The 1687 annexation of the Bijapur Sultanate and the subsequent 1687 annexation of the Golconda Sultanate removed the buffer states that had previously contained the Maratha expansion. The 1689 capture and execution of Sambhaji by Mughal forces did not break the Maratha movement; under Sambhaji's brother Rajaram (r. 1689-1700), under Rajaram's widow Tara Bai (regent 1700-1707), and under the multi-commander Maratha military leadership, the Marathas adopted guerrilla tactics and multi-year delaying-and-attrition warfare across the Deccan that drained the Mughal treasury and the Mughal manpower across the eighteen years.

The structural crisis the Deccan campaign produced was the *jagirdari* crisis. The expanded Mughal nobility under Aurangzeb — including the recruited Deccani Muslim and Maratha nobles that the Mughal expansion had integrated into the mansabdari ranks — required more *jagirs* than the Mughal revenue-and-administrative apparatus could deliver. The excess mansabdars chasing insufficient jagirs produced the crisis that the Mughal economic historian Irfan Habib has documented in *The Agrarian System of Mughal India*. The post-1700 Mughal apparatus could no longer pay its officers from the revenue-base; the officers extracted more aggressively from the peasants and the zamindars; the peasants-and-zamindars revolted; the revenue base contracted; the apparatus extracted more aggressively. The 1707 Mughal collapse began before Aurangzeb's death.

Aurangzeb died on 3 March 1707 at Ahmednagar in the Deccan, aged eighty-eight, after a forty-nine-year reign. His funeral instructions, which he wrote himself in 1706 during the long retreat from the Deccan campaign, instructed that he be buried in an unmarked grave at the Sufi tomb of Sheikh Zainuddin at Khuldabad and that the funeral be funded only from the earnings of the copies of the Quran he had hand-written across his life and from the caps he had sewn. The funeral was conducted as specified. The Mughal Empire he left was, on the record, at its territorial peak — and, on the fiscal record, structurally insolvent.

Twelve weeks after Aurangzeb's death, on 12 June 1707, his eldest surviving son Bahadur Shah I defeated his brother Azam Shah at the Battle of Jajau near Agra; both sides fielded roughly 100,000 combatants per the Britannica account. The subsequent Mughal succession instability — Bahadur Shah I (r. 1707-1712), Jahandar Shah (r. 1712-1713), Farrukhsiyar (r. 1713-1719), Muhammad Shah (r. 1719-1748), Ahmad Shah Bahadur (r. 1748-1754), Alamgir II (r. 1754-1759) — produced the subsequent Maratha-and-Afghan-and-British partitioning of the Mughal political-class apparatus. By 1750, forty years after Aurangzeb's death, the Mughal Emperor's effective authority extended roughly 250 miles around Delhi per the historical analysis. The post-1707 Mughal political-class trajectory — and the parallel rise of the Maratha confederacy, the Awadhi nawabate, the Bengali nawabate, the Hyderabadi nizamate — is the subject of Chapter 3.

## The late Ming collapse and the Manchu Qing conquest 1644

While Aurangzeb consolidated the Mughal apparatus in northern India, the late Ming political class in China collapsed. The Wanli Emperor's 1572-1582 Zhang Juzheng Single Whip reforms (Chapter 1) had initially stabilised the late-Ming fiscal apparatus; the 1582 death of Zhang Juzheng and the post-1582 Wanli withdrawal from administration produced the Ming fiscal-and-administrative decline that ran across the six decades. The Wanli succession crisis, the eunuch-faction dominance of the Tianqi reign (1620-1627), the persecution of the Donglin scholar-faction reformers, and the Chongzhen reign's unsuccessful attempts to restore administrative integrity across 1627-1644 produced a political-class settlement that could not collect sufficient revenue, could not pay its armies, and could not suppress the peasant rebellions the fiscal extraction had ignited.

The peasant rebellions of the 1630s and 1640s — under Li Zicheng in Shaanxi from 1631 and under Zhang Xianzhong in Sichuan — were the agricultural-population response to the Ming tax-extraction-and-famine settlement. In April 1644, Li Zicheng's rebel army captured Beijing. The last Ming emperor, Chongzhen, hanged himself on a tree in the imperial garden outside the Forbidden City. The Ming dynasty, after 276 years, ended.

The political-class apparatus that filled the vacuum was not Han Chinese. It was Manchu. The Manchu political class had been built across the prior half-century by the Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci (1559-1626) and his son Hong Taiji (1592-1643). Nurhaci had created the banner system on an informal basis from 1601 and formally established it with four banners in 1615; Hong Taiji had expanded the system to eight Manchu banners, eight Mongol banners (created 1635), and eight Han-martial (*Hànjūn*) banners (completed 1642). Hong Taiji had renamed the Jurchens "Manchus" and the dynasty "Qing" in 1636. The banner system was the political-and-military-and-social apparatus that integrated the multi-ethnic Manchu political class into the single hereditary military caste.

Hong Taiji died in 1643. His six-year-old son Fulin was chosen by a Manchu princely committee as successor; co-regents Dorgon (Nurhaci's fourteenth son) and Jirgalang were appointed. In 1644, after Li Zicheng's rebel army had captured Beijing, the Ming general Wu Sangui — commanding the border garrison at Shanhai Pass at the eastern end of the Great Wall — opened the pass to the Manchu forces. At the Battle of Shanhai Pass, Dorgon and the bannermen joined Wu Sangui to defeat Li Zicheng; Beijing was secured for the Qing; the six-year-old Fulin was enthroned in Beijing in November 1644 as the Shunzhi Emperor, the first Qing emperor of China proper.

Dorgon's political-class consolidation was institutional. One of his first orders was that the northern part of Beijing be vacated of its Han population and assigned to the bannermen, including the Han bannermen. The Yellow Banners were given the place of honor north of the imperial palace, the White Banners east, the Red Banners west, and the Blue Banners south — mirroring the pre-conquest Manchu homeland geographical arrangement. The 1648 Shunzhi decree allowed Han civilian men to marry Manchu women from the Banners with Board of Revenue or banner-company-captain permission; the late-Qing reversal of intermarriage policy did not erase the early-Qing dyarchy institutional arrangement.

The banner system at the 1644 conquest totalled roughly 168,900 soldiers per the WarHistory.org analysis. The subsequent expansion produced roughly 250,000 bannermen by the late seventeenth century. The banner population was hereditary — banner men and their families lived in walled "Manchu Cities" inside major Chinese cities or in the over 100 banner garrisons established at strategic locations along the Grand Canal, the Yellow and Yangtze Rivers, the coastal regions, and the northeastern and northwestern frontiers. The Manchu City system was the pre-modern world's most systematic experiment in the physical-spatial separation of the conquering political class from the conquered population.

The 1650 consolidation under the Shunzhi Emperor — following Dorgon's death — produced the Three Upper Banners (Bordered Yellow, Plain Yellow, Plain White) under direct imperial control and the Five Lower Banners under Manchu princely command. The Han bannermen filled the majority of governor and governor-general posts under Shunzhi and the subsequent Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661-1722). The post-1644 Manchu-Han dyarchy political-class arrangement — the banner-aristocratic Manchu political class operating the empire alongside the recruited Han bureaucratic political class — was institutionalised across the two and a half centuries until the 1912 fall of the Qing.

The 1650 Guangzhou massacre of Ming-loyalist Han forces and civilians — entirely carried out by Han Bannermen under generals Shang Kexi and Geng Jimao — documented the multi-ethnic character of the Manchu political-class apparatus. The 1673-1681 Revolt of the Three Feudatories under Wu Sangui himself and the southern Han bannermen princes was suppressed under the Kangxi Emperor. The 1683 Qing conquest of Taiwan from the Zheng family resistance completed the territorial consolidation. The subsequent Kangxi-Yongzheng-Qianlong long peace and Qing territorial expansion across the eighteenth century is the subject of Chapter 3.

## The Tokugawa bakuhan settlement from 1603

Across the East Asian sea, the Japanese political class produced a third institutional answer to the seventeenth-century question. After Tokugawa Ieyasu's 21 October 1600 victory at the Battle of Sekigahara over the Western Army under Ishida Mitsunari, Ieyasu had consolidated political authority across Japan. On 24 March 1603, Emperor Go-Yōzei appointed Ieyasu *shōgun*. The Edo bakufu (Tokugawa Shogunate) was founded. The 1615 Siege of Osaka eliminated the Toyotomi line — Toyotomi Hideyori, Hideyoshi's son, was forced to commit *seppuku* — and the Tokugawa hegemony was secured for the 250-year period that the bakufu subsequently administered.

The *bakuhan* (bakufu-han) system was the governing architecture of the Tokugawa political class. The central Tokugawa government in Edo coexisted with roughly 270 daimyo-administered *han* domains. The daimyo were categorised into three classes: the *shinpan* (Tokugawa relatives), the *fudai* (loyal pre-Sekigahara retainers), and the *tozama* (outer lords who submitted only after Sekigahara). The shinpan and fudai daimyo were preferentially assigned to strategic domains near Edo and the other Tokugawa power centres; the tozama daimyo were preferentially assigned to peripheral domains (Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa, Saga, Hizen) where they could be watched but could not easily coordinate among themselves.

The *Buke Shohatto* (Laws for the Military Houses), first promulgated under Ieyasu in 1615 and reiterated under the third shogun Iemitsu (r. 1623-1651) in 1635, codified the daimyo-and-samurai conduct-and-obligation framework. The 1635 Iemitsu revision institutionalised the *sankin kōtai* (alternate attendance) system: daimyo were required to alternate yearly residence between their han and Edo; daimyo wives and heirs were permanently resident in Edo as hostages. The fiscal pressure on the daimyo from the dual-residence requirement — including the costs of elaborate processional travel between the han and Edo, the maintenance of two administrative establishments, and the fixed yearly contributions to bakufu public-works projects — was designed to limit the daimyo capacity to fund independent military operations. The arrangement worked. From the 1615 Siege of Osaka until the 1651 Keian Uprising — and in practice until the 1853 Perry Expedition forced the reopening of Japan — the Tokugawa bakuhan settlement maintained the internal-peace settlement that subsequent historians have called the *Pax Tokugawa*.

The Tokugawa direct domains exceeded seven million *koku* (a koku being the annual rice-production unit equivalent to roughly one person's annual rice consumption) — roughly one-fourth of the country's total agricultural production per the Britannica analysis. Over four million koku were under direct Tokugawa control; three million were distributed among the *hatamoto* and *gokenin* — the liege vassals of the bakufu. The remaining three-fourths of the country's agricultural production was distributed across the 270 daimyo-administered han.

The 1633-1639 *sakoku* (closed-country) policy expelled the Christian missionaries, suppressed Japanese Christianity following the 1637-1638 Shimabara Rebellion of Catholic peasants on Kyushu, and restricted international trade. The Dutch VOC was retained as the sole European trading partner at the artificial-island Dejima factory in Nagasaki harbour. The Chinese trade continued at Nagasaki on the Tōjin-yashiki Chinese-quarter basis. The Korean trade was conducted via the Tsushima domain and the Pusan *Waegwan* Japanese-quarter basis. The Ainu trade was conducted via the Matsumae domain. The Ryūkyū kingdom was administered as a tributary of the Satsuma domain. The 250-year *sakoku* settlement was not the absolute isolation that the nineteenth-century European-and-American historiography characterised it as — it was a carefully regulated four-route external-contact policy that the Tokugawa political class operated to maintain its internal authority. The arrangement was structurally analogous to the post-1683 Qing *haijin* sea-ban and to the post-1592 Joseon Korean closure of most foreign contact.

The Tokugawa political class was a hereditary military caste — the *samurai* class — that across the 250-year *Pax Tokugawa* increasingly operated as a bureaucratic-and-administrative caste rather than as a fighting force. The *bushidō* code that the nineteenth-century writers institutionalised as the characteristic samurai ideology was, in its actual seventeenth-century-and-eighteenth-century institutional form, the administrative-and-civil-service code of a class that no longer fought wars. The late Tokugawa samurai 1850s-1860s crisis — in which the arrival of European-and-American gunboat pressure exposed the administrative samurai incapacity to defend the bakufu militarily — is the subject of Chapter 4.

## The Joseon political class — post-Imjin recovery and the Manchu invasions

The Korean Joseon political class entered the seventeenth century under the burden of the 1592-1598 Imjin War (Chapter 1). The recovery period across the decades produced the 1623 Injo Coup that deposed the Gwanghaegun (r. 1608-1623) and installed Injo (r. 1623-1649). The Injo regime aligned itself with the declining Ming against the rising Manchu Qing — a political-class judgement that produced the 1627 First Manchu Invasion of Korea (under Hong Taiji's pre-Qing forces) and the 1636-1637 Second Manchu Invasion.

The Second Manchu Invasion was triggered by the Joseon refusal to recognise Hong Taiji's 1636 dynastic founding of the Qing. Hong Taiji's forces invaded; Injo retreated to the mountain fortress Namhansanseong south of Hanyang; the fortress was besieged across the winter of 1636-1637. On 30 January 1637 at Samjeondo, Injo formally surrendered to Hong Taiji and accepted Joseon's tributary status under the Qing. The subsequent 1637 peace settlement bound the Joseon political class to the Qing for the remainder of the Qing period.

The post-1637 Joseon political-class settlement consolidated under the Confucian-Neoconfucian *yangban* hereditary scholar-official class (Chapter 1). The factional politics across the two centuries — Westerner-Southerner-Northerner-Old-Young factional divisions, the literati purges, the succession disputes — operated within the settled institutional framework that the post-1392 *yangban-gwageo* apparatus had built. The cultural production of the late Joseon period — including the post-1750 *silhak* (practical learning) intellectual movement under figures including Jeong Yak-yong — proceeded under the tributary-Qing settlement. The subsequent nineteenth-century Joseon-isolation policy and the late-nineteenth-century Japanese intervention is the subject of Chapter 4.

## The Ottoman political class — Köprülü reforms and the 1683 Vienna defeat

The Ottoman political class entered the seventeenth century under Süleyman I's 1566 successors — Selim II (r. 1566-1574), Murad III (r. 1574-1595), Mehmed III (r. 1595-1603). The 1603-1617 Ahmed I reign produced the abolition of the fratricide succession practice — the Mehmed-II *Kanunname* of 1451 had authorised new sultans to execute their brothers to prevent civil war; Ahmed replaced the practice with the *kafes* (cage) confinement system under which royal princes were kept in palace seclusion until they were either summoned to succession or died.

The 1622 deposition and assassination of Osman II by Janissary revolt was the first Ottoman regicide. The Janissary corps — the recruited-and-converted Christian-peasant infantry caste that Chapter 1 documented at the peak Süleyman period — emerged across the seventeenth century as the decisive internal-political force. The *valide sultan* (queen-mother) regencies — Kösem Sultan (regent 1623-1651) and Turhan Hatice Sultan (regent 1651-1683) — operated through the Janissary-and-harem-political-class alliance that the subsequent "Sultanate of Women" historiography has documented.

The 1656-1683 Köprülü era — under Köprülü Mehmed Pasha (grand vizier 1656-1661) and the subsequent Köprülü family viziers (Köprülü Fazıl Ahmed Pasha, vizier 1661-1676; Merzifonlu Kara Mustafa Pasha, vizier 1676-1683) — produced the last structural reform of the Ottoman apparatus before its multi-century decline. The Köprülü viziers suppressed corruption in the provincial administration, restored fiscal discipline, and reasserted central authority over the provincial pashas and the Janissary corps.

The 1683 Battle of Vienna — under Kara Mustafa Pasha — was the decisive end of the Köprülü reform period and of the pre-1683 Ottoman expansion. Ottoman forces besieged Vienna for two months; a Christian relief army under the Polish king Jan III Sobieski defeated the Ottomans on 12 September 1683. Kara Mustafa was executed by Sultan Mehmed IV's order on 25 December 1683. The subsequent 1683-1699 Great Turkish War ended with the 1699 Treaty of Karlowitz, in which the Ottomans ceded Hungary, Transylvania, and Slavonia to Habsburg Austria — the first major territorial loss to European powers. The post-1683 Ottoman political-class trajectory was the multi-century retreat that produced the eighteenth-century *malikâne* life-tenure tax-farming privatisation, the nineteenth-century Tanzimat reforms, and the 1922 collapse of the Ottoman state — the subject of Chapter 4.

## Safavid Persia — Abbas II and the seventeenth-century continuation

The Safavid Persian political class continued the Abbas-I *ghulam-and-silk-monopoly* apparatus (Chapter 1) under Abbas II (r. 1642-1666), Suleiman I (r. 1666-1694), and Sultan Husayn (r. 1694-1722). The Abbas II reign produced the peak of the post-Abbas-I Safavid state — continued Isfahan urban development, continued silk-monopoly revenue, continued diplomatic engagement with the European trading companies. The post-1666 Safavid decline under weak successors produced the 1722 Ghilzai Afghan invasion under Mahmud Hotak; Isfahan fell after a seven-month siege; the Safavid dynasty collapsed. The post-1722 Iranian political-class trajectory — Nader Shah (r. 1736-1747), the Zand interregnum, the Qajar establishment from 1789 — is the subject of Chapter 3.

## The Songhai collapse 1591 and the West African seventeenth-century reorganisation

The Songhai Empire (Chapter 1) collapsed at the 1591 Battle of Tondibi under Moroccan Saadian invasion by Judar Pasha. The post-1591 West African political-class reorganisation across the seventeenth century produced the smaller-scale political-class formations: the Bambara kingdoms of Ségou and Kaarta in the interior, the Mossi kingdoms of Burkina, the Wolof and Mandinka senatorial classes of the Senegambian coast, the Kingdom of Asante consolidating under Osei Tutu from the 1670s in the Gold Coast hinterland, the Kingdom of Dahomey under Houegbadja from the 1620s in the Bight of Benin, the Yoruba states centred on Oyo. The West African political classes of the seventeenth century operated within the commercial-and-political circuits that the expanding Atlantic slave trade had reorganised — the West African coastal-state political classes was the supply-side political class of the Atlantic slave-trade apparatus that the panels of this chapter document.

## The Spanish American viceregal political class and Potosí silver mining

The Spanish American political class that the post-1521 Cortés conquest of central Mexico and the post-1532 Pizarro conquest of Andean Peru had installed across the post-conquest period operated by the seventeenth century through the Madrid-based Council of the Indies (Consejo de Indias, founded 1524) and the viceregal apparatus on the ground. The Viceroyalty of New Spain was established 1535 under Antonio de Mendoza; the Viceroyalty of Peru was established 1542 under Blasco Núñez Vela. Each viceroyalty was administered through a network of *audiencias* (regional high courts) and subordinate *corregidor* and *alcalde mayor* district administrators. The *encomienda* system granted Spanish *encomenderos* the right to indigenous-population labour and tribute in exchange for nominal Christianisation. The 1542 New Laws under the Las Casas advocacy attempted to abolish the encomienda; the partial reversal under settler pressure left the encomienda functioning across the century in modified form before the eighteenth-century Bourbon Reforms further restructured the arrangement.

The structural pillar of the Spanish American extraction apparatus across the seventeenth century was the Potosí silver mining complex. Silver was discovered at the Cerro Rico (Rich Hill) of Potosí (in modern Bolivia) in 1545. The first boom from 1545 to roughly 1560 was based on the surface ores refined with the indigenous *guayra* smelting ovens. The 1571 Toledo Reforms under Viceroy Francisco de Toledo (1515-1582) restructured the Potosí operations on the basis of the mercury amalgamation process (the *patio* process), which required mercury from the Huancavelica mine in central Peru and which allowed the deeper-and-lower-grade ores to be processed.

The 1573 colonial *mita* labour-draft system — instituted by Viceroy Toledo and named after the Inca *mit'a* (Chapter 1) that the Spanish had repurposed — conscripted indigenous men aged 18-50 from 16 designated provinces around Lake Titicaca to Potosí. Up to 14,000 conscripts per year were drafted; the service obligation was one year in seven for the eligible male population of the designated provinces. Labour conditions were coercive; mortality from mercury poisoning, silicosis, altitude exposure, and exhaustion was high. The subsequent demographic contraction across the contributing provinces — driven by the mita-and-disease-and-famine combined effect — produced a gradual fall in Potosí output after 1700 that the subsequent Bourbon Reforms attempted to reverse.

The cumulative 1545-1825 Spanish American silver production has been estimated at roughly 40,000 tonnes of silver per Earl Hamilton's 1934 *American Treasure and the Price Revolution in Spain* and the research. The 80% of the global silver supply across the period flowed through Spanish American mines, of which Potosí was the largest single source until New Spain's eighteenth-century overtaking. The royal *quinto* (one-fifth tax) on silver production funded the Habsburg-and-subsequent-Bourbon Madrid political class. The subsequent 1736 reduction of the quinto to one-tenth stimulated Potosí output growth across the decades.

The silver flowed back to Spain via the treasure-fleet (*flota*) system — annual fleets sailing from Portobelo (on the Caribbean side of Panama, receiving the Potosí silver via the Pacific-to-Panama overland route) and Veracruz (receiving the New Spain silver) to Seville and Cadiz. The parallel Manila Galleon trade from Acapulco to Manila, operating annually from 1565, carried Spanish American silver to Ming and subsequently Qing China in exchange for Chinese porcelain, silk, and other manufactured goods. The Spanish American silver-and-Chinese-manufactures circulation was the pre-1700 world's most systematic transoceanic-commercial-exchange apparatus. The Ming dependency on imported silver (Chapter 1) was the demand-side base for the Spanish American silver-and-mita extraction apparatus. The arrangement is the pre-1700 documentary case of the multi-region political-class integration that the post-1700 European chartered-trading-company apparatus subsequently extended.

## The Atlantic slave trade as political-class system

The Atlantic slave trade across the seventeenth century operated as a political-class apparatus integrating the Portuguese, Dutch, English, French, and subsequent Spanish-asiento-contracted political classes on the European side, and the multiple West African coastal-state political classes on the African side. The 1444 first Portuguese captives delivered to Lagos under Henry the Navigator's expeditions and the 1481 Portuguese establishment of Elmina Castle on the Gold Coast had established the Portuguese supply chain by the turn of the sixteenth century. The 1518 first direct trans-Atlantic shipment of enslaved Africans to the Spanish American territories opened the post-1518 multi-century trans-Atlantic flow.

The Spanish *asiento* system — royal-monopoly contracts granted to various European trading nations to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish American territories — operated as the fiscal-and-administrative integration mechanism between the Spanish Habsburg political class (which had reserved the trans-Atlantic supply right to itself in the 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas) and the actual operators (Portuguese 1595-1640, Dutch West India Company 1662-1675, Portuguese again, French Compagnie de Guinée 1701-1713, British South Sea Company 1713-1750). The asiento was a chartered monopoly. The Spanish political class did not operate the trade directly. The arrangement was structurally analogous to the Mughal *jagir* and to the Roman *publicani* tax-farming — a royal-monopoly right delegated to a private-commercial syndicate that recovered the cost-and-profit from the population the right was exercised against.

The Dutch West India Company (WIC), chartered 1621, remained the single largest seventeenth-century trans-Atlantic slave trader after the 1630-1654 Dutch capture of northern Brazil (Dutch Brazil) and the 1637 capture of Elmina from the Portuguese. The WIC operations since the post-1654 loss of Brazil continued through Curaçao and the other Caribbean bases. The English Royal African Company, chartered 1672 under Stuart-royal patent with the Duke of York (later James II) as major shareholder, operated the English trans-Atlantic supply through the late seventeenth century before its 1698 monopoly was broken by the pressure of independent English-and-British interlopers. The French Compagnie des Indes occidentales (chartered 1664) and its subsequent successor companies operated the French Saint-Domingue (modern Haiti) plantation-and-slave-trade economy that produced, by the late eighteenth century, the most lucrative single colony in the Caribbean.

The cumulative volume of the trans-Atlantic slave trade across the 1500s-1800s is roughly 12.5 million enslaved Africans transported from Africa, of whom roughly 10.7 million survived the Middle Passage and arrived in the Americas — the 1.8 million intermediate death-in-transit is the cumulative shipboard mortality across the multi-century operation per the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database compiled by David Eltis and the Emory University consortium. The seventeenth century alone accounted for roughly 1.9 million of those 12.5 million per the same database.

The African coastal-state political classes operated the supply side of the trans-Atlantic slave trade. The Kingdom of Kongo across the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries — initially Catholic-aligned after the 1491 conversion of King Nzinga a Nkuwu and the integration with the Portuguese commercial-and-religious apparatus — supplied captives via the Mpinda port. The Kingdom of Dahomey under Houegbadja and his successors operated from the 1620s onward as the single largest West African supply-side political-class system on the Bight of Benin "Slave Coast." The Kingdom of Asante, consolidating from the 1670s under Osei Tutu, operated the Gold Coast hinterland supply. The Aro Confederacy in the Igbo hinterland operated the Bight of Biafra supply. The Yoruba states of Oyo and the successor states operated the central-Slave-Coast supply.

The political-class character of the Atlantic slave-trade apparatus is the evidence. The European chartered companies did not capture the enslaved Africans themselves. The captives were captured, transported to the coastal forts, and exchanged for imported goods (firearms, textiles, alcohol, manillas, cowries) by the West African coastal-state political classes operating on their own institutional logic. The arrangement was not a unilateral European extraction operation. It was a extraction operation in which the European political classes operated the trans-Atlantic shipping-and-plantation-and-asiento apparatus, in which the West African coastal-state political classes operated the continental-interior capture-and-transport apparatus, and in which the population that bore the cost was the West African agricultural population — and, on the plantation-and-mining destinations, the indigenous American and African agricultural-and-mining population alike.

The arrangement reorganised the West African political class on the basis of captive-supply revenue. The political-class formations that did not participate in the trade — including the interior states resisting coastal political-class expansion, the maroon-and-marronage communities that escaped from the plantation system — were structurally smaller and poorer than the participating political classes. The multi-century reorganisation of the West African political-class settlement on the basis of the trans-Atlantic trade is the pre-colonial demographic-and-political-class fact that the post-1880 European colonial scramble (Chapter 4) extended in modified institutional form.

## The Dutch regenten and the chartering of the VOC

The Dutch Republic was a federal confederation of seven provinces that had revolted against the Habsburg empire across the long Eighty Years' War (1568-1648). The political class that governed it was not a court. It was an oligarchy of urban patrician families — the regenten — who held the city councils, the provincial deputations to the States General, and the financial offices of the major Dutch cities. Amsterdam was first among them, with Leiden, Haarlem, Delft, Rotterdam, and Dordrecht following. The regenten were merchants. They had not given up being merchants when they entered public office. They sat on city councils, on provincial deputations, on the directorate of the Wisselbank (the Amsterdam exchange bank founded 1609 to serve as the proto-central-bank of Dutch commerce), and, after 1602, on the Heeren XVII — the seventeen-man governing board of the Dutch East India Company, which the regenten had chartered and which they continued to operate.

The chartering of the VOC on 20 March 1602 was the work of Johan van Oldenbarnevelt, Land's Advocate of the States of Holland, who had dominated Dutch politics since 1586 and who would dominate it for sixteen more years. Oldenbarnevelt's biography is the type of the seventeenth-century regenten political-class operator. He was born in 1547 in Amersfoort, into a family of minor nobility; he trained in law at Louvain, Bourges, Cologne, Heidelberg, and probably Padua, returning to practise law in The Hague in 1569. By his late thirties he was Land's Advocate, the chief legal-political officer of the Province of Holland — the position in the Republic's most populous and wealthy province, and the position from which Oldenbarnevelt was the *de facto* prime minister of the Republic for three decades. He had been the political architect of the truce-and-treaty negotiations that had carried the Republic through the period of the Spanish wars. He had been the political architect of the consolidation of six smaller Dutch trading companies into the single chartered VOC in 1602. He had been the political architect of the 1609 Twelve Years' Truce that had ended the Spanish wars long enough for the Dutch trading empire to consolidate. He had run a government, on behalf of a regenten political class for whom government and trade were the same activity, for thirty years.

In August 1618, the Stadtholder Maurice of Orange — Oldenbarnevelt's political rival, the commander of the Republic's standing army, and the institutional figurehead of the Counter-Remonstrant party in the religious-and-political conflict that had split the Dutch political class across the previous decade — arrested him. The charge was treason. The actual offence was a political-and-religious-and-constitutional dispute about provincial autonomy that had been running between Oldenbarnevelt's Holland faction and Maurice's army faction since the Sharp Resolution of August 1617, in which Oldenbarnevelt had empowered the towns of Holland to raise their own troops independently of the army Maurice commanded. Arrested with Oldenbarnevelt were four of his closest political associates: Hugo Grotius, the jurist (and Oldenbarnevelt's successor as Pensionary of Rotterdam), Gilles van Ledenberg, Rombout Hogerbeets, and Jacob Dircksz de Graeff. The trial was conducted by a special court of the States General whose jurisdiction over a servant of the States of Holland Oldenbarnevelt denied to the end. The trial dragged for months. The verdict was death.

On the morning of 13 May 1619, the seventy-one-year-old Oldenbarnevelt was led to a scaffold in the Binnenhof in The Hague, before the Great Hall now known as the Ridderzaal, in front of a crowd of thousands. His last words, to a servant who had come to bid him farewell, were "Make it short, make it short." He was beheaded. Maurice had refused to grant a pardon unless the family asked for one, which would have meant admitting guilt; the family refused. Oldenbarnevelt's son Reinier was beheaded four years later for plotting to assassinate Maurice in revenge.

The Oldenbarnevelt execution is the foundational fact of the seventeenth-century European political class as a category that this book takes seriously. The political class is not a single faction. It is not a unified actor. It contains factions that will turn on one another with capital prosecutions when the stakes are sufficient. The Dutch political class in 1619 split between the regenten merchant faction Oldenbarnevelt led and the Orangist military-Calvinist faction Maurice led, and the merchant faction lost. The Republic did not stop being run by regenten — it could not, the regenten were the urban political class that filled every elected and appointed office below the Stadtholderate — but the period from 1619 to Maurice's death in 1625 saw the Orangist faction consolidate. After Maurice's death the regenten consolidated again, and the seventeenth-century Dutch Golden Age — the VOC's expansion, the Amsterdam Wisselbank's emergence as the European reserve banking institution, the Dutch capture of Brazil and the Cape and Ceylon and the East Indies trade — proceeded under regenten governance.

The pattern was straightforward and the documentation is comprehensive. The Amsterdam families — the Trips, the Bickers, the De Geers, the Pauws, the Witsens — held seats simultaneously on the Amsterdam city council, on the directorate of the VOC, on the Wisselbank's governing board, and on the deputation to the States General. There was no separation of the public role from the commercial role. The arrangement was understood as the constitution of the Republic. The merchants ran the Republic for the same reason the Republic existed: because the Republic was, by its own foundational political logic, the political instrument of a commercial class that had successfully refused Habsburg sovereignty over its commerce. The seventeenth-century Dutch political class is the cleanest documented case in European history of the arrangement this book documents in its modern form. The regenten did not need to pretend that they were not the commercial class. The constitution of the Republic acknowledged that they were.

The Dutch Republic was, by 1700, exhausted. The Anglo-Dutch wars of 1652-1674 had drained Dutch finances. The 1672 French invasion under Louis XIV — the *rampjaar*, the disaster year, during which the regenten Grand Pensionary Johan de Witt and his brother Cornelis were lynched by an Orangist mob in The Hague on 20 August 1672 — had broken the constitutional balance the regenten had operated under, restoring the Orangist Stadtholderate under William III. The Dutch chapter of the seventeenth-century political class ended with the political-class operator who would shortly become King of England, taking the Dutch institutional apparatus with him across the North Sea.

## The English political class — Civil War, Glorious Revolution, Bank of England 1694

The English political class across the seventeenth century moved through three institutional inflection points: the 1603 Stuart succession, the 1642-1649 Civil War, and the 1688-1694 Glorious-Revolution-to-Bank-of-England settlement. Each was the product of the political-class conflict between the factions the English settlement contained — the Crown-and-Court faction, the parliamentary-Whig faction that organised around the chartered-trading interests, the parliamentary-Tory faction that organised around the landed-aristocratic interests, and the religious-and-constitutional factions that cross-cut the other three.

The 1603 Stuart succession brought James VI of Scotland to the English throne as James I (r. 1603-1625). The subsequent Stuart conflict with Parliament over taxation-and-prerogative across the 1620s and 1630s under Charles I (r. 1625-1649) produced the 1642-1649 English Civil War. The Parliamentary forces under Oliver Cromwell defeated the Royalist forces; on 30 January 1649, Charles I was executed at Whitehall — the first formal trial-and-execution of a reigning European monarch by his own political class. The 1649-1660 Commonwealth and Protectorate under Cromwell extended the parliamentary-faction control until Cromwell's 1658 death and the subsequent 1660 Restoration of Charles II.

The 1660-1688 Restoration period under Charles II (r. 1660-1685) and James II (r. 1685-1688) produced the chartering of the major English colonial-and-commercial companies including the Royal African Company (1672, chartered with the Duke of York — later James II — as major shareholder). The 1688 Glorious Revolution — a parliamentary-Whig invitation to William III (Dutch Stadtholder and husband of Mary Stuart, James II's daughter) to invade England with a Dutch army — produced the deposition of James II and the 1689 Bill of Rights constitutional settlement. William III and Mary II were declared joint monarchs by Parliament; the settlement institutionalised parliamentary sovereignty over royal prerogative on taxation, the maintenance of standing armies, and the administration of justice.

The 1694 chartering of the Bank of England was the structural institutional product of the 1688-1689 settlement. William III's 1689-onward war against Louis XIV (the Nine Years' War, 1688-1697) required continued parliamentary funding. The 1694 Tonnage Act authorised the incorporation of a subscriber-funded joint-stock company — the Bank of England — that would loan the English government £1.2 million at 8% interest, against a perpetual annuity funded from parliamentary taxation. The 1,268 original subscribers included the major London merchants, the major Whig political-class figures, and a small but significant block of the recently-immigrated Huguenot mercantile community (1685 Edict of Fontainebleau refugees from France). The founding charter was based on the 1694 proposal by William Paterson — a Scottish merchant who had drafted the original scheme in 1691 and circulated it through the London commercial-and-political community.

The 1694 chartering of the Bank of England was the most consequential institutional innovation of the seventeenth-century European political class. The arrangement converted royal-government debt — the personal obligation of the reigning monarch that the preceding medieval-banking-house apparatus (Chapter 1) had collected through the Bardi-Peruzzi-Medici-Fugger arrangement — into perpetual-annuity national debt, structurally guaranteed by parliamentary taxation and tradeable in the London capital markets. The 1697 Bank of England Act extended the charter and granted the Bank a monopoly on joint-stock banking in England. The post-1694 English-and-British political class operated on the basis of the Bank-of-England-and-parliamentary-taxation apparatus that this volume's prior treatment of the post-1694 mechanism — as the first half of the four-century debt-money machine — has documented. The parliamentary sovereignty established by the Glorious Revolution was the political-class apparatus through which the Bank of England and the London capital markets operated. The arrangement was indistinguishable from the Dutch regenten arrangement in its structural logic. The English political class chartered the banking apparatus and operated through it. The 1689 settlement made the arrangement constitutional.

## The French political class — Louis XIV and Colbert

Across the Channel, the French political class produced the third major seventeenth-century European institutional form: the centralised absolutist state under Louis XIV (r. 1643-1715) and his Controller-General of Finances Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619-1683). The 1610-1643 reign of Louis XIII — under the 1624-1642 chief ministership of Cardinal Richelieu — had begun the centralisation of royal authority over the provincial nobility and the Huguenot political-religious enclaves. The 1643-1661 minority of Louis XIV under the chief ministership of Cardinal Mazarin had extended the centralisation across the 1648-1653 Fronde rebellions of the parlements and the princes-of-the-blood. The 1661 personal-rule consolidation of Louis XIV upon Mazarin's death produced the pure form of the absolutist French political class.

Colbert as Controller-General from 1665 to 1683 built the mercantilist French fiscal apparatus. The 1664 chartering of the Compagnie des Indes orientales and the Compagnie des Indes occidentales — the French parallels to the Dutch VOC and WIC — was the chartered-trading-company side of the Colbertist programme. The establishment of the royal manufactures, the protective tariff system, the naval-and-shipbuilding expansion under Colbert was the protectionist-industrial side. The 1685 Edict of Fontainebleau revoked the 1598 Edict of Nantes and expelled the roughly 200,000 Huguenot population to the Netherlands, England, Prussia, and North America. The 1685 revocation was a structural political-class error — the expelled Huguenot mercantile community subsequently contributed to the 1694 Bank of England subscriber base and to the Dutch-and-Prussian commercial expansion — and the decline of French commercial-political competitiveness across the eighteenth century has been partially attributed to it by the historiography (Charles Tilly, *Coercion, Capital, and European States*, 1990).

The late-Louis-XIV period — the 1701-1714 War of the Spanish Succession, the bankrupting fiscal pressure that the war produced, the subsequent 1716-1720 Mississippi Bubble and Banque Royale collapse under the Scottish economist John Law — is the subject of Chapter 3.

## The Romanov consolidation under Peter the Great

The Romanov Russian political class consolidated across the seventeenth century from the 1613 election of Mikhail Romanov as first Romanov tsar by the *zemsky sobor* (the assembly of land), through the 1645-1676 reign of Alexis I, and into the 1682-1725 reign of Peter I "the Great." The 1649 *Sobornoye Ulozheniye* (Council Code) under Alexis I codified serfdom as the hereditary status binding peasants to the land — the Russian parallel to the multi-century consolidation of unfree-labour systems across the other regions the chapter documents. The 1654 Russian-Polish War over Ukraine and the 1667 Treaty of Andrusovo gave Russia eastern Ukraine including Kiev — the territorial expansion that the subsequent Romanov period would extend across the two and a half centuries.

Peter I's 1697-1698 Grand Embassy to Western Europe — during which Peter worked incognito as a shipwright at the Dutch and English yards — was the unprecedented reigning-monarch direct-engagement with the northwestern European political-class apparatus. Peter's post-1698 reforms imported the Dutch-and-English-and-Swedish institutional models into the Russian political-class apparatus. The 1700-1721 Great Northern War against Sweden under Charles XII — including the 1709 Russian victory at Poltava — produced the 1721 Treaty of Nystad that gave Russia Estonia, Livonia, and Ingria, and the Russian access to the Baltic. The 1703 founding of Saint Petersburg as the new capital was the physical-and-institutional symbol of the post-1698 Petrine reorientation toward Northwestern Europe.

The 1722 Table of Ranks under Peter I established a 14-rank hierarchy for military, civil, and court service in which rank rather than birth determined official position — the eighth rank conferred hereditary nobility status. The Russian Table of Ranks was the northern-European parallel to the Mughal *mansabdari* (Chapter 1), the Ottoman *devşirme*-and-*timar*, and the Chinese imperial-examination system. The political class was structured as a hierarchical service-and-rank apparatus that the eighteenth-and-nineteenth-century Russian state operated on the basis of. The subsequent post-Peter Russian political-class trajectory — Catherine the Great's 1762-1796 consolidation, the nineteenth-century Romanov continuation, the 1917 collapse — is the subject of subsequent chapters.

## The continuity

The seventeenth-century panels above are seven major regional political-class restructurings — Mughal under Aurangzeb, late Ming collapse and Qing conquest, Tokugawa bakuhan, Joseon post-Imjin recovery, Ottoman Köprülü-to-Vienna, Safavid Abbas II continuation, post-Songhai West African reorganisation — and six northwestern European-and-American restructurings: Spanish American viceregal-and-Potosí, the Atlantic slave trade, Dutch regenten-and-VOC, English Civil-War-and-Glorious-Revolution-and-Bank-of-England, French Colbertist absolutism, Romanov Petrine consolidation. The thirteen are not a comprehensive accounting of every seventeenth-century political-class settlement on earth; the chapter has omitted the Ethiopian Gondarine period (Fasilides r. 1632-1667 onward), the Tibetan Ganden Phodrang under the Fifth Dalai Lama (r. 1642-1682), the Burmese Toungoo and Konbaung dynasties, the multiple smaller Asian-and-African-and-American political-class formations of the period. The thirteen panels cover the largest political-class apparatuses of the century by demographic-and-fiscal weight.

The thirteen panels share an institutional logic. Each political class operated an extraction apparatus on a population that produced agricultural-and-commercial value. Each apparatus was operated by a recruited or hereditary or commercial administrative caste that the chapter has documented by name where the evidence permits. Each apparatus operated on behalf of a superior creditor-or-monarch-or-commercial-investor class whose interests the political class served. Each apparatus extracted in proportion to the administrative-and-coercive capacity it could project. Each apparatus subsequently entered its own decline — some across the seventeenth century itself (the Ming, the Songhai, the pre-Köprülü Ottoman); some across the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries that the chapters document. The political class did not change in role across the thirteen panels. The instruments changed. The subsequent chapters document the changes.

The mask of the seventeenth century was many masks. The Mughal mask was the *padshah*-and-mansabdar Islamic-Hindu syncretic court hierarchy. The Qing mask was the multi-ethnic Manchu-Mongol-Han banner-and-dyarchy bureaucratic settlement. The Tokugawa mask was the shogunal-and-imperial bakuhan-and-sankin-kōtai bureaucratic-feudal arrangement. The Ottoman mask was the Sultan-and-Janissary-and-Köprülü-vizier tripartite settlement. The Spanish American mask was the viceregal-and-encomienda-and-mita Catholic-and-tributary hierarchy. The Dutch mask was the republican-mercantile chartered-corporation regenten apparatus. The English mask was the parliamentary-and-Crown-and-Bank-of-England constitutional settlement. The French mask was the absolute-monarchy mercantilist Colbertist administration. The Russian mask was the Petrine-rank-and-Table-of-Ranks autocratic-service hierarchy. The West African coastal-state mask was the tribute-and-captive-supply chiefly authority. The multiple masks of the Atlantic slave-trade apparatus were the individual chartered companies on the European side and the individual coastal-state political classes on the African side.

The role behind each mask was the same. The chapters that follow document what the masks of the eighteenth, nineteenth, twentieth, and twenty-first centuries put on top of the same role.

---


---

# Chapter 3: The Political Class of the Eighteenth Century

The eighteenth century is where the restructurings that Chapter 2 documented across every region of the world ran into their first sustained tests. The Mughal apparatus that had reached its territorial peak under Aurangzeb collapsed across the subcontinent's transition into the successor-state period of the Marathas, the Awadhi nawabate, the Bengali nawabate, the Hyderabadi nizamate, the Mysorean state under Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan, and the consolidating Sikh confederacies — and, by the end of the century, into a settlement under the East India Company that had begun as a chartered trading post and was now, by the Treaty of Allahabad of 1765, the lawful revenue-collecting authority over Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa. The Qing apparatus that the Manchu political class had installed in 1644 reached its High Qing peak under Kangxi, Yongzheng, and Qianlong — the longest three-emperor continuity in Chinese imperial history, 134 years of consolidated political-class operation — and then, by the 1790s, began to enter the structural decline that produced the corruption scandals of the late Qianlong period and the structural crisis of the Macartney rejection of 1793. The Tokugawa bakuhan settlement that had institutionalised the *Pax Tokugawa* (Chapter 2) cycled through three reform-and-reaction inflections: the Kyōhō Reforms under the eighth shogun Yoshimune from 1716, the Tanuma Era of commercial-development experiments under Tanuma Okitsugu from 1772 to 1786, and the reactionary Kansei Reforms under Matsudaira Sadanobu from 1787 to 1793. The Ottoman political class entered the century in the Tulip Era of Ahmed III, was interrupted by the 1730 Patrona Halil revolt, and ended the century under Selim III's *Nizam-i Cedid* "New Order" reform programme that the Janissary revolt of 1807 would destroy. The Safavid space in Iran collapsed in 1722; the Nader Shah Afsharid interregnum and the Karim Khan Zand interregnum filled the eighteenth century until the Qajar dynasty was founded by Agha Mohammad Khan in 1789. The Wahhabi-Saud religious-and-political pact of 1744 founded the First Saudi State at Diriyah. The Asante Empire on the Gold Coast hinterland reached its eighteenth-century peak under Opoku Ware I, Osei Kwadwo, and Osei Kwame. The Bourbon Reforms in Spanish America restructured the viceregal apparatus and produced the 1780-1783 Tupac Amaru II rebellion and the 1781 Comuneros rebellion. And in northwestern Europe and across the Atlantic, the British political class under Walpole's Robinocracy and the post-Walpole Pitt-the-Elder-and-Pitt-the-Younger Whig settlement, the French political class under Louis XV and Louis XVI through the 1716-1720 Mississippi Bubble under John Law and the 1789-1799 Revolution, and the American political class under Hamilton's import of the Bank-of-England architecture into the new republic produced the three Atlantic chartered-financial-political settlements that the nineteenth century would project outward into the world.

The thirteen panels of Chapter 2 had each operated through a different institutional answer to the same political-class question. The eighteenth-century panels operated through institutional adjustments to those answers under the structural pressures the eighteenth century produced: demographic expansion across every region, military-and-fiscal escalation under intensified inter-state competition, and the commercial-and-extractive penetration of the chartered-trading-company apparatus into the agricultural-and-extraction territories of the non-European regions. The political class did not change in role across the century. The institutional instruments adjusted. The chapter that follows documents the long-nineteenth-century continuation.

## The High Qing political class — Kangxi, Yongzheng, Qianlong

The Kangxi Emperor (Xuanye, born 4 May 1654, reigned 1662-1722) was the longest-reigning emperor in Chinese imperial history at sixty-one years, and the institutional architect of the High Qing political-class settlement. Kangxi had ascended the throne at age eight under the regency of four Manchu princes; he achieved independence from the remaining regent Oboi in 1669 at age fifteen by personally arranging Oboi's arrest in a palace audience. The subsequent fifty-three years of personal rule produced the governing consolidation that Yongzheng and Qianlong extended.

Kangxi's consolidation operated on four institutional axes. First, the suppression of the 1673-1681 Revolt of the Three Feudatories under Wu Sangui (Chapter 2) and the 1683 conquest of Taiwan from the Zheng family resistance completed the territorial consolidation of Qing rule across the formerly Ming territories. Second, the 1689 Treaty of Nerchinsk with the Russian Tsardom — the first formal treaty between China and a European power — established the Qing-Russia border that kept the continental peace between the two land empires for nearly two centuries. The treaty was negotiated in Latin through Jesuit intermediaries (Jean-François Gerbillon and Thomas Pereira); the Qing political class operated with the Latinate-Jesuit administrative infrastructure that Kangxi's reign had institutionalised at the court. Third, the 1696-1697 Dzungar campaign defeated Galdan Boshugtu Khan of the Dzungar Mongols; Outer Mongolia was incorporated into the Qing as tributary territory. Fourth — and most consequentially for the fiscal-and-political character of the High Qing settlement — Kangxi's 1712 imperial decree declared that there would be no additional head taxes on newly added taxable population beyond the 1711 fiscal baseline. The Kangxi decree was the most consequential pre-modern tax-policy commitment: the expanding population across the century would not produce expanding head-tax revenue, which limited the fiscal extraction the Qing state could impose on the agricultural population and produced the demographic-and-economic expansion that the eighteenth century witnessed.

The Yongzheng Emperor (Yinzhen, born 1678, reigned 1723-1735) was Kangxi's fourth son; the succession in 1722 was contested across the decade by Yongzheng's brothers, and Yongzheng's subsequent suppression of the factional opposition was severe. Yongzheng's twelve-year reign was the shortest of the three High Qing emperors but the most  consequential. The 1722 consolidation of the fixed head tax into the land tax (*tan ding ru di*, "merging the head tax into the land tax") completed the multi-century Single-Whip-and-silver-tax conversion that the Ming Zhang Juzheng had begun in 1572-1582 (Chapter 2). The subsequent fiscal architecture taxed land rather than persons; the expanding population was no longer the tax base; the agricultural land was. The arrangement produced the late-Qing demographic explosion — from roughly 150 million in 1700 to roughly 400 million by 1850 — that the late-Qing fiscal apparatus, taxing the fixed land rather than the expanding population, was structurally unable to fund.

Yongzheng also established the Grand Council (军机处, *junjichu*) — a small body of trusted advisers, recruited personally by the emperor, that was the chief executive body of the Qing state, bypassing the larger Outer Court (the Grand Secretariat and the Six Ministries) inherited from the Ming. The Grand Council became the pre-eminent decision-making body of the Qing administration across the two centuries. The arrangement concentrated the administrative authority in the personal-advisory circle around the emperor; the structural problem of the late-Qing apparatus would be the over-concentration of the decision-making in a small body that the dynasty could no longer recruit competent personnel for.

The Qianlong Emperor (Hongli, born 1711, reigned 1735-1796 and abdicated to avoid reigning longer than his grandfather) was the peak of the High Qing era. The Ten Great Campaigns of conquest expanded the Qing territorial reach to the largest single contiguous Chinese imperial territory on the historical record — including the 1755-1758 Dzungar Genocide that eliminated the Dzungar Khanate and incorporated Xinjiang as the new frontier. The multi-decade *Siku Quanshu* (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) compilation, completed 1782, was the largest pre-modern compilation project in world history; the parallel Qing literary inquisition burned or banned roughly 3,000 books deemed politically dangerous to the Manchu political-class settlement. The cultural-and-administrative production of the Qianlong era was structurally Manchu — the Sacred Edict was published in tri-lingual editions (Chinese, Manchu, Mongol); the Manchu genealogies were compiled and consolidated; the Manchu language was institutionalised as the official language of banner-administrative correspondence — even as the Han bureaucratic political class continued to fill the majority of the provincial-administration positions under the Manchu-Han dyarchy that Chapter 2 documented.

By 1790, the Qing silver reserves had peaked at over 70 million taels per the Sng-Moriguchi analysis — roughly two years of total Qing tax revenue, the largest fiscal reserve any pre-modern state had accumulated. The arrangement was structurally fragile in ways the late-Qianlong court did not recognise. The Heshen corruption case — in which the Manchu courtier Heshen, Qianlong's favourite from roughly 1775 onward, accumulated by his 1799 prosecution and forced suicide an estimated 800 million taels of personal wealth, roughly fourteen years of Qing tax revenue — was the largest single personal-corruption case in Chinese imperial history and an institutional warning that the High Qing political-class apparatus had begun to fail at the screening-and-discipline function. The Heshen case was prosecuted by Qianlong's son and successor Jiaqing within weeks of Qianlong's death in February 1799.

The 1793 Macartney Embassy was the emblematic encounter between the High Qing political class at its peak and the post-1688 British political class at the beginning of its own peak. George Macartney (Viscount Macartney, b. 1737), the first British envoy to China, arrived at the Qianlong court at Jehol (Chengde) in September 1793 with requests for expanded trade ports beyond Canton, a permanent British embassy at Beijing, a small island for British trading-residence, and reduced tariffs on British merchants in Guangzhou. The 80-year-old Qianlong Emperor rejected all of the requests; the imperial edicts to George III stated: "Our Celestial Empire possesses all things in prolific abundance and lacks no product within its borders. There is therefore no need to import the manufactures of outside barbarians in exchange for our own produce." The subsequent historiographical reading of the Qianlong rejection as the unaware-isolationist-arrogance error has been revised by the post-2015 Harrison-Waley-Cohen-Hevia scholarship: the 1790-1792 Sino-Nepalese War had produced Gurkha-spy intelligence reaching the Qing court that Britain had militarily conquered Bengal across the 1757-1765 period; Qianlong, on the internal-court documentary record, recognised the British threat and after Macartney's departure issued orders strengthening coastal defence at Zhoushan, Macao, and the other Pacific approaches. The Macartney rejection was a strategic-not-arrogant Qing decision against the open trading-port expansion that the post-1839 Opium Wars and the 1842 Treaty of Nanjing imposed by force. The High Qing political class understood the threat. The subsequent nineteenth-century decline (Chapter 4) was the product of the structural-fiscal-and-demographic crisis that the High Qing's own fiscal architecture had built into the period.

## The Mughal collapse and the successor states

While the Qing apparatus consolidated, the Mughal apparatus collapsed. Chapter 2 documented the 1707 death of Aurangzeb and the 12 June 1707 Battle of Jajau; the Mughal succession instability under Bahadur Shah I, Jahandar Shah, Farrukhsiyar, Muhammad Shah, Ahmad Shah Bahadur, and Alamgir II produced the fragmentation of imperial authority across the subcontinent. By 1750, the Mughal Emperor's effective territorial authority extended roughly 250 miles around Delhi. The remaining territories operated as independent or nominally-Mughal-vassal successor states.

The 1739 sack of Delhi by Nader Shah of Persia was the decisive symbolic event of the Mughal collapse. Nader Shah's Afsharid forces, after defeating the Mughal army at the 24 February 1739 Battle of Karnal, occupied Delhi for fifty-seven days and massacred roughly 30,000 of Delhi's residents over a single day on 22 March 1739. The Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond — symbolic centrepieces of the Mughal-imperial regalia under Shah Jahan — were taken to Iran; the Peacock Throne was subsequently broken up; the Koh-i-Noor passed through the Afsharid-Durrani-Sikh-British sequence and is now held by the British Crown.

The Maratha Confederacy was the largest single successor-state political class. After the 1707 release of Shahuji Maharaj (Shivaji's grandson) by Bahadur Shah I, the Maratha state restored under Shahuji as Chhatrapati. The subsequent 1713 institutionalisation of the Peshwa (chief minister) office as a hereditary position in the Bhat brahmin family produced the Maratha expansion under Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath (1713-1720), Peshwa Bajirao I (1720-1740), and Peshwa Balaji Bajirao "Nanasaheb" (1740-1761). The Maratha territorial expansion across central and northern India through the 1720s, 1730s, 1740s, and 1750s produced Maratha control of the Mughal nominal authority across most of the subcontinent by the late 1750s. The subsequent 14 January 1761 Third Battle of Panipat — Afghan Durrani forces under Ahmad Shah Abdali defeated the Maratha army under Sadashivrao Bhau — halted the Maratha northward expansion. The Maratha Confederacy continued under Peshwa Madhavrao I (1761-1772) and the peshwas until the 1818 Third Anglo-Maratha War terminated the confederacy and incorporated its territories into the East India Company territorial system.

The Awadhi nawabate was the northern Indian successor state operating across the Indo-Gangetic plain. Founded by Saadat Ali Khan Burhan-ul-Mulk (Mughal governor from 1722; nawab *de facto* from 1732), Awadh was the wealthy agricultural-and-commercial state controlling the territory around Lucknow and Faizabad. The subsequent nawabs Safdar Jung (1739-1754) and Shuja-ud-Daula (1754-1775) integrated the nawabate into the post-Mughal political-class settlement; the 22 October 1764 Battle of Buxar — at which the East India Company under Hector Munro defeated the combined forces of Shuja-ud-Daula, the Bengali nawab Mir Qasim, and the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II — was the decisive military fact of the subsequent Company territorial expansion.

The Bengali nawabate had been operated by Murshid Quli Khan from roughly 1717 and his successors. Under Alivardi Khan (r. 1740-1756), Bengal was the wealthiest single successor state — its agricultural-and-commercial fiscal base was the largest single revenue source on the subcontinent. The subsequent 1756 succession of Siraj-ud-Daulah and the 23 June 1757 Battle of Plassey ended Bengali independence. Robert Clive of the East India Company defeated Siraj-ud-Daulah's army of roughly 50,000 with the Company's force of roughly 3,000 through the bribery of the nawab's commander Mir Jafar, who had agreed in advance to withhold his cavalry contingent from the battle. The subsequent 1757 installation of Mir Jafar as nawab, the 1760 replacement of Mir Jafar by his son-in-law Mir Qasim, the 1764 Battle of Buxar against the Awadh-Bengal-Mughal coalition, and the 12 August 1765 Treaty of Allahabad — at which the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II granted the East India Company the *diwani* (revenue rights) of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa for the annual tribute of roughly 2.6 million rupees — produced the foundational legal basis of British India. The East India Company was, from 12 August 1765 onward, the lawful Mughal-imperial revenue-collecting authority across the wealthiest agricultural territories of the subcontinent. The subsequent 1770 Great Bengal Famine — produced by the Company's aggressive revenue-extraction during the 1769-1770 drought — killed roughly ten million Bengalis, roughly one-third of the Bengali population, per the historical analysis. The arrangement is on the record. The British political class's nineteenth-century characterisation of the conquest as the humanitarian-and-civilising project is the historiographical lie. The 1765 Treaty of Allahabad was, in its immediate fiscal-and-political effect, the largest single private corporate seizure of state-revenue authority on the historical record.

The Hyderabadi nizamate, founded by Asaf Jah I Qamar-ud-din Khan as Mughal viceroy of the Deccan from 1713 and *de facto* independent from 1724, operated the multi-ethnic Deccani political-class settlement until the 1948 Indian Operation Polo annexation. The subsequent nizams under the Asaf Jah dynasty governed the largest single princely state on the subcontinent through the subsequent British-paramountcy period.

Mysore under Hyder Ali (r. 1761-1782) and Tipu Sultan (r. 1782-1799) was the most systematic Indian-state resistance to British expansion in the eighteenth century. Hyder Ali had seized Mysore from the Wodeyar dynasty in 1761; his son Tipu Sultan operated the state across the 1780s and 1790s as the single state on the subcontinent that maintained a modernised-and-disciplined army, a diplomatic correspondence with France, a commercial-development programme, and a consistent military resistance to the East India Company. The Anglo-Mysore Wars (1767-1769, 1780-1784, 1790-1792, 1799) ended on 4 May 1799 with the fall of Seringapatam and Tipu Sultan's death in combat at the age of forty-eight. The subsequent partition of Mysore between the Company, the Hyderabad nizamate, and the restored Wodeyar dynasty incorporated the Mysorean territories into the subsequent British paramountcy.

The Sikh confederacies (*misls*) consolidated across the eighteenth century in Punjab — twelve major misls operated throughout the post-1716 period after the execution of Banda Singh Bahadur. The subsequent Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) consolidated the Sikh Empire from 1799 onward, ruling from Lahore until his death in 1839; the subsequent 1845-1846 First Anglo-Sikh War and the 1848-1849 Second Anglo-Sikh War terminated the Sikh Empire and incorporated Punjab into the British Indian territorial system.

The eighteenth-century Indian political class was, by the 1799 fall of Tipu Sultan and the 1818 termination of the Maratha Confederacy, restructured into the East-India-Company-and-Mughal-puppet-emperor-and-princely-state-and-treaty-state political-class apparatus that the nineteenth-century British Raj formalised after the 1857 Rebellion. The subject of Chapter 4 includes the post-1857 Raj consolidation.

## The Tokugawa political class — Kyōhō, Tanuma, and Kansei reform cycles

The Tokugawa bakuhan settlement (Chapter 2) entered the eighteenth century under structural fiscal pressure. The *Pax Tokugawa* had institutionalised the 270-daimyo *bakuhan* settlement, the 1635 *sankin kōtai* alternate-attendance hostage system, and the 1633-1639 *sakoku* closed-country policy. The peace had produced demographic expansion — Japan's population rose from roughly 12 million in 1600 to roughly 31 million by 1721 per the demographic estimates — that the fixed-koku agricultural-tax base could not support. The bakufu fiscal crisis across the 1700s and 1710s produced the reform cycle.

The Kyōhō Reforms under the eighth shogun Tokugawa Yoshimune (r. 1716-1745) — the longest-reigning Tokugawa shogun after Iemitsu — were launched 1716 onward. The reforms emphasised austerity: reduced bakufu expenditure, daimyo *agemai* (rice-contribution) levies imposed on daimyo, new-field development to expand the agricultural tax base, copper coinage reform. The 1721 Edo poor-relief commission (*meyasubako* petition-box) and the 1722 Koishikawa Charity Hospital were the welfarist elements of the reform programme. The subsequent 1721-1730 bakufu fiscal position improved measurably under Yoshimune's reforms; the 1745 Yoshimune retirement into inkyo (retired-shogun) administrative status maintained the reform direction across the reigns of the ninth and tenth shoguns Ieshige (r. 1745-1760) and Ieharu (r. 1760-1786).

The Tanuma Era under Tanuma Okitsugu (1719-1788) was the most consequential institutional inflection of the eighteenth-century Tokugawa political class. Tanuma had risen from hatamoto (direct Tokugawa-vassal retainer) status to chamberlain (1767) and *rōjū* councillor (1772) under Ieharu. The subsequent fourteen-year Tanuma political-class dominance (1772-1786) launched a reform programme that departed from the Kyōhō-and-Kansei agrarian-austerity model. Tanuma's reforms emphasised commercial-and-monetary development: monopoly-licensing of the commercial guilds (*kabu nakama*) for bakufu fees; expansion of copper-and-iron coinage; cautious reopening of limited foreign trade through the Nagasaki Dutch-and-Chinese contacts; new-land development including the 1782-1786 attempt to drain the Inba and Tega marshes near Edo for agricultural expansion. The Tanuma programme was the most systematic pre-Meiji Japanese experiment in commercial-development political economy.

The Tanuma programme collapsed in 1786. The 1782-1788 Tenmei Famine — produced by the 1783 Mount Asama eruption that destroyed agricultural land across central Honshu and disrupted the agricultural cycle for multiple years — killed roughly 920,000 peasants across the northeastern domains per the demographic analysis. The 1786 Ieharu death and the subsequent 1787 installation of the eleventh shogun Ienari (r. 1787-1837) under the regency of the reactionary daimyo Matsudaira Sadanobu (1759-1829, grandson of Yoshimune) terminated the Tanuma programme. Tanuma was stripped of his offices, his castle was destroyed, and his son was executed. The Kansei Reforms (1787-1793) under Sadanobu reversed the Tanuma commercial-development programme: the kabu nakama monopolies were suspended; the commercial-development projects were terminated; the Neo-Confucian Zhu Xi orthodoxy was reimposed on the bakufu academies via the 1790 Kansei Edict on Heterodoxy; austerity-and-agrarian policy was restored. The subsequent bakufu fiscal apparatus operated under the Kansei settlement until the 1837 Ōshio Heihachirō rebellion and the 1841-1843 Tenpō Reforms produced the reform-and-reaction cycle — the subject of Chapter 4.

The eighteenth-century Tokugawa political-class settlement was, on the structural-fiscal record, increasingly unable to fund itself from the fixed agricultural tax base. The alternative commercial-development path that Tanuma had attempted was reversed by Sadanobu. The subsequent bakufu apparatus operated under the structural-fiscal crisis that the 1853 Perry Expedition would convert into the collapse that Chapter 4 documents.

## The Ottoman political class — Tulip Era through Nizam-i Cedid

The Ottoman political class entered the eighteenth century under Sultan Mustafa II (r. 1695-1703) and his successor Ahmed III (r. 1703-1730). The post-1699 Karlowitz settlement (Chapter 2) and the 1711 Pruth Campaign that recovered Azov from Russia produced the 1718 Treaty of Passarowitz settlement under Damat Ibrahim Pasha (grand vizier 1718-1730). The 1718-1730 Tulip Era (*Lale Devri*) was the peace-and-cultural-import period of the eighteenth-century Ottoman political class. Ibrahim Müteferrika established the first Ottoman Turkish-language printing press in 1727 (Greek, Armenian, and Hebrew presses had operated in Ottoman territories from the late fifteenth century). The 1729-1742 Müteferrika press published 17 books on history, geography, language, and natural philosophy. The Tulip Era architectural production at the Topkapı and the Saadabad palace complex represented the selective adoption of European-influenced cultural forms within the continuing Ottoman institutional framework.

The 1730 Patrona Halil revolt — a Janissary-and-Istanbul-artisan uprising against the Tulip Era's perceived European-cultural-import-and-fiscal-extraction policies — ended the Tulip Era. Ahmed III was deposed; Ibrahim Pasha was strangled. The subsequent Sultan Mahmud I (r. 1730-1754) operated the post-Tulip reaction settlement that maintained the fiscal-and-institutional status quo across the period.

The eighteenth-century Ottoman political-class crisis was the structural one: the *timar* land-grant cavalry system (Chapter 1) had collapsed across the seventeenth century into the *iltizam* tax-farming privatisation and the *malikâne* life-tenure tax-farming contracts. The subsequent eighteenth-century Ottoman fiscal apparatus relied on private tax-farmers (*mültezim*) operating across the provincial territories under progressively weaker central oversight. The *ayan* (provincial notables) — local landholding-and-tax-farming political-class operators in the provinces — emerged across the eighteenth century as the *de facto* operators of the provincial administration. The arrangement was structurally analogous to the multi-century Mughal *jagirdari* crisis under Aurangzeb (Chapter 2): expansion of the tax-farming political class produced the fiscal-and-political-class fragmentation that the central authority could not reverse.

The 1768-1774 Russo-Turkish War, ended by the 1774 Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca, was the decisive Ottoman territorial loss to Catherine the Great's Russia. The Crimean Khanate became Russian protectorate (formally annexed by Russia in 1783); Russia gained territorial-and-commercial access to the Black Sea; Russia obtained the "protector of Orthodox Christians" status within the Ottoman territories that the nineteenth-century Russian intervention in the Balkans would build on. The subsequent 1787-1792 Russo-Turkish War extended the Russian gains under the 1792 Treaty of Jassy.

The *Nizam-i Cedid* ("New Order") reform programme launched under Selim III (r. 1789-1807) was the most systematic eighteenth-century Ottoman institutional reform. Selim, who had succeeded his uncle Abdülhamid I in April 1789 — three months before the 14 July 1789 storming of the Bastille and four months before Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar's coronation at Tehran — recognised that the cumulative 1768-1792 Russian territorial advances had exposed the structural inferiority of the Ottoman military-and-fiscal apparatus to the post-Petrine Russian state. The subsequent Nizam-i Cedid reforms established a new infantry corps trained on European lines and equipped with modern weapons; a new tax structure to fund the new corps (the *İrad-ı Cedid* treasury); diplomatic missions established in London, Paris, Vienna, and Berlin from 1793; translation programmes for European military, scientific, and administrative texts.

The Nizam-i Cedid programme was destroyed by the 1807 Janissary revolt. The Janissary corps — which had proved the decisive internal-political force across the seventeenth century (Chapter 2) and across the eighteenth century — recognised the new infantry corps as a structural threat to the Janissary monopoly on infantry military-and-political authority. The 1807 Janissary revolt deposed Selim III; the subsequent 28 July 1808 assassination of Selim by Janissary forces ended the eighteenth-century Ottoman reform attempt. Selim's cousin Mahmud II (r. 1808-1839) launched the reform programme — including the decisive 1826 "Auspicious Incident" suppression of the Janissary corps — that the nineteenth-century Tanzimat would extend. The subject of Chapter 4 includes the post-1808 Mahmud II reforms and the subsequent Tanzimat period.

## The Wahhabi-Saud pact and the founding of the First Saudi State

The 1744 pact between Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703-1792), the religious reformer of the Najdi-and-central-Arabian Salafi-Wahhabi movement, and Muhammad ibn Saud (d. 1765), the emir of Diriyah in central Arabia, founded the First Saudi State (Emirate of Diriyah, 1744-1818). The pact established the dual religious-and-political settlement that has continuously defined the Saudi state apparatus across the subsequent 282 years through the Second Saudi State (Emirate of Nejd, 1824-1891) and the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (founded 1932 by Abdulaziz ibn Saud, descended from Muhammad ibn Saud).

The arrangement institutionalised the religious authority of the Wahhabi clerical descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and the political-and-military authority of the House of Saud descendants of Muhammad ibn Saud as the dual co-foundation of the state. The subsequent Wahhabi-Saud expansion across the eighteenth century brought the centralArabian tribes under the dual religious-and-political authority. The 1803-1804 First Saudi State sack of Mecca and Medina under Abdulaziz ibn Muhammad ibn Saud established the Wahhabi religious authority over the Hejazi pilgrimage cities; the subsequent 1811-1818 Ottoman-Egyptian campaign under Muhammad Ali Pasha's son Ibrahim Pasha (commissioned by Sultan Mahmud II) destroyed the Diriyah capital and ended the First Saudi State.

The 1744 Wahhabi-Saud pact is the most consequential single eighteenth-century political-class formation on the twenty-first-century geopolitical record. The subsequent twentieth-and-twenty-first-century Saudi political-class settlement — covered in detail in the Middle Eastern dossiers of Part VII of this volume — operates on the continuous 282-year institutional logic that the 1744 pact established. The Saudi political-class apparatus, the Saudi-American alignment from 1933 onward, and the multi-trillion-dollar Saudi oil-and-financial-and-political-class apparatus that the post-1973 Saudi state operated are the direct continuations of the 1744 institutional foundation.

## The Qajar founding 1789 — Agha Mohammad Khan

The 1722 Safavid collapse (Chapter 2) was followed by the 1736-1747 Nader Shah Afsharid interregnum and the 1747-1779 Karim Khan Zand interregnum based at Shiraz. The subsequent post-Karim-Khan succession crisis was resolved by Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar across the 1779-1796 period.

Agha Mohammad Khan was born roughly 1742 at Astarabad on the southeastern Caspian coast — son of Mohammad Hasan Khan Qajar, chieftain of the Quwanlu (Qavanlu) clan of the Qajar tribe, and grandson of Fath-Ali Khan Qajar (executed by Nader Shah Afsharid's order).
