Agha Mohammad was castrated aged six on the order of Adil Shah Afsharid to eliminate his political-rivalry potential as an heir. He held administrative positions in northern Iran across the 1750s and 1760s; in 1762 he was captured by Karim Khan Zand and held as political hostage at Shiraz for the sixteen years.

The 2 March 1779 death of Karim Khan Zand provided the opening. Agha Mohammad Khan escaped Shiraz and fled to Astarabad, where he consolidated the Qajar tribal base across the decade. By 1786 he had designated Tehran as the new capital — central position, defensible, and removed from the Shiraz Zand-loyalist political base. In 1789 he was enthroned as chief of the Qajars and named his nephew Baba Khan (future Fath-Ali Shah) as heir. The 1789 date marks the founding of the Qajar dynasty per the subsequent Iranian historiography.

The subsequent multi-year consolidation against the last Zand ruler Lotf Ali Khan ended at the 1794 siege of Kerman. Agha Mohammad Khan ordered the blinding or massacre of roughly 20,000 Kermani inhabitants on the city's surrender — the largest single atrocity of the late-eighteenth-century Iranian unification. The 1796 formal coronation as Shah completed the dynastic foundation. The 1797 assassination of Agha Mohammad Khan by personal servants near Shusha — during the 1795-1797 Georgia-and-Caucasus campaign that had re-asserted Iranian sovereignty over former Iranian territories there — transferred the throne to his nephew Fath-Ali Shah (r. 1797-1834).

The Qajar political-class apparatus operated through tribal alliances and familial appointments. The subsequent persistent internal divisions and the fiscal-and-administrative weakness of the Qajar state produced the nineteenth-century territorial losses to Russia under the 1813 Treaty of Gulistan (Georgia, Dagestan, northern Azerbaijan) and the 1828 Treaty of Turkmenchay (Yerevan, Nakhchivan, the remaining Caucasian territories) — the subject of Chapter 4.

## The Asante Empire — eighteenth-century peak

The Asante Empire (Chapter 2) reached its eighteenth-century peak under Opoku Ware I (r. 1720-1750), Osei Kwadwo (r. 1764-1777), and Osei Kwame (r. 1777-1803). The Asante administrative apparatus operated through the Asantehene (king at Kumasi) and the subordinate amanhene (paramount chiefs) of the coalesced Akan-and-other states. The Asante constitution, codified through the Golden Stool tradition that Osei Tutu I had established in the 1690s, stood as the most systematic pre-modern West African political-class institutional form.

The Asante gold-and-slave trade with the coastal European factories — Cape Coast Castle (British, Royal African Company until 1750 and subsequent Company of Merchants Trading to Africa), Elmina Castle (Dutch West India Company, formerly Portuguese), Christiansborg (Danish), and the other Gold Coast coastal posts — produced the fiscal base of the eighteenth-century Asante state. The 1701 Asante victory over the Denkyira state at the Battle of Feyiase had secured the Asante hinterland-to-coast trade access; the eighteenth-century Asante expansion across the northern hinterland (Bono, Banda, Gonja, Dagomba) and across the central coastal interior produced the largest single West African political-class apparatus of the eighteenth century.

The 1807 British abolition of trans-Atlantic slave trade (the US Congress passed its parallel abolition the same year) began the Asante economic-and-political reorientation. The subsequent nineteenth-century Anglo-Asante Wars (1823-1831, 1863-1864, 1873-1874, 1895-1896, 1900) terminated the Asante state and incorporated its territories into the British Gold Coast colony in 1902 — the subject of Chapter 4.

## The Bourbon Reforms in Spanish America

The Spanish American viceregal apparatus (Chapter 2) was restructured across the 1750s-1780s under the Bourbon Reforms of Charles III of Spain (r. 1759-1788). The reforms responded to the 1763 Treaty of Paris that had terminated the Seven Years' War and ceded Florida to Britain; the subsequent Bourbon political class recognised the strategic vulnerability of the Spanish American territories to subsequent British expansion.

The Bourbon Reforms operated on four institutional axes. First, the 1776 creation of the Viceroyalty of Río de la Plata at Buenos Aires divided the southern half of the Viceroyalty of Peru into a new administrative unit. Second, the 1782 *intendencias* system replaced the prior *corregidor* and *alcalde mayor* district-administrative system with provincial intendants recruited from Spain-born *peninsulares* rather than from locally-born *criollos*. The arrangement displaced the generations of locally-rooted criollo administrative-class operators and produced the structural alienation of the criollo political class that the subsequent 1810s-1820s independence movements built on. Third, the 1778 *Reglamento de Libre Comercio* (Free Trade Regulation) extended trading rights between Spanish American ports and multiple Spanish ports beyond the Seville-Cadiz monopoly. Fourth, the expanded *quinto* enforcement and the 1780 tribute-rate increases extracted more aggressively from the indigenous-and-mestizo population.

The 1780-1783 Tupac Amaru II rebellion in Peru, led by José Gabriel Condorcanqui Túpac Amaru (a Quechua mestizo claiming descent from the 1572-executed Túpac Amaru I), was the largest indigenous-and-mestizo uprising in colonial Latin American history. The rebellion mobilised roughly 100,000 indigenous-and-mestizo combatants across the Andean highlands; roughly 100,000 people died in the multi-year suppression. Túpac Amaru II was captured March 1781 and executed at Cusco 18 May 1781 — drawn and quartered in the public plaza after witnessing the execution of his wife Micaela Bastidas, his sons, and his other relatives. The subsequent 1781 Comuneros rebellion in New Granada extended the pattern of eighteenth-century Spanish American structural fiscal-and-political-class rebellion.

The 1780-1783 Tupac Amaru rebellion was suppressed; the 1781 Comuneros rebellion was negotiated to a settlement that the Bourbon administration subsequently reversed. The subsequent 1810s-1820s independence movements — under Bolivar, San Martín, Sucre, O'Higgins, Iturbide, and the other creole-mestizo military-political-class operators — produced the 1810-1825 independence of most of the Spanish American territories. The post-independence Latin American political class is the subject of Chapter 4 and of the dossiers of Part VIII.

## The British political class — Walpole through the Pitts

### The Robinocracy of Sir Robert Walpole (1721-1742)

Sir Robert Walpole — First Lord of the Treasury and Chancellor of the Exchequer from 3 April 1721 to 11 February 1742, the longest single tenure of any British prime minister — built the post-1720 British political class on the foundation that the 1694 Bank of England and the 1688-1689 Glorious Revolution settlement had established (Chapter 2). The 1720 South Sea Bubble — in which the South Sea Company, chartered 1711 as the holder of the asiento contract for Spanish American slave-trade supply, had been used as the vehicle for a debt-conversion scheme that produced the speculative bubble and the 1720 collapse — had discredited the preceding Stanhope-Sunderland administration. Walpole, who had opposed the South Sea scheme and weathered the collapse, was installed in 1721 to manage the recovery. He stayed for twenty-one years.

Walpole's twenty-one-year tenure institutionalised the parliamentary-Whig political-class settlement on the basis of parliamentary patronage. The arrangement operated on three structural mechanisms. First, Crown civil-list payments to MPs and to peers — the places, pensions, and sinecures distributed through the Treasury — bought the parliamentary majorities that Walpole needed for the fiscal-and-administrative legislation. Second, the commercial-favour distribution through the chartered companies (Bank of England, East India Company, South Sea Company, Royal African Company until 1750) — directorships, share allocations, contracts — bound the London commercial-political class to the parliamentary-Whig administration. Third, the parliamentary elections (manageable, given the pre-1832 small-borough constituency system that gave disproportionate parliamentary weight to small controlled boroughs that Walpole's treasury could afford to purchase) were systematically managed across the 1721-1742 period through the centralised electoral-influence apparatus that Walpole had institutionalised.

The eighteenth-century British political class operated on the arrangement Walpole had built. The administrations of Henry Pelham (1743-1754) and the Duke of Newcastle (1754-1762 with interruptions) continued the Walpolean institutional logic. The 1756-1763 Seven Years' War — under William Pitt the Elder (Secretary of State, 1756-1761 with interruptions) — extended the British global-strategic position; the 1763 Treaty of Paris transferred French Canada, Spanish Florida, and several Caribbean islands to Britain.

### The American Revolutionary crisis and Pitt the Younger (1783-1806)

The post-1763 North American taxation crisis — produced by the parliamentary attempt to fund the post-war military-and-administrative apparatus across the North American colonies through direct parliamentary taxation (the 1765 Stamp Act, the 1767 Townshend Acts, the 1773 Tea Act) — produced the subsequent 1775-1783 American Revolutionary War and the independence of the thirteen colonies under the 1783 Treaty of Paris.

William Pitt the Younger (1759-1806), son of Pitt the Elder, was prime minister 1783-1801 and 1804-1806. Pitt's post-1783 administration consolidated the post-American-Revolutionary British political class on the continued Walpolean-and-Pittian institutional foundation. The subsequent 1801 Acts of Union with Ireland incorporated the Irish parliament into the Westminster parliament; the subsequent 1804-1806 Pitt administration died with Pitt in January 1806. The nineteenth-century British political class operated on the Pittian institutional foundation that the 1806 death had left.

## The French political class — Law's System through the Revolution

### John Law's System (1716-1720)

The post-Louis-XIV French political class — under the 1715-1723 regency of Philippe d'Orléans for the child king Louis XV — inherited the bankrupt fiscal apparatus that the 1701-1714 War of the Spanish Succession had produced. The debt was roughly 3 billion livres against an annual revenue of roughly 150 million livres — twenty years of total state income.

John Law (1671-1729), the Scottish economist who had been exiled from Britain after a 1694 duel-killing and had spent the two decades developing his monetary theories across the European courts, proposed to the regent in 1715 a systematic restructuring of the French fiscal apparatus on the basis of paper-money issuance backed by chartered-trading-company commercial profits. The regent approved. The subsequent 1716-1720 Law system operated on three integrated institutional structures.

First, the 1716 Banque Générale (renamed Banque Royale in 1718 when the state took ownership) — the first French note-issuing bank — issued banknotes backed by gold-and-silver reserves. The subsequent 1718 Banque Royale status converted the notes into official state currency.

Second, the 1717 Mississippi Company (Compagnie d'Occident) — the monopoly on Louisiana trade — was consolidated with the other French chartered companies (Compagnie des Indes orientales, Compagnie de Chine, Compagnie de Sénégal, Compagnie d'Afrique) into the 1719 Compagnie des Indes ("Mississippi Company"). The consolidated company held the monopoly on all French overseas trade.

Third, the Compagnie des Indes was used as the debt-conversion vehicle. The holders of the pre-1716 royal debt were invited to convert their holdings into Mississippi Company shares. The subsequent subscription-and-secondary-market trading produced the speculative bubble: the Mississippi Company share price rose from roughly 500 livres in early 1717 to roughly 18,000 livres in late 1719. The subsequent 1720 collapse was immediate. The Banque Royale suspended payments in May 1720 after the over-issuance of banknotes had produced the currency collapse. John Law fled France in December 1720 disguised as a commoner, leaving the French fiscal apparatus and the French commercial class in the speculative-and-creditor ruin that produced the eighteenth-century French structural-fiscal weakness.

The Law system was the continental European parallel to the 1720 British South Sea Bubble. The twin 1720 collapses across the twin Channel jurisdictions terminated the pre-1720 speculative-financial era and produced the more cautious post-1720 financial-political settlements: the Walpolean British settlement on the basis of the continuing Bank of England, and the French settlement on the basis of the royal-treasury-and-tax-farmers-general (*ferme générale*) apparatus that the eighteenth-century French political class operated until 1789.

### The 1789-1799 French Revolution

The post-Law French fiscal apparatus depended on the tax-farmers-general — a consortium of roughly forty financiers who held the contracts to collect the royal indirect taxes (gabelle, aides, traites). The arrangement extracted from the agricultural-and-commercial population at the margins the tax-farmers needed for their commercial profits; the monarchy received the contract revenue without the administrative-and-political cost of direct extraction. The arrangement was structurally analogous to the Roman *publicani* and the Yuan *ortoq* and the Spanish-American *asiento* — a private-commercial syndicate paid for the right to extract from the population on behalf of the state.

The Seven Years' War (1756-1763) and the 1778-1783 American Revolutionary War financial-support cost produced the 1788 fiscal crisis. Jacques Necker, Louis XVI's Director-General of Finances 1777-1781 and 1788-1789, was unable to negotiate the necessary fiscal restructuring through the parlements. The 5 May 1789 calling of the Estates-General at Versailles was the decisive structural inflection.

The 17 June 1789 Third Estate declaration as the National Assembly, the 20 June Tennis Court Oath, the 14 July 1789 storming of the Bastille, the 4-11 August 1789 abolition of feudal privileges, and the 26 August 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen produced the restructuring that converted the absolutist French state into the constitutional monarchy of 1791-1792.

The subsequent 21 September 1792 declaration of the First Republic, the 21 January 1793 execution of Louis XVI, the 16 October 1793 execution of Marie Antoinette, the 1793-1794 Reign of Terror under the Committee of Public Safety (Robespierre, Saint-Just, Couthon), the 28 July 1794 execution of Robespierre, the 1795-1799 Directory, and the 9 November 1799 Napoleon 18 Brumaire coup produced the 1799-1815 Napoleonic period that the nineteenth-century European political-class settlement built on. The subject of Chapter 4 includes the post-1815 Restoration-and-Orleanist-and-Second-Empire-and-Third-Republic French political-class sequence.

## The Hamilton-Jefferson settlement

The American Revolutionary political class — under George Washington, John Adams, Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, James Monroe, Alexander Hamilton, John Jay, Benjamin Franklin, and the other signatories of the 1787 Constitution — built the post-1783 American institutional apparatus on the conscious import of the British post-1694 Bank-of-England-and-parliamentary-taxation settlement that the thirteen colonies had just fought to escape.

Alexander Hamilton (1755-1804), born in Nevis in the British West Indies and educated at King's College (now Columbia University) in New York, was appointed first Secretary of the Treasury under President Washington on 11 September 1789. Hamilton's subsequent multi-year programme — encoded in the three foundational reports of January 1790, December 1790, and December 1791 — replicated the 1694-1697 British apparatus in the new American republic.

The 14 January 1790 "Report on Public Credit" proposed federal assumption of the state Revolutionary War debts (roughly $25 million in state debts, plus the roughly $54 million of federal debts including foreign creditors) and the consolidation of the combined roughly $79 million debt into funded national debt at interest. The arrangement converted the pre-1790 state-and-federal-debt obligations — which had traded at 15-25 cents on the dollar in the post-war secondary markets — into at-par federal obligations backed by federal tax revenue. The subsequent speculative gain to the holders of the depreciated debts — including multiple congressmen, multiple Treasury officials, and the connected commercial-political class around Hamilton — was, on the historical-financial analysis, roughly $40 million in 1790 dollars. The arrangement was the single largest pre-modern American political-class enrichment transaction.

The 14 December 1790 "Second Report on Public Credit" proposed the chartering of the First Bank of the United States — a 20-year-charter joint-stock bank with $10 million capital, of which the federal government would hold $2 million and private subscribers would hold $8 million, with the monopoly on federal-government banking. The 25 February 1791 chartering of the First Bank of the United States — modeled on the 1694 Bank of England in its institutional structure — completed the import. The post-1791 American political class operated on the basis of the chartered-central-bank-and-funded-national-debt apparatus that the 1688-1694 British architecture had built.

The 5 December 1791 "Report on Manufactures" proposed the protectionist-industrial development programme — tariffs on imported manufactured goods, subsidies for domestic manufacturing, infrastructure development through federal-investment programmes — that the nineteenth-century American industrial expansion built on.

The Hamilton programme was opposed by Thomas Jefferson (Secretary of State 1790-1793) and James Madison (member of the House of Representatives 1789-1797) — the subsequent Democratic-Republican faction that organised against the Federalist-Hamilton centralisation programme. The Jefferson-Madison opposition was grounded in the agrarian-republican political-class settlement of the southern slaveholding political class — which depended on decentralised state-level political-class authority and which opposed the centralisation that the Hamilton programme required.

The 1800 election produced the Jefferson presidency (1801-1809). Jefferson did not dismantle the First Bank of the United States — its 20-year charter ran to 1811 — but his Democratic-Republican administration shifted the federal-administrative-and-tax-and-spending policy toward the agrarian-decentralised model that the Jefferson-Madison opposition had advocated. The 1811 expiration of the First Bank charter and the subsequent 1816-1836 Second Bank of the United States — chartered under James Madison in 1816, vetoed for re-charter by Andrew Jackson in 1832, terminated in 1836 — is the subject of Chapter 4.

The eighteenth-century American political class produced, by 1800, the single largest political-class enrichment transaction on the pre-modern American historical record (the 1790 debt-assumption) and the single most systematic import of the post-1694 British chartered-bank-and-funded-national-debt apparatus into the new republic. The subsequent nineteenth-century American commercial-and-industrial expansion operated on the basis of the Hamilton apparatus.

## The Rothschild rise

Mayer Amschel Rothschild (1744-1812) was born to a modest Jewish-merchant family in the Frankfurt ghetto. From 1769 he operated as court factor for William, Prince-Elector of Hesse-Kassel — providing commercial-and-financial services to the wealthy central-German princely court. The subsequent Rothschild expansion across the 1770s, 1780s, and 1790s produced the structural foundation of the nineteenth-century Rothschild banking dominance.

The key institutional innovation was the multi-capital family network. Mayer Amschel's five sons were established across five European capitals: Amschel Mayer (1773-1855) at Frankfurt; Salomon Mayer (1774-1855) at Vienna from 1820; Nathan Mayer (1777-1836) at London from 1798; Carl Mayer (1788-1855) at Naples from 1820; James Mayer (1792-1868) at Paris from 1812. The arrangement produced the cross-border banking network that could move capital, intelligence, and commercial-political influence across the continental European jurisdictions faster than any single-jurisdiction bank.

The 1810s Rothschild consolidation of cross-border European banking dominance was institutionalised through Napoleonic-War financing. Nathan Mayer Rothschild's 1810-1815 financing of Wellington's Peninsular campaign — moving gold across enemy lines through the Rothschild family network — produced the post-Waterloo Rothschild position as the dominant British government creditor. The 1815 Rothschild role in post-Waterloo British financing institutionalised the nineteenth-century Rothschild-London-Paris-Vienna-Frankfurt-Naples banking dominance that the nineteenth century operated on — the subject of Chapter 4.

## The continuity

The thirteen panels of Chapter 2 had institutionalised the different regional answers to the same political-class question. The subsequent fourteen panels of this chapter operated through the adjustments that the eighteenth century imposed: Qing High Qing under Kangxi-Yongzheng-Qianlong; Mughal collapse and the six successor states (Maratha, Awadh, Bengal, Hyderabad, Mysore, Sikh) with the East India Company conquest; Tokugawa Kyōhō-Tanuma-Kansei reform cycles; Ottoman Tulip-Era-and-Nizam-i-Cedid; Wahhabi-Saud 1744 pact; Qajar founding 1789; Asante eighteenth-century peak; Bourbon Reforms in Spanish America with the Tupac Amaru and Comuneros rebellions; British Walpolean Robinocracy through the Pitts; French Law system and the 1789-1799 Revolution; Hamilton-Jefferson American settlement; and the Rothschild rise. The political class did not change in role across the century. The instruments adjusted.

The largest single eighteenth-century political-class transaction on the historical record was the 1765 Treaty of Allahabad, at which the East India Company — a chartered joint-stock corporation — became the lawful Mughal-imperial revenue-collecting authority across roughly 30 million Bengali, Bihari, and Orissan subjects. The arrangement was, in its immediate fiscal-and-political effect, the single largest private corporate seizure of state-revenue authority on the pre-1800 historical record. The next-largest single eighteenth-century political-class transaction was the 1789-1799 French Revolution that terminated the pre-1789 French absolutist political class and produced the subsequent Napoleonic-and-Restoration period. The third-largest was the 1790 Hamilton debt-assumption that converted the pre-1790 American Revolutionary debt into funded national debt and produced the single largest pre-modern American political-class enrichment transaction.

The masks of the eighteenth century continued to be many masks. The High Qing mask was the Confucian-Manchu syncretic-imperial *tianzi* "son-of-heaven" court. The post-Mughal Indian masks were the multiple nawab-and-nizam-and-peshwa-and-sultan masks that the fragmented subcontinent operated through. The Tokugawa mask continued to be the shogunal-and-imperial bakuhan-and-sankin-kōtai bureaucratic-feudal apparatus. The Ottoman mask was the continuing Sultan-and-Janissary-and-ulema-and-ayan tripartite settlement under the Selim III reform attempt. The 1744 Wahhabi-Saud mask was the religious-and-political dual foundation. The Qajar mask was the tribal-and-familial dynastic-foundation apparatus. The Asante mask was the Golden Stool chiefly-and-imperial constitutional settlement. The Bourbon Spanish-American mask was the reformed-viceregal *intendencia* apparatus. The Walpolean British mask was the parliamentary-Whig-and-Bank-of-England-and-civil-list patronage system. The Bourbon French mask was the tax-farmers-general absolutist-mercantile state until 1789, and the National-Assembly-and-Committee-of-Public-Safety-and-Directory revolutionary settlement until 1799. The Hamilton-Jefferson American mask was the Federalist-Democratic-Republican-and-First-Bank-of-the-United-States constitutional settlement. The Rothschild mask was the cross-border family-banking network that did not require any single masking institutional form to operate.

The role behind each mask was the same. The chapters that follow document the masks of the long nineteenth century and of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries that put different masks on top of the same role.

---


---

# Chapter 4: The Political Class of the Long Nineteenth Century

The long nineteenth century — from the 1789-1799 French Revolution to the 1914-1918 World War — was the century in which the northwestern European political-class apparatus that the chapters of this volume have documented projected itself outward into every region of the world the political-class apparatus had not previously reached. The Qing political class that had operated the largest single pre-modern state at its 1790 peak (Chapter 3) entered the decline that the 1839-1842 First Opium War, the 1850-1864 Taiping Rebellion that killed an estimated 20-30 million people, the 1856-1860 Second Opium War, the 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War, the 1899-1901 Boxer Uprising, and the 1911 Xinhai Revolution converted into the end of the 268-year dynasty and of the 2,133-year imperial Chinese institutional form. The Mughal-successor Indian political class that the East India Company had partially absorbed by 1799 (Chapter 3) was completely absorbed across the 1818 Maratha defeat, the 1849 Sikh defeat, and the 1857 Indian Rebellion — and reorganised under direct British Crown rule from 1858 onward under the Raj. The Tokugawa bakuhan settlement that the Tanuma-Kansei cycle had failed to reform (Chapter 3) collapsed under the 1853 Perry Expedition shock; the subsequent Meiji Restoration of 1868 produced the single most successful nineteenth-century non-European political-class restructuring on the historical record — a institutional rebuild that converted Japan into the fifth modern industrial-and-military power and produced the 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War victory and the 1904-1905 Russo-Japanese War victory. The Ottoman political class entered its Tanzimat reform period in 1839 and ended the century under the 1908 Young Turk Revolution. The Qajar Iranian political class lost the Caucasian territories to Russia under the 1813 and 1828 treaties and produced the 1906 Constitutional Revolution that the 1908-1909 Russian-and-shahist counter-revolution suppressed. The Latin American independence wars of 1810-1825 produced the republican settlements that the caudillismo of the 1830s-1880s and the intensified US-and-European commercial penetration of the 1890s-1914 converted into the dependent-republic political-class apparatus that the twentieth century inherited. The Scramble for Africa in the post-1880 period — formalised at the 15 November 1884 - 26 February 1885 Berlin Conference under Bismarck and Leopold II — partitioned the continent across the thirty years from roughly 10% European control to roughly 90% European control. The American political class consolidated across the 1861-1865 Civil War, the 1865-1877 Reconstruction, the post-1877 Gilded Age robber-baron settlement, the 1898 Spanish-American War, and the 22-30 November 1910 Jekyll Island meeting that produced the 1913 Federal Reserve Act. The Bismarckian German political class consolidated across the 1864-1870 wars and the 1871 imperial proclamation. The French political class moved across the 1852-1870 Second Empire and the 1870-1940 Third Republic. The Romanov political class emancipated the serfs in 1861 and survived the 1905 Revolution to face the 1914-1917 collapse the chapter documents. And across the pre-1914 international financial-and-commercial system the Rothschild-Baring-Schröder-Morgan-Warburg cross-border banking network operated the gold-standard architecture that the pre-1914 world traded under.

The political class did not change in role across the century. The institutional instruments adjusted under the unprecedented industrial-and-military-and-financial pressures the nineteenth century produced.

## The Qing political class — collapse from peak to dynasty's end

The 1796 abdication of the Qianlong Emperor (Chapter 3) and the 1799 death of Qianlong began the Qing decline. The Jiaqing Emperor (Hongli's son Yongyan, r. 1796-1820) faced the 1796-1805 White Lotus Rebellion in central China — the first major Qing internal rebellion, driven by the late-Qianlong fiscal-and-administrative deterioration. The Daoguang reign (1820-1850) operated under the continuing demographic pressure of the 400-million population that the Yongzheng land-tax base could not support. The structural fiscal-and-administrative crisis was complete before the British intervention began.

The British intervention began over opium. Across the late eighteenth century, the British East India Company had developed Bengal-grown opium as the commodity that reversed the multi-century British silver outflow to China for tea, silk, and porcelain. By the 1830s, British opium shipments through Canton totalled roughly 40,000 chests per year — roughly 2.4 million kilograms — and had produced millions of Chinese opium addicts and the reversal of the China-Britain silver balance. In 1839, the Daoguang Emperor dispatched Lin Zexu to Canton as imperial commissioner with authority to suppress the trade. Lin's 3 June 1839 confiscation and destruction of roughly 20,000 chests of British opium at Humen — a value of roughly £2 million in 1839 sterling — was the immediate trigger of the First Opium War.

The British Royal Navy expeditionary force dispatched in 1840 defeated the Qing coastal forces at multiple engagements across the 1840-1842 period. The 29 August 1842 Treaty of Nanjing, signed aboard HMS Cornwallis at the Yangtze mouth, was the single most consequential nineteenth-century imposition of treaty-port-and-extraterritoriality terms on a non-European state. The treaty ceded Hong Kong Island to the British Crown; opened five treaty ports (Canton, Amoy, Foochow, Ningpo, Shanghai); imposed a £21 million indemnity; abolished the Cohong (公行) merchant monopoly through which the Qing had previously controlled European trade at Canton; and established the extraterritoriality principle under which British subjects in China were subject to British rather than Chinese law. The 1843 Treaty of the Bogue extended the extraterritoriality and added the most-favoured-nation provision under which any subsequent concession to any other power was automatically extended to Britain. The 1844 Treaty of Wanghia (US) and the 1844 Treaty of Whampoa (France) extended the concessions to the US and French political classes.

The 1842 Treaty of Nanjing was the structural inflection of the nineteenth-century Qing political-class apparatus. The subsequent Qing institutional decline ran on the basis of the treaty-port system that the 1842 settlement had established. The 1856-1860 Second Opium War (Arrow War) extended the arrangement. The 18 October 1860 sack of the Yuanmingyuan (Old Summer Palace) by British-and-French forces under Lord Elgin and General Charles Cousin-Montauban — three days of looting and burning that destroyed the single most architectural-and-artistic complex of the late-Qing imperial court — was the single most emblematic atrocity of the nineteenth-century European intervention. The 1858 Treaty of Tianjin and the 1860 Convention of Beijing expanded the treaty-port system to 11 additional ports, legalised the opium trade, opened the Chinese interior to European merchants and missionaries, and ceded Kowloon to the British Crown.

The Taiping Rebellion that ran across the 1850-1864 period was the largest internal Qing crisis. Hong Xiuquan (1814-1864), Hakka from Guangdong, had failed the imperial civil-service examinations multiple times and, after an 1837 illness produced a visionary experience, had constructed a syncretic Christianity in which he was the younger brother of Jesus Christ commissioned to overthrow the Manchu dynasty and establish the Heavenly Kingdom of Great Peace (Taiping Tianguo). Hong's God Worshipping Society (拜上帝會) recruited across Guangxi and Guangdong from the late 1840s; the 11 January 1851 Jintian Uprising launched the rebellion. The subsequent Taiping advance across the Yangtze valley captured Wuchang in 1852, Nanjing in 1853 (renamed Tianjing, "Heavenly Capital"); the 11-year Taiping rule over the Yangtze territories operated as an alternative political-class settlement that the Qing could not suppress with the collapsed Banner and Green Standard armies.

The Qing response was the multi-province militia-army apparatus. Zeng Guofan organised the Xiang Army (Hunan Army) from 1853; Li Hongzhang organised the Huai Army (Anhui Army) from 1862; subsequent provincial armies operated under recruited gentry-officer command rather than under the centralised Banner command. The Charles "Chinese" Gordon's Ever Victorious Army (Western-officered Chinese mercenaries) reinforced the Qing forces from 1860 onward. The 1 June 1864 death of Hong Xiuquan at Tianjing — by food poisoning from wild vegetables eaten during the siege, with suicide-or-illness contested — preceded the July 1864 fall of Nanjing to the Qing forces under Zeng Guofan's brother Zeng Guoquan. The massacre of the Taiping civilian population in Nanjing produced the single largest urban atrocity of the nineteenth century by death toll.

The cumulative Taiping Rebellion death toll across the 14-year period and the post-Taiping famines is estimated at 20-30 million deaths across roughly 17 provinces — roughly 5-10% of the contemporary Chinese population per the modern demographic estimates including Britannica (roughly 20 million), Wikipedia (20-30 million), the subsequent History.com and Vaia and History Chronicler analyses. The Taiping is the single bloodiest civil war on the historical record; it killed more people than the 1914-1918 World War.

The post-1864 Self-Strengthening Movement (洋务运动 *yangwu yundong*) under Prince Gong, Li Hongzhang, Zeng Guofan, and Zuo Zongtang ran from 1861 to 1895. The limited institutional reform focused on military-industrial modernisation: Jiangnan Arsenal (1865), Fuzhou Navy Yard (1866), China Merchants Steam Navigation Company (1872), subsequent arsenal-and-shipyard establishment across multiple provincial capitals. The Self-Strengthening did not produce the comprehensive Meiji-style institutional restructuring that Japan was simultaneously building. The 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War demonstrated the failure of the Self-Strengthening apparatus. The 17 April 1895 Treaty of Shimonoseki ceded Taiwan, the Pescadores, and the Liaodong Peninsula (returned under the Triple Intervention of France, Germany, and Russia in exchange for an additional 30 million tael indemnity) to Japan and imposed a 200 million tael indemnity on the Qing.

The Empress Dowager Cixi (1835-1908) was the *de facto* ruler of the late-Qing state from 1861 to 1908 through the regencies of Tongzhi (r. 1861-1875) and Guangxu (r. 1875-1908). The 11 June - 21 September 1898 Hundred Days' Reform under Guangxu and the reformers Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao attempted a Meiji-style restructuring; the 21 September 1898 Cixi coup deposed Guangxu *de facto* and executed six of the reformers (Liu Guangdi, Lin Xu, Yang Shenxiu, Yang Rui, Kang Guangren, Tan Sitong). Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao escaped to Japan.

The 1899-1901 Boxer Uprising (Yihequan, "Righteous and Harmonious Fists") was the peasant-and-martial-arts-society movement against foreign-and-Christian presence in northern China. On 21 June 1900, Cixi declared war on eight foreign powers. The subsequent Eight-Nation Alliance (Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Japan, Italy, Austria-Hungary, US) invaded; 14 August 1900 relieved the besieged Legation Quarter in Beijing; subsequent looting and atrocities by the allied forces across the north Chinese plain. The 7 September 1901 Boxer Protocol imposed a 450 million tael indemnity on the Qing — roughly $333 million in 1901 dollars, payable across 39 years with interest — and authorised the multi-power garrison at Beijing.

The 10 October 1911 Wuchang Uprising launched the Xinhai Revolution. The subsequent multi-province secession from the Qing produced the 1 January 1912 inauguration of Sun Yat-sen as provisional president of the Republic of China at Nanjing. The 12 February 1912 abdication of the six-year-old Puyi (Xuantong Emperor) ended the Qing dynasty after 268 years and the Chinese imperial institutional form after 2,133 years (since the Qin Shi Huang's 221 BCE unification).

The Qing political-class collapse was the single largest pre-1914 nineteenth-century institutional collapse on the historical record by population affected (roughly 400 million subjects at the 1912 fall) and by absolute death toll across the collapse period (Taiping plus Opium Wars plus Boxer plus subsequent famines: roughly 30-40 million cumulative deaths). The subsequent twentieth-century Chinese political-class trajectory under the 1912-1928 Republic, the 1927-1949 Nationalist period, and the post-1949 People's Republic is the subject of Volume IV of this nine-volume series.

## The Meiji Restoration and the Japanese political-class restructuring

The Tokugawa bakuhan settlement (Chapter 3) collapsed under external pressure. On 8 July 1853, Commodore Matthew Perry of the United States Navy arrived at Edo Bay with four Black Ships and a demand for commercial-and-diplomatic relations. The 31 March 1854 Treaty of Kanagawa opened Shimoda and Hakodate to US trade and terminated the 1633-1639 *sakoku* policy that Chapter 2 had documented. The 1858 Treaty of Amity and Commerce (Harris Treaty) extended the commercial-and-extraterritorial provisions to US merchants; the subsequent 1858 treaties with Britain, France, Russia, and the Netherlands imposed the unequal treaty system on Japan that the subsequent Meiji political class spent the next forty years renegotiating.

The 1860s multi-domain anti-bakufu movement consolidated around two tozama domains that had operated on the peripheral basis Chapter 2 documented: Satsuma (under Saigō Takamori, Ōkubo Toshimichi, Matsukata Masayoshi) and Chōshū (under Kido Takayoshi, Itō Hirobumi, Yamagata Aritomo, Inoue Kaoru). The 1866 Satsuma-Chōshū Alliance produced the coalition that defeated the bakufu across the subsequent 1868-1869 Boshin War.

The 3 January 1868 Meiji Restoration declaration restored Emperor Meiji (Mutsuhito, b. 1852, r. 1867-1912) to political authority; the bakufu was abolished; the shogun Tokugawa Yoshinobu resigned. The Boshin War of 1868-1869 — the Battle of Toba-Fushimi (January 1868), the fall of Edo (May 1868), the Aizu campaign (September-November 1868), the Battle of Hakodate (May 1869) — defeated the pro-bakufu daimyo and consolidated the new imperial-government authority.

The Meiji institutional restructuring was the single most successful nineteenth-century non-European political-class rebuild on the historical record. The 1871 *Haihan chiken* abolition of the han converted the 270 daimyo domains into 47 prefectures under central-government administrators recruited from the former samurai class and the post-1871 professionalised civil service. The daimyo were compensated with government bonds and aristocratic-title settlements that the 1884 kazoku peerage formalised. The 1873 conscription law replaced the hereditary samurai class with universal male conscription; the 1873-1876 abolition of samurai stipends and sword-wearing privileges terminated the 250-year samurai institutional form. The 1877 Satsuma Rebellion under Saigō Takamori — the last samurai rebellion against the Meiji settlement — was suppressed at the Battle of Shiroyama on 24 September 1877; Saigō committed suicide; roughly 13,000 subordinate samurai casualties across the six-month rebellion ended the samurai class as a military force.

The Meiji land tax reform of 1873 converted the agricultural revenue base from rice-in-kind to monetary payment at 3% of land value — the single largest pre-modern non-European fiscal restructuring. The 1881 Matsukata fiscal reform under Matsukata Masayoshi (Finance Minister 1881-1892, prime minister twice) stabilised the post-1877 inflation through deflation, subsequent establishment of the Bank of Japan in 1882, and subsequent gold-standard adoption in 1897 (funded by the 200 million tael indemnity received from the 1895 Treaty of Shimonoseki).

The 1889 Meiji Constitution under Itō Hirobumi was modelled on the 1871 Prussian Constitution and on the multi-year Itō study mission to Berlin and Vienna. The bicameral Imperial Diet — the House of Representatives elected by limited male suffrage (initially roughly 1% of the population), the House of Peers appointed from the kazoku aristocracy — operated under the subsequent Meiji-and-Taisho settlement until the 1947 post-occupation Constitution.

The 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War, the 1904-1905 Russo-Japanese War (in which the 27-28 May 1905 Battle of Tsushima destroyed the Russian Baltic Fleet under Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō and produced the 5 September 1905 Treaty of Portsmouth recognising Japanese position in Korea and southern Manchuria), and the 22 August 1910 Japan-Korea Annexation Treaty completed the Meiji-era establishment of Japan as the fifth modern industrial-and-military power. The 30 July 1912 death of Emperor Meiji ended the 45-year personal-imperial period; the subsequent Taisho period (1912-1926) and Showa period (1926-1989) are the subject of Volume IV.

## The post-1857 British Raj

The 1857-1858 Indian Rebellion (Sepoy Mutiny) — triggered on 10 May 1857 at Meerut by Bengal Army sepoys reacting to the new Pattern 1853 Enfield rifle cartridges greased with pork-and-beef fat (offensive to both Muslim and Hindu sepoys) and to the EIC territorial-and-fiscal-extraction crisis that the 1856 Doctrine of Lapse annexations had escalated — was the single largest nineteenth-century anti-colonial uprising on the historical record. The rebellion mobilised roughly 200,000 sepoys, multiple princely states (Awadh, Jhansi under Rani Lakshmibai, Kanpur under Nana Sahib, the Mughal court at Delhi under Bahadur Shah II), and the peasant-and-zamindar populations of northern and central India.

The subsequent British suppression was severe. The 30 March 1858 recapture of Delhi, the 16 March - 16 July 1858 Lucknow siege and recapture, the 30 May - 6 June 1858 Gwalior recapture, and the multi-year atrocity-and-famine sequence killed roughly 800,000 Indians since the post-rebellion period per the demographic analysis. The Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah II was tried for treason in January 1858, exiled to Rangoon, and died there in 1862 — the end of the 332-year Mughal dynastic continuity that Chapter 1 had documented from Babur's 1526 foundation.

The 2 August 1858 Government of India Act dissolved the East India Company and transferred its territorial-and-administrative authority to the British Crown. The post-1858 Raj operated under the Viceroy of India (first viceroy: Lord Canning, 1858-1862; subsequent viceroys including Lord Lytton 1876-1880, Lord Ripon 1880-1884, Lord Curzon 1899-1905) and under the Secretary of State for India in London. The 1 May 1876 Royal Titles Act proclaimed Queen Victoria Empress of India (*Kaisar-i-Hind*) — the formal symbolic completion of the 1765-1858 multi-century EIC-to-Crown territorial-and-political-class transfer.

The Raj operated through three institutional layers. The directly-administered British India (roughly 60% of the subcontinent's territory and roughly 75% of the population) was governed through the Indian Civil Service — the elite competitive-examination administrative service recruited from British university graduates with limited Indian-recruit access. The roughly 565 princely states (roughly 40% of the territory, roughly 25% of the population) operated under British paramountcy through subsidiary alliance and treaty relationships that the 1947 partition would terminate. The provincial administrative apparatus integrated the post-1858 zamindar-and-talukdar landlord class into the new revenue-collecting structure.

The late-nineteenth-century Raj famine record was the single most structural indictment of the British political-class apparatus in India. The 1876-1878 Great Famine of southern India killed roughly 5.5-10 million people; the subsequent 1896-1902 Late Victorian Holocausts famines killed cumulative roughly 12-29 million people across multiple El-Niño-driven drought cycles per Mike Davis's *Late Victorian Holocausts* (Verso, 2001) — driven by the continued export of Indian grain to Britain at peak famine prices, by the late-Victorian liberal-economic refusal to intervene in the market-distribution apparatus, and by the continued land-revenue extraction from the famine-affected territories that the prior Mughal-and-EIC-and-Raj fiscal apparatus had built. The late-Victorian Indian famines killed more people than the 1914-1918 World War and have been almost entirely expunged from the subsequent British popular historical memory.

The 1885 founding of the Indian National Congress at Bombay by Allan Octavian Hume and subsequent moderate-and-extremist factions, the 1905 partition of Bengal under Lord Curzon and the swadeshi (self-sufficiency) movement, the 1906 founding of the All-India Muslim League at Dacca, and the 1909 Indian Councils Act (Morley-Minto Reforms) that introduced limited Indian representation under separate-electorate communal arrangements opened the twentieth-century Indian independence movement — the subject of Chapter 5 and of the dossiers of Part V of this volume.

## The Ottoman political class — Tanzimat through Young Turks

The 1808 assassination of Selim III by Janissary forces (Chapter 3) had ended the eighteenth-century Nizam-i Cedid reform attempt. The cousin Mahmud II (r. 1808-1839) launched the reform programme that ran across the next thirty-one years. The 15 June 1826 "Auspicious Incident" (Vaka-i Hayriye) destroyed the Janissary corps that had was the decisive internal-political force across the preceding two centuries (Chapters 2 and 3). Mahmud II's new infantry corps (Asakir-i Mansure-i Muhammediye, "Victorious Soldiers of Muhammad") surrounded the Janissary barracks at Istanbul on 15 June 1826, refused the Janissary demand to reverse the new-corps establishment, opened cannon fire, and killed roughly 6,000 Janissaries across the next 24 hours. The subsequent provincial suppression killed roughly 100,000 Janissaries and associated personnel across the Ottoman territories. The 1826 Auspicious Incident was the single most violent internal political-class purge of the nineteenth century outside the Qing Taiping period.

The subsequent 3 November 1839 Gülhane Edict (Hatt-ı Şerif of Gülhane) launched the Tanzimat ("Reorganisation") under Sultan Abdülmecid I (r. 1839-1861). The Tanzimat reforms produced the comprehensive institutional restructuring: new commercial code (1850, modelled on the 1807 French Code de Commerce), new criminal code (1858, modelled on the 1810 French Code Pénal), new land code (1858), 1856 Hatt-ı Hümayun reform extending the equality of Ottoman subjects irrespective of religion, new education system, new provincial administration system, new census-and-tax-collection apparatus. The subsequent 1876 First Ottoman Constitution under Midhat Pasha was the most systematic pre-1900 non-European constitutional document — and was suspended by Abdülhamid II (r. 1876-1909) in 1878 following the 1877-1878 Russo-Turkish War crisis.

The 1877-1878 Russo-Turkish War was the single most consequential nineteenth-century Ottoman territorial loss. The Russian forces under Grand Duke Nicholas defeated the Ottoman forces at multiple engagements across the Balkans and the Caucasus; the 3 March 1878 Treaty of San Stefano imposed expansive territorial-and-political concessions that the subsequent 13 July 1878 Treaty of Berlin under Bismarck's chairmanship of the Congress of Berlin partially reversed in favour of British and Habsburg interests. The 1878 Berlin settlement recognised Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro as independent states; established Bulgaria as autonomous principality under Ottoman suzerainty; assigned Bosnia-Herzegovina to Habsburg administration; recognised Russian territorial gains in the Caucasus. The subsequent Ottoman Balkan retreat continued across the 1908-1913 period.

The 1875 Ottoman default on foreign debt — produced by the Crimean-War-and-subsequent-borrowing fiscal pressure — produced the 1881 establishment of the Ottoman Public Debt Administration (Düyun-u Umumiye). The Public Debt Administration operated under European creditor control — representatives of the British, French, German, Austrian, Italian, and Dutch bondholders — and collected roughly one-third of the Ottoman state revenue across the subsequent 1881-1914 period to service the foreign debt. The Ottoman political class operated, from 1881 onward, under the partial fiscal-and-administrative supervision of the European creditor consortium — the nineteenth-century parallel to the late-twentieth-century IMF structural-adjustment programmes that the Part VII Middle Eastern dossiers and Part VIII Latin American dossiers will document in modified institutional form.

The 24 July 1908 Young Turk Revolution restored the 1876 Constitution. The Committee of Union and Progress (CUP, İttihat ve Terakki Cemiyeti) — founded as a secret society at the 1889 Imperial Medical Academy and consolidated across the exile-and-organisational period — produced the 1908 multi-city revolt that forced Abdülhamid II to restore the 1876 Constitution. The subsequent 13 April 1909 counter-revolutionary uprising at Istanbul was suppressed by the CUP-and-Action Army (Hareket Ordusu) under Mahmud Şevket Pasha; the 27 April 1909 deposition of Abdülhamid II installed the 65-year-old Mehmed V (r. 1909-1918) as figurehead Sultan.

The 1908-1918 CUP political-class settlement operated through the triumvirate of Enver Pasha (War Minister), Talaat Pasha (Interior Minister and Grand Vizier), and Cemal Pasha (Navy Minister and Syrian governor). The CUP entered the 1914 World War on the Central Powers side. The 1915-1923 Armenian Genocide — roughly 1.5 million Armenian deaths from CUP deportation orders of 24 April 1915 onward and the multi-year systematic massacres-and-death-marches — is the single most consequential atrocity of the late-Ottoman political-class apparatus. The CUP triumvirate fled Istanbul on 1-2 November 1918 after the Ottoman armistice; Talaat was assassinated in Berlin in 1921 by Soghomon Tehlirian (an Armenian Genocide survivor); Cemal was assassinated in Tbilisi in 1922 by Armenian operatives; Enver was killed in 1922 fighting Bolshevik forces in Tajikistan. The 1923 establishment of the Turkish Republic under Mustafa Kemal Atatürk and the 1922 abolition of the Sultanate ended the 623-year Ottoman dynastic continuity that the 1299 Osman foundation (Chapter 1) had begun.

## The Qajar Constitutional Revolution 1906

The 1797 assassination of Agha Mohammad Khan (Chapter 3) had transferred the Qajar throne to Fath-Ali Shah (r. 1797-1834). The subsequent 1804-1813 First Russo-Persian War and the 1826-1828 Second Russo-Persian War produced the 1813 Treaty of Gulistan (cession of Georgia, Dagestan, and northern Azerbaijan to Russia) and the 1828 Treaty of Turkmenchay (cession of Yerevan, Nakhchivan, and the remaining Caucasian territories). The Qajar territorial collapse to Russia was the single largest pre-1900 Iranian territorial loss.

The 1848-1896 Naser al-Din Shah Qajar reign produced the modernisation attempts that the structural-fiscal weakness of the Qajar state could not fund. The 1872 Reuter Concession granted Baron Julius de Reuter (founder of the Reuters news agency) a near-total monopoly over Iranian natural resources (mining, banking, railways, irrigation, public utilities, and most state revenues) in exchange for £40,000 cash and a 20% share of the net profits. The subsequent internal clerical-and-Russian protest produced the 1873 cancellation of the concession. The 1890 Tobacco Protest — a nationwide boycott of tobacco led by Shia clergy against the 1890 tobacco monopoly granted to Major Talbot of the British Imperial Tobacco Corporation for £15,000 — forced Naser al-Din Shah to cancel the concession in January 1892. The 1890 Protest was the single most consequential pre-1906 Iranian public-political mobilisation; it established the twentieth-century precedent of Shia-clergy-and-bazaar-merchant political-class coalition against foreign-and-shahist commercial concessions.

The 1 May 1896 assassination of Naser al-Din Shah by Mirza Reza Kermani (an associate of Jamal al-Din al-Afghani) at the Shah Abdol-Azim Shrine south of Tehran ended the 48-year reign. The subsequent succession of Muzaffar al-Din Shah (r. 1896-1907) and the intensifying fiscal-and-political crisis produced the 1906 Constitutional Revolution.

The December 1905 - August 1906 Persian Constitutional Revolution was driven by bazaar merchants, Shia ulema (including Mohammad Tabatabai and Abdollah Behbahani), and secular-and-religious intellectuals demanding constitutional government. The protest sequence ran from December 1905 bazaar-and-clerical sit-in at Shah Abdol-Azim Shrine (a *bast* — the traditional Iranian sanctuary-protest form); through the July 1906 14,000-person bast at the British Legation in Tehran (organised under tacit British diplomatic protection that subsequently shaped the post-revolutionary Anglo-Persian political-class arrangement); and produced the 5 August 1906 decree by Muzaffar al-Din Shah establishing the Majlis (parliament).

The 30 December 1906 First Iranian Constitution promulgated. The subsequent 1907 Supplementary Fundamental Laws extended the constitutional framework. The 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention divided Iran into Russian (northern) and British (southern) spheres of influence with a central neutral zone — and ended the prior Anglo-Russian "Great Game" rivalry that had constrained the late-Qajar political space.

The 23 June 1908 Mohammad Ali Shah's Russian-officered Persian Cossack Brigade under Colonel Vladimir Liakhov shelled the Majlis building at Baharestan, killed multiple deputies, and arrested the surviving constitutionalists. The 1908-1909 civil war between pro-constitutional forces (centred at Tabriz and Rasht) and shahist forces produced the 16 July 1909 deposition of Mohammad Ali Shah; the 11-year-old Ahmad Shah was installed (r. 1909-1925). The 1911 Russian ultimatum forced the dismissal of American treasury adviser Morgan Shuster — who had been hired by the post-revolutionary government to reform Iranian finances and whose aggressive reform programme had threatened Russian-and-British commercial interests — and ended the 1906 constitutional experiment in practice. The 1907-1925 Qajar political class operated under progressive Anglo-Russian tutelage until the 31 October 1925 Majlis vote that ended the Qajar dynasty and installed Reza Khan (formerly of the Persian Cossack Brigade) as Reza Shah Pahlavi. The 1925-1979 Pahlavi political class is the subject of Chapter 5 and of the Iranian dossiers of Part VII.

## Latin American independence and the republican settlements

The 1808 Napoleonic invasion of Iberia produced the multi-year crisis of Spanish colonial authority across Latin American territories. The 16 September 1810 "Grito de Dolores" of parish priest Miguel Hidalgo at Dolores, Guanajuato launched the Mexican War of Independence; the 25 May 1810 May Revolution at Buenos Aires displaced the Spanish viceroy of Río de la Plata; the independence wars across 1810-1825 produced the independence of roughly fifteen post-Spanish-American republics.

The Bolivarian campaigns in northern South America — Simón Bolívar (1783-1830), born to wealthy Caracas creole family — produced the 7 August 1819 Battle of Boyacá that liberated Colombia, the 24 June 1821 Battle of Carabobo that liberated Venezuela, and the 24 May 1822 Battle of Pichincha under Antonio José de Sucre that liberated Ecuador. The 1817-1818 José de San Martín Crossing of the Andes from Mendoza to Chile produced the 12 February 1818 Chilean Declaration of Independence. The 26-27 July 1822 Guayaquil meeting between Bolívar and San Martín — at which San Martín withdrew from the northern-South-American campaign — left Bolívar in single command of the post-1822 independence sequence. The 9 December 1824 Battle of Ayacucho under Sucre — the decisive defeat of the royalist forces in Peru — completed the continental independence. The 6 August 1825 Bolivian independence at Chuquisaca named the new republic for Bolívar himself.

The Brazilian independence path was distinct. The 1808 Portuguese royal family flight from Lisbon to Rio de Janeiro under British naval escort produced the 13-year Portuguese-court-in-Brazil period. The 7 September 1822 "Grito do Ipiranga" by Pedro I (son of João VI of Portugal) declared Brazilian independence as constitutional monarchy. The subsequent 1822-1889 Brazilian Empire under Pedro I (r. 1822-1831) and Pedro II (r. 1831-1889) was the only post-independence Latin American monarchy. The 15 November 1889 military coup under Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca deposed Pedro II; the First Brazilian Republic was declared.

The post-independence republican settlements across most of Latin America operated under the decades-long *caudillismo* settlement — strongman-and-faction political-class settlements that the post-1820s territorial-and-economic instability produced. The 1846-1848 Mexican-American War — driven by the US territorial-expansionist Manifest Destiny doctrine and the 1845 US annexation of Texas — produced Mexican loss of roughly 55% of territory under the 2 February 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, California, Utah, Nevada, and parts of Colorado, Wyoming, Kansas, and Oklahoma). The 1853 Gadsden Purchase added southern Arizona and southwestern New Mexico for $10 million. The 1864-1867 French intervention in Mexico under Emperor Maximilian, installed at the request of Mexican conservatives by Napoleon III in 1864, ended on 19 June 1867 with the execution of Maximilian at Querétaro on orders of Benito Juárez.

The 1879-1884 War of the Pacific — Chile against Peru and Bolivia over nitrate-rich coastal territories — ended with Chilean victory; Bolivia lost its Pacific coast (Atacama region) and subsequent landlocked status. The 1864-1870 War of the Triple Alliance (Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay against Paraguay under Francisco Solano López) killed roughly 60-90% of the pre-war Paraguayan adult male population and reduced Paraguay to roughly one-quarter of its pre-war territory.

The 1898 Spanish-American War — triggered by the 15 February 1898 USS Maine explosion in Havana harbour (cause disputed; contemporary US press blamed Spain) — produced the US acquisition of Cuba (1898-1902 occupation, then protectorate under the 1901 Platt Amendment until 1934), Puerto Rico (incorporated as US territory), Philippines (acquired from Spain for $20 million; 1899-1902 Philippine-American War killed roughly 250,000-1 million Filipinos), and Guam from Spain. The 1898 war marked the decisive US emergence as the continental-hemisphere dominant political-class apparatus. The 1903 US-engineered Panamanian secession from Colombia (to enable the 1903 Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty granting US canal-zone sovereignty) and the 1904 Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine institutionalised the US-and-Latin-American dependency political-class arrangement that the twentieth century would extend.

## The Scramble for Africa and the 1884-1885 Berlin Conference

The 1830 French invasion of Algiers launched the 132-year French colonial period in Algeria. The 1869 Suez Canal completion (under Ferdinand de Lesseps's Suez Canal Company) and the 1882 British occupation of Egypt following the Urabi revolt produced the decisive northeastern African strategic-and-commercial-and-political-class transformations of the preceding century. The 1879 Anglo-Zulu War — the 22 January 1879 Battle of Isandlwana (British defeat with roughly 1,300 British casualties) and the 4 July 1879 Battle of Ulundi (British victory and Zulu Kingdom defeat) — was the most dramatic single-battle European-and-African military encounter of the pre-1885 period.

The 15 November 1884 - 26 February 1885 Berlin West Africa Conference, convened at 77 Wilhelmstrasse Berlin under German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck at the request of King Leopold II of Belgium, was the single most consequential institutional event of the nineteenth-century Scramble for Africa. Fourteen countries attended: Austria-Hungary, Belgium, Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Portugal, Russia, Spain, Sweden-Norway, the Ottoman Empire, the United Kingdom, and the United States. No African nation attended or was consulted. The General Act of Berlin, signed on 26 February 1885, established the "effective occupation" principle under which European claims to African territories were recognised only on demonstration of actual administrative control through treaties with local leaders, administrative establishment, or military/police deployment.

The subsequent 1885-1914 partition of Africa moved European territorial control from roughly 10% of the continental territory in 1884 to roughly 90% by 1914 — across the 30-year period that the 1885 Berlin framework legitimated. The post-Berlin arbitrary territorial boundaries cut through roughly 190 cultural-and-linguistic groups per the demographic analysis. The partition was structurally analogous to the 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas Spanish-Portuguese division of the pre-Columbian Americas — a European political-class cartographic settlement that the populations of the territories partitioned had no input into.

The single most atrocious post-Berlin administrative settlement was the Congo Free State (1885-1908), operated as the personal property of King Leopold II of Belgium across 23 years. The rubber-and-ivory extraction apparatus, operating through corporate concession companies (Compagnie du Kasaï, ABIR Congo Company, and others) and through the Force Publique mercenary army, killed roughly 10 million Congolese across the 1885-1908 period per Adam Hochschild's *King Leopold's Ghost* (Houghton Mifflin, 1998). The 1908 international outcry over Leopold's atrocities — driven by the Congo Reform Association under Edmund Dene Morel and Roger Casement's 1903-1904 Congo report — forced the 15 November 1908 transfer of the Congo Free State to Belgian government control. The Belgian Congo continued to operate the extraction apparatus under reduced atrocity levels until the 30 June 1960 independence.

The 1904-1907 Herero and Nama Genocide in German Southwest Africa (modern Namibia) was the single most systematic post-Berlin atrocity. The 12 January 1904 Herero uprising against German settler land seizures triggered the multi-year suppression under General Lothar von Trotha. Von Trotha's 2 October 1904 *Vernichtungsbefehl* (extermination order) directed the elimination of the Herero population; the pursuit into the Omaheke Desert and the concentration-camp internment killed roughly 65,000 Herero (roughly 80% of the pre-1904 population) and roughly 10,000 Nama (roughly 50% of the pre-1904 population) across the 1904-1908 period. The Herero-and-Nama Genocide was the first twentieth-century genocide on the historical record; it preceded the 1915-1923 Armenian Genocide and the 1941-1945 Holocaust by multiple decades and prefigured both in its systematic-administrative character.

The 1899-1902 Second Boer War — British forces against the Boer republics of the Transvaal and the Orange Free State — produced the 31 May 1902 Treaty of Vereeniging that incorporated the Boer territories into the British Empire. The subsequent 1910 Union of South Africa under British dominion status created the segregationist political-class settlement that the 1948 apartheid system formalised — the subject of the African dossiers of Part VI.

By 1914, the partition of Africa was complete in all but symbolic detail. Liberia (the US-Black-American settlement founded 1822 by the American Colonization Society) and Ethiopia (which on 1 March 1896 had defeated the Italian invasion under General Oreste Baratieri at the Battle of Adwa — the single most consequential African military victory over a European power on the pre-1945 historical record, producing the Treaty of Addis Ababa that recognised Ethiopian independence) were the only independent African states. The subsequent 1935-1936 Italian invasion of Ethiopia under Mussolini reversed the 1896 settlement temporarily; the 1941 British-and-Ethiopian liberation restored Haile Selassie I. The subsequent twentieth-century African political class is the subject of Chapters 5 and 6 and of the African dossiers of Part VI.

## The American political class — Civil War through Gilded Age through Jekyll Island 1910

The 19th-century US political class moved through three institutional inflections: the 1861-1865 Civil War, the post-1877 Gilded Age robber-baron consolidation, and the 22-30 November 1910 Jekyll Island meeting that produced the 1913 Federal Reserve Act.

The pre-Civil-War American political class operated under the North-South sectional crisis that the 1820 Missouri Compromise, the 1850 Compromise, the 1854 Kansas-Nebraska Act, and the 6 March 1857 Dred Scott decision had progressively escalated. The 6 November 1860 election of Abraham Lincoln on a Republican platform opposed to the territorial extension of slavery triggered the 20 December 1860 secession of South Carolina; ten other Southern states followed across the six months.

The 12 April 1861 - 9 April 1865 American Civil War was the single most consequential nineteenth-century internal political-class restructuring in the trans-Atlantic world. The roughly 750,000 cumulative military deaths (recently revised upward from the earlier 620,000 estimate per the 2011 J. David Hacker demographic analysis) made it the single deadliest war on North American territory. The 14 April 1865 assassination of Lincoln at Ford's Theatre by John Wilkes Booth, five days after the 9 April 1865 Appomattox surrender of Robert E. Lee, transferred the post-war political-class settlement to Vice President Andrew Johnson. The subsequent 18 December 1865 13th Amendment abolished slavery; the 9 July 1868 14th Amendment established birthright citizenship and equal-protection-and-due-process guarantees; the 3 February 1870 15th Amendment prohibited racial-and-prior-servitude voting restrictions.

The 1865-1877 Reconstruction period attempted the comprehensive reconstruction of the Southern political-class settlement on the basis of universal-male-suffrage-and-Federal-occupation enforcement. The 1868 impeachment of Andrew Johnson (acquitted in the Senate by single vote) and the 1869-1877 Ulysses S. Grant administration extended the Reconstruction period. The 1873-1877 multi-scandal period (Crédit Mobilier, Whiskey Ring, Black Friday gold-corner attempt under Jay Gould and Jim Fisk) discredited the Grant administration; the 1876 contested election between Rutherford B. Hayes (Republican) and Samuel J. Tilden (Democrat) was resolved through the Compromise of 1877 — Hayes's inauguration in exchange for end of Reconstruction. The subsequent post-Reconstruction racial-restoration regime in the South — through the 1890s Plessy-v.-Ferguson "separate but equal" doctrine, the 1890s-1900s black-disenfranchisement constitutional conventions, and the 1890s-1960s Jim Crow segregation system — converted the 14th and 15th Amendments into dead-letter constitutional provisions for the subsequent 80 years.

The 1877-1900 Gilded Age produced the single most concentrated commercial-political-class consolidation in the American historical record before the post-1980 tech-and-finance consolidation (which the dossiers of Part III document). The John D. Rockefeller-and-Standard Oil consolidation from 1870 onward, the Andrew Carnegie-and-Carnegie Steel consolidation through the 1901 US Steel merger, the J.P. Morgan-and-First-National-Bank-of-New-York financial-political consolidation, the Vanderbilt-and-Astor commercial-and-political dynasties — produced by 1900 a single-digit number of multi-billion-dollar commercial-political family fortunes that owned the majority of US commercial-and-industrial production. The 1890 Sherman Antitrust Act, the 1893 Panic, the 1896 Bryan-McKinley "Cross of Gold" election (in which William Jennings Bryan's populist anti-gold-standard campaign was defeated by William McKinley's pro-gold-and-Eastern-business platform funded by roughly $3.5 million from Rockefeller, Morgan, and other Gilded Age fortunes), and the 1907 Panic produced the banking-political crisis that the 1910 Jekyll Island plan addressed.

The 22-30 November 1910 Jekyll Island meeting was the single most consequential pre-1913 US political-class transaction. Senator Nelson Aldrich (Republican of Rhode Island, chair of the National Monetary Commission established after the 1907 Panic), A. Piatt Andrew (Assistant Secretary of the Treasury), Henry P. Davison (J.P. Morgan & Co. Partner), Frank Vanderlip (National City Bank president, Rockefeller-aligned), Charles D. Norton (First National Bank of New York president), Benjamin Strong (Bankers Trust vice president), and Paul Warburg (Kuhn, Loeb & Co. Partner) met at the Jekyll Island Club (Georgia, a winter resort owned by the J.P. Morgan-and-Rockefeller-and-Vanderbilt commercial-political-class collective) to draft what became the Federal Reserve Act.

The subsequent 23 December 1913 Federal Reserve Act signed by Woodrow Wilson — passed in the days before Christmas recess when multiple opposed congressmen had left Washington — established the US central bank as the 12-regional-reserve-bank network under the Federal Reserve Board appointed by the President. The arrangement was the North American institutional parallel to the 1694 Bank of England chartering (Chapter 2). The 1913 settlement was the decisive institutional product of the 1910 Jekyll Island meeting. The subsequent twentieth-century US political-class apparatus operated on the basis of the 1913 Federal Reserve architecture — the subject of the Atlantic dossiers of Part III and of Chapter 5.

## Bismarckian Germany

The 23 September 1862 appointment of Otto von Bismarck as Minister-President of Prussia under Wilhelm I launched the German unification. The 1864 Second Schleswig War defeated Denmark; the 1866 Austro-Prussian War (Seven Weeks War) defeated Habsburg Austria at the 3 July 1866 Battle of Königgrätz; the 1870-1871 Franco-Prussian War defeated Second Empire France and produced the 2 September 1870 capture of Napoleon III at Sedan. The 18 January 1871 proclamation of the German Empire at the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles installed Wilhelm I as Kaiser and Bismarck as Chancellor of the unified empire.

The subsequent 19-year Bismarck Chancellorship operated through three institutional axes. First, the 1872-1878 *Kulturkampf* against the Catholic Centre Party and the Catholic political-class establishment was the failed attempt to subordinate the Catholic Church to the state. Second, the 21 October 1878 Anti-Socialist Laws (renewed every four years until 1890) suppressed the Social Democratic Party organisational activity while permitting individual SPD parliamentary candidates. Third — and most consequential for the twentieth-century European political-class settlement — the 1883-1889 welfare-state legislation under Bismarck created the first comprehensive modern welfare state: 1883 Sickness Insurance Law, 1884 Accident Insurance Law, 15 June 1889 Old Age and Disability Insurance Law. The Bismarckian welfare-state apparatus was institutionalised explicitly as a counter-socialist strategy — Bismarck's 1881 parliamentary statement: "Those who have a pension for their old age are far more contented and easier to handle than those who have no such prospect."

The 18 March 1890 dismissal of Bismarck by the new Kaiser Wilhelm II (who had succeeded his father Friedrich III after Friedrich's 99-day reign in 1888) ended the 28-year Bismarck era. The subsequent pre-1914 Wilhelmine German political class operated under the multi-faction parliamentary-and-imperial settlement that the 1914 World War crisis converted into the 1918 collapse — the subject of Chapter 5.

## French Second Empire and Third Republic

The 2 December 1851 coup of Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte (nephew of the first Napoleon) terminated the 1848-1852 Second Republic; the 2 December 1852 proclamation of the Second Empire installed Louis-Napoléon as Emperor Napoleon III. The subsequent 18-year reign produced the 1853-1870 Paris reconstruction under Baron Georges-Eugène Haussmann (Prefect of the Seine 1853-1870) that created the modern boulevard-and-rue Paris and displaced roughly 350,000 poor Parisians to the peripheral districts.

The 1870-1871 Franco-Prussian War (covered in the Bismarck section above) terminated the Second Empire. The 4 September 1870 proclamation of the Third Republic at the Paris Hôtel de Ville produced the subsequent 70-year French republican settlement that lasted until the 10 July 1940 Vichy installation. The 18 March - 28 May 1871 Paris Commune — a 72-day radical-republican-and-socialist insurrection produced by the post-Sedan governmental collapse — was suppressed by the Versailles-government forces under Adolphe Thiers. The "bloody week" of 21-28 May 1871 killed roughly 20,000 Communards; the multi-year repression imprisoned roughly 38,000 and deported roughly 4,000 to New Caledonia.

The 1894-1906 Dreyfus Affair — triggered by the 15 October 1894 arrest of Captain Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish artillery officer on the French General Staff, on the charge of treason — divided the Third Republic political class for twelve years between pro-Dreyfus (Dreyfusard) republicans-and-intellectuals (including Émile Zola, whose 13 January 1898 *J'Accuse...!* open letter to President Faure produced the multi-year public reopening of the case) and anti-Dreyfus (anti-Dreyfusard) clerical-and-military-and-monarchist forces. Dreyfus's 1899 re-trial-and-pardon and 1906 full exoneration produced the Republican-and-secularist consolidation that the 9 December 1905 law on the separation of churches and state institutionalised. The Dreyfus Affair is the single most consequential nineteenth-century French political-class internal struggle and the structural foundation of the twentieth-century French secular-republican settlement.

## Romanov Russia — Alexander II through 1905 Revolution

The 1801-1825 Alexander I reign and the 1825-1855 Nicholas I reign maintained the post-Peter-the-Great autocratic-service-class settlement (Chapter 2). The 1853-1856 Crimean War — Russia against Ottoman Empire, Britain, France, and Sardinia-Piedmont — ended in Russian defeat under the 30 March 1856 Treaty of Paris; the subsequent 1855 accession of Alexander II (1855-1881) launched the reform period.

The 19 February 1861 Emancipation Manifesto under Alexander II freed roughly 23 million privately-owned serfs (and another 27 million state peasants) from the pre-1861 hereditary-servile status that the 1649 Sobornoye Ulozheniye (Chapter 2) had codified. The emancipation was the largest single pre-modern legal-status restructuring on the historical record — affecting roughly 50 million people. The agricultural-and-political settlement produced peasant-redemption-payment fiscal apparatus that the 1906 reform partially reversed and the 1917 Bolshevik Revolution completely eliminated.

The subsequent Alexander II reforms produced the 1864 judicial reform (jury trial and independent judiciary), the 1864 zemstvo reform (local-government councils with limited elected representation), the 1870 municipal reform (city councils), the 1874 universal military conscription, and the 1864 education reform. The 13 March 1881 assassination of Alexander II by the Narodnaya Volya bomb at the Catherine Canal embankment in St. Petersburg ended the reform period and produced the counter-reforms under Alexander III (1881-1894).

The 1894-1917 Nicholas II reign — the last Romanov reign — produced the 1898 establishment of the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party, the 1903 split between the Bolshevik (Lenin-led) and Menshevik (Martov-led) factions, the 1904-1905 Russo-Japanese War defeat, and the 1905 Russian Revolution. The 22 January 1905 "Bloody Sunday" massacre — peaceful petition march to the Winter Palace under Father Georgy Gapon was fired upon by the Imperial Guard, killing roughly 130-200 marchers — triggered the nationwide strike-and-soviet-and-mutiny sequence. The 30 October 1905 October Manifesto under Nicholas II established the State Duma (parliament) with limited elected representation and constitutional-and-civil-rights guarantees. The subsequent 1906-1914 State Duma period operated under progressive shahist-and-bureaucratic erosion of the 1905 settlement until the 1914 World War produced the 1917 collapse — the subject of Chapter 5.

## Pre-1914 financial integration

The pre-1914 international financial-and-commercial system operated under the gold-standard architecture that the 1816 British Coinage Act had institutionalised and that the multi-country gold-standard adoption (Germany 1871, France 1873, US 1879 *de facto* and 1900 *de jure*, Russia 1897, Japan 1897, India 1898 gold-exchange standard) had extended into the global pre-1914 monetary settlement. The gold-standard architecture facilitated the unprecedented pre-1914 cross-border capital movement: roughly 60% of British savings were invested abroad by 1913; roughly $44 billion (1914 dollars) of international investment circulated under the pre-1914 gold-standard settlement.

The London City was the dominant pre-1914 international capital market. The Rothschild banking network (Chapter 3) — operating across the London, Paris, Vienna, Frankfurt, and Naples family branches — was the largest single pre-1914 international bank. The Barings Brothers (Argentina-and-South-America exposure that produced the 1890 Barings Crisis under William Lidderdale's Bank of England rescue), the J. Henry Schröder & Co., the Hambros, the Kleinwort Sons & Co., and the London merchant-banking establishment operated the secondary-and-tertiary financial intermediation. The Paris-Vienna-Frankfurt-Berlin-New York secondary financial centres operated under the pre-1914 gold-standard arbitrage architecture.

Carroll Quigley's *Tragedy and Hope* (Macmillan, 1966) — drawing on the pre-1965 internal Rhodes-Trust archives that Quigley had access to as a approved historian — documented the pre-1914 international banker network as a coherent political-class apparatus operating across multiple national jurisdictions to coordinate monetary, fiscal, and political-class outcomes.
