# Dossier 2: Donald J. Trump

Donald John Trump was born on 14 June 1946 in Queens, New York City, the fourth of five children of Fred Trump and Mary Anne MacLeod Trump. Fred Trump was the post-war New York City outer-borough residential real-estate developer, the operator of over 27,000 New York apartments by the 1970s, the documented subject of a 1954 US Senate profiteering investigation and a 1966 New York State investigation. Mary Anne MacLeod was a Scottish immigrant from the Isle of Lewis. Donald Trump attended the New York Military Academy from 1959 to 1964, Fordham University from 1964 to 1966, and the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania from 1966 to 1968, taking a Bachelor of Science degree in economics in 1968. He received five Vietnam War draft deferments — four educational and one medical, the medical deferment for "bone spurs" in the heels, the diagnosis provided by Dr Larry Braunstein, a Queens podiatrist who was documented a tenant of Fred Trump's at the time the deferment was issued. He joined the Fred Trump real-estate organisation upon his graduation from Wharton and became its president in 1971. He renamed it the Trump Organization. He has been on the public stage of American commercial-and-political life continuously since.

He was elected the 45th President of the United States in November 2016 and inaugurated on 20 January 2017. He lost the 2020 presidential election to Joe Biden, contested the result across a documented sixty-court-filing legal challenge that was unanimously rejected by every American court that heard it, and incited the 6 January 2021 attack on the United States Capitol that delayed the congressional certification of Joe Biden's election by roughly six hours. He was impeached by the House of Representatives twice — the first time in December 2019 for the Burisma-related pressure on the Ukrainian government documented in Dossier 1, the second time in January 2021 for the incitement of the Capitol attack. The Senate acquitted him in both cases. He was criminally indicted four separate times across 2023-2024. He was convicted on 30 May 2024 by a New York County jury on all 34 felony counts of falsifying business records in the first degree, becoming the first former or sitting US president in American history to be convicted of a criminal offence. He was found civilly liable for sexual abuse and defamation of E. Jean Carroll on 9 May 2023 by a federal jury in the Southern District of New York; the jury's finding of sexual abuse was subsequently clarified by the trial judge to be equivalent to "rape" in the everyday meaning of the term. He was found civilly liable for persistent business fraud in the Trump Organization on 16 February 2024 by Judge Arthur Engoron of the New York State Supreme Court, who ordered disgorgement of $354.8 million in ill-gotten gains plus pre-judgment interest. He was elected the 47th President of the United States in November 2024 and inaugurated on 20 January 2025. The legal liabilities did not prevent the re-election. The captured-press apparatus did not prevent the re-election. The post-2008 American political-class settlement, which the post-2008 captured press had been built to defend, did not prevent the re-election. The record this evidence covers is the documented record of what the political-class operator did with the access of American political office across the seventy-five years between his birth and the second inauguration.

## The Fred Trump fortune

The first fact of the Donald Trump biographical record is that he is not the self-made commercial figure he has presented himself as for fifty years. He inherited the bulk of the fortune from his father Fred Trump. The documentary establishment of this fact is the *New York Times* investigation by David Barstow, Susanne Craig, and Russ Buettner published on 2 October 2018, which drew on over 100,000 pages of confidential family tax returns and financial records supplied to the *Times* by a confidential source.

Donald Trump received the present-day equivalent of $413 million from his father Fred Trump's real-estate empire across the course of his life. The transfers began in 1949, when Donald was three years old; by age three, Donald Trump was receiving the equivalent of $200,000 per year (in 2018 dollars) from his father's real-estate empire. Fred Trump made Donald Trump a millionaire by the time Donald was eight. The total transfers from Fred and Mary Trump to their five children across their lifetimes were over $1 billion in 2018 dollars. The mechanisms of transfer were the standard wealth-transfer arrangements that the American tax-and-estate planning industry has developed over the past century: trusts established when the children were young, below-market property transfers, a 1992 subsidiary called "All County Building Supply & Maintenance" set up to funnel Fred Trump's fortune to his children through padded equipment-and-supplies invoicing, and the systematic undervaluation of properties for gift-and-estate tax purposes. The *Times* investigation documented that the family avoided over $500 million in gift and estate taxes through these arrangements — taxes that, on the American tax-law analysis, should have been paid at the 55% rate on transfers over $550 million. The post-Times-investigation legal-academic analysis (Jonathan Chait in *New York* magazine, October 2018) concluded that "many of Fred's contributions to Donald were by definition criminal in nature." The "self-made man" presentation that the public-facing Trump narrative has rested on for fifty years is documentary fiction. The dossier reports the fact.

The late-Fred-Trump succession story is documented of what the political-class operator did when he had the chance to seize the creditor's estate while the creditor was still alive. In December 1990, while Donald Trump was in the middle of his first divorce from Ivana Trump and while his Atlantic City casino debt was about to detonate into the bankruptcy sequence the next section documents, Donald Trump attempted to amend his father's will. The amendment, according to his sister Maryanne Trump Barry's later sworn legal testimony, "was basically taking the whole estate and giving it to Donald," allowing him to "sell, do anything he wants … with the properties." The *Washington Post* documentary record established that the proposed amendment "was designed to protect Donald Trump's inheritance from efforts to seize it by creditors and Ivana." Fred Trump refused the amendment. He drafted his own final will the following year, making three of his surviving children — Donald, Maryanne, and Robert — co-executors. The Fred-Trump-fortune-succession arrangement that resulted is the arrangement the 2018 *Times* investigation documented.

The point is that Donald Trump, at the moment his Atlantic City casino empire was about to collapse into the corporate bankruptcies of the next section, attempted to use his cognitively-declining father's estate as the asset against which the bankruptcy creditors could not collect. The attempt failed. The bankruptcy creditors collected. The Fred-Trump fortune nevertheless transferred to Donald Trump across the decade through the documented tax-avoidance mechanisms the 2018 *Times* investigation established. The political-class operator's behaviour in the late-Fred-Trump succession episode is the documented pattern of the political-class operator across the rest of the career: the arrangements that the creditor class makes are used to extract the assets from the creditor class itself. The arrangement is documented.

## The Atlantic City casinos and the four corporate bankruptcies

The principal commercial period of the Donald Trump career was the Atlantic City casino period, running from the 1982 grant of his New Jersey casino licence through the 1990 opening of the Trump Taj Mahal and the bankruptcy sequence that followed. The period is the documented type of the leveraged-property-development operator who pulls personal capital out of projects through development-management fees and brand-licensing payments while the projects' debt loads bankrupt the public bondholders and the subcontractor class.

Donald Trump began purchasing Atlantic City boardwalk properties in the early 1980s and received his casino licence from the New Jersey Casino Control Commission on 15 March 1982. Trump Plaza Hotel & Casino opened in 1984. Trump Castle (later renamed Trump Marina) was acquired in 1985. The flagship project of the period was the Taj Mahal, an unfinished property Trump purchased from Resorts International for $230 million in 1988 through negotiations with Merv Griffin in which the two men divided the assets of the failing Resorts company. The Taj Mahal, when it opened in April 1990, was the largest casino property in Atlantic City at the time, with a total construction cost approaching $1 billion. The financing was roughly $675 million in junk bonds at an interest rate of 14% — the financial analyst Marvin Roffman of Janney Montgomery Scott had publicly predicted before the opening that the Taj would need to generate daily revenue of $1.3 million to service the debt and would not generate the revenue. Trump publicly attacked Roffman, who lost his job at Janney Montgomery Scott; Roffman later sued and won an arbitrated settlement. Roffman's prediction was correct. The Taj Mahal filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy within a year of opening.

The 10 October 1989 helicopter crash in northern New Jersey killed three of the most Trump casino executives — Stephen F. Hyde, the CEO of Trump's casino operations; Mark Grossinger Etess, the President and Chief Operating Officer of the Taj Mahal; and Jonathan Benanav, the Trump Plaza Executive Vice President. The commercial impact of the loss of the three executives at the moment of the Taj Mahal opening is documented of Atlantic City commercial history. The Trump organisation never replaced the day-to-day management with figures of comparable seniority.

The four bankruptcies followed.

**The first bankruptcy:** Trump Taj Mahal Associates filed Chapter 11 in 1991, within a year of the Taj's opening. The debt load was roughly $1 billion across the Taj alone, $3 billion across the broader Trump corporate structure. The reorganisation: Donald Trump surrendered 50% of the Taj Mahal property to the bondholders in exchange for restructured debt at lower interest rates. The bondholders received new debt instruments worth materially less than the original bond claims — the haircut was around one-third. Donald Trump personally surrendered assets — the Trump Princess yacht, the Trump Shuttle airline, parts of the personal-asset portfolio — to the creditor banks in the parallel personal-finance restructuring. He did not personally file for bankruptcy. The personal-finance position was preserved through the creditor-bank negotiations and through the Fred-Trump-fortune transfers that, documented, supplied the cash flow that kept the personal-finance position alive across the period.

**The second bankruptcy:** Trump Plaza Associates and the related Plaza Hotel Manhattan entity filed Chapter 11 in 1992 with combined debt above $1 billion. Trump surrendered 49% of the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan to a group of six lender banks. He surrendered additional equity in Trump Castle. The bondholders again accepted restructured debt at lower interest rates. The Atlantic City subcontractor class, which had supplied the construction-and-services labour to the Trump properties across the late 1980s, received cents on the dollar in the bankruptcy reorganisations. The subcontractors developed, across the post-1992 period, the documented practice of bidding 10-20% above their target prices on Trump construction projects on the assumption that Trump would not pay the invoices in full. The piano dealer J. Michael Diehl of Freehold Music Center, who supplied eight Yamaha grand pianos to the Taj Mahal opening at a total invoiced cost of $100,000, received cents on the dollar in the bankruptcy reorganisation. Diehl's commercial position is the documented type of the Atlantic City subcontractor experience across the period. The list of subcontractors owed money at the time of the 1991 Taj bankruptcy ran to over 200 names with a collective unpaid invoice total of roughly $69 million.

**The third bankruptcy:** Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts, the umbrella public company that Trump had established in 1995 with the New York Stock Exchange ticker symbol DJT to consolidate the Atlantic City casino properties, filed Chapter 11 in 2004 with $1.8 billion in debt. The reorganisation reduced Trump's ownership stake from 56% to 28%. The company was renamed Trump Entertainment Resorts. The public bondholders again accepted restructured debt at lower interest rates and reduced face values.

**The fourth bankruptcy:** Trump Entertainment Resorts filed Chapter 11 again in 2009 with $500 million in debt. Trump resigned as chairman. His ownership stake was reduced to 10%. The creditor class — by this point dominated by Carl Icahn, who had acquired the secured debt since the post-2009 period — took control of the remaining Atlantic City properties. Trump Entertainment Resorts filed Chapter 11 a third time in 2014. Trump's name was removed from the remaining properties in 2014-2017. The commercial residue of the Trump Atlantic City period was zero — every Trump-branded Atlantic City casino property was closed or sold or demolished by 2021.

The commercial-class assessment of the Trump Atlantic City period is the documented pattern that this volume's voice rules treat as proven. The leveraged property developer puts 90% of the project finance on the public bond market, pulls management fees and brand-licensing payments out of the project across the years it is running, then files corporate bankruptcy when the debt load exceeds the available cash flow. The public bondholders take the haircut. The subcontractor class takes the cents-on-the-dollar settlement. The banks renegotiate. The promoter walks away with the management fees already collected, the personal-finance position structurally protected from corporate liabilities, and the brand-equity position intact for the next commercial venture. The arrangement is the standard procedure of the late-twentieth-century American leveraged property-development apparatus. Donald Trump was the principal public exemplar of the arrangement. The four bankruptcies are documented.

## The Apprentice and the brand-licensing period

The post-bankruptcy period of the Donald Trump career was the brand-licensing period running from around 2000 through the 2015 presidential campaign announcement. The commercial vehicle of the period was *The Apprentice*, the Mark Burnett Productions reality-television programme that premiered on NBC in January 2004 and ran for fourteen seasons. *The Apprentice* paid Trump roughly $213 million across 2004-2015 in salary, executive producer fees, and brand-licensing royalties — the figure is from Trump's own 2015 disclosures. The commercial point of *The Apprentice* was the rehabilitation of the Trump brand-equity position after the 1990s bankruptcies, which the commercial press had documented as the business-failure pattern of the period.

The post-Apprentice arrangement was the brand-licensing model. Trump licensed his name to real-estate developers and consumer-product manufacturers in exchange for licensing fees that, on the Forbes and Bloomberg analyses of the period, ran in the range of 15-25% of the project value or product gross revenue. The Trump Organization did not develop the properties. The Trump Organization did not manufacture the consumer products. The Trump Organization licensed the Trump name. The commercial risk of the projects was carried by the licensee developers and manufacturers. The commercial upside, when there was upside, was split with the licensee. The arrangement was the post-bankruptcy commercial arrangement that the leveraged-property-developer figure had to construct after the creditor class had concluded that direct lending to Trump-owned-and-operated entities was the loss-making arrangement of the 1990s Atlantic City period.

The Trump-licensed projects of the period included Trump SoHo in Manhattan (2010), the Trump International Hotel and Tower Toronto (2012), Trump Vancouver (2017), and a series of consumer-product ventures: Trump Steaks, Trump Vodka, Trump University, Trump magazine, Trump Mortgage, the Trump Network multi-level-marketing scheme. Most of the consumer-product ventures failed within five years of launch. Trump University, the for-profit real-estate-seminar business marketed as a university across 2005-2010, was the documented commercial-fraud venture of the period. The fraud allegations — that Trump University had marketed itself through documented deceptive sales practices and that the product delivered to the students was the unaccredited seminar-and-mentorship arrangement that the promotional materials had concealed — produced lawsuits from the New York State Attorney General Eric Schneiderman (filed in 2013) and class-action plaintiffs in California. Trump settled the lawsuits in November 2016, after his election but before his inauguration as 45th President, for $25 million. The settlement was completed in 2018.

The post-2008 period was, documented of the Trump-Organization-and-licensed-property portfolio, the period of post-bankruptcy brand-equity rebuild that the 2015 presidential campaign was the commercial-political extension of. The public-facing Trump narrative across the period was the "successful businessman" presentation that *The Apprentice* had established and that the 2016 presidential campaign was built on. The documentary record is that the commercial position was the brand-licensing position resting on the reality-television-celebrity position resting on the post-1990s-bankruptcy reconstruction of the public-facing presentation that the 1990s commercial collapse had documented as fictional.

## The first presidency (2017-2021)

Donald Trump announced his presidential campaign at Trump Tower on 16 June 2015. He defeated sixteen Republican Party primary candidates across the 2015-2016 primary cycle and was nominated as the Republican presidential candidate at the Cleveland convention in July 2016. He defeated Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton on 8 November 2016 with 304 Electoral College votes to her 227, while losing the popular vote by roughly 2.9 million votes. He was inaugurated as the 45th President of the United States on 20 January 2017. He served one four-year term.

The principal legislative output of the first term was the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of December 2017, which reduced the federal corporate income tax rate from 35% to 21% and produced tax benefits for high-income individuals that the post-2017 fiscal-policy analysis (Tax Policy Center, Joint Committee on Taxation, Congressional Budget Office) has documented as having delivered the principal fiscal benefit to the American corporate-and-investor class. The principal judicial output of the first term was three Supreme Court nominations — Neil Gorsuch (2017, replacing Antonin Scalia), Brett Kavanaugh (2018, replacing Anthony Kennedy), Amy Coney Barrett (2020, replacing Ruth Bader Ginsburg) — all confirmed by the Republican Senate, which together produced the 6-3 conservative majority that decided *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* in June 2022, overturning *Roe v. Wade* and returning the abortion-regulation question to the states. The principal foreign-policy output of the first term included the withdrawal from the Iran nuclear deal (May 2018), the withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement (June 2017), the renegotiation of NAFTA into the USMCA (signed 2018, in force 2020), the trade war with China (2018-2020), the Abraham Accords (2020), and Operation Warp Speed, the COVID-19 vaccine-development programme (2020).

The first impeachment proceedings in December 2019 to February 2020 turned on the 25 July 2019 phone call between Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in which Trump asked Zelensky to investigate the Burisma arrangement that the Biden dossier above has documented. The House impeachment articles were abuse of power and obstruction of Congress. The Senate acquittal was along party lines, with the exception of Senator Mitt Romney of Utah, who voted to convict on the abuse-of-power article. The public-record point of the first impeachment is that Trump was impeached for asking the Ukrainian government to investigate the documented Burisma arrangement involving Joe Biden's son. The Burisma arrangement, as Dossier 1 has established, was on the public record. The political-class arrangement of the first impeachment is the documented instance of the political-class apparatus moving to suppress the investigation of a documented arrangement involving a fellow political-class operator. The impeachment failed. The 2020 presidential election proceeded.

The COVID-19 pandemic response across 2020 was the visible operational failure of the Trump first term. The documented record includes the Trump public statements across January-March 2020 minimising the severity of the pandemic ("It's going to disappear. One day, it's like a miracle, it will disappear" — 27 February 2020), the contemporaneous Trump private statements to Bob Woodward acknowledging the severity of the pandemic ("This is deadly stuff," 7 February 2020; "I wanted to always play it down," 19 March 2020), and the late-2020 acceleration of the Operation Warp Speed vaccine-development programme that produced the first authorised COVID-19 vaccines in December 2020. The US COVID-19 death toll across 2020 was roughly 350,000.

The 2020 presidential election produced Trump's defeat by Joe Biden — 232 Electoral College votes for Trump to 306 for Biden; popular vote margin of roughly 7 million for Biden. The post-election period, from 3 November 2020 through 20 January 2021, was the documented sequence of legal challenges to the election result. Trump's post-election legal team filed around sixty court challenges across the swing states. Every court that heard the challenges rejected them, including the federal courts staffed with judges Trump had appointed. The public claims of "election fraud" that Trump's post-election political-class apparatus pushed across the period were rejected on every institutional front. The election-administration apparatus of the swing states rejected them (including Republican Secretaries of State in Georgia, Arizona, and elsewhere). The federal courts rejected them. The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency rejected them — its then-director Christopher Krebs was dismissed by Trump on 17 November 2020 for declaring the election "the most secure in American history." And the Trump Department of Justice itself rejected them: Attorney General William Barr told the Associated Press on 1 December 2020 that the DOJ had found no evidence of election fraud sufficient to overturn the result.

The 2 January 2021 phone call between Trump and Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger is the documented audio record of the post-election episode. Trump on the call: "I just want to find 11,780 votes, which is one more than we have." The 6 January 2021 events were the documented attempt to interrupt the congressional certification of the 2020 election result. Trump's rally near the White House that morning included the public statement to the crowd: "If you don't fight like hell, you're not going to have a country anymore." The crowd marched to the Capitol. Roughly 2,000 of the crowd breached the Capitol building. Five people died in the immediate aftermath, including Capitol Police officer Brian Sicknick. Four additional Capitol Police officers died by suicide in the months following. The congressional certification of Joe Biden's election was delayed by the six hours noted earlier.

The second impeachment in January-February 2021 was for incitement of insurrection. The House vote was 232-197, with ten Republicans voting to impeach — the largest cross-party impeachment vote in American history. The Senate vote on conviction was 57-43, with seven Republican senators voting to convict — the largest cross-party Senate impeachment vote in American history. The conviction failed by ten votes (the constitutional threshold being two-thirds of the Senate, 67 votes). The political-class settlement of the post-2021 Trump position was that Trump remained the dominant Republican-party political figure across the 2021-2024 period, defeated thirteen Republican primary opponents in the 2024 primary cycle, and returned to the 2024 general election as the Republican nominee.

## The four indictments and the civil judgments (2023-2024)

Across the period from March 2023 to August 2023, four separate prosecutorial authorities — the Manhattan District Attorney's office (Alvin Bragg), the federal Special Counsel's office (Jack Smith), the Fulton County District Attorney's office (Fani Willis), and the federal Special Counsel's office a second time (Jack Smith) — indicted Donald Trump on a combined ninety-one criminal counts across four separate cases. The cumulative case load was the largest documented prosecutorial mobilisation against a former American president in American history. The parallel civil-case load — the E. Jean Carroll cases, the New York Attorney General civil-fraud case — added the civil-liability dimension. The period from 30 March 2023 to 11 January 2025 was the public-record period of legal exposure that the Trump political-class position simultaneously absorbed and converted into the 2024 re-election campaign.

The 30 March 2023 indictment by the Manhattan District Attorney Alvin Bragg charged Trump with 34 felony counts of falsifying business records in the first degree under New York Penal Law § 175.10. The factual basis was the $130,000 payment from Trump's personal lawyer Michael Cohen to adult-film actress Stormy Daniels in October 2016, made to prevent Daniels from publicly disclosing her allegation of a 2006 sexual encounter with Trump, and Trump's subsequent recording of his reimbursement payments to Cohen as "legal expenses" rather than as the campaign-finance violations that the post-trial finding established the payments factually constituted. The six-week trial in April-May 2024 was the first criminal trial of a former American president in American history. The 30 May 2024 jury verdict — guilty on all 34 felony counts after nine and a half hours of deliberation — was the first criminal conviction of a former American president in American history. The 10 January 2025 sentencing by Judge Juan Merchan, ten days before the second inauguration, was the "unconditional discharge" — the lightest possible sentence available under New York state law — citing the constitutional impracticality of imposing a meaningful penalty on a President-elect. Trump remains a convicted felon under New York law. He filed a formal appeal of the conviction in October 2025.

The 8 June 2023 federal indictment by Special Counsel Jack Smith in the Southern District of Florida charged Trump with retention of national defense information under the Espionage Act and obstruction of justice. The factual basis was the post-presidency retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago and the obstruction of federal recovery efforts. The 15 July 2024 dismissal by Judge Aileen Cannon — on the documented ground that Special Counsel Smith had been unlawfully appointed — was on appeal at the Eleventh Circuit when Trump won the November 2024 election. The Justice Department under Smith subsequently moved to dismiss the case with prejudice consistent with longstanding Justice Department policy against the prosecution of a sitting president. The factual record of the Mar-a-Lago documents — including the photographic record of classified documents stored in unsecured boxes in a Mar-a-Lago bathroom and storage room — remains on the public record.

The 1 August 2023 federal indictment by Special Counsel Jack Smith in the District of Columbia charged Trump with four counts: conspiracy to defraud the United States, conspiracy to obstruct an official proceeding, obstruction of an official proceeding, and conspiracy against rights. The factual basis was the post-2020-election conspiracy to overturn the election result. The 1 July 2024 Supreme Court decision in *Trump v. United States* — the immunity decision, 603 US 593 — held that former presidents have absolute immunity for "official acts" and presumptive immunity for "all official conduct." The decision was 6-3 along the conservative-liberal axis. Justice Sotomayor in dissent: "the President is now a king above the law." The decision materially narrowed the indictable conduct in the January 6 case. The post-November-2024-election dismissal motion by the Smith team again followed DOJ policy.

The 14 August 2023 state indictment by Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis charged Trump and 18 co-defendants under the Georgia state racketeering statute (RICO) with the post-2020-election conspiracy to overturn the Georgia state results. The factual basis included the 2 January 2021 phone call to Brad Raffensperger. The December 2024 Georgia Court of Appeals removal of DA Willis from the case over the documented romantic relationship with special prosecutor Nathan Wade left the case in legal limbo. The status of the case as of this volume's publication remains unresolved.

The parallel civil cases produced the parallel civil-liability findings. The 9 May 2023 federal jury verdict in the Carroll II case found Trump civilly liable for sexual abuse and defamation of E. Jean Carroll, awarded $5 million in damages, and was upheld by the Second Circuit on 30 December 2024. The 26 January 2024 federal jury verdict in the Carroll I defamation case awarded Carroll an additional $83.3 million in damages for Trump's 2019 defamatory statements; the Second Circuit rejected Trump's appeal in April 2026. The 16 February 2024 New York civil-fraud judgment by Judge Arthur Engoron ordered Trump and the Trump Organization to disgorge $354.8 million in ill-gotten gains plus $98.6 million in pre-judgment interest, banned Trump from serving as officer or director of any New York company for three years, and imposed an independent monitor on the Trump Organization for three years. The Engoron opinion: "the frauds shock the conscience"; the defendants' "complete lack of contrition and remorse borders on pathological." The August 2025 appellate decision by the New York Appellate Division, First Department, voided the disgorgement penalty on Eighth Amendment proportionality grounds while preserving the underlying findings of fraud and the bar on Trump's service as a New York corporate officer.

The documentary point is direct. The American legal-investigative apparatus — federal prosecutors, state prosecutors, federal civil juries, state civil judges — produced, across the period from May 2023 to February 2024, a cascading set of findings that Donald Trump had committed sexual abuse, defamation, business fraud, and 34 felony counts of falsifying business records. The US Supreme Court, in the 6-3 immunity decision of 1 July 2024, established that the President had absolute immunity for "official acts" and presumptive immunity for all "official conduct" — a doctrine that, as Justice Sotomayor's dissent recorded, makes the post-2024 American Presidency the office above the law. The American voters, in the 5 November 2024 election, elected Donald Trump to a second term despite the 34-count felony conviction, the civil-liability findings, and the pending federal indictments. The political-class settlement of the post-2024-election period is the record of the American political class accepting that the American legal apparatus does not have the authority to prevent a political-class operator with the record from returning to the political office that the evidence concerns. The arrangement is the 2024-2025 American political-class settlement. The dossier reports it.

## The second presidency (2025-present)

Donald Trump was inaugurated as the 47th President of the United States on 20 January 2025. The personnel of the second term reflect the post-2020 Trump-aligned political-class network: Pam Bondi as Attorney General, Marco Rubio as Secretary of State, Pete Hegseth as Secretary of Defense, Kristi Noem as Secretary of Homeland Security, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. As Secretary of Health and Human Services, Tulsi Gabbard as Director of National Intelligence. The 2025 legislative-and-executive output of the second term has included the tariff programme imposed across March-September 2025 on imports from China, the European Union, Canada, and Mexico at rates materially above the pre-2025 trade-policy baseline; the Department of Government Efficiency operations co-headed by Elon Musk through July 2025; the personnel-and-policy reorganisations of the federal civil service. The commercial-political arrangements of the second term — the Riviera proposal for Gaza in February 2025, the Gaza Great Trust arrangement involving Tony Blair and Saudi sovereign-wealth funds, the Smart Zone arrangement involving Elon Musk and the Arctic territories — are covered in the Prologue of this volume. The point of the second presidency is that the political-class operator returned to the political office that the legal record of the post-2021 period had documented as outside the structural control the office had previously been understood to be subject to, and operated the office in the pattern that the pre-2021 dossier had established.

The closing point of this dossier is the evidence of the figure. Donald Trump inherited the post-war Fred Trump fortune through documented tax-avoidance arrangements. He transformed the inheritance into the 1980s leveraged-property-development position; lost the development position into the four corporate bankruptcies of the 1990s and 2000s; and rebuilt the commercial position through the brand-licensing model and the reality-television-celebrity position across the 2000s and 2010s. He converted the celebrity position into the 2016 presidential election; operated the presidency through the 2017-2021 first term that produced the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and the three Supreme Court appointments; lost the 2020 presidential election; and incited the 6 January 2021 attack on the congressional certification of the election result. He absorbed the post-presidency criminal-and-civil legal exposure of the 2023-2024 period that included the 34-count felony conviction, the Carroll sexual-abuse-and-defamation findings, and the $354.8 million civil-fraud judgment. He then returned to the 2024 presidential election; won the 2024 presidential election; and resumed the political office that the legal record had documented as outside the control the office had previously been understood to be subject to.

The public-facing presentation across the entire seventy-five-year career — the "self-made man" presentation, the "successful businessman" presentation, the "law-and-order President" presentation, the "drain the swamp" presentation — is the presentation that the record this dossier has assembled documents as fictional. The dossier method this volume follows treats the records as separate and reports both. The institutional record speaks for itself.

# Dossier 3: Barack Hussein Obama II

Barack Hussein Obama II was born on 4 August 1961 in Honolulu, Hawaii, the son of Barack Hussein Obama Sr., a Kenyan economist who had come to the United States as an exchange student, and Stanley Ann Dunham, an American anthropologist from Wichita, Kansas. His parents separated in 1963, divorced in 1964; his mother remarried in 1965 and moved the family to Jakarta, Indonesia, in 1967. Obama returned to Honolulu in 1971 to live with his maternal grandparents and attended the Punahou School from 1971 to 1979. He took his undergraduate degree from Columbia University in 1983 after transferring from Occidental College in 1981, worked as a community organiser in New York from 1983 to 1985 and in Chicago from 1985 to 1988, and took his JD magna cum laude from Harvard Law School in 1991, becoming the first African-American president of the *Harvard Law Review* in February 1990. He married Michelle Robinson, a Princeton-and-Harvard-Law-educated attorney from the south side of Chicago, on 3 October 1992. He practised law in Chicago and lectured at the University of Chicago Law School from 1992 to 2004. He served in the Illinois State Senate from 1997 to 2004, representing the 13th district on the south side of Chicago. He was elected to the United States Senate from Illinois in November 2004 and served from January 2005 until his inauguration as the 44th President of the United States on 20 January 2009. He served two four-year terms and left office on 20 January 2017.

The Obama biographical record is the public-facing record on which the post-2008 American political-class settlement was sold to the American electorate. The first African-American President. The community-organiser-turned-Harvard-Law-Review-president biographical arc. The "Hope" and "Change" 2008 campaign slogans. The post-Bush rejection of the Republican neoconservative foreign-policy apparatus that had produced the 2003 Iraq invasion. The progressive-Democratic alternative to the post-1968 conservative settlement that the Reagan-Bush-Clinton-Bush sequence had operated. The record across the eight years of the Obama presidency was, documented this dossier assembles, the continuation of the post-1971 financial-political settlement, the post-2008 recapitalisation of the creditor class at the expense of the debtor class, the expansion of the covert-warfare apparatus that the Bush administration had begun, the preservation of the surveillance-state apparatus that the post-2001 War on Terror had constructed, and the post-presidency monetisation of the governing access that the Presidency had supplied. The biographical record is the public-facing presentation. The institutional record reveals what the administration actually did.

## The 2008 campaign and the Wall Street financing position

The Obama 2008 campaign raised roughly $745 million across the primary and general-election cycle — the largest American presidential campaign fundraising total in American history at the time. The campaign's public-facing presentation was the small-donor populist alternative to the corporate-funded Clinton (Democratic primary) and McCain (Republican general election) campaigns. The public-facing presentation was that Obama had built the small-donor online-fundraising apparatus that liberated him from the corporate-finance dependencies of the pre-2008 American political class. The actual fundraising record establishes the public-facing presentation as documentary fiction.

The 2008 PolitiFact analysis of the fundraising data established that roughly 43% of Obama's 2008 campaign contributions came from donors who gave more than $1,000 — the threshold above which a donor cannot reasonably be described as a small donor. Obama's public statement during the 2008 campaign was that "the vast majority of the money I got was from small donors all across the country." The PolitiFact verification established that the majority of Obama's 2008 campaign money came from donors above the $200 small-donor threshold, with 43% above the $1,000 threshold.

The Wall Street fundraising record was the largest in American presidential history at the time. Per the Center for Responsive Politics / OpenSecrets data, Obama raised roughly $17.3 million from the "securities and investment" sector in 2008 — over $7 million more than his Republican opponent John McCain raised from the same sector. Per the documented sector breakdown: $15 million from securities-and-investment firms, $3 million from commercial banks, $6 million from other financial institutions. The largest single corporate-employee contribution group was Goldman Sachs, whose employees and PAC contributed $994,795 to Obama's 2008 campaign — over four times the $230,095 they contributed to McCain. Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase, and Citigroup combined contributed over $2.5 million to Obama in 2008. McCain's three top Wall Street firms combined contributed roughly $1.5 million.

The PolitiFact verification of Hillary Clinton's 2016 statement that "President Obama took more money from Wall Street in the 2008 campaign than anybody ever had" was "Mostly True" — using the "securities and investment" sector measure, Obama set the new Wall Street fundraising record in 2008. The commercial-political point is that the creditor class of the post-2008 American political-class settlement, the same creditor class that had produced the 2008 financial collapse through the subprime mortgage securitisation apparatus and the pre-2008 derivatives-trading apparatus, financed the 2008 Democratic presidential campaign of the post-Bush candidate who was, on the public-facing presentation, the progressive alternative to the creditor class. The arrangement was visible on the documented FEC contribution records before the election. The captured commercial press did not treat the arrangement as the structural fact about the 2008 Obama campaign that it was. The arrangement was the structural fact.

## The post-2008 financial-crisis response

The post-2008 financial-crisis response was the policy operation that established what the Obama presidency would be documented. The pre-inauguration choice — the staffing of the incoming Obama Treasury and National Economic Council with figures from the pre-2008 banking apparatus — was the signalling choice that established the policy continuity with the pre-2008 Bush-administration banking response that had produced the October 2008 TARP bailout. Treasury Secretary Timothy Geithner had served, prior to the Obama appointment, as President of the New York Federal Reserve Bank across the 2003-2008 period of pre-2008 banking-regulatory failure. National Economic Council Director Lawrence Summers had served, prior to the Obama appointment, as Clinton Treasury Secretary who had signed the 1999 Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act repeal of the 1933 Glass-Steagall Act — the regulatory action that had permitted the pre-2008 commercial-and-investment-banking integration that the post-2008 critical analysis (Stoller; Yves Smith / *Naked Capitalism*; Sheila Bair) has documented as a structural cause of the 2008 collapse. White House Chief of Staff Rahm Emanuel had served as a Wasserstein Perella investment banker who had earned over $18 million in 2.5 years between his Clinton administration exit and his Obama administration appointment.

The Goldman Sachs alumni in the Obama administration staffing — Mark Patterson as Geithner's Chief of Staff at Treasury, Eileen Rominger as SEC Director of Investment Management, Gary Gensler as CFTC Chairman, Robert D. Hormats as Under Secretary of State for Economic Affairs, Gene Sperling as Director of the National Economic Council — established the post-2008 Obama-administration position that structural reform of the banking apparatus was not on the policy agenda. The figures had spent their pre-Obama careers inside the pre-2008 banking apparatus. They returned to their post-Obama careers inside the same apparatus. The Obama presidency was, on the career-trajectory record of the staffing figures, the Treasury-and-NEC operation of the banking-apparatus revolving door.

The TARP bailout that the Obama administration inherited continued through 2009-2010 as the recapitalisation of the creditor banks. The 2008 Justice Department fraud investigations of the subprime-mortgage-securitisation operations of the major banks were not prosecuted to conviction of individual executives. By the end of the Obama presidency in January 2017, no executive of any systemically-important American financial institution had been criminally prosecuted for conduct related to the 2008 collapse. The failure to prosecute was, on the post-presidency analytical literature (Jeff Connaughton's *The Payoff*, 2012; Neil Barofsky's *Bailout*, 2012; David Dayen's journalistic work for *The Intercept* and *The American Prospect*), a deliberate Obama Treasury choice rather than a constraint imposed by the facts of the cases.

## HAMP — the Home Affordable Modification Program

The signature programmatic operation of the post-2008 Obama housing policy was the Home Affordable Modification Program, announced by Obama in Mesa, Arizona, on 18 February 2009. The public-facing presentation of HAMP was that the program would assist 3 to 4 million American homeowners modify their mortgages to avoid foreclosure across the 2009-2012 period — the post-2008 housing-crisis-management programme that would be the matching component to the TARP recapitalisation of the creditor banks. The authorised funding was $50 billion from the TARP allocation, supplemented by $25 billion from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

The actual programmatic outcome was the documented failure. By the end of the Obama presidency, under 1 million American homeowners had received ongoing modification assistance through HAMP. Nearly one in three of those who had received modifications had subsequently re-defaulted after receiving inadequate modifications. Roughly 6 million American families lost their homes to foreclosure across the period 2008-2016. The $50 billion authorised programme had spent roughly $4 billion of its budget by 2012 — under 10% utilisation. The programme terminated on 31 December 2016 with the 2015 omnibus spending package's expiration clause that had been inserted at the Treasury request.

The documentary record of what HAMP was actually built to do was established in the 2012 book *Bailout: An Inside Account of How Washington Abandoned Main Street While Rescuing Wall Street* by Neil Barofsky, the Special Inspector General for TARP from 2008 to 2011. Barofsky was a Manhattan federal prosecutor, a self-described Democrat and 2008 Obama campaign donor, appointed to the SIGTARP role by George W. Bush in November 2008 and continuing through the Obama transition. Barofsky's documented account of the 2009 Congressional Oversight Panel meeting between Geithner, Elizabeth Warren (then chair of the Congressional Oversight Panel), and the TARP oversight bodies establishes what Geithner said when Warren pressed him on whether HAMP was actually designed to help homeowners. Geithner: "We estimate that they [the banks] can handle ten million foreclosures, over time. This program will help foam the runway for them."

The public statement of the Obama Treasury Secretary documented was that the purpose of the $50 billion housing-crisis-management programme was to allow the banks to absorb the foreclosure load over time, with the homeowners as the foam being steamrolled by the runway-clearing jumbo jet. The banks were the beneficiary of the programme. The homeowners were the delivery mechanism through which the programme delivered the benefit to the banks. The arrangement is documented of Barofsky's book and Warren's contemporaneous account.

The servicer-misconduct documentation in the post-HAMP litigation establishes how the arrangement worked. Bank of America's mortgage-servicing employees testified in the class-action lawsuit *In re Bank of America Home Affordable Modification Program Litigation* that they were directed to lie to homeowners about the status of their modification applications, to deliberately misplace homeowner documentation, to deny loan modifications without explanation, and to push the homeowners into foreclosure on the pretext of the missing documentation. Bank of America managers rewarded the employees for the misconduct with Target gift cards. The Treasury Department, documented of the post-HAMP analytical literature, never permanently sanctioned a mortgage servicer for HAMP violations by clawing back the incentive payments the programme had paid to the servicers.

The April 2009 cramdown amendment — the proposed amendment to the bankruptcy code that would have allowed bankruptcy judges to modify mortgage terms in personal-bankruptcy proceedings, analogous to the 1934 Frazier-Lemke Act for the Depression-era farmers — was the alternative legislative approach that would have allowed the post-2008 American homeowner class to renegotiate mortgage terms with the bankruptcy courts rather than with the mortgage servicers whose financial incentives ran against the modification approach. The cramdown amendment failed in the Senate 45-51 on 30 April 2009. The Obama administration, documented of the post-vote analytical literature, had lobbied Democratic senators against the amendment. Senate Majority Whip Dick Durbin, who had offered the amendment and who had been the Democratic-leadership operator pushing for the amendment's passage, said publicly in the post-vote period that the banks "frankly own the place." The statement was the Democratic-leadership documentary acknowledgment of the post-2008 American political-class settlement.

The post-presidency analytical conclusion (David Dayen, *The Intercept*, 28 December 2015) was that HAMP "was designed and implemented entirely by the White House" and "prioritized the health of financial institutions over homeowners." The conclusion is documented. The structural beneficiary of the post-2008 housing policy was the creditor class. The structural loser was the class of foreclosed-upon American households. The 6 million American families who lost their homes across 2008-2016 are the record of what HAMP delivered to the American electorate that the 2008 Obama campaign had been sold to as the progressive alternative to the creditor-class political-class settlement.

## Dodd-Frank and the post-2010 banking-reform settlement

The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act, signed by Obama on 21 July 2010, was the post-2008 financial-reform legislation. The public-facing presentation was that Dodd-Frank constituted the post-2008 structural reform of the American banking apparatus that would prevent the next 2008-style collapse. The post-passage analytical record establishes the arrangement on different terms.

The achievements of Dodd-Frank were the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (the Elizabeth Warren signature contribution from her Congressional Oversight Panel work), the Volcker Rule restricting proprietary trading by deposit-taking banks, the Office of Financial Research, and the Financial Stability Oversight Council. The post-passage analytical position of the post-2008 critical-finance-academic class (Yves Smith / *Naked Capitalism*; Matt Stoller; David Dayen; the anti-trust faction of the American Economic Liberties Project) was that Dodd-Frank left the post-2008 banking apparatus structurally intact. The post-Dodd-Frank American banking-sector concentration was higher in 2017 than in 2008 — the largest American banks were larger and held a larger share of American banking deposits at the end of the Obama presidency than they had held at the moment of the 2008 collapse. The Glass-Steagall restoration was not enacted. The break-up of the too-big-to-fail banks was not enacted. The criminal prosecution of the pre-2008 banking executives was not pursued.

The implementing-regulation rollout of the Volcker Rule — the 2013-2014 administrative-rulemaking process that translated the legislative text into enforceable regulation — was the moment at which the banking-industry lobbying apparatus reversed the structural intent of the original legislation. The published implementing rule, finalised in December 2013, was widely characterised by the financial-policy academic literature of the time as materially weakened from the original legislative intent. The Trump first-term 2018-2020 Volcker Rule rollbacks completed the bank-lobby reversal of the post-2008 reform. The arrangement is documented.

## The drone war and the kill list

The covert-warfare apparatus that the Bush administration had begun in the post-2001 War on Terror period was the policy area in which the Obama presidency departed from the 2008 campaign's public-facing presentation. The 2008 Obama campaign had been sold to the American electorate as the anti-war Democratic alternative to the Bush-Cheney Republican foreign-policy apparatus. The actual policy record across the eight years of the Obama presidency was the 10x expansion of the covert-drone-strike programme that the Bush administration had established at small scale in the closing months of the Bush presidency.

The pre-Obama American drone-strike record under Bush totalled roughly 57 strikes across 2004-2008, almost entirely in the tribal areas of Pakistan. The Obama presidency's first authorised military action — on 23 January 2009, three days into the presidency — was a pair of drone strikes in Waziristan, Pakistan, three hours apart, that killed up to twenty civilians. Across the full 2009 year, the Obama administration ordered 54 drone strikes in Pakistan alone — more strikes in the first year of the Obama presidency than the Bush administration had ordered across its full eight years. The peak year of 2010 saw 128 CIA drone strikes in Pakistan with at least 89 civilians killed in Pakistan per the Bureau of Investigative Journalism figures. The cumulative Obama two-term total was roughly 540 drone strikes across Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia per the Council on Foreign Relations final tally — roughly ten times the Bush total.

The civilian casualty estimates across the Obama drone war differ between the administration's published figures and the independent-monitoring class. The Bureau of Investigative Journalism recorded between 384 and 807 civilian deaths in Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia across the Obama presidency. The New America Foundation recorded between 276 and 368 civilian deaths in Pakistan and Yemen across 2004-2013. The Obama administration's public-record figures, released in July 2016, claimed only 64-116 "non-combatants" killed in 473 counter-terrorism strikes between January 2009 and end of 2015. The gap between the administration figures and the independent figures was the product of the "guilt by association" counting method that the 29 May 2012 *New York Times* investigation by Jo Becker and Scott Shane established the Obama administration was using: the counting method counted all military-age males in a strike zone as militants unless the intelligence proved otherwise.

The 29 May 2012 *New York Times* investigation, "Secret 'Kill List' Proves a Test of Obama's Principles and Will," established the documented operations of the kill list. The reporting, based on interviews with current and former Obama administration officials, established that Obama personally oversaw the kill list of al-Qaeda suspects targeted for death by unmanned drone aircraft. Obama personally approved every drone strike in Yemen and Somalia and every "risky" strike in Pakistan. The kill-list management process operated through the weekly "Terror Tuesday" White House meetings at which the list was reviewed and approved. Obama personally decided whether to launch strikes when the targets were with their families.

The kill list included American citizens. The 30 September 2011 drone strike in Yemen killed Anwar al-Awlaki, a US-born American citizen, without judicial process. The 14 October 2011 follow-up drone strike in Yemen killed Abdulrahman al-Awlaki, Anwar's 16-year-old American-born son, without judicial process and without public Obama administration explanation. The public response from Robert Gibbs, the former White House Press Secretary, when asked about the death of the 16-year-old American citizen at a public forum in October 2012, was that Abdulrahman "should have had a more responsible father." White House Chief of Staff William Daley, in the *New York Times* report, described Obama's decision to assassinate Anwar al-Awlaki, a US citizen, as "an easy one."

The point is documentary. The post-2008 Obama presidency operated, documented, a personal-presidential kill list of targeted-for-death individuals that included American citizens, that was reviewed in weekly White House meetings, that produced roughly 540 drone strikes across three countries, and that killed between several hundred and over a thousand civilians across the eight years. The arrangement was the expansion of the Bush-era covert-warfare apparatus, not its dismantlement. The 2008 Obama campaign promise to dismantle the post-2001 War on Terror apparatus was not delivered. The apparatus was expanded.

## Foreign policy — Libya, Syria, the JCPOA

The March 2011 NATO intervention in the Libyan civil war was the principal foreign-policy operation of the first Obama term. The intervention was launched under UN Security Council Resolution 1973's "responsibility to protect" mandate, with US, UK, and French air strikes beginning 19 March 2011. The outcome on 20 October 2011 was the killing of Muammar Gaddafi by Libyan opposition forces after his convoy was disabled by a US drone strike and a French air strike near Sirte. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton's public statement on the killing, in the CBS News interview that aired 20 October 2011, was: "We came, we saw, he died." The subsequent Libyan trajectory was the multi-faction civil war that has continued through the period of this volume's publication, the collapse of the North African oil producer that had the highest pre-2011 per-capita income on the African continent, and the post-2014 Mediterranean migration crisis that drove the post-2015 European political-class realignment. Obama himself, in the April 2016 interview with Chris Wallace on *Fox News Sunday*, identified the failure to plan for post-Gaddafi Libya as "the worst mistake" of his presidency.

The Syrian uprising that began on 15 March 2011 as part of the Arab Spring sequence produced the longer Obama-administration foreign-policy episode. Obama publicly demanded the Assad regime "step aside" in August 2011. On 20 August 2012, Obama announced the "red line" on chemical weapons: "We have been very clear to the Assad regime, but also to other players on the ground, that a red line for us is we start seeing a whole bunch of chemical weapons moving around or being utilized." On 21 August 2013, Syrian government forces conducted the Ghouta chemical attack with sarin nerve gas in the eastern Damascus suburbs, killing between 281 and 1,729 people across the varying estimates produced at the time. The Obama administration announced on 31 August 2013 that it would seek congressional authorisation for military strikes against the Assad regime. The authorisation effort collapsed within days as the Republican-controlled House of Representatives indicated it would not approve it, and Obama then accepted a Russian-Syrian proposal for the surrender of the declared Syrian chemical-weapons stockpile to the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons.

The parallel Obama-administration covert operation in Syria across 2013-2017 — the Timber Sycamore CIA programme, which armed Syrian opposition groups against the Assad regime — produced the unintended consequence of supplying weapons to jihadist factions including the al-Nusra Front and the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIL). The post-2014 ISIL surge, the June 2014 ISIL capture of Mosul, and the re-engagement of American forces in Iraq are the documented downstream consequences of the Obama-administration Syria policy. The arrangement is documented.

The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action — the 14 July 2015 nuclear agreement between Iran and the P5+1 (the US, UK, France, Russia, China, plus Germany) — was the diplomatic-political success of the second Obama term. The agreement committed Iran to limit its uranium enrichment to 3.67% (the civilian-power-generation level), reduce its stockpile of enriched uranium by 98%, and submit to International Atomic Energy Agency inspection in exchange for the lifting of international sanctions. The Trump first-term withdrawal from the JCPOA in May 2018 — covered in Dossier 2 — terminated the arrangement. The post-2018 Iranian nuclear programmeme has since exceeded the JCPOA enrichment limits.

## Domestic policy — the ACA, immigration, surveillance

The signature domestic-legislative achievement of the Obama presidency was the Affordable Care Act, signed 23 March 2010. The ACA expanded Medicaid eligibility, established state-and-federal health-insurance exchanges, mandated individual purchase of health insurance through the individual mandate, and prohibited insurer practices including the exclusion of pre-existing conditions. The public-option provision that the left-Democratic faction had pushed for was removed from the final bill in the negotiation with the centrist Democratic senators Joe Lieberman and Ben Nelson. The single-payer proposal was never seriously considered.

The ACA outcome reduced the American uninsured rate from roughly 18% in 2010 to roughly 8% by 2016. The structural outcome preserved the dominance of the American private health-insurance industry. The post-ACA American per-capita healthcare expenditure remained the highest in the OECD by a wide margin. The ACA was, on comparative-policy analysis, the continuation of the post-1945 American private-insurance healthcare apparatus, not its structural reform. The alternative — the single-payer model that operates across the OECD comparison set — was not on the Obama policy agenda.

The immigration-enforcement record of the Obama presidency was, documented, the deportation of roughly 3 million unauthorised immigrants across the two terms — more deportations than any prior American administration in absolute numerical terms. The immigrant-rights advocacy class labelled Obama the "Deporter-in-Chief" across 2014-2016. The 2014 surge in deportations of Central American migrants — the "border crisis" of summer 2014 — was managed through the expansion of the immigration-detention facilities that the Trump first-term immigration policy would subsequently inherit and expand. The DACA programme, announced 15 June 2012, protected roughly 800,000 unauthorised immigrants who had been brought to the US as children from deportation. The programme was the administrative-action response to the failed DREAM Act legislative effort.

The mass-surveillance disclosures of the Edward Snowden leaks, published by the *Guardian* and the *Washington Post* in June 2013, established that the National Security Agency was conducting mass surveillance of American citizens through the PRISM programme and the bulk-telephone-metadata-collection programme under Section 215 of the USA PATRIOT Act. The Obama administration's response to the Snowden disclosures was to charge Snowden under the Espionage Act, to defend the surveillance programmes as necessary national-security measures, and to reform the bulk-metadata-collection programme through the 2015 USA FREEDOM Act after the Second Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in May 2015 that the bulk-collection programme was unlawful. The Obama administration charged whistleblowers under the 1917 Espionage Act more often than all prior American administrations combined: Snowden, Chelsea Manning, John Kiriakou, Thomas Drake, Stephen Jin-Woo Kim, Jeffrey Sterling, Donald Sachtleben, James Hitselberger. The pattern was documented by the Committee to Protect Journalists analysis (October 2013), which concluded that the Obama administration was the worst administration for press freedom since the Nixon administration.

## The post-presidency monetisation

The post-presidency commercial-political arrangements of the Obamas establish the terms on which the post-Presidency American political-class operator monetises the access of the office. The arrangements have been the largest post-presidency commercial portfolio in modern American political history.

The February 2017 joint book deal with Penguin Random House for $65 million — split between the Barack Obama and Michelle Obama memoirs — was the largest post-presidency book deal in American publishing history. Michelle Obama's *Becoming*, published November 2018, became the best-selling memoir in American publishing history within twelve months. Barack Obama's *A Promised Land*, the first of his planned two-volume Presidential memoir, was published in November 2020.

The 22 May 2018 Higher Ground Productions deal with Netflix established the post-presidency film-and-television-production commercial vehicle. The financial terms were not publicly disclosed; industry estimates placed the deal value at roughly $50 million across the initial term, with the comparable Netflix talent deals (Shonda Rhimes' $150 million 5-year deal; Ryan Murphy's $300 million 5-year deal) providing the comparable benchmarks. The Higher Ground output across the years — *American Factory* (Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, 2020), *Crip Camp*, *Worth*, the *Waffles + Mochi* children's series — established the post-presidency Netflix programming pipeline.

The post-presidency speaking fees were documented at roughly $400,000 per appearance. The 2017 disclosure of a $400,000 speaking fee paid by Wall Street firm Cantor Fitzgerald to Obama for a September 2017 speech was the post-presidency commercial transaction that drew public attention to the post-presidency Wall Street monetisation. Senator Elizabeth Warren's 2017 public comment on the speaking fee: "I was troubled by that. One of the things I talk about in the book is the influence of money. I describe it as a snake that slithers through Washington." The arrangement is documented.

The post-presidency property acquisitions — the $11.75 million estate at 79 Turkeyland Cove Road, Edgartown, Martha's Vineyard, purchased in August 2019; the $8.1 million townhouse at 2446 Belmont Road NW, Kalorama, Washington DC, purchased in May 2017; the Honolulu acquisition in 2021 — establish the post-presidency residential-real-estate portfolio. The portfolio is, on comparative-historical analysis of post-presidency American Presidential households, the largest post-presidency real-estate portfolio in modern American political history.

The Obama Foundation, founded in 2014, is the post-presidency civic-engagement-and-leadership foundation; the Obama Presidential Center construction in Jackson Park, Chicago, has been the subject of ongoing community-and-historic-preservation disputes through 2017-2025. The original 2017 projected completion date of 2021 has been revised multiple times.

## The verdict

Barack Hussein Obama II was, across the eight years of the Obama presidency, the public-facing presentation of the first African-American President, the "Hope and Change" 2008 campaign candidate, the progressive-Democratic alternative to the post-1968 conservative-Democratic settlement that the Clinton-and-pre-Obama Democratic apparatus had operated. The record across the eight years was the continuation of the post-1971 American financial-political settlement across three threads.

Financial-crisis response: the post-2008 recapitalisation of the creditor class through the Treasury-and-NEC staffing arrangements; the post-2008 abandonment of the 6 million American foreclosed-upon households through HAMP; and the post-2008 preservation of the too-big-to-fail banking apparatus through the Dodd-Frank reform that did not break up the banks.

National-security expansion: the 10x expansion of the covert-drone-warfare apparatus that the Bush administration had begun; the personal-presidential kill list that included American citizens; the 3 million deportations; and the post-2013 Espionage-Act-prosecution record against whistleblowers.

Post-presidency monetisation: the post-presidency commercial monetisation of institutional access through the Penguin Random House and Netflix deals.

The Hope and Change slogan ran in 2008. The bank recapitalisation, the drone kill list, and the Netflix deal ran for the next fifteen years. The dossier reports which one was the operation.
