# Dossier 11: The Cheneys

Richard Bruce Cheney was born on 30 January 1941 in Lincoln, Nebraska, and died on 3 November 2025 at his home in McLean, Virginia, aged 84. His daughter Elizabeth Lynne Cheney, born 28 July 1966 in Madison, Wisconsin, served as US Representative for Wyoming's at-large congressional district from 2017 to 2023 and as Vice Chair of the House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6 Attack on the US Capitol from 2021 to 2023. The Cheneys are the evidentiary case of the American Republican political-class family whose father's record across the Nixon-Ford-Bush-41-Halliburton-Bush-43 sequence established the post-1969 neoconservative executive-power architecture, and whose daughter's 2017-2023 congressional career and 2021-2023 January 6 Committee work produced the post-2021 Republican-against-Trump faction that the 2022 Wyoming Republican primary defeated. The father-daughter combined trajectory documents the pre-Trump-and-post-Trump Republican-political-class generational rupture that the post-2016 American partisan realignment produced.

This dossier is organised in two parts: Part A documents Richard Bruce Cheney; Part B documents Elizabeth Lynne Cheney; the closing point integrates the two.

## Part A — Richard Bruce Cheney (1941-2025): the post-1969 executive-power architecture

### The pre-political-career trajectory

Richard Bruce Cheney was raised in Sumner, Nebraska, and Casper, Wyoming. His father was an Agriculture Department soil-conservation employee. He attended Natrona County High School in Casper, graduating in 1959. He began dating his future wife Lynne Vincent during high school. He enrolled at Yale University in autumn 1959 but dropped out. He worked as a power lineman in Wyoming. In November 1962 at age 21 he was convicted of driving while intoxicated; a second DWI conviction followed in 1963. He returned to academic study, graduating from the University of Wyoming with a BA in political science in 1965 and a MA in political science in 1966. He pursued doctoral studies at the University of Wisconsin-Madison from 1966 to 1968 without completing the degree.

He married Lynne Vincent on 29 August 1964. Their two daughters were born during the Wisconsin period: Elizabeth on 28 July 1966 and Mary on 14 March 1969. The Wisconsin period — the same 1966-1968 academic environment that produced multiple subsequent neoconservative figures — was the formation context for the Cheney intellectual trajectory.

### The Nixon-Ford apprenticeship 1969-1977

Cheney won a congressional fellowship in 1969 and arrived in Washington as an intern in the office of Congressman William A. Steiger (R-Wisconsin). His Washington trajectory accelerated rapidly under the Donald Rumsfeld patronage: aide to Rumsfeld at the Nixon-administration Office of Economic Opportunity (1969-1970); Cost of Living Council staff under Rumsfeld (1970-1973); White House Deputy Chief of Staff under Rumsfeld in the Ford administration (1974-1975); and White House Chief of Staff under President Gerald Ford from November 1975 to January 1977. At age 34 he was the youngest White House Chief of Staff in US history.

The Ford White House Chief of Staff position was the formation period for Cheney's executive-power doctrine. Cheney's subsequent public statements — including the 2002 Fox News interview in which he said "We've seen the War Powers Act, an anti-impoundment control act, and time after time after time, administrations have traded away the authority of the president to do his job. We're not going to do that in this administration. The president is bound and determined to defend those principles and to pass on this office, his and mine, to future generations in better shape than we found it" — established the Cheney project: the restoration of the post-Watergate executive-power restrictions to the pre-Watergate position. The 1973 War Powers Resolution, the 1974 Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act, the 1976 National Emergencies Act, and the 1978 Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act were the post-Watergate-and-Vietnam congressional reforms Cheney sought to reverse across his subsequent career.

### The Wyoming congressional career 1979-1989

Cheney was elected to the US House of Representatives from Wyoming's at-large congressional district in November 1978 and served from January 1979 to March 1989 across six terms. His House voting record was documented as among the most conservative in the chamber, including opposition to the 1983 House resolution calling for Nelson Mandela's release from prison and opposition to the 1985 House resolution calling for economic sanctions against apartheid South Africa. He served as House Republican Conference Chairman 1988-1989 and briefly as House Minority Whip in early 1989 before leaving the House for the Defense Department.

### The Defense Department 1989-1993

Cheney was nominated as Secretary of Defense by President George H. W. Bush after the Senate rejected the initial Bush nominee John Tower on 9 March 1989. Cheney was confirmed on 17 March 1989 and served as Secretary of Defense from 21 March 1989 to 20 January 1993.

His tenure included the December 1989 - January 1990 invasion of Panama (Operation Just Cause) to depose Manuel Noriega; the 1990-1991 Gulf War response to the 2 August 1990 Iraqi invasion of Kuwait, in which Cheney coordinated the Pentagon-side operational planning while George H. W. Bush coordinated the 35-nation coalition diplomacy (Dossier 10 covers the Bush 41 side of the record); and the post-1991 multi-year reduction in US military personnel and base capacity following the collapse of the Soviet Union.

As outgoing Secretary of Defense in 1992, Cheney commissioned a $3.9 million study from Brown & Root Services — the engineering and construction subsidiary that became Kellogg, Brown & Root, a Halliburton subsidiary — on the privatisation of US Army logistical functions. The study recommended that private firms including Brown & Root should take over logistical-support programmes for US military operations globally. Brown & Root subsequently won a five-year LOGCAP (Logistics Civil Augmentation Program) contract from the US Army Corps of Engineers in 1992-1995. The commissioning relationship between Cheney as outgoing Secretary of Defense and Brown & Root as recipient of the multi-million-dollar privatisation-study contract — and Brown & Root as subsequent recipient of the multi-year LOGCAP contract from the Pentagon Cheney had just commanded — was the foundation for the subsequent 1995 Cheney-Halliburton CEO appointment.

### The Halliburton CEO tenure 1995-2000

After two years as a Senior Fellow at the American Enterprise Institute (1993-1995), Cheney was appointed Chairman and CEO of the Halliburton Company on 1 October 1995. He served as combined Chairman-and-CEO from October 1995 to September 1998, and resumed the combined role from 1 February 2000 to 25 July 2000 following the interim chairmanship of William Bradford during the post-Dresser-merger period. The September 1998 merger between Halliburton and Dresser Industries — the company founded by Henry Neil Mallon, the Yale-and-Skull-and-Bones classmate of Prescott Bush who had given George H. W. Bush his first oil-industry job in 1948 — produced the merged Halliburton that became the largest US oil-services-and-engineering firm.

Halliburton's record under Cheney's chairmanship is on the documented public record: the company rose from 73rd to 18th on the Pentagon's list of top contractors per the Center for Public Integrity 2003 analysis, benefiting from at least $3.8 billion in federal contracts and taxpayer-insured loans across the 1995-2000 period. The offshore subsidiary structure expanded from 9 to 44 Caribbean tax-haven subsidiaries under Cheney's chairmanship per the Common Dreams 2005 analysis; Halliburton tax payments to the US dropped from $302 million in 1998 to zero in 1999, with an $85 million IRS refund.

The Halliburton operations under Cheney's chairmanship included subsidiary activity in countries subject to US sanctions: a Cayman Islands subsidiary (Halliburton Products and Services Ltd.) operated roughly $40 million per year in oil-field service work for the Iranian government across the 1995-2000 period. Per the *Washington Post*: "Halliburton held stakes in two firms that signed contracts to sell more than $73 million in oil production equipment and spare parts to Iraq while Cheney was chairman and chief executive officer." Halliburton operations also continued in Libya, Burma, Azerbaijan, Indonesia, and Nigeria across the Cheney chairmanship period.

The 1998-onward Halliburton accounting changes — the company switched to a more aggressive revenue-realisation method for disputed costs on major construction projects — produced a subsequent SEC investigation that closed in 2004 with a $7.5 million settlement for fraud charges. The 2011 US Supreme Court reversed a lower-court ruling and allowed the shareholder class-action suit to continue; the class action was subsequently settled. Cheney was not personally named as a defendant in the SEC settlement or the shareholder class action. Cheney was personally named in the December 2010 Nigerian government corruption complaint against Halliburton, which the company settled for $250 million.

Cheney's compensation across the 1995-2000 Halliburton tenure totalled roughly $44 million per the Common Dreams analysis. His July 2000 severance package was $33.7 million per the subsequent Wikipedia citation. He retained 433,000 unexercised Halliburton stock options worth more than $10 million at the time of his 2001 vice-presidential inauguration per the 2003 Senator Frank Lautenberg analysis. He continued to receive annual deferred-compensation payments from Halliburton across the first VP term: $205,298 in 2001; $162,392 in 2002; $178,437 in 2003; $194,852 in 2004.

The operational conclusion on the Halliburton period: Cheney as outgoing Secretary of Defense in 1992 commissioned the privatisation study that recommended Brown & Root take over Army logistical operations; Brown & Root won the LOGCAP contract in 1992-1995; Cheney as Halliburton CEO from 1995 to 2000 oversaw the company's rise from 73rd to 18th on the Pentagon's contractor list and the Halliburton-Dresser merger; Cheney as Vice President from 2001 to 2009 oversaw the Iraq War in which Halliburton-KBR became the #1 Pentagon contractor with $39.5 billion in federal contracts between 2003 and 2013, including the no-bid LOGCAP III contract. The institutional revolving-door pattern across the Cheney-DoD-Halliburton-VP sequence is documented.

### The Vice Presidency 2001-2009

Cheney chaired the Bush vice-presidential search committee in early 2000 while still Halliburton CEO; on 25 July 2000 he was named by George W. Bush as the Republican vice-presidential nominee. Cheney changed his driver's licence and voter registration from Texas to Wyoming during the same period — a step required to allow Texas presidential electors to vote for both Bush and Cheney without contravening the Twelfth Amendment, which forbids electors from voting for a president and vice president who are both inhabitants of the same state. The Bush-Cheney ticket won the 2000 election after the *Bush v. Gore* Supreme Court decision of 12 December 2000 (Dossier 10).

On 11 September 2001 Cheney was at the White House when the attacks began. Secret Service personnel carried him to the Presidential Emergency Operations Center bunker. From the bunker Cheney coordinated the post-attack response, including the phone calls to Bush telling Bush to delay his return to the White House. Per Cheney's subsequent Meet the Press interview: "I said, 'Delay your return. We don't know what's going on here but it looks like, you know, we've been targeted.'"

The post-9/11 Cheney programme produced the architecture of the Global War on Terror in seven components. The September 2001 Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF). The October 2001 USA PATRIOT Act. The October 2001 launch of the Bush-administration warrantless-surveillance programme by the National Security Agency under direct Cheney authorisation outside the FISA framework — the programme was disclosed publicly by the *New York Times* in December 2005. The establishment of the post-2002 Guantanamo Bay detention facility for indefinite extra-judicial detention of post-9/11 captures. The Office of Legal Counsel "torture memos" by John Yoo and Jay Bybee in 2002 establishing the legal framework for the CIA "enhanced interrogation" programme. The November 2002 establishment of the Department of Homeland Security. And the multi-year extra-judicial-rendition programme that transported captured detainees to third-country sites for interrogation outside US legal protection.

The CIA "enhanced interrogation" programme — the torture programme documented in the 2014 Senate Select Committee on Intelligence Study of the CIA's Detention and Interrogation Program (the redacted public summary is the "Torture Report") — was authorised under direct Cheney advocacy. The techniques included waterboarding (used at least 83 times on Abu Zubaydah and at least 183 times on Khalid Sheikh Mohammed per the Torture Report), stress positions, sleep deprivation up to 180 hours, rectal feeding, and confinement in coffin-sized boxes. Cheney's subsequent public position — in the 14 December 2014 *Meet the Press* interview after the Torture Report's release — was that the techniques were not torture and that he would "do it again" and that those who carried out the interrogations were "heroes."

Cheney was the public advocate for the 2003 invasion of Iraq. He delivered the August 2002 Veterans of Foreign Wars convention speech that included the assertion: "Simply stated, there is no doubt that Saddam Hussein now has weapons of mass destruction." He delivered the September 2002 *Meet the Press* interview in which he asserted that Iraq had "reconstituted nuclear weapons." He continued the assertion of an operational Saddam-al-Qaeda link through 2006 despite the multiple intelligence-community assessments documenting that no such link existed. The 2005 Robb-Silberman Commission and the 2008 Senate Intelligence Committee Phase II Report established that the WMD claims were false.

In May 2005, Cheney told CNN's Larry King that the Iraqi insurgency was in its "last throes." The US service-member death toll in Iraq at the time of the statement was 1,661. The subsequent 2005-2011 Iraq War US service-member death toll exceeded 4,400; the subsequent 2003-2011 Iraqi civilian death toll exceeds 200,000 per the Iraq Body Count and Lancet epidemiological estimates.

On 11 February 2006 Cheney accidentally shot Texas attorney Harry Whittington while quail-hunting at the Armstrong Ranch in South Texas. Whittington survived. The Cheney office's delayed public disclosure of the shooting — the public learned of the incident through the *Corpus Christi Caller-Times* roughly 18 hours after the shooting — became the subject of international media attention.

The Cheney-chaired National Energy Policy Development Group of 2001 — the energy-policy task force whose membership and meeting records the Cheney office refused to disclose — was the subject of GAO and Sierra Club lawsuits to compel disclosure. The Supreme Court's 2004 ruling in *Cheney v. United States District Court for the District of Columbia* upheld the Cheney office's non-disclosure position.

### The post-Vice-Presidency 2009-2025

Cheney's post-2009 public profile was an active and aggressive defence of the Bush-Cheney administration record. His 2011 memoir *In My Time* and his post-2009 *Meet the Press* and Fox News appearances maintained the Cheney public position on the Iraq War, the torture programme, and the executive-power expansion.

He received a heart transplant in March 2012 after multiple decades of cardiovascular disease — his first heart attack was at age 37 in 1978, followed by attacks in 1984, 1988, 2000, and 2010.

His post-2016 public position on Trump was the multi-year break from the Republican Party's Trump-aligned realignment. The January 2022 video for his daughter Liz Cheney's 2022 Wyoming Republican primary campaign included the statement: "In our nation's 246-year history, there has never been an individual who is a greater threat to our republic than Donald Trump. He is a coward. A real man wouldn't lie to his supporters. He lost his election, and he lost big. I know it. He knows it. And, deep down, I think most Republicans know it."

On 6 September 2024 Cheney publicly endorsed Kamala Harris over Trump. The endorsement was the most prominent Republican-political-class endorsement of the 2024 Harris candidacy. He died on 3 November 2025, aged 84. His 20 November 2025 funeral at the Washington National Cathedral was attended by George W. Bush, Laura Bush, Joe Biden, Jill Biden, Kamala Harris, Mike Pence, and Hillary Clinton. Trump and JD Vance were not invited; Trump did not issue a public statement on Cheney's death.

## Part B — Elizabeth Lynne "Liz" Cheney (b. 1966): the Wyoming congressional career and the January 6 Committee

### The pre-2017 record

Liz Cheney was born on 28 July 1966 in Madison, Wisconsin, where her parents Dick and Lynne Cheney were doctoral students at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. She attended Colorado College (BA 1988) and the University of Chicago Law School (JD 1996). She married Philip Perry on 28 May 1993; they have five children.

Her pre-congressional career was the case of the political-class daughter trajectory: 1989-1993 State Department employee under her father's tenure as Secretary of Defense; 1996-1998 lawyer at White & Case; 1999-2001 International Finance Corporation consultant; 2002-2009 multiple positions at the State Department under the George W. Bush administration, including Principal Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Near Eastern Affairs and Chair of the Iran-Syria Operations Group from 2006. The Near Eastern Affairs portfolio since the post-2003 period positioned her in the Iraq-and-Iran policy operational role during her father's Vice Presidency.

She was a Fox News commentator from 2009 to 2011. In 2009 she co-founded Keep America Safe with William Kristol — the neoconservative advocacy organisation whose mandate was the public defence of the Bush-Cheney administration's torture-and-Guantanamo-and-Iraq-War record. In 2014 she launched a primary campaign against Wyoming Republican incumbent Senator Mike Enzi but withdrew before the primary after the intra-party reaction to the primary challenge.

### The Wyoming House campaign and the 2017-2021 leadership ascent

In 2016 Liz Cheney was elected US Representative from Wyoming's at-large congressional district, taking office 3 January 2017. She was Republican Conference Vice Chair 2017-2019 and Chair of the House Republican Conference 2019-2021 — the third-ranking House Republican position behind Minority Leader Kevin McCarthy and Minority Whip Steve Scalise. Across the first Trump term her voting record aligned with the Trump position 93% of the time per the *Time* analysis of her congressional record.

### The post-2020 break and the January 6 Committee

On 13 January 2021, Liz Cheney voted yes on the second Trump impeachment article of incitement of insurrection — one of 10 House Republicans who voted yes. Her public statement before the vote: "The President of the United States summoned this mob, assembled the mob, and lit the flame of this attack. Everything that followed was his doing." On 12 May 2021 she was removed as Republican Conference Chair by a House Republican Conference vote following her continued public criticism of Trump on the 2020 election results.

On 1 July 2021, Speaker Nancy Pelosi (Dossier 7) appointed Cheney to the House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6 Attack on the US Capitol. On 2 September 2021 she was named Vice Chair of the Select Committee.

On 4 February 2022 the Republican National Committee — at its Salt Lake City meeting — formally censured Cheney and Adam Kinzinger for "actions in their positions as members of the January 6th Select Committee not befitting Republican members of Congress." The censure was the evidentiary case of the post-2020 institutional Republican-Party realignment toward the Trump position.

The Select Committee's eight public hearings between 9 June and 13 October 2022 produced what *Time* described as "must-watch television" of the multiple witness testimonies including Cassidy Hutchinson (the Mark Meadows aide), Pat Cipollone, Mike Pence's staff, and the Trump White House communications operation. The hearings produced the multi-channel documentary record of the Trump-administration internal operation on 6 January 2021 — the contemporaneous Trump-Pence phone call, the multiple Trump-administration internal Cabinet objections to the post-election Trump-campaign legal strategy, the multiple Trump-administration internal expressions of post-attack alarm.

The 18 December 2022 Select Committee final report recommended that Trump be criminally prosecuted on multiple federal charges including obstruction of an official proceeding, conspiracy to defraud the United States, conspiracy to make a false statement, and incitement of insurrection. The Department of Justice subsequently brought federal charges against Trump on 1 August 2023; the case was dismissed without prejudice after Trump's 5 November 2024 election victory and the Department of Justice's subsequent 25 November 2024 motion to dismiss under the Department's longstanding position against prosecuting a sitting president.

### The 2022 Wyoming primary defeat

On 16 August 2022, Liz Cheney was defeated in the Wyoming Republican primary by Trump-endorsed Harriet Hageman, 66.3% to 28.9% — a 37-point margin. Per Wikipedia: "Cheney's margin of defeat marked the second-worst for a House incumbent in the last 60 years, behind that of South Carolina Republican Bob Inglis in a 2010 primary runoff."

The pre-primary Casper Star-Tribune polling produced the documented account of the primary-defeat structure: only 30% of respondents said they would vote for Hageman because of Trump's endorsement, whereas 60% said it was because they disapproved of Cheney's role on the January 6 panel. The defeat was the case of the post-2020 Republican-primary-electorate position on the post-6-January-2021 Republican-Party-internal accountability process: the primary electorate rejected the member who had led the accountability process. Hageman's victory speech included the statement: "What Wyoming has shown today is that, while it may not be easy, we can dislodge entrenched politicians who believe they've risen above the people they are supposed to represent." Trump's Truth Social statement on the result: "This is a wonderful result for America, and a complete rebuke of the Unselect Committee of political Hacks and Thugs."

Hageman was the fourth consecutive Republican woman to represent Wyoming's at-large congressional district, succeeding Barbara Cubin (1995-2009), Cynthia Lummis (2009-2017), and Liz Cheney (2017-2023). Liz Cheney's term ended 3 January 2023.

### The post-2023 trajectory and the 2024 Harris endorsement

Liz Cheney's 5 December 2023 memoir *Oath and Honor: A Memoir and a Warning* — published by Little, Brown — was the documentary account of her January 6 Committee work and the post-2020 Republican-Party-internal accountability period. The memoir reached the *New York Times* bestseller list.

On 4 September 2024 Liz Cheney publicly endorsed Kamala Harris over Trump. Two days later, on 6 September 2024, Dick Cheney issued the parallel public endorsement of Harris. The father-and-daughter Cheney endorsement of the 2024 Democratic nominee was the documentary marker of the Cheney family departure from the post-2016 Trump-aligned Republican Party. Liz Cheney made multiple public appearances with Harris in Wisconsin and Michigan across October 2024.

Trump won the 5 November 2024 general election. The post-election analysis quantified the Cheney-aligned Republican voter-defection effect as insufficient to swing the outcome — the post-2020 Republican primary electorate had already rejected the Cheney accountability position; the general-election Republican base aligned with Trump.

Liz Cheney delivered the eulogy at her father's 20 November 2025 funeral.

## The structural consequence

The Cheneys document the American Republican political-class family with a father-daughter arc. The father's record across the 1969-2009 Nixon-Ford-Bush-41-Halliburton-Bush-43 sequence established the post-1969 neoconservative executive-power architecture — the post-Watergate-reform reversal, the post-9/11 Global War on Terror, the Iraq War, the CIA torture programme, the warrantless-surveillance programme, and the no-bid Halliburton-KBR reconstruction-contractor distribution. The daughter's 2017-2023 Wyoming congressional career, 2019-2021 Republican Conference Chairship, 2021-2023 January 6 Committee vice chairship, and 2022 Wyoming Republican primary defeat by Trump-endorsed Harriet Hageman produced the post-2021 Republican-against-Trump faction that the 2024 Trump victory and the 5 November 2024 general-election outcome rejected.

Both records — Dick Cheney's 1969-2009 architecture of the post-Watergate executive-power restoration and the post-9/11 war-on-terror apparatus, and Liz Cheney's 2021-2023 January 6 Committee accountability work that recommended Trump's criminal prosecution — are documented. The father whose 2001-2009 Vice Presidency normalised the extra-legal executive-power-and-torture-and-warrantless-surveillance apparatus produced the daughter who in 2021-2023 led the congressional accountability process for the post-2020 Trump-administration extra-legal effort to overturn the 2020 election. The institutional irony is documented. The post-2024 Republican-Party realignment under Trump's second term has continued the consolidation of the executive-power expansion that Dick Cheney established as the post-1969 Republican-Party operating mode and that Liz Cheney sought to limit when it was applied against the Republican electoral-process apparatus she had served for four decades.

The father built the torture programme, the warrantless-surveillance programme, and the no-bid Halliburton contracts. The daughter led the congressional accountability process against the next Republican who tried to break the structural guardrails. The irony requires no editorial comment.

# Dossier 12: Henry Alfred Kissinger

Henry Alfred Kissinger was born Heinz Alfred Kissinger on 27 May 1923 in Fürth, Bavaria, Germany. His Jewish family fled Nazi Germany in 1938 when Kissinger was 15, resettling in the Washington Heights neighbourhood of Manhattan. He became a US citizen in 1943, served in the US Army in occupied Germany 1945-1946, and took his BA, MA, and PhD in government from Harvard University between 1950 and 1954. He served on the Harvard faculty from 1954 to 1969. He died on 29 November 2023 at his home in Kent, Connecticut, aged 100.

Across the years 1969-1977 Kissinger served as the seventh US National Security Adviser (20 January 1969 - 3 November 1975) and the 56th US Secretary of State (22 September 1973 - 20 January 1977), under Presidents Richard Nixon and Gerald Ford. From 22 September 1973 to 3 November 1975 he held both positions simultaneously — the first and only such combined incumbency in US history. From 1982 to 2023 he ran Kissinger Associates, the New York-based international consultancy firm that across the period advised US administrations from Carter through Trump.

The Kissinger biographical record is the public-facing record of the post-Second-World-War American foreign-policy intellectual whose institutional career integrated the post-1945 American national-security state with the post-1955 Rockefeller patronage network, the post-1968 Nixon-Ford government period, the post-1973 Paris Peace Accords Nobel Peace Prize laureate position, and the post-1982 Kissinger Associates consultancy. The record across the 1969-2023 period includes six items. The 1969-1973 secret bombing of Cambodia and the post-1975 Khmer Rouge genocide. The 1970-1973 multi-year covert US destabilisation campaign against Salvador Allende and the subsequent 11 September 1973 Chilean military coup that produced the Pinochet dictatorship. The 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War and the US-aligned Pakistani military's genocide against Bengali civilians that Kissinger and Nixon backed against the US Consulate Dhaka "Blood Telegram" objections. The 1975-1983 Operation Condor cross-border-assassination apparatus that the Kissinger State Department tacitly supported. The 6 December 1975 Jakarta meeting that produced US tacit approval for the Indonesian invasion of East Timor. And the post-1977 Kissinger Associates consultancy whose corporate-and-foreign-government client relationships have been the subject of multiple investigative reports throughout the post-2002 9/11 Commission resignation period. The record traces the record at the standard of the prior eleven Atlantic dossiers.

## The pre-government academic-and-consultancy period 1954-1969

Kissinger's pre-1969 career was the evidentiary case of the post-1945 American academic-foreign-policy-consultant trajectory. The Harvard PhD dissertation on Castlereagh and Metternich; the Defense Studies Program directorship at the Harvard Center for International Affairs; the 1955-1956 Study Directorship at the Rockefeller Brothers Fund Special Studies Project; the 1957 publication of *Nuclear Weapons and Foreign Policy* that established Kissinger as a public intellectual on post-1945 strategic doctrine; the 1957-1968 multi-campaign adviser role to Nelson Rockefeller across his three Republican presidential primary campaigns (1960, 1964, 1968); the 1961-1968 consultant role to Presidents Kennedy and Johnson on foreign-policy and nuclear-strategy questions — the governing Kissinger position by 1968 was the documented case of the post-1945 American foreign-policy intellectual whose Rockefeller patronage and Harvard institutional standing positioned him for the 1968 Nixon appointment.

The 1968 Nixon-Kissinger appointment was the inflection point. Nixon and Kissinger had previously had limited personal contact; Nixon's subsequent public statements and the 1970s-onward Nixon White House recordings document Nixon's antisemitic comments about Kissinger in the period of their closest collaboration. The Kissinger position across the 1969-1974 period was the documented case of the Harvard-Rockefeller-Republican intellectual operating within an institutional Nixon-administration framework whose public-facing foreign-policy operation Kissinger increasingly controlled.

## The National Security Adviser period 1969-1975

### The Nixon-Kissinger doctrine of concentrated foreign-policy power

The 1969-1973 Kissinger institutional position was the documented case of the National-Security-Council-staff expansion that produced the post-1969 parallel-and-sometimes-conflict relationship between the NSC under Kissinger and the State Department under William P. Rogers. The Kissinger-Nixon institutional doctrine — the concentration of foreign-policy power in the White House at the expense of the State Department, the Pentagon, and the CIA — established the pattern that the subsequent Republican-administration foreign-policy operations (the Reagan administration, the George H. W. Bush administration, the George W. Bush administration) extended.

### The Cambodia bombing 1969-1973

In March 1969, Nixon and Kissinger authorised Operation Menu — the 14-month secret B-52 bombing of Cambodian territory targeting alleged North Vietnamese supply lines. Across the March 1969 - May 1970 Operation Menu period, US Air Force B-52s flew 3,875 sorties dropping 108,823 tons of bombs on Cambodian territory. The B-52 campaigns across the 1970-1973 period — including Operation Freedom Deal — produced an estimated 540,000 to 2.7 million tons of ordnance on Cambodia per the 2006 Owen-Kiernan analysis published in *The Walrus*. The Cambodian civilian death toll from the bombing alone is estimated at 50,000-150,000.

The 17 April 1975 Khmer Rouge seizure of Phnom Penh and the 1975-1979 Cambodian genocide — the systematic Khmer Rouge campaign against the Cambodian urban population, ethnic minorities, and politically suspect segments of the population that killed an estimated 1.5 to 2 million Cambodians, roughly a quarter of the pre-1975 Cambodian population — was the post-bombing political-and-social rupture that the multi-year US bombing destabilisation contributed to. The subsequent debate over the degree of Kissinger's responsibility for the post-1975 Khmer Rouge ascent is documented; the multi-year US bombing campaign that Kissinger authorised is documented uncontested.

### The Vietnam negotiations and the 1973 Nobel Peace Prize

The 1969-1973 parallel secret negotiations between Kissinger and North Vietnamese negotiator Le Duc Tho produced the 27 January 1973 Paris Peace Accords. The 1973 Nobel Peace Prize was awarded jointly to Kissinger and Le Duc Tho; Le Duc Tho refused the prize. The 30 April 1975 fall of Saigon to North Vietnamese forces — and the post-1973 US military disengagement that the Accords had codified — produced the debate over whether the 1973 Accords had produced peace or had codified the subsequent North Vietnamese takeover. Christopher Hitchens's 2001 *Trial of Henry Kissinger* argued that the 1969-1972 Kissinger-Nixon delay of the Vietnam negotiations had produced thousands of additional US-and-Vietnamese deaths beyond the outcome the 1969 negotiations could have produced.

### The China opening 1971-1972

In July 1971 and October 1971, Kissinger made two secret trips to Beijing to negotiate the US-China rapprochement. The February 1972 Nixon visit to Beijing and the meeting with Mao Zedong and Zhou Enlai produced the Shanghai Communiqué and the 1972 normalisation of US-China relations. The China opening was the single most public-facing achievement of Kissinger's National Security Adviser period and the central pillar of the Kissinger institutional reputation in the post-1972 period.

The documented record on the China opening is clear: the diplomatic achievement of the 1971-1972 Kissinger-Mao-Zhou-Nixon negotiations is established. The institutional reputation that the post-1972 China-opening produced for Kissinger across the fifty-one years of his life — the post-1977 access to Beijing, the post-1982 Kissinger Associates client relationships with US corporate principals seeking China-market access, the post-2017 Trump-administration consultation on China policy — established the China opening as the central institutional platform for the post-1977 Kissinger consultancy career.

### The Bangladesh genocide 1971

In March 1971 the Pakistani military government of General Yahya Khan launched Operation Searchlight — the systematic military campaign against the Bengali population of East Pakistan that produced the 1971 Bangladesh genocide. The estimated death toll across the March-December 1971 period is 300,000 to 3 million Bengali civilians, depending on the estimate methodology, per the subsequent Bangladeshi government figures and independent academic analyses.

Kissinger and Nixon maintained US arms transfers to the Pakistani military government across the period despite the US Consulate Dhaka April 1971 "Blood Telegram" — the cable sent by US Consul General Archer Blood documenting the atrocities and requesting US policy change. Kissinger's position, per the declassified 7 March 1971 Nixon-Kissinger Oval Office recording in conversation with Nixon: "the Indians are bastards anyway." The post-2013 Gary Bass book *The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide* documented the Kissinger-Nixon administration internal correspondence on the Bangladesh question.

The cumulative conclusion on the Bangladesh record: the US arms transfers to the Pakistani military government across the period of the Pakistani military's documented genocidal campaign against the Bengali civilian population are documented. The Blood Telegram and the Kissinger-Nixon internal correspondence are on the documented declassified record. The interpretation of Kissinger's degree of personal responsibility for the post-1971 Bangladesh atrocities is contested; the institutional US support for the Pakistani military government across the period is documented.

### The Chile coup 1970-1973

The 1970-1973 multi-year covert US destabilisation campaign against Salvador Allende is the documented case of the Kissinger-coordinated post-1970 institutional US foreign-policy operation against a democratically elected Latin American government. Allende won the September 1970 Chilean presidential election; the post-election Nixon-Kissinger position was the documented case of immediate institutional opposition.

Kissinger's position to the 27 June 1970 NSC 40 Committee on Chile, per the declassified documents: "I don't see why we need to stand by and watch a country go Communist due to the irresponsibility of its own people." The November 1970 CIA cable to its Santiago station — following a Kissinger meeting — stated that "it is firm and continuing policy that Allende be overthrown by a coup."

On 27 October 1970, Chilean Army Commander-in-Chief General René Schneider — an Allende-loyal military officer — was assassinated by CIA-armed Chilean military officers under Operation FUBELT. The post-Schneider assassination period of US economic-and-political destabilisation against the Allende government — what Nixon, per the declassified record, called the effort to "make the economy scream" — culminated in the 11 September 1973 military coup against Allende. Allende died on 11 September 1973 at the La Moneda presidential palace; the official cause was suicide.

The subsequent Pinochet military junta governed Chile from 1973 to 1990. The estimated death toll under Pinochet repression is 3,000-30,000 Chileans killed or disappeared; the subsequent 1976 Letelier-Moffitt assassination on Washington DC's Embassy Row — carried out by Chilean DINA intelligence operatives — was the documented case of the Pinochet regime extending its repression operations onto US soil.

Kissinger's documented position to Pinochet at the 8 June 1976 Organisation of American States meeting in Santiago, per the declassified State Department transcript: "We want to help, not undermine you. You did a great service to the West in overthrowing Allende." Kissinger's marginal note on a cable from the US ambassador to Chile relaying his efforts to persuade the Chilean military to act less brutally against political prisoners: "cut out the political science lectures."

The documentary conclusion on the Chile record: the multi-year US covert destabilisation campaign against Allende, the assassination of General Schneider, the structural US support for the Pinochet military government across the 1973-1989 period, and Kissinger's documented public statements to Pinochet are on the documented declassified record. The Pinochet regime's documented repression — the 3,000-30,000 killed or disappeared Chileans, the Letelier-Moffitt Washington-DC assassination — is documented. The Kissinger institutional role in the multi-year US support for the Pinochet regime is on the documented declassified record.

### The Iraqi Kurdish operation 1973-1975

The 1973-1974 covert US-Iranian support for the Iraqi Kurdish independence movement under Mustafa Barzani against the Baghdad Baathist government — and the 1975 Algiers Accord between Iran (under the Shah) and Iraq that produced the US-Iranian withdrawal of support and the subsequent Baathist repression of the Iraqi Kurdish population — is documented. Kissinger's subsequent public defence to congressional testimony: "Covert action should not be confused with missionary work."

## The Secretary of State period 1973-1977

### The dual-hat tenure 1973-1975

On 22 September 1973, Kissinger became the 56th US Secretary of State while retaining the National Security Adviser position. He held both positions simultaneously from 22 September 1973 to 3 November 1975 — the only such combined incumbency in US history. The combination concentrated US foreign-policy authority in a single official to a degree that subsequently produced the public-and-congressional concern about institutional balance.

### The October 1973 Yom Kippur War and the post-war shuttle diplomacy

The 6-25 October 1973 Yom Kippur War between Israel and the Egyptian-Syrian Arab coalition produced the Kissinger-coordinated US emergency military airlift to Israel (Operation Nickel Grass) that helped turn the war in Israel's favour. The subsequent OPEC oil embargo against the United States — in response to the US support for Israel — produced the 1973-1974 oil crisis and the post-1973 multi-year global energy-and-economic disruption.

In November 1973 Kissinger launched the first "shuttle diplomacy" — the personal travel between Israeli, Egyptian, Syrian, and other Middle Eastern capitals — that produced the January 1974 Egypt-Israel disengagement agreement and the May 1974 Syria-Israel disengagement agreement. The shuttle-diplomacy method was the documented case of the Kissinger personal-diplomacy operational mode that the US Secretaries of State have invoked as the institutional model.

Per the State Department official history: Kissinger flew 565,000 miles across his 1973-1977 Secretary-of-State tenure, made 213 foreign-country visits, and spent 33 consecutive days in the Middle East following the October 1973 war negotiating disengagement between Israel and Syria.

### The Indonesian invasion of East Timor 1975

On 6 December 1975, Kissinger and Ford met with Indonesian President Suharto in Jakarta. Per the declassified record published by the National Security Archive in 2001, Kissinger and Ford gave tacit US approval for the Indonesian invasion of East Timor that began on 7 December 1975. The subsequent 24-year Indonesian occupation of East Timor (1975-1999) produced an estimated 100,000-200,000 East Timorese deaths from military violence, famine, and disease.

The closing assessment of the record on the East Timor record: the 6 December 1975 Jakarta meeting transcript — declassified in 2001 — documents Kissinger's advance knowledge of the planned Indonesian invasion and the tacit US approval. The subsequent 24-year occupation and the documented East Timorese death toll are documented. The institutional Kissinger position on the East Timor invasion is on the documented declassified record.

### Operation Condor 1975-1983

Operation Condor was the intelligence-sharing-and-cross-border-assassination programme operated by the military dictatorships of Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, Bolivia, and Uruguay from 1975 to 1983. The operation produced the multi-year campaign of cross-border-and-international assassinations against left-wing dissidents, with estimated victims numbering in tens of thousands across the participating Southern Cone states. The 1976 Letelier-Moffitt Washington-DC assassination was the most prominent Operation Condor operation conducted on US soil.

The September 1976 State Department documents — released subsequently by the National Security Archive — include Kissinger's order to cancel a planned US warning to the Latin American governments about Operation Condor assassinations. The cable, dated 16 September 1976, ordered that "no further action be taken on this matter" by the State Department. The 21 September 1976 Letelier-Moffitt assassination by Chilean DINA operatives in Washington DC followed five days later.

The overall conclusion on the Operation Condor record: the Operation Condor apparatus and the US State Department awareness of the apparatus across the 1975-1983 period are documented. Kissinger's 16 September 1976 cable cancelling the planned warning to the Latin American governments — and the 21 September 1976 Washington-DC Letelier-Moffitt assassination that followed five days later — are on the documented declassified record.

### The Halloween Massacre November 1975

On 3 November 1975, Ford executed the "Halloween Massacre" cabinet reshuffle. Kissinger retained the Secretary of State portfolio; Brent Scowcroft replaced him as National Security Adviser. The Halloween reshuffle was the governing response to the public-and-congressional concern about the dual-hat concentration of foreign-policy authority. Donald Rumsfeld was named Secretary of Defense; George H. W. Bush was named Director of Central Intelligence; Dick Cheney was named White House Chief of Staff. The Halloween Massacre was the moment at which the post-1969 Nixon-Ford-administration foreign-policy-and-national-security personnel — Rumsfeld, Cheney, Scowcroft, Bush, Kissinger — were positioned in the posts that the Republican-administration foreign-policy operations operated from. The 1989-1993 Bush 41 administration recreated the post-Halloween-1975 personnel constellation in modified form (Scowcroft as NSA, Cheney as Secretary of Defense, Bush as President); the 2001-2009 Bush 43 administration recreated it again (Cheney as Vice President, Rumsfeld as Secretary of Defense).

## The post-government period 1977-2023

### Kissinger Associates and the consultancy

Kissinger left office at the end of the Ford administration in January 1977 following Jimmy Carter's November 1976 victory over Ford. The post-1977 Kissinger trajectory was the documented case of the post-government foreign-policy consultancy career that the US foreign-policy officials have followed. In 1982 Kissinger founded Kissinger Associates, the New York-based consultancy firm whose client list included major multinational corporations, foreign governments, and US corporate principals seeking access to Kissinger's post-1969 international relationships.

Kissinger Associates' reported revenues across the 1982-2023 period have not been public. Multiple subsequent journalistic estimates have placed Kissinger's personal wealth at tens-to-hundreds of millions of dollars at the time of his 2023 death. The post-1982 Kissinger Associates client relationships included reported engagements with Coca-Cola, American Express, ITT, JPMorgan Chase, and foreign-government clients across the period. The Kissinger-Associates client-list non-disclosure across the period has been the documented case of the post-1982 American consultancy-practice institutional model that the US former-Secretaries-of-State have followed.

### The consultative role to subsequent US administrations

Across the post-1977 period, Kissinger maintained advisory relationships with every subsequent US administration: Reagan (1981-1989, including the 1983-1984 Kissinger Commission chairship on Central America and the 1984-1990 membership on the President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board); George H. W. Bush (1989-1993); Clinton (1993-2001); George W. Bush (2001-2009, including the 2002 9/11 Commission chairship resignation and the multi-year consultative role on Iraq policy and Middle East strategy); Obama (2009-2017, including consultations on China policy); Trump (2017-2021, including multiple White House visits); and Biden (2021-2023, including continued public commentary on China and Russia policy until Kissinger's November 2023 death).

The post-2017 Trump-Kissinger relationship was the documented case of the multi-administration multi-party Kissinger institutional positioning. Kissinger was the documented elder-statesman figure consulted by both Republican and Democratic administrations across the period despite the documented Kissinger record of post-1969 Republican-administration policy choices. The pattern is documented.

### The 9/11 Commission resignation December 2002

In November 2002, George W. Bush appointed Kissinger as Chair of the National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States — the "9/11 Commission." Kissinger resigned the chairship two weeks later, in December 2002, after the public concern about the Kissinger Associates client list and the potential conflicts of interest. The Kissinger refusal to disclose the Kissinger Associates client list was the position. Thomas Kean replaced Kissinger as 9/11 Commission Chair.

### The pursuit-of-accountability efforts

The post-1998 period produced multiple legal-accountability efforts directed at Kissinger:

- 2001: French judge Roger Le Loire issued a summons for Kissinger to testify in Paris regarding the disappearance of five French citizens in Chile under Pinochet. Kissinger declined to appear and promptly left Paris.
- 2002: multiple subsequent summons issued by Chilean, Argentine, Spanish, and French judges regarding Kissinger's role in Operation Condor and the Chile coup. Kissinger declined to respond.
- 2001-2002: Christopher Hitchens's *The Trial of Henry Kissinger* (Verso, 2001) and the 2002 documentary film *The Trials of Henry Kissinger* by Eugene Jarecki and Alex Gibney laid out the legal case for war-crimes prosecution.

No legal proceedings against Kissinger were brought to completion. He died on 29 November 2023 at his Kent, Connecticut, home at age 100. The cause was not publicly disclosed by the family or Kissinger Associates.

## What remains

Henry Alfred Kissinger has been, across the fifty-four years 1969-2023, the case of the post-Second-World-War American foreign-policy intellectual whose institutional trajectory integrated the post-1945 American national-security state, the post-1955 Rockefeller patronage network, the post-1968 Nixon-Ford government period, the post-1973 Paris Peace Accords Nobel laureate position, and the post-1982 Kissinger Associates consultancy that advised every US administration from Carter through Trump. The institutional record across the 1969-1977 period — the Cambodia bombing, the Vietnam negotiations, the China opening, the Chile coup, the Bangladesh genocide, the East Timor invasion, Operation Condor — established Kissinger as the post-1945 American foreign-policy operator whose documented record produced the Christopher Hitchens, Greg Grandin, Gary Bass, and Peter Kornbluh published-accountability literature and the multiple post-2001 European judicial summonses that Kissinger declined to answer.

The record across the 1977-2023 post-government period — Kissinger Associates, the 1983-1984 Reagan Kissinger Commission, the 2002 9/11 Commission resignation, the post-2017 Trump White House visits, the post-2009 multi-administration consultative role — established Kissinger as the documented elder-statesman figure of the post-1977 American foreign-policy establishment whose institutional access bridged the Cold War period and the post-Cold-War American unipolar moment.

The Nobel Peace Prize laureate. The Cambodia bomber. The Chile coup architect. The Bangladesh genocide enabler. The East Timor invasion greenlighter. The Kissinger Associates consultant to every subsequent administration. He declined the European judicial summonses and died at 100 in Connecticut. The documented record survived him.
