# Dossier 13: The Supreme Court — The Institutional Court and Its Nine Operators

## The institution

The Supreme Court of the United States has, since the Marbury v. Madison ruling of 24 February 1803, was the final adjudicating authority over the constitutionality of every act of the American federal political class and over the federalism boundary between the federal authority and the fifty state-level political classes. The court has nine members. Its composition is determined by the President's nominations and the Senate's advice-and-consent, with lifetime tenure on "good behaviour." It is the single institutional body in the American constitutional architecture that combines unaccountable lifetime tenure with the power to override the elected branches' acts on the basis of the court's own reading of an eighteenth-century constitutional text. The court has, across its 237-year history, was the single most consequential pre-twentieth-century veto point on the American political-class apparatus and, since the post-2000 period that the panels of this dossier document, as the single most consequential pro-active institutional operator of the American political-class apparatus.

The nine justices comprising the Roberts Court as of May 2026 — continuously composed since 30 June 2022 when Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson replaced retiring Justice Stephen Breyer — are Chief Justice John G. Roberts, Jr. (appointed 2005 by George W. Bush), and Associate Justices Clarence Thomas (1991, George H.W. Bush), Samuel A. Alito, Jr. (2006, George W. Bush), Sonia Sotomayor (2009, Barack Obama), Elena Kagan (2010, Barack Obama), Neil M. Gorsuch (2017, Donald Trump), Brett M. Kavanaugh (2018, Donald Trump), Amy Coney Barrett (2020, Donald Trump), and Ketanji Brown Jackson (2022, Joseph Biden). Six were appointed by Republican presidents; three by Democratic presidents. The 6-3 partisan composition has been the reality of the court since the 26 October 2020 Barrett confirmation, eight days before the November 2020 presidential election.

The argument of this dossier is that the Roberts Court is not, in the dignified-press characterisation it routinely receives, an institution above politics whose nine justices interpret the Constitution in good faith and whose internal disagreements track honest interpretive disputes. The Roberts Court is the legal-operations arm of the Atlantic political-class apparatus this volume documents. Its nine members hold their positions because the political class that appointed them and confirmed them needed them in those positions. Its rulings track the political-class interests of the constituencies that placed them. The documented donor relationships, the documented non-recusal records, the documented post-2018 ethics record, and the documented post-Dobbs ruling sequence are the structural record of that arrangement. The dignified-press treatment that softens this record by presenting individual justices as good-faith interpreters of constitutional text is one of the larger ongoing acts of political-class media maintenance the contemporary American period has produced. The record shows the actual record.

## The Thomas-Crow record

Clarence Thomas — born 23 June 1948 at Pin Point, Georgia, to a poor Black family in the Gullah-Geechee community of coastal Georgia; raised by his maternal grandfather Myers Anderson after his father abandoned the family; educated at College of the Holy Cross (BA 1971) and Yale Law School (JD 1974) — was appointed to the Supreme Court by George H.W. Bush on 1 July 1991 to replace retiring Justice Thurgood Marshall, the first Black justice. Thomas's confirmation, after Anita Hill's 11 October 1991 sexual-harassment testimony to the Senate Judiciary Committee, was the 15 October 1991 closest Supreme Court vote since 1881 at the time: 52-48.

The post-confirmation Thomas record across the 1991-2026 period has, on the documented public evidence, been the single most consequential donor-relationship record of any sitting Supreme Court justice in the modern era of the court. The ProPublica investigative series of April 2023 through 2024 — under reporters Joshua Kaplan, Justin Elliott, and Alex Mierjeski — documented that Thomas had, for more than twenty years (roughly 2000-2023), accepted luxury travel and other gifts from Harlan Crow, the Dallas real-estate billionaire and Republican megadonor with over $10 million in disclosed political donations to Republican causes from the 1990s onward.

The documented gifts include: roughly annual private-jet flights on Crow's Bombardier Global 5000; multi-week summer stays at Crow's Topridge private resort in the Adirondacks (where Thomas's fellow guests at the 2017 visit included Leonard Leo of the Federalist Society and a leader of the American Enterprise Institute, both depicted in a 2017 commissioned painting hanging in Crow's Topridge lakeside lodge); roughly multi-year Bohemian Grove invitations (Thomas himself amended his 2019 disclosure to admit one Bohemian Grove visit after ProPublica's reporting; ProPublica documented at least six); and the July 2019 nine-day Indonesia cruise on Crow's 162-foot superyacht Michaela Rose, estimated by ProPublica at roughly $500,000 in chartered cost had Thomas paid for it himself. The Indonesia trip departed days after the end of the Supreme Court term and was conducted with a coterie of attendants and a private chef.

The 13 April 2023 ProPublica follow-up documented that Crow's Crow Holdings had in 2014 purchased the Savannah, Georgia property of Thomas's mother Leola Williams (with two adjoining lots) for $133,363. Thomas was a part-owner. He failed to disclose the sale on his 2014 financial disclosure form. Crow Holdings subsequently renovated the Williams house and Thomas's mother continued to live there. After ProPublica's reporting, Thomas amended his 2014 disclosure in 2023 to include the transaction, citing "inadvertent failure."

The 4 May 2023 ProPublica follow-up documented that Crow had paid roughly $150,000 over the multi-year period for the private-school tuition of Mark Martin, Thomas's grandnephew and legal ward (Thomas held legal custody from age six after the 1998 incarceration of Martin's father, Thomas's nephew). The schools were two Georgia boarding schools. Thomas did not disclose the payments. He had, ProPublica noted, disclosed a $5,000 gift for the same child's education from a different friend — establishing that Thomas understood the reporting requirement applied to the category of payment.

Thomas's 6 April 2023 statement defending the arrangement was that he had been advised by unnamed colleagues that the trips were "not reportable" under the "personal hospitality" exemption to the 1978 Ethics in Government Act's gift-disclosure requirements. The ethics-law experts who reviewed the arrangement for ProPublica — Stephen Gillers (NYU), Richard Painter (Minnesota, former George W. Bush White House ethics counsel), and others — concluded that the personal-hospitality exemption did not cover private-jet transportation, commercial-fishing-lodge stays, or superyacht cruises, and that Thomas's failure to disclose the gifts violated the 1978 Act.

Crow's public statements characterised the arrangement as the "hospitality we have extended to the Thomases over the years is no different from the hospitality we have extended to our many other dear friends." Crow stated that he had "never asked about a pending or lower court case" and that "we have never sought to influence Justice Thomas on any legal or political issue." Crow did not dispute any of ProPublica's factual reporting. Crow holds board positions at the American Enterprise Institute and the Atlantic Council. His over-$10-million disclosed political donations across the period went to multiple Republican causes including the 2010 Citizens United-and-subsequent dark-money political infrastructure.

The structural question the Crow-Thomas record raises is whether a arrangement under which a Supreme Court justice receives roughly $500,000-$1 million or more in undisclosed gifts from a billionaire whose political-donor network includes the major financers of the conservative legal movement — including the Federalist Society, of which Leonard Leo is the co-chair, and which has organised the judicial-nomination pipeline that placed the current six-justice Republican majority on the Court (covered in Dossier 15) — constitutes a structural conflict of interest. The 2023 congressional response — the Senate Judiciary Committee chair Dick Durbin investigation; the Senate Finance Committee chair Ron Wyden 24 April 2023 letter to Crow demanding full gift accounting and gift-tax-return records — produced no enforcement action. The 13 November 2023 Supreme Court Code of Conduct — the first written code of conduct in the court's 234-year history — was adopted with no enforcement mechanism. Thomas remains on the Court. The arrangement remains.

## The Alito-Singer-Leo record

Samuel Alito — born 1 April 1950 at Trenton, New Jersey, to an Italian-American family; educated at Princeton (AB 1972) and Yale Law School (JD 1975) — was appointed to the Supreme Court by George W. Bush on 31 October 2005 to replace retiring Justice Sandra Day O'Connor. Confirmation 31 January 2006 by 58-42 vote.

The 20 June 2023 ProPublica investigation by Joshua Kaplan, Justin Elliott, and Alex Mierjeski documented that Alito had, in July 2008, flown on the private jet of Paul Singer — the hedge-fund-billionaire founder of Elliott Management — to King Salmon Lodge, a commercial fishing lodge in Alaska owned by Robin Arkley II, a major conservative legal-movement donor. The estimated one-way private-jet cost had Alito chartered it himself: over $100,000. The trip was at a lodge that charged more than $1,000 per day. Alito caught a king salmon nearly the size of his leg. He posed for a photograph with Singer and Leo. He did not disclose the trip on his 2008 financial disclosure form.

Leonard Leo of the Federalist Society (covered in Dossier 15) organised the trip. Leo invited Singer to join, according to a source familiar with the trip, and asked Singer if Leo and Alito could fly on the billionaire's jet. Leo had recently played a role in Alito's 2005-2006 confirmation. Singer and Arkley were both major donors to Leo's political groups.

Singer's Elliott Management subsequently had business before the Supreme Court at least 10 times across the 2008-2023 period per the ProPublica accounting. The single largest such case was the 2014 Republic of Argentina v. NML Capital, in which Singer's NML Capital subsidiary had spent more than a decade pursuing repayment from Argentina on debt purchased at post-2001-default deep discount. The Supreme Court ruled 7-1 in NML Capital's favour on 16 June 2014. Alito did not recuse. NML Capital collected $2.4 billion from Argentina.

Alito's 20 June 2023 Wall Street Journal op-ed, published hours before the ProPublica story appeared, defended the arrangement on three grounds: that he had followed "standard practice" of non-disclosure for "personal hospitality"; that he had "no obligation to recuse in any of the cases that ProPublica cites" because he was unaware of Singer's connection at the time of the rulings; and that ProPublica had misled its readers in its account. The ethics-law experts who reviewed the arrangement for ProPublica concluded that the personal-hospitality exemption did not cover private-jet transportation across continental US distance and that Alito's non-disclosure violated the 1978 Act.

The structural feature of the Alito-Singer-Leo arrangement is that it integrates three nodes of the conservative legal movement: the justice (Alito), the billionaire-donor with business before the court (Singer), and the pipeline-operator who selected the justice for the court (Leo). The 2008 Alaska trip was not an incidental social occasion. It was a multi-day gathering of three operators of the architecture that placed Alito on the court, that placed Singer's cases before Alito on the court, and that has subsequently produced the 5-4 and 6-3 rulings that converted the pre-2018 American constitutional settlement on reproductive rights, affirmative action, federal-agency regulatory authority, and presidential immunity into the post-2022 settlement that Dossier 15 covers in detail.

## The Sotomayor-Penguin-Random-House record

Sonia Sotomayor — born 25 June 1954 at the Bronx, New York, to a Puerto Rican family; raised by her mother after her father's 1963 death; educated at Princeton (AB 1976) and Yale Law School (JD 1979) — was appointed to the Supreme Court by Barack Obama on 26 May 2009 to replace retiring Justice David Souter. Confirmation 6 August 2009 by 68-31 vote.

Sotomayor's post-confirmation Penguin Random House record is, on the documented financial-disclosure evidence, the single largest source of income above the judicial salary of any sitting Supreme Court justice throughout the post-2010 period. Her 2013 memoir *My Beloved World*, published by Knopf Doubleday (a Penguin Random House subsidiary), produced an initial $1.2 million advance (reported on her 2010 financial disclosure) and a two-payment additional advance totalling $1.9 million (reported on her 2012 disclosure). Cumulative pre-publication advance: $3.1 million. The subsequent 2017-2021 royalty-and-advance payments totalled roughly $500,000 across the five-year window, per the disclosure forms. Cumulative reported Penguin Random House income through 2022: roughly $3.6 million. The arrangement is, by an order of magnitude, the largest single income source above Sotomayor's judicial salary across the 2010-2022 period.

In 2013, Aaron Greenspan v. Random House — a copyright-infringement case in which Greenspan, a Harvard classmate of Mark Zuckerberg, alleged Random House had rejected his book proposal about the founding of Facebook and had instead awarded a deal to a different author whose book became the 2010 David Fincher film *The Social Network* — came before the Court on cert petition. Sotomayor did not recuse. Justice Stephen Breyer, who held stock in a company with a large stake in Penguin, did recuse. The cert petition was denied; the lower-court ruling for Random House stood.

In October 2019, children's author Jennie Nicassio petitioned the Court to hear her copyright-infringement case against Penguin Random House — alleging the publisher had copied her 2014 children's book *Rocky* about a small evergreen tree dreaming of becoming the Rockefeller Center Christmas tree, and had published a nearly identical book. On the same day the Nicassio cert petition was distributed to the justices, Sotomayor received a $10,586 check from Penguin Random House per the disclosure forms. The Supreme Court denied the cert petition on 24 February 2020. Sotomayor did not recuse. Her next check from Penguin Random House, in May 2020, was the largest she had received from the publisher: $82,807.

The Penguin Random House also paid for Sotomayor's travel to multiple promotional events, including the speaking engagements that accompanied the memoir's multi-year promotional tour.

The non-recusal record is, on the structural-ethics question that Justice Breyer's parallel recusal raises, anomalous. The reasonable-person standard under 28 U.S.C. § 455 requires a justice to recuse in any proceeding in which "his impartiality might reasonably be questioned." A reasonable person knowing that Penguin Random House had paid Sotomayor more than $3.5 million across the 2010-2020 period would question her impartiality in a copyright case in which Penguin Random House was the named defendant. The 2009 Judicial Conference Advisory Opinion No. 107 cited by Sotomayor's defenders permits non-recusal in cases involving a spouse's employer where the employer is not a party — but Penguin Random House was the named party in both the 2013 Greenspan case and the 2019-2020 Nicassio case. Justice Breyer's recusal in the 2013 Greenspan case establishes that the reasonable-person standard applied to the fact pattern. Sotomayor's non-recusal in the same fact pattern is the anomaly.

## The Gorsuch-Penguin-Random-House record

Neil Gorsuch — born 29 August 1967 at Denver, Colorado; son of Anne Gorsuch Burford (Reagan EPA Administrator 1981-1983); educated at Columbia (AB 1988), Harvard Law School (JD 1991), and Oxford (DPhil 2004) — was appointed by Donald Trump on 31 January 2017 to replace the 13 February 2016-deceased Justice Antonin Scalia. Confirmation 7 April 2017 by 54-45 vote after Mitch McConnell (covered in Dossier 9) triggered the "nuclear option" eliminating the 60-vote cloture threshold for Supreme Court nominations.

Gorsuch's 2019 book *A Republic, If You Can Keep It* was published by Crown Forum (a Penguin Random House subsidiary). Total reported Penguin Random House income through 2020 per his 2018, 2019, and 2020 disclosure forms: $655,000. In the 2019-2020 Nicassio v. Penguin Random House cert petition (covered in the Sotomayor section above), Gorsuch did not recuse. The cert denial of 24 February 2020 was unanimous in result; the court does not publicly record which justices voted to deny cert, but it does record recusals. Gorsuch did not recuse.

The separate 27 April 2017 Walden Group property transaction documented by Politico in April 2023: days after Gorsuch's Senate confirmation, Gorsuch sold his 40-acre Colorado property co-owned with other partners (the Walden Group LLC) to Brian Duffy, CEO of Greenberg Traurig (a law firm with repeated business before the Supreme Court). Gorsuch reported the sale on his 2017 disclosure form but did not name the buyer.

## The Roberts-Jane-Roberts record

John Roberts, Jr. — born 27 January 1955 at Buffalo, New York; raised in Indiana; son of John Glover Roberts Sr., a Bethlehem Steel plant manager and pension-plan administrator; educated at Harvard (AB 1976) and Harvard Law School (JD 1979) — was appointed by George W. Bush on 19 July 2005 to replace the 1 July 2005-retiring Sandra Day O'Connor, and on 5 September 2005 (after the 3 September 2005 death of Chief Justice William Rehnquist) was renominated as Chief Justice. Confirmation 29 September 2005 by 78-22 vote. He is the 17th Chief Justice.

The Roberts dossier-worthy record runs across two overlapping institutional axes. The first is the Jane Sullivan Roberts legal-recruiter income. The second is the record of the Chief Justice's major-rulings authorship — the 2010 Citizens United concurrence, the 2012 NFIB v. Sebelius opinion upholding the Affordable Care Act on the taxing-power ground, the 2013 Shelby County v. Holder gutting the Voting Rights Act, the 2022 Dobbs concurrence (in which Roberts would have upheld the Mississippi statute on narrower grounds without overturning Roe), the 2023 Students for Fair Admissions ruling (Roberts as author), the 2024 Trump v. United States immunity ruling (Roberts as author), the 2024 Loper Bright ruling overturning the 1984 Chevron framework (Roberts as author). The post-2005 court has, on the major rulings, was the Roberts Court in the straightforward sense: Roberts has personally authored the most consequential rulings of the court's post-2005 institutional production.

The Jane Sullivan Roberts record is, on the 2022-2023 documented evidence, the single most consequential disclosed-spousal-income record of any sitting Chief Justice. Per the December 2022 Kendal Price whistleblower complaint to the House and Senate Judiciary Committees and the Department of Justice — Price was a managing director at the Major, Lindsey & Africa legal-recruiting firm where Jane Roberts worked from 2007 to 2014 — Jane Roberts earned $10,323,842.70 in commissions over the 2007-2014 period on $13,309,433 of attributed firm revenue. A MLA partner characterised Jane Roberts in sworn testimony as "the highest earning recruiter in the entire company by a wide margin."

The documented placements that Jane Roberts brokered during the 2007-2014 period include former Interior Secretary Ken Salazar to WilmerHale; Robert Bennett to Hogan Lovells; former US Attorney Neil MacBride to Davis Polk; and New York Federal Reserve general counsel Michael Held to WilmerHale.

WilmerHale alone had 18 Supreme Court cases in the 2016 term. Seth Waxman of WilmerHale has argued more than 85 Supreme Court cases across his career. Hogan Lovells had 8 Supreme Court cases in the 2024 term and has represented roughly 10% of the court's docket in recent terms per the reporting. Across the 20+ years Roberts has sat on the court, the law firms paying his household in commissions through Jane Roberts have argued more than 500 cases before him. He recused from none of them on spousal-income grounds.

In 2015, Jane Roberts moved from MLA to Macrae and opened the Macrae Washington DC office. Her 2015-2025 income has not been disclosed in dollar terms. John Roberts's 2007-2021 financial-disclosure forms listed Jane Roberts's compensation as "salary." Pace University law professor Bennett Gershman, in a memo supporting the Price complaint, characterised the characterisation as factually-and-legally incorrect: "Characterizing Mrs. Roberts' commissions as 'salary' is not merely factually incorrect; it is incorrect as a matter of law. The legal distinction between these terms is clear, undisputed, and legally material. If the Chief Justice's inaccurate financial disclosures were inadvertent, presumably he should file corrected and amended disclosures." From 2022 onward, Roberts's disclosures changed the characterisation to "recoverable base salary and commission" without amending the prior 15 years of disclosures.

The structural feature of the Roberts-Jane-Roberts arrangement is that the Chief Justice of the United States has, across more than fifteen years on the court, ruled in cases brought by law firms that have paid his household upwards of $20 million in commissions through his spouse's recruiter role. He has not recused from any of those cases. The Judicial Conference Advisory Opinion No. 107 (2009) on which Roberts's defenders rely permits non-recusal in cases involving a spouse's employer where the employer is not a named party — but the law firms that have paid Jane Roberts commissions are the law firms that have argued the cases before John Roberts, not the employer (MLA, Macrae). The 2009 advisory opinion does not address the fact pattern. The reasonable-person standard under 28 U.S.C. § 455 — that a justice must recuse in any proceeding in which his impartiality might reasonably be questioned — is the governing standard, and a reasonable person would question the Chief Justice's impartiality in cases argued by law firms that have paid his household more than $20 million.

## The other current justices

### Elena Kagan
Born 28 April 1960 at New York City; educated at Princeton (AB 1981), Worcester College, Oxford (M.Phil. 1983), and Harvard Law School (JD 1986). White House Counsel's Office associate counsel and deputy assistant to the President 1995-1999 (Clinton administration). Harvard Law School Dean 2003-2009. Solicitor General 2009-2010. Appointed by Obama 7 August 2010; confirmation by 63-37 vote.

The Kagan disclosure record across the 2010-2026 period has not, on the documented public evidence, produced a single-donor-or-spouse-or-publisher arrangement of the magnitude documented in the Thomas, Alito, Sotomayor, Gorsuch, and Roberts records above. She has not published a commercial book on the Penguin Random House or comparable commercial-publisher advance scale. The absence-of-major-scandal record should not be confused with the absence of structural alignment with the Democratic-appointing political class; Kagan's pre-court 1995-1999 Clinton White House service and her Obama-administration Solicitor General service establish the structural integration of her pre-court career with the Atlantic-Democratic political class. The subsequent dissent record on the post-2022 major rulings (Dobbs, Students for Fair Admissions, Trump v. United States, Loper Bright) places her, with Sotomayor and Jackson, in the three-justice minority bloc that the 6-3 Republican-appointee majority routinely overrides on constitutional questions of consequence.

### Brett Kavanaugh
Born 12 February 1965 at Washington, D.C.; educated at Yale (BA 1987) and Yale Law School (JD 1990). Clerked for Justice Anthony Kennedy 1993-1994. White House Counsel's Office associate counsel under George W. Bush 2001-2003. White House Staff Secretary under George W. Bush 2003-2006. U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit 2006-2018. Appointed by Trump 9 July 2018 to replace retiring Justice Anthony Kennedy. Confirmation 6 October 2018 by 50-48 vote — the closest Supreme Court confirmation since 1881 at the time.

The confirmation hearings of September 2018 included Christine Blasey Ford's 27 September 2018 testimony to the Senate Judiciary Committee alleging that Kavanaugh had sexually assaulted her at a high-school party in 1982. Kavanaugh's 27 September 2018 testimony denied the allegation in emotional terms and characterised the allegation as a "calculated and orchestrated political hit" by Democrats and Clinton political operatives. The subsequent FBI supplemental background investigation was limited in scope and duration by the Trump White House per the reporting. The 6 October 2018 50-48 confirmation vote put Kavanaugh on the court.

Kavanaugh's pre-confirmation $200,000-$250,000 in credit-card-and-loan debts reportedly paid off in 2017 — disclosed but with the source of the payoff not publicly identified per the 2018 disclosure forms — remains the unresolved financial-disclosure question that the pre-confirmation Senate Judiciary Committee did not pursue.

### Amy Coney Barrett
Born 28 January 1972 at New Orleans, Louisiana; educated at Rhodes College (BA 1994) and Notre Dame Law School (JD 1997). Clerked for Justice Antonin Scalia 1998-1999. Notre Dame Law School faculty from 2002. U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit 2017-2020. Appointed by Trump 26 September 2020, eight days after the 18 September 2020 death of Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg. Confirmation 26 October 2020 by 52-48 vote — eight days before the 3 November 2020 presidential election.

The 2020 Barrett confirmation reversed the 2016 McConnell "Biden rule" position that election-year confirmations were inappropriate (McConnell's 2016 blockade of Merrick Garland is covered in Dossier 9). The reversal converted the 5-4 conservative majority into the 6-3 conservative supermajority that has subsequently authored the 2022 Dobbs, the 2023 Students for Fair Admissions, the 2024 Trump v. United States, and the 2024 Loper Bright rulings.

Barrett is a member of the People of Praise charismatic Catholic community, founded 1971 at South Bend, Indiana, that operates a "head" hierarchical structure under which married women are required to accept the spiritual authority of their husbands. Barrett did not disclose her People of Praise membership on her 2017 Seventh Circuit nomination Senate Judiciary Committee questionnaire; the 2020 Senate Judiciary Committee questionnaire likewise did not address it.

### Ketanji Brown Jackson
Born 14 September 1970 at Washington, D.C.; raised at Miami; educated at Harvard (AB 1992, magna cum laude) and Harvard Law School (JD 1996, cum laude). Clerked for Justice Stephen Breyer 1999-2000. Federal Public Defender 2005-2007. United States Sentencing Commission vice chair 2010-2014. U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia 2013-2021. U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit 2021-2022. Appointed by Biden 25 February 2022 to replace retiring Justice Stephen Breyer. Confirmation 7 April 2022 by 53-47 vote (three Republican votes: Collins, Murkowski, Romney). First Black woman to serve on the Supreme Court.

Jackson's 2023 financial disclosure included a $893,750 advance from Random House (a Penguin Random House subsidiary) for her 2024 memoir *Lovely One*. The arrangement matches the pattern documented in the Sotomayor and Gorsuch records above. The subsequent cert-petition recusal record on Penguin Random House cases remains to be tested across the post-2024 period.

## The post-2022 ruling sequence

The 24 June 2022 Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization ruling — 5-4 majority opinion authored by Alito (Thomas, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett joined; Roberts concurred separately on narrower grounds; Breyer, Sotomayor, Kagan dissented) — overturned Roe v. Wade (22 January 1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (29 June 1992). The subsequent abortion restriction sequence has, as of May 2026, produced comprehensive abortion bans in 13 states and major restrictions in 7 additional states per the reporting.

The 29 June 2023 Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard / UNC ruling — 6-3 ruling authored by Roberts (Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett joined; Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson dissented in the UNC case; Jackson recused in the Harvard case because she had served on the Harvard Board of Overseers) — ended race-conscious admissions in higher education. The 1978 Bakke and 2003 Grutter framework that had governed affirmative action for 45 years was overturned.

The 30 June 2023 303 Creative LLC v. Elenis ruling — 6-3 ruling that a Colorado web designer had a First Amendment right to refuse to create wedding websites for same-sex couples — extended the 2014 Hobby Lobby and 2018 Masterpiece Cakeshop religious-exemption framework into the anti-discrimination context.

The 1 July 2024 Trump v. United States ruling — 6-3 ruling authored by Roberts (Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett joined; Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson dissented) — held that former presidents have absolute immunity for official acts within the "exclusive constitutional authority" of the presidency and presumptive immunity for other official acts. The ruling functionally terminated the pre-2024 Jack Smith special-counsel federal criminal prosecutions of Donald Trump (the 4 January 6 case and the classified-documents Mar-a-Lago case). Justice Sotomayor's dissent concluded: "With fear for our democracy, I dissent." The ruling was the single most consequential pre-November-2024 election-relevant ruling of the post-2022 court. The subsequent November 2024 Trump re-election (covered in Dossier 2) was structurally enabled by the decision.

The 28 June 2024 Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo ruling — 6-3 ruling authored by Roberts (Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett joined; Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson dissented) — overturned the 1984 Chevron v. NRDC framework that had governed federal-agency regulatory authority for 40 years. The subsequent post-2024 administrative-state restructuring will be the subject of subsequent litigation across the next decade.

## The structural relationship to the Federalist Society pipeline

Five of the six Republican-appointed justices currently sitting — Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett — were Federalist-Society-vetted or Federalist-Society-affiliated nominees. Roberts is not formally Federalist Society but has the conservative-legal-movement institutional positioning that has produced the majority opinions since the post-2022 period. The Federalist Society judicial-pipeline operation under Leonard Leo — covered in detail in Dossier 15 (Federalist Society and Heritage apparatus) — is the architecture that placed the 6-3 majority on the court. The $1.6 billion 2021 donation from Barre Seid to a Leo-controlled 501(c)(4) — the largest politically-directed gift in US history per the reporting — funds the continuing operation of the pipeline.

## The structural verdict

The Roberts Court is a political-class operating apparatus. Its nine justices hold their positions because the political class that appointed them and confirmed them needed them in those positions. Its major rulings track the political-class interests of the constituencies that placed them. Its post-2018 ethics record — the Thomas-Crow $500,000-$1-million-plus undisclosed gift trove, the Alito-Singer Alaska trip, the Sotomayor-Penguin-Random-House $3.6 million non-recusal, the Gorsuch-Penguin-Random-House non-recusal, the Roberts-Jane-Roberts $20-million-plus household income from law firms practicing before the chief justice — is the governing record of the post-2018 court's structural compromise.

The 13 November 2023 Supreme Court Code of Conduct — the first written code in the court's 234-year history — has no enforcement mechanism. The subsequent 2024-2025 congressional efforts to legislate enforceable judicial ethics standards have failed under the post-2024 Republican-majority Senate. The Court remains, as of May 2026, the single most powerful institutional component of the Atlantic political-class apparatus this volume documents, operating with lifetime tenure, unrecused conflicts of interest, and no enforceable accountability mechanism.

The mask of the Roberts Court is the dignified-press characterisation of the court as a good-faith constitutional adjudicator above politics. The actual record is the subject of this dossier.

## Bridge to Dossier 14

The post-2001 intelligence-community principals — Brennan, Clapper, Burns, Haines, Wray, Patel, Gabbard, and the NSA leadership throughout the post-2001 period — operate the parallel apparatus to the Supreme Court at the intelligence-and-national-security end of the unaccountable-institutional spectrum. The next dossier documents the IC principal record.

# Dossier 14: The Intelligence Community Principals — The Institutional Apparatus and Its Operators

## The institution

The American intelligence community is the post-1947 apparatus that the National Security Act of 26 July 1947 built on the foundation of the wartime Office of Strategic Services. It consists of 18 federal agencies and offices coordinated since 2005 under the Office of the Director of National Intelligence. Its three pre-eminent components — the Central Intelligence Agency, the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and the National Security Agency — operate the human-intelligence, domestic-counterintelligence, and signals-intelligence functions of the post-1947 architecture. Across the post-2001 period that this dossier covers, the IC has expanded its budget from roughly $30 billion in fiscal 2001 to roughly $107.7 billion in fiscal 2024 (combined National Intelligence Program plus Military Intelligence Program per the Office of the Director of National Intelligence's annual disclosure), and its personnel from roughly 100,000 in 2001 to over 200,000 cleared employees and contractors. The post-9/11 expansion produced the institutional and legal architecture — Patriot Act 2001, FISA Amendments Act 2008, Section 702, Section 215 bulk metadata collection, the Stellar Wind warrantless wiretap programme, the Disposition Matrix drone-strike targeting database — that the contemporary American national-security state operates on.

The IC principals this record covers — the post-2001 CIA Directors, FBI Directors, Directors of National Intelligence, and NSA Directors — are the operators of that architecture. Their pre-government careers, their conduct in office, and their post-government commercial integrations constitute the record of a single coordinated political-class formation that has, across the period, transferred billions of dollars in personal compensation from the post-9/11 intelligence-budget expansion to the operators' subsequent private-sector positions. The dignified-press characterisation of the post-9/11 IC as a non-partisan technical-professional national-security apparatus is, documented, false. The IC is a political-class institutional layer with its own distinct interests, its own donor and corporate-board integrations, and its own routine post-government commercial monetisation through cable-news-network contributor appointments, defence-contractor board memberships, private-equity advisory positions, and authoritative-text memoir publications through the same Penguin Random House and Macmillan corporate publishing apparatus documented in Dossier 13. The dossier that follows documents the named operators.

## John Brennan — the CIA Director who institutionalised the Disposition Matrix

John Brennan, born 22 September 1955 at North Bergen, New Jersey to a Catholic working-class family, joined the CIA in 1980 after a Fordham BA, an University of Texas at Austin MA, and an American University-Cairo year abroad with Arabic study. He rose across the 21-year pre-2001 career through analyst, station-chief Saudi Arabia 1996-1999 (during the 1996 Khobar Towers bombing investigation), chief of staff to CIA Director George Tenet 1999-2001, and deputy executive director CIA 2001-2003. Under Tenet's CIA, Brennan was structurally adjacent to the decisions that produced the September 2001 intelligence-coordination failure that the 9/11 Commission documented and to the 2002 "slam dunk" Iraqi WMD briefing that Tenet personally delivered to George W. Bush as the justification for the March 2003 Iraq invasion. Brennan was not the signatory of those failures but was a member of the CIA leadership that produced them.

From 2004 to 2005 Brennan served as the first director of the National Counterterrorism Center, then from 2005 to 2008 as president of The Analysis Corporation, a CIA contractor producing intelligence-analysis product on contract terms that were not made public. The 2008 Obama campaign brought Brennan into the transition team as the intelligence-adviser-of-record; Obama's 20 January 2009 appointment of Brennan as Assistant to the President for Homeland Security and Counterterrorism made him the coordinator of the post-2009 counter-terrorism programme that the eight-year Obama presidency expanded into the most systematic American extraterritorial-killing programme on the post-1945 historical record.

The 2011-2012 Brennan-led restructuring of the US drone-strike targeting process — what came to be called the "Disposition Matrix" after the 23 October 2012 Washington Post Greg Miller-Karen DeYoung reporting — concentrated authority over the US kill lists in the White House counter-terrorism office under Brennan. The 30 April 2012 Brennan speech at the Woodrow Wilson Center in Washington was the first public Obama-administration acknowledgement of the CIA drone programme in Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, Libya, and Afghanistan. Brennan's speech defended the programme on legality, morality, and effectiveness grounds; he characterised civilian casualties as "exceedingly rare" and claimed that the CIA targeting "literally" did not kill civilians — the characterisation that the subsequent Bureau of Investigative Journalism multi-year database documentation revised to roughly 2,500-4,000 cumulative US drone-strike deaths in Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia 2002-2017 with roughly 400-1,000 civilian deaths among the cumulative total. The 29 May 2012 New York Times Becker-Shane reporting documented Brennan as the "principal coordinator" of US kill lists; former Obama counter-terrorism official Daniel Benjamin characterised Brennan as having "probably more power and influence than anyone in a comparable position in the last 20 years."

The September 2011 killing of US citizen Anwar al-Awlaki in Yemen by US drone strike — and the subsequent 14 October 2011 killing of al-Awlaki's 16-year-old US-citizen son Abdulrahman al-Awlaki in a separate US drone strike — was the single most consequential pre-2017 US extraterritorial-killing-of-US-citizens episode that the Brennan-coordinated programme produced. The 29 January 2017 US special-operations raid in Yemen authorised in Trump's first week killed Awlaki's 8-year-old US-citizen daughter Nawar al-Awlaki — completing the elimination of three US-citizen members of the same family across the preceding six-year period.

Brennan was nominated CIA Director on 8 March 2013. Senator Rand Paul's 13-hour 6-7 March 2013 filibuster on the Senate floor demanded an Obama-administration commitment that the US would not deploy drone strikes against US citizens on US soil. Eric Holder's 7 March 2013 letter confirmed that the administration would not. The 7 March 2013 Senate confirmation vote was 63-34. Brennan's 8 March 2013 - 20 January 2017 CIA Director tenure consolidated the architecture he had built in the prior four years at the White House.

The 9 December 2014 Senate Intelligence Committee Torture Report — under chair Dianne Feinstein and the multi-year staff investigation under Daniel Jones — documented the CIA detention-and-interrogation programme of 2001-2009 including the multi-site black-site programme that the subsequent CIA Director Gina Haspel had personally overseen at Detention Site Green (Thailand) in 2002-2003. The March 2014 Feinstein floor speech accused the CIA of unauthorised searches of Senate staff computers during the Torture Report investigation. Brennan initially denied: "we wouldn't do that." The 31 July 2014 CIA Inspector General report confirmed five CIA employees had accessed Senate staff drives. Brennan apologised privately to Feinstein. He was not removed. The CIA inspector-general accountability process produced no consequences for the CIA officers responsible.

After the 20 January 2017 end of his CIA tenure, Brennan moved into the cable-news commercial circuit. The 27 February 2018 NBC News and MSNBC announcement that Brennan had been hired as the "national security and intelligence analyst" institutionalised the multi-year Brennan commentary-and-political-attack platform. Brennan's 16 July 2018 Twitter post characterising Trump's Helsinki conference performance as "nothing short of treasonous and is a betrayal of our nation" produced Trump's 15 August 2018 revocation of Brennan's security clearance. Brennan's 2020 memoir *Undaunted: My Fight Against America's Enemies, at Home and Abroad* (Celadon Books / Macmillan) extended the commercial integration. The 2018-present University of Texas at Austin distinguished-fellow appointment, the multi-year MSNBC contributor income, and the speaking-circuit appearances constitute the post-CIA Brennan commercial portfolio.

The structural feature of the Brennan record is that the architect of the US extraterritorial-killing programme — including the killing of three US-citizen members of the al-Awlaki family — moved into the post-government cable-news commentary role from which he characterised the subsequent Trump political-class apparatus as treasonous. The dignified-press treatment of Brennan as a good-faith post-political national-security adult speaking on MSNBC against the Trump excess softens to the point of erasure the previous six-year Obama-administration record under which the same Brennan personally coordinated the killing of roughly 2,500-4,000 people across five countries with roughly 400-1,000 civilian deaths in the cumulative total. The structural function of the post-2017 Brennan position is not analysis. It is institutional rehabilitation for the preceding career.

## James Clapper — the DNI who lied under oath

James Clapper, born 14 March 1941 at Fort Wayne, Indiana, served in the USAF intelligence functions from his 1963 commission through his 1995 retirement as Lieutenant General — a 32-year career across the Vietnam, post-Vietnam, and post-Cold-War periods. Defense Intelligence Agency Director 1991-1995. National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency Director 2001-2006. Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence 2007-2010 under George W. Bush and Obama. Senate confirmation as Director of National Intelligence 5 August 2010 produced the longest DNI tenure at 6.5 years — the six-and-a-half-year period in which Clapper coordinated the post-Bush IC during the post-9/11 architecture's maturation.

The 12 March 2013 Senate Intelligence Committee hearing produced the single most consequential pre-Snowden Clapper testimony. Senator Ron Wyden, whose reform-and-disclosure efforts produced the 2013 bulk-metadata-programme controversy, asked Clapper: "Does the NSA collect any type of data at all on millions or hundreds of millions of Americans?" Clapper's 12 March 2013 recorded response was: "No, sir." Wyden pressed: "It does not?" Clapper: "Not wittingly. There are cases where they could inadvertently perhaps collect, but not wittingly." The 5 June 2013 Edward Snowden leaked documents — first published by Glenn Greenwald and Ewen MacAskill in The Guardian — established that the NSA was under FISA Court order collecting Section 215 bulk telephony metadata on tens of millions of Americans through a daily bulk-data delivery from Verizon and other major telecommunications carriers.

The 30 June 2013 Clapper letter to Senator Feinstein characterised his 12 March 2013 testimony as "visibly erroneous" and the 9 June 2013 Andrea Mitchell NBC News interview characterised the March answer as "the least untruthful" he could give in the unclassified setting. No perjury charges were brought. The subsequent multi-year Department of Justice review concluded the factual-falsity-and-intentionality threshold for 18 U.S.C. § 1621 perjury was not met. The statute of limitations expired March 2018.

Clapper remained DNI through 20 January 2017 — the nearly four-year post-perjury-question continuation of his tenure that the accountability architecture did not interrupt. After the 20 January 2017 end of his DNI tenure, Clapper moved to the CNN national-security analyst contributor role from which he, like Brennan, became a multi-year anti-Trump cable-news commentator. The 23 May 2018 Clapper memoir *Facts and Fears: Hard Truths from a Life in Intelligence* (Viking / Penguin Random House) extended the commercial integration. The speaking-circuit and multi-year media-appearance income constitute the post-DNI Clapper commercial portfolio.

The structural feature of the Clapper record is that the longest-tenured DNI in the post-2005 institutional history lied under oath to the Senate Intelligence Committee on the single most consequential intelligence-collection question of the post-9/11 period; faced no accountability for the false testimony; completed his tenure with full institutional support from the Obama administration; and moved into the post-government cable-news commentary role from which he, like Brennan, became an anti-Trump moral-authority figure. The dignified-press treatment of Clapper as a good-faith post-political national-security adult speaking on CNN against Trump excess elides the prior perjury record entirely.

## Christopher Wray — the FBI Director who served Trump-and-Biden-and-was-fired

Christopher Wray, born 17 December 1966 at New York City, educated at Yale (BA 1989) and Yale Law (JD 1992), built a pre-FBI career as a federal prosecutor (US Attorney's Office Northern District of Georgia 1997-2001), Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General DOJ 2001-2003, and Assistant Attorney General Criminal Division 2003-2005. His 2003-2005 Criminal Division tenure oversaw the Enron prosecution and the corporate-fraud enforcement programme.

Between 2005 and 2017 Wray was a King & Spalding partner — the 12-year corporate-defence practice. King & Spalding's major clients during the Wray tenure included multi-national corporate clients on FCPA-enforcement, antitrust, and white-collar criminal matters. Wray personally represented Chris Christie during the 2014 Bridgegate investigation, a multi-million-dollar legal-representation engagement that the multi-year Christie-and-Wray professional relationship continued. King & Spalding's corporate-defence practice operated on the compensation structure that the law-firm-and-Supreme-Court material in Dossier 13 documented — equity-partner compensation in the multi-million-dollar annual range during the 2012-2017 period.

Trump nominated Wray as FBI Director on 7 June 2017, after the 9 May 2017 firing of James Comey. Senate confirmation 1 August 2017 by 92-5 vote — the bipartisan support reflecting the pre-Trump-era Wray reputation as a conventional Republican-appointed law-enforcement professional. Wray's multi-year FBI Director tenure ran across both the remainder of the Trump first term and the full Biden administration — the 7.5-year tenure that included the 6 January 2021 Capitol attack and the FBI investigation that produced roughly 1,500 federal charges across the multi-defendant cases.

The 8 August 2022 FBI search of Trump's Mar-a-Lago residence under federal search warrant — produced by the multi-year National Archives recovery effort and the special-counsel investigation under Jack Smith — was the single most politically consequential FBI operation of the Wray tenure. The subsequent 8 June 2023 classified-documents indictment of Trump, the 13 July 2024 Aileen Cannon dismissal of the case, and the subsequent November 2024 Trump re-election produced the 11 December 2024 Wray resignation announcement — effective 20 January 2025, the day before Trump's second-term inauguration.

The structural feature of the Wray record is that the conventional Republican-appointed law-enforcement professional who served as FBI Director across the 2017-2024 period was, by the end of his tenure, on the wrong side of the post-2024 Trump-political-class settlement that produced the 20 February 2025 Kash Patel confirmation. Wray's pre-political career — King & Spalding partner, Chris Christie attorney, conventional federalist-society-adjacent corporate-defence lawyer — was, on the pre-2017 assessment, the most  conservative possible FBI Director appointment. The subsequent 2017-2024 tenure produced the firing-and-replacement sequence that the subsequent Patel installation completed.

## William Burns — the career diplomat who ran the CIA under Biden

William Burns, born 11 April 1956 at Fort Bragg, North Carolina, son of Major General William F. Burns USA, educated at La Salle University (BA 1978) and Oxford as a Marshall Scholar (D.Phil. 1982), entered the US Foreign Service in 1982. His 33-year career across multiple State Department appointments spanned the post-1980 multi-administration diplomatic establishment: Ambassador to Jordan 1998-2001 under Clinton; Assistant Secretary of State for Near Eastern Affairs 2001-2005 under George W. Bush; Ambassador to Russia 2005-2008 under George W. Bush; Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs 2008-2011 under Obama; Deputy Secretary of State 2011-2014 under Obama. The 2014 retirement transitioned to the 2014-2021 presidency of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace — the establishment foreign-policy think tank that the post-Cold-War multi-administration foreign-policy elite operates through.

Biden's 11 January 2021 nomination of Burns as CIA Director — the unusual choice of a career diplomat rather than a career intelligence officer — was confirmed unanimously by the Senate on 18 March 2021. Burns's 19 March 2021 - 20 January 2025 tenure produced three major operational records.

First, the 23 August 2021 secret meeting in Kabul with Taliban leader Abdul Ghani Baradar on the 31 August 2021 US military withdrawal deadline. The subsequent 26 August 2021 Kabul airport bombing killed 13 US service members and roughly 170 Afghan civilians. The 30 August 2021 US withdrawal completed the 20-year US presence in Afghanistan.

Second, the November 2021 trip to Moscow to notify Nikolai Patrushev, the secretary of Putin's security council, that the US believed Putin was considering a full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Burns warned that if Putin invaded, the West would respond in a way that would have severe consequences for Russia. The 24 February 2022 Russian invasion proceeded. The subsequent multi-year US intelligence-and-weapons support to Ukraine — which Burns coordinated from the CIA side — has produced the roughly $175 billion cumulative US assistance commitment to Ukraine 2022-2025 per the reporting.

Third, the Israel-Hamas mediation 2023-2024 — Burns's multi-trip Doha-and-Cairo shuttle diplomacy on the multi-round hostage-negotiation efforts that produced the 24 November 2023 week-long ceasefire and the unsuccessful follow-on negotiation rounds.

Burns is, on the documented public record, the most  conventional and least scandal-producing CIA Director of the post-2001 period. The 2019 memoir *The Back Channel: A Memoir of American Diplomacy and the Case for Its Renewal* (Random House) is the pre-CIA-tenure career retrospective. No post-CIA commercial-board or media-contributor appointment has been announced as of May 2026.

The structural feature of the Burns record is that it stands as the counter-pattern to the other CIA Directors documented in this dossier — Tenet's Iraq WMD failure, Goss's brief tenure and subsequent commercial integration, Hayden's post-NSA Chertoff Group co-founding and CNN commercial integration, Petraeus's multi-year KKR Global Institute chair, Brennan's MSNBC contributor and memoir, Pompeo's subsequent State-Department-and-Trump-administration trajectory, Haspel's 2002-2003 Thailand Detention Site Green station-chief command and 2005 destruction of 92 interrogation tapes documented in the 2014 Senate Torture Report. The Burns counter-pattern is institutionally instructive: a career diplomat without prior CIA service can run the CIA without generating the commercial-rehabilitation pattern that the other CIA Directors documented in this dossier have all produced. The structural question this raises is whether the CIA-Director-to-cable-news-contributor pipeline is structural — produced by the commercial-news-network and post-government compensation economics that the other directors have all monetised — or whether it is personal. The Burns case suggests the pattern is at least partially personal.

## Avril Haines — the DNI under Biden

Avril Haines, born 27 August 1969 at New York City, educated at the University of Chicago (BA 1992, physics) and Georgetown (JD 2001), built a pre-DC career that included running the Adrian Book Store in Baltimore — an independent bookstore she co-owned with her subsequent husband David Davighi from 1992 to 1999. The career inflection at age 32 from bookstore-and-judo-academy operator to Senate Foreign Relations Committee staffer under Joe Biden 2003-2007 produced the subsequent 18-year national-security establishment career.

State Department Office of the Legal Adviser 2007-2008. White House Counsel Office 2010-2013. Deputy National Security Advisor 2013-2015 under Obama. Deputy CIA Director 2013-2015 — first woman in the role. Pre-2021 commercial integration: WestExec Advisors (founded by Antony Blinken and Michèle Flournoy) consulting; Brookings Institution multi-year fellow positions.

Biden's 8 January 2021 nomination of Haines was confirmed by the Senate 84-10 on 20 January 2021 — the first DNI confirmed on inauguration day. Haines's 20 January 2021 - 20 January 2025 DNI tenure ran across the full Biden presidency. The multi-year intelligence-coordination during the 2022-2024 Russia-Ukraine war and the 2023-2024 Israel-Hamas conflict were the most consequential operational features. Haines's 18 October 2023 US Senate briefing on the Israel-Hamas conflict and the subsequent US intelligence support to Israeli forces — the intelligence-and-targeting support that the 2023-2025 Gaza operations relied on — is the single most consequential operational episode of the Haines DNI tenure that the subsequent Gaza chapter of this volume will document.

No post-DNI commercial integration has been announced as of May 2026.

## Tulsi Gabbard — the DNI under Trump second-term

Tulsi Gabbard, born 12 April 1981 at Leloaloa, American Samoa, to Anglo-American mother Carol Porter and Samoan-American father Mike Gabbard (a Republican-then-Democratic Hawaii state senator), entered Hawaii politics aged 21 with her 2002 election to the Hawaii State House. Hawaii Army National Guard from 2003 with multiple Middle East and Africa deployments; she now holds the rank of Lieutenant Colonel US Army Reserve. US House of Representatives (HI-2) 2013-2021 for four terms as a Democrat. 2020 Democratic presidential primary campaign withdrew before most major primary contests. 2022 departure from the Democratic Party. 2024 endorsement of Trump.

The pre-2025 Gabbard public record included two particularly contested episodes. First, the January 2017 trip to Damascus and the meeting with Bashar al-Assad — at a moment when US policy formally opposed the Assad regime and when the multi-year Syrian civil-war atrocities had been documented by multiple international bodies. Gabbard publicly characterised the trip as a fact-finding mission. Second, the 2020 bill she introduced as a congresswoman to amend the Espionage Act to provide protections for whistleblowers including Edward Snowden — the bill that the 2025 confirmation hearings characterised as a functional Snowden pardon.

Trump nominated Gabbard as DNI in November 2024. The 4 February 2025 Senate Intelligence Committee 9-8 party-line vote advanced her nomination after multi-week pressure on Senators Todd Young (Indiana) and Susan Collins (Maine) from Vice President JD Vance, Elon Musk, former Senator Kyrsten Sinema, and Senator Tom Cotton. Young announced his support in committee after outlining commitments Gabbard made: not to seek a pardon for Snowden; regularly update the Senate Intel committee on her foreign travel; work with the committee to reauthorise Section 702 of FISA. The 12 February 2025 full-Senate confirmation by 52-48 vote made Gabbard the eighth Senate-confirmed DNI and the first female combat veteran in the role. Senator Mitch McConnell was the only Republican to vote against her — his 12 February 2025 post-vote statement said that Gabbard "failed to demonstrate" she was prepared for the role and that the country "should not have to worry that the intelligence assessments the President receives are tainted by a Director of National Intelligence with a history of alarming lapses in judgment."

Gabbard's 12 February 2025 swearing-in by Attorney General Pam Bondi at the Oval Office produced her characterisation of her mandate: "President Trump's re-election is a clear mandate from the American people to end the weaponization and politicization of the IC."

The structural question the Gabbard appointment raises is whether the IC under a DNI whose pre-2025 public record included the Snowden-protection legislation and the Assad meeting can perform the coordinating function that the 2004 IRTPA established the position for. The subsequent 2025 IC-internal-reorganisation activity under Gabbard has produced multiple media reports of personnel changes, budget reallocations, and subsequent internal-restructuring efforts that this dossier records as in-progress as of May 2026.

## Kash Patel — the FBI Director under Trump second-term

Kashyap Pramod "Kash" Patel, born 25 February 1980 at Garden City, New York, to Indian-American Gujarati parents — his father immigrated from East Africa via Canada — was educated at the University of Richmond (BA in criminal justice and history) and Pace University Law School (JD). He began his career as a public defender in Miami-Dade County in 2005; he served as a federal public defender for the Southern District of Florida from 2009 to 2014; and then transitioned to DOJ National Security Division as a trial attorney 2014-2017.

The 2017 inflection that built Patel's subsequent trajectory was his appointment as counsel to the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence under Chairman Devin Nunes — a position from which Patel was the primary author of the 2 February 2018 Nunes Memo. The memo alleged FBI abuse of the FISA process in obtaining the October 2016 surveillance warrant on Carter Page (the Trump campaign foreign-policy adviser whose 2013-2016 contacts with Russian intelligence the FBI was investigating). The memo's public release was authorised by Trump over the FBI and DOJ objection. The subsequent 2019 DOJ Inspector General Michael Horowitz report on the FISA process found 17 significant errors in the FBI's FISA applications on Page; the IG report did not, however, find the systemic bias that the Nunes Memo had alleged.

After the 2018 Nunes-Memo period, Patel moved into the Trump administration's national-security apparatus: National Security Council director for Counterterrorism 2019-2020; ODNI principal deputy 2020 (under the brief Ratcliffe DNI tenure); DoD Chief of Staff to acting Defense Secretary Christopher Miller November 2020 - January 2021 (during the post-election turbulent 6 January 2021 period).

The 2017 Trump administration consideration of Patel as FBI Deputy Director was blocked by Attorney General Bill Barr — Barr's 2022 memoir *One Damn Thing After Another* characterised the proposal as one Barr rejected: "over my dead body."

In the post-2021 period, Patel built a media-and-commercial portfolio: 2022 book *Government Gangsters: The Deep State, the Truth, and the Battle for Our Republic* including a named list of 60 "deep state" officials; podcast *Kash's Corner*; multi-platform conservative-media appearances; reported $1 million-plus speaking-and-media income across the 2022-2024 period per the subsequent 2024-2025 disclosure reporting. The NBC News content analysis of 79 *Kash's Corner* episodes documented "detailed but unfounded claims of conspiracies involving government officials, law enforcement agencies, the media and tech companies, among others, all aiming to rig elections, silence conservative voices and undermine Trump's presidency and re-election." Patel also engaged QAnon community content: appearances on QAnon podcasts; signing copies of *The Plot Against the King* with "#WWG1WGA"; creating the #FlannelFridays trend.

Trump's 30 November 2024 announcement of Patel as the nominee for FBI Director produced two responses from preceding officials. Wray's 11 December 2024 resignation announcement preceded the nomination's Senate confirmation. Roughly 24 former Republican government officials sent a 28 January 2025 letter to senators urging them to reject Patel's nomination on the grounds of his pre-nomination conduct.

The 20 February 2025 Senate confirmation by 51-49 — with Senators Susan Collins (Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) the only Republican defections — installed Patel as the 9th FBI Director on a 10-year term. The subsequent March 2025 internal FBI restructuring under Patel — including the reorganisation of FBI field offices to report to branch directors rather than the Deputy Director, and the February 2025 call with FBI officials in which Patel proposed altering the FBI physical fitness test and partnering with Ultimate Fighting Championship — has produced the reporting on the 2025 FBI internal turbulence.

The 24 February 2025 - April 2025 acting ATF Director appointment in addition to the FBI Director role was the unusual dual-agency leadership arrangement that the subsequent April 2025 replacement at ATF terminated. Trump's March 2025 speech announced the plan to move FBI headquarters from the J. Edgar Hoover Building to the "old Department of Commerce building" — a continued reduction in the FBI's Washington-headquarters footprint.

The structural feature of the Patel installation is that the FBI Director position — designed under the 1976 post-Hoover reforms as a 10-year term insulated from political appointment — has, under the 2024-2025 Trump second-term transition, become a politically-loyal-to-Trump appointment held by a pre-nomination podcast-and-conspiracy-content producer. The subsequent 2025-2035 Patel FBI tenure will be the subject of subsequent reporting; the pre-confirmation record is the subject of this dossier.

## John Ratcliffe — the only person to hold both DNI and CIA Director

John Ratcliffe, born 20 October 1965 at Mount Prospect, Illinois, served as US Representative for Texas's 4th congressional district 2015-2020. His 2019 questioning of Special Counsel Robert Mueller during the 24 July 2019 House Judiciary Committee testimony — in which Ratcliffe pressed Mueller on the procedural framing of the Special Counsel report's obstruction-of-justice section — produced the subsequent Trump-personal-favourite status that the 2020 DNI nomination institutionalised.

Trump's 2019 first nomination of Ratcliffe as DNI was withdrawn after bipartisan questioning of his qualifications. The 2020 re-nomination produced confirmation 21 May 2020 by 49-44 vote — the narrowest DNI confirmation in the history of the position, with no Democratic support. The 21 May 2020 - 20 January 2021 eight-month DNI tenure included the September 2020 declassification of the CIA memo on alleged 2016 Clinton-Russia operation — declassification opposed by CIA Director Gina Haspel and other CIA officials, who characterised the intelligence as unverified and potentially Russian-disinformation product.

Trump's November 2024 nomination of Ratcliffe as CIA Director — and the 23 January 2025 Senate confirmation by 74-25 with 21 Democrats and Independent Angus King supporting — made Ratcliffe the first person to hold both DNI and CIA Director positions in US history. The 23 January 2025 swearing-in by Vice President JD Vance made Ratcliffe the second confirmed Cabinet member of Trump's second term after Rubio. The subsequent 2025 CIA-Director tenure has identified China as the US "greatest geopolitical rival" and pledged to develop offensive cyber tools.

## The NSA leadership pipeline

The NSA Directors in the post-2001 period are: Michael Hayden (1999-2005); Keith Alexander (2005-2014); Michael Rogers (2014-2018); Paul Nakasone (2018-2024); Timothy Haugh (2024-April 2025).

Hayden's 1999-2005 NSA tenure oversaw the post-9/11 warrantless surveillance programmes including Stellar Wind — the multi-component domestic-surveillance programme that the 16 December 2005 New York Times James Risen-Eric Lichtblau reporting first publicly disclosed. Hayden subsequently served as CIA Director 2006-2009 and, after retiring from federal service in 2009, co-founded the Chertoff Group (with former DHS Secretary Michael Chertoff) — the security-consultancy that the post-9/11 security-industrial-complex commercial integration has built. Hayden was also a CNN national-security contributor and Carnegie Mellon distinguished visiting professor since the post-2009 period.

Alexander's 2005-2014 NSA tenure was the longest in NSA history. Under Alexander, the agency operated the PRISM, Upstream, and bulk metadata programmes documented in the 2013 Snowden disclosures. After Alexander's 28 March 2014 retirement, he founded IronNet Cybersecurity — the publicly-traded (NYSE: IRNT, 2021-2023) commercial cybersecurity firm that monetised the post-NSA Alexander commercial trajectory. Alexander has also served on the Amazon Web Services Public Sector Advisory Board.

Rogers's 2014-2018 tenure as both NSA Director and US Cyber Command commander bridged the Obama and Trump first administrations. Nakasone's 2018-2024 tenure continued the dual-hat NSA-and-Cyber-Command arrangement. Haugh's April 2024 succession of Nakasone produced the briefest NSA Director tenure in recent history: Haugh and NSA Deputy Wendy Noble were fired by Trump on 3 April 2025 after Trump met with right-wing activist Laura Loomer at the Oval Office. Lieutenant General William Hartman was named acting NSA Director.

The structural feature of the NSA leadership pipeline throughout the post-2001 period is the documented Hayden-Alexander commercial integration with the security-industrial-complex contractor base. The Chertoff Group, IronNet Cybersecurity, and the multiple subsequent NSA-alumni cybersecurity startups operate on the integration that the NSA tenures built. The NSA leadership has been, on the documented post-retirement record, the most uniformly commercially-integrated component of the post-2001 IC principals — more uniformly so than the CIA Directors documented above. The structural question this raises is whether the signals-intelligence capabilities that NSA Directors learn in office have a private-sector commercial value that the post-retirement security-industrial-complex compensates for — and what consequences that compensation pattern has for the in-office decisions of NSA Directors who can anticipate the post-government integration.

## The integrated institutional record

The 24 IC principals across the post-2001 period — CIA Directors Tenet, Goss, Hayden, Panetta, Petraeus, Brennan, Pompeo, Haspel, Burns, Ratcliffe; FBI Directors Mueller, Comey, Wray, Patel; DNIs Negroponte, McConnell, Blair, Clapper, Coats, Ratcliffe, Haines, Gabbard; NSA Directors Hayden, Alexander, Rogers, Nakasone, Haugh — comprise the operating leadership of the post-9/11 IC. The cumulative budget under their tenures was, summed across the 2001-2024 period, roughly $1.6 trillion in National Intelligence Program plus Military Intelligence Program disclosed totals per the ODNI annual disclosures. The cumulative personnel under their direction grew from roughly 100,000 in 2001 to over 200,000 cleared employees and contractors by 2024. The cumulative legal and architecture they built — Patriot Act, FISA Amendments Act, Section 702, Section 215 (until 2020), Stellar Wind, PRISM, Upstream, Disposition Matrix, the extraterritorial-drone-strike programme that killed roughly 2,500-4,000 people in Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia alone — is the structural product of their coordinated career.

Their post-government commercial portfolios — Hayden's Chertoff Group, Petraeus's KKR Global Institute, Alexander's IronNet, Brennan's MSNBC, Clapper's CNN, Comey's *A Higher Loyalty* memoir at Macmillan, the multi-million-dollar speaking-circuit appearances, the revolving-door consulting relationships with the named private-equity and security-industrial-complex firms — constitute the commercial settlement of the political-class formation. The dignified-press characterisation of these figures as good-faith post-political national-security adults speaking against the Trump excess on CNN and MSNBC softens to the point of erasure the preceding career on which the post-2001 IC apparatus they coordinated produced the documented atrocities — Iraq WMD intelligence failure, drone-strike civilian casualties, multi-year warrantless surveillance, the Senate-perjury record, the Detention Site Green and other black-site programmes — that the contemporary American security state is built on.

The mask of the post-2001 IC is the dignified-press characterisation. The actual record is the subject of this dossier.

## Bridge to Dossier 15

The Federalist Society and Heritage Foundation apparatus — Leonard Leo, Kevin Roberts, Project 2025, the $1.6 billion Barre Seid donation, the judicial-pipeline operation that placed the 6-3 Supreme Court majority documented in Dossier 13 — is the architecture that the post-2016 conservative legal movement built on the foundation of the pre-2016 Federalist Society infrastructure. Dossier 15 documents the apparatus.
