# Dossier 15: The Federalist Society and Heritage Foundation Apparatus — The Judicial Pipeline and the Policy Blueprint

## The institution

The Federalist Society for Law and Public Policy Studies and the Heritage Foundation are the two pillars of the post-1973 institutional infrastructure that the American conservative political class built to circumvent the majority-electorate constraints on conservative policy. The Federalist Society is the judicial-nominee pipeline, the legal-academic credentialling network, and the conservative-lawyer-and-judge professional society that has placed six of the nine current Supreme Court justices on the court — Thomas, Alito, Roberts, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett — and roughly 85% of the Trump first-administration circuit-court nominees in the reshaped federal appellate judiciary. The Heritage Foundation is the policy-blueprint provider and personnel-pipeline for the incoming Republican administration. The 1980 Heritage *Mandate for Leadership* was implemented roughly 60% by the Reagan administration across the 1981-1989 period. The 2023 Heritage *Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise* — the central 920-page document of the subsequent 100-plus-organisation Project 2025 operation — has been implemented roughly 50% by the Trump second administration in its first year per the advocacy-organisation tracking.

The two institutions operate in coordinated complementarity. The Federalist Society places the judges who interpret the policies the Heritage Foundation blueprints. The product is the contemporary American political-class settlement that the dossiers of this volume document. The $1.6 billion 2021 Barre Seid donation to the Marble Freedom Trust, which Leonard Leo personally controls as trustee and chairman, is the single largest donation on the pre-2024 record to any politically-focused American nonprofit. The subsequent 100-plus-organisation Project 2025 coalition, the Federalist-Society-vetted judicial-nomination operation across the 2017-2026 Trump first-and-second administrations, the Heritage Foundation policy-and-personnel pipeline from Reagan onward, and the dossiers of Part III of this volume comprise the governing architecture this evidence covers.

## The Federalist Society — founding 1982 and ideological architecture

The Federalist Society was founded in 1982 by Steven G. Calabresi (Yale Law '83), David M. McIntosh (University of Chicago Law), and Lee Liberman Otis (University of Chicago Law). The founding faculty advisers were Antonin Scalia (then an University of Chicago Law professor, Supreme Court Justice 1986-2016) and Robert Bork (then a Yale Law professor, failed 1987 Supreme Court nominee defeated 58-42 in the Senate). The April 1982 inaugural three-day "Symposium on Federalism: Legal and Political Ramifications" at Yale Law School — funded $25,000 by the Institute for Educational Affairs and attended by roughly 200 people — produced the founding statement of the organisational expansion. The symposium speakers included Scalia, Bork, and Theodore Olson (Solicitor General under George W. Bush 2001-2004 and the Bush v. Gore 2000 lead counsel).

The ideological architecture of the Federalist Society operates on two interconnected legal-interpretive doctrines that the organisational publishing-and-conference programme institutionalised as the conservative-legal-movement orthodoxy. Originalism — the interpretive doctrine that the US Constitution should be read according to the meaning the text had at the 1787-1791 framing-and-ratification period — and textualism — the interpretive doctrine that the statutory text should be read according to the plain-meaning of the words at the moment of the legislative passage — comprise the twin doctrinal pillars on which the subsequent Federalist-Society-and-Republican-judicial-appointee jurisprudence operated.

The practical effect of originalism-and-textualism doctrines was the systematic narrowing of the post-1937 progressive-administrative-state constitutional-interpretation that the prior Roosevelt-and-Warren-Court period had built. The subsequent Federalist-Society-affiliated jurisprudence reversed Roe v. Wade in 2022 (Dobbs), the Chevron deference doctrine in 2024 (Loper Bright), the Section 4(b) Voting Rights Act preclearance formula in 2013 (Shelby County), and the race-conscious higher-education admissions doctrine in 2023 (Students for Fair Admissions). The originalism-and-textualism doctrines that the 1982 Yale-and-Chicago founders had built into the Federalist Society institutional framework produced, across the forty-two years, the reshaping of the American constitutional-interpretive landscape that the Dossier 13 SCOTUS analysis documents.

## The funding pipeline — Olin, Scaife, Bradley, Koch, Mercer

The Federalist Society's post-1982 institutional expansion was funded by the coordinated network of conservative-foundation and conservative-family-fortune donors that the decades made the central financial infrastructure of the American conservative movement.

The John M. Olin Foundation provided roughly $5.5 million to the Federalist Society across the 1982-2005 period; the Olin Foundation was deliberately wound down on planned schedule in 2005 after distributing its endowment per the founder John M. Olin's instructions, which the conservative-foundation literature (James Piereson's 2005 *Commentary* essay; Olin Foundation president John J. Miller's 2003 *A Gift of Freedom*) characterised as the model of strategic philanthropy. The Scaife Foundation under Richard Mellon Scaife (heir to the Mellon banking-and-industrial fortune; died 2014), the Lynde and Harry Bradley Foundation of Milwaukee (with the roughly $850 million endowment from the 1985 Rockwell International acquisition of Allen-Bradley), the Koch family foundations under Charles Koch and David Koch (David died 2019), and the Mercer family foundation under Robert Mercer (the Renaissance Technologies hedge-fund co-CEO and the Cambridge Analytica funder) constituted the donor network. By 2017, the Federalist Society reported $20 million in annual revenue; by 2024, the annual budget exceeded $20 million.

The chapters-and-membership infrastructure grew from the two original chapters at Yale and University of Chicago in 1982 to chapters at all 204 ABA-accredited law schools by 2024, with 70,000 practising-attorney members organised in lawyer chapters across 90 cities. The three divisions — Student Division (law-school chapters), Lawyer Division (post-graduation professional network), and Faculty Division (legal academics) — comprise the integrated career-tracking apparatus that the subsequent Federalist Society pipeline operated.

## The Meese DOJ integration 1985-1988

The decisive Reagan-administration integration of the Federalist Society into the federal-government legal apparatus operated under Edwin Meese III (born 1931; Reagan chief of staff in California 1969-1974; Counsellor to the President 1981-1985; US Attorney General 1985-1988). Meese was a cofounder-and-early-adviser of the Federalist Society and operated, from his 1985 confirmation as Attorney General, the systematic recruitment of early Federalist Society members into DOJ leadership positions.

The multi-year Meese DOJ apparatus installed Federalist Society members in key DOJ positions including William Bradford Reynolds (Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights 1981-1989, opposed 1965 Voting Rights Act extensions), Stephen Markman (Assistant Attorney General for Legal Policy 1985-1989, subsequent Michigan Supreme Court Justice), Charles Cooper (Assistant Attorney General for the Office of Legal Counsel 1985-1988, subsequent partner at Cooper & Kirk and the Trump-administration anti-LGBT litigator), and multiple subsequent Federalist Society-affiliated DOJ appointees. The Meese DOJ Office of Legal Policy under Markman and subsequent Federalist Society-credentialled successors became the systematic operations centre for the conservative-legal-movement institutional building.

The 1986 confirmation of Antonin Scalia to the Supreme Court — the first Italian-American justice and the first Federalist Society faculty-adviser-turned-Supreme-Court-Justice — was the decisive symbolic event of the Meese-and-Federalist-Society integration. The 1987 defeat of Robert Bork's Supreme Court nomination by 58-42 Senate vote following Senator Edward Kennedy's 1 July 1987 "Robert Bork's America" floor speech became, in the conservative-legal-movement narrative, the emblematic injustice that justified the intensification. The subsequent "to bork" verb entered the American political vocabulary as the shorthand for systematic political-attack-and-defeat of a judicial nominee.

The George H.W. Bush administration (1989-1993) extended the Federalist Society integration through Lee Liberman Otis's appointment as associate White House counsel — Otis (the 1982 cofounder) was placed in the position from which she vetted judicial nominees for the Bush 41 administration. The 1991 Thomas Senate confirmation, the 1990 Souter Senate confirmation (which subsequent conservatives characterised as the conservative-movement disappointment that the intensified Federalist Society vetting was designed to prevent), and the Bush 41 lower-federal-court appointments comprised the Bush 41 contribution to the Federalist Society pipeline.

The George W. Bush administration (2001-2009) operated the most systematic pre-Trump Federalist Society judicial-nomination operation. Brett Kavanaugh (subsequent Supreme Court Justice from 2018) and Viet Dinh (Assistant Attorney General for Legal Policy 2001-2003; Patriot Act principal author) held the key judicial-selection positions in the George W. Bush administration. Roughly 50% of George W. Bush's appointments to the US Courts of Appeals were Federalist Society members or affiliates. The 2005 John Roberts Chief Justice confirmation and the 2006 Samuel Alito Associate Justice confirmation — both Federalist Society affiliates — completed the pre-Obama Federalist-Society-on-Supreme-Court placement.

## Leonard Leo — 1991-present

Leonard Leo (born 1965 in Long Branch, New Jersey) is the single most consequential post-1991 operator of the Federalist Society pipeline and the architect of the post-2016 conservative-legal-movement intensified judicial-nomination operation. Leo's father died young; his mother remarried when he was five and the family moved to New Jersey. Leo attended Cornell University for both undergraduate and law school (BA 1986, JD 1989). During law school Leo interned with Senator Orrin Hatch (R-UT) on the Senate Judiciary Committee — the formative professional experience that shaped Leo's subsequent career in conservative judicial-nomination operations.

Leo married Sally Schroeder; the couple had seven children. Their first daughter Margaret was born with spina bifida and died aged fourteen — the single most formative personal event that Leo's subsequent public commentary and subsequent Catholic-anti-abortion convictions trace to.

Leo joined the Federalist Society in 1991 as one of the first paid employees in the national office. Across the thirty-three years, Leo rose through the positions of Director of Lawyers Division, Executive Vice President, and (from 2020 onward) Co-chair. Under Leo's operational leadership, the Federalist Society's law-school chapters grew from roughly two student chapters to over 90 (subsequent expansion to all 204 ABA-accredited law schools followed); the annual budget grew from $100,000 in 1991 to over $20 million by 2024; the membership network reached 70,000 practising attorneys.

The 2017 Clarence Thomas characterisation of Leo as "the third most powerful person in the world" — reported by New York Times — captures the post-Trump-election institutional position Leo achieved through the coordinated 2016 Supreme Court nominee-list operation. In May 2016, candidate Trump released the list of 11 potential Supreme Court nominees compiled with Leo's and Heritage Foundation Federalist-Society-aligned personnel input. In September 2016, Trump released the expanded list of 21 names. The subsequent 31 January 2017 nomination of Neil Gorsuch (on the list), the 9 July 2018 nomination of Brett Kavanaugh (on the list), and the 26 September 2020 nomination of Amy Coney Barrett (on the list) completed the 6-3 Federalist Society majority on the Supreme Court.

Leo also chairs CRC Advisors (formerly Creative Response Concepts), a PR-and-political-consulting firm that manages multiple conservative nonprofits. In 2020, Leo stepped back from daily Federalist Society management to focus on CRC Advisors and the Marble Freedom Trust operations.

## The Barre Seid donation 2021 — $1.6 billion

The single most consequential post-2020 funding event in the American conservative-legal-movement record was the 2021 Barre Seid donation of $1.6 billion to the Marble Freedom Trust.

Barre Seid (born 1932) was the longtime CEO of Tripp Lite — a Chicago-based electronics-manufacturing company specialising in surge protectors and power-management equipment. Seid had operated, across the preceding decades, as the secretive donor to conservative-and-pro-Israel causes — including Bar-Ilan University (which awarded him a 2010 honorary degree for "supporting those organizations which will fortify Israel's position in the world"), Aish HaTorah, the Heartland Institute (the climate-denial think tank), and multiple subsequent conservative-movement institutions.

In 2020, Leo founded the Marble Freedom Trust as a 501(c)(4) charity organised as a trust rather than as a corporation. The trust structure was selected for three specific institutional reasons. First, the trust founding documents are harder to change than corporate governing documents per the subsequent Notre Dame Law School professor Lloyd Hitoshi Mayer analysis — protecting the donor's ideological direction against subsequent governance changes. Second, the trust can operate with a single trustee rather than the corporate-board minimum of three directors — concentrating the decision-making authority. Third, the trust structure has reduced disclosure requirements compared to corporate structures. Leo is the trustee and chairman of Marble Freedom Trust — he personally controls the $1.6 billion endowment.

In 2021, Seid donated 100% of his Tripp Lite shares to the Marble Freedom Trust. The trust subsequently sold the shares to Ireland-based Eaton Corporation for $1.6 billion. The structured stock-donation-then-sale arrangement allowed Seid to avoid roughly $400 million in capital-gains taxes that would have been imposed on a cash sale.

The $1.6 billion is the single largest donation on the pre-2024 record to any politically-focused American nonprofit. For comparative scale: the 2020 election cycle top 15 GOP-and-Democratic-aligned political-advocacy nonprofits combined spent roughly $750 million in election-cycle expenditures per the subsequent Center for Responsive Politics tabulation. The single 2021 Seid donation to Leo's Marble Freedom Trust exceeded the cumulative 2020 cycle top-15 political-nonprofit expenditures by a factor of roughly 2x. The donation gave Leo personal control of an endowment that, deployed at modest annual disbursement rates of 5-7% of the corpus per the standard private-foundation disbursement model, generates roughly $80-110 million in annual political-advocacy capacity in perpetuity.

The Marble Freedom Trust 501(c)(4) status — distinct from the 501(c)(3) charity status of most conservative-foundation operations — permits the trust to fund overtly political work including lobbying, election-related advertising, and partisan political campaigns. Gifts to 501(c)(4) organisations are not tax-deductible for the donor (unlike 501(c)(3) gifts) but they may operate with the expanded political-advocacy scope that the 501(c)(3) status prohibits. The Marble Freedom Trust is therefore the optimal institutional vehicle for the $1.6 billion Seid donation to be deployed in the political-advocacy programme that Leo's subsequent multi-organisation network operates.

## The Heritage Foundation — founding 1973 and Reagan integration

The Heritage Foundation was founded on 16 February 1973 by Paul Weyrich, Edwin Feulner, and Joseph Coors (heir to the Coors Brewing Company fortune) at Washington DC. The initial $250,000 founding contribution came from Joseph Coors. The founding rationale: the prior American Enterprise Institute (founded 1938) was perceived by Weyrich and Feulner as insufficiently politically-aggressive in its policy-advocacy role; the new Heritage Foundation was institutionalised explicitly as the more activist conservative-policy think tank that would operate the systematic legislative-and-administrative advocacy that the preceding AEI academic-research-orientation had not.

The 1980 Heritage publication *Mandate for Leadership: Policy Management in a Conservative Administration* — a 1,093-page policy blueprint for the incoming Reagan administration — became the single most consequential pre-2023 Heritage publication. The Reagan administration implemented roughly 60% of the Heritage recommendations across the 1981-1989 period per Heritage institutional assessments. The Heritage personnel pipeline supplied multiple Reagan-administration appointees including Edwin Meese (Heritage Distinguished Fellow Emeritus from post-government period).

The subsequent Heritage presidencies operated under Edwin Feulner (1977-2013, with brief 2017-2018 second tenure during the transition), Jim DeMint (2013-2017, former senator from South Carolina, resigned after leadership dispute over Trump alignment), and Kay Coles James (2017-2021, first Black president of Heritage).

## Kevin Roberts and the Heritage presidency from 2021

Kevin Roberts (born 1974 in Lafayette, Louisiana) became the Heritage Foundation president on 1 December 2021. Roberts holds a PhD in History from the University of Texas at Austin. His pre-Heritage career included founding of the John Paul the Great Academy (a Catholic K-12 school in Lafayette), the presidency of Wyoming Catholic College 2013-2016, and the CEO position at the Texas Public Policy Foundation 2016-2021. Roberts's Heritage appointment institutionalised the post-2016 Heritage realignment around Trumpist national-conservatism and the subsequent Project 2025 operation.

In April 2023, Roberts and the Heritage Foundation launched Project 2025 (formally the "2025 Presidential Transition Project") with a coalition of 100+ conservative organisations as partners. The central document of the operation — *Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise*, a 920-page policy blueprint — was published April 2023. The subsequent Project 2025 operation organised around four institutional pillars: the 920-page policy agenda, a personnel database of vetted political appointees (over 20,000 names by subsequent reporting), a training programme for incoming appointees, and a 180-day playbook for the incoming administration's first six months.

The Project 2025 policy programme covered the comprehensive reshaping of the federal administrative state: the dismantling of the Department of Education; the expansion of executive-branch authority over independent agencies under the unitary-executive theory; the comprehensive immigration-enforcement programme including mass deportations and reduced legal immigration; the abortion restrictions including reversal of FDA approval of mifepristone; the elimination of diversity-and-inclusion programmes across federal agencies; the loyalty tests for federal employees through Schedule F reclassification of career civil servants; the comprehensive anti-trans-and-anti-LGBTQ provisions across multiple agency rules.

Roberts characterised the Project 2025 operation in a 2 July 2024 public statement: "We are in the process of the second American Revolution, which will remain bloodless if the left allows it to be." The characterisation as "the second American Revolution" became the single most quoted Roberts public statement.

In July 2024, candidate Trump publicly distanced himself from Project 2025: "I have nothing to do with Project 2025. I have not read it. I do not want to read it." The distancing was tactical campaign positioning; post-election, multiple Project 2025 architects were placed in Trump second-administration positions. Russell Vought (Project 2025 contributor and OMB Director under Trump first administration) returned as OMB Director in the second administration. Tom Homan (Project 2025 chapter author on immigration) was appointed Border Czar. Stephen Miller (Project 2025 contributor) was appointed Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy. Brendan Carr (Project 2025 FCC chapter author) was appointed FCC Chair.

Kevin Roberts's subsequent April 2025 NPR statement closed the arc: "If the Heritage Foundation has a tiny role in providing some ideas and some of the people to help that happen, then we're very happy about that." Per The 19th News and subsequent advocacy-organisation tracking, roughly 50% of the 920-page Project 2025 agenda was implemented across the first year of Trump's second administration.

## The judicial pipeline — Federalist Society on the federal courts

The cumulative product of the Federalist Society judicial-nomination pipeline across the 1986-2026 period is the reshaping of the federal judiciary. Six of the nine current Supreme Court justices are Federalist Society members or affiliates: Clarence Thomas (Federalist Society affiliate; confirmed 1991), Samuel Alito (Federalist Society affiliate; confirmed 2006), John Roberts (Federalist Society affiliate; confirmed 2005), Neil Gorsuch (Federalist Society member; confirmed 2017), Brett Kavanaugh (Federalist Society member; confirmed 2018), Amy Coney Barrett (Federalist Society member; confirmed 2020). Antonin Scalia, the 1982 founding faculty adviser of the University of Chicago Federalist Society chapter, served on the Supreme Court from 1986 until his 13 February 2016 death.

The Trump first-administration (2017-2021) judicial appointments included 234 Article III federal judges: 3 Supreme Court Justices, 54 US Courts of Appeals judges, 174 US District Court judges, and 3 US Court of International Trade judges. Roughly 85% of the 54 appellate appointments were Federalist Society members or affiliates per the subsequent Federalist Society and media reporting. The 54 circuit appointments shifted multiple appellate courts from Democratic-appointed-majorities to Republican-appointed-majorities — including the decisive reshaping of the Second, Third, Fifth, Sixth, Eighth, Ninth, and Eleventh Circuit balances.

The subsequent 2025-2026 Trump second-administration judicial-nomination operation continued the Federalist-Society-vetted pipeline under Leo's coordination and the White House Counsel office.

## The major rulings the Federalist Society pipeline produced

The Federalist Society institutional architecture has produced, across the 1986-2026 period, the reshaping of the American constitutional-interpretive landscape. The 2008 *District of Columbia v. Heller* 5-4 decision (Scalia opinion) established the individual-right Second Amendment interpretation. The 2010 *Citizens United v. FEC* 5-4 decision opened the corporate-and-union independent political-expenditure floodgates. The 2013 *Shelby County v. Holder* 5-4 decision invalidated the Section 4(b) Voting Rights Act preclearance formula. The 2014 *Burwell v. Hobby Lobby* 5-4 decision granted closely-held religious-objector corporations the contraceptive-coverage-mandate exemption. The 2018 *Janus v. AFSCME* 5-4 decision invalidated public-sector union agency fees on First Amendment grounds.

The post-2022 Federalist Society 6-3 supermajority on the Supreme Court accelerated the reshaping. The 24 June 2022 *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* 6-3 decision overturned *Roe v. Wade* (1973) and *Planned Parenthood v. Casey* (1992). The 23 June 2022 *NYSRPA v. Bruen* 6-3 decision expanded the Second Amendment to public-carry licensing. The 29 June 2023 *Students for Fair Admissions* 6-3 decision ended race-conscious higher-education admissions. The 30 June 2023 *303 Creative LLC v. Elenis* 6-3 decision granted First Amendment exemption from public-accommodations law. The 1 July 2024 *Trump v. United States* 6-3 decision (Roberts opinion) granted the president absolute immunity for official acts and presumptive immunity for "outer perimeter" official acts. The 28 June 2024 *Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo* 6-3 decision overturned the 1984 *Chevron v. NRDC* deference doctrine — reducing administrative-agency interpretive authority across the federal regulatory system.

The rulings constitute the cumulative jurisprudential product of the 1982 Federalist Society founding. The originalism-and-textualism doctrines that Scalia and Bork articulated at the first 1982 Yale symposium became, across the forty-two years, the dominant interpretive doctrines on the federal judiciary — and the basis on which the 1973-2022 abortion-rights settlement, the 1965-2013 Voting Rights Act preclearance settlement, the 1984-2024 Chevron deference administrative-law settlement, the 1978-2023 race-conscious admissions settlement, and the 1976-2024 (Mills v. United States onward) presidential criminal-liability settlement were reversed.

## The integrated institutional record

The Federalist Society and the Heritage Foundation, taken together with Leonard Leo's subsequent Marble Freedom Trust and CRC Advisors operations, constitute the single most institutionally-consequential post-1973 non-governmental American conservative-political-class apparatus. The product is: a 6-3 Federalist Society majority on the Supreme Court; roughly 85% of the Trump first-administration circuit appointments; a 920-page Project 2025 policy blueprint of which roughly 50% has been implemented by the Trump second administration in its first year; the single largest political-advocacy donation on the pre-2024 record at $1.6 billion under Leo's personal trustee control.

The architecture is the model that the dossiers of this volume document at the individual-operator level. The masks are multiple — the Federalist Society's originalism-and-textualism doctrinal facade, the Heritage Foundation's policy-research think-tank facade, the Marble Freedom Trust's 501(c)(4) charity facade. The operations under the masks are the coordinated conservative-political-class capture of the federal judiciary, the federal executive-branch personnel, and the federal policy-design apparatus.

The Federalist Society's 1982 founding by three Yale-and-Chicago law students was not the debate society its public characterisation has claimed. The 234 Trump first-administration judicial appointments, the six current Supreme Court justices, the $1.6 billion personal endowment under Leo's trustee control, and the 920-page Project 2025 implementation under the Trump second administration are the actual record. The dossiers document the individual operators of the apparatus the 1982 founding institutionalised.

The 2024-2026 Department of Government Efficiency, the Elon Musk involvement, the JD Vance vice-presidential apparatus, and the Vivek Ramaswamy operational role comprise the dossier.

# Dossier 16: DOGE, Musk, Vance, Ramaswamy — The Tech-Capital Integration with the Second Administration

## The institution

The Department of Government Efficiency, the JD Vance vice presidency, the Vivek Ramaswamy candidacy-and-Ohio-gubernatorial transition, and the Elon Musk special-government-employee tenure are not four separate political-class operators. They are four faces of the same integration. The 5 November 2024 election produced a Trump-Vance administration in which the financial-class principals of Volume One — Peter Thiel, Elon Musk, Marc Andreessen, Eric Schmidt, the tech-capital network whose dossiers Volume One documented — were not merely advisers but operational principals of the federal political-administration apparatus itself. Peter Thiel personally funded JD Vance's 2022 Senate campaign with roughly $15 million, the single largest single-donor Senate-race investment of the 2022 cycle, and Thiel-Andreessen-Schmidt jointly backed Vance's Narya Capital venture fund. Elon Musk held the 130-day special-government-employee position from 20 January 2025 to 28 May 2025 as the face of DOGE. Vivek Ramaswamy, despite being announced as DOGE co-leader on 12 November 2024, departed the position on the morning of 20 January 2025 — the inauguration day — and announced his Ohio gubernatorial campaign within hours. The subsequent operation of the Trump second administration's political-administration apparatus has run on the coordinated Federalist-Society-and-Heritage-Foundation architecture that Dossier 15 documented, with the Vance vice presidency providing the tech-capital integration that the preceding pre-2024 conservative-movement apparatus had not had at the cabinet-level operational position.

The product is the single most consequential post-1933 federal-administrative-state restructuring under way at the date of writing. The 17 inspectors general dismissed within the first week of the Trump second administration; the Schedule F restoration on inauguration day; the subsequent DOGE-Team penetration of multiple federal agencies including Treasury, USAID, OPM, and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau; the roughly 50,000 career civil servants potentially subject to at-will reclassification under Schedule F; the implementation of roughly 50% of the 920-page Project 2025 agenda — these are the cumulative product of the Federalist-Society-Heritage-Foundation-and-tech-capital integration that this dossier and the prior Dossier 15 document.

The masks are multiple. The DOGE acronym — derived from dogecoin meme cryptocurrency that Musk had promoted, with subsequent backronym to "Department of Government Efficiency" — was the public mask for the systematic reduction of federal-administrative capacity. The *Hillbilly Elegy* mask of Vance's 2016 memoir was the public mask for the Yale-Law-Mithril-Capital-Narya-Capital-Thiel-Andreessen-Schmidt tech-capital career trajectory that the memoir's public characterisation of Vance as the Appalachian-poverty-to-Senate self-made operator concealed. The Roivant-pharma-anti-woke mask of Ramaswamy's career was the public mask for the failed Axovant Alzheimer's-drug speculation, the Sinovant-and-Cytovant China-focused subsidiaries, the Sumitomo Dainippon $3 billion deal, and the anti-woke pivot. The operations behind the masks are the subject of this dossier.

## Elon Musk and DOGE — January to May 2025

The 12 November 2024 Trump announcement of DOGE established Musk and Ramaswamy as co-leaders of an advisory body operating "from outside the government" — an arrangement that Trump's communication explicitly stated would "advise" the Office of Management and Budget rather than constitute a federal agency. The arrangement was structurally analogous to the 1981-1983 Reagan-era Grace Commission (Private Sector Survey on Cost Control under J. Peter Grace) but operated at multiple-orders-of-magnitude greater scale.

The 20 January 2025 Executive Order 14158 renamed the pre-existing United States Digital Service — a 2014 Obama-era technology unit within the Executive Office of the President — to "United States DOGE Service" (USDS) and established DOGE Teams of at least four employees in each federal agency. The structural arrangement gave DOGE access to multiple agency data-and-personnel systems while maintaining the fiction that DOGE itself was not a real federal agency — preserving the absence of congressional-authorisation requirement that the Federal Advisory Committee Act would have imposed on an actual federal advisory body.

The cost-cutting targets across the operation were progressively reduced. The October 2024 Madison Square Garden Trump rally Musk claim of "at least $2 trillion" in savings — exceeding the entire 2023 federal discretionary budget — was the initial promise. The February 2025 first cabinet meeting target was $1 trillion (roughly 15% of the federal budget). The April 2025 Musk claim was $150 billion in cuts. The 5 June 2025 House DOGE caucus leader Blake Moore (R-UT) acknowledgement — "Republican members always knew it was a massive exaggeration" — closed the gap between the public claims and the actual record.

The structural relationship between Musk's special-government-employee status and the commercial integrity of the Musk corporate portfolio became the single most consequential conflict-of-interest record of the post-2025 administrative-state restructuring. Musk was the CEO of Tesla, the chairman of SpaceX, the owner of X (formerly Twitter), the founder of xAI, the owner of Neuralink, and the owner of The Boring Company. Tesla stock peaked at roughly $479 per share in December 2024 in the post-election Musk-Trump optimism period; Tesla stock subsequently lost roughly 48% across the first-quarter 2025 period as the Tesla shareholder base recognised that Musk's DOGE involvement was producing measurable commercial damage to the Tesla brand. Tesla European sales plunged 45% in January 2025. The 17 February 2025 under-oath statement by Office of Administration Director Joshua Fisher — that Musk was an employee of the White House Office and not employed by DOGE or USDS, an arrangement that meant the actual DOGE administrator was Amy Gleason rather than Musk — protected Musk's legal-and-fiduciary position from the fuller conflict-of-interest exposure that a formal DOGE-administrator position would have entailed.

The 17 inspectors general dismissed within the first week of the Trump second administration included IGs of multiple agencies that were actively investigating Musk companies at the time of dismissal — including the IGs of the Department of Defense, the State Department, and multiple other agencies whose ongoing investigations into SpaceX, Tesla, and other Musk-related entities had been in progress at the time of dismissal. The structural arrangement that the fired IGs represented was the elimination of the internal-watchdog apparatus that would have monitored the conflict-of-interest record between DOGE and Musk's personal commercial interests.

The 23 April 2025 Musk announcement of reduced DOGE involvement and the 28 May 2025 end of Musk's 130-day special-government-employee tenure produced the 30 May 2025 Trump-Musk Oval Office press conference and the multi-week Trump-Musk public feud over Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" multi-trillion-dollar spending legislation. The subsequent late-2025 Tesla shareholder approval of a pay package that could make Musk the first trillionaire in pre-modern human history closed the arc: the 130-day political-administration involvement that had cost Musk's commercial portfolio roughly 48% of its post-election valuation was subsequently compensated by the commercial-and-political-class endorsements that the post-DOGE recovery enabled. The December 2025 Musk statement on the "Katie Miller Podcast" — DOGE "was only somewhat successful" and "I wouldn't do it again" — was the public acknowledgement that the 130-day operation had not produced its advertised savings.

The more consequential structural product of the Musk DOGE tenure was the penetration of multiple federal agencies by DOGE Teams: the Treasury Department (the 8 February 2025 federal-court temporary restraining order blocked DOGE access to Treasury payment systems before being subsequently modified); USAID (the dismantling of the 1961-founded foreign-development agency that John F. Kennedy's executive order had created); the Office of Personnel Management; the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (subsequent shutdown). The administrative-state restructuring continued under OMB Director Russell Vought's coordination with the post-Musk-departure DOGE leadership.

## JD Vance — biography and the Thiel pipeline

James Donald Bowman was born 2 August 1984 in Middletown, Ohio. His mother Beverly Aikins — subsequently Bev Vance, then Bev Hamel — struggled with multi-year opioid-and-alcohol addiction across the 1980s and 1990s. His biological father Donald Bowman departed when Vance was a young child. He was raised primarily by maternal grandparents Bonnie ("Mamaw") and James ("Papaw") Vance in Middletown.

Vance served in the US Marine Corps 2003-2007 — primarily as public-affairs Marine in the Iraq War. He attended Ohio State University BA 2009 (political science and philosophy) and Yale Law School JD 2013, where he served as editor of the *Yale Law Journal*. At Yale he met his future wife Usha Chilukuri — subsequently Usha Vance — who subsequently served as law clerk to Chief Justice John Roberts. The Yale-Law-and-Roberts-clerkship connection between Usha Vance and Roberts is one of the multiple personal-network connections between the contemporary American political-class operators that this volume's dossiers document.

Vance's post-law-school career operated through five institutional positions. First, the 2011 three-month law clerkship for Senator John Cornyn (R-TX). Second, the 2013-2014 law clerkship for federal Judge David Bunning (US District Court Eastern District of Kentucky). Third, subsequent litigation-associate position at an international law firm in Washington DC. Fourth, the May 2015 Director of Operations and Business Development position at Circuit Therapeutics, a biotech firm.

The decisive institutional inflection was the April 2016 position as Principal at Mithril Capital Management. Mithril, the Silicon Valley venture-capital firm co-founded by Peter Thiel and Ajay Royan in 2012, was Thiel's late-stage growth-investment vehicle. Thiel had given a speech at Yale Law School during Vance's time there that Vance subsequently characterised as the formative professional encounter; the Mithril hiring was the operational continuation of the Yale-Thiel mentorship. The Mithril position is documented in subsequent Wall Street Journal reporting (July 2024) as absentee in practice: a former Mithril colleague said he "never once saw" Vance in the office during the year Vance was there, and a second former colleague said Vance "spent a good deal of time promoting *Hillbilly Elegy*." Vance's actual work at Mithril, documented, was the promotion of his memoir rather than the operation of the Thiel venture-capital portfolio.

The June 2016 publication of *Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis* (HarperCollins) coincided with the 2016 presidential election and produced the cultural-political artefact that subsequent Vance career operated on. The memoir was a *New York Times* bestseller through 2016 and 2017; a 2017 Dayton Literary Peace Prize finalist; the subject of Ron Howard's 2020 Netflix adaptation. The memoir's public characterisation of Vance as the Appalachian-poverty-to-Yale-Law success operator who had pulled himself out of the Rust Belt by hard work and self-discipline became the political-class biographical narrative on which the subsequent Senate-and-vice-presidential campaigns ran. The Eastern-Kentucky critics who characterised Vance as "not a hillbilly" given his Middletown, Ohio upbringing — Middletown is in southwestern Ohio roughly 70 miles north of Cincinnati and roughly 150 miles from the Eastern Kentucky Appalachian counties Vance claimed to represent — were correct on the geographical record. The Appalachian biographical claim was constructed for political purposes.

Spring 2017 Vance left Mithril for Revolution, the Washington DC investment firm founded by AOL co-founder Steve Case. In 2019 Vance founded Narya Capital (Cincinnati, Ohio) — a tech-startup venture fund named after one of the Three Rings of the Elves in J.R.R. Tolkien's *Lord of the Rings*. The Narya Capital backers were Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, and Eric Schmidt — all three covered in Volume One dossiers as principal operators of the post-2000 American tech-capital political-class apparatus. The Narya Capital structure was the vehicle through which Vance was institutionalised as the political-class candidate of the tech-capital network.

The January 2021 Vance announcement of his Republican Ohio Senate campaign — for the open seat vacated by retiring senator Rob Portman — produced the documented Thiel investment pattern. Per March 2021 Cincinnati Enquirer reporting under communications adviser Bryan Lanza, Peter Thiel donated $10 million to the Protect Ohio Values PAC — the Vance-supporting Super PAC. Robert Mercer (Renaissance Technologies / Cambridge Analytica funder; covered in Dossier 15 as the Federalist Society donor network member) made a "significant contribution" per the same Lanza confirmation. Subsequent additional Thiel donations brought the total Thiel investment in the Vance 2022 Senate campaign to roughly $15 million across the 2021-2022 cycle — the single largest single-donor Senate-race investment of the 2022 cycle, exceeding the next-largest single-donor commitments by multiples.

The 8 November 2022 election produced Vance's 53-47% defeat of Tim Ryan (D-OH). On 3 January 2023 Vance was sworn in as US Senator from Ohio. On 15 July 2024, the opening day of the Republican National Convention at Milwaukee, Trump selected Vance as Vice-Presidential nominee. The Trump-Vance ticket defeated Harris-Walz 312-226 in the 5 November 2024 election. On 10 January 2025 Vance resigned the Ohio Senate seat. On 20 January 2025 Vance was sworn in as the 50th Vice President of the United States by Chief Justice Brett Kavanaugh — closing the circuit through the Kavanaugh-Roberts-Usha-Vance personal network.

The subsequent Vance vice-presidential operations included two consequential public episodes. First, the 14 February 2025 Munich Security Conference keynote in which Vance attacked European allies on free-speech-and-immigration grounds — producing diplomatic-and-political fallout across European capitals and the strategic re-evaluation of US-European alignment by multiple European governments. Second, the 24 March 2025 *Atlantic* exposure by editor Jeffrey Goldberg of the "Houthi PC Small Group" Signal chat group — to which National Security Advisor Mike Waltz had accidentally added Goldberg — that included Vance, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, DNI Tulsi Gabbard, CIA Director John Ratcliffe (Dossier 14), and other Trump second-administration principals. The chat included pre-strike operational details for 15 March 2025 US strikes on Houthi targets in Yemen including weapons-systems and aircraft-launch times. Vance's Signal messages included criticism of European NATO allies and contempt for European NATO commitments — consistent with the Munich keynote.

## Vivek Ramaswamy — Roivant trajectory and the anti-woke pivot

Vivek Ganapathy Ramaswamy was born 9 August 1985 in Cincinnati, Ohio. His parents were Tamil Brahmin immigrants from the Palakkad-Kerala region of southern India. His father Vadakanchery Ganapathy Ramaswamy graduated from the National Institute of Technology Calicut and worked as an engineer at General Electric. His mother Geetha Ramaswamy graduated from Mysore Medical College and worked as a geriatric psychiatrist at Merck and Schering-Plough. Ramaswamy attended St. Xavier High School (Cincinnati) as valedictorian and nationally-ranked junior tennis player. He attended Harvard College BA 2007 (biology, summa cum laude), where he worked in the Douglas Melton stem-cell laboratory. He attended Yale Law School JD 2013, where he met his future wife Apoorva Tewari — subsequently throat surgeon — and where he was a law-school classmate of JD Vance.

The 2007-2014 period at QVT Financial — a New York hedge fund — produced Ramaswamy's first major wealth (reportedly roughly $7 million across seven years per multiple subsequent reporting accounts). The 2008 Pharmasset trade was the institutional emblem: Ramaswamy bought Pharmasset shares at $5 per share; Gilead acquired Pharmasset at $137 per share in 2011, producing the structural credentialling moment that launched the subsequent Roivant operation.

The 2014 founding of Roivant Sciences was the operational pivot. Roivant was incorporated in Bermuda — a tax-haven jurisdiction with no corporate income tax — with roughly $100 million in start-up capital from QVT, RA Capital Management, Visium Asset Management, D. E. Shaw, and Falcon Edge Capital. The Roivant business model was the systematic purchase of shelved drug patents from larger pharmaceutical companies for development through subsidiary "Vant" structures: Dermavant (dermatology), Urovant (urological disease), Myovant (women's health), Axovant (Alzheimer's), Sinovant (China), Cytovant (Asia).

The 2015 Axovant Sciences operation produced the single largest controversy of the Roivant trajectory. Ramaswamy raised $360 million for Axovant to develop intepirdine as Alzheimer's drug — a compound that GlaxoSmithKline had previously shelved after the post-Phase-II development failures. The Axovant Forbes cover September 2015 with Ramaswamy's claim that Axovant would be "the highest return on investment endeavor ever taken up in the pharmaceutical industry" produced the IPO that raised $315 million; the Axovant market capitalisation reached roughly $3 billion despite the company having only eight employees — including Ramaswamy's brother Shankar and his mother Geetha on the employment roster. Ramaswamy subsequently reported $37 million in capital gains in 2015 from the partial sale of his Roivant shares to Viking Global Investors.

The September 2017 announcement that intepirdine had failed its large Phase III clinical trial produced the Axovant collapse. Axovant stock lost 75% in a single day. Institutional losers included the California State Teachers' Retirement System pension fund. Axovant subsequently attempted a gene-therapy pivot, failed, and dissolved in 2023. Ramaswamy was structurally insulated from most of the Axovant losses because he held his economic exposure through Roivant rather than through direct Axovant shareholding — a structural arrangement that protected Ramaswamy's personal balance sheet while exposing institutional pension funds and retail investors to the Axovant collapse.

The 2017 SoftBank investment of $1.1 billion in Roivant by Masayoshi Son (covered in Volume One Dossier 22) and the 2017 CITIC Private Equity partnership creating Sinovant — under which Ramaswamy partnered with the Chinese state-owned CITIC Group — extended the Roivant institutional capital. The 2019 Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma acquisition of roughly 10% Roivant stake for roughly $3 billion validated the broader Roivant valuation and sharply increased Ramaswamy's personal net worth. The 2021 Roivant IPO via SPAC merger on Nasdaq completed the sequence.

In January 2021 Ramaswamy stepped down as Roivant CEO. The August 2021 publication of *Woke, Inc.: Inside Corporate America's Social Justice Scam* (Center Street, a Hachette conservative imprint) launched the anti-woke career pivot. The 2022 founding of Strive Asset Management — an "anti-woke" index-fund provider backed by Peter Thiel — institutionalised the pivot as ongoing commercial operation. Strive marketed itself as the antithesis of BlackRock-Vanguard-State Street ESG-aligned asset managers — a positioning that Larry Fink's BlackRock (covered in Volume One Dossier 18) had defined across the 2018-2022 stakeholder-capitalism period.

The 21 February 2023 announcement of the 2024 Republican presidential campaign and the multi-debate primary performance positioned Ramaswamy as the youngest serious candidate in the 2024 Republican field. The platform — federal-bureaucracy reduction, end of birthright citizenship for children of undocumented immigrants, withdrawal of Ukraine support, raising the federal voting age to 25 — was the subsequent Project-2025-aligned policy programme. The 15 January 2024 Iowa caucus fourth-place finish at roughly 8% of the vote produced the campaign suspension and Trump endorsement.

The structural irony of the Ramaswamy anti-China campaign rhetoric against the documented Sinovant-and-Cytovant-CITIC China-focused Roivant subsidiaries was not addressed in the campaign's public messaging. The Ramaswamy political-class trajectory operated through the selective public-record presentation that the Roivant subsidiary structure had previously facilitated.

The 12 November 2024 Trump announcement of Ramaswamy as DOGE co-leader with Musk produced the 20 January 2025 conflict that ended the DOGE involvement on inauguration day. Per Politico reporting, Ramaswamy was forced out by Musk after conflict with DOGE staff over Ramaswamy's X post characterising Americans as "venerated mediocrity over excellence." A Republican strategist quoted in subsequent reporting characterised the arrangement: "everyone wants him out." Within hours of the inauguration-day departure, Ramaswamy announced his Ohio gubernatorial campaign. The May 2026 Republican primary produced Ramaswamy's nomination. Per the May 2026 Forbes estimate, Ramaswamy's net worth had reached $2.5 billion across the pre-gubernatorial period — largely built on the 2017-2021 Roivant-and-SoftBank-and-Sumitomo institutional capital that the anti-woke commercial pivot built on.

## Russell Vought and the OMB-Schedule F integration

The DOGE-Musk-Vance-Ramaswamy public-mask architecture operates above a more consequential federal-administrative restructuring under OMB Director Russell Vought. Vought served as OMB Director under the Trump first administration 2020-2021. Post-2021 he founded the Center for Renewing America (CRA), a 501(c)(3) policy-and-litigation organisation that drafted executive orders, regulations, and memoranda for the incoming Trump second administration. The CRA was one of the 100-plus organisations participating in the Project 2025 coalition (Dossier 15). In January 2025 Vought was confirmed as OMB Director for the Trump second administration.

The 20 January 2025 Trump executive order restoring Schedule F was the single most consequential pre-DOGE personnel restructuring of the post-1883-Pendleton-Act period. Schedule F — first created by October 2020 Trump first-administration executive order and revoked by Biden in January 2021 — established a new excepted-service classification for career civil servants in "confidential, policy-determining, policy-making, or policy-advocating" positions. The subsequent Schedule F implementation could allow reclassification of roughly 50,000 career civil servants to at-will employment status — eliminating the post-1883 Pendleton Act merit-system protections that had insulated the federal career civil service from arbitrary partisan-political dismissal.

Vought's public characterisation of career civil servants as figures to "put in trauma" through policy — a statement made multiple times across the pre-2025 CRA period — set the tone for the subsequent Schedule F implementation. The CRA strategic logic — coordinated with the Heritage Foundation Project 2025 (Dossier 15), the Federalist-Society-vetted judicial-nomination pipeline that protects the executive actions from judicial reversal, and the tech-capital DOGE penetration of multiple federal agencies — comprises the coordinated structural assault on the post-1883 federal-administrative-state architecture.

## The Volume One continuity

The Vance Narya Capital backers — Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, Eric Schmidt — are all principal subjects of Volume One dossiers. Peter Thiel (Volume One Dossier 11) is the PayPal co-founder, the Palantir co-founder, the Founders Fund co-founder, and the conservative-political-class donor whose multi-million-dollar donations to Vance's 2022 Senate campaign exceeded $15 million. Elon Musk (Volume One Dossier 12) is the CEO of Tesla, the chairman of SpaceX, the owner of X, and the 130-day DOGE operational principal across the January-May 2025 period. Marc Andreessen (Volume One Dossier 14) is the co-founder of Andreessen Horowitz and the tech-capital political-class operator who backed Narya Capital. Eric Schmidt (Volume One Dossier 18) is the former Google CEO and chairman whose post-Google political-class operations included the Schmidt Futures and multiple other policy-advisory roles. Larry Fink (Volume One Dossier 18) is the BlackRock founder-and-CEO whose post-2018 stakeholder-capitalism advocacy produced the Ramaswamy Strive Asset Management anti-woke commercial counter-positioning.

The Vance vice presidency operates on the continuous Volume-One tech-capital network institutionalised at the cabinet-level operational position. The Musk DOGE tenure operates on the continuous Volume-One commercial-political integration institutionalised at the special-government-employee position. The Ramaswamy 2024 campaign and subsequent Ohio gubernatorial campaign operate on the continuous Volume-One Roivant-SoftBank-Sumitomo institutional capital integrated with the anti-woke conservative-movement positioning. The 2024-2026 political-administration apparatus of the Trump second administration is the post-Federalist-Society-Heritage-Foundation operational continuation of the Volume-One tech-capital political-class apparatus.

## The assessment

The DOGE-Musk-Vance-Ramaswamy integrated apparatus is the single most consequential post-1933 federal-administrative-state restructuring under way at the date of writing. The cumulative product across the 20 January 2025 - 18 May 2026 post-inauguration period — the 17 inspectors general dismissed within the first week, the 1 February 2025 Treasury payment-system access blocked by federal court, the dismantling of USAID, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau shutdown, the Office of Personnel Management restructuring, the Schedule F reclassification proceedings under way against roughly 50,000 career civil servants, the Project 2025 implementation at roughly 50% in the first year per the advocacy-organisation tracking — is the cumulative institutional outcome of the coordinated Federalist-Society-Heritage-Foundation-and-tech-capital integration that this dossier and the preceding Dossier 15 document.

The masks were the public characterisations. The Hillbilly Elegy mask of Vance's 2016 memoir. The dogecoin-meme mask of the DOGE acronym. The anti-woke commercial mask of Ramaswamy's Strive Asset Management. The efficiency-and-cost-cutting mask of the DOGE operation. The conservative-policy-research mask of the Heritage Foundation. The debate-society mask of the Federalist Society.

The role behind the masks is what this dossier and the prior dossiers of Part III document.

## Bridge to Dossier 17

The American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) apparatus — the named donor network, the congressional client list, the post-1947 institutional history, the post-2018 United Democracy Project Super PAC operations, and the subsequent 2024-2026 congressional-primary intervention pattern — is the subject of Dossier 17.
