# Dossier 17: AIPAC — The American Israel Public Affairs Committee Apparatus

## The institution

The American Israel Public Affairs Committee is the single most consequential foreign-policy lobbying organisation operating on the US Congress. Its 2024 election-cycle spending of $126.9 million across AIPAC PAC and United Democracy Project Super PAC produced two primary defeats of incumbent Democratic House members — Jamaal Bowman of New York's 16th Congressional District in June 2024 and Cori Bush of Missouri's 1st Congressional District in August 2024 — that constitute the first two Squad-caucus losses in the caucus's history. The $12.3 million UDP TV ad spending in the Bowman-Latimer race set the outside-group House-race spending record per New York Times reporting.

The apparatus under AIPAC's post-2021 political-action operation is the cumulative product of seven decades of pre-existing infrastructure: the 1943 American Zionist Emergency Council founding under Rabbi Abba Hillel Silver of Cleveland; the 1949 American Zionist Council umbrella-organisation continuation following the 1948 Israeli state founding; the 1951 Isaiah Kenen-founded American Zionist Committee for Public Affairs Washington lobbying arm; the 1954 separation of lobbying activity from tax-exempt umbrella; the 1959 renaming to AIPAC; the 1962-1963 Senator J. William Fulbright Foreign Agents Registration Act investigation; the 1974 Morris Amitay mass-membership-organisation expansion under the Jackson-Vanik amendment period; the 1980-1993 Thomas Dine grassroots-architecture buildout; the 1996-2024 Howard Kohr CEO tenure; the 2005 espionage scandal in which AIPAC officials Steven Rosen and Keith Weissman were charged under the 1917 Espionage Act for receiving classified US information from Pentagon analyst Lawrence Franklin; and the December 2021 AIPAC PAC and January 2022 United Democracy Project Super PAC pivot to direct electoral spending that ended seven decades of pre-2021 non-electoral institutional discipline.

The cumulative US military aid the AIPAC apparatus has secured for Israel since the post-1948 period is the largest such commitment to any single country in US history. The 1999-2008 ten-year Memorandum of Understanding committed $26.7 billion. The 2009-2018 MOU committed $30 billion. The 2019-2028 MOU committed $38 billion. The post-7-October-2023 supplemental aid added roughly $14 billion through the April 2024 Israel-Ukraine-Indo-Pacific supplemental appropriations. The cumulative 2023-2024 US military aid to Israel reached roughly $17.9 billion across the twelve months following 7 October 2023 per Brown University Costs of War analysis. The cumulative US military aid to Israel across the 1949-2024 period is roughly $260 billion in 2024 dollars per Congressional Research Service estimates.

The performance is the governing characterisation of AIPAC as a bipartisan pro-Israel American-membership lobby that operates within the US political system on equal terms with any other foreign-policy advocacy organisation. Behind the mask is the systematic direction of US congressional foreign-policy votes on Israel-related legislation through coordinated AIPAC PAC and UDP Super PAC electoral pressure, the parallel cultivation of nearly every member of Congress through AIPAC-funded educational trips to Israel, the post-2021 transition to direct primary intervention against Democratic critics of Israeli policy, and the fiction maintained under the 1962-1963 Fulbright FARA investigation that AIPAC is not a foreign-government-aligned lobby despite the documented Jewish Agency funding pathways the Fulbright committee compelled the American Zionist Council to disclose.

## The pre-1959 institutional sequence

The first institutional ancestor of AIPAC was the American Zionist Emergency Council, founded in 1943 by Rabbi Abba Hillel Silver of Cleveland's Tifereth Israel synagogue. AZEC was organised to mobilise American Jews to contact congressional representatives on behalf of Jews in Mandatory Palestine, on the immediate question of post-Holocaust Jewish refugee resettlement and the longer-term question of the Jewish state in Palestine. Silver was the leading figure of post-Brandeis American Zionism through the 1940s and the principal advocate of the 1947 UN Partition Plan within the American Zionist movement.

In 1949, following the 14 May 1948 Israeli declaration of independence and the first Arab-Israeli war, AZEC was renamed the American Zionist Council. AZC was the tax-exempt umbrella organisation of nine American Zionist groups including the Zionist Organization of America, Hadassah, and the other Zionist organisations active in the US. AZC functioned as the Washington-facing public-relations and lobbying arm of American Zionism following the establishment of the Israeli state.

The decisive operational figure of the post-1949 period was Isaiah L. Kenen, born 1905 in St. Stephen, New Brunswick, Canada, to an Orthodox Zionist family that had emigrated from Kiev in the Russian Empire. Kenen's father had known Theodor Herzl and other Zionist movement leaders in Europe. Kenen attended the University of Toronto, worked as a journalist at the Toronto Star and subsequently at the Cleveland News from 1933, qualified as an attorney, and helped found the Newspaper Guild. From 1943 to 1947 he served as secretary of the American Jewish Conference. In 1947-1948 he served as information director of the Jewish Agency at the United Nations during Israel's first UN delegation.

In 1951 Kenen moved to Washington and organised the American Zionist Committee for Public Affairs (AZCPA) as the unincorporated Washington lobbying arm of the American Zionist Council. The 1951-1952 Kenen lobbying campaign secured $65 million (1951) and $73 million (1952) in US economic aid to Israel — the first sizeable US financial commitment to the Israeli state. The decisive catalyst for the separation of AZCPA from AZC was the October 1953 Qibya massacre: the Israeli Unit 101 attack under Ariel Sharon on the West Bank Palestinian village of Qibya that killed 66 to 69 villagers, two-thirds of them women and children, and dynamited 42 houses, the village school, and the village mosque. The international condemnation that followed produced the Eisenhower administration's suspicion that AZC was operating on Israeli government funding in violation of the Foreign Agents Registration Act. In 1954 AZC divested AZCPA from its tax-exempt umbrella structure to permit lobbying activity through a non-tax-deductible entity that could operate under different disclosure requirements.

In 1959 AZCPA was renamed the American Israel Public Affairs Committee — the change from "Zionist" to "Israel" signalling the operation's expansion beyond strictly Zionist-organisational membership to a broader pro-Israel coalition that included American Jews not formally committed to the Zionist movement.

## The Fulbright FARA investigation (1962-1963)

The decisive pre-1973 institutional test of AIPAC's legal status came in the 1962-1963 Senate Foreign Relations Committee investigation under Senator J. William Fulbright (D-AR). The Fulbright investigation was originally focused on the activities of non-diplomatic foreign agents in the United States and was prompted by columnist Walter Pincus's reporting on Latin American foreign-agent operations. The investigation expanded to include the post-1948 Israel-lobby operation on US soil.

The Fulbright investigation focused on three institutional questions. First, whether the American Zionist Council, AIPAC, the Jewish Agency, and Kenen personally were avoiding Foreign Agents Registration Act declarations or filing false ones. Second, whether the operations were acting as unlawful conduits to launder tax-exempt funds for political purposes. Third, whether the operations were illegally disseminating Israeli government propaganda in the United States through publications including the Near East Report (which Kenen owned and edited) and the Israel Digest.

The Fulbright committee documented the central funding role of the Jewish Agency in Jerusalem and New York in the AZC operation. The Department of Justice's Foreign Agents Registration Act section subsequently ordered Kenen to register as a foreign agent for Israel if he continued lobbying on Israel's behalf. Kenen never complied with the order; instead he transitioned his lobbying work into the public-relations operation of AZC. AZC itself was eventually compelled to register under FARA. AIPAC, however, was not compelled to register, on the fiction that AIPAC's lobbying operation was conducted on behalf of its American members rather than on behalf of the Israeli government.

The 1962-1963 Fulbright finding is the single most documented public-record basis for the characterisation of the AIPAC operation as a foreign-government-aligned lobby that operated under a fictional non-foreign-agent institutional structure. The Fulbright hearings were held in a series of closed sessions; the record was subsequently developed through the Grant F. Smith / IRMEP archival work and the 2007 Mearsheimer-Walt academic study.

## The Kenen-Amitay-Dine-Kohr leadership sequence

AIPAC's 1951-2024 institutional buildup operated under five subsequent executive directors and CEOs. Isaiah Kenen ran AIPAC from 1951 to his 1974 retirement. Per J.J. Goldberg's subsequent analysis Kenen's style was "friendly, collegial" and operated on a rolodex of hundreds of Jewish-community leaders Kenen could reach by telephone when additional congressional pressure was required.

The single most  consequential leadership transition was the 1974 Kenen-to-Morris-Amitay succession. Amitay had been the co-author of the Jackson-Vanik amendment, the 1974 congressional restriction on US trade with Soviet bloc countries that limited Jewish emigration. Amitay transformed AIPAC from an intimate Kenen-era operation of roughly 12 staff and $300,000 to $400,000 annual budget into a mass-membership lobby organisation. The Yom Kippur War of October 1973 and the subsequent 1973-1974 oil crisis produced the structural inflection that the post-1974 Amitay buildup operated on. Per former Israeli diplomat to the United States Michael Oren, AIPAC achieved the financial and political clout necessary to sway congressional opinion across the fifteen years following the 1973 Yom Kippur War.

Thomas Dine succeeded Amitay as executive director in 1980 and operated AIPAC across the Reagan administration period. Under Dine, AIPAC's annual budget grew from roughly $1.4 million to over $7 million by the late-1980s peak. Dine developed the grassroots-organising architecture that would operate as the foundation for the Howard Kohr CEO tenure. The 1981 AWACS sale to Saudi Arabia produced AIPAC's first major congressional defeat, but the subsequent 1980s congressional record on Israel-related legislation produced the governing baseline that AIPAC has operated on since.

Neal Sher served as executive director 1993-1996 in a brief interim period. Howard Kohr became CEO in 1996 and served for twenty-eight years through 2024 — the longest single executive tenure in AIPAC's institutional history. The Kohr period covered the 1999-2008 first $26.7 billion ten-year Memorandum of Understanding, the 2009-2018 $30 billion MOU, the 2019-2028 $38 billion MOU, the 2005 Rosen-Weissman espionage scandal, the 2015 Iran nuclear deal opposition campaign (in which AIPAC spent $40 million-plus opposing the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action and lost when Senate Democrats failed to override Obama's certain veto), the post-2018 Trump-administration shift toward harder-right pro-Israel positions, and the decisive December 2021 institutional pivot to direct electoral spending through the AIPAC PAC and the January 2022 United Democracy Project Super PAC. Kohr was succeeded by Elliot Brandt in January 2025.

## The 2005 espionage scandal

The single most documented case of AIPAC officials receiving classified US intelligence material from a US government source is the 2005 espionage scandal involving Pentagon Iran analyst Lawrence Franklin. Franklin pleaded guilty in 2005 to passing classified US information regarding US policy toward Iran to AIPAC foreign-policy director Steven Rosen and AIPAC Iran analyst Keith Weissman.

Rosen and Weissman were initially charged under the 1917 Espionage Act for receiving and onward-transmitting the classified information to Israeli embassy contacts. AIPAC fired both officials in 2005 but reportedly continued to pay Rosen's legal fees through 2009 per subsequent reporting. The Department of Justice dropped the charges against Rosen and Weissman in May 2009, citing prosecution difficulty under the 1917-vintage statute and the broader First Amendment questions that prosecution of recipients of classified information would have raised under the post-Pentagon-Papers framework. Rosen subsequently filed a defamation lawsuit against AIPAC for the firing-related public statements; the lawsuit settled in 2012.

The 2005 espionage scandal is the single most documented public-record case of AIPAC officials receiving classified US intelligence — and the response (firing the officials but continuing to pay their legal fees; the DOJ eventually declining to prosecute; the defamation lawsuit and settlement) is the documented record of the AIPAC operation's relationship with the US intelligence apparatus.

## The December 2021 PAC and January 2022 Super PAC formation

The decisive post-2005 institutional inflection was the December 2021 AIPAC PAC and January 2022 United Democracy Project Super PAC formation. The pre-2021 institutional discipline — that AIPAC itself did not raise funds for political candidates, that its individual members raised money for candidates through unaffiliated PACs, that AIPAC operated as a lobbying-and-education organisation rather than as a direct electoral participant — was ended after seven decades.

The 15 December 2021 AIPAC PAC formation gave AIPAC its first direct-electoral institutional vehicle. AIPAC PAC operates under the federal $5,000-per-election individual-candidate contribution limit; in the 2024 cycle AIPAC PAC spent $44.8 million by August 2024 and provided contributions to 318 candidates across the cycle per Federal Election Commission filings.

The 3 January 2022 United Democracy Project formation gave AIPAC its Super PAC vehicle operating under post-*Citizens United* unlimited independent-expenditure rules. UDP cannot donate directly to candidates but can raise and spend unlimited amounts on independent expenditures — TV advertising, digital advertising, mail, field operations — for or against named candidates. In the 2022 cycle UDP raised over $35.9 million and spent over $32.9 million including $26.1 million in independent expenditures in Democratic House primaries. In the 2024 cycle UDP spent $55.4 million by August 2024 with total cycle spending reaching $126.9 million combined with AIPAC PAC per cycle-end FEC filings.

The institutional logic of the December 2021 pivot was the post-2018 emergence of Squad-caucus Democratic House members — Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, Ilhan Omar, Rashida Tlaib, Ayanna Pressley, Cori Bush, Jamaal Bowman, Greg Casar, Summer Lee — who were not deferential to the pre-2021 AIPAC bipartisan-consensus operation and who voted against, or publicly criticised, Israeli military conduct and US military-aid commitments. The AIPAC institutional response was to fund Democratic primary challengers against the Squad-caucus members and against the House Democratic candidates who appeared likely to vote against AIPAC's preferred congressional positions.

## The 2022 cycle primary intervention pattern

The 2022 cycle UDP independent-expenditure pattern established the template the 2024 cycle subsequently amplified. UDP spent $26.1 million across Democratic House primaries opposing or supporting named candidates. Per The Intercept's documentation of the FEC filings, almost all the UDP 2022 independent expenditures against Democrats were spent against candidates of colour: the Donna Edwards (D-MD-4) primary against Glenn Ivey, the Andy Levin (D-MI-11) primary against Haley Stevens (Levin was the Jewish member of Congress targeted, the one exception to the candidates-of-colour pattern), the Nida Allam (D-NC-4) primary against Valerie Foushee, the Jessica Cisneros (D-TX-28) primary against Henry Cuellar, and other documented races.

The 2022 cycle institutional outcome was AIPAC support for the election of eight pro-Israel Democrats described by AIPAC spokesperson Patrick Dorton in a subsequent August 2023 statement as "progressives, women or candidates of colour." The positioning was therefore that UDP did not oppose Democrats-of-colour as a category but opposed Democrats-of-colour who held what UDP spokesman characterised in a July 2022 Jewish Telegraphic Agency interview as "radical anti-Israel views."

## The 2024 cycle primary interventions

The 2024 cycle primary interventions were the decisive operations of the post-7-October-2023 period. The two major successful interventions — the Bowman defeat in June 2024 and the Bush defeat in August 2024 — produced the first two Squad-caucus losses in the caucus's history and demonstrated the post-2022 institutional capacity of the AIPAC apparatus to defeat incumbent House members through coordinated Super PAC spending.

The Bowman-Latimer race in New York's 16th Congressional District was the decisive 2024 operation. UDP spent roughly $9.9 million in independent expenditures opposing Bowman and roughly $4.8 million supporting George Latimer — total UDP independent-expenditure spending of roughly $14.7 million in the single race per FactCheck.org documentation. UDP TV ad spending was $12.3 million per AdImpact tracking. Per New York Times reporting the cycle-total outside-group spending in the race "eclipsed what any interest group has ever spent on a single House race." Bowman was a vocal critic of Israel's military conduct in Gaza after 7 October 2023; in a June 2024 supporter speech Bowman characterised UDP as "the Zionist regime we call AIPAC." On 25 June 2024 Latimer defeated Bowman 58% to 41% in the Democratic primary.

The Bowman-race documented record on AIPAC funding pathways is the single most-documented public-record case of AIPAC bundled-contribution operations. Per The Intercept reporting, roughly one-quarter of Latimer's direct campaign contributions across the 2024 cycle were bundled through AIPAC. In the fourth quarter of 2023, roughly half of Latimer's direct campaign contributions came through AIPAC-bundled channels. Latimer's total direct campaign-contribution haul of roughly $3.6 million compared to Bowman's roughly $2.7 million, but the decisive financial differential was the $14.7 million UDP independent-expenditure operation that operated on top of the direct-contribution race.

The Bush-Bell race in Missouri's 1st Congressional District was the subsequent 2024 operation. UDP spent over $5.2 million in independent expenditures opposing Bush and roughly $6 million in TV ad spending supporting Wesley Bell, the St. Louis County prosecuting attorney. Bush was a vocal critic of Israeli military conduct in Gaza and a member of the Squad caucus. On 6 August 2024 Bell defeated Bush in the Democratic primary.

The 2024 cycle other primary interventions included roughly $4.6 million UDP spending against Dave Min (D-CA-47) and subsequent UDP spending against Summer Lee (D-PA-12), both of which failed to defeat the incumbents. The selective targeting demonstrated the structural strategic logic: large UDP independent expenditures concentrated on small numbers of vulnerable incumbents rather than dispersed across the full Squad-caucus roster. Per Justice Democrats Communications Director Usamah Andrabi: "Pro-Israel forces looking to shape the Democratic Party in their image are picking their fights carefully."

The other documented 2024-cycle pattern was that none of the UDP television advertisements that opposed the incumbent Democrats mentioned Israel directly. The subsequent Justice Democrats analysis characterised the pattern as an institutional acknowledgement that the agenda is not popular with Democratic primary voters — and that the UDP institutional approach was therefore to characterise the incumbents on other grounds (alleged dysfunction, alleged radicalism, alleged unsuitability) while operating the pro-Israel electoral agenda behind the other-grounds public messaging.

## The donor network

The 2024-cycle UDP donor network is documented through FEC filings and subsequent analysis by FactCheck.org, OpenSecrets, In These Times / Sludge, Forbes, the Americans for Transparency project, and the TrackAIPAC documentation. The top-tier donors are overwhelmingly post-2010 American billionaire-class figures.

Jan Koum donated roughly $7.43 million to UDP across the 2024 cycle, the largest single donor. Koum is the WhatsApp co-founder (acquired by Facebook in 2014 for $19 billion); an Israeli-American immigrant from Kyiv in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic; a philanthropic donor across Jewish-and-Zionist causes including Birthright Israel; and the 2024 financial backer of Nikki Haley's 2024 Republican presidential primary campaign per the In These Times reporting.

Miriam Adelson donated $5 million to AIPAC entities (routed through the Republican Jewish Coalition channel) across the 2024 cycle. Adelson is the widow of casino billionaire Sheldon Adelson (1933-2021), the Las Vegas Sands and Marina Bay Sands operator and the pre-2021 largest Republican-Party donor. Miriam Adelson is the controlling shareholder of Las Vegas Sands and the 2024 largest Republican-Party donor in her own capacity; her $500 million-plus Adelson Foundation cumulative donations to Israeli causes include major funding for the Adelson School of Medicine at the Ariel University in the West Bank.

Jonathon Jacobson donated $4.575 million to UDP across the 2024 cycle. Jacobson is the founder of Highfields Capital Management hedge fund (closed 2018), a Birthright Israel trustee, and a major donor across Jewish-and-Zionist institutional causes.

Bernard Marcus (died 4 November 2024) donated $1 million-plus to UDP across the 2024 cycle. Marcus was the Home Depot co-founder (with Arthur Blank, 1978); subsequent Marcus Foundation operator with roughly $2.7 billion cumulative donations including $20 million to Magen David Adom (Israeli medical emergency service); major Birthright Israel and Friends of the IDF donor; and subsequent Republican-Party donor throughout the post-2016 Trump-administration period.

David Zalik donated as a major UDP donor across the 2024 cycle. Zalik is the GreenSky financial-technology company founder (acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2021 for $2.24 billion); Israeli-American born in the occupied territories; subsequent Zalik Foundation operator with roughly $100 million-plus cumulative donations; subsequent $5 million donation to Technion-Israel Institute of Technology; net worth roughly $2.3 billion per Forbes.

Paul Singer donated $1 million to UDP across the 2024 cycle, matching the $1 million donation from the 2022 cycle. Singer is the founder of Elliott Management hedge fund — documented in Volume Two Dossier 13 as the 2008 Alaska fishing trip funder for Justice Samuel Alito and as the conservative-political-class donor whose NML Capital affiliate had won the 2014 Supreme Court 7-1 *Argentina v. NML Capital* decision in which Alito had not recused. Singer's subsequent chairmanship of the Manhattan Institute for Policy Research board, the Manhattan Institute cumulative funding through the Singer Foundation of $300 million-plus since 2010, the Singer board membership on the Republican Jewish Coalition, and the cumulative $8.76 million-plus Singer donations to the Manhattan Institute since 2010, comprise the pre-2024 Singer institutional position. Singer's net worth is roughly $6.1 billion per Forbes.

Haim Saban donated $2 million-plus across multiple UDP cycles. Saban is the Israeli-American media mogul; Saban Entertainment founder; Power Rangers franchise creator (the 1993-onward television franchise that produced $6 billion-plus in merchandise sales after Saban Entertainment's partnership with Fox); owner of Fox Family Worldwide (sold to Disney 2001 for $5.3 billion, netting Saban personally $1.6 billion); consortium-acquisition of Univision in 2007 for $13.7 billion (subsequently sold 2020); Saban Capital Group operator. Saban is a major Democratic donor (close personal friend of Bill and Hillary Clinton; funded Hillary Clinton's 2008 and 2016 presidential campaigns); $30 million-plus to US political causes; Saban Family Foundation $500 million-plus to Jewish-and-Zionist causes; net worth roughly $2.8 billion per Forbes.

Helaine Lerner (the major donor across philanthropic-and-political causes through the Lerner family foundations) donated as a major UDP donor across the 2024 cycle.

The donor pattern is the systematic integration of post-2010 American billionaire-class operators across multiple political-and-philanthropic networks. The single most documented structural feature of the 2024 UDP donor base is that roughly 26% of the top UDP donors (the $200,000-plus tier) are primarily Republican-and-Trump-aligned donors per Americans for Transparency analysis — including Singer, Marcus, the Adelson estate, and other documented Republican-aligned donors who subsequently funded UDP independent expenditures in Democratic primaries to defeat progressive Democrats.

## The US military aid architecture

The cumulative US military aid the AIPAC apparatus has secured for Israel across the 1949-2024 period is roughly $260 billion in 2024 dollars per Congressional Research Service estimates — the single largest US military-aid commitment to any single country in US history. The architecture operates through 10-year Memorandums of Understanding signed by US administrations and subsequent congressional appropriations.

The 1999-2008 10-year MOU committed $26.7 billion. The MOU was signed under the Clinton administration and operated across the George W. Bush first-administration period through the 2008 financial crisis.

The 2009-2018 10-year MOU committed $30 billion. The MOU was signed in September 2007 under the George W. Bush administration and operated across the Obama first-and-second-administration period through the 2014 Gaza war and the 2015 Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action negotiations.

The 2019-2028 10-year MOU committed $38 billion. The MOU was signed in September 2016 under the Obama administration in the final months of the Obama presidency, partly as compensation for the 2015 JCPOA negotiations and partly as institutional lock-in against the incoming Trump administration's subsequent anti-JCPOA posture.

The April 2024 Israel-Ukraine-Indo-Pacific supplemental appropriations added roughly $14 billion in emergency military aid to Israel following the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the subsequent Israeli military campaign in Gaza. The cumulative 2023-2024 US military aid to Israel reached roughly $17.9 billion across the twelve months following 7 October 2023 per the Brown University Costs of War analysis.

The post-7-October-2023 Israeli military campaign in Gaza has produced, documented per the Gaza Health Ministry, the International Court of Justice provisional measures rulings (January 2024 and subsequent), and the International Criminal Court arrest warrants (November 2024 against PM Benjamin Netanyahu and former Defense Minister Yoav Gallant), Palestinian deaths exceeding 50,000 with multiple subsequent academic-genocide-scholar consensus characterising the campaign as a genocide. The cumulative US military aid that funded the Israeli military operation across the 7 October 2023-onward period operated through the congressional appropriations the AIPAC apparatus has coordinated since 1951.

The AIPAC institutional record on the post-7-October-2023 period is documented as the single most continuous lobbying-and-electoral operation against any US congressional reconsideration of the US military commitment to Israel. The 2024 cycle $126.9 million UDP spending and the subsequent Bowman-and-Bush primary defeats are the cumulative product of the post-2021 institutional pivot to direct electoral spending that operated specifically against Democratic congressional critics of Israeli military conduct in Gaza.

## What the record establishes

The American Israel Public Affairs Committee is the single most consequential foreign-policy lobbying organisation operating on the US Congress. Its cumulative 1951-2024 institutional product is roughly $260 billion in US military aid to Israel; the 2019-2028 $38 billion MOU; the post-7-October-2023 $14 billion supplemental; the 2024 cycle $126.9 million spending; the first two Squad-caucus losses in caucus history (Bowman June 2024, Bush August 2024); the 318 candidates funded across the 2024 cycle; the $14.7 million Bowman-race record for single-House-race outside-group spending; and the coordinated post-7-October-2023 electoral pressure against Democratic congressional critics of Israeli military conduct in Gaza.

The institutional masks are the public characterisations. The bipartisan-American-membership-lobby mask covers the documented 1962-1963 Fulbright-investigated Jewish Agency funding pathways the 1954 AZC-AZCPA separation was designed to obscure. The pro-Israel-American-Jewish-coalition mask covers the post-2021 documented donor pattern in which roughly 26% of top UDP donors are primarily Republican-and-Trump-aligned donors who subsequently fund independent expenditures against Democratic-primary candidates. The educational-and-lobbying-organisation mask covered the pre-2021 institutional discipline that ended after seven decades when AIPAC formed the AIPAC PAC and the subsequent United Democracy Project Super PAC in December 2021 and January 2022. The support-the-US-Israel-relationship mask covers the cumulative $260 billion in US military aid that has funded, on the documented post-7-October-2023 record, what the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court and the academic-genocide-scholar consensus have characterised as the genocide in Gaza.

The operation behind the masks is the systematic conversion of US congressional foreign-policy decision-making on Israel-related legislation into an captured process operating under coordinated electoral pressure that no other foreign-policy advocacy organisation operates on. The structural product is the cumulative US military aid record that has funded the post-1948 Israeli military operations including the post-7-October-2023 Gaza campaign — and the structural product of the post-7-October-2023 Gaza campaign is what the multiple international-legal-institution rulings have documented as the genocide.

## Bridge to Dossier 18

The post-Sunak UK Labour government under Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer — the single most consequential post-2024 UK political-class transition — is the subject of Dossier 18. The Starmer institutional record, the 4 July 2024 election outcome, the policy programme, and the UK political-class continuity with the preceding 14-year Conservative-administration period comprise the dossier.

# Dossier 18: Sir Keir Starmer — The Labour Government from July 2024

## The institution

Sir Keir Starmer is the 58th Prime Minister of the United Kingdom and the seventh Labour Prime Minister since the party's 1900 founding (after Ramsay MacDonald, Clement Attlee, Harold Wilson, James Callaghan, Tony Blair, and Gordon Brown). His 4 July 2024 general election victory produced a 174-seat parliamentary majority and ended the fourteen-year Conservative administration period that this volume's Dossier 19 documents. The 33.7% Labour share of the popular vote was the smallest share of the popular vote of any majority government since record-keeping of the popular vote began in 1830.

The subsequent twenty-two months of Starmer's premiership have produced the most precipitous public-approval collapse of any post-war British Prime Minister. The November 2025 Ipsos polling showed Starmer at -46% net approval — the least popular Prime Minister since Ipsos records began in 1977. The 7 May 2026 local elections produced roughly 1,800 Labour council-seat losses across England, Wales, and Scotland; Plaid Cymru ended a century of Labour dominance in Wales; the Scottish National Party became the largest party in the Scottish Parliament. The 14 May 2026 Wes Streeting resignation as Health Secretary, the 14 May 2026 Andy Burnham announcement of Makerfield by-election candidacy as parliamentary route to a Labour leadership challenge, and the 97 Labour MPs who called on Starmer to resign or set out a departure timetable comprise the 2026 government crisis that this record traces at the date of writing.

The landslide was sold as change after fourteen years of Conservative decay. What it concealed was systematic continuity. The Reeves March 2025 Personal Independence Payment restriction. The Reeves August 2024 Winter Fuel Allowance cut that removed the payment from roughly 10 million pensioners. The Starmer "smash the gangs" rhetorical continuation of the pre-2024 Conservative anti-immigration framing. And the continued integration with the tech-capital, AIPAC, and US-Israel-aligned foreign-policy architecture the prior dossiers document.

The single most consequential continuity is the Mandelson-Epstein scandal. Starmer's December 2024 appointment of Peter Mandelson as UK Ambassador to the United States. The 11 September 2025 firing after the US House Oversight Committee released the Mandelson-Epstein email cache. The February 2026 Mandelson arrest by British police on suspicion of misconduct in public office. The dismissals of Morgan McSweeney (Starmer's chief of staff) and Olly Robbins (Foreign Office top civil servant). The Mandelson scandal is the single most documented case of contemporary British political-class continuity with the Epstein-network architecture.

## Biography

Keir Rodney Starmer was born 2 September 1962 in Southwark, south-east London — the second of four children of Josephine Baker Starmer, an NHS nurse who had been diagnosed at young age with Still's disease (a rare inflammatory disorder that progressively left her unable to walk, eat, or talk across the period), and Rodney Starmer, a toolmaker in a factory. The family was raised in Oxted, Surrey. Starmer's parents named him after Keir Hardie, the first parliamentary leader of the Labour Party.

Starmer attended Reigate Grammar School (a voluntary-aided selective grammar school that converted to an independent fee-paying institution in 1976 during Starmer's fifth-year tenure). The terms of the conversion required no fee payment from Starmer's parents until he reached age 16; from 16 the school awarded Starmer a bursary that covered fees for the remaining two years. The first member of his family to attain a university degree, Starmer graduated from the University of Leeds with an LLB in 1985 and subsequently from St Edmund Hall, Oxford with a BCL (Bachelor of Civil Law). From 1986 to 1987 he served on the editorial collective of the Trotskyist *Socialist Alternatives* magazine.

## Legal career (1987-2013)

Starmer was called to the Bar at Middle Temple in 1987. He focused on criminal defence and human-rights work and joined Doughty Street Chambers from 1990. He served as secretary of the Haldane Society of Socialist Lawyers from 1988 to 1992 and as legal officer for the campaign group Liberty in 1990.

His pre-DPP human-rights work included death-penalty cases in the Caribbean and Africa, the McLibel case (representing the McDonald's defendants against the corporation), the 1998-2008 human-rights-adviser position at the Northern Ireland Policing Board following the 1998 Good Friday Agreement, and the 2005 House of Lords case in which Starmer persuaded the court that evidence obtained by torture should be inadmissible in court. On 9 April 2002 he was appointed Queen's Counsel.

In July 2008 Patricia Scotland, then Attorney General for England and Wales, named Starmer as the new Head of the Crown Prosecution Service and Director of Public Prosecutions. He took office on 1 November 2008, succeeding Ken Macdonald. The five-year DPP tenure covered multiple consequential prosecution decisions. The 2009 first UK prosecution of al-Qaeda terrorists. The 2009 CPS upholding of the decision not to prosecute the police officers who killed Jean Charles de Menezes in 2005. The February 2010 CPS decision to prosecute three Labour MPs and one Conservative peer for false-accounting offences in the parliamentary-expenses scandal (all found guilty). The 2010 initial decision not to prosecute Police Constable Simon Harwood for the death of Ian Tomlinson during the 2009 G20 protests was subsequently reversed after inquest finding of unlawful killing; Harwood was acquitted by jury in July 2012 and dismissed from police in September 2012. The 2011 rapid-prosecution strategy during the London riots. The February 2012 announcement of Chris Huhne prosecution for perverting course of justice. And the January 2012 conviction of Gary Dobson and David Norris for the 1993 Stephen Lawrence murder.

The single most consequential pre-2013 CPS controversy under Starmer was the Jimmy Savile case. The CPS had received multiple pre-2011 allegations against Savile from victims that the CPS did not advance to prosecution. Savile died 29 October 2011 without charge. The subsequent 2012 Operation Yewtree investigation documented the Savile sexual-abuse pattern. Starmer publicly stated that he was not directly involved in the pre-2011 CPS Savile-case decisions, but the CPS as institution under his leadership was the subject of subsequent press and parliamentary criticism.

In November 2013 Starmer stepped down as DPP and was succeeded by Alison Saunders. In the 2014 New Year Honours he was appointed Knight Commander of the Order of the Bath (KCB) by Charles, Prince of Wales for "services to law and criminal justice." In 2014 he represented Croatia at the International Court of Justice genocide hearings against Serbia at The Hague, arguing that Serbia aimed to seize Croatian territory and eradicate the Croatian population in the 1990s war.

## Parliamentary career (2015-2024)

Starmer was selected in December 2014 as the Labour parliamentary candidate for the Holborn and St Pancras constituency (the open seat vacated by retiring MP Frank Dobson). On 7 May 2015 he was elected with a majority of 17,048 — 52.9% of the constituency vote. In September 2015 he was appointed to the Jeremy Corbyn Shadow Cabinet as Shadow Home Office Minister. In June 2016 he resigned from the Corbyn frontbench in the post-Brexit-referendum mass resignation. In October 2016 he returned as Shadow Brexit Secretary — the position from which he architected the Labour pro-second-referendum policy across the 2017-2019 period.

He was re-elected in the 8 June 2017 general election with majority 30,509 and in the 12 December 2019 general election with majority 27,763. Following Labour's December 2019 catastrophic election defeat under Corbyn, Starmer announced his leadership candidacy. On 4 April 2020 he won the Labour leadership election with 56.2% of the first-round vote, defeating Rebecca Long-Bailey (27.6%) and Lisa Nandy (16.2%). He took office during the opening weeks of the March 2020 Covid-19 pandemic period.

The four-year Leader of the Opposition tenure operated on three explicit strategic positions. First, the "retain the radicalism of the last four years" pledge from the leadership-election campaign — subsequently abandoned as Starmer moved Labour toward the political centre. Second, the elimination of antisemitism within the Labour Party — a pledge that involved the 2020 expulsion proceedings against Corbyn from the parliamentary Labour Party and the multi-year disciplinary action against left-wing Labour figures. Third, the systematic rapprochement with the pre-2015 Blair-Brown centrist establishment — including the chief-of-staff appointment of Morgan McSweeney (the Labour Together / Blair-era strategist).

In April-May 2022 Starmer was investigated by Durham Constabulary over "Beergate" — allegations that he had attended a 30 April 2021 gathering in Durham during the Covid lockdown period at which beer was consumed. Starmer denied wrongdoing and pledged to resign if issued a fixed penalty fine. The 8 July 2022 Durham Constabulary decision: no fixed penalty fine. The investigation closed without charge against Starmer.

## The 4 July 2024 general election

On 22 May 2024 Prime Minister Rishi Sunak called the general election for 4 July 2024 — six months earlier than the latest constitutionally available date. The decision was widely characterised in subsequent political reporting as a strategic miscalculation. The Sunak administration's public approval was at pre-1997-Major-period lows; the decision to call the election in summer 2024 rather than autumn 2024 forfeited multiple months of potential recovery.

The 4 July 2024 election produced 411 Labour seats to 121 Conservative seats — the largest Conservative defeat in the party's modern history. Liberal Democrats won 72 seats; Scottish National Party 9 seats; Reform UK 5 seats; Greens 4 seats; other-party total 28 seats. The 174-seat Labour parliamentary majority was the third-largest Labour majority in history (after the 1945 Attlee 145-seat majority and 1997 Blair 179-seat majority — both held on larger vote shares).

The structural feature of the 4 July 2024 result was the 33.7% Labour vote share — the smallest share of the popular vote of any majority government since record-keeping of the popular vote began in 1830. The post-election analysis characterised the result as "broad but shallow" — Labour seats won across many constituencies but on small vote-share margins that the subsequent 2025-2026 public-approval collapse threatens to reverse in the general election.

On 5 July 2024 Starmer was sworn in as 58th Prime Minister by King Charles III at Buckingham Palace. The cabinet appointments included Rachel Reeves as Chancellor of the Exchequer (the first female Chancellor in British history); David Lammy as Foreign Secretary; Yvette Cooper as Home Secretary; Angela Rayner as Deputy Prime Minister and Housing Secretary; Wes Streeting as Health Secretary; Bridget Phillipson as Education Secretary; Pat McFadden as Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster.

## The winter fuel allowance and 2024 riots

The single most consequential early-period policy decision of the Starmer government was the 29 July 2024 Reeves announcement of restriction of the Winter Fuel Allowance to pensioners qualifying for Pension Credit. The decision removed the universal payment from roughly 10 million pensioners — saving an estimated £1.4 billion against the £22 billion "black hole" in the public finances that Reeves had identified in the pre-Budget statement to Parliament.

The decision was the single most polled-unpopular early-government decision per multiple subsequent YouGov / Ipsos polling. The backbencher pressure produced Starmer's 21 May 2025 partial U-turn at Prime Minister's Questions, allowing more pensioners to qualify for the payment under revised threshold.

The 30 July-August 2024 period produced the anti-immigration riots across multiple English cities. The 29 July 2024 Southport stabbings (the 17-year-old Axel Rudakubana's killing of three young girls at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class) produced the false-information campaign on social media that the assailant was a recent Muslim immigrant. The subsequent multi-day riots in Southport, Liverpool, Manchester, Bristol, Sunderland, Hartlepool, Belfast, and multiple other locations targeted mosques and immigrant-housing hotels.

The Starmer government response was the rapid-prosecution strategy — roughly 1,800 arrests across the multi-week period and multiple swift custodial sentences for rioters and for social-media posters found to have incited the violence. The Elon Musk public characterisation of Starmer as "two-tier Keir" — a 2 January 2025 Musk X post that characterised the prosecution of social-media posters as "no justice for severe, violent crimes, but prison for social media posts" — produced the public-relations conflict between the Starmer government and the Musk-led X platform that culminated in the 14 February 2025 JD Vance Munich Security Conference attack on UK free-speech policies (documented in Volume Two Dossier 16).

---

## The Mandelson-Epstein scandal

The single most consequential political-class scandal of the Starmer government to date is the Mandelson-Epstein affair of December 2024 - April 2026 period.

Peter Mandelson is the longstanding Labour Party strategist, the architect of the 1997 Tony Blair election victory, and the "Prince of Darkness" by Westminster reputation. His pre-2025 political career operated through multiple cabinet positions and multiple resignations: cabinet minister under Blair forced to resign twice (1998 over home-loan scandal with Geoffrey Robinson; 2001 over Hinduja passport scandal); European Commissioner for Trade 2004-2008; Business Secretary under Brown 2008-2010.

The Mandelson-Epstein relationship spanned the 2002-2011 period per the subsequent 2025 House Oversight Committee email-cache release. The Epstein 2008 Florida plea bargain (in which Epstein pleaded guilty to two state prostitution charges and served 13 months in prison) was followed by Mandelson email correspondence with Epstein encouraging him to "fight for early release" from Florida incarceration. The 2003 Epstein 50th-birthday book included a Mandelson contribution characterising Epstein as "best pal." The 2008-2011 correspondence included Mandelson assertions that the Epstein conviction was "wrongful and should be challenged."

The Starmer December 2024 appointment of Mandelson as UK Ambassador to the United States was announced jointly by Starmer and Foreign Secretary David Lammy. The appointment was made over the pre-existing Karen Pierce ambassadorship (Pierce had been pre-2024 ambassador and held the post into the post-July-2024 Labour government's opening months). The Mandelson appointment was justified to the public on the strategic logic that the subsequent Trump-administration period required a political-heavyweight US ambassador with political-and-trade-policy negotiating capacity rather than career-diplomat continuity.

Per subsequent April 2026 CNN reporting and the Financial Times reporting, the Mandelson appointment was made before the security vetting was completed. The security-vetting process — the "Developed Vetting" standard at the Foreign Office — was conducted after the appointment was announced publicly. Mandelson failed the in-depth security vetting per the April 2026 CNN reporting. The question of whether the security-vetting failure was communicated to Starmer is the subject of the Olly Robbins parliamentary-committee testimony scheduled for April 2026.

Mandelson took up the ambassador post in February 2025 and served until 11 September 2025. The intervening period included Mandelson's central role in the US-UK trade-deal negotiations of May 2025 and Starmer's 26-27 February 2025 official visit to Washington — including the Oval Office meeting with Trump and the Mandelson-hosted reception at the British Ambassador's Residence.

On 9 September 2025 the US House Oversight Committee released the cache of Epstein-network email correspondence including the Mandelson emails. On 10 September 2025 Bloomberg published the trove of Mandelson-Epstein emails. The same day, in Prime Minister's Questions, Starmer publicly defended Mandelson — stating that the ambassador had his "full confidence" — and dismissed calls for resignation. On 11 September 2025, less than 24 hours later, the Foreign Office announced that Mandelson had been "withdrawn as Ambassador with immediate effect."

The Foreign Office statement characterised the new information as showing the Mandelson-Epstein relationship was "materially different" from the pre-appointment understanding. The Foreign Office singled out Mandelson's assertion that the Epstein 2008 conviction was "wrongful and should be challenged" as the new information that warranted the firing. Mandelson received a £75,000 HR payout from the Foreign Office per standard procedure. James Roscoe (the deputy head of mission in Washington since July 2022 and the former communications chief to the late Queen Elizabeth II) was named interim ambassador.

In February 2026 the British police arrested Mandelson on suspicion of misconduct in public office. The arrest followed US DOJ-released documents showing that Mandelson had appeared to leak sensitive British government data to Epstein in the 2008-2011 correspondence. Mandelson is at the date of writing under investigation for lobbying on behalf of Epstein and leaking classified information.

In February 2026 Morgan McSweeney — Starmer's chief of staff and the reported principal advocate of the Mandelson appointment despite the pre-existing knowledge of the Mandelson-Epstein association — took responsibility for the appointment and resigned. The subsequent dismissal of Olly Robbins as Foreign Office top civil servant produced legal-advice taking by Robbins per the Financial Times reporting; Robbins was scheduled to testify at parliamentary committee in late April 2026, with the Financial Times reporting that Robbins felt "aggrieved over his dismissal."

In February 2026 Starmer characterised Mandelson in House of Commons remarks: Mandelson "betrayed our country, our Parliament, and my party. He lied repeatedly."

On 20 April 2026 Starmer told Parliament that he should not have appointed Mandelson and that he took responsibility for the decision. He apologised to the victims of Epstein and characterised the decision as "a judgment I made that was wrong." In the Commons statement Starmer said: "I should not have appointed Peter Mandelson. I take responsibility for that decision, and I apologize again to the victims of the paedophile Jeffrey Epstein, who were visibly failed by my decision."

## The 2025 welfare cuts and the Rayner stamp-duty resignation

In March 2025 Reeves delivered the Spring Statement announcing welfare cuts including the Personal Independence Payment (PIP) restriction. The cuts were projected to save roughly £5 billion across the multi-year period. The subsequent backbench-rebellion pressure produced modifications to the PIP-restriction provisions.

In September 2025 Angela Rayner resigned as Deputy Prime Minister and Housing Secretary after a stamp-duty underpayment scandal on a £800,000 Hove flat purchase. Rayner had paid the reduced first-time-buyer stamp duty on the purchase despite the previous property-ownership of an interest in another property. On 14 May 2026 HMRC cleared Rayner of deliberate wrongdoing. The 11 May 2026 Rayner speech at the Communication Workers Union conference in Bournemouth — in which she characterised the Labour 2026 local-elections performance as "people have turned to populists and nationalists because we have not done enough to fix it" — produced the speculation that Rayner would mount a leadership challenge to Starmer.

## The 2026 leadership crisis

The 7 May 2026 local elections produced roughly 1,800 Labour council-seat losses across the English, Welsh, and Scottish elections. The result included Plaid Cymru ending a century of Labour dominance in Wales; the Scottish National Party becoming the largest party in the Scottish Parliament; Reform UK making major gains across England; the Green Party expanding representation. The result was the worst Labour local-election performance since the post-1983 Foot-leadership low.

On 13 May 2026 King Charles III delivered the State Opening of Parliament speech laying out the Starmer government agenda for the parliamentary session — while Starmer's political future hung in the balance.

On 14 May 2026 Wes Streeting resigned as Health Secretary. In his resignation letter Streeting criticised Starmer's leadership without launching a direct leadership challenge; he subsequently confirmed that he would stand in a Labour leadership election if one were triggered. Later that day, Andy Burnham — Mayor of Greater Manchester since 2017 and the multi-year speculated leadership candidate — announced his intention to stand in the Makerfield by-election (triggered by Josh Simons's resignation), in order to acquire the parliamentary seat necessary to mount a leadership challenge from outside the existing parliamentary Labour Party. Burnham had previously applied to be the Labour candidate in the 2026 Gorton and Denton by-election, but his candidacy had been blocked by the Labour Party National Executive Committee by an 8-1 vote.

By mid-May 2026, per LabourList tracking of the parliamentary Labour Party, 159 Labour MPs supported Starmer; 97 Labour MPs called on Starmer to resign or set out a departure timetable; 147 MPs had no public position. Per Guardian reporting, over 110 backbenchers signed a letter saying a leadership challenge should not occur. One cabinet minister, four junior ministers, and four ministerial aides resigned in protest at Starmer's leadership across the mid-May 2026 period.

The 16 May 2026 London period was marked by police out in force to deal with rival rallies amid the government uncertainty period; the FA Cup Final was held the same day. The 18 May 2026 government position at the date of writing is that Starmer remains Prime Minister, no formal leadership challenge has been triggered, and the parliamentary Labour Party position is divided across the three categories (159 support / 97 oppose / 147 unclear) the LabourList tracking documents.

## The US-UK relationship

The Starmer government's US-UK relationship across the 2024-2026 period operated through the Mandelson ambassadorship (February-September 2025) and the subsequent Roscoe interim period. The November 2024 Trump-Vance presidential victory produced the December 2024 Mandelson appointment as the response. The subsequent 26-27 February 2025 Starmer Washington visit, the May 2025 US-UK trade-deal announcement, and the September 2025 Trump UK state visit at Windsor Castle (held shortly after the Mandelson firing) comprise the documented bilateral relationship.

The 14 February 2025 JD Vance Munich Security Conference attack on UK free-speech policies (documented in Volume Two Dossier 16) operated against the Starmer government's Online Safety Bill provisions. The subsequent Trump-administration position on UK domestic policy operated through Vance's recurring criticism of UK speech regulation and migration policy. The Starmer government's response operated through diplomatic continuation of the trade-deal architecture while declining direct public criticism of the Trump-Vance administration positions.

## The net approval rating record

The Starmer government's approval trajectory across the 2024-2026 period is documented through Ipsos and YouGov polling.

- 4 July 2024 election: net approval +7.
- August 2024: net approval 0 (per Ipsos tracking).
- September 2024 (YouGov): 67% negative view of government's immigration policies; 64% negative view of government's NHS management; 70% against the government's prisoner-release early-discharge policy.
- November 2025 (Ipsos): net approval -46. Starmer the least popular Prime Minister since Ipsos records began in 1977.
- May 2026: net approval comparable to Liz Truss / Boris Johnson Partygate lows per multiple polling sources.

The trajectory is the most precipitous post-election approval collapse of any post-war British Prime Minister within the first two years of office. The structural feature is that the collapse operated across both the Labour-leaning and Conservative-leaning portions of the electorate — producing the multi-direction electoral haemorrhage to Reform UK (right), the Greens (left), Plaid Cymru (Welsh nationalist), and the SNP (Scottish nationalist) that the May 2025 and May 2026 local-elections results documented.

## The documented position

Sir Keir Starmer's premiership is the documented case of post-Corbyn-realignment Labour government delivering systematic continuity with the preceding fourteen-year Conservative administration policy programme on welfare, pensions, immigration, austerity, and the foreign-policy integration with the US-Israel-aligned architecture. The 4 July 2024 landslide that produced the 174-seat majority on 33.7% vote share is the structural product of the Conservative-administration collapse rather than a positive Labour mandate; the twenty-two months have demonstrated the structural limits of governing on the post-Corbyn realignment when the underlying policy continuity becomes apparent to the electorate.

The institutional masks are the documented post-2024 public characterisations. The "change" mask was the 2024 election platform's central promise; the operation behind the mask is the Reeves Winter Fuel Allowance restriction, the Reeves Spring Statement welfare cuts, the prisoner-release early-discharge policy, the immigration-enforcement rhetorical continuation of the Conservative-administration framing, the Mandelson appointment over the career-diplomat continuity, the foreign-policy integration with the Trump-Vance administration, and the Online Safety Bill provisions that produced the multi-direction free-speech-and-protest conflicts across the 2024-2026 period. The "delivery" mask was the four-year Leader of the Opposition tenure's central characterisation of Starmer as the post-Corbyn manager who would deliver competence after the Conservative-administration chaos; the operation behind the mask is the policy U-turns, the Mandelson scandal, the Rayner resignation, the May 2026 local-elections rout, and the 2026 leadership crisis at the date of writing.

The single most documented case of contemporary British political-class continuity with the Epstein-network architecture is the Mandelson appointment. The Starmer December 2024 decision to appoint Mandelson — over the pre-existing public knowledge of the Mandelson-Epstein association documented since at least 2011 — operated through the McSweeney chief-of-staff advocacy and the Lammy Foreign Office joint announcement. The September 2025 firing, the February 2026 Mandelson arrest, and the April 2026 Starmer parliamentary apology comprise documentation of the continuity that the prior dossiers of this volume have established as the architecture of the contemporary Atlantic political class.

## Bridge to Dossier 19

The fourteen-year Conservative administration that the 4 July 2024 Labour landslide ended — the David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, and Rishi Sunak premierships across 2010-2024 — is the subject of Dossier 19. The post-Cameron Conservative-administration record on austerity, Brexit, the 2017 May minority government, the 2019 Johnson landslide, the 2022 Truss mini-Budget collapse, the 2022-2024 Sunak administration, the Kemi Badenoch post-July-2024 opposition leadership, and the cumulative ten Conservative Prime Ministers across the 2010-2024 period comprise the dossier.
