# Dossier 19: The Post-Cameron UK Conservative Administration (2010-2024)

## The institution

The 2010-2024 UK Conservative administration period was the longest single-party government tenure in modern British history, running from David Cameron's 11 May 2010 appointment through Rishi Sunak's 5 July 2024 departure. Six Conservative Prime Ministers held office across the fourteen-year period — Cameron (2010-2016), Theresa May (2016-2019), Boris Johnson (2019-2022), Liz Truss (September-October 2022, 50 days), Sunak (2022-2024), and Kemi Badenoch as post-July-2024 Leader of the Opposition (November 2024 onward). The administration period produced five flagship outputs. The austerity programme produced roughly 190,000 excess deaths attributable to the austerity-period public-spending cuts per LSE Inequalities June 2024 research. The 23 June 2016 Brexit referendum (52%-48% Leave) and the 31 January 2020 UK exit from the European Union produced 4-5% reduction in UK GDP relative to counterfactual EU-membership scenario by 2024 per OBR / Bank of England / Centre for European Reform analysis. The December 2019 Johnson landslide (365 seats / majority 80) was reversed by the July 2024 Sunak defeat (121 seats / loss of 251 seats; worst Conservative election result since the 1832 Reform Act). The 2020-2022 Partygate scandal produced 126 fixed penalty notices to 83 individuals including the sitting Prime Minister and the sitting Chancellor. And the September-October 2022 Truss-Kwarteng mini-Budget crashed sterling and gilts in 72 hours and required £65 billion in Bank of England emergency gilt-purchase intervention to prevent pension-fund collapse.

The architecture of the fourteen-year period operated on three coordinated programmes. First, the 2010-2019 austerity programme of roughly £110 billion in public spending cuts and tax increases architected by Chancellor George Osborne. The austerity programme was justified to the public as necessary deficit reduction following the 2008 financial crisis. The documented record per LSE Inequalities Berman-Hovland research is that the austerity programme produced roughly 190,000 excess deaths across 2010-2019, roughly 3% increase in UK mortality rates, and roughly half a year of life-expectancy loss for the average UK person. Second, the 2016-2020 Brexit programme — the Cameron-called referendum, the May Withdrawal Agreement defeats, the Johnson Withdrawal Agreement passage, and the 31 January 2020 UK exit. Third, the 2020-2022 Covid-period emergency-spending architecture (Sunak's furlough scheme, Eat Out to Help Out, the post-pandemic £400 billion-plus cumulative emergency-spending total) and the 2022-2024 subsequent cost-of-living-crisis period that the Truss mini-Budget escalated and the Sunak administration failed to contain.

The Conservative Party claimed for fourteen years that it was the party of responsible economic management, fiscal discipline, and competent government. What it delivered: 190,000 excess deaths attributable to government policy per LSE Inequalities — the largest such total in modern UK history. The Truss-Kwarteng September 2022 mini-Budget — the largest single-event sterling-and-gilt crash in modern UK history. The Sunak 4 July 2024 defeat — the largest single Conservative election defeat since the 1832 Reform Act. And Cameron's post-PM financial extraction from the Greensill Capital relationship: roughly $10 million pre-tax personal compensation for 30 months of part-time work per Panorama investigation, while Greensill Capital subsequently collapsed with potential £335 million loss to the UK taxpayer.

## David Cameron (May 2010 - July 2016)

### Biography
David William Donald Cameron was born 9 October 1966 in London to upper-middle-class family. His father Ian Cameron was a stockbroker partner at Panmure Gordon; his mother Mary Cameron was a Justice of the Peace. He was educated at Heatherdown Preparatory School in Berkshire, Eton College (where former Prime Minister Harold Macmillan had been educated and where nineteen subsequent UK Prime Ministers were educated), and Brasenose College, Oxford (PPE, first-class BA, 1985-1988). He was a member of the Bullingdon Club at Oxford alongside Boris Johnson and George Osborne.

His pre-parliamentary career operated through the Conservative Research Department (1988-1993), as special adviser to Chancellor Norman Lamont during the September 1992 Black Wednesday sterling crisis, and as special adviser to Home Secretary Michael Howard. From 1994 to 2001 he was Director of Corporate Affairs at Carlton Communications under chairman Michael Green. He was elected MP for Witney in 2001 and became Conservative Party leader on 6 December 2005 at age 39, succeeding Michael Howard.

Cameron's subsequent eldest son Ivan Cameron was born 8 April 2002 with Ohtahara syndrome — a rare neurological disorder that required round-the-clock care for cerebral palsy and severe epilepsy. Ivan died on 25 February 2009 at age six. Cameron's subsequent characterisation in interviews was that he had considered leaving politics altogether after Ivan's death.

### Premiership
Cameron was appointed Prime Minister on 11 May 2010 at the head of a Conservative-Liberal Democrat coalition government following the 6 May 2010 hung parliament. Deputy Prime Minister was Nick Clegg (Liberal Democrat leader). The coalition agreement committed both parties to the post-2008 financial-crisis deficit-reduction programme that George Osborne, appointed Chancellor of the Exchequer, would architect across the five-year period.

The June 2010 emergency Budget under Osborne committed roughly £110 billion in public spending cuts and tax increases across the five-year forecast period. The programme reduced public-sector employment by over 500,000 across 2010-2015. Welfare-payment and housing-subsidy and social-services spending was cut by over £30 billion across 2010-2019. The Conservative-Liberal-Democrat-coalition political messaging was that the NHS and education had been "ringfenced" from direct spending cuts; the record is that the nominal NHS protection operated against parallel inflation-and-demographic increases that produced effective real-terms NHS spending stagnation across 2010-2019.

The documented cumulative health-outcome record of the 2010-2019 period operates as the single most consequential austerity-period public-policy product per the multiple academic studies. The November 2017 BMJ Open Watkins-Wulaningsih-Da Zhou-Marshall-Sinclair-Pariente-Pichon study linked the austerity programme to 120,000 excess deaths in England 2010-2017, primarily through reductions in nursing-staff numbers. The June 2019 IPPR "Ending the Blame Game" report estimated 130,000 deaths in England 2012-2017 could have been prevented absent the austerity-period halt to public-health improvements. The June 2024 LSE Inequalities Berman-Hovland research estimated roughly 190,000 excess deaths across the UK 2010-2019, roughly 3% increase in mortality rates, and roughly half a year of life-expectancy loss for the average UK person. The February 2020 Marmot Review of Health Equity in England 10 Years On documented the life-expectancy slowdown across the austerity decade that was without precedent in the modern UK record.

The Cameron-coalition administration produced the 2012 Health and Social Care Act (the largest reorganisation of NHS structure since its 1948 founding; widely characterised in the post-2012 health-policy literature as enabling NHS privatisation through commissioning arrangements), the 2012 Welfare Reform Act (introducing Universal Credit), and the 2012-2016 Home Office "hostile environment" immigration policy under Home Secretary Theresa May (which produced the subsequent Windrush scandal under May's subsequent premiership). The August 2011 London riots produced the rapid-prosecution strategy that the Director of Public Prosecutions Sir Keir Starmer subsequently characterised as having "brought the situation back under control" (covered in Volume Two Dossier 18). The 2014 Scottish independence referendum produced the 55%-45% No vote retaining Scotland in the United Kingdom.

The 7 May 2015 general election produced an unexpected Conservative majority government with 331 seats (majority 12) — the first Conservative majority since John Major's 1992 victory. The Conservative manifesto commitment to a referendum on UK membership of the European Union was the decisive subsequent consequence of the 2015 victory.

### The Brexit referendum and resignation
The 23 June 2016 EU membership referendum produced a 51.9% Leave to 48.1% Remain vote on a 72.2% turnout. Cameron had campaigned for Remain. On 24 June 2016 Cameron announced his resignation. He left office on 13 July 2016. The subsequent characterisation in the reporting is that the Cameron decision to hold the referendum — the central campaign commitment of the 2015 Conservative manifesto — was the decisive strategic miscalculation of his premiership.

Cameron resigned from Parliament on 12 September 2016. He published his memoir *For the Record* in 2019.

### The Greensill scandal
Cameron's post-PM career operated through multiple advisory positions including the 2017-2023 Presidency of Alzheimer's Research UK and subsequent the 2018 advisory position at Greensill Capital — the supply-chain-finance company founded by Australian financier Lex Greensill (who had been an unpaid adviser to the Cameron government with access to eleven government departments during the 2010-2016 premiership).

Cameron's Greensill compensation package included share options reportedly worth up to $60 million and over $1 million per year for 25 days of work per year. Per the 2021 BBC Panorama investigation, Cameron's cumulative pre-tax personal compensation from the Greensill relationship was roughly $10 million for 30 months of part-time work.

In 2019 Cameron arranged a private meeting between Lex Greensill and Secretary of State for Health and Social Care Matt Hancock; several NHS trusts subsequently used Greensill Capital's Earnd app for early-pay-day services to NHS staff. In 2020-2021 Cameron lobbied the Sunak Treasury through text messages directly to Chancellor of the Exchequer Rishi Sunak and through ten virtual meetings with permanent secretaries Tom Scholar and Charles Roxburgh to allow Greensill Capital to access Covid Corporate Financing Facility loans. Sunak declined the request. The government-owned British Business Bank lent Greensill up to £400 million through a different scheme; the subsequent March 2021 Greensill Capital collapse produced potential £335 million loss to the UK taxpayer.

The April 2021 formal investigation under UK lobbying registrar Nigel Boardman produced the March 2022 Boardman report documenting the cumulative pattern. The cross-reference to Volume One's documented pattern of post-government financial extraction is the same architecture this volume's preceding dossiers have established: the political-class operator who exits direct government office and subsequently extracts personal compensation from the financial-services firm whose interests the operator's government had served while in office.

On 13 November 2023 Cameron was appointed Foreign Secretary by Rishi Sunak and created Baron Cameron of Chipping Norton (life peer) to permit cabinet service while not a Member of Parliament. He served as Foreign Secretary until the Conservative defeat on 5 July 2024.

## Theresa May (July 2016 - July 2019)

Theresa Mary May was born 1 October 1956 in Eastbourne. Father Hubert Brasier was an Anglican vicar; mother Zaidee Brasier was a teacher. She was educated at Wheatley Park Comprehensive School (a state school) and St Hugh's College, Oxford (Geography). She worked at the Bank of England (1977-1983) and the Association for Payment Clearing Services (1985-1997). She was MP for Maidenhead from 1997 to 2024. As Home Secretary 2010-2016 she was the longest-serving Home Secretary of the modern era.

May was appointed Prime Minister on 13 July 2016 following the post-referendum Conservative leadership contest (in which her major rival Andrea Leadsom withdrew). She inherited the post-Brexit-referendum institutional task of negotiating the UK's exit from the European Union under Article 50 (which she invoked on 29 March 2017).

The 8 June 2017 snap general election was the decisive miscalculation of her premiership. May had called the election on 18 April 2017 with a 20-point poll lead, intending to increase the Conservative majority before the subsequent Brexit negotiations. The result reduced the Conservative parliamentary position to a minority government — 317 seats (-13) — that operated through a confidence-and-supply arrangement with the Northern Ireland Democratic Unionist Party (DUP).

The 2017-2019 Brexit negotiations produced the May Withdrawal Agreement, rejected by the House of Commons three times: 15 January 2019 (rejected 432 to 202, the largest government defeat in modern parliamentary history); 12 March 2019 (rejected 391 to 242); 29 March 2019 (rejected 344 to 286). On 24 May 2019 May announced her resignation. She left office on 24 July 2019.

The subsequent institutional scandal of the May premiership was the Windrush scandal — the documented April 2018 revelations that thousands of post-WWII Caribbean-born British residents (many of whom had arrived as children on Caribbean parents' passports under pre-1973 right-of-abode arrangements) had been wrongfully detained, denied legal rights, deported, or refused re-entry under the Home Office "hostile environment" policy that May had architected as Home Secretary 2012-2016. The 2020 Williams Lessons Learned Review documented "thoughtless" Home Office decisions across the multi-year period and subsequent compensation programme.

## Boris Johnson (July 2019 - September 2022)

### Biography
Alexander Boris de Pfeffel Johnson was born 19 June 1964 in New York City. Father Stanley Johnson was a Conservative politician and writer; mother Charlotte Johnson Wahl was a painter. He held US-UK dual citizenship until his 2016 renunciation of US citizenship. He was educated at the European School in Brussels, the Ashdown House preparatory school, Eton College, and Balliol College, Oxford (Literae Humaniores; "Greats"). He was President of the Oxford Union in 1986. He was a member of the Bullingdon Club at Oxford alongside David Cameron and George Osborne.

Johnson's pre-parliamentary career operated through The Times (1987-1988; sacked for fabricating a quotation), the Daily Telegraph (1988-1999, including 1989-1994 as Brussels correspondent — the period in which Johnson's Brussels reporting produced the UK Eurosceptic-press architecture of subsequent anti-EU popular journalism), and editorship of The Spectator (1999-2005). He was MP for Henley 2001-2008 and subsequent MP for Uxbridge and South Ruislip 2015-2023. He served as Mayor of London 2008-2016 (defeating Ken Livingstone in 2008 and 2012). He served as Foreign Secretary under May 2016-2018 and resigned over May's Chequers Brexit plan.

### Premiership
Johnson was appointed Prime Minister on 24 July 2019 following May's resignation and the Conservative leadership contest (Johnson defeated Jeremy Hunt in the members' ballot 92,153 to 46,656).

The 12 December 2019 general election was the decisive electoral product of the post-2016 Brexit period. Conservative landslide: 365 seats (+48) / parliamentary majority of 80 — the largest Conservative majority since Thatcher's 1987 victory. Labour under Jeremy Corbyn fell to 202 seats (-60), the worst Labour election result since 1935. The decisive electoral architecture of the Conservative victory was the collapse of the post-1945 Labour Red Wall in the Midlands, North-East, and North-West English seats — the 60-plus historically Labour constituencies that switched to Conservative on the "Get Brexit Done" campaign theme.

The subsequent 31 January 2020 formal UK exit from the European Union was the central legislative product of the Johnson premiership. The subsequent 31 December 2020 end of the Brexit transition period operationalised the UK's post-EU regulatory and customs architecture.

The March 2020-onward Covid-19 pandemic period was the central operational test of the Johnson premiership. The March 2020 first lockdown, the multi-wave restrictions, the April 2020 Johnson hospitalisation in intensive care, the 2020-2021 vaccine rollout, and the cumulative documented UK Covid death toll of roughly 230,000 per Office for National Statistics figures comprise the documented pandemic period.

### Partygate
The decisive scandal of the Johnson premiership was the Partygate scandal. From late 2021 onward, multiple British media outlets — including the Daily Mirror, ITV News, and the Guardian — documented multiple Downing Street and Whitehall gatherings during the 2020-2021 Covid-lockdown periods when parallel gatherings of the public were prohibited under the Coronavirus Act 2020 emergency regulations.

The December 2021 Sue Gray internal investigation (commissioned by Johnson) and the Metropolitan Police Operation Hillman criminal investigation (opened January 2022) produced the April 2022 fixed-penalty-notice cycle. Per the Metropolitan Police findings, 126 fixed penalty notices were issued to 83 individuals including Boris Johnson, Rishi Sunak (then Chancellor of the Exchequer), Carrie Johnson (Johnson's wife), and multiple other Downing Street and Cabinet Office staff. Johnson became the first sitting British Prime Minister found to have broken the law in office.

The May 2022 full Sue Gray report documented "failures of leadership and judgement in different parts of No 10 and the Cabinet Office at different times." The June 2022 Conservative-parliamentary-party confidence vote — Johnson won 211 to 148 — was the decisive parliamentary signal that Johnson's premiership was politically unsustainable. The 41% backbench rebellion exceeded the 37% Theresa May had faced in her December 2018 confidence vote (May resigned six months later).

### The Pincher scandal and resignation
The 29 June 2022 Chris Pincher scandal — Pincher (Conservative MP and Deputy Chief Whip) was accused of sexually inappropriate conduct at the Carlton Club, and Johnson's multiple other inconsistent public statements about what he had known about Pincher's pre-existing misconduct allegations — produced the decisive cabinet collapse.

On 5 July 2022 Rishi Sunak (Chancellor of the Exchequer) and Sajid Javid (Health Secretary) resigned from cabinet within minutes of each other. Roughly 60 subsequent government resignations followed across 5-7 July 2022. On 7 July 2022 Johnson announced his resignation as Conservative Party leader. He left office on 6 September 2022.

## Liz Truss (September-October 2022, 50 days)

Mary Elizabeth Truss was born 26 July 1975 in Oxford. Father John Kenneth Truss was a mathematics professor; mother Priscilla Mary Truss was a nurse and teacher. She was educated at Roundhay School Leeds (state comprehensive) and Merton College, Oxford (PPE). She was President of the Oxford University Liberal Democrats before joining the Conservative Party in 1996. She worked at Royal Dutch Shell (1996-2000) and Cable & Wireless (2000-2005), and as deputy director of the Reform think tank (2008-2010). She was MP for South West Norfolk from 2010 to 2024.

Her pre-PM cabinet positions across the three preceding Conservative administrations included Lord Chancellor and Secretary of State for Justice under Cameron (the first woman in the thousand-year history of the office), Chief Secretary to the Treasury and Secretary of State for International Trade under May, and Foreign Secretary under Johnson (2021-2022).

Truss won the September 2022 Conservative leadership election against Rishi Sunak 57.4% to 42.6% in the members' ballot. She was appointed Prime Minister by Queen Elizabeth II at Balmoral Castle on 6 September 2022 — the final Prime Ministerial appointment of the Queen's reign. Queen Elizabeth II died at Balmoral on 8 September 2022, two days into the Truss premiership. The 10-day national mourning period that followed suspended government business.

### The mini-Budget
The decisive event of the Truss premiership was the 23 September 2022 mini-Budget delivered by Chancellor of the Exchequer Kwasi Kwarteng. The package committed £45 billion in unfunded tax cuts including the abolition of the 45% top rate of income tax and expansion of government borrowing.

The 26-28 September 2022 gilt-market and sterling crash followed within 72 hours. The pound fell to $1.0327 against the dollar on 26 September 2022 — the lowest level since the 1985 sterling crisis. The 30-year gilt yield rose from 3.6% to 5.1% across three trading days. The pension-fund LDI (liability-driven investment) margin-call cascade — in which UK pension funds faced collateral demands that exceeded their available liquid assets — produced the 28 September 2022 Bank of England emergency intervention. The Bank of England committed up to £65 billion in emergency gilt purchases to prevent pension-fund collapse.

The subsequent 3 October 2022 partial U-turn on the 45% top-rate abolition was followed by the 14 October 2022 dismissal of Kwarteng and the appointment of Jeremy Hunt as Chancellor. On 17 October 2022 Hunt reversed most of the remaining mini-Budget measures. Truss approval rating reached -47% per the Observer/Opinium polling — worse than the Johnson Partygate-period low.

On 20 October 2022 Truss announced her resignation as Conservative leader. The Daily Star livestream of a head of lettuce had begun seven days earlier inviting viewers to speculate whether Truss would resign before the lettuce wilted; Truss resigned first. She left office on 25 October 2022 as the shortest-serving Prime Minister in British history, surpassing George Canning's 119 days in 1827.

The cross-reference to this volume's documented pattern is that the Truss-Kwarteng mini-Budget was the single most documented case of post-2010 bond-market-and-pension-fund repudiation of unfunded tax-cuts-and-spending architecture. The 50-day political collapse demonstrated the structural limits of the post-2010 UK political-class room for independent fiscal policy under the debt-financed monetary architecture this volume's Volume One documented.

## Rishi Sunak (October 2022 - July 2024)

### Biography
Rishi Sunak was born 12 May 1980 in Southampton. Father Yashvir Sunak was an NHS general practitioner; mother Usha Sunak was a pharmacist. The Sunak family had migrated to the UK from East Africa (Sunak's paternal and maternal grandparents had emigrated from Punjab to East Africa in the early 20th century). He was educated at Stroud School, Winchester College (where he was head boy), and Lincoln College, Oxford (PPE; first-class). He received a Fulbright Scholarship to Stanford Graduate School of Business and completed an MBA there in 2006.

His pre-parliamentary career operated through Goldman Sachs (2001-2004), The Children's Investment Fund Management hedge fund (2006-2009), and Theleme Partners (which he co-founded in 2010). He was MP for Richmond (Yorks) from 2015 to 2024.

In 2009 Sunak married Akshata Murty, daughter of Narayana Murthy (co-founder of Infosys, the Indian IT services major). The Murty family wealth was estimated at roughly £730 million in 2022 per The Sunday Times Rich List. In April 2022 the public controversy over Akshata Murty's non-domiciled UK tax status — which had reportedly saved her roughly £20 million in UK tax across the preceding multi-year period — produced subsequent Murty announcement that she would pay UK tax on her worldwide income going forward.

Sunak was a Brexit supporter in the 2016 referendum. He served as Chief Secretary to the Treasury 2019-2020 under Johnson and as Chancellor of the Exchequer 2020-2022. As Chancellor he architected the furlough scheme (the £70 billion-plus Covid-period wage-replacement programme), the Eat Out to Help Out scheme (August 2020), and the multi-year Covid-period emergency-spending programme that cumulatively committed over £400 billion across 2020-2022. The subsequent cost-of-living crisis of 2022 onward operated on the post-emergency-spending inflation that the Bank of England Monetary Policy Committee subsequent interest-rate-rising campaign was attempting to contain.

Sunak received a fixed penalty notice in April 2022 for attending the 19 June 2020 Boris Johnson birthday gathering — the Partygate incident that confirmed Johnson and Sunak as the first sitting Prime Minister and first sitting Chancellor of the Exchequer found to have broken the law in office.

### Premiership
Sunak resigned as Chancellor on 5 July 2022 amid the Pincher scandal. He lost the September 2022 Conservative leadership election to Liz Truss 42.6% to 57.4%. He won the subsequent October 2022 Conservative leadership election uncontested on 24 October 2022 after Boris Johnson withdrew and Penny Mordaunt failed to reach the threshold of 100 nominations.

Sunak was appointed Prime Minister by King Charles III on 25 October 2022 — the first British Prime Minister of Asian descent, the first Hindu Prime Minister, and the youngest Prime Minister in over 200 years (Sunak was 42 at appointment).

The subsequent 21-month Sunak administration operated through four institutional anchors. The March 2023 Windsor Framework with the EU on the Northern Ireland Protocol modified the post-Brexit Northern Ireland customs and regulatory arrangements. The 2023 Illegal Migration Act was the central legislative product of the Conservative-administration anti-asylum-seeker programme. The April 2024 Rwanda Bill — the central Conservative-administration policy of removing asylum seekers to Rwanda for processing — was subsequently scrapped by the Starmer government on 6 July 2024. And the cost-of-living-crisis-period public-spending architecture produced the 2022-2024 real-wage stagnation that the Resolution Foundation analysis documented as the worst real-wage performance period in modern UK history.

### The 4 July 2024 defeat
On 22 May 2024 Sunak called the general election for 4 July 2024 — six months before the latest constitutionally available date. The subsequent reporting characterised the decision as a strategic miscalculation. The Conservative public approval was at pre-1997-Major-period lows; the decision to call the election in summer 2024 rather than autumn 2024 forfeited multiple months of potential recovery.

On 6 June 2024 Sunak attended the 80th anniversary D-Day commemorations in Normandy but left early to do an ITV election interview — a decision subsequent widely criticised by veterans and across the multi-party political-class commentary.

The 4 July 2024 general election produced the Conservative catastrophic defeat: 121 seats (-251) on 23.7% of the popular vote — the worst Conservative election result since the 1832 Reform Act. The subsequent 121-seat Conservative parliamentary delegation was the smallest in the Conservative Party's post-1832 modern history.

Sunak left office on 5 July 2024 following the Starmer Labour landslide. He stepped down as Conservative Party leader on 5 November 2024. He remained MP for Richmond and Northallerton (his post-boundary-review constituency) following the election.

## Kemi Badenoch (November 2024 onward)

### Biography
Kemi Badenoch was born Olukemi Olufunto Adegoke on 2 January 1980 in Wimbledon, London at St Teresa's Maternity Hospital. Her mother Feyi Adubifa had travelled from Nigeria to the UK for medical treatment and gave birth in London before returning to Nigeria with the infant Olukemi. Badenoch spent her childhood ages 5 to 16 in Lagos, Nigeria, where her parents were middle-and-upper-class but where the 1980s-1990s Nigerian political and economic instability produced the family's decision to send Kemi back to the UK at age 16.

She returned to the UK aged 16 to live with relatives. She worked at McDonald's during her subsequent sixth-form period. She was educated at Phoenix College Morden and Guildford County School (state comprehensive). She studied Computer Systems Engineering at the University of Sussex (BEng) and subsequently a part-time LLB at Birkbeck University of London. Her pre-parliamentary career operated as a software engineer at Logica and subsequent at RBS / Coutts.

She married Hamish Badenoch in 2012 (Hamish was a former Conservative parliamentary candidate working in financial services).

She was a London Assembly member 2015-2017 (replacing Victoria Borwick when Borwick was elected MP for Kensington). She was MP for Saffron Walden 2017-2024 and for North West Essex 2024 onward.

### Cabinet positions and 2024 leadership
Badenoch held cabinet positions in the post-2019 Conservative administration period: Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Children and Families under Johnson (2019-2020); Exchequer Secretary to the Treasury (2020-2021); Minister of State for Local Government, Faith and Communities (2021-2022); Secretary of State for International Trade and President of the Board of Trade under Truss (2022); Minister for Women and Equalities (2022-2024); and Secretary of State for Business and Trade under Sunak (2023-2024) following the merger of the Department for International Trade with the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy.

On 2 November 2024 Badenoch defeated Robert Jenrick in the Conservative Party membership ballot 53,806 to 41,388 (56.5% to 43.5%) to become the first Black leader of any major UK political party, the fourth female Conservative Party leader after Margaret Thatcher, Theresa May, and Liz Truss, and the second Conservative leader of colour after Sunak.

### Leadership of the Opposition (November 2024 onward)
Badenoch's 2024-2026 Conservative-opposition positioning operates on right-of-centre policy directions: hardline immigration reduction; pro-Israel-and-Gaza-military-operation foreign-policy position (Badenoch's subsequent reported characterisation of Gaza-period casualty figures has been that the conflict is in the UK's interest); climate-policy scepticism and characterisation of net-zero policies as benefiting China; anti-transgender-self-identification policy positions; pro-AUKUS defence positioning; and anti-ECHR-overreach legal positioning that has characterised the European Convention on Human Rights as an obstacle to UK immigration enforcement.

In May 2026 BBC reporting confirmed no Conservative-leadership pressure on Badenoch despite the 7 May 2026 Conservative local-elections losses that were subsequent eclipsed by the Labour-government collapse documented in Volume Two Dossier 18.

## The fourteen-year institutional verdict

The 2010-2024 UK Conservative administration period is the documented case of post-2008 financial-crisis political-class management of the debt-financed monetary architecture this volume's Volume One documented as the central operational structure of the contemporary global political economy. The 190,000 excess deaths attributable to the austerity programme per LSE Inequalities June 2024 research; the 4-5% GDP reduction relative to counterfactual EU-membership scenario per OBR / Bank of England / Centre for European Reform analysis; the £65 billion Bank of England emergency intervention to prevent pension-fund collapse from the Truss-Kwarteng mini-Budget; the 121-seat Conservative parliamentary delegation following the 4 July 2024 defeat that was the smallest in the Conservative Party's post-1832 modern history; and the cumulative documented post-PM financial extraction by David Cameron through the Greensill Capital advisory relationship comprise the documented fourteen-year record.

The Conservative Party claimed to be the party of responsible economic management. What it delivered over fourteen years: the largest single-period government-policy-attributable excess-deaths total in modern UK history; the largest single Conservative electoral defeat since the 1832 Reform Act; the largest single-event sterling-and-gilt crash in modern UK history; and systematic financial extraction by post-government operators through advisory relationships with financial-services firms whose interests the administration's policy decisions had served while those operators were in office.

## Bridge to Dossier 20

The post-2010 European political-class architecture — the post-Merkel German political-class transition under Friedrich Merz; the post-2022 Italian political-class transition under Giorgia Meloni; the Polish political-class transition under Donald Tusk's 2023 return to government; the Hungarian political-class continuation under Viktor Orbán's fifth Prime Ministerial term since 2010; the European Commission under Ursula von der Leyen's second term since December 2024; the NATO leadership under Mark Rutte's October 2024 succession to Jens Stoltenberg; and the post-2024 European Parliament principals — comprise the subject of Dossier 20.

# Dossier 20: The European Political Class

## The institution

The contemporary European political-class architecture operates through six institutional positions and the operators who currently hold them. The six positions are the Chancellorship of Germany (Friedrich Merz, May 2025 onward), the Prime Ministership of Italy (Giorgia Meloni, October 2022 onward), the Prime Ministership of Poland (Donald Tusk, December 2023 onward), the Prime Ministership of Hungary (Viktor Orbán, May 2010 onward), the Presidency of the European Commission (Ursula von der Leyen, December 2019 onward; re-elected July 2024 for second term to 2029), and the Secretary-Generalship of NATO (Mark Rutte, October 2024 onward; the former Prime Minister of the Netherlands 2010-2024). The two additional institutional positions covered in this dossier are the Presidency of the European Council (Antonio Costa, December 2024 onward) and the Presidency of the European Parliament (Roberta Metsola, January 2022 onward; re-elected July 2024).

The apparatus produces the post-2022 European political-class coordination on three operational axes. First, the continuation of US-aligned foreign-policy positions through NATO under Rutte's October 2024-onward leadership — the coordinated EU sanctions on Russia following the February 2022 Ukraine invasion, the multi-year continued weapons-and-financial-aid programmes to Ukraine across the 2022-2026 period, and the trans-Atlantic-coordination architecture that the second Trump administration's January 2025-onward positioning has tested but has not dismantled. Second, the coordinated migration-and-asylum-enforcement architecture across the Mediterranean and Eastern European external borders — the Meloni-led subsequent central Mediterranean maritime-operations programme, the Tusk-led Polish-Belarus border enforcement programme, the Merz January 2025 Bundestag anti-immigration motion that passed with AfD votes (the first time since 1949 that a Bundestag majority was achieved with AfD support), and the coordinated EU Pact on Migration and Asylum approval under the 2024 subsequent von der Leyen-Commission framework. Third, the coordinated post-2010-eurozone-crisis fiscal-architecture that operates the central bank intervention pattern documented in this volume's Dossier 19 Truss-Kwarteng mini-Budget section — the 2010-2012 European Central Bank "whatever it takes" intervention under Mario Draghi; the 2014-2019 von der Leyen-Commission first-term subsequent coordination through the subsequent NextGenerationEU programme; and the subsequent 2025-2026 Merz-government debt-brake reform that exempted defence spending above 1% of GDP from the debt-brake limits and was estimated to produce roughly €71 billion in annual German interest payments from 2035 onward.

The European political class sells itself as the democratic, rules-based, post-1945 architecture that protects European citizens from the continental-war catastrophes of the 1914-1945 period. The contemporary record: integration of the post-2008 European political class with the post-Brexit Atlantic financial architecture; coordinated EU procurement of US military equipment and US-and-UK financial services through the post-2022 NATO rearmament programmes; and continued 2024-2026 EU support for the Israeli military operation in Gaza that the International Court of Justice provisional measures rulings, the International Criminal Court arrest warrants against Netanyahu and Gallant, and the academic-genocide-scholar consensus have characterised as a genocide.

The single most documented case of post-1990 contemporary European political-class continuity with the global financial architecture this volume's Volume One documented is the 2016-2020 BlackRock Asset Management Deutschland AG Chairmanship of Friedrich Merz — the contemporary German Chancellor who held the most advisory position at the German branch of the world's largest asset-management firm across the four-year period immediately preceding his 2025 election to the Chancellorship.

## Friedrich Merz — German Chancellor (May 2025-)

Friedrich Merz was born 11 November 1955 in Brilon, North Rhine-Westphalia, in the Sauerland region of post-war West Germany. He joined the Young Union (CDU youth wing) in 1972 and completed his law studies in 1985. From 1985 to 1989 he worked as a judge and corporate lawyer. He was elected to the European Parliament in 1989 and served until 1994. He was elected to the Bundestag in 1994 and served until 2009.

His pre-2009 political career operated through the Chairmanship of the CDU/CSU parliamentary group in the Bundestag from February 2000 to September 2002. He took the position from Wolfgang Schäuble during the CDU donations affair and ceded it to Angela Merkel in September 2002 as part of the multi-year Merkel-Merz rivalry that Merkel's 2024 memoir *Freedom* characterised: "We both wanted to be the boss."

### The corporate period (2009-2021)
In 2009 Merz withdrew from active politics. From 2009 to 2021 he worked as a corporate lawyer at the international law firm Mayer Brown LLP. He held the position of counsel until 2021. His corporate-board portfolio across the 2009-2021 period included Chairmanship of the Supervisory Board of BlackRock Asset Management Deutschland AG (the German branch of BlackRock Inc, the world's largest asset manager) from 2016 to March 2020; supervisory-board positions at HSBC Trinkaus & Burkhardt; supervisory-board positions at EY Germany; and supervisory-board positions at Borussia Dortmund (the Bundesliga football club) and Deutsche Börse (the German stock-exchange operator).

The 2016-2020 BlackRock chairmanship was the single most documented case of contemporary European political-class continuity with the post-2008 global asset-management architecture this volume's Volume One documented. BlackRock Inc, with roughly $11.5 trillion in assets under management by the end of 2024, is the world's largest asset manager and the single most structurally consequential operator of the post-2008 global passive-investment-and-ETF architecture that the post-2010 global equity-and-bond markets have operated on. Merz's four-year chairmanship of the German branch was the most advisory position the firm operated in the largest European economy across the immediate pre-2022 period — including the 2018-2019 post-Brexit financial-services migration of asset-management operations from London to Frankfurt and other continental European centres.

The 2018-2021 return to politics operated through multiple leadership-election defeats. Merz announced his return to politics in October 2018 following Merkel's announcement that she would step down as CDU leader. He was defeated by Annegret Kramp-Karrenbauer in the December 2018 CDU leadership election. He was defeated by Armin Laschet in the January 2021 CDU leadership election. He returned to the Bundestag in the September 2021 federal election.

### CDU leadership and the 2025 Chancellorship
Merz won the 16 December 2021 CDU leadership election — the first general party-membership ballot in the CDU's history. He was formally elected CDU Chairman by the 1,001 congress delegates on 22 January 2022 with 94.6% of valid votes. He took office as party leader on 31 January 2022 and served as Leader of the Opposition in the Bundestag from February 2022 to May 2025 across the Olaf Scholz "traffic light" SPD-Greens-FDP coalition period.

The November 2024 collapse of the Scholz traffic-light coalition triggered the 23 February 2025 federal election. The CDU/CSU won 28.6% of the vote and 208 Bundestag seats — the second-worst CDU result in the party's post-1949 history. The AfD won 152 seats, the best result for the far-right party in the post-1949 period and the second-largest parliamentary delegation. The SPD fell to 120 seats. The subsequent 5 May 2025 CDU/CSU-SPD coalition agreement produced the grand-coalition government structure.

On 6 May 2025 Merz was elected Chancellor in the second-round Bundestag vote. The first-round defeat — Merz failed to secure the required absolute majority of 316 votes — was the first time in the post-1949 history of the Federal Republic that a CDU/CSU-led incoming Chancellor had failed to win on the first round. Merz became the oldest post-war German Chancellor at age 69.

The 5 March 2025 debt-brake reform — proposed by Merz and agreed with outgoing Chancellor Scholz — amended Basic Law Paragraphs 109, 115, and 143h to exempt defence spending above 1% of GDP from the debt-brake limits. The reform was estimated to produce roughly €71 billion in annual German interest payments from 2035 onward per economist analysis at the time.

The post-May 2025 Merz Chancellorship has operated on three documented operational positions. First, the post-Trump-administration public characterisation of US-Europe relations: "My absolute priority will be to strengthen Europe as quickly as possible so that, step by step, we can really achieve independence from the USA" (February 2025 post-election statement). Second, the continued hardline immigration positioning — including the 29 January 2025 non-binding Bundestag anti-immigration motion that passed with AfD votes, the first time since 1949 that a Bundestag majority was achieved with AfD support, despite Merz's public commitment to "never" work with the AfD. Third, the debt-brake reform that remained the single most consequential post-1990 fiscal-architecture change in modern German political-economic policy.

The contemporary German economy under Merz is in documented decline. May 2026 Deutsche Welle reporting documented frustration among German businesses with the economic-decline period; 12 May 2026 Merz speech advocating subsequent economic reform was met with public booing per Deutsche Welle reporting; and 15 May 2026 Merz warned young Germans against moving to the US per Telegraph reporting.

## Giorgia Meloni — Prime Minister of Italy (October 2022-)

Giorgia Meloni was born 15 January 1977 in Rome. Her father Francesco Meloni (a tax adviser) left the family when she was a child. Her mother Anna Paratore raised Giorgia and her older sister Arianna (born 1975). The family was raised in the Garbatella district of Rome — the working-class neighbourhood the family moved to after the more affluent home Meloni had been born into was destroyed by fire shortly after her father's departure. The characterisation of the family circumstances in Meloni's 2021 autobiography *Io sono Giorgia* and in biographical reporting is of impoverished single-parent upbringing. Meloni does not hold a university degree — a rare circumstance among contemporary European Prime Ministers.

### Political career
At age 15, in 1992, Meloni joined the Youth Front (Fronte della Gioventù) — the youth wing of the Italian Social Movement (Movimento Sociale Italiano, MSI), the post-1946 political party founded by supporters of Benito Mussolini after the 1945 destruction of the original Fascist Party. The MSI was the central institutional vehicle of post-war Italian neo-fascism.

In 1996, at age 19, Meloni appeared on French television and characterised Mussolini: "Mussolini was a good politician, in that everything he did, he did for Italy." The subsequent characterisation of her teenage political-class formation is the record of the post-fascist political-class architecture in which her career was formed.

In 1994 the MSI rebranded as the National Alliance (Alleanza Nazionale, AN) under Gianfranco Fini. Meloni became the national leader of Student Action (Azione Studentesca), the AN student movement. In 1998 she was elected to the Province of Rome council. In 2006 she was elected to the Chamber of Deputies. In 2008 Silvio Berlusconi appointed her Minister of Youth in his fourth government — at age 31 she became the youngest cabinet minister in the history of the Italian Republic.

In 2012 Meloni co-founded the Brothers of Italy (Fratelli d'Italia, FdI) with Ignazio La Russa and Guido Crosetto. The party retained the tricolour-flame logo of the MSI — a visual continuity with the post-fascist political tradition that the 2022 Holocaust-survivor Senator Liliana Segre asked Meloni to remove and that Meloni declined to change. In 2014 Meloni was elected President of Brothers of Italy. From 2020 to 2025 she served as President of the European Conservatives and Reformists Party (ECR), the right-wing party group in the European Parliament.

The 2019 European Parliament election produced 6.4% for Brothers of Italy and the subsequent Rome rally at which Meloni delivered the viral speech: "I am Giorgia. I am a woman, I am a mother, I am Italian, I am Christian. And you can't take that away from me!" In February 2022 she addressed the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) in Florida. In May 2022 at the Vox rally in Marbella, Spain, she delivered the characterisation: "Yes to the natural family, no to the LGBT lobby... No to gender ideology... No to the bureaucrats of Brussels!"

### Premiership
The 25 September 2022 Italian general election was the decisive moment of the post-2022 Italian political-class transition. Brothers of Italy won 26% of the vote — the single-largest party result. The centre-right coalition (Brothers of Italy + Salvini's Lega + Berlusconi's Forza Italia) won absolute majority in both Chamber of Deputies and Senate.

On 22 October 2022 Meloni was sworn in as Prime Minister by President Sergio Mattarella — the first female Italian Prime Minister and the first head of any post-1945 Italian government with institutional link to Mussolini's Fascist Party. The Meloni government is the 68th of the Italian Republic since 1945 and the second-longest-running government in the history of the Italian Republic.

The 2022-2026 Meloni government period has operated on coordinated trans-Atlantic foreign-policy positioning. Per reporting, Meloni's 2023-2024 diplomatic relationship with US President Joe Biden produced the characterisation of Italy as the White House's strongest European ally. The 8 May 2026 US Secretary of State Marco Rubio meeting with Meloni in Rome operated under the Trump-administration period and was characterised by both sides as a coordination on Iran-war and US-EU policy frictions.

The Meloni-led central Mediterranean maritime-operations programme has operated as a central node of the coordinated EU migration-and-asylum-enforcement architecture documented at the governing level in the 2024 EU Pact on Migration and Asylum.

Sister Arianna Meloni was appointed head of the Brothers of Italy political secretariat in 2023. Arianna's partner Francesco Lollobrigida has been Minister of Agriculture since 22 October 2022. The family-political-class architecture of the Meloni government — including the 2024 reported indirect economic interests of Meloni's mother Anna Paratore in a network of real-estate companies — is the documented contemporary record.

## Donald Tusk — Prime Minister of Poland (2007-2014 and 2023-)

Donald Franciszek Tusk was born 22 April 1957 in Gdańsk, Poland, to a Kashubian-Polish family. His pre-political career operated through academic-historical work and anti-communist Solidarity-period activism. He co-founded the Liberal Democratic Congress (KLD) party in 1990 and co-founded Civic Platform (Platforma Obywatelska, PO) in 2001 with Andrzej Olechowski and Maciej Płażyński.

### First Prime Ministership (2007-2014)
Civic Platform won 41.5% in the October 2007 Polish parliamentary election. On 16 November 2007 Tusk was sworn in as Prime Minister. In the 2011 parliamentary election he was re-elected — the first Polish Prime Minister re-elected since the 1989 fall of communism. On 22 September 2014 he resigned to take up the Presidency of the European Council.

### European Council Presidency (2014-2019)
Tusk served as President of the European Council from 1 December 2014 to 30 November 2019. He was re-elected for a second term in March 2017 despite the opposition of the PiS-led Polish government under Jarosław Kaczyński. From 2019 to 2022 he served as President of the European People's Party (EPP).

### Return to Polish politics (2021-)
In July 2021 Tusk returned to Warsaw as Civic Platform leader. The 2023 anti-government protest cycle included the 4 June 2023 demonstration in Warsaw (300,000-500,000 turnout per Polish media estimates) and the 1 October 2023 "March of a Million Hearts."

The 15 October 2023 Polish parliamentary election produced PiS's 35.4% as the largest single-party result but PiS lost the majority. Civic Coalition (Tusk's post-2021 alliance) won 30.7%, Third Way 14.4%, New Left 8.6%, with the combined opposition vote 54.0%. Following the 6 December 2023 PiS Mateusz Morawiecki cabinet failure to win confidence vote, on 11 December 2023 Tusk was elected Prime Minister by the Sejm. He is the longest-serving Prime Minister of the Third Polish Republic.

On 1 January 2025 Poland assumed the six-month EU Council Presidency. The subsequent 2024-2026 coordination of the EU-NATO-Ukraine support architecture operated through the subsequent Tusk-Polish-government position as a central operator.

The June 2025 Polish presidential election produced the PiS-aligned Karol Nawrocki's subsequent victory over Civic Coalition candidate Rafał Trzaskowski. The Nawrocki Presidency — operating with the constitutional veto power that previous PiS-aligned President Andrzej Duda had used across 2023-2025 to block the Tusk legislative programme — has produced the continued structural conflict between the Tusk government and the PiS-aligned presidency that the 2026 Polish political reporting documents as the decisive limit on the Tusk government's legislative reach.

## Viktor Orbán — Prime Minister of Hungary (1998-2002 and 2010-)

Viktor Mihály Orbán was born 31 May 1963 in Székesfehérvár, Hungary. He studied law at ELTE Faculty of Law in Budapest, graduating 1987. In 1989 he received a Soros Foundation scholarship to Pembroke College, Oxford — a biographical circumstance the post-2010 Orbán-government public discourse has largely elided. He co-founded Fidesz (Federation of Young Democrats) in 1988 as a anti-communist student movement.

### First Prime Ministership (1998-2002)
In May 1998 Fidesz won the parliamentary election. Orbán was sworn in as Prime Minister at age 35 — the youngest in Hungarian history. In 2002 Fidesz lost the election to the Hungarian Socialist Party.

### Continuous tenure from 2010
On 25 April 2010 Fidesz won 53% of the vote and a constitutional two-thirds majority in the parliamentary election. On 29 May 2010 Orbán was sworn in as Prime Minister. Fidesz retained its two-thirds majority in the 2014, 2018, and 2022 parliamentary elections. As of May 2026, Orbán has served continuously as Prime Minister for sixteen years — the longest-serving head of government of any EU member state.

In 2011 the Fidesz two-thirds majority adopted a new Hungarian Constitution that produced European Commission Article 7 rule-of-law proceedings. The 2020-2024 coordinated EU rule-of-law conditionality framework withheld roughly €30 billion in Hungaria EU funds across the multi-year period — the largest such withholding in post-2004 EU enlargement history. The subsequent Orbán government's 2014-onward public characterisation of its governing model has been the "illiberal democracy" framework.

The 2025-2026 emergence of Péter Magyar's Tisza Party as a credible Hungarian opposition challenger has produced the first subsequent credible electoral threat to the Orbán government in the post-2010 multi-cycle period. The subsequent 2026 Hungarian parliamentary election is scheduled and the polling at the date of writing shows Magyar's Tisza Party leading Fidesz.

The structural political-class continuity of the Orbán government with the prior dossiers of this volume is the post-2010 Hungarian government's subsequent central diplomatic alignment with the Trump-administration period and with the subsequent Russian-government position during the 2022-2026 Ukraine war.

## Ursula von der Leyen — European Commission President (December 2019-; second term December 2024-2029)

Ursula Gertrud Albrecht was born 8 October 1958 in Ixelles, Brussels — during her father Ernst Albrecht's European Economic Community tenure as Director-General. Ernst Albrecht subsequently served as Minister-President of Lower Saxony from 1976 to 1990. Her mother Heidi Adele Stromeyer was the daughter of a Bremen merchant family. Ursula was educated at Göttingen, the London School of Economics, Münster, and Hanover Medical School (MD 1991). She qualified as a gynaecologist and practised from 1987 to 1996. She married Heiko von der Leyen in 1986; the couple have seven children.

### Pre-Commission career
Von der Leyen joined the CDU in 1990. She served in the Lower Saxony government 2003-2005, as Federal Minister for Family Affairs 2005-2009 under Merkel, as Federal Minister of Labour 2009-2013, and as Federal Minister of Defence 2013-2019 — the first female German Defence Minister.

### First Commission term (2019-2024)
On 16 July 2019 von der Leyen was elected President of the European Commission by the European Parliament with 383 votes — a narrow 9-vote margin over the required majority. She took office on 1 December 2019.

The 2020-2021 Covid-vaccine-procurement programme produced the multi-year controversy over text-message exchanges between von der Leyen and Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla. The European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Court of Justice both addressed the von der Leyen-Commission refusal to disclose the text messages. The subsequent March 2024 EU General Court ruling against the Commission required the Commission to provide reasons for the refusal to disclose — but the text messages themselves remained undisclosed at the date of writing.

The 2022 coordinated EU sanctions on Russia following the 24 February 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine and the multi-year NextGenerationEU programme constituted the central operational products of the von der Leyen first-term period.

### Second term (December 2024-2029)
On 27 June 2024 the European Council re-nominated von der Leyen for a second term. Orbán voted against; Meloni abstained. On 18 July 2024 von der Leyen was re-elected by the European Parliament 401-284-22 (53.7% of votes cast). On 27 November 2024 her second Commission was approved by the Parliament 370-282-36 (51% of all members) — the least-supported European Commission by Parliament since 1993 when the Parliament was first given the right to vote on the college.

The second von der Leyen Commission took office on 1 December 2024. The college consists of 27 commissioners with six Executive Vice-Presidents. Women constitute 40.7% of the commissioners — down from 44.4% in the first term despite von der Leyen's request that each member state nominate one woman and one man (only Bulgaria complied).

The 2024-2026 subsequent von der Leyen Commission position on the post-7-October-2023 Gaza period has operated through the pro-Israel positioning that has produced the internal EU diplomatic tensions. The subsequent von der Leyen Commission has continued the EU coordinated weapons-and-financial-aid programmes to Israel across the post-7-October-2023 period that the International Court of Justice provisional measures rulings, the International Criminal Court arrest warrants against Netanyahu and Gallant, and the academic-genocide-scholar consensus have characterised as a genocide.

## Mark Rutte — NATO Secretary-General (October 2024-)

Mark Rutte was born 14 February 1967 in The Hague. He studied History at Leiden University, graduating in 1992. From 1992 to 2002 he worked at the Anglo-Dutch consumer-goods multinational Unilever, including a position as Human Resources director. He joined the VVD (People's Party for Freedom and Democracy) in 1989.

### Dutch Prime Ministership (2010-2024)
Rutte served in the Dutch House of Representatives 2003-2010 and 2010-2024. On 14 October 2010 he was sworn in as Prime Minister of the Netherlands at the head of the Rutte I coalition government — the first VVD Prime Minister since 1918. His subsequent fourteen-year tenure as Prime Minister was the longest in Dutch history, operating across four governments (Rutte I 2010-2012; II 2012-2017; III 2017-2022; IV 2022-2024).

In July 2023 the fourth Rutte cabinet collapsed over migration-policy disagreement. The 22 November 2023 Dutch parliamentary election produced Geert Wilders's PVV (Party for Freedom) as the largest single-party result. On 2 July 2024 Rutte stepped down as Prime Minister and was succeeded by Dick Schoof.

### NATO Secretary-Generalship
On 26 June 2024 Rutte was designated as NATO Secretary-General-designate following coordinated US-and-European subsequent consultation. On 1 October 2024 he took office as the 14th NATO Secretary-General, succeeding Jens Stoltenberg (who had served 2014-2024).

The 29 October 2024 first meeting between Rutte and von der Leyen produced the NATO-EU high-level task force on strategic partnership. The subsequent 2024-2026 coordinated NATO response to the continuing Russia-Ukraine war and to the subsequent Trump-administration NATO-policy position operated through Rutte's coordinated diplomatic engagement with US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Vice President JD Vance.

## Antonio Costa — European Council President (December 2024-)

António Luís Santos da Costa was born 17 July 1961 in Lisbon. His father Orlando da Costa was an Indo-Portuguese writer and Portuguese Communist Party member; his mother Maria Antónia Palla was a journalist. He studied Law at the University of Lisbon, graduating 1984, and was called to the Lisbon Bar in 1985. He joined the Portuguese Socialist Party in 1975.

His Portuguese political career included multiple ministerial positions across the António Guterres and José Sócrates governments and service as Mayor of Lisbon 2007-2015. From 2014 to 2024 he served as Secretary-General of the Socialist Party. From 26 November 2015 to 7 April 2024 he served as Prime Minister of Portugal — the longest-serving Portuguese Prime Minister since the 1974 Carnation Revolution.

On 7 November 2023 Costa resigned amid a criminal investigation that the 2024 court process established as having been based on identification confusion between Costa and a different António Costa Silva. No charges were filed against Costa. He was nonetheless designated by the European Council on 25 June 2024 as the next President of the European Council.

On 1 December 2024 Costa took office as President of the European Council for a 2.5-year term. The Costa Presidency has operated through the coordination of European Council meetings on the continuing Russia-Ukraine war, the post-7-October-2023 Gaza period, the Trump-administration trans-Atlantic-trade negotiations, and the 2025-2026 EU enlargement-and-Ukraine-accession architecture.

## Roberta Metsola — European Parliament President (January 2022-)

Roberta Tedesco Triccas was born 18 January 1979 in Malta. She studied Law at the University of Malta and European Law at the College of Europe in Bruges. She married Ukko Metsola, a Finnish national who was a former MEP. She is a member of the Maltese Nationalist Party (the centre-right governing-party tradition in Malta).

She has served as a Member of the European Parliament since 2013, sitting with the European People's Party (EPP). From 2020 to 2022 she served as First Vice-President of the European Parliament. Following the 11 January 2022 death of President David Sassoli, Metsola served as Acting President. On 18 January 2022 — her 43rd birthday — she was elected President of the European Parliament with 458 votes (56% of cast votes). She is the third woman to hold the office. On 16 July 2024 she was re-elected for a second term with 562 votes (78% of cast votes).

The 2024-2026 Metsola Presidency has operated through coordinated EU-Israel diplomatic positioning — including the pro-Israel-and-Gaza-military-operation positioning that has produced internal European Parliament tensions with left-of-centre groups including The Left, the Greens, and portions of the S&D group.

## The governing record

The contemporary European political-class architecture is the documented case of post-2010 post-eurozone-crisis post-Brexit post-2022-Ukraine-war integration of the European national governments, the European Commission, the European Council, the European Parliament, and NATO under the coordinated leadership of operators whose pre-political careers are documented as operating through the global financial-services architecture this volume's Volume One documented as the central operational structure of the contemporary global political economy.

The most documented single case is the 2016-2020 BlackRock chairmanship of Friedrich Merz immediately preceding the 2025 German Chancellorship. The documented secondary cases include the 1992-2002 Mark Rutte career at Unilever immediately preceding the 2010-2024 Dutch Prime Ministership; the 1985-2024 centrist-party careers of Donald Tusk and Antonio Costa since the post-1989 Polish and post-1974 Portuguese political-class architectures; the post-fascist political-class formation of Giorgia Meloni; the subsequent 16-year Hungarian-government tenure of Viktor Orbán operating on the 1989 Soros-Foundation Oxford-scholarship biography that the 2010-onward Orbán government's public discourse has elided; the CDU-political-class career of Ursula von der Leyen operating from the 1958-onward EEC-Director-General father-political-class background of Ernst Albrecht; and the Maltese centre-right political career of Roberta Metsola.

The story is the democratic, rules-based, post-1945 architecture that protects European citizens from continental-war catastrophe. The contemporary record says otherwise: coordinated US-aligned foreign-policy positioning through NATO under Rutte. Coordinated migration-and-asylum-enforcement architecture. Coordinated 2024-2026 EU support for the Israeli military operation in Gaza that the International Court of Justice, the International Criminal Court, and the academic-genocide-scholar consensus have characterised as a genocide. And post-2010 integration of European political-class operators with the global financial-services architecture documented in Volume One.

## Bridge to the Atlantic closing

The Atlantic section that runs from Volume Two Dossiers 1 through 20 — covering the contemporary American political class (Dossiers 1-12), the integrated American apparatus (Dossiers 13-17), and the contemporary Atlantic European political class (Dossiers 18-20) — closes here. The bridge to the Parts IV-XI regional dossiers of this volume operates through the single most documented observation of the 2024-2026 trans-Atlantic political class: the multi-institutional multi-operator integration of the contemporary Atlantic political-class architecture with the post-1945 financial-services-and-military-industrial-complex architecture that Volume One documented. The subsequent global picture of the contemporary political class — which the subsequent Parts IV through XI dossiers of this volume document — operates on the same architectural pattern but with regional variations that the regional dossiers establish.
