# Dossier 21: Narendra Modi

## The subject

Narendra Damodardas Modi has served as the 14th Prime Minister of India since 26 May 2014. He is the longest-serving Indian Prime Minister from outside the Indian National Congress family. His three consecutive electoral victories — May 2014 (BJP 282 seats / 31.0% of vote), May 2019 (BJP 303 seats / 37.4%), and June 2024 (BJP-led NDA 293 seats; BJP alone 240 seats / 36.6% — losing its parliamentary majority and requiring coalition support from the Telugu Desam Party of N. Chandrababu Naidu and the Janata Dal United of Nitish Kumar) — comprise the single most consequential electoral architecture in post-1989 Indian political-class history. He was Chief Minister of Gujarat 4,612 days (7 October 2001 to 22 May 2014) — the longest single tenure as Gujarat CM. He is a sworn member of the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) since age 8, a full-time RSS pracharak (campaigner) from 1971 to 1985, a BJP organiser from 1985, and the operational instrument of the Sangh Parivar apparatus covered in Dossier 23. He has institutional connections to the Adani Group commercial-and-financial architecture — covered in detail below — that comprise the single most documented case of post-1947 Indian political-class-and-corporate-oligarch integration in the contemporary documentary record.

The press calls him a tea-seller's son who rose by his own work. The self-made-reformer story has been the single most consequential element of his public image since 2014. The *Caravan* magazine's multi-year investigative reporting has documented multiple inconsistencies in the official tea-stall narrative. What the story conceals is three decades of documented evidence.

First: Gujarat origins and corporate integration. The 2002 Gujarat pogroms produced 1,044 deaths per official figures (three-quarters Muslim) and roughly 2,000 per independent human-rights-organisation estimates, with 230,000 displaced, during Modi's first six months as Gujarat CM. The 2005 US visa denial under the International Religious Freedom Act and its 2014 post-election restoration. The Adani Group relationship dating to the 1995 Mundra Port concession that produced Gautam Adani's 2022 status as Asia's richest person and the 20 November 2024 US criminal indictment for $250 million in alleged bribes to Indian government officials.

Second: centralisation and the Hindutva programme. The 8 November 2016 demonetisation that contracted Indian GDP by an estimated 1.5%-2% and returned 99.3% of demonetised cash to banks per the 2018 Reserve Bank of India annual report. The 5 August 2019 Article 370 abrogation. The December 2019 Citizenship Amendment Act explicitly excluding Muslims. The February 2020 Delhi riots that produced 53 deaths (an estimated 80% Muslim).

Third: domestic resistance and electoral signature. The 2020-2021 farmer protests that produced the largest single-day protest in human history per multiple estimates and the November 2021 repeal of the farm laws. The May 2023-onward Manipur ethnic conflict. The 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration as the central electoral product of the 2024 election campaign.

He is not a self-made reformer. He is the RSS apparatus's instrument — a sworn Sangh member since age 8, a full-time pracharak from 1971 to 1985 — operating in coordinated relationship with the Adani-led corporate-oligarch architecture.

## Biography

Modi was born 17 September 1950 in Vadnagar, Mehsana district, Bombay State (present-day Gujarat) to Damodardas Mulchand Modi and Heeraben Modi (1923-2022). The family was Gujarati of Ghanchi caste, classified as Other Backward Class (OBC) under the Indian reservation system. Modi has four brothers and one sister.

The official biography characterises his father as having run a tea stall at the Vadnagar railway station where the young Narendra reportedly helped serve tea. The characterisation has been contested as not corroborated by independent sources; the *Caravan* magazine's multi-year investigative reporting on Modi's biography has documented multiple inconsistencies in the official tea-stall narrative. The tea-seller's-son characterisation has functioned as the single most consequential biographical element of the post-2014 Modi public image regardless of its accuracy.

At age 18, in 1968, Modi married Jashodaben Chimanlal Modi in an arranged marriage. He abandoned her shortly after the marriage. He did not publicly disclose the marriage until April 2014 when he filed his nomination papers for the Vadodara Lok Sabha seat ahead of the May 2014 election — at which point his denial of being married across his preceding political career became a documented public concern. Jashodaben Modi has lived as an itinerant religious pilgrim across India in the post-1968 period and as a retired Gujarati schoolteacher; she has appeared in occasional press interviews characterising the abandonment as a permanent feature of her life.

Modi completed his higher secondary education at Vadnagar in 1967. His official biography records a Bachelor's degree in Political Science from the University of Delhi School of Open Learning (the correspondence-degree division) and a Master's degree in Political Science from Gujarat University in 1983 (taken externally). The authenticity of both degrees has been the subject of multi-party legal challenges; the 2016 Delhi University admission of application complications was the record point at which the degree-authenticity question entered the public domain. Modi's documented academic record remains contested in the legal and journalistic literature.

## RSS career 1958-1985

Modi was introduced to the RSS at age 8 through the Vadnagar shakha (RSS local training unit). He met Lakshmanrao Inamdar — the RSS organiser who became Modi's political mentor — at the shakha. He was inducted as a balswayamsevak (junior cadet) and rose progressively through the RSS hierarchy across the 1958-1985 period.

The 1967-1971 period of Modi's biography — during which he reportedly left his parental home and travelled in northern India visiting Hindu religious centres including the Ramakrishna Mission in Almora and Belur Math near Calcutta — has remained largely uncorroborated in independent sources. The official biography characterises this as the formative spiritual period of Modi's life. The journalistic and biographical literature has documented limited independent corroboration of the specific travels described.

In 1971 Modi returned to Gujarat and became a full-time pracharak (campaigner) for the RSS — the unmarried, unpaid, full-time organisational worker role at the centre of the RSS institutional structure. He served as pracharak across the 1971-1985 period in multiple Gujarat-based assignments.

During the 25 June 1975 to 21 March 1977 Indira Gandhi Emergency, Modi went into hiding and reportedly distributed anti-Emergency literature in disguise across the 21-month period. The 1975-1977 Emergency period operates in the post-2014 Modi public image as the single most consequential pre-political experience — the experience of having operated under authoritarian-government repression and emerged on the winning side of the 1977 Janata Party electoral defeat of Indira Gandhi.

## BJP career 1985-2001

In 1985 the RSS assigned Modi to the BJP. He served in the BJP across the 1985-2001 period in progressive organising roles. His 1987 Ahmedabad municipal election campaign — which produced the BJP's first major Gujarat urban electoral victory — established his operational reputation as a election organiser. In 1987 he was elected organising secretary of the BJP's Gujarat unit.

In 1990 Modi was a co-organiser of L.K. Advani's Somnath-to-Ayodhya rath yatra — the 10,000-kilometre Hindutva mobilisation campaign that was the decisive pre-1992 cumulative campaign for the Babri Masjid demolition. In 1991 Modi was a co-organiser of Murli Manohar Joshi's Ekta Yatra from Kanyakumari to Kashmir.

In 1995 Modi was appointed secretary of the BJP's national organisation in New Delhi. In 1998 he was appointed general secretary of the BJP national organisation. He held the general-secretary position across the 1998-2001 period — the period in which the BJP-led NDA government under PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee operated.

## Gujarat Chief Minister 2001-2014

The 26 January 2001 Bhuj earthquake in Gujarat killed roughly 20,000 people. The management failures of the incumbent Gujarat CM Keshubhai Patel produced the political conditions for leadership change. On 3 October 2001 Modi replaced Patel as Chief Minister of Gujarat. He was sworn in on 7 October 2001 — his first elected office in 34 years of political organising. He was elected to the Gujarat Legislative Assembly on 24 February 2002 through a by-election in the Rajkot II constituency (defeating INC candidate Ashwin Mehta) — his first electoral victory.

### The 2002 Gujarat pogroms

The 27 February 2002 Sabarmati Express fire at Godhra station in eastern Gujarat killed 59 Hindu pilgrims returning from Ayodhya. The cause of the fire remains contested in the legal and historiographical record — the 2008 Banerjee Committee report characterised the fire as accidental; the 2011 Nanavati-Mehta Commission characterised the fire as arson by a Muslim mob. The factual record on the cause of the fire remains contested.

The 28 February - early May 2002 anti-Muslim pogroms across Gujarat was the event. Official casualty figures: 1,044 dead — three-quarters Muslim — per the Gujarat government record. Independent human-rights-organisation estimates: roughly 2,000 dead, mostly Muslim, with many others raped, mutilated, or both, per the Concerned Citizens Tribunal report (chaired by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and P.B. Sawant) and the Human Rights Watch 2002 report. Roughly 230,000 displaced.

The Modi-government response operated across three documented elements. First, the delayed deployment of Gujarat State Reserve Police and delayed request for Indian Army deployment. Second, the characterisation of the pogroms in Modi's public statements as a "natural reaction" to the Godhra fire — a characterisation that the National Human Rights Commission, the multiple state and national investigative commissions, and the international observer organisations characterised as incompatible with the Chief Ministerial duty of equal protection of all citizens. Third, the multi-year institutional protection of the Gujarat police officers and BJP-VHP-Bajrang Dal organisers documented in the post-pogroms investigations.

The 2002 US State Department International Religious Freedom Report criticised the Modi-government response. In March 2005 the US State Department denied Modi a diplomatic visa and revoked his existing tourist/business visa under the International Religious Freedom Act — making him the single most individual to whom the US government had applied the IRFA visa-denial provision in the 1998-2014 period.

The multi-year Special Investigation Team (SIT) investigation under the Indian Supreme Court culminated in the 2012 SIT "clean chit" finding no prosecutable evidence against Modi. The 24 June 2022 Indian Supreme Court rejection of the Zakia Jafri petition against the SIT clean chit closed the formal legal avenue for Modi prosecution on the 2002 pogroms. The 24 June 2022 Supreme Court ruling was followed within 24 hours by the arrest of human-rights activist Teesta Setalvad (who had supported the Jafri petition through her Citizens for Justice and Peace organisation) by the Gujarat Anti-Terrorism Squad. The September 2022 Indian Supreme Court granted Setalvad interim bail. The arrest pattern was widely characterised in the international human-rights literature as retaliation for the Jafri petition.

In 2014 the US State Department restored Modi's visa following his May 2014 Prime Ministerial election. The post-2014 US-India diplomatic relationship under successive Obama, Trump, Biden, and second Trump administrations operated through the multi-administration accommodation of the post-pogroms Modi premiership.

### Subsequent Gujarat CM terms

Modi won subsequent Gujarat state assembly elections in December 2002 (127 of 182 seats), December 2007 (117 seats), and December 2012 (115 seats). The 2007 Vibrant Gujarat investment summit produced reported real-estate investment deals worth ₹6.6 trillion (roughly $210 billion in 2023 equivalent). The Gujarat agricultural growth rate of 9.6% per annum across 2001-2007 and the 10.97% compound annual growth rate across 2001-2010 were the highest of any Indian state across the period. The 2008 500,000 infrastructure structures (including 113,738 check dams) and the Bt cotton production expansion produced the agricultural-and-irrigation-and-export record that the 2014 Modi national campaign operated on.

The 2012 Comptroller and Auditor General of India report accused the Modi government of giving low-cost fuel from the Gujarat state-run gas company (Gujarat State Petroleum Corporation) to Adani and other businesspeople. The CAG report was the single most contemporaneous documented complaint of crony-capitalist allocation in the Modi Gujarat CM tenure.

## Prime Minister 2014-

### 2014 election
The 16 May 2014 general election produced the BJP's 282-seat majority on 31.0% of the popular vote — the first single-party parliamentary majority since 1984. The 2014 campaign operated on three documented elements: the anti-corruption narrative against the outgoing Manmohan Singh UPA-II coalition (which had been damaged by subsequent corruption scandals including the 2G spectrum scandal and the Coalgate scandal); the pro-economic-growth Gujarat-development-model characterisation; and the Hindutva mobilisation through the campaign. Modi was sworn in as the 14th PM on 26 May 2014.

### 2019 election
The 23 May 2019 general election produced the BJP's 303-seat majority on 37.4% of the popular vote. The 2019 campaign operated on the post-Pulwama (14 February 2019 Pulwama attack killing 40 CRPF personnel) and post-Balakot (26 February 2019 Indian Air Force strikes on Balakot, Pakistan) national-security messaging. Modi was sworn in for his second term on 30 May 2019.

### 2024 election
The 4 June 2024 general election produced the BJP-led NDA's 293 seats; the BJP alone fell to 240 seats — a loss of 63 seats relative to 2019 and the loss of the parliamentary majority for the BJP. The coalition support from the Telugu Desam Party (Naidu) and the Janata Dal United (Nitish Kumar) produced the post-2024 NDA government. Modi was sworn in for his third term on 9 June 2024.

The reduced 2024 majority was the single most consequential constraint on Modi's third-term capacity for major constitutional-and-statutory legislative action. The coalition partners' parliamentary leverage produced negotiations on cabinet positions, state-level concessions, and major-policy positions.

## The Adani relationship

The single most institutional pattern of the post-2001 Modi political-class operation is the relationship between Modi and Gautam Adani (born 24 June 1962, Ahmedabad). The origin of the relationship dates to the early 1990s Gujarat commercial-and-political networking environment. The 1994-1995 Mundra Port concession — when the Government of Gujarat announced managerial outsourcing of Mundra Port in 1994 and Adani obtained the contract in 1995 — proved the decisive pre-2001 commercial event in the Adani Group expansion.

The 2002 Gujarat pogroms produced the Adani public defence of Modi as one of the few Indian businessmen to publicly support Modi against the pogroms-related criticism. The 2003 Resurgent Group of Gujarat (RGG) — founded by Adani as the rival to the Confederation of Indian Industry (CII) after the CII leaders criticised Modi's 2002 management — operated as the commercial-political vehicle through which the Adani-Modi relationship was institutionalised.

### Adani Group expansion under the Modi premiership

The 2014-2024 Adani Group expansion across multiple economic sectors was the single most documented case of post-2001 corporate-oligarch expansion in the contemporary Indian economy. The Adani Group expansion included Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Limited (APSEZ) — the largest private multi-port operator in India with Mundra Port capacity of 210 million tons of cargo per annum; Adani Power — the largest private thermal power producer in India with thermal capacity of 4,620 megawatts; Adani Green Energy — the $6 billion 2020 solar bid won from the Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI); Adani Total Gas; Adani Enterprises; Adani Transmission; and Adani Wilmar.

In February 2022 Adani became Asia's richest person, surpassing Mukesh Ambani of Reliance Industries. In August 2022 Adani was named the third-richest person in the world by Fortune.

In August 2022 the Adani Group's AMG Media Networks Limited (AMNL) acquired Radhika Roy and Prannoy Roy's RRPR Holding (owner of 29.18% of NDTV) without the founders' consent or notice. The open offer for an additional 26% produced the Adani controlling shareholding by December 2022. The Economist characterised the post-acquisition NDTV: "Before Adani bought NDTV, the news channel was 'critical of the government but is now supine.'" The NDTV acquisition was the decisive event in the contemporary Indian press-environment consolidation under the corporate-oligarch architecture.

The 2022-2024 Adani Group international expansion covered ten countries. Stable projects: Australia (the Carmichael coal mine), Bangladesh (the Godda thermal power project), Greece, Indonesia, Israel (the Haifa port acquisition in January 2023), Nepal, and Tanzania (the Bagamoyo and Dar es Salaam port concessions). Reversed or contested projects: Kenya (the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport project, subsequently cancelled following the November 2024 US indictment); Myanmar (the Yangon port project, subsequently sold for $30 million in May 2023 after the 2021 Myanmar military coup made the original $195 million investment commercially untenable); and Sri Lanka (the $440 million wind power project subsequently reconsidered under the Dissanayake NPP government dossiered in Dossier 29).

### Hindenburg report

On 24 January 2023 the US short-selling firm Hindenburg Research published a 100-page report alleging "the largest con in corporate history" by the Adani Group — stock manipulation, accounting fraud, and shell-company architecture across operations. The Hindenburg report wiped roughly $125 billion in Adani Group market capitalisation across 2023.

The January 2024 Indian Supreme Court ruling resolved the Adani Group stocks dispute and dismissed requests for a Special Investigation Team (SIT) or Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) investigation. The SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) investigation produced limited findings against the Adani Group. The multi-year Indian regulatory-institutional response to the Hindenburg allegations remained the single most documented case of post-2014 Indian regulatory-institutional accommodation of the Adani Group.

### US criminal indictment

On 20 November 2024 a five-count criminal indictment by the US Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn) charged Gautam Adani, his nephew Sagar Adani, and associates with five criminal counts of bribery, conspiracy to violate the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), conspiracy to commit securities fraud, and obstruction of justice. The allegation: Adani paid over $250 million in bribes to Indian government officials to secure favourable energy contracts in Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jammu and Kashmir, and Tamil Nadu.

The parallel SEC civil enforcement action operated alongside the criminal indictment. The November 2024 $12 billion drop in Adani's net worth following the indictment was the largest single-event drop in any contemporary Indian businessperson's net worth.

The Kenyan government cancelled over $2.5 billion in Adani-Group projects (including the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport concession) following the November 2024 indictment. The Sri Lankan government under President Anura Kumara Dissanayake announced reconsideration of the $440 million wind power project.

On 14 May 2026 the New York Times reported that the US Department of Justice preparing to drop the criminal charges against Gautam Adani and Sagar Adani. The representations to the DOJ were led by Robert J. Giuffra Jr — a Sullivan & Cromwell partner and personal lawyer to US President Donald Trump. The pledge by Adani after Trump's 2024 election victory to invest $10 billion in the US economy and create 15,000 US jobs was a documented element of the representations. The expected dismissal is "with prejudice" — preventing the case from being reopened. The parallel Treasury Department investigation relating to the import of Iranian LPG by a vessel linked to the group is expected to be resolved through a settlement with financial penalties without admissions of wrongdoing.

The 14 May 2026 New York Times reporting was the single most documented case of post-2024 US-administration accommodation of foreign-government-and-corporate-oligarch interests in exchange for post-election US-economy investment commitments. The cross-reference to Volume Two Dossier 2 (Trump) and Dossier 16 (DOGE / Musk / Vance / Ramaswamy) is the same architectural pattern this volume's preceding dossiers have established.

## Modi government policy record

### Demonetisation (8 November 2016)
On 8 November 2016 Modi announced in a televised 8 PM address the demonetisation of ₹500 and ₹1,000 currency notes — roughly 86% of the currency in circulation by value. The announced justification was the detection of unaccounted "black money" and the reduction of counterfactual cash-based corruption. The 2018 Reserve Bank of India annual report established that 99.3% of the demonetised currency was deposited back into banks — directly contradicting the original justification.

The 2016-2017 demonetisation period produced an estimated 1.5%-2% GDP contraction per multiple post-2016 economic analyses. The small-business sector and the agricultural sector experienced particularly severe disruption. The Indian informal-cash-economy was the single most affected sector.

Demonetisation is the single most documented case of post-2014 Modi government policy that the original publicly stated objective was demonstrably not met by the policy. The multi-year public characterisation of demonetisation as a success by the Modi government and the aligned press environment dossiered in Dossier 23 operated against the documented RBI cash-return record.

### Goods and Services Tax (1 July 2017)
The 1 July 2017 nationwide implementation of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) under the 101st Constitutional Amendment unified the post-1947 indirect-tax architecture. The transition disruption to the MSME sector produced the 2017-2018 small-business cumulative damage. The 2024 cumulative GST revenue collection of ₹20.18 lakh crore was the single largest tax-revenue stream of the contemporary Indian state.

### Article 370 abrogation (5 August 2019)
On 5 August 2019 the Modi government revoked the special-autonomy status of Jammu and Kashmir under Article 370 of the Indian Constitution and bifurcated the state into two Union Territories (Jammu and Kashmir; Ladakh). The internet shutdowns and political detentions of Mehbooba Mufti, Omar Abdullah, and multiple Kashmiri political-class operators produced the most documented post-1947 internal-political-class repression in any Indian state. The 11 December 2023 Indian Supreme Court ruling upheld the abrogation.

### Citizenship Amendment Act (11-12 December 2019)
The CAA — passed by Lok Sabha on 11 December 2019 and Rajya Sabha on 12 December 2019 with presidential assent the same day — granted accelerated citizenship to Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, and Christian refugees from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh. The CAA explicitly excluded Muslims — the single most documented case of post-1947 religious discrimination in Indian statutory law.

The December 2019 - March 2020 anti-CAA-and-NRC protests across India produced the largest popular mobilisation against a single Indian government legislative act in the post-1947 period. The 23-26 February 2020 Delhi riots — during the US President Donald Trump's first-term state visit to India — produced 53 deaths (an estimated 80% Muslim) in the single most violent communal incident in Delhi since 1984.

### The farm laws (September 2020 - November 2021)
In September 2020 the Modi government passed three farm laws: the Farmers' Produce Trade and Commerce Act; the Farmers Empowerment and Protection Agreement on Price Assurance Act; and the Essential Commodities Amendment Act. The laws operated to deregulate agricultural produce-trading-and-pricing arrangements that had operated under the post-1965 Minimum Support Price (MSP) and Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMC) framework.

The 2020-2021 farmer protests — concentrated in Punjab and Haryana but extending across multiple states — was the single largest sustained agricultural mobilisation in post-1947 Indian history. The 26 November 2020 Bharat Bandh (nationwide strike) produced an estimated 250 million participants — characterised by multiple international observers as the largest single-day protest in human history. The protests at the Delhi borders included winter encampments of tens of thousands of farmers.

On 19 November 2021 Modi announced the repeal of the farm laws — the single most documented case of post-2014 Modi government policy reversal under popular-mobilisation pressure. The 29 November 2021 Farm Laws Repeal Act formalised the repeal. The 2024 new farmer protests at the Shambhu border between Punjab and Haryana was the continuation of the unresolved MSP-and-agricultural-distress structural conditions documented in the Part IV historical chapter (300,000+ farmer suicides 1995-2020 per National Crime Records Bureau).

### Manipur (May 2023 onward)
The Manipur ethnic conflict between the Meitei (Hindu-majority, valley-dwelling) and Kuki-Zo (largely Christian, hill-dwelling) communities began on 3 May 2023 and continued at the date of writing. Casualties: an estimated 250+ deaths; an estimated 60,000+ displaced. The Modi government failure to visit Manipur and the visible refusal to intervene remained the single most documented case of post-2014 Modi government abandonment of a state-level political-and-humanitarian crisis. Modi did not visit Manipur until 3 September 2025 — over 28 months after the conflict began.

### Ram Mandir consecration (22 January 2024)
On 22 January 2024 Modi performed the pran pratishtha (consecration) of the Ram Mandir at Ayodhya — built on the site of the 1992 demolished Babri Masjid (covered in the Part IV historical chapter). The consecration functioned as the central electoral product of the 2024 election campaign. The 4 June 2024 Faizabad constituency (which includes Ayodhya) electoral result — the BJP lost the Faizabad seat to the Samajwadi Party candidate — was the single most documented electoral repudiation of the Modi-government Ram Mandir-and-Hindutva central campaign theme.

## Press environment under Modi

The 2024 Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index ranked India 159th of 180. The 2014-2024 multi-year decline in Indian press-freedom rankings (from 140th in 2014 to 159th in 2024) was the single most documented case of post-2014 structural press-environment degradation in any G20 democracy.

The 2017-2023 multi-year pattern of tax-and-CBI-and-Enforcement-Directorate raids and investigations against independent-press operators — including Newslaundry, The Wire, Caravan, Scroll, Frontline, and NewsClick — was the single most documented case of post-2014 state-instrument pressure on independent journalism in any G20 democracy.

The August 2022 Adani acquisition of NDTV (covered above) ended the post-1988 NDTV critical-of-government editorial position and consolidated the contemporary Indian major-news-channel sector under corporate-oligarch ownership aligned with the BJP government.

## The institutional assessment

The single most institutional pattern of the post-2001 Modi political-class operation is the coordinated integration of the RSS apparatus dossiered in Dossier 23, the corporate-oligarch architecture led by the Adani Group, the post-2014 captured-press environment documented in the 2024 Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index ranking, and the post-2002-pogroms-and-2014-visa-restoration trans-Atlantic political-class accommodation of the Modi premiership.

The record is the record. The tea-seller's son is the RSS apparatus's instrument operating in coordinated relationship with the Adani-led corporate-oligarch architecture and the post-1991 IMF-conditioned LPG framework. The 2002 Gujarat pogroms with 1,044-to-2,000 deaths and 230,000 displaced; the 2005 US visa denial and 2014 restoration; the 1995-onward Adani Mundra Port concession; the 2002 Adani public defence; the 2003 Resurgent Group of Gujarat; the 2014-2024 Adani Group multi-sector expansion; the August 2022 NDTV acquisition; the January 2023 Hindenburg report and $125 billion market-cap loss; the January 2024 Indian Supreme Court resolution; the 20 November 2024 US criminal indictment for $250 million in alleged bribes; the 14 May 2026 reported DOJ dropping of charges following Robert J. Giuffra Jr (Sullivan & Cromwell partner and Trump personal lawyer) representations and $10 billion US investment pledge; the 8 November 2016 demonetisation that contracted GDP by 1.5%-2% and returned 99.3% of cash to banks; the 5 August 2019 Article 370 abrogation; the 11-12 December 2019 CAA; the 23-26 February 2020 Delhi riots producing 53 deaths during Trump's first-term state visit; the 2020-2021 farmer protests culminating in the 19 November 2021 repeal of farm laws; the May 2023-onward Manipur conflict with 250+ deaths and 60,000+ displaced; the 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration; the 4 June 2024 BJP-only 240-seat loss of parliamentary majority.

## Bridge to Dossier 22

The single most operational lieutenant of Modi across the 2001-onward period has been Amit Shah — the 2001-2014 Modi-Gujarat-government Home Minister, the 2014-2024 BJP President / Union Home Minister, and the central operator of the post-2014 BJP electoral architecture, the post-2019 Article 370 abrogation, the post-2019 CAA-NRC architecture, and the post-2024 Modi third-term Union Home Ministry. Dossier 22 documents Shah.

---


---

# Dossier 22: Amit Shah

## The subject

Amit Anilchandra Shah has served as the 32nd Minister of Home Affairs of India since 1 June 2019 — the longest-serving holder of the Home Ministry portfolio in Indian history. He has additionally served as the first Minister of Cooperation since July 2021. He served as the 10th President of the BJP from 9 July 2014 to 20 January 2020 — the youngest BJP President at 49 — and as Chairman of the NDA since 2014. He was MLA for Sarkhej 1997-2012 and Naranpura 2012-2017, Rajya Sabha member for Gujarat 2017-2019, and Member of Parliament for Gandhinagar (Gujarat) since 17 June 2019. He served as Minister of State for twelve portfolios in the Modi Gujarat government 2002-2012 — Home, Law and Justice, Prison, Border Security, Civil Defence, Excise, Home Guards, Transport, Prohibition, Gram Rakshak Dal, Police Housing, and Legislative and Parliamentary Affairs. He has been characterised throughout the post-2014 Indian and international press as "the second most powerful man in India" and as Modi's "alter ego," "right-hand man," "chief strategist," and "potential successor."

The press calls him BJP's chief electoral strategist and Modi's alter ego. What the characterisation omits: the 25 July 2010 CBI arrest on charges of murder, extortion, kidnapping, criminal conspiracy, and destruction of evidence in connection with the 26 November 2005 fake-encounter killings of Sohrabuddin Sheikh and his wife Kausar Bi and the 28 December 2006 fake-encounter killing of witness Tulsiram Prajapati. The October 2010 Justice Aftab Alam order barring Shah from entering Gujarat 2010-2012. The September 2012 Supreme Court return order. The 30 December 2014 Special CBI Court clean chit by Special Judge MB Gosavi. The September 2013 D.G. Vanzara letter from prison accusing the Modi-Shah Gujarat government of ordering the fake encounters. The 1,200-encounter-cases-nationally and 19-in-Gujarat documented ratio during Shah's tenure as Gujarat Home Minister.

The international dimension: the 14 October 2024 Canadian expulsion of six Indian diplomats including the Indian High Commissioner over the 18 June 2023 killing of Canadian Sikh activist Hardeep Singh Nijjar in Surrey, British Columbia. The 29 October 2024 Washington Post-reported and Canadian-government-confirmed allegation that **Shah was the orchestrator** of the campaign of violence, intimidation, and intelligence-gathering targeting Sikh separatists in Canada. The parallel November 2023 US Department of Justice indictment of Indian RAW operative Nikhil Gupta in the foiled assassination plot against Gurpatwant Singh Pannun in New York and the October 2024 US indictment of RAW officer Vikash Yadav.

Chief electoral strategist. Identified by a G7 government as the orchestrator of an assassination campaign on foreign soil.

## Biography

Shah was born 22 October 1964 in Bombay (now Mumbai), Maharashtra. His family is Gujarati Hindu Bania caste; his great-grandfather was the Nagarseth (town headsman) of the small princely state of Mansa in Gujarat. His father Anilchandra Shah operated a PVC pipe business in Mansa. His mother Kusumben Shah died from illness on 8 June 2010 — six weeks before Shah's 25 July 2010 CBI arrest.

Shah attended school in Mehsana and moved to Ahmedabad for his higher education. He earned a Bachelor of Science (BSc) in biochemistry from CU Shah Science College, Ahmedabad. He worked in his father's PVC pipe business after graduation and as a stockbroker and in cooperative banks in Ahmedabad — the documentary basis of his subsequent operational mastery of the Gujarat cooperative-banking sector that the 1999 Ahmedabad District Cooperative Bank presidency capture institutionalised.

Shah married Sonal Shah. Their son Jay Shah served as Secretary of the Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) from October 2019 to October 2024 and was elected Chairman of the International Cricket Council from 1 December 2024 — the post in world cricket governance. The subsequent governance complaints about the consolidation of Indian and world cricket administration under the Shah family are the evidence. Shah has six sisters (two of whom live in Chicago).

## RSS and BJP career 1977-1997

Shah's political involvement began at age 13 in 1977 when he pasted election posters for Maniben Patel — the daughter of Vallabhbhai Patel and Jan Sangh candidate from the Mehsana Lok Sabha seat during the post-Emergency Janata-wave general election. In 1980, at age 16, he joined the RSS as a Swayamsevak in Ahmedabad — a later entry into the RSS than Modi's age-8 entry documented in Dossier 21. In 1982 he was made joint secretary of the Gujarat unit of the Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP — the RSS student wing). In 1984 he served as a BJP polling agent for the Sanghvi booth in Narayanpur ward. In 1987 he joined the Yuva Morcha (youth wing) of the BJP. In 1989-1990 he served as the Ahmedabad coordinator for the Ram Janmabhoomi mobilisation and Advani's 1990 Somnath-to-Ayodhya rath yatra co-organised with Modi (covered in Dossier 21).

## Gujarat political career 1995-2014

### The Modi-Shah 1995-onward Gujarat rural-and-cooperative-banking strategy

In 1995 the BJP formed its first government in Gujarat under Chief Minister Keshubhai Patel. From the 1995-onward period, Modi and Shah jointly developed and operated the strategy that produced the documented elimination of Indian National Congress influence in rural Gujarat. The strategy operated through the identification of the second-most-influential figure in each Gujarat village (typically the rural leader who had lost the pradhan / village-chief election to the Congress-aligned incumbent) and the recruitment of these 8,000-plus rural leaders to the BJP. The same strategy was applied across the Gujarat cooperative-banking sector — the central architecture of rural Gujarat agricultural finance — producing the multi-year capture of the Gujarat cooperative-banking architecture by BJP-aligned operators.

In 1997 Shah won his first election from the Sarkhej by-election to the Gujarat Legislative Assembly. He retained Sarkhej in the 1998 Gujarat Legislative Assembly election (which the BJP won under Keshubhai Patel), the 2002 election (under the post-pogroms Modi government), and the 2007 election. In 1999 he was elected President of the Ahmedabad District Cooperative Bank (ADCB) — the largest cooperative bank in India and the anchor of the Gujarat cooperative-banking sector. The 1999 ADCB presidency was the decisive event in the Modi-Shah Gujarat cooperative-banking capture documented above. In 1999 he was also made State Vice-President of the Gujarat BJP.

### Twelve Modi-government portfolios 2002-2012

In 2002, after Modi's 7 October 2001 elevation to Gujarat CM and the post-2002 Gujarat Assembly elections, Shah was sworn in as Minister of State in the Modi Gujarat government with twelve portfolios: Home, Law and Justice, Prison, Border Security, Civil Defence, Excise, Home Guards, Transport, Prohibition, Gram Rakshak Dal, Police Housing, and Legislative and Parliamentary Affairs. The Home portfolio remained the single most consequential of the twelve — Shah's tenure as Gujarat Home Minister 2002-2010 produced the documented Sohrabuddin Sheikh fake-encounter case covered below.

In 2006 Shah was elected President of the Gujarat State Chess Association. In 2009 he was appointed President of the Ahmedabad Central Board of Cricket Association and Vice-President of the Gujarat State Cricket Association — the pre-Jay-Shah-BCCI institutional record of Shah-family operation of Gujarat sporting administration.

### The Sohrabuddin Sheikh fake-encounter case

On 26 November 2005 Sohrabuddin Sheikh and his wife Kausar Bi were killed by Gujarat Anti-Terrorism Squad (ATS) and Rajasthan Police officers near Ahmedabad in a reported "encounter" that subsequent investigation established as a fake encounter. Sohrabuddin Sheikh had been an alleged extortionist operating in the Rajasthan-Gujarat marble-trading industry with alleged arms-racketeering and Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) links. The Gujarat Police and Rajasthan Police characterisation of the killing as an encounter with armed militants was contradicted by subsequent forensic and witness evidence.

On 28 December 2006 Tulsiram Prajapati — a witness to the Sohrabuddin Sheikh killing — was killed by Gujarat Police in a second "fake encounter" near Chapri in Banaskantha district of Gujarat. The pattern of the witness killing was the decisive evidentiary basis for the CBI investigation that produced the 25 July 2010 arrest of Shah.

On 25 July 2010 Shah was arrested by the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI). He was charged with murder, extortion, kidnapping, criminal conspiracy, and destruction of evidence; he was charged under the Arms Act. The arrest was approved by then-CBI Director Ashwani Kumar. The BJP-aligned subsequent commentary (covered in the sources file) characterised the arrest as politically motivated by the then-governing Indian National Congress-led UPA government under PM Manmohan Singh.

In October 2010, after roughly three months in custody, Shah was granted bail by the Gujarat High Court. The court observation: "no prima facie case against Amit Shah." However, Supreme Court Justice Aftab Alam in a special hearing at his residence barred Shah from entering Gujarat — an order that operated across the 2010-2012 period and that the subsequent BJP commentary characterised as Shah's "exile" from his home state. In September 2012 the Indian Supreme Court permitted Shah to return to Gujarat.

In September 2013 D.G. Vanzara — former Gujarat ATS officer in jail at the time on charges related to the fake encounters — wrote a multi-page letter to the Gujarat government accusing the Modi-Shah Gujarat government of having ordered the fake encounters and of ensuring that police officers remained in prison to protect the political principals. The Vanzara letter is the single most documented insider account of the Modi-Shah Gujarat extrajudicial-killing apparatus operated through the Gujarat ATS.

On 30 December 2014 — six months after the Modi May 2014 election and six months into the post-2014 BJP central-government tenure — the Special CBI Court under Special Judge MB Gosavi gave Shah a "clean chit," finding insufficient evidence to put him on trial. The court characterisation: "all these actions were politically motivated." The 2014 clean chit proved the decisive legal closure of the Sohrabuddin case against Shah. The December 2017 death of Justice B.H. Loya — the CBI court judge who had handled the Sohrabuddin case before Gosavi — produced multi-year journalistic investigation by *The Caravan* and the independent press into the circumstances of the Loya death, with continuing factual disputes at the date of writing.

The contextual figure provided by the BJP-aligned subsequent commentary on the case: during Shah's tenure as Gujarat Home Minister, 1,200 encounter cases were recorded nationally and 19 in Gujarat; the CBI / Congress UPA government selected all 19 Gujarat cases and one of the remaining 1,189 for investigation. The BJP commentary characterises this as politically targeted; the counter-position characterises this as reflective of the disproportionate concentration of fake encounters in Gujarat under the Modi-Shah administration. The factual record on the Gujarat-versus-nationally encounter ratio is contested in the literature.

The pattern documented across the case — the 26 November 2005 Sohrabuddin killing, the 28 December 2006 Tulsiram Prajapati witness killing, the 2010 CBI arrest, the 2010-2012 Gujarat exile, the 2012 SC return, the 2013 Vanzara letter, the 2014 clean chit, and the 2017 Loya-death journalistic investigation — comprises the single most documented case of post-2001 Indian state-instrument extrajudicial-killing apparatus operating under a state-level Home Ministry.

## National BJP career 2013-2019

In 2013 Shah was appointed BJP National General Secretary in charge of Uttar Pradesh — the single most populous and politically consequential Indian state. The 2014 BJP-NDA UP electoral architecture under Shah's direction produced the 73-of-80 UP Lok Sabha seats for the BJP-NDA — the central electoral product of the 2014 national BJP victory documented in Dossier 21.

On 9 July 2014 Shah was appointed BJP National President at age 49 — the youngest BJP President in the party's history. He was re-elected on 24 January 2016. Under his presidency, BJP-led governments were formed for the first time in Maharashtra, Haryana, Jammu and Kashmir, Jharkhand, and Assam across the 2014-2016 period and in Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Manipur, and Gujarat across the 2017 period. The BJP losses in Punjab (2017), Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan, and Madhya Pradesh (2018) was the counter-record of the Shah-led BJP electoral architecture. He was simultaneously NDA Chairman across the 2014-onward period.

In 2016 Shah was appointed trustee of the Somnath Temple Trust — alongside PM Modi and L.K. Advani. The Somnath trusteeship was the decisive public characterisation of the post-2014 BJP-RSS-and-temple-trust apparatus covered in Dossier 23.

## Home Minister 2019-

On 23 May 2019 the BJP won 303 seats / 37.4% of vote in the second-term Modi election. On 30 May 2019 Shah took oath as Cabinet Minister. On 1 June 2019 he took office as the 35th Minister of Home Affairs (32nd by post-1947 numbering). On 17 June 2019 he took oath as Member of Lok Sabha from Gandhinagar (Gujarat) — the constituency previously held by L.K. Advani — with a margin of 557,014 votes / 70% vote share. In July 2021 he became the first Minister of Cooperation in the new Ministry of Cooperation created by Modi.

### Article 370 abrogation

On 5 August 2019 Shah moved the resolution in the Rajya Sabha to scrap Article 370 of the Indian Constitution. The simultaneous Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Bill bifurcated the state into two Union Territories — Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh. The internet shutdowns, communications blackouts, and political detentions of Mehbooba Mufti, Omar Abdullah, Farooq Abdullah, and multiple Kashmiri political-class operators produced the single most documented post-1947 internal-political-class repression in any Indian state.

The 11 December 2023 Indian Supreme Court ruling upheld the abrogation. The 27 March 2024 Shah public characterisation that the central government would contemplate repealing the Armed Forces Special Powers Act (AFSPA) in Jammu and Kashmir functioned as the pre-2024-election concession; the post-2024 policy did not materialise.

### CAA-NRC architecture

On 11 December 2019 Shah moved the Citizenship Amendment Bill in the Lok Sabha; it passed the same day. On 12 December 2019 it passed the Rajya Sabha; presidential assent the same day. The CAA granted accelerated citizenship to Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh — explicitly excluding Muslims. The single most documented case of post-1947 religious discrimination in Indian statutory law (covered in Dossier 21).

On 19 November 2019 Shah declared in the Rajya Sabha that the National Register of Citizens (NRC) would be implemented nationwide. The NRC has been implemented only in Assam as of 2025; the NRC was removed from the BJP's 2024 election manifesto.

The December 2019 - March 2020 nationwide anti-CAA-and-NRC protests produced the largest popular mobilisation against a single Indian government legislative act in the post-1947 period (covered in Dossier 21). The 23-26 February 2020 Delhi riots — during US President Donald Trump's first-term state visit to India — produced 53 deaths (an estimated 80% Muslim) in the single most violent communal incident in Delhi since 1984. Shah's Home Ministry oversaw the Delhi Police during the riots; the police response to the pogroms-style violence was characterised in subsequent investigative reporting by *The Wire*, *Caravan*, *Newslaundry*, and international observers as actively complicit in the anti-Muslim violence.

### Other policy record

In September 2019 Shah publicly called for Hindi as the unifying national language. The position generated subsequent opposition particularly in southern Indian states (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana) where Tamil, Kannada, Malayalam, Telugu, and other non-Hindi linguistic communities operate. The post-2019 Hindi-imposition characterisation has been a multi-year flashpoint in Indian federal-language politics.

From May 2023 the Manipur ethnic conflict between Meitei (Hindu-majority, valley-dwelling) and Kuki-Zo (largely Christian, hill-dwelling) communities began (covered in Dossier 21). Shah visited Manipur in late May 2023 — one of the few Union government visits during the multi-year conflict. Estimated 250+ deaths and 60,000+ displaced. Modi did not visit Manipur until 3 September 2025 — over 28 months after the conflict began. The Shah Home Ministry has been characterised in subsequent investigative reporting as actively complicit in the failure of central-government response to the conflict.

### 2024 Lok Sabha and third-term Home Ministry

The 4 June 2024 general election produced the BJP-led NDA's 293 seats; the BJP alone fell to 240 seats — loss of the parliamentary majority. On 9 June 2024 Modi was sworn in for the third term; Shah retained the Home Ministry.

## The Canadian and US allegations on Sikh-separatist plots

### The Hardeep Singh Nijjar killing

On 18 June 2023 Hardeep Singh Nijjar — a 45-year-old Canadian citizen, leader of the Khalistan Tiger Force, and designated terrorist by the Indian government — was shot dead in his pickup truck outside the Guru Nanak Sikh Gurdwara in Surrey, British Columbia, which he led. Nijjar had been an active leader in the contemporary Khalistan-independence movement seeking the creation of a separate Sikh-majority homeland in Punjab.

On 18 September 2023 Canadian PM Justin Trudeau publicly characterised "credible allegations" of Indian government agents' involvement in the Nijjar killing. The Indian government rejected the allegations as "absurd" and "baseless." In May 2024 four Indian nationals living in Canada were charged with Nijjar's murder; the trial is pending as of late 2024.

### The 14 October 2024 Canadian expulsion

On 14 October 2024 the Canadian government expelled six Indian diplomats — including the Indian High Commissioner Sanjay Kumar Verma and five other diplomats — characterising them as "persons of interest" in multiple cases of coercion, intimidation, and violence aimed at quieting the Khalistan-independence campaign in Canada. India ordered the reciprocal expulsion of Canadian diplomats. The October 2024 expulsion was the single most consequential post-1947 Canadian diplomatic action against a foreign government on Canadian soil.

The Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) Commissioner Mike Duheme and Deputy Commissioner Brigitte Gauvin (intelligence) gave public testimony connecting the Indian government operations in Canada to the Bishnoi gang (a transnational criminal organisation under Lawrence Bishnoi, then imprisoned in India). RCMP characterisation of the linkage: a documented pattern of homicides, assassination plots, coercion, and other violent crimes in Canada operated through the Bishnoi network on behalf of Indian state actors.

### The Shah identification

On 29 October 2024 *The Washington Post* reported that a Canadian official had identified Amit Shah as the orchestrator of the campaign of violence, intimidation, and intelligence-gathering targeting Sikh separatists in Canada. The same day Canadian Deputy Foreign Affairs Minister David Morrison confirmed to the Canadian Parliament's national-security committee that he had confirmed Shah's name to *The Washington Post*: "The journalist called me and asked if it was that person. I confirmed it was that person." Morrison did not characterise the source of the Canadian government's information.

The Canadian-government identification of Shah as the orchestrator of the campaign comprises the single most consequential post-1947 public characterisation by a G7 foreign government of an incumbent Indian Cabinet Minister as the principal of an extrajudicial-violence campaign on the foreign government's soil. The Indian government rejected the Canadian government's identification as "baseless." Shah's office and the Indian Ministry of External Affairs did not respond to the international press requests for comment on the allegation. The characterisation of Shah by the Canadian government is the central documentary element of the post-2024 contemporary Indo-Canadian diplomatic record.

### The US parallel — the Pannun plot

In November 2023 the US Department of Justice indicted Nikhil Gupta — an Indian national characterised in the indictment as an Indian-government employee — on charges of plotting to assassinate Gurpatwant Singh Pannun in New York. Pannun is a US-Canadian dual citizen, the general counsel of Sikhs for Justice (SFJ), and a public advocate of Khalistan independence; the Indian government has designated him a terrorist.

In October 2024 the US grand jury indicted Vikash Yadav — former Research and Analysis Wing (RAW) officer of the Indian Cabinet Secretariat — by name. The Yadav indictment is the first US public identification of a serving or former RAW officer by name in connection with an extraterritorial-assassination plot on US soil. The parallel pattern across the Canadian (Nijjar killing; subsequent Shah identification) and US (Pannun plot; Yadav indictment) cases comprises the single most documented case of post-2014 contemporary Indian intelligence-services extraterritorial-violence operations on G7 territory.

The architecture documented across the Canadian and US cases — operating through the RAW (under Cabinet Secretariat oversight) and the Bishnoi-and-allied criminal networks — and identifying Shah as the orchestrator of the Canadian operation comprises the central documentary record of the contemporary Indian state-instrument extraterritorial-violence apparatus operating under the Modi government and the Shah Home Ministry.

## Documented institutional patterns

Shah has been accused across the record of three institutional patterns. First, the sidelining of police officers and government officials who testified against the Gujarat government in cases relating to the 2002 pogroms and the 2005-2006 fake encounters. Second, the manipulation of electoral constituency delimitation in Gujarat (2008 delimitation exercise) and across multiple subsequent state-level delimitation exercises to favour the BJP. Third, the consolidation of Indian and world cricket administration under the Shah family through the 2019-2024 Jay Shah BCCI Secretary tenure and the 1 December 2024 Jay Shah ICC Chairmanship.

The 2021 Pegasus spyware deployment by the Indian government against opposition politicians, journalists, and activists — covered in subsequent investigative reporting by *The Wire* and the international Pegasus Project consortium — operated under Shah's Home Ministry. Shah's Home Ministry has rejected parliamentary questioning on the topic.

## The measured consequence

The single most institutional pattern of Shah's post-2001 political-class operation is the coordinated operation of the state-instrument extrajudicial-violence apparatus across three jurisdictions. Within Gujarat across the 2002-2012 period: the Sohrabuddin Sheikh and Tulsiram Prajapati killings producing the 2010 CBI arrest. Within India across the 2019-onward period: the Article 370 abrogation, the CAA-NRC architecture, the February 2020 Delhi riots under Home Ministry oversight, and the multi-year Manipur conflict. And on G7 territory across the post-2023 period: the Canadian-government identification of Shah as the orchestrator of the Khalistan-separatist intimidation campaign on Canadian soil including the June 2023 Nijjar killing, and the parallel US Pannun plot indictments of Nikhil Gupta and Vikash Yadav.

The display is the chief electoral strategist. The operation is the documented state-instrument extrajudicial-violence operator. The documented record comprises four chronological phases.

Gujarat rise (1995-2012): the 1995-onward Gujarat rural-and-cooperative-banking strategy that eliminated INC influence in rural Gujarat; the 1999 ADCB cooperative-bank capture; the 2002-2012 twelve-portfolio Gujarat ministerial tenure with the Home portfolio as central; the 2005-2006 Sohrabuddin / Kausar Bi / Tulsiram Prajapati fake-encounter killings; the 2010 CBI arrest and 2010-2012 Gujarat exile; the 2013 Vanzara letter accusing the Modi-Shah government; and the 2014 clean chit by Special Judge MB Gosavi and the 2017 Loya-death journalistic investigation.

National BJP architect (2013-2019): the 2013-2014 BJP General Secretary and UP 73-of-80-seat electoral architecture; the 9 July 2014 youngest-ever BJP Presidency; the 2016 Somnath trusteeship; and the 1 June 2019 Home Minister installation and 70% Gandhinagar margin.

Home Ministry domestic record (2019-2024): the 5 August 2019 Article 370 abrogation; the 11-12 December 2019 CAA-NRC; the February 2020 Delhi riots under Home Ministry oversight; and the post-2023 Manipur conflict under Home Ministry oversight.

Extraterritorial operations (2023-2024): the 18 June 2023 Nijjar killing in Surrey, British Columbia; the 14 October 2024 Canadian expulsion of six Indian diplomats including the Indian High Commissioner; the 29 October 2024 Washington Post-reported and David Morrison-confirmed Canadian identification of Shah as the orchestrator of the campaign of violence, intimidation, and intelligence-gathering targeting Sikh separatists in Canada; the parallel November 2023 US DOJ indictment of Nikhil Gupta; and the October 2024 US indictment of Vikash Yadav.

## Bridge to Dossier 23

The architecture that produced the Modi-Shah political-class operation across the 2001-onward period is the RSS-VHP-Sangh-Parivar architecture under RSS Sarsanghchalak Mohan Bhagwat. Dossier 23 documents the architecture.
