---

# Dossier 23: The RSS-VHP Institutional Architecture (Bhagwat and the Sangh Parivar)

## The subject

The Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) is a Hindu nationalist paramilitary organisation founded 27 September 1925 in Nagpur, Central Provinces, British India, by Keshav Baliram Hedgewar (1889-1940). At the date of writing it is, by membership, the largest far-right organisation on Earth. Its membership operates through an 80,000-plus-shakha (local-branch) network across India and through the affiliated Hindu Swayamsevak Sangh (HSS) operating in 40-plus countries. Its architecture — the Sangh Parivar ("Family of the Sangh") — comprises 2,500-plus organisations covering political (the BJP), religious (the VHP), youth (the ABVP, the Bajrang Dal, the BJYM), women's (the Rashtra Sevika Samiti, the Durga Vahini), labour (the BMS), agricultural (the BKS), educational (the Vidya Bharati network — the largest non-government school network in India), legal (the Akhil Bharatiya Adhivakta Parishad), and tribal (the Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram) operations.

The 6th and current RSS Sarsanghchalak (chief) is Mohan Madhukar Bhagwat (born 11 September 1950). He has held the office since 21 March 2009. The current Sarkaryavah (general secretary) is Dattatreya Hosabale (born 1 December 1955), who has held the office since 2021 and was re-elected for a second three-year term in March 2024.

The RSS has been banned three times by the post-1947 Indian state. First, on 4 February 1948 after Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated 30 January 1948 by Nathuram Vinayak Godse — a documented former RSS member; lifted 12 July 1949. Second, on 4 July 1975 during the Indira Gandhi Emergency; lifted on the Emergency's end in March 1977. Third, on 10 December 1992 following the 6 December 1992 Babri Masjid demolition; lifted 4 June 1993.

The mask is the public characterisation of the RSS as a cultural-and-character-building organisation devoted to Hindu unity, discipline, national service, and social harmony. The 2 October 2025 RSS centenary on Vijayadashami was the structural public characterisation of the "world's largest voluntary organisation."

The reality behind the mask is the record across 100 years. The 1925-1947 colonial period: the RSS collaborated with the British Raj and played no documented role in the Indian independence movement. The 1938 Golwalkar text *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* praised Nazi Germany's "purging" of "the semitic Races" and held the Hindustan analogous treatment of Muslims as "a good lesson for us in Hindustan to learn and profit by." The December 1947 Nehru letter to provincial chief ministers characterised the RSS as "an organisation which is in the nature of a private army, and which is definitely proceeding on the strictest Nazi lines, even following the techniques of the organisation." The 30 January 1948 Gandhi assassination by a documented former RSS member produced the 4 February 1948 first ban; the 11 September 1948 Patel letter to Golwalkar: "All their (RSS) speeches were full of communal poison." The 1969 Jeevan Lal Kapur Commission found: "All these facts taken together were destructive of any theory other than the conspiracy to murder by Savarkar and his group." The 1992 Babri Masjid demolition (covered in the Part IV historical chapter): documented RSS-BJP-VHP-Bajrang Dal organisation per the Liberhan Commission and the IB Maloy Krishna Dhar disclosure. The 2002 Gujarat pogroms (covered in Dossier 21): documented VHP and Bajrang Dal participation per Human Rights Watch. The 2003-2008 nine-attack terrorism campaign: roughly 150 dead (mostly Muslims) at Samjhauta Express (68 dead), Malegaon (10 dead), Modasa, Mecca Masjid Hyderabad (9 dead), Ajmer Sharif Dargah (3 dead), and other documented attacks operating through the Sangh Parivar apparatus. The 2022 Yashwant Shinde sworn affidavit by a former RSS pracharak characterising training to carry out false-flag terrorist attacks in India to scapegoat Muslims.

The performance is the world's largest voluntary cultural organisation. The operation is the architecture of post-1925 Hindu nationalist political-violence operation — the governing parent organisation of the contemporary BJP government and the 2024-onward Indian state.

## The founding 1925

Keshav Baliram Hedgewar (1889-1940) — a Nagpur-based physician and 1921 sedition convict — founded the RSS on 27 September 1925 on Vijayadashami (the Hindu festival marking Rama's victory over Ravana). The founding meeting of roughly 17 men at the Mohite Bada in Nagpur operated under Hedgewar's documented inspiration drawn from Vinayak Damodar Savarkar's 1923 *Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?* — the foundational text of the Hindutva movement.

Hedgewar had been associated with the Indian National Congress in the 1920s before breaking from Congress over Congress's support for the 1919-24 Khilafat movement (a pan-Islamic campaign led by Indian Muslims to pressure the British leadership to protect the Ottoman caliphate). His pre-RSS revolutionary background included secret revolutionary organisations in Bengal — Anushilan Samiti and Jugantar — and the Hindustan Republican Association. The 1921 sedition conviction had institutionalised his break with the anti-colonial constitutional politics of the Congress.

The 1925 documented objective: provide character training and instil "Hindu discipline" to unite the Hindu community and establish a Hindu Rashtra (Hindu nation). The central operational mechanism: the shakha — the daily one-hour pre-dawn local-branch meeting combining physical drill, Hindu cultural training, and ideological instruction.

## Hedgewar period 1925-1940

The 1925-1940 Hedgewar RSS operated through the shakha-based local-branch system and through the avoidance of any anti-British political activity. Per the RSS biographer C.P. Bhishikar, Hedgewar "talked only about Hindu organisations and avoided any direct comment on the colonial government." The 1930 Congress "Independence Day" (26 January 1930) was celebrated by the RSS only that year and subsequently avoided. The Indian Tricolour was shunned in favour of the RSS bhagwa (saffron) flag. Hedgewar personally participated in the April 1930 Salt March but did not get the RSS involved in the movement.

The 1934 Indian National Congress passed a resolution prohibiting Congress members from joining the RSS, the Hindu Mahasabha, or the Muslim League. The RSS-Congress institutional separation was institutionalised across the 1934-onward period. Hedgewar died 21 June 1940.

## Golwalkar period 1940-1973

Madhav Sadashiv Golwalkar (1906-1973) — "Guruji" — became the 2nd Sarsanghchalak in June 1940 following Hedgewar's death. The Golwalkar period operated across 33 years and produced the decisive institutional expansion of the RSS into the pre- and post-independence Indian political-class architecture.

### *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* (1938)

Golwalkar's 1938 text *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* — published two years before he became Sarsanghchalak — is the single most documented foundational ideological text of the post-Hedgewar RSS architecture. Its central passages on the non-Hindu populations of India: "The non-Hindu people in Hindustan must adopt the Hindu culture and language, must learn to respect and hold in reverence Hindu religion, must entertain no idea but those of the glorification of the Hindu race and culture..."

Its documented passages on Nazi Germany's treatment of the European Jewish population: "German race pride has become the topic of the day. To keep up the purity of the Race and its culture Germany shocked the world by purging the country of the semitic Races – the Jews. Race pride at its highest has been manifested here. Germany has shown how well nigh impossible it is for the Races and cultures having differences going to the roots, to be united into one united whole, a good lesson for us in Hindustan to learn and profit by."

The 1938 Golwalkar characterisation was the decisive textual foundation of the RSS institutional ideology. The post-Golwalkar editions of the text were partially revised and republished as *Bunch of Thoughts* (1939, revised editions) but the central passages were not retracted across the Golwalkar leadership of the RSS.

### The 1940-1947 colonial period

Golwalkar continued the Hedgewar policy of RSS non-participation in the Indian independence movement. Per the RSS biographer C.P. Bhishikar, Golwalkar "did not want to give the British an excuse to ban the RSS." The 1942 Quit India Movement (which Gandhi launched on 8 August 1942 and during which the INC leadership was imprisoned) produced no organised RSS participation. The 1930-1947 documented British Raj policy treated the RSS as a non-political cultural organisation; no pre-1948 ban was imposed on the RSS by the colonial government.

### The 1947-1948 transition and the Nehru characterisation

The December 1947 Nehru letter to provincial chief ministers — written four months after the 14-15 August 1947 transfer of power and one month before the 30 January 1948 Gandhi assassination — characterised the RSS in terms that remain the decisive public characterisation by the first PM of India:

"We have a great deal of evidence to show that RSS is an organisation which is in the nature of a private army, and which is definitely proceeding on the strictest Nazi lines, even following the techniques of the organisation."

The Nehru characterisation was the post-1947 institutional position of the post-independence Indian state on the RSS — the position that the 1948 first ban operationalised six weeks later.

## The 1948 Gandhi assassination and the first ban

On 30 January 1948 at roughly 5:17 PM, Mahatma Gandhi was shot and killed at Birla House (now Gandhi Smriti), New Delhi, by Nathuram Vinayak Godse — a documented former RSS member and former Hindu Mahasabha activist. Godse's documented co-conspirators included his brother Gopal Godse, Narayan Apte, Madanlal Pahwa, Vishnu Karkare, Shankar Kistayya, Digambar Badge, Dattatraya Parchure, and Vinayak Damodar Savarkar himself.

The court proceedings at trial in 1948-1949 documented Godse's claim to have left the RSS in 1946 — a claim that the RSS characterisation has used to characterise Godse as a non-RSS individual at the time of the assassination. The counter-position per the documented record: Godse's multi-year RSS training, his continuing relationship with the Sangh and Hindu Mahasabha networks at the time of the assassination, and the 1969 Jeevan Lal Kapur Commission finding: "All these facts taken together were destructive of any theory other than the conspiracy to murder by Savarkar and his group."

On 4 February 1948 the RSS was banned under the Indian Criminal Law Amendment Act 1908. Many prominent RSS leaders were arrested.

The 11 September 1948 Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel letter to Golwalkar — the single most documented post-1947 institutional Indian government characterisation of the RSS — recorded: "All their (RSS) speeches were full of communal poison. As a final result of the poison, the country had to suffer the sacrifice of the invaluable life of Gandhiji."

The 12 July 1949 ban-lifting agreement required the RSS to adopt a constitution swearing loyalty to the Constitution of India and the national flag. The 1949 RSS constitution was adopted under the continuing Golwalkar leadership; the RSS practice on the Indian Tricolour and Constitution operated against the 1949 formal agreement.

## The Bharatiya Jana Sangh and the BJP succession

In 1951 Shyama Prasad Mukherjee — a former Hindu Mahasabha leader and former Cabinet Minister under Nehru who had resigned in April 1950 over Indo-Pakistan policy — founded the Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJS) as the political wing of the RSS. The 1951-1977 BJS was the decisive post-1948 institutional vehicle of the RSS political-class operation.

The 1977 post-Emergency Janata Party coalition (which the BJS joined alongside the Bharatiya Lok Dal, Congress (O), and Socialist Party) defeated Indira Gandhi in the March 1977 general election (covered in the Part IV historical chapter). The 1977-1979 Janata Party government under PM Morarji Desai collapsed.

In 1980 the BJS was reconstituted as the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) under L.K. Advani and Atal Bihari Vajpayee. The 1980-onward BJP proved the decisive post-1980 political vehicle of the Sangh Parivar architecture — the 1998-2004 Vajpayee NDA governments and the 2014-onward Modi governments documented in Dossier 21.

## The 1975-1977 second ban and the 1992-1993 third ban

On 4 July 1975 — nine days after the 25 June 1975 declaration of the Emergency by President Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed on Indira Gandhi's advice (covered in the Part IV historical chapter) — the RSS was banned for the second time. The 1975-1977 Emergency-period RSS underground operations included the anti-Emergency literature distribution that the Modi 1975-1977 hiding-period biography is based on (Dossier 21). The 21 March 1977 end of the Emergency produced the lifting of the 1975 ban.

On 10 December 1992 — four days after the 6 December 1992 Babri Masjid demolition — the RSS, the VHP, and the Bajrang Dal were banned by the PM P.V. Narasimha Rao Congress government under the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act 1967. The 4 June 1993 Indian tribunal ruled there was no evidence of unlawful activities by the RSS and lifted the ban — a ruling that the scholarly literature has characterised as incompatible with the contemporaneous Liberhan Commission record on the demolition.

## Mohan Bhagwat (Sarsanghchalak since 2009)

Mohan Madhukar Bhagwat was born 11 September 1950 in Chandrapur, then in undivided Madhya Pradesh (present-day Maharashtra). He is from a Marathi Karhade Brahmin family of multi-generational RSS activists. His father Madhukar Rao Bhagwat served as Karyavah (secretary) for the Chandrapur RSS zone and subsequently in multiple RSS positions. Bhagwat earned a Bachelor of Veterinary Sciences and Animal Husbandry from Government Veterinary College, Nagpur; he dropped out of his postgraduate course in Veterinary Sciences in late 1975 to become a full-time RSS pracharak.

Bhagwat is unmarried per the pracharak convention. He owns no home, car, or property and lives in RSS hostels. His 50-year daily routine — documented in the RSS hagiographic literature — includes 4 AM rising for yoga, meditation, and study. He has visited RSS / HSS chapters in the US, UK, Kenya, and multiple other countries.

### RSS career

- 1975-onward: full-time pracharak.
- 1991-1999: Akhil Bharatiya Pracharak Pramukh (All-India Chief of Pracharaks).
- 1999-2000: Sah Sarkaryavah (Joint General Secretary).
- 2000-2009: Sarkaryavah (General Secretary) under K.S. Sudarshan.
- 21 March 2009: chosen as 6th Sarsanghchalak following K.S. Sudarshan — the chosen-by-predecessor's-nomination succession architecture that operates the RSS leadership.

In June 2015 the Government of India (under the post-2014 Modi government) provided Bhagwat with Z+ VVIP security cover through the Central Industrial Security Force (CISF). The characterisation of the threat was multiple Islamic militant organisations.

### Documented public positions

In 2017 Bhagwat became the first RSS chief to be officially invited to the Rashtrapati Bhawan by then-President Pranab Mukherjee — a decisive post-1948 institutional accommodation of the RSS by the Office of the President of India. In September 2018 he presided over a three-day session at Vigyan Bhawan, Delhi at which he publicly characterised the RSS as having "discarded some parts of M.S. Golwalkar's *Bunch of Thoughts* which were no longer relevant to the current circumstances" — a partial 80-year post-1938 public characterisation that did not retract the Nazi-Germany passage of the 1938 text.

In February 2017 at a Madhya Pradesh rally Bhagwat characterised: "Everyone born in the country is a Hindu — of these some are idol-worshipers and some are not." The position — that all Indian citizens regardless of declared religion are "Hindu" — was the decisive public characterisation of the Hindu Rashtra (Hindu nation) framework as non-negotiable.

In November 2021 Bhagwat publicly opposed the 1947 Partition of India and advocated reunification of the Indian subcontinent: "The only solution to the pain of Partition lies in undoing it." The characterisation was the single most documented post-1947 public characterisation by an incumbent Indian non-government leader of territorial irredentism toward the 1947 partitioned territories of Pakistan and Bangladesh.

In January 2025 Bhagwat characterised India as having achieved "true independence" with the 22 January 2024 consecration of the Ram Mandir at Ayodhya (covered in Dossier 21). The characterisation operated to reclassify the 15 August 1947 transfer of power as partial and the 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration as decisive.

In a 2019 book Bhagwat characterised homosexuality as a "private matter" while arguing gay marriages should not be "institutionalised for it will institutionalise homosexuality." In January 2023 he characterised LGBTQ individuals as having "right to live as others." In March 2023 Sarkaryavah Dattatreya Hosabale reiterated the RSS opposition to legalisation of gay marriage; the post-2023 BJP-ruled Government of India affidavit before the Indian Supreme Court against same-sex marriage operated in coordinated alignment with the Sangh Parivar position.

At the October 2025 RSS centenary event in Mumbai Bhagwat characterised: "1.25 crore Hindus in Bangladesh should unite and fight for their rights" — a public characterisation that operated within the post-Hasina-ouster Bangladeshi political-class context dossiered in Dossier 27 and that paralleled the November 2021 Bhagwat call for the undoing of the 1947 Partition.

## Dattatreya Hosabale (Sarkaryavah since 2021)

Dattatreya Hosabale was born 1 December 1955 in Sorab, Shivamogga district, Karnataka. He has been an RSS pracharak since 1978. He served in multiple ABVP (RSS student-wing) positions through the 1980s and 1990s. He was elected Sarkaryavah (General Secretary) — the operational head of the RSS — by the Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha in March 2021 and re-elected for a second three-year term in March 2024.

Hosabale's documented public positions across the 2021-onward Sarkaryavah period have included opposition to same-sex marriage (March 2023; covered above), advocacy for caste-based reservation reform, and multi-year public characterisation on population policy and religious-conversion legislation that has operated in coordinated alignment with the BJP-government policy framework.

## RSS organisational architecture

### The shakha system

The shakha (local branch) is the central operational unit of the RSS. The pre-dawn one-hour daily meeting combines physical drill, Hindu cultural training, and ideological instruction. Each shakha is operated by a shakha pramukh (branch chief). The pre-2014 shakha count had fallen from roughly 51,000 in 2004 to roughly 40,000 by mid-2014 (per multi-source academic literature) before the post-2014 BJP government tenure produced the documented expansion to the 80,000-plus figure of the post-2025 RSS centenary period.

### Internal governance

The Sarsanghchalak (chief) is chosen by the predecessor's nomination — the succession architecture that has operated across the six post-1925 Sarsanghchalaks (Hedgewar 1925-1940, Golwalkar 1940-1973, Deoras 1973-1994, Rajendra Singh 1994-2000, Sudarshan 2000-2009, Bhagwat 2009-). The Sarkaryavah (general secretary) is elected by the Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha every three years and serves as the head. Four Sah-Sarkaryavah (joint general secretaries) operate the central administration. The Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha — roughly 1,500 representatives meeting annually — is the highest deliberative body.

### The pracharak cadre

The pracharak (full-time missionary) is the backbone of the RSS. Pracharaks are unmarried, unpaid, full-time RSS workers operating under the assignment system that places RSS-trained individuals across the Sangh Parivar 2,500-plus organisations. The Modi 1971-1985 pracharak tenure (Dossier 21) and the RSS pracharak careers of figures since the post-2014 BJP government — including Sarkaryavah Hosabale, post-Bhagwat-period successors-in-waiting, multiple BJP Chief Ministers (Yogi Adityanath of UP from 2017 and others), and cabinet ministers — operate the pracharak-to-political-class pipeline that is the mechanism of the post-2014 Sangh Parivar political-class architecture.

## The Sangh Parivar

The Sangh Parivar ("Family of the Sangh") comprises 2,500-plus organisations throughout the post-1925 apparatus. The major organisations are:

**Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)** — Indian People's Party; political wing; 202 million members as of 2024 per BJP figures; founded 6 April 1980 (successor to BJS founded 21 October 1951). The single largest electoral political party in modern world history by membership. Operates the post-2014 Modi government covered in Dossier 21 and the Shah Home Ministry covered in Dossier 22.

**Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP)** — World Hindu Council; founded 29 August 1964 at Sandipani Sadhanalaya, Bombay on Krishna Janmashtami by RSS Sarsanghchalak M.S. Golwalkar and S.S. Apte with Swami Chinmayananda. The 14th Dalai Lama attended the founding meeting. The VHP is the religious-and-cultural arm of the Sangh Parivar. Its documented role in the 1992 Babri Masjid demolition and the 2002 Gujarat pogroms is covered in the Part IV historical chapter and Dossier 21. The 4 June 2018 CIA *World Factbook* entry classified the VHP and the Bajrang Dal as militant religious organisations.

**Bajrang Dal** — Army of Lord Hanuman; founded 8 October 1984 in Ayodhya as the youth wing of the VHP. Operated as the foot-soldier organisation of the 1989-1992 Ram Janmabhoomi mobilisation that produced the Babri Masjid demolition. The Bajrang Dal operates major training-camp shakhas across multiple Indian states; the international scholarly literature characterises the organisation as paramilitary. The 1999 Graham Staines killing (covered below) was committed by a Bajrang Dal-affiliated cell.

**Durga Vahini** — women's wing of the Bajrang Dal; founded 1991 under Sadhvi Rithambara. Operates the "Beti Bachao Bahu Lao" / "love jihad" campaign (covered below).

**Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP)** — All India Students' Council; founded 9 July 1949; 2.8 million members as of 2011 per the organisation's figures. The student wing of the RSS; the single largest student political organisation in India; the institutional pipeline through which RSS-trained university students enter the post-graduation Sangh Parivar architecture.

**Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS)** — Indian Workers' Union; founded 23 July 1955; 10 million members as of 2009 per the organisation's figures. The single largest trade union in India.

**Bharatiya Kisan Sangh (BKS)** — Indian Farmers' Association; founded 4 March 1979; 8 million members as of 2002. Operated through the post-1979 Indian agricultural-policy debates. The 2020-2021 farmer protests against the Modi farm laws (covered in Dossier 21) operated through opposition farmer unions; the BKS position on the farm laws was internally divided and operated as a complicating factor for the Modi government's farm-laws repeal calculations.

**Rashtra Sevika Samiti** — women's wing of the RSS; founded 1936 under Lakshmibai Kelkar. The single largest women's Hindu nationalist organisation in India.

**Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram** — for tribal (adivasi) communities; founded 1952. Operated in the post-1952 Indian tribal-belt period through the Christian-missionary-counter-conversion programme and the ghar wapsi ("homecoming") religious-conversion-to-Hinduism programme. Documented operations in Odisha, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, the North East, and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.

**Vidya Bharati** — the RSS schools network; the largest non-government school network in India. Roughly 25,000 schools and 3.4 million students across India per the organisation's figures.

**Hindu Swayamsevak Sangh (HSS)** — international wing; operates in 40-plus countries including the US, UK, Canada, Australia, Kenya, Mauritius, Trinidad and Tobago, Singapore, and the UAE. The HSS America is the US institutional affiliate; HSS UK is the British affiliate.

**Bharatiya Janata Yuva Morcha (BJYM)** — BJP youth wing.

**Akhil Bharatiya Adhivakta Parishad** — All India Lawyers' Council; operated through the post-1992 multi-year Hindu-nationalist legal-architecture programme.

**Deen Dayal Shodh Sansthan** — rural development; 1.7 million members per the organisation's figures.

**Bharat Vikas Parishad** — Indian Development Council.

The Sangh Parivar additionally includes multiple sectoral organisations across journalism (Vishwa Samvad Kendra), economics (Swadeshi Jagaran Manch), and multiple regional and state-level organisations.

## Documented violence record

The record of Sangh Parivar political-violence operations since the post-1925 period comprises six major elements.

### The 1948 Gandhi assassination

Covered above. Nathuram Vinayak Godse — documented former RSS member — assassinated Mahatma Gandhi on 30 January 1948. The 1969 Jeevan Lal Kapur Commission established the multi-individual Savarkar-group conspiracy. The 4 February 1948 first RSS ban was the post-1948 institutional Indian state position on the assassination.

### The 1992 Babri Masjid demolition

Covered in the Part IV historical chapter and Dossier 21. Documented RSS-BJP-VHP-Bajrang Dal organisation of the 6 December 1992 demolition. Liberhan Commission 2009 report: "neither spontaneous nor unplanned." 2005 Maloy Krishna Dhar (former IB Joint Director) disclosure: 10-month pre-planning known to PM P.V. Narasimha Rao and Home Minister Shankarrao Chavan.

### The 2002 Gujarat pogroms

Covered in Dossier 21. Documented VHP and Bajrang Dal participation per Human Rights Watch *We Have No Orders to Save You: State Participation and Complicity in Communal Violence in Gujarat* (April 2002). The 1,044-2,000 documented deaths and 230,000 displaced operated under Modi's Gujarat Chief Ministership and Shah's Home Ministership.

### The 1999 Graham Staines killing

On the night of 22-23 January 1999, Australian Christian missionary Graham Staines (58) and his sons Philip (10) and Timothy (6) were burned alive in their station wagon in Manoharpur, Odisha, by Bajrang Dal-affiliated Dara Singh and accomplices. The 2003 conviction of Dara Singh produced a life-imprisonment sentence; the 21 January 2011 Indian Supreme Court upheld the conviction. The 12 accomplices were acquitted. The Justice D.P. Wadhwa Commission (1999) reportedly found no evidence of orchestrated VHP-Sangh Parivar conspiracy in the killing; the scholarly characterisation of the Wadhwa finding has been contested.

### The 2003-2008 bombing campaigns

The documented record across the 2003-2008 period: nine terror attacks linked to the RSS or other Sangh Parivar organisations killed roughly 150 people, mostly Muslims; many attacks happened near or inside Muslim places of worship or on Islamic holidays. The documented major attacks: the 18 February 2007 Samjhauta Express bombing (68 dead — the India-Pakistan train running between Delhi and Lahore); the 29 September 2008 Malegaon bombing (10 dead); the 29 September 2008 Modasa bombing; the 18 May 2007 Mecca Masjid Hyderabad bombing (9 dead); the 11 October 2007 Ajmer Sharif Dargah bombing (3 dead).

The multi-year investigations under the 2008-2014 UPA government — through the Maharashtra Anti-Terrorism Squad (under chief Hemant Karkare who was killed in the 26 November 2008 Mumbai attacks) and the National Investigation Agency (NIA) established in December 2008 — identified the Abhinav Bharat organisation (founded 2006 by Sameer Kulkarni and others), Sadhvi Pragya Singh Thakur (subsequent 2019 BJP Lok Sabha winner from Bhopal), Lt. Col. Prasad Shrikant Purohit (serving Indian Army officer), and multiple Sangh-affiliated individuals as the operational network. The post-2014 BJP government period produced the systematic dismantling of multiple cases by the NIA — the 2017 dropping of charges against multiple accused in the Mecca Masjid case; the 2019 acquittal of all accused in the Mecca Masjid case; the multi-year delay of the Malegaon trial. The pattern was characterised in the 2008-2014 Indian counter-terrorism investigation as "Hindu terror" or "saffron terror."

### The 2022 Yashwant Shinde sworn affidavit

In September 2022 former RSS pracharak Yashwant Shinde gave an interview and sworn affidavit characterising training to carry out covert operations in Pakistan and false-flag terrorist attacks in India that could then be used to scapegoat Muslims. Shinde characterised himself and fellow trainees as having bombed mosques in Jalna, Purna, and Parbhani in Maharashtra. Shinde implicated key members of the RSS and the larger Sangh Parivar in directly planning or having knowledge of these attacks, including a former RSS national executive member and a national secretary of the BJP. The 2022-onward Indian counter-terrorism response to the Shinde affidavit operated through limited institutional engagement.

### The "Beti Bachao Bahu Lao" / "love jihad" campaign

The VHP-Bajrang Dal-Durga Vahini campaign — tracking Hindu women in inter-faith relationships with Muslim men — has operated throughout the post-2014 period through the anti-conversion-laws architecture. The 2020 Uttar Pradesh Unlawful Religious Conversion Ordinance; 2020 Madhya Pradesh Freedom of Religion Act; 2021 Karnataka Protection of Right to Freedom of Religion Act; 2021 Gujarat Freedom of Religion (Amendment) Act; 2018-2022 Uttarakhand Freedom of Religion Act amendments; 2022 Haryana Prevention of Unlawful Conversion of Religion Act; 2022 Himachal Pradesh Freedom of Religion (Amendment) Act; 2023 Maharashtra and 2025 Chhattisgarh equivalent statutes. The pattern of criminalisation of inter-faith marriage under the "love jihad" framework was the single most documented post-2017 state-level Hindu nationalist legislative-policy programme.

### The post-2024 government-employee participation rule

In July 2024 the Government of India Department of Personnel and Training rescinded the six-decade-old 30 November 1966 ban on central government employees participating in RSS activities. The 2024 administrative order produced the single most documented post-1947 institutional incorporation of the RSS into the central-government employment architecture.

## The RSS centenary 2 October 2025

The RSS centenary on Vijayadashami (Dussehra) 2 October 2025 was the structural public characterisation of the post-1925 record. The two-day centenary conference at New Delhi attended by diplomats, scholars, journalists, and business leaders produced the multi-day Bhagwat public characterisation programme. The Mumbai centenary event Bhagwat speech on the "1.25 crore Hindus in Bangladesh" (covered above) escalated the multi-year post-2021 Bhagwat public characterisation programme on territorial irredentism toward the 1947 partitioned territories.

## The operating verdict

The single most institutional pattern of the post-1925 RSS-VHP-Sangh-Parivar architecture is the coordinated operation of cultural-and-character-building public characterisation alongside documented of Hindu nationalist political-violence operation across the 100-year period.

The display is the world's largest voluntary cultural organisation. The operation is the architecture of post-1925 Hindu nationalist political-violence operation. The documented record comprises four phases.

Origins and the Gandhi assassination (1925-1969): the 1925 Hedgewar founding under Savarkar's Hindutva framework; the 1925-1947 colonial-period collaboration with the British Raj and non-participation in the independence movement; the 1938 Golwalkar *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* praise of Nazi Germany's purging of European Jews as a "good lesson for us in Hindustan"; the December 1947 Nehru characterisation as "a private army definitely proceeding on the strictest Nazi lines"; the 30 January 1948 Godse assassination of Gandhi; the 4 February 1948 first ban and the 11 September 1948 Patel "communal poison" letter; and the 1969 Kapur Commission finding on the Savarkar-group conspiracy.

Family expansion and Ayodhya (1951-1992): the 1951 BJS founding; the 1980 BJP reconstitution; the 1964 VHP founding by Golwalkar and Apte; the 1984 Bajrang Dal founding; the 1989-1992 Ram Janmabhoomi mobilisation; the 6 December 1992 Babri Masjid demolition by 150,000 RSS-BJP-VHP-Bajrang Dal activists per the Liberhan Commission "neither spontaneous nor unplanned" finding; and the December 1992 third ban and the June 1993 lift.

Atrocities and the terrorism cases (1999-2014): the 1999 Bajrang Dal-affiliated Graham Staines and sons killing; the 2002 Gujarat pogroms under VHP-Bajrang Dal participation per Human Rights Watch; the 2003-2008 nine-attack bombing campaign killing 150 mostly Muslims per the Maharashtra ATS and NIA investigations identifying the Abhinav Bharat-Sadhvi Pragya-Lt. Col. Purohit operational network.

Government capture (2014-2025): the multi-year post-2014 NIA dismantling of the cases; the 2022 Yashwant Shinde sworn affidavit on false-flag terrorism training; the post-2014 anti-conversion legislative programme under the "love jihad" framework; the 2018 CIA *World Factbook* militant-organisation classification of the VHP and Bajrang Dal; the July 2024 government-employee participation rule rescission; and the 2 October 2025 centenary on Vijayadashami with the Mumbai Bhagwat "1.25 crore Hindus in Bangladesh" public characterisation.

## Bridge to Dossier 24

The cross-border operational pattern of the post-2014 BJP-RSS-and-Indian-state-security architecture documented in Dossiers 21 and 22 — including the 18 June 2023 Nijjar killing in Surrey, BC, the 14 October 2024 Canadian expulsion of six Indian diplomats, the 29 October 2024 Canadian government identification of Shah as the orchestrator, and the parallel November 2023 US DOJ indictment of Nikhil Gupta and October 2024 indictment of Vikash Yadav — operates against the contemporary Pakistani political-class architecture covered in Dossier 24 (General Asim Munir and the Pakistani military political class).

# Dossier 24: General Asim Munir and the Pakistani Military Political Class

## The subject

Syed Asim Munir Ahmed Shah has served as the 11th Chief of Army Staff (COAS) of the Pakistan Army since 29 November 2022. On 20 May 2025 he was promoted to Field Marshal by unanimous federal cabinet approval — the second Field Marshal in Pakistan's history (after Mohammad Ayub Khan in 1959) and the first Field Marshal in Pakistan's history to be appointed by a civilian government. In November 2025 the Parliament of Pakistan passed the 27th Constitutional Amendment creating the new post of Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) with authority over all three armed services; Munir became the first CDF. He is the first Pakistani officer in history to hold both COAS and CDF simultaneously. His tenure as COAS has been extended to five years — until late November 2027.

The mask is the public characterisation of Pakistan as a parliamentary democracy with civilian elected governments operating under the 1973 Constitution as periodically amended. The recurring public characterisation: Pakistan as the world's fifth most populous country (240 million people), the world's second largest Muslim-majority country, a nuclear-armed state with a strategic relationship with the US (post-September 2001) and China (post-2013 CPEC), operating under the multi-party electoral framework of the PML-N (Pakistan Muslim League-Nawaz), the PPP (Pakistan People's Party), the PTI (Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf), the MQM (Muttahida Qaumi Movement), and the multiple regional and religious parties.

The machinery behind the mask is the record. Direct military rule has covered roughly 33 of Pakistan's 75 post-1947 years (44%). Three successful coups: 1958 (Ayub Khan), 1977 (Zia ul-Haq's Operation Fair Play against the elected Bhutto government), and 1999 (Musharraf against the elected Sharif government). One additional martial law declaration through internal military transfer: 1969 (Yahya Khan). The Pakistani Supreme Court's doctrine of necessity has provided judicial ratification of every successful coup. The Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) — the Pakistani intelligence service — operated the 1980-1988 Operation Cyclone CIA-funded mujahideen architecture that produced the post-1989 Taliban and al-Qaeda. The Hudood Ordinances under Zia and the Eighth Amendment (1985) and the Legal Framework Order 2002 under Musharraf concentrated presidential and military power. The documented Pakistani human rights record across the period includes documented extrajudicial killings, enforced disappearances, and torture, particularly in Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The 2 May 2011 US Navy SEAL operation that killed Osama bin Laden at his Abbottabad compound roughly 1 km from the Pakistan Military Academy at Kakul produced multi-year US Congressional and intelligence-community concerns about ISI knowledge of bin Laden's residence.

The post-2022 Munir tenure has institutionalised the pattern. The 10 April 2022 ouster of PM Imran Khan by no-confidence vote — the first PM in Pakistan history to be removed by no-confidence vote — produced the Shehbaz Sharif PDM government under which Munir was appointed COAS in November 2022. The 9 May 2023 PTI attacks on military installations including the Lahore Corps Commander House and the GHQ Rawalpindi gates produced the PTI crackdown: thousands of arrests, 100-plus PTI defections, December 2023 removal of PTI's electoral symbol, January 2026 anti-terrorism court life sentences for eight journalists and social media commentators in absentia for online content supporting Khan. The May 2025 four-day India-Pakistan conflict produced the 20 May 2025 Munir promotion to Field Marshal. The November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment institutionalised Munir's dual-post architecture.

The performance is parliamentary democracy. The operation is the Pakistani military political class operating through documented coups, judicial doctrine of necessity ratification, ISI political-class management, and the post-2022 Munir architecture institutionalised through the 9 May 2023 crackdown and the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment.

## Biography

Munir was born in 1968 in Rawalpindi — the garrison city of the Pakistan Army adjacent to the federal capital Islamabad. His family is originally from Jalandhar, Punjab; they migrated to Pakistan after the 14-15 August 1947 Partition (covered in the Part IV historical chapter). He received early religious education; he became a hafiz of the Quran (memorised the entire Quran) while serving as a military attaché in Saudi Arabia — the decisive personal religious-credentialing element that operates the multi-year Munir public characterisation programme. He is a Syed (claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through his daughter Fatima and his son-in-law Ali) — a personal lineage characterisation that operates within the Pakistani Sunni Muslim political-class hierarchy.

His military education was at the Officers Training School (OTS) at Mangla — not the Pakistan Military Academy (PMA) at Kakul. The Mangla-versus-Kakul distinction was the decisive institutional challenge to his 2022 promotion to COAS by his intra-institutional opponents. He was commissioned into the Frontier Force Regiment — the multi-generational Pakistani Army regiment with the multi-year counter-insurgency operational history in the Pakistani Pashtun belt.

His advanced military training was conducted in Pakistan, Japan, and Malaysia. He earned an MPhil in Public Policy and Strategic Security Management from the National Defence University (NDU), Islamabad.

He is married to Irum Asim Munir. They have four daughters. His third daughter Mahnoor married Captain Abdul Rehman Qasim — her first cousin (son of Munir's brother Syed Qasim Munir) — on 26 December 2025 at an official residence near GHQ Rawalpindi. The wedding was kept discreet because of security concerns; roughly 400 high-profile guests attended including serving and retired Pakistani Army generals; the ceremony was held without public photos and coincided with the visit to Pakistan of the UAE president.

## Military career

### Early career

Munir's early career assignments included commanding troops in the Northern Areas (Gilgit-Baltistan), including operations in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir, and service as military attaché in Saudi Arabia. The Saudi-attaché posting was the decisive personal-religious-credentialing tour during which he became a hafiz. As a brigadier he commanded an infantry brigade in the Northern Areas and was identified by then-X Corps Commander Lt. Gen. Qamar Javed Bajwa (who subsequently became COAS in November 2016) as the officer to whom Bajwa entrusted intelligence and operational appointments. He served as Chief of Staff of the I Strike Corps at Mangla under Bajwa. The position proved the decisive operational-staff appointment that institutionalised the multi-year Bajwa-Munir patronage relationship.

### DG MI 2017 and DG ISI 2018-2019

In 2017 Munir was appointed Director General of Military Intelligence (DG MI) — the internal-security and counter-intelligence wing of the Pakistani Army. He was promoted to Lieutenant General in 2018.

In October 2018 — eight months into the August 2018 PTI government of PM Imran Khan — Munir was appointed Director General of the Inter-Services Intelligence (DG ISI). The appointment was made by PM Khan in consultation with COAS Bajwa.

In June 2019, after only eight months as DG ISI, Munir was removed by PM Khan and replaced by Lt. Gen. Faiz Hameed — the shortest tenure in the ISI's post-1948 history. The removal followed Munir's reported corruption allegations against PM Khan's wife Bushra Bibi's family. The Khan-Munir conflict that the 2019 removal produced was the decisive personal-and-institutional basis of the multi-year post-2022 Munir-Khan operational confrontation documented below.

Khan's subsequent characterisation across the 2023-2024 post-arrest period: "I think that maybe he has a grudge because I asked him to resign as ISI chief in 2019."

### Corps and GHQ assignments 2019-2022

Following the June 2019 removal as DG ISI, Munir was assigned to the XXX Corps as corps commander and subsequently to Quartermaster General at GHQ Rawalpindi. He was due to retire two days before Bajwa retired in late November 2022. He was not initially on the list of probable successors to Bajwa as COAS — his intra-institutional challenge from the PMA-Kakul-graduate factions remained operationally outstanding.

### 24-29 November 2022 — COAS appointment

On 24 November 2022 the Shehbaz Sharif PDM (Pakistan Democratic Movement) government — the multi-party coalition that had taken power following the April 2022 ouster of Imran Khan via no-confidence vote — promoted Munir to General. On 29 November 2022 Munir took office as the 11th COAS, succeeding Bajwa.

The PDM government's selection of Munir over the alternative candidates operated under the decisive consideration of Munir's 2019 conflict with ousted PM Khan — the selection that produced the post-2022 Munir-Khan operational confrontation. The Sharif government's calculation: a COAS with personal grievances against the dominant opposition leader operated as a decisive structural alignment against the PTI political resurgence.

### 20 May 2025 — Field Marshal

Following the four-day India-Pakistan military confrontation of early May 2025, the federal cabinet on 20 May 2025 unanimously approved the promotion of Munir to Field Marshal — the highest military rank in Pakistan. The official characterisation: recognition of Munir's leadership during the Pakistani Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos in response to the Indian Operation Sindoor.

Munir became the second Field Marshal in Pakistan's history after Mohammad Ayub Khan in 1959 — and the first Field Marshal to be appointed by a civilian government. The Vivekananda International Foundation analyst characterisation: "It would be a rare occasion in military history when an army general was promoted to a five-star rank for his role in an unsuccessful operation primarily dominated by the Air Force and that too for a period of less than four full days."

### November 2025 — Chief of Defence Forces (CDF)

In November 2025 the Pakistan Parliament passed the 27th Constitutional Amendment creating the new post of Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) with authority over all three armed services (Army, Navy, Air Force). Munir became the first CDF — the first Pakistani officer in history to hold both COAS and CDF simultaneously. His tenure as COAS was extended to five years (until late November 2027) — five years from his 29 November 2022 appointment.

The simultaneous decision extended the tenure of Chief of Air Staff Air Chief Marshal Zaheer Ahmed Babar Sidhu by two years (from his original March 2026 end-date). The parallel dual extensions was the decisive institutional consolidation of the Pakistani military political-class architecture under the post-2022 Munir tenure.

## The May 2025 India-Pakistan conflict

In early May 2025 a four-day military confrontation between India and Pakistan — the fifth war averted between the two nuclear-armed neighbours — proved the decisive recent operational event of the Pakistani military political class.

The trigger was India's Operation Sindoor — Indian Air Force strikes against alleged militant infrastructure in Pakistan and Pakistan-administered Kashmir. The Pakistani response was Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos (Arabic for "iron wall" / "leaden wall" — a Quranic phrase from Surah As-Saff verse 4). The conflict was primarily air-and-missile based with drone exchanges over the four days.

The multi-day nuclear-armed confrontation between India and Pakistan operated under the India-Pakistan crisis-management architecture — the 1947-48 first India-Pakistan war; the 1965 second war; the 1971 third war that produced Bangladesh; the 1999 Kargil fourth war (covered in the Part IV historical chapter and Dossier 21). The 2025 near-fifth-war was the decisive recent India-Pakistan crisis under the Modi government (Dossier 21), the Shah Home Ministry (Dossier 22), and the Munir COAS tenure.

The 20 May 2025 Munir Field Marshal promotion — eight days after the 12 May 2025 ceasefire — was the decisive institutional product of the 2025 conflict.

## Pakistan military coup history

The documented Pakistani military political-class architecture comprises three successful coups, one internal military transfer, and multiple failed coup attempts. Direct military rule has covered roughly 33 of Pakistan's 75 post-1947 years.

### 1958 — Ayub Khan

On 7 October 1958 the Governor-General Iskander Mirza declared martial law and named the Commander-in-Chief General Mohammad Ayub Khan as Chief Martial Law Administrator. On 27 October 1958 — thirteen days after the initial declaration — Ayub Khan deposed Mirza, who was exiled to England, and assumed the presidency himself. Ayub ruled from 1958 to 1969. He was promoted to Field Marshal in 1959 — Pakistan's first.

### 1969 — Yahya Khan

On 25 March 1969 General Yahya Khan declared martial law and took power from Ayub Khan through internal military transfer rather than coup against a civilian government. The 1970 national elections — the most open in Pakistan's history at the time — produced Sheikh Mujibur Rahman's Awami League victory (160 of 313 National Assembly seats, on the strength of 167 of 169 East Pakistan seats). Yahya Khan and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto's PPP refused to transfer power.

On 25 March 1971 Yahya Khan launched Operation Searchlight against East Pakistan (covered in the Part IV historical chapter). The 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War produced 300,000-3,000,000 Bengali deaths (the Bangladesh government's post-1971 figure of 3 million is at the upper end; independent scholarly estimates cluster at 300,000-500,000) and 7,235,916 refugees fleeing to India per Indian government figures. On 16 December 1971 Pakistan surrendered at Dhaka in the single largest military surrender since World War II.

Yahya Khan resigned in December 1971; he transferred power to Zulfikar Ali Bhutto.

### 1977 — Operation Fair Play and Zia ul-Haq

On the night of 4-5 July 1977 Operation Fair Play — the coup conducted by the Pakistan Army under COAS General Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq against PM Zulfikar Ali Bhutto — institutionalised the second successful Pakistani military coup. Zia ordered the arrest of Bhutto, his ministers, and other PPP and PNA (Pakistan National Alliance) leaders. In a nationally televised address Zia announced that the National Assembly and provincial assemblies were dissolved and that the Constitution of Pakistan was suspended.

In April 1979 Zulfikar Ali Bhutto was hanged after the Pakistan Supreme Court upheld his death sentence on contested murder charges. The Bhutto hanging was the decisive judicial-killing of an elected former PM by a military government — the single most documented post-1947 Pakistani political-class instance.

Zia's eleven-year rule 1977-1988 produced the Hudood Ordinances (1979) that institutionalised the Islamisation of Pakistani criminal law including the discriminatory provisions on women, the Eighth Amendment (1985) that gave the President unilateral authority to dismiss elected governments, the 1980-1988 Operation Cyclone CIA-ISI mujahideen architecture, and the multi-year suppression of the PPP and the broader Pakistani opposition.

On 17 August 1988 Zia was killed in a plane crash near Bahawalpur along with several Pakistani military officials and US Ambassador to Pakistan Arnold Raphel. The circumstances of the crash remain contested.

### 1995 attempted coup against Benazir Bhutto

In 1995 a coup attempt against PM Benazir Bhutto led by Major General Zaheer ul-Islam Abbasi was foiled. The attempt operated within the pattern of military-versus-civilian tension in Pakistan.

### 1999 — Musharraf

On 12 October 1999 — during the Kargil-aftermath period (the Kargil war covered in the Part IV historical chapter and Dossier 21) — PM Nawaz Sharif attempted to dismiss COAS General Pervez Musharraf and replace him with Lt. Gen. Khwaja Ziauddin Butt. Sharif denied Musharraf's PIA flight permission to land on his return from a visit to Sri Lanka (the 50th anniversary celebrations of the Sri Lankan Army).

officers loyal to Musharraf arrested Sharif and his ministers in a coordinated operation that was the third successful Pakistani military coup. Musharraf landed in Karachi and announced a state of emergency. He placed Sharif under house arrest. He appointed himself Chief Executive of Pakistan and subsequently President.

The Pakistan Supreme Court subsequently ratified Musharraf's coup under the doctrine of necessity — the judicial precedent operationalised by the Pakistani Supreme Court since the 1955 Federation of Pakistan v. Maulvi Tamizuddin Khan case.

Musharraf assumed the presidency formally in June 2001. The April 2002 referendum extended his rule by five years. The Legal Framework Order 2002 institutionalised the concentration of presidential power. The post-September 2001 Musharraf alignment with the US War on Terror produced the multi-year US-Pakistan operational relationship.

Musharraf was forced to resign as President on 18 August 2008 under threat of impeachment by the post-2008-election Pakistan People's Party-Pakistan Muslim League-N coalition government. He departed Pakistan for self-imposed exile. He died on 5 February 2023.

### The doctrine of necessity

The Pakistani Supreme Court's doctrine of necessity — originated by Chief Justice Muhammad Munir in the 1955 Federation of Pakistan v. Maulvi Tamizuddin Khan case — provided judicial ratification for the 1958 Ayub coup, the 1977 Zia coup, and the 1999 Musharraf coup. The pattern of judicial pliability proved the decisive apparatus of Pakistani military-civilian relations.

The November 2007 Musharraf state of emergency required Pakistani Supreme Court judges to swear loyalty under a new Provisional Constitutional Order. Those who refused — including Chief Justice Iftikhar Muhammad Chaudhry and roughly 60 other judges — were dismissed. The Lawyers' Movement and the 2009 restoration of Chief Justice Chaudhry institutionalised a multi-year tension between the Pakistani Supreme Court and the military political class.

## The ISI architecture

The Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) is the Pakistani intelligence service. It was founded in 1948 by Major General Robert Cawthome (Australian deputy chief of staff in the Pakistan Army). The ISI operates under the dual formal-and-operational reporting structure — the formal reporting line to the Prime Minister; the reporting line to the Chief of Army Staff.

### Operation Cyclone 1980-1988

From 1979 the Soviet occupation of Afghanistan produced the CIA-ISI Operation Cyclone — the multi-year joint operation funding and arming Afghan mujahideen against the Soviet occupation. Estimated US$3 billion in CIA funding plus equivalent Saudi matching funds was channelled through the ISI to multiple mujahideen factions including Gulbuddin Hekmatyar's Hezb-e-Islami, Ahmad Shah Massoud's Jamiat-e-Islami, and Burhanuddin Rabbani's Jamiat-e-Islami.

The operational legacy of Operation Cyclone: the post-1989 Taliban (founded 1994 by Mullah Mohammed Omar from Kandahar-area Pashtun madrasa students) and al-Qaeda (founded 11 August 1988 by Osama bin Laden and Ayman al-Zawahiri). The multi-year post-Cyclone Afghan civil war 1989-1996 produced the Taliban government of Afghanistan that subsequently provided sanctuary to al-Qaeda for the 11 September 2001 attacks.

The Pakistani military and ISI operational architecture across the post-1979 period was the decisive structural element of the post-1989 Islamist-militant landscape across South Asia, Central Asia, and the Middle East.

### Post-9/11 War on Terror

Following the 11 September 2001 attacks, US Secretary of State Colin Powell delivered the ultimatum to Musharraf that produced the Pakistani alignment with the US War on Terror. The 2001-2014 US war in Afghanistan — the longest war in US history — operated through the ISI as the primary Pakistani intelligence partner of the CIA.

The documented dual role of the ISI across the period: simultaneous ISI support for Taliban factions including the Haqqani Network even as Pakistan formally allied with the US. The multi-year US Congressional and intelligence-community concerns about ISI support for the Taliban was the decisive structural element of the post-2001 US-Pakistan relationship.

### 2 May 2011 — bin Laden killing at Abbottabad

On 2 May 2011 — at roughly 1 AM Pakistan Standard Time — a US Navy SEAL Team 6 operation under President Barack Obama's authorisation killed Osama bin Laden at his compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan. The compound was located roughly 1 km from the Pakistan Military Academy (PMA) at Kakul — the central officer-training institution of the Pakistani Army.

The location of bin Laden's compound — within walking distance of the central Pakistani military training institution — produced multi-year US Congressional and intelligence-community concerns about ISI knowledge of bin Laden's residence in Abbottabad. The post-2011 multi-year US-Pakistan diplomatic and intelligence relationship operated under the unresolved question.

### Documented human rights record

The documented Pakistani military and ISI human rights record includes documented extrajudicial killings, enforced disappearances, and torture, particularly in Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The multi-year reporting by the Human Rights Commission of Pakistan, Amnesty International, and Human Rights Watch documents the pattern. The Baloch enforced-disappearances pattern — operating since the post-2004 Baloch insurgency under Akbar Bugti and successor leaders — comprises the single most documented Pakistani military-and-intelligence-services human-rights case.

## The Munir-Imran Khan operational confrontation

### April 2022 ouster of Imran Khan

On 10 April 2022 PM Imran Khan was ousted via vote of no confidence — the first PM in Pakistan history to be removed by no-confidence vote. The Pakistan Democratic Movement (PDM) — a multi-party coalition combining the PML-N (under Shehbaz Sharif, brother of three-term PM Nawaz Sharif), the PPP (under Bilawal Bhutto Zardari), the JUI-F (under Maulana Fazl-ur-Rehman), and multiple smaller parties — produced the no-confidence vote and the governing coalition.

Khan's subsequent characterisation: the US-Pakistan diplomatic correspondence (the "cipher" that subsequently produced the Cipher case against him) demonstrated US-Pakistan-military coordination to remove him. The multi-year PTI public characterisation of the April 2022 ouster as a US-orchestrated regime change was the decisive PTI public characterisation of the post-April-2022 period.

The 29 November 2022 Munir COAS appointment by the PDM government proved the decisive institutional consolidation of the post-Khan architecture.

### 9 May 2023 — PTI attacks on military installations

On 9 May 2023 Imran Khan was arrested at the Islamabad High Court in connection with the Al-Qadir Trust Case — the case in which Khan was accused of using his position as PM to facilitate a settlement between the Pakistani government and businessman Malik Riaz after the UK National Crime Agency returned roughly £190 million (US$239 million) to Pakistan. In exchange Riaz allegedly donated 458 kanals of land to the Al-Qadir University Trust — a charity associated with Khan and his wife Bushra Bibi.

PTI supporters across Pakistan responded to the arrest with nationwide demonstrations that descended into violent riots. The major incidents: the attack on the Lahore Corps Commander House (Jinnah House) which was torched; the attack on the gates of Pakistan military headquarters (GHQ) in Rawalpindi; attacks on military installations in Karachi, Peshawar, Sargodha, Gujranwala, and other cities. The total damage was estimated at Rs. 2.5 billion (US$8.9 million) per official figures.

The Pakistan Army subsequently termed 9 May 2023 "Pakistan's 9/11." The 9 May 2023 attacks was the decisive event of the post-2022 Munir-Khan operational confrontation — the subsequent PTI crackdown was institutionalised through the "Pakistan's 9/11" public characterisation.

### The 2023-2026 PTI crackdown

Imran Khan was released two days after the 9 May arrest; he was arrested again on 5 August 2023 in the Toshakhana corruption case. The subsequent prosecutions included more than 100 cases against Khan including the Toshakhana case (foreign-gifts sale), the Cipher case (classified diplomatic cable disclosure), and the Al-Qadir Trust Case. Thousands of PTI workers and party leaders were arrested. More than 100 PTI figures defected from the party.

In December 2023 the Pakistan Election Commission removed PTI's electoral symbol (the cricket bat) over characterised "irregularities" in PTI intra-party elections — a decisive pre-electoral institutional action that produced the 8 February 2024 PTI-as-independent-candidates electoral architecture.

The subsequent media crackdown operated through multiple documented elements. Army officials summoned Pakistani media houses to secret meetings (per Intercept reporting). Khan's name was banned from print and electronic Pakistani media; journalists referred to him euphemistically as "Qasim ke Abba" (Qasim's father). Journalists Imran Riaz Khan (kidnapped outside Sialkot Central Jail on 11 May 2023; Punjab Police chief Usman Anwar blamed military agencies for his disappearance in testimony before the Lahore High Court) and Sami Ibrahim (Bol News head, picked up 24 May 2023 from Islamabad) was the central cases.

Reporters Without Borders and the International Federation of Journalists raised multi-year concerns. Trials of civilians in military courts were initiated and subsequently challenged before the Pakistan Supreme Court.

In January 2026 a Pakistani anti-terrorism court convicted and sentenced eight journalists and social media commentators to life imprisonment in absentia for terrorism-related offences linked to online content supporting Imran Khan — the single most documented post-1947 Pakistani institutional press-suppression event.

### February 2024 election

On 8 February 2024 the Pakistani general election was held with PTI-backed independent candidates contesting (PTI banned from contesting under its electoral symbol). PTI-backed independents won roughly 93 of 266 directly elected National Assembly seats — the largest single bloc but unable to form government. The PML-N (Sharif) and the PPP (Bhutto-Zardari) formed a coalition government with Shehbaz Sharif as PM for his second term from 4 March 2024.

The PTI characterised the election as fraudulent; subsequent disputes over seat allocations operated through the Election Commission of Pakistan, the Pakistani Supreme Court, and the Islamabad High Court. The PTI public characterisation that the 9 May 2023 attacks were a "false flag operation" engineered by the military operated alongside the official Pakistani government characterisation of the attacks as a PTI-engineered mutiny against COAS Munir.

## The post-2022 architecture

The 2023-2026 Munir tenure has institutionalised four documented elements of the contemporary Pakistani military political-class architecture.

First, the November 2022 COAS appointment under the PDM government following the April 2022 Khan ouster — the single most documented post-1947 selection of a COAS by an incoming civilian government to operate the governing confrontation with the ousted political-class predecessor.

Second, the 9 May 2023 "Pakistan's 9/11" public characterisation and the PTI crackdown operating through thousands of arrests, 100-plus PTI defections, the December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal, the media crackdown, the multi-year civilian-prosecutions-in-military-courts framework, and the January 2026 anti-terrorism court life sentences for eight journalists in absentia — the single most documented post-1947 Pakistani institutional press-and-political-class suppression event.

Third, the 20 May 2025 Field Marshal promotion following the four-day India-Pakistan May 2025 conflict — the single most documented post-1947 promotion of a serving Pakistani Army Chief to Field Marshal under a civilian government, and the first Field Marshal promotion since Ayub Khan in 1959.

Fourth, the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment creating the Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) post with Munir as the first holder simultaneously with the COAS — the single most documented post-1947 institutional consolidation of central command authority over the Pakistani armed forces under a single officer.

## The analytical verdict

The single most institutional pattern of the post-1947 Pakistani military political class is the pattern of military supremacy over civilian government. It operates through five mechanisms. First, three successful coups (1958 Ayub, 1977 Zia, 1999 Musharraf). Second, the Pakistani Supreme Court's doctrine of necessity ratification architecture. Third, the ISI political-class management apparatus (the 1980-1988 Operation Cyclone CIA-ISI mujahideen architecture producing the Taliban and al-Qaeda; the post-2001 dual role; the 2011 bin Laden Abbottabad killing). Fourth, the Baloch and Pashtun enforced-disappearances and extrajudicial-killings pattern. And fifth, the military political-class economic operation (the officer corps' extensive landholdings, business interests, and post-retirement positions across Pakistani public-and-private institutions).

The post-2022 Munir tenure has institutionalised the pattern through the record. The display is parliamentary democracy. The operation is the Pakistani military political class — and the November 2022 Munir-as-COAS / November 2025 Munir-as-CDF dual-post architecture is the post-2022 culmination.

The biographical record comprises three phases.

Origins and rise through the ranks (1968-2017): the 1968 Rawalpindi birth and the Jalandhar-Partition family migration; the Saudi-attaché hafiz credentialing and the Syed lineage; the Mangla OTS commissioning and the Frontier Force Regiment career; the Bajwa patronage relationship; and the 2017 DG MI.

DG ISI, Khan removal, and COAS (2018-2023): the October 2018 DG ISI under Imran Khan and the June 2019 removal by Khan that institutionalised the multi-year Munir-Khan operational basis; the November 2022 COAS appointment by the post-Khan PDM government; and the 9 May 2023 PTI attacks on military installations producing the "Pakistan's 9/11" public characterisation and the multi-year PTI crackdown including thousands of arrests, the December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal, the January 2026 life sentences for eight journalists in absentia, and the multi-year civilian-prosecutions-in-military-courts framework.

Field Marshal promotion and CDF post (2025-2026): the May 2025 four-day India-Pakistan conflict producing the 20 May 2025 Field Marshal promotion (the first Field Marshal under a civilian government, the second in Pakistan's history after Ayub Khan 1959); and the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment producing the CDF post with Munir as first holder simultaneously with COAS until November 2027.

## Bridge to Dossier 25

The Pakistani political-class architecture documented in Dossier 24 operated alongside the Sharif-PML-N political-class architecture covered in Dossier 25.
