# Dossier 25: Shehbaz Sharif and the Sharif-PML-N Political-Class Continuity

## The subject

Mian Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif has served as the 23rd Prime Minister of Pakistan since 4 March 2024 (second term). His first term ran 11 April 2022 to 14 August 2023 under the Pakistan Democratic Movement (PDM) coalition government that followed the no-confidence ouster of Imran Khan. He served as Chief Minister of Punjab — Pakistan's most populous and economically dominant province — three times: 20 February 1997 to 12 October 1999, 2008 to 2013, and 2013 to 2018. He has been President of the Pakistan Muslim League (Nawaz) / PML-N since 2017, when his brother Nawaz Sharif was disqualified from holding public office over the Panama Papers case. He has been Member of the National Assembly since 1990 (with intervals during the 2000-2007 Saudi exile).

The mask is the public characterisation of Shehbaz Sharif as the efficient administrator, the Punjab CM who built the Lahore Metro Bus and multiple infrastructure projects across the Sharif Punjab governance period, the cancer-survivor moderate political-class operator who replaced the controversial Imran Khan through legitimate parliamentary no-confidence procedure in April 2022, the post-PDM coalition consensus PM who navigated Pakistan through the 2022-2024 economic crisis and the IMF programme, and the second Sharif brother to serve as Pakistani PM (following Nawaz Sharif's three terms 1990-93, 1997-99, 2013-17).

The actual operation is the documented record across four interlocking threads.

Dynastic-economic base: the Sharif family operation as Pakistan's central dynastic political-and-economic class operating from Lahore (Raiwind Palace) and through the Ittefaq Group steel conglomerate (multimillion-dollar holdings nationalised by Zulfikar Ali Bhutto in 1974 and returned by Zia ul-Haq in 1977); the 1980 Sharif family political emergence through Punjab Governor Lt. Gen. Ghulam Jilani Khan (former DG ISI) under the Zia martial-law regime — producing the documented characterisation by political opponents as "the product of martial law and General Jilani."

Saudi-Gulf integration: the 2000-2007 Saudi-brokered family exile under King Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz; and the family integration with the Saudi political-and-economic class operating across the multi-Sharif-PM tenure period.

Corruption and accountability cases: the 2015 Panama Papers leak implicating Nawaz Sharif and three of his children (Maryam, Hassan, Hussain) in offshore-shell-company London real-estate holdings; the 28 July 2017 Pakistan Supreme Court disqualification of Nawaz; and the 14 July 2019 Daily Mail allegation that Shehbaz had stolen UK DFID aid funds for the 2005 Kashmir earthquake victims and laundered £1 million through frontman Aftab Mehmood (rejected by the Sharif family, subject to multi-year libel litigation).

Army-state co-management: the documented Sharif family operation of the 4 March 2022-onward Munir COAS appointment that produced the post-9 May 2023 PTI suppression covered in Dossier 24; the 8 February 2024 election characterised by PTI and multiple international observers as having been rigged through PTI electoral-symbol removal, pre-election Khan conviction sentences, election-day internet shutdowns, multi-day result delays, late-night seat-count swings, and the Form-45 / Form-47 discrepancy; and the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment passed by the Sharif government creating the Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) post for Munir.

The performance is the efficient administrator and consensus PM. The operation is the documented dynastic-political-class operator of the Sharif family — the Pakistani political dynasty that operates alongside the Pakistani military political class as the dual apparatus of post-1947 Pakistani politics.

## Biography

Shehbaz Sharif was born on 23 September 1951 in Lahore, West Punjab — four years after the 14-15 August 1947 Partition. His family is Punjabi with Kashmiri ancestry. The Sharif family ancestors emigrated from Anantnag, Kashmir for business reasons at the beginning of the twentieth century and settled in the village of Jati Umra, Amritsar district, Punjab. His mother's family came from Pulwama, Kashmir. The 1947 Partition produced the family's documented migration from Amritsar (now in Indian Punjab) to Lahore (now in Pakistani Punjab).

Shehbaz's father, Mian Muhammad Sharif, was an upper-middle-class businessman and industrialist who founded the Ittefaq Group steel conglomerate — the central economic anchor of the Sharif family's political emergence. His mother was Shamim Akhtar Sharif. Shehbaz is the second of three brothers: Nawaz Sharif (eldest, three-time PM and the central political principal of the Sharif family throughout the post-1985 period); Shehbaz Sharif; and Abbas Sharif (businessman, deceased).

Shehbaz attended Saint Anthony's High School in Lahore — one of Pakistan's central Catholic-mission elite schools — and Government College University, Lahore (GCU), where he earned a Bachelor of Arts. GCU is the central institutional university of the Punjabi upper-class.

Shehbaz married Nusrat Shehbaz Sharif in 1973 (his first wife). He married Tehmina Durrani in 2003 (his second wife — legally permitted under Pakistani law). He married a third time. All four of his sons and his two daughters are by Nusrat. The eldest son and documented political heir, Hamza Shahbaz Sharif, served two terms in the National Assembly 2008-2018, briefly served as Chief Minister of Punjab in 2022, and returned to the National Assembly after the 8 February 2024 election. The Sharif family residence is the Raiwind Palace compound near Lahore — the central physical location of the Sharif political dynasty.

## The Sharif family economic and political architecture

The Sharif family economic-and-political architecture operates through three documented elements.

First, the Ittefaq Group steel conglomerate. Founded by Mian Muhammad Sharif before Partition. Operated as a multimillion-dollar steel conglomerate in the post-1947 period. Nationalised by PM Zulfikar Ali Bhutto in 1974 under the documented PPP nationalisation policy. Returned to the Sharif family in 1977 following the Zia-ul-Haq coup against Bhutto and the privatisation. The documented Ittefaq-to-Zia operational architecture is the central institutional basis of the Sharif family's political relationship with the Pakistani military political class covered in Dossier 24.

Second, the Sharif Group conglomerate. Multi-sector holdings across agriculture, transport, and sugar mills — operating alongside the Ittefaq Group steel holdings as the central economic basis of the Sharif dynasty. The documented Sharif business expansion since the post-1977 period operated under the family-political-class integration with the Pakistani regulatory and tax architecture.

Third, the Raiwind Palace family residence near Lahore. The central physical location of the Sharif political dynasty. Hosts the documented multi-year visiting heads of state and PML-N political-class meetings.

The documented 1980 political emergence of the Sharif family operated through then-Punjab Governor Lt. Gen. Ghulam Jilani Khan (former DG ISI 1971-1977 under Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, subsequently Punjab Governor under Zia). In May 1980 Jilani appointed Nawaz Sharif (age 30) as Punjab Finance Minister. The documented characterisation of the Sharif family by post-1985 political opponents — "the product of martial law and General Jilani" — captures the architecture of the family's political emergence under the post-1977 Zia regime. The Sharif family political-class operation is documented from the post-1977 Zia martial-law period continuously through the post-2024 second Shehbaz PM tenure.

## Shehbaz political career 1988-2018

### Early career 1988-1996

Shehbaz entered electoral politics in 1988 — the first general election after Zia-ul-Haq's 17 August 1988 plane-crash death (Dossier 24). He was elected to the Provincial Assembly of the Punjab in 1988 and to the National Assembly in 1990. He was re-elected to both the National Assembly and the Provincial Assembly in 1993. From 1993 to 1996 he served as Leader of the Opposition in the Punjab Assembly under the Benazir Bhutto PPP government.

### First Punjab CM term 1997-1999

On 20 February 1997 — following the PML-N landslide in the 3 February 1997 general election that returned Nawaz Sharif to the PM office for his second term — Shehbaz was elected Chief Minister of Punjab for the first time. His first CM term operated from 20 February 1997 to 12 October 1999 — when the Musharraf coup (covered in Dossier 24) toppled the Nawaz Sharif federal government and ended Shehbaz's first Punjab tenure simultaneously.

The Sharif brothers were arrested following the 12 October 1999 coup and tried by Musharraf-controlled courts. Nawaz was sentenced to life imprisonment in April 2000 for the documented charges of issuing orders telling ground control to refuse landing permission to the Karachi-bound airliner carrying Musharraf returning from Sri Lanka on 12 October 1999 — orders that Nawaz subsequently rescinded but that the Musharraf regime characterised as the central pretext for the coup. Shehbaz was simultaneously prosecuted on multiple related charges.

### Saudi exile 2000-2007

In December 2000 the Sharif family went into Saudi exile under an agreement brokered by then-Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz (subsequent King Abdullah, 2005-2015). The documented Saudi-brokered exile operated under a 10-year agreement; the family lived in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia for roughly seven years (2000-2007).

In August 2006 Shehbaz was re-elected PML-N President for his second term while still in Saudi exile. On 23 August 2007 the Pakistan Supreme Court ruled that the Sharif brothers were free to return to Pakistan. On 10 September 2007 Nawaz attempted to return from London exile to Islamabad; he was prevented from leaving the aeroplane and was deported within hours to Jeddah, Saudi Arabia under the documented continuing Saudi-Musharraf bilateral political arrangement. In November 2007 the Sharif brothers returned to Pakistan following the deterioration of Musharraf's domestic political position.

### Second Punjab CM term 2008-2013

Shehbaz was unable to contest the 18 February 2008 general election due to the Sabzazar case (a Musharraf-era prosecution); he was acquitted months later. In 2008 he was elected Chief Minister of Punjab for his second term following the PML-N victory in Punjab in the 2008 election that produced the PPP-led federal government under PM Yousaf Raza Gilani (with Asif Ali Zardari subsequently President from 9 September 2008).

On 25 February 2009 the Supreme Court of Pakistan disqualified both Nawaz and Shehbaz from holding public office. The disqualification operated on documented charges related to the 2000 hijacking case against Shehbaz and the 1993 Lahore High Court conviction against Nawaz. PPP President Asif Ali Zardari attempted to place Nawaz under house arrest; the Punjab police left Nawaz's residence after an angry crowd gathered outside. The Lawyers' Movement Long March on Islamabad — led by Nawaz Sharif from his Lahore home — operated alongside the pressure on Zardari from the Pakistan Army (including from then-COAS Gen. Ashfaq Parvez Kayani), American and British envoys, and internal protests.

On 16 March 2009 PM Yousaf Raza Gilani delivered a televised speech promising to reinstate Chief Justice Iftikhar Chaudhry (covered in Dossier 24) and to reverse the Sharif disqualifications. The PPP-PML-N secret agreement to restore the PML-N government in Punjab operated alongside the Lawyers' Movement victory.

Shehbaz completed his second CM term 2008-2013. The infrastructure-and-development programme of the second term included the documented Lahore Metro Bus, the Multan and Rawalpindi Metro projects, multi-year highway construction, multi-year schools-and-hospitals construction, and the multi-year load-shedding-management programme.

### Third Punjab CM term 2013-2018

Following the PML-N national victory in the 11 May 2013 general election that returned Nawaz to the PM office for his third term, Shehbaz was re-elected Chief Minister of Punjab for his third term. The third term operated 2013-2018.

In 2015 the Panama Papers leak — the international Consortium of Investigative Journalists publication of roughly 11.5 million documents from the Panamanian law firm Mossack Fonseca — implicated Nawaz Sharif and three of his children (Maryam Nawaz, Hassan Nawaz, Hussain Nawaz) in offshore shell companies used to purchase London real estate (the documented Avenfield apartments at One Hyde Park and other London properties).

Shehbaz was not directly implicated in the Panama Papers leak but faced deposition in the Supreme Court investigation in June 2017. On 28 July 2017 the Pakistan Supreme Court disqualified Nawaz Sharif from holding public office. Nawaz was replaced as PM by Shahid Khaqan Abbasi. In August 2017 Nawaz left for London (with wife Kulsoom Nawaz seeking medical treatment for the throat cancer that produced her September 2018 death); Maryam joined them in September. Their absence left Shehbaz to take the reins of PML-N leadership.

In 2017 Shehbaz was formally nominated as President of the PML-N — the position he has retained continuously from 2017 to the date of writing.

### 2018 election and opposition period 2018-2022

The PML-N was defeated by Imran Khan's PTI in the 25 July 2018 general election. Shehbaz was elected MNA and served as Leader of the Opposition in the National Assembly across the 2018-2022 period.

The 2018-2022 opposition period produced multi-year prosecutions against Shehbaz under the PTI government:
- 4 December 2018: arrested by the National Accountability Bureau (NAB) on charges related to the Ashiana-i-Iqbal housing scheme case;
- 14 February 2019: released on bail;
- 14 July 2019: the Daily Mail article by David Rose alleging Shehbaz stole UK DFID aid funds for 2005 Kashmir earthquake victims and laundered £1 million through frontman Aftab Mehmood — covered separately below;
- 28 September 2020: arrested in a money-laundering case;
- April 2021: released on bail.

The PML-N rejected the prosecutions as politically motivated by the PTI government; the multi-year legal record operated through documented Pakistani institutional patterns of post-electoral prosecution of opposition political-class operators.

### Pakistan Democratic Movement 2020-

In September 2020 the Pakistan Democratic Movement (PDM) was formed — a multi-party coalition combining the PML-N (under Shehbaz Sharif), the PPP (under Bilawal Bhutto Zardari), the Jamiat Ulema-e-Islam (F) (under Maulana Fazl-ur-Rehman), the Awami National Party (ANP), the Balochistan National Party (Mengal), the National Party, the Pashtoonkhwa Milli Awami Party, Jamaat-e-Islami (briefly), and multiple smaller parties. The PDM stated goal: reduce military establishment interference in civilian government.

Nawaz Sharif — from London — gave video-conference speeches at PDM rallies across the 2020-2022 period accusing the Pakistan Army of orchestrating the 2018 election of Imran Khan and PTI. The Nawaz video-conference speeches was the public characterisation by a former three-time Pakistani PM of the Pakistani military political class' documented operation of post-2018 Pakistani politics.

The PDM coalition produced the no-confidence vote that ousted Imran Khan on 10 April 2022.

## First PM term April 2022 - August 2023

On 11 April 2022 Shehbaz Sharif was elected the 23rd Prime Minister of Pakistan with 174 votes against 0 — PTI walked out of the National Assembly chamber rather than participate in the vote. He took oath the same day.

The 16-month first PM term 11 April 2022 - 14 August 2023 operated through:
- The post-Khan-ouster PDM coalition government navigating the 2022-2023 Pakistani economic crisis (currency depreciation, IMF programme, fiscal deficit, multi-year inflation);
- The November 2022 COAS appointment of Asim Munir succeeding Bajwa (covered in Dossier 24) — the central institutional Shehbaz government decision of the period;
- The 9 May 2023 PTI attacks on military installations following Imran Khan's arrest at the Islamabad High Court (covered in Dossier 24);
- The post-9 May 2023 PTI crackdown operating alongside the Munir-led Pakistan Army (Dossier 24);
- The August 2023 IMF Stand-By Arrangement of $3 billion.

On 14 August 2023 Shehbaz resigned three days before the end of the parliamentary term — the documented manoeuvre that permitted a caretaker government to operate from August 2023 to March 2024 and that delayed the general elections beyond the original October 2023 date. Anwaar-ul-Haq Kakar (Balochistan Awami Party / BAP) served as caretaker PM from 14 August 2023 to 4 March 2024.

## The 8 February 2024 election

The 8 February 2024 general election was held following the documented delay engineered by the August 2023 Shehbaz early resignation and the caretaker government tenure. The election was conducted under the architecture that the multi-year Munir-led Pakistan Army and the Shehbaz PML-N political-class jointly operated.

PTI-backed independent candidates — running as independents because the Election Commission of Pakistan had removed PTI's electoral symbol (cricket bat) on 22 December 2023 over "irregularities" in PTI intra-party elections — won roughly 93 of 266 directly elected National Assembly seats, the largest single bloc but unable to form the government. The PML-N won 75 seats (second-largest). The PPP won 54 seats (third-largest). Other parties and remaining independents accounted for the remaining seats.

The 8 February 2024 election was characterised by PTI and multiple international observers — including the Financial Times, The Economist, Reuters, and the EU Election Observation Mission preliminary report — as having been rigged in favour of the PML-N-PPP coalition through institutional means:

- The 22 December 2023 removal of PTI's electoral symbol;
- The pre-election arrest and conviction sentences against Imran Khan operating in coordinated parallel with the post-9 May 2023 PTI crackdown architecture;
- The 8 February 2024 election-day nationwide internet and mobile-services shutdown ordered by the caretaker Interior Ministry;
- The multi-day delay in result announcement following the closing of polling stations;
- The documented late-night swings in seat counts in disputed constituencies — multiple PTI-backed independent candidates who had been leading at polling-station counts on the evening of 8 February 2024 found themselves trailing or losing by the 10-11 February 2024 final consolidated counts;
- The Form-45 / Form-47 discrepancy — the PTI characterisation that the polling-station Form 45 results (signed by polling agents at the polling-station level) were systematically altered in the consolidated Form 47 results announced by the Returning Officers.

On 20 February 2024 the PML-N and PPP formed a minority coalition government. Shehbaz was chosen as the PM candidate over Nawaz Sharif (who had been the PML-N's pre-election expected candidate). Asif Ali Zardari (PPP) was chosen as the coalition's presidential candidate — Zardari's second term as President of Pakistan after his first term 2008-2013.

On 3 March 2024 Shehbaz Sharif was elected PM for his second term with 201 votes, defeating PTI's Omar Ayub Khan. On 4 March 2024 Shehbaz took oath of office. On 9 March 2024 Asif Ali Zardari was sworn in as President.

## Second PM term March 2024-

The post-March 2024 second Shehbaz PM term has institutionalised four documented elements of the contemporary Pakistani political architecture.

First, the multi-year IMF Extended Fund Facility programme — the September 2024 $7 billion IMF EFF approved by the IMF Executive Board is the central institutional fiscal architecture of the post-2024 Shehbaz government. The 37-month IMF programme operates through multi-year quarterly reviews requiring documented fiscal discipline, energy-and-tax reform implementation, and the multi-year monetary tightening.

Second, the multi-year continuing PTI crackdown (covered in Dossier 24). The prosecutions against Imran Khan and PTI figures, the multi-year civilian-prosecutions-in-military-courts framework, the December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal, the multi-year media crackdown, and the January 2026 anti-terrorism court life sentences for eight journalists in absentia — all operate under the Shehbaz government and the Munir-led Pakistan Army in documented coordinated parallel.

Third, the May 2025 Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos and the 20 May 2025 Munir Field Marshal promotion (Dossier 24). The unanimous federal cabinet approval of Munir's Field Marshal promotion by the Shehbaz government is the documented post-1959 civilian endorsement of military institutional consolidation.

Fourth, the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment passed by the Pakistan Parliament under the PML-N-PPP coalition government. The amendment created the new post of Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) with authority over all three armed services; Munir became the first holder and the first Pakistani officer to hold both COAS and CDF simultaneously; Munir's COAS tenure extended to five years (until late November 2027); Air Chief Marshal Zaheer Ahmed Babar Sidhu's tenure extended by two years. The 27th Amendment institutionalises the documented Shehbaz-Munir civilian-military operational alignment of the post-2024 period.

## The Daily Mail allegation 2019

On 14 July 2019 the Daily Mail published a David Rose article headlined: "Did the family of Pakistani politician who has become the poster boy for British overseas aid STEAL funds meant for earthquake victims?"

The Rose allegation: Shehbaz Sharif stole aid funds from the UK's Department for International Development (DFID) following the 8 October 2005 Kashmir earthquake (which killed an estimated 87,350 people across Pakistan, India, and Afghanistan). Rose's documented allegation: Shehbaz used the aid funds to send £1 million through money laundering to his frontman Aftab Mehmood, a British citizen of Pakistani descent.

The contextual background: the UK had donated more than £500 million to earthquake victims in Pakistan through DFID across the 2005-2010 period. The DFID-Pakistan post-earthquake aid programme operated under the multi-year UK-Pakistan bilateral aid architecture.

The Sharif family rejected the Rose allegations and pursued multi-year libel litigation against the Daily Mail in the UK courts. The multi-year UK libel proceedings operated across the 2019-onward period; the litigation outcome remains contested in the multi-year legal record.

## The Sharif-Saudi relationship

The documented Sharif-Saudi political-and-economic relationship operates from the December 2000 Saudi-brokered family exile under then-Crown Prince and subsequent King Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz. The multi-year Saudi-Pakistani economic relationship — operating through Saudi loans to the State Bank of Pakistan, Saudi deposits in the SBP foreign exchange reserves, Saudi deferred oil payments, and Saudi direct foreign investment — has been operationally institutionalised across the multiple Sharif PM tenures.

The November 2025 Saudi investment announcements under the Shehbaz second term — including the multi-billion-dollar Reko Diq copper-gold mining investment, the multi-billion-dollar Saudi Aramco refinery investment, and the multi-year Saudi sovereign-wealth-fund partnerships — operate as the structural continuation of the Sharif-Saudi relationship.

The Sharif-Saudi-and-UAE-and-Gulf relationship is the central institutional element of the Sharif family's external political-economy architecture — operating alongside the Sharif family's documented relationship with the US political-class (the post-1999 Bill Clinton intervention against the Musharraf death sentence for Nawaz; multi-year US bilateral engagement with successive Sharif PM tenures).

## The Sharif dynastic political class

The contemporary Sharif political dynasty operates through four institutional positions.

First, the PM post under Shehbaz Sharif since 4 March 2024 (second term). The PM is the documented operational head of the Pakistani civilian government under the architecture established by the 1973 Constitution.

Second, the PML-N presidency under Shehbaz Sharif since 2017. The PML-N is one of the two post-1985 Pakistani political parties (alongside the PPP); its electoral base operates from Punjab and from the documented Punjabi industrial-and-business political class.

Third, the Chief Ministership of Punjab under Maryam Nawaz Sharif (Nawaz's eldest daughter) since 26 February 2024 — the first woman Chief Minister of Punjab in Pakistani history. Maryam's CM tenure is the central post-2024 Sharif family institutional consolidation of Punjabi political-class authority.

Fourth, the multi-year PML-N supremo role of Nawaz Sharif following his 21 October 2023 return from London exile. Nawaz is the central decision-maker of the PML-N political class; his subsequent decision to nominate Shehbaz as the 2024 PM candidate (over himself) functioned as the central strategic decision of the post-October-2023 Sharif political restoration.

The architecture documented across the four positions — the Shehbaz PM, Maryam Punjab CM, Shehbaz PML-N presidency, and Nawaz PML-N supremo — comprises the most fully consolidated dynastic political-class architecture in contemporary Pakistani politics. The Hamza Shahbaz Sharif (Shehbaz's eldest son, MNA) and the multiple PML-N-aligned Sharif family-and-political-class operators across the governing period operate the dynastic operational depth.

## The documented assessment

The single most institutional pattern of the Sharif political dynasty is the coordinated operation of dynastic political-class authority alongside the Pakistani military political class throughout the post-1985 period. It runs from the 1980 Jilani-appointed Nawaz Punjab Finance Ministership under the Zia regime through the Sharif-army institutional cooperation-and-conflict cycles: 1990-93 Nawaz first term; 1993 dismissal; 1997-99 Nawaz second term; 12 October 1999 Musharraf coup against Nawaz; 2000-2007 Saudi exile; 2007-2013 Zardari PPP partnership; 2013-17 Nawaz third term; 28 July 2017 Panama Papers disqualification; 2017-2022 Imran Khan PTI tenure; 11 April 2022 Shehbaz first term under PDM; and 4 March 2024 Shehbaz second term under PML-N-PPP coalition with the documented post-2024 Sharif-Munir institutional alignment.

The display is the efficient administrator and consensus PM. The operation is the documented dynastic political-class operator — the central element of the dual Pakistani political-class architecture (alongside the Pakistani military political class documented in Dossier 24) that has served as the central institutional structure of post-1947 Pakistani politics. The biographical record comprises four chronological blocks.

Origins and Punjab entry (1951-1999): the 1951 Lahore birth and the Kashmiri-Punjabi family migration from Anantnag through Amritsar to Lahore; the Ittefaq Group steel conglomerate as the central economic basis; the 1980 Jilani-Zia patronage that produced the Sharif political emergence; the 1988 Punjab Provincial Assembly entry; the 1990 National Assembly entry; the 1997-99 first Punjab CM term ended by the Musharraf coup.

Exile and return (2000-2017): the 2000-2007 Saudi-brokered exile under King Abdullah; the 2007 return; the 2008-13 second Punjab CM term; the 2013-18 third Punjab CM term; the 2015 Panama Papers leak; the 28 July 2017 Nawaz disqualification and the 2017 Shehbaz elevation to PML-N President.

Opposition and first PM term (2018-2023): the 2018-22 opposition period and the multi-year NAB prosecutions; the 14 July 2019 Daily Mail Kashmir-earthquake-aid theft allegation; the September 2020 PDM formation; the 10 April 2022 Khan ouster and the 11 April 2022 Shehbaz first PM term; the 24 November 2022 cabinet approval of Munir's elevation to General and 29 November 2022 COAS appointment; the 9 May 2023 PTI attacks and the post-9 May crackdown; the 14 August 2023 early resignation and caretaker-government delay.

Second PM term (2024-2026): the 22 December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal; the 8 February 2024 election characterised as rigged by PTI and international observers; the 4 March 2024 second Shehbaz PM term; the May 2025 Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos and the 20 May 2025 Munir Field Marshal promotion; the November 2025 27th Constitutional Amendment creating the CDF post for Munir; the multi-year IMF EFF programme; and the Sharif-Saudi-Gulf apparatus.

## Bridge to Dossier 26

The Sharif political-class architecture documented in Dossier 25 operates against the contemporary Pakistani opposition political class architecture under Imran Khan and the PTI documented in Dossier 26 (Imran Khan and the PTI record).
