# Dossier 26: Imran Khan and the PTI Record

## The subject

Imran Ahmed Khan Niazi served as the 22nd Prime Minister of Pakistan from 18 August 2018 until 10 April 2022. He was the first PM in Pakistan's history to be removed by a no-confidence vote rather than by coup or constitutional dissolution. He is the founder of Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI — "Pakistan Movement for Justice"), founded 25 April 1996, and served as its Chairman from 1996 until his 2023 imprisonment. He was previously the international cricketer who captained Pakistan to its only ICC Cricket World Cup victory in 1992. He has been imprisoned at Adiala Jail, Rawalpindi continuously since 5 August 2023.

The story sold to the public is the cricketing icon turned anti-corruption populist. The Oxford-educated reformer who founded the country's first cancer hospital in memory of his mother. The third-political-force operator who broke the two-party PML-N-PPP duopoly to form a government in 2018. The populist who promised to dismantle the post-1947 Pakistani corruption and the Sharif-Bhutto dynastic architecture. The imprisoned former PM characterised by the UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention as having been detained in violation of international law.

The record underneath runs in three phases.

Pre-PM army-political alignment (1994-2013): Khan's late-1994 alliance with former DG ISI Lt. Gen. Hamid Gul (the central architect of Operation Cyclone Afghan-mujahideen support; covered in Dossier 24) and Pasban head Muhammad Ali Durrani that produced the pre-PTI institutional architecture of the 1996 PTI founding; Khan's October 1999 support for Musharraf's coup against Nawaz Sharif on the premise that Musharraf would "end corruption and clear out the political mafias"; and the multi-year 2013-2018 Pakistan Army-aligned electoral expansion of PTI that produced the documented Munir-and-Khan-period 2018 PTI federal government formation under documented military political-class facilitation.

PM tenure (2018-2022): the multi-year contested PTI alignment with the Pakistan Army's architecture across the 2018-2021 period — the period that PTI subsequently characterised as one of institutional cooperation and that opposition political-class operators characterised as one of "selected" governance; and the documented multi-year 2018-2022 Khan PM tenure economic and political record (multi-year IMF programmes, multi-year Saudi-and-Gulf bilateral support, the 2020-2022 COVID-19 response, the 22-23 February 2022 Moscow visit to Putin on the day Russia invaded Ukraine that produced the post-March-2022 "Cipher" controversy, and the April 2022 no-confidence ouster).

Post-ouster confrontation (April 2022-onward): the post-April-2022 multi-year PTI institutional confrontation with the Munir-led Pakistan Army (Dossier 24) and the Sharif-led PML-N-PPP coalition (Dossier 25) that produced the 3 November 2022 Wazirabad assassination attempt, the 21 October 2022 ECP disqualification, the 9 May 2023 first arrest and the multi-city PTI attacks on military installations covered in Dossier 24, the 5 August 2023 second arrest, the documented continuous imprisonment of Khan at Adiala Jail since 5 August 2023, and the multi-year cascade of roughly 200 cases against Khan producing the multiple convictions across the Toshakhana, Cipher, Iddat, and Al-Qadir Trust cases with cumulative sentences totalling roughly 65 years (subject to multiple ongoing appeals and partial acquittals).

Cricketing icon. Anti-corruption populist. The record shows a post-2018 political-class operator whose multi-year pre-2018 Pakistan Army alignment produced the architecture of his PM tenure, whose 2018-2022 governance operated through the documented post-1947 Pakistani military-and-civilian political-class architecture, and whose post-April-2022 ouster stands as the central case study of post-Musharraf Pakistani institutional confrontation between a former PM and the military political class.

## Biography

Imran Ahmad Khan Niazi was born on 5 October 1952 in Lahore — the central city of Pakistani Punjab. His family is Niazi Pashtun. His father Ikramullah Khan Niazi (died March 2008, age 85) was a civil engineer. His mother Shaukat Khanum died from cancer in 1985 — the documented motivation for the Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital and Research Centre founded by Khan in 1994. Khan is the only son with four sisters: Aleema Khanum, Uzma Khanum, Rubina Khanum, and Rani Khanum.

The cricketing background of Khan's mother's family is the context of Khan's cricket career. His maternal cousins Javed Burki and Majid Khan both captained the Pakistan national cricket team and both attended Oxford University before Khan — a documented multi-generational pattern of upper-class Pakistani cricketing-and-Oxford political-and-sporting credential operating across the post-1947 period.

Khan attended Cathedral School and Aitchison College in Lahore — Aitchison being the central elite Pakistani boarding school institution of the post-1947 period — before continuing his education at the Royal Grammar School, Worcester, England. He earned a Bachelor of Arts in Philosophy, Politics and Economics (PPE) from Keble College, University of Oxford, graduating in 1975. He captained the Oxford University Blues Cricket team in 1974.

## Marriages

Khan married Jemima Goldsmith — daughter of British tycoon Sir James Goldsmith and a close friend of Princess Diana — in Paris in May 1995, with a London civil ceremony on 21 June 1995. Goldsmith converted to Islam for the marriage. The couple had two sons: Sulaiman Isa (born 1996) and Kasim (born 1999). They divorced on 22 June 2004 after nine years of marriage; the public characterisation: "it was difficult for Jemima to adapt to life in Pakistan." Sulaiman and Kasim live in London with their mother. The Jemima Goldsmith marriage produced the documented multi-year early political-fundraising architecture of the Shaukat Khanum Hospital and the PTI founding period.

In 1997 a California court ruled in the Sita White paternity case that Tyrian Jade was Khan's daughter from his former girlfriend Sita White. Khan publicly denied the paternity claim for multiple years.

In January 2015 — by some accounts October 2014 — Khan married British-Pakistani journalist Reham Khan. The marriage was announced on 8 January 2015. On 22 October 2015 the couple announced their divorce — a ten-month marriage.

On 18 February 2018 — six months before Khan's August 2018 elevation to the PM office — PTI confirmed Khan had married Bushra Bibi (formerly Bushra Maneka), his documented spiritual guide. The first nikah on 1 January 2018 was controversial because Bushra was still in iddat — the mandatory Islamic post-divorce waiting period for the woman. The mufti who conducted the marriage subsequently testified that he had believed marrying on that date would help Khan become PM. Bushra had previously been married 28 years to Khawar Maneka, with whom she had five children. The Maneka family is from Pakpattan, Punjab. The documented iddat controversy produced the subsequent 3 February 2024 "Iddat case" 7-year sentence covered below.

## Cricket career 1971-1992

Khan made his first-class cricket debut at age 16 in Lahore. By the early 1970s he played for Lahore A, Lahore B, Lahore Greens, and Lahore domestic teams. He was part of the Oxford University Blues Cricket team 1973-1975. Between 1971 and 1976 he played English county cricket for Worcestershire. He also represented Dawood Industries and Pakistan International Airlines in Pakistani domestic cricket.

In 1971 he made his Pakistan international Test debut in a Test series against England. He did not take a permanent place on the team until after his Oxford graduation in 1976. By the early 1980s he had distinguished himself as an exceptional bowler and all-rounder. He was named captain of the Pakistani national team in 1982 — age 30 — and served as captain intermittently between 1982 and 1992.

The 1987 Pakistan tour produced the first-ever Pakistani Test series wins in India and England. The 1982-83 home series against India produced Khan's 88 wickets in 13 Tests in a single year as captain, followed by a multi-year stress-fracture absence and the documented late-1984 experimental treatment funded by the Pakistani government that produced his comeback.

On 25 March 1992 at the Melbourne Cricket Ground, Pakistan defeated England by 22 runs in the ICC Cricket World Cup final — Khan's man-of-the-match performance produced Pakistan's only ICC Cricket World Cup victory. He retired from international cricket immediately after the 1992 World Cup. He was subsequently inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame.

The 1992 Cricket World Cup victory is the central documented Khan public characterisation. The "kaptaan" (captain) characterisation operating across the PTI political-class architecture is the documented Khan public identity that the Pakistani electoral political-class period 2011-2024 operated through.

## Post-cricket period 1992-1996

### Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital

In December 1994 the Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital and Research Centre opened in Lahore. Founded and funded through Khan's multi-million-dollar fundraising campaign across the early 1990s. The first cancer hospital in Pakistan. The documented hospital fundraising operation produced Khan's central post-cricket public characterisation as anti-corruption philanthropist across the 1992-1996 pre-PTI political-class period.

### The 1994-1995 Bhutto interference

In 1995 PM Benazir Bhutto, the central institutional concern that Khan could become PM through a military-coup pathway, responded with documented punitive measures. State television refused to air archival cricket footage of Khan; banned fundraising advertisements for Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital during Ramadan. Authorities planted rumours that Khan was under investigation for embezzlement and tax irregularities. Donations to the hospital halved that year as a documented result.

### 1994 Hamid Gul alliance

In late 1994 Khan formed an alliance with former DG ISI Lt. Gen. Hamid Gul (DG ISI 1987-1989; the central architect of Operation Cyclone Afghan-mujahideen support documented in Dossier 24) and Muhammad Ali Durrani (heading Pasban — the breakaway Jamaat-e-Islami youth wing). The stated objective: civil society "pressure group" / watchdog. The documented Hamid Gul-Khan alliance produced the structural architecture of the 1996 PTI founding and was the documented multi-year early Pakistani military political-class facilitation of Khan's political emergence.

## PTI founding and early years 1996-2011

### 25 April 1996 founding

On 25 April 1996 Khan founded Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI — "Pakistan Movement for Justice"). Nawaz Sharif had previously offered Khan second-in-command position in PML-N and 30 National Assembly seats; Khan declined. The PTI initial apparatus operated through the documented Hamid Gul-Khan alliance and the multi-year Pakistani military political-class facilitation documented above.

### 1997 election

PTI contested NA-53 Mianwali (Khan's family region) and NA-94 Lahore in the 3 February 1997 general election. Khan lost both seats to PML-N. PTI won less than 1% of the vote nationally. Zero NA seats.

### 1999 Musharraf coup support

In October 1999 Khan publicly supported General Pervez Musharraf's coup against PM Nawaz Sharif (covered in Dossier 24). Khan's institutional position: Musharraf would "end corruption and clear out the political mafias." The documented Khan-Musharraf 1999-2002 alignment operated through the multi-year Khan-and-Pakistan-Army cooperation that produced the PTI apparatus.

### 2002 election

In the 10 October 2002 general election Khan won NA-71 Mianwali — the only PTI seat in the 2002 elections. He served as a National Assembly opposition member from Mianwali 2002-2007. In October 2007 he resigned from the National Assembly along with multiple opposition politicians protesting Musharraf's presidential re-election.

### 2008 boycott

PTI boycotted the 18 February 2008 general election along with multiple opposition parties protesting the November 2007 Musharraf emergency and the deposition of Chief Justice Iftikhar Chaudhry. Multi-year low PTI electoral profile across the 2008-2011 period.

### 30 October 2011 Minar-e-Pakistan rally

On 30 October 2011 Khan held a mass public rally at Minar-e-Pakistan, Lahore. Hundreds of thousands of attendees per multiple contemporaneous estimates. The 2011 Minar-e-Pakistan rally produced the documented multi-year rapid PTI electoral expansion across the 2011-2013 period and the multi-year documented PTI emergence as the central Pakistani electoral political-class third force.

## PTI 2013-2018

### 2013 general election

In the 11 May 2013 general election PTI emerged as second-largest party by popular vote (3rd by seats with 35 NA seats) under documented Pakistan Army political-class facilitation that Time magazine reporting characterised as the central Army calculation of PTI's role: "the military, which loathes the Sharif political machine and has adopted strong-arm tactics to boost Khan's leadership bid."

PTI formed the provincial government in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) — the central Pashtun province bordering Afghanistan. The KP provincial government 2013-2018 produced documented police-and-judicial-reform programmes that functioned as the central PTI governance record of the 2013-2018 pre-PM period.

### 2014 dharna

In August 2014 PTI and Pakistan Awami Tehreek (PAT under Tahir-ul-Qadri) launched a joint sit-in in Islamabad — the "Azadi March" and the "Inqilab March" — protesting alleged 2013 election rigging by PML-N. The 126-day sit-in operated outside the Pakistani Parliament. The confrontation with the Nawaz Sharif PML-N federal government produced the documented multi-year deterioration of PML-N-PTI institutional relations and the documented continuing Pakistan Army political-class facilitation of PTI's electoral expansion.

### 2017-2018 Panama Papers period

The 28 July 2017 Pakistan Supreme Court disqualification of Nawaz Sharif over the Panama Papers case (Dossier 25) produced the opening that the 25 July 2018 PTI electoral victory operationalised. Khan's multi-year anti-Sharif and anti-corruption rhetoric produced the documented PTI electoral mobilisation across the 2017-2018 pre-election period.

### 25 July 2018 election

In the 25 July 2018 general election PTI emerged as the largest party with 149 of 272 directly elected National Assembly seats and 16.9 million votes (largest single-party vote share). The PTI coalition with independents and smaller parties (MQM-P, BNP-M, BAP, PML-Q, GDA, and others) produced the federal government formation. On 18 August 2018 Khan was sworn in as the 22nd Prime Minister of Pakistan.

The 2018 election was characterised by multiple international observers (the EU Election Observation Mission preliminary report; the multi-year commentary by Nawaz Sharif at PDM rallies covered in Dossier 25) as having been facilitated by the Pakistan Army political-class. The documented "selected not elected" characterisation operating from the post-2018 opposition political-class is the central institutional context of Khan's 2018 PM tenure.

## Prime Minister tenure 2018-2022

### Economic record

Khan inherited a balance-of-payments crisis from his PML-N predecessor in August 2018. The multi-year IMF bailouts included the 2019 $6 billion IMF Extended Fund Facility (EFF). Multi-year Saudi Arabia and UAE bilateral economic support operated through documented Saudi loans to the State Bank of Pakistan, Saudi deposits in SBP foreign exchange reserves, and Saudi deferred oil payments. The Khan administration figures characterised the period as one of shrinking current account deficit, limited defence spending, and increased tax collection.

The 2020-2022 COVID-19 pandemic produced multi-year economic turmoil and rising inflation. The Khan administration COVID-19 response — operated through the National Command and Operation Centre (NCOC) under chief minister of defence Asad Umar with multi-year Pakistan Army involvement — avoided full lockdown and operated through "smart lockdowns." The multi-million-vaccine programme operated across 2021-2022. The post-COVID-19 inflation was the central economic deterioration of Khan's 2020-2022 PM tenure period.

### Ehsaas Programme and policy initiatives

The Ehsaas Programme — Khan's flagship social safety net programme launched 2019 — produced multi-million-rupee cash-transfer architecture across millions of beneficiaries. The "Plant for Pakistan" / Ten Billion Tree Tsunami initiative operated multi-year reforestation programmes. The expansion of protected areas produced multi-year ecological-policy programmes. The Khan administration's renewable-energy-transition commitments operated within the multi-year IMF-and-fiscal-discipline framework.

### Foreign policy

In July 2019 Khan visited Washington and met President Donald Trump. The visit produced multi-year US-Pakistan diplomatic re-engagement after the multi-year Trump-administration multi-year tensions with Pakistan on Afghanistan policy. The multi-year China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) Belt-and-Road framework continued under Khan's PM tenure.

The 22-23 February 2022 Khan visit to Moscow — on the day Russia invaded Ukraine — is the central documented foreign-policy event of Khan's PM tenure. The Moscow visit produced multi-year diplomatic tension between the Khan administration and the Biden administration. The 7 March 2022 documented conversation between Pakistan's then-Ambassador to the US Asad Majeed Khan and US Assistant Secretary of State Donald Lu — the conversation summarised in the cable subsequently characterised by Khan as "the Cipher" — operated within the documented post-Moscow-visit US-Pakistan diplomatic period.

## The April 2022 no-confidence ouster

### The Cipher controversy

On 7 March 2022 a documented conversation took place between Pakistan's then-Ambassador to the US Asad Majeed Khan and US Assistant Secretary of State for South and Central Asian Affairs Donald Lu. The conversation reportedly characterised US administration concern about Khan's Moscow visit and indicated US administration preference for Khan's removal from power. The cable summarising the conversation — "the Cipher" — was reportedly received by the Pakistan Foreign Ministry.

On 27 March 2022 — at a public rally in Islamabad — Khan brandished the Cipher paper and characterised it as documented US-orchestrated regime-change conspiracy against him. The August 2023 publication of the purported Cipher text by The Intercept produced multi-year international commentary on the documented US-Pakistan diplomatic episode. The US State Department and the Pakistan Army rejected Khan's characterisation; PTI maintained the characterisation across the multi-year period.

### The no-confidence vote

On 8 March 2022 the opposition (the PDM coalition plus dissident PTI MNAs) filed a no-confidence motion against Khan in the National Assembly. On 3 April 2022 PTI Deputy Speaker Qasim Suri dismissed the motion as "unconstitutional" on the documented basis of the Cipher allegations; Khan dissolved the National Assembly through President Arif Alvi. On 7 April 2022 the Pakistan Supreme Court (5-0 ruling under Chief Justice Umar Ata Bandial) restored the National Assembly and ordered the no-confidence vote.

The 9-10 April 2022 overnight National Assembly session produced the no-confidence vote shortly after midnight on 10 April 2022. The vote: 174 votes for the motion; 0 against (PTI walked out). Khan was removed — the first PM in Pakistan history to be ousted by no-confidence vote. On 11 April 2022 Shehbaz Sharif was elected the 23rd PM (Dossier 25).

## Post-ouster political and legal architecture 2022-2025

### November 2022 Wazirabad assassination attempt

On 3 November 2022 Khan was shot in the leg during his Haqeeqi Azadi Long March near Wazirabad, Punjab. One supporter was killed; multiple wounded. Khan recovered from Lahore's Shaukat Khanum Hospital. He subsequently characterised the attempt as a documented assassination plot involving PM Shehbaz Sharif, then-Interior Minister Rana Sanaullah, and ISI officer Maj. Gen. Faisal Naseer. The documented public characterisation produced the multi-year confrontation between Khan and the post-2022 Pakistan Army political-class architecture.

### 21 October 2022 ECP disqualification

On 21 October 2022 the Election Commission of Pakistan disqualified Khan under Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution for false declarations of assets in the documented Toshakhana case — Khan had admitted in his 8 September 2022 ECP filing to having sold four state gifts (Graff watch, cufflinks, ring, pen, and four Rolex watches) for roughly 58 million rupees after purchasing them from Toshakhana for 21.56 million rupees.

### 9 May 2023 first arrest

On 9 May 2023 Khan was arrested at the Islamabad High Court in connection with the Al-Qadir Trust Case (covered below). The arrest came one day after the Inter-Services Public Relations (ISPR) — the Pakistan Army media wing — rejected Khan's 7 May 2023 public allegations against ISI officer Maj. Gen. Faisal Naseer for the November 2022 Wazirabad attempt and for the documented killing of journalist Arshad Sharif (killed 23 October 2022 in Kenya in circumstances that PTI characterised as orchestrated by Pakistani state actors).

The 9-12 May 2023 nationwide PTI demonstrations descended into the documented multi-city attacks on military installations covered in Dossier 24 — the Lahore Corps Commander House (the historic Jinnah House) torched; the GHQ Rawalpindi gates attacked; multi-city attacks in Karachi, Peshawar, Sargodha, Gujranwala. The Pakistan Army characterised 9 May 2023 as "Pakistan's 9/11." On 11 May 2023 the Pakistan Supreme Court deemed Khan's arrest unlawful and ordered his release; on 12 May 2023 he was released.

### 5 August 2023 second arrest

On 5 August 2023 Khan was arrested at his Zaman Park residence in Lahore in connection with the Toshakhana case conviction. He has been confined at Adiala Jail, Rawalpindi continuously since 5 August 2023. The UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention 2024 opinion characterised Khan's detention as in violation of international law and lacking legal basis; UN human rights experts have characterised the conditions of Khan's imprisonment as "inhumane and undignified."

## The cases against Imran Khan

The Pakistani courts have filed roughly 200 cases against Khan since his April 2022 ouster. The central convictions:

### Toshakhana cases

The Toshakhana ("treasure house") cases concern Khan's documented retention and sale of state gifts received during his 2018-2022 PM tenure. On 5 August 2023 a trial court convicted Khan with a 3-year sentence (subsequently suspended by the Islamabad High Court in 2024). On 31 January 2024 the NAB Toshakhana case produced 14 years' imprisonment and a roughly 1.5 billion rupee fine; the April 2024 Islamabad High Court suspended the sentence on bail. On 13 July 2024 Khan was re-arrested in the Toshakhana 2 case. On 17 December 2025 the Toshakhana 2 case produced a 17-year sentence for both Khan and Bushra Bibi over the documented Bulgari jewellery set valued at $285,000 received from Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman during the 2021 state visit (paid only $10,300); each fined 10 million rupees; 10-year disqualification from public office.

### Cipher case

On 30 January 2024 Khan and former Foreign Minister Shah Mahmood Qureshi were each sentenced to 10 years' imprisonment under the Official Secrets Act for "leaking state secrets" through the documented Cipher disclosure. On 3 June 2024 the Islamabad High Court (Chief Justice Aamer Farooq and Justice Miangul Hassan Aurangzeb) acquitted both Khan and Qureshi in the Cipher case.

### Iddat case

On 3 February 2024 Khan and Bushra Bibi were each sentenced to 7 years' imprisonment for "contracting marriage during iddat" — the mandatory Islamic post-divorce waiting period for the woman. The case operated through the documented January 2018 first nikah controversy covered above. In July 2024 the district and sessions court acquitted both.

### Al-Qadir Trust case

The Al-Qadir Trust Case operated through the documented UK National Crime Agency £190 million / US$239 million property settlement returned to Pakistan in 2019 and the alleged donation of 458 kanals of land to the Al-Qadir University Trust (a charitable trust set up by Khan and Bushra Bibi in 2018 while Khan held office) by property developer Malik Riaz. The land value: up to 7 billion rupees ($25 million). On 17 January 2025 the NAB accountability court sentenced Khan to 14 years' imprisonment; Bushra Bibi sentenced to 7 years.

### 9 May 2023 cases

Multi-year anti-terrorism charges against Khan and PTI leadership for the 9 May 2023 attacks on military installations. Multi-year continuing prosecutions in military and anti-terrorism courts under the documented post-2023 architecture covered in Dossier 24.

### Cumulative

Roughly 200 total cases filed against Khan since April 2022. Total cumulative sentences across six-plus convictions: roughly 65 years (subject to multiple ongoing appeals and partial acquittals). Multi-month acquittals and reversals across cases. Khan has been imprisoned continuously since 5 August 2023.

## PTI under the 2023-2025 crackdown

### 22 December 2023 electoral symbol removal

On 22 December 2023 the Election Commission of Pakistan removed PTI's electoral symbol (the cricket bat) over alleged "irregularities" in PTI intra-party elections — operating under a 13 December 2023 ECP requirement that PTI had not complied with per ECP. The appeals to the Islamabad High Court and the Pakistan Supreme Court produced the 13 January 2024 Pakistan Supreme Court upholding of the ECP decision.

### 8 February 2024 election

PTI-backed independent candidates — running as independents because of the December 2023 electoral-symbol removal — won roughly 93 of 266 directly elected NA seats in the 8 February 2024 general election (Dossier 25). The largest single bloc but unable to form government. PML-N (75 seats) and PPP (54 seats) formed the coalition government. The Sunni Ittehad Council (SIC) — to which PTI-backed independents subsequently joined — was denied reserved seats by the ECP and (in a documented 6-5 ruling) by the Pakistan Supreme Court — a ruling that produced multi-year commentary on Pakistani Supreme Court PTI-related decisions.

### November 2024 Islamabad protests

On 24 November 2024 the PTI-led march on Islamabad — led by Bushra Bibi from Peshawar following Khan's documented directives from Adiala Jail — produced thousands of PTI supporters reaching D-Chowk in central Islamabad by 25-26 November 2024. The post-midnight 26 November 2024 Rangers-led crackdown produced multiple PTI-worker deaths (PTI claimed 12; the documented government characterisation rejected the figure; the actual death count remains contested). Multi-month detentions of PTI workers across Punjab and KP.

### July 2025 PTI protest campaign

In July 2025 PTI announced a 90-day street-level protest campaign across Pakistan. Rallies and road blockades in multiple provinces. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa then-Chief Minister Ali Amin Gandapur led announcements. The second anniversary of Khan's jailing in August 2025 produced nationwide rallies; over 240 arrests per official Pakistani figures.

### Documented international response

The UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention's 2024 opinion: Khan's detention violates international law and lacks legal basis; Khan should be released and compensated. UN human rights experts have characterised the conditions of Khan's imprisonment at Adiala Jail — solitary confinement, restricted family-and-lawyer access, restricted communal-prayer attendance — as "inhumane and undignified." Reporters Without Borders, Amnesty International, and Human Rights Watch have produced multi-year reporting on the Pakistani institutional crackdown on PTI, journalism, and judicial independence under the post-2023 architecture (covered in Dossiers 24 and 25).

## The PTI electoral and political record

PTI's electoral and political record since the 25 April 1996 founding:

- 1996-2002: less than 1% national vote share; zero NA seats.
- 2002: Khan's first NA seat from Mianwali.
- 2008: PTI boycotted election.
- 2013: 7.6 million votes (second-largest by popular vote); 35 NA seats; KP provincial government.
- 2018: 16.9 million votes (largest); 149 NA seats; coalition federal government with Khan as PM.
- 2022: April Khan ouster by no-confidence vote.
- 2024: PTI-backed independents 93 of 266 directly elected NA seats; largest single bloc; unable to form government.

The PTI apparatus has operated through three documented phases. First, the 1996-2011 wilderness period of low electoral profile and multi-year Pakistan Army political-class facilitation. Second, the 2011-2022 electoral expansion period producing the 2013 KP provincial government and the 2018 federal government under documented Pakistan Army political-class alignment. Third, the post-April-2022 confrontation period producing the multi-year Munir-Sharif PTI crackdown documented across Dossiers 24, 25, and the present dossier.

## The net conclusion

The single most institutional pattern of post-2018 Pakistani politics is the governing cycle of Pakistan Army-Khan relations, running through three phases. Phase one: Pakistan Army political-class facilitation of Khan's electoral expansion 2011-2018. Phase two: the multi-year deterioration of the Khan-Army relationship 2018-2022 producing the April 2022 no-confidence ouster. Phase three: the multi-year post-April-2022 confrontation that has produced the documented continuous Khan imprisonment since 5 August 2023, the multi-year cascade of roughly 200 cases against Khan, the multi-year cumulative sentences of roughly 65 years, the December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal, the February 2024 election that PTI and international observers characterised as rigged, the November 2024 Islamabad protests producing multiple PTI worker deaths, the July 2025 90-day protest campaign, and the multi-year UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention characterisation of Khan's detention as violating international law.

The mask is the cricketing icon turned anti-corruption populist. The operation is the documented political-class operator whose apparatus operated through the post-1947 Pakistani military political-class facilitation — and whose post-April-2022 confrontation with the same military political-class apparatus has produced the central documented case study of post-Musharraf Pakistani institutional confrontation between a former PM and the Pakistani military political class.

The biographical record comprises four chronological blocks.

Origins and cricket (1952-1992): the 1952 Lahore birth and the Niazi Pashtun family lineage; the multi-generational Burki-Khan cricketing-and-Oxford institutional credentialing; the Cathedral-Aitchison-Worcester-Keble-Oxford 1975 PPE education; the 1971 international Test debut and 1982-1992 captaincy producing the 25 March 1992 Cricket World Cup victory.

Philanthropy and political entry (1994-2011): the 1994 Shaukat Khanum Hospital founding and the multi-year fundraising programme; the late-1994 Hamid Gul alliance institutionalising the multi-year Pakistan Army political-class facilitation of the 1996 PTI founding; the 1995-2004 Jemima Goldsmith marriage and the 1997 Sita White paternity ruling; the 1999 Khan support for the Musharraf coup; the 2002 Mianwali NA seat; the October 2007 Musharraf-era resignation; the 2008 PTI boycott; the 30 October 2011 Minar-e-Pakistan rally producing the multi-year PTI electoral expansion.

PM term and ouster (2013-2022): the 2013 KP provincial government and the 2014 126-day dharna; the 18 February 2018 Bushra Bibi marriage producing the Iddat case; the 25 July 2018 PTI 149-seat federal government victory under documented Pakistan Army political-class facilitation; the 18 August 2018 elevation to PM; the multi-year 2018-2022 IMF, Saudi, UAE, China, and US diplomatic architecture; the 2020-2022 COVID-19 response; the 22-23 February 2022 Moscow visit producing the post-March-2022 Cipher controversy; the 27 March 2022 Cipher rally; the 9-10 April 2022 no-confidence vote ousting Khan.

Post-ouster confrontation (2022-2025): the 3 November 2022 Wazirabad assassination attempt; the 21 October 2022 ECP disqualification; the 9 May 2023 first arrest and the multi-city PTI attacks on military installations; the 5 August 2023 second arrest and the continuous Adiala Jail imprisonment from 5 August 2023; the multi-year cascade of roughly 200 cases with cumulative sentences of roughly 65 years across multiple Toshakhana / Cipher / Iddat / Al-Qadir Trust convictions; the 22 December 2023 PTI electoral-symbol removal; the 8 February 2024 election with PTI-backed independents at roughly 93 of 266 NA seats; the November 2024 D-Chowk crackdown; the July 2025 90-day protest campaign; the 17 December 2025 Toshakhana 2 case 17-year sentence; and the UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention 2024 opinion characterising Khan's detention as violating international law.

## Bridge to Dossier 27

The Pakistani political-class architecture documented across Dossiers 24, 25, and 26 operates against the contemporary Bangladeshi political-class architecture under former PM Sheikh Hasina (the 5 August 2024 exile from Dhaka to Delhi covered in the Part IV historical chapter) and the post-2024 interim Muhammad Yunus government documented in Dossiers 27 and 28.

---


---

# Dossier 27: Sheikh Hasina and the Bangladeshi Political-Class Architecture

## The subject

Sheikh Hasina Wazed served as the 10th Prime Minister of Bangladesh across two non-consecutive tenures: first from 23 June 1996 to 15 July 2001, and second through fifth consecutive terms from 6 January 2009 to 5 August 2024. Her combined roughly 20-year tenure makes her the longest-serving PM in Bangladesh's history; at the time of her resignation in August 2024 she was the world's longest-serving female head of government. She has been President of the Bangladesh Awami League since 1981 — 44 years and continuing as of the date of writing. She is the daughter of Sheikh Mujibur Rahman ("Bangabandhu") — founding leader and first President of Bangladesh (1971-1975). Since 5 August 2024 she has been in self-imposed exile in India.

On 17 November 2025 the Bangladesh International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) in Dhaka — the same tribunal that her 2009-2024 government had revived in 2010 to prosecute 1971 Liberation War crimes — convicted her in absentia of crimes against humanity for ordering the July-August 2024 crackdown on student-led protests and sentenced her to death. She is the first Bangladeshi head of government, sitting or former, to have been given capital punishment through judicial proceedings.

The public story is the surviving daughter of Bangabandhu, inheritor of the 1971 Liberation War dynastic legitimacy. The PM who delivered 6-7% annual GDP growth, the world's second-largest garment exporter status after China, the Padma Bridge, the Dhaka Metro, the Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant. The ICT-architect who delivered judicial accountability for the 1971 war crimes. The 2024 ouster victim who has rejected the post-Yunus interim government's November 2025 death sentence as the product of a "kangaroo court."

What the public story omits runs across five threads.

Dynasty and Indian-state alignment: the Mujibur Rahman dynastic political-class operating from the post-1971 Bangladesh founding through Hasina's Awami League presidency since 1981; and the Awami League-and-Indian-state institutional alignment operating from Hasina's 1975-1981 Indira Gandhi-protected New Delhi exile through the post-2014 multi-year Modi government bilateral architecture.

Paramilitary-and-extrajudicial record: the documented multi-year human rights record of the 2009-2024 PM tenures producing the December 2021 US Treasury Magnitsky-style sanctions on the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB — the elite Bangladeshi paramilitary force) and seven current and former officials for documented hundreds of enforced disappearances and extrajudicial killings since 2009.

Electoral architecture: the documented electoral architecture across the 2014 BNP-boycotted election (Awami League 234 of 300 seats), the 30 December 2018 vote-rigging-and-ballot-stuffing election (Awami League 257 of 300 with documented 96% vote share in contested seats and pre-election jailing of BNP leader Khaleda Zia), and the 7 January 2024 BNP-boycotted election (Awami League 224 of 300 seats with roughly 40% turnout widely contested) that produced the documented "authoritarian leader of a one-party state" characterisation by multiple international observers; and the multi-year institutional crackdown on opposition political parties, journalists, and civil society documented across multi-year US State Department, EU, UN OHCHR, Human Rights Watch, and Amnesty International reporting.

Quota crackdown and ouster: the July-August 2024 quota reform movement crackdown producing up to 1,400 dead and up to 25,000 injured per UN OHCHR figures including the iconic 16 July 2024 Abu Sayed killing at Begum Rokeya University in Rangpur, the Chankharpul massacre that the November 2025 ICT verdict personally implicated Hasina in ordering, and the 5 August 2024 Ashulia immolation killings.

Exile and verdict: the 5 August 2024 Hasina resignation and flight from Dhaka by Bangladesh Air Force helicopter to Hindon Air Force Base near Delhi; the continuing exile in Delhi under documented post-2024 Modi government protection; and the 17 November 2025 ICT death sentence in absentia.

Surviving daughter of Bangabandhu. Dynastic political-class operator. The central documented case study of post-1971 Bangladeshi democratic backsliding — whose July-August 2024 crackdown ordered the documented massacre of roughly 1,400 protesters and produced the ICT's November 2025 in absentia death sentence.

## Biography

Sheikh Hasina was born on 28 September 1947 in Tungipara, East Bengal (then within the newly-formed Dominion of Pakistan; subsequently East Pakistan; now Gopalganj District in Bangladesh). Her birth occurred six weeks after the 14-15 August 1947 Partition. Her father Sheikh Mujibur Rahman was a young political activist of the Bengal Muslim Students' League who subsequently became the founding leader and first President of Bangladesh (1971-1975) and the central iconic figure of the post-1971 Bangladeshi political imagination as "Bangabandhu" ("Friend of Bengal"). Her mother was Sheikh Fazilatunnesa Mujib.

Hasina was the eldest of five surviving Mujibur Rahman children. Her three brothers — Sheikh Kamal, Sheikh Jamal, and Sheikh Russel (the youngest, killed at 10) — were all assassinated alongside their parents on 15 August 1975. Her younger sister Sheikh Rehana survived the family massacre because both were in West Germany at the time visiting Hasina's husband. Sheikh Rehana's daughter Tulip Siddiq has been a UK Labour MP for Hampstead and Highgate since 2015; she served as UK Treasury Minister for Anti-Corruption under PM Keir Starmer from July 2024 until her 14 January 2025 resignation over allegations linking her to Hasina family London property dealings.

Hasina married Dr. M. A. Wazed Miah (1942-2009) — a Bangladeshi nuclear scientist who died 9 May 2009. The couple had one son — Sajeeb Ahmed Wazed ("Sajeeb Wazed Joy"), US-based ICT expert who served as ICT advisor to multiple Hasina governments — and one daughter — Saima Hossain Wazed, psychologist who became Regional Director of WHO South-East Asia on 1 February 2024. The Hasina family has five grandchildren.

### Education

Hasina attended Tungipara primary school and Government Intermediate Girls' College, Azimpur in Dhaka. She studied at Eden College, Dhaka — where she served as Vice-President of the College Students' Union. She earned a Bachelor of Arts in Bengali Literature from the University of Dhaka in 1973.

### Early political activism

In 1969 Hasina was an active student leader at Eden College during the mass upsurge against Pakistani military ruler Ayub Khan. During the 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War, Hasina, her mother Fazilatunnesa, her sister Sheikh Rehana, and her brothers were imprisoned by the Pakistani occupation army at the Mujibur Rahman Dhanmondi residence in Dhaka. Her husband M.A. Wazed Miah was also detained.

## The 15 August 1975 family massacre

On 15 August 1975 — at roughly 4:30 AM — Bangladesh Army officers led by Major Syed Faruque Rahman and Major Khondaker Abdur Rashid assassinated Sheikh Mujibur Rahman at his Dhanmondi 32 residence in Dhaka. The massacre killed roughly 18 family members and aides: Mujibur Rahman; his wife Fazilatunnesa; their sons Sheikh Kamal, Sheikh Jamal, and 10-year-old Sheikh Russel; daughters-in-law Sultana Kamal and Rosy Jamal; Mujibur Rahman's brother Sheikh Naser; and multiple aides and family members. The military coup that followed installed Khondaker Mostaq Ahmad as President; the post-1975 Bangladeshi military-and-political-class architecture operating across the General Ziaur Rahman (1975-1981) and General Hussain Muhammad Ershad (1982-1990) periods is the central institutional context of Hasina's pre-1996 political career.

Hasina and Sheikh Rehana survived the family massacre because both were in West Germany at the time visiting Hasina's husband M.A. Wazed Miah. The 1975-1981 exile in India operated under the Indira Gandhi government's documented protection. The post-1981 Hasina-and-Indian-state institutional relationship is the central documented architecture of the Awami League political-class' Indian government alignment.

The 1996-2001 Hasina government enacted the Bangabandhu Murder Trial proceedings; 12 convictions were obtained; multi-year executions of multiple convicted assassins were carried out across 2009-2020 under the second Hasina premiership.

## Political career 1981-1996

In 1981 — while still in New Delhi exile — Hasina was unanimously elected President of the Bangladesh Awami League in her absence. On 17 May 1981 she ended six years of exile and returned to Bangladesh. The 1981-1990 period operated through the multi-year opposition leadership during the military dictatorships of General Ziaur Rahman (the husband of the future BNP leader Khaleda Zia; Ziaur Rahman was assassinated 30 May 1981 in Chittagong) and General Hussain Muhammad Ershad (1982-1990 military dictator).

In the 1986 parliamentary election Hasina won three seats and was elected Leader of the Opposition in the Third Parliament. The 1990 mass movement against Ershad — in which Hasina led the Awami League opposition movement — produced Ershad's December 1990 resignation. Hasina announced the constitutional formula for peaceful transfer of power through Articles 51 and 56. In the 1991 general election the Awami League lost to the BNP under Khaleda Zia; Hasina became Leader of the Opposition in the Fifth Parliament.

The Hasina-Khaleda Zia rivalry is the central architecture of post-1990 Bangladeshi politics. Hasina is the daughter of Mujibur Rahman (Awami League founder); Khaleda Zia is the widow of Ziaur Rahman (BNP founder). The 1991-onward two-party Awami League-BNP architecture is the documented dynastic-and-apparatus of post-1990 Bangladeshi democracy.

## First Premiership 1996-2001

On 23 June 1996 Hasina became the 10th PM of Bangladesh after the Awami League's victory in the 12 June 1996 general election. The first premiership produced documented achievements: the 30-year Ganges Water Sharing Treaty with India (December 1996) — the central institutional Indo-Bangladeshi water-sharing architecture; the Chittagong Hill Tracts Peace Accord (December 1997) — ending the armed conflict between the Bangladeshi government and the Parbatya Chattagram Jana Samhati Samiti; the Bangabandhu Bridge over the Jamuna river (opened June 1998) — the central transportation infrastructure connecting eastern and western Bangladesh; multi-year food security programmes; and the social safety net programmes (widow/disabled/freedom-fighter allowances; Shanti Nibas elder care; Ashrayan homeless programme; One House-One Farm scheme).

In the 1 October 2001 general election the Awami League lost to the BNP-Jamaat-e-Islami coalition under Khaleda Zia. On 15 July 2001 Hasina left office.

## Opposition period 2001-2008

The 2001-2008 opposition period operated under the Khaleda Zia BNP-Jamaat government. The central documented event of the period was the 21 August 2004 grenade attack on an Awami League rally in Dhaka — 24 party supporters killed including Awami League Women's Secretary Ivy Rahman. Hasina survived. The multi-year investigation produced the October 2018 special-court verdict finding BNP Senior Vice-Chairman Tarique Rahman (in absentia; eldest son of Khaleda Zia) and former intelligence officials guilty of orchestrating the attack through abuse of state power. The 1 December 2024 Bangladesh High Court (post-Hasina ouster) acquitted Tarique Rahman and others — a multi-year legal reversal that operated under the post-2024 Yunus interim government architecture covered in Dossier 28.

In 2004 SAMS Kibria — Hasina's close advisor and former Finance Minister under the 1996-2001 government — was assassinated in a grenade attack in Sylhet.

The 2007-2008 period operated under the military-backed caretaker government under Lt. Gen. Moeen U Ahmed. In 2007 Hasina was arrested on corruption charges by the caretaker government and detained; released July 2008. The 29 December 2008 general election under the caretaker government produced an Awami League landslide victory with 230 of 300 parliamentary seats. On 6 January 2009 Hasina took oath as PM for her second term.

## Second-through-fifth Premiership 2009-2024

### The 2009 Bangladesh Rifles revolt

On 25-26 February 2009 — six weeks into Hasina's second premiership — the Bangladesh Rifles (BDR) mutinied at the BDR headquarters at Pilkhana, Dhaka. The two-day revolt killed 74 people including 57 Bangladesh Army officers. The multi-year ICT BDR mutiny trials produced multiple convictions. The 30 November 2025 re-investigation commission report (formed by the post-Hasina interim government on 23 December 2024) characterised the incident as a planned operation rather than a spontaneous revolt and alleged involvement of Awami League figures including Sheikh Fazle Noor Taposh (Hasina's nephew) and Hasina herself.

### Economic record 2009-2024

The second-through-fifth Hasina premiership operated through multi-year GDP growth averaging 6-7% annually — making Bangladesh one of the fastest-growing economies in the world across the multi-year period. The multi-year ready-made garment (RMG) export expansion made Bangladesh the world's second-largest garment exporter after China — operating on the documented multi-year labour-cost advantage and the multi-year Bangladeshi export-oriented industrialisation. The remittance-driven foreign exchange architecture operated through millions of Bangladeshi migrant workers in the Gulf, Malaysia, and Singapore. By 2022 GDP growth reached 7.2%; the wealthiest 10% of the population controlled 41% of total income.

The flagship infrastructure projects produced documented multi-year Hasina-era achievements: the Padma Bridge (opened 25 June 2022 — multi-billion-dollar bridge over the Padma river connecting Dhaka with the southwestern region; self-financed by the Bangladeshi government after the World Bank's 2012 cancellation amid corruption allegations against Hasina associates); the Dhaka Metro Rail Line 6 (opened 28 December 2022); the Bangabandhu Tunnel under the Karnaphuli River in Chittagong (opened 28 October 2023); the Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant (Russian-built; under construction from 2017 with multi-year operational launch); and multi-year power-generation expansion from roughly 5,000 MW (2009) to over 11,000 MW (2014) and over 27,000 MW (2024) per Hasina government figures.

### The International Crimes Tribunal 2010-2016

In 2010 the Hasina government revived the International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) under the International Crimes (Tribunals) Act 1973 to prosecute 1971 Liberation War crimes. The multi-year ICT trials produced multiple convictions and executions of BNP-Jamaat figures: Abdul Quader Mollah (Jamaat; executed 12 December 2013); Mohammad Kamaruzzaman (Jamaat; executed 11 April 2015); Ali Ahsan Mohammad Mujahid (Jamaat; executed 22 November 2015); Salahuddin Quader Chowdhury (BNP; executed 22 November 2015); Motiur Rahman Nizami (Jamaat-e-Islami chief; executed 11 May 2016); and Mir Quasem Ali (Jamaat; executed 3 September 2016).

In 2013 the Bangladesh Supreme Court banned Jamaat-e-Islami from electoral participation citing inconsistency with Bangladesh's secular constitution. The multi-year 2013-2014 BNP-Jamaat-led violence opposing the ICT trials produced hundreds of deaths; nationwide protests; the multi-year Awami League-BNP-Jamaat institutional confrontation. The Shahbag movement of February-April 2013 — the youth-and-civil-society mass mobilisation demanding Jamaat leaders' execution — was the documented post-2011 institutional support architecture for the ICT trials.

The ICT trials operated under documented multi-year fair-trial concerns from Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, and the UN OHCHR; the multi-year international scholarly literature on the ICT has characterised the tribunal as having had "lack of independence and history of unfair proceedings."

### Electoral record under fraud allegations

The 5 January 2014 general election was boycotted by the BNP and other opposition parties citing the Hasina government's removal of the caretaker-government provision (constitutional amendment passed June 2011). The Awami League won 234 of 300 seats. The election was characterised by multiple international observers as not free and fair.

The 30 December 2018 general election produced an Awami League landslide of 257 of 300 seats with documented 96% vote share in contested seats. The election was widely characterised by Bangladeshi media, international observers (the Commonwealth Observer Group, the EU Election Observation Mission), and the multi-year scholarly literature as marred by vote-rigging, ballot-stuffing, and intimidation. The pre-election context: BNP leader Khaleda Zia had been jailed in February 2018 on multi-year embezzlement and graft cases (the Zia Orphanage Trust corruption case and others) and was not permitted to contest. The BNP won only seven seats. Hasina rejected the rigging allegations.

The 7 January 2024 general election was boycotted by the BNP and other opposition parties. The Awami League won 224 of 300 seats. Voter turnout was roughly 40% per official Election Commission figures; multiple international observers, Bangladeshi journalists, and post-election scholarly analysis characterised the actual turnout as significantly lower than the official figure. On 11 January 2024 Hasina was inaugurated for her fourth consecutive term and fifth overall. The documented multi-year post-2014 characterisation of Hasina by multiple international observers — that she had "the authoritarian leader of a one-party state" — is the central institutional characterisation of the 2014-2024 Bangladeshi political architecture.

### Human rights record 2009-2024

The multi-year documented human rights record of the 2009-2024 Hasina tenures includes documented hundreds of enforced disappearances, extrajudicial killings, custodial torture, and the multi-year crackdown on opposition political parties, journalists, and civil society.

In December 2021 the US Department of the Treasury imposed Magnitsky-style sanctions on the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB — the elite Bangladeshi paramilitary force) and seven current and former officials including the then-Inspector General of Police Benazir Ahmed for documented hundreds of enforced disappearances and extrajudicial killings since 2009. The sanctions were the central documented US institutional action against the Hasina government across the period.

The multi-year US State Department human rights reports, the multi-year EU Parliament resolutions, the multi-year UN OHCHR and Special Rapporteur reporting, the multi-year Human Rights Watch reporting, the multi-year Amnesty International reporting, and the multi-year Odhikar (Bangladeshi human rights organisation) reporting documented the pattern across the 2009-2024 period.

The Digital Security Act 2018 produced multi-year documented prosecutions of journalists, opposition activists, and civil society figures. The Cyber Security Act 2023 (the documented post-2023 replacement) operated under international press-freedom criticism. Reporters Without Borders ranked Bangladesh 162 of 180 in its 2024 Press Freedom Index — among the lowest globally.

### Foreign policy

The documented Hasina-and-Indian-state institutional relationship functioned as the central foreign-policy architecture of her 2009-2024 tenures. The 2014 onward Modi government in India operated multi-year bilateral economic, security, and intelligence cooperation with the Hasina government. The September 2023 G20 New Delhi summit produced the documented Hasina-Modi bilateral meeting.

The multi-year Russian, Chinese, and Japanese bilateral relationships operated alongside the central Indian relationship. The Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant operated under Russian Rosatom construction; the multi-year Chinese Belt-and-Road infrastructure investment (Padma Bridge Rail Link; Dhaka-Chittagong Highway; multiple ports); the multi-year Japanese ODA infrastructure (Dhaka Metro Rail; multiple highways and ports).

In May 2024 — three months before her ouster — Hasina made a documented public characterisation that "a white country" was plotting to topple her government and had offered her trouble-free elections in January 2024 if she allowed the country to set up an airbase in Bangladesh. The characterisation was widely interpreted as referring to the United States; the documented US-Bangladesh relationship across the 2021-2024 period had produced multi-year US pressure on Hasina over the documented human rights record, the December 2021 RAB sanctions, the multi-year visa restrictions on Bangladeshi officials for "undermining the democratic election process," and the multi-year US Department of State public characterisation of the 2024 election as unfair.

## The July 2024 quota reform movement and uprising

### 1-15 July 2024 protests

On 5 June 2024 the Bangladesh High Court reinstated a 30% government-jobs quota for descendants of 1971 Liberation War "freedom fighters" — the quota that the 2018 quota reform movement had previously suspended. On 1 July 2024 students at the University of Dhaka began protests against the reinstatement. The protests spread to multiple universities across Bangladesh through early July under the documented Students Against Discrimination (Boishommo Birodhi Chhatra Andolon) banner. The 14 July 2024 Hasina public statement characterising the protesters as "razakar" (Bengali pejorative for 1971 Pakistani collaborators) was the documented incendiary characterisation that produced the post-14 July escalation.

### 15-21 July 2024 — the crackdown begins

On 15-16 July 2024 the Awami League's Bangladesh Chhatra League (BCL — the AL student wing) attacked protesters on multiple campuses including Dhaka University. On 16 July 2024 protester Abu Sayed was shot dead at Begum Rokeya University, Rangpur — the documented iconic image of the movement that produced multi-year international coverage. On 19 July 2024 the documented Rampura Thana crackdown killed at least 11. On 21 July 2024 the Bangladesh Supreme Court reduced the quota system from 30% to 5% — but the protests continued and expanded into a movement demanding Hasina's resignation.

### 22 July - 4 August 2024 — the nationwide crackdown

The 22 July - 4 August 2024 period operated through the documented nationwide deployment of Bangladesh Police, the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB), the Border Guard Bangladesh (BGB), and the Awami League Bangladesh Chhatra League (BCL) against protesters. Multi-day internet blackouts; multi-day mobile-services shutdowns. On 4 August 2024 the documented nationwide internet blackout commenced at noon; an indefinite curfew was imposed from 6 PM; all courts were closed; a three-day general holiday was declared from 5 August. On 4 August 2024 alone nearly 100 were killed.

### 5 August 2024 — "July 36"

On 5 August 2024 the documented "Long March to Dhaka" by Students Against Discrimination produced protesters storming the Ganabhaban Prime Minister's official residence in Dhaka. Around mid-morning Hasina resigned as PM. In the afternoon Hasina and Sheikh Rehana fled Dhaka by Bangladesh Air Force helicopter and subsequently by C-130 transport aircraft to Hindon Air Force Base near Delhi, India. The day — 5 August 2024 — has been commemorated as "July 36" in Bangladeshi post-2024 institutional commemoration.

The Awami League Dhanmondi 32 residence (Mujibur Rahman's home and the central post-1975 commemorative memorial) was burned down by protesters. The multi-day commemorative-vandalism architecture targeting Awami League institutional sites across Bangladesh was the documented post-5-August symbolic-political-class repudiation of the Mujibur Rahman dynastic political-class.

### Death toll

The UN OHCHR fact-finding report produced the documented estimate: up to 1,400 killed between 15 July and 5 August 2024, with up to 25,000 injured. Per Prothom Alo reporting: 624 deaths between 16 July and 16 August 2024, of which 354 occurred before Hasina's resignation; 66 children among the dead. Documented atrocities: the Chankharpul massacre (Hasina personally implicated by the November 2025 ICT verdict for ordering); the 5 August 2024 Ashulia immolation killings (viral video documented police officers stacking dead bodies on a van at Ashulia Police Station).

The July-August 2024 crackdown produced the largest documented post-1971 episode of state-led mass killing of Bangladeshi civilians by Bangladeshi state forces. The documented death toll of roughly 1,400 in three weeks is the central documented post-1947 episode of South Asian state violence against a peaceful protest movement.

## The Yunus interim government and the November 2025 ICT death sentence

On 5 August 2024 evening Bangladesh Army Chief of Staff General Waker-uz-Zaman convened a meeting with BNP, Jatiya Party, and Jamaat-e-Islami representatives. On 8 August 2024 Muhammad Yunus (Nobel Peace Prize laureate 2006) was sworn in as Chief Adviser of the interim government — covered in Dossier 28.

The post-August 2024 Yunus interim government's apparatus produced four documented actions targeting the Awami League and the Hasina family. First, the 23 October 2024 banning and designation of the Bangladesh Chhatra League as a terrorist organisation under the Anti-Terrorism Act. Second, the 23 December 2024 commission to re-investigate the 2009 BDR mutiny — the 30 November 2025 report alleging Hasina involvement. Third, the May 2025 banning of the Awami League's political activities. Fourth, the multi-year ICT proceedings against Hasina that produced the 17 November 2025 death sentence.

### The ICT proceedings

On 19 August 2024 the ICT initiated investigations into "mass murder" cases against Hasina including charges related to the killing of 450 protesters. In October 2024 the ICT issued an arrest warrant for Hasina and 45 others. In November 2024 Hasina failed to appear before the tribunal as ordered. In June 2025 prosecutors filed formal charges; a second arrest warrant was issued. A state lawyer was appointed to defend Hasina in absentia.

The trial was characterised by Amnesty International, the UN OHCHR, Human Rights Watch, and Hasina's spokesperson former Minister of Information and Broadcasting Mohammad Ali Arafat as having multi-year fair-trial deficits: the ICT was characterised as having "lack of independence and history of unfair proceedings"; the rapid trial pace was characterised as raising "significant fair trial concerns for a case of this scale and complexity"; the documented time to prepare a defence was characterised as "manifestly inadequate"; the documented denial of defence cross-examination of evidence was characterised as a documented violation of fair-trial standards.

### 17 November 2025 verdict

On 17 November 2025 the ICT in Dhaka — three-judge panel — found Sheikh Hasina guilty of crimes against humanity on five charges: incitement of murder of protesters; ordering demonstrators be hanged; ordering the use of lethal weapons, drones, and helicopters against largely unarmed protesters; bearing command responsibility for mass killings including the Chankharpul massacre and the Ashulia immolation killings; and failure to prevent or punish documented atrocities. The sentence: death penalty in absentia.

Former Home Minister Asaduzzaman Khan Kamal was also sentenced to death in absentia. Former Inspector General of Police Chowdhury Abdullah Al-Mamun — the only accused in custody — was pardoned from the death penalty after turning state witness; he was sentenced to five years' imprisonment. The court ordered confiscation of Hasina's and Asaduzzaman's properties in favour of the state.

Hasina's 17 November 2025 statement from Delhi rejected the verdict as "biased and politically motivated." She characterised the ICT as a "kangaroo court" and the verdict as having been "made by a rigged tribunal established and presided over by an unelected government with no democratic mandate." She characterised the call for the death penalty as revealing "the brazen intent of extremist figures within the interim government to remove Bangladesh's last elected prime minister and nullify the Awami League as a political force."

Amnesty International's Secretary General Agnès Callamard characterised the verdict: "this trial and sentence is neither fair nor just"; "the death penalty simply compounds human rights violations"; "the unprecedented speed of this trial in absentia and verdict raises significant fair trial concerns for a case of this scale and complexity." The UN OHCHR characterised the verdict as "an important moment for victims" while regretting "the imposition of the death penalty, which we oppose in all circumstances."

The November 2025 verdict made Hasina the first Bangladeshi head of government, sitting or former, to have been given capital punishment through judicial proceedings. The multi-year ironic institutional context: the ICT that issued the November 2025 verdict was the same tribunal that Hasina's 2009 government had revived in 2010 to prosecute 1971 Liberation War crimes. The BNP, Jamaat-e-Islami, and the post-2024 National Citizen Party (NCP — the political formation produced from the July 2024 movement leadership) welcomed the verdict.

## The India extradition demands

In November 2025 the Bangladeshi government demanded that India hand over Hasina under the 2013 India-Bangladesh extradition treaty. Article 6 of the treaty allows extradition refusal "if the offence of which it is requested is an offence of a political character" — the documented provision that the multi-year Indian legal characterisation has operated through.

India has not formally responded to the multiple Bangladeshi extradition demands across the 2024-2026 period. The April 2026 South China Morning Post reporting characterised India as having opened bilateral talks on extradition with Bangladesh — the documented post-2024 multi-year India-Bangladesh institutional architecture.

The documented Indian-state protection of Hasina — from the 1975-1981 Indira Gandhi-protected Delhi exile through the post-5-August-2024 Modi government protection — is the central architecture of the Awami League-and-Indian-state alignment that Dossier 21 documents from the Indian side. The post-2024 continuing Hasina exile in Delhi is the central documented contemporary case study of South Asian dynastic political-class operating through cross-border state-instrument protection.

## The Bangladeshi political-class architecture

The Bangladeshi political-class architecture documented across this dossier operates through four institutional pillars.

First, the dynastic-party architecture. The Awami League under the Mujibur Rahman-Hasina dynasty is the dynastic-political party of post-1971 Bangladesh. The BNP under the Ziaur Rahman-Khaleda Zia-Tarique Rahman dynasty is the documented alternating dynastic party. The Jamaat-e-Islami serves as the post-1971 Islamist opposition (Bangladeshi affiliate of the Pakistani Jamaat-e-Islami covered in Dossier 24). The multi-year alternation of power between the Awami League and the BNP across the 1990-2024 period is the documented dynastic alternation architecture.

Second, the military political-class architecture. The Bangladesh Army has produced documented influence over Bangladeshi politics: the 15 August 1975 Mujibur Rahman assassination; the post-1975 military rule under General Ziaur Rahman; the 1982-1990 General Ershad dictatorship; the 2007-2008 military-backed caretaker government; the documented Army institutional autonomy. The 5 August 2024 Hasina ouster operated through the decision by COAS General Waker-uz-Zaman not to fire on protesters — the documented military institutional non-intervention that produced Hasina's flight.

Third, the paramilitary-and-security-services architecture. The Bangladesh Rifles / Border Guard Bangladesh (BGB); the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB) under US Treasury sanctions since December 2021; the Police Special Branch; the Directorate General of Forces Intelligence (DGFI); the National Security Intelligence (NSI). The multi-year documented enforced-disappearance and extrajudicial-killing record of the security architecture is the pattern.

Fourth, the India-Bangladesh apparatus covered above.

## The record's verdict

The single most institutional pattern of post-2009 Bangladeshi politics is the Hasina-led Awami League consolidation of dynastic-and-authoritarian political-class authority. The consolidation operated on two tracks. The economic track: the multi-year 6-7% GDP growth and the flagship infrastructure architecture. The authoritarian track: the multi-year electoral fraud architecture (2014 BNP boycott; 2018 vote-rigging and pre-election Khaleda Zia jailing; 2024 BNP boycott), the US-sanctioned RAB-and-paramilitary extrajudicial-killing record, the Digital Security Act press-freedom crackdown, the December 2021 US Treasury Magnitsky-style RAB sanctions, and the July-August 2024 crackdown on the quota reform movement that produced up to 1,400 dead and the 5 August 2024 ouster.

The performance is the surviving daughter of Bangabandhu and the dynastic political-class operator of post-1971 Bangladesh. The operation is the documented dynastic-and-authoritarian political-class operator whose post-2009 apparatus produced the central documented case study of post-1971 Bangladeshi democratic backsliding — and whose July-August 2024 crackdown produced the documented massacre of roughly 1,400 protesters in three weeks.

The biographical record comprises five chronological blocks.

Origins and exile (1947-1981): the 1947 Tungipara birth as Mujibur Rahman's eldest daughter; the 1969 mass upsurge student activism; the 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War family imprisonment at Dhanmondi; the 15 August 1975 family massacre that killed her father and 17 other family members; the 1975-1981 New Delhi exile under Indira Gandhi protection; the 1981 in-absentia election as Awami League President; the 17 May 1981 return.

Opposition and first term (1981-2001): the 1986 three-seat election and Opposition Leadership; the 1990 mass movement against Ershad; the 1991 Opposition Leadership; the 23 June 1996 - 15 July 2001 first PM term producing the 1996 Ganges Water Sharing Treaty, the 1997 CHT Peace Accord, and the 1998 Bangabandhu Bridge.

Return to power (2001-2014): the 2001-2008 Opposition period; the 21 August 2004 grenade attack producing 24 Awami League deaths including Ivy Rahman; the 2007 caretaker-government arrest; the 29 December 2008 Awami League 230-seat landslide; the 6 January 2009 second PM term inauguration; the 25-26 February 2009 BDR revolt; the 2010 ICT revival producing the multi-year executions of BNP-Jamaat figures; the 2013 Jamaat-e-Islami electoral ban and Shahbag movement; the 5 January 2014 BNP-boycotted 234-seat election.

Consolidation (2014-2024): the flagship infrastructure projects (Padma Bridge 2022; Dhaka Metro 2022; Bangabandhu Tunnel 2023; Rooppur NPP); the multi-year RAB-and-paramilitary documented enforced-disappearance and extrajudicial-killing record; the December 2021 US Treasury RAB sanctions; the 30 December 2018 257-seat landslide with Khaleda Zia pre-election jailing; the 2018 Digital Security Act crackdown; the 7 January 2024 BNP-boycotted 224-seat election; the 11 January 2024 fourth-consecutive inauguration; the May 2024 "white country" airbase claim.

Collapse and aftermath (July 2024 onward): the 1 July 2024 quota reform protests; the 14 July 2024 "razakar" statement; the 16 July 2024 Abu Sayed killing at Begum Rokeya University Rangpur; the 19 July 2024 Rampura Thana 11 deaths; the 21 July 2024 SC quota reduction; the 22 July - 4 August 2024 nationwide crackdown producing up to 1,400 dead per UN OHCHR; the documented Chankharpul massacre and the 5 August 2024 Ashulia immolation killings; the 5 August 2024 Hasina resignation and Air Force helicopter flight to Hindon AFB Delhi with Sheikh Rehana; the 5 August 2024 storming of Ganabhaban and the burning of the Dhanmondi 32 Mujibur Rahman residence; the 8 August 2024 Yunus interim government; the 23 October 2024 Bangladesh Chhatra League terrorist designation; the 23 December 2024 BDR re-investigation commission; the May 2025 Awami League political-activities ban; the 17 November 2025 ICT death sentence in absentia for crimes against humanity covering Chankharpul and Ashulia among five charges; the November 2025 Bangladesh extradition demands to India; and the continuing exile in Delhi under documented Modi government protection.

## Bridge to Dossier 28

The post-Hasina interim government under Muhammad Yunus and the post-2024 Bangladeshi political-class architecture are documented in Dossier 28.

# Dossier 28: Xi Jinping

## The operating principal of the contemporary Chinese political-class architecture

Xi Jinping has operated as General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party since November 2012, Chairman of the Central Military Commission since the same date, and President of the People's Republic of China since March 2013. Three terms have run to date. A fourth term is expected to begin at the 21st Party Congress in autumn 2027. As of the date of writing in May 2026, Xi is 72 years old and has held supreme operational position in the Chinese political-class architecture for 13 years.

His tenure has produced the most centralised political-class consolidation in the PRC since Mao Zedong. The 2018 constitutional amendments removing presidential term limits eliminated the structural check that the post-Deng Chinese architecture had built into its succession system after 1982. The 20th Politburo Standing Committee announced October 2022 contains seven members all loyal to Xi: Xi himself, Li Qiang (Premier), Zhao Leji, Wang Huning, Cai Qi, Ding Xuexiang, Li Xi. No counterweight, no faction-balancing, no institutional brake operates inside the apex body. Steve Tsang, Director of the SOAS China Institute at the University of London, characterised the 20th PSC after its constitution as a body where "there is no counterweight or checks and balances in the system at all."

The contemporary Chinese political class is not the Maoist architecture and is not the Dengist architecture. The contemporary Chinese political class is the architecture Xi has built across his three terms and is currently building toward his fourth. The dossier documents who he is, what he has done, who is around him, and what the architecture he has produced operates to extract.

## Lineage and the princeling architecture

Xi Jinping was born on 15 June 1953 in Beijing. He is a princeling — the son of a CCP revolutionary and the offspring of the apex layer of the founding revolutionary architecture of the PRC.

His father Xi Zhongxun (15 October 1913 – 24 May 2002) joined the Communist Party at age 14 while imprisoned for an assassination attempt on a pro-government teacher at his school in Shaanxi. He participated in the Weihua Uprising of March 1928, the Liangdang Uprising of March 1932, and the founding of the Shaanxi-Gansu Border Region Soviet Area in March 1933 alongside Liu Zhidan. He was arrested in 1935 in a Communist internal purge and released after Mao Zedong's arrival in the Long March in November 1935. Mao gave him calligraphy in 1943 reading "the party's interests come first." Xi Zhongxun held positions in the PRC after the 1949 founding including head of the CCP Publicity Department, vice-premier, and vice-chairperson of the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress. He was a member of the Eight Elders — the most revolutionary leadership cohort — in the 1980s. He was one of the architects of the Special Economic Zones programme in the early 1980s.

His mother Qi Xin was Xi Zhongxun's second wife, married in 1944. Xi Jinping's older half-siblings from his father's first marriage include Xi Zhengning (researcher in the Ministry of Defence), Xi Heping (died during the Cultural Revolution, most likely by suicide under duress), and Xi Ganping. His older full sisters are Qi Qiaoqiao (born 1949) and Qi An'an (born 1952). Xi Jinping has one younger full brother, Xi Yuanping.

Xi's early childhood was passed in the guarded Beijing compound architecture of the apex CCP leadership. He attended the elite Beijing Bayi (August 1st) School — a facility linked to the People's Liberation Army and frequented by children of high-ranking officials — and Beijing No. 25 School. In 1962, when Xi was nine, his father was purged by the Mao apparatus for allegedly supporting a sympathetic novel about the CCP and was imprisoned. The 1966 Cultural Revolution intensified the persecution. The family's privileged position collapsed; the children of disgraced officials were targeted as "counter-revolutionary offspring." In January 1969, aged 15, Xi was sent down to Liangjiahe village in Yan'an Prefecture, Shaanxi, under Mao's "Up to the Mountains and Down to the Villages" movement.

He lived in a yaodong (a cave-house) at Liangjiahe. He submitted ten unsuccessful applications to join the CCP — the applications repeatedly denied because his father remained politically rehabilitated. The applications succeeded only in early 1974 when a new commune secretary recognised what biographers have characterised as Xi's organisational capability. He served as Party branch chairman of the Liangjiahe Brigade. In 1975 he was recommended by the CCP Yanchuan County Committee to one of the slots Tsinghua University allocated to Yanchuan County as part of the worker-peasant-soldier student programme.

From 1975 to 1979 he studied chemical engineering at Tsinghua University in Beijing. The 1975-1979 Tsinghua training under Cultural Revolution conditions has been characterised by biographers as politically formative rather than academically rigorous. The chemical engineering degree was not the basis of his subsequent career. The Tsinghua connection was — Tsinghua and Peking University operate as the central recruiting institutions for the contemporary Chinese political-class architecture, parallel to the Ivy League / Oxbridge architecture across the Atlantic and UK dossiers.

The princeling formation produced an operator with three institutional features: lineage access to the most CCP revolutionary network; Cultural Revolution biographical legitimacy (sent-down youth, rural party-branch experience, manual labour); and elite educational credentialing through the Tsinghua programme. The combination operated to qualify him for the institutional sponsorship that subsequent princelings would receive across the 1980s-2000s as the founding revolutionary generation transitioned out of operational positions.

## Provincial career 1979-2007

Upon graduation from Tsinghua in 1979, Xi became personal secretary to General Geng Biao — then concurrently vice-premier, Defence Minister, and secretary of the military affairs commission. Geng Biao had been purged alongside Xi Zhongxun during the Cultural Revolution; the appointment operated through the rehabilitated network. In this position Xi likely had contact with Zeng Qinghong, then secretary to State Planning Commission chief Yu Qiuli, himself a leading military figure. Zeng would subsequently operate as Xi's sponsor in the apex CCP architecture and as Vice President under Hu Jintao.

In 1982 Xi was appointed county-level party boss in Zhengding County, Hebei province. He held the position from 1982 to 1985. In 1985 he visited the United States as part of a corn delegation from Hebei. He traveled to Iowa, Oregon, and California. He stayed two nights with an American family in a town south of Des Moines, Iowa, and was reportedly impressed by the summertime local baseball game and community barbecue — the small-town-Iowa cultural exposure operating as the rare extended biographical contact with the US population of any contemporary apex Chinese political-class operator.

From 1985 to 2002 Xi served in Fujian province across a sequence of positions: party secretary of Xiamen (1985-1988), party secretary of Ningde Prefecture (1988-1990), party secretary of Fuzhou (1990-1996), Fujian deputy governor (1995-1999), Fujian acting governor (1999-2000), and Fujian governor (2000-2002). The 17-year Fujian tenure was characterised by biographers as stalled. Xi's career took off only after his father's death in May 2002. In October 2002 he was reassigned to Zhejiang province as deputy party secretary; in November 2002 he became acting governor; in 2003 he became Zhejiang party secretary, the top provincial position. The Zhejiang appointment placed him in the apex provincial-secretary tier of the CCP architecture.

During the 2002-2007 Zhejiang tenure, the province reportedly averaged 13-14% GDP growth per year. The province transitioned increasingly away from heavy industry toward private-sector export processing. Xi's anti-corruption posture in Zhejiang earned him national media attention. His chief of staff from 2004 to 2007 was Li Qiang, then secretary-general of the Zhejiang Party Committee, who would subsequently rise to operate as Xi's Premier in the 20th PSC.

In March 2007 Xi was reassigned to Shanghai as party secretary, replacing Chen Liangyu — who had been fired and was being investigated for mishandling of municipal pension funds. The Shanghai appointment placed Xi in the commercial-and-financial-centre party-secretary position immediately before the 17th Party Congress. In October 2007, at the First Plenary Session of the 17th Central Committee, Xi was elected to the Politburo Standing Committee at age 54 — ranked sixth of nine members, ahead of Li Keqiang (seventh). The PSC appointment marked him as Hu Jintao's heir apparent.

## Vice presidency and ascent 2008-2012
