In March 2008 Xi was elected Vice President of the PRC. He focused publicly on conservation efforts and international relations during his vice presidency. In October 2010 he was named Vice Chairman of the Central Military Commission — the governing step that had been the gateway to the presidency under both Jiang Zemin and Hu Jintao.

The 2008-2012 vice presidency was the apprenticeship in the apex architecture. Xi's friendships with the generation of party elders consolidated. Wang Qishan, who had been a close friend of Xi since both were sent-down youths in neighbouring counties of Yan'an in the early 1970s, operated as Xi's closest peer across the period. Wang's father-in-law Yao Yilin had been a major supporter of Jiang Zemin in the apex CCP architecture. Wang's close friendship with Jiang Zemin's son Jiang Mianheng further connected the Xi-Wang network to the Jiang Zemin architecture.

In November 2012, at the 18th Party Congress, Xi was elected General Secretary of the CCP — succeeding Hu Jintao — and assumed the chairmanship of the CMC. On 14 March 2013, the 12th National People's Congress confirmed him as President of the PRC. The transition was the most orderly transition of the PRC apex apparatus to date.

## First and second terms 2012-2022: the consolidation

The first term (2012-2017) was characterised by three operational features.

First, the anti-corruption campaign. From January 2013, Xi launched the most intensive anti-corruption campaign in PRC history. Between 2012 and 2022, roughly 4.7 million CCP members were investigated for corruption per the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) cumulative data. targets included former PSC member Zhou Yongkang (former internal-security chief, life imprisonment 2015); former CMC vice-chairmen Xu Caihou (died 2015 awaiting trial) and Guo Boxiong (life imprisonment 2016); former Chongqing party secretary Bo Xilai (life imprisonment 2013); and roughly 200+ officials at the ministerial or vice-ministerial level. The campaign's posture was characterised by Xi as targeting both "tigers and flies" — the apex and the mid-level officials simultaneously. Outside analysts characterised the campaign as a combination of genuine anti-corruption operation and apex political-class consolidation that eliminated potential rivals.

Second, the centralisation of decision-making. Xi created leading small groups across major policy areas (economic reform, foreign affairs, national security, Internet, military reform) with himself as chairman, displacing the Premier-led State Council coordination architecture that had operated since Deng. The Politburo Standing Committee that had operated as a collective-leadership architecture under Jiang and Hu was reorganised around Xi's preferences. The September 2014 dropping of the post-Tiananmen "seven up, eight down" age retirement rule's strict application began to operate as the apparatus reshaped to Xi's preferences.

Third, the assertive foreign-policy posture. The "China Dream" framework articulated 29 November 2012 — Xi's first major speech as General Secretary, delivered at the National Museum of China — invoked the "great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation" by 2049 (the centenary of the PRC). The May 2014 framework "major-country diplomacy with Chinese characteristics" replaced the Deng-era "hide your strength, bide your time" posture with an explicitly assertive international position. The Belt and Road Initiative announced September 2013 in Astana (now Astana) was the vehicle for projecting Chinese economic and diplomatic influence across Eurasia and Africa. The South China Sea island-building programme accelerated through 2014-2016 — Central Party School's *Study Times* reported the decision was personally taken by Xi.

The second term (2017-2022) consolidated the architecture. The 19th Party Congress in October 2017 enshrined "Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era" in the CCP constitution — a designation parallel to "Mao Zedong Thought" and "Deng Xiaoping Theory" but applied while the operator was still in office, unique in the post-1949 architecture. The 19th PSC was constituted without an obvious successor at age 54 or younger (the threshold for a candidate to serve two PSC terms before assuming the General Secretary position) — the operational signal that Xi intended to remain beyond two terms.

In March 2018 the 13th National People's Congress passed constitutional amendments including the removal of term limits on the President and Vice President positions and the creation of the National Supervisory Commission. The term-limit amendment was the architecture for Xi's continued operation beyond the 2023 second-term endpoint that the post-Deng architecture would otherwise have required.

The 19 January 2018 launch of the three-year Special Campaign to Crack Down on Organized Crime and Eliminate Evil ran through 2020. The 2018 People's Armed Police reassignment to sole CMC control consolidated the internal-security architecture under Xi's command.

The COVID-19 institutional response (January 2020 onward) — characterised externally as the "zero-COVID" architecture — served as the central public-health programme of the Xi second term. The November 2022 "white paper" protests across Shanghai, Beijing, and other cities produced the reversal of the zero-COVID posture by early December 2022. The reversal was the only operational concession by the Xi apparatus to mass-mobilisation pressure across the three-term tenure.

The Hong Kong architecture was reorganised under Xi. The 1 July 2020 National Security Law for Hong Kong was imposed by the NPC Standing Committee, bypassing the Hong Kong Legislative Council. The arrests of pro-democracy figures including Jimmy Lai, the Apple Daily executives, and the Stand News editorial staff dismantled the pro-democracy political and media architecture that had operated in Hong Kong since the 1990s. The 2024-2025 Hong Kong Article 23 domestic security legislation completed the architecture.

The Xinjiang programme — the mass internment, ideological-reeducation, and labour-discipline programme operating against the Uyghur Muslim population since roughly 2017 — has been characterised by the US State Department (January 2021), the UK Parliament (April 2021), the Canadian House of Commons (February 2021), the Dutch Parliament (February 2021), and roughly 12 other state-level designations as "genocide and crimes against humanity." The August 2022 UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights report characterised the operations as "serious human rights violations" possibly constituting "crimes against humanity." Multi-year corporate-supply-chain pressure including the 2021 US Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act has produced the pressure that Xi's apparatus has rejected as Western interference in Chinese domestic affairs. The internal architecture has continued operating.

## Third term 2022-2027

In October 2022, at the 20th Party Congress, Xi was unanimously elected to his third term as CCP General Secretary. The 20th PSC was announced consisting of seven members all loyal to Xi: Xi himself, Li Qiang (Premier), Zhao Leji, Wang Huning, Cai Qi, Ding Xuexiang, Li Xi. The composition was the most centralised PSC since the early Mao period.

On 10 March 2023 Xi secured an unprecedented third five-year term as President of China — the sole candidate, receiving votes of all 2,952 NPC delegates.

The third term has produced four major operational features.

**The PLA purges.** Since 2023, Xi has overseen the most extensive military anti-corruption campaign of his tenure. targets have included:
- Li Shangfu (former Defence Minister): expelled from CCP 27 June 2024.
- Miao Hua (CMC member, Director of CMC Political Work Department): suspended for investigation 28 November 2024.
- Wei Fenghe (former Defence Minister): purged.
- He Weidong (CMC Vice Chairman): purged.
- Ma Xingrui (former Xinjiang Party Secretary, Politburo member): placed under investigation in 2026.

Bloomberg News reported October 2025 that at least 14 of the 79 generals appointed under Xi's leadership had been ousted. The PLA purges have intensified rather than slowed in the third term. Multiple analyses have characterised the pattern as reflecting either continued anti-corruption operation or apex political-class consolidation against potential rivals — including potentially Zhang Youxia, the CMC Vice Chairman widely characterised as the second-most-PLA operator after Xi.

**The PLA modernisation programme.** The 2024 PLA reforms dissolved the Strategic Support Force and established three new arms: the Aerospace Force, the Cyberspace Force, and the Information Support Force. Chinese military spending has more than doubled since 2012 and reached a record US$282 billion in 2026. The PLA Navy has grown rapidly under Xi — China has added more warships, submarines, support ships, and major amphibious vessels during certain years than the UK or US. Aircraft carriers Shandong (2019) and Fujian (2025) entered service under Xi. A third carrier (Type 004, nuclear-powered) is reported in construction.

**The economic management.** Chinese GDP has grown from ¥54.8 trillion (US$8.7 trillion) in 2012 to ¥140.2 trillion (US$20.1 trillion) in 2024. Chinese nominal GDP per capita has risen from ¥40,431 (US$6,408) in 2012 to ¥99,665 in 2024. The Made in China 2025 plan's majority of goals were considered achieved by 2024 — Chinese electric vehicle, solar panel, battery, drone, and select semiconductor production operate at global leadership scale, with multi-year US-led restrictions on advanced semiconductor and AI-chip exports operating to constrain the most advanced subset. The Chinese property sector stress (Evergrande default September 2021; Country Garden default 2023; multi-firm subsequent sector contraction) is the central economic-management challenge of the third term. The 2023-2025 Chinese deflationary pressure, youth-unemployment elevation, and consumer-confidence decline operate as additional pressures the Xi apparatus has managed without producing the kind of large-scale fiscal stimulus that Western analysts have repeatedly characterised as required.

**The international posture.** The 16 May 2024 Putin state visit to Beijing produced the joint statement characterising the Sino-Russian relationship as "no limits" partnership. The 3 September 2025 military parade in Tiananmen Square featured Xi, Putin, and Kim Jong Un standing side by side — the unprecedented public solidarity display of the three operators against the US-led architecture. The 11 April 2025 Xi first public response to the US-China trade war (under Trump 2's January 2025 reescalation) struck a defiant note. The May 2026 Xi-Trump summit produced an agreement under which China would boost US beef and poultry trade — the bilateral concession operating against the escalating tariff and export-control architecture. Trump's 16-17 May 2026 characterisation of Taiwan as a "good negotiating chip" with China produced the Taiwanese institutional response — President William Lai's 17 May 2026 defence of Taiwanese US arms purchases — and reflected the structural ambivalence of the contemporary US administration position on Taiwan.

The Sino-Indian relationship remains contested. The 2020 Galwan Valley deadly clash on the Line of Actual Control has been followed by disengagement negotiations producing partial pullbacks. The Sino-Japanese relationship deteriorated in 2025 following Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi's comments on potential defence of Taiwan. The Sino-EU relationship is contested across the EU "de-risking" framework versus the most member-state engagement with China including the 2025 EU summit visit by member-state leaders to Beijing.

## Fourth term 2027-2032 and the succession question

The 21st Party Congress is expected to convene in autumn 2027. The base-case expectation across China-leadership analysts is that Xi will be elected to a fourth term as General Secretary, Chairman of the CMC, and (in March 2028) President.

The Politburo Standing Committee composition at the 21st Party Congress will operate as the central institutional indicator of Xi's succession planning. The "seven up, eight down" age rule — strict under Jiang and Hu, increasingly nominal under Xi — would require six of the seven current PSC members to retire if applied strictly: only Ding Xuexiang (born 1962, age 65 in 2027) would qualify to remain. Xi at 74 in 2027 would be exceeding the rule, but the rule has been treated as not applying to him since the 2018 constitutional amendments.

Ding Xuexiang is the most-named potential successor in the contemporary China-leadership analytical literature. Ding's profile: born 1962 in Jiangsu province; engineer by training (materials science); worked his way up through Shanghai municipal apparatus from the 1990s; became Xi's chief of staff (Director of the CCP General Office) in 2017; promoted to PSC at the 20th Party Congress 2022 as the youngest member. Ding's institutional position as Xi's longtime personal chief of staff places him in the structural pathway that has historically produced successors (Hu Jintao, Xi Jinping himself). Ding would be 65 in 2027, 70 by 2032 (when Xi's fourth term ends), and 73 by 2035 (Xi's announced midpoint year for "socialist modernization").

Outside the 20th PSC, the next-generation candidates are provincial party secretaries and deputy provincial party secretaries born in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Zhou Zuyi, Fujian Provincial Communist Party Committee Secretary (born 1965), and roughly a dozen 1970s-born deputy provincial party secretaries appointed across 2024-2026 — Zhuge Yujie (Hubei), Shi Guanghui (Inner Mongolia), Zhu Zhongming (Shanghai), Yang Jinbai (Hainan), Liu Xiaotao (Jiangsu), Guo Ningning (Fujian), Lu Dongliang (Shanxi) — operate as the candidate pool from which the 2027 Politburo or PSC elevations would draw.

Politicians born in the late 1960s would have been university students during the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests. The Xi apparatus has characterised this cohort as potentially having been influenced by what the apparatus characterises as Western ideas — the position that has produced the preference for the late-1950s cohort that built the current 20th PSC and the preference for the 1970s cohort that may build the 21st PSC successor architecture, with the late-1960s cohort partially bypassed.

The question of whether Xi will name an heir apparent at the 21st Party Congress is the central succession-question indicator. The 19th Party Congress in 2017 produced no obvious successor; the 20th Party Congress in 2022 produced no obvious successor. If the 21st Party Congress in 2027 produces no obvious successor, the succession question would be deferred to the 22nd Party Congress in 2032 (when Xi would be 79) or to the 23rd Party Congress in 2037 (when Xi would be 84).

The historical analogues for political-class operators serving beyond the most post-revolutionary architecture's institutional retirement window include Mao Zedong (operated until death at age 82 in 1976), Deng Xiaoping (operated as paramount leader in practice until 1989, formally retained "Chairman of the CMC" title until 1989, died 1997 at age 92), Jiang Zemin (retained "Chairman of the CMC" until 2004 at age 78, died 2022 at age 96), and Hu Jintao (retired completely 2012 at age 70 — the anomalous orderly retirement of the post-Mao architecture). The Xi trajectory points more toward the Mao, Deng, and Jiang models than the Hu model.

## The personal architecture

Xi's first marriage was to Ke Lingling, daughter of Ke Hua, the former PRC ambassador to the United Kingdom. The marriage produced no children. Ke Lingling now lives in the UK. The first marriage ended in divorce.

Xi's second marriage was to Peng Liyuan on 1 September 1987. Peng is a famous Chinese folk and opera singer who held the rank of major general in the People's Liberation Army. She served as president of the PLA General Political Department Song and Dance Troupe and as president of the PLA Art Institute. Peng has appeared in the Chinese state-media architecture across the Xi tenure as the central first-spouse figure. The couple has one daughter, Xi Mingze, born 1992. Xi Mingze received her undergraduate degree in psychology from Harvard University 2010-2014 — the Harvard-undergraduate enrolment operating as the most anomalous Western-elite-credentialing for the daughter of an apex Chinese political-class operator during the period in which the Xi administration was building the anti-Western posture that has defined the Xi tenure. Xi Mingze subsequently pursued graduate studies in psychology at a university in Beijing.

The media architecture in China has carefully managed the public profile of the Xi family. Xi's daughter has been kept entirely outside the public profile. The contrast with the US presidential-family architecture (the public Trump family architecture; the public Biden family architecture across Dossier 1) is institutional rather than personal.

## The architecture Xi has produced

The cumulative architecture Xi has produced across the three-term tenure operates with eight institutional features.

**Feature one: term-limit removal.** The March 2018 constitutional amendments removed presidential term limits. The structural architectural brake that the post-Deng PRC had built against indefinite tenure was eliminated.

**Feature two: PSC composition.** The October 2022 20th PSC was constituted with seven members all loyal to Xi, with no faction-balancing operator. The collective-leadership architecture that operated under Jiang, Hu, and the post-Mao reform tradition has been replaced with the centralised-apex architecture.

**Feature three: Politburo composition.** The 20th Politburo (24 members) was constituted with operators heavily drawn from Xi's Zhejiang, Fujian, and Shanghai networks. The Tuanpai (Communist Youth League) faction that operated as a counterweight under Hu Jintao has been largely eliminated from the apex architecture.

**Feature four: PLA reorganisation.** The 2015-2016 PLA structural reorganisation (Theater Commands replacing Military Regions; restructuring of services and arms; CMC reform) plus the 2024 PLA reforms (dissolution of Strategic Support Force; establishment of Aerospace Force, Cyberspace Force, Information Support Force) have produced the most reorganised PLA in PRC history. The PLA purges have removed roughly 14 of the 79 generals Xi himself had appointed — the pattern reflecting either continued anti-corruption operation or apex political-class consolidation against potential rivals or both.

**Feature five: ideological centralisation.** "Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era" was enshrined in the CCP constitution at the 19th Party Congress in October 2017 and in the PRC state constitution in March 2018. The most ideological architecture has operated to enforce "Xi Thought" study across CCP members, government employees, academic institutions, and increasingly across most Chinese-state-controlled media. The cult-of-personality architecture — books, cartoons, pop songs, dance routines honouring Xi — operates at scale not seen since Mao.

**Feature six: anti-corruption institutional weaponisation.** The CCDI architecture has investigated roughly 4.7 million CCP members across the 2012-2022 first-and-second-term period. The selective-targeting of potential rivals — most visibly in the military purges — has produced the governing pattern that Western analysts have characterised as combining genuine anti-corruption operation with apex political-class consolidation.

**Feature seven: parallel external architecture.** The Belt and Road Initiative architecture (operating since 2013), the BRICS+ architecture (the Iran, UAE, Egypt, Ethiopia, Saudi Arabia full/partial membership 2024-2026 reflecting the most Chinese diplomatic position), the AIIB architecture, the CIPS payment-system architecture, the mBridge architecture, the gold-accumulation architecture, and the Yuan-internationalisation programme operate together as the parallel infrastructure to the post-1944 Bretton Woods institutional order across Volume One and Volume Two Part XIII.

**Feature eight: domestic-surveillance integration.** The Chinese social-credit-system architecture (operating since the 2014 planning-document phase; implementation across multi-municipal pilots), the facial-recognition architecture (roughly 700+ million surveillance cameras nationally per 2023 estimates), the Great Firewall architecture, and the most Xinjiang-internment architecture operate together as the most multi-year operational domestic-population-management architecture of any contemporary state.

The cumulative architecture has produced the most centralised political-class apparatus in the PRC since Mao. The ECON-architecture (state-owned-enterprise reform partially stalled; private-sector pressure under the 2020-2021 Jack Ma / Alibaba episode; property-sector stress; youth-unemployment elevation) operates under the architecture's continued institutional position. The foreign-policy assertiveness (the 3 September 2025 Tiananmen Square parade; the Taiwan pressure exercises; the Senkaku/Diaoyu pressure; the South China Sea island-building) operates under the architecture's assertive posture.
---


---

# Dossier 29: The Chinese Politburo Standing Committee — Six Operators Around Xi

## The Chinese PSC institutional position

The Politburo Standing Committee (PSC) of the Chinese Communist Party is the apex of the Chinese political-class architecture. It is constituted formally by the Politburo of the CCP Central Committee, in practice through the paramount leader's selection, and is the body that conducts policy discussions and decisions on major issues when the larger 24-member Politburo is not in session. The 20th PSC, announced 23 October 2022 immediately after the 20th Party Congress, consists of seven members: Xi Jinping (rank 1, covered in Dossier 28); Li Qiang (rank 2, Premier); Zhao Leji (rank 3, NPC Chairman); Wang Huning (rank 4, CPPCC Chairman); Cai Qi (rank 5, First Secretary of Central Secretariat); Ding Xuexiang (rank 6, Executive Vice Premier); and Li Xi (rank 7, CCDI Secretary).

Of the six members other than Xi, two were retained from the 19th PSC (Zhao Leji, Wang Huning); four are new entrants (Li Qiang, Cai Qi, Ding Xuexiang, Li Xi). All seven have direct personal or professional ties to Xi from his Zhejiang, Fujian, Shanghai, or Beijing chief-of-staff career. None is from the Communist Youth League (Tuanpai) faction that operated as a counterweight under Hu Jintao. None is from the Jiang Zemin Shanghai Gang faction (with the partial exception of Wang Huning, whose career began under Jiang and continued under Hu before consolidating under Xi).

The dismantling of the collective-leadership architecture that operated under Deng, Jiang, and Hu is complete with the 20th PSC. The selective application of the "seven up, eight down" age rule produced four PSC members (Zhao Leji 69, Wang Huning 70, Cai Qi 70, Li Xi 69 — all over 67 at appointment) and removed Li Keqiang and Wang Yang despite their eligibility. The Politburo was reduced from 25 to 24 members; 13 of the 17 non-PSC seats are new; the CYL faction was in practice eliminated at the apex level.

The 20th PSC is the architecture for Xi's third term and his planned fourth term in 2027. The six members around him are the operators whose biographies and institutional positions this record covers.

## Li Qiang (rank 2): Premier of the State Council

Born July 1959 in Rui'an County, Wenzhou, Zhejiang Province. Joined the CCP in 1983. Undergraduate education in agricultural mechanisation at the Zhejiang Institute of Agriculture's Ningbo campus, 1978-82.

Li's career was built in Zhejiang province across roughly three decades. His first provincial government position was in the civil affairs department, providing welfare to the rural poor — the kind of grassroots administrative position that produces the Chinese political-class operator's biographical legitimacy.

In 2002, at age 43, he was appointed party boss of Wenzhou — the youngest person to take that position in over three decades. He established a reputation as a keen supporter of the city's private sector. Wenzhou's private-enterprise architecture had operated since the early reform era as one of the principal experimental zones for non-state economic activity; Li's tenure there built his pro-business credentials.

In 2003 he joined Zhejiang's provincial party committee, less than a year after Xi Jinping took control of Zhejiang as provincial party secretary. In 2004 he was promoted to secretary general of the Zhejiang Party Committee — Xi's chief of staff. From 2004 to 2007, Li served directly under Xi as his chief of staff in the Zhejiang Provincial Party Committee. The 2004-2007 working relationship was the formative connection of Li's apex career. Victor Shih, the China-politics specialist at the University of California San Diego, has characterised the chief-of-staff role in the Chinese provincial architecture as "the enforcer or go-between the party secretary of the province and all the subordinate units." Xi was on his way up but fighting factional struggles in Zhejiang at the time; Li stood by his side. Xi's trust of Li grew through the working partnership.

When Xi left for Shanghai in March 2007 and then for the Politburo Standing Committee in October 2007, Li remained in Zhejiang. He became provincial governor at the end of 2012, immediately after Xi assumed the General Secretary position. In June 2016 he was named Party Secretary of Jiangsu province — an important coastal province that had been beset by scandals involving provincial leaders. The 15-month Jiangsu tenure was the positioning for the subsequent Shanghai appointment.

In October 2017, after the 19th Party Congress, Li was appointed Party Secretary of Shanghai — the most provincial-level municipal-party-secretary position in China. The 2017-2022 Shanghai tenure produced the pro-business institutional record that has defined Li's national profile. He opened the Shanghai Stock Exchange STAR Market (the Chinese equivalent of NASDAQ). He oversaw foreign investment expansion including the Tesla Gigafactory Shanghai construction (operational 2019) — the first wholly foreign-owned car company in China. He eased internal-migrant residency permit requirements. He created five new towns to address the city's land-supply shortage.

In 2022 Li managed the two-month Shanghai COVID lockdown that produced major economic damage. He was criticised across the Chinese commercial-class and the Western analytical literature for the lockdown's economic consequences. The Wall Street Journal reporting characterised Li as one of the few people in the top Chinese leadership who wanted China to introduce Western mRNA vaccines against COVID-19 — the kind of pragmatic posture that distinguished him from the Xi apparatus's official zero-COVID architecture but did not damage his promotion trajectory.

In October 2022, at the 20th Party Congress, Li was promoted to the PSC at rank 2 — bypassing the conventional requirement of prior PSC service. In March 2023, the 14th NPC appointed him as the 8th Premier of the State Council, succeeding Li Keqiang.

Li is the visible operational economic-policy face of the Xi apparatus. He has hosted the foreign business delegations since the post-2023 period — the December 2023 EU foreign-policy delegation, the major-economy commercial-class engagement. He travelled to Australia in June 2024 — the first Chinese leader of his seniority to visit since 2017. The Australian visit was the reset of the China-Australia relationship after the Morrison-era pressure.

Li's institutional position is ranked second on the PSC but his operational position is widely characterised as below the rank-2 formal title would suggest. The pattern by Lowy Institute (Richard McGregor, June 2024), Asia Society Policy Institute (Neil Thomas, October 2024), and Brookings (Cheng Li) is that under the Xi architecture the Premier's State Council leadership operates with less institutional autonomy than the equivalent position operated under Wen Jiabao or Li Keqiang. The Xi-chaired Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission has displaced the State Council leadership in major economic policymaking. The annual Premier press conference at the National People's Congress — a fixture under Wen and Li Keqiang — was cancelled by Xi in 2024. The apparatus surrounding Li Qiang reflects Xi's consolidation rather than Li's autonomy.

Li's wife worked in the Zhejiang provincial transportation bureau before retirement. The couple has one daughter, who studied in Australia.

## Zhao Leji (rank 3): Chairman of the Standing Committee of the NPC

Born March 1957 in Xining, Qinghai Province. Ancestral province Shaanxi. Age 69 as of 2026.

Zhao's career was built in the western provinces. He spent his early decades in Qinghai, rising through the provincial party architecture. He became Qinghai Party Secretary at age 42 — at the time, the youngest provincial party secretary in China. He was subsequently transferred to Shaanxi province as Party Secretary, where the Shaanxi connection — Xi Jinping's ancestral province — was the foundation for his subsequent connection to the Xi apparatus.

From 2012 to 2017 Zhao served as head of the CCP Organization Department — the body responsible for personnel selection and promotion across the CCP architecture. The Organization Department position was the structural position through which Xi's personnel-selection preferences were operationalised across the apex of the CCP architecture during the first-term consolidation.

From 2017 to 2022 Zhao served as Secretary of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) — the party's anti-corruption watchdog. He succeeded Wang Qishan in this role. The CCDI under Zhao was the vehicle for Xi's continuing anti-corruption campaign, including the investigations of officials at the ministerial and vice-ministerial levels. Roughly 4.7 million CCP members were investigated for corruption across the 2012-2022 first-and-second-term period; Zhao's 2017-2022 CCDI tenure managed the second-term phase.

In October 2022 Zhao was retained on the PSC and promoted to rank 3. In March 2023 he succeeded Li Zhanshu as Chairman of the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress — the largely ceremonial legislature that meets in full session once a year and whose deliberations are mainly conducted behind closed doors by the smaller standing committee.

Zhao's institutional position as NPC Chairman places him at the apex of the legislative architecture, but the legislative architecture itself is the governing ratification mechanism for the PSC's apex decisions rather than as an independent law-making body. The real institutional weight of Zhao's position is in his apex CCP rank, his Shaanxi-Qinghai network connections, his Organization Department personnel-selection legacy, and his CCDI anti-corruption record.

## Wang Huning (rank 4): Chairman of the CPPCC, chief ideologue

Born October 1955 in Shanghai. Age 70 as of 2026. Native Shanghainese.

Wang is the most anomalous PSC operator — the only PSC member in the post-1989 architecture who has never held a regional leadership position. His career was built in academia (Fudan University, where he served as Dean of the Law School) and in the apex CCP Central Policy Research Office.

Wang has served as the principal political-theory operator for three paramount leaders: Jiang Zemin (whose "Three Represents" ideological framework Wang helped craft), Hu Jintao (whose "Scientific Outlook on Development" Wang helped craft), and Xi Jinping (whose "China Dream" framework and "Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era" Wang helped craft). The pattern of serving multiple paramount leaders without faction-loss is unusual in the Chinese political-class architecture; Wang's institutional position has operated as the political-theory continuity throughout the post-Deng leadership transitions.

From 2012 to 2017 Wang served as 19th Politburo member and Director of the CCP Central Policy Research Office. From 2017 to 2022 he served as First Secretary of the Central Secretariat under the 19th PSC. In October 2022 he was retained on the PSC and promoted to rank 4. In March 2023 he was appointed Chairman of the CPPCC — the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference, the party's advisory body that brings together non-CCP political parties, academics, religious figures, and business representatives under CCP supervision.

Wang's institutional position is the chief-ideologue role. His operational outputs include the Xi Jinping Thought enforcement materials, the ideological-education programmes across CCP members and government employees, and the doctrinal foundations for the foreign-policy and domestic-governance positions the Xi apparatus has adopted.

## Cai Qi (rank 5): First Secretary of the CCP Central Secretariat — Xi's enforcer

Born December 1955 in Yongtai County, Fujian Province. Age 70 as of 2026.

Cai's career was built in Fujian and Zhejiang — the two provinces in which Xi Jinping served during 1985-2007. The Fujian-Zhejiang overlap with Xi was the foundation of Cai's apex position.

Cai rose through Fujian provincial positions across the 1980s-2000s and Zhejiang positions across the 2000s. From 2014 he rose rapidly through Beijing positions. From 2017 to 2022 he served as Party Secretary of Beijing — the apex provincial-level position in the capital. The 2017-2022 Beijing tenure produced two institutional outputs: the establishment of the Xiong'an special economic zone (a Xi flagship project intended as a alternative urban centre to relieve Beijing of non-capital functions) and the hosting of the Beijing Winter Olympics in February 2022.

In October 2022 Cai was promoted to the 20th PSC at rank 5 and elected First Secretary of the CCP Central Secretariat — the position that operates as the party's organisational nerve centre. The First Secretary role coordinates the day-to-day operation of the CCP apparatus across organisation, propaganda, united-front, and law enforcement.

The analysis pattern: while Li Qiang is ranked 2 formally, multiple analysts (Lowy Institute, Asia Society, Brookings) have characterised Cai Qi as the real second-most-powerful operator on the PSC. The characterisation operates through two institutional pathways. First, Cai serves as First Secretary of the Central Secretariat — the role that under Hu Jintao was held by Xi himself before his apex elevation. Second, Cai is the visible enforcer of Xi's preferences across the apex CCP architecture — coordinating personnel decisions, propaganda outputs, and institutional discipline across the operations. Richard McGregor at Lowy Institute (June 2024) characterised Cai as "the most powerful person after Xi" — "not because Cai has any independent political personality of his own, nor any achievements in office to match those of Li, but because he has been handed the role of Xi's enforcer."

## Ding Xuexiang (rank 6): Executive Vice Premier — Xi's longtime chief of staff

Born September 1962 in Nantong, Jiangsu Province. Age 63 as of 2026 — the youngest member of the 20th PSC.

Ding's educational and early-career background is unusual for an apex Chinese operator. He trained as a materials-science engineer at Northeast Heavy Machinery Institute and Fudan University. He spent the 1990s in Shanghai municipal positions in research-institution administration. From the late 1990s onwards he rose through Shanghai municipal Party Committee staff positions.

Ding's relationship with Xi began in 2007 when Xi served as Shanghai Party Secretary from March to October 2007. Ding worked as Xi's top political secretary in Shanghai during that period. The brief Shanghai working relationship was the foundation of what has become the longest continuous Xi-staff working relationship in the apex architecture.

In 2013 Xi brought Ding to Beijing as his personal secretary — the Director of the Office of the General Secretary. From 2017 to 2022 Ding served as Director of the CCP General Office — Xi's chief of staff at the central apparatus level. The General Office position operates with sweeping control over information flowing to Xi and access to Xi by officials. Ding has been often the few officials attending sensitive meetings alongside Xi — earning him the sobriquets "Xi's alter ego" and "Xi's chief of staff."

Ding's October 2022 promotion to the PSC was characterised across the analytical literature as unusual because he had never served as a provincial-level party boss or governor — the conventional institutional pathway to PSC promotion. The promotion operated as a reward for personal loyalty to Xi rather than for institutional record. In March 2023 he was appointed Executive Vice Premier — the first-ranked Vice Premier of the State Council, with oversight of development and reform, public finance, taxation, and the environment.

Ding's name has appeared across the succession analysis literature as a potential successor to Xi or to Li Qiang at the 21st Party Congress in 2027. Ding would be 65 in 2027 — within the conventional age range for PSC retention. The base-case among China-leadership analysts (Cheng Li at Brookings, Neil Thomas at Asia Society, Joseph Torigian at American University) is that Ding is the most structurally positioned candidate for the Premier role at the 21st Party Congress if Li Qiang is rotated out (Li will turn 68 in 2027, the conventional retirement age).

## Li Xi (rank 7): Secretary of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection

Born October 1956 in Liangdang, Gansu Province. Age 69 as of 2026.

Li Xi's career was built across the western and southern provinces — Gansu, Shaanxi, Shanghai, Liaoning, Guangdong. The Shaanxi connection — through Xi Jinping's ancestral province — was the foundation of his apex connection.

Li served as Party Secretary of Liaoning province from 2015 to 2017, then as Party Secretary of Guangdong province from 2017 to 2022. The 2017-2022 Guangdong tenure produced the structural record of overseeing the development of the Greater Bay Area economic region — the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao integration project that Xi has characterised as one of his apex economic-development priorities.

In October 2022 Li Xi was promoted to the 20th PSC at rank 7 and appointed Secretary of the CCDI — succeeding Zhao Leji in the anti-corruption position. The CCDI under Li Xi has overseen the expansion of the anti-corruption operations into the PLA architecture in Dossier 28: the expulsion of Li Shangfu (June 2024); the suspension of Miao Hua (November 2024); the purges of Wei Fenghe, He Weidong; the investigation of Ma Xingrui (2026); the Bloomberg-reported ousting of roughly 14 of 79 Xi-appointed generals.

## The surrounding architecture: the broader Politburo

The 20th Politburo consists of 24 members — the 7 PSC members plus 17 additional Politburo members. The 17 non-PSC Politburo members operate as the layer immediately below the PSC, with operational responsibilities across the central CCP departments and the State Council.

named operators in the 17 non-PSC Politburo positions include:

- **Zhang Youxia** (born 1950, age 75 in 2026): CMC Vice Chairman; Shaanxi native princeling; widely characterised as the second-most-PLA operator after Xi; reportedly under investigation in 2026 (though unconfirmed).
- **He Weidong** (born 1957, age 69 in 2026): CMC Vice Chairman; purged.
- **Wang Yi** (born 1953, age 72 in 2026): CCP Central Foreign Affairs Commission Office Director; de facto top foreign-policy operator; concurrently Foreign Minister.
- **He Lifeng** (born 1955, age 71 in 2026): Vice Premier; Fujian Xi network; economic-policy operator.
- **Chen Wenqing** (born 1960, age 66 in 2026): Secretary of the Central Politics and Law Affairs Commission; law-enforcement and security-architecture chief.
- **Li Shulei** (born 1964, age 62 in 2026): Director of Propaganda Department.
- **Shi Taifeng** (born 1956, age 69 in 2026): Director of United Front Work Department; personnel chief.
- **Ma Xingrui** (born 1959, age 66 in 2026): former Xinjiang Party Secretary; placed under investigation in 2026.

The Politburo Secretariat (the body that handles the day-to-day operations of the Central Committee) is led by Cai Qi as First Secretary, with Shi Taifeng, Li Ganjie, Li Shulei, Chen Wenqing, Liu Jinguo, and Wang Xiaohong as additional Secretaries. The Wang Xiaohong appointment as Minister of Public Security plus Secretariat member increased the security architecture's representation in the Secretariat from one to two — an institutional indicator of the security-architecture consolidation under Xi.

## The princeling decline

The 20th PSC contains one princeling: Xi himself. The 18th PSC (October 2012) had contained four princelings; the 19th PSC (October 2017) had contained two; the 20th PSC contains one. The princeling architecture that was the founding-revolutionary-generation institutional pathway is in decline — partly because the founding generation has aged out, partly because Xi's anti-corruption operations have weakened multiple princeling networks, and partly because the governing pathway has shifted toward the provincial-cadre architecture rather than the princeling-network architecture.

The 20th Central Committee has 10 princeling members — down from 41 in the 18th Central Committee per *The Economist* analysis (2022). The Maoist-era princeling-network institutional pathway is no longer the operational route into the apex architecture. The operational route is Xi's personal preference, which operates through the Fujian-Zhejiang-Shanghai network operationally established across the 2007-2022 period.
---


---

# Dossier 30: The Hong Kong Political Class

## The Hong Kong institutional position

Hong Kong became a Special Administrative Region (HKSAR) of the People's Republic of China on 1 July 1997 under the "one country, two systems" framework agreed in the 1984 Sino-British Joint Declaration. The 1997 Basic Law was the territory's mini-constitution and was supposed to guarantee Hong Kong's distinct legal, economic, and political architecture until 2047. The 2014 Umbrella Movement, the 2019-2020 anti-extradition-bill protests, the 2020 National Security Law imposed by the NPC Standing Committee, and the 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance — the four institutional inflection points in the post-1997 period — have together produced the dismantling of the political-class architecture the 1997 framework had been designed to preserve.

The contemporary Hong Kong political class is a categorically different architecture from the pre-2020 architecture. The pro-democracy political-class (the Democratic Party, the Civic Party, Demosistō, the legislator network running from Martin Lee through Joshua Wong) has been dismantled through prosecution, imprisonment, and forced exile. The pro-Beijing political-class (the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong / DAB, the Federation of Trade Unions / FTU, the Liberal Party, the New People's Party) now is the only  permitted political-class in the territory. The 2021 electoral changes excluded pro-democracy candidates from the LegCo and District Council elections; the 2021 LegCo turnout of 30.2% and the 2023 District Council turnout of 27.5% were the lowest in HKSAR history.

## John Lee Ka-chiu — the operating principal

John Lee was born on 7 December 1957 in Hong Kong. His ancestral roots are in Panyu, Guangzhou. He grew up in a working-class family in So Uk Estate, Kowloon. He graduated from Wah Yan College, Kowloon — a Roman Catholic Jesuit secondary school whose alumni include Hong Kong professional-class operators. He achieved 4A grades in the Hong Kong Certificate of Education Examination and 1A in the Hong Kong Advanced Level Examination — academic results sufficient to qualify him for the undergraduate engineering programme at the University of Hong Kong.

Lee declined the HKU offer. He has said that being robbed during a school mountain-climbing trip produced the conviction that public safety was the priority of his career. In 1977, at age 19, he joined the Hong Kong Police Force as a Probationary Inspector. He would remain in the police force for 35 years.

While serving in the police force, Lee completed a Master's degree in Public Policy and Administration from Charles Sturt University, Australia. He rose through the force across the career: Assistant Commissioner (Crime), Director of Crime and Security, Deputy Commissioner (Management). His operational record produced two visible career episodes that the post-2022 Chinese state-media architecture has emphasised.

The 1994-1995 drinks-spiking gang case: a gang injected drugs into paper-packaged drinks, committed over 20 crimes in six months. Lee led the case; the gang leader was subsequently sentenced to 14 years.

The 1998 Zhang Ziqiang case: Lee was head of the Organized Crime and Triad Bureau. Zhang was the most-wanted criminal in Hong Kong at the time, with charges including looting, kidnapping, and murder. Lee arranged 24/7 monitoring of Zhang's gang, which had stored 800 kilograms of explosives and over 2,000 detonators in a village in the New Territories. On 17 January 1998 the explosives were seized; on 22 July 1998 Zhang and 17 suspects were arrested; the following week another 13 were arrested. The case operated through close cooperation between Hong Kong Police and mainland Chinese police — the pathway that would define Lee's subsequent career trajectory.

In 2003 Lee became Assistant Commissioner. In 2010 he was promoted to Deputy Commissioner of Police. In 2012, with the new Leung Chun-ying administration, he was appointed Under Secretary for Security — the cross-over from career police into government appointment that the Hong Kong architecture has used for select police operators since the post-1997 period.

In August 2012 Lee relinquished his British citizenship to satisfy the legal requirement for the Under Secretary position. His wife and two children retained their British citizenship — making Lee himself eligible for right of abode in the UK through dependency. The asymmetry between Lee's institutional position as a Hong Kong national security operator and his family's continued UK citizenship has been characterised by some Hong Kong observers as illustrative of the broader pattern in which the pro-Beijing operating class maintains foreign-citizenship escape routes for their families while imposing patriotism requirements on the population.

In 2017, under the new Carrie Lam administration, Lee was promoted to Secretary for Security — the cabinet position responsible for the police, immigration, customs, fire services, correctional services, and the multi-pathway disciplined services architecture. The 2017-2021 Secretary for Security tenure produced the two operational events that defined Lee's national profile.

The first was the 2019 extradition bill. Lee was instrumental in pushing the bill that would have allowed extradition from Hong Kong to mainland China, sparking the most extensive protests in HKSAR history. Protests began June 2019; estimated peak participation reached roughly 2 million on 16 June 2019 — roughly 27% of Hong Kong's population, well above the Chenoweth-Stephan 3.5% threshold in Part XIV Chapter 1. The Hong Kong police response, under Lee's institutional supervision as Secretary for Security, included tear gas, rubber bullets, water cannons, beanbag rounds, and at times live ammunition. Multi-thousand protesters were arrested. The October 2019 anti-mask law operated to identify protesters through facial recognition. Lee characterised the protests at various points as "terrorism" and "violence."

The second was the 2020 National Security Law. Imposed by the NPC Standing Committee on 30 June 2020, bypassing the Hong Kong Legislative Council, and effective 1 July 2020, the NSL criminalised four categories of conduct (secession, subversion, terrorism, collusion with foreign forces) with a maximum penalty of life imprisonment and extraterritorial reach. The Office for Safeguarding National Security was established in Hong Kong directly under Beijing's Central People's Government — the architecture that has operated outside HKSAR judicial control since.

Lee oversaw the NSL's operational implementation as Secretary for Security through 2021. On 7 August 2020 the US Treasury OFAC sanctioned Lee — alongside Carrie Lam and 9 other Hong Kong and mainland officials — for "undermining Hong Kong's autonomy." Lee subsequently characterised the sanctions as "bullying" and said they "strengthened my belief that what I am doing is right."

In June 2021 Lee was promoted to Chief Secretary for Administration — the number-two position in the HK government, succeeding Matthew Cheung. The Chief Secretary role oversees 9 policy bureaux and coordinates across the multi-bureau apparatus. Lee held the position until 6 April 2022, when he resigned to enter the Chief Executive race.

## The 2022 Chief Executive election

The 2022 Chief Executive election was conducted under the post-2021 electoral framework. The Election Committee had been restructured from 1,200 to 1,500 members, with new sectors added (e.g., "national" representatives appointed by Beijing) and pro-democracy representation eliminated. The Candidate Eligibility Review Committee — established under the 2021 electoral changes — was the structural gatekeeper for who could stand.

Lee was the sole candidate approved to stand. On 8 May 2022 he won 1,416 votes from the 1,500-member Election Committee — roughly 99%. On 20 May 2022 he was appointed by the Central People's Government as the 6th-term Chief Executive. He assumed office on 1 July 2022 — the 25th anniversary of the handover.

The selection of a career security operator as Chief Executive — rather than the career-civil-servant pattern that had produced Donald Tsang, Leung Chun-ying, and Carrie Lam — was the signal that Beijing's preference for the post-2020 Hong Kong was the security-architecture priority over the commercial-architecture priority. Sonny Lo, a Hong Kong political analyst, characterised the selection to the *New York Times*: "It conforms with the trend of securitisation in mainland China in the past 10 years, especially after Xi Jinping came to power."

Lee became the third Hong Kong Chief Executive to come from a policing background. The pattern is institutional: the contemporary HKSAR is governed through the security architecture rather than the pre-2020 commercial-and-bureaucratic architecture.

## The Lee administration 2022-2026

The Lee administration has produced four operational features.

**The 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance.** Article 23 of the 1997 Basic Law required HKSAR to enact its own national security legislation. The obligation had not been fulfilled in 27 years — Tung Chee-hwa's 2003 attempt produced a 500,000-person protest that forced withdrawal. Lee's administration fulfilled the obligation: public consultation began 30 January 2024; the bill was proposed 8 March 2024; signed into law 23 March 2024.

The 2024 Ordinance expanded the NSL architecture: treason, insurrection, sedition, theft of state secrets and espionage, sabotage, and external interference all added as offences with up to life imprisonment. Detention without charge was extended to 16 days. Access to lawyers during detention was restricted. The Ordinance operates alongside the 2020 NSL.

**The continued NSL prosecutions.** The Jimmy Lai trial began in December 2023. Lai, founder of the *Apple Daily* newspaper that had been forced to close in June 2021, faces collusion-with-foreign-forces charges and could receive life imprisonment. The trial has run through 2024-2025 with delays. The Hong Kong 47 trial — the mass prosecution of 47 pro-democracy operators arrested in January 2021 — produced May 2024 convictions and November 2024 sentencing across the 47 individuals. By late 2024 roughly 290+ individuals had been arrested under the NSL since July 2020.

**The bounty programme.** In July 2023 the Hong Kong government announced bounties of HK$1 million each for 8 democrats living overseas: Nathan Law (UK), Ted Hui (Australia), Anna Kwok (US), Elmer Yuen (US), Dennis Kwok (US), Mung Siu-tat (UK), Finn Lau (UK), Kevin Yam (Australia). Lee said: "we will pursue them for the rest of our lives even if they run to the ends of the earth"; he called them "street rats." Subsequent bounty rounds in December 2023 and December 2024 expanded the list. The bounties operate as the cross-jurisdictional extension of the NSL — the governing pathway through which the Hong Kong national security architecture reaches into the diaspora.

**The economic-rebranding programme.** Lee's administration has prioritised attracting foreign investment to offset the multi-year departure of professionals (roughly 200,000-500,000 emigrants 2020-2024 by various estimates) and the multi-year departure of multinational regional headquarters to Singapore, Tokyo, and Dubai. The February 2023 Middle East trip — including the campaign to encourage Saudi Aramco's secondary IPO in Hong Kong — operated as one institutional component. The 2023-2024 "Hello Hong Kong" tourism-recovery programme operated as another. The November 2024 Hong Kong-Saudi Arabia ETF cross-listing operated as another.

The cumulative operational record of the Lee administration is the consolidation rather than the reversal of the post-2020 architecture. The pro-democracy political-class has been dismantled. The press architecture has been dismantled (*Apple Daily* closed June 2021; *Stand News* closed December 2021; *Citizen News* closed January 2022; multiple smaller outlets closed subsequent). The civil-society architecture has been dismantled (the Confederation of Trade Unions disbanded September 2021; the Professional Teachers' Union disbanded August 2021; the Hong Kong Alliance — which had organised the annual 4 June Tiananmen commemoration since 1990 — disbanded September 2021). The judicial architecture has been compromised (foreign judges from the UK, Australia, and Canada have resigned from the Court of Final Appeal — Lord Sumption in June 2024, additional resignations).

## The pro-democracy political-class dismantled

The pro-democracy political-class architecture that operated from 1990 to 2020 has been  dismantled.

**Martin Lee** (born June 1938), the "father of Hong Kong democracy," co-founded the Democratic Party in 1994. Across a 30-year career he was the principal voice of pro-democracy operation in the LegCo and the international advocacy network. In April 2021 he was convicted alongside activists Margaret Ng, Cyd Ho, and Albert Ho for organising an unauthorised assembly in August 2019. The conviction was the reduction of pro-democracy political-class leadership to criminal-conviction status.

**Jimmy Lai Chee-ying** (born December 1947), the founder of *Apple Daily* and one of the principal Hong Kong commercial and media operators of the pre-2020 period, has been imprisoned since 2020. His NSL trial began December 2023; the verdict has been subsequently delayed. Lai's HK$5.8 billion in personal and business assets were frozen. He faces life imprisonment.

**Joshua Wong Chi-fung** (born October 1996), the student-activist leader of the 2014 Umbrella Movement and the international face of Hong Kong democracy, has been imprisoned multiple times since 2020. He pleaded guilty to NSL charges in 2023 and was sentenced in May 2024.

**Agnes Chow Ting** (born December 1996), Joshua Wong's Demosistō co-founder, fled to Canada in December 2023 after being released on bail. Hong Kong subsequently issued an arrest warrant for her and threatened to revoke her passport.

**Nathan Law Kwun-chung** (born July 1993), the youngest-ever Hong Kong legislator (elected 2016, age 23), fled to the UK in 2020. The Hong Kong government placed a bounty on him in July 2023.

**Benny Tai Yiu-ting** (born July 1964), the legal scholar who co-founded Occupy Central with Love and Peace (the Umbrella Movement's institutional predecessor), was convicted in the Hong Kong 47 case; sentenced to 10 years in November 2024.

**Cardinal Joseph Zen Ze-kiun** (born January 1932), retired Bishop of Hong Kong and one of the most Catholic figures in East Asia, was arrested under the NSL in May 2022 at age 90 for his role as trustee of the 612 Humanitarian Relief Fund. He was convicted in November 2022 and fined.

**Cardinal Zen, Margaret Ng, Cyd Ho, Sze Ching-wee, Hui Po-keung, Denise Ho** (the trustees of the 612 Humanitarian Relief Fund) faced charges that was the reduction of Hong Kong religious, legal, civil-society, and entertainment-industry figures to criminal-conviction status.

## The pro-Beijing political-class operating

The contemporary pro-Beijing political-class operates through the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB), the Federation of Trade Unions (FTU), the Liberal Party, the New People's Party, and the post-2021 multi-sector Election Committee architecture.

**Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor** (born May 1957), Lee's predecessor as Chief Executive, retired from the position 30 June 2022 and was appointed to the CCP Central Committee at the 20th Party Congress in October 2022. Her appointment to the apex CCP architecture was the rehabilitation of the operator who had presided over the 2019 protests and the 2020 NSL imposition.

**Regina Ip Lau Suk-yee** (born August 1950), the former Security Secretary under Tung Chee-hwa who had been forced out in 2003 over the failed Article 23 legislation, now serves as Convenor of the Executive Council since 2022 — the advisory body to the Chief Executive.

**Andrew Leung Kwan-yuen** (born April 1951), a textile industrialist, has served as President of the LegCo since 2016. Under his presidency the LegCo has passed the post-2021 electoral framework legislation, the 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, and the legislative consolidation of the post-2020 architecture.

**Erick Tsang Kwok-wai** (born 1970), Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs since 2020, has overseen the 2021 electoral changes and the electoral architecture.

**Chris Tang Ping-keung** (born 1965), Secretary for Security since 2021, former Commissioner of Police 2019-2021, oversaw the operational police response to the 2019 protests. Tang now operates as Lee's Secretary for Security — the structural continuity of the security-architecture operators that produced Lee's own rise.

**Paul Chan Mo-po** (born 1955), Financial Secretary since 2017, a PwC career operator before entering government. Chan has produced the budgets that have maintained the Hong Kong fiscal position despite the structural challenges of post-2020 capital and professional outflow.

**Eric Chan Kwok-ki** (born 1961), Chief Secretary for Administration since 2022, a career civil servant who succeeded Lee in the position when Lee became Chief Executive. Chan is the bureaucrat coordinating across the multi-bureau apparatus.

**Joe Chow Yat-ming**, Commissioner of Police since April 2025, succeeded Raymond Siu (Commissioner April 2021-April 2025). Chow is the police head under Lee's apex command.

## The Hong Kong institutional pattern

The contemporary Hong Kong political class is the governing extension of the Beijing political-class architecture in Dossiers 28 and 29. The pathway from Beijing to Hong Kong runs through the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office (HKMAO) — the central-government office in Beijing that oversees Hong Kong policy. HKMAO Director Xia Baolong (since February 2020) has operated as Beijing's apex Hong Kong operator across the multi-year period covering the NSL imposition, the Lee administration installation, and the consolidation. The Liaison Office of the Central People's Government in Hong Kong — headed by Director Zheng Yanxiong since January 2023 — is the central-government operational presence in the territory.

The architecture that the 1997 framework had been designed to constrain — the multi-pathway Chinese-state apparatus operating directly inside Hong Kong — now operates openly. The "one country, two systems" framework operates formally; the reality is the direct integration of Hong Kong governance into the Beijing political-class architecture. The 2047 expiration of the Basic Law guarantees is no longer the inflection point that the Hong Kong commercial and professional class had treated it as. The institutional inflection points have been the 2020 NSL, the 2021 electoral changes, the 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, and the operational implementation. The post-2047 Hong Kong has already been  constituted in the post-2020 period.

## The 2027 succession question

John Lee's 5-year Chief Executive term ends 30 June 2027. The 7th-term Chief Executive election will be held in spring 2027 under the same post-2021 electoral framework that produced Lee's selection. Lee is eligible to stand for a second term. As of May 2026, Lee has not publicly indicated whether he will stand again.

The pattern of post-2020 Hong Kong Chief Executive selection is the same as the pattern of apex CCP succession in Dossier 28: the formal electoral architecture is the ratification mechanism for the apex political-class decision made in Beijing. The 7th-term Chief Executive will be whoever Beijing's apex architecture — operating through HKMAO Director Xia Baolong, the Liaison Office, and the apex CCP Politburo — designates. If Lee stands again, he will be re-elected by the Election Committee under the same roughly 99% vote pattern that produced his 2022 selection. If Beijing prefers a different operator, Lee will not stand and the alternative will be designated.

The candidate pool from which the 7th-term Chief Executive will be drawn includes Lee himself; Eric Chan Kwok-ki (Chief Secretary for Administration, who would be the structural successor under the pre-2020 pattern); Paul Chan Mo-po (Financial Secretary, who is the most commercial-architecture operator in the cabinet); Regina Ip (Convenor of the Executive Council, the pro-Beijing political operator outside the cabinet); and Chris Tang (Secretary for Security, who would represent the continuation of the security-architecture-priority pattern that Lee himself represents).

The 21st CCP Party Congress in autumn 2027 will precede the Hong Kong 7th-term Chief Executive election (spring 2027) by roughly six months. The institutional priority for the Beijing architecture during the period leading up to the apex CCP succession will be operational stability in Hong Kong. The base-case expectation is that Lee will stand for and win a second term — operating the multi-year stability that the Beijing apparatus prefers across the succession period.
---


---

# Dossier 31: The Taiwan Political Class

## The Taiwanese institutional position

The Republic of China on Taiwan is the contemporary East Asian counter-pole to the Hong Kong architecture in Dossier 30. Whereas Hong Kong has been integrated into the PRC apparatus through the 2020 National Security Law and the 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, Taiwan has continued operating as a constitutionally distinct democracy with its own elected president, Legislative Yuan, judiciary, and armed forces. The contemporary Taiwan political class is the architecture that operates this democracy under continuous PRC pressure.

Taiwan's political-class architecture is unique in East Asia for one institutional reason: it is the only Chinese-cultural-sphere polity that has produced a functioning multi-party democracy with peaceful transfer of power between rival political parties. The Republic of China relocated to Taiwan in 1949 after the Kuomintang lost the Chinese Civil War to the CCP. The 1949-1987 martial-law period under KMT one-party rule operated under conditions through the 1947 "228 Incident" and the multi-decade "White Terror" — estimated 18,000-28,000 killed across the KMT crackdown. Martial law was lifted in July 1987 by President Chiang Ching-kuo (son of Chiang Kai-shek). The Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) had been founded in September 1986 in defiance of the still-operative martial-law architecture. The first direct presidential election was held in March 1996.

In the eight presidential elections since 1996, the KMT has won three (Lee Teng-hui 1996, Ma Ying-jeou 2008 and 2012) and the DPP has won five (Chen Shui-bian 2000 and 2004, Tsai Ing-wen 2016 and 2020, Lai Ching-te 2024). The 2024 DPP victory marked the first time since democratisation that any party had won three consecutive presidential elections.

The contemporary Taiwan political class is divided across three institutional formations: the DPP (presently governing); the KMT (the historically dominant party, now in opposition but holding the Legislative Yuan plurality); and the Taiwan People's Party (TPP, founded 2019, operating as the kingmaker third force). The three operate in a fractured Legislative Yuan configuration that has produced the governing pattern characterised by Lai Ching-te's first two years as president-versus-legislature confrontation.

## Lai Ching-te (William Lai) — the operating principal

Lai Ching-te was born on 6 October 1959 in Wanli, then a coastal coal-mining town in Taipei County (now Wanli District, New Taipei City). His father was a coal miner who died in a gas-leak mining accident in 1960 when Lai was an infant. His mother raised six children alone through manual labour. The biographical foundation — working-class origin, single-mother household, coal-mining death — is the distinctive personal background among contemporary East Asian apex political-class operators across this and the prior East Asian dossiers.

Lai was the first student from Wanli Junior High School to be admitted to Taipei Municipal Chien Kuo High School — Taipei's premier public boys' high school. He attended National Taiwan University and initially studied veterinary medicine, later switching to physical medicine and rehabilitation; bachelor's degree 1984. He worked as a tutor through college to pay tuition. After military service he attended National Cheng Kung University in Tainan, earning a medical degree in 1991. He practised as an internal medicine chief physician at two hospitals.

Lai's political involvement began in 1994 when he served as head of a Tainan physicians' association supporting DPP gubernatorial candidate Chen Ding-nan. The 1994 engagement was the pathway from medical practice into political career. In 1996 he won election as Tainan City representative in the National Assembly. In 1998 he was elected to the Legislative Yuan; he was re-elected three times and served from 1999 to 2010. The Citizen Congress Watch civil-society organisation recognised him as "Best Legislator" four years in a row. In 2003 he obtained a Master of Public Health degree from Harvard University while serving as a legislator.

In 2010, Tainan City and Tainan County were amalgamated into the Tainan special municipality under the 2010 Taiwan municipal reorganisation. Lai won the 27 November 2010 mayoral election with 60.41% of the vote. He took office 25 December 2010 as the first Mayor of Tainan. He prioritised fiscal sustainability through zero-based budgeting and abolished the discretionary project funds for city councillors. The fiscal reform produced political friction with the Tainan City Council but reduced city debt and produced a balanced budget by 2015. His infrastructure programme focused on regional integration and disaster resilience — 2013 consolidation of fragmented transit routes into six major bus trunk lines, and a ten-year flood-protection standard.

The 29 November 2014 mayoral re-election produced the largest victory margin of any Taiwanese municipal race that year — Lai won by 45 percentage points with roughly 73% of the vote, the highest percentage in any county or city election since the 1987 lifting of martial law. The 2014 result established him as the most popular DPP politician in Taiwan and the successor candidate to Tsai Ing-wen.

In 2015 Lai produced a localised constitutional crisis by boycotting the Tainan City Council for nearly eight months over Speaker Lee Chuan-chiao's vote-buying allegations. The Control Yuan impeached Lai on 4 August 2015, ruling that his refusal to submit to interpellation violated the Local Government Act. The episode was the early institutional indicator of the confrontational political-class posture that has characterised Lai's subsequent career.

In 2015 Lai declared himself a "pragmatic worker for Taiwanese independence" — the position that has since defined his political-class identity in the cross-Strait context. The pro-independence position is the institutional red line for Beijing, which characterises Taiwanese independence as one of the four institutional triggers for the use of force against Taiwan under the 2005 PRC Anti-Secession Law.

On 8 September 2017 Lai was appointed 26th Premier of the Republic of China by President Tsai Ing-wen. His Premier tenure produced one major institutional controversy: the December 2017 Labor Standards Act amendments increased allowable overtime from 46 hours per month to 138 hours over a three-month period. Lai's public comment characterising longer working hours as workers "doing good deeds" produced national mockery. The protests around the reform contributed to the DPP's major defeat in the November 2018 local elections. Lai resigned as Premier on 11 January 2019.

In November 2019 Lai lost the DPP presidential primary to Tsai Ing-wen. He became her running mate in the 2020 presidential election and was elected Vice President. Tsai's 2020 re-election with 57.13% of the vote — the highest percentage for any president in Taiwan's democratic history — was the structural foundation for the multi-year second-term DPP consolidation.

In 2022 Tsai resigned as DPP Chair after the party's poor showing in the November 2022 local elections. Lai was elected DPP Chair by acclamation in late 2022. On 12 April 2023 the DPP announced Lai as its 2024 presidential candidate. His running mate was Hsiao Bi-khim, the former Taiwan de facto ambassador to the United States who had operated as one of the most diplomatic-network operators for the DPP across the 2020-2023 period.

## The 2024 presidential election

The 13 January 2024 presidential election produced a three-way contest:
- Lai Ching-te (DPP) with running mate Hsiao Bi-khim: 5,586,019 votes — 40.1%.
- Hou Yu-ih (KMT) with running mate Jaw Shaw-kong: 4,671,021 votes — 33.5%.
- Ko Wen-je (TPP) with running mate Cynthia Wu: 3,690,466 votes — 26.5%.

Hou Yu-ih, the New Taipei City Mayor since 2018 and former Director-General of the National Police Agency, was the KMT candidate. He campaigned on a more conciliatory cross-Strait posture — characterised by his opponents as Beijing's preferred candidate. Ko Wen-je, the former Mayor of Taipei 2014-2022 and founder of the TPP in 2019, ran as the third-force candidate appealing primarily to younger voters disenchanted with both the DPP and the KMT. Ko led among voters aged 20-29 in the pre-election polling (43% support per TVBS) versus Hou (26%) and Lai (22%) — the generational divide that has produced the TPP as the operative kingmaker formation in the post-2024 Legislative Yuan.

China characterised the election as a choice between "peace and war" and characterised Lai as a "dangerous separatist." Lai's campaign maintained the position that Taiwan was already an independent sovereign country and that no formal declaration was needed. His running mate Hsiao operated her US-network connections (she had been Taiwan's de facto ambassador to the US for the multi-year pre-campaign period) to maintain US institutional support during the campaign.

The Legislative Yuan election held the same day produced a different result. The 113-seat assembly split: KMT 52 seats plus 2 Blue independents (54 total Blue camp), DPP 51 seats, TPP 8 seats. The DPP lost its Legislative Yuan majority that it had held since 2016. The TPP became the operative kingmaker — its 8 seats could swing votes to either the DPP or the KMT.

The first institutional test was the election of the Legislative Yuan President. The KMT proposed Han Kuo-yu — the 2020 KMT presidential candidate, former Mayor of Kaohsiung 2018-2020 (recalled June 2020), characterised as one of the most divisive operators in the KMT post-2020 apparatus. The DPP proposed You Si-kun (the incumbent). The TPP first voted for its own candidate, then abstained on the second ballot — lowering the quorum and producing Han's election as Legislative Yuan President with KMT and TPP votes. Johnny Chiang (a "light Blue" moderate former KMT Chair) became Vice President.

The outcome: the 2024 elections produced a divided government in which the DPP holds the presidency and the KMT-TPP coalition holds the Legislative Yuan. The cross-Strait pressure operates through the KMT institutional preference for accommodation with Beijing; the DPP institutional preference for autonomy from Beijing; the TPP institutional preference for ambiguity that has produced the coalition with the KMT on legislative matters and the independence from the KMT on cultural and youth-policy matters.

## The Lai presidency 2024-2026

Lai's 20 May 2024 inauguration address was the position-statement for his presidency. He stated that "the Republic of China and the People's Republic of China are not subordinate to each other" — the position the Beijing apparatus characterised as a provocation. He continued the pre-inauguration position that Taiwan was an independent sovereign country and that no formal declaration was needed.

Three days later, 23-24 May 2024, the PLA conducted "Joint Sword-2024A" exercises around Taiwan. The exercises was the immediate institutional response to the inaugural address. Subsequent PLA exercises under Lai's tenure: "Joint Sword-2024B" in October 2024; "Strait Thunder-2025A" in April 2025; near-daily PLA aircraft and naval incursions into Taiwan's Air Defense Identification Zone through 2024-2026.

The DPP-versus-Legislative-Yuan confrontation began in May 2024. The KMT-TPP coalition passed parliamentary bills strengthening Legislative Yuan investigation and review powers — characterised by the DPP and the protest mobilisation ("Bluebird Movement" / 青鳥行動) as an unconstitutional expansion of legislative power against the executive. Lai filed a constitutional challenge; in June 2024 the Constitutional Court upheld Lai's challenge and declared the bills unconstitutional.

In September 2024, TPP leader Ko Wen-je was arrested and investigated for bribery allegations connected to his Taipei mayoral tenure. The TPP characterised the arrest as political persecution. Public opinion was divided. Huang Kuo-chang (former NPP convenor) became the new TPP leader and continued the strengthening of the TPP-KMT legislative coalition.

Late 2024: the KMT-TPP coalition passed revisions to the Constitutional Interpretation Act, the Election and Recall Act, and the Budget Act. The cumulative effect: restriction of executive power; expansion of legislative-investigation power; restriction of constitutional-court referral capacity. In late 2024 the terms of seven Constitutional Court justices expired; the Legislative Yuan rejected Lai's nominees for the seven vacancies. The Constitutional Court entered functional paralysis from late 2024 into 2025-2026 — apparatus that has constrained Lai's capacity to refer legislation for constitutional review.

2025 produced the escalation. Lai announced plans to bring back military trials for active-duty soldiers — a position the KMT institutional opposition characterised as authoritarian. Lai designated China as a "foreign hostile force" under the Anti-Infiltration Act — the governing position that operates the cross-Strait posture into Taiwan's domestic legal architecture.

The opposition parties have compared Lai's posture to that of Yuan Shikai — the historical figure associated with autocratic rule who briefly attempted to restore monarchy in 1916. On 23 December 2025 the KMT and TPP confirmed they would propose an impeachment motion at the plenary session. On 26 December 2025 the motion to initiate impeachment proceedings passed with all KMT and TPP members in support and all DPP members against. The impeachment proceedings against Lai were under way through early 2026.

The 16-17 May 2026 Trump characterisation of Taiwan as a "good negotiating chip" with China produced a institutional ambiguity in the US-Taiwan relationship. On 17 May 2026 Lai defended the multi-year US arms purchases that Taiwan had been making. The Trump administration's apparent willingness to characterise Taiwan as transactional with respect to the US-China relationship is the concern of Lai's post-May-2026 position.

## The DPP architecture

The Democratic Progressive Party was founded 28 September 1986 by roughly 130 pro-democracy operators in defiance of the KMT martial-law architecture. The founding figures included Hsu Hsin-liang, Lin Yi-hsiung, Shih Ming-teh, Yao Chia-wen, and the activist network that had produced the 1979 Kaohsiung Incident. The DPP was the principal opposition party throughout the post-1987 democratisation period.

Tsai Ing-wen (born August 1956), Lai's immediate predecessor, served as DPP Chair 2008-2012 and 2014-2018, and as President 2016-2024. Tsai's 2020 re-election with 57.13% of the vote (8.17 million votes) was the highest percentage for any president in Taiwan's democratic history. Her multi-year tenure produced the architecture of Taiwan's contemporary international positioning, the multi-year arms-procurement programme with the US, and the pandemic response that operated as one of the most successful in the global record.

Hsiao Bi-khim (born August 1971), Lai's Vice President, was Taiwan's de facto ambassador to the United States 2020-2023 — the position that operates as one of the most in the Taiwanese diplomatic network. Hsiao operates the US-Taiwan diplomatic relationship from the multi-year diaspora-network connection (she is half-American, her mother is from Ohio) and the Washington-network connections. She is sanctioned by Beijing alongside Lai.

The internal DPP factional architecture includes the New Tide faction (Lai's faction; pro-independence; institutional pathway), the Tsai/Su system (Tsai Ing-wen and Su Tseng-chang network), the Justice Alliance and Welfare State Alliance factions, and the multi-year Taiwan Normal University and National Taiwan University academic networks. The DPP holds 51 Legislative Yuan seats — one short of the KMT and well below the majority threshold.

Cho Jung-tai (born July 1959), former DPP Chair, was appointed Premier by Lai on 20 May 2024. The Premier role under Lai operates with limited institutional autonomy given the KMT-TPP Legislative Yuan coalition and the president-versus-legislature confrontation.

## The KMT architecture
