The Kuomintang of China was founded 1894 by Sun Yat-sen as the Revive China Society; reorganised multiple times across the 1894-1949 period; ruled mainland China 1927-1949 under Chiang Kai-shek; relocated to Taiwan 1949 and ruled the territory under one-party authoritarian architecture 1949-1987. The 1987 lifting of martial law and the 2000 first DPP presidential victory operated together as the structural transition of the KMT from ruling party to one of two principal political-class operators.

The contemporary KMT operates as the principal opposition party under the Lai presidency. Eric Chu Li-luan (born June 1961), KMT Chair since October 2021, previously the 2016 KMT presidential candidate and Mayor of New Taipei City 2010-2014, is the institutional KMT leader.

Hou Yu-ih (born June 1957), the 2024 KMT presidential candidate, is Mayor of New Taipei City since 2018 and former Director-General of the National Police Agency. The KMT's selection of Hou as 2024 presidential candidate was the governing preference for a moderate-conciliatory cross-Strait posture rather than the explicit pro-Beijing posture associated with the KMT mainland-network architecture.

Han Kuo-yu (born June 1957), President of the Legislative Yuan since 2024, is the most visible KMT operator in the contemporary period. Han served as Mayor of Kaohsiung 2018-2020 (recalled by referendum June 2020 — the first successful recall of an elected official in Taiwan's democratic history). Han was the 2020 KMT presidential candidate (lost to Tsai with 38.6%). His post-2020 institutional career was the multi-year preparation for the 2024 Legislative Yuan leadership position.

Ma Ying-jeou (born July 1950), KMT President 2008-2016, has was the most visible elder statesman of the contemporary KMT. Ma's 2023 and 2024 visits to mainland China — the first former ROC president visits to the PRC — was the pathway through which the KMT has maintained the cross-Strait relationship that the DPP government has not been able to maintain. Ma's position on cross-Strait policy is the KMT institutional preference for the "1992 Consensus" framework that the DPP has rejected.

The KMT holds 52 Legislative Yuan seats plus 2 Blue independents (54 total Blue camp).

## The TPP architecture

The Taiwan People's Party was founded 6 August 2019 by Ko Wen-je. Ko Wen-je (born August 1959), a trauma surgeon at National Taiwan University Hospital before entering politics, served as Mayor of Taipei 2014-2022. His September 2024 arrest for bribery investigation produced the crisis that brought Huang Kuo-chang to the TPP leadership.

Huang Kuo-chang (born March 1973), the current TPP Chair, was a legal scholar and former convenor of the New Power Party (NPP) — the pro-democracy formation that emerged from the 2014 Sunflower Movement. The post-Sunflower-Movement NPP faded across the period; Huang transitioned to TPP leadership through the 2023-2024 political-class reconfiguration.

The TPP holds 8 Legislative Yuan seats — the kingmaker position in the post-2024 fractured Legislative Yuan. The TPP-KMT legislative coalition has been the operative architecture of the post-2024 period.

## Cross-Strait architecture and the contemporary trajectory

The cross-Strait relationship is the central apparatus of the contemporary Taiwan political-class. The PRC operates the One China principle — the position that there is only one China and Taiwan is part of it. The PRC's 2005 Anti-Secession Law identifies four institutional triggers for the use of force: formal Taiwan independence; major event leading to Taiwan independence; loss of any possibility of peaceful reunification; third-state intervention. The PLA modernisation in Dossier 28 operates against this framework.

The US "one China policy" since the 1979 Taiwan Relations Act commits to maintaining unofficial relations with Taiwan and arms sales. The Trump administration's 2025-2026 posture has been ambiguous: the January 2025 arms-package approval continued the multi-year pattern; the May 2026 "negotiating chip" characterisation produced institutional concern.

Taiwan's diplomatic recognition has declined from 22 recognising states in 2017 to roughly 12 in 2025-2026 — Beijing diplomatic pressure has produced the multi-year switching of recognition to the PRC.

TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) is the most strategic Taiwanese commercial-class asset. TSMC produces roughly 90% of advanced semiconductor wafers (3nm, 5nm, 7nm process nodes) — the strategic position for the global supply chains. The Trump administration's multi-year posture has characterised TSMC US investment commitments as the strategic priority; TSMC has committed roughly $200 billion+ in US investment since 2020 (Arizona fabrication facilities; 2025-2026 expansion announcements).

## The 2028 succession question

Lai Ching-te's 4-year presidential term ends 20 May 2028. The Taiwanese constitutional architecture permits two consecutive terms; Lai would be eligible to stand for re-election in January 2028 if not impeached or otherwise removed.

The impeachment proceedings initiated 26 December 2025 operate as the question of whether Lai serves the full first term. The Republic of China constitution requires a two-thirds Legislative Yuan vote to refer impeachment to the Constitutional Court, and a Constitutional Court ruling to remove the President. The Constitutional Court's functional paralysis since late 2024 is the structural architecture that complicates the impeachment outcome.

The 2028 KMT and TPP candidates are not yet selected. The multi-year candidate pool for the KMT includes Eric Chu, Lu Shiow-yen (Mayor of Taichung), Chiang Wan-an (Mayor of Taipei since December 2022), and Han Kuo-yu. The multi-year TPP candidate pool will depend on the Ko Wen-je legal-case outcome and the Huang Kuo-chang leadership operation.

The DPP candidate for 2028 is likely to be a figure from the current cabinet — possibly Premier Cho Jung-tai, possibly Hsiao Bi-khim if Lai is constitutionally prevented from continuing, possibly other DPP operators.
---


---

# Dossier 32: The Japanese Political Class

## The Japanese institutional position

Japan operates as a constitutional monarchy with parliamentary democracy under the 1947 Constitution drafted during US occupation. The Liberal Democratic Party (LDP), founded in 1955 through the merger of the Liberal Party and the Japan Democratic Party, has governed Japan almost continuously throughout the post-war period. The post-war institutional pattern is the longest single-party dominance of any G7 democracy.

The contemporary Japanese political class is the architecture that has governed under the LDP framework for roughly 68 of the 71 years since 1955. The exceptions were two brief interludes: the 1993-1994 coalition government under Morihiro Hosokawa (8 months) and the 2009-2012 Democratic Party of Japan government under Yukio Hatoyama, Naoto Kan, and Yoshihiko Noda (3 years 3 months). Every other prime minister since 1955 has been an LDP operator.

The contemporary LDP architecture has produced six prime ministers across the post-Abe period: Shinzō Abe (returned 2012-2020); Yoshihide Suga (2020-2021); Fumio Kishida (2021-2024); Shigeru Ishiba (2024-2025); and Sanae Takaichi (October 2025–present). The 8 July 2022 assassination of Abe in Nara during a campaign speech was the inflection point for the LDP architecture: it produced the exposure of the LDP-Unification Church institutional connection that the assassin Tetsuya Yamagami had cited as motivation, the slush-fund scandal that engulfed the LDP factional architecture across 2023-2024, the dissolution of most LDP factions in January 2024, and the LDP electoral underperformance that produced the loss of the Lower House majority in October 2024 and the Upper House majority in July 2025.

The Takaichi premiership from October 2025 has was the governing reset of the LDP architecture. The 8 February 2026 snap general election produced a landslide LDP victory with 316+ Lower House seats — the most ever won by a single party in Japanese electoral history, and the first single-party two-thirds House supermajority since World War II. This section covers the operating principal of the contemporary Japanese political class, the LDP architecture surrounding her, the post-Abe context, and the contemporary geopolitical position of the Japanese political class.

## Sanae Takaichi — the operating principal

Sanae Takaichi was born on 7 March 1961 in Yamatokōriyama, Nara Prefecture. Her biographical foundation is unusual for an apex Japanese political-class operator: she did not come from a political family. Her parents were working-class operators who opposed her studying away from home. She attended Kobe University by commuting long distances rather than relocating.

After graduating Kobe University, in 1984 Takaichi entered the Matsushita Institute of Government and Management — the Konosuke Matsushita-founded leadership-training institute that has operated as one of the central recruiting institutions for the Japanese political-class architecture since the post-1980 period. Through a Matsushita dispatch programme she was sent to Washington DC, where she worked on the staff of a Democratic Party US House Representative. The US-staffing experience operated as her early international-network exposure.

From 1989 Takaichi worked as a newscaster for TV Asahi, building her reputation through political analysis. The broadcast-journalism background is unusual for the post-war LDP operator pathway (which has traditionally produced operators from elite bureaucratic and family-political backgrounds) but consistent with the post-Reagan global pattern of political-class operators emerging from television-broadcast careers.

In 1992 she ran for House of Councillors (Upper House) seat in Nara district as an independent candidate. She lost. In 1993 she ran for House of Representatives (Lower House) in Nara district as an independent candidate; she won with the highest vote count in the Nara district. She initially joined Shinshintō (the New Frontier Party) — a liberal opposition formation intent on challenging the LDP. After Shinshintō collapsed following the 1996 general election, the LDP Secretary General identified Takaichi as a lawmaker to recruit. She joined the LDP in 1996.

Takaichi served as Member of the House of Representatives 1993-2003 and again from 2005 onward. The 2003-2005 gap reflects the intermittent electoral fortunes that characterised her early career. Her political mentor was Shinzō Abe. Across the Abe premierships (2006-2007 and 2012-2020), Takaichi held a succession of ministerial portfolios. She served as Minister for Internal Affairs and Communications under Abe 2014-2017 and again 2019-2020 — the longest-serving operator in the MIC role throughout the post-war record. The MIC portfolio operates with responsibility for telecommunications policy and broadcast media regulations — the architecture that aligned with Takaichi's broadcast-journalism background and her Abe-aligned media-policy posture.

Under the Kishida administration 2022-2024, Takaichi served as Minister of Economic Security — a newly-created portfolio that managed the Japanese semiconductor, AI, and critical-infrastructure security architecture. The Economic Security portfolio was the pathway through which Takaichi built her contemporary policy profile on the China-strategic-competition architecture.

In 2021 Takaichi made her first run for the LDP presidency. She lost; Kishida won. She put forward a "Plan to Strengthen the Japanese Economy" — characterised as "New Abenomics" or "Sanaenomics" — built on three institutional pillars: expansionary monetary policy; flexible fiscal spending in response to crises; and bold investment in crisis management and growth.

In 2024 Takaichi ran for the LDP presidency for the second time. She lost again, this time to Shigeru Ishiba. Ishiba's premiership lasted roughly one year before he announced on 7 September 2025 that he would step down. The LDP leadership election on 4 October 2025 produced five candidates in the first round and a runoff between Takaichi and Shinjirō Koizumi (the 44-year-old son of former Prime Minister Junichirō Koizumi). Takaichi won the runoff 185-156 (149 Diet members + 36 prefectural votes versus Koizumi's 145 Diet members + 11 prefectural votes). The runoff result reflected the institutional preference for political experience over generational change — Koizumi at 44 would have been the youngest-ever Prime Minister; concerns about his lack of experience appeared to operate against his candidacy.

On 21 October 2025 the National Diet elected Takaichi the 104th Prime Minister of Japan. She became the first woman to serve as either LDP President or Prime Minister in Japanese history.

The context of her premiership operated under three multi-year pressures. First, the LDP-Kōmeitō coalition that had operated since 1999 ended just before her premiership — Kōmeitō (the Buddhist Sōka Gakkai-affiliated party) ended its 26-year alliance with the LDP. Takaichi negotiated a new coalition agreement with the Japan Innovation Party (Nippon Ishin no Kai, the right-leaning populist formation). Second, the LDP had lost its absolute Lower House majority in the October 2024 election and its Upper House majority in the July 2025 election — making the early Takaichi premiership a minority-government operation. Third, the slush-fund scandal had produced public-trust deficit for the LDP across the 2023-2024 period.

## The 24 October 2025 policy speech and the consolidation

Takaichi's first policy speech at the National Diet on 24 October 2025 was the structural position-statement for her premiership. The speech emphasised five priorities:

1. **Tackling inflation.** Inflation in Japan had operated at multi-year elevated rates relative to the pre-2022 deflationary architecture. Takaichi's response operated through fiscal expansion rather than monetary tightening — the position consistent with her multi-year Sanaenomics framework.

2. **Major fiscal spending.** Takaichi committed to expansionary fiscal policy. Tokyo stocks had rallied to a new record after she took over the LDP leadership in October 2025, reflecting the market expectation of the fiscal expansion.

3. **Defence spending acceleration.** Takaichi announced the target of 2% GDP defence spending would be brought forward from 2027 (the post-Kishida 2022 National Security Strategy target) to March 2026 — the acceleration of roughly one year.

4. **US-Japan alliance continuation.** Takaichi committed to maintaining the US-Japan alliance. President Trump visited Japan in late October 2025; Takaichi hosted Trump in their first meeting in Tokyo. The US-Japan alliance under the Trump 2 architecture has operated through the Trump preference for transactional bilateral commitments rather than the governing alliance architecture; Takaichi's accommodation of the Trump posture produced the characterisation across the foreign-policy commentary as the alignment of the LDP with the Trump 2 administration.

5. **Immigration acknowledgment.** Takaichi acknowledged the need for foreign workers to supplement Japan's declining population — the unusual position for an LDP hard-conservative operator. The declining-population architecture (Japan's fertility rate of roughly 1.2, the demographic contraction, the immigration-policy debate) is the central institutional challenge of the contemporary Japanese political-class architecture.

The post-policy-speech popularity was the highest of any Japanese government in roughly 20 years. Late October and early November 2025 polling produced government approval ratings between 65% and 83%. Approval among young voters was particularly high: 84% in their 20s, 78% in their 30s, against 67% overall.

## The 8 February 2026 snap election

On 23 January 2026 Takaichi dissolved the House of Representatives. The snap general election held 8 February 2026 produced a historic LDP landslide. The LDP won at least 316 seats in the 465-seat Lower House — an outright two-thirds supermajority and the most seats ever won by a single party in Japanese Lower House electoral history. The result marked the first time since World War II that a single party controlled more than two-thirds of the Lower House.

The analysis: the LDP electoral underperformance had operated under the post-Abe-assassination conditions, the slush-fund-scandal exposure, and the cycle of three short-tenure prime ministers (Suga, Kishida, Ishiba). Takaichi's high personal popularity, her policy speech, and the reset of the LDP brand operated together to produce the electoral recovery. Analysts credited her popularity among young voters as a particular institutional factor — the cohort that had traditionally been demobilised in Japanese electoral participation had been mobilised by the Takaichi candidacy.

The post-February 2026 apparatus: Takaichi operates the most secure parliamentary majority of any post-war Japanese Prime Minister. The two-thirds Lower House supermajority enables her government to pass constitutional amendments (which require two-thirds in each chamber plus a national referendum) and to override Upper House rejections of legislation. The LDP-Japan-Innovation-Party coalition operates with the capacity to advance the Takaichi multi-year agenda without the parliamentary-coalition constraints that operated against Suga, Kishida, and Ishiba.

## The defence and security architecture

Takaichi's central institutional priority is the acceleration of the Japanese defence and security architecture. Three institutional features have operated.

**Defence spending acceleration.** The multi-year Kishida 2022 National Security Strategy had committed Japan to doubling defence spending over 5 years to reach 2% GDP by 2027. Takaichi's October 2025 announcement brought the target forward to March 2026 — roughly one year ahead of schedule. The acceleration operates against the post-war Article 9 constraint that had constrained Japanese defence spending to roughly 1% GDP across most of the post-war period.

**Strategic-sectors investment.** Takaichi's "crisis management investment" framework directs heavy government investment to artificial intelligence, semiconductors, nuclear fusion, biotechnology, and defence. The strategic-sectors framework is the Japanese analogue to the US Stargate Project (in Part XIV Chapter 2), the Chinese Made in China 2025 framework (in Dossier 28), and the EU strategic-autonomy architecture. The strategic-sectors competition is one institutional indicator of the tripartite-competition architecture across Part XII Chapter 7.

**Spy-prevention legislation.** In May 2025 Takaichi presented a spy-prevention law proposal. The post-October-2025 government has continued advancing the legislation. The 11 May 2026 South China Morning Post coverage characterised the proposed law as either "a shield against China" or "militarist surveillance" depending on institutional perspective. The May 2026 Takaichi government also faced anti-war protests over the defence-buildup posture; BBC reported 7 May 2026 anti-war protests rocking Japan as the public-opinion architecture diverged from the elite-policy consensus on defence acceleration.

## The Taiwan and China posture

Takaichi's post-October-2025 statements on potential defence of Taiwan produced the deterioration of China-Japan relations that the Xi dossier documented. The position has been the most explicit Japanese statement of the contemporary period on Taiwan — departure from the post-1972 Joint Communiqué framework under which Japan acknowledged the PRC position on Taiwan without formally accepting it.

The pathway operates through the US-Japan alliance security architecture, the multi-year 2015 Japanese reinterpretation of Article 9 that permitted collective self-defence, the multi-year 2022 National Security Strategy that characterised China as the strategic challenge to Japan, and the 2024-2025 Taiwan-related institutional preparations across the US-Japan-South-Korea-Australia-Philippines architecture.

Takaichi's institutional position aligns with the Trump 2 US posture on China. Whether the alignment continues operating after the Trump May 2026 "negotiating chip" characterisation of Taiwan (in Dossier 31) is the contemporary institutional question. Japan's institutional position on Taiwan is the central indicator of whether the US-Japan alliance continues operating as the strategic anchor of East Asian architecture or whether the alliance enters the reconfiguration that the Trump 2 transactional posture has produced across the architecture.

## The Trump-Takaichi relationship

President Trump visited Japan in late October 2025. The first Trump-Takaichi meeting in Tokyo was characterised across the foreign-policy commentary as a institutional success — Takaichi "seemed to win him over" per Sheila Smith of CFR. The meeting operated through Trump's preference for personal-relationship-based diplomacy and Takaichi's preparation through her US-staffing background and Matsushita-Institute dispatch experience.

The apparatus of the Trump-Takaichi relationship is the contemporary US-Japan alliance manifestation. The multi-year Trump 2 administration has demanded allied burden-sharing increases across NATO, US-South-Korea, and US-Japan; the Takaichi October 2025 acceleration of the 2% GDP defence target to March 2026 is the structural compliance with that demand. The Takaichi posture on China and Taiwan is the alignment with the Trump administration's hardline China posture.

The 16-17 May 2026 Trump characterisation of Taiwan as a "good negotiating chip" (Dossier 31) introduced institutional ambiguity into the alignment. The Takaichi government response will operate as a central institutional indicator of whether the alignment continues or enters reconfiguration.

## The LDP architecture surrounding Takaichi

The contemporary LDP architecture operates under post-faction conditions. The factional system that had was the governing architecture of the LDP from the 1955 founding through the post-Abe assassination period was dissolved across January 2024 in response to the slush-fund scandal. The major factions — Seiwakai (Abe faction, the largest), Heisei Kenkyūkai (Motegi faction), Kōchikai (Kishida faction), Shisuikai (Nikai faction) — were formally dissolved. The Shikōkai (Asō faction) was the only major faction that did not dissolve.

The post-faction LDP operates through informal "policy groups" that have continued the factional architecture under different formal names. The key operators around Takaichi include:

**Tarō Asō** (born 1940, age 85 in 2026), Vice President of the LDP since 2024, former Prime Minister 2008-2009. Asō family is one of the most political-class dynasties in Japan — Tarō is the grandson of Yoshida Shigeru (Prime Minister 1946-1947 and 1948-1954, one of the architects of post-war Japanese statehood). Asō is the remaining LDP elder.

**Yoshihide Suga** (born 1948), former Prime Minister September 2020 – October 2021. Suga operates as kingmaker in the LDP architecture, particularly the alternative-to-Abe-faction network. He had been Abe's Chief Cabinet Secretary across the 2012-2020 second Abe premiership.

**Shinjirō Koizumi** (born 1981), Takaichi's runner-up in the 2025 LDP leadership election. Son of former Prime Minister Junichirō Koizumi (2001-2006). The Koizumi family is one of the most political-class dynasties (Junichirō's father Junya Koizumi was a Diet member; his grandfather Matajiro Koizumi was a Meiji-era political-class operator). Shinjirō has held cabinet positions including Minister of the Environment 2019-2021 under Abe. His preference for the Koizumi-family pro-market posture distinguishes him from Takaichi's fiscal-expansion posture.

**Toshimitsu Motegi** (born 1955), current Foreign Minister. Motegi has held positions in the post-Abe period including LDP Secretary-General 2021-2024.

**Yoko Kamikawa** (born 1953), former Foreign Minister under Kishida; multi-year Justice Minister.

**Itsunori Onodera** (born 1960), former Defense Minister; member of LDP Policy Research Council.

## The opposition architecture

The contemporary Japanese opposition operates across five main parties.

The **Constitutional Democratic Party of Japan (CDP)** — the largest opposition party, centre-left in posture — is led by Yoshihiko Noda (former Prime Minister 2011-2012). The CDP has was the opposition to the LDP defence-buildup posture and the LDP fiscal-expansion architecture.

The **Japan Innovation Party (Nippon Ishin no Kai)** is the coalition partner of the LDP under Takaichi — the right-leaning populist formation founded in 2015 by former Osaka Mayor Tōru Hashimoto. The alignment with the LDP operates through the shared posture on constitutional reform, immigration restriction, and defence buildup.

**Kōmeitō**, the Buddhist Sōka Gakkai-affiliated party that was the LDP coalition partner from 1999 to October 2025, has moved into opposition. The 26-year coalition ending operated as one of the inflection points of the Takaichi transition.

The **Democratic Party for the People** is the centrist formation that has captured moderate centrist voters since the post-2024 period.

The **Japanese Communist Party** and **Reiwa Shinsengumi** operate as the small-party formations on the left.

The **Sanseitō** is the far-right populist formation that has captured nationalist voters throughout the post-2024 period.

## The Imperial Household and the male-succession question

The Imperial Household of Japan is constitutionally separate from the political class but operates as a institutional architecture. The current Emperor is Naruhito, who assumed the throne on 1 May 2019 upon the abdication of his father Akihito (the first abdication in roughly 200 years).

The question that has operated across the period is the succession crisis: the male-only succession rules of the Imperial Household Law have produced only one male heir presumptive — Prince Hisahito (born 2006), grandson of Akihito through Akihito's second son Crown Prince Akishino. Naruhito's only child is Princess Aiko, who is constitutionally barred from succession.

Takaichi's institutional position supports the male-only succession rules. The multi-year reform proposals from the opposition and from progressive public-opinion architecture have not been advanced under Takaichi's premiership. The succession architecture is the indicator of the conservative-nationalist multi-year alignment between Takaichi's posture and the Imperial-Household-tradition architecture.

## The 2030 succession question

Takaichi's post-February-2026 two-thirds supermajority is the most secure parliamentary architecture of any post-war Japanese Prime Minister. The institutional base case is that Takaichi will serve out the four-year Lower House mandate (until early 2030) and possibly continue beyond.

The multi-year succession candidates include Shinjirō Koizumi (the 2025 runner-up; would be 49 in 2030), Toshimitsu Motegi (would be 75 in 2030), and the next-generation LDP cohort that the Takaichi government has been cultivating across the post-October-2025 period.

The structural challenges that operate against the Takaichi government — the demographic decline, the public-debt architecture at 230% of GDP, the energy-import dependency, the Chinese strategic-competition pressure, the US-Japan alliance uncertainty under Trump 2 — operate as the central architecture against which the Takaichi tenure will be judged.
---


---

# Dossier 33: The South Korean Political Class

## The South Korean institutional position

South Korea operates as a presidential democracy under the 1987 Constitution of the Sixth Republic. The constitution was produced through the June Democratic Uprising of 1987 that ended the military rule of the Republic of Korea (1948-1987) and established the contemporary architecture: direct presidential election; single 5-year non-renewable term; 300-seat National Assembly; multi-party system; independent judiciary; Constitutional Court.

The contemporary South Korean political class operates across two principal poles. The progressive pole runs through the Democratic Party of Korea (DPK / Minjudang) and its institutional predecessors (the Kim Dae-jung-Roh Moo-hyun-Moon Jae-in lineage). The conservative pole runs through the People Power Party (PPP) and its institutional predecessors (Grand National Party, Saenuri, Liberty Korea). The two poles have alternated presidential power across the Sixth Republic in a regular ten-year-ish rhythm. The 2017 impeachment of Park Geun-hye and the 2025 impeachment of Yoon Suk Yeol — both conservative presidents removed by Constitutional Court ruling — operate as the contemporary institutional pattern: the conservative pole has produced two presidents removed from office within an 8-year period.

The current operating principal is Lee Jae-myung, who was elected 3 June 2025 in a snap election triggered by Yoon's 4 April 2025 removal. Lee's election ended a 6-month political crisis that had begun on 3 December 2024 when Yoon declared martial law and was forced to withdraw it within hours under National Assembly pressure. The 2024-2025 crisis is as the most consequential institutional episode in the South Korean democratic record since the 1987 transition.

## Lee Jae-myung — the operating principal

Lee Jae-myung was born around 8 December 1963 in Andong, North Gyeongsang Province. His exact birthdate is uncertain — his father registered the birth late, a common practice in the period when infant mortality in Korea was elevated and parents delayed birth registration to confirm survival. South Korea's GDP per capita at Lee's birth was comparable with sub-Saharan African economies; the country's transition to high-income status would not begin until the 1970s industrialisation programme.

Lee was the fifth of seven children in a working-class household. His early biography includes stints as an underage factory labourer — the kind of working-class formation rare among contemporary East Asian apex political-class operators and distinctive from the bureaucratic-elite or family-political backgrounds that have produced most contemporary Korean presidents. He did not complete formal secondary schooling. He obtained the equivalent qualification through self-study and was admitted to Chung-Ang University in Seoul in 1982. He passed the bar exam in 1986 — the structural pathway out of working-class formation into the elite legal profession.

Lee built his early legal career as a labour lawyer in Seongnam, a major city south of Seoul. He championed the rights of industrial-accident victims and residents facing eviction during urban-redevelopment projects. The labour-lawyer practice operated as his institutional foundation and the source of his pro-worker policy orientation.

His entry into electoral politics began in 2006 with an unsuccessful run for Seongnam mayor. He lost a 2008 parliamentary bid. In 2010 he won the Seongnam mayoralty on his second attempt; he was re-elected in 2014. From 2018 to 2021 he served as Governor of Gyeonggi Province — the country's most populous province (roughly 14 million residents) surrounding Seoul. The Seongnam mayoral and Gyeonggi gubernatorial tenures established his institutional record on social welfare expansion, basic-income pilot programmes, and the confrontation with the chaebol architecture that has characterised his career.

In the 2022 presidential election Lee was the DPK nominee against Yoon Suk Yeol of the PPP. The result: Yoon 48.6%, Lee 47.8% — a 0.8-percentage-point margin, the narrowest in South Korean presidential history. Lee's loss was the foundation for his subsequent positioning as the leader of the parliamentary opposition.

In August 2022 Lee was elected DPK Chair. He held the position through April 2025. In January 2024 he survived an assassination attempt — stabbed in the neck during a visit to a construction site in Busan. He recovered and returned to political operation within weeks.

In November 2024 Lee was convicted under the Public Official Election Act for falsely denying a connection to Kim Moon-ki, a former Seongnam Development Corporation executive, during his 2022 presidential campaign. The conviction operated as institutional pressure on his candidacy. He maintained that the charges were politically motivated. The legal cases against him have continued through 2025-2026 with multiple unresolved proceedings on bribery, election-law violations, and encouraging false testimony.

## The 3 December 2024 martial law crisis

The apparatus of contemporary South Korean political life was reshaped on the evening of 3 December 2024. President Yoon Suk Yeol declared martial law in a televised address, citing "anti-state forces" and accusing the opposition-controlled National Assembly of paralysing the government. Yoon ordered the deployment of military troops to the National Assembly building.

The crisis ran for roughly six hours. The National Assembly building was surrounded by troops; lawmakers were physically prevented from entering. Lee Jae-myung — then DPK Chair — climbed over the National Assembly fence and livestreamed the event. Other lawmakers followed. The footage of South Korean parliamentarians scaling the fence to defy the martial law order was the governing galvanising image of the crisis.

The lawmakers pushed past the soldiers and convened the National Assembly. They voted unanimously to annul the martial law decree. Under the 1987 Constitution, the National Assembly has the power to annul martial law by majority vote; the unanimous vote of the 190+ legislators present made the annulment immediate. Yoon withdrew the martial law order within hours of his declaration.

The consequence was immediate. The National Assembly began impeachment proceedings the same week. On 14 December 2024 the Assembly voted to impeach Yoon. The post-14-December period operated under acting presidents: Prime Minister Han Duck-soo served as acting president initially; subsequent rotations through the constitutional succession architecture continued through the impeachment proceedings.

The Constitutional Court of Korea — the architecture that is the final arbiter of impeachment — deliberated through the early months of 2025. On 4 April 2025 the Court unanimously upheld the impeachment. Yoon was removed from office. He subsequently faced trial on insurrection charges — a crime punishable under South Korean law by death or life imprisonment.

The pattern of two conservative presidents removed by Constitutional Court ruling within 8 years (Park 2017, Yoon 2025) is the contemporary record of the South Korean impeachment architecture. The 1987 Constitution's institutional check on presidential power — judicial review through Constitutional Court — has was the architecture through which the South Korean political class has constrained executive overreach.

## The 2025 snap election

Under the 1987 Constitution, a presidential vacancy requires an election within 60 days. Acting President Han Duck-soo called the snap election for 3 June 2025.

The DPK primary produced Lee Jae-myung as nominee with roughly 90% of the primary vote on 27 April 2025. The PPP nomination produced a complex institutional drama. Former Labour Minister Kim Moon-soo was selected as the PPP nominee. On 10 May 2025 the PPP leadership attempted to replace Kim with Han Duck-soo, the acting president and former Prime Minister who had announced his presidential candidacy a week earlier. Han had no PPP standing; his candidacy operated as an institutional intervention by Yoon-aligned PPP operators against Kim, who had criticised Yoon's martial law declaration but opposed his impeachment.

PPP rank-and-file members rejected the resolution designating Han as the party's candidate. Kim's nomination was reinstated. Han ended his campaign on 11 May 2025 and endorsed Kim. The PPP institutional crisis was the second institutional self-inflicted wound — after Yoon's December 2024 martial law declaration — of the period.

On 17 May 2025 Yoon left the PPP and endorsed Kim. The institutional disassociation operated as both a tactical PPP move to distance the candidate from the discredited former president and as Yoon's institutional exit from formal party politics.

The 27 May 2025 final televised debate produced the multi-week institutional positioning of the candidates. The 3 June 2025 election produced:

- **Lee Jae-myung (Democratic Party): 49.42%** — just under 50%.
- **Kim Moon-soo (People Power Party): 41.2%**.
- **Lee Jun-seok (New Reform Party): 8.34%**.
- **Kwon Yeong-guk (Democratic Labor Party): 0.98%**.

The margin between Lee and Kim was roughly 2.89 million votes — the widest in nearly three decades of Sixth Republic presidential elections.

On 4 June 2025 at 11:00 KST, Lee Jae-myung was sworn in as the 14th President of the Republic of Korea. The inauguration was held in the Rotunda Hall of the National Assembly — apparatus reflecting the parliamentary-centric nature of the crisis from which his presidency emerged. His inauguration address emphasised national unity and a pledge to be "a president who ends the politics of division." The position of unifier rather than partisan was the tone of his early presidency.

## The Lee presidency 2025-2026

Lee took office under three simultaneous institutional pressures. First, the aftermath of the martial law crisis required institutional reconciliation and the prosecution of Yoon and the military and political operators who had supported the declaration. Second, the Trump 2 administration's tariff architecture required institutional negotiation: a 25% "reciprocal" tariff on Korean exports was set to take effect 9 July 2025. Third, the chaebol architecture, the demographic crisis (the lowest fertility rate in any major economy), the rising cost of living, and the global protectionism produced the context against which Lee's domestic agenda would operate.

Lee's early foreign-policy operations produced four bilateral institutional engagements that established the architectural foundation of his presidency.

**The 10 June 2025 Xi-Lee phone call.** The first major bilateral call of Lee's presidency was with Xi Jinping. Lee invited Xi to attend the 2025 APEC summit in Gyeongju, South Korea (the 2025 host). Xi stressed the importance of stable supply chains. The institutional posture: Lee's rebalancing of the Yoon-administration alignment with the US-Japan trilateral architecture toward a more balanced position with China (South Korea's largest trading partner).

**The 18 June 2025 Ishiba meeting at G7.** Lee's first in-person meeting with Japanese PM Shigeru Ishiba at the G7 summit produced agreement to restart shuttle diplomacy. The meeting marked the 60th anniversary of normalised Japan-Korea diplomatic ties (1965). The institutional position: Lee continued the Yoon-administration Japan-Korea reconciliation framework while removing the nationalist edge that had characterised the Korean political-class architecture's posture on Japan. The institutional reconciliation operates against the historical-grievance architecture (Japanese colonial rule 1910-1945; comfort women issue; forced labour reparations) that has periodically produced multi-year Japan-Korea institutional crises.

**The 15 August 2025 "People's Mandate Ceremony".** Lee held a public inauguration ceremony at Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul commemorating the 80th anniversary of Korean liberation from Japan (15 August 1945). The architecture: Lee received appointment letters from 80 selected notable people. The ceremony operated as the popular ratification of his presidency, two months after his official inauguration. The choice of Gwanghwamun Square — the central plaza of Seoul historically associated with Korean democratic mobilisation — was the structural positioning of Lee's presidency as a return to the post-1987 democratic-mass-mobilisation tradition.

**The Cheong Wa Dae return.** In December 2025 Lee commuted to Cheong Wa Dae (the Blue House) for the first time. The 2022-2025 Yoon administration had moved the presidential office to the Defence Ministry compound in Yongsan — a controversial relocation that Yoon had justified as opening the presidential complex to the public. Lee's return to Cheong Wa Dae was the reset of the Yoon-era architectural arrangements. It was the first time a president had commuted to Cheong Wa Dae since 9 May 2022 — the last day of Moon Jae-in's term.

Lee's approval ratings reached roughly 70% by April 2026 — among the highest of any modern South Korean president. The institutional honeymoon has operated against the conservative-opposition criticism of his foreign-policy rebalancing and his chaebol-reform proposals.

## The DPK architecture

The Democratic Party of Korea is the contemporary governing apparatus. Its institutional lineage runs through:

- The National Congress for New Politics (1995-2000), founded by Kim Dae-jung.
- The Millennium Democratic Party (2000-2003).
- The Uri Party (2003-2007) under Roh Moo-hyun.
- The Democratic Party (2008-2014).
- The New Politics Alliance for Democracy (2014-2015).
- The Minjoo Party of Korea (2015-2024).
- The Democratic Party of Korea / DPK (2024-present, formally renamed).

The DPK is the governing pole of the Korean centre-left progressive tradition. Its founders and leaders — Kim Dae-jung (Nobel Peace Prize 2000), Roh Moo-hyun (committed suicide May 2009 under corruption investigation), Moon Jae-in (President 2017-2022), Lee Jae-myung — operate as the continuity of the democratic-movement architecture.

In the 2024 general election the DPK won 168 of 300 National Assembly seats. With smaller allied parties (the Rebuilding Korea Party, the Democratic Labor Party, others), the broader progressive coalition reached roughly 185 seats — a comfortable working majority but short of the 200-seat threshold that would enable constitutional amendment without a referendum.

The DPK architecture's institutional priorities under Lee include:
- Chaebol reform: restructuring the Korean conglomerate architecture (Samsung, Hyundai, LG, SK, etc.) that operates as the Korean commercial-class apparatus.
- Economic equity: expanded social welfare, basic-income pilot programmes, public-housing construction.
- North Korea engagement: cautious return to Moon-Roh-Kim-Dae-jung "Sunshine Policy" architecture, though constrained by Kim Jong Un's hardline posture (see Dossier 34).
- China rebalancing: reduced dependence on US-Japan trilateral against the Yoon-era architecture.
- US relations: navigating the Trump 2 transactional architecture.

## The PPP architecture

The People Power Party is the contemporary conservative apparatus. Its institutional lineage runs through the Democratic Justice Party (1981-1990), Democratic Liberal Party (1990-1995), New Korea Party (1995-1997), Grand National Party (1997-2012), Saenuri (2012-2017), Liberty Korea Party (2017-2020), and PPP (2020-present).

The PPP is the pole of the Korean centre-right conservative tradition. Its history is connected to the post-Park-Chung-hee military-era architecture; the chaebol-friendly economic policy framework; and the hard-line anti-DPRK and pro-US-alliance security architecture.

The contemporary PPP is in institutional crisis after Yoon's removal. The post-2025 leadership architecture has not yet stabilised. Kim Moon-soo, the 2025 presidential candidate, retains institutional standing but lost the election. Yoon left the party in May 2025. The party reconstruction is the central institutional question of the post-2025 PPP architecture.

The 2026-2027 institutional question for the PPP is whether the party can reset its conservative brand without the Yoon legacy, or whether the PPP undergoes the factional restructuring that the post-Park-Geun-hye 2017 period had produced. The 2027 National Assembly election (mid-term to Lee's presidency) and the 2030 presidential election will operate as the indicators of whether the PPP recovers or fractures.

## The New Reform Party and the third-force architecture

Lee Jun-seok (born March 1985), former PPP leader, founded the New Reform Party in 2024. He has was the face of the generational change in Korean conservative politics. His 8.34% in the 2025 presidential election established the third-force position in the contemporary Korean political-class architecture. The multi-year question is whether the New Reform Party reaches an institutional accommodation with the post-Yoon PPP or whether it operates as an institutional reconstruction of the Korean conservative tradition under younger leadership.

## The Yoon legacy and the insurrection trial

Yoon Suk Yeol, born December 1960, operates the post-removal institutional position of the most conservative apex operator to face criminal trial in South Korean history. He was a career prosecutor for roughly 30 years before entering politics in 2021. Under the Moon administration he had served as Prosecutor General from 2019 to 2021 — the structural position from which he launched his presidential campaign.

His 9 March 2022 presidential election victory over Lee Jae-myung was the narrowest in South Korean history. His 2022-2024 presidency operated under confrontation with the DPK-majority National Assembly, personal-scandal architecture (above all First Lady Kim Keon-hee's multi-year allegations on jewellery and stock manipulation), and approval-rating decline from the initial 50%+ to the sub-20% range by late 2024.

The 3 December 2024 martial law declaration was the self-immolation of his presidency. His insurrection trial — proceeding through 2025 and into 2026 — operates under the multi-year 1980 Gwangju Uprising historical precedent: the trial of former Presidents Chun Doo-hwan and Roh Tae-woo in the 1990s on related charges had produced precedent for the judicial accountability of Korean political-class operators on insurrection-related charges.

## The contemporary East Asian institutional position

South Korea operates as one of the Asia-Pacific apex architectures. Its position in the contemporary period is institutional under three pressures:

**The US-South-Korea alliance.** The 1953 Mutual Defense Treaty has was the governing anchor. Roughly 28,500 US troops are stationed in Korea. The Trump 2 administration's demand for increased Korean burden-sharing has produced the pressure on the alliance architecture. The 25% tariff that came into effect 9 July 2025 operates against the US-Korea Free Trade Agreement framework that had operated since 2012.

**The China relationship.** China is South Korea's multi-year largest trading partner — roughly $250 billion in bilateral trade. The US-China strategic competition across Dossier 28 produces institutional pressure on the Korean position. Lee's rebalancing toward China has operated against the Yoon-era US-Japan-Korea trilateral architecture.

**The North Korea question.** Kim Jong Un's multi-year hardline posture (in Dossier 34) has operated against the periodic engagement architecture of progressive Korean administrations. Lee's cautious approach has not yet produced the breakthrough that the progressive tradition has historically pursued. The September 2025 Tiananmen parade with Putin and Kim (in Dossier 28) was the positioning of North Korea within the China-Russia-DPRK solidarity architecture against the US-Japan-Korea trilateral.
---


---

# Dossier 34: The North Korean Political Class

## The North Korean institutional position

The Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) is the contemporary world's only hereditary communist autocracy. The state was founded 9 September 1948 under Kim Il Sung. Three generations of the Kim dynasty have ruled across 77 years: Kim Il Sung (1948-1994), Kim Jong Il (1994-2011), and Kim Jong Un (2011-present). The apparatus is constructed around the Workers' Party of Korea (WPK / KWP) as the dominant party, the Supreme People's Assembly as nominal legislature, and the State Affairs Commission as the formal apex of state authority. The Kim family is the institutional fusion of party leadership, state authority, military command, and the Juche-ideology architecture that has produced the most isolated political-class regime in the contemporary global record.

The contemporary North Korean political class is the apparatus that has consolidated under Kim Jong Un across 2011-2026. The Kim Jong Un period has produced three institutional features: the most aggressive nuclear and missile development programme in the post-1945 record; the formal abandonment of the Korean reunification framework in December 2023; and the structural alignment with Russia from June 2024 that has produced the deployment of roughly 11,000 DPRK troops to Russia for the Ukraine war and the arms transfer of roughly 6 million heavy artillery rounds and 100 ballistic missiles.

The succession question — who will inherit the dynastic position when Kim Jong Un either dies or becomes operationally incapacitated — has produced the South Korean National Intelligence Service's February 2026 assessment that Kim's daughter Kim Ju Ae (roughly 13 years old) is the designated successor. The record shows Kim Jong Un as the operating principal, the brutal-consolidation architecture he built across 2011-2017, the nuclear and missile programme, the Russia alignment from 2023-2024, the Ju Ae succession architecture, the Kim Yo Jong (sister) institutional position, and the broader North Korean political-class apparatus.

## Kim Jong Un — the operating principal

Kim Jong Un was born on 8 January in either 1982, 1983, or 1984. The DPRK state media has used different dates at different points; the contested birth year reflects the pattern of opaque biographical information about the Kim dynasty. He is the third son of Kim Jong Il (the second-generation supreme leader, 1994-2011) and Ko Yong Hui (Kim Jong Il's consort who died in 2004).

Kim Jong Un's early biography is contested. He was reportedly educated at the International School of Berne in Gümligen, Switzerland under a pseudonym across the late 1990s. He returned to North Korea and attended Kim Il Sung National War College in Pyongyang from 2002 to 2007 — the governing training pathway for the North Korean military leadership.

Kim Jong Il had three sons. Kim Jong Nam (eldest, 1971-2017) was reportedly initially the heir apparent but fell out of favour after his 2001 attempt to enter Japan on a forged Dominican Republic passport to visit Tokyo Disneyland. Kim Jong Chul (second son, born 1981) was reportedly considered too effeminate for the position. Kim Jong Un was selected as the heir apparent around 2008-2009.

The succession architecture operated through a compressed institutional timeline relative to the Kim Jong Il succession (which had taken roughly 20 years across the 1970s-1990s under Kim Il Sung's grooming). In January 2009 Kim Jong Un was appointed to a leadership position over the WPK and the Korean People's Army. In September 2010 he was publicly identified as heir apparent; appointed Vice Chairman of the WPK Central Military Commission and given the rank of 4-star general despite no prior military service. On 17 December 2011 Kim Jong Il died; within days Kim Jong Un was declared supreme leader. In April 2012 he acquired an array of titles: First Secretary of the WPK; Chairman of the Central Military Commission; Chairman of the National Defense Commission. The title-acquisition was the ratification of his apex position.

He was 27 or 28 years old at accession — the youngest head of state in the world at the time of his elevation.

## The brutal-consolidation architecture 2011-2017

The first six years of Kim Jong Un's rule produced the brutal consolidation of his apex position through purges of operators inherited from his father's regime.

The most purge was of his uncle Jang Song-thaek. Jang had been the longtime regent and one of the architects of the 2010-2011 succession transition. He was Vice Chairman of the National Defense Commission, Director of the WPK Administrative Department, and reportedly the second-most-powerful operator in the DPRK during the early Kim Jong Un period. On 8 December 2013 Jang was dragged out of a Politburo meeting on televised state media. On 12 December 2013 he was executed by anti-aircraft machine guns after a trial that characterised him as a "traitor for all ages." The execution was the signal to the DPRK political-class architecture that no operator — including the Kim family's in-laws — was protected from elimination.

In February 2017 Kim's half-brother Kim Jong Nam was assassinated at Malaysia's Kuala Lumpur International Airport using the VX nerve agent. Two women approached him in the airport and smeared the agent on his face. He died within roughly 20 minutes. The Malaysian authorities concluded the assassination had been organised by DPRK operatives. South Korean intelligence subsequently said Kim Jong Un ordered the assassination. Kim Jong Nam's killing was the structural elimination of the only remaining adult male in the Kim dynasty who had any potential as a successor-claim alternative.

Across the 2011-2017 period, an estimated multiple military and political operators were executed in the consolidation. The institutional pattern: Kim Jong Un eliminated the generation he had inherited from his father, replaced them with operators loyal to himself, and constructed the apex architecture that has operated through 2026.

## The nuclear and missile programme

The nuclear and missile programme has been the centrepiece of Kim Jong Un's tenure. Under Kim Jong Il, North Korea had conducted two nuclear tests (October 2006, May 2009) with limited success. Under Kim Jong Un, the programme accelerated dramatically.

Nuclear tests:
- 12 February 2013: third DPRK nuclear test, first under Kim Jong Un.
- 6 January 2016: fourth test, claimed hydrogen bomb.
- 9 September 2016: fifth test.
- 3 September 2017: sixth test, claimed thermonuclear.

Missile tests:
- More than 200 missile tests under Kim Jong Un — over triple the combined total under his father and grandfather.
- 4 July 2017: first Hwasong-14 ICBM test; brought parts of US mainland within theoretical range.
- 28 November 2017: Hwasong-15 ICBM test; claimed to bring entire US mainland within range.
- 2018-2019: missile test moratorium during Trump-Kim diplomatic period.
- 2022: ICBM and IRBM tests resumed.
- 2023-2026: continued testing of advanced systems including hypersonic glide vehicles, solid-fuel ICBMs, submarine-launched ballistic missiles, and tactical nuclear weapons.

The post-2017 institutional posture: DPRK characterises itself as a permanent nuclear weapons state. Kim has repeatedly stated that the nuclear weapons are not negotiable with the United States. The position represents a comprehensive rejection of the post-1953 framework under which the United States and South Korea had pursued denuclearisation as the condition for normalisation.

## The Trump-Kim diplomacy 2018-2019

The Trump-Kim diplomatic episode of 2018-2019 was the governing anomaly in the otherwise hostile US-DPRK relationship. The episode produced three direct meetings:

- 12 June 2018: Singapore Summit. The first meeting between a sitting US president and a sitting DPRK leader in the post-1953 record.
- 27-28 February 2019: Hanoi Summit. The summit collapsed without agreement when Kim refused to dismantle Yongbyon nuclear facility in exchange for the sanctions relief Trump was willing to provide.
- 30 June 2019: brief meeting at the Korean DMZ. Trump briefly stepped into DPRK territory — the first sitting US president to do so.

The outcome: no denuclearisation agreement, no peace treaty, no formal change to the 1953 Korean War Armistice. The episode was the indicator that the Trump 1 administration's transactional diplomacy could not produce the concessions that the framework had required. The post-2019 period has produced no resumption of high-level US-DPRK diplomacy.

The Trump 2 administration's posture on the DPRK question through 2026 has been operationally low-priority compared to the Ukraine, Gaza, and China-Taiwan questions across this volume. Trump has made periodic statements about wanting to "reconnect" with Kim but has not produced an institutional re-engagement.

## The December 2023 unification reversal

In December 2023 Kim Jong Un made the most consequential institutional declaration of his tenure on the inter-Korean relationship. At a WPK plenum he declared that the DPRK formally abandoned the goal of Korean reunification and characterised South Korea as a "hostile state" rather than a "fraternal nation."

The consequences:
- The Arch of Reunification in Pyongyang — a monument constructed in 2001 to symbolise the reunification framework — was symbolically demolished.
- The DPRK constitution was amended to characterise South Korea as a hostile state.
- Multi-decade peace-and-reunification agencies within the DPRK government were dissolved or reorganised.
- 2024 onwards: artillery drills and missile drills targeted at South Korea increased.

The institutional analysis: Kim's declaration formally ended the pretence that the two Koreas might reunify. The pretence had operated since 1945 as the architecture under which both Koreas claimed sovereignty over the entire peninsula. Kim's December 2023 declaration replaced the unification framework with a two-state framework — operationally consistent with the reality but doctrinally significant for the structural logic of the DPRK regime.

The reasoning advanced by Carnegie Endowment analysts Jung H. Pak and others: Kim's declaration reflects the acceptance that DPRK's economic and conventional-military inferiority to South Korea makes reunification on DPRK terms impossible. The DPRK's institutional position depends on permanent isolation, nuclear deterrence, and the systemic differentiation from South Korea. The reunification framework operated against these institutional requirements; the December 2023 declaration aligned the doctrinal architecture with the architecture.

## The Russia alignment 2023-2026

The alignment with Russia has was the most consequential foreign-policy reorientation of Kim Jong Un's tenure. The architecture has produced:

**Arms transfers:** Beginning August 2023, DPRK exported over 1 million artillery shells to Russia. By end of 2024, DPRK had delivered roughly 6 million heavy artillery rounds and 100 ballistic missiles to Russia, per US and allied assessments.

**Troop deployments:** In October 2024 DPRK sent military personnel to Russia who fought against Ukraine in Russian uniforms under Russian command. By end of 2024 roughly 11,000 DPRK troops had been deployed. In April 2025 Kim publicly acknowledged that DPRK troops were fighting alongside Russian forces to "annihilate and wipe out the Ukrainian neo-Nazi occupiers." On 27 April 2026 Kim unveiled a towering bronze sculpture of DPRK and Russian soldiers in combat at a Pyongyang public ceremony; he praised troops who chose death over capture in Ukraine.

**Diplomatic architecture:** Kim visited the Russian Far East 12-17 September 2023. Putin visited Pyongyang 18-19 June 2024 — his first visit since 2000. The two signed the "Treaty on Comprehensive Strategic Partnership" — a mutual-defence commitment that the DPRK characterised as the most consequential alliance treaty since the 1961 Soviet-DPRK Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation, and Mutual Assistance.

**Returns to DPRK:** Russia provided commodities, technology transfer, and assistance accessing the global financial system to pay DPRK workers, circumventing UN sanctions (per former US diplomat and historian Jung H. Pak). The technology transfer is the most consequential institutional return — DPRK has reportedly received Russian assistance with satellite launches, advanced missile guidance, and submarine technology.

**The September 2025 Tiananmen parade:** On 3 September 2025 Kim travelled to Beijing for the Chinese military parade commemorating the end of World War II. He shared the parade stand with Xi Jinping and Vladimir Putin — the unprecedented public display of the three operators against the US-led architecture in Dossier 28. The positioning was the formal alignment of the DPRK within the China-Russia-DPRK solidarity architecture that has operated since 2023.

## The Kim Ju Ae succession architecture

The succession question has been the central institutional uncertainty of the Kim Jong Un period. Until November 2022 the question had been unanswered: Kim's wife and children had not been publicly displayed; the existence of any children had not been confirmed by DPRK state media.

On 18 November 2022 Kim Jong Un appeared at an ICBM launch site with his daughter Kim Ju Ae — the first public appearance of any of his children. The consequences have unfolded across 2022-2026 as a structured introduction of Ju Ae as the designated successor.

The state-media linguistic progression has been the governing marker:
- Late 2022: "saranghaneun jajebun" ("beloved child") — the standard honorific for a daughter of a operator.
- Early 2023: "jongyeonghaneun jajebun" ("respected child") — the elevation to a more honorific.
- 2024 onwards: "hyangdo" ("guidance") — historically reserved for leaders.
- March 2024: officially referred to as "great person of guidance" alongside Kim Jong Un — the most honorific.

The state-media visual progression has been the indicator of her training:
- November 2022: ICBM launch site debut.
- 2023: Day of the Shining Star celebrations (Kim Jong Il's birth anniversary); 75th anniversary of DPRK foundation parade; New Year's Eve May Day Stadium celebrations.
- 2024: WPK 79th anniversary celebrations October 2024.
- 25 April 2025: Choe Hyon destroyer launch ceremony.
- 9 May 2025: 80th Victory Day at Russian Embassy in Pyongyang.
- 3 September 2025: travelled to China for the Victory Day Parade.
- Early 2026: state media showed her firing a sniper rifle with party and military officials.
- 19 March 2026: photographed operating a new battle tank during a tactical drill.

In February 2026 the South Korean National Intelligence Service (NIS) assessed in its annual briefing to the South Korean National Assembly that Ju Ae had completed her training and become the designated successor. The reasoning: the structured introduction; the honorific progression; the policy-input indicators; the visit to the 2025 Beijing Victory Day Parade — the international debut.

The institutional novelty: Ju Ae would be the first woman to serve as DPRK supreme leader. The DPRK political-class architecture has been patriarchal in the post-1948 record. The institutional legitimation programme for a female successor has produced the state-media campaign of "mythmaking, linguistic and visual manipulation, and maternal statecraft" (per Foreign Policy May 2026 analysis) — drawing on the DPRK's fusion of dynastic symbolism and deified maternal leadership.

The institutional pace is unprecedented. Kim Jong Il was in his 30s when his public succession grooming began. Kim Jong Un was in his mid-20s. Kim Ju Ae is roughly 13. The accelerated timeline is likely driven by Kim Jong Un's reported health concerns — weight fluctuations, obesity-related conditions including high blood pressure and diabetes (per July 2024 NIS assessment), and the facial growth that has been visible since late 2020.

The succession question is not closed. Kim Jong Un may have other children. The retired NIS official February 2024 statement that Kim has an older son who is "too pale and thin" to be displayed publicly leaves open the possibility that the older son could re-emerge as a successor candidate. The pattern in the DPRK architecture has been patrilineal — passing from father to son across the Kim Il Sung → Kim Jong Il → Kim Jong Un transitions. The institutional novelty of a female successor would require multi-year legitimation work that the current state-media programme is operationally constructing.

## Kim Yo Jong — the second

Kim Yo Jong, born 1987 or 1989 (the date is contested), is Kim Jong Un's younger sister. She has was the institutional second-most-powerful operator in the DPRK across the tenure. Her institutional positions include:
- Deputy Director of the WPK Agitation and Propaganda Department.
- Vice Department Director of the Central Committee of the WPK.

Yo Jong has been instrumental in creating Kim Jong Un's public image and organising state events. She has frequently issued public statements on behalf of Kim Jong Un — often hostile statements toward South Korea, the US, and Japan. The institutional pattern: Kim Yo Jong operates as Kim Jong Un's spokeswoman, propagandist, and operational deputy.

In February 2018 Yo Jong travelled to Seoul for the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics — the first member of the Kim ruling dynasty to visit South Korea since the Korean War (1950-1953). The visit was the structural warm-up for the Trump-Kim 2018-2019 diplomacy.

Yo Jong has been touted in foreign press as a potential successor to Kim Jong Un. Her institutional position, her family lineage, and her multi-year operational role make her the obvious alternative to Ju Ae in any succession scenario. The analysis: Yo Jong may operate as the regent for Ju Ae during the period until Ju Ae is mature enough for direct apex leadership — a architectural model that operated for the Kim Jong Un succession through Jang Song-thaek before Jang's December 2013 execution.

## The political-class operators

The contemporary DPRK political class operates around Kim Jong Un through a small apex circle. Notable operators include:

- **Choe Ryong Hae**: First Vice Chairman of the State Affairs Commission; longtime political operator who has survived multiple multi-year consolidation rounds.
- **Kim Tok Hun**: Premier of the DPRK since August 2020. Manages the economic apparatus.
- **Pak Jong Chon**: former Vice Chairman of the Central Military Commission.
- **Ri Pyong Chol**: Vice Chairman of the WPK Central Military Commission.
- **Choe Son Hui**: Foreign Minister since June 2022 — first female foreign minister of the DPRK.

The apparatus operates with limited institutional autonomy. The multi-year pattern has been the periodic purging of operators who have accumulated too much institutional weight; the cult-of-personality architecture concentrates authority on the Kim family.

## Domestic conditions

The DPRK domestic architecture has produced the humanitarian crisis that the Carnegie Endowment, the multi-year 38 North, the North Korea Studies academic literature have continued to document. The conditions include:

**Economic conditions:** Roughly 25-30% of GDP spent on defence, including WMD development. Endemic food and energy shortages. De facto breakdown of state rationing system; rise of jangmadang (small local markets, including illicit trade). The multi-year economic crisis has been acknowledged by Kim Jong Un — at a January 2024 WPK meeting he characterised it as a "serious political issue" since the government was unable to "provide even basic necessities."

**Demographic conditions:** Population roughly 26 million. Multi-year life expectancy below 75 years (versus South Korea's 83+). Multi-year infant mortality elevated relative to the global average. Multi-year chronic malnutrition rates elevated.

**Political conditions:** The indoctrination architecture has been weakening (per Carnegie analysis). The hatred and fear of South Korea, the US, and Japan has been operating less reliably as the DPRK population has acquired the smuggled access to South Korean and international media. The concentration-camp architecture continues operating but with reduced multi-year institutional efficiency. The multi-year loyalty of the WPK nomenklatura, the armed forces, the security services, and the donju (moneyed elite) is no longer guaranteed.

**Military conditions:** Roughly 1.1 million armed forces. Multi-month continued nuclear and missile development. Multi-month Russia arms-and-personnel cooperation operating against UN sanctions. The Korean People's Army is the pillar of the Kim dynasty.

## The succession timeline

The institutional question for the 2026-2031 period in Part XIV is whether Kim Jong Un dies or becomes incapacitated during the window. The South Korean NIS assessments suggest his health is deteriorating. The Foreign Policy May 2026 analysis characterised the Ju Ae succession acceleration as likely driven by health concerns. The multi-year weight fluctuations, the obesity-related conditions, and the facial growth are operating as the visible indicators of the succession-pressure architecture.

If Kim Jong Un dies or becomes incapacitated before 2031:
- Ju Ae (roughly 13-18 years old by 2031) would be the formally designated successor.
- Kim Yo Jong would likely operate as regent or co-leader.
- The multi-year apparatus around Kim Jong Un (Choe Ryong Hae, Kim Tok Hun, the military leadership) would operate as the governing council during the succession transition.
- The China and Russia architectures would shape the international response.

If Kim Jong Un survives the 2026-2031 period:
- The Ju Ae succession architecture continues developing.
- The multi-year DPRK-Russia alignment continues operating.
- The multi-year DPRK nuclear and missile architecture continues developing.
- The multi-year inter-Korean architecture continues operating under the December 2023 hostile-state framework.
---


---

# Dossier 35: Vladimir Putin and the Kremlin Apex Architecture

## The Kremlin institutional position

Vladimir Putin has was the apex institutional figure of the Russian Federation since 31 December 1999, when Boris Yeltsin resigned and Putin assumed acting presidential authority. As of May 2026, this represents 26 years and 5 months of continuous apex position. Putin is currently in the fifth presidential term, having been inaugurated 7 May 2024 for a term that runs until 2030. Under the 2020 constitutional amendments, he is eligible to stand for a sixth term in 2030 and would remain eligible through 2036, when he would be 83.

The duration of Putin's apex position is unprecedented in the post-Soviet Russian record. It surpasses Brezhnev's 18-year tenure as General Secretary (1964-1982) and Stalin's 24 years as the Soviet operator (1929-1953). Among contemporary apex political-class operators in the global G20 architecture, Putin's tenure is exceeded only by the hereditary monarchs (the Saudi king, the UK monarchy, and a handful of other dynastic positions) and by Paul Biya of Cameroon. Putin's position is the second-longest tenure among contemporary elected (formally) heads of state in any G20 economy.

Putin's institutional position parallels Xi Jinping's in the contemporary tripartite-competition architecture across Part XII Chapter 7. The two apex operators have built personalised authoritarian-state architectures around themselves; both have removed constitutional term limits to enable indefinite tenure; both have produced apparatuses in which no successor is publicly designated; both manage the confrontation with the US-led architecture across Volume One and the prior Volume Two dossiers. The principal institutional differences: Putin operates a single-resource extractive economy (energy exports) rather than the multi-sector Chinese commercial-class architecture; Putin has launched and is conducting an active multi-year war (against Ukraine since February 2022) where Xi has not yet done so against Taiwan; Putin's apex apparatus is built around the siloviki (security-services-trained) operators rather than the Chinese provincial-and-bureaucratic Tuanpai architecture.

## Putin — biographical foundation

Vladimir Vladimirovich Putin was born 7 October 1952 in Leningrad, then in the Russian SFSR of the Soviet Union (now St Petersburg, Russian Federation). His parents had lost two children before his birth, one during the prolonged Nazi siege of Leningrad (1941-1944) that killed roughly one million civilians. His father Vladimir Spiridonovich Putin (1911-1999) was a Red Army veteran and factory worker; his mother Maria Ivanovna Shelomova (1911-1998) was a factory worker. The family lived in modest conditions — by Putin's own account, in a "rat-infested communal apartment building."

Putin has repeatedly recalled a formative childhood incident in interviews — the cornered-rat episode. As a boy he and friends chased rats through the corridors of his apartment building with sticks. One day a large rat he had cornered turned on him and chased him back to his quarters. He said in a 2000 interview: "I got a quick and lasting lesson in the meaning of the word cornered." Multi-decade Kremlinology has produced the consensus interpretation that Putin identifies with the cornered rat as the metaphor for his political posture — willing to lash out catastrophically when he perceives his survival threatened.

Putin studied law at Leningrad State University from 1970 to 1975. His thesis adviser was Anatoly Sobchak, who would later become the first democratically-elected Mayor of St Petersburg and Putin's institutional patron. After graduation in 1975 Putin was assigned to the KGB.

## The KGB years 1975-1991

Putin's KGB career lasted 16 years and culminated at the rank of lieutenant colonel. He spent his first decade in internal Soviet operations. From 1985 to 1990 he was stationed in Dresden, East Germany — a relatively minor KGB outpost compared to the East Berlin or Moscow assignments. The Dresden assignment has been characterised by analysts as undistinguished; Putin operated as a mid-level intelligence officer rather than as the apex spymaster the post-2000 Russian state-media architecture has retrospectively constructed.

Two events from the Dresden period operated as institutional formation for Putin's subsequent political-class trajectory. First, the 1989 collapse of East German communism. Putin witnessed the dissolution of the East German state and observed that Moscow failed to respond to the crisis — the indicator that the Soviet empire was failing operationally. Second, the December 1989 incident in which East German protesters attempted to enter the KGB Dresden compound. Putin reportedly held them off at gunpoint while waiting for Moscow instructions that never came. The incident was the formation of his subsequent posture: the structural response to threat must be ruthless and immediate; the weakness of central authority produces collapse.

Putin retired from the KGB in 1991 with the rank of lieutenant colonel. The Soviet Union itself dissolved later that year.

## St Petersburg 1990-1996

Putin returned to Leningrad in 1990 as assistant to the rector of Leningrad State University responsible for international affairs. He subsequently became advisor to Anatoly Sobchak, his former law professor who had been elected chairman of the Leningrad City Council. When Leningrad was renamed St Petersburg and Sobchak became Mayor, Putin rose through the city government: Chairman of the St Petersburg City Council's International Relations Committee from June 1991; First Deputy Chairman of the St Petersburg City Government (First Deputy Mayor) from 1994.

The St Petersburg years operated as Putin's institutional formation in post-Soviet governance. He managed the city's external relations, oversaw the foreign-investment promotion programme, and built the governing network with operators who would later constitute his Moscow apparatus. figures who emerged from the St Petersburg city government include Alexei Kudrin (Finance Minister 2000-2011), German Gref (Sberbank chairman from 2007), Dmitry Medvedev (subsequent President), and the St Petersburg group that is the core of the contemporary Russian political class.

In 1996 Sobchak lost his re-election bid for Mayor of St Petersburg. Putin resigned from the city government. The institutional pathway from St Petersburg to Moscow was opened by Anatoly Chubais — the Yeltsin operator and architect of the privatisation programme — and Pavel Borodin, the Kremlin's chief administrator.

## The Moscow rise 1996-1999

In August 1996 Putin was appointed Deputy Chief of the Presidential Property Management Department under Pavel Borodin. The role managed the transfer of former Soviet assets to the Russian Federation — the pathway through which Putin gained operational access to the Russian financial and asset architecture.

In March 1997 Putin became Deputy Chief of Presidential Staff and Head of the Main Control Directorate. The institutional ascent was rapid: from St Petersburg deputy mayor to Kremlin staff in roughly 18 months.

On 25 July 1998 Yeltsin appointed Putin Director of the Federal Security Service (FSB) — the primary domestic successor agency of the KGB. Putin held the position until August 1999. The FSB tenure was  formative for two reasons.

First, Putin reorganised and strengthened the FSB after the post-Soviet period of perceived decline. The institutional restoration of the security architecture — the siloviki position that Putin would build into the apex of his subsequent Russian political-class architecture — was operationally inaugurated during the FSB Director period.

Second, Putin demonstrated his institutional loyalty to Yeltsin in the most consequential way possible. The Russian Prosecutor General Yuri Skuratov had been investigating corruption in Yeltsin's inner circle. Putin obtained and released a videotape showing Skuratov with two young naked women. Skuratov was removed from his position. The corruption investigation collapsed. The pattern: Putin's FSB protected Yeltsin and the Yeltsin Family circle from prosecution. The institutional value Putin demonstrated was the same institutional value that would later operate as the apex requirement of the post-Yeltsin transition — protection of the oligarchic and political class from accountability for the multi-year 1990s asset-acquisition record.

In October 1998 Putin became a permanent member of the Russian Security Council. In March 1999 he became Secretary of the Security Council. On 9 August 1999 he was appointed acting Prime Minister; on 16 August 1999 he was confirmed. The institutional rise from FSB Director to Prime Minister occurred in roughly 13 months.

## "Operation Successor" 1999

The apparatus of Putin's 1999 ascent is now documented in the subsequent Kremlinology literature. Yeltsin's circle ("The Family" — daughters, son-in-law, oligarchs) had multiple institutional concerns about the 2000 presidential transition: Yeltsin himself was in poor health, alcoholic, and constitutionally barred from running for a third term; the corruption allegations against Yeltsin and his inner circle would expose the Family to prosecution if a hostile successor emerged; the oligarchs (Boris Berezovsky, Vladimir Gusinsky, Mikhail Khodorkovsky, Roman Abramovich, and others who had acquired the 1990s privatised assets at discounts) faced potential expropriation under any alternative successor.

The Family conducted "Operation Successor" — the institutional programme to identify and install a loyal apex operator who would protect Yeltsin and the 1990s oligarchic architecture. Sergei Stepashin (former Interior Minister and FSB Director) was the structural test candidate; he failed the loyalty tests by seeking compromises with Yeltsin's opponents. He was removed as Prime Minister in August 1999 and Putin replaced him.
