# Dossier 37: The Caucasus Political Class — Aliyev, Pashinyan, Kavelashvili

## The regional institutional position

The South Caucasus comprises three independent states sandwiched between Russia, Iran, and Turkey: Azerbaijan, Armenia, and Georgia. Each state operates a distinct political-class architecture, and the three architectures stand at sharply divergent points along the contemporary Russia-versus-West axis that has structured post-Soviet politics in the post-1991 period.

Azerbaijan operates as a consolidated authoritarian dynasty under Ilham Aliyev, who has held the presidency since 2003 and inherited the position from his father Heydar Aliyev (President 1993-2003). The Aliyev architecture is one of two clear hereditary successions in the post-Soviet space (alongside Turkmenistan). Azerbaijan has used its oil and gas wealth to build a military capability that decisively reversed the Armenian control of Nagorno-Karabakh in two wars (2020 and 2023), produced the ethnic cleansing of roughly 100,000+ ethnic Armenians from the territory in October 2023, and consolidated the apex regional position.

Armenia is the most  pluralistic state in the South Caucasus. Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan came to power through the 2018 Velvet Revolution — a peaceful protest mobilisation that displaced the Republican Party architecture. Pashinyan has presided over the two military defeats (2020 Second Karabakh War; 2023 Nagorno-Karabakh capitulation) and the strategic reorientation of Armenia away from the Russia-led CSTO architecture and toward the EU accession process. The 8 August 2025 Trump-brokered Armenia-Azerbaijan peace framework is the formalisation of this trajectory. The June 2026 parliamentary election is the central institutional inflection point of the contemporary Armenian trajectory.

Georgia is the contested case. The Georgian Dream party, founded in 2012 by Russia-fortune billionaire Bidzina Ivanishvili, has won every parliamentary election since. The 26 October 2024 election produced contested results that the opposition refused to recognise. The 14 December 2024 election of former footballer Mikheil Kavelashvili as president (by a 300-seat electoral college rather than direct election) consolidated Georgian Dream's institutional position. Former President Salome Zourabichvili refused to recognise the result. The subsequent mass protests, the suspension of EU accession negotiations, and the US sanctions on Georgian operators have produced the most dramatic post-2003 reversal of any post-Soviet Western-oriented state.

## Ilham Aliyev — Azerbaijan

### Biographical foundation and the dynastic architecture

Ilham Heydar oglu Aliyev was born on 24 December 1961 in Baku, Azerbaijan SSR. He is the son of Heydar Aliyev (President of Azerbaijan 1993-2003) and Zarifa Aliyeva (ophthalmologist, died of cancer 1985). He has one older sister, Sevil Aliyeva. His biographical foundation is the governing formation of a Soviet political-class princeling: his father had risen to one of the most Azerbaijani positions in the Soviet apparatus, becoming a candidate Politburo member in 1976 and a full Politburo member plus First Deputy Chairman of the Council of Ministers of the USSR under Andropov in 1982 — the highest position ever reached by an Azerbaijani in the Soviet Union.

Heydar Aliyev's biographical foundation is the architectural pre-history of the Ilham trajectory. Heydar was born 10 May 1923 in Nakhchivan (the autonomous Azerbaijani exclave separated from the rest of Azerbaijan by Armenian territory). His family had relocated from Comardlı village (modern-day Tanahat in Syunik Province of Armenia) before his birth. Heydar joined the Soviet Ministry of State Security (predecessor to the KGB) in 1944 and trained at the Leningrad academy. He rose through the Azerbaijani KGB apparatus across the 1950s-1960s, became Chairman of the KGB of the Azerbaijan SSR in 1967, and First Secretary of the Communist Party of Azerbaijan in 1969 — the apex Soviet position in the republic. Mikhail Gorbachev dismissed him in 1987 over the Perestroika anti-corruption campaign. After the 1991 Soviet dissolution Heydar returned to Nakhchivan and was the apex regional figure until the 1993 Azerbaijani political crisis brought him back to Baku as President.

Ilham studied at Moscow State Institute of International Relations (MGIMO-MSIIR) from 1977 to 1985, earning a PhD in history. From 1985 to 1990 he lectured at MGIMO. From 1991 to 1994 he led private industrial-commercial enterprises in Moscow, struggling to find institutional position; he subsequently sought opportunity in Istanbul. After his father's 1993 rise to the Azerbaijani presidency, Ilham returned to Baku.

On 22 January 1983 Ilham married Mehriban Pashayeva (now Mehriban Aliyeva). The couple has three children: Leyla, Arzu, and Heydar. Heydar (named after his grandfather) is the apparent third-generation successor candidate.

From 1994 to 2003 Ilham served as Vice President, then First Vice President, of SOCAR (the State Oil Company of the Azerbaijan Republic) — the pathway through which he built the relationship with Western petroleum operators and constructed the architecture of contemporary Azerbaijani oil wealth. The Caspian-Mediterranean BTC pipeline agreement was concluded in 2000 with SOCAR involvement; the pipeline opened 2005. He was also elected to the Milli Majlis (Parliament) in 1995 and 2000.

### Succession 2003

The dynastic succession architecture was constructed across 2002-2003. The 2002 constitutional referendum made the Prime Minister next in line for the presidency if the president became incapacitated. By early 2003 Heydar's health had significantly declined. On 4 August 2003 Ilham was appointed Prime Minister; the National Assembly confirmed him 101-24 with opposition boycott. Less than two weeks before the October 2003 election Heydar withdrew his candidacy and appointed Ilham as the party's candidate. Ilham won the 15 October 2003 election with 76% of the vote — characterised by international observers as fraudulent. He was inaugurated 31 October 2003. Heydar died at the Cleveland Clinic on 12 December 2003.

The 2003 transition is one of two clear hereditary successions in the post-Soviet space (the other being Turkmenistan's Niyazov-to-Berdymukhamedov-to-Berdymukhamedov-Jr trajectory). The apparatus has been characterised across the literature as the post-Soviet template for dynastic-authoritarian succession.

### Five terms and the constitutional architecture

Ilham has been re-elected five times: 2003 (76%), 2008 (88.73%, opposition boycott), 2013 (84.54%), 2018 (86.02%), and 7 February 2024 (92.12%, called 20 months early). The 2009 constitutional referendum removed term limits and added new media restrictions. The 2016 constitutional referendum further consolidated apex authority, extended presidential terms from 5 to 7 years, lowered the minimum age for president from 35 to 18 ( clearing the path for the next-generation succession to son Heydar), and created the Vice President position. In February 2017 Aliyev appointed his wife Mehriban as Vice President — the architectural step that placed her constitutionally next in line for the presidency.

The Mehriban Aliyeva institutional position is the contemporary indicator that the dynasty has multiple succession candidates: Mehriban is the second-in-line operator; son Heydar (born roughly 1997) is the longer-term succession candidate. The Aliyev architecture has been characterised by Western analysts as the most fully consolidated post-Soviet dynastic-authoritarian architecture.

### The two Karabakh wars

The most consequential institutional events of Ilham's tenure have been the two Nagorno-Karabakh wars. The 1988-1994 First Nagorno-Karabakh War had produced Armenian military control of the predominantly Armenian-populated Nagorno-Karabakh region of Azerbaijan plus seven surrounding Azerbaijani districts. Roughly 700,000+ Azerbaijanis were displaced. The frozen conflict architecture operated from 1994 to 2020 under the OSCE Minsk Group (US, Russia, France) framework that produced no resolution.

The Second Nagorno-Karabakh War (27 September - 9 November 2020) reversed the Armenian position. The 44-day Azerbaijani offensive was enabled by three factors: the Azerbaijani military modernisation funded by oil and gas revenues; the decisive deployment of Turkish Bayraktar TB2 armed drones that systematically destroyed the Armenian armoured and air-defence systems; and the Russian failure to intervene on the Armenian side despite the CSTO mutual-defence framework. Russia brokered the 10 November 2020 ceasefire under which Azerbaijan retained the seven surrounding districts plus the southern third of Nagorno-Karabakh, while Russian peacekeepers deployed to the remaining Armenian-controlled portion. Aliyev hosted a December 2020 Victory Parade in Baku attended by Erdoğan — the formalisation of the Turkey-Azerbaijan strategic partnership.

The 2020-2023 institutional period operated under the frozen-conflict 2.0 architecture. Azerbaijani forces blockaded the Lachin Corridor (the road connecting Armenian-controlled Nagorno-Karabakh to Armenia) from December 2022 to April 2023, despite the Russian peacekeeping presence. Russia did little to intervene — the structural indicator that the Russian apparatus, under Ukraine-war pressure in Dossier 35, no longer operated the post-1994 institutional architecture as a regional security guarantor for Armenia.

The 19-20 September 2023 Azerbaijani military operation produced the surrender of the self-declared Republic of Artsakh. The aftermath: more than 100,000 of roughly 120,000 ethnic Armenians fled to Armenia by October 2023. The ethnic-Armenian population of Nagorno-Karabakh was eliminated. The outcome operates as one of the most consequential post-Cold-War ethnic cleansings — and operates without the international intervention that the 1990s humanitarian-intervention architecture had previously produced under comparable circumstances (Bosnia 1995, Kosovo 1999). The contemporary architecture has demonstrated that the 1990s norms have eroded.

### Repression and the 2024-2025 architecture

Freedom House's 2025 assessment rates Azerbaijan as "Not Free" with a score of 7 out of 100 for political rights and civil liberties. The multi-year post-2020 architecture has produced the intensification of repression. Reporters Without Borders characterised the late 2023 onwards as "a new wave of fierce repression against the country's last remaining journalists." Seven journalists from Abzas Media received long prison sentences in 2024-2025 — widely seen as retaliation for the outlet's subsequent investigations of corruption in the Aliyev family.

The contemporary Azerbaijani apparatus operates through the consolidated authoritarian apex (Ilham as President; Mehriban as Vice President; son Heydar as successor candidate), the oil and gas wealth that funds the military and patronage architecture, the alliance with Turkey under the "one nation, two states" framework, and the contemporary positioning between Russia and the West that maximises strategic optionality.

The 25 December 2024 Azerbaijan Airlines Flight 8243 incident — in which an Azerbaijani commercial aircraft was shot down over Grozny by Russian air defence (initially attributed to bird strike) killing 38 — produced tension in the Russia-Azerbaijan relationship. Aliyev demanded a Russian apology and compensation. Putin expressed condolences. The incident was the indicator that the contemporary Russia-Azerbaijan relationship has limits despite the strategic partnership.

## Nikol Pashinyan — Armenia

### Biographical foundation and the 2018 Velvet Revolution

Nikol Pashinyan was born on 1 June 1975 in Ijevan, Tavush Province, Armenian SSR. His biographical foundation is unusual among contemporary post-Soviet apex operators in that he came to power through mass mobilisation rather than through institutional succession, KGB pathway, or oligarchic patronage. His father Vova Pashinyan (1940-2020) was a football and volleyball coach and physical education teacher. His mother Svetlana died when Nikol was 12; he was primarily raised by his stepmother Yerjanik.

Pashinyan studied journalism at Yerevan State University. He built his career as a journalist across the 1990s-2000s, founding the *Haykakan Zhamanak* newspaper that operated as one of the independent Armenian media outlets. He produced multi-year critical coverage of the post-Soviet Armenian political-class architecture under Levon Ter-Petrosyan (President 1991-1998), Robert Kocharyan (President 1998-2008), and Serzh Sargsyan (President 2008-2018). He was charged multiple times across the 1990s and 2000s for journalism critical of authorities.

In 2007 Pashinyan was convicted of "incitement to mass disorder" related to the 2008 post-election protests against Sargsyan's first-term election. He was imprisoned 2009-2011. The imprisonment was the governing formation that built his multi-year reputation as the opposition figure who had been targeted by the Sargsyan apparatus.

In 2012 Pashinyan was elected to the National Assembly via the Armenian National Congress and subsequently the Civil Contract party predecessor. He operated as a opposition member throughout 2012-2018.

### The 2018 Velvet Revolution

The subsequent 2018 Velvet Revolution was the peaceful mass-mobilisation event that displaced the Republican Party architecture. The immediate trigger: Serzh Sargsyan's 2018 attempt to extend his tenure as Prime Minister after the 2015 constitutional referendum had moved Armenia from a presidential to a parliamentary system. The April 2018 parliamentary vote made Sargsyan Prime Minister; Pashinyan led protests against the move.

The protests grew across April 2018. Pashinyan led the "My Step" march from Gyumri to Yerevan. Mass mobilisation in Yerevan reached an estimated 250,000+ at peak — well above the Chenoweth-Stephan 3.5% threshold for Armenia's population. The Armenian military refused to intervene against the protests. On 23 April 2018 Sargsyan resigned. On 8 May 2018 the National Assembly elected Pashinyan as Prime Minister.

The 2018 Velvet Revolution was the most consequential institutional displacement of a post-Soviet political-class architecture through peaceful mass mobilisation. The comparison with the 2003 Georgian Rose Revolution, the 2004 Ukrainian Orange Revolution, and the 2014 Ukrainian Maidan Revolution is the pattern of post-Soviet mass-mobilisation displacement of authoritarian or oligarchic architectures. The Armenia case is distinctive in that the post-Velvet trajectory has produced a consolidated democratic-apparatus rather than the reversal that the Georgian and Ukrainian cases produced.

### The 2020 and 2023 Karabakh defeats

The most consequential institutional events of Pashinyan's tenure have been the 2020 and 2023 Nagorno-Karabakh defeats. The 2020 Second Karabakh War produced Armenian military collapse over 44 days. The outcome: Russian-brokered ceasefire that left Armenia with the northern half of Nagorno-Karabakh under Russian peacekeeping protection but lost the southern half plus all seven surrounding districts.

The 2023 Azerbaijani offensive produced the collapse of the remaining Armenian-controlled territory and the ethnic cleansing of roughly 100,000+ ethnic Armenians from the ancestral homeland. The outcome: complete Azerbaijani control of Nagorno-Karabakh; total demographic transformation; subsequent multi-year strategic reorientation of Armenia.

The Armenian political-class architecture had been built around the Nagorno-Karabakh question. The 1988-1994 First Karabakh War had been the founding institutional event of independent Armenia; the Karabakh leadership architecture (Kocharyan and Sargsyan were both born in Nagorno-Karabakh and operated through the Karabakh political-class pathway) had produced the pre-2018 architecture. The 2020-2023 multi-year loss of Karabakh  displaces the architecture and requires the reorientation that Pashinyan's "Real Armenia" project has been pursuing.

### The Russia-pivot and EU orientation

The post-2020 institutional indicator of Russian failure to protect Armenia under the CSTO mutual-defence framework produced the Armenian strategic reorientation. The key institutional steps:

- 18 February 2024: at the Munich Security Conference, Pashinyan characterised Armenia as not a Russian ally in the Ukraine invasion.
- 22 February 2024: announcement that Armenia was freezing participation in the CSTO.
- 28 February 2024: National Assembly speech characterising CSTO as "threat to the national security of Armenia."
- 9 September 2024: confirmation that EU membership process was on Armenian political agenda.
- 12 February 2025: Armenian parliament approved bill endorsing EU accession process.

The strategic reorientation is the most consequential institutional shift in the post-Soviet Armenian trajectory. The Armenia-Russia alliance — built around the 1992 Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance, the CSTO framework, the Russian military base in Gyumri, and the multi-billion-dollar economic dependency — has been weakened. The contemporary architecture: Armenia is the most Western-oriented former Soviet republic outside the Baltic states.

### The 8 August 2025 Trump-brokered peace framework

The central institutional output of the Pashinyan trajectory has been the 2024-2025 Armenia-Azerbaijan peace negotiations. In July 2025 Pashinyan met Aliyev in the United Arab Emirates as part of the pre-summit architecture. On 8 August 2025 both leaders signed a framework peace agreement in a White House ceremony hosted by Trump.

The framework agreement included provisions on:
- Mutual recognition of territorial integrity.
- Diplomatic normalisation.
- Border delimitation.
- Zangezur Corridor connectivity (Azerbaijan-Nakhchivan transit through southern Armenian territory).
- Mutual withdrawal of international claims.

The unresolved element: Azerbaijan demands Armenia revise its constitution to remove the indirect reference to the Armenia-Karabakh union. The constitutional revision requires a referendum, scheduled separately from the June 2026 parliamentary election. The 2026 trajectory depends on whether Pashinyan can win the parliamentary election with the majority required to deliver the constitutional revision.

### Domestic political conflicts and the 2026 election

The post-2020 multi-year Pashinyan approval has declined from the 2018-2020 peak (60-70%) to the 11-17% range in mid-2025 polls. The contemporary opposition architecture includes:

- **Robert Kocharyan's Armenia Alliance**: former President 1998-2008; Karabakh-born; Russia-aligned operator.
- **Samvel Karapetyan's Strong Armenia**: Armenian-Cypriot-Russian businessman; arrested 18 June 2025 after defending the Armenian Apostolic Church; house arrest extended April 2026 (running through the June 2026 election). Karapetyan characterised by Pashinyan as instrument of Russian "hybrid operation."
- **Gagik Tsarukyan's Prosperous Armenia**: businessman-politician; opposition operator.

The 2024-2026 institutional disputes have produced episodes:

- **18 September 2024**: National Security Service announced foiled pro-Russian coup attempt.
- **June 2025**: open conflict with Armenian Apostolic Church; Pashinyan call for removal of Catholicos of All Armenians Karekin II.
- **October 2025**: Archbishop Mikael Ajapahyan arrested; sentenced 2 years November 2025; moved to house arrest February 2026.
- **2025-2026**: pro-Russian Matryoshka bot network produced 343 fake videos against Armenian political leadership ahead of the June 2026 election.
- **Putin statement**: Armenia's EU plans "require special consideration"; parallel drawn with Ukraine.

The June 2026 Armenian parliamentary election is the central institutional indicator of whether Pashinyan's multi-year "Real Armenia" project consolidates institutionally or whether the post-Karabakh opposition reverses the 2018 Velvet Revolution trajectory. The outcome will determine whether Armenia continues operating as the most pluralistic post-Soviet South Caucasus state or whether the contemporary architecture fractures under Russia-pressure and domestic-opposition combined operation.

## Mikheil Kavelashvili — Georgia

### Biographical foundation

Mikheil Kavelashvili was born on 22 July 1971. His biographical foundation is unique among contemporary post-Soviet heads of state — he is a former professional footballer with no political-class background. He emerged from Dinamo Tbilisi's youth system as a promising young footballer in 1989. He played for Dinamo Tbilisi (Georgian league), Manchester City (English Premier League, mid-1990s), Grasshopper Club Zürich (Switzerland), and other clubs. He retired from football in 2006.

In 2016 Kavelashvili was elected to the Georgian Parliament on the Georgian Dream ticket. In 2022 he co-founded the People's Power political movement, allied with Georgian Dream. People's Power has been characterised by Western observers as the most strongly anti-Western Georgian political formation. Kavelashvili was one of the authors of the controversial 2024 "foreign agents" law that required organisations receiving more than 20% of their funding from abroad to register as "pursuing the interest of a foreign power" — a law modeled on the Russian 2012 foreign-agents legislation that has was the architecture for multi-year Russian repression of independent civil-society architecture.

Kavelashvili lacks higher education. He has been frequently mocked by the Georgian opposition for this — on the day of his election as President, protesters outside the Parliament building brought their own university diplomas to display, while others kicked footballs around to mock his pre-political career.

### The 2024 election crisis and the Kavelashvili elevation

The 26 October 2024 Georgian parliamentary elections produced contested results. Georgian Dream officially won; the opposition coalitions, the Western election observers, and the then-incumbent President Salome Zourabichvili refused to recognise the result as legitimate. The allegations included ballot stuffing, voter intimidation, vote buying, and Russian interference.

The post-October 2024 protests in Tbilisi reached 100,000+ at peak. The protests continued across November-December 2024 and beyond, becoming the largest sustained mass mobilisation in Georgian history. The Georgian Dream government's response included mass arrests, police violence, water cannons, and wave of post-2024 repressive legislation.

The November 2024 Georgian Dream announcement that Georgia would postpone EU accession negotiations until 2028 was the trigger that intensified the protests. The post-1991 Georgian institutional trajectory had been characterised by multi-year Western orientation, NATO partnership, and the 2003 Rose Revolution that displaced the Shevardnadze architecture. The 2012 Georgian Dream electoral victory had operated initially as the continuation of the Western orientation; the post-2020 trajectory has been the shift to Russia-aligned positioning.

On 14 December 2024 the 300-seat Georgian electoral college — which replaced direct presidential elections under the 2017 constitutional amendments — elected Kavelashvili as President. He was the only candidate on the ballot. He was elected with 224 votes from the 300-member electoral college. He was inaugurated 29 December 2024.

President Zourabichvili refused to recognise the election. She refused to leave the Presidential Palace initially; she subsequently left under protest. She has operated as the opposition figurehead since December 2024.

### The Ivanishvili apex architecture

The contemporary Georgian apex operator is not formally Kavelashvili or Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze. The institutional apex is Bidzina Ivanishvili — the billionaire founder of Georgian Dream. Ivanishvili was born in 1956 in the rural Georgian Sachkhere district. He made his multi-billion-dollar fortune in Russia across the 1990s in the post-Soviet privatisation architecture. He returned to Georgia in 2003. He founded Georgian Dream in 2012 and led the party's victory in the 2012 parliamentary elections that displaced the Saakashvili-led United National Movement architecture. He served as Prime Minister from October 2012 to November 2013. He has subsequently was the Honorary Chairman of Georgian Dream and the actual decision-maker behind the party.

The Ivanishvili fortune is roughly $5 billion — relative to Georgian GDP of roughly $30 billion, the wealth-to-economy ratio is one of the highest in any contemporary state. The Ivanishvili architecture is the most consequential post-Soviet oligarch-party architecture. The multi-year contemporary Georgian Dream apparatus operates as Ivanishvili's personal political-class architecture.

other Georgian Dream operators:

- **Irakli Kobakhidze**: Prime Minister since 8 February 2024. Former Speaker of Parliament. The post-2024 PM.
- **Irakli Garibashvili**: PM 2013-2015 and 2021-2024. Multi-decade Ivanishvili operator.
- **Shalva Papuashvili**: Speaker of Parliament.

### The contemporary trajectory

The 2024-2026 Georgian institutional trajectory has produced four consequences:

**First**, the consolidation of Georgian Dream's post-2024 architecture. The parliamentary, presidential, and government positions are all held by Georgian Dream operators. The judicial architecture has been absorbed. The multi-year opposition is fragmented across multiple parties (United National Movement, Coalition for Change, Strong Georgia, others) without unified leadership.

**Second**, the suspension of EU accession. Georgia had been granted EU candidate status in December 2023. The November 2024 postponement to 2028 was the structural indicator that the Georgian Dream architecture would not pursue the Western orientation. The EU formally suspended Georgia's accession-track acceleration. The US sanctions on Georgian operators (Ivanishvili, other operators) operate as the Western diplomatic pressure architecture.

**Third**, the alignment with the Russia-China-Iran architecture. Georgia has not formally rejoined the CSTO or the CIS but has aligned with the Russia-led architecture on critical institutional questions. The 2024-2026 Georgian Dream architecture is the most Russia-aligned former Soviet republic apart from Belarus.

**Fourth**, the mass-mobilisation architecture that has continued operating from December 2024 through 2026. The protests have not displaced the Georgian Dream architecture, but they have was the continuing institutional indicator that the Georgian Dream consolidation has not been fully accepted by the Georgian majority. The multi-year trajectory operates as one of the central institutional indicators of contemporary post-Soviet architecture: whether post-Soviet states with pre-existing Western-orientation can be re-institutionalised toward Russia-alignment through parliamentary-and-governmental capture, or whether mass mobilisation is the check on reversal.

## The contemporary South Caucasus architecture

The three apex operators of the contemporary South Caucasus — Aliyev (Azerbaijan), Pashinyan (Armenia), Kavelashvili / Ivanishvili (Georgia) — operate within shared structural pressures.

**The Russia question.** Russia's regional position has been weakened by the post-2022 Ukraine war. Russian peacekeepers failed to protect Nagorno-Karabakh in 2022-2023. Russia has not been able to block Pashinyan's 2024 CSTO withdrawal or his multi-year EU pivot. The Russia-Azerbaijan relationship has been tested by the December 2024 plane incident. The Russia-Georgia relationship is the most Russia-aligned of the three but operates without institutional capture.

**The Turkey question.** Turkey is the most consequential regional partner for Azerbaijan ("one nation, two states") and as a strategic challenge for Armenia (the Armenia-Turkey closed border; the Genocide-recognition question; the Zangezur Corridor question). The Turkey-Georgia relationship is the pragmatic partnership.

**The Iran question.** Iran operates as the major regional power adjacent to all three states. The Iran-Armenia relationship is the pragmatic partnership (despite the religious-cultural differences). The Iran-Azerbaijan relationship is tense (multi-year Iranian anxiety about Azerbaijani Turkic-nationalist sentiment in Iran's millions of Azerbaijani population). The Iran-Georgia relationship is the most limited.

**The Western architecture.** The EU is the aspirational architecture for Armenia and (less so) Georgia, and as the contested architecture for Azerbaijan (which operates as a selective EU partner on energy questions but not on democratic-governance questions). The US under Trump 2 is the unpredictable architecture — the 8 August 2025 White House peace ceremony stood as the most consequential Trump-administration intervention in the post-Soviet South Caucasus.

The 2026-2028 trajectory operates under the central institutional indicators. The June 2026 Armenian parliamentary election will determine whether the Pashinyan trajectory consolidates. The 2027 Georgian parliamentary election will operate as the central institutional indicator of whether the Georgian Dream architecture can be displaced. The 2031 Azerbaijan presidential election — under the 7-year term — will operate as the central institutional indicator of whether the Aliyev dynasty consolidates the next-generation succession (Heydar Aliyev II, roughly 34 years old in 2031) or whether the dynastic architecture continues operating under Ilham.
---


---

# Dossier 38: The Central Asian Political Class — Five States, Five Operators

## The regional institutional position

Central Asia comprises five states that emerged from the December 1991 Soviet dissolution: Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Turkmenistan. Combined population roughly 80 million. Combined GDP roughly $500 billion as of 2024. The five-state architecture has produced the most consolidated post-Soviet authoritarian regional architecture, with all five operating as personalised authoritarian-leader systems under operators whose tenures span the post-Soviet period.

The contemporary Central Asian political class operates at the intersection of four competing apparatuss. The Russian architecture, dominant since the Tsarist conquests of the 19th century and consolidated through the Soviet period, has been weakening since 2022 under the pressure of the Ukraine war (in Dossier 35). The Chinese architecture, expanding since the 2013 Belt and Road Initiative launch at Astana, has become the largest economic operator across the region. The Turkish architecture, advancing through the Organisation of Turkic States since the 2021 reorganisation, has captured the cultural-and-political-class affinities of Turkic-speaking Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Turkmenistan as observer). The Western architecture, marginal during the Karimov and Niyazov authoritarian-isolationist period, has been re-engaging through the C5+1 framework launched 2015 and considerably expanded under Biden's September 2023 first leaders summit.

The contemporary five operators are: Kassym-Jomart Tokayev (Kazakhstan, since 2019); Shavkat Mirziyoyev (Uzbekistan, since 2016); Emomali Rahmon (Tajikistan, since 1992); Sadyr Japarov (Kyrgyzstan, since 2020); and Serdar Berdymukhamedov (Turkmenistan, since 2022, with his father Gurbanguly continuing as Chairman of the People's Council in an institutional position above the presidency). Three of the five (Rahmon, the Berdymukhamedov dynasty, and in practice Mirziyoyev under the Karimov-clan continuation) operate as entrenched architectures; two (Tokayev and Japarov) emerged from the contemporary post-2019 reshuffle.

## Kassym-Jomart Tokayev — Kazakhstan

### Biographical foundation

Kassym-Jomart Tokayev was born on 17 May 1953 in Alma-Ata (now Almaty), Kazakh SSR. His biographical foundation is distinctive among contemporary post-Soviet apex operators: he came from an intellectual rather than political-class family. His father Kemel Tokayev (1923-1986) was a Great Patriotic War veteran and a well-known writer who founded the detective-adventure genre in Kazakh literature. His mother Turar Shabarbayeva (1931-2000) worked at the Alma-Ata Teacher Training Institute of Foreign Languages. The intellectual-family formation produced Tokayev as the Soviet diplomatic-corps operator rather than the regional party operator.

In 1975 Tokayev graduated from the Moscow State Institute of International Relations (MGIMO) — the Soviet diplomatic-training institution. His pre-degree internship was at the USSR Embassy in the People's Republic of China. In 1983-1984 he interned at the Beijing Linguistic Institute. The China-focused early training produced his diplomatic-class fluency in Chinese affairs that has operated across the contemporary Kazakhstan-China relationship.

Tokayev's career as a Soviet diplomat began in 1975 in the USSR Ministry of Foreign Affairs. He was posted to the Soviet Embassy in Singapore (1975-1979). He returned to the USSR Foreign Ministry from 1979. In 1992 — after Kazakhstan's December 1991 independence — he was appointed Deputy Foreign Minister of the new Kazakh state. He became First Deputy Foreign Minister in 1993 and Foreign Minister in 1994.

Tokayev's institutional rise across the 1990s-2010s operated under Nazarbayev's patronage. He became Deputy Prime Minister in March 1999 and Prime Minister on 12 October 1999, holding the post until January 2002. Kazakhstan's GDP grew 13.5% in 2001 under his premiership. He was Foreign Minister again 2003-2007. In January 2007 he was elected Chairman of the Senate. From 2011 to 2013 he served as Director-General of the UN Office at Geneva — the first Asian to hold the position. He returned to Kazakhstan in 2013 as Senate Chairman.

### Presidential succession 2019

On 19 March 2019 Nursultan Nazarbayev — President since 1990 and the apex Kazakh operator since the post-Soviet period — announced his surprise resignation. Tokayev, as Senate Chairman, was constitutionally designated the acting president. He assumed acting authority on 20 March 2019. The subsequent snap election was held 9 June 2019; Tokayev won with 70.96%, was inaugurated 12 June 2019. He was initially seen as a placeholder for the Nazarbayev clan architecture.

The Nazarbayev "Elbasy" (Leader of Nation) framework was the governing architecture of indirect post-resignation rule. Nazarbayev retained chairmanship of the Security Council; his daughter Dariga Nazarbayeva chaired the Senate; his nephew Samat Abish operated as deputy head of the KNB intelligence service; the Nazarbayev family and clients controlled major portions of the Kazakh economic apparatus. Tokayev operated formally as president but operationally as the Nazarbayev-clan placeholder across 2019-2022.

### The January 2022 "Bloody January" inflection point

The most consequential institutional event of Tokayev's tenure was the January 2022 Qandy Qantar ("Bloody January") protests. The trigger: on 2 January 2022 a doubling of the state-regulated price of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) in the western oil town of Zhanaozen produced rolling protests. The protests spread to Almaty within three days. On 5 January 2022 protesters torched the Almaty city hall and the presidential residence. The entire cabinet resigned.

Tokayev's institutional response was the reset that transformed him from Nazarbayev-clan placeholder to operationally apex Kazakh operator. He took three decisive actions. First, he sacked Nazarbayev as chair of the powerful Security Council — eliminating the architecture of Nazarbayev influence. Second, he invoked the Collective Security Treaty Organisation (CSTO), which deployed roughly 2,500 Russian-led troops for 8 days. Third, he publicly authorised security forces to "fire without warning." At least 238 people were killed across the 8-day crisis; thousands were detained. Nazarbayev relatives, including nephew Samat Abish, were subsequently prosecuted.

The consequence: Tokayev consolidated apex Kazakh authority. The Nazarbayev-clan architecture was displaced. The subsequent June 2022 constitutional referendum (77% approval) curbed presidential powers, bolstered parliamentary authority, banned relatives from government positions, and stripped Nazarbayev's "Leader of Nation" status. The Nur-Sultan capital reverted to its original name Astana. On 20 November 2022 Tokayev was re-elected with 81.31% under reset clock for a single 7-year non-renewable term running to 2029.

### The post-2022 architecture

The post-2022 Tokayev architecture has operated through three institutional features. First, the Nazarbayev family dismantling. On 15 February 2023 Tokayev signed the law repealing the "Law on the First President" / Elbasy framework, removing Nazarbayev family lifetime immunity. The prosecutions of Nazarbayev-clan operators continued across 2023-2024. The financial holdings of the Nazarbayev family in Western jurisdictions ($8B+ identified per investigative-journalism reporting) were subjects of asset recovery efforts.

Second, the multi-vector foreign policy. Tokayev has pledged to continue the Nazarbayev-framework multi-vector approach — balancing relations with Russia, China, the United States, the EU, and the regional architectures. The consequences: 200,000 Russian draft-dodgers absorbed since September 2022 producing subsequent demographic and economic effects; 2023 China "permanent comprehensive strategic partnership" upgrade; 2024 Xi visit plus Scholz and Macron visits to Astana; $15.7B FDI attracted 2024 (the highest level in North and Central Asia); parallel-import architecture operating despite US Treasury sanctions warnings.

Third, the post-2022 reform programme. October 2024 nuclear power plant referendum approved construction near Lake Balkhash. 18 March 2026 constitutional amendment passed with 87% strengthened presidential power including right to dissolve parliament — the reversal of the 2022 parliamentary-strengthening trajectory. June 2025 face-covering ban (niqab/burqa) in public spaces. 2026 declared digitalization year. GDP doubling goal by 2029. The subsequent April 2024 Saltanat law restored criminal liability for domestic violence (after outcry over Saltanat Nukenova murder).

The Kazakhstan position: the largest, wealthiest, and most consequential Central Asian state. Tokayev's post-2022 architecture is the contemporary Central Asian model — the modernising authoritarian operator who has consolidated apex authority, eliminated the predecessor clan, and balanced multi-vector relations.

## Shavkat Mirziyoyev — Uzbekistan

### Biographical foundation

Shavkat Miromonovich Mirziyoyev was born on 24 July 1957 in Zaamin District, Jizzakh Region, Uzbek SSR — to a family of doctors. The intellectual-family formation parallels Tokayev's. He has higher education in engineering rather than diplomacy or law: 1981 graduate of the Tashkent Institute of Engineers of Irrigation and Mechanization of Agriculture, degree in mechanical engineering, PhD in Technical Sciences, Associate Professor. From 1981 to 1990 he held academic positions at the Tashkent Institute — junior research fellow, lecturer, associate professor, vice rector for academic affairs.

His political career began in 1990 with election as deputy of the Supreme Council of the Republic. In 1992 — under President Islam Karimov's architecture — he was appointed khokim (governor) of Mirzo Ulugbek District of Tashkent. From 1996 to 2001 he served as hokim of Jizzakh Region (his home region); from 2001 to 2003 hokim of Samarqand Region. The Samarkand-clan affiliation has been one institutional indicator of the Uzbek political-class architecture.

On 12 December 2003 Karimov nominated Mirziyoyev as Prime Minister; the parliament confirmed. He served as PM for 13 years (2003-2016) under Karimov, re-approved in 2005, 2010, and 2015. The PM tenure produced his economic-management profile and built the network that would underpin his 2016 succession.

### The 2016 succession

Islam Karimov — President 1989-2016, authoritarian-isolationist operator — died officially on 2 September 2016. (The subsequent speculation that Karimov died earlier than reported has not been conclusively confirmed.) Karimov's death produced the succession question. The Constitution of Uzbekistan designated Senate Chairman Nigmatilla Yuldashev as constitutional successor.

On 8 September 2016, however, a joint session of the Legislative Chamber and Senate of the Oliy Majlis appointed Mirziyoyev as interim president. Yuldashev formally proposed Mirziyoyev's appointment, citing Mirziyoyev's "many years of experience." The constitutional bypass was the indicator that the Karimov-clan apex apparatus had selected Mirziyoyev as successor — likely through coordination among Karimov's widow Tatyana Karimova, the National Security Council chairman Rustam Inoyatov, and other apex operators of the Karimov architecture.

On 4 December 2016 Mirziyoyev was elected President with 88.61% of the vote. He was inaugurated 14 December 2016. The subsequent transition was the structural consolidation of the post-Karimov architecture under Mirziyoyev's authority.

### The post-2016 reform period

The 2016-2022 Mirziyoyev period produced the most ambitious post-Soviet Central Asian reform programme. The key institutional features:

**Economic liberalisation.** Mirziyoyev lifted trade barriers, allowed the currency to float (a Karimov-era restriction), and courted foreign investment. The November 2018 "Reform Roadmap" 2019-2021 impressed foreign investors. December 2019: *The Economist* named Uzbekistan "country of the year" for the reform trajectory.

**Human-rights reforms.** Mirziyoyev ended the Karimov-era state-mandated cotton-picking forced-labour architecture in 2017 — the most consequential post-Soviet labour-rights reform. The US removed Uzbekistan from its list of "countries of particular concern" for religious freedom. The public call to prayer was allowed in 2017 (a Karimov restriction). The ban on forced and child labour was institutionalised. Multi-year subsequent laws on gender equality, access to justice, and ratification of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities followed.

**Regional reconciliation.** Mirziyoyev settled border disputes with Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan that had operated as frozen-conflict zones across the Karimov tenure. Regular flights between Tashkent and Dushanbe resumed January 2017 (the first since 1992). The 2017-2021 Mirziyoyev-Rahmon and Mirziyoyev-Japarov relationship-building was the foundation of contemporary regional integration.

**International re-engagement.** Mirziyoyev visited 36 countries in 2019. The Tashkent hosting of summits, the EBRD 2023 annual meeting in Tashkent (the first since 2003), and the Organisation of Turkic States chairmanship from 2022 was the re-engagement architecture.

### The post-2022 consolidation

The post-2022 institutional period has produced two central features. First, the April 2023 constitutional referendum extended presidential terms from 5 to 7 years and reset the term-clock for Mirziyoyev. On 9 July 2023 Mirziyoyev was re-elected with 87.05% in a snap presidential election held days after the constitutional revision. His current term runs to 2030; under the reset clock he is eligible to stand again in 2030 and serve to 2037.

Second, the authoritarian-consolidation pattern. The 1 July 2022 Karakalpakstan protests over proposed constitutional amendments that would have ended the Karakalpakstan autonomous-region status produced brutal suppression: at least 18 killed. The post-2022 institutional pattern has been characterised by observers (Gaszprom Institute, the Human Rights Watch coverage, the EBRD reservations) as the Karimov-era authoritarian-architecture continuation rather than the liberalisation-of-2016-2022 trajectory.

The current Mirziyoyev architecture operates as the modernising-authoritarian model — reform-oriented economic policy combined with consolidated apex authority and repression of organised political opposition. The 2030 succession question (whether Mirziyoyev stands for another term or whether the next-generation succession architecture operates) is the central uncertainty of the contemporary Uzbek political-class architecture.

## Emomali Rahmon — Tajikistan

### Biographical foundation and the Civil War rise

Emomali Rahmon was born on 5 October 1952 in Dangara, Kulob district, Tajik SSR. He is the longest-serving contemporary head of state in Central Asia and the second-longest contemporary post-Soviet head of state after Lukashenko (Dossier 36). His tenure spans 33+ years as of May 2026 — encompassing the Tajik Civil War (1992-1997), the post-Civil-War reconstruction, and the authoritarian consolidation that has operated through to the contemporary 2024-2026 succession transition.

Rahmon's pre-1992 background was as a Soviet operator and a collective farm director in his native Dangara region. He had no major political-class profile before the Soviet dissolution. The 1991-1992 Tajik political crisis — produced by the Soviet collapse, the regional-clan competition among the Khojent (north), Kulob (south), Garm (east), and Pamir (east) regional factions, and the emerging Islamist-secular cleavage — produced the 1992 Civil War.

The 1992-1997 Tajik Civil War killed roughly 50,000-100,000 people and displaced over 1.2 million. The conflict pitted the post-Soviet government (with Russian and Uzbek support) against the United Tajik Opposition (Islamist and democratic factions). Rahmon emerged from the Kulob faction as the apex operator. He was elected Chairman of the Supreme Soviet of Tajikistan in November 1992 — the position that made him head of state. He won the November 1994 presidential election (after the constitutional shift to direct elections).

### Multi-decade consolidation

Rahmon has been re-elected in 1999, 2006, 2013, and 2020. The post-1997 Civil War peace agreement initially produced power-sharing with the United Tajik Opposition. The Rahmon trajectory has been the governing dismantling of the 1997 power-sharing framework. The Islamic Renaissance Party of Tajikistan (the post-Soviet moderate Islamic opposition that was a 1997 peace-agreement signatory) was banned in 2015. The other opposition parties have been systematically marginalised or banned across the tenure.

The 2003 constitutional referendum extended presidential terms to 7 years. The 2016 constitutional referendum removed all term limits for Rahmon — granting him the title of "Founder of Peace and National Unity — Leader of the Nation" with lifetime authority to stand. He is eligible to remain president indefinitely.

The contemporary Tajikistan apparatus has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consolidated authoritarian state in Central Asia (with Turkmenistan). The Freedom House assessments rate Tajikistan as "Not Free." The political-prisoner architecture, the banned opposition parties, and the independent-media restrictions operate as the indicators.

### The Rustam Emomali succession architecture

The 2024-2026 institutional question for Tajikistan is whether the Rahmon-to-son succession architecture is being prepared. Rustam Emomali (born 30 December 1987) — Rahmon's eldest son — is the succession candidate. The positioning:

- 2017-2020: Mayor of Dushanbe.
- 2020-present: Chairman of the National Assembly (Majlisi Milli, the upper house of parliament). The position makes him constitutionally first-in-line for the presidency.
- international visibility: visited Iran January 2024 ($120M agreements with Raisi); travelled to Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan (meetings with heads of state); travelled to Kyrgyzstan, Russia, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan (meetings with heads or deputy heads of state).

The pattern parallels the Berdymukhamedov dynasty: father grooming son for succession through institutional positioning. The Eurasianet February 2024 analysis cited Tajik sources suggesting the succession timeline could be operating: "If internal political issues do not arise in the near future, then after the border issues with Kyrgyzstan are resolved, early elections will be announced."

The Kyrgyz-Tajik border resolution was produced in March 2025 (the Japarov-Rahmon State Border Treaty). The succession timeline is now operationally clear: the 2025-2027 period may produce the Rahmon-to-son transition. The Tajik succession would be the third post-Soviet dynastic succession (after Azerbaijan's Heydar-to-Ilham 2003 and Turkmenistan's Gurbanguly-to-Serdar 2022).

### The February 2026 health episode

In February 2026 Rahmon disappeared from public view for over two weeks — producing speculation about whether he was alive. He reportedly travelled to China for medical treatment for an undisclosed illness. The institutional consequence: the first known occasion when an infirm Central Asian leader opted to receive treatment in China rather than in Moscow or in the West — the indicator of China's rising regional influence relative to Russian and Western architectures.

Rahmon reappeared 14 February 2026, looking relatively fit, at an Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank delegation meeting. The subsequent 26 March 2026 state visit to Uzbekistan (inaugurating the new Tajik embassy in Tashkent with Mirziyoyev) produced the indicator that Rahmon's tenure continues operating — but the succession timeline has been operationally placed on the agenda.

## Sadyr Japarov — Kyrgyzstan

### Biographical foundation

Sadyr Japarov was born on 6 December 1968 in Kek-Saray, Tien-Shan region, Kyrgyz SSR. His biographical foundation is distinctive among contemporary Central Asian apex operators in that he came to power through mass mobilisation rather than through institutional succession, party-apparatus rise, or dynastic continuity. Kyrgyzstan is the post-Soviet Central Asian state with the most pluralistic architecture — three mass-mobilisation events have displaced presidents (2005 Tulip Revolution, 2010 protests, 2020 protests). Japarov emerged from the 2020 protest cycle.

His pre-political career was as a businessman, then politician. He served as Member of Parliament 2005-2007. From 2008-2010 he headed the National Agency for Combating Corruption. After 2010 he operated as a opposition operator. In 2013 he was imprisoned for "hostage-taking" — a contested charge widely characterised by observers as politically motivated.

The October 2020 Kyrgyz political crisis followed contested parliamentary elections. Protests in Bishkek produced the collapse of President Sooronbay Jeenbekov's authority. Protesters freed Japarov from prison on 6 October 2020. On 10 October 2020 the parliament appointed him Prime Minister. On 14 October 2020 he also became Acting President. On 10 January 2021 he was elected President with 79.2%. The 11 April 2021 constitutional referendum shifted Kyrgyzstan from a parliamentary system to a presidential system, expanding presidential powers.

### Japarov presidency

The Japarov tenure has produced the consolidation of presidential power against the pluralistic Kyrgyz apparatus. The 2021 constitutional referendum served as the architectural reset. The subsequent 2021-2026 trajectory has included media restrictions, NGO restrictions (2024 foreign-agents law similar to Russian 2012 architecture), and opposition-operator prosecutions.

The Japarov foreign policy has operated under the multi-vector framework. Kyrgyzstan hosts four Russian military installations including Kant Air Base. The post-2022 Japarov posture has been careful — maintaining Russian institutional relations without endorsing the Russian Ukraine invasion. The 9 May 2025 attendance at the Moscow Victory Day Parade operated as symbolic alignment with Russia.

The March 2025 Japarov-Rahmon State Border Treaty resolved the Kyrgyz-Tajik border dispute that had produced 2021 and 2022 border clashes (250+ killed across the two incidents). The diplomatic achievement was the most consequential regional-security accomplishment of 2025.

The 2026 institutional trajectory: parliamentary elections in 2026; presidential election scheduled 2027. Japarov has stated his intention to seek a second term. The Kyrgyz post-Soviet pattern of mass mobilisation displacing presidents may operate again — or the Japarov consolidation may operate to absorb potential opposition mobilisation.

## Serdar Berdymukhamedov — Turkmenistan and the dynastic architecture

### The Niyazov-Berdymukhamedov dynasty

Turkmenistan operates the most closed-authoritarian Central Asian apparatus and one of the most closed-authoritarian contemporary states globally. The architecture has produced three apex operators across the 1991-2026 period: Saparmurat Niyazov ("Turkmenbashi"), Gurbanguly Berdymukhamedov, and Serdar Berdymukhamedov.

**Saparmurat Niyazov** (1940-2006) was the founding apex operator. He had been First Secretary of the Communist Party of Turkmenistan from 1985 and was elected President of the Turkmen SSR in October 1990. He continued as President of independent Turkmenistan from December 1991 to his death on 21 December 2006. His tenure produced the most eccentric cult-of-personality architecture in the post-Soviet space: he renamed days of the week and months after himself and family members; he wrote *Ruhnama* (the required-reading text combining biography, history, and philosophical reflection); he banned ballet, opera, and other cultural forms; he renamed the month of April after his mother.

**Gurbanguly Berdymukhamedov** (born 29 June 1957) was a Health Minister under Niyazov — the dentist by training who emerged as succession candidate during Niyazov's final illness. He became acting president on Niyazov's death in December 2006. He was elected President in February 2007 with 89% (Niyazov-era official statistics) and re-elected 2012 (97%) and 2017 (97.6%). On 19 March 2022 he handed the presidency to his son Serdar — the first post-Soviet father-to-son peaceful succession transfer. He continues as Chairman of the Halk Maslahaty (People's Council) — an institutional position above the presidency in the post-2022 architecture.

**Serdar Berdymukhamedov** (born 22 September 1981) is the contemporary apex operator (though operating under his father's architectural authority). His career was the bureaucratic operator pathway under his father. He held various ministerial positions across 2010s-2021. In November 2020 he was appointed Deputy Prime Minister. On 12 March 2022 he was elected President with 72.97% of the vote — the lower official percentage than his father's previous elections operating as the indicator of the dynastic-succession architectural calibration. He was inaugurated 19 March 2022.

### Turkmenistan position

The contemporary Turkmenistan position operates through three institutional features. First, the decades-long "permanent neutrality" foreign policy — Turkmenistan has not joined CSTO, EAEU, or other Russia-led architectures. It is an OSCE member with observer status at SCO and the Organisation of Turkic States. Second, the heavy China dependence: Turkmenistan has 4th-largest global natural gas reserves; the majority of gas exports flow to China via the Central Asia-China gas pipeline (operational since 2009). Third, the most closed authoritarian architecture: independent media is largely banned, religious practice is restricted, travel abroad requires state permission.

The Berdymukhamedov dynasty's 2022 transition has been characterised as the model for post-Soviet father-to-son succession that may be emulated by Rahmon (Tajikistan), and that has already operated under Aliyev (Azerbaijan, Dossier 37). The dynastic-succession architecture serves as the post-Soviet authoritarian-architecture template that has now produced three consolidated dynasties in the period.

## The regional architecture

### The Russia-Central Asia summit October 2025

On 8-9 October 2025 the 2nd Russia-Central Asia Summit was held in Dushanbe under Rahmon's hosting. All five Central Asian leaders attended: Tokayev, Mirziyoyev, Rahmon, Japarov, and Serdar Berdymukhamedov. Putin sought to deepen institutional ties through bilateral and multilateral agreements.

The Eurasianet assessment: Central Asian leaders "largely left empty-handed." Putin had rekindled relations with Azerbaijan's Aliyev (after the December 2024 plane incident in Dossier 37). But the Central Asian operators received limited new commitments beyond reiteration of strategic partnership formulas. The summit was the indicator of Russia's weakening regional position.

### The Chinese architecture

China is the most consequential economic operator across Central Asia. The September 2013 Belt and Road Initiative launch at Astana — Xi's first major foreign-policy announcement — placed Central Asia as the architectural starting point of the BRI. The consequences across the 2013-2026 period:

**Kazakhstan**: largest BRI partner; Khorgos border hub; 2023 "permanent comprehensive strategic partnership" upgrade; 2024 Xi visit to Astana.

**Uzbekistan**: major BRI partner; China-Kyrgyzstan-Uzbekistan railway project advancing (groundbreaking December 2024); natural-gas exports to China.

**Turkmenistan**: most dependent on Chinese gas-export market; Central Asia-China gas pipeline carries majority of gas exports.

**Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan**: smaller economies; recipients of Chinese infrastructure investment.

The contemporary 2024-2026 architecture: China has emerged as the most  consequential external operator across Central Asia. The 2026 Rahmon medical-treatment visit to China (rather than Moscow) is the most consequential symbolic indicator.

### The Turkish architecture

The Organisation of Turkic States (founded 2009 as Turkic Council, reorganised 2021) is the Turkey-led architecture. Members: Turkey, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan; Turkmenistan as observer; Hungary as observer; TRNC as observer. Mirziyoyev has chaired since 2022.

The Erdoğan-led Turkish post-2022 push for Turkic integration has produced shared institutional frameworks: diplomatic-passport agreements, commercial integration, shared Turkic-language cultural architectures. The Turkish position has was the third leg of the post-Russia regional architecture, alongside China and the Western re-engagement.

### The Western architecture — C5+1

The C5+1 framework launched in 2015 brings the US together with Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan. The September 2023 first C5+1 leaders summit at the UN General Assembly in New York with Biden was the structural architectural pivot. The subsequent 2024-2026 re-engagement architecture has produced critical-minerals partnerships (Kazakhstan), energy-cooperation expansion, and counter-terrorism partnerships (against ISIS-K and other regional threats).

The Trump 2 administration's 2025-2026 Central Asia engagement has been less institutional than the Biden-era architecture but has continued operating through bilateral engagements. The post-2024 trajectory operates under uncertainty.

### The parallel-import architecture

The post-2022 institutional pattern: Central Asian states operate as transit hubs for Western goods reaching Russia despite the Russian-sanctions architecture. Kazakhstan is the most consequential operator. The 2023-2024 US Treasury sanctions warnings to Central Asian banks operated as the Western pressure architecture. The parallel-import flows have continued operating across the 2024-2026 period.

## The succession architecture

The contemporary 2026-2031 succession architecture operates across five trajectories.

**Kazakhstan**: Tokayev's single 7-year term runs to 2029; he is constitutionally barred from re-election. The 2029 succession will be the most consequential post-2019 institutional transition. The succession candidates have not been publicly designated.

**Uzbekistan**: Mirziyoyev's current term runs to 2030; he is eligible to stand again (under reset clock he could serve to 2037). The 2030 election will operate as the central institutional indicator of post-Mirziyoyev architecture.

**Tajikistan**: Rahmon's tenure may transition to son Rustam Emomali in the 2025-2027 period per Eurasianet assessments. The dynastic succession would operate as the third post-Soviet father-to-son transfer.

**Kyrgyzstan**: Presidential election scheduled 2027; Japarov has stated intention to seek second term. The Kyrgyz pattern of mass mobilisation displacing presidents may operate again.

**Turkmenistan**: The Berdymukhamedov dynasty is already in second-generation operation; the 2027 presidential election under Serdar's current term would be expected to produce his re-election under dynastic continuation.
