# Dossier 39: The Iranian Supreme Leadership Office and the IRGC Apparatus

## The architecture

The Islamic Republic of Iran was established 1 April 1979 following the overthrow of the Pahlavi monarchy. The 1979 Constitution institutionalised the doctrine of velayat-e faqih (Guardianship of the Jurist) developed by Ruhollah Khomeini across the Najaf and Qom exile period. The doctrine produced the apex institutional position of Supreme Leader (rahbar) — the jurist who operates as commander-in-chief, appoints the heads of the judiciary, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, state media, and the Guardian Council, and exercises final authority across the apparatus.

The Supreme Leader is selected and supervised by the Assembly of Experts — 88 clerics elected by popular vote from candidates vetted by the Guardian Council. The Assembly can dismiss the Supreme Leader; in practice it has never done so. The Guardian Council (12 members: 6 clerics appointed by the Supreme Leader, 6 jurists nominated by the Chief Justice) vets electoral candidates and parliamentary legislation. The Expediency Discernment Council mediates between parliament and the Guardian Council. The President is directly elected for a 4-year term renewable once; the position is subordinate to the Supreme Leader.

Parallel to the regular armed forces (Artesh), the IRGC is the governing military arm of the Supreme Leader. The IRGC reports directly to the Supreme Leader. It controls major economic holdings throughout the post-1979 architecture, oversees Iran's ballistic missile arsenal, and operates the Quds Force as its expeditionary arm — the vehicle through which Iran has built and sustained the Axis of Resistance network across Lebanon, Iraq, Syria, Yemen, and the Palestinian territories.

The contemporary 2026 Iranian institutional architecture operates under conditions without precedent in the post-1979 period. On 28 February 2026 Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — who had held the position since 1989 — was assassinated by a joint US-Israeli airstrike during the 2026 Iran war. His son Mojtaba Khamenei was injured in the same strike. The Assembly of Experts conducted the succession process from 1 to 8 March 2026 and elected Mojtaba Khamenei as the third Supreme Leader. The contemporary post-Khamenei architecture operates under conditions of active war, decimated IRGC leadership, collapsed Axis of Resistance, and uncertain nuclear-weapons trajectory.

## Ali Khamenei — biographical foundation

Ali Khamenei was born on 17 April 1939 in Mashhad, Khorasan, in the Imperial State of Iran. His biographical foundation is the classical Shia clerical formation. His father Sayyid Javad Khamenei was a Twelver Shia cleric. Khamenei studied at the Qom Seminary — the apex institutional training centre for Iranian Shia clergy — under Ruhollah Khomeini, who was already operating as the dissident-cleric opponent of the Pahlavi monarchy. Khamenei was arrested multiple times during the pre-1979 dissident activity. He was a founding member of the post-1979 Islamic Republic apparatus.

In 1981 Khamenei was shot in an assassination attempt at a mosque in Tehran. He lost the use of his right arm — the physical signifier that operated across his tenure as the indicator of his revolutionary credentials.

From 9 October 1981 to 16 August 1989 Khamenei served as the 3rd President of Iran under Khomeini's Supreme Leader authority. The presidential tenure was the training period for the apex position. From 7 February 1988 to 4 June 1989 he was the first Chairman of the Expediency Discernment Council.

On 3 June 1989 Khomeini died. On 4 June 1989 — the day after Khomeini's death — the Assembly of Experts elected Khamenei as the second Supreme Leader of Iran. The succession was constitutionally irregular: Khamenei held the religious rank of hojjat al-islam (a cleric), not ayatollah (a marja, an institutional cleric authorised to issue independent religious rulings). The 1989 constitutional amendment process retroactively elevated his religious authority to fit the structural position. The clerical establishment did not unanimously accept the architectural improvisation.

## The Khamenei tenure

Khamenei held the Supreme Leader position from 4 June 1989 to 28 February 2026 — a tenure of 36 years 8 months. The architecture has been characterised across the analytical literature as the consolidation of apex authority across the post-Khomeini period. The architectural features:

**Sidelining factional rivals.** Khamenei systematically displaced multi-year leftist factions (the post-1979 Islamist-leftist faction associated with Mir-Hossein Mousavi), moderate clerics (Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani, despite multi-year alliance), reformist operators (Mohammad Khatami's 1997-2005 period), and political dissidents through the post-1989 architecture. The 2009 Green Movement protests against the contested Mahmoud Ahmadinejad re-election was the most consequential institutional challenge to his authority; the protests were suppressed; Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi remain under house arrest as of 2026.

**State militarisation.** The Khamenei period produced the governing expansion of the IRGC across the Iranian state and economy. The IRGC has become the most consequential economic operator in Iran since the post-1989 period; it controls major holdings in construction, energy, telecommunications, and finance. The IRGC operates the Iranian ballistic missile programme. The post-2009 protest suppression  militarised the post-1979 Islamic Republic.

**Axis of Resistance expansion.** The Khamenei tenure produced the Iranian regional architecture. Hezbollah in Lebanon (built from Iranian Revolutionary Guard 1982 deployment to Beqaa Valley), Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in the Palestinian territories, Houthis in Yemen, Popular Mobilization Forces in Iraq, and the 2011-2024 Assad-regime architecture in Syria was the Iranian extended-deterrence and regional-influence architecture. The Khamenei doctrine of the "Resistance" — against Western, Israeli, and Gulf-monarchy influence — was the architectural underpinning.

**Nuclear architecture.** Iran's nuclear programmeme expanded across the Khamenei tenure. The 2002 IAEA disclosure of the Natanz enrichment facility produced the international-pressure architecture. The 2015 Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) — negotiated with the Western powers plus Russia and China — produced the temporary multi-year nuclear-deal architecture that froze Iran's enrichment programme below weapons-grade thresholds in exchange for sanctions relief. The May 2018 Trump withdrawal from JCPOA reversed the architecture; Iran began subsequent enrichment expansion to 60% U-235. By 2024 IAEA reports indicated Iran held sufficient highly-enriched uranium for multiple weapons; the weaponisation timeline was the contested architectural question.

**Domestic repression.** Khamenei's tenure has been characterised by political-prisoner architectures, execution of political opponents, restrictions on women's rights and religious-minority rights, and restrictions on independent media. The 2022 Mahsa Amini protests produced suppression: more than 500 protesters killed per independent reporting; thousands detained. The January 2026 nationwide protests produced similar suppression and functioned as the pre-war institutional indicator of the contemporary Iranian crisis.

## The 2025-2026 Israeli-Iranian war

### Senior pre-war architecture

The 2023-2026 Israeli-Iranian conflict trajectory has been in Part XII Chapter 5 of this volume (Gaza) and Part XII Chapter 7 (Proxy War Architecture). The summary architectural points: the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel produced the Israeli operations against Hamas (Gaza), Hezbollah (Lebanon), and the Iranian regional architecture. The April and October 2024 Iran-Israel exchanges (direct missile and drone attacks) was the escalation. The 27 September 2024 Israeli assassination of Hezbollah Secretary-General Hassan Nasrallah produced the Hezbollah leadership decimation. The October 2024 Hashem Safieddine killing eliminated the immediate-successor Hezbollah operator.

The 8 December 2024 collapse of the Assad regime in Syria — under the HTS-led offensive in the Middle East dossiers of this volume — produced the most consequential geopolitical reversal of the post-1979 Iranian regional position. The Iran-Hezbollah ground supply line through Syria was severed. The 4+1 coalition architecture (Iran, Iraq, Syria, Russia, Hezbollah) was dissolved. Iran's regional architecture was degraded across the 2023-2024 period.

### The June 2025 12-day war

In June 2025 Israel launched the 12-day war against Iran. The strikes targeted Iranian nuclear facilities (Natanz, Fordow, Isfahan), ballistic missile sites, and the IRGC apex apparatus. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu characterised the operation as targeting the "heart of Iran's nuclear enrichment program."

The 12-day war produced the decapitation of the IRGC apex apparatus:

- **Major General Hossein Salami** (IRGC Commander-in-Chief since 21 April 2019, born 25 August 1960): killed.
- **Major General Mohammad Bagheri** (Iranian Armed Forces Chief of Staff since 28 June 2016): killed.
- **Brigadier General Amir Ali Hajizadeh** (IRGC Aerospace Forces commander since 2009): killed.
- **Major General Gholam Ali Rashid** (Khatam Al-Anbiya Central Headquarters commander): killed.
- **Brigadier General Mohammad Kazemi** (IRGC Intelligence Organization chief since 2022): killed.
- Multiple nuclear scientists: killed.

The post-June-2025 IRGC restructuring produced the succession appointments:

- **Major General Mohammad Pakpour** replaced Salami as Commander-in-Chief.
- **Brigadier General Mohammad Karami** assumed the Ground Force command vacated by Pakpour.
- **Brigadier General Majid Mousavi** replaced Hajizadeh as Aerospace Force commander.
- **Brigadier General Majid Khademi** replaced Kazemi as Intelligence Organization head.
- The Quds Force command under Esmail Qaani continued operating; the speculation about Qaani's survival and possible Mossad compromise continued operating across the 2025-2026 period.

The 12-day war was concluded by ceasefire architecture. Iran's nuclear programmeme status emerged as multi-year contested: Israeli claims of major damage; Iranian claims of partial damage with preserved capacity; IAEA-access disputes served as the architectural indicator.

### The January 2026 nationwide protests

The January 2026 nationwide Iranian protests operated under multiple triggers: economic collapse from sanctions; post-June-2025 humiliation from the decapitated IRGC architecture; repression accumulating from the Amini-protest legacy; and subsequent generational opposition to the clerical architecture. The protests produced IRGC suppression characterised by international institutional condemnation. The European Union and other bodies designated the IRGC as a terrorist organisation across the January-February 2026 period.

The US military buildup in the Middle East began January 2026 in response to the protests and the Iranian nuclear-programme rebuilding architecture.

### The 28 February 2026 Khamenei assassination

On 28 February 2026 a joint US-Israeli airstrike killed Ali Khamenei. The strike occurred during the expanded 2026 Iran war architecture. Khamenei was 86 years old at the time of death. His son Mojtaba Khamenei was injured in the same strike.

The assassination was the most consequential post-1989 event in the Iranian political architecture. The comparison points: 1953 Mossadegh coup; 1979 revolution; 1989 Khomeini death and Khamenei succession; 2024 Raisi helicopter crash. The 28 February 2026 assassination is the most consequential post-1979 institutional inflection point.

The consequences operated across the immediate period. The Iranian regional attack architecture intensified: IRGC strikes targeted Israel, US installations, and Gulf-Arab oil and water facilities. The US-Israeli strikes continued across March 2026.

## The 2026 succession process

### The Interim Leadership Council

The 1979 Constitution provides that when the Supreme Leader leaves office, his powers transfer temporarily to a Leadership Council comprising the President, the Chief Justice, and a cleric from the Guardian Council. The 1 March 2026 Interim Leadership Council operated under the constitutional provision.

**President Masoud Pezeshkian.** Born 29 September 1954 in Mahabad, Iran. Ethnically Azerbaijani. Career heart surgeon. Minister of Health and Medical Education 2001-2005 under Khatami. Multi-year Consultative Assembly member (Tabriz constituency) since May 2008. Reformist political affiliation. 9th President of Iran since 28 July 2024 — elected in the 5 July 2024 runoff after Raisi's 19 May 2024 helicopter death. The Pezeshkian platform: moderate foreign policy, JCPOA-style nuclear-deal negotiation, economic reform, modest social opening. The contemporary 2026 institutional position: reformist president operating under conditions of intensifying war and clerical-establishment crisis.

**Chief Justice Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Eje'i.** Born 1956. Chief Justice since 1 July 2021. Multi-decade career intelligence and judicial operator. The judicial-establishment representative in the Council.

**Ayatollah Alireza Arafi.** Born 1959 in Meybod, Yazd Province. Head of Iranian Seminaries since 2016. Qom Friday Prayer Imam since 2015. Member of the Guardian Council since 15 July 2019 (appointed by Khamenei). Member of the Assembly of Experts since 8 March 2022 (Tehran Province, 1,293,072 votes). The Guardian Council representative in the Council. He was also a candidate for the Supreme Leader position.

### The Assembly of Experts election

From 3 to 8 March 2026 the Assembly of Experts conducted the succession election. The Assembly is the 88-member elected body that constitutionally selects the Supreme Leader; 59 votes were required for selection. The Chairman of the Assembly is Mohammad-Ali Movahedi Kermani — Tehran's Ephemeral Friday Prayer Imam since December 2012, an Assembly member since 1983, and Chairman since 21 May 2024.

The candidates per subsequent reporting: Mojtaba Khamenei (Ali Khamenei's second son); Asghar Hijazi (Khamenei aide); Ali Larijani (former parliament speaker and Expediency Council Secretary); Sadiq Larijani (former Chief Justice); Alireza Arafi; and Mohammad-Mahdi Mirbagheri.

On 8 March 2026 the Assembly elected Mojtaba Khamenei as the third Supreme Leader of Iran. The result was announced 9 March 2026. The outcome is the most consequential succession in the 47-year post-1979 Iranian apparatus.

### The architectural questions

The Mojtaba succession produced multiple institutional questions. First, the father-to-son succession question. The Iranian clerical-establishment doctrine has frowned upon father-to-son succession as monarchical — particularly in a revolutionary Iran that came to power against the Pahlavi monarchical architecture. The 2026 succession violates the norm. The comparison: the post-Soviet dynastic-succession architectures (Aliyev in Azerbaijan, Berdymukhamedov in Turkmenistan, prospective Rahmon-to-Rustam in Tajikistan in Dossiers 37-38). The Iranian succession is the fourth contemporary dynastic-succession architecture but the first within an explicitly anti-monarchical revolutionary institutional framework.

Second, the religious-authority question. Mojtaba's religious title of ayatollah was granted roughly 2023 — viewed by other clerics as politically motivated promotion rather than scholarly accomplishment. The comparison: Ali Khamenei's 1989 succession also operated under religious-authority irregularity (hojjat al-islam promoted to ayatollah to fit the position). The pattern is the structural indicator that the post-1979 Iranian architecture has consistently subordinated religious authority to political requirements.

Third, the IRGC-relationship question. Mojtaba served as the central IRGC and Basij relationship operator across the pre-2026 period. His Vakil position (representative of the Supreme Leader) from 21 August 2008 to 28 February 2026  embedded him in the IRGC apex apparatus. The contemporary IRGC architecture under Pakpour's commander-in-chief authority is the foundation of his new Supreme Leader authority. Atlantic Council analytical characterisation: Mojtaba is more hardline than his father; he has close ties to "most ideologically extremist clerics."

Fourth, the wartime-succession question. Mojtaba assumed the apex position under conditions of active war with the US-Israeli coalition. The post-8-March 2026 architecture is the most consequential wartime succession in the post-1979 Iranian institutional history. The outcome trajectory: continued war; IRGC strikes against the US-Israeli coalition and the Gulf monarchies; nuclear-programme architecture under conditions of ongoing damage and rebuilding.

## The Mojtaba Khamenei architecture

Mojtaba Hosseini Khamenei was born on 8 September 1969 in Mashhad, Iran — the same city as his father. His mother is Mansoureh Khojasteh Bagherzadeh. He is the second son of Ali Khamenei. He married Zahra Haddad-Adel in 1999; she died in 2026. Her father, Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel, is a former Iranian parliament speaker — the political-family connection that embedded Mojtaba in the hardline parliamentary architecture. The couple has three children.

Mojtaba's education was at the Qom Seminary — the architectural training institution of the Iranian Shia clerical establishment. His religious title of ayatollah was granted roughly 2023.

His military service: IRGC Ground Forces 1987-1988 during the Iran-Iraq War; Basij 2009-2026 (the post-2009-Green-Movement period). The 2009 Green Movement context: Mojtaba operated as a central figure in the suppression of the protests under his father's authority.

His political authority operated through the Vakil of the Supreme Leader position from 21 August 2008 to 28 February 2026. The position  embedded him as the representative of his father across the IRGC, Basij, and clerical-establishment institutional network. The position chief of staff Mohammad Mohammadi Golpayegani was the key bureaucratic operator.

Mojtaba operates as the patron of the Front of Islamic Revolution Stability — the hardline political faction that has consolidated under his patronage throughout the post-2008 period. The faction's central institutional positions: maximalist opposition to Western engagement; maximalist support for the Axis of Resistance architecture; maximalist domestic-repression posture against reformist and moderate factions.

## The contemporary IRGC and Quds Force architecture

### The contemporary IRGC under Pakpour

The post-June-2025 IRGC restructuring has produced the contemporary leadership architecture:

**Major General Mohammad Pakpour** operates as IRGC Commander-in-Chief since June 2025. Prior to the promotion he had operated as Commander of the IRGC Ground Force. The architectural feature: Pakpour represents the IRGC Ground Force tradition rather than the IRGC aerospace and intelligence traditions that produced his immediate predecessors. The 2025-2026 IRGC operates under conditions of decimated aerospace, intelligence, and central-command architecture.

**Brigadier General Mohammad Karami** operates as Ground Force commander. **Brigadier General Majid Mousavi** operates as Aerospace Force commander. **Brigadier General Majid Khademi** operated as Intelligence Organization head until his 6 April 2026 assassination by airstrike. The post-Khademi Intelligence Organization position is currently vacant — the most consequential continuing institutional vulnerability of the contemporary IRGC architecture.

The surviving IRGC operators: **Brigadier General Gholamreza Soleimani** (Basij commander); **Brigadier General Mohammad Hossein Nejat** (Tharallah Headquarters for Tehran security); **Rear Admiral Alireza Tangsiri** (IRGC Navy).

### Esmail Qaani and the Quds Force question

The Quds Force — the IRGC expeditionary arm responsible for extraterritorial operations and the Axis of Resistance architecture — is commanded by Esmail Qaani since 3 January 2020. Qaani was born 8 August 1957 in Mashhad. He joined the IRGC in 1980. He served in the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988) commanding the 5th Nasr Brigade and 21st Imam Reza Armored Brigade. He met Qasem Soleimani during the war. In 1997 he was appointed deputy Quds Force commander under Rahim Safavi (commander) and Qasem Soleimani (subsequent commander).

Qaani assumed Quds Force command on 3 January 2020 following the 3 January 2020 US drone-strike assassination of Soleimani in Baghdad. He has been on the US Treasury OFAC Specially Designated Nationals list since March 2012.

The contemporary Qaani question is the central uncertainty of the contemporary IRGC architecture. Across the multi-year 2024-2026 period Qaani has been declared dead multiple times in international and Iranian media reporting, only to reappear at public events. The October 2024 Safieddine-strike speculation; the interrogation reports; the 24 June 2025 public reappearance in Tehran; the March 2026 Mossad-agent speculation: all operate as the continuing institutional pattern of survival under conditions in which contemporaries have been systematically eliminated.

The analytical characterisation per Newsweek and the other 2026 reporting: Qaani is "no longer a central figure, reflecting the broader decline of the IRGC's regional proxy network." The contemporary Quds Force operates under conditions of degraded Axis of Resistance architecture — Hamas decimated, Hezbollah decimated, Assad regime collapsed, Houthi partially degraded, PMF reduced.

### The Axis of Resistance collapse

The Iranian Axis of Resistance architecture has been decimated across the multi-year 2023-2026 period. The detailed architectural casualties:

**Hamas**: Politburo Chief Ismail Haniyeh assassinated 31 July 2024 in Tehran during official visit; military commander Yahya Sinwar killed October 2024; subsequent leadership decimation; Hamas continues operating under degraded conditions. Documented in Part XII Chapter 5.

**Hezbollah**: September 2024 pager-attack architecture decimated thousands of operatives; Secretary-General Hassan Nasrallah killed 27 September 2024 in Beirut airstrike; successor Hashem Safieddine killed October 2024; subsequent leadership under Naim Qassem operates under conditions of decimated architectural foundations. Documented in Part XII Chapter 7.

**Syria/Assad regime**: collapsed 8 December 2024 under the HTS-led offensive. Bashar al-Assad fled to Russia. The Iran-Hezbollah ground supply line through Syria was severed. Documented in subsequent Middle East dossiers of this volume.

**Houthi (Ansar Allah) Yemen**: continues attacking Israeli and international shipping; partial degradation under US-UK-Israeli operations; remains the most  durable Axis member.

**Iraqi Popular Mobilization Forces**: reduced operational tempo; continued operating but under conditions of degraded supply lines and reduced Quds Force direction.

The 2026 Axis of Resistance architecture is the most consequential post-1979 institutional regional reversal of Iranian power. The architecture of the "Shi'ite Crescent" or "Resistance Front" — constructed across the Khomeini and Khamenei tenures — has been operationally degraded by the 2023-2026 Israeli-and-allied operations.

## The contemporary trajectory

The contemporary May 2026 Iranian institutional position operates under the conditions documented:

**Apex authority.** Mojtaba Khamenei is the third Supreme Leader since 8 March 2026. The architectural authority operates under conditions of wartime crisis and contested religious-authority foundation. The Mojtaba absence-from-public-view period (he was injured in the 28 February assassination strike) was the immediate institutional vulnerability. The contemporary Mojtaba architecture operates under conditions of unconsolidated apex authority.

**IRGC apparatus.** The contemporary IRGC operates under Major General Mohammad Pakpour's commander-in-chief authority after the June 2025 decapitation of the previous IRGC apex apparatus. The contemporary IRGC operates under conditions of reduced institutional capacity and continued Israeli targeting (Khademi killed 6 April 2026).

**President Pezeshkian.** Operates as the reformist president under conditions of wartime crisis. The reformist platform institutionally contradicts the Mojtaba and IRGC apex maximalist architecture. The subsequent contemporary trajectory is the reformist-and-clerical-hardliner institutional contestation.

**Nuclear architecture.** The nuclear programmeme status operates as contested architectural question. Israel and the US claim major damage from the June 2025 and 2026 strikes; Iran claims preserved capacity; IAEA-access disputes operate as the architectural indicator. The weaponisation timeline is the contested question; some analyses suggest Iran may have accelerated the weaponisation architecture under conditions of existential threat.

**Axis of Resistance**: decimated across the multi-year 2023-2026 period; continued operating in degraded form; subsequent rebuilding architecture is the medium-term institutional uncertainty.

**Regional position.** Iran's post-1979 regional position has been degraded. The Iran-Saudi-Arabia rivalry has been moderated by the 2023 China-brokered Iran-Saudi normalisation. The Iran-UAE relationship operates pragmatically. The Iran-Turkey relationship operates under wartime pressure. The Iran-Russia relationship has been strained by the 2024-2026 Russian inability to protect Iranian architectural assets (Syria, IRGC, Khamenei).

# Dossier 40: Mohammed bin Salman and the Saudi Arabian Apex Apparatus

## The operator and the architecture

The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia was founded on 23 September 1932 by Abdulaziz ibn Saud through the unification of the Arabian Peninsula political-class architecture under the House of Saud. The post-1932 apparatus has produced one of the most consequential apex apparatuses in the contemporary world. Saudi Arabia operates as the Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques (Mecca and Medina), the apex Sunni Muslim political-class architecture, the largest oil exporter in the contemporary period, and the apex Arab Gulf state.

The architecture operates without a constitution in the post-1992 sense. The 1992 Basic Law of Government declares that the Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet operate as the governing constitution; the King exercises combined temporal and religious authority. The House of Saud architecture includes an estimated 15,000-20,000 princes in the post-Abdulaziz period.

The institutional post-1953 succession architecture operated through fraternal rotation among Abdulaziz's sons across the period: Saud (1953-1964), Faisal (1964-1975), Khalid (1975-1982), Fahd (1982-2005), Abdullah (2005-2015), and Salman (2015-present). Mohammed bin Salman displaces the fraternal-rotation framework. The pivot is from rotational-fraternal succession to filial-vertical succession — from King Salman to his son.

## Mohammed bin Salman — biographical foundation

Mohammed bin Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud was born on 31 August 1985 in Riyadh. His father is King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al-Saud (born 31 December 1935, King since 23 January 2015). His mother is Fahda bint Falah bin Sultan Al Hithalayn — Salman's third wife. Salman had 13 sons across 4 wives. MBS was Salman's 6th son and the eldest son of Fahda's six children. He has five full brothers and multiple half-brothers.

Among the Salman sons, the pre-2015 succession architecture had favoured the sons of Salman's first wife Sultana — Fahd, Sultan, Ahmed, Abdulaziz, Faisal, and Saud — who had operated as governmental and business figures across the pre-2015 period. The MBS rise reversed the internal House-of-Salman seniority architecture.

MBS received his early schooling in Riyadh. In 2007 he graduated from King Saud University with a BA in Law. He studied public administration and economics. He has been characterised by Western analysts as the only contemporary Saudi apex operator without Western education — an architectural feature that operates as institutional indicator of his contemporary nationalist disposition. In 2008 he married his first cousin Princess Sara bint Mashhoor bin Abdulaziz Al Saud; the couple has five children.

His pre-2015 career operated through advisory positions under his father. From 2007 he worked for the Bureau of Experts at the Council of Ministers. From 2009 he was a formal adviser to Salman, then-Governor of Riyadh. In 2011 he established the MiSK Foundation — the youth-education and leadership nonprofit that has was the vehicle for his youth-engagement architecture. From March 2013 he served as head of the Crown Prince Court and special adviser to the Crown Prince (Salman) — the immediate institutional pre-2015 positioning.

## The rise 2015-2017

The contemporary MBS architecture  began on 23 January 2015 with the death of King Abdullah and the accession of King Salman. Salman immediately named MBS as Minister of Defence at age 29 — the youngest Defence Minister in the contemporary period. MBS operated immediately as the second-most-powerful operator behind his father, and the Defence Ministry portfolio controlled the largest Saudi institutional budget and the most national-security architecture.

In March 2015 — only weeks after his appointment — MBS launched Operation Decisive Storm against the Houthi forces in Yemen. The 25 March 2015 operation was the institutional architectural indicator of MBS personalised foreign policy. The multi-year Yemen war architecture would become the most consequential foreign-policy commitment of his tenure and the most consequential humanitarian-crisis production of the contemporary period (377,000+ deaths per UN estimates).

In April 2015 King Salman named MBS as Deputy Crown Prince, with Salman's nephew Mohammed bin Nayef remaining as Crown Prince. The institutional positioning placed MBS as second-in-line behind Nayef. The pre-2017 architecture operated under the assumption that Nayef — born 30 August 1959, Interior Minister 2012-2017, close US institutional partner including CIA cooperation against AQAP — would succeed Salman.

On 25 April 2016 MBS officially unveiled Vision 2030 — the architectural framework that transformed his international profile from Defence Minister to reformer. The subsequent June 2016 Council of Ministers approval of the National Transformation Program was the structural implementation framework.

The architectural inflection point: 21 June 2017. King Salman issued a royal decree replacing Mohammed bin Nayef with MBS as Crown Prince. Nayef was placed under house arrest. The Allegiance Council approved the change 31-3. The decree consolidated MBS as First Deputy Prime Minister, Defence Minister, and Crown Prince simultaneously — the positions that functioned as the architectural foundation of the contemporary apex authority.

## The November 2017 Ritz-Carlton purge

The 4 November 2017 purge is the most consequential institutional event of MBS's pre-2022 architecture. Hundreds of princes, businessmen, ministers, and other royal-family operators were detained at the Riyadh Ritz-Carlton hotel under the formal framework of an "anti-corruption" operation. Detainees included Prince Alwaleed bin Talal (Kingdom Holding, multi-billion-dollar global business architecture), Prince Mutaib bin Abdullah (former National Guard commander), other royal operators.

The subsequent extraction was the governing process under which detainees agreed to settlements in exchange for release. Per Western reporting, roughly $100B+ was extracted across the period. The operation eliminated the independent royal-family economic-power architecture that had operated as potential institutional check on MBS authority.

The 2017 purge has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential consolidation of authoritarian-personalised authority in the post-1953 Saudi apparatus. No previous Saudi post-1953 succession episode produced the comparable systematic institutional elimination of royal-family rivals.

The subsequent 2018-2020 consolidation continued. Prince Ahmed bin Abdulaziz (King Salman's younger brother, born 1942, Interior Minister 1975-2012, Allegiance Council member) was detained in March 2020. Mohammed bin Nayef (former Crown Prince) was also detained in March 2020 and remains under house arrest as of 2026. The 2020 detentions completed the elimination of the post-1953 rotational-fraternal architecture as an institutional check on MBS authority.

On 27 September 2022 King Salman named MBS as Prime Minister — the position usually held by the King himself. The position formalised MBS as operational head of government and operational apex authority under the formal Salman kingship.

## Vision 2030 — the architectural framework

Vision 2030 was officially launched on 25 April 2016. The framework is the most consequential post-1932 Saudi institutional transformation programme. The architectural goals: reduce oil dependency; diversify economy; develop tourism, entertainment, technology, and manufacturing; double private-sector contribution to GDP; build the Public Investment Fund as the global sovereign-wealth-fund apex operator; transform the post-1932 Wahhabi-clerical-establishment cultural architecture.

The implementation operates through three 5-year phases: 2016-2020, 2020-2025, and 2025-2030. The contemporary 2026 institutional position operates at the beginning of the third phase.

The 2024 Vision 2030 Annual Report indicates 93% of vision realisation programmes are "on target or close" and 85% of initiatives are "complete or progressing according to plan." Non-oil GDP reached 56% of the economy by 2025. Female workforce participation exceeded 36% by 2025. Tourism reached 122 million annual visitors by 2025.

The architectural criticisms operate as institutional indicators of the contradictions of the framework. The 2024 investigative reporting indicates roughly 21,000+ workers died on Vision 2030 projects between 2017 and 2024. The migrant-worker architecture is the structural foundation of the construction-and-development sector. Human-rights criticisms since the post-2015 period have continued operating across the contemporary period.

### The Public Investment Fund

The PIF architecture has produced the apex sovereign-wealth-fund operator of the contemporary period. The 2024 year-end assets stood at $925 billion. The early 2026 assets reached $1.15 trillion — a $226 billion increase across a single year. The PIF is the 5th-largest sovereign wealth fund globally per the Sovereign Wealth Fund Institute assessment. The 2030 target operates at $3 trillion.

The PIF Governor is Yasir Al Rumayyan, in post since September 2019. He is the central economic operator under MBS's apex authority. The PIF investment architecture has  shifted from domestic-holding-company architecture to global-portfolio architecture spanning technology, entertainment, sports, infrastructure, and strategic-industry positions.

PIF investments operating as institutional indicators include: the October 2021 80% acquisition of Newcastle United (English Premier League); the $400 million LIV Golf disruption of PGA Tour architecture and the 2023 PGA Tour-LIV Golf merger framework; the SoftBank Vision Fund $45 billion commitment; the Uber $3.5 billion stake; the 2034 FIFA World Cup hosting rights secured in 2023; and the August 2024 MOU with six Chinese financial institutions for $50 billion investments — the indicator of economic alignment with Beijing alongside the US strategic partnership.

### NEOM and the megaprojects

NEOM — the $500 billion futuristic megacity in northwest Saudi Arabia announced October 2017 — is the indicator of the MBS architectural ambition. The Line component — a 170-kilometre mirrored linear city planned to house 9 million people — has produced Western architectural and operational scrutiny. As of late 2025 only 2.4 kilometres had been completed. The PIF suspended The Line construction in September 2025. Revised plans project fewer than 300,000 residents by the end of the decade.

The June 2020 killing of Abdulraheem al-Huwaiti of the Huwait tribe for refusing eviction from the NEOM area was the structural indicator of the human-rights costs of the megaproject architecture.

other megaprojects include: Qiddiya — the 334 square-kilometre sports, culture, and entertainment complex south-west of Riyadh announced April 2017, with Six Flags Qiddiya City theme park opening 2025; the Red Sea Project; and the Diriyah Gate development.

## The Khashoggi assassination

The 2 October 2018 Khashoggi assassination is the most consequential international incident of MBS's tenure. Jamal Khashoggi — Saudi journalist, Washington Post columnist, former Saudi government adviser, subsequently dissident — entered the Saudi Consulate in Istanbul to obtain marriage documents for his planned wedding to Hatice Cengiz, who waited outside.

A 15-member Saudi team killed and dismembered him inside the consulate. The Turkish authorities obtained audio recordings of the operation. The subsequent international reporting  established the architectural facts.

Saudi government initial response: denied. The subsequent November 2018 acknowledgement: Khashoggi had died inside the consulate; the operation was characterised as a "rogue operation" of operators acting without authorisation.

The CIA assessment of November 2018: MBS approved the operation. The 24 February 2021 US Office of the Director of National Intelligence declassified report: MBS approved the operation. MBS has publicly denied ordering it; he has stated the operation was "carried out by Saudi officials."

The subsequent international architecture produced US-Saudi institutional damage. The Trump 1 administration declined to  sanction MBS. The Biden campaign (2019-2020) characterised Saudi Arabia as a "pariah" state. The subsequent Biden institutional position moderated; the 16 July 2022 Biden Jeddah visit and the fist-bump with MBS was the rehabilitation indicator.

The 17 November 2022 US Department of Justice filing determined MBS qualified for immunity since he had been named Prime Minister of Saudi Arabia on 27 September 2022. The 6 December 2022 dismissal of the Hatice Cengiz and DAWN human-rights organisation case institutionally closed the US-legal architecture of the matter.

The consequence: the architectural rehabilitation of MBS across the 2022-2025 period operated under conditions of systemic Western institutional accommodation to Saudi oil-and-strategic-partnership requirements. The 18 November 2025 White House visit with full honours — the dark-horse-escorted limousine on the South Lawn, the Trump-hosted black-tie East Room dinner — was the governing indicator of the architectural rehabilitation completion.

## The multi-year Yemen war

The 25 March 2015 Operation Decisive Storm was the foundation of the Yemen war. The trigger: 2014 Houthi seizure of Sanaa; subsequent Houthi advance toward Aden; March 2015 collapse of the Yemeni government under President Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi. Hadi fled to Saudi Arabia and requested international assistance.

The Saudi-led coalition included UAE, Egypt, Morocco, Jordan, Sudan, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar. The multi-year operations targeted Houthi positions, infrastructure, and populations. The architectural consequences: per UN estimates 377,000+ deaths across the period (mostly indirect from blockade-produced famine and cholera and healthcare-collapse architecture); the most consequential humanitarian crisis of the contemporary period; the Iran-Houthi missile-and-drone architecture targeting Saudi Aramco facilities (the 14 September 2019 Abqaiq-Khurais attacks served as the architectural inflection point that temporarily halved Saudi oil production).

The April 2022 UN-mediated truce architecture operated as the reduction in operational tempo. The 10 March 2023 China-brokered Saudi-Iran normalisation was the architectural indicator of the re-orientation away from the 2015-2022 maximalist Saudi-versus-Iran architectural framework. The continued multi-year Houthi operations across the 2024-2026 period (including attacks on Red Sea shipping and Israel) operate as the indicator of the partial resolution.

## The contemporary 2024-2026 trajectory

The contemporary 2024-2026 apparatus operates under four central features.

**First**, the US-Saudi strategic-partnership intensification under Trump 2. The May 2025 Trump visit to Saudi Arabia produced the $142 billion defence cooperation agreement — characterised as the "largest defence cooperation agreement in US history." The package includes advanced military equipment. The F-35 fighter jet sale — long blocked across the Israeli Qualitative Military Edge protection framework — was cleared as part of MBS's November 2025 Washington visit. The US 2025 budget defence allocation: $78 billion (21% of total government spending; 7.1% of GDP; 7th-largest military spender globally; largest in the Middle East per SIPRI data). The Vision 2030 target operates at 50% of defence expenditure localised by 2030.

**Second**, the China-alignment expansion. The 10 March 2023 China-brokered Saudi-Iran normalisation was the structural indicator of Chinese regional diplomatic-architecture insertion. The Belt and Road Initiative architecture operates across Saudi infrastructure and energy architecture. The August 2024 PIF MOU architecture with six Chinese financial institutions for $50 billion investments is the foundation. The contemporary Saudi position is the multi-vector alignment — US strategic partnership combined with Chinese economic alignment.

**Third**, the contemporary Gaza war position. MBS has condemned Israeli bombardment as disproportionate and as "collective punishment." Saudi Arabia has provided $500 million to the UN Yemen Response Plan, $200 million to Jordan for refugee support, and $13 million to the domestic Ehsan charitable platform. The Saudi position has  affirmed Palestinian statehood as the precondition for Saudi-Israeli normalisation. The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Gaza war — the most consequential genocidal architecture of the post-Cold-War period per the authorial position of this volume, in Part XII Chapter 5 — paused the pre-October-2023 Saudi-Israeli normalisation negotiations. The institutional resumption operates under conditions of contested architectural feasibility.

**Fourth**, the contemporary Iran-architecture re-positioning. The 28 February 2026 Khamenei assassination and the Iranian institutional crisis (in Dossier 39) operate as the most consequential regional inflection point of the contemporary period. The Saudi position operates under conditions of ambivalence — the Saudi-Iran rivalry favoured Iranian weakening; the contemporary Iran institutional collapse risks producing regional instability that operates against the Vision 2030 economic-development framework. The Saudi position has remained moderate across the 2025-2026 Iran-war architecture.

## Senior other House-of-Saud operators

The contemporary Saudi institutional apex apparatus operates through Mohammed bin Salman as Crown Prince and Prime Minister under his father King Salman's formal authority. The other House-of-Saud operators:

- **King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al-Saud**: born 31 December 1935; King since 23 January 2015; Governor of Riyadh 1963-2011; Defence Minister 2011-2015; health concerns producing speculation about transition timing across the 2024-2026 period.

- **Khalid bin Salman bin Abdulaziz**: MBS's younger full brother; Saudi Ambassador to US 2017-2019 (post-Khashoggi); Defence Minister since 2022. Operates as the MBS-aligned principal operator on defence and US-relations architecture.

- **Yasir Al Rumayyan**: PIF Governor since September 2019; central economic operator.

- **Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud**: Foreign Minister since 23 October 2019; career diplomat.

- **Adel Al-Jubeir**: career diplomat; Foreign Minister 2015-2018; Minister of State for Foreign Affairs 2018-present.

- **Turki Al Sheikh**: Chairman of General Entertainment Authority; central entertainment-sector operator; subsequent MBS confidant.

- **Saud al-Qahtani**: MBS confidant; central Khashoggi-operation operator; subsequently dismissed and sanctioned; operates in contemporary uncertain position.

The contemporary institutional apex apparatus operates with operational authority concentrated under MBS's personal architecture. The royal-family institutional checks have been eliminated through the 2017 purge and the 2020 detentions. The Allegiance Council operates as an institutional residual rather than as an operational succession-architecture check. The Wahhabi-clerical establishment operates under reduced institutional authority following the 2016 religious-police reduction and the cultural-liberalisation architecture.

## The trajectory

MBS has been Crown Prince since 21 June 2017 and Prime Minister since 27 September 2022. The consolidation ran: Defence Minister (25 January 2015), Deputy Crown Prince (April 2015), Vision 2030 launch (25 April 2016), Crown Prince elevation displacing Mohammed bin Nayef (21 June 2017), Ritz-Carlton purge of royal-family rivals and wealthy businessmen (4 November 2017), detentions of Ahmed bin Abdulaziz and Mohammed bin Nayef (2018-2020), Prime Minister appointment (27 September 2022).

Vision 2030 runs across three 5-year phases. The PIF is the 5th-largest sovereign wealth fund globally ($1.15 trillion early 2026; $3 trillion 2030 target). NEOM and The Line produced architectural ambition combined with operational scaling-back. Women driving. Entertainment. Tourism.

The costs: the Yemen war (377,000+ dead per UN estimates). The Khashoggi assassination (2 October 2018; CIA and DNI assessments: MBS approved). Vision 2030 project worker deaths (21,000+ across 2017-2024 per investigative reporting). The dissident-prosecution apparatus.

The 2024-2026 trajectory: US-Saudi strategic-partnership intensification under Trump 2 ($142 billion defence cooperation agreement May 2025; F-35 sale clearance November 2025; White House visit with full honours 18 November 2025). China-alignment expansion through PIF MOUs and BRI. Palestinian statehood affirmed as precondition for normalisation. Iran repositioning after the 28 February 2026 Khamenei assassination.

When Salman dies, MBS becomes King. The Crown Prince position passes to one of his brothers, sons, or other House-of-Saud operators. Vision 2030 is scaling back. NEOM is scaling back. The post-2030 architecture is the open question.

# Dossier 41: Mohammed bin Zayed and the UAE Apex Apparatus

## The UAE institutional foundation

The United Arab Emirates was founded on 2 December 1971 as a federal absolute monarchy comprising seven emirates: Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al Quwain, Fujairah, and Ras Al Khaimah (which joined in February 1972). The Federal Supreme Council — composed of the seven emirate rulers — elects the President. By institutional convention the Abu Dhabi ruler serves as UAE President and the Dubai ruler serves as Prime Minister.

The Al Nahyan dynasty of Abu Dhabi (descended from the Bani Yas tribal federation) and the Al Maktoum dynasty of Dubai operate as the governing architecture. Abu Dhabi controls roughly 96% of UAE oil reserves and is the and economic apex. The UAE population is roughly 10 million, of which roughly 90% are expatriates and 10% are Emirati citizens. The architecture has produced one of the most distinctive contemporary state forms: a federal absolute monarchy with concentrated apex authority, a rentier-state economic foundation, a migrant-labour architecture, and the contemporary global financial-hub and strategic-operator position.

The founding architecture was constructed by Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan (~1918-2 November 2004), who became Ruler of Abu Dhabi on 6 August 1966 (after deposing his older brother Shakhbut) and 1st President of UAE on 2 December 1971. His tenure produced the architecture of contemporary UAE: federal architecture across the seven emirates, oil-revenue accumulation through ADIA (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority), education and infrastructure expansion, and moderate Sunni Islamic institutional framework distinct from Saudi Wahhabi architecture.

Zayed had three wives producing sons across multiple Nahyan branches. The contemporary apex governing branch is "Bani Fatima" — the six sons of his third wife Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi. Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (MBZ) is the eldest Bani Fatima brother and apex contemporary operator. The five other Bani Fatima brothers operate as apparatus supporting the MBZ apex authority.

## Biographical foundation

Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born on 11 March 1961 in Al Ain, Abu Dhabi emirate (then part of the Trucial States under British protection). He is the third son of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi — Zayed's third wife and the matriarch of the contemporary apex governing branch. He has five full brothers, the "Bani Fatima" architecture, and multiple half-brothers from his father's two other wives.

His education operated through Abu Dhabi and Al Ain primary institutions. In April 1979 he graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in the United Kingdom — the training framework for the Commonwealth and Gulf military elite. The Sandhurst formation has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the structural foundation of his contemporary military-strategic disposition and Anglo-American institutional networking.

His career operated through the UAE Armed Forces from 1979. He served as a pilot in the UAE Air Force. He held various positions across the 1980s-1990s period. From 1993 to 2005 he served as Chief of Staff of the UAE Armed Forces — the position that gave him operational control of the military apparatus across the foundational period of contemporary UAE military expansion. In 2005 he was promoted to general. He subsequently served as Deputy Supreme Commander of the UAE Armed Forces.

Across the pre-2014 period MBZ pursued institutional military reform under his Chief-of-Staff and institutional positions: hiring foreign officers to lead components of the armed forces; instituting a year of compulsory service for male UAE citizens (subsequently extended to 16 months); acquiring advanced and customised fighter aircraft from the United States including the F-16 Desert Falcon programme. The military-modernisation programme produced the most consequential Gulf military force outside Saudi Arabia.

## The rise to apex authority

The contemporary MBZ apex authority operated through three institutional steps. In 2003 he was appointed Deputy Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi under his older half-brother Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1948). On 2 November 2004 — the day of Sheikh Zayed's death — Khalifa became Ruler of Abu Dhabi and 2nd President of UAE, and MBZ was appointed Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi. He simultaneously became Chairman of the Abu Dhabi Executive Council — the governing body governing Abu Dhabi day-to-day operations.

The institutional inflection point: 24 January 2014. Khalifa suffered a major stroke and was incapacitated for the multi-year period. MBZ assumed de facto executive authority across both Abu Dhabi and the UAE federal apex architecture. The multi-year 2014-2022 period operated under the architecture in which Khalifa remained formal President but MBZ exercised operational apex authority. The Western analytical literature characterised the period as the most consequential pre-presidency exercise of authority in the contemporary Gulf apparatus.

On 13 May 2022 Khalifa died. On 14 May 2022 the Federal Supreme Council elected MBZ as the 3rd President of UAE and Ruler of Abu Dhabi. The succession formalised the multi-year 2014-2022 architecture under which MBZ had operated as de facto apex.

## The Bani Fatima architecture

The contemporary UAE apex apparatus operates through the Bani Fatima architecture — the six sons of Sheikh Zayed and Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi, who control the apex institutional positions across the contemporary UAE state. The architecture distributes authority across the six brothers while consolidating apex authority under MBZ:

**Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (MBZ)**: President of UAE; Ruler of Abu Dhabi. Eldest Bani Fatima.

**Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan**: Ruler's Representative in Al Dhafra Region (western Abu Dhabi emirate).

**Hazza bin Zayed Al Nahyan**: Deputy Ruler of Abu Dhabi; National Security Advisor 2006-2016.

**Tahnoon bin Zayed Al Nahyan (TBZ)**: National Security Advisor since 2016; Deputy Ruler of Abu Dhabi; oversees over $1.5 trillion in UAE assets including International Holding Company (IHC) and ADQ sovereign wealth fund. TBZ is the most consequential economic operator under MBZ's apex authority and as the contemporary intelligence-and-financial-architecture controller.

**Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan**: UAE Vice President since 29 March 2023; Deputy Prime Minister; Minister of Presidential Affairs; chairman of Mubadala Investment Company (~$300B AUM); owner of Manchester City Football Club; international sports and entertainment operator.

**Abdullah bin Zayed Al Nahyan**: Minister of Foreign Affairs since 2006 — the foreign-policy operator under MBZ's authority.

The Bani Fatima architecture is the most consolidated apex-family architecture in the contemporary Arab Gulf — distinct from Saudi architecture under MBS (where rivals were eliminated through the November 2017 Ritz-Carlton purge), the Qatari Al Thani architecture (where internal-family dynamics operate differently), and the other Gulf architectures. The coordinated brother-architecture has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential contemporary Arab Gulf family-power architecture.

## MBZ's family and succession

MBZ married Sheikha Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. The couple has eight children. The succession architecture operates through the eldest son. **Sheikh Khaled bin Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan** (born 1982) was appointed Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi on 29 March 2023 — the indicator that MBZ has  identified his eldest son as successor.

The Khaled architecture operates under conditions of Bani Fatima institutional patronage. The institutional check operates through the five Bani Fatima brothers; the post-MBZ trajectory will operate as the central institutional question of whether the Khaled architecture consolidates  beyond MBZ's lifetime or whether the Bani Fatima brothers operate as institutional alternative-succession architecture.

The other MBZ sons include: Sheikh Theyab bin Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (alternative succession-architecture operator); Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1988); Sheikh Zayed bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1995, Sandhurst graduate December 2020). His daughters: Sheikha Fatima bint Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1989), Sheikha Shamma bint Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1992), and Sheikha Wadeema bint Zayed Al Nahyan (born 18 January 2025).

## The economic architecture

The UAE economic architecture operates as one of the most consequential contemporary sovereign-wealth-fund operators. The institutional vehicles:

- **ADIA (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority)**: founded 1976; ~$1 trillion AUM; institutional sovereign wealth fund.
- **Mubadala Investment Company**: ~$300B AUM; managed by Mansour bin Zayed; strategic investments in technology, energy, healthcare.
- **ADQ (Abu Dhabi Developmental Holding Company)**: formed 2018; managed by Tahnoon bin Zayed; ~$300B AUM.
- **International Holding Company (IHC)**: publicly-listed mega-conglomerate; chaired by Tahnoon bin Zayed; diversified holdings.
- **2PointZero**: Tahnoon-controlled investment vehicle; architecture acquisitions.
- **ADNOC**: Abu Dhabi National Oil Company; majority-state-owned oil and gas operator; roughly 4 million barrels per day production capacity.

Total UAE sovereign-and-quasi-sovereign assets exceed $2 trillion — making the UAE the most consequential per-capita sovereign-wealth operator globally. The assets are concentrated under Tahnoon and Mansour management with MBZ as apex authority.

The contemporary economic transition has produced the non-oil sector exceeding the hydrocarbons sector in Abu Dhabi GDP for the first time throughout the post-1971 period. The diversification operates through technology investment, financial services, logistics, tourism, and strategic-industry positions including subsequent advanced AI-chip architecture.

## Senior foreign policy operations

The MBZ foreign-policy architecture operates through the pattern of backing secessionist movements, fueling fragmentation across regional states, and supporting strongman operators against central governments. The pattern has operated across Libya, Yemen, Sudan, and other regional theatres.

The strategic foundation operates through opposition to the 2011 Arab Spring uprisings. MBZ has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential Arab strategic operator opposed to mass-mobilisation movements and Muslim Brotherhood-aligned architectures. The 2013 UAE94 trial — in which 94 Emirati Islamists were tried for plotting against the state and 69 were convicted — was the structural indicator of systematic domestic-suppression architecture.

### Yemen war

The UAE participated as coalition partner with Saudi Arabia in the Yemen war from 25 March 2015 onwards. The UAE withdrew most ground forces 2019-2020 but continued operating through proxy architectures. The Southern Transitional Council (STC) — the Yemeni separatist movement — operates as the UAE primary proxy. The UAE continues training and arming STC forces across the 2024-2026 period.

In 2022 MBZ disagreed with Saudi Arabia over the Al-Islah (Yemeni Muslim Brotherhood-linked party) — Saudi Arabia maintained support; UAE opposed. The January 2022 Houthi drone-and-missile attacks on Abu Dhabi (3 killed at the ADNOC oil depot, plus the architectural attacks) was the indicator of Houthi escalation against the UAE.

### Libya

The UAE foreign-policy architecture operated as major backer of General Khalifa Haftar's Libyan National Army (LNA). The 2014 onwards: arms, drones, mercenary support to Haftar. The UAE operations against the UN-recognised Government of National Accord violated the UN arms embargo (in UN Panel of Experts investigations). The UAE alignment with Egypt's Sisi on Libya is the governing pattern of Arab strongman coordination against central governments.

### Sudan civil war 2023-2026

The Sudan civil war is the most consequential contemporary UAE foreign-policy operation and the most consequential institutional liability of MBZ's tenure. The war began April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF, General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF, paramilitary commander Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo "Hemedti").

The UAE has been characterised across the Western analytical literature, the UN sanctions monitors, and the independent investigators as the most consequential foreign backer of the RSF. The UAE-RSF architecture has produced:

- **Weapons shipments**: drones, howitzers, multiple rocket launchers, antiaircraft missiles via the UAE-controlled airfield in Chad (Amdjarass).
- **Cover operations**: UAE Red Crescent humanitarian-relief framework used as institutional cover for weapons shipments.
- **Gold-mining architecture**: Hemedti South Darfur gold-mining operations supply UAE markets; subsequent flows have funded continued RSF operations.
- **Sheikh Tahnoon Bin Zayed investments**: Kush gold-mine investments (SAF-aligned) — UAE operates economic positions on both conflict parties.

The RSF Darfur ethnic-cleansing operations across the 2024 period produced mass atrocities including large-scale sexual violence, Masalit-tribal-targeted ethnic cleansing, and famine architectures. The ICJ ruled on 6 May 2025 that it lacks jurisdiction over Sudan's genocide case against the UAE. The US Treasury sanctioned Hemedti personally on 7 January 2025.

In December 2024 the UAE stated to the US that "it is not now transferring any weapons to the RSF and will not do so going forward." The subsequent UN sanctions monitors and independent investigators have continued documenting UAE-RSF weapons flows across the 2025-2026 period. The UAE foreign-policy advisor Anwar Gargash speech on 2 November 2025 admitting "mistakes in Sudan policy" was characterised by some observers as prelude to a meaningful policy shift; subsequent operational architecture remains contested.

### Abraham Accords and Israel

The 13 August 2020 UAE-Israel normalisation announcement and the 15 September 2020 White House signing under Trump 1 produced the most consequential Arab-Israeli diplomatic event since the 1979 Egypt-Israel and 1994 Jordan-Israel peace treaties. The UAE became the first Arab state to normalise with Israel since the 1994 Jordan agreement. The subsequent Bahrain (15 September 2020), Sudan (October 2020), and Morocco (December 2020) normalisations completed the Abraham Accords architecture.

The UAE-Israel economic, technology, and intelligence cooperation has produced major institutional positions across the subsequent 2020-2026 period. The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the Gaza war did not break UAE-Israel diplomatic relations — the UAE maintained diplomatic relations despite public criticism of Israeli operations. The November 2024 IMEC (India-Middle East-Europe Corridor) architecture continued advancing despite Gaza disruption.

### Iran

The UAE-Iran relationship operates through contradictory architectures. The long-standing UAE territorial claim over Abu Musa, Greater Tunb, and Lesser Tunb islands (Iranian-occupied since 1971) is the institutional dispute. The UAE pragmatic economic relationship operates through Dubai — the largest Iranian trading hub outside Iran.

MBZ has been characterised as the most consequential Arab Gulf operator opposed to Iranian regional expansion. He fell out with the Obama administration on the Iran nuclear deal and supported the Trump 1 withdrawal from JCPOA in May 2018. The post-2023 China-brokered Saudi-Iran normalisation produced UAE moderation across the subsequent 2024-2026 period. The contemporary 2025-2026 Iran war architecture has produced cautious UAE public position.

### Russia

The pragmatic UAE-Russia relationship has produced critical contemporary apparatus. Post-2022 Ukraine invasion: the UAE refused to join Western sanctions; positioned as financial-hub for Russian assets; subsequent UAE Ukraine-mediation operations have continued.

In August 2025 MBZ met Putin in Moscow; bilateral trade $11.5 billion with pledge to double. The UAE position has operated as strategic-hedge architecture — maintaining US strategic partnership while operating Russian-economic-architecture assets and Russian-and-Western-sanctions adherence simultaneously.

### China

The UAE operates as major Belt and Road Initiative partner. The Chinese investment in UAE technology, infrastructure, and advanced-industry positions has produced Western concerns about UAE technology-transfer architecture. The 2023 Chinese ARM-class chip access disputes and subsequent US concerns about UAE-China technology cooperation was the indicator. The 2024 UAE-China strategic-cooperation expansion has continued.

### India

The UAE-India Comprehensive Strategic Partnership operates through roughly 3.5 million Indian expatriates in UAE and bilateral commerce exceeding $80 billion annually. The July 2025 Modi-MBZ phone call expanded trade, defence, and connectivity cooperation. The September 2025 MBZ conferral of First-Class Order of Zayed II on Indian Ambassador Sunjay Sudhir operated as institutional indicator.

### BRICS+

The January 2024 UAE BRICS+ founding membership (alongside Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran, and the Saudi Arabia formal-membership declination) was the indicator of multi-vector strategic positioning. MBZ attended the 16th BRICS Summit in Kazan, Russia in October 2024. The UAE BRICS+ architecture operates alongside the US strategic-partnership architecture — the pattern of multi-vector positioning that has distinguished the UAE from the Saudi Arabia under MBS.
