# Dossier 42: Recep Tayyip Erdoğan and the Turkish AKP Apex Apparatus

## The Qatari governing structure

The Republic of Turkey was founded on 29 October 1923 under Mustafa Kemal Atatürk following the Turkish War of Independence (1919-1923). The Kemalist architecture constructed a secular, nationalist, Westernising republic. The 1924 Constitution abolished the Caliphate on 3 March 1924 and severed the Sunni Islamic universalist architecture that had operated through the Ottoman Sultanate-Caliphate framework.

The post-1923 architecture operated under military-tutelage architecture in which the Turkish Armed Forces was the structural guardian of Kemalist secularism. The 1960, 1971, 1980, and 1997 "post-modern" coups operated as institutional indicators. The Cold War period operated under Turkey's NATO membership since 1952 and tension between the Kemalist secular establishment and the emerging Islamist political-class architecture.

The contemporary AKP architecture is the displacement of the Kemalist-and-military-tutelage architecture. Erdoğan's AKP came to power in November 2002 and has been consolidated across the period. The 16 April 2017 constitutional referendum transformed the parliamentary system into the presidential system, abolished the Prime Minister position, and vested the presidency with expanded executive powers — the architectural pivot from parliamentary-Kemalist framework to contemporary Erdoğan-personalised authoritarian-presidentialist architecture.

## Erdoğan — biographical foundation

Recep Tayyip Erdoğan was born on 26 February 1954 in Beyoğlu, Istanbul. His father Ahmet Erdoğan (1905-1988) was a Turkish Coast Guard officer; his mother Tenzile Erdoğan (1924-2011) was a housewife. The family's origins were in Rize Province on Turkey's eastern Black Sea coast — the pious-conservative Sunni region distinct from the secular-Kemalist establishment that operated in Istanbul and Ankara.

Erdoğan's education operated through the imam-hatip religious vocational school — the governing architecture for Islamic clerical-and-religious training that operated as alternative to the Kemalist secular-education architecture. He subsequently graduated from the Aksaray Academy of Economic and Commercial Sciences (now Marmara University) with a degree in business administration. He worked as a consultant and manager in the private sector before entering politics.

He married Emine Erdoğan in 1978. The couple has four children: Ahmet Burak, Necmettin Bilal, Esra, and Sümeyye. Bilal Erdoğan operates as a businessman with multi-year allegations regarding oil trade with ISIS (December 2015 Russian government accusations during the Turkish-Russian rupture period). Esra Erdoğan married Berat Albayrak, who served as Finance Minister 2018-2020 under a heterodox monetary-policy programme that produced the 2021-2023 lira depreciation crisis.

## The pre-2002 Islamist-movement foundation

Erdoğan's pre-2002 political formation operated through the National View Movement (Milli Görüş) founded by Necmettin Erbakan in 1969. The National View architecture is the Turkish Islamist political-class institutional foundation. The National View political-party architecture operated through successive party formations that were systematically banned by the Constitutional Court and the military-tutelage architecture: National Order Party (1970-1971), National Salvation Party (1972-1981), Welfare Party (Refah Partisi, 1983-1998), Virtue Party (Fazilet, 1997-2001), Felicity Party (Saadet, 2001-present).

Erdoğan joined the National Salvation Party youth wing in 1976. He was elected Welfare Party Beyoğlu district chair in 1984 and Istanbul chair in 1985. In the 1994 local elections he was elected Mayor of Istanbul. His 1994-1998 mayoral tenure was the foundation of his subsequent national rise: he produced infrastructure improvements (water supply, waste management, Istanbul Metro expansion), moderate-Islamist governance, and pragmatic-administrative reputation.

In December 1997 Erdoğan was convicted of inciting religious hatred for reciting a poem by Ziya Gökalp at a rally in Siirt: "The minarets are our bayonets, the domes our helmets, the mosques our barracks, and the faithful our army." He was sentenced to 10 months imprisonment and banned from politics. He served 4 months at Pınarhisar Prison from April 1999. He was released July 1999.

The 1997 "post-modern coup" — the 28 February 1997 National Security Council memorandum that produced the collapse of the Necmettin Erbakan coalition government — served as the architectural indicator of the military-tutelage architecture's systematic suppression of Turkish Islamist political-class advancement.

## The 2002 AKP rise

On 14 August 2001 Erdoğan co-founded the AKP (Justice and Development Party, *Adalet ve Kalkınma Partisi*) with Abdullah Gül. The AKP abandoned overtly Islamist positions and adopted a "conservative-democratic" framework that aligned with centre-right political-class formations across the Western democratic architecture.

The 3 November 2002 parliamentary elections produced the AKP landslide victory: 363 of 550 seats. The architectural inflection point operated under conditions of the 2001 economic crisis that produced collapse of established political-class architectures and the unprecedented institutional reset of Turkish party-architecture.

Erdoğan was barred from candidacy under his 1997 conviction. The subsequent constitutional amendment  rescinded the ban. On 9 March 2003 he won the Siirt by-election. On 14 March 2003 he became the 25th Prime Minister of Turkey at age 53.

## The AKP consolidation 2003-2014

The 2003-2014 AKP architecture operated through architectural features that produced the consolidation of personalised authoritarian-presidentialist authority.

**Economic recovery.** The 2003-2014 period produced recovery from the 2001 economic crisis. The IMF-coordinated reform architecture, the export-expansion programme, the infrastructure-investment-led growth architecture, and the moderate-monetary-policy framework produced roughly 5-6% annual GDP growth across the 2003-2013 period.

**EU accession.** In October 2005 the EU formally opened membership negotiations with Turkey. The EU-accession architecture was the framework for pre-2010 AKP reformist trajectory — human-rights legislation, judicial reform, Kurdish-language permissions, civil-military-relations restructuring. The post-2007 trajectory stalled under European political-class opposition (French Sarkozy and German Merkel opposition to Turkish accession) and the AKP institutional re-orientation.

**Civil-military restructuring.** The 2008-2012 Ergenekon and Balyoz trials was the most consequential institutional restructuring of the military-tutelage architecture. Hundreds of military officers were prosecuted for alleged coup-plotting architectures. The 2010 constitutional referendum (57.88% Yes) reduced military influence on judicial appointments.

**Kurdish opening.** The 2012-2015 Erdoğan-Öcalan peace process produced institutional opening on the Kurdish question. PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan (imprisoned at İmralı Island since 1999) was the dialogue interlocutor. The subsequent 7 June 2015 elections produced HDP (pro-Kurdish Peoples' Democratic Party) entry to parliament — the 13.12% vote share that institutionally denied AKP a single-party majority. The July 2015 ceasefire collapse and the November 2015 re-elections under nationalist-coalition framework produced the architectural reversal.

**Direct presidential election.** On 10 August 2014 Erdoğan won Turkey's first direct presidential election with 51.79%. He became the 12th President. The position remained constitutionally largely ceremonial — but Erdoğan operated through personal authority and AKP-coalition-dominant parliamentary majority to expand presidential operational authority across the multi-year period.

## The 15 July 2016 coup attempt and the purge

On 15 July 2016 a faction within the Turkish Armed Forces attempted a coup. Tanks deployed in Istanbul and Ankara; fighter jets bombed parliament and the presidential complex; coup forces seized CNN Türk and state media; tanks crushed civilians at the Bosphorus Bridge.

Erdoğan was on holiday in Marmaris on the southwestern coast. He summoned supporters to the streets via FaceTime broadcast on CNN Türk. Mass mobilisation of AKP supporters and loyalist military operators across the overnight period defeated the coup architecture. The 15-16 July 2016 outcome: 251 killed; 2,194 injured. The Erdoğan apex authority survived institutionally.

The Turkish government blamed the coup attempt on the Gülen movement — the Sufi-Sunni religious-and-educational network led by Fethullah Gülen, a Turkish cleric in self-imposed exile in Saylorsburg, Pennsylvania since 1999. The Gülen network had was the AKP institutional ally across the 2002-2013 period; the Ergenekon-Balyoz trials were constructed through Gülenist-aligned prosecutors and judges. The 2013 corruption-investigation rupture — when Gülenist-aligned prosecutors launched corruption investigations against AKP ministers and Erdoğan family members — produced the Erdoğan-Gülen institutional rupture.

The post-2013 architecture characterised the Gülen movement as FETÖ ("Fethullah Terrorist Organisation"). Fethullah Gülen died on 20 October 2024 in Pennsylvania.

The 15 July 2016 coup attempt produced the most consequential purge architecture in the contemporary Turkish institutional history. The subsequent architecture:

- **State of emergency** 21 July 2016 - 18 July 2018 (24 months).
- **Detentions**: over 150,000 across the period.
- **Imprisonments**: roughly 50,000.
- **Public-service dismissals**: roughly 130,000.
- **Targeted institutional architectures**: military (one-third of generals dismissed); judiciary (one-third of judges dismissed); police; media (newspapers, TV stations, news agencies closed); academia (thousands of academics dismissed; Academics for Peace petition signers prosecuted); civil society (hundreds of NGOs closed).

The 2016-2018 purge architecture eliminated the Gülenist network and secular-Kemalist architectures simultaneously. The purge has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential institutional purge in the contemporary post-Cold-War period.

## The 16 April 2017 constitutional referendum

On 16 April 2017 the Turkish electorate approved 18 constitutional amendments by 51.41% to 48.59%. The amendments transformed the parliamentary system into the presidential system: Prime Minister position abolished; executive powers vested in the presidency; cabinet appointment under presidential authority; judicial appointment under expanded presidential control through restructured Supreme Board of Judges and Prosecutors; parliament seats expanded from 550 to 600; candidacy age lowered from 25 to 18.

The 2017 referendum functioned as the architectural pivot from Kemalist-parliamentary institutional framework to Erdoğan-personalised authoritarian-presidentialist architecture. The Western analytical literature has characterised the 2017 amendments as the most consequential institutional transformation of the post-1923 Turkish apparatus.

## The 2018-2023 consolidations and 2024 setback

On 24 June 2018 Erdoğan was re-elected in the first election under the new presidential system with 52.59% in the first round. The AKP-MHP "People's Alliance" achieved parliamentary majority.

The 14 May 2023 and 28 May 2023 elections operated under conditions of the 2021-2023 economic crisis (80%+ lira depreciation under heterodox monetary-policy framework). The first round produced Erdoğan 49.52% versus CHP's Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu 44.88%. The runoff produced Erdoğan 52.18% versus Kılıçdaroğlu 47.82% — the third presidential term.

On 8 March 2024 Erdoğan announced he would retire at the end of his presidential term in 2028.

The 31 March 2024 local elections produced the worst electoral defeat in the 22-year AKP history. The CHP became the largest political-class formation by vote share. The CHP retained Istanbul (Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu) and Ankara (Mayor Mansur Yavaş) and won 36 of 81 provincial capitals.

The İmamoğlu architecture stood as the most consequential opposition challenge. His 2019 Istanbul mayoral election victory (re-run after the March 2019 controversial first-result cancellation) had was the indicator of post-AKP architectural alternative. His 2024 re-election by expanded margin was the 2028 presidential-candidacy positioning.

## The March 2025 İmamoğlu crackdown

On 19 March 2025 Ekrem İmamoğlu was arrested on corruption and "terrorism" charges. The arrest was the most consequential opposition-suppression operation in the contemporary Turkish apparatus. On 11 March 2025 İmamoğlu's Istanbul University diploma had been  annulled — the architectural disqualification under the constitutional requirement that presidential candidates hold a university degree.

The arrest and the disqualification in practice eliminated İmamoğlu from the 2028 presidential election. The subsequent 2025 protests across Turkey have produced the largest mass mobilisations since the 2013 Gezi Park protests. The international criticism from EU, US (under Biden), and human-rights organisations is the structural indicator. The continued 2025-2026 protests operate as the continuing institutional crisis architecture.

## Foreign policy — Syria

The Turkish hostility toward the Assad-regime architecture (Hafez 1971-2000, Bashar 2000-2024) has been characterised as the architectural foundation of contemporary Erdoğan Middle East strategy. The ideological roots operate through the National View Movement: Turkish Sunni Islamists viewed the Alawite-dominated Syrian Ba'athist regime as secular socialists dangerously aligned with the Soviet architecture.

From 2011 Turkey supported the Syrian opposition. The subsequent backing of Sunni Islamist factions including the Free Syrian Army (FSA, subsequently renamed Syrian National Army) was the foundation. From 2012 onwards the Turkish border was the key transit route for foreign fighters and Sunni Islamist factions including Jabhat al-Nusra (Al-Qaeda affiliate, subsequently renamed Hayat Tahrir al-Sham or HTS).

From 2016 onwards: Turkish military operations targeted both ISIS and the Kurdish YPG (US-aligned Syrian Democratic Forces principal component, characterised by Turkey as PKK affiliate). The operations: August 2016 Operation Euphrates Shield (against ISIS and SDF); January 2018 Operation Olive Branch (Afrin); October 2019 Operation Peace Spring (north-east Syria post-Trump withdrawal); 2022 onwards Idlib operations.

The 2017 onwards: cooperative relationship between Turkey and HTS in Idlib (despite Turkey's official designation of HTS as terrorist organisation). The 2017-2024 architecture enabled HTS to consolidate its Idlib governance, accumulate weapons and forces, and subsequent train and prepare for the 2024 offensive against the Assad regime.

In November 2024 HTS launched the offensive. On 8 December 2024 the Assad regime collapsed; HTS took Damascus; Bashar al-Assad fled to Moscow. On 13 December 2024 Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan visited Damascus — the first major foreign-power-government delegation. On 25 December 2024 Turkey reopened its embassy in Damascus.

The 2025 architecture has produced Turkish institutional influence over post-Assad Syria: the Turkish lira operates as unofficial currency in Syrian population centres; Turkish minders attend HTS meetings with foreign diplomats; Ahmed al-Sharaa (formerly Abu Mohammad al-Julani, HTS leader who dropped the nom de guerre in 2025) serves as the architectural beneficiary of Turkish backing. The post-Assad Syria architecture is the most consequential geopolitical achievement of the Erdoğan tenure.

## Foreign policy — Libya

In January 2020 Turkish troops deployed to support the Tripoli-based Government of National Accord (GNA) against the Russia-UAE-Egypt-Saudi-backed Haftar Libyan National Army architecture. The 27 November 2019 maritime boundary agreement between Turkey and the GNA — controversial for disrupting Greek, Cypriot, Egyptian, and Israeli maritime claims — served as the architectural foundation of the Turkish-GNA relationship.

The subsequent Turkish-UAE-Egypt-Saudi proxy contestation across Libya produced 2020 ceasefire architecture. From 2021 onwards: Turkish reconciliation with UAE and Saudi Arabia produced moderation. The 2024-2026 continued Turkish operational presence in western Libya is the governing indicator of strategic positioning.

## Foreign policy — Nagorno-Karabakh and the Caucasus

The 2020 Second Karabakh War: Turkey decisively armed Azerbaijan with Bayraktar TB2 drones; Azerbaijani victory across the 44-day operation. The "one nation, two states" rhetoric consolidated the Turkey-Azerbaijan strategic partnership. The December 2020 Victory Parade in Baku attended by Erdoğan was the indicator.

The September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive against Nagorno-Karabakh and the ethnic cleansing of 100,000+ ethnic Armenians (in Dossier 37) operated under the Turkish-aligned architecture. The Organisation of Turkic States 2021 reorganisation (Turkey, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan; Turkmenistan and Hungary observers) is the Turkey-led architectural framework for Turkic-cultural and economic integration across the Caucasus-and-Central-Asian region.

## Foreign policy — NATO, Russia, and Ukraine

Turkey has been a NATO member since 1952. The November 2015 Turkish F-16 shooting down of a Russian Su-24 over the Syrian-Turkish border was the most consequential post-Cold-War Turkey-Russia confrontation. The subsequent 2015-2016 rupture produced Russian sanctions against Turkey; the subsequent 2016 reconciliation (after Erdoğan apology to Putin) restored the bilateral relationship.

The July 2017 onwards Turkish acquisition of the Russian S-400 missile system was the most consequential institutional rupture with the Western strategic architecture. The 2019 US suspension of Turkey from the F-35 programme was the architectural consequence. The contemporary Turkish position combines NATO membership with Russian strategic-systems-acquisition and multi-vector positioning.

The post-22 February 2022 Ukraine invasion produced architectural test. Turkey supplied Ukraine with Bayraktar TB2 drones — the indicator of Ukrainian-aligned positioning. Turkey closed the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits to Russian warships under the Montreux Convention — the architectural application that prevented Russian Black Sea Fleet reinforcement from the Mediterranean. Turkey declined to join Western sanctions architecture; expanded trade with Russia; positioned as the multi-vector strategic mediator.

The July 2022 Black Sea Grain Initiative brokered by Turkey and the UN stood as the most consequential Russia-Ukraine diplomatic architecture. The subsequent Turkish-mediated prisoner exchanges was the indicator. The 2025 Trump-administration-initiated Ukraine ceasefire negotiations were hosted in Turkey — the structural consolidation of Turkey's mediator architecture.

In May 2022 Erdoğan temporarily blocked Finland and Sweden NATO membership. He approved Finnish membership in March 2023. He approved Swedish membership in January 2024 after Sweden approved new counterterrorism legislation and after the Biden administration approved modernised F-16 fighter jet sale to Turkey.

## Foreign policy — Israel, Gaza, and Hamas

The Turkish-Israeli relationship operated under conditions of intermittent normalisation and rupture across the post-1949 period. The 2009 Davos Erdoğan-Peres confrontation over Gaza; the 2010 Mavi Marmara incident (10 Turkish activists killed by Israeli commandos on Gaza-bound flotilla); the intermittent diplomatic-relations management functioned as the pre-2023 architecture.

The Turkey-Hamas relationship was the architecture. Hamas Political Bureau Chief Ismail Haniyeh operated his office from Istanbul under Turkish institutional protection across the 2014-2024 period. Haniyeh met Erdoğan in Istanbul months before his 31 July 2024 Tehran assassination by Israel. Turkey has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential foreign supporter of Hamas — providing diplomatic, financial, and political support since the post-2006 period.

The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Israeli genocide architecture (in Part XII Chapter 5) produced the Turkey-Israel rupture. On 2 May 2024 Turkey suspended all trade with Israel. The Erdoğan rhetoric calling Israel "Nazi" and "terror state" and comparing Netanyahu to Hitler produced the most consequential post-1949 Turkish-Israeli diplomatic rupture. The 2024-2026 trajectory has produced continued ruptured relations.

## Foreign policy — BRICS and strategic positioning

On 2 September 2024 AKP spokesperson Ömer Çelik announced that Turkey had filed an official application to become a member of BRICS+. The architectural significance: Turkey would be the first NATO member to join BRICS+. The subsequent continued ambivalence on the membership trajectory is the governing indicator of multi-vector positioning.

The January 2024 BRICS+ expansion (UAE, Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran joining; Saudi Arabia declining formal membership; Argentina withdrawing under Milei) produced the architectural framework within which the Turkish application operates. The continued BRICS+ membership question is the central institutional indicator of the contemporary Turkish strategic-positioning trajectory.

## Foreign policy — PKK and the 2025 peace process

The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) conflict has operated since 1984. Roughly 40,000+ have been killed across the conflict. PKK founder Abdullah Öcalan was captured in February 1999 in Kenya and imprisoned at İmralı Island; he has was the dialogue interlocutor across the period.

In February 2025 Öcalan called for the PKK to disarm and dissolve. In March 2025 the PKK announced unilateral ceasefire. In May 2025 the PKK announced dissolution. The subsequent Erdoğan domestic-political opening is the architectural foundation of Iraqi-Kurdish trade-and-energy expansion and Syria-Kurdish architectural negotiations under the post-Assad Sharaa-led Damascus architecture.

The 22 October 2024 Devlet Bahçeli (MHP leader; AKP-MHP "People's Alliance" partner) surprise call for PKK reconciliation served as the architectural opener. The subsequent 2024-2025 architectural opening has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential institutional opening on the Kurdish question throughout the post-1984 period.

## Senior other AKP operators

- **Devlet Bahçeli**: MHP leader; AKP-MHP "People's Alliance" partner; key Erdoğan ally.
- **Hakan Fidan**: Foreign Minister since 3 June 2023; MİT head 2010-2023; central Erdoğan operator on intelligence, Syria, and foreign-policy architectures.
- **İbrahim Kalın**: MİT head since June 2023 (replacing Fidan); Erdoğan spokesman pre-2023.
- **Cevdet Yılmaz**: Vice President since 3 June 2023.
- **Mehmet Şimşek**: Finance Minister since 3 June 2023; orthodox-monetary-policy architect of post-2023 lira-stabilisation programme.
- **Hulusi Akar**: Defence Minister 2018-2023; Chief of General Staff 2015-2018 (operated through 15 July 2016 coup attempt as loyalist).
- **Yaşar Güler**: Defence Minister since 3 June 2023.
- **Numan Kurtulmuş**: Speaker of Grand National Assembly since June 2023.
- **Bilal Erdoğan**: Erdoğan's son; businessman; controversial public profile.

## Economic architecture

The 2021-2023 economic crisis under the heterodox monetary-policy framework (interest-rate-cuts-as-inflation-cure architecture pursued under Berat Albayrak Finance Ministry 2018-2020 and subsequent Erdoğan personal direction) produced 80%+ lira depreciation against the dollar across the 2021-2023 period.

In June 2023 Mehmet Şimşek was appointed Finance Minister; orthodox monetary policy was restored. The 2024-2025 stabilisation produced inflation declining from a peak of roughly 75% to roughly 40% by 2025. The 2026 forecast operates under continued monetary tightening.

# Dossier 43: Abdel Fattah el-Sisi and the Egyptian Apex Apparatus

## The machineryure

Egypt was declared a republic on 18 June 1953 following the 1952 Free Officers Movement that overthrew King Farouk. The post-1953 apparatus has produced six presidents: Mohamed Naguib (1953-1954), Gamal Abdel Nasser (1956-1970), Anwar Sadat (1970-1981), Hosni Mubarak (1981-2011), Mohamed Morsi (2012-2013), and Abdel Fattah el-Sisi (2014-present). The contemporary apparatus operates under the pattern in which the Egyptian Armed Forces operate as the apex institutional architecture and the civilian presidency operates under military-establishment authority.

The pre-2011 architecture operated through five architectural periods. The Nasser period (1956-1970) constructed the Arab nationalist architecture, the 1956 Suez Crisis, the 1967 Six-Day War defeat, and the pan-Arab leadership framework. The Sadat period (1970-1981) produced the 1973 October War, the 1978 Camp David Accords, the 1979 Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty, and the Sadat assassination on 6 October 1981. The Mubarak period (1981-2011) operated under authoritarian consolidation, US strategic partnership ($1.3 billion annual military aid since 1979), and corruption-and-clientelist architecture that produced the 25 January 2011 Egyptian Revolution.

The 2011-2013 architectural transition operated through the 11 February 2011 Mubarak resignation, the Supreme Council of Armed Forces (SCAF) under Field Marshal Mohamed Hussein Tantawi, the 30 June 2012 Muslim Brotherhood candidate Mohamed Morsi presidential election victory, and the 3 July 2013 Sisi-led coup d'état. The coup operated under conditions of 30 June 2013 Tamarod ("Rebel") mass anti-Morsi protests and UAE-Saudi institutional backing.

The contemporary Sisi architecture is the consolidation of post-2013 military-establishment authority under the 2014 Constitution and the 2019 constitutional referendum (extending presidential terms from 4 to 6 years and allowing Sisi to stand for a third term). The post-1979 US strategic partnership has been maintained across the Sisi tenure; the Camp David framework operates under conditions of contemporary Gaza-war architecture tested by the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the Israeli Gaza genocide architecture in Part XII Chapter 5.

## Sisi — biographical foundation

Abdel Fattah Saeed Hussein Khalil el-Sisi was born on 19 November 1954 in Gamaleya district, Cairo. His father Saeed Hussein el-Sisi was a woodworker who owned a bazaar shop near the Khan el-Khalili market. He has eight siblings. He married Entissar Amer (his first cousin); the couple has three sons and one daughter.

His career operated through the Egyptian military architecture. He graduated from the Egyptian Military Academy in 1977 and served in the infantry. He never saw combat in the pre-2013 period. The subsequent military-intelligence career operated through institutional positions including the 1987 UK Joint Services Command and Staff College, the 1992 Egyptian Command and Staff College, and the 2006 US Army War College in Carlisle, Pennsylvania. His 15 March 2006 thesis "Democracy in the Middle East" is the indicator of his pre-2011 strategic-formation architecture.

He advanced to command a mechanized infantry division and operated as Commander of the Northern Military Zone. From 2008 to 2010 he served as Head of Military Intelligence and Reconnaissance — the position that built his intelligence-architecture authority across the pre-2011 Egyptian Armed Forces.

## The 2011-2013 rise

The 25 January 2011 Egyptian Revolution was the structural inflection point. Mass mobilisation in Tahrir Square and subsequent protests across Egyptian cities produced the 11 February 2011 Mubarak resignation. The subsequent Supreme Council of Armed Forces (SCAF) under Field Marshal Mohamed Hussein Tantawi was the transitional architecture.

Sisi was the youngest member of the SCAF. He was characterised in the Western analytical literature as moderate Islamist and pragmatic security operator. The 30 June 2012 Muslim Brotherhood candidate Mohamed Morsi was inaugurated as the 5th President of Egypt — the first democratically elected post-1953 Egyptian President.

In August 2012 Morsi appointed Sisi as Minister of Defence and Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces — replacing Tantawi. The architectural significance: Morsi  believed Sisi was aligned with the Brotherhood-leaning architecture; the subsequent 12 months produced the reversal under which Sisi was the architect of the coup against Morsi.

## The 3 July 2013 coup

The 30 June 2013 mass anti-Morsi protests under the Tamarod ("Rebel") campaign produced multi-million-person mobilisation in Cairo, Alexandria, and Egyptian cities. The 1 July 2013 Sisi ultimatum to Morsi gave the President 48 hours to "meet the demands of the people."

On 3 July 2013 Sisi led the Egyptian Armed Forces in the coup that overthrew Morsi. Adly Mansour (Supreme Constitutional Court Chief Justice) was appointed interim President. Morsi was detained.

The 2013 coup operated under UAE-Saudi institutional backing. The $12 billion subsequent Saudi-UAE-Kuwait financial assistance package was the governing foundation of the post-Morsi architecture. Leaked tapes subsequently indicated Egyptian military involvement in organising the pre-coup protests and high-level coordination between the coup plotters and the UAE.

The Western analytical literature has characterised the 2013 coup as the most consequential institutional reversal of Arab Spring democratic-architecture aspirations and the most consequential UAE-Saudi institutional intervention in Arab political-class architecture.

## The 14 August 2013 Rabaa massacre

The 14 August 2013 Rabaa massacre is the most consequential institutional event of Sisi's pre-2014 architecture. Egyptian security forces dispersed Muslim Brotherhood sit-ins at the Rabaa al-Adawiya Square in Cairo and the al-Nahda Square in Giza. At least 817 protesters were killed at Rabaa per Human Rights Watch documentation; estimates of total Rabaa-and-al-Nahda casualties exceed 1,000.

Human Rights Watch characterised the Rabaa massacre as a crime against humanity. The Egyptian government characterised the operations as counter-terrorism architecture under conditions of Muslim-Brotherhood institutional violence.

The subsequent Egyptian apparatus  banned the Muslim Brotherhood (December 2013 designation as terrorist organisation), prosecuted tens of thousands of Brotherhood-aligned operators, and subsequently sentenced individuals to death across mass-trial architectures. On 17 June 2019 Morsi died in the courtroom during his trial under conditions of medical neglect that observers characterised as institutional homicide.

## The Sisi presidential tenure

### The 2014 election and first term

On 26-28 May 2014 the Egyptian presidential election produced Sisi 96.9% (against Hamdeen Sabahi). On 8 June 2014 he was sworn in as the 6th President of Egypt. The 2014 election operated under conditions of post-Rabaa repression and Brotherhood-prosecution architecture.

The 2014-2018 first term operated through architectural features: the 2014 Constitution institutional construction, the August 2015 Suez Canal Expansion ($8.5 billion infrastructure programme), the 2015 IMF programme architecture, the Sinai counter-insurgency operations against the ISIS-affiliated Wilayat Sinai architecture, and the authoritarian consolidation including expanded political-prisoner architecture.

### The 2018 election and second term

On 26-28 March 2018 Sisi was re-elected with 97.1% — the election operated under conditions of systematic disqualification of viable opposition candidates. Ahmed Shafik (the last Prime Minister under Mubarak and Morsi's main 2012 presidential rival) was reportedly forced out of the 2018 race after threats from an intelligence officer. Abdel Moneim Aboul Fotouh was detained after calling for a boycott. General Sami Anan was detained for over a year. Mostafa Mousa was the token candidate. The Western analytical literature characterised the 2018 election as plebiscite rather than competitive election.

### The 2019 constitutional referendum

The 19-22 April 2019 constitutional referendum produced 88.83% Yes (on 44.33% turnout). The amendments extended presidential terms from 4 to 6 years, added 2 years to current term, and allowed Sisi to stand for a third and final term in 2024 —  exempting him from the 2-term limit.

### The 2023 election and third term

On 18 December 2023 Sisi was re-elected with 89.6% (against three largely unknown candidates). The election operated under conditions of the 2022-2023 economic crisis and the subsequent 7 October 2023 Gaza war regional architecture. The Ahmed Tantawi 2023 presidential candidacy — opposition candidate who subsequently withdrew under conditions of insufficient endorsements after systematic intimidation of his signature-gathering operators — was the indicator of systematic suppression of the 2023 opposition.

On 2 April 2024 Sisi was sworn in for the third 6-year term running to 2030. The 2030 succession architecture is the central institutional uncertainty of the contemporary Egyptian architecture.

## The authoritarian consolidation

The contemporary Egyptian architecture under Sisi is the most consequential post-Mubarak authoritarian consolidation. The architectural indicators:

- Freedom House 2025 assessment: Egypt "Not Free" (18/100 political-rights-and-civil-liberties score).
- Human Rights Watch characterisation: Sisi's government "relies on naked coercion and the military and security services as his main vehicles of control."
- Tens of thousands of political prisoners across the tenure.
- Multi-decade Brotherhood-prosecution architecture.
- secular-opposition prosecution including journalists, academics, and civil-society operators.
- 2 October 2019: 2,300+ arrests including 111+ children — the largest crackdown to that point.
- 2024: "Dignity Revolution" online protests; subsequent crackdown with hundreds detained.
- June 2025: Global March to Gaza movement; Egyptian border guards clashed with international activists.

The post-2013 architecture has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as more draconian than the Mubarak architecture across the 1981-2011 period.

## The contemporary Gaza war architecture

The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Israeli Gaza genocide architecture (in Part XII Chapter 5) produced the most consequential test of the Camp David framework. Egypt borders Gaza at the 12-kilometre Rafah crossing — the only non-Israeli land border of the 2.3-million-person Gaza Strip.

The Sisi institutional position on Hamas: characterised as enemy; Brotherhood-affiliated; 2012 Egyptian-soldier-killing in Sinai blamed on Hamas. The pre-October-2023 Egyptian-Hamas relationship operated under conditions of hostility despite the shared Brotherhood-roots architecture.

The post-October-2023 Egyptian institutional position operated under conditions of architectural contradictions:

**Refusal to allow Palestinian mass displacement into Sinai**: Sisi rejected Israeli pressure to allow mass Palestinian displacement across the Rafah border. The Egyptian position characterised the proposed Palestinian transfer as architectural risk of Egyptian security destabilisation and permanent alteration of Gaza demographics.

**Boosted border forces**: Egyptian Armed Forces conducted subsequent border-fortification operations along the Sinai-Gaza border.

**Maintained Camp David framework**: the 1979 Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty is the foundation; Egyptian-Israeli diplomatic and security cooperation continued operating despite public condemnation of Israeli operations.

**Operated as central Arab mediator**: Egypt functioned as the central Arab mediator between Israel and Hamas across the period. The co-sponsorship architecture: November 2023 hostage pause; January 2025 ceasefire framework; 13 October 2025 Sharm El Sheikh peace summit formalising the Gaza ceasefire framework.

**Rafah crossing operations**: on 7 May 2024 Israeli forces seized the Rafah crossing — reducing Egyptian leverage. Ibrahim al-Organi, an Egyptian tycoon with close ties to the Sisi regime, operated lucrative smuggling operations bringing goods into Gaza via Rafah at 4x market prices (in 2022 a diesel liter cost Gaza residents $2.50 — double the Israeli-imported fuel price).

The Western analytical literature has characterised the contemporary Egyptian position as the most consequential Arab architectural contradiction — formally opposed to Israeli genocide operations while institutionally maintaining the Camp David framework, the US strategic partnership, the Rafah-blockade architecture, and the smuggling-profit architecture.

## The foreign-policy architecture

### UAE-Saudi alignment

The UAE-Egypt close alignment under MBZ (in Dossier 41) and the Saudi-Egypt close alignment under MBS (in Dossier 40) operate as the architectural foundation of contemporary Egyptian foreign policy. The 2013 coup operated under UAE-Saudi financial backing ($12 billion package); Egyptian institutional dependence has continued operating.

The February 2024 Ras El-Hekma deal: $35 billion Mediterranean tourism mega-project funded by UAE — the most consequential single foreign-investment commitment in Egyptian history. UAE institutional economic-architecture is the central foreign-investment-and-budget-support framework under conditions of 2022-2024 Egyptian currency collapse.

### US relations

The US-Egypt strategic partnership has operated since the 1979 Camp David architecture. The annual $1.3 billion US military aid (second-largest US aid recipient after Israel since 1979) is the foundation.

The 2017 Trump 1 administration: Trump characterised Sisi as "my favourite dictator." The Biden administration continued US military aid despite human-rights concerns. The 2024-2025 Trump 2 Gaza-mediation architecture has produced cooperation across the Sharm El Sheikh peace summit and the ceasefire-framework architecture.

### Russia, China, BRICS+

The post-Morsi Russia-Egypt rapprochement operated under conditions of US-Egypt institutional tension following the 2013 coup. The 2015 onwards Russia-Egypt military cooperation expanded. The $30 billion Dabaa Nuclear Power Plant Russian-financed architecture operates with 2030 delivery target.

The Belt and Road Initiative architecture operates across Egyptian infrastructure investment. Chinese investment in the New Administrative Capital and Suez Canal Chinese-shipping architecture operate as the foundation.

In January 2024 Egypt became a BRICS+ founding member alongside UAE, Ethiopia, and Iran (Saudi Arabia declined formal membership; Argentina withdrew under Milei). In October 2024 Sisi attended the 16th BRICS Summit in Kazan, Russia.

### Sudan civil war

The Egyptian-Sudanese apparatus has been  critical to contemporary Sisi foreign-policy operations. The April 2023 onwards Sudan civil war between SAF (Burhan) and RSF (Hemedti) produced the architectural contradiction.

Sisi backed the SAF — opposed to the UAE-RSF architecture in Dossier 41 — despite the UAE-Egypt close alignment. Egyptian Air Force operations against the RSF have been in subsequent reports. The Sudan war is the most consequential UAE-Egypt institutional divergence under the contemporary period.

### Ethiopia and the Nile

The Egyptian-Ethiopian Nile River dispute operates through the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam architecture. The $4.8 billion Ethiopian-built dam (operational from 2022) is the structural architecture that Egypt characterises as existential threat to Egyptian water security. The 2024-2026 ongoing diplomatic and architectural negotiations operate as the continuing institutional dispute.

### Syria

In February 2025 Sisi congratulated Ahmed al-Sharaa on his appointment as President of Syria — the Brotherhood-aligned operator's ascendance under the post-Assad architecture. The architectural opening: Egyptian anti-Brotherhood architectural framework  accommodated the post-Assad Damascus architecture.

### Senior other operators

The contemporary Egyptian apex apparatus operates through architectural operators:

- **Mostafa Madbouly**: Prime Minister since 7 June 2018; architect of economic-management architecture.
- **Sameh Shoukry**: Foreign Minister 17 June 2014 - 3 July 2024.
- **Badr Abdelatty**: Foreign Minister since 3 July 2024.
- **Abbas Kamel**: Director General Mukhabarat (General Intelligence Directorate) since 2018; central security operator.
- **Mahmoud Tawfik**: Interior Minister since 14 June 2018.
- **Abdel Magid Saqr**: Defence Minister since 13 August 2024.
- **Galal el-Haridy**: Speaker of House of Representatives since October 2025.

## The economic architecture

The Sisi economic-management architecture has produced contradictory institutional outcomes. The infrastructure mega-project programme serves as the architectural foundation:

- **2015 Suez Canal Expansion**: $8.5 billion.
- **New Administrative Capital**: $58 billion east of Cairo; some ministries moved by mid-2025; Sisi held the third-term inauguration there in 2024.
- **New Delta agricultural project**: 2.5 million acres of desert reclamation west of the Nile; Mostaqbal Misr sustainable city.
- **El-Dabaa Nuclear Power Plant**: $30 billion Russian-financed; 2030 delivery target.
- **Ras El-Hekma**: $35 billion UAE-funded Mediterranean tourism hub; construction began 2025.

The external-debt expansion has produced $160+ billion debt by early 2026 — roughly tripled under Sisi. The 2022-2024 currency-collapse architecture operated through three successive devaluations; the pound depreciated roughly 65% against the dollar since 2022. The March 2024 IMF programme — Egypt's fourth — operated under conditions of $8 billion subsequent package.

In June 2024 the subsidised bread price was increased 300% (the first increase in 35 years), affecting roughly 65 million Egyptians dependent on subsidised bread. The pre-2024 5-piastre bread price had been subsidised since the 1977 bread riots that nearly toppled Sadat.

# Dossier 44: Iraq Post-2003 — The Sudani-to-Zaidi Architecture and the PMF-KRG Apparatus

## The Iraqi institutional structure

The Republic of Iraq was founded on 14 July 1958 following the Free Officers' overthrow of the Hashemite monarchy under King Faisal II. The post-1958 architecture operated through military-and-Ba'athist political-class periods. The Ba'ath Party seized power in the 17 July 1968 coup. Saddam Hussein operated as President from 16 July 1979 to 9 April 2003 — the authoritarian tenure that produced the 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq War (roughly 500,000+ Iraqi-Iranian casualties), the 1988 Anfal campaign against the Kurdish population (50,000-180,000 killed including the 16 March 1988 Halabja chemical attack), the 1990 invasion of Kuwait, the 1991 Gulf War, and the post-1991 sanctions architecture under UN Security Council Resolution 661.

The 19 March - 1 May 2003 US-UK-led invasion under the Bush 2 administration produced the most consequential institutional transformation of Iraq in the post-1958 period. The Coalition Provisional Authority (CPA) under Paul Bremer dissolved the Iraqi state through the 16 May 2003 CPA Order Number 1 (de-Ba'athification of Iraqi society) and the 23 May 2003 CPA Order Number 2 (dissolution of the Iraqi Armed Forces). The Sunni-and-secular institutional architecture was eliminated; the insurgency, civil war, and 2014 ISIS rise have been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the architectural consequences. Saddam Hussein was executed on 30 December 2006.

The post-2003 architecture operates under the 2005 Constitution — the federal parliamentary republic framework that constructed the muhasasa (sectarian power-sharing) architecture. The tripartite political-class architecture: Prime Minister Shia (Arab); President Kurd; Speaker of Parliament Sunni (Arab). The 329-seat Council of Representatives is the parliamentary architecture.

## Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani — biographical foundation

Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani was born on 9 March 1970 in Baghdad. His family is from Maysan Province in southern Iraq — the poor, predominantly Shia, marsh-Arab region that was the Da'wa Party stronghold under the Saddam regime. His father was a Da'wa Party operator who was executed by the Saddam regime — the architectural foundation of his Shia-Islamist political-class formation.

His education operated through Baghdad University with an agriculture degree. He worked as a civil servant under the Saddam regime through the 1990s and pre-2003 period. He joined the Islamic Da'wa Party (Tanzim al-Iraq faction) after the 2003 invasion. The Da'wa Party — the Shia Islamist political party founded in 1957 by Mohammed Baqir al-Sadr (cousin of Muqtada al-Sadr's father Mohammed Sadeq al-Sadr) — was the foundation of post-2003 Iraqi Shia political-class architecture; Jaafari, Maliki, and Abadi all operated through the Da'wa institutional framework.

### Pre-PM career

Sudani's pre-PM career operated through administrative and ministerial positions: Mayor of Al-Amarah (Maysan Province capital) 2004-2009; Governor of Maysan Province 2009-2010; Minister of Human Rights 2010-2014 under Maliki; Minister of Labour and Social Affairs 2014-2018 under Abadi. In 2018 he split from Da'wa and formed the Furatayn Movement — the political-class formation that operated as his post-2018 institutional vehicle.

## The path to PM 2022

The 10 October 2021 Iraqi parliamentary election produced the Sadrist Movement (Muqtada al-Sadr's anti-corruption populist Shia cleric architecture) victory: 73 seats — the largest bloc. Sadr formed the tripartite "Save the Homeland" alliance with the KDP (Masoud Barzani) and the Sunni Sovereignty coalition (Mohammed al-Halbousi).

The pro-Iran Coordination Framework (CF) — including Maliki's State of Law, the Fatah Alliance (Hadi al-Amiri, pro-Iran militia operators), and allies — blocked the tripartite majority via the Federal Supreme Court ruling. On 12 June 2022 Sadr withdrew his 73 MPs from parliament in the dramatic political-class move; on 30 August 2022 Sadrist supporters stormed the Green Zone in clashes that killed 30+.

The subsequent architecture: on 13 October 2022 Abdul Latif Rashid (PUK) was elected President; on 27 October 2022 CF-backed Sudani was sworn in as PM with Maliki support. The architectural significance: Sudani was the Da'wa-aligned moderate operator positioned to balance the CF pro-Iran architecture with the US-aligned Iraqi-state architecture.

## Sudani's PM tenure 2022-2026

Sudani's tenure operated through architectural features that produced relative stability after the pre-2022 institutional crises:

**Economic stabilisation.** The Iraqi oil revenues reached roughly $115 billion in 2024 — the architectural foundation of the contemporary Iraqi apparatus. The 2023 federal budget was roughly 198.9 trillion Iraqi dinars (~$153 billion). In December 2023 the Council of Ministers approved the third oil-for-development deal with TotalEnergies. The 2024-2025 China Belt and Road expansion and the China-Iraq strategic-cooperation agreement consolidated the economic architecture.

**Gaza war non-involvement.** The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Israeli Gaza genocide architecture in Part XII Chapter 5 produced PMF Iranian-aligned operations against Israel and US targets. Sudani steered Iraq away from formal involvement in the regional confrontation despite the PMF operations. The January 2024 US strikes on pro-Iran militia operators in Iraq following the 28 January 2024 Tower 22 attack on a Jordan-Syria border base (3 US service members killed) functioned as the architectural escalation.

**US relations management.** Sudani conducted the November 2024 trip to Washington for Biden meeting. The 17 May 2025 hosting of the 34th Arab League Summit in Baghdad — the first since 2012 — served as the architectural indicator of Iraqi institutional re-emergence on the Arab political-class architecture. The 2025 US drawdown of the coalition forces under Operation Inherent Resolve transition was the architectural feature of the Sudani-US relationship.

**The 2025-2026 Iran-US war architecture.** The contemporary 2025-2026 Iran-US war architecture in Dossier 39 produced the most consequential test of the post-2003 Iraqi apparatus. The PMF — particularly Kata'ib Hezbollah (Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps proxy) and the Badr Corps — launched drone attacks and ballistic missile attacks on US targets in Iraq. Roughly 1,000 attacks were per Jerusalem Post reporting. The 24 March 2026 Sudani declaration that the Iranian-backed Iraqi militias should respond to attacks on their headquarters functioned as the architectural inflection point —  signalling Sudani's accommodation of the PMF Iranian-aligned architecture under conditions of Iranian institutional pressure.

## The November 2025 election and the post-election architecture

The 11 November 2025 parliamentary election produced the architectural reset. Sudani's Reconstruction and Development Coalition (RDC) won 46 seats — the largest bloc. Maliki's State of Law won 29 seats. With CF coalition partners, the Maliki total approached Sudani's RDC.

The post-election architecture operated under Iran-versus-US institutional pressure:

- Iran pressed the CF to deny Sudani a second term.
- Trump 2 publicly rejected the Maliki candidacy through Trump Envoy for Iraq Mark Savaya.
- The 5-month post-election stalemate served as the architectural deadlock under conditions of US-Iran competing institutional pressure.

In April 2026 both Sudani and Maliki withdrew their candidacies to end the stalemate. The subsequent architecture:

- **April 2026**: Council of Representatives elected Nizar Amedi (PUK) as President.
- **27 April 2026**: CF nominated Ali Falah Kazim al-Zaidi (a little-known banker) as PM-designate.
- **April-May 2026**: cabinet-formation process ongoing.

The al-Zaidi nomination operated as the consensus compromise under conditions of US-Iran competing institutional pressure. The architectural significance: the contemporary Iraqi architecture operates under conditions of weak prime-ministerial authority, continued PMF Iranian-aligned operations, and US-pressure architecture that rejected the Maliki candidate.

The Western analytical literature has characterised the contemporary Iraqi institutional position as the most consequential test of the post-2003 architecture — and the central institutional indicator of whether the Iranian-aligned regional architecture  consolidates in Iraq or whether the US-aligned architecture institutionally re-emerges under conditions of 2025-2026 Iranian-institutional-collapse architecture in Dossier 39.

## The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF / Al-Hashd al-Shaabi)

The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF / Al-Hashd al-Shaabi) operate as the most consequential Iranian-aligned apparatus in contemporary Iraq. The PMF was formed on 15 June 2014 in response to the ISIS capture of Mosul (10 June 2014) and Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani's 13 June 2014 fatwa calling for volunteer mobilisation against ISIS.

The PMF is composed of roughly 67 militias — predominantly Shia, with Sunni and Christian units. The November 2016 Iraqi parliamentary act integrated the PMF into the Iraqi security architecture — the architectural development that  legitimised the Iranian-aligned militias as official Iraqi state institutions.

The dominant factions:

- **Badr Organization**: led by Hadi al-Amiri; Iranian-aligned Shia Islamist political-and-military operator; institutional foundation from Badr Brigades that operated in Iran during the 1980s Iran-Iraq War.
- **Kata'ib Hezbollah**: Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps proxy; US Treasury sanctions architecture; Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis (deputy PMF commander, killed 3 January 2020 US drone strike with Soleimani) operated as founding architect.
- **Asa'ib Ahl al-Haq**: Qais al-Khazali; Iranian-aligned Shia Islamist political-and-military operator.
- **Saraya al-Salam**: Sadr-aligned Mahdi Army successor; subsequently withdrew from PMF integration.

The 3 January 2020 US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport killed Quds Force Commander Qasem Soleimani and PMF deputy commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis. The subsequent PMF operations against US targets was the governing consequence. The 2024-2026 architecture operates under Kata'ib Hezbollah characterisation by US Treasury as Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps proxy.

The contemporary 2025-2026 architecture: the PMF has been entrenched. BBC Monitoring analysis notes that several PMF elements, after the US strikes in 2026, now see the struggle with the US as "existential" and "enduring." In 2026 some members of the Shia Coordination Framework have reportedly discussed conditions for potential disarmament — but Kata'ib Hezbollah and the Iranian-aligned militias have rejected disarmament.

The outgoing Sudani had previously linked PMF disarmament to the continued US drawdown across 2025 and 2026. The architecture operates under conditions of continuing institutional tension.

## The Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG)

The Kurdistan Regional Government operates as the constitutionally autonomous region of Iraq under the 2005 Constitution — comprising the Erbil, Sulaymaniyah, and Duhok provinces. The two-party architecture: the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP, Barzani family) controls Erbil and Duhok; the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK, Talabani family) controls Sulaymaniyah.

The 25 September 2017 independence referendum produced 93% Yes vote — the Baghdad-led Kirkuk recapture (16 October 2017) and economic sanctions architecture was the consequence. The independence aspiration was suspended.

The KRG operators:

- **Masoud Barzani**: KDP leader since 1979; former KRG President 2005-2017; architect of contemporary Iraqi-Kurdish political-class architecture.
- **Masrour Barzani**: Masoud's son; KRG Prime Minister since 10 July 2019.
- **Nechirvan Barzani**: Masoud's nephew; KRG President since 10 June 2019.
- **Bafel Talabani**: Jalal Talabani's son; PUK leader; KDP-PUK rivalry under his leadership.
- **Lahur Talabani**: Bafel's cousin; PUK internal rival; 2021 internal-leadership struggle institutional consequences.

The 2024-2026 KRG architecture operates under KDP-PUK tensions over oil revenues and parliamentary representation. The 17 September 2025 ICJ ruling against the KRG over the Turkish oil pipeline architecture produced $1.4 billion Iraqi-government damages award. The contemporary KRG architecture operates under conditions of reduced autonomous-architectural authority and continued internal KDP-PUK contestation.

Masoud Barzani has been characterised across the Western analytical literature as the most consequential Iraqi-Kurdish operator and a trusted Washington partner. Barzani helped US officials mediate the de-escalation of the 2024-2025 fighting between the post-Assad Damascus architecture under Ahmed al-Sharaa and the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF, the Kurdish-aligned architecture in subsequent Middle East dossiers).

# Dossier 45: Ahmed al-Sharaa and the Post-Assad Syrian Apex Apparatus

## The Syrian operator

The Syrian Arab Republic was founded on 17 April 1946 with independence from French Mandate authority. The post-1946 architecture operated under political-class instability across the 1946-1963 period, the 8 March 1963 Ba'ath Party coup that constructed the Ba'athist architecture, and the Alawite-dominated Assad family architecture under Hafez al-Assad (President 12 March 1971 - 10 June 2000, 29 years) and Bashar al-Assad (President 17 July 2000 - 8 December 2024, 24 years).

The Assad architecture produced minority-Alawite consolidated authoritarian architecture under conditions of majority-Sunni population (roughly 70% Sunni, 12% Alawite, 10% Christian, 3% Druze, other minorities). The foundation operated through the Mukhabarat intelligence-and-repression architecture, the Sednaya Prison torture facility, the 1982 Hama massacre under Hafez (10,000-40,000 killed in suppression of Muslim Brotherhood uprising), and the 2011-2024 civil war architectural collapse.

The 2011-2024 Syrian civil war was triggered by the March 2011 Arab Spring uprising in Daraa and produced roughly 500,000+ killed and 13+ million displaced. The September 2015 Russian military intervention saved the Assad regime under conditions of Iranian-Hezbollah ground operations that consolidated the 4+1 coalition architecture (Iran, Iraq, Syria, Russia, Hezbollah) in earlier dossiers.

The 27 November - 8 December 2024 HTS-led offensive — under conditions of Iranian institutional weakening (12-day Israel-Iran war architectural antecedents), Hezbollah decimation (Nasrallah killed 27 September 2024, Safieddine killed October 2024), and Russian Ukraine-war distraction — produced the most consequential post-Cold-War regional architectural transformation. Assad fled to Moscow under Russian asylum arrangement.

## Ahmed al-Sharaa — biographical foundation

Ahmed Hussein al-Sharaa was born on 29 October 1982 in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. His family is Syrian Sunni Muslim from Daraa (the southern Syrian governorate that was the 2011 Syrian uprising founding-location) and the Golan Heights. His father was a petroleum engineer working in Saudi Arabia. The family returned to Syria in 1989; al-Sharaa was raised in Damascus.

The al-Sharaa career operated under the nom de guerre Abu Mohammad al-Julani — the pseudonym referencing the Golan Heights (al-Jawlan in Arabic) family-origin architecture. He dropped the nom de guerre in 2025 and re-assumed his birth name.

In 2003, shortly before the US invasion of Iraq, al-Sharaa travelled to Iraq and joined al-Qaeda in Iraq under Abu Musab al-Zarqawi. He fought for three years in the Iraqi insurgency. From 2006 to 2011 he was imprisoned by US forces at Camp Bucca and Abu Ghraib. The Camp Bucca imprisonment was the indicator of the post-2003 US detention architecture that institutionally networked jihadist operators including Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi (future ISIS Caliph), al-Sharaa, and other architectural figures.

The 2011 release coincided with the Syrian uprising against the Ba'athist regime.

## The militant career

In January 2012 al-Sharaa founded the Jabhat al-Nusra (al-Nusra Front) with the Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi institutional backing — al-Qaeda's Syrian affiliate. The December 2012 US State Department designation of al-Nusra as a Foreign Terrorist Organisation was the indicator of the Western architectural opposition.

The April 2013 Baghdadi unilateral announcement of merger of al-Nusra and the Islamic State of Iraq into the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIL) produced the architectural rupture. Al-Sharaa rejected the merger and affirmed his al-Qaeda allegiance. The Nusra-ISIL competition was the architectural foundation of the contemporary Syrian-jihadist architectural divide.

On 28 July 2016 al-Sharaa announced the split from al-Qaeda; the group was renamed Jabhat Fateh al-Sham. In January 2017 Jabhat Fateh al-Sham merged with other factions to form Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS) — the architectural foundation of the contemporary post-Assad Syrian apparatus.

In November 2017 HTS announced the Syrian Salvation Government (SSG) in the Idlib Governorate. The Sharaa-led Idlib consolidation built governance institutions in Idlib (roughly 3 million population): schools, hospitals, courts, civil-defence Syrian White Helmets, and economic-development architecture. The architectural significance: HTS was the structural state-building architecture under conditions of official FTO designation; the 2017-2024 Idlib architecture functioned as the pre-Damascus institutional rehearsal for the contemporary post-Assad governance.

The Turkish-HTS accommodating relationship operated across the 2017-2024 period under conditions of official Turkish FTO designation and actual operational cooperation. The Turkey-HTS architectural foundation was the architecture that produced the 27 November - 8 December 2024 offensive (in Dossier 42 — most consequential geopolitical achievement of Erdoğan tenure).

## The December 2024 HTS offensive and the Assad fall

On 27 November 2024 HTS-led "Operation Deterrence of Aggression" launched the offensive from Idlib. The architectural conditions: Iranian institutional weakening under Israel-Iran 12-day war architectural antecedents; Hezbollah decimation (Nasrallah killed 27 September 2024; Safieddine killed October 2024); Russian Ukraine-war institutional distraction. The 4+1 coalition architecture (Iran, Iraq, Syria, Russia, Hezbollah) operated under conditions of institutional inability to intervene.

The offensive timeline:

- **30 November 2024**: HTS captured Aleppo (Syria's second-largest city).
- **5 December 2024**: HTS captured Hama.
- **7 December 2024**: HTS captured Homs.
- **8 December 2024**: HTS captured Damascus; Assad fled to Moscow under Russian asylum arrangement.

The 11-day offensive from Aleppo to Damascus was the most consequential rapid-collapse architectural event in the contemporary post-Cold-War Middle East. Total HTS forces were roughly 30,000.

On 8 December 2024 al-Sharaa delivered a speech at the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus calling the Assad fall "a new chapter in the history of the region." Mohammed al-Bashir (former Prime Minister of the HTS Syrian Salvation Government in Idlib) was appointed caretaker Prime Minister of the transitional government. On 12 December 2024 al-Sharaa met the Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan in Damascus — the first foreign diplomatic delegation since the Assad fall (in Dossier 42). On 20 December 2024 the US removed the $10 million bounty on al-Sharaa after his meeting with US delegates.

## The Sharaa rise to president

On 29 January 2025 the Syrian Revolution Victory Conference was held at the People's Palace in Damascus. Al-Sharaa was appointed President of Syria for the transitional period. The architectural arrangements:

- The 2012 Constitution was suspended.
- HTS and other armed factions were formally dissolved.
- The legislative council was authorised to operate until the new constitution.
- Sharaa expected a 3-year subsequent constitution-drafting period; elections would not take place before the pre-election census.

On 13 March 2025 the Constitutional Declaration was signed, outlining a 5-year transitional period and announcing the transitional government formation.

The architectural significance: al-Sharaa was the President of a transitional architecture under conditions of contested institutional legitimacy. The Western analytical literature has characterised the arrangements as the most consequential post-2011 Arab Spring transition — comparable to the Tunisian 2011-2014 transition ( rolled back by Kais Saied 2021-) but operating under conditions of HTS-led architectural foundations rather than secular-and-Islamist coalition architectures.

## The foreign-policy architecture

### Turkey

The Turkish-aligned architectural foundation continues operating as the governing foundation of the contemporary post-Assad architecture. The 12 December 2024 Fidan visit was the first foreign diplomatic engagement. The 25 December 2024 Turkey embassy reopening in Damascus formalised the architecture. The Turkish lira is the unofficial currency in Syrian population centres; Turkish minders attend HTS meetings with foreign diplomats. The post-Assad Turkey-Sharaa close institutional relationship is the most consequential contemporary Middle East strategic alignment.

### Saudi Arabia and UAE

On 2 February 2025 al-Sharaa and Foreign Minister Asaad al-Shaibani visited Saudi Arabia and met MBS — the first foreign visit since the Assad fall. The UAE engagement: 13 April 2025 MBZ met al-Sharaa (in Dossier 41), the UAE first major Gulf engagement with post-Assad Syria. The Saudi-Egypt-UAE post-Assad accommodation architecture operated under conditions of Brotherhood-aligned official Saudi-and-UAE opposition to HTS-architectural framework — reversed under post-Iran institutional collapse architecture.

### Qatar

On 30 January 2025 Qatari Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani became the first head of state to visit Damascus since the Assad fall. The Qatar-HTS architectural foundation was the architecture across the 2017-2024 Idlib period — distinct from the Saudi-UAE Brotherhood-opposition architecture.

### Russia

On 12 February 2025 al-Sharaa held his first phone call with Putin since the Assad fall. The architectural negotiations operated under Russian retention of the Hmeimim Air Base and the Tartus Naval Facility — the architectures of Russian Mediterranean military projection. Assad operated under Russian asylum in Moscow; subsequent Sharaa demands for Assad extradition was the continuing institutional contestation.

### Iran

The Iranian architecture in Syria was eliminated under the Assad fall. The Iran-Hezbollah ground supply line through Syria was severed. The Iranian-aligned architecture dissolved across the Damascus-and-multi-state-Syrian-regional-architecture post-December-2024 framework.

### United States

The 20 December 2024 US bounty removal served as the architectural opener. In May 2025 the Trump 2 Middle East visit produced the Trump promise to lift US sanctions on Syria. The Syria-US institutional opening has functioned as the architectural pivot from US Assad-regime opposition framework to contemporary post-Assad cooperation framework.

### European Union

On 9 January 2026 EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen visited Damascus. The €620 million EU financial assistance package for 2026-2027 was announced — the most consequential institutional shift in EU-Syria relations. The subsequent EU sanctions architecture subsequent lifting was the consequence.

### Ukraine

On 6 April 2026 Volodymyr Zelensky visited Damascus — the first Ukrainian leader visit. The Syria-Ukraine diplomatic relations were re-established at the end of 2025. The architectural significance: Syria is the architectural-aligned Turkey-Ukraine strategic positioning under conditions of Russia-Iran institutional contraction.

### Israel

The 14 December 2024 onwards Israeli strikes against Syrian military assets operated as the Israeli post-Assad architectural operation. IDF operations into the Golan buffer zone and beyond the border  extended Israeli operational architecture. On 17 December 2024 al-Sharaa pledged adherence to the 1974 Disengagement of Forces Agreement that ended the Yom Kippur War. Sharaa stated that Israel had "no more excuses" for strikes; diplomatic-solutions approach.

On 16 July 2025 Itamar Ben-Gvir (Israel Minister of National Security) publicly urged al-Sharaa's assassination, characterising him as "head of the snake." The subsequent Israeli architectural framework operated under conditions of continued Israeli-Syrian tension despite Sharaa institutional pledges.

## The domestic apparatus

### Transition framework

The 13 March 2025 Constitutional Declaration outlined a 5-year transitional period. The transitional government was formed under HTS-and-multi-state-allied operators. In October 2025 the parliamentary election was held — the first since the Assad fall. The People's Assembly was formed under pre-permanent-constitution architecture.

### The SDF / Kurdish question

The Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) operate under Mazloum Abdi command. The YPG (People's Protection Units) Kurdish architectural foundation operates through the self-declared Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria (AANES). The SDF controls roughly 25% of Syrian territory in the northeast — the architectural region containing major oil fields, US-presence (anti-ISIS deployment), and PKK-linked architectural elements that Turkey  opposes.

On 10 March 2025 al-Sharaa signed an agreement with Mazloum Abdi at Damascus to integrate the SDF into state institutions. The subsequent 2025 negotiations remained inconclusive. In January 2026 the military campaign was launched against the SDF in eastern Aleppo Governorate (Deir Hafir, Maskanah) and subsequently extended to Raqqa, Deir ez-Zor, and Al-Hasakah. The 6-10 January Aleppo operation produced Syrian forces taking control of the city's Kurdish districts (Sheikh Maqsood).

The 20 and 30 January 2026 ceasefires paused the operations. On 18 January 2026 the 14-point agreement was signed:

- SDF cedes territorial control over Deir al-Zour and Raqqa provinces.
- Hasakah province retained by SDF under central-government integration.
- Kurdish-led forces become part of single Syrian armed force.
- Fighters join as individuals, not units.
- Oil fields and border crossings to state administration.
- Non-Syrian PKK fighters to leave Syria.
- Kurdish-language and Newroz holiday recognised.

The architectural significance: the Sharaa-aligned framework aligns with Turkish opposition to Kurdish-autonomous-architecture and aligns with the PKK-dissolution architecture (in Dossier 42 — February-May 2025 Öcalan-and-Bahçeli-led PKK dissolution).

### The Druze, Bedouin, and Alawite minority architectures

In July 2025 armed clashes broke out between Syrian Druze and Bedouin in Suwayda. The Syrian Army intervention produced clashes with militants; subsequent ceasefire. Sharaa criticised Israeli attacks on government forces during the operations.

The 2025 coastal-region violence was the most consequential minority-architectural crisis. The March 2025 coastal Latakia and Tartus governorate violence killed roughly 1,500 Alawites per Human Rights Watch documentation. The subsequent allegations of government-linked troop involvement was the structural indicator of the contemporary minority-architectural contestations. The subsequent accountability mechanisms operate.

### Civil-rights architecture

The Sednaya Prison — the Assad-regime torture facility — was closed. The subsequent commitments to women's empowerment, civil rights, and rule of law operate as the framework. On 18 February 2026 al-Sharaa signed his first amnesty decree for minor offenders, excluding serious violations against the Syrian people.

### Currency

On 29 December 2025 the new national currency was launched at the Conferences' Palace in Damascus. Sharaa's wife Latifa al-Droubi attended; Central Bank Governor Abdulkader Husrieh unveiled the banknotes.

## Senior other operators

The contemporary apex apparatus operates through architectural operators:

- **Asaad al-Shaibani**: Foreign Minister since March 2025.
- **Anas Khattab**: Intelligence head; subject of ISIS assassination plots.
- **Mohammed al-Bashir**: caretaker PM December 2024 - March 2025.
- **Murhaf Abu Qasra**: Defence Minister.
- **Mazloum Abdi**: SDF commander-in-chief.
- **Latifa al-Droubi**: Sharaa's wife.

## Assassination plots and security architecture

The contemporary Sharaa security architecture operates under conditions of continuing assassination plots:

- 10 November 2025: Syria thwarted 2 ISIS plots.
- 5 January 2026: IDF claimed Iran planning Sharaa assassination.
- 12 February 2026: UN Secretary-General Guterres said Sharaa, Khattab, and Shaibani were targets of 5 foiled ISIS and Saraya Ansar al-Sunnah plots.
- 6 March 2026: Turkey's MİT asked British MI6 to take a larger role in protecting Sharaa.

The Western analytical literature has characterised the contemporary security architecture as the most consequential transitional-leader-protection architecture in the contemporary Middle East.
