# Dossier 51: Abdelmadjid Tebboune and the Algerian Apex Apparatus

## The Algerian institutional design

The People's Democratic Republic of Algeria was founded on 5 July 1962 after the 8-year war of independence (1 November 1954 - 19 March 1962) against French colonial rule. The war killed roughly 1.5 million Algerians (Algerian government estimates; Western estimates 250,000-400,000) — the most consequential anti-colonial war in post-1945 African history.

The post-1962 institutional order ran under the FLN (Front de Libération Nationale) single-party framework. The principal architects: Ahmed Ben Bella (President 1962-1965, first President); Houari Boumediene (1965-1978, the 13-year military framework that consolidated the FLN order after the 19 June 1965 military coup); Chadli Bendjedid (1979-1992).

The rupture: in December 1991 the FIS (Islamic Salvation Front) won the first round of legislative elections; on 11 January 1992 the military cancelled the second round through a coup. The subsequent Algerian Civil War 1992-2002 killed roughly 150,000-200,000 — the "Black Decade" / "Sale Decade" foundation of contemporary Algerian security-and-repression policy.

The 20-year Abdelaziz Bouteflika presidency 1999-2019 was the post-Civil-War consolidation. On 22 February 2019 the Hirak (Movement) protests began after Bouteflika announced a fifth-term candidacy. On 2 April 2019 Bouteflika was forced to resign after losing military backing under Chief of Staff Ahmed Gaïd Salah. On 12 December 2019 Tebboune was elected (39.93% in first round under the Hirak boycott). On 19 December 2019 he was sworn in.

Algeria is roughly 46 million people (2024 estimate); Arab-Berber population mix; Sunni Muslim majority (~99%); Berber/Amazigh ~30% of population under Kabylie regional contestation; French-Arabic bilingual.

## Abdelmadjid Tebboune — biographical foundation

Abdelmadjid Tebboune was born on 17 November 1945 in Méchéria, Naâma Province (French Algeria). His father was a school teacher. He graduated from the École Nationale d'Administration (ENA) Algiers in 1969.

His civil-service career ran through ministerial positions. In 1991 he was Minister Delegate of Local Communities under the Mouloud Hamrouche government — the pre-coup reformist government. From 1999 to 2002 he was Minister of Communication and Culture under Bouteflika; from 2001 to 2002 Minister of Housing and Town Planning; from 2012 to 2013 Minister of Housing; from 2013 to 2017 Minister of Housing, Urban Planning and Town Planning.

On 25 May 2017 he was appointed Prime Minister by Bouteflika. On 15 August 2017 he was dismissed after 3 months — under pressure from the Algerian business elite (the Said Bouteflika faction). The Hirak-driven 12 December 2019 presidential election was the reset that brought him back.

## The first presidency 2019-2024

The first Tebboune presidency ran under Hirak contestation. Features:

**COVID-19 Berlin treatment.** On 24 October 2020 Tebboune tested positive for COVID-19 in Berlin; received treatment. The significance: Tebboune was absent from Algeria for an extended period; Chengriha consolidated control across that period.

**November 2020 constitutional referendum.** The 2020 constitutional referendum produced roughly 66.8% Yes and roughly 33.2% No on roughly 23.7% turnout — the lowest turnout in Algerian electoral history and the clearest indicator of the Hirak boycott.

**2021 Algerian legislative elections.** The 12 June 2021 elections produced FLN 105 seats, MSP 64 seats, Independents 84 seats, RND 57 seats. The FLN-RND-MSP institutional configuration continued under the Hirak boycott.

**Hirak effective ban.** From 22 February 2021 to 30 April 2021 the Hirak anniversary protests demanded Tebboune-Djerrad resignation. In May 2021 the Hirak movement was in practice banned. The significance: institutional repression of the post-2019 pro-democracy mobilisation.

**Morocco diplomatic rupture.** On 24 August 2021 Algeria severed diplomatic relations with Morocco over Western Sahara, Pegasus spyware, and Kabylie wildfires accusations. On 22 September 2021 Algeria closed airspace to Moroccan civilian and military aircraft. On 31 October 2021 Algeria refused to renew the Maghreb-Europe gas pipeline transiting Morocco. The significance: the most consequential post-1962 Algeria-Morocco rupture.

**Russian arms architecture.** Algeria is the largest African arms importer — roughly $10+ billion in total arms purchases 2015-2025 from Russia. The foundation: Algerian-Russian strategic alignment under Cold-War-era FLN continuity.

**BRICS positioning.** On 31 August 2023 Algeria's BRICS expansion application was denied alongside Argentina. In 2024 the BRICS New Development Bank authorised Algeria as a member. The significance: Algerian Global-South positioning under Russian-Chinese alignment.

## The 7 September 2024 presidential election

The 7 September 2024 presidential election was originally scheduled December 2024; Tebboune brought it forward to 7 September 2024 on 21 March 2024. The results:

- **Tebboune (Independent)**: 5.32 million votes (84.30%).
- **Abdellah Hassan Cherif (MSP)**: 904,642 (9.56%).
- **Youcef Aouchiche (FFS/Socialist Forces Front)**: 580,495 (6.14%).
- **Turnout**: 46.10% (~24.4M registered).

The significance: the election was widely characterised in Western analytical literature as a foregone conclusion under conditions of Chengriha-led military selection of Tebboune as figurehead. The National Independent Electoral Authority's published turnout figures produced contestation; all three candidates (including the Tebboune campaign) criticised them. Aouchiche and Cherif challenged the results.

On 17 September 2024 Tebboune was inaugurated for a second term.

## The military foundation under Saïd Chengriha

Lieutenant-General Saïd Chengriha has been Chief of Staff of the Algerian National People's Army (ANP) since 2 January 2020 — after the 23 December 2019 death of Ahmed Gaïd Salah (the figure who produced Bouteflika's 2019 resignation).

The military foundation: the ANP is described as the "true power broker" in Algeria across Western analytical literature. The Algerian Department of Intelligence and Security (DRS) under Chengriha operational control is the contemporary institutional backbone.

The Middle East Forum January 2026 analysis characterised the Tebboune presidency as "ceremonial figure operating under de facto guardianship imposed by Army Chief of Staff Saïd Chengriha and presidential office director Boualem Boualem." The Chengriha-Boualem Boualem duo is the most consequential post-Bouteflika institutional configuration.

## The July 2025 General Mobilization Law

In July 2025 the General Mobilization Law was signed by Tebboune. The provisions: places economy, media, and public discourse under a "state of war" logic; criminalisation of press silence; encouragement of denunciation; treatment of dissent as treason.

The significance: the General Mobilization Law is the most consequential post-Civil-War piece of repressive legislation — extending the Black Decade security framework into the contemporary apparatus.

## The Boualem Sansal arrest November 2024

In November 2024 security forces arrested French-Algerian writer Boualem Sansal. The allegations: threatened national unity through a controversial statement questioning Algeria's sovereignty over eastern territory.

The significance: a press-freedom flashpoint under the Tebboune presidency; a France-Algeria flashpoint amid 2024 Macron recognition of Moroccan Western Sahara sovereignty.

## Western Sahara

The Western Sahara conflict has run since the 1975 Spanish withdrawal. The Algerian-backed Polisario Front declared the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR) in 1976; ~165,000 Sahrawi refugees remain in Tindouf camps inside Algeria. Moroccan occupation covers roughly 80% of the territory; 1991 ceasefire under MINURSO (UN Mission for Referendum in Western Sahara).

The inflection points:

- **November 2020**: the first Trump administration recognised Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara as part of the Abraham Accords (Israel-Morocco normalisation).
- **2024**: Macron recognised Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara — France-Algeria rupture.
- **November 2024**: Crisis Group documented continuing Morocco-Algeria-backed Polisario hostilities.
- **2025**: UN Security Council Resolution 2797 — first time the UNSC recognised Morocco's autonomy proposal as "serious and credible" basis for negotiation.
- **May 2026**: Tebboune continued to re-assert the Algerian Polisario-Sahrawi position.

The significance: Western Sahara is the most consequential post-1975 territorial dispute in North Africa — the Algeria-Morocco rupture constrains all contemporary Maghreb regional cooperation.

## Foreign policy 2024-2026

The contemporary foreign policy runs through several features:

- **Italy gas relationship**: on 25 March 2024 Meloni visited Algiers; agreed Algerian gas increased deliveries to Italy. Algeria is the largest African gas supplier to Italy and Europe after the 2022 Russia-EU rupture.
- **Oil and gas licensing**: on 14 March 2026 the government announced "Algeria Bid Round 2026" oil and gas exploration licensing.
- **BRICS**: 31 August 2023 expansion application denied; 2024 NDB admission.
- **Sahel-junta opposition**: Algeria opposes the Mali-Niger-Burkina Faso juntas; Algeria opposes the Russian Africa Corps presence in the Sahel despite the Algerian-Russian arms relationship.
- **Mali contestation**: October 2024 Algeria-Mali confrontation; Bamako accused Algiers of interference in the Tuareg rebellion.
- **UN Security Council**: non-permanent member through 2025.
- **France**: 2024 Macron recognition of Moroccan Western Sahara sovereignty produced the France-Algeria rupture; the Boualem Sansal arrest deepened it.

## Cabinet and other operators

- **Sifi Ghrieb**: Prime Minister since 28 August 2025 (succeeded Larbaoui).
- **Nadir Larbaoui**: Prime Minister 11 November 2023 - 28 August 2025.
- **Boualem Boualem**: Director of Cabinet of Presidency since 11 November 2023; co-guardian with Chengriha per Middle East Forum.
- **Ahmed Attaf**: Minister of Foreign Affairs.
- **Saïd Chengriha**: Chief of Staff ANP since 2 January 2020.

## The economic context

The 2024 GDP: roughly $260 billion. The hydrocarbon economy generates roughly 95% of export revenue and roughly 60% of government revenue from oil and gas. Sonatrach (state oil company) is the centrepiece. The 2024 inflation rate was roughly 4.0%; unemployment is roughly 12-15%; youth unemployment is roughly 30%; housing shortage persistent; subsidies remain a structural feature of the budget.

# Dossier 52: Mohammed VI and the Moroccan Apex Apparatus

## Who runs iture

The Kingdom of Morocco has been ruled by the Alaouite dynasty since 1631 — the longest continuously-ruling royal house in contemporary Africa and among the longest in global institutional history. The 1912-1956 French and Spanish Protectorate period ran under colonial administration; independence was achieved on 2 March 1956 under King Mohammed V (Mohammed VI's grandfather).

The Hassan II monarchy (26 February 1961 - 23 July 1999, a 38-year authoritarian rule) produced the "Years of Lead" human-rights repression and the 6 November 1975 Green March (350,000 Moroccans walked into Western Sahara). On 23 July 1999 Mohammed VI acceded the throne after Hassan II's death from cardiac arrest.

The 2011 constitutional reform under the 20 February Movement (Arab Spring) reshaped the Moroccan constitutional framework — although king-dominated executive power persisted. The 2011 Constitution requires the king to appoint the Prime Minister from the largest party in parliament; bicameral Parliament operates with 395-seat House of Representatives (elected) and 120-seat Council of Councillors (indirectly elected). The king also chairs the Supreme Council of the Judiciary and operates as Commander of the Faithful (Amir al-Mu'minin, religious authority).

Morocco is roughly 37 million people (2024 estimate); Arab-Berber ethnic mix (~65% Arab, ~30% Berber/Amazigh); Sunni Muslim majority (~99%); Tamazight became official second language under the 2011 Constitution; French and Arabic bilingual administration.

## Mohammed VI — biographical foundation

Mohammed VI of Morocco was born Sidi Mohammed on 21 August 1963 in Rabat. His father was Hassan II (King 1961-1999); his mother was Lalla Latifa Hammou. He is the eldest son of Hassan II. He was educated at Mohammed V University, Rabat (Law) and the University of Nice, France (PhD International Law 1993).

In 1985 he was appointed Coordinator of Royal Armed Forces General Staff Offices. On 12 July 1994 he was promoted to Major General by Hassan II. On 23 July 1999 he acceded the throne after Hassan II's death.

On 21 March 2002 Mohammed VI married Salma Bennani (commoner, computer engineer) — a consequential break from dynastic-marriage tradition; Salma became Lalla Salma, first publicly-acknowledged consort of a Moroccan monarch. The couple has two children: Crown Prince Moulay Hassan (born 8 May 2003) and Princess Lalla Khadija (born 28 February 2007). In February 2018 the couple separated formally; Salma Bennani has not been seen in public since.

## The early reign 1999-2010

On 30 July 1999 Mohammed VI delivered his first throne speech committing to rule of law and human rights — the public break from the Hassan II "Years of Lead" period.

Features of the early reign:

**Equity and Reconciliation Commission.** On 4 February 2004 the Equity and Reconciliation Commission (IER) was established to investigate Hassan II "Years of Lead" human-rights abuses — the most consequential post-Hassan II transitional-justice mechanism in contemporary North African institutional history.

**Family Code (Mudawana) reforms.** On 5 February 2004 the Family Code reforms were enacted — landmark women's rights reforms across the Maghreb: raised marriage age, restricted polygamy, expanded divorce rights, joint family responsibility.

**16 May 2003 Casablanca bombings.** The 16 May 2003 Casablanca bombings (~45 killed) produced the subsequent crackdown and Anti-Terrorism Law.

## The 20 February Movement and 2011 constitutional reform

On 20 February 2011 the 20 February Movement protests began as part of the Arab Spring. On 9 March 2011 Mohammed VI announced constitutional reforms in a royal address. On 1 July 2011 the 2011 Constitution referendum produced 98.5% Yes on 90% turnout (figures questioned in Western analytical literature).

The constitutional reforms:

- Tamazight (Berber) recognised as official language.
- PM from largest party.
- Strengthened parliament.
- Independent judiciary provisions.

**BUT**: the king retained power to dissolve the legislature, rule by decree, and dismiss or appoint cabinet. The significance: the 2011 reforms preserved king-dominated executive power under a reformist veneer.

The 25 November 2011 elections produced PJD (Islamist Justice and Development Party) victory. Abdelilah Benkirane served as PM 2012-2017; Saadeddine Othmani 2017-2021. On 8 September 2021 the PJD was trounced; RNI (Rally of Independents) and Istiqlal won. On 7 October 2021 Aziz Akhannouch (RNI) was appointed PM.

## The Hirak Rif protests 2016-2017

On 28 October 2016 fish-vendor Mouhcine Fikri was crushed in a garbage truck in Al Hoceima — the incident that sparked the Hirak Rif protests. The Rif region's long-standing socio-economic grievances (marginalisation of the Berber-majority Rif region by the Moroccan state) were the foundation.

The 2016-2017 protests demanded development and public services. In 2018 leaders were convicted; Nasser Zefzafi was sentenced to 20 years. In November 2024 Mohammed VI pardoned roughly 5,000 cannabis farmers and 708 other detainees — BUT Hirak Rif activists serving up to 20 years were NOT included.

## Western Sahara

The Western Sahara conflict has run since the 1975 Spanish withdrawal. The Polisario Front, Algerian-aligned, declared the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR) in 1976 from Tindouf, Algeria. The 6 September 1991 ceasefire under MINURSO (UN Mission for Referendum in Western Sahara) suspended active armed conflict.

The contemporary inflection points:

- **November 2020**: the first Trump administration recognised Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara as part of the Abraham Accords (Israel-Morocco normalisation 10 December 2020).
- **13 November 2020**: Guerguerat border crossing clashes; Polisario declared the ceasefire ended; 2020-present Western Saharan clashes resumed.
- **7 April 2022**: Spain joined recognising Morocco's autonomy plan as "most serious, realistic and credible" basis (PM Pedro Sánchez letter to Mohammed VI).
- **30 July 2024**: France's Macron letter to Mohammed VI: "the present and future of Western Sahara fall within the framework of Moroccan sovereignty" and Morocco's autonomy plan is "the only basis" for a just and lasting political solution — the most consequential post-2020 recognition.
- **October 2025**: UN Security Council Resolution 2797 — first time the UNSC recognised Morocco's autonomy proposal as "serious and credible" basis for negotiation; renewed MINURSO mandate.
- **December 2025**: Akhannouch hailed the UN resolution as "major turning point" and "fair opportunity."

The consequences: roughly 30+ countries have opened consulates in Laayoune and Dakhla — the clearest indicator of pro-Morocco diplomatic alignment. The $1.2 billion Dakhla Atlantic Port is the infrastructure consolidation. The Algerian-backed Polisario position now operates from increasing isolation.

## The Abraham Accords

On 10 December 2020 Israel and Morocco normalised diplomatic relations under US-brokered Abraham Accords — in exchange for the US Western Sahara sovereignty recognition. The features:

- Israel-Morocco intelligence, security, and defence cooperation.
- November 2021: Israeli Defense Minister Benny Gantz visited Rabat — first official defence visit.
- Pegasus spyware allegations (NSO Group Israeli sale to Morocco for surveillance use).
- October 2023 Gaza war: Moroccan pro-Palestine public protests; the Moroccan public's long-standing pro-Palestine position operates against palace Israel-Morocco consolidation.

## The 8 September 2023 Al Haouz earthquake

On 8 September 2023, 23:11 local time, a 6.8 magnitude earthquake struck the High Atlas Mountains. The epicentre was Al Haouz Province, southwest of Marrakesh. The consequences:

- **~2,946 killed**.
- **~5,674 injured**.
- **~50,000+ buildings damaged**.
- Hundreds of thousands of survivors displaced.

Mohammed VI was initially in Paris; he returned to Morocco roughly 24 hours after the earthquake. The subsequent 13.7 billion EUR reconstruction plan was announced. Morocco initially declined international assistance offers (Spain, France, UK accepted; others were declined).

Subsequent allegations of fund mismanagement, bribery, and favouritism in reconstruction contracts surfaced — the indicator of contemporary Moroccan corruption patterns. In January 2025 activist Said Ait Mahdi was sentenced to 3 months imprisonment for protesting the government's earthquake response — a press-freedom flashpoint.

## The 27 September - 18 October 2025 Gen Z 212 protests

From 27 September to 18 October 2025 the Gen Z 212 protests were the most consequential post-2011 youth-led mobilisation. The causes: social discontent, public services deterioration, social inequality, youth unemployment, corruption, and 2030 FIFA World Cup stadium spending.

The features:

- Slogan: "Stadiums are here, but where are the hospitals?"
- Decentralised leadership.
- Discord and TikTok organising.
- Mohammed VI criticised the government's focus, calling Morocco a "two-speed country."

The outcomes: increased health and education budgets, financial incentives for young political candidates, construction and renovation of hospitals.

The significance: the Moroccan Gen Z mobilisation aligns with Kenya (Dossier 49), Nigeria (Dossier 48), and Bangladesh Gen Z protest waves — the contemporary Global-South youth-protest pattern.

## The 2030 FIFA World Cup co-hosting

In October 2023 FIFA confirmed the Morocco-Spain-Portugal joint bid (with 3 South American matches in Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay — centenary commemoration). The bid requires massive stadium and infrastructure expenditure. The 2025 Gen Z protests centered partly on expropriations, stadium spending considered non-essential, and stray-dog culling controversy.

## Aziz Akhannouch and the cabinet

Aziz Akhannouch has been Prime Minister since 7 October 2021 — he heads the Akwa Group oil-and-gas business. He is reportedly worth $1.5 billion (early 2023) — second-richest person in Morocco after the king. He is RNI President. The cabinet operates under king-led direction.

## The Mohammed VI personal wealth

Mohammed VI is reportedly worth $5.7-8.2 billion (Forbes estimates) — the most consequential personal-wealth figure in contemporary North Africa. The Royal Holding structure is the privately-owned royal-business framework; SNI (Société Nationale d'Investissement) and the subsequent Al Mada structure are the structural royal-business vehicles.

Maroc Telecom, Attijariwafa Bank, OCP (Office Chérifien des Phosphates), and other royal-business holdings consolidate the king's economic position.

## Other operators

- **Crown Prince Moulay Hassan**: born 8 May 2003; successor.
- **Prince Moulay Rachid**: Mohammed VI's brother (born 1970); royal-business figure.
- **Nasser Bourita**: Minister of Foreign Affairs since 2017.
- **Abdellatif Loudiyi**: Minister Delegate of National Defence Administration.
- **Mohammed Berrid**: Chief of Staff Royal Moroccan Armed Forces since 2023.
- **Abdellatif Hammouchi**: Director General of National Security (DGSN, DGST).

## Foreign policy

The France, Spain, US strategic alignment is the foundation of contemporary Moroccan foreign policy. The 2017 Moroccan re-joining of the African Union (after the 1984 withdrawal over Western Sahara) is the foundation of contemporary Moroccan African engagement.

The 2024 France-Morocco alignment under Macron's 30 July 2024 Western Sahara recognition is the most consequential recent inflection. The Spain-Morocco alignment under 2022 Sánchez recognition operates alongside.

The Gulf states (Saudi Arabia, UAE) alignment is the regional foundation. The 2020 Abraham Accords with Israel is the regional pivot. The Algeria contestation is the most consequential regional rivalry.

## The economic context

The 2024 GDP: roughly $157 billion. Tourism, phosphates, agriculture, textiles, and automotive are the principal sectors. OCP (Office Chérifien des Phosphates) controls 70%+ of global phosphate reserves — the most consequential strategic mineral.

The automotive sector under Renault and PSA/Stellantis manufacturing is the contemporary diversification base. The 2024 inflation rate was roughly 1-3%; unemployment is roughly 12-14%; youth unemployment is roughly 30%. The $1.2 billion Dakhla Atlantic Port and the Tanger-Med port complex are the trade-infrastructure anchors. The Morocco-Nigeria Atlantic Gas Pipeline is a proposed long-term project.

---

# Dossier 53: The Sahel Juntas — Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso under the Goïta-Tiani-Traoré Architecture

## The structure behind the performanceure

Mali, Niger, and Burkina Faso operate as the three Sahel coup-state cluster under the contemporary Alliance of Sahel States (AES) Confederation framework. All three are former French colonies that achieved independence in 1960: Mali on 22 September 1960 (after dissolution of the Mali Federation with Senegal); Niger on 3 August 1960; Burkina Faso on 5 August 1960 (as Upper Volta — renamed Burkina Faso ("Land of Upright People") on 4 August 1984 under Thomas Sankara). The post-1960 institutional order ran under the French Françafrique framework: CFA franc monetary architecture, French military presence, French Treasury reserve-deposit arrangement, and French-aligned political class.

ECOWAS founding membership (1975 Lagos Treaty) and Sahel G5 founding membership (2014: Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Mauritania) embedded the three states in a Western-aligned regional framework. The French Operation Serval (11 January 2013 - 1 August 2014) and Operation Barkhane (1 August 2014 - 15 August 2022) ran at ~$1+ billion annual cost and ~5,100 French troops across the Mali-Burkina-Niger counter-insurgency theatre. The UN MINUSMA peacekeeping mission (2013-2023) was the most consequential UN peacekeeping mission — roughly 13,000 personnel; roughly 300 peacekeepers killed (deadliest UN mission in contemporary history).

## The coup sequence

The inflection points:

- **18 August 2020**: Mali — Assimi Goïta deposed President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta (IBK).
- **24 May 2021**: Mali — Goïta second coup against transitional civilian leadership (Bah N'Daw and Moctar Ouane).
- **24 January 2022**: Burkina Faso — Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba deposed Roch Marc Christian Kaboré.
- **30 September 2022**: Burkina Faso — Ibrahim Traoré counter-coup against Damiba.
- **26 July 2023**: Niger — General Abdourahamane Tiani deposed President Mohamed Bazoum.

The significance: the 2020-2023 coup wave is the most consequential post-Cold-War regional transformation in contemporary West Africa — driven by French withdrawal, Islamist insurgency, Russian Wagner alignment, and pan-African youth mobilisation.

## Assimi Goïta — biographical foundation

Assimi Goïta was born in 1983 in Kati, Mali. He is Fulani ethnicity. He was educated at the Joint Military School Koulikoro and trained by US special operations under US Africa Command (AFRICOM). The significance: Goïta is a US-trained special-operations officer who came up under the pre-2020 US-aligned framework.

His Malian Armed Forces special-operations career ran through command of the Autonomous Special Forces Battalion (BAFS) and command of Anti-Terrorism Special Forces. He served alongside Operation Barkhane under the French-led counter-insurgency umbrella.

On 18 August 2020 (age 37) Goïta led the coup deposing IBK. On 21 September 2020 Bah N'Daw was appointed transitional President; Goïta served as Vice President. On 24 May 2021 Goïta executed the second coup against N'Daw and Moctar Ouane. On 7 June 2021 the Supreme Court declared Goïta President. On 23 July 2024 Goïta was legitimised under National Transition Charter; his tenure was extended by 5 years. On 6 July 2024 he became first AES rotating presidency chair. On 24 December 2025 he handed the AES presidency to Traoré.

## Abdourahamane Tiani — biographical foundation

Abdourahamane Tiani was born roughly 8 November 1960 (some sources 1962) in Filingué Department, Niger. He is Hausa ethnicity. He was educated at the Joint Military School Niamey, trained by US special operations, and trained at the ECOWAS Conflict Resolution Training Centre. The significance: Tiani is also a US-trained officer who came up under the pre-2023 US-aligned framework.

His Nigerien Armed Forces career ran under his 2011 appointment as Commander of Presidential Guard under Mahamadou Issoufou. From 2018 to 2023 he commanded the Presidential Guard. The Bazoum-Tiani contestation over Bazoum's Presidential Guard dissolution announcement was the inflection that produced the 26 July 2023 Tiani-led Presidential Guard coup against Bazoum.

On 28 July 2023 Tiani became CNSP (National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland) President; self-appointed Head of State. On 25 March 2024 the Charter for the Transition was signed — 3-year transition period. On 26 March 2025 General Tiani was inaugurated President; 5-year transition extended.

## Ibrahim Traoré — biographical foundation

Ibrahim Traoré was born on 14 March 1988 in Kéra, Bondokuy department, Mouhoun Province, Burkina Faso. He is Mossi ethnicity. He is Christian (Catholic). He was educated at the University of Ouagadougou (BSc Geology) and the Georges-Namoano Military Academy. He joined the Burkina Faso Army in 2009; Captain by 2020.

His military career ran through Mali War deployment and UN MINUSMA peacekeeping participation in 2018. He participated in the 2014 Compaoré-ouster events. He was a member of the "Cobra" Special Forces Unit (disputed across Western analytical literature).

On 22 January 2022 he participated in the Damiba coup against Kaboré. On 30 September 2022 (age 34) he executed the counter-coup against Damiba — making him the youngest head of state in the contemporary world at the time of his ascent. On 21 October 2022 he was inaugurated President. On 22 May 2024 the national dialogue extended Traoré's 5-year tenure to July 2029. On 24 December 2025 he assumed the AES rotating presidency from Goïta.

## The Sankara-influenced framework

The Thomas Sankara (Burkina Faso President 4 August 1983 - 15 October 1987) revolutionary record is the political foundation of the contemporary Traoré project. The Sankara features: pan-African anti-imperialism; economic self-reliance; opposition to French Françafrique arrangements; women's emancipation and literacy programmes.

On 15 October 1987 Blaise Compaoré executed the coup that assassinated Sankara. The Compaoré 27-year authoritarian rule (1987-2014) was the opposite of the Sankara project. On 31 October 2014 Compaoré was ousted by mass uprising. On 27 April 2022 Compaoré was convicted of the 1987 Sankara assassination; life imprisonment in absentia.

The Traoré presidency invokes the Sankara legacy: red beret (Sankara signature), anti-imperialist rhetoric, pan-African framing. Tricontinental Institute and Modern Diplomacy analyses characterise Traoré as the most consequential post-Sankara revolutionary figure in contemporary Africa.

## The French withdrawal sequence

The French Françafrique collapse runs through the following inflections:

- **2 February 2022**: Mali expelled French Ambassador.
- **18 February 2022**: Macron announced France-led withdrawal from Mali; Operation Barkhane ended.
- **15 August 2022**: final French troops withdrew from Mali.
- **23 January 2023**: Burkina Faso terminated 1961 military cooperation agreement with France.
- **February 2023**: Burkina Faso expelled French forces.
- **30 June 2023**: MINUSMA mandate ended; UN peacekeeping mission withdrawal.
- **19 February 2024**: Niger withdrew from US base agreement.
- **16 September 2024**: last US troops left Niger.
- **22 December 2024**: last French troops left Côte d'Ivoire.
- **2 January 2025**: last French troops left Chad.
- **13 February 2025**: last French troops left Senegal.

The significance: the 2022-2025 French withdrawal is the most consequential post-Cold-War French Françafrique collapse. The $110 million US Air Base 201 in Agadez (constructed under the Obama-Trump-Biden US counter-terrorism programme) was vacated. The ~1,000+ US military personnel in Niger were withdrawn.

## The Russian Wagner / Africa Corps replacement

The Wagner Group mercenary outfit under Yevgeny Prigozhin operated across the 2014-2023 period. The inflection: December 2021 Wagner began operations in Mali under the Goïta junta — the clearest indicator of Russian-aligned replacement of French presence.

On 23 August 2023 Prigozhin died in a plane crash in Russia under suspicious circumstances (consequence of the Wagner Rebellion). Post-2023 the Russian Ministry of Defence absorbed Wagner under the Africa Corps (Afrikanskiy Korpus) framework. Africa Corps operates across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Central African Republic, Libya, Sudan, and Equatorial Guinea.

On 25 July 2024 the Tinzaouaten ambush killed roughly 80 Wagner mercenaries by JNIM and Tuareg rebels — the most consequential Wagner loss in African history.

The 25 April 2026 Mali offensives began under coordinated JNIM and Azawad Liberation Front attacks; Africa Corps casualties: 1 Mil Mi-35 helicopter (with crew and mobile fire group killed), 1,000+ killed per Africa Corps assessments. The significance: the 2026 Mali offensives test the viability of the Africa Corps presence under continuing Tuareg-rebellion contestation.

## The Alliance of Sahel States (AES)

The AES Confederation Treaty is the most consequential post-Cold-War West African regional consolidation. The sequence:

- **16 September 2023**: AES founding agreement signed in Bamako — initially a defensive alliance.
- **28 January 2024**: Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso announced ECOWAS withdrawal.
- **May 2024 Niamey meeting**: draft text creating AES finalised.
- **6 July 2024**: AES Confederation Treaty signed at first summit Niamey — Niamey Declaration with 25-point communiqué; 1-year rotating presidency; Goïta first chair.
- **15 December 2024**: ECOWAS granted 6-month withdrawal-period extension.
- **29 January 2025**: ECOWAS withdrawal formalised.
- **22 February 2025**: AES flag launched.
- January 2025: joint parliament creation work began.
- **23 December 2025**: AES second summit Bamako.
- **24 December 2025**: Traoré assumed AES rotating presidency from Goïta.
- **27 December 2025**: AES unified military force launched.
- Early 2026: joint AES passport introduced.
- January-May 2026: autonomous financial-infrastructure construction; AES single-currency ambition (post-CFA franc).
- May 2026: vision of federation of the three states.

The significance: AES is the most consequential post-Cold-War African regional alternative to the ECOWAS Western-aligned framework. The 27 December 2025 unified military force launch and the joint AES passport are the governing foundations of contemporary AES consolidation.

## The Russian and alternative partnerships

The contemporary AES framework operates through Russian, Turkish, Chinese, and Iranian partnerships:

- **Russia**: Burkina Faso-Russia Agreement at St. Petersburg International Economic Forum (Rosatom nuclear power plant, regulations, training, Rosatom scholarships). Mali-Russia Agreement (nuclear infrastructure, safety regulations, training, medicine, agriculture, industry).
- **Turkey**: Turkish FM, Defense, Energy ministers visited Niger for new deals — Turkish Sahel engagement.
- **China**: Belt and Road engagement across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger.
- **Iran**: 2024-2025 engagement with the pre-2026 Iranian institutional framework (documented in Dossier 39).

The significance: AES has pivoted from the French-aligned framework to a Russian-Turkish-Chinese-Iranian-aligned framework under contemporary multipolar conditions.

## The 2024-2026 picture

The contemporary picture runs through several features:

- Mali GDP 2024: roughly $18 billion; population roughly 22.5 million.
- Burkina Faso GDP 2024: roughly $20 billion; population roughly 23.5 million.
- Niger GDP 2024: roughly $17 billion; population roughly 26.2 million.
- CFA franc monetary arrangement under French Treasury reserve deposits.
- Mali gold (3rd-largest in Africa); Niger uranium (5-7% global supply); Burkina Faso gold and cotton.
- French Areva/Orano Niger uranium contestation; subsequent Russian Rosatom replacement.
- 2024-2026 Islamist insurgency contestation under JNIM, ISGS, ISWAP.
- 2024-2026 Tuareg-rebellion contestation in northern Mali.
- 2025-2026 Nigeria and Côte d'Ivoire ECOWAS opposition to the juntas.
- 2025-2026 Algeria opposition to AES (under the post-1992 Algerian framework).

## The pan-African popular framework

The Ibrahim Traoré presidency is the most consequential post-Sankara revolutionary figure under the contemporary pan-African popular framework. The features:

- Traoré's Sankara-influenced posture.
- Online memes, videos, film tributes across the African diaspora.
- Pan-African youth support across the African continent.
- Black Agenda Report, Tricontinental, Peoples Dispatch, and Modern Diplomacy coverage characterising Traoré as the most consequential contemporary African revolutionary figure.

The significance: Traoré's popularity is the indicator of contemporary pan-African youth mobilisation — alongside the Kenyan, Nigerian, Bangladesh, and Moroccan Gen Z protest waves (documented in Dossiers 47-52).

# Dossier 54: Sudan Civil War, Democratic Republic of the Congo, and the African Union — Closing the Africa Block

## The governing apparatusure

The three architectures operate together as the central institutional indicators of contemporary continental African architectural framework. The contemporary Sudan civil war is the world's largest displacement and hunger crisis. The contemporary DRC crisis is the most consequential post-Mobutu conflict-mineral-extraction architectural framework. The contemporary African Union institutional consolidation operates under external-funding-dependency architectural framework. The dossier closes the Africa regional block of the manuscript.

## PART A: SUDAN

### Sudan institutional foundation

The Republic of Sudan was founded on 1 January 1956 (independence from Anglo-Egyptian Condominium). The post-1956 architecture operated through the First Sudanese Civil War (1955-1972), the Second Sudanese Civil War (1983-2005) under Omar al-Bashir, and the Bashir presidency 30 June 1989 - 11 April 2019 (30 years) — the most consequential post-Cold-War Sudanese authoritarian framework.

The 2003-2020 Darfur conflict killed roughly 300,000 and displaced roughly 2.5 million under Bashir-mobilised Janjaweed Arab militia architectural framework. The 2009 ICC arrest warrant against Bashir for war crimes and the 2010 second warrant for genocide produced architectural inflection: Bashir operated as first sitting head of state indicted by ICC.

On 9 July 2011 South Sudan achieved independence after the 2005 Comprehensive Peace Agreement. On 11 April 2019 Bashir was deposed by military amid popular uprising. On 21 August 2019 the Sovereignty Council was formed under transitional architecture; Burhan Chairman, Hemedti Deputy. On 25 October 2021 Burhan seized full control via coup; Hemedti retained as Deputy.

### Burhan and Hemedti — biographical foundations

Abdel Fattah al-Burhan was born in 1960 in Gandato, Sudan. He operates as SAF career officer; Inspector General of SAF. He commanded Sudanese forces deployed to Yemen 2015-2019 alongside Saudi-led coalition under Bashir-era US-Saudi-aligned architectural framework. On 11 April 2019 he led SAF role in Bashir overthrow. On 21 August 2019 he became Chairman of Sovereignty Council. On 25 October 2021 he executed full coup. From April 2023 to present he operates as SAF commander in civil war.

Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (known as "Hemedti") was born in 1974 in Chad-Darfur border region. He is Rizeigat Arab (Mahariya clan). He operated as Janjaweed militia commander during 2003-2020 Darfur genocide architectural participation. In 2013 Janjaweed was institutionally restructured into Rapid Support Forces (RSF) under Bashir framework with Hemedti as commander. The RSF gold-mining revenue architecture in Darfur (Jebel Amer goldfield primary architectural foundation) produced Hemedti financial independence — the architectural foundation of RSF parallel-state architectural framework.

From 2017 to 2019 Hemedti deployed RSF forces to Yemen alongside Saudi coalition. On 11 April 2019 he led RSF role in Bashir overthrow. On 3 June 2019 RSF executed Khartoum massacre — roughly 120 pro-democracy protesters killed at sit-in. On 21 August 2019 he became Deputy of Sovereignty Council. From April 2023 to present he operates as RSF commander.

### 15 April 2023 civil war outbreak and escalation

The tensions over integration of RSF into SAF produced architectural inflection. On 15 April 2023 explosions in Khartoum operated as architectural opening; SAF and RSF accused each other of firing first. The external architectural framework was activated: UAE-Egypt-Saudi-Iran-Russia-Turkey-Qatar architectural contestation.

In June 2023 West Darfur Governor Khamis Abakar was assassinated by RSF militants after accusing RSF of renewed genocidal attacks against minorities in Darfur. In October 2023 Iran renewed diplomatic ties with Sudan after rupture; Iran provided SAF with attack drones. In July 2024 Emirati passports were discovered in plane wreckage linked to RSF per UN Security Council documents leaked.

### 2025 escalation architecture

- **January 2025**: Biden administration sanctioned 7 UAE-based companies funding Hemedti.
- **February 2025**: Russia-Sudan Red Sea naval base deal confirmed by Sudan FM.
- **May 2025**: Amnesty International found UAE sending advanced Chinese-made Norinco guided bombs and howitzers into Darfur.
- **August 2025**: UN-backed IPC declared famine in displacement camps outside El Fasher.
- **October 2025**: SAF-backed government announced Saudi Arabia planned $50 billion additional investment in Sudan on top of $35 billion already invested; US intelligence agencies reported UAE increased Chinese drones and weapons supply to RSF per Wall Street Journal.
- **Late October 2025**: El Fasher fell to RSF after 500+ day siege.
- **30 October 2025**: Hemedti admitted "some violations" against civilians during El Fasher capture in 25-minute Telegram video.

### 2026 escalation architecture

- **Early January 2026**: RSF seized strategic Heglig oilfield; threatened Sudan economy.
- **January 2026**: SAF responded with airstrikes on RSF positions in Darfur; 100+ civilian deaths in week from market bombings and clinic attacks.
- **2026**: armoured corps defence in south Khartoum.
- **April 2026**: Sudan war entered 4th year unabated.
- functional partition: RSF west, SAF east, central zones fluid.

### Casualty architecture

- 150,000+ deaths estimated overall.
- 8.6 million internally displaced.
- 3 million refugees.
- roughly 12 million total displaced per UN OCHA early 2026.
- 15,000 bodies exhumed in Khartoum since April 2024.
- systematic gender-based violence epidemic; RSF-controlled areas rapes.
- ethnic cleansing of non-Arab communities in Darfur.
- cholera outbreaks in camps worsened by rainy season floods.

### External architecture support

**RSF backers**:
- UAE: primary external backer; weapons, financing, Chinese-made Norinco bombs and howitzers, Chinese drones.
- Russian Wagner remnants (Africa Corps) via Central African Republic networks.
- Libya Khalifa Haftar (Russia-backed) supplied military supplies.

**SAF backers**:
- Egypt: warplanes and pilots; GERD-aligned Ethiopia opposition.
- Saudi Arabia: $35 billion invested plus $50 billion planned; Iran-containment architectural framework.
- Qatar, Turkey.
- Iran: attack drones; diplomatic ties renewed October 2023.
- Russia: Sudan Red Sea naval base deal February 2025; diplomatic and military support.

### Mediation collapse architecture

- US-Saudi Arabia Jeddah negotiations: more than dozen failed ceasefires.
- African Union mediation collapsed.
- Sudan suspended IGAD (Intergovernmental Authority on Development) membership 2024 over outreach to Hemedti.
- "Hope Government" formed in Port Sudan under SAF architecture in 2025.
- UN Security Council seat retention.

The architectural significance: the contemporary Sudan civil war is the world's largest displacement and hunger crisis, the most consequential post-Cold-War African armed conflict outside DRC and Ethiopia Tigray War, and the most consequential post-2003 external-architectural contestation. The UAE-Saudi-Egypt-Iran-Russia-Turkey-Qatar architectural framework produces 4-year contemporary war architectural framework.

## PART B: DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF THE CONGO

### DRC institutional foundation

The Democratic Republic of the Congo was founded on 30 June 1960 (independence from Belgium). On 17 January 1961 Patrice Lumumba was assassinated under Belgian-CIA architectural framework — the most consequential post-1945 colonial-architectural assassination.

The Mobutu Sese Seko 32-year authoritarian framework (1965-1997, the Zaire architectural era) consolidated Cold-War-aligned kleptocratic architectural framework. In May 1997 Laurent-Désiré Kabila overthrew Mobutu; country renamed back to DRC.

The 1996-1997 First Congo War and 1998-2003 Second Congo War killed roughly 5.4 million per International Rescue Committee (IRC) estimates — the most consequential post-WWII armed conflict in global institutional history. In January 2001 Laurent Kabila was assassinated; Joseph Kabila succeeded. The Joseph Kabila 2001-2019 architectural framework produced mineral-extraction architectural framework.

On 24 January 2019 Félix Tshisekedi was inaugurated President — the first peaceful transfer of power in DRC institutional history. On 9 January 2024 Tshisekedi was re-elected for second term.

### Tshisekedi and Kagame — biographical foundations

Félix Tshisekedi was born on 13 June 1963 in Léopoldville (Kinshasa). His father Étienne Tshisekedi was opposition leader to Mobutu and Kabila architectural frameworks. He lived in Belgium for exile period and returned to DRC in 2011. He led Union for Democracy and Social Progress (UDPS).

On30 December 2018 he was declared winner amid fraud allegations against Martin Fayulu (Catholic Church Observation Mission documented Fayulu actually won).

Paul Kagame was born on 23 October 1957 in Gitarama, Rwanda. Tutsi ethnicity. He spent Uganda exile under Idi Amin and Obote architectures. He operated as Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF) founding architect. During April-July 1994 Rwandan genocide roughly 800,000+ Tutsi were killed by Hutu Power architecture. In July 1994 RPF military victory ended genocide; Kagame became Vice President and Minister of Defence. In 2000 he became President. Re-elected 2003, 2010, 2017 with 93-99% margins. On 15 July 2024 re-elected with 99.18% — most consequential indicator of Rwandan authoritarian architecture.

### M23 architecture

The March 23 Movement operates under Tutsi-aligned architecture; Rwandan-backed (Rwanda denies). Sequence:

- 2012: M23 seized Goma temporarily.
- 2013: defeated; dormant period.
- 2022: resurgence under Rwandan support architecture.
- 2024: continuation under Bertrand Bisimwa leadership and Corneille Nangaa Congo River Alliance (AFC) integration.

### 2024-2025 M23 offensives

- October 2024: M23 resumed offensive after pause.
- **23-30 January 2025 Goma offensive**: M23 seized Goma (capital North Kivu, 1M population); 4,000-7,000 Rwandan soldiers participated.
- 2,000+ FARDC soldiers and police surrendered; 20 UN peacekeepers killed; 2 mercenaries killed and 280+ Romanian mercenaries captured.
- 28 January 2025: M23 captured Goma International Airport.
- **February 2025 Bukavu offensive**: South Kivu provincial capital seized.
- 2,300+ Congolese National Police defected.
- 2025 Walikale offensive: M23 advanced into Walikale (North Kivu).
- EU sanctioned Bertrand Bisimwa and 3 Rwandan military commanders plus Francis Kamanzi (Rwanda Mines, Petroleum and Gas Board CEO) for conflict mineral exploitation and illegal gold importation from M23-controlled areas.

### Diplomatic mediation

- 2022 AU invited Angola João Lourenço to mediate.
- July 2024 Luanda ceasefire (4 August effective); collapsed within weeks.
- December 2024 Kagame failed to attend Luanda peace meeting; Angola mediation halted.
- **18 March 2025 Tshisekedi-Kagame first direct meeting since M23 escalation hosted in Doha, Qatar**; called for immediate and unconditional ceasefire.
- M23 representatives pulled out of Angola talks after EU sanctions.

### Conflict-mineral architecture

DRC operates as world's largest cobalt producer (~70% global supply); coltan, gold, copper, lithium extraction architecture. Multi-decade Rwanda-DRC Tutsi-Hutu architectural contestation operates under conditions of DRC mineral extraction. M23 Rubaya coltan smuggling to Rwanda produces Rwandan economic-extraction architecture (Kagame denies).

At Munich Security Conference Tshisekedi decried Western powers for failing to sanction Rwanda. The Chinese mining company architectural framework operates under conditions of Tshisekedi 2024-2025 Saudi-investment-attraction architecture. **2024 Future Minerals Forum**: Saudi Arabia announced $15 billion mining stakes commitment globally including DRC.

Population architecture: roughly 7 million humanitarian crisis affected; 3.5+ million children affected per UN; 200+ armed groups vying for territorial control in eastern DRC.

The architectural significance: contemporary DRC institutional crisis is the most consequential post-Mobutu conflict-mineral-extraction architectural framework — the Rwandan, Chinese, Western, Saudi, and Russian architectural frameworks jointly constitute contemporary DRC extractive-architectural contestation.

## PART C: AFRICAN UNION

### African Union institutional foundation

The African Union was founded on 9 July 2002 (Durban Summit) — successor to Organisation of African Unity (OAU, founded 25 May 1963). AU operates with 55 member states. AU Commission Chairperson serves 4-year terms.

Multi-decade Chairperson sequence:
- 2003-2008: Alpha Oumar Konaré (Mali).
- 2008-2012: Jean Ping (Gabon).
- 2012-2017: Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma (South Africa).
- 2017-2025: Moussa Faki Mahamat (Chad).
- **15 February 2025: Mahmoud Ali Youssouf elected** at 38th AU Assembly (defeated Raila Odinga among others — see Dossier 49).

### Mahmoud Ali Youssouf biographical foundation

Mahmoud Ali Youssouf was born in 1965 in Djibouti. He operated as Djiboutian Foreign Minister 2005-2025 (20 years — longest-serving FM architectural framework in contemporary African institutional history). Multi-decade Djibouti US-China-French military-base architectural framework is the foundation of his contemporary diplomatic positioning. He was elected AU Commission Chairperson on 15 February 2025. He welcomed Doha Tshisekedi-Kagame talks March 2025.

### 2024-2026 AU architecture

Multi-state African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) has operated since 1 January 2021 — the most consequential post-2002 AU economic-architecture. AU Peace and Security Council operates as architectural framework. African Standby Force (ASF) operates under conceptual framework.

2024-2026 Sudan mediation failed; 2024-2026 DRC mediation outsourced to Angola then Qatar. 2024 AU Permanent Mission to UN in New York and AU-China FOCAC architectural framework operate as contemporary architectural alignment. 2024 AU Russia-Africa Summit architectural framework operates alongside.

### AU institutional limitations

Multi-decade AU funding crisis: roughly 80% from external donors. 2023-2024 AU institutional decline relative to ECOWAS, IGAD, SADC sub-regional architectures operates as architectural foundation. Multi-decade Sahel Alliance of Sahel States (AES — Dossier 53) operates as contemporary architectural challenge to AU institutional framework.

The architectural significance: contemporary African Union under Youssouf operates as contemporary continental institutional consolidation under conditions of external-funding-dependency, Sahel-AES architectural fragmentation, contemporary Sudan-civil-war mediation failure, and contemporary DRC mediation outsourcing to Qatar.

# Dossier 55: Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva and the Brazilian Workers' Party Architecture

## What was constructedure

The Federative Republic of Brazil operates under the 1988 Constitution that closed the 1964-1985 military dictatorship period. The presidential system permits two consecutive terms of four years, with non-consecutive re-election unrestricted. The 39th President, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, took office for a third term on 1 January 2023 after winning the 30 October 2022 runoff against incumbent Jair Bolsonaro by 1.8% — the closest margin in Brazilian electoral history.

The parliamentary architecture: a 513-seat Chamber of Deputies and an 81-seat Federal Senate, both elected on staggered cycles that produce permanent coalition arithmetic. The president is elected by direct two-round vote.

The Lula 2023-2026 cabinet was assembled around a coalition centred on the Workers' Party (Partido dos Trabalhadores, PT), the centre-right Brazilian Socialist Party (PSB) supplying Vice President Geraldo Alckmin, and the centrist *Centrão* bloc supplying legislative votes in exchange for ministry and budget access. The 2026 cabinet apex includes Wellington Lima e Silva (Minister of Justice since 15 January 2026, previously general counsel of Petrobras 2024-2026), Fernando Haddad (Finance), Mauro Vieira (Foreign Affairs), and a Defence-Ministry-and-Armed-Forces relationship that remains the central post-2023 constitutional pressure point given the role of military officers in the 8 January 2023 Brasília government-building attack three weeks into Lula's third term.

## Lula — biographical foundation

Luiz Inácio da Silva was born on 27 October 1945 in Garanhuns, Pernambuco state, to sharecropping parents. The nickname "Lula" was added to his legal name in 1982. He worked as a shoe-shine boy, street vendor, and factory worker. During the post-1964 military-dictatorship period he found employment at the Villares Metalworks in São Bernardo do Campo, the industrial belt of São Paulo. In 1972 he left the factory to work full-time for the Metalworkers' Union, becoming union president in 1975.

The 1978-1980 metalworkers' strikes at São Bernardo do Campo positioned Lula as the central labour leader of the late-military-dictatorship period. The strikes broke the official wage controls of the military government and produced his arrest under the 1969 National Security Law in April 1980. In February 1980 he co-founded the Workers' Party (Partido dos Trabalhadores) at the Colégio Sion in São Paulo. The party brought together Catholic-base-community militants, Trotskyist organisations, dissident Communist Party operatives, and the new São Paulo industrial union movement.

Lula ran for the presidency in 1989, 1994, and 1998 and lost each time. The 1989 first-round elimination by Fernando Collor de Mello, the 1994 first-round defeat to Fernando Henrique Cardoso (52.6% to Lula's 27%), and the 1998 first-round defeat to Cardoso again (53.1% to 31.7%) bracketed the centre-left's two-decade construction of the PT as a national governing party.

## The 2002 victory and the first two terms 2003-2011

In October 2002 Lula won the runoff against PSDB candidate José Serra with 61.3% of the vote. He took office on 1 January 2003 as the 35th President of Brazil. He was re-elected in October 2006 against PSDB candidate Geraldo Alckmin with 60.8% of the runoff vote.

The 2003-2011 record had three principal threads.

Social-policy expansion: the *Bolsa Família* conditional-cash-transfer programme reached roughly 12 million households by 2010 and was credited by the World Bank and the UN ECLAC with lifting roughly 20 million Brazilians out of extreme poverty across the period. The Programa Universidade para Todos expanded federal higher-education access. Minimum-wage increases ran above inflation across the entire eight-year period.

Macroeconomic management within the Cardoso framework: the Lula government maintained the inflation-targeting regime, the floating exchange rate, and the fiscal-primary-surplus target that Cardoso had institutionalised. The PT's pre-2002 platform of debt-renegotiation and IMF withdrawal was abandoned in the August 2002 "Letter to the Brazilian People" that committed Lula to honouring existing contracts. The 2007-2010 commodity supercycle produced Brazilian GDP growth averaging roughly 4.5% per year and the country's investment-grade credit rating award in 2008.

The Mensalão vote-buying scandal: in 2005 the Veja magazine and the *Folha de S. Paulo* exposed a monthly-allowance scheme by which the PT government paid coalition deputies for legislative support. The 2012 Supreme Court trial convicted 25 figures including PT chief of staff José Dirceu, PT treasurer Delúbio Soares, and PT president José Genoíno. Lula was not charged.

Lula left office on 1 January 2011 with the highest end-of-term approval rating of any modern Brazilian president — roughly 80% per the Datafolha December 2010 poll.

## Dilma Rousseff, the 2014 election, and the Lava Jato investigation

Lula's chief of staff Dilma Rousseff won the 2010 election and was re-elected in October 2014 against the PSDB's Aécio Neves by a 51.6%-48.4% runoff margin. The 2014 election was contested by Aécio Neves and the PSDB on fraud grounds that Brazil's electoral courts rejected.

Operation Lava Jato ("Car Wash") opened in March 2014 in Curitiba under federal judge Sérgio Moro. The investigation began as a money-laundering case and rapidly expanded into the largest corruption investigation in Brazilian history. The Lava Jato task force identified a multi-year kickback scheme through which the state oil company Petrobras awarded inflated contracts to a cartel of major Brazilian construction firms — Odebrecht (later renamed Novonor), Andrade Gutierrez, Camargo Corrêa, OAS, Queiroz Galvão — that returned a percentage to political parties including the PT, the centre-right Brazilian Democratic Movement Party (PMDB), and the PSDB.

The 2016 impeachment of Dilma Rousseff was carried by Congress on the formal charge of *pedaladas fiscais* (fiscal-decree accounting irregularities). The political record is that the impeachment was led by PMDB Vice President Michel Temer, who succeeded Rousseff in August 2016. The Wikileaks 2019 publication of intercepted Telegram messages between Moro and lead Lava Jato prosecutor Deltan Dallagnol — the "Vaza Jato" leak via *The Intercept Brasil* under Glenn Greenwald — documented coordination between the supposedly impartial judge and the prosecution team on case strategy, witness selection, and public-relations posture.

## The 2017 conviction, the 2018 imprisonment, and the 2021 annulment

On 12 July 2017 federal judge Sérgio Moro convicted Lula of passive corruption and money-laundering in the *triplex* case — the allegation that the OAS construction company had provided Lula with a beachfront apartment in Guarujá, São Paulo state, in exchange for Petrobras contracts. Lula was sentenced to 9 years and 6 months, subsequently extended to 12 years and 1 month on appeal.

On 7 April 2018 Lula surrendered to federal police in São Bernardo do Campo and entered Federal Police custody in Curitiba. He had been polling first in the 2018 presidential race against then-candidate Jair Bolsonaro before his arrest. The Superior Electoral Court barred his 2018 candidacy on 1 September 2018 under the *Ficha Limpa* law. Bolsonaro won the October 2018 election.

Moro was appointed Minister of Justice in the Bolsonaro government on 1 January 2019 — three months after the election that the Lula candidacy bar had enabled. Moro resigned from the Bolsonaro government on 24 April 2020 over an unrelated dispute and ran for Senate in 2022 as a member of União Brasil.

On 8 November 2019 Lula was released from prison after a Supreme Court ruling held that defendants could not be imprisoned until all appeals were exhausted. On 8 March 2021 Supreme Court justice Edson Fachin annulled all four Lava Jato convictions against Lula on the procedural ground that the federal court in Curitiba had lacked jurisdiction over the cases. On 23 March 2021 the Supreme Court's Second Panel ruled separately that Moro had been biased in the Lula trial. The annulments restored Lula's political rights.

The Lava Jato investigation itself was wound down across 2020-2021 under Bolsonaro-era prosecutorial appointments. The 14 March 2024 UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention opinion characterised Lula's 2018-2019 imprisonment as a violation of his political rights under the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights.

## The 2022 election

Lula announced his candidacy in May 2022 in alliance with former PSDB São Paulo governor Geraldo Alckmin — Lula's 2006 runoff opponent — as the vice-presidential nominee. The alliance was the central political signal of the campaign: that Lula would govern from a centrist coalition rather than the PT-PCdoB-PSOL left.

The 2 October 2022 first round produced Lula 48.4% to Bolsonaro 43.2%. The 30 October 2022 runoff produced Lula 50.9% to Bolsonaro 49.1% — a 2,139,645-vote margin and the closest result in Brazilian electoral history. Lula received roughly 60.3 million votes — the largest absolute vote total ever recorded for a Brazilian presidential candidate.

The Bolsonaro post-election period included the 8 January 2023 Brasília attack — eight days into Lula's third term — when Bolsonaro supporters stormed the National Congress, the Supreme Court, and the Planalto presidential palace. The attack mirrored the 6 January 2021 US Capitol attack of which Bolsonaro had been a self-declared admirer. The Federal Police investigation produced multiple indictments. On 5 September 2025 the Federal Supreme Court convicted Bolsonaro and seven co-defendants for plotting a coup; Bolsonaro received a 27-year sentence and entered federal custody.

## The third term 2023-2026

The third term has run through three principal threads.

Coalition management: the post-2023 Lula government has operated through a coalition of the PT, the PSB (Alckmin), the centrist PSD, the agrarian-conservative Republicanos and PP, and the *Centrão* bloc that votes for the government's legislative programme in exchange for ministerial portfolios and budget transfers. The 2023 cabinet had 37 ministries, the highest in Brazilian history. The post-2023 *orçamento secreto* ("secret budget") rapporteur-amendment architecture that the Bolsonaro coalition had institutionalised continued to operate under Lula.

Foreign policy: Lula resumed Brazil's BRICS leadership role and hosted the August 2023 BRICS summit in Johannesburg that institutionalised BRICS+ expansion. He attended the September 2024 BRICS summit in Kazan, Russia. He has called repeatedly across 2023-2026 for a negotiated end to the Russia-Ukraine war and has refused to provide Ukrainian military aid. On 18 February 2024 he characterised the Israeli campaign in Gaza as comparable to "what Hitler decided to do to the Jews," producing the Israeli government's *persona non grata* designation. On 29 December 2023 South Africa filed the ICJ Genocide Convention case against Israel; Brazil joined the case as an intervening party in July 2024. The May 2026 White House meeting with Trump on organised crime and tariffs was the first Lula-Trump direct engagement of the second Trump term.

Domestic record: the 2023 PEC da Transição amended the constitution to permit *Bolsa Família* expansion outside the spending cap. The August 2023 Arcabouço Fiscal law replaced the 2016 spending cap with a graduated framework. The Belo Monte and Itaipu hydroelectric concessions continued. The 14 March 2024 assassination of Rio de Janeiro city councillor Marielle Franco (case in advanced trial through 2024-2026 under former federal police chief Domingos Brazão's indictment) and the Manaus organised-crime expansion produced the May 2026 Trump-Lula organised-crime meeting referenced above.

The April 2026 Senate rejection of Lula's Supreme Court nominee — the first Senate rejection of a presidential STF nominee in Brazilian history — marked the most consequential institutional reversal of the third term. Reuters reporting indicated Lula's intention to submit a second nominee.

## The 2026 fourth-term bid

In March 2026 Lula confirmed Geraldo Alckmin would again be his running mate in the October 2026 election. The AS/COA poll tracker has Lula in a tight contest with Senator Flávio Bolsonaro — Jair Bolsonaro's eldest son and the PL replacement nominee after the September 2025 Bolsonaro coup conviction. Lula would be 81 on inauguration day in January 2027.

The fourth-term bid is structurally framed by the same dynamic that the third term was: the Workers' Party operating as the centre-left pole of a coalition that includes centre-right partners (Alckmin), with the post-Bolsonaro PL operating as the right-wing pole behind a Bolsonaro-family candidate. The 2018 election that produced Bolsonaro's first presidency was enabled by the judicially-engineered exclusion of Lula. The 2022 election that produced Lula's third term was enabled by the Supreme Court's annulment of the Lava Jato convictions. The 2026 election proceeds with Bolsonaro himself excluded by federal coup conviction — the pattern of judicial exclusion of one or the other principal having now structured three consecutive Brazilian presidential elections.

## The final assessment

The Lula biographical record is the public-facing record of the metalworker-turned-union-leader-turned-Workers'-Party-founder-turned-three-time-President. The structural record is the record of a Brazilian centre-left government across roughly twelve cumulative years of executive office. It has operated within rather than against the post-1994 Brazilian macroeconomic framework. It has reduced extreme poverty through transfer payments while leaving the commodity-export-oriented economic base unaltered. It has co-existed with major Petrobras-and-construction-cartel corruption scandals across both PT presidencies. It returned to power after the structural-judicial campaign of the Lava Jato investigation was reversed by the Supreme Court itself. And it now seeks a fourth term against the son of the predecessor whom the judiciary has, in turn, imprisoned.

The metalworker reduced extreme poverty, preserved the commodity-export base, co-existed with the Petrobras corruption architecture, went to prison on judicially-engineered charges, came back when the Supreme Court annulled the convictions, and now seeks a fourth term while the judiciary imprisons his predecessor's predecessor. The Workers' Party record is documented. The biographical arc is the presentation that made the operation electable.

The Brazilian Workers' Party architecture under Lula is, documented, the Latin American case study of a centre-left party operating the same post-1971 financial settlement that the other dossiers in this volume have documented in other jurisdictions. The architecture works.
