# Dossier 56: Claudia Sheinbaum and the MORENA Architecture in Mexico

## The Mexican political architecture

The United Mexican States operates under the 1917 Constitution. The presidency is a single six-year term (*sexenio*) with no re-election permitted — the central post-1917 constitutional commitment that emerged from the post-Porfirio-Díaz revolutionary settlement. The 66th President, Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo, assumed office on 1 October 2024 after winning the June 2024 election with roughly 60% of the vote — the largest absolute vote total in Mexican electoral history. She is the first woman and the first Jewish person to be elected to the post.

The parliamentary architecture: a 500-seat Chamber of Deputies and a 128-seat Senate. The post-2018 MORENA-led coalition has held legislative supermajorities permitting constitutional amendment since the 2024 election. MORENA — Movimiento Regeneración Nacional, the "National Regeneration Movement" — was founded by Andrés Manuel López Obrador in 2014 as the political vehicle for the post-PRD left-populist movement that the 2006 and 2012 elections had failed to bring to office.

The Sheinbaum 2024-2030 cabinet apex includes Luz Elena González Escobar (Energy, since 1 October 2024, previously Sheinbaum's Mexico City finance secretary), Marcelo Ebrard (Economy), Juan Ramón de la Fuente (Foreign Affairs), and Rosa Icela Rodríguez (Public Security). Leticia Ramírez Amaya assumed the Welfare Ministry on 29 April 2026 after the resignation of Ariadna Montiel Reyes.

## Sheinbaum — biographical foundation

Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo was born on 24 June 1962 in Mexico City. Her father Carlos Sheinbaum Yoselevitz was a chemical engineer; her mother Annie Pardo Cemo is a biologist and professor emerita at the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM). Her grandparents on both sides were Jewish immigrants — her paternal grandparents from Lithuania and her maternal grandparents from Bulgaria — who settled in Mexico in the 1920s. She has described her religious background in interviews as Jewish ancestry without active religious practice.

She studied physics at UNAM and received a bachelor's degree in 1989. She completed master's and doctoral degrees in energy engineering at UNAM, with doctoral research conducted at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory in Berkeley, California. Her doctoral dissertation compared Mexican energy-consumption patterns with those of industrialised countries. She joined the UNAM engineering faculty in 1995.

Her academic career has produced over 100 peer-reviewed publications and two books on energy, the environment, and sustainable development. She was a member of the United Nations Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) and contributed to the IPCC's Fourth Assessment Report (2007) and Fifth Assessment Report (2014). The IPCC shared the 2007 Nobel Peace Prize with Al Gore for the Fourth Assessment Report — Sheinbaum's recurring biographical reference point for her scientific credentials.

## Political emergence under López Obrador

Sheinbaum was politically active in UNAM student politics through the 1980s. She was a founding member of the Party of the Democratic Revolution (PRD) in 1989. Her political alignment was with the López Obrador faction of the PRD that emerged from the 1988 Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas presidential campaign and the contested 1988 election result.

In December 2000 López Obrador, then newly elected as Mexico City head of government, appointed Sheinbaum as Mexico City Secretary of the Environment. She held the post until 15 May 2006. Her record at the Mexico City environment secretariat included the introduction of the Metrobús bus rapid-transit system and the construction of the second-deck expansion of the Periférico beltway.

López Obrador resigned the Mexico City mayoralty in mid-2005 to run for the 2006 presidency. He lost narrowly to Felipe Calderón of the PAN — the 2006 election that produced multi-week post-election protests in Mexico City and the López Obrador *gobierno legítimo* parallel-government claim.

Sheinbaum returned to UNAM as a research faculty member from 2006 to 2015. In 2014 she followed López Obrador out of the PRD and into the newly founded MORENA. In 2015 she was elected delegational chief (mayor) of Tlalpan, one of the sixteen Mexico City boroughs. In 2017 she resigned the Tlalpan position to run for Mexico City head of government.

## The Mexico City head of government tenure 2018-2023

On 1 July 2018 Sheinbaum won the Mexico City head-of-government election with 47.1% of the vote — the same day López Obrador won the national presidency in a landslide. She took office on 5 December 2018.

The 2018-2023 Mexico City record had three principal threads.

Infrastructure and transit: the Cablebús aerial cable-car system was inaugurated 2021 across two lines connecting peripheral high-density neighbourhoods to the Metro network. The reconstruction of Line 12 of the Mexico City Metro followed the 3 May 2021 collapse of an elevated section between Olivos and Tezonco that killed 26 passengers. The investigation found the collapse was caused by construction defects in the Line 12 originally built under the 2010-2012 Marcelo Ebrard mayoral term. The political consequence — pressure on Ebrard, then a Sheinbaum rival for the 2024 MORENA presidential nomination — was a central inflection point of the 2023 MORENA primary.

Public security: Mexico City murder rates declined across 2019-2023 per official Mexico City Attorney General figures. The improvement was contested by independent analysts who pointed to data-classification changes. The 20 June 2020 attempted assassination of Mexico City Public Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch in the Lomas de Chapultepec neighbourhood by the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) — García Harfuch was wounded; three others were killed — was the most consequential single security incident of the tenure.

The 2023 MORENA presidential primary: in June 2023 Sheinbaum resigned the Mexico City mayoralty to compete for the MORENA presidential nomination. The internal-poll-based primary system that López Obrador had institutionalised selected Sheinbaum over Ebrard, Adán Augusto López, and three other contenders. Ebrard contested the result and threatened to leave MORENA before reconciling with the Sheinbaum campaign.

## The 2024 election and the first eighteen months

On 2 June 2024 Sheinbaum won the presidential election with roughly 60% of the vote against PAN-PRI-PRD candidate Xóchitl Gálvez (roughly 28%) and Movimiento Ciudadano candidate Jorge Álvarez Máynez (roughly 11%). The MORENA-led "Sigamos Haciendo Historia" coalition won qualified majorities in both chambers of Congress — the threshold required for constitutional amendments.

The post-September 2024 *paquete de reformas* constitutional package — passed by the outgoing López Obrador administration before Sheinbaum's 1 October 2024 inauguration — included the judicial reform that mandated direct election of federal judges including Supreme Court justices, the dissolution of multiple autonomous regulatory bodies (the INAI transparency authority and others), and the integration of the National Guard into the Defence Ministry. The package marked the most consequential post-1917 alteration of the Mexican constitutional architecture.

Foreign policy: the Sheinbaum government has maintained the *política de no intervención* but the dominant foreign-policy challenge has been the second Trump administration. The January 2025 Trump tariff threats produced a coordinated Mexican response on migration and fentanyl-precursor interdiction. The April-May 2026 sequence covered below is the central institutional record of the bilateral relationship.

Macroeconomic management: the 2024 fiscal deficit reached 5.7% of GDP — the largest since the 1980s debt crisis. The Sheinbaum government has committed to deficit reduction across 2025-2026 while maintaining the post-2018 MORENA flagship programmes including Pensión para Adultos Mayores (universal old-age pension) and Becas Benito Juárez (universal student stipends).

## The 2026 organised-crime architecture

The April-May 2026 sequence has been the most consequential institutional pressure point of the Sheinbaum presidency.

On 29 April 2026 the US Department of Justice unsealed indictments against Sinaloa state Governor Rubén Rocha Moya and other officials for alleged Sinaloa Cartel involvement. Rocha Moya is a MORENA party member and close ally of López Obrador. On 30 April 2026 Sheinbaum announced Mexico would investigate the indictment but did not commit to extradition.

In February 2026 the Adán Augusto López Hernández resignation as MORENA Senate coordinator followed mounting corruption-scandal pressure and reported tensions with Sheinbaum. The Brookings Institution February 2026 analysis characterised the López Hernández resignation alongside the early-February 2026 arrests of the Tequila mayor and three local officials for collaboration with the Jalisco New Generation Cartel as evidence of a Sheinbaum-led restructuring of MORENA's relationship with subnational political-criminal networks.

In April 2026 the death of two CIA officers during a Drug Enforcement Administration-and-Chihuahua-state drug-laboratory raid produced the Sheinbaum government's consideration of sanctions against the Chihuahua state government. The 13 May 2026 CNN report — that the CIA was conducting operations on Mexican soil — was denied by Sheinbaum, with CNN standing by the reporting. On 18 May 2026 The Guardian reported the surrender to US authorities of two further ex-officials over alleged cartel ties.

The Mitofsky March 2026 poll registered Sheinbaum approval at 68.4% — the sixth consecutive month of marginal decline from the September 2025 peak. The Brazilian AtlasIntel poll of the same period registered approval at 53.9%. The AS/COA approval tracker has consistently described Sheinbaum as one of Latin America's most popular incumbent leaders.

## The residue

The Claudia Sheinbaum biographical record is the public-facing record of the climate physicist who became the first woman and the first Jewish person elected to the Mexican presidency. She is the protégée of López Obrador, the credentialed technical operator with a Lawrence Berkeley doctoral training, the IPCC contributor with a share in the 2007 Nobel Peace Prize, and the constitutional-reform president who has used the post-2024 MORENA legislative supermajority to enact the most consequential post-1917 alteration of the Mexican constitutional architecture.

The operating record is the operation of a Mexican left-populist government across the second Trump administration that has, documented, expanded social-transfer programmes; institutionalised judicial election; dissolved multiple autonomous regulatory bodies; folded the National Guard into the Defence Ministry; and continued to confront the post-2006 Mexican narcotrafficking architecture under direct US institutional pressure.

The climate physicist, the IPCC contributor, the first woman president, the MORENA supermajority architect who dissolved the autonomous regulators and elected the judges. The credentials were real. So was the constitutional rewrite.

The post-2018 Mexican MORENA architecture under Sheinbaum is, documented, the Latin American case study of a left-populist constitutional-reform party operating the same post-1971 financial settlement that the other dossiers in this volume have documented in other jurisdictions. The architecture works.


---

# Dossier 57: Javier Milei and the La Libertad Avanza Architecture in Argentina

## How the apparatus operatesure

The Argentine Republic operates under the 1853 Constitution as amended in 1994. The presidential system permits a single four-year term with one consecutive re-election. The 60th President, Javier Gerardo Milei, took office on 10 December 2023 after winning the 19 November 2023 runoff against Sergio Massa with 55.7% of the vote. The 26 October 2025 mid-term legislative elections produced a 41% vote share for Milei's La Libertad Avanza (LLA) party and its allies — the largest single-party result and the confirmation of the Milei programme one year into the *sexenio*.

The parliamentary architecture: a 257-seat Chamber of Deputies and a 72-seat Senate, both elected on staggered cycles. The post-October 2025 result gave LLA enough seats to sustain presidential vetoes against legislative override — the procedural foundation of the post-2025 Milei austerity programme's protected status.

The Milei cabinet apex includes Vice President Victoria Villarruel, Cabinet Chief Guillermo Francos, Economy Minister Luis Caputo (architect of the December 2023 currency devaluation and the post-2024 fiscal-shock programme), and a Cabinet structure reduced from 18 ministries to 9 by emergency decree in the first weeks of the government.

## Milei — biographical foundation

Javier Gerardo Milei was born on 22 October 1970 in Buenos Aires. His father Norberto was a bus driver; his mother Alicia a housewife. He has described his childhood in repeated interviews as marked by paternal physical violence — a public account his father has not contested. He has been estranged from his parents and has identified his younger sister Karina as his closest confidante; Karina serves as General Secretary of the Presidency in his government.

Milei attended Cardenal Copello Institute high school, then the University of Belgrano for an economics degree (1989-1993). He completed two master's degrees in economics from the Instituto de Desarrollo Económico y Social (IDES) and the Universidad Torcuato Di Tella. He has not completed a doctorate. His academic-publication record is thin by international economics-faculty standards but he served as a faculty contributor at multiple Buenos Aires universities through the 2000s and 2010s.

From the late 1990s through 2021 Milei worked as an economist at multiple Argentine financial-services firms including Estudio Broda, HSBC Argentina, and Máxima AFJP. From the late 2010s he became a regular Argentine broadcast-television commentator, building a public profile through profanity-laden interview appearances attacking what he termed the *casta política* (political caste). The television persona — the "loose-cannon" libertarian economist — was the central asset of his rapid political ascent.

## Political emergence 2021-2023

In November 2021 Milei was elected to the Chamber of Deputies representing the City of Buenos Aires on the Avanza Libertad ticket. He served a single term. His congressional record was limited — he attended sessions inconsistently and submitted few bills. The deputyship served as the governing platform for the 2023 presidential run.

In August 2023 Milei finished first in the Argentine PASO open primary election with 30.0% of the vote — ahead of both the centre-right Juntos por el Cambio coalition (Patricia Bullrich) and the incumbent Peronist Unión por la Patria (Sergio Massa). The PASO result was the political shock of the 2023 cycle and reordered the runoff calculation.

On 22 October 2023 the first round produced Massa 36.7%, Milei 30.0%, and Bullrich 23.8%. On 19 November 2023 the runoff produced Milei 55.7% to Massa 44.3%. The Macri-led PRO faction of Juntos por el Cambio endorsed Milei between rounds — the central institutional realignment that produced the Milei victory.

Milei took office on 10 December 2023. His inaugural address — delivered from the steps of the National Congress rather than from inside the chamber — told Argentines directly that "the situation will get worse in the short term" before improvement could begin. The Milei electoral coalition was, at inauguration, the smallest legislative caucus of any modern Argentine president.

## The first year: the chainsaw

The Milei programme operated in four immediate moves across the first months.

The currency devaluation: on 12 December 2023 Economy Minister Luis Caputo devalued the official Argentine peso by 54% — from 366.50 to 800 pesos per dollar. The blue-chip swap and parallel-market rates closed the Argentine exchange-rate divergence. Monthly inflation peaked at over 25% at the end of December 2023.

The emergency decree package: on 20 December 2023 Milei issued the Decreto de Necesidad y Urgencia 70/2023 (DNU 70/2023) — a 366-article emergency decree that scrapped or modified over 300 regulations, including rent controls, price controls, and post-2020 labour protections. The DNU was the largest single emergency decree in modern Argentine history. The May 2024 Bases Law (Ley Bases), passed by Congress in modified form after the original *Ley Ómnibus* failed, codified portions of the DNU into statute.

The state contraction: nine of the eighteen pre-2023 cabinet ministries were dissolved or merged. Over 30,000 public-sector workers were dismissed across 2024. The hiring freeze was institutionalised. Energy and transport subsidies were cut, producing utility-rate increases that absorbed the bulk of the inflationary impact on household budgets in the first half of 2024.

The fiscal-surplus achievement: by mid-2024 the Argentine federal government had run consecutive monthly fiscal surpluses for the first time in over a decade. The 2024 calendar-year primary surplus reached roughly 1.8% of GDP — the first annual primary surplus since 2010. The financial-market response was a recovery of the peso against the parallel-market dollar and a sharp compression of Argentine sovereign-bond yields.

The 2024-2025 macroeconomic record produced what Caputo and Milei characterised as the "Argentine miracle": monthly inflation declining from over 25% in December 2023 to roughly 2-3% by late 2025 — the lowest level in seven years per the Phenomenal World November 2025 analysis. The cost of the disinflation has been the post-2024 expansion of registered poverty (peaking at roughly 53% of the population in early 2024 before declining), the post-2024 contraction of real wages, and the documented absolute reduction of provincial-government transfers that the December 2025-January 2026 protests have foregrounded.

## The October 2025 mid-terms and the Trump alignment

The 26 October 2025 mid-term legislative elections were the central institutional inflection point of the Milei presidency to date. La Libertad Avanza and its allies took roughly 41% of the vote, producing the largest single-party result and a Chamber-of-Deputies seat allocation sufficient to sustain Milei vetoes against override.

The pre-election architecture included a US Treasury commitment of $20 billion in currency-swap support to the Argentine Central Bank — the largest such facility ever extended to Argentina by the US Treasury. Trump's public position — articulated by Trump directly and by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent — was that the swap was contingent on the LLA winning the mid-terms. The post-election extension of the swap was confirmed by Bessent in the days following the LLA victory.

The Trump-Milei bilateral relationship has been the central post-2025 Argentine foreign-policy architecture. Trump has characterised Milei in repeated public statements as the most consequential US-aligned Latin American head of state of the post-Cold-War period. The proposed US-Argentina free-trade agreement that Milei restated in his March 2025 state-of-the-nation address remains in negotiation as of May 2026 per the May 2026 *Buenos Aires Times* and *Foreign Policy* reporting cycles.

## The 2026 architecture

The 2026 calendar-year record has had four threads.

The 2026 budget: pitched by Milei in late 2025 as "the smallest in the last thirty years," it reduced social spending, federal-employee salaries, and federal transfers to the provinces. The post-2026-budget provincial-government protests produced the May 2026 mass demonstrations covered in the *Foreign Policy* reporting.

The ministerial-salary controversy: Decree DNU 931/2025, signed by Milei on 2 January 2026, doubled cabinet-minister and senior-undersecretary salaries to over eight million pesos per month — explicitly excluding the President and Vice-President, whose pay remained frozen. The decree was characterised by opposition and centrist critics as the institutional contradiction of the *austeridad* programme. The *Buenos Aires Times* April 2026 reporting documented the staged rollout across the first months of the year.

The Spagnuolo allegations: corruption accusations against Milei's chief of staff Karina Milei (the President's sister and General Secretary of the Presidency) and against former cabinet figure Diego Spagnuolo have run across early 2026. The accusations have been denied by the Presidency. The documented fact remains that no judicial proceedings have been finalised through May 2026.

The science-and-university record: Milei's "chainsaw" applied to the Argentine higher-education and public-science sectors — frozen budgets, multi-year inflation-driven real-terms cuts, CONICET research-grant restrictions, and university-administration confrontations. The February 2026 Times Higher Education investigation characterised the Argentine science-sector reductions as the most consequential government-imposed contraction in Argentina's modern history. The Bolsonaro Brazilian comparison framing is the article's own.

The polling: the AtlasIntel April 2026 reading gave Milei roughly 35% approval — the lowest level of the presidency to date. Per the May 2026 *Foreign Policy* reporting, more than half the Milei electoral term remains, with the 2027 presidential election scheduled for October 2027.

## The summary position

The Javier Milei biographical record is the public-facing record of the libertarian-economist-turned-television-personality-turned-President of Argentina. He brandished a chainsaw at the September 2023 La Plata campaign rally and translated that imagery into a one-and-a-half-year programme that has, documented, halved monthly inflation, run consecutive monthly fiscal surpluses, dismissed over 30,000 public-sector workers, cut over half the cabinet ministries, and scrapped or modified over 300 federal regulations by emergency decree.

The documented record is the operation of an Argentine right-libertarian government that has, documented, restored creditor confidence and serviced the Argentine sovereign debt held principally by international institutional creditors; aligned Argentine foreign policy with the second Trump administration including the $20 billion currency-swap facility; produced the deepest post-1983 contraction of the Argentine state by raw employment and ministerial-headcount figures; and expanded registered poverty in the first half of its first year to the highest level recorded since the 2001-2002 *corralito* collapse before declining as the 2024-2025 disinflation took hold.

The chainsaw was a campaign prop. The 30,000 dismissals, the halved inflation, the expanded poverty rate, and the restored creditor-confidence framework were the operation. The libertarian presentation and the structural-adjustment outcome are the same programme described in two registers.

The Milei programme is, documented, the most explicit Latin American post-2023 implementation of the structural-adjustment-and-creditor-priority framework that the prior dossiers in this volume have documented in other jurisdictions. The architecture works.


---

# Dossier 58: Gustavo Petro and the Pacto Histórico Architecture in Colombia

## The Colombian institutional operator

The Republic of Colombia operates under the 1991 Constitution that closed the post-1958 National Front bipartisan settlement and opened the contemporary multi-party era. The presidential system permits a single four-year term — the 2015 constitutional amendment having reversed the 2005 Uribe-era amendment that had permitted one consecutive re-election. The 35th President, Gustavo Francisco Petro Urrego, assumed office on 7 August 2022 after winning the 19 June 2022 runoff against Rodolfo Hernández with 50.4% of the vote. Vice President Francia Márquez became the first Afro-Colombian woman elected to the post.

The parliamentary architecture: a 188-seat Chamber of Representatives and a 108-seat Senate, both elected on the same four-year cycle as the presidency. The March 2022 legislative elections gave Petro's Pacto Histórico coalition the largest single bloc but not a working majority — the central institutional constraint of the entire 2022-2026 term. The 8 March 2026 mid-term legislative elections produced a Pacto Histórico victory in the popular vote without a majority sufficient to pass the pending reforms unilaterally.

The Petro cabinet apex as of May 2026 includes Interior Minister Armando Benedetti (since 3 March 2025, previously Chief of Staff of the Casa de Nariño February-March 2025 and Ambassador to Venezuela 2022-2023), Foreign Minister Rosa Yolanda Villavicencio (since November 2024), and a Justice Ministry that has cycled through five holders during the term — most recently Acting Minister Augusto Ocampo June-October 2025 and current Minister Luis Eduardo Montealegre. The cabinet-turnover pattern is the central institutional indicator of the post-2023 disintegration of the Petro coalition.

## Petro — biographical foundation

Gustavo Francisco Petro Urrego was born on 19 April 1960 in Ciénaga de Oro, a small town in the cattle- and cotton-raising region of the Córdoba department of northwestern Colombia. His family heritage is likely Italian or Roma — a detail that has figured in the Colombian press coverage of his personal background. He grew up in the town of Zipaquirá, north of Bogotá, where his family relocated during his childhood.

He enrolled at Externado University of Colombia in Bogotá to study economics. At age 17, while a student, he was recruited by the 19th of April Movement (M-19) — the Marxist guerrilla organisation founded in 1970 by figures who held that the 1970 presidential election had been stolen from former dictator Gustavo Rojas Pinilla. M-19 was distinct in the Colombian guerrilla landscape: its operations were primarily urban, its leadership middle-class, and its political character explicitly nationalist-Bolivarian rather than rural-peasant Marxist-Leninist.

The M-19 record across the 1976-1990 period included the 1976 kidnapping and killing of labour leader José Raquel Mercado, the 1979 raid on the Cantón Norte Bogotá military arsenal that seized over 5,000 weapons, the February 1980 seizure of the Dominican Republic embassy during a cocktail-party reception, and the catastrophic 6 November 1985 seizure of the Palacio de Justicia in Bogotá that produced the army-counter-assault massacre of over 100 people including 11 of the 25 Supreme Court justices. Petro was not directly involved in the Palacio de Justicia operation — he was imprisoned at the time on weapons-and-armed-conspiracy charges following an October 1985 arrest in Zipaquirá.

In March 1987 Petro was released from prison. He helped promote the peace negotiations between M-19 and the Virgilio Barco government that produced the 9 March 1990 M-19 demobilisation and the founding of the M-19 Democratic Alliance (AD M-19) as a legal political party. AD M-19 became the third-largest force in the 1990-1991 National Constituent Assembly that drafted the 1991 Constitution.

## Pre-presidency political career 1991-2022

Petro served in the Chamber of Representatives 1991-1994 (Cundinamarca constituency) and 1998-2006 (Capital District). He served in the Senate 2006-2010 and 2018-2022. His congressional record was built principally on investigative committee work attacking the Álvaro Uribe presidency (2002-2010). His 2007-2009 Senate denunciations identified what he alleged were links between politicians, security-force figures, paramilitary operators including the AUC (United Self-Defence Forces of Colombia), and the Uribe brother Santiago.

The 2010 presidential election was Petro's first national run; he finished fourth with 9.1% of the vote. The 30 October 2011 Bogotá mayoral election produced his victory with 32.2% of the vote. He took office on 1 January 2012.

The Bogotá mayoralty 2012-2015 was contentious. In December 2013 Inspector General Alejandro Ordóñez removed Petro from office and barred him from holding public office for fifteen years over the 2012 garbage-collection reform that had restructured the Bogotá waste-management system. The 19 March 2014 removal was reversed by an Inter-American Commission on Human Rights precautionary measure of 18 March 2014 and by a subsequent Council of State ruling. Petro returned to the mayoralty on 23 April 2014 and completed his term on 31 December 2015.

The 2018 presidential election was Petro's second national run. He came second in the first round, advanced to the runoff against Iván Duque (the Centro Democrático candidate backed by Uribe), and lost the runoff 54%-42% — the closest left-wing presidential result in Colombian history at that point.

## The 2022 election

The Pacto Histórico coalition was founded in 2021 as a unified left-and-centre-left ticket. The June 2022 first round produced Petro 40.3% to right-populist construction magnate Rodolfo Hernández 28.2%, with Centro Democrático's Federico Gutiérrez falling out of the race in third place at 23.9%. The 19 June 2022 runoff produced Petro 50.4% to Hernández 47.3% — a margin of roughly 700,000 votes. Vice President Francia Márquez, an Afro-Colombian environmental activist from the Pacific coast region of Cauca department, was the first Afro-Colombian VP and the second woman to hold the office.

Petro took office on 7 August 2022. His inaugural address committed his government to *paz total* (total peace) negotiations with the surviving Colombian armed-group landscape — the ELN guerrilla, the FARC dissident factions that had rejected the 2016 peace agreement, the Gulf Clan paramilitary organisation, and assorted regional criminal-armed groups. The post-2016 Colombian armed-group landscape had reconstituted along the territorial seams the FARC demobilisation had created.

## The reforms 2022-2026

The Petro 2022-2026 legislative programme had five flagship reforms: health, labour, pension, agrarian, and education.

Pension reform passed Congress in June 2024 — the central legislative achievement of the term. The reform restructured the multi-pillar Colombian pension system to channel the first roughly 2.3 minimum wages through the public Colpensiones system before private AFP capitalisation, expanding public-system coverage. The reform has been contested before the Constitutional Court across 2024-2026 on procedural grounds.

Labour reform was defeated in the Senate Labour Committee in March 2024. Petro then sought to put it to popular referendum, but the Constitutional Court suspended the referendum decree. A modified version passed Congress in June 2025 in significantly reduced form.

Health reform — the most consequential reform proposal, restructuring the post-1993 Ley 100 EPS-based private-insurance model toward state-led provision — remains stalled in Congress through May 2026. The reform has been the central political-economic battleground of the entire term, with the EPS industry, the medical-professional associations, and the opposition coalition aligned against passage.

Agrarian reform: the multi-year programme to redistribute roughly 3 million hectares of land to peasant communities has produced documented delivery of land totals that the government and independent monitors have disputed at various points. The September 2025 18,000-hectare delivery in the Magdalena Medio region was the occasion of Petro's revival of the National Constituent Assembly proposal.

Education reform: the post-2023 expansion of the public-university system and the cancellation of student-loan-collection arrears for ICETEX borrowers — the central commitment to the 2019-2021 student-protest movement that had backed Petro's 2022 campaign.

## The Constituent Assembly proposal

In 2025 Petro revived a longstanding proposal to convene a National Constituent Assembly to amend the 1991 Constitution in areas including healthcare, pensions, justice reform, environmental policy, and full implementation of the 2016 FARC peace agreement. The proposal followed the legislative defeat of multiple reforms and the judicial blocking of his labour-reform referendum.

The proposal has been characterised by the Atlantic Council and other Western foreign-policy institutions as a "democratic backsliding" risk — the framing that Petro is using popular mobilisation against constitutional institutional checks. The May 2026 SL Guardian reporting characterised the proposal's institutional status as "at an early stage, with no approved law to convene an assembly and an ongoing campaign to collect signatures in support of the initiative."

The political-class point: the Atlantic Council and aligned framings characterise the Constituent Assembly proposal as a "power grab." The Petro framing characterises it as a "popular constituent process." Both framings agree on the underlying institutional fact — Congress and the courts have blocked the principal reforms that the 2022 election was understood by Petro's electoral coalition to mandate.

## Foreign policy and the bilateral architecture

Petro held the *pro tempore* presidency of the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States (CELAC) from 9 April 2025 to 21 March 2026. His foreign policy across the 2022-2026 term has had three principal threads.

The Gaza-and-ICJ position: on 1 May 2024 Petro broke Colombian diplomatic relations with Israel — the most decisive diplomatic break of any major Latin American government throughout the post-October-2023 period before the late-2024 sequence of additional ruptures. Colombia joined the ICJ Genocide Convention case against Israel as an intervening party in mid-2024.

The Venezuela bilateral relationship: relations re-established with the Maduro government in August 2022 after the multi-year Iván Duque-era rupture. The Petro government has hosted multiple rounds of Venezuelan-opposition-Maduro-government dialogue. The April 2026 Petro visit to Caracas — the first Colombian presidential visit to Venezuela in seven years — was the inflection point of the bilateral architecture.

The US relationship under Trump: the post-January-2025 second Trump administration has produced sharp tension over migration, narcotics interdiction, and Colombian co-operation on the Plan Colombia framework. The January 2025 confrontation over the US deportation of Colombian nationals on military aircraft produced Petro's threat to refuse the flights, the Trump tariff-and-sanctions threat in response, and Petro's same-day reversal. The bilateral architecture has remained contested across the entire post-January-2025 period.

## So what

The Gustavo Petro biographical record is the public-facing record of the M-19 guerrilla-turned-demobilised-political-operator-turned-first-left-wing-president of Colombia. He served seventeen months in prison in the mid-1980s on weapons-conspiracy charges. He helped negotiate the 1990 demobilisation that ended M-19's armed period. He spent thirty years in Colombian elective politics before winning the 2022 presidency. And he has spent the four years of his term operating an inherited Colombian state apparatus that the opposition has, documented, used Congress and the judiciary to block at every consequential decision point.

The administrative record is the operation of a Colombian centre-left government that has, documented, passed one of five flagship reforms through the legislature in its enacted form; broken Colombian diplomatic relations with Israel; re-established relations with Venezuela; held the CELAC *pro tempore* presidency for the year April 2025 - March 2026; and faces an October 2026 presidential election in which the constitutional single-term rule prevents Petro's own candidacy.

The M-19 guerrilla spent seventeen months in prison, negotiated the 1990 demobilisation, spent thirty years in elective politics, won the presidency, and passed one of five flagship reforms. The Colombian institutional apparatus blocked the rest. The mandate was electoral. The veto was institutional.

The post-2022 Petro government is, documented, the Latin American case study of a left-wing electoral mandate constrained by the structural veto-points that Colombia's post-1991 constitutional architecture distributes across the legislature, the judiciary, and the autonomous oversight bodies. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 59: Nicolás Maduro, Delcy Rodríguez, and the Venezuelan State Architecture After Operation Resolve

## The Venezuelan power structure

The Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela operates under the 1999 Constitution drafted under Hugo Chávez. The presidential system permits one consecutive re-election; the post-1999 PSUV (United Socialist Party of Venezuela) consolidation has produced four consecutive PSUV presidencies: Chávez 1999-2013, Maduro 2013-2026, and acting president Delcy Rodríguez since 5 January 2026.

The dossier opens with a single fact that re-orders every subsequent claim. On 3 January 2026, US special operations forces conducted a military strike on Caracas. The operation, known under multiple US designations including "Operation Resolve" and "Absolute Resolve," captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores at a fortified compound in the centre of Caracas. The pair were flown to the US warship USS Iwo Jima, then to New York to face Southern District of New York narco-terrorism charges. Maduro appeared in handcuffs at a Manhattan federal courthouse on 5 January 2026.

## Nicolás Maduro — biographical foundation

Nicolás Maduro Moros was born on 23 November 1962 in Caracas. His father was a trade-union militant; the family lived in the Los Chaguaramos neighbourhood of Caracas. He completed secondary school but did not attend university. He worked as a bus driver for the Caracas Metro system. He became active in the trade-union movement and joined the Liga Socialista, a leftist organisation that operated in the post-1958 Punto Fijo party-system landscape.

His political career was built principally on union-and-party operations through the 1980s and 1990s. He met Cilia Flores — the lawyer who would later represent the imprisoned Hugo Chávez after the February 1992 attempted coup — in the early 1990s; they married in 2013. He was a member of the Chávez post-1992 Movimiento Bolivariano Revolucionario 200 (MBR-200) faction that produced the 1999 PSUV-precursor presidential victory.

In the 2000-2005 period he served in the National Assembly. In 2006-2013 he served as Foreign Minister under Chávez. The Foreign Ministry tenure built his international profile and his network with the principal anti-US Latin American governments of the period — the Lula and Rousseff PT governments in Brazil, the Kirchner-and-Kirchner Peronist governments in Argentina, the Correa government in Ecuador, the Morales government in Bolivia, and the Cuban Castro government with which the Venezuelan state had built the multi-year ALBA-and-Petrocaribe regional alignment.

In October 2012 Chávez was diagnosed with the cancer that would kill him five months later. On 8 December 2012, Chávez named Maduro as his political successor in a televised address from Caracas before departing for the final round of Cuban cancer surgery. On 5 March 2013 Chávez died. On 14 April 2013 Maduro won the special presidential election against opposition candidate Henrique Capriles Radonski by 50.6% to 49.1% — a margin of roughly 235,000 votes.

## The Maduro presidency 2013-January 2026

The Maduro presidency operated across thirteen years through five distinct phases.

The oil-price collapse (2014-2016): the post-2014 collapse of the international oil price from over $100 per barrel to roughly $30 per barrel produced the Venezuelan fiscal crisis from which the Bolivarian state has not recovered. Venezuelan GDP contracted by roughly 70% across 2013-2020 per multiple international assessments — the largest peacetime contraction recorded for any modern non-conflict economy.

The 2017 Constituent Assembly and the rupture: in March 2017 the Maduro-aligned Supreme Court attempted to assume the powers of the opposition-controlled National Assembly elected in December 2015. The post-March 2017 protests produced roughly 125 dead. On 4 August 2017 Maduro convoked a National Constituent Assembly under disputed procedures; the assembly assumed parallel-legislative authority. Delcy Rodríguez was its first president (August 2017 - June 2018).

The 2019 Guaidó claim and the Trump first-term sanctions architecture: on 23 January 2019 National Assembly president Juan Guaidó proclaimed himself interim president on the constitutional ground that the May 2018 Maduro re-election had been invalid. The Trump first-term administration, the Lima Group of Latin American governments, and roughly sixty other governments recognised Guaidó. The Maduro government retained operational control. The Trump first-term sanctions architecture imposed comprehensive US Treasury restrictions on Venezuelan oil sales, PDVSA banking access, and the Citgo subsidiary.

The hyperinflation period (2017-2021): annual inflation reached roughly 65,000% in 2018 per the IMF. The bolívar was redenominated three times. The post-2018 cumulative emigration reached roughly 7-8 million Venezuelans per UNHCR — the largest migration crisis in the Western Hemisphere in modern history. The 2021-2023 economic stabilisation produced single-digit inflation by 2023 alongside a roughly 70%-lower GDP than the pre-2013 peak.

The July 2024 election and the post-election rupture: the 28 July 2024 election was the political-economic inflection point of the entire presidency. Per the October 2023 Barbados Agreement between the Maduro government and the opposition coalition Plataforma Unitaria Democrática (PUD), the election was to be conducted under international observation. Opposition primary winner María Corina Machado was barred from candidacy on disputed administrative grounds. The PUD substituted retired diplomat Edmundo González Urrutia.

The 29 July 2024 National Electoral Council (CNE) announcement claimed Maduro 51.2% to González 44.2% without releasing precinct-level vote tallies. The opposition collected and published roughly 80% of the precinct-level acta tallies, which indicated González had won by roughly 67%-30%. The US Department of State, the Carter Center observation mission, and roughly sixty other governments declared González the legitimate winner. The Maduro government did not release the disputed tallies. The post-29-July-2024 protests produced 23 dead and roughly 2,000 detentions per Foro Penal figures. González went into exile in Spain in September 2024. Machado went into clandestinity in Venezuela. The Maduro government's December 2024 estimate of political prisoners reached over 2,000 — the largest political-prisoner population in the Western Hemisphere per the Center for Strategic and International Studies November 2025 analysis.

In October 2024 the Nobel Committee awarded the Nobel Peace Prize to Machado for her organisation of the post-July-2024 opposition documentation campaign. She received the prize while in clandestinity.

## Operation Resolve, 3 January 2026

The post-July-2024 second Trump administration moved across 2025 from sanctions-based pressure to military-deployment pressure. The post-September 2025 US Caribbean military build-up — including the deployment of over 150 military aircraft and an armada of naval vessels — produced the governing architecture from which the 3 January 2026 operation was launched.

The operation sequence per the *Washington Post*, NBC, and the House of Commons Library briefing on the operation. President Trump approved the strike before Christmas 2025 after months of Joint Chiefs planning that included troop training on a model of the Maduro Caracas compound. A CIA team had operated inside Venezuela for months tracking Maduro's daily movements. A late-December 2025 private phone call between Trump and Maduro produced Trump's "you got to surrender" demand; Maduro declined.

The launch was delayed by weather. On the night of 2-3 January 2026 a weather break opened a corridor; US Joint Chiefs Chairman General Dan Caine authorised launch. At roughly 2:00 a.m. local time on 3 January 2026, low-flying US aircraft were observed over Caracas. Seven explosions were reported in the city. US special operations forces breached the Maduro compound and captured Maduro and his wife as they attempted to reach a steel-doored safe room.

The casualties: the Venezuelan Interior Minister Diosdado Cabello stated on 7 January 2026 that roughly 100 Venezuelans had been killed in the operation. The Cuban government acknowledged on Facebook that 32 Cubans operating as security personnel on behalf of Venezuelan counterparts had been killed. Seven US service members were injured; no US service members were killed. Maduro and Flores were flown to the USS Iwo Jima and then to New York for arraignment.

The Trump statements: at a 3 January 2026 Mar-a-Lago press conference Trump stated the US was "going to run the country, until we can do a safe, proper and judicious transition" of power. He compared the operation to the prior US strike against Iranian nuclear facilities. He stated that Maduro and Flores faced Southern District of New York indictment on narco-terrorism charges from the multi-year US DEA-and-FBI Venezuelan-state-criminalisation case.

The institutional response: a spokesperson for the UN Secretary General António Guterres characterised the operation as "a dangerous precedent." The Russian, Chinese, Cuban, and Nicaraguan governments condemned the operation. The UK Government said it had not been involved in the operation but that it had long regarded Maduro as an illegitimate leader. Legal scholars and politicians across multiple jurisdictions questioned the operation's lawfulness under the UN Charter. House Foreign Affairs Committee Chairman Brian Mast and the US congressional Republican leadership endorsed the operation.

## Delcy Rodríguez — the acting president

Delcy Eloína Rodríguez Gómez was born on 18 May 1969 in Caracas. She is the daughter of Jorge Rodríguez Sr. — the founding leader of the Liga Socialista, killed in 1976 under conditions that the Carlos Andrés Pérez first administration's police forces have been documented to have caused. Her brother Jorge Rodríguez Jr. is the speaker of the post-2020 PSUV-controlled National Assembly. The Rodríguez siblings have been the central PSUV operators of the post-Chávez period alongside Cabello and the Maduro family.

Rodríguez studied law at the Central University of Venezuela and at the Sorbonne. She served as Minister of Foreign Affairs (26 December 2014 - 21 June 2017), then as president of the Constituent National Assembly (4 August 2017 - 14 June 2018), then as Vice President under Maduro (14 June 2018 - 5 January 2026), simultaneously holding Economy and Finance (10 September 2020 - 27 August 2024) and Petroleum and Hydrocarbons (27 August 2024 - 11 March 2026).

The 2017 Rodríguez-Trump-first-term opening: per the post-2026 NBC News reporting, in 2017 Rodríguez directed the Citgo subsidiary to donate $500,000 to the Trump first-term inauguration committee. The donation opened a channel of contact between Rodríguez and the early-Trump-first-term Washington political-class architecture. The channel was reactivated after the 3 January 2026 capture.

On 5 January 2026 Rodríguez was sworn in as acting president at a National Assembly ceremony administered by her brother Jorge Rodríguez Jr. She is the first woman to perform the duties of the Venezuelan presidency. In her inaugural address she declared that "there is only one president in this country, and his name is Nicolás Maduro" and characterised the US operation as a violation of the UN Charter. The same week she signalled to the Trump administration a willingness to negotiate.

## The post-capture apparatus

The post-3-January-2026 period has run across multiple competing tracks.

The Trump-Rodríguez bilateral track: the Trump administration has, documented per the *Washington Post* March 2026 reporting and the Ambassador James Story April 2026 *MS NOW* analysis, recognised the Rodríguez government and removed long-standing US sanctions against Rodríguez herself. Trump has referred to Rodríguez in public statements as "the president-elect" — the same designation Trump had previously refused for Machado-González. On 9 January 2026 the Caracas government under Rodríguez announced exploration of a process to restore diplomatic ties with Washington that had been severed in 2019 under the first Trump administration.

The opposition track: María Corina Machado emerged from clandestinity following the Maduro capture. She met Trump at the White House on 15 January 2026. The meeting did not produce a Trump endorsement; Trump signalled willingness to work with Rodríguez and dismissed Machado's credibility to govern Venezuela. González remained in Spanish exile.

The economic-policy track: in April 2026 the Venezuelan National Assembly under PSUV control passed a law opening Venezuelan mining to foreign investors — a stated priority of the Trump administration. The Trump-administration policy goal, articulated repeatedly by Trump himself, has been the resumption of Venezuelan oil exports to the US at 30 to 50 million barrels annually at market price. The post-March 2026 Petroleum Ministry change (Paula Henao replacing Rodríguez on 11 March 2026) was the signal of the Rodríguez government's transition from oil-and-finance management toward presidential operation.

The criminal-proceedings track: Maduro and Flores remain in US federal custody facing the Southern District of New York narco-terrorism indictment. The indictment alleges Maduro operated the Venezuelan state apparatus as a transnational criminal enterprise channelling cocaine through Venezuelan ports to US markets in collaboration with the Cartel de los Soles network of Venezuelan military officers. Maduro has denied the charges. As of May 2026 the case remains in pre-trial discovery.

The democratic-transition track: the *Washington Post* 13 March 2026 Abrams opinion piece characterised the post-3-January Trump-Rodríguez arrangement as "almost invisible" on democratic transition. The Trump administration's stated policy is that the US "will run the country" until a transition is feasible. No timetable has been announced.

## The net effect

The Nicolás Maduro biographical record is the public-facing record of the bus-driver-turned-Chávez-loyalist-turned-Foreign-Minister-turned-President of Venezuela. He held the office for thirteen years across the largest peacetime contraction of any Western Hemisphere economy. He won three presidential elections — 2013 by a thin margin, 2018 by a wide margin under opposition boycott, 2024 by an announced margin contradicted by the precinct-level acta tallies that the opposition collected. He was captured in his own capital by US special operations forces eighteen months after the disputed 2024 election he had refused to concede. He now sits in US federal custody awaiting trial in New York.

The Delcy Rodríguez record is the public-facing record of the multi-portfolio Chavista operator who has held five major Venezuelan cabinet positions since 2014 and who, in the seventy-two hours after the 3 January 2026 strike, transitioned from Maduro's Vice President to the operating partner of the US administration that had removed him.

The political record is the operation of a post-1999 Bolivarian state that consolidated under Chávez, governed under Maduro, faced collapse under the post-2014 oil-price shock and the post-2017 sanctions architecture, and now operates under US military-and-diplomatic supervision following the most consequential single US military intervention in the Western Hemisphere since the 1989 Panama operation.

The bus driver held office for thirteen years across the largest peacetime economic contraction in the Western Hemisphere, won three elections of declining credibility, and was extracted from his own capital by US special operations forces. His Vice President transitioned to US operating partner within seventy-two hours. The Bolivarian presentation ended in a New York federal courtroom.

The post-2026 Venezuelan settlement is, documented, the Latin American case study of an extra-territorial removal of a sitting head of state by the US armed forces, conducted on narco-terrorism predicate, with the active institutional cooperation of the Vice President and acting successor whom the same US administration had previously sanctioned. The architecture works.


---

# Dossier 60: Miguel Díaz-Canel and the Post-Castro Cuban Architecture

## The constructionure

The Republic of Cuba operates under the 2019 Constitution that replaced the 1976 socialist-era constitution. The 2019 reform retained the one-party system under the Communist Party of Cuba (PCC) but introduced a five-year presidential term limited to two consecutive terms — the first modern formal limit on tenure for the Cuban head of state. The 19th President of Cuba is Miguel Mario Díaz-Canel Bermúdez, who succeeded Raúl Castro on 19 April 2018 and was ratified for a second five-year term on 19 April 2023.

Díaz-Canel also serves as First Secretary of the Communist Party of Cuba (PCC) — the position he assumed on 19 April 2021 when Raúl Castro retired from the post. The PCC First Secretaryship is the apex political position of the Cuban state; the presidency is the formal head-of-state position. The 2021 transition closed the post-1959 Castro-family leadership of the Cuban revolutionary state — Fidel Castro from 1959 to 2008, Raúl Castro from 2008 to 2018 as president and from 2011 to 2021 as PCC First Secretary.

## Díaz-Canel — biographical foundation

Miguel Mario Díaz-Canel Bermúdez was born on 20 April 1960 in Placetas, Villa Clara province. His father Miguel Díaz-Canel was a mechanic at a sugar mill; his mother Aida Bermúdez was a schoolteacher. He attended the Central University of Las Villas, where he studied electronic engineering and graduated in 1982. He was a member of the Young Communist League and joined the Communist Party of Cuba during his university years.

His early career was in the Cuban Revolutionary Armed Forces — three years of compulsory military service after graduation — followed by a return to Villa Clara as an engineering instructor. He rose through the provincial PCC structure across the 1990s. In 1994 he became First Secretary of the Villa Clara provincial PCC committee. From 2003 to 2009 he served as First Secretary of the Holguín provincial PCC committee — the second of his two provincial-secretary tenures.

The pre-presidency record was that of a PCC provincial-and-national functionary. From 2009 to 2012 he served as Minister of Higher Education. From 2012 to 2018 he served as First Vice President under Raúl Castro — the position that the 2018 succession institutionalised as the heir-apparent role. He was selected for First Vice Presidency at the April 2013 National Assembly session.

The political-class point: Díaz-Canel rose through the post-Castro PCC by demonstrated reliability rather than charismatic-revolutionary credentials. His distinguishing feature in the PCC apparatus was the absence of the family-or-military-comradeship credentials that the surviving 26 July Movement generation had used to consolidate the post-1959 leadership. He was the first Cuban head of state born after the 1959 Revolution.

## The 2018 succession and the first term 2018-2023

On 19 April 2018 the Cuban National Assembly elected Díaz-Canel as President of the Council of State and the Council of Ministers, succeeding Raúl Castro. Castro retained the PCC First Secretaryship and the position of First Secretary of the Central Committee — institutional anchors that he held until 2021. Castro retained influence through the Cuban Revolutionary Armed Forces (FAR), the Ministry of the Interior (MININT), and the military-economic conglomerate GAESA (Gaviota Group / Grupo de Administración Empresarial S.A.) commanded by Raúl Castro's son-in-law Luis Alberto Rodríguez López-Calleja until his July 2022 death.

The 2018-2023 first term ran through four principal threads.

The 2019 constitutional reform: the referendum of 24 February 2019 approved the new constitution by roughly 87% per the official count. The reform introduced the presidential term limits, expanded recognition of private property, and removed the explicit reference to communism as the system's objective while retaining the one-party institutional structure.

The COVID-19 pandemic 2020-2022: Cuba developed three indigenous mRNA-and-protein-subunit COVID-19 vaccines (Abdala, Soberana 02, Soberana Plus) — the first developing-country indigenous-vaccine development of the pandemic. The vaccines were authorised and deployed across Cuba and exported to Iran, Vietnam, Venezuela, and Nicaragua. The Cuban excess-mortality figure was among the lowest in Latin America per multiple academic-comparative studies through 2022.

The currency unification (2021): on 1 January 2021 the Cuban government unified the dual-currency system (the convertible peso CUC and the Cuban peso CUP) at a single exchange rate against the dollar. The reform produced near-immediate inflationary effects across the Cuban economy that the post-2021 record has been unable to reverse. Annual inflation reached roughly 75% in 2021 and roughly 40% in 2022 per Cuban government figures.

The July 2021 protests: on 11 July 2021 the largest protests in post-1959 Cuban history erupted in San Antonio de los Baños and spread to Havana, Santiago de Cuba, and over 50 other cities. The protests were driven by power-outage frequency, COVID-19 conditions, and food-and-medicine shortages. The government response included internet shutdowns, mass detentions (over 1,400 detainees per multiple international human-rights organisations), and post-protest prosecutions producing sentences up to 30 years. The protests were the central public political-class crisis of the first Díaz-Canel term.

## The second term ratification and the 2023-2026 economic collapse

On 19 April 2023 the National Assembly ratified Díaz-Canel for a second five-year term with 459 votes from 462 legislators present. Per the 2019 Constitution this is his final term; he must leave office on 19 April 2028.

The post-2023 economic crisis has been characterised by multiple comparative analyses as Cuba's worst economic period since the post-1991 *Período Especial* — the post-Soviet-collapse decade-long crisis of the 1990s. GDP contracted in 2023 and 2024 per Cuban government figures. Cuban population data per the Cuban National Statistics Office (ONEI) showed the population falling below 10 million for the first time since the 1985 baseline — driven by emigration figures that the US Customs and Border Protection statistics confirmed at roughly 500,000-700,000 Cubans entering the US across 2022-2024 alone.

The energy crisis: by mid-2024 Cuba was experiencing daily multi-hour power outages across the entire country. The October-November 2024 nationwide grid failures produced rolling blackouts of up to 20 hours per day in some provinces. The crisis was driven by the post-2014 decline in Venezuelan oil shipments under the Petrocaribe-and-ALBA bilateral architecture, the deterioration of the Cuban refining infrastructure, and the post-2017 Trump-first-term sanctions architecture that the Biden administration had only partially relaxed.

## The post-3-January-2026 Trump-era confrontation

The 3 January 2026 US capture of Nicolás Maduro (Dossier 59) was the central inflection point of the post-2018 Cuban geopolitical position. Multiple immediate consequences.

The Cuban security personnel killed in Caracas: on 5 January 2026 the Cuban presidential office acknowledged on Facebook that 32 Cuban citizens had been killed during the US Operation Resolve strike on the Maduro compound. The acknowledgement stated the Cubans had been carrying out missions for Cuban Revolutionary Armed Forces and Ministry of the Interior structures at the request of Venezuelan counterparts. The acknowledgement is the most consequential post-2026 institutional documentation of the Cuban security-services presence in Venezuela.

The Venezuelan oil cutoff: on 11 January 2026 Trump announced that the post-3-January Rodríguez-Trump bilateral arrangement (Dossier 59) would terminate Venezuelan oil shipments and financial transfers to Cuba. The cutoff produced an immediate institutional crisis for the Cuban state apparatus. On 16 January 2026 Díaz-Canel chaired a Council of Ministers meeting that focused on "immediate" economic transformation; Reuters and NBC News coverage characterised the meeting as the most consequential post-2021 economic-policy statement of his presidency.

The 29 January 2026 executive order: Trump signed an executive order designating Cuba an "unusual and extraordinary threat" to US national security under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The order escalated post-2017 sanctions across multiple categories and threatened tariff penalties against countries continuing oil exports to Cuba.

The March 2026 US demand for Díaz-Canel's resignation: per the Al Jazeera 20 March 2026 reporting, Trump bilateral negotiation channels with the Cuban government indicated US willingness to relax energy sanctions if Díaz-Canel stepped down. The Cuban government, through Foreign Ministry spokesperson statements and Díaz-Canel's own April 2026 interviews, rejected the demand "categorically." The Polymarket March 2026 trading market on Díaz-Canel's removal by June 2026 priced low-double-digit probability — institutional confirmation that the consensus market read of the situation was that Díaz-Canel would complete his term to 2028 absent further escalation.

The AP reporting on Castro-family succession scenarios: per the AP March 2026 analysis cited in CiberCuba, multiple analysts have identified Oscar Pérez-Oliva Fraga and Raúl Guillermo Rodríguez Castro — the latter being Raúl Castro's grandson — as potential post-Díaz-Canel successors. The Cuban government has rejected any prospect of externally-induced transition. The 2026 institutional record is that the post-Castro consolidation under Díaz-Canel has not displaced the residual Raúl Castro and FAR-GAESA institutional centre of gravity.

## What it adds up to

The Miguel Díaz-Canel biographical record is the public-facing record of the Villa Clara electronic-engineering graduate who rose through the post-1959 PCC provincial-and-national apparatus to become the first non-Castro Cuban head of state since 1959. He held the First Vice Presidency for six years under Raúl Castro before the 2018 succession. He governed through the COVID-19 pandemic, the 2021 currency unification, the July 2021 protests, the post-2023 deepest economic crisis since the *Período Especial*, and the post-3-January-2026 Trump-era confrontation that has, documented, included the explicit US demand for his resignation and the termination of Venezuelan oil supply to the Cuban state.

The state record is the operation of a post-1959 revolutionary state that has, documented, lost the cumulative economic underpinning of its external alignments — first the Soviet collapse 1991, then the post-2014 Venezuelan oil-supply decline, then the post-3-January-2026 Trump-Rodríguez Venezuelan oil cutoff. The state apparatus continues to operate the one-party-system institutional architecture under the joint Díaz-Canel public-facing presidency and the residual Raúl Castro / FAR-GAESA institutional centre of gravity.

The first non-Castro head of state since 1959 governed through the currency unification, the July 2021 protests, the deepest economic crisis since the *Período Especial*, and the explicit US demand for his resignation. The Soviet subsidy ended in 1991. The Venezuelan oil ended in 2026. The one-party architecture continues. The economic foundation does not.

The post-2018 Cuban state under Díaz-Canel is, documented, the Latin American case study of a one-party post-revolutionary state operating under the sustained US sanctions architecture that the prior dossiers in this volume have documented in their various Latin American and Caribbean implementations. The architecture works as designed.

# Dossier 61: Gabriel Boric, José Antonio Kast, and the Post-2019 Chilean Political-Class Realignment

## The Chilean operational architecture

The Republic of Chile operates under the 1980 Constitution drafted under the Pinochet dictatorship (1973-1990) and amended multiple times since the post-1990 democratic restoration. The presidential system permits a single four-year term with no consecutive re-election. The Chilean Congress operates as a bicameral legislature with a 155-seat Chamber of Deputies and a 50-seat Senate, both elected on staggered cycles that the post-2017 reform realigned with the presidential cycle.

The 38th President is José Antonio Kast Rist, inaugurated on 11 March 2026 after winning the 14 December 2025 runoff against Communist Party candidate Jeannette Jara with 58.2% of the vote — the largest single presidential-election margin in the post-1990 democratic period. He is the leader of the Republican Party of Chile (Republicanos), the post-2019 hard-right party that he founded after his exit from the centre-right Unión Demócrata Independiente (UDI). His term runs to 11 March 2030.

His predecessor Gabriel Boric Font held the presidency 11 March 2022 to 11 March 2026 — a single non-consecutive term that closed with the Boric coalition's defeat in the December 2025 election. The Boric-to-Kast transition is the most consequential single-cycle right-ward realignment of Chilean post-1990 democratic-period politics.

## The 2019 *estallido social* and the 2020-2022 constitutional process

On 18 October 2019 a Santiago Metro fare increase produced the largest sustained protest period in Chilean post-1990 history. The protests across October-November 2019 produced over 30 dead, over 11,000 injured, and roughly 460 documented eye injuries from Carabineros police use of pellet-fire ammunition per the Chilean National Institute of Human Rights (INDH) and the UN OHCHR observation reports. The Piñera second-term government deployed the military to multiple Chilean cities — the first such deployment since the post-1990 democratic restoration.

The 15 November 2019 *Acuerdo por la Paz Social y la Nueva Constitución* — signed by all major Chilean parliamentary parties including the centre-right Coalición Vamos por Chile and the centre-left Concertación-and-Frente-Amplio bloc — produced the opening for the constitutional process. The 25 October 2020 referendum produced 78.3% support for drafting a new constitution. The constitutional convention was elected on 15-16 May 2021 with 155 elected representatives across an unprecedented gender-parity-and-Indigenous-reserved-seat architecture.

The draft constitution was presented on 4 July 2022 — three months after Boric's inauguration. The 4 September 2022 referendum rejected the draft 62%-38%. The second constitutional process produced a draft drawn principally by Republican Party and right-wing convention members in 2023; the 17 December 2023 referendum rejected that draft 55%-44%. Chile remains under the 1980 Pinochet-era constitution as amended.

## Gabriel Boric — biographical foundation and presidency

Gabriel Boric Font was born on 11 February 1986 in Punta Arenas, Magallanes region — Chile's southernmost province. His father Luis Borić Scarpa is of Croatian descent; his mother María Soledad Font of Catalan descent. He attended the British School of Punta Arenas before relocating to Santiago for university. He studied law at the University of Chile but did not complete his degree.

He came to national attention as a leader of the 2011-2012 Chilean student protests demanding free public higher education. As President of the University of Chile Student Federation (FECh) from December 2011 to November 2012, he was one of the four central student-movement leaders alongside Camila Vallejo, Giorgio Jackson, and Camilo Ballesteros. The student-movement cohort produced four of the principal post-2019 Chilean political figures — Boric, Vallejo (Communist Party), Jackson (Boric's first interior-and-then-social-development minister), and Karol Cariola (Communist Party deputy).

Boric was elected to the Chamber of Deputies for the Magallanes region in 2013 and re-elected in 2017. He helped found the Frente Amplio (Broad Front) coalition in 2017. He won the December 2021 presidential election in the second round against José Antonio Kast — 55.9% to 44.1% — having narrowly placed second in the first round behind Kast.

Boric took office on 11 March 2022 at age 36 — the youngest president in Chilean history. His first cabinet had majority-female composition and the average age was the lowest in any post-1990 Chilean government.

The 2022-2026 record had four principal threads.

Economic stewardship had three threads. Persistent inflation peaked at roughly 14% in late 2022 before declining to single digits by 2024. GDP growth held below 2% per year across the term. And the post-2017 Chilean lithium-extraction architecture continued to expand under the public-private-partnership framework that Boric had campaigned against as a candidate.

Constitutional defeats: the September 2022 first-draft rejection and the December 2023 second-draft rejection eliminated the central programmatic commitment of the Boric campaign. The two defeats institutionalised the 1980 Pinochet-era constitutional framework as the operating constraint of any post-2026 Chilean government.

Migration and security: the post-2021 Venezuelan-Colombian-Haitian migration wave to Chile produced the post-2022 increase in registered crime that dominated the 2022-2026 political cycle. The Boric government's response — increased police staffing, new immigration legislation, and tightened northern-border controls — was characterised by both his coalition's left flank as a betrayal and by the right opposition as inadequate.

Cabinet turnover: the multi-cycle cabinet reshuffles across 2022-2026 produced the post-2024 transitions of Carolina Tohá from Interior to candidate, Camila Vallejo from spokesperson to General Secretariat, and Jeannette Jara from Labor to presidential candidate. The 7 April 2025 transition of Labor Minister Jeannette Jara to candidate (succeeded by Giorgio Boccardo) marked the commitment of the post-Boric centre-left to the Communist Party candidacy.

## The 2025 election and the Kast victory

The June 2025 primary of the post-Boric centre-left coalition produced Jeannette Jara as the unified candidate. Jara — born 1973, Communist Party member, Boric's Labor Minister 2022-2025, architect of the post-2023 minimum-wage-and-pension legislative package — became the first Communist Party candidate to advance to a Chilean presidential runoff in the post-1990 democratic period.

The 16 November 2025 first round was the first Chilean election conducted under the post-2022 mandatory-voting-and-automatic-registration reform. The 5-6 million new voters who entered the registered electorate (per pre-election multiple-source estimates) skewed younger, male, and lower-middle-class — a demographic that the post-2021 surveys had identified as anti-establishment and crime-focused.

The first round produced Jara 26.8%, Kast 23.9%, Republican Party-aligned Evelyn Matthei (Chile Vamos) 12.5%, and Franco Parisi (Partido de la Gente) 11.7%. The post-first-round consolidation produced the centre-right Matthei endorsement of Kast and the Parisi voter migration toward Kast.

The 14 December 2025 runoff produced Kast 58.2% to Jara 41.8% — a margin of roughly 16 percentage points and the largest second-round margin in any Chilean post-1990 presidential election. Kast carried every Chilean region — the first such regional sweep in the post-1990 period. Boric phoned Kast to concede on election night; Jara conceded in a social-media statement. On 15 December 2025 Kast met Boric at La Moneda Palace.

The political-class point: the Kast 58.2% result was achieved with the new automatic-registration electorate that had previously not voted under the pre-2022 voluntary-registration system. The same demographic produced the Bukele-style anti-establishment outcomes documented in the regional comparative analyses across the late-2024-to-late-2025 cycle.

## José Antonio Kast — biographical foundation

José Antonio Kast Rist was born on 18 January 1966 in Santiago. His father Michael Kast was a German Wehrmacht soldier who emigrated to Chile in 1950 — a biographical fact that the Chilean and international press has covered across his multiple presidential runs. His brother Miguel Kast was the Pinochet-era Minister of Planning (ODEPLAN) 1978-1980 and the principal architect of the Pinochet-era market-liberalisation programme; Miguel Kast died in 1983.

José Antonio Kast graduated in law from the Pontifical Catholic University of Chile. He has been married to Pía Adriasola since 1990 and is the father of nine children — a biographical fact that has figured in the multi-cycle Chilean political-class portrayal of his social conservatism.

He served on the Buin City Council 1996-2000 and as a Chamber of Deputies member 2002-2018 representing the UDI on three consecutive terms. In 2016 he left the UDI; in 2017 he ran as an independent in the first round, finishing fourth with 7.9%. In 2018 he founded the Acción Republicana social-network movement; in 2019 it formalised as the Partido Republicano (Republican Party). In 2021 he won the first round of the presidential election with 27.9% before losing to Boric in the runoff.

His public position across the multi-cycle campaign period has been opposition to abortion, to same-sex marriage, to the 2019 constitutional reform process, to undocumented immigration, and to the post-2017 lithium-extraction public-private framework as administered by the Boric government. He has been characterised across multiple international-press profiles as the most ideologically explicit post-1990 Chilean political figure to defend the legacy of the Pinochet dictatorship — a characterisation he has rejected in repeated public statements.

## The Kast presidency and the 11 March 2026 inauguration

Kast took office on 11 March 2026. His pre-inauguration December 2025 - March 2026 transition included multiple bilateral conversations with the second Trump administration that have been characterised across the Latin American post-2025 cycle as the central US-Chile bilateral realignment.

His pre-inauguration commitments: deportation of undocumented immigrants beginning on inauguration day; reversal of the post-2023 pension-reform legislation; restoration of the post-2017 lithium-extraction framework to majority-private composition; security-budget expansion; and full alignment of Chilean foreign policy with the US-Argentine-Ecuadorian-Bolivian post-2025 right-wing realignment.

The first-100-days record (March-May 2026) cannot yet be fully audited at the date of writing of this dossier, but the structural posture of the Kast government is the central Latin American post-2025 case of a hard-right party assuming office through democratic-electoral channels after running on an explicit pro-Pinochet-era-economic-architecture platform.

## The last word

The Gabriel Boric biographical record is the public-facing record of the Magallanes-born student-movement leader who entered Congress at 27, helped found the Frente Amplio, and won the 2021 presidency at 35. He presided over a single four-year term that closed with the two-time rejection of the constitutional reform his campaign had centred, and with the December 2025 election of the candidate he had defeated four years earlier.

The José Antonio Kast biographical record is the public-facing record of the UDI Chamber of Deputies member who founded the Republican Party after leaving the centre-right, ran for president three times across 2017, 2021, and 2025, and won on the third attempt with the largest post-1990 runoff margin under the new mandatory-voting electoral architecture.

The apparatus record is the operation of a Chilean political-class realignment that has, documented, displaced the post-1990 Concertación-and-Frente-Amplio centre-left from La Moneda and produced the most explicit post-1990 Pinochet-era-economic-architecture restoration government on offer in any contemporary Latin American jurisdiction.

The student-movement leader became president at 35 and lost the constitutional reform twice. The Pinochet-legacy candidate he had defeated four years earlier won the next election with the largest post-1990 runoff margin. The Chilean electorate chose. The institutional record documents what they chose between.

The post-2025 Chilean political-class realignment is, documented, the Latin American case study of an electorally validated transition from a centre-left student-movement-origin government to a hard-right Pinochet-legacy-defence government within a single four-year cycle. The architecture works.


---

# Dossier 62: The Peruvian Presidential Sequence — Castillo, Boluarte, Jerí, Balcázar, and the 2026 Election

## The administrative machineryure

The Republic of Peru operates under the 1993 Constitution drafted under Alberto Fujimori. The presidential system permits a single five-year term with no consecutive re-election. The 1993 Constitution preserves a unicameral 130-seat Congress until the post-April-2026 transition to the bicameral Senate-and-Chamber-of-Deputies architecture restored under the constitutional reform package that the post-2024 Congress approved.

Peru has had eight presidents in the past ten years. The Council on Foreign Relations April 2026 framing — "the so-called presidential curse" — captures the fact that no Peruvian president since Ollanta Humala (2011-2016) has completed a five-year term. The current incumbent, José María Balcázar, is an 83-year-old transitional figure who took office on 17 February 2026 after Congress impeached his predecessor José Jerí four months into Jerí's tenure.

The 12 April 2026 first round of the post-Castillo-cycle general election produced Keiko Fujimori (Fuerza Popular, 17.19%) and Roberto Sánchez (left coalition) advancing to the 7 June 2026 runoff. The election day produced delivery-of-materials failures, an unprecedented one-day extension at Lima polling stations, a five-week vote-counting period, and the 21 April 2026 resignation of the head of the electoral agency. The post-runoff inauguration scheduled for 28 July 2026 — the 205th anniversary of Peruvian independence — will install Peru's ninth president of the decade.

## Pedro Castillo and the 2021 election

Pedro Castillo Terrones was born on 19 October 1969 in Puña, Cajamarca region. He was a rural primary-school teacher in the Chota district of Cajamarca for over twenty years before his political emergence. He came to national attention as the leader of the 2017 Peruvian teachers' strike. He ran for the presidency in 2021 on the Perú Libre ticket — a small far-left party founded in 2007 by Vladimir Cerrón, a Cuban-trained physician and former regional governor of Junín.
