The June 2021 runoff produced Castillo 50.15% to Keiko Fujimori 49.85% — a margin of roughly 44,000 votes out of roughly 17.6 million cast. Fujimori contested the result on fraud grounds that the Peruvian National Jury of Elections (JNE) rejected after a six-week post-election review. Castillo took office on 28 July 2021. His cabinet was the first cabinet in modern Peruvian history led by a Cabinet Chief — Guido Bellido — from outside the Lima political-class establishment.

The 2021-2022 Castillo tenure ran through five cabinet reshuffles, two failed impeachment attempts (in November 2021 and March 2022), and the multi-month investigative inquiries by the Peruvian Attorney General into the Castillo family's business interests in Chota.

On 7 December 2022 Castillo attempted what Peruvian and international press described as an *autogolpe* (self-coup): he announced the dissolution of Congress, the convocation of a constitutional assembly, and the introduction of emergency-rule decrees. Within hours Congress impeached him by 101-6 vote and his Vice President Dina Boluarte assumed the presidency. Castillo was arrested at the Lima police prefecture and charged with rebellion, conspiracy, and abuse of authority. He has remained in detention at Barbadillo Prison since.

In November 2025 — the month after Boluarte's removal — a Peruvian court sentenced Castillo to 11 years in prison for the December 2022 *autogolpe* attempt. He maintains that he is a political prisoner.

## Dina Boluarte and the 2022-2025 tenure

Dina Ercilia Boluarte Zegarra was born on 31 May 1962 in Chalhuanca, Apurímac region. She practised law and worked for the Peruvian national civil registry RENIEC across the 1980s-2010s. She joined Perú Libre in 2017 and was Castillo's running mate on the 2021 ticket. She served as Minister of Development and Social Inclusion 2021-2022 alongside the Vice Presidency.

She took office on 7 December 2022 within two hours of Castillo's impeachment vote. She became Peru's first female president. Her stated commitment was to serve out Castillo's term to July 2026.

The 2022-2025 tenure had three principal threads.

The post-Castillo protests and the 2022-2023 repression: protests erupted across Peru immediately after Castillo's removal, principally in the Andean southern regions of Cusco, Puno, Apurímac, and Ayacucho — the regional base of Castillo's electoral coalition. The Boluarte government deployed Peruvian National Police and Armed Forces. Over 50 people were killed and over 1,500 injured across the December 2022 - April 2023 period per the post-protest Peruvian Ombudsman's Office count, the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights findings, and the UN OHCHR observation reports. The Juliaca massacre of 9 January 2023 (Puno region, 17 dead in a single police-and-military operation) was the most consequential single incident.

The 2024 *Rolexgate* scandal: investigative reporting in March-April 2024 documented Boluarte's possession of multiple Rolex watches and other high-value jewellery that did not appear in her asset declarations. The investigation produced multiple congressional impeachment motions and Attorney General-led criminal investigations across 2024-2025. Boluarte survived eight impeachment attempts before the eight-attempt sequence's final outcome.

The October 2025 removal: on 10 October 2025 Congress impeached and removed Boluarte from office on grounds of "permanent moral incapacity" — the constitutional provision under which Castillo had been removed and which the 1993 Constitution permits Congress to invoke by simple majority. The president of Congress, José Jerí, was sworn in as her successor within hours.

## José Jerí — four months

José Enrique Jerí Oré was born in Lima. He served as Congressman 2021-2026 representing the Somos Perú party. He was elected President of Congress in mid-2025. On 10 October 2025 he ascended to the presidency upon Boluarte's removal.

His tenure ran for four months. On 17 February 2026 Congress impeached and removed him over corruption allegations including reported undisclosed meetings with a Chinese businessman under government scrutiny per the Rio Times Online April 2026 reporting. The vote installed José María Balcázar — the new president of Congress — as the next president by automatic constitutional succession.

## José María Balcázar — the transitional caretaker

José María Balcázar Jiménez was born in 1942. He served as a Perú Libre congressman across multiple terms. He was elected President of Congress on 17 February 2026 in a contest against Héctor Acuña, María del Carmen Alva, and Edgard Reymundo. The election to the Congress presidency triggered the automatic constitutional ascension to the national presidency upon Jerí's removal the same day.

Balcázar took office on 17 February 2026 at age 83 — the oldest person to hold the Peruvian presidency in the post-1990 democratic period. His tenure was  designated as transitional from inauguration — bridging the gap until the April 2026 general election produced an elected successor for the 28 July 2026 inauguration.

## The April 2026 first round and the June 2026 runoff

The 12 April 2026 first round had 34 registered candidates — a record number per the Peruvian electoral office. Three candidates polled in double digits: Keiko Fujimori (Fuerza Popular), Roberto Sánchez (left coalition), and Rafael López Aliaga (ultra-conservative former mayor of Lima).

The election-day failures: delayed delivery of electoral materials disrupted voting at dozens of polling stations. The Lima electoral district and overseas-voting sites operated under an unprecedented one-day voting extension on 13 April 2026. The review of disputed vote tally sheets produced a five-week count. On 21 April 2026 the head of the electoral agency resigned under public pressure.

The first-round result (confirmed by Peru's National Elections Board on 18 May 2026): Keiko Fujimori 17.19% (2.8 million votes), Roberto Sánchez second, López Aliaga third. Fujimori advanced to her fourth consecutive presidential runoff — the only Peruvian politician of the post-1990 period to compete in four runoffs (2011 against Humala, 2016 against Kuczynski, 2021 against Castillo, 2026 against Sánchez).

The 7 June 2026 runoff between Fujimori and Sánchez will determine Peru's ninth president of the decade. The post-April reinstated bicameral legislative architecture has produced a Congress in which the right-wing parties of Fujimori and López Aliaga together command majorities in both the new Senate and the lower house per the post-April 2026 reporting.

## Keiko Fujimori — biographical foundation

Keiko Sofía Fujimori Higuchi was born on 25 May 1975 in Lima. She is the eldest daughter of Alberto Fujimori (President of Peru 1990-2000) and Susana Higuchi. At age 19 she paused her university education to serve as Peru's First Lady (1994-2000) after her parents' bitter public separation — an unprecedented role for a sitting president's daughter. She completed an MBA at Columbia Business School and at Boston University.

She served one congressional term (2006-2011) for the Alianza por el Futuro coalition. In 2010 she founded Fuerza Popular as the post-Fujimori dynastic political vehicle. She lost the 2011 runoff to Humala (51.4%-48.6%), the 2016 runoff to Kuczynski (50.1%-49.9%), and the 2021 runoff to Castillo (50.15%-49.85%) — a multi-cycle pattern of narrow second-round defeats that has institutionalised the Fujimorista coalition as Peru's principal right-wing electoral force without ever winning the presidency.

She was held in pre-trial detention multiple times across 2018-2022 on charges related to Odebrecht corruption payments to her presidential campaigns. The cases have not produced convictions through May 2026.

The 2026 Fujimori candidacy is her fourth run. Her stated programme: increased security to lower the homicide rate, armed-forces participation in immigration-control operations, police-search expansion, continuation of the post-1990 free-market economic model, and a Salvadoran-Bukele-influenced approach to crime control.

## The endgame

The Pedro Castillo biographical record is the public-facing record of the Cajamarca rural teacher who won the 2021 presidency by a 44,000-vote margin, attempted an *autogolpe* eighteen months later, was impeached within hours, and has remained imprisoned at Barbadillo since.

The Dina Boluarte record is the public-facing record of the Apurímac civil-registry lawyer who succeeded Castillo as Peru's first female president, governed for nearly three years through the 50+-dead post-Castillo protest repression and the *Rolexgate* corruption sequence, and was removed by congressional impeachment on 10 October 2025.

The Jerí-and-Balcázar records are the public-facing records of two successive Congress presidents who became Peruvian president by automatic constitutional succession — Jerí for four months until his February 2026 impeachment over corruption allegations; Balcázar as the 83-year-old transitional figure bridging to the 28 July 2026 inauguration.

What the record documents is the operation of a Peruvian constitutional architecture that has, across the decade 2016-2026, produced eight presidents through the 1993 Constitution's "permanent moral incapacity" impeachment provision and the constitutional-succession architecture that places the President of Congress next in line. The cumulative effect has been the governingisation of congressional power over the executive — a permanent structural feature of the post-1993 Peruvian state that the 1993 Constitution's drafters under the elder Fujimori had not anticipated.

The biographical records do not contradict the record. They are the operating faces of a constitutional architecture that the political-class operators have learned to use, repeatedly, to remove sitting presidents on the floor of Congress without recourse to electoral validation.

The post-2016 Peruvian sequence is, documented, the Latin American case study of a constitutional architecture that delivers executive instability as a permanent institutional output. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 63: Daniel Noboa and the Post-Correa Ecuadorian Realignment

## The frameworkure

The Republic of Ecuador operates under the 2008 Constitution drafted under Rafael Correa. The presidential system has been modified multiple times across the 2008-2018 amendment cycles; the 2008 architecture permits a single four-year term with one consecutive re-election after the 2017 referendum reversal of the unlimited-reelection amendment Correa had pushed through in 2015. The 47th President is Daniel Roy Gilchrist Noboa Azín, who took office on 23 November 2023 to complete the term of resigned predecessor Guillermo Lasso, and was re-elected on 13 April 2025 to a full four-year term running to May 2029.

The parliamentary architecture: a 151-seat National Assembly elected on the same cycle as the presidency. The post-2025 election produced a National Assembly in which Noboa's Acción Democrática Nacional (ADN) holds the largest single bloc but not a working majority; the post-March 2026 government has operated through coalitions with the right-wing Construye party and through bilateral agreements with conservative independent deputies.

## Noboa — biographical foundation

Daniel Roy Gilchrist Noboa Azín was born on 30 November 1987 in Miami, Florida — a fact that the post-2023 Correista opposition repeatedly cited in attempts to challenge his Ecuadorian citizenship credentials. He is the son of Álvaro Noboa, the multi-time Ecuadorian presidential candidate (five unsuccessful runs across 1998-2013) and the heir to the Noboa banana export-and-shipping empire built by his father Luis Noboa Naranjo. The Noboa Group remains one of the largest privately held conglomerates in Ecuador.

Noboa attended high school in the United States. He completed a bachelor's degree at New York University, a master's degree at Harvard Kennedy School, an MBA at the IE Business School in Madrid, and a master's degree at George Washington University. He worked across multiple roles in the Noboa Group business operations across the 2010s, including the international division of the family's container-shipping operations.

His political career began in 2021 when he was elected to the National Assembly for Guayas province on the centre-left CREO-PSC coalition ticket. He chaired the Assembly's Economic Development Commission. His record in the Assembly was limited — he served less than two years before resigning to run for the presidency in the 2023 snap election.

## The 2023 snap election

The 2023 election was triggered by President Guillermo Lasso's invocation of the *muerte cruzada* (mutual death) constitutional provision on 17 May 2023. The provision allows the president to dissolve the National Assembly and trigger new elections, in exchange for facing the same electoral test himself. Lasso invoked the provision in the face of imminent impeachment over the *Caso Encuentro* corruption investigation. The Constitutional Court accepted the invocation; both the executive and the legislative were dissolved.

The 20 August 2023 first round was held under the shadow of the 9 August 2023 assassination of presidential candidate Fernando Villavicencio — the centre-right anti-corruption journalist who had been polling in fourth place. Villavicencio was shot dead at a Quito campaign rally; six Colombian nationals associated with the Los Lobos criminal organisation were arrested. The assassination was widely characterised across Ecuadorian and international press as the inflection point of contemporary Ecuadorian institutional collapse.

The first-round result: Luisa González 33.6%, Daniel Noboa 23.7%, Christian Zurita 16.4% (the post-Villavicencio Movimiento Construye replacement candidate), Otto Sonnenholzner 7.0%, Yaku Pérez 4.0%. Noboa advanced to the runoff as the surprise second-place finisher; he had polled in single digits a month before the election.

The 15 October 2023 runoff produced Noboa 51.8% to González 48.2%. He took office on 23 November 2023 at age 35 — one of the youngest heads of state in Latin American history. His tenure was constitutionally limited to completing the remainder of Lasso's term, which expired on 24 May 2025.

## The first term (November 2023 - May 2025)

The first eighteen months of the Noboa presidency had four principal threads.

The January 2024 *conflicto armado interno* declaration: on 7 January 2024 the criminal leader José Adolfo Macías Villamar (known as "Fito"), head of the Los Choneros organisation, escaped from the Guayaquil regional prison where he had been held since 2011. On 9 January 2024 masked men with explosives stormed the TC Televisión Guayaquil studio during a live broadcast. The same day Noboa issued Decreto Ejecutivo 111 declaring an "internal armed conflict" and designating 22 criminal organisations as terrorist groups. The decree authorised the deployment of the Armed Forces in domestic security operations under terms previously reserved for declared international conflict.

Plan Fénix: the post-January-2024 security architecture included nightly curfews in Guayaquil, Esmeraldas, Manabí, and other coastal-province cities; multi-thousand-arrest sweeps across prison populations; the construction of a Bukele-model maximum-security prison in Santa Elena province; and bilateral security cooperation with the United States including the 2024 negotiation of a US military-bases agreement that the 2008 Correa-era constitutional amendment had previously prohibited.

The April 2024 Mexican-embassy incident: on 5 April 2024 Ecuadorian National Police broke into the Mexican embassy in Quito to arrest former Vice President Jorge Glas, who had been granted asylum by the López Obrador government. The breach of diplomatic immunity produced Mexico's immediate diplomatic break with Ecuador and Nicaragua's parallel break, and produced ICJ proceedings against Ecuador filed by Mexico that the Court has reviewed across 2024-2026.

The 2024 electricity crisis: a multi-month drought across the Andean watershed produced reduced hydroelectric output across Ecuador's principal generation facilities. The October-November 2024 period saw rotating blackouts of up to 14 hours daily across the entire country. The crisis produced sharp public-opinion deterioration that the 2025 campaign re-litigated.

## The April 2025 re-election

The 9 February 2025 first round produced Noboa 44.17% to González 43.97% — a 0.2 percentage-point margin that was the closest first-round result in modern Ecuadorian history. The runoff campaign February-April 2025 ran principally on security and the economy.

On the eve of the 13 April 2025 runoff Noboa declared a 60-day state of emergency in seven of Ecuador's 24 provinces. The decree suspended constitutional protections against warrantless search-and-entry; limited public-gathering rights; and imposed nightly curfews. Phones were prohibited at polling stations under a separate electoral-authority decision that Noboa had requested.

The 13 April 2025 runoff produced Noboa 55.65% to González 44.35%. González and the Revolución Ciudadana party rejected the result. González characterised the election as "the most grotesque fraud in Ecuador's history." The European Union observation mission, the Organization of American States observation mission, and the Carter Center observation mission found procedural irregularities but did not characterise the result as fraudulent.

The political-class point: the 18-percentage-point swing between the first round (statistical tie) and the runoff (11-point Noboa margin) is the central forensic question of the April 2025 election that the post-2025 opposition documentation continues to litigate without institutional resolution.

## The second term institutional consolidation 2025-2026

The post-May 2025 Noboa government has consolidated power across multiple institutional fronts per the Democratic Erosion April 2026 documentation, the Buenos Aires Times April 2026 reporting, and the Human Rights Watch 2026 World Report (February 2026).

Constitutional Court confrontation: in mid-2025 the Noboa government attempted to remove the Constitutional Court from its building by sending a vacate notice from the Ministry of Energy citing an obscure occupancy-agreement technicality. In March 2026 the Noboa-appointed Attorney General opened criminal investigations against two Constitutional Court judges alleging financial misconduct.

Opposition party suspension: on 6 March 2026 an electoral judge acting on the Attorney General's request ordered the suspension of Revolución Ciudadana — the Correa-aligned main opposition party — for nine months. The order was subsequently appealed.

Electoral calendar manipulation: the National Electoral Council moved the local elections scheduled for February 2027 to November 2026, officially citing concerns about El Niño weather disruptions. The post-decision opposition characterisation was that the rescheduling was  designed to capture local offices under conditions of continued Revolución Ciudadana suspension.

Media confrontation: in April 2026 the front pages of two widely circulated Ecuadorian newspapers (*Expreso* and *Extra*) appeared blank. The publisher of both papers stated that the publication had been taken over by alleged government action. The Human Rights Watch 2026 World Report characterised the multi-year Noboa-administration record as "advanced laws and measures that endanger rights, undermine the independence and security of Constitutional Court justices, and threaten constitutional safeguards."

Security record continuation: January 2025 was Ecuador's most violent month on record with 781 homicides per Ecuadorian National Police figures. Homicide totals across 2024 and 2025 remained above pre-2023 levels despite the multi-year *conflicto armado interno* architecture. The forced-disappearance cases linked to military operations — including the case of four Black children kidnapped and killed by a military patrol in Guayaquil in December 2024 — produced the central post-2024 human-rights-litigation record of the security architecture.

## The documented consequence

The Daniel Noboa biographical record is the public-facing record of the heir to Ecuador's largest banana-export fortune who entered the National Assembly at 33, won the snap-election presidency at 35, declared internal armed conflict against organised crime at 36, and was re-elected to a full four-year term at 37 with an unprecedented post-tie 11-point swing.

The functional record is the operation of an Ecuadorian centre-right-to-hard-right government that has, documented, consolidated executive power against the Constitutional Court, the Attorney General's office, the principal opposition party, the electoral calendar, and the principal opposition press; that has militarised domestic security under multi-year states of emergency without producing the homicide-rate reduction that has been the principal stated objective; and that has aligned Ecuadorian foreign policy explicitly with the second Trump administration including the post-2024 US military-bases negotiation.

The banana heir declared internal armed conflict, militarised domestic security under multi-year states of emergency, consolidated executive power against every institutional check, and was re-elected with an unprecedented post-tie swing — while the homicide rate remained above pre-crisis levels. The security emergency was the stated objective. The executive consolidation was the documented outcome.

The post-2023 Noboa Ecuadorian government is, documented, the Latin American case study of a centre-right elected administration consolidating into a semi-authoritarian governing architecture under cover of the public-security emergency. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 64: Xiomara Castro, Nasry Asfura, and the 2026 Honduran Realignment

## The Honduran institutional design

The Republic of Honduras operates under the 1982 Constitution drafted in the post-Cold-War democratic restoration period. The presidential system permits a single four-year term with no consecutive re-election — the constitutional architecture that the 2009 coup against Manuel Zelaya was triggered by Zelaya's pursuit of an amendment. There is no second-round runoff; the candidate with the most votes wins.

The parliamentary architecture: a 128-seat National Congress elected on the same cycle as the presidency. The post-November 2025 election produced a Congress in which the centre-right Partido Nacional (PN) and the Partido Liberal (PL) together hold majorities over the post-2011 left-wing Libertad y Refundación (Libre) party founded by Manuel Zelaya and Xiomara Castro.

The 40th President is Nasry Juan Asfura Zablah, sworn in on 27 January 2026 after winning the 30 November 2025 election by a margin of 0.74 percentage points — roughly 30,000 votes out of roughly 4 million cast. He succeeded the 39th President, Iris Xiomara Castro Sarmiento, whose 27 January 2022 - 27 January 2026 term marked the first time a left-wing president had completed a full term in Honduras.

## Xiomara Castro — biographical foundation

Iris Xiomara Castro Sarmiento was born on 30 September 1959 in Santa Bárbara, Honduras. She married Manuel Zelaya in 1976. Zelaya served as President of Honduras 2006-2009. On 28 June 2009 Zelaya was removed in a military coup carried out by the Honduran Armed Forces and ratified by the National Congress on the constitutional ground that Zelaya had attempted to convoke a non-binding referendum on constitutional revision. The coup was condemned by the OAS, the UN General Assembly, the EU, and the Obama-administration US State Department, although the post-coup interim government held Honduran sovereignty until the November 2009 election.

Castro emerged from First Lady to political leadership in the post-2009 resistance movement against the coup. In 2011 she co-founded with Zelaya the Partido Libertad y Refundación (Libre) — the post-coup left-wing party that absorbed multiple smaller groupings into a unified opposition force against the post-coup PN-PL bipartisan consolidation under Porfirio Lobo Sosa (2010-2014) and Juan Orlando Hernández (2014-2022).

She ran for president in 2013 and lost to Hernández. In 2017 she was Salvador Nasralla's running mate on the Alianza de Oposición ticket; the ticket narrowly lost to Hernández in a result that the OAS observation mission and the US State Department characterised as marred by procedural irregularities including a 36-hour vote-tabulation halt that the post-halt count reversed.

The 2021 election: Castro won the 28 November 2021 first-and-only round with 51.1% of the vote against Asfura (36.9%) and Yani Rosenthal (10.1%). She became the first president from outside the post-1982 Partido Nacional - Partido Liberal bipartisan duopoly. Nasralla was her running mate as First Vice President.

## The Castro presidency 2022-2026

Castro took office on 27 January 2022 with a coalition government composed of Libre, Nasralla's Partido Salvador de Honduras (PSH), and the Partido Innovación y Unidad (PINU). The post-2022 institutional record had four principal threads.

The Juan Orlando Hernández extradition: on 21 April 2022 the Honduran government extradited former president Hernández (2014-2022) to the United States. The Southern District of New York indictment charged Hernández with multi-year operation of a transnational cocaine-trafficking enterprise channeling Colombian cocaine through Honduras to US markets. The March 2024 trial produced a guilty verdict on all three counts; the June 2024 sentencing produced a 45-year federal prison sentence. The Hernández extradition was the central post-2022 Honduran institutional act — the first extradition of a sitting or former Honduran head of state to the United States in Honduran history.

The Maras and the post-2022 security situation: the post-2022 Castro government maintained the multi-year *estado de excepción* (state of emergency) covering the *Mara Salvatrucha* (MS-13) and *Barrio 18* operating areas across multiple Honduran departments. Homicide rates declined across 2022-2024 per the Honduran National Police figures from a 2010s peak of roughly 90 per 100,000 to roughly 30 per 100,000 by 2024 — a Bukele-comparable reduction that the Castro government characterised as the Libre security record.

The Taiwan break and the China recognition: on 26 March 2023 the Castro government broke diplomatic relations with Taiwan and recognised the People's Republic of China. The break was the most consequential Honduran foreign-policy realignment of the post-Cold-War period; it followed the Taiwanese-Honduran institutional relationship that dated to the 1985 establishment of relations. The Castro government's stated rationale was access to Chinese infrastructure financing and the Belt-and-Road architecture.

The CELAC presidency: Castro held the *pro tempore* presidency of the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States (CELAC) from 4 March 2024 to 9 April 2025 — the anchor of her foreign-policy profile across the term.

The Nasralla rupture: on 30 April 2024 Vice President Nasralla resigned over multi-year accumulated disagreements with the Libre coalition core. The resignation closed the 2022 coalition architecture and produced a 2024-2025 Libre minority-government posture that hampered legislative execution across the final year and a half of the term.

The Rixi Moncada appointment: in May 2025 Castro elevated her Defence Minister Rixi Moncada — previously her Finance Minister 2022-2024 — to the Libre presidential nomination for the November 2025 election. Castro then assumed acting command of the Defence Ministry herself from 27 May 2025 to 18 December 2025 before transferring it to Roosevelt Hernández Aguilar.

## The November 2025 election and the Trump intervention

The 30 November 2025 election was the central post-Trump-second-term Latin American case of explicit US presidential endorsement intervention.

The three principal candidates: Libre's Rixi Moncada (Castro's Finance-and-Defence-Minister and named successor); Partido Nacional's Nasry "Tito" Asfura (the 2021 loser and former Tegucigalpa mayor 2014-2022); and Partido Liberal's Salvador Nasralla (Castro's former Vice President who had resigned in April 2024 and shifted parties to lead the post-PSH Liberal restoration).

The Trump endorsement: on 26 November 2025 — four days before the vote — Trump issued an explicit endorsement of Asfura on Truth Social. The endorsement statement warned that Trump "cannot work with Moncada and the Communists, and Nasralla is not a reliable partner for Freedom." Trump subsequently asserted that the United States "will not be throwing away good money" sustaining a Moncada-led government. The intervention is documented across the Journal of Democracy December 2025 analysis, the Americas Quarterly post-election coverage, and the multi-source Honduran press record.

The 30 November 2025 result: the post-vote count of 24 hours produced preliminary returns covering 57% of precincts. The post-24-hour pattern showed Moncada eliminated below 20% (Libre failed to win a single department per Americas Quarterly), with Asfura and Nasralla tied within a fraction of a percentage point. The vote count then ran for nearly a month with multiple Trump Truth Social interventions during the count period including the post-narrowing Trump claim that Honduras was "trying to change the results of their Presidential Election" with "hell to pay" if the result was altered.

The 24 December 2025 final result: announced by CNE members Ana Paola Hall and Cossette López, Asfura defeated Nasralla by 0.74 percentage points. The OAS observation mission flagged concerns about the electoral process; the US Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau had warned during the count that the US "would respond swiftly and decisively to anyone who undermines" the result. Moncada signalled she would not recognise the result; Libre filed multiple post-election challenges. The CNE certification stood.

## Asfura — biographical foundation and the 2026 inauguration

Nasry Juan Asfura Zablah was born on 8 June 1958 in Tegucigalpa to a Lebanese-Honduran Christian family. He attended the Universidad Nacional Autónoma de Honduras and ran a construction business across the 1980s and 1990s. He served as Tegucigalpa City Councilman 2006-2010 and as a National Congress deputy for Francisco Morazán department 2010-2014.

He served as Mayor of Tegucigalpa 25 January 2014 - 25 January 2022 — two consecutive four-year terms under Partido Nacional. His Tegucigalpa record was characterised in the centrist press as competent municipal administration; in the post-2017 *Pandora Papers* it was characterised as involving documented offshore-financial-services holdings that Asfura did not declare in his Honduran asset declarations. The associated criminal cases have not produced convictions through May 2026.

He lost the 2021 presidential election to Castro. On 24 May 2025 he was elected president of the Partido Nacional, displacing David Chávez in the post-Hernández-extradition party reconfiguration. He won the November 2025 election by the documented 0.74-point margin and was sworn in as the 40th President of Honduras on 27 January 2026 — the same date Castro left office.

His Vice Presidents: María Antonieta Mejía (First), Carlos Flores Guifarro (Second), Diana Herrera (Third). The three-vice-president constitutional architecture is a 1982 Honduran institutional inheritance.

## The post-January 2026 apparatus

The first 100 days of the Asfura government cannot yet be fully audited at the date of writing of this dossier. The pre-inauguration stated commitments included consideration of resuming diplomatic relations with Taiwan (which would constitute a reversal of the March 2023 Castro recognition of the PRC); continued security cooperation with the United States; renegotiation of post-2023 PRC infrastructure financing arrangements; and full alignment of Honduran foreign policy with the second Trump administration on Caribbean Basin security and migration architecture.

## What survives

The Xiomara Castro biographical record is the public-facing record of the post-2009-coup First Lady who emerged as the leader of the Honduran left-wing resistance movement. She co-founded Libertad y Refundación in 2011; lost the 2013 and 2017 elections; and won the 2021 election with the largest margin in modern Honduran history. She completed the first full presidential term by a left-wing leader in Honduran history, broke diplomatic relations with Taiwan, extradited her predecessor to the United States, and was succeeded by the right-wing candidate she had defeated in 2021 — under conditions of explicit US presidential endorsement intervention.

The Nasry Asfura biographical record is the public-facing record of the Lebanese-Honduran Tegucigalpa construction-business operator who served two terms as Tegucigalpa mayor, lost the 2021 presidential race to Castro, became Partido Nacional president in May 2025, won the November 2025 election by a 0.74-point margin, and was sworn in on 27 January 2026 with the explicit endorsement of the second Trump administration.

The official record is the operation of a Honduran constitutional architecture that has, across the post-2009-coup period, produced the alternation between a post-coup right-wing PN-PL bipartisan governing structure and a single Libre four-year term — with the 2025 election restoring the right-wing alternation under conditions of unprecedented direct US presidential endorsement intervention during a Latin American election.

The post-2009-coup First Lady completed the first full left-wing presidential term in Honduran history. Her successor was elected by a 0.74-point margin under explicit US presidential endorsement intervention. The Honduran constitutional architecture alternated. The US institutional shadow did not.

The post-2009 Honduran political-class architecture is, documented, the Central American case study of an electoral democracy operating under the institutional shadow of US-aligned post-coup political-class consolidation, multi-year transnational-criminal-organisation embedment in the political-class apparatus, and the post-2025 explicit US presidential-endorsement intervention model that the Trump second-term Latin America policy has now institutionalised. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 65: The 2025 Bolivian Realignment — Luis Arce, Evo Morales, and Rodrigo Paz

## Who holds the leversure

The Plurinational State of Bolivia operates under the 2009 Constitution drafted under Evo Morales. The presidential system permits two consecutive five-year terms with one non-consecutive re-election. The 2009 Constitution institutionalised the post-2005 *proceso de cambio* (process of change) under the Movimiento al Socialismo (MAS) — a left-populist political project that drew its base from Bolivia's Indigenous, peasant, and coca-grower social movements.

The 68th President is Rodrigo Paz Pereira, sworn in on 8 November 2025 after winning the 19 October 2025 runoff against former interim president Jorge "Tuto" Quiroga with 54.5% of the vote. He is the first president elected under the runoff mechanism in Bolivian history; the 2009 Constitution introduced the runoff for cases where no first-round candidate exceeds 50% of valid votes (or 40% with a 10-point lead). The Paz inauguration closed the twenty-year MAS hegemony that ran from Evo Morales' January 2006 inauguration through Luis Arce's 8 November 2025 departure.

The parliamentary architecture: a 130-seat Chamber of Deputies and a 36-seat Senate. The post-October 2025 result produced the near-total annihilation of MAS: two seats in the Chamber and zero seats in the Senate — down from over two-thirds of both chambers across the 2014-2020 period. The Partido Demócrata Cristiano (PDC) under Paz, the Libre alliance under Quiroga, and the Alianza Unidad of Samuel Doria Medina are the three principal parliamentary forces of the post-2025 architecture.

## Luis Arce — biographical foundation and the 2020-2025 presidency

Luis Alberto Arce Catacora was born on 28 September 1963 in La Paz. He attended the Higher University of San Andrés for a degree in economics, then completed a master's degree in economics at the University of Warwick in the UK. He joined the Banco Central de Bolivia as a young economist and rose through the Central Bank professional ranks across the 1990s.

He served as Minister of Economy and Public Finance under Evo Morales from January 2006 to June 2017 — the longest tenure of any minister in the post-2005 MAS governments. He was the architect of the post-2006 macroeconomic stabilisation programme that produced multi-year Bolivian GDP growth of roughly 5% annually, the post-2006 nationalisations of hydrocarbons and telecommunications, the post-2006 reserve accumulation that produced the largest Central Bank reserves in Bolivian history by 2014, and the post-2009 *bono Juancito Pinto* and *bono Juana Azurduy* social-transfer programmes.

He returned to the Ministry of Economy briefly in 2019 before the October 2019 election crisis covered below. He was the MAS presidential candidate for the 2020 election after Evo Morales was barred from candidacy and exiled. He won the 18 October 2020 first round with 55.1% of the vote — exceeding the 50%-runoff threshold by a single percentage point — and took office on 8 November 2020 with David Choquehuanca, an Aymara Indigenous-movement leader, as Vice President.

## The 2019 institutional rupture

The October 2019 election crisis is the central institutional inflection point of the entire post-2005 Bolivian sequence.

On 20 October 2019 Bolivia held a presidential election in which Morales sought an unprecedented fourth term — a candidacy that the 2016 referendum had explicitly rejected and that the 2017 Constitutional Court had subsequently authorised on disputed jurisprudential grounds. The official count produced Morales at 47.08% with Carlos Mesa at 36.51% — a 10.57-percentage-point margin that exceeded the 10-point runoff-avoidance threshold by 0.57 points. The OAS observation mission released a preliminary statement alleging irregularities in the vote-counting halt of 21-22 October 2019; the subsequent OAS audit characterised the result as compromised.

The post-election protests across November 2019 included multiple deaths at the Senkata and Sacaba massacres committed by post-Morales-resignation interim security forces. Morales resigned on 10 November 2019 after a Bolivian Armed Forces chief publicly recommended he do so. He fled to Mexico on 11 November 2019. Jeanine Áñez, the second vice president of the Senate, assumed the presidency under contested constitutional-succession provisions on 12 November 2019.

The post-Morales academic-and-institutional reassessment: multiple post-2019 statistical analyses — including the MIT Election Lab study, the Center for Economic and Policy Research analysis, and the *Washington Post* April 2020 reporting on the underlying OAS data — challenged the OAS claim of statistical irregularities. The post-2020 institutional consensus has been that the OAS preliminary statement contributed to the post-October 2019 destabilisation without adequate empirical foundation. The OAS has not formally retracted the original characterisation.

The post-2019 Áñez interim government conducted multi-month prosecutions of MAS-aligned figures and overturned multiple post-2006 institutional commitments. The 18 October 2020 election restored MAS to office under Arce with 55.1% of the vote — the most consequential institutional vindication of the post-2019 Morales-MAS political coalition.

## The Arce presidency 2020-2025

The Arce term ran through three principal threads.

The post-pandemic macroeconomic stabilisation 2020-2022: GDP recovery from the 2020 COVID-19 contraction, restoration of the post-2006 social-transfer architecture, and continuation of the multi-year hydrocarbons-export-dependent budget framework.

The 2023-2025 economic crisis: the cumulative decline in Bolivian natural-gas reserves and the post-2023 collapse of natural-gas export volumes to Argentina and Brazil produced the central post-2023 Bolivian fiscal crisis. The Bolivian Central Bank's dollar reserves collapsed from the post-2014 peak to roughly $1 billion by mid-2025. The dollar parallel-market premium reached over 100% across 2024-2025. Fuel shortages produced multi-day queues at urban filling stations across La Paz, El Alto, Santa Cruz, and Cochabamba throughout 2025. Inflation reached roughly 18% by mid-2025 per the Bolivian National Statistics Institute — the highest level in over thirty years.

The Morales-Arce schism: from 2022 onwards Morales and Arce engaged in a multi-year public dispute over MAS leadership. Morales held the formal party presidency; Arce controlled the government apparatus. The party's October 2023 congress produced two parallel claims to MAS authority that the Bolivian electoral court could not resolve definitively. By 2024 the dispute had produced two warring MAS factions. Morales attempted to register for the 2025 election but was barred by the post-2017 Constitutional Court precedent restricting non-consecutive re-election cycles. He then refused to endorse a MAS candidate and called for null-ballot voting. Arce declined to seek re-election in May 2025 but retained dubious legal control of the MAS brand.

The 17 August 2025 first round: the MAS candidate Eduardo del Castillo, named by the Arce faction, finished sixth with 3.2% of the vote — the party's worst result in any election since its 1997 founding. Roughly 1.2 million voters (about 20% of valid votes) cast null ballots — a near-record rejection figure that Morales characterised as a "victory." The MAS lost both chambers of Congress entirely.

## The 2025 election

The 17 August 2025 first round produced Rodrigo Paz Pereira (PDC) 32%, Jorge "Tuto" Quiroga (Libre alliance) 27%, Samuel Doria Medina 19.7%, Andrónico Rodríguez (Senate president, the post-Morales heir-apparent who broke from MAS) 8.2%, and Eduardo del Castillo (MAS) 3.2%. The result was a major upset; pre-election polls had placed Quiroga and Doria Medina in the lead and had registered Paz below 10%.

Paz advanced to the runoff under the Christian Democratic Party banner. He is the son of former president Jaime Paz Zamora (1989-1993). He served as Senator from Tarija department and built his 2025 campaign on a *capitalismo para todos* (capitalism for all) framework that emphasised fiscal discipline alongside continued recognition of post-2005 social-transfer commitments.

Quiroga (born 5 May 1960) had served as interim president August 2001 - August 2002 after the death of Hugo Banzer Suárez. His 2025 campaign was the most ideologically explicit market-restoration platform: IMF rescue programme, strict austerity, government digitalisation, and the "popular property title" proposal that would issue every Bolivian adult a $1,500 symbolic claim on the country's mineral resources.

The 19 October 2025 runoff: Paz 54.5%, Quiroga 45.5%. Paz won the heavily Indigenous highland departments (Chuquisaca, Cochabamba, La Paz, Oruro, Potosí) where MAS had previously dominated. Quiroga won the eastern lowland departments (Santa Cruz, Beni, Pando, Tarija) plus the city of La Paz upper-class neighbourhoods. The 19 October 2025 result is the first peaceful transfer of national power between non-MAS parties since the post-1982 democratic restoration produced its alternation cycles.

## Paz — the first 100 days

Paz took office on 8 November 2025. His Vice President Edmand Lara is a former police-officer-turned-political-figure from El Alto who emerged as a Paz running-mate selection in the post-first-round consolidation.

The first 100 days of the Paz government — covering November 2025 through February 2026 — have run principally on economic-stabilisation operations: emergency fuel-import arrangements with multiple suppliers, parallel-currency-market interventions by the Central Bank, and IMF preliminary discussions for a multi-billion-dollar facility. The post-MAS lithium-extraction architecture has not yet been altered through May 2026; Paz had campaigned on transparency around the post-2023 Arce-government lithium deals with Chinese and Russian state enterprises.

The post-2025 Bolivian foreign-policy realignment: the post-2020-2025 MAS-era alignment with Cuba, Nicaragua, and Venezuela has been replaced by a post-2025 Paz-government posture of pragmatic neutrality. Bolivia joined BRICS as a "partner country" in January 2025 — the final foreign-policy initiative of the Arce term. The Paz government has signalled willingness to continue the BRICS-partner status while opening new bilateral channels with Washington under the second Trump administration.

## The takeaway

The Luis Arce biographical record is the public-facing record of the Central Bank economist and Warwick-trained macroeconomist who served as Evo Morales' Economy Minister for eleven years, became MAS presidential candidate after the 2019 Morales exile, won the 2020 election with 55.1%, governed for five years since the post-2023 collapse of Bolivian natural-gas reserves and the resulting hyperinflation crisis, and exited office on 8 November 2025 with MAS reduced from two-thirds majorities in both chambers to two seats and zero respectively.

The Rodrigo Paz biographical record is the public-facing record of the son of a former president, Tarija senator, polling below 10% one month before the election, surprise winner of the first round with 32%, and elected on 19 October 2025 with 54.5% of the runoff vote to become the first post-MAS president of Bolivia and the first elected by runoff in Bolivian history.

The performance record is the operation of a Bolivian constitutional architecture that has, throughout the post-2005 period, produced the longest single-party governing run in Bolivian post-1982 history, the 2019 election crisis under disputed OAS findings that contributed to the temporary post-November 2019 institutional rupture, the 2020 electoral restoration of MAS, the post-2023 economic collapse driven by the cumulative depletion of the post-2006 hydrocarbons-export base, and the 2025 electoral realignment that has now ended the MAS hegemony.

The biographical records do not contradict the structural record. They are the operating faces of a constitutional architecture that has, documented, transferred power between left and right multiple times in the post-1982 democratic period.

The post-2025 Bolivian realignment is, documented, the Latin American case study of a two-decade left-populist governing project collapsing under the cumulative weight of its own primary-export-dependent fiscal foundation and the schism between its founder and its administrator. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 66: Prabowo Subianto and the Post-Jokowi Indonesian Apex Architecture

## The apparatusure

The Republic of Indonesia operates under the 1945 Constitution as amended four times (1999-2002) in the post-Suharto reformasi period. The presidential system permits two consecutive five-year terms. The 8th President is Prabowo Subianto Djojohadikusumo, inaugurated on 20 October 2024 after winning the 14 February 2024 first round with 58.6% of the vote against Anies Baswedan and Ganjar Pranowo. His Vice President is Gibran Rakabuming Raka, the eldest son of his predecessor Joko Widodo.

The parliamentary architecture: a 580-seat House of Representatives (DPR) elected on the same cycle as the presidency, plus a 152-seat Regional Representatives Council (DPD) elected as upper chamber. The post-February-2024 result produced the Prabowo-aligned Advanced Indonesia Coalition (KIM) — Gerindra, Golkar, PAN, the Democratic Party, Gelora, PSI, PBB, and Garuda — holding qualified majorities sufficient for constitutional amendment and for the November 2024 elevation of the cabinet from 34 to 53 ministries.

The "Red and White" Cabinet inaugurated on 20 October 2024 was the largest in Indonesian history. Apex positions include Foreign Minister Sugiono, Finance Minister Sri Mulyani Indrawati (retained from the Jokowi government), Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin (former Kopassus officer), and Coordinating Minister for Political and Security Affairs Budi Gunawan (former BIN intelligence chief).

## Prabowo — biographical foundation

Prabowo Subianto Djojohadikusumo was born on 17 October 1951 in Jakarta. His father Sumitro Djojohadikusumo was a prominent Indonesian economist who served as Minister of Trade, Minister of Finance, and Minister of Research under Sukarno and then under Suharto across multiple terms; Sumitro was the first Indonesian to complete a doctorate at Erasmus University Rotterdam. His mother Dora Marie Sigar was of Sulawesi Manado Christian descent. The family went into exile in Switzerland and the United Kingdom during the 1957-1967 Sukarno period after Sumitro fell out with Sukarno.

Prabowo attended schools in Switzerland, Hong Kong, Malaysia, and the United Kingdom. He returned to Indonesia in 1968 after Suharto consolidated power and enrolled in the Magelang Military Academy, graduating in 1974. He served in the Army Strategic Reserves Command (Kostrad), the Special Forces Command (Kopassus), and across multiple Indonesian military deployments to East Timor across 1976-1989. In 1983 he married Titiek Hediati Haryadi, the second daughter of President Suharto — the marriage that produced his integration into the senior New Order political-and-military apparatus.

His rapid promotion through the 1980s and 1990s was widely attributed to his Suharto-family connection. He was promoted to Commandant General of Kopassus on 1 December 1995 (succeeding Subagyo Hadi Siswoyo) and then to Commander of Kostrad on 20 March 1998 — the inflection point of his career. The 22 May 1998 events covered below ended his military career.

## The Tim Mawar disappearances and the 1998 dismissal

The post-1997 Indonesian economic and political crisis produced a wave of student protests against the Suharto government. The military response included Kopassus operations to identify, detain, and "process" pro-democracy activists deemed to be undermining state stability. The operations were conducted by a Kopassus unit designated Tim Mawar (Rose Team), led by Major Bambang Kristiono and comprising ten other officers.

Tim Mawar abducted at least 22 pro-democracy activists across 1997-1998. Nine were released alive; thirteen — including Wiji Thukul of the Folk Culture Work Network, Petrus Bima Anugrah, and Suyat of the Students Solidarity for Democracy in Indonesia (SMID) — have never returned. One activist, Pius Lustrilanang, escaped and disclosed the operation publicly. Released activists testified to beatings and electric-shock torture during their detention.

The sequence: in August 1998 the Indonesian Armed Forces (ABRI) Officers' Honors Council (DKP) examined Prabowo, Major General Muchdi Purwopranjono, and Colonel Chairawan. The DKP concluded that Prabowo had personally ordered the Tim Mawar and Tim Merpati operations to arrest and detain activists. The DKP recommended Prabowo's dismissal from the Army. President B.J. Habibie accepted the recommendation. Prabowo was discharged from the military and went into exile in Jordan and then Germany across 1998-2000.

In April 1999 the Jakarta High Military Court II convicted eleven Tim Mawar members and sentenced them to terms between 12 and 22 months. The sentences were appealed; the appeal outcomes were not made public. All but one of the convicted Tim Mawar members remained in the military and several were subsequently promoted.

The 2008 Komnas HAM (Indonesian National Commission on Human Rights) investigation completed in November 2006 — its executive summary released in 2008 — identified 12 cases of serious human-rights violations, found evidence pointing to crimes against humanity, and concluded that at least 27 persons may be criminally responsible. Three named generals — Wiranto, Prabowo, and Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin (Prabowo's later Defence Minister) — refused to appear before Komnas HAM. The full report has never been released.

The institutional outcome of the post-2008 record: no Indonesian prosecutor has opened a criminal case against Prabowo for the disappearances. Three of the nine returned activists — Faisol Reza, Mugiyanto, and Nezar Patria — have served in Prabowo's government since 2024 in advisory and ministerial positions.

## The post-1998 commercial and political reconstruction

Prabowo returned to Indonesia in 2001. His brother Hashim Djojohadikusumo had built a multi-billion-dollar conglomerate (Arsari Group) across the 1990s commodity-and-finance markets; the post-2001 Prabowo political reconstruction was financed principally through this family conglomerate. The 2004 founding of Gerindra (Gerakan Indonesia Raya, the Great Indonesia Movement Party) was directly funded through the Djojohadikusumo family network.

The political emergence: Prabowo ran as Megawati Sukarnoputri's vice-presidential running mate in the 2009 election — a PDI-P-Gerindra ticket that lost to the incumbent Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono. He ran for the presidency in 2014 against Joko Widodo and lost 47%-53%. He ran again in 2019 against Jokowi and lost 45%-55%. The 2014 and 2019 post-election periods produced multiple Prabowo claims of electoral fraud that the Indonesian Constitutional Court rejected.

The 2019-2024 Jokowi-Prabowo institutional realignment: in October 2019 Jokowi appointed Prabowo as Defence Minister — the governing move that defused the post-2019 Prabowo opposition posture and integrated Gerindra into the Jokowi second-term governing coalition. Prabowo served as Defence Minister 2019-2024. The institutional inheritance of the Jokowi-Prabowo arrangement was the 2024 presidential succession architecture covered below.

## The 2024 election and the Gibran candidacy

The 2024 election was constructed through the October 2023 Constitutional Court ruling that altered the candidate-age requirements to permit Joko Widodo's son Gibran Rakabuming Raka — then 36 years old, below the 40-year minimum — to run as Vice President. The ruling was issued by Chief Justice Anwar Usman, Gibran's uncle by marriage to Jokowi's sister. The Constitutional Court's Ethics Council subsequently found Anwar Usman guilty of a serious ethical violation and removed him from the Chief Justice position; the underlying ruling permitting Gibran's candidacy was retained.

The 14 February 2024 first round produced Prabowo-Gibran 58.6%, Anies Baswedan-Muhaimin Iskandar (Coalition of Change) 24.9%, and Ganjar Pranowo-Mahfud MD (PDI-P) 16.5%. The result exceeded the 50%-plus-one threshold for first-round victory. The 20 March 2024 General Election Commission (KPU) certification confirmed Prabowo as President-elect. The post-March 2024 election-result challenges by both losing campaigns were rejected by the Constitutional Court on 24 April 2024.

Prabowo and Gibran were inaugurated on 20 October 2024 at the People's Consultative Assembly (MPR) in Jakarta.

## The first term institutional record

The first eighteen months of the Prabowo presidency have run through four principal threads.

The cabinet bloat and budget restructuring: the Red-and-White Cabinet of 53 ministries was the largest in Indonesian history, marking a sharp expansion from Jokowi's 34. In January 2025 Prabowo issued Presidential Instruction Number 1 of 2025 that cut Rp 306.6 trillion (roughly $19 billion) in non-essential government spending — official trips, ceremonial events, and similar categories — and redirected the funds toward his flagship Free Nutritious Lunch (Makan Bergizi Gratis, MBG) programme. In September 2025 the central government announced that fiscal transfers to regional governments would be reduced from Rp 920 trillion to Rp 650 trillion for fiscal year 2026 — a 29% cut that has produced sustained subnational political-class friction with the Prabowo administration.

Danantara and the post-2025 sovereign-wealth architecture: on 24 February 2025 Prabowo launched the Daya Anagata Nusantara (Danantara) sovereign-wealth fund consolidating major state-owned enterprises (BUMNs) including Pertamina, Telkom Indonesia, BRI, Mandiri, BNI, and PLN under a single state-investment vehicle. Danantara was capitalised at roughly $900 billion in initial assets — among the largest sovereign-wealth funds in the world by enterprise valuation. On 26 February 2025 Prabowo launched Indonesia's first bullion-bank service through state-owned mining company MIND ID. The institutional intent of both moves was to consolidate Indonesian commodity-and-state-enterprise revenue under presidential direction.

Foreign-policy continuity and BRICS accession: Indonesia became a full BRICS member on 6 January 2025 — the most consequential post-2024 Indonesian foreign-policy realignment. Prabowo attended the 2025 BRICS summit in Kazan and the October 2025 BRICS summit in Brazil. The pre-2024 Jokowi-era multi-alignment posture has continued; Indonesia has not signed multi-year strategic-partnership agreements with the United States since 2024.

The free-nutritious-lunch programme: the central campaign promise of the 2024 ticket has been the universal MBG school-meals programme. The pilot phase launched in January 2025 across selected districts. The full national rollout has been deferred multiple times. Independent academic and civil-society monitors have documented sustained challenges including supply-chain failures, food-poisoning incidents across multiple provinces, and budget shortfalls. The MBG programme remains the principal Prabowo public-facing commitment.

## The post-2025 political-class confrontation

The August 2025 protests over a proposed parliamentary-housing-allowance increase produced multi-province demonstrations and the death of a Jakarta motorcycle-taxi (ojol) driver Affan Kurniawan, killed by a police vehicle on 28 August 2025. The protests produced the multi-week confrontation between the post-Jokowi political-class consensus and the post-2024 reformasi-era civil-society apparatus. Prabowo's public response — characterising the protests as "destabilising" — was the signal of the post-2024 governance posture.

The Jokowi factor: former president Joko Widodo (2014-2024) retains sustained post-presidency political influence through the Gibran vice presidency, through his son Kaesang Pangarep's chairmanship of the PSI party, and through the Jokowi-aligned PDI-P faction. The post-2024 Prabowo-Jokowi institutional relationship has been characterised across multiple analytical sources as the central institutional dynamic of the term — Prabowo as president, Jokowi as the operating co-architect of the political-class consolidation.

## The weight of the record

The Prabowo Subianto biographical record is the public-facing record of the Djojohadikusumo-family heir, son of one of independent Indonesia's most prominent economists. He graduated from the Magelang Military Academy in 1974 and married Suharto's daughter in 1983. He commanded Kopassus from 1995 to 1998 during the period of the documented disappearances of at least 22 pro-democracy activists, thirteen of whom remain missing. He was dismissed from the army by President Habibie in August 1998 and returned to politics through the Gerindra party founded with Djojohadikusumo family financing in 2004. He lost two presidential elections to Jokowi in 2014 and 2019, served as Jokowi's Defence Minister 2019-2024, and won the 2024 presidential election with the Jokowi-family vice president as his running mate.

The power record is the operation of a post-2024 Indonesian government that has, documented, expanded the cabinet from 34 to 53 ministries, consolidated state-owned enterprises under the $900-billion Danantara fund, joined BRICS, cut Rp 270 trillion in fiscal transfers to regional governments, launched and stumbled through the Free Nutritious Lunch programme, and confronted post-August-2025 protest mobilisation under a public posture characterising democracy as "very, very messy and costly."

The Kopassus commander was discharged for ordering enforced disappearances during the closing months of the New Order. Twenty-six years later he was elected president with the predecessor's son as his running mate, expanded the cabinet to 53 ministries, consolidated $900 billion in state assets under presidential direction, and described democracy as "very, very messy and costly." Thirteen of the disappeared activists remain missing.

The post-2024 Indonesian apex architecture under Prabowo is, documented, the Southeast Asian case study of a former senior military officer of the New Order period — discharged for ordering enforced disappearances during the closing months of that regime — returning to executive office through electoral channels, married into the family of his predecessor, with the consolidation of state-enterprise revenue under presidential direction and the reformasi-era civil-society apparatus operating under sustained governmental pressure. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 67: Ferdinand Marcos Jr., Sara Duterte, and the Marcos-Duterte Dynastic Confrontation

## The mechanismure

The Republic of the Philippines operates under the 1987 Constitution drafted in the immediate post-EDSA Revolution period that closed the 1972-1986 Marcos Sr. martial-law regime. The presidential system permits a single six-year term with no re-election — the constitutional commitment that emerged directly from the post-1986 institutional reckoning with the Marcos Sr. authoritarian period. The 17th President is Ferdinand "Bongbong" Romualdez Marcos Jr., son of the former dictator, inaugurated on 30 June 2022 after winning the 9 May 2022 election with 58.8% of the vote against Vice President Leni Robredo (27.9%). His term runs to 30 June 2028.

The parliamentary architecture: a 316-seat House of Representatives and a 24-seat Senate, both elected on staggered three-year cycles. The May 2025 mid-term elections produced a Congress in which Marcos-aligned forces retained working majorities in both chambers but with a documented Duterte-aligned counter-mobilisation across multiple regions, principally in Mindanao and the Visayas.

The Vice President is Sara Zimmerman Duterte-Carpio, daughter of former President Rodrigo Duterte (2016-2022), inaugurated alongside Marcos on 30 June 2022 with 32 million votes — the largest single-candidate vote total in Philippine electoral history. The 2022 election was contested under the UniTeam alliance branding that united the Marcos and Duterte political-dynastic networks; the alliance collapsed across 2023-2024 and produced the confrontation documented in this dossier.

## Marcos Jr. — biographical foundation

Ferdinand "Bongbong" Romualdez Marcos Jr. was born on 13 September 1957 at Our Lady of Lourdes Hospital in Santa Mesa, Manila. He is the only son and second child of Ferdinand Marcos Sr. — then a sitting Philippine representative, subsequently 10th President of the Philippines from 1965 to 1986 — and former First Lady Imelda Romualdez Marcos. His godfathers included Eduardo "Danding" Cojuangco Jr. and pharmaceuticals magnate Jose Yao Campos, both subsequently identified as principal Marcos-Sr.-era cronies.

He attended kindergarten and elementary school at La Salle Green Hills in Manila. In 1970 — two years before his father imposed martial law — he was sent to Worth School in England, a Roman Catholic boys' boarding school, where he completed secondary education in 1974. He then attended St Edmund Hall, Oxford, where he received a special diploma in social studies in 1978. He subsequently enrolled in the MBA programme at the Wharton School of Business at the University of Pennsylvania but did not complete the degree. The educational-credentials question has been raised in multiple Philippine election cycles; Oxford issued public confirmation in 2021 that he received a special diploma rather than the full bachelor's degree his political biography had previously claimed.

The 1972-1986 martial-law period: his father declared martial law on 21 September 1972, ruling by decree until 1981 and as a formally constitutional president until 1986. The Human Rights Violations Victims' Memorial Commission (HRVVMC) — established by the post-2013 Human Rights Reparation and Recognition Act — has documented 11,103 approved claims for compensation. Amnesty International has estimated 107,200 people killed, tortured, or imprisoned across the period. The post-2013 framework distinguishes formally-adjudicated victims (the 11,103 figure) from the larger documented universe of victims (the 107,200 figure).

In 1980 at age 23 Marcos Jr. became Vice Governor of Ilocos Norte, running unopposed under his father's Kilusang Bagong Lipunan party. He became Governor in 1983, holding the post until the family's exile to Hawaii on 25 February 1986 following the EDSA People Power Revolution. Ferdinand Marcos Sr. died in Hawaiian exile on 28 September 1989.

## Post-exile political career 1991-2022

President Corazon Aquino permitted the Marcos family to return to the Philippines after the elder Marcos's death. The family faced multiple Philippine and US court proceedings over the estimated $5-10 billion the Presidential Commission on Good Government (PCGG) has identified as the Marcos-era ill-gotten wealth. The PCGG has recovered roughly $4 billion across the litigation period; multiple claims remain unresolved.

Marcos Jr. was elected Representative of Ilocos Norte's 2nd congressional district 1992-1995. He returned as Governor of Ilocos Norte 1998-2007. He served as Representative again 2007-2010. He served as Senator under the Nacionalista Party 2010-2016.

In 2016 he ran for Vice President and lost to Camarines Sur Representative Leni Robredo by 263,473 votes — a margin of 0.7% that he contested before the Supreme Court Presidential Electoral Tribunal. The tribunal dismissed the contest in February 2021 after multiple recounts produced no material change in the Robredo margin.

The October 2021 presidential announcement: Marcos Jr. announced his bid through a YouTube and Facebook video on 5 October 2021. The campaign was conducted under a stated commitment to "national unity" that critics across the multi-year campaign period characterised as institutional whitewashing of the martial-law record. The May 2022 result produced his 58.8% landslide victory — the first majority-vote first-round Philippine presidential result since the 1981 single-candidate Marcos Sr. re-election.

## The 2022 UniTeam alliance and the Duterte family

The UniTeam alliance was the central apparatus of the 2022 election. Marcos Jr. ran for president; Sara Duterte ran for Vice President under the post-Lakas-CMD coalition with the post-PDP-Laban Marcos Jr. faction. Sara Duterte had been the Davao City mayor since 2016, succeeding her father Rodrigo Duterte who had won the May 2016 presidential election with 39% of a multi-candidate field.

Rodrigo Duterte presidency 2016-2022: his administration's central institutional commitment was the multi-year "drug war" that the Philippine National Police (PNP) Operation Plan Double Barrel and parallel vigilante-style operations conducted across the entire term. The documented death toll across the 2016-2022 period was characterised by the Philippine Drug Enforcement Agency at roughly 6,000 deaths in PNP operations; civil-society organisations including the Catholic Bishops' Conference of the Philippines and Human Rights Watch documented totals at 12,000-30,000 deaths including the vigilante killings the post-2016 framework had institutionalised.

The 2022 UniTeam architecture: Sara Duterte's 32 million votes (61% of valid votes) were the largest single-candidate vote total in Philippine electoral history. Marcos Jr.'s 31 million votes were the second-largest. The UniTeam carried every Philippine region except parts of Metro Manila and a small number of Visayan provinces.

## The post-2023 alliance collapse

Sara Duterte was appointed Marcos's first Secretary of Education in mid-2022. The Department of Education portfolio was not her preferred ministry; she had publicly sought the Department of National Defense. The Defense portfolio went instead to Carlito Galvez Jr. The institutional disagreement over the Defense portfolio was the first documented Marcos-Duterte friction point.

The confidential-funds controversy: across 2023 the Office of the Vice President and the Department of Education under Sara Duterte were allocated 612.5 million pesos (roughly $10 million at exchange rate of the period) in "confidential funds" — a budget category historically reserved for intelligence-and-security operations. The November-December 2023 Congressional Commission on Appointments investigation produced documented questions about the use of those funds for purposes incompatible with the Vice Presidency. Sara Duterte's defence before Congress was that the confidential-funds use had been  proper and that the investigation was politically motivated.

Sara Duterte resigned as Secretary of Education in mid-2024 while retaining the Vice Presidency. The Marcos-aligned House of Representatives began the parallel investigation that produced the first impeachment complaint covered below.

## The Rodrigo Duterte ICC arrest

The International Criminal Court (ICC) had opened an investigation into the Philippine drug-war killings in February 2018 under Prosecutor Fatou Bensouda. The Philippines under Rodrigo Duterte had withdrawn from the Rome Statute in March 2019 — a withdrawal that took institutional effect on 17 March 2019. The ICC investigation continued under the post-2018 Pre-Trial Chamber framework that retained jurisdiction over alleged crimes committed before the withdrawal date.

In March 2025 — the Marcos government having agreed to ICC cooperation — Rodrigo Duterte was arrested at Manila International Airport on an ICC arrest warrant for crimes against humanity in connection with the drug-war killings. He was transferred to the ICC detention facility at The Hague. As of May 2026 he remains in ICC custody awaiting trial. He is 81 years old.

The institutional consequence: the Marcos government's facilitation of the ICC arrest of the former president — and the father of the sitting Vice President — was the central institutional break between the Marcos and Duterte families. Sara Duterte's subsequent statements — covered in the impeachment record below — included her November 2024 declaration that if she were "killed", her allies should arrange the assassination of President Marcos, First Lady Liza Araneta Marcos, and House Speaker Martin Romualdez. The Philippine National Bureau of Investigation opened a criminal investigation into the statement that has run across 2025-2026.

## The first impeachment

On 5 February 2025 the House of Representatives voted 215-66 to impeach Sara Duterte on four articles: culpable violation of the Constitution; betrayal of public trust; graft and corruption; and "other high crimes." An additional 25 House members verified as complainants signed onto the articles. The charges centred on the confidential-funds misuse, the unexplained wealth allegations, and the November 2024 assassination threats.

The impeachment articles were transmitted to the Senate, which was constitutionally obligated to convene as an impeachment court. Procedural disputes across early-2025 over the calendar and the seating of the senator-judges delayed proceedings.

On 25 July 2025 the Philippine Supreme Court issued a unanimous ruling voiding the first impeachment on a constitutional technicality — the one-year ban under Article XI, Section 3(5) of the 1987 Constitution that prohibits the initiation of more than one impeachment proceeding against the same official within a single year. The Court found that earlier complaints filed in late 2024 against Sara Duterte had triggered the one-year ban, rendering the February 2025 impeachment procedurally invalid. The Court did not adjudicate the merits of the underlying charges.

The 25 July 2025 ruling produced two institutional results. First, Sara Duterte remained in office. Second, the Court's ruling explicitly did not preclude future impeachment proceedings on the same underlying charges after the expiration of the one-year ban.

## The second impeachment

The post-25-July 2025 institutional sequence: the one-year ban triggered by the late-2024 complaints expired in early February 2026. Four new impeachment complaints were filed in February 2026 — including a complaint filed by lawyer Nathaniel Cabrera on 18 February 2026 and a complaint led by a group of priests and lawyers. All four cited the misuse of confidential funds, unexplained wealth, abuse of power, bribery, and the threats against Marcos, the First Lady, and Romualdez. The Speaker referred the complaints to the House Committee on Justice on 23 February 2026, formally initiating the second impeachment.

The House Committee on Justice, chaired by Representative Gerville Luistro, began formal hearings on 25 March 2026 through the issuance of subpoenas. Sara Duterte and her legal team did not attend the proceedings, characterising them as politically motivated. On 29 April 2026 the Justice Committee voted unanimously (53-0) to find probable cause and consolidated the four complaints into a single set of articles of impeachment. On 4 May 2026 the consolidated articles were presented to the full House. On 11 May 2026 the House voted 257 in favour of impeachment — exceeding the one-third threshold of 105 members and producing the first double impeachment of any sitting official in Philippine history.

The Senate impeachment trial began on 18 May 2026. The 24 senator-judges were sworn in by Senate President Francis Escudero. Conviction requires a two-thirds vote (16 of 24). A guilty verdict would remove Sara Duterte from office and disqualify her from public office for life.

## The 2025 mid-term elections and the 2028 succession

The May 2025 mid-term elections produced a documented Duterte counter-mobilisation across multiple regions. Rodrigo Duterte — then in ICC custody at The Hague — won the Davao City mayoral race from detention with over 60% of the vote. Multiple Duterte-aligned candidates won in Mindanao and Visayan provinces. The Marcos-aligned slate underperformed pre-election expectations in multiple Senate races, though retained enough seats to proceed with the second-impeachment Senate trial that the post-May 2025 calendar had positioned for early 2026.

The 2028 presidential succession: the 1987 Constitution prohibits Marcos Jr. from seeking a second term. The post-2025 polling has consistently placed Sara Duterte as the frontrunner for the May 2028 presidential election. Her February 2026 announcement of her presidential candidacy — issued during the post-Justice-Committee-vote period — was the structural commitment to the 2028 contest that would proceed under conditions of either Senate acquittal (in which case Sara Duterte runs from the Vice Presidency) or Senate conviction (in which case the lifetime-disqualification eliminates her candidacy).

## The balance sheet

The Ferdinand Marcos Jr. biographical record is the public-facing record of the only son of the 1972-1986 martial-law dictator. He attended Worth School and St Edmund Hall, Oxford; served as Ilocos Norte Vice Governor at 23 under his father's martial-law government; held provincial-and-national offices for over thirty years since the post-1986 exile-and-return cycle; lost the 2016 vice presidential race to Robredo by 0.7%; won the 2022 presidency in coalition with the Duterte family with the largest single-result vote totals in Philippine electoral history; and presides over a government that has, documented, facilitated the ICC arrest of his Vice President's father and twice impeached his Vice President.

The Sara Duterte biographical record is the public-facing record of the Davao City mayor's daughter who became Davao City mayor herself, ran as Vice President on the UniTeam ticket, served seventeen months as Secretary of Education before resigning over the confidential-funds controversy, is the daughter of the former president now in ICC custody at The Hague, and is the first Philippine official to be impeached twice and the leading candidate for the 2028 presidency.

The operational record is the operation of a Philippine constitutional architecture that has, documented, sustained the 1987 single-term presidency through six consecutive electoral cycles; produced the post-2022 collapse of the most consequential dynastic coalition in Philippine electoral history; permitted the post-2024 facilitation of ICC jurisdiction over a former Philippine president; and produced the first double impeachment of any sitting Philippine official in the constitutional record.

The dictator's son won the presidency with the largest vote totals in Philippine electoral history, in coalition with the daughter of the president now in ICC custody at The Hague. Two years later he facilitated the ICC arrest of his running mate's father and impeached his running mate twice. Two dynastic families. One constitutional architecture. The 1987 single-term framework keeps the theatre running on schedule.

The post-2022 Philippine apex architecture under the Marcos-Duterte confrontation is, documented, the Southeast Asian case study of two dynastic political families — one rooted in the 1972-1986 martial-law period, the other in the 2016-2022 drug-war period — operating the post-1987 constitutional architecture as the institutional theatre of their cumulative dynastic confrontation. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 68: To Lam and the Post-2024 Vietnamese Apex Consolidation

## The seats of powerure

The Socialist Republic of Vietnam operates under the 2013 Constitution, the fifth in the post-1945 institutional sequence. The one-party state under the Communist Party of Vietnam (CPV) operates through what the post-1986 *Đổi Mới* (Renovation) period institutionalised as the "four pillars" leadership: the General Secretary of the CPV, the State President, the Prime Minister, and the Chairperson of the National Assembly. The four-pillar architecture distributed apex authority across distinct office-holders — the central institutional commitment to collective leadership that distinguished post-1986 Vietnam from the Chinese single-leader model.

The post-2024 sequence has overturned this architecture. On 7 April 2026 the National Assembly unanimously elected CPV General Secretary To Lam as State President for the 2026-2031 term — making him the first Vietnamese leader to hold both top positions through the normal leadership-selection process rather than by stepping in following an officeholder's death. The consolidation produced what multiple comparative analyses have characterised as the most concentrated personal authority held by any Vietnamese leader since the post-1969 Le Duan period.

The 14th CPV National Congress (Hanoi, 20-23 January 2026) confirmed Lam as General Secretary for the 2026-2031 term, elected a new 180-member Central Committee, and a 19-member Politburo.
