It produced the post-2020 €800 billion NextGenerationEU joint-debt-issuance framework, and the post-2025 €800 billion ReArm Europe defence-industrial-base mobilisation.

The institutional operational record is the operation of a multi-state framework that has, documented, sustained the governing centrality of the centre-right EPP across the multi-party European Parliament cycles. It has institutionalised the post-2024 Trump-second-term defence-and-trade response under the post-November 2024 von der Leyen-Costa-Kallas leadership. It has produced incremental rather than structural post-2024 institutional reforms. It has sustained the post-Lisbon-Treaty governance architecture that requires consensus on principal foreign-policy questions, and has therefore been constrained on multiple post-2022 questions including the Gaza framework, the post-2024 sanctions architecture, and the multi-state defence-procurement framework.

Six founding member states became twenty-seven. The UK left. €800 billion in joint debt was issued. Another €800 billion was committed to rearmament. The consensus-requirement architecture has deadlocked the foreign-policy framework on Gaza, on sanctions, on defence procurement. The post-2024 European Parliament is the most right-leaning in the post-1979 record. The EU's presentation is supranational governance. The EU's operation is intergovernmental negotiation among member states whose domestic electorates are moving faster to the right than the architecture can follow.

The post-2024 European Union apparatus is, documented, the principal regional case study of a supranational-and-intergovernmental hybrid framework restructuring its financial-and-defence framework under the pressure of the post-2025 Trump second-term US realignment, while operating across multiple member states whose post-2024 electoral cycles have produced the most right-leaning composition of the European Parliament in the post-1979 record. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Dossier 80: The International Criminal Court — The Rome Statute Architecture and the Post-2023 Confrontation

## The power apparatusure

The International Criminal Court (ICC) was established under the Rome Statute, adopted on 17 July 1998 at the Rome Diplomatic Conference and entered into force on 1 July 2002 following the 60th ratification. The Court is headquartered in The Hague, Netherlands. It is the first permanent international criminal court — distinct from the ad hoc tribunals (ICTY for the former Yugoslavia, ICTR for Rwanda) that operated under UN Security Council Chapter VII authority across the 1993-2017 period.

The Rome Statute has 125 state parties as of 2026 — roughly two-thirds of UN member states. Non-parties include three of the five UN Security Council permanent members (the United States, China, and the Russian Federation), and multiple major regional powers (India, Pakistan, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Israel, Türkiye, Egypt, and Ethiopia). The post-2002 accession pattern has reflected the tension between the Court's universalist institutional posture and the post-1945 state-sovereignty architecture.

The Court has jurisdiction over four crimes under Article 5 of the Rome Statute: genocide (Article 6); crimes against humanity (Article 7); war crimes (Article 8); and the crime of aggression (Article 8 bis, activated July 2018 with sharp jurisdictional limitations). The Court exercises jurisdiction over crimes committed on the territory of state parties, by nationals of state parties, or referred to the Court by the UN Security Council under Chapter VII.

The institutional structure: the Office of the Prosecutor (OTP) led by the elected Prosecutor; the 18-judge bench organised across the Pre-Trial, Trial, and Appeals Divisions; the Registry; and the Assembly of States Parties as the supervisory body.

The current Prosecutor is Karim Ahmad Khan KC (United Kingdom), in office since 16 June 2021 for a nine-year non-renewable term. The current President of the Court is Tomoko Akane (Japan), in office since March 2024.

## Karim Khan — biographical foundation

Karim Ahmad Khan was born on 30 March 1970 in Edinburgh, Scotland, to a Pakistani Ahmadiyya family. His father was a senior NHS dermatologist. He attended Edinburgh University and King's College London for his law degree. He completed his Bar training at Lincoln's Inn and was called to the Bar in 1992.

His pre-ICC career: a international criminal-law practice including senior counsel positions before the ad hoc tribunals (ICTY, ICTR, Special Court for Sierra Leone, Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia, Special Tribunal for Lebanon) and the ICC. He served as Special Adviser and Head of the UN Investigative Team for the Promotion of Accountability for Crimes Committed by Da'esh/ISIL (UNITAD) 2018-2021. He was elected ICC Prosecutor on 12 February 2021 by the Assembly of States Parties and took office on 16 June 2021.

His prosecutorial record before assuming the ICC Prosecutor position included defence work for multiple ICTY and ICTR defendants — institutional experience that the 2021 election framework treated as cross-examination expertise rather than as institutional bias.

## The post-March 2023 Putin arrest warrant

On 17 March 2023 the ICC Pre-Trial Chamber II issued arrest warrants for Russian President Vladimir Putin and Russian Children's Rights Commissioner Maria Lvova-Belova for the war crime of unlawful deportation and transfer of children from Ukrainian territory to the Russian Federation. The institutional speed of the warrant: the OTP filed the application in early February 2023; the Pre-Trial Chamber issued the warrant three weeks later — the record for ICC warrant-issuance speed.

The institutional consequence: Putin became the first sitting head of state of a UN Security Council permanent member to face an ICC arrest warrant. His subsequent travel was restricted to ICC non-parties — China, multiple Central Asian states (Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan), and the post-September 2024 Mongolia visit that produced sustained international criticism after Mongolia (an ICC state party) failed to arrest him.

The post-2023 ICC enforcement record: Putin has not been arrested. The ICC has no police force; member states are required under Article 86 of the Rome Statute to cooperate with the Court including by arresting suspects on their territory. Multiple member states have publicly committed to arresting Putin if he enters their territory; multiple others (including Hungary, South Africa under previous conditions, and Mongolia) have  signalled that they would not.

## The May 2024 Netanyahu-Gallant warrant application

On 20 May 2024 Prosecutor Khan announced that he was applying to Pre-Trial Chamber I for arrest warrants against five individuals in connection with the post-7 October 2023 Israel-Hamas conflict: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu; Israeli Defence Minister Yoav Gallant; and three Hamas leaders — Yahya Sinwar, Mohammed Deif, and Ismail Haniyeh.

The institutional charges against Netanyahu and Gallant: war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza from at least 8 October 2023, including starvation as a method of warfare; murder; persecution; and other inhumane acts. The institutional charges against the three Hamas leaders: war crimes and crimes against humanity in connection with the 7 October 2023 attacks including murder, hostage-taking, and sexual violence.

The post-application sequence: Haniyeh was killed in Tehran on 31 July 2024 in an Israeli operation. Sinwar was killed in Rafah on 16 October 2024 by Israeli forces. Deif's status remained disputed across mid-2024 — Israel claimed to have killed him in a July 2024 airstrike; Hamas neither confirmed nor denied. The warrants against the three Hamas leaders were subsequently withdrawn upon the structural confirmation of their deaths.

On 21 November 2024 the Pre-Trial Chamber I — a three-judge panel — issued the arrest warrants against Netanyahu and Gallant unanimously. The institutional reasoning: "reasonable grounds to believe that both individuals intentionally and knowingly deprived the civilian population in Gaza of objects indispensable to their survival, including food, water, and medicine and medical supplies, as well as fuel and electricity." The warrants covered the period 8 October 2023 to at least 20 May 2024.

The institutional significance: Netanyahu became the first head of government of a Western-aligned democratic state to face ICC arrest warrants. The warrants triggered the confrontation with the Israeli government, the post-Biden US administration, and (after January 2025) the Trump second-term US administration.

## The post-2024 Khan personal-misconduct allegations

A separate institutional process has developed across 2024-2026. In April 2024 a senior female ICC staff member formally reported to senior ICC officials allegations of sexual misconduct against Khan. The allegations were widely reported across multiple international press outlets in October-November 2024.

The institutional sequence: Khan was reportedly informed of the complaint shortly before the May 2024 announcement of the Netanyahu-Gallant arrest warrant application. He cancelled a planned visit to Israel and Gaza days before the announcement. Multiple senior ICC officials and diplomats subsequently raised institutional questions about whether the May 2024 application timing was influenced by the personal-misconduct complaint.

Khan has categorically denied the allegations. His legal team has stated that his prosecutorial work was based solely on legal and evidentiary considerations. As of May 2026 the ICC Independent Oversight Mechanism investigation remains open; Khan has temporarily stepped aside from certain prosecutorial functions in connection with the investigation.

The consequence: the post-2024 personal-misconduct allegations have produced documented institutional tension within the Court between protection of the prosecutorial independence and accountability for senior-leadership misconduct. Multiple state parties and civil-society organisations have called for institutional reform of the Court's senior-misconduct framework.

## The post-2025 Trump-second-term sanctions architecture

The Trump first-term US administration imposed sanctions on the ICC Prosecutor and senior officials in September 2020 under Executive Order 13928 — sanctions that the Biden administration revoked in April 2021 through Executive Order 14022. The Trump second-term US administration has institutionalised a sharper sanctions architecture.

On 6 February 2025 — sixteen days after the inauguration — Trump signed Executive Order 14203 "Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court." The order designated Khan and additional ICC officials for asset freezes and travel restrictions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The reasoning of the order: the ICC's "illegitimate and baseless actions targeting America and our close ally Israel."

The post-February 2025 institutional consequences: Khan's US-denominated assets have been frozen; he is prohibited from entering the United States; multiple US-headquartered organisations (Microsoft, Westlaw, and other multi-state service providers) have institutionalised compliance frameworks that restrict the Court's access to multiple commercial services. The Court has institutionalised workarounds through European-headquartered alternatives.

The European Union response: multiple member states including France, Germany, and the Netherlands have publicly committed to honouring the ICC arrest warrants and to protecting Court personnel from US-led sanctions. The post-2025 EU "blocking statute" framework has been activated to protect EU-based ICC operations.

## The record — cumulative

The cumulative ICC record since the post-2002 period:

Twelve situations under investigation including the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda, the Central African Republic, Sudan/Darfur, Kenya, Libya, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, Georgia, Burundi, Bangladesh/Myanmar, Afghanistan, Venezuela, the Philippines, Ukraine, and the State of Palestine.

Eleven convictions including Thomas Lubanga (DRC, 2012), Germain Katanga (DRC, 2014), Jean-Pierre Bemba (CAR, 2016, partially overturned 2018), Bosco Ntaganda (DRC, 2019), Dominic Ongwen (Uganda, 2021), and Mahamat Said Abdel Kani (CAR, 2024). The post-2024 docket includes the post-March 2025 Rodrigo Duterte case discussed in Dossier 67.

Multiple high-profile cases withdrawn or dismissed for evidentiary insufficiency including the post-2014 Kenya cases (Kenyatta, Ruto, Kosgey) and the 2019 Côte d'Ivoire Gbagbo case.

Outstanding arrest warrants as of 2026 include Putin, Lvova-Belova, al-Bashir (since 2009), and Netanyahu and Gallant. The cumulative documented outstanding arrest warrants exceed twenty individuals.

## The through-line

The Karim Khan biographical record is the public-facing record of the Edinburgh-born British-Pakistani international criminal lawyer who built a ad-hoc-tribunal practice. He served as Special Adviser to UNITAD on Da'esh/ISIL accountability 2018-2021, was elected ICC Prosecutor in February 2021 for a nine-year non-renewable term, applied for arrest warrants against Netanyahu and Gallant in May 2024 producing the November 2024 warrants, faces a parallel ICC Independent Oversight Mechanism investigation into personal-misconduct allegations, and operates the Office of the Prosecutor under post-February 2025 US Treasury sanctions.

The ICC institutional record is the operation of a post-2002 permanent international criminal court that has, documented, accumulated 125 state parties throughout the post-2002 period. It has investigated twelve situations across the docket and produced eleven convictions. It has issued multiple high-profile arrest warrants including the post-March 2023 Putin warrant and the post-November 2024 Netanyahu-Gallant warrants. It has operated under sustained post-2024 institutional pressure including the post-February 2025 US sanctions architecture. It has demonstrated documented institutional limitations including the lack of an enforcement mechanism, the dependence on state-party cooperation, and the post-2024 senior-leadership-misconduct framework crisis.

125 state parties. Twelve situations. Eleven convictions across twenty-two years. Outstanding arrest warrants for Putin, al-Bashir, Netanyahu, Gallant. No enforcement mechanism. US Treasury sanctions on the Prosecutor's office. A personal-misconduct investigation against the Prosecutor himself. The ICC's presentation is international criminal justice. The ICC's operation is a treaty-based jurisdiction that can indict but cannot compel, can warrant but cannot arrest, and can prosecute but cannot protect its own personnel from the sanctions of non-party states.

The post-2002 ICC architecture under Khan is, documented, the principal global institutional case study of an international criminal-justice framework operating under the structural limitations of a treaty-based jurisdiction without enforcement capacity, in confrontation with the largest non-party state's institutional capacity to impose targeted sanctions on Court personnel, and through the post-2024 personal-misconduct framework crisis that has produced sustained institutional pressure on the prosecutorial independence framework. The architecture works as designed.

# Dossier 81: The International Court of Justice and the Post-2023 South Africa Genocide Case

## The governing machineure

The International Court of Justice (ICJ) is the principal judicial organ of the United Nations, established under Chapter XIV of the UN Charter (Articles 92-96) and operating under the 1945 Statute of the International Court of Justice — annexed to the UN Charter as an integral part of the post-1945 institutional framework. The Court is seated at the Peace Palace in The Hague, Netherlands. Its jurisdiction encompasses two distinct functions: contentious cases (legal disputes between states) and advisory opinions (legal questions referred by UN organs and authorised specialised agencies).

The ICJ comprises 15 judges elected for nine-year renewable terms by separate concurrent votes of the UN General Assembly and the UN Security Council — the governing architecture that produces continuous P5 representation through informal but documented post-1945 convention. The current composition includes judges from China (Xue Hanqin), France (Bogdan Aurescu), the Russian Federation (Kirill Gevorgian), the United Kingdom (Dame Hilary Charlesworth), the United States (Sarah H. Cleveland), plus ten additional judges from across the multi-regional UN membership.

The current ICJ President is Judge Nawaf Salam of Lebanon, elected to the Presidency on 6 February 2024 for a three-year term. Salam resigned as Member of the Court with effect from 14 January 2025 upon his appointment as Prime Minister of Lebanon. His successor as President assumed office in February 2025.

The Court has 193 states parties through their UN membership (all UN members are automatically parties to the ICJ Statute). Acceptance of compulsory jurisdiction under Article 36(2) of the Statute is voluntary; 74 states have accepted such jurisdiction as of 2026. The United States withdrew its 1946 acceptance in 1986 following the *Nicaragua v. United States* judgment. Israel has never accepted compulsory jurisdiction.

## The 1945-2023 institutional record

The ICJ docket across its first eight decades has produced roughly 200 contentious cases and 30 advisory opinions. The principal precedents include the 1949 *Corfu Channel* case (UK v. Albania, the Court's first contentious judgment); the 1986 *Nicaragua v. United States* judgment finding US support for Contra rebels in violation of international law; the 1996 *Legality of Nuclear Weapons* advisory opinion; the 2004 *Wall* advisory opinion finding the Israeli West Bank barrier construction in violation of international law; and the 2007 *Bosnia v. Serbia* judgment under the Genocide Convention.

The 2019 *Gambia v. Myanmar* case under the Genocide Convention — addressing Myanmar military operations against the Rohingya population — established the post-2019 jurisprudence permitting third-state Genocide Convention enforcement. The Court's January 2020 provisional measures order in *Gambia v. Myanmar* required Myanmar to prevent genocidal acts against the Rohingya. The merits proceedings continued across 2023-2026; public hearings on the merits were held 12-29 January 2026. The case is the precedent for the post-2023 South Africa case.

## The 19 July 2024 Wall Advisory Opinion

On 30 December 2022 the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution 77/247 requesting an ICJ advisory opinion on the legal consequences of the prolonged Israeli occupation of Palestinian territory. The Court held public hearings in February 2024 with submissions from 50+ states and three international organisations.

On 19 July 2024 the Court delivered the advisory opinion. The principal findings (by votes of 11-4 to 14-1 depending on the specific point): the Israeli presence in the Occupied Palestinian Territory is unlawful; Israel has an obligation to terminate its presence as rapidly as possible; Israel has an obligation to provide reparations for damage caused; Israel has an obligation to cease all new settlement activities; all UN member states have an obligation not to recognise the unlawful situation and not to assist in maintaining it.

The 19 July 2024 advisory opinion was characterised across the multi-source international-law analytical press as the most consequential post-2004 ICJ Israeli-Palestinian ruling. The consequences: the post-July 2024 multi-state diplomatic responses including the post-2024 wave of state recognitions of Palestine (Ireland, Norway, Spain, Slovenia, Armenia in 2024; multiple post-2024 additional recognitions through 2025-2026); the post-2024 UN General Assembly resolutions citing the advisory opinion; and the post-2024 multi-source academic-legal characterisation of the Israeli presence in the OPT as  illegitimate under international law.

## The 29 December 2023 South Africa filing

On 29 December 2023 — eighty-three days after the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the start of the Israeli military campaign in Gaza — South Africa filed an Application instituting proceedings against Israel under the 1948 Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide. The application alleged that Israeli acts and omissions in Gaza constituted, were intended to constitute, or risked constituting genocide against the Palestinian population of Gaza.

The South African application was unprecedented. Three previous Genocide Convention cases had been filed (*Bosnia v. Serbia*, *Croatia v. Serbia*, *Gambia v. Myanmar*); none had been filed during an active military campaign by the respondent state. The South African legal team — led by Vusi Madonsela (Ambassador to the Netherlands) and including Adila Hassim SC, Tembeka Ngcukaitobi SC, John Dugard SC, Vaughan Lowe KC, and Max du Plessis SC — assembled the 84-page application across the post-October 2023 period.

The 11-12 January 2024 hearings on provisional measures: South Africa's oral submissions (11 January) presented the documentary evidentiary base including statements by Israeli officials (Defence Minister Yoav Gallant's 9 October 2023 "complete siege" statement; President Isaac Herzog's 12 October 2023 "entire nation" statement; Prime Minister Netanyahu's invocation of Amalek), the post-October 2023 Israeli military operations record, and the documented post-October 2023 humanitarian catastrophe. Israel's submissions (12 January) characterised the case as an inversion of reality and argued the proceedings should be dismissed on prima facie jurisdictional grounds.

## The 26 January 2024 provisional measures order

On 26 January 2024 the Court delivered its provisional measures order. The key findings (by majority votes of 15-2 to 16-1):

- The Court has prima facie jurisdiction under Article IX of the Genocide Convention to hear the case
- South Africa has standing to bring the case under the *Gambia v. Myanmar* precedent
- The Palestinians in Gaza constitute a protected group under the Genocide Convention
- A plausible risk exists that some of South Africa's claimed rights under the Genocide Convention are being violated

The Court ordered six provisional measures binding on Israel:

- take all measures within its power to prevent the commission of all acts within the scope of Article II of the Genocide Convention
- ensure with immediate effect that its military does not commit any such acts
- take all measures within its power to prevent and punish direct and public incitement to commit genocide against Palestinians in Gaza
- take immediate and effective measures to enable provision of urgently needed basic services and humanitarian assistance
- take effective measures to prevent the destruction of evidence
- submit a report to the Court within one month on measures taken to comply

The vote breakdown: 15 judges voted in favour of the principal provisional measures (including ICJ President Joan Donoghue of the US, Vice-President Kirill Gevorgian of Russia, and judges from the UK, France, Germany, Australia, Japan, China, India, and other states). Two judges voted against the measures: Judge Sebutinde of Uganda (who appended a dissenting opinion) and Judge ad hoc Aharon Barak (the Israeli appointee).

## The post-January 2024 procedural sequence

The post-26 January 2024 procedural sequence has run across multiple Court orders.

On 16 February 2024 the Court, considering South Africa's additional submissions on the Rafah situation, declined to order additional provisional measures but emphasised that the situation required immediate compliance with the existing provisional measures.

On 28 March 2024 the Court issued an additional provisional measures order (15-2) following the post-February 2024 worsening Gaza famine conditions. The order required Israel to ensure unhindered provision of basic food supplies and humanitarian assistance, including via additional land crossings.

On 24 May 2024 the Court issued the third provisional measures order (13-2) following the May 2024 Rafah ground operation. The order required Israel to immediately halt military operations in Rafah and to maintain the Rafah crossing open for humanitarian aid.

The post-2024 South African memorial: on 28 October 2024 South Africa filed its memorial on the merits — over 750 pages of text and over 4,000 pages of exhibits and annexes. Under ICJ rules the memorial is not public. Israel was initially required to file its counter-memorial by 28 July 2025. Israel requested a six-month extension; in April 2025 the Court granted a five-and-a-half month extension to 12 January 2026. Israel filed its counter-memorial on 12 January 2026 (the Court extended further to 12 March 2026 in some procedural sources).

## The post-2024 intervention wave

The post-26 January 2024 multi-state intervention pattern has been the most consequential in the Court's post-1945 record. As of May 2026 the following states have filed declarations of intervention or applications for permission to intervene under Article 63 of the Statute:

Nicaragua (May 2024), Colombia (April 2024), Libya (June 2024), Mexico (May 2024), Palestine (June 2024), Spain (June 2024), Turkey (August 2024), Chile (September 2024), Maldives (October 2024), Bolivia (November 2024), Ireland (December 2024), Brazil (March 2025), Belize (June 2025), Belgium (2025), Paraguay (2026), and multiple additional states. The cumulative pattern: roughly 17 states intervening on the South African side; no state has intervened on the Israeli side.

The cumulative state participation in the South African case — including the original applicant, the interventions, and the documented post-2024 multi-state statements citing the proceedings — represents the largest international-judicial-proceeding state participation in the post-1945 record.

## The Salam transition and the post-2025 Presidency

Judge Nawaf Salam of Lebanon was elected ICJ President on 6 February 2024 — the first Arab judge to hold the Presidency. He had voted with the majority in the 26 January 2024 provisional measures order. On 13 January 2025 Salam was appointed Prime Minister of Lebanon following the November 2024 Lebanese presidential election; he resigned as Member of the Court with effect from 14 January 2025. The Court's First Vice-President assumed the Presidency on an acting basis pending the post-2025 ICJ election.

## The post-2024 US institutional posture

The post-February 2025 US administration has not imposed sanctions on ICJ judges or personnel — the ICJ remains distinct from the ICC under US Treasury sanctions categorisation. However, the US institutional posture toward the ICJ has been documented as adversarial across multiple post-2024 framings. President Trump's February 2025 Executive Order 14199 on the multi-source UN withdrawal addressed UNHRC and UNRWA specifically; the ICJ was not included.

The post-2024 US position on the South African case: the State Department under Secretary of State Antony Blinken (until January 2025) had characterised the South African application as "meritless." The post-January 2025 State Department under Secretary Marco Rubio has retained the post-Biden-era characterisation. Neither administration has filed an intervention. The US has not formally submitted observations to the Court on the merits.

## The end point

The International Court of Justice institutional record is the public-facing record of the post-1945 principal UN judicial organ. It has 193 states parties through UN membership, 15 judges across nine-year renewable terms, and a jurisprudence across roughly 200 contentious cases. It has produced multiple consequential advisory opinions including the 19 July 2024 ruling that the Israeli presence in the Occupied Palestinian Territory is unlawful. It is conducting the *South Africa v. Israel* genocide proceedings — the largest multi-state intervention case in the Court's post-1945 record — under provisional measures binding on Israel since January 2024.

The operational record is the operation of a Court that has, documented, found prima facie jurisdiction and plausibility under the Genocide Convention against an incumbent UN member state engaged in active military operations; ordered three successive provisional measures binding the respondent; received multi-state interventions on the applicant side without a single intervention on the respondent side; and operated without the formal cooperation of the United States, the United Kingdom (which has not intervened), or the European Union member states that have not joined the post-2024 intervention wave.

The Court found prima facie jurisdiction. It found plausibility under the Genocide Convention. It ordered three successive provisional measures. Multiple states intervened on the applicant side. Zero intervened on the respondent side. Israel continued military operations. The ICJ can issue legally binding orders against a sitting UN member state conducting active military operations. It cannot, in operational practice, compel a single one of those orders to be followed. The gap between the binding legal authority and the enforcement capacity is the architecture.

The post-2023 International Court of Justice is, documented, the principal post-1945 institutional case study of a supranational judicial framework operating at the limit of its enforcement capacity. The Court can issue legally binding provisional measures against a sitting UN member state engaged in active military operations. The Court cannot, in operational practice, compel compliance with those measures. The architecture works as designed.

# Dossier 82: The World Health Organization and the Post-2025 US Withdrawal

## The skeletonure

The World Health Organization (WHO) is the specialised UN agency for international public health, established by the WHO Constitution adopted on 22 July 1946 and entering into force on 7 April 1948 — the date observed annually as World Health Day. The WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, and operates through six regional offices (Africa, the Americas, South-East Asia, Europe, Eastern Mediterranean, Western Pacific) and 150+ country offices.

The architecture: the World Health Assembly (WHA) — the supreme decision-making body comprising delegations from all 194 member states, meeting annually each May in Geneva. The Executive Board — a 34-member technical body elected by the WHA for three-year terms. The Director-General — the chief technical and administrative officer, elected by the WHA for renewable five-year terms.

The current Director-General is Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus of Ethiopia. He was first elected on 23 May 2017 (the first African Director-General in WHO history) and re-elected unopposed on 24 May 2022 for a second term ending in mid-2027. He is a former Ethiopian Minister of Health (2005-2012) and Minister of Foreign Affairs (2012-2016).

The WHO budget architecture: the 2024-2025 biennial budget was set at $6.8 billion, financed through assessed contributions (mandatory member-state payments based on the UN scale) and voluntary contributions (the principal source, often earmarked for specific programmes). The largest historical donor since the post-1948 period has been the United States; the second-largest is the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.

## The 2017-2020 first Tedros term

The 2017 Director-General election was the first WHO contest conducted under the post-2012 reformed election process — direct WHA secret ballot rather than the previous Executive Board nomination. Tedros defeated David Nabarro (UK) in the final round 133-50.

His first-term institutional record: the post-2017 multi-year transformation initiative; the multi-region polio-eradication framework; the post-2018 ebola response across the Democratic Republic of Congo; and the post-2019 COVID-19 response that became thely defining episode of his tenure.

The COVID-19 sequence: on 31 December 2019 the WHO China Country Office was informed by Chinese authorities of multiple cases of pneumonia of unknown aetiology in Wuhan. On 30 January 2020 the WHO declared the outbreak a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) under the International Health Regulations. On 11 March 2020 Tedros characterised the COVID-19 outbreak as a pandemic.

The post-March 2020 US institutional response: on 14 April 2020 President Trump announced the suspension of US funding to the WHO pending review of the WHO's COVID-19 management. On 18 May 2020 Trump sent a formal letter to Tedros stating that US contributions would be halted and that the US would withdraw from the WHO if material improvements were not committed to within 30 days. On 7 July 2020 the US notified the UN Secretary-General of its withdrawal from the WHO, effective 6 July 2021 under the one-year notice requirement.

On 25 January 2021 President Biden retracted the US notice of withdrawal. The US remained a WHO member state throughout the post-2021 Biden administration.

## The 20 January 2025 Trump second-term withdrawal

On 20 January 2025 — the day of his second inauguration — President Trump signed Executive Order 14155 withdrawing the United States from the WHO. The order was the first executive action of the second term.

The order's stated rationale (verbatim): "the organization's mishandling of the COVID-19 pandemic that arose out of Wuhan, China, and other global health crises, its failure to adopt urgently needed reforms, and its inability to demonstrate independence from the inappropriate political influence of WHO member states." The order also cited "unfairly onerous payments" from the United States "far out of proportion with other countries' assessed payments" — singling out China, with 1.4 billion population (300 percent of the US population) contributing "nearly 90 percent less to the WHO."

The operational provisions of Executive Order 14155: (i) pause future US Government funds, support, or resources to the WHO; (ii) recall and reassign US Government personnel or contractors working in any capacity with the WHO; (iii) identify credible US and international partners to assume necessary activities previously undertaken by the WHO; (iv) review, rescind, and replace the 2024 US Global Health Security Strategy; (v) cease negotiations on the WHO Pandemic Agreement and the International Health Regulations amendments.

The withdrawal notification was transmitted to the UN Secretary-General. The withdrawal becomes effective on 22 January 2026 under the one-year notice requirement. The US was the largest single contributor to the 2024-2025 biennial budget; the $600 million already committed for that biennium was not paid in full following the Executive Order.

The consequence: the post-January 2025 WHO budget shortfall was documented at $600 million for the 2024-2025 biennium with materially larger shortfalls projected for 2026-2027. In May 2025 the WHO Executive Board approved a roughly 20% reduction in the proposed 2026-2027 budget. Tedros announced the elimination of about half of the WHO leadership team positions and the scaling back of multiple programmes including the emergency response framework.

## The 20 May 2025 WHO Pandemic Agreement

The post-2021 WHO Pandemic Agreement negotiations had been conducted across multiple cycles by the Intergovernmental Negotiating Body (INB) established by the December 2021 Special Session of the World Health Assembly. The negotiating period ran from 2022 through April 2025 across thirteen INB meetings.

The principal areas of the Agreement: equitable access to vaccines, treatments, and diagnostics during pandemic events; pathogen-and-data-sharing mechanisms; surveillance-and-detection coordination; supply-chain frameworks; and the multi-source One Health approach addressing animal, human, and environmental health interactions.

On 16 April 2025 the INB concluded negotiations and produced the final draft text. On 19 May 2025 the World Health Assembly opened in Geneva with the Pandemic Agreement on the agenda. On 19 May 2025 evening the WHA Committee A vote endorsed the Agreement; the 20 May 2025 formal adoption vote was 124 in favour, 11 abstentions, and 0 opposed.

The United States did not attend the 78th World Health Assembly — the first such absence in WHO history. The post-Executive-Order 14155 US withdrawal process was in effect; US negotiators had withdrawn from INB proceedings following the 20 January 2025 order. The agreement is not binding on the United States.

The Pathogen Access and Benefit-Sharing (PABS) annex — the section governing pathogen-and-data-sharing in exchange for fair access to resulting vaccines, tests, and treatments — was set aside during the May 2025 adoption due to multi-state disagreement. The post-2025 INB continued negotiations on the PABS annex across 2025-2026. On 1 May 2026 member states extended the PABS talks; the annex is now expected to be submitted to the 2027 World Health Assembly (or to a special 2026 WHA session, depending on the post-2026 negotiating pace).

The Pandemic Agreement enters into force following ratification by 60 states. As of May 2026 the ratification process is in early stages; the absence of US participation has been widely characterised across the multi-source public-health analytical press as a limitation on the post-2025 framework.

## The post-2025 institutional posture

The post-January 2025 WHO institutional posture under Tedros has been documented across multiple themes.

The financial restructuring: the post-2025 multi-source documented WHO efforts to fill the post-US-withdrawal funding gap have included expanded contributions from the European Union, Germany, Japan, China, the UK, and multiple post-2024 increased pledges from middle-income states. Tedros stated in December 2025 that he was "confident" the $1 billion budget cut could be filled and that the WHO's "standing is very good."

The bilateral US frameworks: the post-2025 US administration has pursued bilateral health-cooperation arrangements with multiple states — including African states with HIV-and-tuberculosis programme dependencies. Tedros has publicly characterised such bilateral arrangements as "no problem" for the broader multilateral framework. The post-2025 US health-policy architecture under HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has prioritised the post-2025 withdrawal from vaccine-and-public-health frameworks across multiple post-2025 institutional moves.

The post-2025 childhood-mortality concern: Tedros stated in December 2025 that early estimates indicated childhood mortality could increase for the first time this century, driven by post-2025 cuts to donors' foreign-health spending — principally the US framework but also UK, German, and French reductions.

## The documented verdict

The World Health Organization institutional record is the public-facing record of the post-1948 specialised UN agency for international public health. The WHO has 194 member states. It is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, under Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus since 2017. It has produced the post-1948 institutional record of disease-eradication frameworks including the post-1980 smallpox eradication, the post-1988 polio eradication framework, the post-2000s HIV/AIDS treatment scale-up, and the multi-region post-2014 ebola response. It produced the post-March 2020 COVID-19 pandemic response that became the post-2020 multi-source institutional credibility flash-point. It produced the 20 May 2025 Pandemic Agreement — the first such international instrument in WHO history.

The institutional operational record is the operation of an agency that has, documented, sustained the post-1948 institutional framework for global public-health coordination and produced the post-2020 COVID-19 response that has been characterised across multiple analytical sources as both slow on initial-detection and successful on subsequent-coordination. It entered post-January 2025 documented financial crisis following Trump Executive Order 14155, sustained the post-2025 Pandemic Agreement adoption without US participation, documented the post-2025 funding shortfall as $600 million for 2024-2025 and materially larger for 2026-2027, and produced the post-2025 multi-source institutional restructuring including roughly 20% budget cuts and elimination of half the senior leadership positions.

194 member states. Smallpox eradicated. Polio nearly eradicated. The COVID-19 pandemic response that became the credibility flash-point. The May 2025 Pandemic Agreement adopted without the participation of the largest historical donor. A $600 million funding shortfall. Half the senior leadership positions eliminated. The WHO's presentation is global public health. The WHO's operation is a specialised agency that cannot survive the withdrawal of the state whose contributions underwrote it.

The post-2025 World Health Organization is, documented, the principal post-1948 case study of a specialised UN agency operating under documented financial-and-political crisis produced by the withdrawal of its largest historical donor, while sustaining the multi-state multilateral framework for the post-2020 pandemic-preparedness architecture. The architecture works as designed.


---

# Atlantic Closing Bridge

The Atlantic section runs from Dossier 1 through Dossier 20. Twelve American principal-figure dossiers (Biden, Trump, Obama, Hillary Clinton, Bill Clinton, Blair, Pelosi, Schumer, McConnell, the Bush dynasty, the Cheneys, Kissinger). Five integrated American apparatus dossiers (the Supreme Court; the Intelligence Community principals; the Federalist Society and Heritage Foundation; DOGE / Musk / Vance / Ramaswamy; AIPAC). Three Atlantic European dossiers (the Starmer Labour government from July 2024; the post-Cameron UK Conservative administration 2010-2024; the contemporary European leadership — Merz / Meloni / Tusk / Orbán / von der Leyen / Rutte / Costa / Metsola).

The twenty dossiers document the same pattern. The pattern is the answer to the question this volume opened with: who is operating the apparatus that Volume One named? The answer is: an integrated political class that operates the documented apparatus on documented terms.

## The pattern

Twenty dossiers in the Atlantic section. The same pattern repeats across every one:

The dossier subject is positioned by a documented funding pathway — donor names disclosed in Federal Election Commission filings, Companies House filings, Land Registry records, Securities and Exchange Commission disclosures, Office of Government Ethics filings, parliamentary registers of interests, court records, FOIA-released communications, leaked private email caches, House Oversight Committee releases, Senate Intelligence Committee reports.

The dossier subject is staffed by a documented professional-services apparatus — the same Manhattan and Washington law firms (Wachtell, Sullivan & Cromwell, Kirkland & Ellis, Mayer Brown), the same management consulting firms (McKinsey, BCG, Bain), and the same accounting firms (the Big Four). The same lobbying firms recur (the Brunswick Group, FGS Global, K Street's named partners), the same campaign-advisory firms (the Lynton Crosby and David Axelrod and Sergio Bendixen architectures), and the same think-tank-and-foundation pipelines (the Federalist Society, the Heritage Foundation, the Aspen Institute, the Atlantic Council, the Brookings Institution, Chatham House, the European Council on Foreign Relations, the Centre for European Reform).

The dossier subject's policy decisions correlate with the documented financial interests of the funding pathways — the Biden bankruptcy bill of 2005 and the MBNA Delaware credit-card-industry relationship; the Clinton bank-deregulation programme of the 1990s and the Wall Street donor base; the Blair Iraq vote of 2003 and the Carlyle-and-defence-contracting architecture; the Cameron austerity programme of 2010-2016 and the post-2008 financial-services-regulatory-capture continuity; the Truss-Kwarteng mini-Budget of September 2022 and the bond-market repudiation; the Merz BlackRock chairmanship of 2016-2020 and the 2025 German Chancellorship.

The dossier subject's post-government career produces a documented financial extraction — Cameron's $10 million-pre-tax compensation from Greensill Capital; Blair's Tony Blair Institute and the Saudi-Israeli-Trump consulting income; Clinton Foundation's $2 billion-plus cumulative donations including the foreign-government channels; Pelosi family's documented stock-trading outperformance; Kissinger Associates and the Russian and Chinese client list across the 1982-2023 period; the Sunak family's £730 million Murty wealth and the £20 million UK tax saving from the non-dom status.

The dossier subject's public communications operate through the same press apparatus the first volume of this series documented — the Bezos-owned *Washington Post*, the Sulzberger-family *New York Times*, the Murdoch press, the *Atlantic*, the *Economist*, the *Financial Times*, the BBC, CNN, Sky News, France 24, Deutsche Welle, RAI, the Spanish *El País*, the Italian *Corriere della Sera*, the German *Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung*. The fence around the record is staffed by the columnists at these institutions. The fence is not editorial judgement. It is paid work.

The dossier subject's foreign-policy positions correlate with the documented integration of the Atlantic political class with the American military-industrial complex — every Atlantic dossier of the post-2001 period operating on the same post-9/11 Afghanistan-Iraq-Libya-Syria-Yemen-Ukraine-Gaza chain, every UK Conservative and Labour PM operating on the same NATO-aligned framework, every European figure from Merz back to von der Leyen back to Rutte back to Tusk back to Costa back to Metsola operating on the same trans-Atlantic-defence-procurement-and-financial-services architecture.

The dossier subject is captured by the AIPAC apparatus, by the Federalist Society judicial pipeline, by the Heritage Foundation policy blueprint, by the Atlantic Council foreign-policy framework, by the Brookings Institution fellowships, by the Council on Foreign Relations membership lists, by the World Economic Forum Young Global Leaders programme, by the Aspen Institute conference architecture, and by the post-1991 Soros Open Society Foundations programme (including the 1989 Soros scholarship to Pembroke College Oxford of one Viktor Orbán). The capture is documented in each individual case. The cumulative capture is the political class.

## The architecture

The architecture has four documented layers:

1. **The money layer** — the four-century debt-money-creation mechanism documented in Volume One.

2. **The political-class layer** — the twenty Atlantic dossiers of Volume Two and the regional dossiers that follow. The political class operates the mechanism. The political class is captured by the mechanism. The political class is not the mechanism's operator at the highest tier — the political class is the public-facing apparatus through which the operators dossiered in Volume One exercise their power.

3. **The professional-services-and-consensus-knowledge layer** — the Big Four, McKinsey, the major law firms, the major lobbying firms, the major think tanks, the major press, the major academic-publishing oligopoly (covered in Volume One). This layer manufactures the public characterisation of the political class as competent, democratic, rules-based, accountable, and reform-capable.

4. **The enforcement layer** — the intelligence-community principals (Volume Two Dossier 14), the military-industrial complex, the major weapons manufacturers (Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, Boeing, BAE Systems, Northrop Grumman, General Dynamics, Leonardo, Thales, Airbus, Dassault), the major mercenary-and-private-military contractors, the policing apparatus, the prison-industrial-complex apparatus, the migration-enforcement apparatus, the surveillance apparatus.

The four layers operate as a single integrated system. The Atlantic dossiers document the political-class layer in the geographies the Atlantic political class operates on.

## The Gaza test

The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the subsequent Israeli military operation in Gaza produced the single most documented test of the Atlantic architecture across the 2023-2026 period. Twenty-plus months of documented Israeli military operations against the Gaza Strip have produced, documented:

- Over 50,000 documented Palestinian deaths per the Gaza Health Ministry figures (subject to ongoing methodological debate but the central figure that the academic-genocide-scholar consensus, the medical journals including *The Lancet*, and the international-humanitarian-law literature have operated on).
- The 26 January 2024 International Court of Justice provisional measures ruling on South Africa's application under the Genocide Convention, finding plausibility of genocide and ordering Israel to take measures to prevent genocidal acts.
- The 24 May 2024 ICJ ruling ordering Israel to halt the Rafah offensive.
- The 21 November 2024 International Criminal Court arrest warrants against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and former Defense Minister Yoav Gallant.
- The post-2024 academic-genocide-scholar consensus characterisation (Raz Segal, Omer Bartov, A. Dirk Moses, Martin Shaw, Amos Goldberg, Lee Mordechai, and the international-genocide-studies scholarly literature).

The Atlantic response to the 7 October 2023-onward Israeli military operation is the documented test of the architecture this section has covered. The response, documented across every Atlantic operator dossiered in this volume:

- Continued US military aid — roughly $17.9 billion in the twelve months following 7 October 2023 (Dossier 17); the $14 billion April 2024 supplemental; the 2019-2028 $38 billion ten-year MOU continuing.
- Continued UK weapons-export licensing — the September 2024 partial licensing suspension that excluded F-35 components; the continuation of weapons-component supply through the partial-suspension framework.
- Continued German weapons exports — Berlin became the second-largest weapons supplier to Israel in 2023, with roughly €326 million in weapons-export approvals.
- Continued French weapons-component supply — the subsequent 2024 weapons-export controversy at the Eurosatory defence trade show in Paris.
- Continued EU diplomatic positioning — the coordinated von der Leyen-Commission position that produced internal European Parliament tensions; the Metsola European Parliament Presidency's pro-Israel positioning; the coordinated EU sanctions architecture against Russia that the subsequent EU diplomatic refusal to extend to Israel demonstrated the selective application of.
- Continued AIPAC primary intervention against Democratic congressional critics of the Israeli military operation — the June 2024 Bowman defeat and the August 2024 Bush defeat, the first two Squad-caucus losses in caucus history (Dossier 17).
- Continued public characterisation — the Starmer government's Mandelson appointment despite the pre-existing Mandelson-Epstein record (Dossier 18); the post-Conservative-administration UK position under Badenoch's pro-Israel framework (Dossier 19); the Meloni and Merz and Costa and Metsola public positions characterising the Israeli military operation as subsequent in the subsequent European interest (Dossier 20).

The Atlantic response to the 7 October 2023-onward Israeli military operation in Gaza is the single most documented case of coordinated Atlantic continuity in the documented historical record. The 26 January 2024 ICJ ruling, the 21 November 2024 ICC arrest warrants, the cumulative academic-genocide-scholar consensus, and the cumulative civilian-casualty record across the twenty-plus-month period have not produced the structural change the architecture's subsequent nominal commitments to subsequent international law and subsequent human rights would have predicted.

The 2023-2026 Gaza period is the single most documented case of the Atlantic architecture operating in the interest of the money-creation-and-arms-production architecture this volume's Volume One documented, against the nominal commitments the architecture publicly maintains. The Gaza chapter of Part XII will document the period at the length the record requires.

## The regional bridge

The Atlantic political class is half of the global political class. The other half is the regional architecture covered in Parts IV through XI. The pattern is the same. The regional variations are documented in the dossiers that follow.

Part IV documents South Asia — the post-2014 Modi BJP-RSS apparatus in India; the post-1971 Pakistani military leadership under General Asim Munir; the post-2024 Bangladeshi transition from the Sheikh Hasina period to the Muhammad Yunus interim government; the post-2024 Sri Lankan transition from the Rajapaksa period to the Anura Kumara Dissanayake National People's Power government; the contemporary Indian opposition under Rahul Gandhi; and the Sangh Parivar apparatus under Mohan Bhagwat's RSS leadership.

Part V documents East Asia — the CCP Politburo Standing Committee architecture under Xi Jinping; the Japanese ruling-party continuity under the LDP succession from Kishida to Ishiba; the Korean leadership transition; the Taiwanese DPP under William Lai's 2024 Presidential election victory; the post-2024 Mongolian transition.

Part VI documents Russia and the post-Soviet space — the Vladimir Putin architecture across the 2000-2026 period and the surrounding oligarchic-political integration; the Volodymyr Zelensky war presidency in Ukraine; the Aleksandr Lukashenko continuity in Belarus; the Central Asian and Caucasus leadership.

Part VII documents the Middle East — the Khamenei architecture in Iran; the Erdoğan AKP machinery in Turkey; the Mohammed bin Salman political-and-commercial architecture in Saudi Arabia; the Mohammed bin Zayed architecture in the UAE; the Qatari, Kuwaiti, Bahraini, Omani royal-family architectures; the Iraqi, Syrian, Lebanese, and Jordanian leaderships; the Egyptian architecture under Abdel Fattah el-Sisi; the Netanyahu-Smotrich-Ben-Gvir-Likud-Religious-Zionism Israeli architecture beyond what the Atlantic dossiers and the AIPAC dossier have reached.

Part VIII documents Africa — Nigeria under Bola Tinubu; South Africa under Cyril Ramaphosa and the post-2024 ANC-DA Government of National Unity coalition; Kenya under William Ruto; Ethiopia under Abiy Ahmed; and the Ghanaian, Senegalese, Moroccan, Algerian, Tunisian, Libyan, Sudanese, and DRC leaderships.

Part IX documents Latin America — Mexico under Claudia Sheinbaum; Brazil under Lula da Silva's third presidency; Argentina under Javier Milei; the Colombian, Chilean, Peruvian, Venezuelan, Cuban, and Central American leaderships.

Part X documents Southeast Asia, Oceania, and Canada — Indonesia under Prabowo Subianto; the Philippines under Ferdinand Marcos Jr.; the Vietnamese Politburo architecture; the Thai, Malaysian, Singaporean, Australian, New Zealand, and Canadian leaderships.

Part XI documents the international-organisations leadership — the UN principals under Secretary-General António Guterres; the World Bank, IMF, WTO, BIS, FSB, OECD principals; the international-humanitarian-law institutional figures.

The regional dossiers run on the same pattern the Atlantic dossiers have established. The connective tissue between the regional dossiers — the funding pathways, the professional-services apparatus, the policy-staffing pipelines, the consulting-firm advisers, the commercial-and-political integrations — demonstrates the global political-class architecture as a single integrated system rather than a collection of regional silos.

The work that follows is the documentation.

# Part XII Chapter 1: The Cold War Proxy Wars

## The architecture

Between 1945 and 1989 the Cold War produced roughly 30 million deaths. Almost none of those deaths occurred in the United States or the Soviet Union — the two states whose rivalry was the occasion for the killing. The dead were Korean, Vietnamese, Laotian, Cambodian, Indonesian, Chilean, Argentine, Uruguayan, Brazilian, Bolivian, Paraguayan, Salvadoran, Nicaraguan, Guatemalan, Honduran, Angolan, Mozambican, Ethiopian, Eritrean, Afghan, Greek, Malay, and a great many more nationalities besides. The wars were fought on their territory, in their towns, with their bodies. The two states whose rivalry was the occasion for the killing supplied weapons, money, intelligence, training, doctrine, propaganda, advisors, naval and air assets, and — when local proxies were considered inadequate — death squads and assassins.

The record covers the principal Cold War proxy wars. It is the dependency-root chapter of Part XII because every subsequent war chapter runs from the architecture established here. The post-1989 US wars covered in Chapter 2 run from the post-1945 military-and-intelligence apparatus built across the period covered in this chapter. The Iran-Iraq War covered in Chapter 3 runs from the Cold War pattern of great-power dual-side weapons supply. The Ukraine war covered in Chapter 4, the Gaza chapter covered in Chapter 5, and the multi-front contemporary wars covered in Chapter 6 run from the proxy-war architecture institutionalised across 1945-1989. The synthesis triad of Part XIII and the exits triad of Part XIV run from the record of this chapter forward.

The public characterisation of the Cold War was the contest between the "free world" and "international communism." The actual record shows: the post-1945 apparatus of US, Soviet, Chinese, British, French, Cuban, South African, Israeli, Pakistani, Argentine, and Brazilian intelligence services, militaries, and political-class operators producing the post-1945 architecture of proxy wars, covert operations, assassinations, coups, death squads, mass killings, and genocides covered across this chapter.

## Korea 1950-1953

The first major Cold War proxy war began on 25 June 1950, when roughly 75,000 troops of the Korean People's Army (KPA) of North Korea crossed the 38th parallel and invaded South Korea. The 38th parallel had been established in 1945 by the United States and the Soviet Union as the dividing line between their respective occupation zones following the surrender of Japan; the Soviet-American failure to agree on Korean reunification had produced two states, the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) under Kim Il-Sung in the north and the Republic of Korea (ROK) under Syngman Rhee in the south.

On 27 June 1950 the United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 83 calling on member nations to provide military support to the ROK. The Soviet delegate was absent — having walked out of the UNSC earlier in 1950 over the UN's refusal to seat a delegate from the People's Republic of China. President Harry S. Truman committed US air, ground, and naval forces under the UN Command led by General Douglas MacArthur; 15 other nations contributed troops. The US never formally declared war on North Korea, China, or the Soviet Union. Truman described the war as a "police action." The first use of force ever authorised by the Security Council under Chapter VII of the Charter was carried through this Soviet-absent vote.

The context: in April 1950 the US National Security Council had produced NSC-68, a policy directive recommending that the US use military force to "contain" communist expansionism anywhere it seemed to be occurring, "regardless of the intrinsic strategic or economic value of the lands in question." Truman framed the stakes: "If we let Korea down, the Soviet[s] will keep right on going and swallow up one [place] after another." The domino theory ran as the central architecture of US Cold War foreign policy across 1950-1989.

The war's three-year trajectory ran through four phases. First, the initial KPA advance to the Pusan perimeter at the southern tip of the peninsula by August 1950. Second, the 15 September 1950 Inchon landing by MacArthur reversing the KPA advance and driving UN forces to the Yalu River near the Chinese border by October 1950. Third, the Chinese intervention in October 1950 — the People's Volunteer Army under Marshal Peng Dehuai sending hundreds of thousands of troops across the Yalu — driving UN forces back below the 38th parallel by January 1951. Fourth, the May-June 1951 UN counteroffensive that established the front line roughly along the 38th parallel — followed by a two-year stalemate of trench warfare and aerial bombardment until the 27 July 1953 Korean Armistice Agreement at Panmunjom.

The 27 July 1953 armistice established the Korean Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) at the 38th parallel — a two-mile-wide buffer zone that remains the most heavily fortified border in the world. No formal peace treaty has been signed. The war technically continues to the date of writing.

The casualties were catastrophic and disproportionately civilian. Roughly 2.5 million people died according to Britannica's estimate; some estimates reach 5 million. Civilian deaths were estimated at 1.5 to 3 million — roughly 10 percent of the pre-war Korean population. The civilian-casualty rate was higher than that of World War II or the Vietnam War. US military deaths totalled 36,000+; wounded 92,000+; POWs 7,200; missing 8,000+. South Korean military deaths totalled roughly 200,000-250,000. Chinese military deaths totalled hundreds of thousands. North Korean military deaths totalled hundreds of thousands. The partition of the Korean peninsula has run in the post-1953 period as the central case study of Cold War partition.

The sustained US aerial bombing campaign against North Korea is the centrepiece of the war's civilian-casualty record. The US Strategic Air Command and Far East Air Force dropped 635,000 tons of bombs (including 32,557 tons of napalm) on North Korea — more than the total tonnage dropped by the US in the entire Pacific theatre of World War II. Curtis LeMay, then-Strategic Air Command commander, characterised the outcome: "Over a period of three years or so, we killed off — what — 20 percent of the population." General Douglas MacArthur testified to the US Senate in 1951 that the North Korean physical destruction was "the most devastating destruction of any country I had ever seen including Hiroshima and Nagasaki."

## Vietnam, Laos, Cambodia 1954-1975

The Vietnam War was the central Cold War proxy war of the 1955-1975 period. It produced roughly 3 million deaths and the US strategic defeat that the post-1975 architecture has run against ever since. It also produced the "secret wars" in Laos and Cambodia that killed 200,000-500,000 Laotians and 150,000-500,000 Cambodians in US bombing campaigns — and that produced the post-1975 Khmer Rouge genocide of 1.5-3 million Cambodians.

### Vietnam 1954-1975

In May 1954 the French garrison at Dien Bien Phu surrendered to the Viet Minh under General Vo Nguyen Giap after a 56-day siege. The Geneva Conference of July 1954 produced the Geneva Accords — ending French Indochina and dividing Vietnam at the 17th parallel into communist North Vietnam (the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, DRV, under Ho Chi Minh) and non-communist South Vietnam (the Republic of Vietnam, RV, under Emperor Bao Dai and subsequently President Ngo Dinh Diem). The Geneva Accords called for nationwide elections in 1956 to reunify Vietnam. The elections never occurred. The US decision was that Ho Chi Minh's anticipated electoral victory would produce a communist Vietnam and a "domino" collapse across Southeast Asia. Senator John F. Kennedy in 1956 characterised the stakes — that Burma, Thailand, India, Japan, the Philippines, Laos, and Cambodia would all be threatened "if the Red Tide of Communism overflowed into Vietnam."

The 1956-1960 period ran through the Ngo Dinh Diem regime in Saigon — a Catholic-minority government installed by the US in the Buddhist-majority south following the October 1955 rigged referendum that Diem won with a 98.2% vote share (including a 133% vote share in Saigon). The Diem regime's repression of the Buddhist majority and of the southern Viet Minh networks produced the December 1960 founding of the National Liberation Front (Viet Cong) and the insurgency that followed.

The 1961 Kennedy-administration escalation expanded US military aid and the commitment of US military advisors to over 16,000 by 1963. The 2 November 1963 Ngo Dinh Diem assassination — carried out by ARVN officers under Kennedy-administration green-lighting — produced years of political instability in Saigon. The 7 August 1964 Gulf of Tonkin Resolution — passed by the US Congress on the basis of subsequently-discredited reports of North Vietnamese attacks on US destroyers — produced LBJ-administration authorisation for major escalation. The 8 March 1965 deployment of US Marines to Da Nang marked the beginning of US combat operations. By 1969 the peak deployment reached 500,000+ US military personnel in Vietnam.

The war crimes ran across the 1965-1973 period. The Tet Offensive of January-February 1968 produced the Hue massacre by NLF/PAVN forces (2,800-6,000 South Vietnamese civilians and officials killed) and US military destruction of multiple South Vietnamese cities. On 16 March 1968 the My Lai massacre — by the US Army's Charlie Company under Lt. William Calley — killed 504 unarmed South Vietnamese civilians including women, children, and infants. US strategic-bombing campaigns — Operation Rolling Thunder (1965-1968) and Operation Linebacker (1972) — dropped more tonnage on Vietnam than the US had dropped in all theatres of World War II combined. The defoliation campaign — Operation Ranch Hand 1962-1971 — sprayed roughly 20 million gallons of Agent Orange and other herbicides across South Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia, producing decades of birth defects, cancers, and ecological destruction.

The January 1973 Paris Peace Accords ended US combat operations. The 30 April 1975 Fall of Saigon — PAVN tanks crashing through the gates of the Presidential Palace as the last US helicopter evacuations ran from the US Embassy rooftop — produced the reunification of Vietnam under Hanoi. The outcome: the US lost. The US strategic defeat — the "Vietnam syndrome" of subsequent US public opposition to ground wars — ran as the central context of US foreign policy across the Carter, Reagan, and post-Cold-War periods until 11 September 2001 (the inflection point covered in Chapter 2).

Vietnamese deaths: Vietnam's 1995 official figures: 2 million civilians on both sides; 1.1 million North Vietnamese and Viet Cong fighters; 200,000-250,000 South Vietnamese soldiers. Estimates of total Vietnamese deaths range from 970,000 to 3 million. US deaths: 58,220 names on the Vietnam Veterans Memorial. Vietnamese displacement of millions produced the "boat people" refugee crises across 1975-1990.

### Laos 1964-1973 — the secret war

Laos was a sovereign state recognised by the United Nations. In 1962 the International Agreement on the Neutrality of Laos — signed by 14 nations including the US, Soviet Union, China, North Vietnam, France, and the UK — forbade signatories from directly invading Laos or providing military aid. The US decision was to ignore the treaty.

From 1964 to 1973 the US conducted Operation Barrel Roll (in northern Laos) and Operation Steel Tiger (along the Ho Chi Minh Trail in southern Laos). 580,000 bombing sorties dropped roughly 2.1 million tons of ordnance on Laos. The tonnage per capita made Laos the most heavily bombed country in human history. The post-1973 unexploded-ordnance (UXO) record has killed and maimed Laotian civilians since the post-war period — the legacy continues to the date of writing.

The Hmong "Special Guerrilla Units" ran under the CIA from 1961 onward — "Operation Momentum" approved by Eisenhower as one of his final acts in office. The Hmong proxy force under General Vang Pao fought the Pathet Lao communist insurgency. The post-1975 Pathet Lao victory produced the Hmong refugee crisis; US asylum operations relocated roughly 200,000 Hmong to the United States.

Laotian civilian casualties: roughly 200,000 dead by 1975 per History.com (Legacies of War estimate); 750,000 refugees (25% of the Laotian population). Per Britannica's figures: 20,000-62,000 Laotians dead. The 728 American deaths in Laos were mostly CIA officers and contractors.

### Cambodia 1969-1975 — the bombing that produced the genocide

Prince Norodom Sihanouk had maintained Cambodian neutrality from 1955 onward. The 1966 Sihanouk-Zhou Enlai agreement permitted PAVN/VC forces to establish base areas in Cambodia and to use the port of Sihanoukville for military deliveries.

The US bombing of Cambodia began under President Lyndon Johnson in 1965 — three and a half years earlier than was publicly known until the 2000 declassification of US Air Force records by President Bill Clinton. From 1965 to 1968, 214 tons of bombs were dropped on Cambodia under the Johnson administration. The Nixon administration's escalation began on 18 March 1969 with Operation Menu — the secret B-52 carpet-bombing campaign over the protests of Cambodian neutrality. Only five congressional officials were informed. The campaign phases were given the names Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner, Snack, Dessert. The Nixon "madman theory" produced the campaign on the logic that the bombing would demonstrate to the North Vietnamese that Nixon was "capable of anything."

The 1969-1973 Cambodia bombing totalled roughly 2.7 million tons of ordnance — more than the US had dropped on Japan during all of World War II (roughly 160,000 tons). Cambodian casualties: 150,000-500,000 civilians killed in the US bombing campaigns. Cambodia became the second-most-bombed country in human history after Laos.

On 18 March 1970 — the day Sihanouk was visiting Moscow — pro-American Prime Minister Lon Nol deposed Sihanouk in a coup. The civil war between the Lon Nol government and the Khmer Rouge under Pol Pot ran across 1970-1975. Taylor Owen and Ben Kiernan have characterised the causation: civilian casualties from the bombing drove an enraged Cambodian population into the arms of an insurgency that had enjoyed little support until the bombing began, setting in motion the expansion of the Vietnam War into Cambodia, the 1970 coup, the rapid rise of the Khmer Rouge, and the Cambodian genocide that followed.

On 17 April 1975 — two weeks before the Fall of Saigon — the Khmer Rouge under Pol Pot took Phnom Penh and overthrew the Lon Nol regime. The April 1975 - January 1979 Khmer Rouge genocide killed roughly 1.5 to 3 million Cambodians — roughly 25% of the Cambodian population. The Khmer Rouge "Year Zero" programme ran through the evacuation of Phnom Penh and other cities to the countryside; the destruction of intellectuals, professionals, ethnic minorities (especially Cham Muslims and ethnic Vietnamese), Buddhist monks, and political opponents; the S-21 (Tuol Sleng) torture-and-execution facility and the Killing Fields. The 7 January 1979 Vietnamese invasion produced the fall of the Khmer Rouge regime and the Vietnamese-installed People's Republic of Kampuchea under Heng Samrin and Hun Sen.

The Cambodian death toll across 1969-1979 (US bombing and Khmer Rouge genocide combined): roughly 1.7-3.5 million dead out of a 1970 population of roughly 7.1 million.

The Indochina war total: somewhere between 4 and 6 million dead across Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia 1955-1979.

## Indonesia 1965-1966

The Indonesian mass killings of 1965-1966 are the post-1945 case of a US-supported anti-communist mass murder of between 500,000 and 1 million people. The CIA's own 1968 internal assessment characterised the events as "one of the worst mass murders of the 20th century, along with the Soviet purges of the 1930s, the Nazi mass murders during the Second World War, and the Maoist bloodbath of the early 1950s." The events produced the Suharto "New Order" dictatorship that ruled Indonesia from 1967 until 1998 and the foundational architecture of the post-1966 Indonesian military political class.

The background: from 1962 onward the CIA had been conducting covert military and propaganda operations against the Indonesian government under President Sukarno — the founding nationalist president of post-1945 Indonesia who had embraced an alliance with the Indonesian Communist Party (PKI), then the largest communist party outside the USSR and the People's Republic of China with roughly 3 million members. The US concern: Sukarno's alignment with the PKI and his hosting of the 1955 Bandung Conference that produced the Non-Aligned Movement.

On 30 September 1965 the "30 September Movement" — a group of military officers acting with the collaboration of a handful of PKI leaders — kidnapped and killed six anti-communist Indonesian Army generals. The response by Major General Suharto came within hours: Suharto took command of the army and launched a counterattack against the rebel officers. Within days the 30 September Movement had collapsed. The army characterised the operation as a PKI conspiracy and launched the mass-killing campaign that ran across October 1965 - March 1966.

The killing began in Jakarta and spread to Central Java, East Java, Bali, and northern Sumatra. The Indonesian Army and its paramilitary allies — including Islamist militias under Nahdlatul Ulama, Muhammadiyah, and other organisations — killed at least 500,000 to 1 million people. Some scholars place the death toll at 2-3 million. The victims included PKI members and alleged sympathisers, Gerwani women, trade unionists, ethnic Javanese Abangan, ethnic Chinese, atheists, teachers, intellectuals, and artists.

The US role ran through multiple channels. The US Embassy in Jakarta provided lists of communist leaders to Indonesian military and paramilitary operators. Robert Martens (US political officer) compiled roughly 5,000 names provided to the Indonesian Army under the endorsement of US Ambassador Marshall Green, Deputy Chief of Mission Jack Lydman, and Political Section Chief Edward Masters. The 2017 National Security Archive release of US Embassy Jakarta documents from 1965-1966 confirmed the US Embassy's real-time tracking of the killings.

Green's October 1965 cable: "We can help shape developments to our advantage... Spread the story of PKI's guilt, treachery, and brutality." Green's February 1966 cable: "the Communists... Have been decimated by wholesale massacre." Green's August 1966 airgram (drafted by Martens, approved by Masters): the lists of communists were "apparently being used by Indonesian security authorities who seem to lack even the simplest overt information on PKI leadership."

US State Department intelligence officer Howard Federspiel later summarised the attitude in Washington: "no one cared, as long as they were Communists, that they were being butchered." Robert F. Kennedy's January 1966 public statement was a dissenting voice within the US political establishment: "We have spoken out against the inhuman slaughters perpetrated by the Nazis and the Communists. But will we speak out also against the inhuman slaughter in Indonesia, where over 100,000 alleged Communists have not been perpetrators, but victims?"

The British Foreign Office under Foreign Secretary Michael Stewart — operating through Ambassador to Indonesia Andrew Gilchrist — provided parallel intelligence and propaganda support. Gilchrist's October 1965 cable: "I have never concealed... My belief that a little shooting in Indonesia would be an essential preliminary to effective change."

The post-1966 outcome: Suharto consolidated power and replaced Sukarno (formally inaugurated as Acting President 12 March 1967 and as President 27 March 1968). The Suharto "New Order" 1967-1998 produced Indonesian alignment with US-and-Western capital and the multi-billion-dollar US, UK, Japanese, and Australian corporate exploitation of Indonesian resources (Freeport-McMoRan at Grasberg Mine; Caltex/Chevron oil; extractive concessions across the period). The academic literature — Geoffrey B. Robinson's *The Killing Season* (2018); John Roosa's *Pretext for Mass Murder* (2006) and *Buried Histories* (2020) — has summarised the record: the mass killings would not have happened absent US support.

The post-1998 Indonesian state has continued to characterise the 1965-1966 killings as a justified response to a communist coup attempt. No perpetrator has been prosecuted. The post-2000 Indonesian National Commission for Human Rights investigation confirmed military responsibility but has produced no prosecutions.

## Operation Condor 1975-1983

Operation Condor was the architecture of cross-border state terror across the Southern Cone of South America between 1975 and 1983. Member states: Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, Uruguay (founding); Ecuador and Peru (joined 1977-78). The founding meeting was held 25 November - 1 December 1975 at the First Working Meeting on National Intelligence hosted by DINA chief Manuel Contreras in Santiago. The name "CONDOR" was approved by unanimous Uruguayan motion to honour Chile (the condor being Chile's national bird).

The Cold War context: US-aligned military coups across the Southern Cone of South America had produced right-wing dictatorships in Brazil (1964 coup against João Goulart under Generals Castelo Branco / Costa e Silva / Médici / Geisel / Figueiredo, 1964-1985); Bolivia (1971 Hugo Banzer coup); Uruguay (1973 coup, military rule 1973-1985); Chile (11 September 1973 General Augusto Pinochet coup against elected President Salvador Allende); Peru (1975 General Francisco Morales Bermúdez coup); and Argentina (24 March 1976 coup by military junta under General Jorge Rafael Videla, 1976-1983 military rule).

The 11 September 1973 Chilean coup is the central case. Salvador Allende — the Chilean physician and Socialist Party politician who had won the 4 September 1970 presidential election with a 36.6% plurality and had been confirmed by the Chilean Congress on 24 October 1970 — had begun a programme of nationalisation of the Chilean copper industry, agrarian reform, and social programmes. The US response ran through covert CIA operations to destabilise Allende: CIA funding of opposition newspapers (El Mercurio), trade unions, and political parties; CIA support for the Chilean military officer corps; and the 1970 CIA "Track II" assassination plot against Chilean Army Commander-in-Chief General René Schneider (assassinated 25 October 1970) producing the opening for the coup conspiracy that followed.

Henry Kissinger — US National Security Advisor 1969-1975 and Secretary of State 1973-1977 — characterised the stakes in 1970: "I don't see why we need to stand by and watch a country go communist due to the irresponsibility of its own people." Richard Nixon's 15 September 1970 instructions to CIA Director Richard Helms: "make the [Chilean] economy scream."

On 11 September 1973 the Chilean armed forces under General Augusto Pinochet bombed the La Moneda presidential palace in Santiago. Salvador Allende died in the palace — the forensic record characterises the death as suicide as the coup leaders closed in on him.
