The Pinochet dictatorship 1973-1990 produced 3,065 killings and disappearances and roughly 30,000 cases of torture per the Valech Commission (2004-2005) — figures characterised by some scholars as undercounts.

Operation Condor's cross-border state terror ran under DINA chief Manuel Contreras. The operations: cross-border kidnapping, disappearance, torture, and extrajudicial execution of political refugees, leftist activists, trade unionists, peasant leaders, priests and nuns, intellectuals, students, teachers, and guerrillas across the Southern Cone. The "death flights" — victims drugged and dropped from aircraft into the Atlantic Ocean — were the Argentine centrepiece of the Dirty War (1976-1983) under the Videla, Viola, and Galtieri juntas. The record: roughly 30,000 Argentines disappeared.

The Argentine "stolen babies" architecture — children of disappeared mothers given to military families — produced the Abuelas de Plaza de Mayo (Grandmothers of the Plaza de Mayo) campaign that has by 2024 identified 138 of an estimated 500 stolen children.

Operation Condor assassinations ran globally. The 30 September 1974 assassination of Chilean General Carlos Prats and his wife by car bomb in Buenos Aires under DINA orchestration. The 1975 attempted assassination of Chilean Christian Democrat leader Bernardo Leighton in Rome (organised by DINA's Michael Townley with Italian neo-fascists). The 1976 Buenos Aires assassinations of former Bolivian President Juan José Torres and Uruguayan legislators Zelmar Michelini and Héctor Gutiérrez Ruiz. The 21 September 1976 Washington DC car-bomb assassination of former Chilean Foreign Minister Orlando Letelier and his American colleague Ronni Moffitt — the Operation Condor attack on US soil organised by Townley under direct Pinochet order.

The US role ran continuously across the Condor period. Contreras was a paid CIA contact 1974-1977 — including across the period of the Letelier-Moffitt assassination in Washington. Henry Kissinger was briefed on Condor "murder operations" on 5 August 1976 by Deputy Assistant Secretary Harry Shlaudeman; Kissinger considered but did not deliver a warning to Condor regimes to stop the planned assassinations. Kissinger's October 1976 instructions to Argentine Foreign Minister César Guzzetti: "If there are things that have to be done, you should do them quickly."

The outcome: John Dinges in *The Condor Years* (2004) documented 654 victims of Operation Condor kidnapping, torture, and disappearance in the Southern Cone between 1976 and 1980. The Pinochet record: 2,279 murders and 30,000+ tortured for political reasons. The Argentine "Dirty War" record: roughly 30,000 disappeared. The Brazilian, Uruguayan, Paraguayan, and Bolivian records add further thousands.

Post-2000 prosecutions: Pinochet stripped of immunity May 2000 and faced 100+ charges; he died 10 December 2006 still under indictment. Videla convicted of crimes against humanity; he died 17 May 2013 in prison. Contreras convicted of multiple Operation Condor murders; he died 7 August 2015 serving a 526-year sentence. In May 2016 an Argentine court issued the first Latin American ruling characterising Operation Condor as a transnational criminal conspiracy; 14 Argentine military officers and one Uruguayan officer were convicted.

## Central America 1979-1992

The Central American crisis of 1979-1992 produced roughly 360,000-400,000 deaths across El Salvador (75,000-90,000), Guatemala (200,000+), and Nicaragua (30,000-43,000), with Honduran death-squad operations across the period. The US architecture was the Reagan-administration multi-billion-dollar military-and-economic aid programme to right-wing governments and proxy forces under the framework of Cold War anti-communism.

### El Salvador 1979-1992

The Salvadoran civil war began with the 15 October 1979 ousting of military dictator General Carlos Humberto Romero by a reformist junta. The right-wing reaction produced the FMLN (Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front) guerrilla insurgency against the Salvadoran state. Roughly 75,000-90,000 Salvadorans died in the 12-year war. The UN Truth Commission for El Salvador (*From Madness to Hope*, 1993) found 85% of killings were by government forces and right-wing paramilitary death squads; 5% by FMLN guerrillas.

The atrocities ran through institutional channels. On 24 March 1980 — during mass at the chapel of the Hospital de la Divina Providencia in San Salvador — Archbishop Óscar Romero was assassinated by a death squad operating under the orders of Major Roberto D'Aubuisson, the founder of the right-wing ARENA party and the leader of multiple Salvadoran death squads. On 11 December 1981 the El Mozote massacre — by the US-trained Atlácatl Battalion under Lt. Col. Domingo Monterrosa — killed roughly 900-1,200 villagers in the Morazán department of El Salvador; the majority of the dead were children. On 16 November 1989 the Jesuit massacre at the Universidad Centroamericana in San Salvador — by Atlácatl Battalion units — killed six Jesuit priests including theologian Ignacio Ellacuría, the priests' housekeeper Elba Ramos, and her daughter Celina Ramos.

US support ran through roughly $5 billion in military and economic aid 1980-1992. The School of the Americas at Fort Benning, Georgia (renamed Western Hemisphere Institute for Security Cooperation in 2000) trained the Atlácatl Battalion officers and the Salvadoran military elite. Israel was also a major arms supplier 1970-1976. 500,000+ Salvadoran refugees and 500,000 internally displaced (out of a population of roughly 5 million) produced the Salvadoran migration to the United States that produced the post-1990s MS-13 gang formation in Los Angeles and the US-Central-American migration politics.

The Chapultepec Peace Accords were signed 16 January 1992 in Mexico City ending the war.

### Nicaragua 1979-1990

On 19 July 1979 the FSLN (Sandinista National Liberation Front) overthrew the US-backed dictator Anastasio Somoza Debayle — ending more than four decades of Somoza family dictatorship in Nicaragua. President Jimmy Carter initially accepted the new Sandinista government. The 20 January 1981 inauguration of Ronald Reagan produced the reversal: Reagan was intent on overthrowing the FSLN.

The 1981 Reagan-administration covert action programme funded the Nicaraguan Contras — the proxy force assembled from ex-Somoza National Guard officers, anti-Sandinista Nicaraguans, and CIA-trained operatives running from bases in Honduras and Costa Rica. The Reagan-administration multi-billion-dollar Contra operation produced atrocities against Nicaraguan civilians: rape, torture, murder, the destruction of schools, clinics, and infrastructure.

The 1984 Boland Amendment prohibited US funding to the Contras. The Reagan-administration response was the Iran-Contra Affair: illegal arms sales to the Iranian government via Israel during the Iran-Iraq War (covered in Chapter 3) producing proceeds that were illegally funnelled to the Contras after Congress prohibited US funding. The congressional investigation (1986-1987) produced convictions of Oliver North, John Poindexter, Caspar Weinberger, and other Reagan-administration figures — pardoned by George H.W. Bush in 1992.

The 27 June 1986 International Court of Justice ruling in *Nicaragua v. United States of America* found the US in violation of international law: the US had used illegal force, breached customary international law obligations, and violated the 1956 US-Nicaragua Treaty of Friendship, Commerce, and Navigation. The ICJ ordered the US to pay reparations of roughly $370.2 million. The US ignored the ruling and used its UN Security Council veto to block enforcement. The outcome: the central post-1945 international tribunal had found the central post-1945 superpower guilty of state-sponsored terrorism, and the central post-1945 superpower had refused to comply.

Nicaraguan deaths: 30,000-43,000 killed in the war 1979-1990 per the PRIO Battle Deaths Dataset. On 25 February 1990 the FSLN lost the presidential election to Violeta Chamorro. The post-1990 Nicaraguan political architecture has run under multiple Sandinista returns and the Daniel Ortega government covered in the regional dossier inventory.

### Guatemala 1960-1996

The Guatemalan civil war 1960-1996 was the longest civil war in Central American history. The origin: the 1954 CIA-backed Operation PBSUCCESS coup against the elected reformist President Jacobo Árbenz — the case study of US intervention against Latin American land reform that threatened US corporate interests (specifically the United Fruit Company's multi-million-acre Guatemalan landholdings). The military dictatorships that followed under Carlos Castillo Armas (1954-1957), Miguel Ydígoras Fuentes (1958-1963), Enrique Peralta (1963-1966), and others produced the 1960 civil war origin.

The intensification ran under General Romeo Lucas García (1978-1982) and especially under General Efraín Ríos Montt (March 1982 - August 1983). The scorched-earth campaign in the Guatemalan highlands — Ríos Montt's "fusiles y frijoles" ("rifles and beans") strategy — destroyed 626 Maya villages and produced 200,000+ deaths over the conflict per the UN Commission for Historical Clarification (*Memory of Silence*, 1999). The Commission's finding: 93% of killings by government forces; the campaign was characterised as genocide against the Maya population. The December 1982 Dos Erres massacre — by the Kaibiles unit — killed 200+ villagers in the department of Petén; the record included women raped and children murdered.

US support ran through CIA covert operations: $2 million in 1981; $2.5 million in 1982. The Argentine "Operation Charly" — Argentine military advisors operating in Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and Nicaragua as US-CIA proxies in the early 1980s — was the architecture connecting the Operation Condor regimes to Central America.

Ríos Montt was convicted of genocide on 10 May 2013 by a Guatemalan court — the first conviction of a national head of state for genocide by his own country's judicial system — and sentenced to 80 years' imprisonment. The conviction was overturned on procedural grounds on 20 May 2013 by Guatemala's Constitutional Court. Ríos Montt died on 1 April 2018 before retrial.

The Guatemalan Peace Accords were signed 29 December 1996 ending the war.

### Honduras and Battalion 316

Honduras served as the US-CIA base of operations for Central American covert action 1981-1989. US Ambassador John Negroponte (1981-1985) oversaw the second-largest US embassy in Latin America and the largest CIA station in the world. Battalion 316 — the CIA-backed Honduran death squad under General Gustavo Álvarez Martínez — disappeared, tortured, and killed students, academics, labour unionists, clergy, journalists, and indigenous-rights activists. US State Department human rights reports on Honduras under Negroponte were falsified to obscure the death-squad record.

Negroponte's subsequent US national security career — Permanent US Representative to the UN 2001-2004, US Ambassador to Iraq 2004-2005, Director of National Intelligence 2005-2007, US Deputy Secretary of State 2007-2009 — is the case study of post-Cold-War US institutional continuity.

## African proxy wars

The post-1945 African proxy wars ran across the 1960s-1990s period. Three cases are central.

### Angola 1975-2002

The 11 November 1975 Portuguese withdrawal from Angola produced the civil war between three liberation movements that had fought Portuguese colonial rule. The MPLA (Movimento Popular de Libertação de Angola — Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola) under Agostinho Neto operated with Soviet and Cuban support. The FNLA (Frente Nacional de Libertação de Angola — National Liberation Front of Angola) under Holden Roberto operated with US and Zairean support. UNITA (União Nacional para a Independência Total de Angola — National Union for the Total Independence of Angola) under Jonas Savimbi operated with US and apartheid-South-African support.

The Cuban intervention — roughly 36,000 Cuban troops in Angola by 1976 under Operation Carlota — produced the MPLA government's control of Luanda and the Cuban-Angolan military operations against UNITA and the South African Defence Force (SADF). The 1987-1988 Battle of Cuito Cuanavale — the largest African battle since World War II — involved Cuban, Angolan, UNITA, and SADF forces and produced the defeat of the apartheid South African military intervention in Angola. The December 1988 New York Accords — negotiated under US Secretary of State Chet Crocker — linked Cuban withdrawal from Angola to Namibian independence (achieved 21 March 1990).

US support to UNITA continued under the Reagan administration after the 1985 repeal of the Clark Amendment (which had prohibited US covert action in Angola). The Reagan-administration UNITA support produced the post-Namibian-independence Angolan civil war that continued until the 22 February 2002 killing of Jonas Savimbi by Angolan government forces and the 4 April 2002 Luena Memorandum ending the war.

Angolan deaths across 1975-2002: 500,000-800,000 deaths. The 4,300 Cuban deaths in African conflicts (half in Angola) were the Cuban cost of the Castro-era "world revolution" doctrine.

### Mozambique 1977-1992

The Mozambican civil war ran from 1977 onward between FRELIMO (Frente de Libertação de Moçambique) under President Samora Machel (until his 19 October 1986 plane-crash death) and his successor Joaquim Chissano, supported by the Soviet Union and Cuba, and RENAMO (Resistência Nacional Moçambicana) under Afonso Dhlakama, supported by Rhodesia (until 1980) and apartheid South Africa. The RENAMO campaign ran through massacres of civilians, mass rape, child-soldier recruitment, and systematic destruction of schools, health clinics, and infrastructure across rural Mozambique.

Mozambican deaths: roughly 1 million dead. The 4 October 1992 Rome General Peace Accords ended the war.

### Ethiopia, Eritrea, Somalia 1974-1991

The 1974 Derg coup deposed Emperor Haile Selassie (subsequently murdered 27 August 1975 in his palace by Derg orders). The Derg military junta under Mengistu Haile Mariam aligned with the Soviet Union. The Ethiopian Civil War 1974-1991 ran between the Derg and multiple insurgent movements: the EPLF (Eritrean People's Liberation Front), the TPLF (Tigray People's Liberation Front, the precursor of the ruling EPRDF coalition), and the OLF (Oromo Liberation Front).

The 1977-1978 Ogaden War ran between Somalia under Siad Barre (originally Soviet-aligned) and Ethiopia (newly Soviet-aligned following the 1974 Derg coup). The Soviet reversal — abandoning Somalia for Ethiopia — produced the Cuban intervention with roughly 11,000 Cuban troops plus Soviet advisors fighting for Ethiopia. The Somali defeat — the Soviets reversed alliance and the Cubans rolled back the Somali invasion — produced the Somali deterioration toward the 1991 collapse and the post-1991 Somali civil war (covered in Chapter 6).

The 1983-1985 Ethiopian famine — produced by the Derg agricultural-collectivisation and forced-resettlement policies under conditions of civil war — killed roughly 1 million Ethiopians. The 1985 Live Aid concert and the Western humanitarian intervention ran against the continuing Cold War context.

The 1991 Mengistu collapse produced the EPRDF takeover of Addis Ababa under Meles Zenawi and the EPLF takeover of Asmara under Isaias Afwerki. The 1993 Eritrean independence referendum produced independence. The 1998-2000 Eritrean-Ethiopian War produced 70,000-100,000 deaths in a two-year border war over the Badme region. The 2018 peace agreement under Ethiopian PM Abiy Ahmed produced the reversal in the 2020-2022 Tigray War covered in Chapter 6.

Mengistu was convicted of genocide in absentia in Ethiopia in 2008; he remains in Zimbabwean exile under Mugabe-Mnangagwa-era protection.

## Afghanistan 1979-1989 — Operation Cyclone

The Soviet-Afghan War 1979-1989 is covered in detail in Dossier 24 (the Asim Munir / Pakistani political-class dossier). The short summary: on 27 December 1979 Soviet forces invaded Afghanistan to support the Marxist People's Democratic Party of Afghanistan (PDPA) government under Babrak Karmal. The US response — Operation Cyclone, the largest CIA covert operation in history — ran through multi-billion-dollar weapons supplies to the Afghan mujahideen via the Pakistani Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) under DG Akhtar Abdur Rahman (1979-1987) and Hamid Gul (1987-1989).

Reagan-era Operation Cyclone funding reached roughly $3 billion (with Saudi matching funds bringing total to $6 billion+). The 1986 introduction of US Stinger anti-aircraft missiles produced the Soviet helicopter losses and the Soviet decision to withdraw. The 15 February 1989 Soviet withdrawal produced the Afghan civil war that ended with the Taliban takeover of Kabul in 1996.

Afghan deaths across 1979-1989: 1 million+ Afghan civilians; 15,000 Soviet troops; 5-6 million Afghan refugees. The mujahideen network produced the post-1989 Taliban and the 1988 founding of al-Qaeda by Osama bin Laden — the architecture of the post-11-September-2001 global "war on terror" covered in Chapter 2.

## Greek Civil War 1946-1949

The first major Cold War proxy conflict in Europe ran between the Greek government (initially British-backed, subsequently US-backed under the 1947 Truman Doctrine) and the Democratic Army of Greece (the military wing of the Communist Party of Greece, KKE, supported by Yugoslavia and Albania until the 1948 Tito-Stalin split). Roughly 158,000 Greeks died; 40,000-50,000 imprisoned or exiled. The outcome: the Greek monarchical government under subsequent military rule (the 1967-1974 colonels' junta) was the case study of Western European Cold War authoritarianism.

The March 1947 Truman Doctrine — the $400 million US aid programme to Greece and Turkey — was the architecture of the post-1945 US containment doctrine that produced the US foreign policy architecture across all the wars covered in this chapter.

## Malayan Emergency 1948-1960

The British colonial counterinsurgency against the Malayan National Liberation Army (the military wing of the Malayan Communist Party) ran 1948-1960. Roughly 13,000 killed. The British "Briggs Plan" — the strategic-hamlet resettlement programme that relocated 500,000+ ethnic Chinese Malaysians into "New Villages" under military control — produced the template subsequently adopted by the US in Vietnam under the Strategic Hamlet Program. The outcome: 31 August 1957 Malayan independence and 31 July 1960 emergency end with Malaysian government victory.

## Other Cold War proxy wars

The post-1945 Cold War period produced multiple additional proxy wars that this chapter cannot cover in detail. The chronological inventory:

- The 1948-1949 First Arab-Israeli War (covered in Chapter 5 / the Gaza chapter context).
- The 1956 Suez Crisis (UK-France-Israel vs Egypt over Suez Canal nationalisation).
- The 1962 Sino-Indian War.
- The 1965 Indo-Pakistani War (covered in Ch5 historical chapter).
- The 1965 Dominican Republic US intervention.
- The 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War (covered in Ch5 historical chapter and Dossier 27).
- The 1971 Indo-Pakistani War.
- The 1973 Yom Kippur War / October War (Israel vs Egypt-Syria; superpower brinkmanship including US nuclear DEFCON 3 alert).
- The 1974 Cyprus crisis (Turkish invasion).
- The 1980s Lebanon civil war (1975-1990, intersecting with Israeli interventions, US-and-French peacekeeping, Hezbollah formation; covered in Chapter 6 and the Middle East dossier inventory).
- The 1982 Falklands/Malvinas War (UK vs Argentina; Reagan-administration support for UK against the Argentine Condor-junta despite the US-Argentine cooperation across Operation Charly).
- The 1983 US invasion of Grenada.
- The 1989 US invasion of Panama.
- The 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq War — the million-dead war of the period, covered in Chapter 3.

## The verdict

The single largest pattern of the 1945-1989 Cold War period was the production of roughly 30 million proxy-war deaths by the US-Soviet superpower rivalry — almost entirely in the developing world. The post-1945 US national-security state under the 1947 National Security Act — operating through the Central Intelligence Agency, the National Security Council, the Defense Department, the State Department, the military-industrial complex (covered in Chapter 8), and the alliances with right-wing governments, military juntas, dictators, and death squads across Asia, Latin America, Africa, and the Middle East — was the central architecture of the post-1945 proxy-war record covered in this chapter. The Soviet architecture under the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, the KGB, and the Soviet military ran as the counterpart producing the bipolar contest that operationalised the proxy-war architecture.

The mask was the "free world vs international communism" characterisation. Behind the mask was the post-1945 production of:

- 2.5-5 million Korean deaths;
- 3-6 million Indochinese deaths (Vietnam / Laos / Cambodia);
- 1.5-3 million Cambodian deaths in the post-1975 Khmer Rouge genocide (produced by US bombing);
- 500,000 - 1 million Indonesian deaths in the 1965-1966 mass killings;
- 30,000 Argentines disappeared in the Dirty War under Operation Condor;
- 3,065 Chilean killings and disappearances under Pinochet;
- 75,000-90,000 Salvadorans killed (85% by US-supported forces);
- 200,000+ Guatemalans killed (93% by US-supported forces);
- 30,000-43,000 Nicaraguans killed in the Contra war;
- 500,000-800,000 Angolan deaths;
- 1 million Mozambicans dead;
- 1+ million Afghan civilians dead;
- 158,000 Greeks dead in the Greek Civil War;
- 13,000 dead in the Malayan Emergency;
- and millions more deaths across the Cold War proxy-war architecture not covered in detail in this chapter.

The post-1989 architecture of the post-Cold-War "unipolar moment" ran from this record. The post-1989 US wars covered in Chapter 2 — the Gulf War 1991, the Yugoslav wars, the Afghanistan and Iraq wars, the Libya / Syria / Yemen / Somalia interventions, and the drone-war architecture — run from the apparatus, doctrine, personnel, and political-class architecture covered in this chapter.

The mask of post-1989 was the "end of history" and the "rules-based international order" — the characterisation that the post-Cold-War US-led architecture had produced the democratic-and-peaceful global order. The operation behind that mask is the record of Chapter 2 and forward.

## Bridge to Chapter 2

The post-1945 Cold War apparatus did not dissolve at the 26 December 1991 dissolution of the Soviet Union. It expanded. The post-1989 US architecture has produced more war deaths in fewer years than the Cold War period. Chapter 2 covers the post-Cold-War US wars: the Gulf War 1991, the Yugoslav wars 1991-2001, the Afghanistan war 2001-2021, the Iraq war 2003-2011, the 2014-onward war against ISIS, the Libya 2011 war, the Syria intervention 2011-onward, the Yemen war 2015-onward (Saudi-led, US-supported), the Somalia interventions, and the drone-war architecture across Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia.

---


---

# Part XII Chapter 2: The Post-Cold-War US Wars

## The unipolar moment

The Soviet Union dissolved on 26 December 1991. The architecture of the post-1945 US national-security state did not dissolve. It expanded. Across 1990-2024 the United States and its NATO allies have produced more war deaths in fewer years than the Cold War period produced. Brown University's Costs of War project estimates 940,000+ direct deaths and 3.6-3.8 million indirect deaths across the five principal post-9/11 theatres alone — Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Yemen, Pakistan — for a total of 4.5-4.7 million dead between 2001 and 2023. The financial cost: roughly $8 trillion, not including future interest on borrowing. The displacement: 38 million people.

That is one accounting. It excludes Yugoslavia, Kosovo, Libya, Somalia, and the multi-country drone-war architecture. It excludes the 1991-2003 Iraq sanctions deaths estimated by UNICEF at 400,000-500,000 children under five. It excludes Gulf War 1991. The total post-Cold-War US-war death toll across all theatres, direct and indirect, is in the range of 5-6 million people across 35 years.

The post-Cold-War period was characterized as "the end of history" (Francis Fukuyama, 1992) under a "rules-based international order" — the characterisation by US, UK, French, and German political-class operators that the post-1989 world had produced a democratic-and-peaceful order under US leadership. The underlying machinery is the record of this chapter.

The record covers the principal post-Cold-War US wars in chronological dependency order: the 1991 Gulf War and the 1990s Iraq sanctions period; the 1991-2001 Yugoslav wars and NATO interventions; the 2001-2021 Afghanistan war; the 2003-2011 Iraq War and the subsequent ISIS war; the 2011 Libya intervention; the 2011-2024 Syrian civil war and US intervention; the 2015-onward Yemen war (Saudi-led, US-supported); and the multi-country drone-war architecture across Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, and Libya. The Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), the Ukraine war (2014-onward), the Gaza chapter (2023-onward), and the multi-front contemporary wars (Sudan, Sahel, Tigray, Myanmar, DRC, Nagorno-Karabakh) are covered in Chapters 3-6.

## Gulf War 1991 — the template

On 2 August 1990 Iraqi forces under Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait. Roughly 100,000 troops overran the small oil-rich emirate within hours; the Kuwaiti royal family fled to Saudi Arabia. The background: Iraq had emerged from the 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq War (covered in Chapter 3) with $80 billion in debt — including $14 billion owed to Kuwait — and roughly 1 million dead. Iraq accused Kuwait of slant-drilling into the Iraqi Rumaila oil field and of overproducing OPEC quotas to drive down oil prices.

The 25 July 1990 meeting between US Ambassador to Iraq April Glaspie and Saddam Hussein produced US ambiguity: Glaspie told Saddam the US had "no opinion on Arab-Arab conflicts, like your border disagreement with Kuwait." The transcript was characterised by Saddam's advisors as a green light for invasion. The US State Department subsequently characterised the Glaspie meeting as the US conveying that it opposed the use of force; the record remains contested.

The US response to the 2 August invasion ran through President George H.W. Bush and his national-security team — National Security Advisor Brent Scowcroft, Secretary of State James Baker, Secretary of Defense Dick Cheney, and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs Colin Powell. On 6 August 1990 UN Security Council Resolution 661 imposed comprehensive sanctions on Iraq. On 7 August Bush ordered Operation Desert Shield — the deployment of US forces to Saudi Arabia. On 8 August Iraq announced formal annexation of Kuwait; UNSC Resolution 662 next day declared the annexation null and void. On 29 November UNSC Resolution 678 authorised the use of force after 15 January 1991. The vote: 12 in favour, 2 against (Cuba and Yemen), China abstaining.

The 35-nation coalition under US command assembled roughly 750,000 troops — including 540,000 US personnel, 25,000+ British, 20,000 Egyptians, 5,500 French, and contributions from Canada, Saudi Arabia, Syria, Bangladesh, Morocco, and others. Germany and Japan covered $16 billion of war costs under the "checkbook diplomacy" arrangement.

On 17 January 1991 the air campaign of Operation Desert Storm began. Over the next 39 days the US-led coalition flew over 100,000 sorties. The 13 February 1991 Al-A'amiriya shelter strike — two USAF laser-guided missiles fired at a civilian air-raid shelter in the Al-A'amiriya neighbourhood of Baghdad — killed at least 408 civilians sheltering inside. US officials subsequently characterised the shelter as having served as a military command-and-control facility. The Iraqi forensic record characterised the victims as women, children, and elderly civilians.

On 24 February 1991 Operation Desert Sabre — the ground assault — began. The Iraqi military collapsed within 100 hours. On 27 February Kuwait was liberated. On 28 February Bush declared a unilateral ceasefire. The "Highway of Death" — the multi-mile US airstrikes on retreating Iraqi forces and Kuwaiti-civilian hostage convoys on the Kuwait-Iraq highway 26-27 February — produced contested casualty figures from hundreds to thousands.

Gulf War casualties: coalition deaths roughly 250-392 killed (including 47 British, predominantly American). Iraqi battle deaths: 20,000-35,000 (Imperial War Museum and Wikipedia); 8,000-10,000 (History.com). Some estimates ranged from 1,500 to 100,000. Iraqi civilian deaths: 2,278 confirmed killed and 5,965 wounded in coalition air strikes per the Wikipedia Gulf War air campaign record; some estimates from 3,000 to 200,000.

The outcome of the Gulf War: the post-1991 US "unipolar moment" was operationalised. The US had assembled a 35-nation coalition under UN authorisation, executed a military campaign with low US casualties, and produced victory over the multi-million-person Iraqi military. The Powell-Weinberger doctrine of "overwhelming force with clear objectives and exit strategy" appeared to have produced the template for post-Cold-War US warfare. The Bush administration "New World Order" rhetoric characterised the architecture.

The reality: the Gulf War produced the 1990s sanctions regime that killed an estimated 400,000-500,000 Iraqi children, the US/UK military presence in Saudi Arabia (which Osama bin Laden cited as primary motivation for the 1996 fatwa and 1998 fatwa declaring war against the US), the no-fly-zone bombings of the 1990s, the 1998 Operation Desert Fox, and the path to the 2003 Iraq War.

## The 1990s Iraq sanctions

UN Security Council Resolution 661 of 6 August 1990 imposed comprehensive sanctions on Iraq covering all goods entering or leaving the country. The sanctions continued through the 1991 Gulf War, the 1990s, and until the 2003 US invasion. The outcome was the production of one of the worst documented humanitarian crises of the late twentieth century.

In 1995 the UN Food and Agriculture Organization produced a preliminary estimate that 567,000 Iraqi children under five had died as a result of the sanctions. In August 1999 UNICEF published the Iraq Child and Maternal Mortality Survey (ICMMS), based on data from roughly 38,000 households across Iraq. The findings: Iraq's under-5 child mortality rate had more than doubled — from 56 deaths per 1,000 live births in 1984-1989 to 131 deaths per 1,000 live births in 1994-1999. The ICMMS extrapolated to 400,000-500,000 excess deaths among children under 5 in central and southern Iraq across 1991-1998. Independent estimates ranged from 227,000 to 500,000.

The post-2003 academic literature has produced contested counter-estimates. The 2017 *BMJ Global Health* study by Spagat and others challenged the ICMMS methodology and hypothesised that the Saddam regime had manipulated data. The post-invasion Iraq Living Conditions Survey (2004) and Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (2006, 2011) suggested child mortality in 1995-2000 was roughly 40 per 1,000 live births. The debate: whether the sanctions killed 400,000-500,000 children, or 200,000-300,000 children, or whether the mortality increase was attributable to Saddam-regime corruption and the Oil-for-Food Programme failures. UNICEF's November 1999 clarification: the survey figures represented the child deaths that would not have occurred had Iraq's 1980s reductions in child mortality continued through the 1990s, not deaths "caused by" sanctions in the sense of attributable to UNSCR 661 directly.

The centrepiece of the sanctions debate was the 12 May 1996 *60 Minutes* interview by Lesley Stahl of Madeleine Albright, then-US Ambassador to the United Nations. Stahl: "We have heard that a half million children have died. I mean, that's more children than died in Hiroshima. And, you know, is the price worth it?" Albright: "I think this is a very hard choice, but the price, we think the price is worth it." The interview won an Emmy Award. Albright would later state in her 2003 memoir *Madam Secretary* that the response had been "hasty, clumsy and wrong" and that she "had fallen into a trap and said something I did not mean." In a 2020 interview she summarised the lesson: "we learned in many ways that comprehensive sanctions often hurt the people of the country and don't really accomplish what is wanted."

The August 1996 Osama bin Laden fatwa cited "more than 600,000 Iraqi children" as having died as a result of the sanctions and US policy. The 1998 bin Laden fatwa cited the sanctions, the US troop presence in Saudi Arabia, and US support for Israel as the justifications for jihad against the United States. The record: the US-and-UK sanctions architecture was a primary motivation cited for the al-Qaeda attacks including 11 September 2001.

The World Health Organization cancer-programme chief Karol Sikora characterised the record in a 1999 *BMJ* article: requested radiotherapy equipment, chemotherapy drugs and analgesics for Iraqi cancer patients were consistently blocked by US and British advisors to the UN Sanctions Committee. Sikora: "There seems to be a rather ludicrous notion that such agents could be converted into chemical or other weapons."

The UN Humanitarian Coordinator for Iraq Denis Halliday resigned in September 1998 in protest, characterising the sanctions as "genocide." His successor Hans von Sponeck resigned in 2000 in protest under the same characterisation.

The Oil-for-Food Programme began deliveries in March 1997 — six years after the sanctions began. The UN/Iraq Memorandum of Understanding permitted Iraq to sell limited oil to fund humanitarian imports under UN supervision; the programme ran through 2003 and was the subject of subsequent corruption scandals involving multiple UN and Iraqi officials.

## The 1990s no-fly zones

The US/UK aerial campaign against Iraq did not end with the 1991 ceasefire. In April 1991 the US, UK, and France established the Iraqi-Kurdish "no-fly zone" north of the 36th parallel (Operation Provide Comfort, later Northern Watch). In August 1992 the US, UK, and France established the southern no-fly zone south of the 32nd parallel (Operation Southern Watch). France withdrew in 1996-1998.

The 1991-2003 US/UK no-fly-zone enforcement bombings produced thousands of sorties against Iraqi air-defence and command-and-control facilities, with continuous Iraqi civilian casualties.

On 16-19 December 1998 the Clinton administration launched Operation Desert Fox — a 70-hour US-UK bombing campaign against Iraq in response to the Iraqi expulsion of UN weapons inspectors (UNSCOM). The campaign struck roughly 100 targets. The context: the Clinton administration was facing the Monica Lewinsky impeachment proceedings. The bombing was characterised by Republican critics as an attempt to distract from the impeachment ("wag the dog").

## Yugoslav wars 1991-2001

The dissolution of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia produced the bloodiest European wars since World War II. The context: the death of Tito (1980), the economic crisis of the 1980s, the rise of Slobodan Milošević through the Serbian Communist Party (1986-onward), the rise of Franjo Tuđman in Croatia, the rise of Alija Izetbegović in Bosnia, and the unravelling of the federal architecture.

### Slovenia and Croatia 1991-1995

In June 1991 Slovenia and Croatia declared independence. The Slovenian Ten-Day War (27 June - 7 July 1991) produced 62 deaths and Slovenian withdrawal from Yugoslavia. The Croatian War of Independence 1991-1995 produced roughly 20,000 deaths and Serb-Croatian ethnic-cleansing campaigns including the November 1991 Vukovar massacre.

### Bosnian War 1992-1995

The Bosnian War began in April 1992 after the Bosnian referendum on independence (boycotted by Bosnian Serbs). The war ran between Bosniaks (Bosnian Muslims) under President Alija Izetbegović, Bosnian Serbs under Republika Srpska President Radovan Karadžić and military commander Ratko Mladić (backed by Belgrade), and Bosnian Croats (backed by Zagreb).

The Siege of Sarajevo (April 1992 - February 1996) was the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare — 1,425 days. Roughly 13,952 people killed including 5,434 civilians under Bosnian Serb artillery and sniper fire.

The Srebrenica massacre ran 11-13 July 1995. Republika Srpska forces under General Ratko Mladić systematically rounded up Bosniak men and boys aged 12-77 from the UN-declared "safe haven" of Srebrenica. The operation killed roughly 8,000 within 48-72 hours. The Dutch UNPROFOR peacekeeping contingent in Srebrenica was forced to surrender; Dutch parliamentary recriminations followed. The 2004 ICTY (International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia) ruling characterised Srebrenica as genocide; the 2007 International Court of Justice ruling concurred. Srebrenica is the worst atrocity in Europe since World War II.

In response to Srebrenica the 30 August - 20 September 1995 NATO Operation Deliberate Force air campaign — the first major NATO combat operation in history — struck Bosnian Serb forces. The November 1995 Dayton Peace Accords negotiated at Wright-Patterson AFB by US envoy Richard Holbrooke produced the partition of Bosnia into the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina (Bosniak-Croat) and Republika Srpska under a single state. The Dayton Accords were signed in Paris on 14 December 1995.

Bosnian War casualties: roughly 100,000-110,000 killed; roughly 2.2 million displaced.

### Kosovo War 1998-1999 and NATO bombing

The Kosovo War began on 28 February 1998 between the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia under Slobodan Milošević and the Kosovo Liberation Army (KLA). The context: the post-1989 Milošević suppression of Kosovar Albanian autonomy and the Ibrahim Rugova-led non-violent resistance had given way to the KLA armed insurgency.

The 24 March - 10 June 1999 NATO Operation Allied Force — a 78-day bombing campaign — ran against the FRY without UN Security Council authorisation. Russia and China would have vetoed. The bombing struck targets across Yugoslavia including Belgrade. The outcome: 10 June 1999 Kumanovo Agreement; Yugoslav military withdrawal from Kosovo; UN Security Council Resolution 1244 establishing the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo (UNMIK).

Operation Allied Force casualties: Human Rights Watch documented 488-528 Yugoslav civilians killed in NATO bombing. The Humanitarian Law Centre 2014 list named 454 civilian victims. The Yugoslav government claimed 1,200-5,000 civilians dead. Yugoslav military deaths roughly 1,000. The NATO bombing used 9-11 tonnes of depleted uranium munitions, dropped by A-10 Thunderbolts and Tomahawk cruise missiles; uranium-soil contamination at Cape Arza and other sites has persisted.

The NATO bombing produced multiple incidents of mass civilian deaths. The 14 April 1999 Đakovica refugee column bombing killed roughly 75 ethnic Albanian refugees fleeing Kosovo. The 7 May 1999 NATO bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade killed 3 Chinese journalists; the US characterised the bombing as a "mistake" attributed to outdated CIA maps. The 13 May 1999 Korisa village bombing killed roughly 87 people; the Yugoslav forensic record disputed NATO's characterisation that the village had been a military command-and-control facility.

The post-bombing outcome: 164,000+ ethnic Serbs and 24,000 Roma left Kosovo in the weeks after the Yugoslav military withdrawal. The KLA was subsequently accused of war crimes including organ trafficking per the 2010 Council of Europe Marty Report. Kosovo declared independence 17 February 2008; not recognised by Serbia, Russia, China, India, Brazil, or several EU states (Spain, Cyprus, Greece, Romania, Slovakia).

### Milošević and the ICTY

Slobodan Milošević was indicted by the ICTY on 27 May 1999 for crimes against humanity and war crimes during the Kosovo War. He was arrested on 1 April 2001 in Belgrade and extradited to the ICTY in The Hague on 28 June 2001. The trial began February 2002. Milošević died of a heart attack in ICTY detention on 11 March 2006 before verdict.

The ICTY operated 1993-2017. Convictions included Radovan Karadžić (40 years 2016, life sentence 2019), Ratko Mladić (life sentence November 2017), and 90+ other individuals. The record: the ICTY was the first international war-crimes tribunal since Nuremberg and Tokyo.

## 11 September 2001 and the AUMF

On 11 September 2001 al-Qaeda operatives under Osama bin Laden conducted the suicide hijackings of four US commercial aircraft. Two struck the World Trade Center towers in New York; one struck the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia; one crashed in a field in Shanksville, Pennsylvania after passenger resistance. The casualties: 2,977 killed including 343 New York City firefighters and 71 New York City police officers.

The context: al-Qaeda had been founded by bin Laden in 1988 from the mujahideen networks created by US-Saudi-Pakistani Operation Cyclone (covered in Chapter 1 and Dossier 24). The al-Qaeda architecture had run previous attacks: the 1993 World Trade Center bombing; the 1998 US embassy bombings in Nairobi (Kenya) and Dar es Salaam (Tanzania) that killed 224 people; the 12 October 2000 USS Cole bombing in Aden, Yemen that killed 17 US sailors.

On 14 September 2001 the US Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force. The House vote: 420-1 (one dissenting vote, Representative Barbara Lee, D-CA). The Senate vote: 98-0. The statutory authorisation: the President could "use all necessary and appropriate force against those nations, organizations, or persons he determines planned, authorized, committed, or aided the terrorist attacks that occurred on September 11, 2001, or harbored such organizations or persons."

The outcome: the 2001 AUMF has been cited by Bush, Obama, Trump (first term), Biden, and Trump (second term) administrations as the legal basis for US military operations in roughly 19 countries through 2024. The military operations have included Afghanistan, Iraq (under the separate 2002 AUMF for Iraq), Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, Libya, Syria, Iraq again (2014), the Philippines, and elsewhere. The architecture has produced extended legal disputes over executive war powers.

The 26 October 2001 USA PATRIOT Act produced the architecture of US-domestic mass surveillance, financial intelligence collection, expanded FBI authority, and the revelations through Edward Snowden (2013) and others.

## The Afghanistan War 2001-2021

On 7 October 2001 the US under President George W. Bush launched Operation Enduring Freedom. The US air campaign supported the Northern Alliance ground offensive. By 13 November 2001 the Northern Alliance had captured Kabul. By 7 December the Taliban surrendered Kandahar. The US decision to focus on Tora Bora and bin Laden in December 2001 produced the failure to capture bin Laden — who escaped into Pakistan's Federally Administered Tribal Areas.

The post-2001 architecture ran through the Bonn Agreement (December 2001) installing Hamid Karzai as interim leader; the International Security Assistance Force (ISAF) under NATO command from 2003; the US military presence reaching peak deployment of roughly 100,000 troops under Obama in 2010-2011; the Taliban regroupment in Pakistan tribal areas under the Quetta Shura and Haqqani Network leadership; and the Afghan civilian casualties recorded by the UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA) from 2007 onward.

The Pakistani role — covered across Dossier 24 (the Asim Munir / Pakistani political-class dossier) — ran through the Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) under multiple DG ISIs. *Der Spiegel*'s 2010 characterisation of the Wikileaks Afghan War Logs was that the leaked documents established the ISI as "the most important accomplice the Taliban has outside of Afghanistan."

On 2 May 2011 US Navy SEAL Team Six conducted Operation Neptune Spear in Abbottabad, Pakistan. Osama bin Laden was killed in his Abbottabad compound. The Pakistani embarrassment — bin Laden had been living roughly 1.2 km from the Pakistan Military Academy in Kakul — produced the US-Pakistani crisis that followed. The Pakistani government denied prior knowledge.

The 29 February 2020 Doha Agreement was signed by the Trump administration through negotiator Zalmay Khalilzad and the Taliban under Mullah Abdul Ghani Baradar. The terms: US troop withdrawal within 14 months; Taliban commitments not to harbour terrorist groups; intra-Afghan negotiations. The agreement excluded the Afghan government of President Ashraf Ghani.

The 14 April 2021 Biden announcement of full US withdrawal by 11 September 2021 (subsequently moved to 31 August) produced the Taliban offensive across Afghanistan. On 15 August 2021 Taliban forces captured Kabul. President Ashraf Ghani fled. The US embassy evacuation ran through Hamid Karzai International Airport. The 26 August 2021 ISIS-K suicide bombing at the airport killed 13 US service members and roughly 170 Afghan civilians during the evacuation. On 30 August 2021 the last US troops left Afghanistan.

The outcome: the longest war in US history at roughly 20 years and 6 months — six months longer than Vietnam. The Taliban returned to power. The post-2001 US-backed Afghan Republic collapsed within hours of the Ghani flight. The Taliban "Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan" has run since 15 August 2021 with no international recognition.

Afghanistan War casualties: US military deaths 2,459 (1,922 killed in action); US wounded 20,769; US contractors killed 1,822; CIA officers killed 18. UK military deaths 457; other coalition deaths bringing total coalition military fatalities to roughly 3,621. Afghan civilian casualties recorded by UNAMA from 2007: 100,000+ civilian casualties (killed and wounded) 2009-2020 alone. UNAMA attribution: 61-80% of civilian deaths attributed to "anti-government elements" (Taliban and other insurgents) per year. The Uppsala Conflict Data Programme total: 212,191 killed across the war. The Costs of War 2015 estimate of indirect deaths: 360,000+ additional. Taliban fighter deaths: 80,000+. Total Afghan dead across the conflict (including pre-2001 and post-2021 periods): estimates range from 176,000 to 1+ million.

## The Iraq War 2003-2011

The architecture of the 2003 Iraq War ran through the post-9/11 neoconservative political-class operators within the Bush administration: Vice President Dick Cheney (former Halliburton CEO whose former company would receive $39.5 billion in war contracts); Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld; Deputy Secretary of Defense Paul Wolfowitz (the Project for a New American Century neoconservative architect from 1992 onward); Under Secretary of Defense for Policy Douglas Feith (who created the Office of Special Plans); National Security Advisor Condoleezza Rice; and Vice Presidential Chief of Staff Lewis "Scooter" Libby.

The 29 January 2002 Bush "Axis of Evil" State of the Union speech identified Iraq, Iran, and North Korea as a threat. The campaign within the Bush administration to invade Iraq ran through intelligence manipulation. The 2002 Office of Special Plans within the Pentagon under Feith was created to assemble the WMD case for war — bypassing the intelligence-community channels at the CIA and Defense Intelligence Agency.

On 5 February 2003 Secretary of State Colin Powell — the most-trusted figure within the Bush administration — appeared before the UN Security Council with vials, satellite photos, intercepted communications, and a case alleging Iraqi WMD programmes. The post-2004 record: the intelligence had been manipulated, cherry-picked, and in some cases fabricated. Powell would subsequently characterise the presentation as a "blot" on his record.

In October 2002 the US Congress passed the Iraq Resolution (AUMF Iraq) on bipartisan support. The House vote: 296-133. The Senate vote: 77-23. The record: Senator Hillary Clinton, Senator Joe Biden, Senator John Kerry, Senator John Edwards, Senator Charles Schumer, and most Democratic-party operators voted yes. The Senate dissenters included Senator Robert Byrd, Senator Edward Kennedy, Senator Russ Feingold, and Senator Lincoln Chafee (the only Republican).

On 20 March 2003 the US-led invasion began. The ground campaign reached Baghdad on 9 April 2003; the Firdos Square statue of Saddam Hussein was toppled by US Marines in a staged media event. On 1 May 2003 Bush delivered the "Mission Accomplished" speech on the USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier off San Diego under a banner reading "Mission Accomplished." The Bush speech became a symbol of presidential delusion across the insurgency and civil war that followed.

On 23 May 2003 Coalition Provisional Authority (CPA) Administrator Paul Bremer issued CPA Order Number 2 disbanding the Iraqi military. The consequence: roughly 400,000 armed, trained Iraqi soldiers — predominantly Sunni — were suddenly unemployed without pensions. The subsequent insurgency ran through these former Iraqi military personnel. The architecture of ISIS — formed initially under Abu Musab al-Zarqawi as al-Qaeda in Iraq, subsequently rebranded multiple times, and finally as the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant under Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi declaring the "caliphate" in June 2014 — ran through the networks created by Bremer Order Number 2. The post-2014 ISIS war is covered later in this chapter.

The Iraq War casualties ran through multiple phases. The 13 December 2003 Saddam Hussein capture at a farmhouse near Tikrit. The April 2004 First Battle of Fallujah. The April 2004 Abu Ghraib torture scandal — exposed through photographs of US Army personnel torturing Iraqi prisoners — produced damage to US international standing that has persisted. The November 2004 Second Battle of Fallujah produced US-Marine urban combat with extensive civilian casualties and the use of white phosphorus munitions. The 22 February 2006 Samarra mosque bombing (al-Askari Mosque) triggered the Sunni-Shia sectarian war.

The 30 December 2006 Saddam Hussein execution by hanging was conducted by the Shia-led Iraqi government on the first day of Eid al-Adha. The 2007 "Surge" — a 30,000-troop additional US deployment under General David Petraeus and counter-insurgency doctrine — produced reductions in US casualty rates and a characterisation of relative success. The post-2008 US-Iraq Status of Forces Agreement under Bush, and the Obama-administration 2011 final withdrawal, produced the 18 December 2011 last-US-troop departure from Iraq.

Iraq War casualties 2003-2011: US military deaths 4,431 (warcosts.org) or 4,488 (Brown University) or 4,492 (DoD). US contractors killed 3,400+. US wounded 32,000+. UK military deaths 179. Iraqi deaths: Iraq Body Count documented violent deaths from 2003 onward 207,841-288,000 by March 2020 (including post-2014 ISIS period). Brown University Costs of War 2013 figure: 190,000+ direct deaths. Updated Costs of War 2019 figure for Iraq alone 2003-2019: roughly 268,000 direct deaths (Crawford and Lutz). Indirect deaths from preventable disease, infrastructure collapse, and the ISIS war are characterised by Costs of War as "several times" the direct deaths.

Financial cost of the Iraq War: direct US spending roughly $2.2 trillion through 2013 (Brown) or $1.79 trillion through 2023 (Crawford). With future veterans' care through 2050: $2.89 trillion+. With cumulative interest on borrowing through 2053: $3.9 trillion+ on top. The cost: more than the Vietnam War, the Korean War, World War I, and most other major wars in US history — second only to World War II in inflation-adjusted terms.

## The post-2014 ISIS war

The rise of ISIS ran through the channels created by Bremer Order Number 2 and the Iraqi sectarian war. Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi declared the "Islamic State Caliphate" on 29 June 2014 in Mosul, Iraq — after ISIS forces had captured Mosul (Iraq's second-largest city) from the collapsed Iraqi Army.

The ISIS conquest produced peak territorial control across roughly 100,000 square kilometres of Iraq and Syria including Mosul, Raqqa, Fallujah, Tikrit, and large parts of Anbar, Salah ad-Din, Diyala, Nineveh, Aleppo, Deir ez-Zor, and Raqqa governorates. Roughly 10 million people lived under ISIS rule at peak.

The August 2014 US-led Operation Inherent Resolve began airstrikes against ISIS positions in Iraq and Syria. The coalition operations included the US, UK, France, Australia, Canada, the Netherlands, Belgium, Denmark, and 60+ other countries with varying contributions. The Iraqi-government ground campaign — coordinated with Iranian-backed Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF / Hashd al-Shaabi) under Qasem Soleimani's IRGC Quds Force, and with Kurdish Peshmerga in the north — produced the sequential liberation of major cities: Fallujah (June 2016), Mosul (July 2017 after a 9-month battle), Raqqa (October 2017 by the Syrian Democratic Forces under US air support), and the March 2019 final fall of Baghuz, the last ISIS territorial holding.

Baghdadi was killed in Operation Kayla Mueller in Barisha, Idlib Governorate, Syria on 27 October 2019 by US Delta Force operators.

ISIS-war casualties: Brown University Costs of War 2023 figure for Iraq + Syria 2003-2023: 550,000-580,000 direct deaths. ISIS sleeper-cell operations continue across Iraq, Syria, and globally to the date of writing.

## The Libya intervention 2011

On 17 February 2011 Libyan anti-Gaddafi demonstrations began in Benghazi as part of the Arab Spring. The first weeks of violence by both regime and protesters produced escalation. On 17 March 2011 UN Security Council Resolution 1973 — passed 10-0 with five abstentions (Russia, China, Germany, Brazil, India) — authorised "all necessary measures" to protect Libyan civilians. The understanding at the time of the vote did not include regime change.

On 19 March 2011 French air strikes began Operation Harmattan; US-UK joined within hours under Operation Odyssey Dawn (US) and Operation Ellamy (UK). On 31 March NATO took command under Operation Unified Protector. The architecture ran under the 2005 UN World Summit "Responsibility to Protect" (R2P) doctrine.

The reversal ran through the NATO mission expansion. In April 2011 US President Barack Obama, UK PM David Cameron, and French President Nicolas Sarkozy issued a joint statement that protection of Libyan civilians required removal of Gaddafi. The NATO bombing escalated to support of rebel ground operations.

NATO flew roughly 26,500 sorties over eight months, including 7,000 bombing sorties. Top contributors by sortie share: France 33%, UK 21%, US 16%, Italy 10%, Denmark/Canada/Norway 31% combined. On 20 August 2011 Tripoli fell to rebel forces. On 20 October 2011 Muammar Gaddafi was captured in a drainage pipe near Sirte by rebel fighters, beaten, sodomised with a bayonet, and shot. The response by US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton: "We came, we saw, he died" (laughing). NATO ended operations 31 October 2011.

The Libyan collapse ran across 2011-2025. The 2014 Second Libyan Civil War produced competing governments: the UN-recognised Government of National Accord in Tripoli (later Government of National Unity), backed by Turkey and Qatar; and the Libyan National Army (LNA) under General Khalifa Haftar in Tobruk/Benghazi, backed by the UAE, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Russia (through the Wagner Group), and France. The 23 October 2020 Geneva ceasefire ended major fighting; fragmentation has continued.

Libya intervention casualties: 2011 fighting per the Uppsala Conflict Data Programme produced 1,914-3,466 deaths; per Libyan-government 2013 estimates roughly 4,700 rebel and 4,700 Gaddafi-loyalist dead plus 2,100 missing. Per ACLED 6,109 fatalities 15 February - 23 October 2011. NATO civilian casualties: roughly 60-72 confirmed civilian deaths from NATO bombing per Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International investigations.

Post-2011 Libyan war deaths: the subsequent civil wars produced tens of thousands of dead; per the New America/Airwars database 624-915 civilians killed in 4,517+ post-2011 air, drone, and artillery strikes by multiple actors 2012-2020.

The Alan Kuperman / Belfer Center 2013 *International Security* analysis characterised the outcome: "NATO's action magnified the conflict's duration about sixfold and its death toll at least sevenfold, while also exacerbating human rights abuses, humanitarian suffering, Islamic radicalism, and weapons proliferation in Libya and its neighbors. If Libya was a 'model intervention,' then it was a model of failure." Up to 15,000 MANPADS (man-portable surface-to-air missiles) from Gaddafi arsenal were unaccounted for as of 2012 — leaked to radical Islamists across the Sahel, Egypt, Syria, and beyond.

The 2016 UK House of Commons Foreign Affairs Committee report on Libya concluded that the UK government "failed to identify that the threat to civilians was overstated and that the rebels included a significant Islamist element."

The consequence: Russia and China cited the Libyan precedent as the reason for vetoing every UN Security Council resolution on Syria 2011-onward. The damage to R2P, to UN Security Council cooperation, and to the US-Russia-China relationship has been severe and lasting. Across all R2P interventions since 2011 — Côte d'Ivoire, Syria, Yemen, South Sudan, Myanmar, Tigray, Sudan — the record has been catastrophic.

## Syria 2011-2024

The Syrian civil war began on 15 March 2011 when anti-government protests erupted in Deraa following the torture of teenagers who had written anti-regime graffiti. The regime crackdown by the Assad government produced escalation across multiple Syrian cities. By late 2011 the civil war was running between the Assad regime (backed by Iran and Russia and Hezbollah), the Free Syrian Army and other rebel groups (backed by Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, US, UK, France through the CIA Timber Sycamore covert programme 2013-2017), the Kurdish YPG/SDF in the north, and ISIS in the east.

The Russian and Iranian roles ran continuously. On 30 September 2015 Russia under President Vladimir Putin launched air operations from Hmeimim airbase in Latakia to support the Assad regime. The Russian intervention used air power, Wagner Group mercenaries, and naval support from Tartus. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) Quds Force under Qasem Soleimani ran ground operations alongside Hezbollah, Iraqi Shia militias, and Afghan Shia "Fatemiyoun" militias.

The chemical-weapons attacks by the Assad regime ran across 2013-2018. The 21 August 2013 Ghouta sarin attack killed roughly 1,400 in Eastern Ghouta. The Obama-administration "red line" — Obama's 2012 statement that chemical-weapons use would produce US military response — was not enforced; the Obama-administration decision to seek congressional authorisation, the Russian-mediated deal for Syria to declare its chemical-weapons stockpile, and the OPCW operations produced incomplete chemical-weapons elimination. The 4 April 2017 Khan Shaykhun sarin attack killed roughly 89. The Trump-administration response: 6 April 2017 Tomahawk strike on Shayrat air base. The 7 April 2018 Douma chlorine attack killed roughly 49; the 14 April 2018 US-UK-France strike on Syrian chemical facilities followed.

The ISIS war (covered above) ran through the US-led Operation Inherent Resolve and the Syrian Democratic Forces under Mazloum Abdi.

The stalemate ran from 2018 through 2024. The regional developments: the 2024 Israeli-Lebanese war that severely weakened Hezbollah (including the 27 September 2024 killing of Hezbollah Secretary-General Hassan Nasrallah); the Russian military focus on Ukraine since 24 February 2022; the Iranian weakening after Israeli strikes — produced the opening for the rebel offensive.

On 27 November 2024 Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS) under Abu Mohammad al-Jolani (Ahmad al-Sharaa) launched an offensive from Idlib. On 30 November HTS captured Aleppo. On 5 December HTS captured Hama. On 7 December HTS captured Homs. On 8 December 2024 HTS entered Damascus; Bashar al-Assad fled to Moscow with his family. The end of Ba'ath Party rule (54 years across the father-son Assad dynasty 1971-2024).

The post-Assad architecture has run through the HTS-led interim government under Mohammed al-Bashir then al-Sharaa as President from January 2025. The coastal massacres of Alawites by HTS-aligned forces (February-March 2025) killed roughly 1,400 per the Syrian Network for Human Rights.

Syrian war casualties ran through multiple accountings. The Syrian Network for Human Rights documented 230,000+ civilians killed across 2011-2024; attribution: 87% by Assad regime and Iranian militias (201,260 deaths), 3% by Russian forces (6,969), 2.2% by ISIS (5,056), 1.8% by armed opposition factions (4,227), 1.3% by the US-led International Coalition (3,055), 0.7% by SDF (1,491), 0.2% by HTS (538). The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights documented 618,000+ killed total including combatants. The UN September 2021 verified-deaths figure: 350,000+ (acknowledged as undercount). Brown Costs of War estimate for Iraq + Syria 2003-2023: 550,000-580,000 direct deaths.

Russian military deaths in Syria 2015-2024: 543 confirmed per BBC Russia, of which 346 were Wagner Group; 116 officially confirmed by the Russian Defence Ministry. The displacement: 12.5 million Syrians displaced — roughly 5.5-6.4 million refugees and 6.7-7.2 million internally displaced.

## Yemen 2015-onward

The Yemen war began on 26 March 2015 with the Saudi-led intervention against the Houthi (Ansar Allah) movement that had captured Sana'a in September 2014. The context: the collapse of the 2011-2012 Arab Spring transition under President Abd Rabbu Mansour Hadi; the Houthi-Saleh alliance (former President Ali Abdullah Saleh, ousted 2012, allied with the Zaidi Shia Houthi movement that had fought multiple wars against the Saleh government); and Saudi fear of Iranian influence on the Arabian Peninsula's southern border.

The Saudi coalition under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman (MBS) included Saudi Arabia (100 warplanes, 150,000 soldiers), the UAE under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Zayed (MBZ), Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar (until 2017), Egypt, Jordan, Morocco, Sudan, and Senegal. US support ran through logistical assistance, intelligence sharing, aerial refuelling (2015-2018), weapons sales (tens of billions of dollars), and targeting assistance. US-trained Saudi and Emirati pilots flew air operations using US-manufactured F-15s, GBU-12 bombs, and other munitions.

The Yemen Data Project: the Saudi-led coalition carried out roughly 24,600 air raids 2015-2022 — an average of roughly 10 per day. Per the Yemen Data Project: roughly 9,000 civilians killed by direct coalition air strikes — including the 8 October 2016 Sana'a funeral hall bombing (~155 killed), the 9 August 2018 Dahyan school bus bombing (~51 killed including 40 children, by a US-made Lockheed-Martin laser-guided bomb), and the 22 April 2018 Al-Raqa wedding bombing (~30 killed) by a US-made GBU-12 bomb.

Yemen war casualties: by the end of 2021 the UN Development Programme estimated 377,000+ Yemenis dead — roughly 60% from indirect causes (starvation, disease, lack of medical care) under the Saudi-led naval and aerial blockade. Roughly 150,000 deaths from direct armed conflict. Roughly 16+ million Yemenis on the brink of famine — the world's worst humanitarian crisis per the UN. Roughly 21.6 million Yemenis (66% of population) needed humanitarian aid. 4.5+ million Yemenis internally displaced.

The outcome: the US, UK, French, and Canadian support to the Saudi-and-UAE air campaign produced the worst humanitarian crisis on Earth. The Trump administration declined to suspend arms sales. The February 2021 Biden announcement of an end to US "offensive support" was characterised by humanitarian organisations as inadequate; US arms sales to Saudi Arabia continued.

The Columbia Law School Security Force Monitor investigation with the *Washington Post* in 2018 concluded that a major share of airstrikes that killed civilians in Yemen were carried out by jets developed, sold, and maintained by US companies and flown by US-trained pilots.

The April 2022 UN-mediated truce produced a reduction in major hostilities. The Saudi-Iranian rapprochement (March 2023 China-mediated agreement) produced reductions in coalition air strikes. The Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping from late October 2023 onward (in coordination with the post-7-October-2023 Gaza war covered in Chapter 5) produced US, UK, and Israeli air strikes against Houthi positions across 2023-2025. The March 2025 Trump-administration redesignation of the Houthis as a Foreign Terrorist Organisation followed.

## The drone war architecture

The post-2001 US-drone-war architecture ran as the centrepiece of the "global war on terror." The 6 January 2002 first US drone strike outside an active war zone killed Abu Ali al-Harithi (alleged USS Cole bombing planner) in Yemen. The CIA-and-JSOC drone-strike architecture ran across Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, Libya, the Philippines, and other theatres.

### Pakistan

The 2004-2018 CIA drone-strike campaign in Pakistan's Federally Administered Tribal Areas ran under Bush, Obama, and Trump administrations. The New America database: 414 strikes producing 2,366-3,702 deaths including 245-303 civilians. The Bureau of Investigative Journalism: 430+ strikes producing 2,515-4,026 deaths including 424-969 civilian deaths. "Double-tap" strikes — where second strikes targeted rescuers responding to first strikes — produced civilian-casualty patterns characterised by independent investigators as war crimes.

The Pakistani ambiguity — public condemnation paired with private cooperation including Shamsi Airfield in Balochistan used by US drones until December 2011 — produced political-class operator complicity covered in Dossier 24.

### Yemen

The post-2002 US drone-strike campaign against al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) ran under Bush, Obama, Trump (first term), Biden, and Trump (second term) administrations. The New America database: 376+ strikes producing 1,390-1,779 deaths including 125-151 civilian deaths.

The 30 September 2011 al-Awlaki strikes produced legal disputes. Anwar al-Awlaki — a US citizen, born in New Mexico, US-radical-cleric who had moved to Yemen — was killed by a US drone in Al-Jawf Governorate. Two weeks later on 14 October 2011 a separate US drone strike killed Anwar al-Awlaki's 16-year-old son Abdulrahman al-Awlaki — also a US citizen, born in Denver, who had no documented militant ties. The US record: Abdulrahman's death was characterised as "collateral damage." Then-White House Press Secretary Robert Gibbs in 2012 characterised the case: Abdulrahman should "have a far more responsible father." The ACLU lawsuit on behalf of the al-Awlaki family was dismissed in 2014.

The 29 January 2017 first Trump-administration drone raid in Yakla, Yemen — Operation Yakla — killed Navy SEAL William "Ryan" Owens and roughly 30 Yemeni civilians including 8-year-old US citizen Nawar al-Awlaki (Anwar al-Awlaki's daughter).

### Somalia

The post-2007 US drone-strike campaign against al-Shabaab in Somalia produced 200+ strikes per the New America database; the Trump-administration escalation reached 47 strikes in 2019 alone. Roughly several thousand deaths. The Biden-administration 2023 policy restrictions reduced strike rates.

### Libya

US drone strikes against ISIS in Sirte ran across 2016-2019 under Obama Administration Presidential Policy Guidance (PPG). UAE, Egyptian, and Russian (Wagner Group) drone operations across Libya have run with no accountability.

### The architecture

The legal framework ran through the 2001 AUMF, the Obama-era Presidential Policy Guidance (May 2013), and the Trump-administration easing of constraints. The architecture produced operations across a 19+ country footprint including the Philippines, Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Mozambique, and others.

The 1 February 2020 Trump-administration designation of US "battlefield" expanded to include Niger and the broader Sahel region was an expansion of the AUMF framework. The African-coup-belt collapse (covered in Chapter 6 and the Sahel dossier 53) ran under the context of the US-French-and-other counterterrorism architecture.

## Casualties and costs

The Brown University Costs of War project synthesis for the post-9/11 wars across the five principal theatres (Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Yemen, Pakistan) 2001-2023:

- Direct deaths: 940,000+ including 432,000+ civilians.
- Indirect deaths (from preventable disease, malnutrition, infrastructure collapse, displacement): 3.6-3.8 million.
- Total deaths: 4.5-4.7 million.
- Direct US cost: roughly $8 trillion (not including future interest on borrowing).
- Future interest costs on borrowing for the wars through 2053: estimated multi-trillion-dollar additional.
- Displaced people: 38 million.

Yugoslav-wars (Bosnia + Kosovo + Croatia) deaths: roughly 130,000-140,000 total across 1991-1999.

Libya 2011-2025 deaths: roughly 25,000-50,000 across the 2011 war, the 2014-2020 second civil war, and the continuing instability.

Iraq sanctions deaths (1990-2003): contested figures from 200,000 to 500,000 child deaths (under five), excluded from post-9/11 totals.

Gulf War 1991 deaths: roughly 25,000-35,000 Iraqi military and 2,278-200,000 Iraqi civilian deaths, plus 250-392 coalition military deaths.

Combined post-Cold-War US-war death toll across all theatres direct and indirect: in the range of 5-6 million people across 35 years.

## Personnel continuity

The post-1989 US national-security architecture has run continuously across Republican and Democratic administrations. Personnel continuity has produced architectures.

John Negroponte — US Ambassador to Honduras during the 1980s Contra wars (covered in Chapter 1) — served as Permanent US Representative to the UN 2001-2004, US Ambassador to Iraq 2004-2005, Director of National Intelligence 2005-2007, and US Deputy Secretary of State 2007-2009. The continuity from the Central American dirty wars to the Iraq War ran through Negroponte and multiple other figures.

Dick Cheney — Secretary of Defense 1989-1993 (Gulf War) and Vice President 2001-2009 (Iraq War) — was the architect of both post-Cold-War US-Iraq engagements. The Cheney record produced multi-billion-dollar Halliburton contracting; his former company received $39.5 billion in Iraq War contracts.

Hillary Clinton — Senator who voted yes on the 2002 AUMF Iraq; Secretary of State 2009-2013 who pushed for the 2011 Libya intervention; 2016 Democratic-party presidential nominee — was the centrepiece of the Democratic-party post-Cold-War foreign-policy political class.

Joe Biden — Senator who voted yes on the 2002 AUMF Iraq; Vice President 2009-2017 who supported the Libya intervention; President 2021-2025 who completed the Afghanistan withdrawal under embarrassment — was a Democratic-party foreign-policy centrepiece. Joe Biden is covered in detail in Dossier 1.

Barack Obama — President 2009-2017 — ran the escalation of the drone war (542 drone strikes during his administration per the Bureau of Investigative Journalism), the 2009-2011 Afghanistan surge, the 2011 Libya intervention, the 2013 CIA Timber Sycamore Syria covert programme, the 2014 Iraq re-intervention against ISIS, and the Yemen and Somalia campaigns. The Obama-administration Nobel Peace Prize (October 2009) ran against the record of thousands killed under Obama-administration policies.

Donald Trump (R, 2017-2021, 2025-) ran continuity. The 29 February 2020 Doha Agreement on Afghanistan withdrawal. The 3 January 2020 drone-strike assassination of IRGC Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani at Baghdad International Airport — the first US-targeted killing of a foreign military officer. The 2018 US withdrawal from the JCPOA (Iran nuclear deal). The second-term escalations 2025-onward across Iran, Yemen, and the Caribbean (2025 anti-Maduro Venezuela operations covered later).

## The verdict

The post-Cold-War US-led architecture has produced roughly 5-6 million deaths across the 1991-2025 period — within the range of the Cold War proxy-war architecture covered in Chapter 1. The cost: roughly $8 trillion in direct US war spending, plus future veterans' care and interest costs running into trillions more.

The public presentation was the "rules-based international order," "humanitarian intervention," "responsibility to protect," "spreading democracy," and "ending tyranny." The reality is documented in this chapter:

- 2,278 to 200,000+ Iraqi civilian deaths in the 1991 Gulf War;
- 200,000-500,000 Iraqi child deaths from 1990s sanctions;
- roughly 100,000-110,000 Bosnian deaths and the Srebrenica genocide;
- roughly 500-3,000 Yugoslav civilian deaths in NATO bombing under no UN authorisation;
- 2,977 US deaths in 9/11 attacks produced by US-Saudi-Pakistani Operation Cyclone alumni;
- roughly 212,000-940,000 deaths across the Afghanistan and Iraq wars;
- 4.5-4.7 million total direct-and-indirect deaths across the Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Yemen, Pakistan theatres 2001-2023;
- 25,000-50,000 deaths in Libya and the continuing fragmentation;
- 377,000+ Yemeni deaths across the Saudi-led US-supported war;
- thousands of drone-strike civilian deaths across Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, and Libya;
- 38 million displaced.

The post-1989 architecture has not produced the "rules-based international order." It has produced the industrial-scale production of war and refugees by the US-led national-security state.

## Bridge to Chapter 3

The Iran-Iraq War 1980-1988 — the reference war of the late twentieth century, producing roughly 1 million deaths, that ran under US-and-Western dual-side weapons supply and US-and-Iraqi facilitation of chemical-weapons use — is the dependency for understanding the architecture of the Middle East, the 1991 Gulf War and 2003 Iraq War, the Iranian post-1988 posture, and the regional war architecture that followed. The Iran-Iraq War is covered in Chapter 3.
