# Part XII Chapter 3: The Iran-Iraq War 1980-1988

## The forgotten million-dead war

Between 22 September 1980 and 20 August 1988 the Republic of Iraq and the Islamic Republic of Iran fought the bloodiest interstate war of the second half of the twentieth century. Roughly one million people died — some estimates reach two million. Roughly 500,000 combatants killed across both armies; roughly 100,000-200,000 civilians killed. Roughly one million wounded.

The war ran for eight years across thousand-kilometre frontages. It produced First-World-War-style trench warfare with twenty-first-century-equivalent technology. It produced the largest sustained use of chemical weapons by any state since World War I — chemical weapons used by Iraq with the full knowledge of the United States, the United Kingdom, France, the Soviet Union, West Germany, Italy, and other Western governments that supplied Iraq with weapons, intelligence, financial credits, precursor chemicals, and political cover.

The mask was the characterisation of the war as a regional sectarian conflict between Sunni Arab Iraq and Shia Persian Iran — a conflict in which the Western position operated under the Kissinger characterisation: "It's a shame they can't both lose." What runs behind the mask was the Reagan-administration covert architecture of arming Iraq while the Iran-Contra Affair simultaneously armed Iran — producing US complicity in chemical-weapons attacks that killed roughly 20,000 Iranian troops and roughly 5,000 Kurdish civilians at Halabja.

The Iran-Iraq War is the dependency chapter for understanding the post-1989 Middle East. The 1991 Gulf War (covered in Chapter 2) was a direct consequence of Iraq's $80-billion war debt and Saddam's belief that the West would tolerate his Kuwait invasion as it had tolerated the eight-year war against Iran. The 2003 Iraq War (covered in Chapter 2) ran on the WMD case that depended on the Iraqi chemical-weapons programmes the US had armed and protected across the 1980s. The Iranian posture since 1988 — the "axis of resistance," the nuclear-programme acceleration, the IRGC's consolidated power, the sponsorship of Hezbollah and other regional armed movements — runs from the Iran-Iraq War foundation.

The record covers the war in full: origins, military phases, the Iraqi chemical-weapons programme and use, the Halabja massacre and Anfal genocide, the Tanker War and US naval intervention, the USS Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air Flight 655, the Reagan-administration arming of both sides, and the postwar consequences.

## The Iranian Revolution and the road to war

The context begins with the Iranian Revolution of February 1979. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini returned from exile in Paris on 1 February 1979. Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi — the US-and-UK-installed monarch since the 1953 CIA-MI6 coup against Mohammad Mosaddegh — fled into exile on 16 January 1979. The 1 April 1979 Iranian referendum produced the founding of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

On 4 November 1979 Iranian student militants stormed the US Embassy in Tehran, beginning the 444-day hostage crisis (ending 20 January 1981). The US-Iranian rupture has continued to the date of writing. On 24-25 April 1980, Operation Eagle Claw — the failed US hostage rescue attempt — left eight US service members dead in a helicopter-aircraft collision in the Iranian desert.

In Iraq, Saddam Hussein had been the de facto power since the 1968 Ba'athist revolution under President Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr. On 16 July 1979 al-Bakr resigned; Saddam became President. The Ba'ath Party purges produced mass executions of suspected rivals as Saddam consolidated power.

The context for war ran through multiple dynamics. Khomeini called for Shia uprising against Saddam's secular Ba'athist regime — Iraq's population was roughly 60% Shia, predominantly southern, ruled by a Sunni-minority Ba'athist political class. The Iranian post-revolutionary military purges had produced disarray; Saddam calculated that Iran was militarily weakened and politically isolated. The Shatt al-Arab waterway dispute — and the 1975 Algiers Agreement under which Saddam (as Vice President) had ceded shared sovereignty over the waterway to Iran in exchange for an end to Iranian support for Iraqi Kurds — was the pretext. On 17 September 1980 Saddam tore up the Algiers Agreement on Iraqi state television.

## The invasion and initial phase 1980-1982

On 22 September 1980 Iraqi forces invaded Iran along the 644-kilometre Iran-Iraq border. The Iraqi objective: the oil-producing Khuzestan province (largely populated by ethnic Arabs, which Saddam called "Arabistan"), the Shatt al-Arab waterway, and the overthrow of the Khomeini government. Iraq claimed Iran had begun the war on 4 September 1980 with border-post shelling.

The Iraqi advance captured Iranian territory including the port city of Khorramshahr (after a 35-day siege ending 24 October 1980), Susangerd, Bostan, and frontier villages. Iraqi forces failed to capture Abadan after a siege.

The Iranian response ran through emergency mobilisation. The regular military — the Artesh — had been purged of thousands of officers during the post-revolutionary period. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (Pasdaran) had been founded by Khomeini decree on 5 May 1979 as a counter-force to the Artesh. The Basij volunteer paramilitary force (Mobilization Resistance Force) was created in November 1979.

Iranian counter-offensives across 1981 — Operation Tariq al-Quds (Path of Jerusalem) in November 1981 recaptured Bostan; Operation Fatah al-Mobin (Undeniable Victory) in March 1982 routed Iraqi forces in Khuzestan; Operation Beit ol-Moqaddas in April-May 1982 liberated Khorramshahr — produced the Iraqi reversal. By June 1982 Saddam had withdrawn Iraqi forces from most Iranian territory and proposed a ceasefire. Khomeini refused.

The Iranian decision in June 1982 to invade Iraq produced the attritional war that followed. The Iranian objective expanded beyond restoration of pre-war borders to include the overthrow of the Saddam regime, the installation of a Shia theocratic government in Baghdad, the liberation of Karbala and Najaf (Shia holy cities in southern Iraq), and the export of the Islamic Revolution.

## The attritional phase 1982-1987

The war ran through First-World-War-style trench warfare across the Iran-Iraq border. Iranian "human wave" attacks — Basij volunteer waves sent against Iraqi fortified positions — produced hundreds of thousands of Iranian casualties. Iranian ideology cast the martyrdom of Basij volunteers as religious obligation; thousands of Basij volunteers, sometimes children aged 12-16, were given plastic "keys to paradise" before being sent into minefields to clear paths for Iranian regular forces.

Iranian offensives ran under codenames: Operation Ramadan (July 1982) attempting to capture Basra; the Operation Wal-Fajr ("By the Dawn") series (Operations 1 through 10, 1983-1987); Operation Khaybar (February-March 1984) capturing the Majnoon Islands and oil fields; Operation Badr (March 1985); Operation Dawn 8 (February 1986) capturing the Fao Peninsula; the Karbala series (Karbala-1 through Karbala-10, 1986-1987) producing hundreds of thousands of casualties.

The Iraqi response ran through superior firepower, multi-billion-dollar Western weapons supplies, air superiority via French Mirage F-1 aircraft and Soviet-supplied MiG-23s and Su-22s, and the chemical-weapons programme.

## The Iraqi chemical-weapons programme

The Iraqi chemical-weapons programme had been founded in the early 1970s. Its expansion ran through foreign assistance: West German firms (including Karl Kolb, Pilot Plant, W.E.T., Wahdat Khorshid) supplied production equipment for sulphur mustard and nerve agents; French firms supplied glass-lined reactors; Dutch firm Melchemie supplied precursors; British firms supplied dual-use chemicals; Italian firm Saes Getters supplied production equipment; US firms shipped multiple biological agents (anthrax strains, botulinum toxin) under US Commerce Department licences 1985-1989 (per the 1994 Riegle Report from the US Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs).

The Iraqi production centre at Al-Muthanna State Establishment (also called Samarra Drug Industries) manufactured thousands of tonnes of sulphur mustard, tabun (GA), sarin (GB), and limited quantities of VX across the war. The Iraqi stockpile reached an estimated 1,000+ tonnes of bulk agent and roughly 30,000 chemical munitions.

The first Iraqi chemical-weapons use against Iran took place in 1983 (small-scale). By November 1983, Iraqi chemical-weapons use against Iranian troops was "almost daily" per declassified US intelligence later cited in the 2002 National Security Archive document release at George Washington University. On 5 March 1984 the US State Department acknowledged: "available evidence indicates that Iraq has used lethal chemical weapons." The 30 March 1984 *New York Times* reported US intelligence had "what they believe to be incontrovertible evidence that Iraq has used nerve gas in its war with Iran and has almost finished extensive sites for mass producing the lethal chemical warfare agent."

Iraq's February 1984 press release framed the chemical-weapons use as anti-pest control: "the invaders should know that for every harmful insect there is an insecticide capable of annihilating it whatever their number and Iraq possess[es] this annihilation insecticide." Iraqi Foreign Minister Tariq Aziz in 1988 acknowledged Iraq's chemical warfare while characterising it as response to Iranian chemical use — a characterisation for which no evidence has been produced.

Iranian casualties from Iraqi chemical weapons across the war: roughly 20,000 Iranian troops killed by mustard gas and nerve agents; roughly 100,000 Iranian troops wounded; thousands of civilians killed in Iraqi chemical attacks on Iranian cities and on Iraqi-Kurdish villages.

## US support for Iraq

The pivot in US policy ran through the Reagan-administration reversal of Carter-administration neutrality. On 10 March 1982 the Reagan administration removed Iraq from the State Department list of state sponsors of terrorism — the opening for US-Iraqi cooperation.

On 26 November 1983 Reagan signed National Security Decision Directive 114. The objective: projecting US military force in the Persian Gulf and protecting oil supplies. The directive did not mention chemical weapons or human rights concerns. On 1 January 1984 the *Washington Post* reported the US had "informed friendly Persian Gulf nations that the defeat of Iraq in the three-year-old war with Iran would be 'contrary to US interests' and has made several moves to prevent that result."

The centrepiece of the US-Iraq architecture ran through Donald Rumsfeld — then a private-sector Searle Pharmaceutical executive serving as Reagan's special Middle East envoy. On 19-20 December 1983 Rumsfeld travelled to Baghdad and met Saddam Hussein. The Iraqi-press photographs of Rumsfeld and Saddam shaking hands became the public image of the US-Iraqi alliance. The US cable record of the meeting confirmed Rumsfeld did not raise the chemical-weapons issue with Saddam — despite US intelligence-community awareness of "almost daily" Iraqi chemical-weapons use against Iranian forces.

On 24 March 1984 Rumsfeld returned to Baghdad to meet Iraqi Foreign Minister Tariq Aziz. The Rumsfeld mission per newly declassified 2003 documents: delivering a private US message that "the United States' public criticism of Iraq for using chemical weapons would not derail Washington's attempts to forge a better relationship." The day before Rumsfeld's arrival, Iran had accused Iraq of poisoning 600 Iranian soldiers with mustard gas and tabun.

Roughly 25 days after Rumsfeld's December 1983 trip, Reagan signed a secret order instructing the US administration to do "whatever was necessary and legal" to prevent Iraq losing the war.

In November 1984 the US restored full diplomatic relations with Iraq — broken since 1967.

US support to Iraq ran through multiple channels:

- Roughly $1+ billion in US Commodity Credit Corporation agricultural credits 1983-1988 — economic life-support that freed Iraqi resources for war spending.
- US satellite intelligence sharing: imagery and maps of Iranian troop movements, Iranian logistics facility locations, Iranian air defence details.
- Thousand-page intelligence reports per Howard Teicher (1995 affidavit, the Reagan White House National Security Council Iraq specialist).
- CIA-arranged third-country arms transfers (per Teicher: CIA Director William Casey used the Chilean firm Cardoen to send cluster bombs to Iraq against Iranian human-wave attacks).
- Commerce Department export licences for dual-use chemicals, biological agents (anthrax strains, botulinum), and computer technology — per the 1994 Riegle Report from the US Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs.
- Diplomatic cover at the United Nations: US opposition to UN resolutions condemning Iraqi chemical-weapons use. Iran filed a UN draft resolution after the March 1984 mustard-gas-and-tabun attacks; the US lobbied for a "no decision" stance.

The August 2013 *Foreign Policy* magazine investigation by Shane Harris and Matthew M. Aid — based on declassified CIA documents and interviews — produced the evidentiary record that in 1988 US intelligence officials provided Iraq with the locations of Iranian troops via satellite imagery, fully aware that the Iraqi military would attack those positions with chemical weapons including sarin. The US intelligence-sharing preceded four major Iraqi offensives in early 1988 — including the recapture of the Fao Peninsula in April 1988 — that used mustard gas and sarin. Retired US Air Force Colonel Rick Francona, the 1988 US military attaché in Baghdad, summarised the record: "The Iraqis never told us that they intended to use nerve gas. They didn't have to. We already knew."

## The other side: Iran-Contra

While the Reagan administration was arming Iraq, the same Reagan administration was secretly arming Iran. The Iran-Contra Affair ran from 1985 to 1986 through secret US arms sales to the Iranian government via Israeli intermediaries. Thousands of TOW anti-tank missiles and HAWK anti-aircraft missiles were transferred to Iran in exchange for the release of US hostages held by Hezbollah in Lebanon. The proceeds were illegally funnelled to the Nicaraguan Contras (covered in Chapter 1) in violation of the Boland Amendment.

The Iran-Contra arms transfers were exposed by the Lebanese magazine *Ash-Shiraa* on 3 November 1986. The congressional investigation under the Tower Commission (1987) and the Walsh independent counsel investigation (1986-1993) produced convictions of Oliver North, John Poindexter, Robert McFarlane, Caspar Weinberger, Elliott Abrams, and other Reagan-administration figures. The George H.W. Bush 1992 pardons of all Iran-Contra convicts produced full impunity.

The Henry Kissinger characterisation of the US position on the Iran-Iraq War: "It's a shame they can't both lose."

## Halabja massacre 16 March 1988

The Halabja massacre took place in the Kurdish town of Halabja in northern Iraq, roughly 15 miles from the Iranian border, with a Kurdish population of roughly 60,000. The context ran through the Anfal campaign (covered below) and the Iraqi-Kurdish-Iranian military operations of early 1988.

On 14 March 1988 a combined force of Iranian Pasdaran (IRGC Revolutionary Guards) and Iraqi Kurdish peshmerga militias (predominantly Patriotic Union of Kurdistan, PUK, under Jalal Talabani) captured the entire Halabja district from Iraqi forces during Operation Zafar 7. Many Iraqi troops drowned in Darbandikhan Lake; others fled or were captured.

On the morning and evening of 16 March 1988 Iraqi MiG-23 and Mirage F-1 aircraft began conventional rocket and napalm attacks on Halabja. The conventional bombing was timed to break windows and force civilian populations underground into cellars. The chemical attack followed — Iraqi aircraft dropped multiple chemical bombs containing sulphur mustard, sarin, tabun, and VX nerve agent on Halabja's residential areas. Five-hour attack across the late afternoon and evening of 16 March 1988.

Eyewitness accounts: a smell like sweet apples; sheep and goats falling in the streets; birds dropping from tree limbs; stabbing pain in the eyes, uncontrollable vomiting, tremors, confusion. Smoky clouds of gas hovering over the ground; white powder dusting on streets and rooftops. Residents in cellars suffocated by mustard and nerve gas (heavier than air, settled in basements).

Halabja casualties: roughly 5,000 killed within hours; 10,000+ injured. Roughly 75% of the dead were women and children. The health effects on survivors included cancers, neurological disorders, birth defects, miscarriages, and respiratory ailments persisting to the date of writing.

The Halabja attack is characterised by the Kurdistan Regional Government as "the deadliest chemical weapons attack ever directed at a civilian population" — and as the worst use of chemical weapons against civilians since the 1915-1918 World War I gas attacks.

The aftermath ran through Western diplomatic concealment. The US Defense Intelligence Agency initially attributed the Halabja attack to Iran — disinformation subsequently disproven by overwhelming forensic evidence including the chemical-agent profile (Iranian forces had no VX or tabun) and the Iraqi flight patterns. The British Foreign and Commonwealth Office briefing paper concluded: "We believe it better to maintain a dialogue with others if we want to influence their actions. Punitive measures such as unilateral sanctions would not be effective in changing Iraq's behaviour over chemical weapons, and would damage British interests to no avail." Tony Benn MP characterised the UK response when raising the issue in Parliament: he was told that "Saddam was an ally."

Joost Hiltermann (subsequently of the International Crisis Group) summarised the consequence: "Iraq took the US disinformation about Halabja as another green light...to gather up and methodically kill tens of thousands of Kurds" over the course of the Anfal campaign that continued through September 1988.

## The Anfal genocide February-September 1988

"Anfal" is a Qur'anic term meaning "spoils of war" — the eighth chapter of the Qur'an. The Iraqi Ba'athist military-counter-insurgency campaign under the codename al-Anfal ran against Iraqi Kurds from February to September 1988 in eight stages.

The campaign architecture ran under Ali Hassan al-Majid — Saddam Hussein's cousin, subsequently known as "Chemical Ali." On 18 March 1987 the Iraqi Revolutionary Command Council issued Decision Number 160 granting al-Majid sweeping authority over northern Iraq, including the Kurdistan Region, as head of the Ba'ath Party Northern Bureau. On 3 June 1987 al-Majid signed a proclamation declaring an area containing more than 1,000 Kurdish villages out-of-bounds for the delivery of food and machinery — the deliberate starvation of Iraqi-Kurdish rural areas.

The eight-stage Anfal campaign ran February-September 1988 with roughly 200,000+ Iraqi ground troops, air support including chemical-weapons aircraft, and systematic village destruction. Roughly 4,000-5,000 Kurdish villages were destroyed during the operation. Chemical-weapons attacks on Kurdish villages preceded each Anfal stage to flush civilians into Iraqi army hands.

The Anfal procedure ran through systematic processing of captured Kurdish civilians. Iraqi armed forces transported captured Kurds to transit centres, sorted them by age and sex, and conducted multi-day executions. Mass graves containing tens of thousands of Kurdish civilians have been excavated across northern Iraq from 2003 onward.

Anfal casualties: 50,000-182,000 Kurds killed (Human Rights Watch lower estimate; Iraqi Kurdistan parliament higher estimate). Roughly 100,000 Kurds fled to Turkey during the campaign.

The earlier 1983 disappearance of 8,000 Barzani men aged 8-70 — rounded up from camps and trucked to undisclosed locations, never seen again — was the precursor of Anfal.

The postwar accountings: the 2007 Iraqi High Tribunal characterised Anfal as genocide and convicted al-Majid; the 2010 Iraqi Supreme Criminal Tribunal additionally characterised the Halabja attack as genocide. Al-Majid was executed by hanging on 25 January 2010 after multiple death sentences. International recognitions of the Anfal genocide include the UK House of Commons (28 February 2013), Sweden (December 2012), Norway (2013), South Korea (2013), the Netherlands, and the European Parliament.

The Dutch businessman Frans van Anraat — who had supplied Iraq with thousands of tonnes of chemical-weapons precursors (thiodiglycol for mustard gas) — was convicted on 17 December 2005 by a Dutch court in The Hague and sentenced to 15 years' imprisonment for complicity in war crimes. The van Anraat case is the exception: no US official, no UK official, no French official, and no other Western official has ever been prosecuted for complicity in the Iraqi chemical-weapons programme.

## The Tanker War 1984-1988

The Tanker War ran from 1984 to 1988 as the maritime phase of the Iran-Iraq War in the Persian Gulf. Iraq initiated the tanker attacks in early 1984 by attacking Iran's main oil-export facility at Kharg Island. The Iraqi objective: provoke Iranian retaliation against neutral shipping that would force US intervention against Iran.

On 13 May 1984 Iran first retaliated by attacking a Kuwaiti tanker carrying Iraqi oil. On 16 May 1984 Iran attacked a Saudi tanker in Saudi waters. The Tanker War produced roughly 451 ship attacks: 283 by Iraq, 168 by Iran. The Seawise Giant — the largest ship ever built — was struck by Iraqi Exocet missiles while carrying Iranian crude oil.

The US-Soviet-French naval presence in the Persian Gulf ran from 1984 onward to protect oil trade routes. The escalation ran across 1986-1988.

### USS Stark incident

On 17 May 1987 an Iraqi Mirage F-1 jet fired two French-made Exocet missiles at the USS Stark — a US Navy guided-missile frigate operating in the Persian Gulf. The Iraqi pilot characterised the Stark as an Iranian tanker. The Exocet impact killed 37 US sailors and wounded 21. Saddam apologised; the US response was strategic patience — the US did not retaliate against Iraq and instead blamed the Iran-Iraq War for escalation.

The reversal: the US Joint Chiefs of Staff issued revised rules of engagement giving US warships in the Persian Gulf broader protective authority — the precondition for the 3 July 1988 USS Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air Flight 655.

### Operation Earnest Will

On 20 July 1987 UN Security Council Resolution 598 was adopted unanimously demanding ceasefire. On 24 July 1987 the US Navy launched Operation Earnest Will — the escort of reflagged Kuwaiti oil tankers through the Persian Gulf. The operation was the largest US naval convoy operation since World War II.

The naval architecture ran through joint US-French-British naval operations. The US Navy Seventh Fleet — augmented by US Air Force AWACS radar planes, US Army special-operations helicopters, and US Navy SEAL teams — provided protection of Kuwaiti and other Gulf-state tankers. The Iranian response ran through mine warfare, Boghammar speedboat attacks, and Silkworm anti-ship missile strikes.

On 24 September 1987 US helicopters caught and sank the Iran Ajr mine-laying vessel; nine mines and a logbook recovered implicating Iran in mining international waters. On 15 October 1987 the reflagged US tanker Sea Isle City was struck by an Iranian Silkworm missile near Kuwait City; 17 crewmen and the American captain were wounded. On 18 October 1987 Operation Nimble Archer — US naval retaliation against Iranian Revolutionary Guard oil platforms in the Rostam oil field — destroyed two platforms.

### USS Samuel B. Roberts and Operation Praying Mantis

On 14 April 1988 the USS Samuel B. Roberts — a US Navy frigate — struck an Iranian mine in the Persian Gulf. The damage nearly broke the vessel in two; 10 US sailors injured. On 18 April 1988 the US launched Operation Praying Mantis — the largest US surface naval engagement since World War II. The operation destroyed two Iranian oil platforms; sank Iranian frigate Sahand; severely damaged Iranian frigate Sabalan; destroyed multiple Iranian Boghammar speedboats. The Iranian naval losses produced the Iranian recognition that the US naval intervention had escalated to direct conflict with Iran.

## The USS Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air Flight 655 — 3 July 1988

The 3 July 1988 USS Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air Flight 655 is the signature US atrocity of the naval intervention in the Iran-Iraq War. The attack killed all 290 passengers and crew on the Iran Air Airbus A300 — including 66 children — operating the scheduled Tehran-Bandar Abbas-Dubai route.

The context ran through weeks of pre-shootdown tensions in the Persian Gulf. On the morning of 3 July 1988 the USS Vincennes — a Ticonderoga-class guided-missile cruiser equipped with the Aegis combat system, under the command of Captain William C. Rogers III — engaged Iranian Revolutionary Guard speedboats near the Strait of Hormuz. The USS Vincennes had a reputation as an aggressive ship — Rogers III had been characterised by other US Navy officers as the "Robo-Cruiser." On 3 July 1988 the USS Vincennes — operating in Iranian territorial waters — engaged Iranian speedboats and was simultaneously tracking Iran Air Flight 655.

Iran Air Flight 655 had departed Bandar Abbas International Airport at 10:17 AM local time on the daily scheduled Tehran-Bandar Abbas-Dubai commercial route. The Airbus A300 was operating its civilian IFF transponder (Identification Friend or Foe Mode III) broadcasting commercial-aircraft identification. The aircraft was ascending within the established commercial air corridor over Iranian territorial waters.

The USS Vincennes Aegis combat system characterised the tracking of Iran Air Flight 655 as a descending Iranian F-14 fighter on an attack profile against the USS Vincennes. The characterisation was wrong on every dimension: Iran Air Flight 655 was ascending, not descending; was on a commercial route, not an attack profile; was a wide-body Airbus A300 with hundreds of passengers, not a single-seat F-14 fighter; was broadcasting civilian IFF transponder, not its military IFF.

At roughly 10:24 AM local time the USS Vincennes fired two SM-2MR surface-to-air missiles at Iran Air Flight 655. One missile struck the aircraft. All 290 people on board were killed. Persian-Gulf debris recovery ran for several days.

The USS Vincennes was within Iranian territorial waters at the moment of firing.

The US government's response ran through deception. US officials initially claimed Iran Air Flight 655 had been descending and was outside its normal commercial air corridor. Both claims were disproven by the US Navy investigation report of 28 July 1988 (released in redacted form 19 August 1988). The final US Navy report concluded the Iranian aircraft had been "ascending within the established air route" at a slower speed than reported by the USS Vincennes. The Aegis combat-system errors were characterised by US Navy authorities as "stress...and unconscious distortion of data."

The US response continued the deception:

- Vice President George H.W. Bush represented the US at the UN Security Council and defended the USS Vincennes action as appropriate to the circumstances.
- The US never apologised. Reagan issued a written diplomatic note to Iran "expressing deep regret."
- Captain William C. Rogers III received the Legion of Merit award in April 1990 for "exceptionally meritorious conduct in the performance of outstanding service as commanding officer...from April 1987 to May 1989." The USS Vincennes Air Warfare Coordinator Lt. Cmdr. Scott Lustig also received the Commendation Medal.
- Iran filed a lawsuit against the US at the International Court of Justice on 17 May 1989.
- 1996 ICJ settlement: US paid $61.8 million to victims' families ($131.8 million total to Iran). The US never admitted legal liability for the shootdown.
- The compensation per victim ($213,000) was one-tenth the amount the US had demanded from Iraq for the USS Stark's dead.

The consequence: Iran widely believed the USS Vincennes shootdown was a deliberate US attack. The reading in Tehran was that the shootdown was the signal that the US would join Iraq in the war. Iran accepted UN Security Council Resolution 598 on 18 July 1988 — two weeks after the USS Vincennes shootdown. Khomeini characterised the Iranian acceptance as "drinking the poisoned chalice."

The USS Vincennes shootdown is the foundational atrocity in the US-Iranian relationship — cited continuously by Iranian political-class operators across the Khamenei-Rafsanjani-Khatami-Ahmadinejad-Rouhani-Raisi-Pezeshkian period.

## The "War of the Cities"

The War of the Cities ran across the war but reached peak intensity in 1988. The Iraqi strategic bombing of Iranian cities — Tehran, Tabriz, Mashhad, Isfahan, Shiraz, and others — ran through Scud missile attacks and aircraft strikes. The Iranian retaliation ran through Scud strikes against Baghdad, Mosul, Kirkuk, and Basra.

The 1988 escalation produced thousands of civilian casualties and millions of Tehran residents fleeing to the countryside. The Iranian inability to match Iraqi air superiority — produced by Western and Soviet arms supplies — was the pressure on Iran to accept the ceasefire.

## Ceasefire and the end

On 20 July 1987 UN Security Council Resolution 598 had been adopted unanimously demanding ceasefire. The Iraqi acceptance was conditional on Iranian acceptance. The Iranian rejection ran through 17 July 1988 — until the combined pressure of the Iraqi chemical-weapons attacks (April-July 1988 Fao recapture and four major offensives), the USS Vincennes shootdown (3 July 1988), the economic exhaustion of Iran, the casualty exhaustion of the Iranian military, and the recognition that the US was operating as an effective co-belligerent for Iraq produced the Iranian decision.

On 18 July 1988 Iran formally accepted Resolution 598. Khomeini's public characterisation: "drinking the poisoned chalice."

On 20 August 1988 the ceasefire took effect. The war ended with the status quo ante bellum — the border-and-territory questions unresolved.

On 16 August 1990 — 14 days after Iraq invaded Kuwait — Iran and Iraq signed a formal peace agreement. Saddam restored the 1975 Algiers Agreement border arrangements, withdrew all Iraqi forces from Iranian territory, and conceded all war gains. The reversal ran through Saddam's need to secure his eastern border in advance of the US-led coalition response to the Kuwait invasion.

## Casualties and costs

Iran-Iraq War casualties:

- Total deaths: 1,000,000 to 2,000,000.
- Total combatant deaths both sides: roughly 500,000.
- Iranian deaths: roughly 300,000-500,000.
- Iraqi deaths: roughly 150,000-340,000.
- Civilian deaths: roughly 100,000-200,000 (including roughly 50,000-182,000 Kurds killed in Anfal).
- Total wounded: roughly 1,000,000-1,500,000.
- Iranian troops killed by chemical weapons: roughly 20,000.
- Iranian troops wounded by chemical weapons: roughly 100,000.
- Halabja deaths: roughly 5,000 (with 10,000+ wounded and thousands of subsequent deaths from health complications).
- Roughly 100,000+ Basij volunteers killed in human-wave attacks.

Economic cost: roughly $1.19 trillion combined (Iraq $452 billion + Iran $644 billion in 1988 dollars). Damage to oil infrastructure, agricultural infrastructure, water systems, and industrial facilities across both countries was a generational economic burden.

Iraq emerged from the war with $80+ billion in foreign debt — including $14 billion owed to Kuwait, $25 billion to Saudi Arabia, and multi-billion-dollar debts to the Soviet Union, France, and other Western and Eastern bloc creditors. The Iraqi inability to service that debt produced the 2 August 1990 invasion of Kuwait (covered in Chapter 2).

## Postwar consequences

### Iraq

- $80-billion foreign debt produced the path to the 2 August 1990 Kuwait invasion (Chapter 2).
- Chemical-weapons stockpile produced the UN inspection regime under UNSCOM (1991-1998) and UNMOVIC (2002-2003) — the architecture against which the 2003 Iraq War WMD case ran (Chapter 2).
- Saddam's consolidation, the 1991 Shia uprising (suppressed with multi-thousand deaths), and Kurdish autonomy under no-fly-zone protection (covered in Chapters 2 and 5).
- Postwar Iraqi emergence as the "moderate Sunni Arab" Western ally framework collapsed after 1991.

### Iran

- Consolidation of the Islamic Republic.
- 3 June 1989: Khomeini died. Ali Khamenei (President 1981-1989, formerly Friday-prayer leader in Tehran) became Supreme Leader. The Khamenei rule has continued to the date of writing (covered in the Iran dossier in the regional dossier inventory).
- Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani became President 1989-1997.
- IRGC consolidation as the dominant Iranian political-class force.
- Iranian nuclear-programme acceleration as the response to the chemical-weapons experience.
- "Axis of resistance" architecture: Iranian sponsorship of Hezbollah (Lebanon, founded 1982 during the Israeli invasion of Lebanon and the Iran-Iraq War); Palestinian armed factions; post-2003 Iraqi Shia militias; the Houthi movement in Yemen; the Syrian Assad regime alliance (covered in Chapter 2 and the regional dossier inventory).
- Regional rivalry with Saudi Arabia, Israel, UAE, and the US covered in subsequent chapters.

### Kurds

- Iraqi Kurdish autonomy under the 1991 no-fly-zone protection following the Gulf War and the Saddam-suppressed 1991 Kurdish uprising.
- Founding of the Kurdistan Regional Government in 1992.
- Iraqi-Kurdish political-class consolidation under the Barzani (KDP) and Talabani (PUK) parties.
- Kurdish autonomy embedded in the post-2003 Iraqi Constitution.
- The Kurdish question covered in the regional dossier inventory.

### US institutional record

- The Reagan-administration US-Iraq-arming architecture was fully exposed across the 1991 Gulf War, the 2003 Iraq War, and the subsequent National Security Archive document releases.
- No US official has ever been prosecuted for complicity in the Iraqi chemical-weapons programme.
- The pattern of US arming Iraq, then attacking Iraq, then occupying Iraq, then arming Iraqi militias against ISIS, then withdrawing — became the signature architecture of post-Cold-War US Middle East policy.

## The verdict

The Iran-Iraq War was the forgotten million-dead war of the Cold War endgame. The architecture behind it ran through US, UK, French, Soviet, West German, Italian, and Dutch arms supplies to Iraq; US, Israeli, and other arms supplies to Iran; US-and-Western diplomatic cover for the Iraqi chemical-weapons programme; and the US decision to engineer the eight-year war's continuation to mutual exhaustion.

The mask was the characterisation of the war as a regional sectarian conflict between Sunni Arabs and Shia Persians — a conflict in which the Western position was the Kissinger-characterised "It's a shame they can't both lose."

The actual operation: the industrial-scale production of roughly one million deaths through arms supplies, intelligence sharing, diplomatic cover for chemical-weapons use, and cynicism. The Reagan-administration arming of both sides simultaneously — Iraq through multi-billion-dollar agricultural credits, intelligence sharing, and weapons-supplier facilitation; Iran through Iran-Contra arms transfers — is the case study of the post-1945 US institutional architecture covered across Chapters 1-3.

## Bridge to Chapter 4

The post-1989 Western engagement with Iraq and Iran has run continuously from the Iran-Iraq War foundation. The post-2014 Ukraine war (covered in Chapter 4) runs through partially analogous dynamics: Western arms and intelligence supplies to one belligerent (Ukraine) in a war with a major regional power (Russia), with risks of escalation including nuclear thresholds. The architecture of war prolongation under Western intervention — and the question of whether the war is being prolonged in the interest of Western arms manufacturers and geopolitical positioning, rather than in the interest of the combatants — is the foundation for the Ukraine chapter.

# Part XII Chapter 4: The Ukraine War

## The largest land war in Europe since 1945

Between 24 February 2022 and the date of writing in May 2026 the Russian Federation and Ukraine have fought the largest interstate land war in Europe since World War II. Roughly 400,000-550,000 people have been killed across the combined combatant-and-civilian totals; multi-thousand civilians have been killed in Russian air, missile, drone, and artillery strikes; hundreds of thousands of Russian soldiers have been killed; multi-tens-of-thousands of Ukrainian soldiers have been killed; 6+ million Ukrainian refugees have fled abroad and 6+ million have been internally displaced; 1,897 square miles of Ukrainian territory have been captured by Russia in the last 12 months alone of writing.

The public characterisation of the war was a sudden 2022 Russian aggression against an innocent Western-democratic Ukraine — a straightforward case of authoritarian invasion versus rules-based-international-order defence. The actual operation spans the architecture: the post-1991 NATO expansion eastward despite Russian objections; the February 2014 Maidan Revolution that removed an elected pro-Russian Ukrainian president; the Russian annexation of Crimea and the Donbas war that killed 14,000 before 2022; the Western military training of Ukrainian forces 2014-2022; the Western institutional rejection of the Putin demands of December 2021; the 24 February 2022 Russian full-scale invasion; the Western institutional decision to arm Ukraine to strategic-attrition exhaustion of Russia rather than negotiate; and the question of whose interests have been served by the prolongation of the war.

The record documents the war in dependency order: the context; the Maidan Revolution and Crimea annexation 2013-2014; the Donbas war 2014-2022; the 24 February 2022 invasion; the combat phases through May 2026; the Bucha and Mariupol atrocities; the Wagner Group mutiny and Prigozhin death; the Nord Stream pipeline sabotage; the 2024 Kursk incursion; the 2025 Trump-administration policy shift; the current battlefield situation; the casualty accounting; and the architecture of war prolongation.

## The institutional context

Ukraine — meaning "borderland" in the Slavic etymology — has operated for periods as a contested zone between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, the Ottoman Empire, the Cossack Hetmanate, and the Russian Empire. The 1654 Treaty of Pereyaslav produced the Cossack-Russian alliance that was the Russian institutional foundation for incorporating eastern Ukraine. The Russian and Soviet rule produced the Russification of eastern and southern Ukraine; the Ukrainian-language suppression; the Stalinist 1932-1933 Holodomor famine that killed roughly 3.9 million Ukrainians; and the Soviet apparatus across 1922-1991.

On 24 August 1991 Ukraine declared independence as the Soviet Union collapsed. The 1 December 1991 referendum produced 92.3% in favour of independence with majority support across every region of Ukraine — including Crimea (54%) and the Donbas regions.

In 1994 Ukraine signed the Budapest Memorandum on Security Assurances. Ukraine — which had inherited the world's third-largest nuclear-weapons stockpile from the Soviet Union (roughly 1,900 strategic warheads plus tactical weapons) — surrendered the entire nuclear stockpile to Russia for dismantlement in exchange for security assurances from the US, UK, and Russia. The Budapest Memorandum guaranteed Ukraine's "existing borders" and the commitment to "refrain from the threat or use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence of Ukraine." Russia signed.

The post-1991 Ukrainian political architecture operated through alternating pro-Western and pro-Russian presidencies: Leonid Kravchuk (1991-1994); Leonid Kuchma (1994-2005); Viktor Yushchenko (2005-2010) elected after the 2004 Orange Revolution that overturned the falsified election win of Viktor Yanukovych; Viktor Yanukovych (2010-2014); Petro Poroshenko (2014-2019); Volodymyr Zelensky (2019-present).

The NATO eastward expansion operated continuously across the 1991-2024 period despite Russian objections. The 1990 James Baker assurance to Mikhail Gorbachev that NATO would not move "one inch eastward" was disputed by the US institutional position that the assurance had referred only to East Germany and had been verbal rather than treaty-bound. The NATO expansion: 1999 (Czech Republic, Hungary, Poland); 2004 (Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia, Slovenia); 2009 (Albania, Croatia); 2017 (Montenegro); 2020 (North Macedonia); 2023 (Finland); 2024 (Sweden).

The 2008 NATO Bucharest Summit declared that Ukraine and Georgia "will become members of NATO" — without granting either a Membership Action Plan. Russian institutional responses operated through the August 2008 Russo-Georgian War (Russia recognised South Ossetia and Abkhazia as independent states); the Russian institutional characterisation of the NATO expansion as an existential threat; and the Putin institutional rhetoric across the 2007 Munich Security Conference speech, the 2014 Crimea annexation, and the 2022 invasion.

The US institutional position operated through mixed characterisations. The Western political-class characterisation operated as: NATO expansion was a sovereign choice of post-Soviet states, not Western projection; Russia had no veto over alliance membership; the Russian objections were institutional pretexts for the Putin authoritarian consolidation. The realist political-class characterisation — operated by John Mearsheimer, Stephen Walt, George Kennan, Henry Kissinger, William Burns (then-US Ambassador to Moscow, in the 2008 "Nyet means nyet" cable), and others — operated as: NATO expansion was an institutional miscalculation; Russia had credible security concerns; the Western institutional rejection of those concerns produced the war.

Both characterisations operate against the structural record. The question of which characterisation is correct is operated across the conversations in Western political-class architectures. The fact is that NATO expanded; Russia objected; and the war operated to the benefit of US arms manufacturers, the US apparatus for weakening Russia (in 2019 RAND Corporation report "Extending Russia"), and the US-EU geopolitical positioning against the Russia-China alignment.

## The Maidan Revolution 2013-2014

The institutional precipitant of the multi-year war was the February 2014 Revolution of Dignity (Euromaidan), which removed the elected pro-Russian president Viktor Yanukovych.

On 21 November 2013 — after Yanukovych's 9 November meeting with Putin — the Yanukovych government suspended preparations for the EU Association Agreement that Ukraine had been negotiating since 2007. The institutional reversal had operated through Russian pressure including natural-gas price discounts and the offer of $15 billion in Ukrainian bonds.

Mass protests began in Kyiv's Independence Square (Maidan Nezalezhnosti) on 21 November 2013 and operated continuously for three months. The Euromaidan was the largest democratic mass movement in Europe since 1989. Multi-hundred-thousand demonstrators participated; on 8 December 2013 800,000 people gathered in Kyiv and the Lenin statue was toppled.

The escalation operated through Berkut riot-police crackdowns; anti-protest laws passed by show of hands on 16 January 2014; the first protester deaths on 22 January 2014. On 18-20 February 2014 — during the Sochi Winter Olympics — the Berkut snipers shot dead roughly 100 protesters across the three-day institutional violence. The Heavenly Hundred became the Ukrainian institutional martyrs.

The question of who fired the Maidan-protester-killing shots — Berkut snipers under Yanukovych orders per the Ukrainian institutional position; or multi-year-disputed third parties per the contested ballistics analysis — operates across Ukrainian-court proceedings.

On 21 February 2014 EU foreign ministers from Germany, France, and Poland brokered an agreement between Yanukovych and the Maidan opposition. The night Yanukovych fled Kyiv. On 22 February 2014 the Verkhovna Rada voted 328-0 to remove Yanukovych and restore the 2004 Constitution. Oleksandr Turchynov became interim president; Arseniy Yatsenyuk became prime minister. New elections were called for 25 May 2014.

The characterisation of the Maidan Revolution operates through contested institutional accounts. The Russian political-class characterisation operated as: a Western-organised "coup" with US institutional involvement including the Victoria Nuland-Geoffrey Pyatt phone call leaked February 2014 (Nuland: "Yats is the guy who's got the economic experience..." and "Fuck the EU"). The Western political-class characterisation operated as: a popular democratic revolution against a corrupt pro-Russian authoritarian government, with US support having been democracy-promotion rather than coup-making.

The fact: Yanukovych was the elected president of Ukraine. He was removed through street protests. The Western political-class characterisation of the Maidan as a democratic revolution against the Russian political-class characterisation as a Western coup operates across contested institutional ground.

## Crimea annexation March 2014

The Russian institutional response operated immediately. On 27 February 2014 Russian soldiers in unmarked uniforms — the "little green men" — seized the Crimean Parliament and Council of Ministers buildings in Simferopol. Russian special forces operated takeovers of Crimean government buildings, blockades of Ukrainian military bases, and the installation of a pro-Russian Crimean government.

Putin denied Russian military involvement. He admitted in an April 2014 television interview that the "little green men" had been Russian troops.

On 16 March 2014 a Crimean referendum was held under Russian military occupation. The institutional result — characterised by Russia as a 97% vote in favour of joining Russia — was characterised by the US, EU, UN, and most international architecture as illegitimate. On 18 March 2014 Putin signed the bill annexing Crimea into the Russian Federation. Sevastopol was reorganised as a Russian federal city; Crimea was reorganised as a Russian republic.

On 27 March 2014 UN General Assembly Resolution 68/262 affirmed the "territorial integrity of Ukraine within its internationally recognised borders." The vote: 100 in favour, 11 against, 58 abstentions. The position of the UN, the US, the EU, and most architectures has continued through the date of writing.

The institutional Russian rationale operated through characterisations: connection between Russia and Crimea since the 1783 Catherine the Great annexation from the Ottoman Empire; the 1954 Khrushchev administrative transfer of Crimea from Russian SFSR to Ukrainian SSR within the Soviet Union; the Sevastopol Black Sea Fleet base; the Russian-speaking majority population. The institutional Ukrainian response operated through characterisations: Ukraine's 1991 referendum had been won in Crimea (54%); the Budapest Memorandum had guaranteed Ukraine's borders; the 1997 Russian-Ukrainian Treaty had reaffirmed Russian recognition of Ukraine's borders including Crimea.

The annexation produced the governing rupture in the Russian-Western institutional relationship. Western sanctions operated continuously from March 2014 onward. NATO suspended "practical civilian and military cooperation" with Russia. The Council of Europe suspended Russia's voting rights. The G8 expelled Russia (became G7).

## The Donbas war 2014-2022

The Crimea annexation, pro-Russian protests began in eastern Ukrainian cities including Donetsk, Luhansk, and Kharkiv. On 7 April 2014 separatists declared the Donetsk People's Republic (DPR); the Luhansk People's Republic (LPR) followed. The apparatus operated through Russian institutional support — weapons, training, intelligence, command-and-control — paired with denial of direct Russian military presence.

On 11 May 2014 the DPR and LPR held independence referendums producing institutional claims of overwhelming yes votes. The institutional results were not recognised by Ukraine, the US, the EU, or most international apparatuss.

The Ukrainian government launched the "Anti-Terrorist Operation" (ATO) in April 2014 to recapture Donbas. The Ukrainian architecture deployed the post-Maidan reorganised Ukrainian Armed Forces alongside volunteer militias — including the Azov Battalion (founded in May 2014 and integrated into into the Ukrainian National Guard, with contested institutional characterisations regarding far-right elements) and the Right Sector (Pravy Sektor). The Russian political-class characterisation operated through "denazification" rhetoric drawing on the presence of these far-right elements.

On 17 July 2014 Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17 — operating the Amsterdam-Kuala Lumpur route — was shot down over eastern Ukraine. All 298 people on board were killed including 193 Dutch citizens, 38 Australians, 10 Britons. The Dutch-led Joint Investigation Team concluded the MH17 had been struck by a Buk surface-to-air missile fired from the Russian 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade launcher operating in Russia-backed-separatist-held territory. The Russian institutional denial operated through multiple competing alternative narratives. The 2022 Dutch court convicted three defendants in absentia for murder.

The August 2014 Russian institutional escalation produced direct-Russian-military operations in eastern Ukraine — the Battle of Ilovaisk (August-September 2014) produced Ukrainian-encirclement disaster with roughly 360 Ukrainian dead. The Battle of Donetsk Airport (May 2014 - January 2015) produced Ukrainian institutional resistance under the "cyborgs" characterisation.

The 5 September 2014 Minsk I Agreement was signed by Russia, Ukraine, France, and Germany. The institutional ceasefire framework failed within weeks. On 12 February 2015 the Minsk II Agreement was signed under the Normandy Format (Putin, Poroshenko, Merkel, Hollande) after the Battle of Debaltseve. The Minsk II framework — calling for ceasefire, constitutional reforms in Ukraine, special status for Donbas regions, and border restoration to Ukraine — was never fully implemented. The Russian and Ukrainian political-class characterisations of who had failed to implement Minsk II operated through contested institutional ground. In December 2022 Angela Merkel characterised the Minsk II institutional purpose as having given Ukraine "time to get stronger" against an inevitable Russian attack — confirming Russian political-class characterisations of the Minsk process as a Western strategic-stalling architecture.

The Donbas war operated continuously from April 2014 to 24 February 2022 with low-intensity ceasefire violations. The UN OHCHR 14,000+ deaths across the period — including roughly 3,400 Ukrainian military, roughly 5,700 separatist fighters, and roughly 3,400 civilians.

The US apparatus operated training of Ukrainian forces. From 2015 onward US, UK, Canadian, and Polish forces operated training programmes at the Yavoriv Combat Training Centre and other facilities. US security assistance to Ukraine 2014-2022 totalled roughly $2.7 billion. The Ukrainian institutional military transformation operated from the post-Soviet conscript force to a NATO-trained professional military by the 2022 invasion.

## The pre-invasion buildup 2021-2022

In March-April 2021 Russia conducted the first major military buildup along the Ukrainian border — roughly 100,000 troops. De-escalation followed.

In October-November 2021 Russia conducted the second major military buildup. By December 2021 roughly 100,000+ Russian troops were positioned along the Ukrainian border, in Belarus, and in occupied Crimea.

On 17 December 2021 Putin issued multi-page institutional demands to the US and NATO: legally-binding guarantees that Ukraine would never join NATO; withdrawal of NATO forces from post-1997 NATO member states; revision of the post-Cold-War apparatus in Europe. The NATO institutional response operated through refusal — NATO would not concede a veto power over alliance membership; NATO would not withdraw from post-1997 members.

The January-February 2022 institutional diplomacy operated through US, French, and German meetings with Putin. The Western institutional warnings that Russia would invade were characterised by Ukrainian President Zelensky as overstated; the Russian institutional position characterised the invasion warnings as Western "hysteria."

On 21 February 2022 Putin recognised the independence of the DPR and LPR. He ordered Russian troops into eastern Ukraine as "peacekeepers."

On 22 February 2022 German Chancellor Olaf Scholz suspended Nord Stream 2 certification.

On 24 February 2022 at roughly 04:00 local time Russia launched the full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

## The 24 February 2022 invasion

Putin's 24 February 2022 address characterised the military operation as a "special military operation" with institutional objectives of "denazification" and "demilitarisation" of Ukraine. The Russian institutional rhetoric operated through characterisations of Ukrainian architecture as "Nazi" — drawing on the presence of far-right elements within the Ukrainian Armed Forces (Azov, Right Sector) and the characterisation of the Bandera-aligned Ukrainian nationalist history. The characterisation operated as institutional propaganda against the fact that Ukrainian President Zelensky was Jewish and the Ukrainian government had operated under Jewish President; the far-right elements within the Ukrainian military operated at multi-percent institutional level rather than as the dominant institutional architecture.

The Russian military objectives operated through multi-axis invasion:
- North from Belarus toward Kyiv — the decapitation operation aimed at capturing Kyiv within 72 hours and installing a puppet government.
- Northeast through Sumy and Kharkiv — the institutional supporting axis.
- East in Donbas — the structural consolidation of Russian-backed-separatist territory.
- South from Crimea — the land-bridge operation toward Mariupol and Kherson.

The Kyiv assault failed catastrophically. The Antonov Airport (Hostomel) air assault on the first day of the invasion produced Russian VDV (Airborne) casualties without securing the governing foothold needed for the Kyiv assault. The 40-mile Russian armoured column north of Kyiv stalled in traffic and was destroyed by Ukrainian artillery, drones, and Javelin anti-tank missiles. By the end of March 2022 Russian forces had withdrawn from Kyiv Oblast.

The Russian institutional embarrassment operated through multi-thousand casualties, hundreds of captured Russian armoured vehicles, and the multi-day-long initial Russian institutional underestimation of Ukrainian resistance.

The Western institutional response operated through massive arms transfers, intelligence sharing, and economic sanctions. On 27 February 2022 the EU announced first weapons supplies (the precedent of EU funding of arms supplies to a third country in active war). The SWIFT financial-messaging ban on selected Russian banks. The freezing of roughly $300 billion in Russian central bank reserves held in Western financial institutions.

## The Bucha massacre April 2022

Russian forces occupied Bucha in Kyiv Oblast from 27 February to 31 March 2022. Following the Russian withdrawal in early April 2022 mass civilian killings were discovered.

Local authorities recovered 458 bodies including 9 children under the age of 18. Of those, 419 were killed with weapons; 39 appeared to have died of natural causes possibly related to the occupation. Many victims were found with hands tied behind their backs; torture marks; gunshot wounds to the head.

Mass graves were excavated. Evidence of executions, sexual violence, and systematic atrocities was assembled by the Ukrainian institutional investigations and the International Criminal Court.

The Russian institutional response operated through denials and characterisations of the atrocities as Ukrainian "stagings." The German foreign intelligence service (BND) intercepted Russian troop communications, "completely refuting" the Russian denials. Wagner Group forces were reportedly involved.

Similar atrocities were found across Russian-occupied territories: in Izium (Kharkiv Oblast, September 2022) Ukrainian forces uncovered a mass grave of roughly 440 bodies, many showing signs of torture, execution, and mutilation; in Borodyanka Russian airstrikes flattened residential blocks; in Kherson and Zaporizhzhia torture, electric shocks, beatings, and psychological abuse were documented in eyewitness testimony.

President Zelensky characterised the Bucha massacre as genocide. President Biden characterised Putin's actions as a "war crime." On 17 March 2023 the International Criminal Court issued an arrest warrant for Putin and Russian Children's Commissioner Maria Lvova-Belova for the unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children to Russia.

## The Mariupol siege

The siege of Mariupol operated from 24 February to 20 May 2022 — 86 days. The Russian siege of the Sea of Azov port city of roughly 430,000 prewar population produced the signature institutional atrocity of the war.

Russian forces operated relentless bombing of civilian infrastructure. On 9 March 2022 Russian airstrikes hit the Mariupol maternity hospital; images of pregnant women injured by the strike produced international condemnation. On 16 March 2022 a Russian airstrike hit the Mariupol Drama Theatre where hundreds of civilians had been sheltering. The word "ДЕТИ" (Russian for "CHILDREN") had been painted in large letters outside the theatre to indicate civilian shelter. Estimates of the Drama Theatre bombing dead range from 300 to 600+.

The siege reduced Mariupol to rubble. Thousands of civilians were killed. The "filtration camps" operated by Russian forces produced multi-thousand Mariupol residents forcibly deported to Russia.

The final Ukrainian defenders — including the Azov Battalion — operated resistance at the Azovstal steelworks. On 16 May 2022 the final defenders were ordered by the Ukrainian government to surrender. The Russian institutional treatment of the Azovstal prisoners produced prisoner-of-war controversies; the 29 July 2022 Olenivka prison massacre killed roughly 50 Ukrainian POWs in Russian custody.

On 20 May 2022 Mariupol fell to Russian forces. The Russian institutional reconstruction of Mariupol operated through propaganda; the city has remained under Russian control through the date of writing.

## The counter-offensives 2022-2023

The post-Kyiv-withdrawal Russian apparatus refocused on Donbas operations. The April-July 2022 Russian institutional advance produced gains in Luhansk Oblast — the fall of Lysychansk on 3 July 2022 gave Russia control of roughly 95% of Luhansk Oblast.

In April 2022 the Russian cruiser Moskva — the flagship of the Black Sea Fleet — was sunk by Ukrainian Neptune anti-ship missiles. The Ukrainian institutional naval and drone operations produced Russian Black Sea Fleet withdrawal from western Black Sea operations; by 2024 most of the Russian Black Sea Fleet had been withdrawn from Sevastopol to Russian ports.

The September 2022 Ukrainian Kharkiv counter-offensive produced institutional success: roughly 12,000 square kilometres recaptured in operations. A Russian institutional rout produced abandonment of weapons and equipment. The September 2022 Russian institutional response: Putin announced mass mobilisation on 21 September 2022, calling up 300,000 reservists. Mass emigration of military-age Russian men to Kazakhstan, Georgia, Armenia, and Turkey. Protests in Russian cities.

In November 2022 Ukrainian forces conducted the Kherson counter-offensive. Russian forces withdrew across the Dnipro River; the Ukrainian recapture of Kherson city operated on 11 November 2022.

In September 2022 Russia conducted sham referendums in Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, and Kherson oblasts and annexation of all four despite Russian non-control of significant portions of each. The 30 September 2022 Putin signing-ceremony characterised the annexation as "forever."

The 17 March 2023 International Criminal Court arrest warrant against Putin was the first against a sitting permanent UN Security Council member head of state. Russia is not party to the Rome Statute; the structural warrant operates against the possibility of Putin travel to ICC-state-party countries.

The 26 September 2022 Nord Stream pipeline sabotage produced investigation across Sweden, Denmark, and Germany. Four explosions at the Nord Stream 1 and Nord Stream 2 pipelines in the Baltic Sea rendered three of the four pipelines inoperable. The investigations have produced competing characterisations: the February 2023 Seymour Hersh report alleged Biden-administration operation via Norwegian Navy divers; the German investigation produced arrest warrants for Ukrainian nationals; the February 2026 Der Spiegel report characterised the operation as approved by then-Ukrainian commander-in-chief Valerii Zaluzhny without Zelensky's knowledge; Zelensky has denied Ukrainian state involvement. The institutional fact: the Nord Stream sabotage destroyed the European energy-relationship-with-Russia apparatus and the European dependence on US-supplied LNG.

The August 2022 - May 2023 Battle of Bakhmut produced WWI-style trench-and-artillery war. The Wagner Group under Yevgeny Prigozhin operated "human wave" assault tactics using multi-tens-of-thousands of recruited Russian prison-inmate-convicts. Wagner Group deaths in Bakhmut: roughly 20,000 (per Prigozhin May 2023 statement). On 9 May 2023 Russian forces claimed control of Bakhmut.

The 4-9 June 2023 Ukrainian summer counter-offensive — armed with Western Leopard 2 tanks, Bradley IFVs, and Western training — failed to achieve operational breakthroughs against the Russian defensive layered fortifications across the Surovikin Line. The military failure was the turning point: Russian institutional position shifted from defensive to offensive; Western institutional confidence in Ukrainian capacity to recapture occupied territory declined.

On 6 June 2023 the Kakhovka Dam in Kherson Oblast collapsed. Multi-million-cubic-metre flooding inundated downstream Ukrainian and Russian-occupied territory. Widely-attributed to Russian sabotage; Russian-attributed to Ukrainian strikes.

## The Wagner mutiny June 2023

On 23-24 June 2023 Wagner Group founder Yevgeny Prigozhin launched the armed rebellion against the Russian Ministry of Defence under Sergei Shoigu and Chief of General Staff Valery Gerasimov. The Prigozhin institutional grievances had operated across ammunition shortages, characterisations of Russian Defence Ministry incompetence, and the Prigozhin institutional rivalry with the Russian architecture.

The Wagner mutiny operated through seizure of Rostov-on-Don (headquarters of Russian Southern Military District) and march toward Moscow via Voronezh. Wagner forces shot down at least 6 Russian military helicopters and 1 Il-22M command-and-control aircraft, killing 13 Russian aircrew.

On 24 June 2023 Belarusian President Lukashenko mediated the agreement. Prigozhin agreed to halt the advance in exchange for amnesty and exile to Belarus. The Wagner Group apparatus operated through transitional Russian Ministry of Defence integration.

On 23 August 2023 — 60 days after the mutiny — Prigozhin and seven other Wagner figures were killed in an Embraer Legacy 600 plane crash near Tver. The Russian institutional position characterised the crash as an accident; the Western political-class characterisation operated as a Kremlin-ordered assassination.

## 2024: Kursk and grinding attrition

Across 2024 the grinding attritional war continued. Russian forces operated slow advances in eastern Ukraine, capturing Avdiivka in February 2024 after months of fighting.

On 6 August 2024 Ukraine launched the Kursk offensive — the first foreign occupation of Russian territory since World War II per the BBC characterisation. Multi-thousand Ukrainian forces operated territory captures in Kursk Oblast; by 15 August Sudzha (Kursk Oblast) had been captured. By the end of the first week Ukraine claimed roughly 1,000 square kilometres and 28 settlements.

The Kursk operation diverted multi-tens-of-thousands of Russian troops from the Donbas frontline. The Russian institutional response operated through deployment of roughly 50,000 Russian troops plus the deployment of roughly 10,000+ North Korean troops from October 2024 onward.

The North Korean troop deployment — under June 2024 Russian-North-Korean "Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty" — was the governing first deployment of North Korean troops in foreign combat. North Korean casualties operated at high levels; in February 2025, North Korean troops were withdrawn after the decimation.

By the end of 2024 Russian forces had recaptured roughly half of the Kursk territory. By 11 March 2025 most Ukrainian forces had retreated from Kursk under Russian counterattack.

On 17 December 2024 Lt. Gen. Igor Kirillov — the head of Russian NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) Protection Troops, who had directed Russian chemical-weapons-related institutional positions — was killed by a bomb in Moscow. The Ukrainian SBU (Security Service) claimed responsibility.

## 2025: Trump administration policy shift

On 5 November 2024 Donald Trump won the US presidential election against Kamala Harris. The question of US military and financial support for Ukraine was the signature institutional uncertainty.

On 20 January 2025 Trump's second inauguration. The Trump-administration institutional posture operated through characterisations: the war must end; Ukraine must accept territorial concessions; Putin and Trump should negotiate directly; Zelensky was "a dictator" (Trump characterisation, February 2025); Ukraine should pay for US aid through minerals deal.

On 28 February 2025 Trump and Zelensky operated the Oval Office confrontation with Vice President JD Vance present. A multi-minute live-broadcast escalation produced Ukrainian institutional embarrassment and US institutional escalation.

Early March 2025 the Trump administration paused US military aid and intelligence sharing with Ukraine. The Russian military operated advances in eastern Ukraine and the Kursk recapture. Zelensky characterized of the war as requiring "just peace" rather than military victory.

In April 2025 Lt. Gen. Yaroslav Moskalik — an officer in the Russian General Staff's Main Operational Directorate — was killed by a car bomb in a Moscow suburb. In December 2025 Lt. Gen. Fanil Sarvarov — head of the Operational Training Directorate of the Russian General Staff — was killed in a Moscow car bomb.

The ACLED 2025 figure: roughly 78,000 people killed in Ukraine across 2025 — the highest single-conflict death toll globally. December 2025 Russian institutional claims of control of Kupyansk produced a visit by Zelensky to the city.

## 2026 events to the date of writing

The architecture has operated through escalating Russian advance and Ukrainian attrition.

On 31 January 2026 OHCHR recorded 56,550 civilian casualties since 24 February 2022: 15,172 killed and 41,378 injured. OHCHR characterised the real numbers as higher.

February 2026 Russian Ministry of Defence claimed Ukrainian military casualties exceeded 1.5 million. The *Telegraph* report: Russia losing 40,000 soldiers per month since November 2025 while recruiting roughly 35,000; total Russian casualties exceeded 1.25 million (more than US WWII casualties).

On 10 March 2026 Zelensky and Ukrainian military intelligence Lt. Gen. Oleh Ivashchenko said per captured Russian documents 1,315,000 Russian soldiers had been killed or wounded since 24 February 2022.

On 31 March 2026 Lt. Gen. Alexander Otroschenko — commander of the mixed aviation corps of the Russian Northern Fleet — was killed in an An-26 transport aircraft crash in Crimea.

By end of March 2026 OHCHR had verified 58,930 civilian casualties since 24 February 2022 (roughly 15,578 killed; 43,352 injured). Real numbers characterised as higher.

In early April 2026 Russia claimed full control of Luhansk Oblast — though only roughly 0.2% of the oblast's prewar population remained in Russian-controlled areas (the multi-million-person displacement having operated continuously).

On 3 April 2026 Zelensky said Russia had suffered its highest single-month losses since the invasion in March 2026 — over 35,000 killed or wounded Russian soldiers.

On 10-12 April 2026 an Easter ceasefire was announced. Within 48 hours of taking effect it had been violated 2,299 times — including 747 attack drone strikes and 1,045 FPV drone strikes (per Ukraine Armed Forces).

Across 4-8 May 2026 Russian strikes killed 73+ civilians and injured 400+ across Zaporizhia, Kramatorsk, Dnipro, Merefa, and Sumy — the deadliest civilian-strike week of 2026.

On 9-11 May 2026 a Trump-mediated three-day Victory Day ceasefire took effect; it was violated.

On 9 May 2026 Ukrainian General Staff figures of Russian losses since 24 February 2022: 1,340,270 troops; 11,920 tanks; 24,541 armoured vehicles; 95,252 vehicles and fuel tanks; 41,712 artillery systems; 1,780 multiple launch rocket systems; 1,371 air defence systems; 435 aircraft; 352 helicopters; 281,208 drones; 33 ships and boats; 2 submarines.

On 9 May 2026 the Mediazona/Meduza/BBC Russian published estimate (based on Russian Probate Registry data): 352,000 Russian men aged 18-59 had died since the full-scale invasion. Of those, 217,808 names had been verified including 7,094 officers, 27,475 Motorized Rifle Troops, 5,135 VDV Airborne. Prisoner-recruits represented roughly 10.7% of deaths.

May 2026: Russia had captured roughly 1,897 square miles over the past 12 months; territorial momentum had slowed sharply — only 17 square miles changed hands in the four weeks from 10 March to 7 April 2026 per the Russia Matters War Report Card. Ukrainian forces had retaken roughly 480 square kilometres in southern regions since January and roughly 180 square kilometres around Kupyansk in late 2025. Ukrainian long-range drone strikes had reduced Russian oil export capacity by roughly 40%. Every Ukrainian power plant had been damaged; electricity generation had fallen from 33.7 GW prewar to roughly 14 GW.

On 16 May 2026 the US waiver permitting Russian oil purchases stranded at sea expired without renewal.

## Casualty synthesis

The war casualties through May 2026:

**Russian military**:
- Independent estimate (Mediazona/Meduza, May 2026, Probate Registry): roughly 352,000 killed.
- Ukrainian General Staff (9 May 2026): 1,340,270 total casualties.
- Western estimates (CSIS January 2026, *Telegraph* February 2026): 1.25 million+ casualties; 2-2.5x Ukrainian casualties.
- Total Russian killed: probably in range 200,000-400,000.

**Ukrainian military**:
- Zelensky (December 2024): 43,000 killed; 370,000 injured.
- UALosses project (April 2026): 91,559 named killed + 95,165 missing = 186,724.
- Independent estimates (early 2026): 250,000-300,000 total casualties.
- Russian MoD claim (February 2026): 1,500,000 (significantly inflated).
- Total Ukrainian killed: probably in range 75,000-150,000.

**Civilians**:
- UN OHCHR verified (end March 2026): roughly 15,578 killed; 43,352 injured.
- Real numbers characterised as higher; independent estimates 20,000-40,000+ civilian deaths.

**Combined**: roughly 400,000-550,000 deaths across the multi-year combined combatant-and-civilian totals.

**Displacement**: roughly 6+ million Ukrainian refugees in Europe; 6+ million internally displaced.

**ACLED 2025 alone**: roughly 78,000 killed in Ukraine — the highest single-conflict death toll globally for 2025.

**Russian generals killed in covert operations 2024-2026**: Lt. Gen. Igor Kirillov (December 2024 Moscow bomb); Lt. Gen. Yaroslav Moskalik (April 2025 Moscow car bomb); Lt. Gen. Fanil Sarvarov (December 2025 Moscow car bomb); Lt. Gen. Alexander Otroschenko (March 2026 An-26 crash Crimea). Maj. Gen. Andrei Sukhovetsky and Maj. Gen. Vladimir Frolov killed in opening weeks; Maj. Gen. Roman Kutuzov killed June 2022.

## Western military support

The Western military and financial support to Ukraine 2022-2026:

- US aid authorised: roughly $175+ billion across multi-year supplemental appropriations.
- EU and member states' total aid: roughly €130+ billion.
- UK: roughly £12+ billion.

Major weapon systems transferred 2022-2026: Javelin and NLAW anti-tank; Stinger MANPADS; HIMARS multiple rocket launchers; M777 and PzH 2000 howitzers; Caesar self-propelled artillery; Patriot air defence; Leopard 2, Challenger 2, and M1 Abrams tanks; F-16 fighters (2024 onward); ATACMS long-range missiles (2023-2024); Storm Shadow / SCALP cruise missiles; Bradley and Stryker IFVs; multi-thousand drones; Western intelligence, satellite, electronic warfare, and training support.

The question of who has benefited from the war operates across architecture. The US arms industry — Lockheed Martin, Raytheon (RTX), General Dynamics, Northrop Grumman, Boeing — has operated multi-hundred-billion-dollar contracts under the Ukraine aid appropriations. The European arms industry has undergone a re-expansion after the post-Cold-War contraction. The US natural-gas industry has operated multi-billion-dollar replacement of the Russian gas supplies to Europe — the reversal that the Nord Stream sabotage produced.

## Nuclear thresholds and escalation

The question of nuclear escalation has operated continuously. The Putin institutional rhetoric has produced multi-occasion characterisations of Russian willingness to use nuclear weapons. The Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant — Europe's largest, captured by Russian forces in March 2022 — has produced IAEA safety concerns. The Russian "tactical nuclear weapon" rhetoric has been characterised by Western political-class operators as institutional bluff; the characterisation operates against the uncertainty.

The "doomsday clock" Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists set to 89 seconds to midnight in 2025 — the closest setting since the institutional founding in 1947. The institutional rationale: Ukraine war, Middle East war, AI, climate.

## The summary verdict

The Ukraine war is the signature institutional war of the post-2022 period. The combined combatant-and-civilian death toll of roughly 400,000-550,000 across four years operates at annual rates comparable to the 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq War (Chapter 3) and the 1955-1975 Vietnam War (Chapter 1).

The war was presented as a sudden 2022 Russian aggression against an innocent Western-democratic Ukraine. The actual architecture involves: NATO expansion against Russian objections; February 2014 Maidan Revolution removing an elected pro-Russian president; Russian annexation of Crimea; Donbas war and Western training of Ukrainian forces; Russian institutional warnings of red lines that were crossed; 24 February 2022 Russian institutional escalation to full invasion; Western institutional decision to arm Ukraine to strategic-attrition exhaustion of Russia rather than negotiate a settlement; US arms industry, European arms industry, and US LNG industry's institutional benefit from war prolongation.

The question — operated across multiple political-class characterisations from John Mearsheimer, Stephen Walt, Henry Kissinger, William Burns, Jeffrey Sachs, Pope Francis, the UN Secretary-General António Guterres, the Brazilian President Lula da Silva, the Chinese institutional position, the Indian institutional position, the African Union institutional positions, and others — is whether the war has been prolonged in the interest of the Ukrainian people. The answer per the casualty figures, the territorial outcomes, the demographic collapse of Ukraine (the prewar population of roughly 41 million has declined to roughly 28-32 million through death, emigration, and occupation), the destruction of the Ukrainian power grid, and the absence of progress toward territorial restoration — is contested and operated against the structural fact that the war continues.

The institutional alternative — characterised by realist political-class operators — was a negotiated settlement involving Ukrainian neutrality, acceptance of limited Russian-controlled territory, and the prevention of the war prolongation. The rejection of that institutional alternative by the Western political-class architecture — and the Russian political-class architecture — has operated continuously. The institutional cost has operated through the casualty figures, the displacement figures, and the nuclear-threshold uncertainties.

## Bridge to Chapter 5

The Ukraine war is contested institutional ground. The Gaza war — operated by the State of Israel against the Palestinian population of the Gaza Strip from 7 October 2023 onward, characterised by the International Court of Justice as a plausible case of genocide, characterised by the International Criminal Court arrest warrants against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and former Defence Minister Yoav Gallant — operates without contested ground. Chapter 5 documents the Gaza chapter.
