# Part XII Chapter 5: The Gaza Genocide

## The most extreme genocide in human history

Between 7 October 2023 and the date of writing in May 2026 the State of Israel — armed, financed, and politically protected by the United States; tolerated by the United Kingdom, Germany, France, Italy, Canada, Australia, and most of the Western political-class architecture — has conducted military operations against the Palestinian population of the Gaza Strip. The International Court of Justice has characterised them as plausibly violating the 1948 Genocide Convention. The International Criminal Court has characterised them as the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare, intentionally directing attacks against the civilian population, and the crimes against humanity of murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts. The UN Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory has characterised them as a case of genocide constituting four of the five acts as defined in the 1948 Genocide Convention. The International Association of Genocide Scholars has characterised them as genocide. Amnesty International has characterised them as genocide. Human Rights Watch has characterised them as genocide.

Per the casualty figures: as of 3 May 2026 at least 75,811 people have been reported killed including roughly 73,770+ Palestinians and 2,039+ Israelis. The Gaza Ministry of Health figure accepted by the IDF in January 2026: 71,667 killed, 171,000+ wounded, 9,500+ missing under rubble — characterised by the Israeli political-class architecture itself as "conservative" relative to the unaccounted dead under rubble and the starvation deaths. The US healthcare workers' October 2024 conservative estimate of 62,413 starvation deaths alone — based on the IPC Phase 5 mortality rates — exceeded the Gaza Health Ministry direct-violence counts. The *Economist* indirect-death analysis: Gaza life expectancy fell 35 years, rivalling the Rwandan genocide. The scholar estimates: 80% of Palestinians killed were civilians; 10,429 women, 20,179 children, 4,813 elderly among the dead.

The war was framed as Israel's "right to defend itself" against the Hamas atrocity of 7 October 2023. The reality is built on: the Israeli occupation of the Palestinian territories captured in 1967; the Gaza blockade since 2007; the Israeli political-class architecture of settler-colonial expansion across the West Bank; the characterisation by Israeli political-class operators of the Palestinian population as "human animals" (Yoav Gallant, 9 October 2023) and as "Amalek" (Netanyahu, citing the biblical genocide injunction); the operation of starvation as method of warfare; the destruction of roughly two-thirds of the Gaza building stock; the forced displacement of roughly 2 million of Gaza's 2.3 million residents; the US apparatus of weapons, intelligence, diplomatic cover, and the UN Security Council vetoes that operated continuously throughout the Biden and Trump administrations.

The record documents the war in dependency order: the context; the 7 October 2023 Hamas-led attack; the Israeli military operations October 2023 onward; the famine; the International Court of Justice proceedings; the International Criminal Court arrest warrants; the UN Commission of Inquiry findings; the Hamas leadership killings; the Lebanon war and Hezbollah leadership decapitation; the Iran-Israel direct exchanges and the June 2025 Twelve-Day War and the February 2026 Iran war; the West Bank operations; the Trump 20-point Comprehensive Plan and October 2025 ceasefire; the current state through May 2026; and the architecture of US institutional complicity.

## The context

The Palestinian apparatus throughout the post-1948 period operated through millions of population displacement (the Nakba); Israeli occupation of the 1967-captured territories (West Bank, Gaza Strip, East Jerusalem, Golan Heights, Sinai); the Palestine Liberation Organisation institutional architecture; the 1987-1993 First Intifada; the 1993 Oslo Accords; the 2000-2005 Second Intifada; the 2005 Israeli unilateral disengagement from Gaza; the January 2006 Hamas electoral victory; the June 2007 Hamas military takeover of Gaza from Fatah; and the Israeli-Egyptian blockade of Gaza from June 2007 onward.

The Gaza architecture operated through Israeli-Egyptian land, sea, and air blockade; Israeli military operations against Gaza in 2008-2009 (Operation Cast Lead — roughly 1,400 Palestinians killed), 2012 (Operation Pillar of Defense), 2014 (Operation Protective Edge — roughly 2,200 Palestinians killed), 2021 (Operation Guardian of the Walls — roughly 250 Palestinians killed), and 2022 (Operation Breaking Dawn). The 2018-2019 Great March of Return Gaza protests produced IDF sniper killings of hundreds of Palestinian protesters and multi-thousand injuries. The Hamas operational planning for what became the October 7 attack began after the May 2021 conflict.

The Israeli political-class architecture across the 2022-2023 period operated through the December 2022 Netanyahu sixth government — the most right-wing government in Israeli history, including the Itamar Ben-Gvir (Otzma Yehudit, Minister of National Security with police authority), Bezalel Smotrich (Religious Zionism, Minister of Finance with West Bank settlement authority), and the Religious Zionism-Likud apparatus (in Volume Two Dossier 18 — Smotrich; Dossier 19 — Ben-Gvir; Dossier 20 — Religious Zionism-Likud).

The Israeli apparatus across the 2023 period operated through domestic political crisis. The judicial reform protests — the largest mass protests in Israeli history — operated continuously across the January-September 2023 period. The Israeli institutional architecture of the Gaza envelope security operated through complacency; intelligence failures; the Egyptian institutional warnings; the Israeli political-class assumption that Hamas was deterred.

## The 7 October 2023 Hamas-led attack

On the morning of 7 October 2023 — during the Jewish holiday of Simchat Torah and Shemini Atzeret on Shabbat, and the 50-year anniversary the day after of the 6 October 1973 Yom Kippur War — Hamas and several other Palestinian militant groups launched Operation Al-Aqsa Flood from the blockaded Gaza Strip into the Gaza envelope of southern Israel.

The opening barrage operated through roughly 4,300+ rockets fired into Israel in 20 minutes. Hamas militants breached the Gaza-Israel barrier through vehicle-transported, motorcycle, paraglider, motorboat, and bulldozer infiltration. Per the IDF revised estimate, roughly 6,000 Gazans breached the border in 119 locations including roughly 3,800 from the elite Nukhba forces of the al-Qassam Brigades.

Militants attacked 21 communities including:
- Be'eri kibbutz: 108 killed.
- Kfar Aza kibbutz: roughly 50 killed.
- Netiv HaAsara moshav: 15 killed.
- Alumim kibbutz: 16-17 Thai and Nepalese agricultural workers killed.
- Nir Oz kibbutz: multi-dozen killed and taken hostage.

The Supernova Sukkot Gathering ("Nova music festival") near kibbutz Re'im, the largest single-target massacre of the 7 October attack, produced 378 dead (344 civilians and 34 security personnel) per the Israeli investigation. Roughly 44 festival attendees were taken hostage to Gaza. Roughly 20 of the attackers were killed by Israeli security forces in the area of the festival.

Bomb shelter massacres operated continuously around the festival site. Aner Shapira fought Hamas militants at the Re'im Junction shelter with a broken bottle. Hersh Goldberg-Polin lost his hand and was kidnapped (later killed in Hamas captivity in Gaza; his body was recovered in August 2024). Yuval Raphael survived the shelter outside kibbutz Be'eri where 40 of 51 people were killed.

The total killed in Israel and at the festival on 7 October 2023: roughly 1,200 people, primarily Israeli citizens but including multi-dozen foreign nationals from over 20 countries. The total taken hostage: roughly 251 people (some sources cite 240+).

A March 2024 UN report found evidence of sexual violence committed against Israeli victims during the attack. On 12 May 2026 the Israeli NGO Civil Commission on October 7 Crimes by Hamas Against Women and Children published "Silenced No More" — a roughly 290-page report concluding that sexual and gender-based violence had been "systematic, widespread, and integral" to the attack and its aftermath, identifying 13 recurring patterns including rape and gang rape, sexual torture and mutilation, forced nudity, executions linked to sexual violence, and abuse during captivity.

Specific atrocities were contested. The ZAKA volunteer accounts characterised by US Secretary of State Antony Blinken at the 9 November 2023 Manama Dialogue — including the Be'eri "young boy and girl, 6 and 8 years old, and their parents around the breakfast table" with "father's eye gouged out" and the decapitation accounts — were disproved by Haaretz reporting that "no children 6 or 7 or near those ages were killed on Be'eri." The Sara Netanyahu letter to US First Lady Jill Biden — characterising a "heavily pregnant woman" taken hostage — was identified as a Thai worker who was not pregnant. The contested specific allegations did not undermine the governing fact of the hundreds of Israeli civilian killings, the hundreds of hostage-taking, and the Hamas atrocity.

The response across international architecture: universal condemnation of the Hamas attack as a war crime; institutional commitment to Israeli "right to defend itself"; characterisation of the Israeli military operations as legitimate.

## The Israeli military operations October 2023 onward

The 7 October attack, Israel declared war (the first formal Israeli war declaration since the 1973 Yom Kippur War). The Israeli institutional military mobilisation operated through massive air-strike campaign followed by ground invasion.

On 9 October 2023 Defence Minister Yoav Gallant announced: "there will be no more electricity, no more food, no more fuel... We are fighting against human animals and will behave accordingly." The statement was the charter for the siege. The Netanyahu institutional rhetoric invoked the biblical Amalek injunction — "you shall blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven" — operating as the rhetorical scaffolding for the operations.

On 27 October 2023 Israeli ground forces invaded Gaza. The Israeli operations across Gaza Strip operated through bombardment, ground invasions of multiple cities, sieges of hospitals (Al-Shifa, Al-Ahli Arab, Indonesian Hospital, Kamal Adwan, Nasser Hospital), and forced displacement of millions of Palestinians from northern Gaza to southern Gaza to Rafah and back.

Signature institutional atrocities operated continuously:
- 17 October 2023: Al-Ahli Arab Hospital strike — hundreds killed; contested attribution.
- 31 October 2023: Jabalia refugee camp bombing of six-story apartment building — at least 106 civilians killed including 54 children.
- 2023-2024: Khan Younis battles — destruction of the city.
- May 2024 onward: Rafah offensive despite ICJ provisional measures order to halt it.
- 2024-2025: siege of North Gaza.
- August 2025: Gaza City offensive — mass displacement.

The Israeli apparatus continuous violation of the International Humanitarian Law:
- Indiscriminate bombing of densely populated civilian areas using multi-thousand US-supplied weapons including Mark 84 2,000-pound bombs and BLU-109 bunker busters.
- siege blockade of humanitarian aid into Gaza.
- destruction of all Gaza universities, hundreds of mosques, hundreds of churches and cemeteries, Gaza electricity grid, Gaza water and sewage infrastructure, Gaza hospital network.
- killings of 270+ journalists, 120+ academics, 560+ humanitarian aid workers (including 391 UNRWA employees).
- killings of hundreds of Palestinian doctors, nurses, paramedics.
- torture, sexual violence, and arbitrary detention of multi-thousand Palestinian detainees in Sde Teiman and other Israeli detention facilities.

The Israeli institutional position characterised the military operations as Hamas-targeting under international humanitarian law. The fact: per UN OCHA, more than two-thirds of Gaza's buildings had been damaged or destroyed by November 2025; per Wikipedia synthesis of multi-year data, scholar estimates put 80% of Palestinians killed as civilians.

## The famine

The siege architecture operated through complete blockade of humanitarian aid into Gaza for periods. Despite ICJ provisional measures orders (January 2024, March 2024, May 2024), Israel did not fully comply with the orders to facilitate humanitarian relief.

By August 2025 the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) confirmed Phase 5 (Catastrophe / Famine) in parts of Gaza City. Deir Al Balah and Khan Younis were projected to reach famine by end September 2025. UN experts characterised the famine as "entirely man-made."

The US healthcare workers' letter of October 2024 — based on IPC Phase 5 mortality rates (2 deaths per 10,000 population per day) — was the institutional estimate of roughly 62,413 starvation deaths in Gaza, conservative; the additional roughly 5,000 chronic-disease-related deaths from denied medical access.

The Israeli apparatus characterised the famine as Hamas-induced rather than Israeli-imposed. The fact: the Gaza Strip had been blockaded by Israel; the humanitarian aid trucks crossing the Kerem Shalom and Rafah crossings had been reduced to a fraction of prewar quantities; the Israeli Gaza Humanitarian Foundation (GHF) — the US-Israeli-operated aid distribution architecture established 2025 — produced hundreds of Palestinian killings as multi-thousand civilians were shot while seeking food.

As of late 2025 per ECCHR: 1.6 million Palestinians faced acute food insecurity; 100,000+ remained in Catastrophic (Phase 5) conditions. The entire Gaza Strip was projected to remain in Emergency (IPC Phase 4) through mid-April 2026 with renewed famine risk if humanitarian aid were further restricted.

The Gaza Health Ministry direct famine-and-malnutrition death count: 428 adults and 146 children per a TIME report (autumn 2025) — institutional underestimate relative to the IPC-projected mortality.

## The ICJ — South Africa v. Israel

On 29 December 2023 the South African government instituted proceedings against the State of Israel at the International Court of Justice alleging violations of the 1948 Genocide Convention.

The South African application produced evidentiary catalogue of:
- The Israeli military operations killings of Palestinian civilians.
- The Israeli government and military officials' genocidal rhetoric — including the Gallant "human animals" statement, the Netanyahu Amalek invocation, the Israeli president Isaac Herzog's "it's an entire nation out there that is responsible" statement, and the statements by multi-dozen Israeli officials.
- The Israeli operations targeting "conditions of life" for the Palestinian population: starvation, denial of water, denial of medical care, denial of shelter.
- The Israeli military operations forcing mass displacement.

On 11-12 January 2024 the ICJ public hearings produced South African and Israeli legal presentations. The international institutional attention was the structural turning point.

On 26 January 2024 the ICJ — in a 15-2 / 16-1 / 17-0 / unanimous range of votes across the six provisional measures — ordered Israel to:
1. Take all measures within its power to prevent the commission of acts of genocide.
2. Ensure with immediate effect that its military does not commit genocidal acts.
3. Prevent and punish public incitement to genocide.
4. Take immediate and effective measures to enable the provision of urgently needed humanitarian assistance.
5. Take effective measures to prevent destruction and ensure preservation of evidence.
6. Submit a report on all measures taken within one month.

The ICJ provisional measures operated continuously:
- 28 March 2024: ICJ ordered Israel to take measures to ensure unhindered humanitarian aid and to prevent famine.
- 24 May 2024: ICJ ordered Israel to halt its Rafah offensive and to keep Rafah crossing open.

Israel did not fully comply with any of the ICJ provisional measures across the period. The Counter-Memorial originally due 28 July 2025 was extended to 12 January 2026 and again to 12 March 2026. On 12 March 2026 Israel filed its Counter-Memorial.

In March 2026 18 states had filed Article 63 ICJ Statute interventions, including:
- Netherlands: argued obligation to prevent genocide is a "due diligence obligation"; forced displacement and "deliberate withholding of humanitarian aid" could be indicators of genocidal intent.
- Iceland: intent may be inferred from "patterns of conduct" rather than requiring the only reasonable inference.
- Namibia, Spain, Mexico, Turkey, Ireland, Bolivia, Colombia, Belgium, Maldives, Cuba, Libya, Palestine.

The US, Hungary, and Fiji filed Article 63 interventions in support of Israel. The US characterised the genocide allegations as "false" and argued that international bodies were being "misused" as part of a campaign to "delegitimise" Israel.

Hearings on the merits are not expected until possibly 2027.

## The ICC arrest warrants

On 12 October 2023 ICC chief prosecutor Karim Khan stated that both Israeli and Palestinian war crimes within the ICC Palestine investigation jurisdiction would be investigated.

On 20 May 2024 Khan filed applications for arrest warrants against:
- Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
- Israeli Minister of Defence Yoav Gallant.
- Hamas leaders Yahya Sinwar, Mohammed Deif, Ismail Haniyeh.

On 21 November 2024 the ICC Pre-Trial Chamber I — composed of Judges Nicolas Guillou (France, presiding), Reine Alapini-Gansou (Benin), and Beti Hohler (Slovenia) — issued arrest warrants for Netanyahu, Gallant, and Mohammed Deif.

The Pre-Trial Chamber I found reasonable grounds that from "at least 8 October 2023 until at least 20 May 2024" Netanyahu and Gallant bear criminal responsibility:
- As co-perpetrators jointly with others: the war crime of starvation as a method of warfare; and the crimes against humanity of murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts.
- As civilian superiors: the war crime of intentionally directing an attack against the civilian population.

The warrant against Deif was cancelled in January 2025 after Hamas confirmed his 13 July 2024 death in an Israeli airstrike. The warrants against Sinwar and Haniyeh had been withdrawn following their respective deaths.

All 125 ICC member states — including France, the United Kingdom, Germany, Italy, Canada, Australia, and most of the Western political-class architecture — are obligated to arrest Netanyahu and Gallant if they enter their territory. Netanyahu skipped the 56th World Economic Forum (Davos 2026) — Switzerland being a Rome Statute signatory. He visited Hungary; Hungary did not arrest him; Hungary announced its withdrawal from the ICC.

In 2026 Haaretz reported that the Office of the Prosecutor had secretly requested arrest warrants for Ben-Gvir, Smotrich, Settlements Minister Orit Strook, and two unnamed IDF officers. The ICC denied that any new warrants had been issued.

International law professors Matthias Goldmann and Kai Ambos characterised Germany as legally obliged to enforce the arrest warrants. US defense lawyer Alan Dershowitz characterised the ICC decision as "the rule of law has been disgraced." International law professor Eliav Lieblich of Tel-Aviv University characterised the warrants as "the most dramatic legal development in Israel's history."

The ICC architecture is subject to US Trump-administration sanctions — including sanctions on ICC officials and the US apparatus of pressure against ICC member states to prevent arrest.

## The UN Commission of Inquiry

On 16 September 2025 the UN Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory — chaired by Navi Pillay (former UN High Commissioner for Human Rights and South African Constitutional Court judge) — concluded "on reasonable grounds that the Israeli authorities and Israeli security forces have committed and are continuing to commit" the crime of genocide against Palestinians in the Gaza Strip.

The Commission found that Israel had committed four of the five genocidal acts as defined in the 1948 Genocide Convention since 7 October 2023:
1. Killing members of the protected group.
2. Causing serious bodily or mental harm to members of the protected group.
3. Deliberately inflicting on the group conditions of life calculated to bring about its physical destruction in whole or in part.
4. Imposing measures intended to prevent births within the group.

The Commission named Israeli leadership — Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, President Isaac Herzog, and former Minister of Defence Yoav Gallant — as inciters of genocide.

Israel rejected the Commission report as "distorted and false" and called for the Commission's abolition.

The genocide determinations:
- June 2024 prior UN Commission report: Israeli authorities responsible for war crimes (starvation as method of warfare, murder, attacks on civilians, forcible transfer, sexual violence, torture, arbitrary detention); crimes against humanity (extermination of Palestinians, gender persecution of Palestinian men and boys).
- December 2024 Amnesty International: Israel committing genocide.
- December 2024 Human Rights Watch: Israel responsible for crime of genocide through deprivation of safe water and sanitation.
- March 2025 UN Commission: Israeli attacks on women's healthcare facilities amounted to genocidal acts.
- September 2025 International Association of Genocide Scholars: war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide.
- UN Special Committee.
- Hundreds of scholars and human rights organisations.

The institutional consensus across international apparatus — excluding Israel, the United States, Hungary, Fiji, and a declining minority — is that the Israeli operations in Gaza constitute a genocide.

## The Hamas leadership killings

Ismail Haniyeh — Hamas Political Bureau chief, Doha-based — was assassinated in Tehran on 31 July 2024 in an Israeli-claimed operation. The strike on the IRGC guesthouse where Haniyeh was staying produced Iranian institutional embarrassment and Iranian institutional response.

Mohammed Deif — commander of the al-Qassam Brigades (Hamas military wing) — was killed on 13 July 2024 in an Israeli airstrike in Khan Younis. His death was confirmed by Hamas in January 2025.

Yahya Sinwar — Gaza Hamas leader, October 7 architect, Haniyeh successor — was killed by Israeli forces in Gaza on 16 October 2024. IDF reports identified him as having been killed during a routine patrol that engaged a building where Sinwar was sheltering. The images of his death produced international attention.

Following Sinwar's death the Hamas leadership transitioned to a five-man council led by Khalil al-Hayya (Sinwar's former deputy), with Khaled Meshaal, Zaher Jabarin, Muhammad Darwish, and an unidentified fifth figure. Most members are based in Qatar or Turkey.

In September 2025 Israeli airstrikes targeted Hamas leaders meeting in Qatar — Israel's institutional breach of the US-Qatar institutional architecture. Although Hamas said its top officials survived, five Hamas members and one Qatari security officer were killed. The strike on a US ally's sovereign territory produced Qatari institutional embarrassment.

In December 2025 Israeli forces killed Raed Saad, characterised as a Hamas military wing figure spearheading Hamas rearmament.

On 7 May 2026 Hamas reported that the son of its chief negotiator had been killed in an Israeli strike on Gaza.

## The Lebanon war 2023-2024

On 8 October 2023 — one day after the Hamas attack — the Lebanese Hezbollah movement began rocket and missile strikes against Israeli military positions in northern Israel, characterised by Hezbollah as "solidarity with the Palestinians." The Hezbollah institutional position operated continuously: strikes would continue until Israel ended its operations in Gaza.

The cross-border exchanges across the 7 October 2023 - 20 September 2024 period: 10,200 cross-border attacks (8,300 by Israel, 1,900 by Hezbollah). The displacement: roughly 60,000 Israelis from northern Israel; roughly 100,000+ Lebanese from southern Lebanon.

The late July 2024 Israeli institutional escalation: Israeli assassination of Fuad Shukr (Hezbollah commander) in southern Beirut; Israeli assassination of Ismail Haniyeh in Tehran days later.

On 17 September 2024 Israeli intelligence operated the pager attack across Lebanon. Roughly 5 months before the explosions Hezbollah had purchased Gold Apollo AR-924 pagers; the Mossad had secretly manufactured the devices and integrated PETN explosive, selling them to Hezbollah through a shell company. Thousands of pagers exploded simultaneously across Lebanon and parts of Syria. Killed: at least 12 people including 2 Hezbollah members, 2 children, and Iran's ambassador to Lebanon. Wounded: 2,750+.

On 18 September 2024 Israeli intelligence operated the walkie-talkie attack — Icom devices. Killed: 30+. Wounded: 750+.

UN human rights experts condemned the attacks as potential war crimes — the indiscriminate nature of the thousands of simultaneous explosions violating international law and the right to life.

On 10 November 2024 Netanyahu confirmed Israeli responsibility for the pager attack, characterising the operation as having been carried out "despite the opposition of officials in the defense establishment and those responsible for them in the political echelon."

On 20 September 2024 Ibrahim Aqil (Hezbollah Radwan Force commander) was killed in an Israeli airstrike on Beirut along with 54 others.

On 23 September 2024 Israel began Operation Northern Arrows — air-strike campaign across Lebanon. 558 killed first day including 50 children and 94 women. 800+ killed and 5,000+ injured first week.

On 27 September 2024 Hassan Nasrallah — Hezbollah Secretary-General for over three decades — was killed in an Israeli airstrike on Hezbollah headquarters in Dahieh (southern Beirut suburbs). Roughly 80+ bombs including US-made 2,000-pound (910 kg) bunker-buster bombs were dropped by at least 8 Israeli aircraft. Nasrallah was meeting in a room 20 metres below ground; the explosion was felt 40 kilometres away. Also killed: Ali Karaki (Hezbollah Southern Front commander), Mohammed Hussein Sarour (Hezbollah air force commander), Ibrahim Kobeissi (head of Hezbollah missile division). Successor Hashem Safieddine was targeted and killed (confirmed October 2024).

On 1 October 2024 Iran fired 181 ballistic missiles at Israel in retaliation for the assassinations of Abbas Nilforoushan (IRGC officer killed alongside Nasrallah), Hassan Nasrallah, and Ismail Haniyeh. At least one building was hit in Tel Aviv. Most projectiles were intercepted by Israeli air defences with US, UK, and Jordanian assistance. At least 2 Israelis were injured in Tel Aviv; one Palestinian was killed by shrapnel in Nu'eima, West Bank.

On 1 October 2024 Israeli ground forces invaded southern Lebanon — the sixth Israeli invasion of Lebanon since 1978. Ground operations operated across southern Lebanon. Roughly 1.2 million Lebanese (20% of population) were displaced.

On 26 October 2024 Israel deployed roughly 100 fighter jets to strike roughly 20 sites in Iran (anti-aircraft systems, missile-manufacturing sites, and other military targets including Tehran's nuclear programme facilities). Destruction of most Russian-supplied S-300 air defence systems paving the way for Israeli airpower escalation.

On 27 November 2024 the US-France-mediated ceasefire took effect. Total: roughly 4,000 Lebanese dead; 49 Israeli dead. Across November 2024 - March 2026 the ceasefire violations operated continuously: Israeli near-daily strikes in Lebanon killed 331 by November 2025 (127 civilians); total reached 500.

## The Iran-Israel direct exchanges and the Twelve-Day War

In April 2024 an Israeli airstrike on the Iranian consulate in Damascus killed Iranian IRGC officers — the first major direct exchange. Iran retaliated with the hundreds of missile and drone strikes on Israel (April 2024). Most projectiles were intercepted.

In October 2024 the Iran-Israel second direct exchange operated. Iran fired 181 ballistic missiles on 1 October; Israel struck Iran on 26 October.

In March 2025 US Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard testified that US intelligence assessed that "Iran is not building a nuclear weapon."

On 31 May 2025 the IAEA reported that Iran had enough 60%-enriched uranium for 9 nuclear weapons. On 12 June 2025 the IAEA Board of Governors passed a resolution drafted by the US/UK/France/Germany declaring Iran non-compliant with its nuclear obligations for the first time since September 2005.

On 13 June 2025 Israel launched Operation Rising Lion. 200 fighter jets struck Iranian nuclear sites at Natanz, Fordo, and Isfahan and hundreds of military and government targets across 27 Iranian provinces. Mossad operatives sabotaged Iranian air defences using precision weapons and a covert drone base near Tehran. The opening "Operation Red Wedding" killed 30 Iranian generals and 9 nuclear scientists in minutes.

Iran responded with missile salvos: 500+ Iranian missiles, roughly 36 of which landed in populated areas in Israel. The Haifa oil refinery and Israeli military sites were struck.

On 22 June 2025 the US struck Iranian nuclear facilities at Natanz, Fordo, and Isfahan using GBU-57 A/B "bunker-buster" bombs. Trump claimed Iranian nuclear capabilities were "completely and totally destroyed." A US Defense Intelligence Agency preliminary report assessed that Iran had moved much of its enriched uranium stockpile before the strikes and that the strikes had set back Iran's nuclear programme by only a matter of months. CIA director John Ratcliffe asserted that new information indicated severe damage.

On 23 June 2025 Iran fired missiles at Al-Udeid airbase in Qatar (advance warning given to Washington; no casualties).

On 24 June 2025 the US-brokered ceasefire took effect — Trump's "Twelve-Day War" framing.

Twelve-Day War casualties: 1,100+ Iranians killed (Al Jazeera), 610 (Iran initial); 28 Israelis killed. Roughly 1,000 Iranian ballistic missiles destroyed. Roughly 30+ Iranian military commanders and 11+ nuclear scientists killed.

On 28 August 2025 France/UK/Germany triggered the "snapback" mechanism reinstating UN sanctions on Iran for the first time in a decade.

## The 2026 Iran war

On 27 February 2026 Oman's foreign minister stated Iran had agreed to degrade its nuclear stockpile "to the lowest level possible" — to unrefined levels. Trump said he preferred diplomacy but warned "all options" remained available.

On 28 February 2026 — one day after the Omani diplomatic announcement — Israel and the United States launched 900+ strikes in 12 hours targeting Iranian missiles, air defences, military infrastructure, and leadership. The initial wave killed Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and dozens of other officials. The strike also killed roughly 170 people at a girls' school adjacent to a naval base in Minab, near Bandar Abbas.

The institutional successor as Iranian Supreme Leader: Mojtaba Khamenei (Khamenei's second son). The IRGC and other key leaders pledged loyalty.

Iran responded with missile and air strikes across the region including Israel, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the UAE, and Iraq. Spain — a NATO member already in dispute with the second Trump administration — refused to allow US use of bases for the operation.

The 2026 Iran war operated continuously through the date of writing. Trump oscillated between demanding "unconditional surrender" of Iran, calling for popular uprising, and offering amnesty to Iranian military commanders. The diplomatic off-ramps were characterised by Al Jazeera analysis as "erased" by the decapitation strategy.

On 2 March 2026 Hezbollah launched projectiles at northern Israel — the first time since the November 2024 ceasefire — targeting a missile defence site south of Haifa. Hezbollah characterised the strikes as response to Israeli aggression and as unrelated to the Iran war.

On 16 March 2026 Israeli ground operations in southern Lebanon began; 5 Israeli divisions were deployed.

On 1 April 2026 an Israeli Navy strike in Beirut killed Hezbollah Southern Front commander Hajj Yusuf Ismail Hashem.

On 4 April 2026 an Israeli airstrike on Ain Saade (a Christian locality near Beirut) killed Lebanese Forces politician Pierre Moawad, his wife, and their female neighbour. The IDF claimed Hezbollah target.

In November 2025 Hezbollah figure Haytham Ali Tabatabai had been killed.

## The West Bank during the Gaza war

The West Bank architecture operated continuously across the Gaza war period. At least 806 West Bank Palestinians were killed by Israeli forces or settlers between 7 October 2023 and 2025 (Wikipedia); 1,000+ per the PA Health Ministry as of November 2025; 240 killed by IDF in 2025 (down from 500 in 2024 and 504 in 2023). 143 of the dead were children.

UN: 800+ Israeli settler attacks on Palestinians between October 2023 and May 2024. 867 incidents of nationalistic crime and settler violence in 2025 (up from 682 in 2024); 128 severe incidents in 2025 (up from 83 in 2024 and 54 in 2023). The IDF identified roughly 300 Jewish extremists involved including 70 "hardcore" extremists; 39 were under restraining orders. The 300 extremists live largely in 42 illegal outposts across the West Bank.

10,000 West Bank Palestinians were arrested between 7 October 2023 and August 2024.

On 21 January 2025 — days after the January 2025 Gaza ceasefire — Israel launched Operation Iron Wall in Jenin, expanding to Tulkarm. UNRWA characterised the operation as "the longest and most destructive" Israeli operation in the West Bank since the Second Intifada. 40,000+ Palestinians were displaced — the highest displacement rate since the 1967 Palestinian expulsions.

The IDF demolished roughly 180 homes in Jenin and dozens in Tulkarm. The Jenin refugee camp was depopulated by the end of February 2025. Jenin camp has been sealed off by the IDF since early 2025.

Between 1 January and 29 September 2025 Israeli forces carried out nearly 7,500 raids in Palestinian towns and villages — a 37% increase compared with the corresponding period in 2024.

Between 7 October 2023 and 29 September 2025 OCHA Israeli forces withholding the bodies of 199 Palestinians from the West Bank, of whom 7 had been handed over and 192 remained withheld. 70 Palestinians (45 from Gaza, 25 from West Bank) died in Israeli custody.

On 26 November 2025 Israel launched Operation Five Stones targeting the "Pentagon of Villages" (Tubas, Tammun, al-Fara'a camp, Aqaba, Tayasir) in Tubas Governorate.

The Palestinian Authority — under President Mahmoud Abbas (term-expired 2009) — participated in the Iron Wall operation for the first time alongside the IDF, marking the capture of the Palestinian Authority security architecture by the Israeli occupation regime.

## The Trump 20-point Comprehensive Plan and the October 2025 ceasefire

On 29 September 2025 — with the Gaza death toll exceeding 67,000 and the famine confirmed — President Donald Trump unveiled his "Comprehensive Plan to End the Gaza Conflict" at a White House press conference with Netanyahu.

The 20-point plan operated through multi-section apparatus: ceasefire; phased hostage release; phased Israeli withdrawal to pre-designated lines; Palestinian administration of Gaza by "independent technocrats"; Palestinian Authority takeover by 2027; international "Board of Peace" with Trump as chair; International Stabilization Force (ISF) with US two-star general command; Civil Military Coordination Center (CMCC) under US military leader; Gaza reconstruction architecture.

Trump gave Hamas a deadline of 5 October 2025 for acceptance. On 3 October 2025 Hamas agreed to release all hostages and "hand over the administration of the Gaza Strip to a Palestinian body of independent technocrats," but did not agree to disarm.

On 8 October 2025 Trump announced an agreement on the first phase. Secretary of State Marco Rubio passed a note to Trump: "You need to approve a Truth Social post soon so you can announce the deal first." Trump multi-minute posted the deal announcement on Truth Social.

On 9 October 2025 the agreement was signed in Sharm el-Sheikh. On 10 October 2025 the ceasefire took effect.

By 13 October 2025 all 20 living hostages had been released in exchange for 2,000 Palestinian prisoners including 250 serving life sentences and 1,700 detainees. The return of 25 deceased hostage remains by mid-October. Hamas missed the deadline to return the last 28 deceased hostages, citing inability to access the bodies without time and special equipment. The dispute over the remaining hostage remains was an institutional friction across October-December 2025. By January 2026 Israel confirmed all hostages and hostage remains had been returned.

On 17 November 2025 the UN Security Council adopted Resolution 2803 endorsing Trump's plan.

In January 2026 the Board of Peace charter was signed at the World Economic Forum in Davos. The charter (obtained by the *Times of Israel*) did not limit the Board's scope to Gaza — a concern that the Board might compete with the Palestinian Authority and other Middle East architectures.

On 18 February 2026 the Board of Peace held its first meeting in Washington — 27 signatory countries with envoys from roughly 50 countries (some only as observers). US NATO allies broadly declined Board membership citing concerns about charter scope and invitations to ICC-wanted leaders such as Russia.

The US sent 200 troops to Israel in late 2025 to monitor the ceasefire and aid delivery. No US troops were stationed directly inside Gaza per official statements. Israeli troops were stationed at the Rafah crossing reopened in February 2026.

Across October 2025 - May 2026 the ceasefire violations operated continuously: 442 Palestinians were killed by Israel in 1,200+ truce violations since 10 October 2025 per Wikipedia; 500+ Palestinians killed by Israel per other counts. On 13 December 2025 Israeli forces killed Hamas commander Raed Saad. On 7 May 2026 the son of Hamas chief negotiator was killed in an Israeli strike on Gaza.

## The casualty synthesis

Gaza Strip Palestinian casualties through 3 May 2026 per Wikipedia "Gaza war":
- 75,811 total reported killed (73,770+ Palestinians and 2,039+ Israelis).
- 171,000+ wounded (per IDF-accepted GHM figures January 2026).
- 9,500+ missing under rubble.
- 270 journalists and media workers killed.
- 120 academics killed.
- 560+ humanitarian aid workers killed (including 391 UNRWA employees).
- Roughly 10,429 women, 20,179 children, 4,813 elderly among the dead per ECCHR.
- Roughly 80% of Palestinians killed were civilians per scholar estimates.
- US healthcare workers' October 2024 conservative estimate of 62,413 starvation deaths.
- Roughly 5,000 chronic disease deaths.
- Indirect death studies (per *Economist*): Gaza life expectancy fell 35 years.

Israeli casualties:
- October 7, 2023: roughly 1,200 killed; roughly 251 taken hostage.
- IDF military: 460+ soldiers killed in Gaza operations (per October 2025 figures).
- Total Israeli dead (per Wikipedia): 2,039+.
- Additional Israeli civilians killed in rocket strikes, West Bank attacks, Lebanon strikes.

Lebanese casualties (2023-2024 war and continuing operations):
- Roughly 4,000+ killed by November 2024 ceasefire.
- 331+ killed by November 2025; reached 500.
- Roughly 1.2 million displaced.

Iranian casualties (June 2025 Twelve-Day War):
- 1,100+ killed (Al Jazeera) or 610+ (Iran initial estimate).
- 30+ military commanders killed.
- 11+ nuclear scientists killed.

Iranian casualties (February 2026 Iran war onward):
- Khamenei plus dozens of officials killed in 28 February strikes.
- 170 killed at Minab girls' school strike.
- ongoing operations.

West Bank casualties:
- 806+ Palestinians killed (Wikipedia); 1,000+ per PA Health Ministry.
- 143 children among dead.
- 70 Palestinian detainees died in Israeli custody.
- 192 Palestinian bodies remain withheld by Israel.

**Combined deaths from the multi-front war complex spanning Gaza, Lebanon, Iran, West Bank, and Israeli territory: roughly 80,000-90,000+.**

## Western institutional complicity

The US apparatus operated through weapons transfers totalling multi-tens of billions of dollars during the Biden administration alone, continuing into the Trump administration. US-supplied weapons used in Gaza operations: Mark 84 2,000-pound bombs, BLU-109 bunker busters, JDAM precision-guidance kits, GBU-39 small-diameter bombs, M982 Excalibur GPS-guided shells, M141 bunker defeat munitions, M72 LAW anti-tank, multi-thousand small arms, F-15I, F-16I, and F-35I fighter aircraft.

The US institutional diplomatic cover operated through multi-occasion UN Security Council vetoes of Gaza ceasefire resolutions during both the Biden administration (October 2023, December 2023, February 2024, March 2024 abstention) and the Trump administration. The US institutional pressure against the ICC, ICJ, and other international architectures operated continuously.

The June 2024 US House of Representatives amendment — banning US officials from using State Department resources to cite Gaza Health Ministry casualty figures — was sponsored by Rep. Jared Moskowitz (D-Fla.) whose all-time top campaign contributor was AIPAC. Moskowitz characterised the Gaza Health Ministry as "the Hamas Ministry of Health." The US apparatus of casualty-figure denial operated continuously until the IDF January 2026 acceptance of the GHM count of 71,667 killed — the admission of the US institutional position's collapse.

The AIPAC apparatus across the period was the signature US-Israel political-class lobbying architecture (in Volume Two Dossier 17 — AIPAC). The AIPAC institutional position operated continuously through multi-million-dollar campaign contributions to US officials including hundreds of Democrats and Republicans; funding of US-Israel parliamentary delegations; architecture of pressuring US officials to oppose Gaza ceasefires and arms embargoes.

The UK Foreign Secretary David Lammy on 2 September 2024 suspended roughly 30 of 350 UK arms export licences to Israel after a government review concluded high risk of being used for severe IHL violations. The UK institutional position retained the hundreds of remaining licences.

The 3 September 2025 Scottish Government ban on funding to arms companies provided to countries with plausible evidence of genocide — and the Scottish Parliament vote for a boycott of Israel and any companies providing military support — was the governing first by a UK devolved government.

In February 2026 the UK Public Interest Lawyers organisation noted that 2,000+ UK nationals had served in the IDF during the genocide — raising UK legal questions about complicity.

Sweden's police task force dedicated to war-crimes investigations received 700+ Gaza-related reports per Prosecutor Reena Devgun.

## The balance

The Gaza war is the signature institutional genocide of the post-2023 period. The architecture of US, UK, German, French, Italian, Canadian, Australian, and most Western political-class operators has produced the record of complicity — through weapons transfers, diplomatic cover, UN Security Council vetoes, rejection of the ICJ provisional measures, sanctions on the ICC and the ICC officials, and rhetorical characterisation of the Israeli operations as legitimate "self-defence."

The conflict was presented as Israel's "right to defend itself" against the Hamas atrocity. The reality is documented:
- The ICJ proceedings characterising the Israeli operations as plausibly violating the Genocide Convention.
- The ICC arrest warrants against the Israeli Prime Minister and former Defence Minister for the war crime of starvation and the crimes against humanity of murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts.
- The UN Commission of Inquiry finding of four of five genocidal acts.
- The Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, and International Association of Genocide Scholars determinations of genocide.
- The Gaza death toll exceeding 75,000 with 80% civilian.
- The destruction of roughly two-thirds of Gaza's building stock.
- The forced displacement of roughly 2 million of Gaza's 2.3 million residents.
- The siege architecture producing famine.
- The Israeli political-class genocidal rhetoric.
- The Western institutional complicity.

The fact: the Israeli operations in Gaza constitute the most genocide in human history — the recorded across millions of social-media posts, multi-thousand journalist reports, hundreds of academic studies, UN documentation, and the International Court of Justice proceedings. The inability of the international architecture to stop the genocide constitutes the failure of the post-1945 international order.

The question of who has benefited from the war operates across apparatus: the US arms industry; the Israeli political-class architecture (Netanyahu government extension through wartime emergency powers); the Israeli settler-colonial expansion across the West Bank; the re-architecture of the Middle East regional balance through Hamas decapitation, Hezbollah decapitation, Iranian decapitation, and Assad-regime collapse.

The Palestinian institutional question — operated across Palestinian political-class operators (Hamas, Fatah, the PA, the multi-year diaspora architectures), Arab institutional positions (Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt, Jordan, Lebanon, Iraq), broader Global South institutional positions (South Africa, Bolivia, Colombia, Brazil, Malaysia, Indonesia, Turkey, Iran, China, the UN General Assembly multi-occasion majorities) — is whether the genocide will produce the reorganisation of the Israel-Palestine question. The answer per the Trump 20-point plan, the Board of Peace architecture, the inability of the international apparatus to arrest Netanyahu and Gallant, and the absence of progress toward a Palestinian state — is contested and operated against the fact that the siege continues.

## Bridge to Chapter 6

The Ukraine war (Chapter 4) and the Gaza war (this chapter) operate as the signature contested-and-uncontested institutional wars of the post-2022 period. The contemporaneous wars across the African Sahel, the Sudan civil war, the Tigray war (Ethiopia), the Myanmar civil war, the DRC war, and the Nagorno-Karabakh war — each producing multi-tens-of-thousands to millions of dead — operate as the unseen wars of the post-2022 period. Chapter 6 documents these wars.

# Part XII Chapter 6: The Unseen Wars

## The wars without cameras

Between 2020 and the date of writing in May 2026 an archipelago of major wars has operated across the African Sahel, the Horn of Africa, the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, the Myanmar civil war, the Sudan civil war, and the 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh — each producing multi-tens-of-thousands to hundreds of thousands of deaths; each producing millions of displacement; each producing systematic atrocities including characterisations of ethnic cleansing, war crimes, crimes against humanity, and in multiple cases, genocide.

These wars operate without the Western political-class attention given to Ukraine (Chapter 4) and Gaza (Chapter 5). The Sudan civil war from April 2023 onward — with a former US envoy estimate of up to 400,000 dead, 14 million displaced (the world's largest displacement crisis), and the October 2025 El Fasher massacre characterised by Yale Humanitarian Research Lab satellite analysis as possibly the most lethal massacre of the 21st century — operates as an institutional case of what gets covered and what does not. The 2020-2022 Tigray war (Ethiopia) — with a Council on Foreign Relations estimate of roughly 600,000 dead by the Pretoria Agreement — is the "forgotten genocide" per the June 2024 New Lines Institute 120-page report calling for Ethiopian prosecution at the International Court of Justice. The Myanmar civil war from February 2021 onward — with UN figures of 75,000+ total dead and 3 million+ displaced — is the unseen war of Southeast Asia. The DRC eastern Congo war — with Rwanda-backed M23 capture of Goma in January 2025 and millions of civilian impact — operates as the war of the African Great Lakes. The September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh — expelling 100,400 ethnic Armenians within a week — operates as the ethnic cleansing characterised by international architecture as war crime or crime against humanity. The Sahel insurgencies and coup architecture across Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger — with JNIM blockade of Bamako since September 2025 and Russian Africa Corps deployment — is the unseen war of West Africa.

The display is the characterisation of these wars as African problems, Asian problems, Caucasus problems — operating outside the Western political-class apparatus. What runs behind the mask is the architecture: Western arms exports fuelling the wars (UAE arms to RSF in Sudan, Russian Africa Corps in Sahel, Western arms to Rwanda and Azerbaijan, Chinese and Russian arms to Myanmar junta); extractive-industry interests (gold and minerals in Sudan, critical minerals in DRC and Myanmar, oil and gas in Azerbaijan); geopolitical contests (US-Russia-China-UAE-Turkey-Iran-EU-India proxy positioning); and the failure of the post-1945 international order to stop the atrocities through UN Security Council vetoes, inadequate humanitarian funding, and non-prosecution.

The record documents each war in dependency order: the Sudan civil war 2023-present; the Sahel insurgencies and coup architecture; the Tigray war and Ethiopian post-war conflicts; the Myanmar civil war 2021-present; the eastern DRC war and M23 capture of Goma; the September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh.

## The Sudan civil war 2023-present

On 15 April 2023 fighting broke out in Khartoum between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) under Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) under Gen. Mohamed Hamdan "Hemedti" Dagalo — the two military factions of the post-2019 Sudanese transitional architecture that had jointly overthrown the Omar al-Bashir 30-year regime in April 2019 and the civilian-led transitional government in October 2021.

The RSF traces its institutional architecture to the Janjaweed militia founded by Bashir during the 2003-2008 Darfur genocide. The Janjaweed had killed hundreds of thousands of non-Arab civilians (Fur, Masalit, Berti, Zaghawa tribes); the campaign had displaced roughly 3 million people. US President Biden in January 2025 characterised the 2003-2008 Darfur events as genocide.

The April 2023 institutional war operated across Sudan. RSF forces operated rapid initial captures of Khartoum and large portions of western Sudan including the Darfur region. SAF forces operated retreat from Khartoum and reorganisation in Port Sudan.

**Mass atrocities:**

In November 2023 the RSF captured Geneina (West Darfur). The Ardamata massacre killed up to 2,000 people per local rebel groups. A UN panel estimated 10,000-15,000 killed by RSF in Geneina alone in 2023. The targeting of the Masalit and other non-Arab communities operated systematically.

In August 2024 the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) confirmed famine at the Zamzam IDP camp — the structural first such famine declaration. The famine expanded to five localities across El Fasher and Kadugli. By November 2025 the IPC identified famine in two states with 20+ more at severe threat.

In March 2025 the SAF recaptured Khartoum after operations from Port Sudan and Omdurman. The SAF advance operated through March-October 2025 across Khartoum, Omdurman, and toward central Sudan.

**The El Fasher massacre October 2025:**

On 26-27 October 2025 the RSF captured El Fasher — the capital of North Darfur and the last major SAF stronghold in Darfur — after an 18-month siege. The siege had systematically operated through starvation, deprivation, confinement, and drone strikes.

The capture produced mass atrocities. The UN Human Rights Office confirmed 6,000 killings; characterised the actual toll as "undoubtedly significantly higher." The Yale Humanitarian Research Lab satellite analysis 30,000-60,000 dead based on satellite imagery of closely packed objects and ground discoloration consistent with human bodies; estimates ranged 60,000-70,000 dead, and via satellite-based methods, as possibly up to 150,000 dead. The El Fasher massacre operated as possibly the most lethal massacre of the 21st century.

The Human Rights Watch documentation of the RSF treatment of disabled civilians at El Fasher: point-blank executions of amputees, point-blank executions of people with Down syndrome, characterisation by RSF fighters of disabled people as "insane" or "not a complete person."

The US institutional finding of 2023: the RSF and allied militias had committed genocide in Darfur with systematic targeting and killings, and sexual violence against ethnic Massalit and other non-Arab communities.

The UN institutional characterisation: the RSF operations bear the "hallmarks of genocide" per UN investigator Christopher Lockyear and others.

**Current state May 2026:**

On 11 January 2026 Sudan's military-led government returned to Khartoum from Port Sudan; Prime Minister Kamil Idris began service restoration. The city remained badly damaged after displacement, looting, occupation of homes, and near-collapse of basic services.

In early 2026 intensifying fighting operated in the Kordofan region with almost-daily drone strikes resulting in hundreds of civilian casualties striking markets, health facilities, and residential areas. The SAF advanced into Bara (North Kordofan's second-largest city) and operated positioning in the state capital El-Obeid. The RSF captured the last SAF stronghold in West Kordofan and pushed into parts of South Kordofan.

On 23 February 2026 Chad shut its eastern border with Sudan indefinitely citing repeated incursions by Sudanese armed groups.

**Casualty synthesis:**
- ACLED tracked 58,000+ fatalities — actual number characterised as "certainly much higher."
- Former US envoy estimate: up to 400,000 dead.
- Range: 150,000-400,000.
- Total displaced: ~14 million; ~11 million IDPs; ~4 million refugees in Chad/Egypt/South Sudan/Ethiopia.
- 28.9 million in acute food insecurity; 10+ million in severe/extreme hunger.
- 2.9 million children acutely malnourished; 729,000 children under-five severe acute malnutrition.
- 4 million children acutely malnourished; 770,000 at imminent risk of death.
- Sudan population ~50 million; ~60% of population in humanitarian need.

**Foreign involvement:**

The UAE has was the primary RSF backer through weapons, financing, and logistical support channelled largely through Chad, Libya, and recently Ethiopia. The UAE architecture has operated denials despite evidence including US-supplied UAE drones appearing in RSF inventories. Sudan has considered action against the UAE at the International Court of Justice for supplying the RSF.

Egypt, Iran, and Turkey have operated SAF support. China has operated weapons to both sides. Russia has operated presence through Wagner / Africa Corps gold-mining and mineral interests.

Humanitarian funding has never exceeded 38% of need since 2023; in 2026, only 8% of need has been met. The Trump-administration USAID cuts produced closure of 60%+ of Sudanese-run emergency food kitchens.

## The Sahel insurgencies and coup architecture

Between August 2020 and August 2023 a coup architecture operated across the African Sahel: Mali (August 2020 and May 2021 — Assimi Goïta); Guinea (September 2021); Burkina Faso (January and September 2022 — Capt. Ibrahim Traoré); Niger (July 2023 — Gen. Abdourahamane Tchiani); Gabon (August 2023). The coup architecture operated through post-colonial French apparatus, Islamist insurgency pressure, economic crisis, and popular dissatisfaction with French-aligned governance.

The realignment operated through French troop expulsions: Mali (December 2022), Burkina Faso (February 2023), Niger (December 2023). US troops were withdrawn from Niger in 2024. The Russian Africa Corps (the successor architecture to Wagner Group after the August 2023 Prigozhin death) operated deployment to Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger. 3,500 Russian troops in Mali by early 2026 per Jeune Afrique; Mali paying $35 million monthly for Africa Corps maintenance.

In January 2024 Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger formally established the Alliance of Sahel States (AES) and withdrew from ECOWAS effective 2025.

**The Islamist insurgency:**

The Sahel Islamist insurgencies operate through two apparatuss:

- Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wa al-Muslimeen (JNIM): al-Qaeda-affiliated coalition under Iyad ag Ghaly. JNIM imposed a blockade on Bamako since September 2025 — major attacks on fuel convoys across southern and western Mali; 60%+ of Mali's total trade passes along the Northern Bamako-Dakar Corridor. JNIM operated 2024 attacks on the main military air base and a gendarmerie base in Bamako — the first such attacks since the jihadi insurgency began.

- Islamic State Sahel Province (ISSP): ISIS-affiliated. The UN reported ISSP doubled territory in Mali 2022-2023; expansion into Niger 2023-2024. ISSP control of significant Mali-Niger border. IS recruiters and facilitators established transit corridors between southern Europe and the Sahel; attack cell operated out of Morocco and Spain.

The Russian Africa Corps has failed to degrade the strengthening Salafi-jihadi insurgencies. Mali and Niger's capitals have come under direct threat in the period.

## The Tigray war and Ethiopian post-war conflicts

The Tigray war operated from November 2020 to November 2022 between the Ethiopian federal government under Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed (the 2019 Nobel Peace Prize laureate for the Eritrea-Ethiopia peace agreement), Eritrean Defense Forces (EDF) under Isaias Afwerki, and Amhara regional forces — against the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) and Tigray Defense Forces (TDF). The war was the deadliest single armed conflict of the post-2020 period until the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.

The war casualty estimate per Council on Foreign Relations: roughly 600,000 dead by the November 2022 Pretoria Agreement. Per Congressional Research Service: 100,000+ battle-related deaths in 2022 alone; roughly 500,000 civilians may have died from killings, starvation, or lack of healthcare linked to Ethiopian-government humanitarian siege.

The September 2022 Ethiopia-Eritrea joint offensive: Ethiopian army reportedly suffered 90,000 casualties in one month.

Internal displacement 2021: 5.1 million Ethiopians — the record for most internally displaced in any country in any single year at the time.

**Atrocities:**

US Secretary of State Antony Blinken characterised the March 2021 events in western Tigray as "ethnic cleansing." In March 2023 Blinken characterised the conflict events as war crimes, crimes against humanity, and ethnic cleansing committed by multiple parties.

The June 2024 New Lines Institute 120-page report "Genocide in Tigray" concluded strong evidence of genocidal acts committed by Ethiopian forces and allies, calling for Ethiopian prosecution at the International Court of Justice. The findings: systematic mass killings of Tigrayan civilians; widespread incidents of torture, rape, and severe physical and psychological abuse; imposing starvation tactics and other measures aimed at destroying Tigrayan communities; actions intended to prevent births within the Tigrayan population; destruction and looting of heritage.

The UN International Commission of Human Rights Experts on Ethiopia (ICHREE) — mandated December 2021 — issued the findings before termination in October 2023 after the Ethiopian diplomatic campaign against renewal. ICHREE: all parties committed war crimes; Ethiopian federal government and allies committed crimes against humanity and used starvation as a weapon of war; "further independent investigation is warranted to determine the possible commission of the crime against humanity of extermination and/or the crime of genocide."

Characterisations of the Tigray events as genocide: Pope Francis; Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus (WHO Director-General, ethnic Tigrayan); Abune Mathias (Ethiopian Orthodox Church Patriarch, ethnic Tigrayan, censored by the Ethiopian government); June 2024 New Lines Institute; sociology of genocide scholars.

The sexual violence: used as weapon of war; maternal mortality rates in Tigray rose from 186 deaths per 100,000 prewar to 840 per 100,000 postwar; 81% of mothers in the Seharti Samre woreda had birth defects; infant mortality regression to 2001 levels.

The Mai Kadra and Axum massacres was the governing reference points for the multi-massacre architecture.

**Post-war Ethiopian conflicts:**

The 2 November 2022 Pretoria Peace Agreement between Ethiopia and the TPLF — Eritrea not a party — produced ceasefire. Eritrean troops and Amhara forces have remained in parts of Tigray, implicated in serious abuses.

The Amhara region war operated continuously from 2023 onward: Fano militia vs Ethiopian National Defense Force (ENDF). The Oromia region Oromo Liberation Army (OLA) insurgency operated continuously. Roughly 2,000 killed in 2023 in Amhara/Oromo/inter-ethnic conflicts.

The 2025-2026 power struggle within Tigray operated between the TPLF (under Debretsion Gebremichael) and the Tigray Interim Administration (TIA, appointed by Abiy under the Pretoria Agreement). Eritrea reportedly supporting dissident TPLF factions. The threatened re-escalation operated continuously through May 2026.

## The Myanmar civil war 2021-present

On 1 February 2021 the Myanmar military (Tatmadaw) under Senior Gen. Min Aung Hlaing executed a military coup overthrowing the elected Aung San Suu Kyi National League for Democracy (NLD) government. The civil war operates continuously through the date of writing.

**Casualty figures:**
- Civilian deaths per Assistance Association for Political Prisoners (AAPP) to April 2025: 6,486 civilians killed including 1,494 women and 751 children.
- UN OHCHR verified deaths 2024: 1,824 people killed including 531 women and 248 children — sharply up from the previous peak of 1,639 in 2023.
- UN Global Conflict Tracker total deaths: 75,000+ across the conflict.
- Displaced: 3 million+ internally; additional multi-million-Rohingya displacement.
- ACLED tracks 2,600+ armed groups emerged since the 2021 coup.

**The resistance:**

The National Unity Government (NUG) — formed April 2021 by ousted NLD parliamentarians and ethnic representatives — is the opposition government. The People's Defence Force (PDF) under the NUG operated 65,000 fighters in 2022 rising to 85,000 by 2024.

The ethnic armed organisations across Myanmar's 135 ethnic groups operated against the Tatmadaw: Kachin Independence Army (KIA); Shan State Army; Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA); Arakan Army; Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA); Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA). A 2024 survey: 93% of respondents in Myanmar held a favourable opinion of the NUG.

On 27 October 2023 the Three Brotherhood Alliance (MNDAA, TNLA, Arakan Army) launched Operation 1027 in northern Shan State — the turning-point coordinated offensive against the Tatmadaw. By late 2023 resistance forces controlled more than half of Myanmar's territory.

Throughout 2024 and 2025 the Tatmadaw used superior air power, divisions within the resistance, and war of attrition to retain control over major urban areas. The Chinese institutional intervention operated continuously through aid to the junta, pressure on opposition forces, and brokered ceasefires.

**Atrocities:**

The 11 April 2023 Pa Zi Gyi village massacre (Sagaing): Myanmar military airstrike on People's Administration Team gathering killed 175 including 33 women and 42 children. Human Rights Watch concluded the use of a thermobaric bomb.

The 18 March 2024 Thar Dar village airstrike (Rakhine): 22 Rohingya civilians killed including children.

Airstrikes on churches, schools, residential areas across operations.

**The Rohingya:**

The 2017 ethnic cleansing of the Rohingya minority in Rakhine State produced 1 million+ Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh (Cox's Bazar). The case of The Gambia v Myanmar before the International Court of Justice is the accountability architecture; Aung San Suu Kyi defended Myanmar against the genocide charges at the ICJ in December 2019 before her removal in the 2021 coup. The ICJ proceedings operate continuously.

**2025-2026 events:**

In January 2025 the junta announced general elections by end of 2025 or early 2026 to consolidate political control. Observers feared the elections would lead to more violence; extended state of emergency.

On 28 March 2025 a magnitude-7.7 earthquake struck Sagaing region near Mandalay. Major destruction; further humanitarian crisis on top of the civil war.

## The eastern DRC war and M23

The eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo war operated continuously from the 1994 Rwandan Genocide aftermath. The Kivu conflict — across North Kivu and South Kivu provinces — operates through 100+ armed groups including the FDLR (Hutu remnants of the Rwandan génocidaires), ADF (Allied Democratic Forces), Mai-Mai militias, and the M23 (March 23 Movement).

The M23 architecture operates as a Tutsi-led armed group backed by Rwanda. The M23 captured Goma briefly in November 2012 before international pressure produced withdrawal. The M23 architecture operated continuously through peace agreements that failed.

**2024-2026 escalation:**

In December 2024 peace talks in Angola between Presidents Tshisekedi (DRC) and Kagame (Rwanda) were cancelled over disagreements about M23. Rwanda insisted on direct dialogue between DRC and M23, which the Congolese government refused.

On 27 January 2025 M23 captured Goma — the eastern DRC's largest city, the crucial humanitarian hub near the Rwandan border, home to 2+ million people. The capture was the worst escalation in a decade or more.

On 16 February 2025 M23 captured Bukavu, the South Kivu provincial capital. On 19 March 2025 M23 captured the Walikale mining hub in North Kivu — the farthest west M23 has reached.

The UN reports: 3,000-4,000 Rwandan Defense Forces (RDF) troops fighting alongside M23 in eastern DRC; "de facto control" of M23 operations by Rwanda.

**Casualty and displacement figures:**
- At least 7,000 people killed in recent months (early 2025).
- Nearly 780,000 displaced November 2024 - January 2025.
- 100,000+ refugees crossed into neighbouring countries since 1 January 2025: 69,000 to Burundi, 29,000 to Uganda, ~1,000 each to Rwanda and Tanzania.
- 21 million in DRC needed humanitarian aid pre-escalation — the highest figure globally per UN OCHA.

**International response:**

Widespread international condemnation of M23 occupation of Goma; calls for resumption of peace talks; few countries explicitly condemned Rwanda or considered sanctioning Rwanda. The Western apparatus — Rwanda institutional support for Kagame as the "rebuilder of Rwanda after the 1994 genocide" — operated reluctance to apply institutional pressure.

The Trump-administration Washington Peace Accords (mid-2025) was the architecture for DRC-Rwanda normalisation. Rwanda violations: late 2025 M23 offensive on Uvira town in South Kivu.

In late January 2026 Rwanda and M23 publicly acknowledged ties for the first time. The Trump-administration sanctions package on Rwandan officials operated under consideration. In February 2026 the Congolese army (FARDC) killed a M23 military leader in a drone strike in Masisi district. Rwanda reduced force deployment after US pressure.

The question of who benefits from the DRC war: mineral extraction (cobalt, coltan, tin, gold, tungsten — the supply chain for the global electronics and battery industries); Rwandan apparatus of resource-rich-DRC-east access; Western institutional architecture of mineral supply at low cost; Chinese institutional architecture of mineral acquisitions.

## The September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh

On 19-20 September 2023 Azerbaijan launched a "anti-terrorist operation" against the self-declared Republic of Artsakh in Nagorno-Karabakh. The conflict — over the Armenian-majority enclave de jure within Azerbaijan but de facto governed by ethnic Armenians since the 1991-1994 First Nagorno-Karabakh War — operated termination through institutional ethnic cleansing.

**Institutional context:**

The 1988-1994 First Nagorno-Karabakh War was the conflict that produced Azerbaijani territorial loss of roughly 13.6% — displacement of roughly 500,000 Azerbaijanis from Nagorno-Karabakh and surrounding occupied territories, 186,000 from Armenia, and 300,000-500,000 Armenians from Azerbaijan.

The 27 September - 9 November 2020 Second Nagorno-Karabakh War operated as the Azerbaijani military reclamation of the surrounding territories and roughly one-third of Nagorno-Karabakh itself. The 9 November 2020 tripartite ceasefire — Pashinyan, Aliyev, Putin — installed Russian peacekeepers and the Lachin Corridor as the sole road connecting Nagorno-Karabakh to Armenia.

**The 2022-2023 Lachin blockade:**

From December 2022 to September 2023 Azerbaijan blocked the Lachin Corridor for nine months. The blockade was operated through Azerbaijani "environmental protesters" with state backing. Russian peacekeepers operated inability or unwillingness to enforce the 2020 ceasefire terms.

The blockade produced humanitarian crisis: severe shortages of food, medicine, fuel, gas, electricity; bread rationed to one loaf per family per day by early September 2023. In February 2023 the International Court of Justice ordered Azerbaijan to ensure free movement to Nagorno-Karabakh, ruling that the blockade posed a "real and imminent risk" to the "health and life" of Nagorno-Karabakh's Armenian population. Azerbaijan did not comply.

**The 19-20 September 2023 offensive:**

On 19 September 2023 Azerbaijan launched the "anti-terrorist operation" — drone offensive, artillery, ground operations. Hundreds of casualties operated on both sides. Five Russian peacekeepers were killed.

The 20 September 2023 surrender: Russian-mediated agreement for the complete disarmament and dissolution of the Republic of Artsakh.

On 28 September 2023 Artsakh President Samvel Shahramanyan signed a decree to dissolve all state institutions by 1 January 2024.

**The mass exodus:**

The population exodus operated within days. Pre-offensive Artsakh population: roughly 120,000 ethnic Armenians (95% of region). By the end of September 2023 — from 24 September onward — 100,400 ethnic Armenians (99% of remaining population) had fled to Armenia through the Lachin Corridor. Only a couple of dozen Armenians remained.

The mass forced displacement was characterised by international experts as war crime or crime against humanity. The Lemkin Institute for Genocide Prevention had urged Artsakhi women and children to refuse separation from men and older boys — citing the risk of mass execution patterns consistent with prior genocides.

The Azerbaijani institutional position: Armenians who chose to remain would be "reintegrated" with rights and security. The fact: 99% of the 120,000 chose to leave.

**International response:**

Condemnations: Armenia, the diaspora architectures, the Lemkin Institute, the University Network for Human Rights, Armenian-American institutional networks. Muted responses: Russia (Russian peacekeepers unwilling or unable to enforce 2020 ceasefire), the US (Blinken tweet calling for "immediate end to hostilities and direct dialogue" but no sanctions), the EU ("irreversible normalization" rhetoric from Charles Michel), Turkey (Erdoğan-Aliyev meeting on 25 September discussing Zangezur land corridor and natural gas pipeline).

**The August 2025 Washington Peace Accords:**

In August 2025 the Trump administration hosted Armenian PM Nikol Pashinyan and Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev at the White House. Peace deal: joint declaration of peace; joint request to dissolve the OSCE Minsk Group; provision granting the United States sole development rights over a transit route from the Nakhchivan exclave through southern Armenia to Azerbaijan — the "Trump Route for International Peace and Prosperity."

The Azerbaijani trials of 16 former ethnic Armenian officials including former state minister Ruben Vardanyan operated through restricted media access and arbitrary terrorism charges. Armenian officials characterised the proceedings as "mock trials."

The Nagorno-Karabakh outcome: multi-million-Armenian-diaspora architecture, Armenian displacement to Armenia and elsewhere, cultural-heritage destruction across the Armenian region, inability of the international architecture to enforce protections.

## Casualty synthesis

The combined deaths across the six unseen wars:

- Sudan civil war 2023-present: 150,000-400,000 dead.
- Tigray war 2020-2022: ~600,000 dead.
- Myanmar civil war 2021-present: 75,000+ dead.
- DRC M23 2024-2026 escalation alone: 7,000+ dead in recent months (continuing).
- Sahel insurgencies and coup-zone violence 2020-2026: multi-tens-of-thousands dead.
- Nagorno-Karabakh 2023 offensive: hundreds killed; 100,400 ethnically cleansed.

Combined deaths: roughly 800,000-1.1 million across the period. Combined displacement: roughly 25-30 million across the period.

By comparison: Ukraine war (Chapter 4) — roughly 400,000-550,000 deaths; Gaza war (Chapter 5) — roughly 75,000-100,000+ deaths.

The unseen wars have killed roughly twice as many people as the Ukraine and Gaza wars combined. The Western political-class institutional attention has been roughly one-tenth.

## Foreign involvement architecture

The foreign involvement architecture across the six unseen wars:

**Sudan:** UAE primary RSF backer; Egypt, Iran, Turkey to SAF; China to both; Russia (Wagner / Africa Corps gold/mineral interests); Chad accused; US (Trump-administration USAID cuts).

**Sahel:** Russia (Africa Corps, ~3,500 troops in Mali, $35M/month); France (expelled); US (withdrawn from Niger 2024); China (multi-year mineral interests).

**Tigray/Ethiopia:** Eritrea (direct military participation); UAE (drones to Ethiopia); US/EU (sanctions and aid suspensions); China (multi-year economic interests).

**Myanmar:** China (primary supporter of junta; critical minerals, oil and gas, BRI infrastructure, deep-sea port access, Strait of Malacca alternative trade route); Russia (multi-year arms); India (multi-year ambivalent).

**DRC:** Rwanda (direct M23 support); Uganda (multi-year ambivalent); China (multi-year mineral acquisitions); EU/US (multi-year mineral supply chain for electronics and batteries); Trump administration (Washington Peace Accords).

**Nagorno-Karabakh:** Turkey (direct Azerbaijani support including 2020 Bayraktar drones and Syrian-mercenary deployment); Russia (peacekeepers who did not protect Armenians); Israel (arms supplier to Azerbaijan including Harop loitering munitions and intelligence cooperation); US (Trump-administration deal).

## The institutional reckoning

The unseen wars operate as the architecture of the post-2020 period that the Western political-class operators have characterised as African problems, Asian problems, Caucasus problems — operating outside the Western political-class apparatus.

The public framing is the characterisation. The actual operation involves: Western arms exports; Western extractive interests; Western institutional positioning against Russia and China; Western institutional silence on Western-allied perpetrators (Rwanda, UAE, Azerbaijan, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Myanmar military before the 2021 coup).

The question: why does the Ukraine war receive the multi-hundred-billion-dollar Western financial and weapons commitment, the multi-week front-page coverage, the UN General Assembly resolutions, and the ICC arrest warrant for Putin — while the Sudan war receives 8% of humanitarian funding need, the Tigray war receives the characterisation of "forgotten genocide," the DRC war receives the mineral-supply-chain dependency without institutional accountability, the Myanmar war receives the Chinese institutional protection of the junta, and the Nagorno-Karabakh ethnic cleansing receives the "Trump Route for International Peace and Prosperity"?

The institutional answer: the Western political-class architecture has operated selective institutional attention based on geopolitical alignment, resource interests, racial-civilisational hierarchy of which populations matter, and benefit from war prolongation and resource extraction.

## Bridge to Chapter 7

The unseen wars operate as the evidence of the proxy-war architecture. The Sudan war operates through UAE-Russia-Iran-Turkey-China proxy positioning. The Sahel wars operate through Russia-France-US-China proxy positioning. The Tigray war operated through US-China-UAE-Eritrea proxy positioning. The Myanmar war operates through China-US-Russia-India proxy positioning. The DRC war operates through Rwanda-EU-US-China proxy positioning. The Nagorno-Karabakh war operated through Turkey-Russia-Israel-US proxy positioning.

Chapter 7 documents the proxy-war architecture itself — the pattern across the post-WWII period, the Cold War proxy wars (Chapter 1), the post-Cold-War US wars (Chapter 2), the Iran-Iraq War (Chapter 3), the Ukraine war (Chapter 4), the Gaza war (Chapter 5), and the unseen wars (this chapter) — and the architecture of how great-power proxy positioning operates as mask for institutional interests.
