# Part XII Chapter 7: The Proxy-War Architecture

## The pattern

Across the six previous chapters of Part XII — the Cold War proxy wars (Chapter 1), the post-Cold-War US wars (Chapter 2), the Iran-Iraq War (Chapter 3), the Ukraine war (Chapter 4), the Gaza war (Chapter 5), and the unseen wars (Chapter 6) — a pattern operates continuously: the Western political-class architecture, the Russian / Soviet political-class architecture, and the Chinese political-class architecture have consistently operated major wars through proxy positioning rather than through direct great-power-on-great-power combat.

The combined death toll across the wars covered in Chapters 1-6 alone: tens of millions dead. The Cold War proxy wars: roughly 15-25 million dead (1945-1991). The post-Cold-War US wars: 2-3 million dead (1991-2021 in Iraq, Afghanistan, Libya, Syria, Yemen alone). The Iran-Iraq War: 1-1.5 million dead (1980-1988). The Ukraine war: 400-550K dead (2022-present). The Gaza war and Middle East war complex: 75-100K+ dead (2023-present). The unseen wars: roughly 800K-1.1M dead (2020-present).

The performance is the characterisation of each war as a specific local conflict — a Korean conflict, a Vietnam conflict, a Soviet-Afghan conflict, a Gulf War, an Iraq War, an Ukraine war, an Israel-Hamas war, a Sudan civil war. The underlying operation is the architecture: the great-power competition for resource extraction, geographical positioning, arms-industry profit, financial-system positioning, and management of populations across the developing world.

The record documents the proxy-war architecture as an institutional system. The Hoover Institution characterisation of the current US position — "Today, the United States is engaged in multiple proxy wars: supplying arms to the Ukrainians to repel and degrade the Russian military; supplying arms to Israel so it can dismantle Hamas (an Iranian proxy); and directly confronting a wider array of Iranian proxies in Syria, Iraq, and the Red Sea" — is the admission of what the operations actually are.

The record documents the proxy-war architecture in dependency order: the structural definition of proxy war; the Cold War pattern; the post-Cold-War pattern; the arms-industry profit architecture; the theatre matrix; the academic and political-class critiques; the verdict.

## The institutional definition

A proxy war operates as an armed conflict in which one or more great powers support local belligerents to advance the great power's own interests while avoiding direct combat with other great powers. The institutional rationale: avoidance of nuclear escalation between the Cold War superpowers; political-cost-reduction relative to direct military intervention; deniability of institutional involvement.

The architecture per the RAND Corporation analyses operates through mechanisms: weapons transfers; intelligence sharing; training of local forces; financial support; diplomatic cover at the UN Security Council and other international apparatuss; sanctions on opposing local belligerents and their great-power patrons; covert operations through intelligence services and Special Operations Forces; private military contractor deployments; information-operations campaigns.

The historical scope per Britannica: "Proxy wars became a more acceptable way for the two superpowers to compete for world influence." The RAND Corporation analysis: "Its use in the modern era peaked in the late Cold War."

Pre-Cold-War precedent: France used American colonists as proxies against Britain in the 1775-1783 American Revolution — more French soldiers and sailors than Continental Army troops at the 1781 Battle of Yorktown. Russia used Serbia as proxy against the Austro-Hungarian Empire pre-1914. Spain used the Spanish-American war 1898 — and was operated as proxy by US architecture against post-1898 Cuba, Philippines, Puerto Rico, Guam.

## The Cold War pattern 1945-1991

The Cold War proxy-war pattern operated continuously across the 1945-1991 period (in Chapter 1). The architecture: the US-Soviet-Chinese tripartite competition produced armed conflicts across Asia, Africa, Latin America, and the developing world generally.

The Korean War 1950-1953 (2-4 million dead): US/UN forces vs DPRK forces backed by USSR weapons and PRC direct military intervention.

The Vietnam War 1955-1975 (2-3 million dead): US direct military intervention against DRV (North Vietnam) and Viet Cong, with Soviet weapons and Chinese support to DRV.

The Indochina wars: Cambodia (US bombing 1969-1973 then Khmer Rouge genocide 1975-1979 with Chinese support); Laos secret war 1959-1975 (500K-1M dead).

The Greek Civil War 1946-1949: US-UK support to monarchist forces against communist Democratic Army of Greece backed by Yugoslavia, Bulgaria, and Albania.

The Indonesian mass killings 1965-1966 (500K-1M dead): US-UK-Australian institutional support for Suharto military coup and mass killings of Indonesian Communist Party (PKI) members and ethnic Chinese.

The Angolan Civil War 1975-2002 (500K-1.5M dead): US-South Africa-Zaire backing of UNITA (Savimbi) vs Soviet-Cuban backing of MPLA (Neto, dos Santos) — the Cuban military intervention with 50,000+ troops at peak.

The Mozambican Civil War 1977-1992 (~1M dead): Rhodesia-South Africa backing of RENAMO vs Soviet-allied FRELIMO government.

The Ethiopian Civil War 1974-1991 (1.4M dead) and Ogaden War 1977-1978: Soviet-Cuban backing of Mengistu Derg regime; US-Egyptian-Saudi backing of Somali and Eritrean insurgents.

The Nicaraguan Sandinista Revolution and Contra War 1972-1990 (~75K dead): Soviet-Cuban backing of Sandinista government; US-CIA-Argentina-Saudi backing of Contras through Iran-Contra arms-for-hostages-and-cocaine architecture.

The El Salvador Civil War 1979-1992 (~75K dead): US-Israeli backing of Salvadoran junta and death squads; Soviet-Cuban-Nicaraguan-allied FMLN insurgency.

The Guatemala Civil War 1960-1996 (~200K dead, mostly indigenous Mayan): US-CIA backing of Guatemalan military government across Ríos Montt period.

The Soviet-Afghan War 1979-1989 (1-2M dead): US-Saudi-Pakistani-Chinese-UK Mujahideen support; Operation Cyclone CIA programme. The Afghan civil war 1989-2001 produced Taliban rise through Pakistani ISI support. The 2001 US invasion produced 20-year occupation (Chapter 2).

The pattern across the Cold War: great-power weapons; local proxies; millions of dead; impunity; characterisations of the wars as internal conflicts or liberation struggles or anti-communist struggles depending on the political-class characterisation.

## The post-Cold-War pattern 1991-present

The post-Cold-War proxy-war pattern operated through characterisations as unipolar US institutional moment — the "end of history" thesis (Fukuyama 1989), the "Wolfowitz Doctrine" (1992 Defense Planning Guidance), the RAND Corporation strategic planning, and the US-NATO institutional expansion.

The institutional pattern operated through direct US-NATO interventions: 1991 Gulf War; 1992-1995 Bosnia; 1998-1999 Kosovo; 2001-2021 Afghanistan; 2003-2011 Iraq invasion and occupation; 2011 Libya NATO intervention; 2011-present Syria intervention; Yemen intervention through Saudi-UAE coalition; Somalia drone strikes and ground operations through AFRICOM architecture (Chapter 2).

The proxy-war architecture continued through continued operations: US support to Syrian opposition through CIA Timber Sycamore programme and Pentagon programs; US support to Saudi-UAE Yemen war through arms and intelligence; US support to Iraqi government against ISIS through Combined Joint Task Force - Operation Inherent Resolve; US-Israeli support against Iranian regional architecture (Hezbollah, Hamas, Houthis, Iraqi militias, IRGC); US-UK-French support to African counterterrorism operations through French Operation Barkhane and US AFRICOM Operation Enduring Freedom Trans-Sahara; US support to Kurdish forces in northeastern Syria through YPG/SDF architecture.

The Russian / Soviet-successor proxy-war architecture operated continuously through post-1991 institutional reconstitution: Chechen wars 1994-2009; 2008 Russo-Georgian War; 2014 Crimea annexation and Donbas war; 2015-present Russian intervention in Syria; Wagner Group / Africa Corps operations across Mali, Central African Republic, Libya, Sudan, Burkina Faso, Niger; 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.

The Chinese proxy-war architecture operated through subtler institutional mechanisms: North Korean support; arms exports to Pakistan, Algeria, Bangladesh, Myanmar, multiple Sub-Saharan African states; Belt and Road Initiative economic positioning; African gun-running and resource-extraction architecture.

The Iranian proxy-war architecture operated through the "Axis of Resistance": Hezbollah (Lebanon, founded 1982), Hamas (Gaza, multi-year support since 1990s), Palestinian Islamic Jihad, Houthis (Yemen, multi-year support post-2014), Iraqi militias (Kata'ib Hezbollah, Asa'ib Ahl al-Haq, Badr Organisation, post-2003), Syrian Assad government, Afghan Fatemiyoun (Hazara Shia volunteers in Syria), Pakistani Zainabiyoun (Pakistani Shia volunteers in Syria). The Iranian apparatus has operated through IRGC Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani (killed 3 January 2020 by US drone strike under Trump 1 administration) and successors.

The Saudi / UAE / Qatari / Turkish / Egyptian / Israeli / South African / Indian / Pakistani / Brazilian regional-power proxy-war architectures operated continuously across specific theatres.

## The arms-industry profit architecture

The arms-industry profit architecture operates as the beneficiary of the proxy-war pattern.

**SIPRI Arms Transfers Database (March 2025 release, 2020-2024 data):**

Global major-arms exports 2020-2024:
- US: 43% (up from 35% in 2015-2019; +21% volume).
- France: 9.6% (+11%).
- Russia: 7.8% (-64%).
- China: 5.9% (-5.4%).
- Germany: 5.6%.
- Italy: 4.8% (+138%).
- UK: 3.6%.
- Israel: 3.1%.
- South Korea: 2.2%.
- Spain: 2.1%.

NATO + US allies (US, France, Germany, Italy, UK, Israel, South Korea, Spain, Turkey 1.7%, Netherlands 1.2%, Australia, Ukraine): 75%+ of global arms exports.

16 of top 20 exporters are US allies. Non-US-allied in top 20: Russia (7.8%), China (5.9%), Iran (0.4%).

**top arms importers 2020-2024:**
- Ukraine: 8.8% (+9,627% vs 2015-2019). World's biggest arms importer.
- India: 8.3% (-9.3%).
- Qatar: 6.8% (+127%).
- Saudi Arabia: 6.6% (-41%).
- Pakistan: 4.6% (+61%).
- Japan: 3.9%.
- Australia, Egypt, Kuwait, Norway round out top 10.

Middle East: 27% of global imports; 52% from US.
European NATO imports: +105%; 64% from US.
Saudi Arabia: 74% from US.
Qatar: 48% from US.

**SIPRI March 2026 release (2021-2025 data):**

- US: 42% of global major arms exports (+27% vs 2016-2020).
- Russia: 6.8% (down from 21% in 2016-2020).
- France: second largest supplier 2021-2025.
- Ukraine: 9.7% of global imports 2021-2025 (vs 0.1% in 2016-2020).
- European NATO arms imports: +143% from 2016-2020 to 2021-2025.
- 25+ states agreed in 2025 to buy major arms from US for transfer to Ukraine — the Trump-era proxy-arms-transfer architecture.
- Saudi Arabia: 77% from US.
- Russia received Irania one-way attack drones and DPRK ballistic missiles (violating UN arms embargo on DPRK).

**US military spending 2023:**

- 37% of global military expenditure.
- More than next 9 largest spenders combined.
- 3x China; 8x Russia.
- ~$880 billion baseline (excluding off-book appropriations and military aid packages).
- Trump 2nd-term demand: NATO allies 5% of GDP on defence.

**Trump 2nd-term arms deals (May 2025):**

- $142 billion Saudi Arabia deal — characterised by Trump administration as "the largest defence sales agreement in history."

**US-Israel military aid:**

- Costs of War Brown University: $21.7 billion in first 2 years of Gaza war (October 2023 - October 2025).
- Multi-decade $3.8 billion/year baseline under 2016 MOU.
- 70%+ of Israeli arms imports from US over period.

**US-Ukraine military aid:**

- US authorised roughly $175+ billion in supplemental appropriations 2022-2024.
- Ukraine 9% of all US conventional arms transfers 2020-2024.
- 30+ states transferred arms to Ukraine 2022-2023.

**major US arms manufacturers:**

- Lockheed Martin: F-35, HIMARS, Javelin, Patriot, JASSM, THAAD architectures.
- RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon): Patriot, AMRAAM, Stinger, Tomahawk architectures.
- General Dynamics: Abrams tanks, naval shipbuilding (Bath Iron Works, Electric Boat).
- Northrop Grumman: B-21 Raider, Global Hawk, ICBM, naval programmes.
- Boeing: F-15EX, AH-64 Apache, KC-46, P-8 Poseidon.
- L3Harris.
- BAE Systems (UK).

Stock-price performance during 2022-2024 Ukraine war and 2023-2024 Gaza war: gains across all major defence contractors.

The architecture of the US Military-Industrial-Congressional Complex (MICC, or expanded to MICIMATT per Ray McGovern: Military-Industrial-Congressional-Intelligence-Media-Academia-Think-Tank Complex) operates continuously through political contributions, revolving-door personnel architecture, congressional district employment, multi-billion-dollar lobbying, and institutional documentation that the arms-industry profit architecture is the structural feature of the US political-class architecture.

## The theatre matrix of the multi-year 2020-2026 period

The 2020-2026 theatre matrix:

**Ukraine** — Open proxy war. US/NATO institutional support to Ukraine vs Russia.

**Gaza/Israel/Lebanon/Iran** — Open proxy war. US/UK/Germany/Italy/France/Canada/Australia institutional support to Israel vs Iran (Hamas, Hezbollah, Houthis, Iraqi militias, IRGC).

**Syria** — Multi-year proxy war until December 2024 Assad collapse. US/Turkey/Gulf states vs Russia/Iran. Post-Assad architecture: US/Turkey/Israel positioning vs Iranian-Russian institutional withdrawal.

**Yemen** — Multi-year proxy war. Saudi Arabia/UAE (with US/UK arms and intelligence) vs Houthis (with Iranian arms). UN-377K+ dead per UN; Houthi Red Sea blockade producing US/UK Operation Prosperity Guardian.

**Libya** — Multi-year proxy war. UAE/Russia/Egypt backing Haftar/LNA vs Turkey/Italy backing Government of National Unity (GNU). Multi-year stalemate.

**Sudan** — proxy war. UAE/Russia (RSF) vs Egypt/Iran/Turkey/China (SAF). 150-400K dead.

**Sahel** — proxy war. Russia (Africa Corps with 3,500 troops in Mali) + AES juntas vs JNIM (al-Qaeda) and ISSP (IS); French/US withdrawal.

**Tigray/Ethiopia** — proxy involvement. UAE drones to Ethiopian government; Eritrea direct participation; US/EU sanctions and aid tensions.

**Myanmar** — proxy involvement. China primary backer of junta with critical minerals, oil and gas, BRI infrastructure, deep-sea port access, Strait of Malacca alternative trade route; Russia arms; India ambivalent; US multi-year sanctions but limited intervention.

**DRC** — proxy involvement. Rwanda direct M23 support; EU/US mineral supply dependency (cobalt, coltan, tin, gold, tungsten); China mineral acquisitions. 6+ million dead across Kivu conflict cumulatively.

**Nagorno-Karabakh** — proxy war. Turkey (Bayraktar drones, Syrian mercenaries) + Israel (Harop loitering munitions, multi-year intelligence cooperation) backing Azerbaijan; Russian peacekeeping failure; Armenian institutional isolation.

**South China Sea / Taiwan Strait** — positioning. US/Japan/Australia/Philippines vs China.

**Korean Peninsula** — positioning. US/South Korea vs China/Russia/DPRK; DPRK ICBM tests and 2024-2025 DPRK troop deployment to Russia for Ukraine war.

**Latin America** — institutional positioning. US sanctions against Cuba, Venezuela (Maduro), Nicaragua (Ortega) vs Russia-China-Iran institutional positioning.

The pattern across the theatre matrix: great-power weapons; local proxies; multi-tens-of-thousands to millions of dead per theatre; impunity for great-power patrons; characterisations of the local wars as internal conflicts or terrorism or humanitarian interventions or anti-authoritarian struggles depending on the political-class characterisation.

## The institutional critique

Academic and political-class critiques of the proxy-war architecture operate continuously across the period.

**Realist critique** — operated by John Mearsheimer, Stephen Walt, Henry Kissinger, William Burns, Jeffrey Sachs, George Kennan, and realist political-class operators. The position: great-power proxy positioning operates as behaviour but the specific US foreign-policy decisions (NATO expansion, Iraq invasion, Libya intervention, Syria intervention, Ukraine war prolongation) operated as institutional miscalculations that harmed US interests, produced millions of deaths, and accelerated Russian-Chinese-Iranian alignment.

**Anti-imperial critique** — operated by Noam Chomsky, Edward Said, William Blum, Andrew Bacevich, Michael Mann, Vijay Prashad, and anti-imperial operators. The position: the US architecture operates as an imperial system producing systematic mass violence in pursuit of economic and geopolitical interests.

**Military-Industrial-Complex critique** — operated since President Eisenhower's 17 January 1961 farewell address warning of the "military-industrial complex." operators: Seymour Melman, Chalmers Johnson ("Blowback," "The Sorrows of Empire," "Nemesis: The Last Days of the American Republic"), James Risen, Andrew Cockburn ("The Spoils of War"), William Hartung ("Prophets of War"). The position: the arms-industry profit architecture is the structural driver of US foreign policy.

**Global South critique** — operated by Non-Aligned Movement architectures (1961-present), BRICS architectures (2009-present), African Union institutional positions, Indian-Brazilian-South-African-Indonesian institutional positions. The position: the Western proxy-war architecture operates as neo-colonial system; Global South sovereignty has been systematically violated by great-power proxy positioning.

**specific Putin/Xi/Khamenei institutional positions:**

- Putin (2007 Munich Security Conference speech): characterisation of US apparatus as "unipolar dictatorship."
- Xi Jinping characterisation of US institutional architecture as "Cold War mentality."
- Khamenei characterisation of US architecture as "Great Satan."

The Western political-class institutional characterisation of these positions as authoritarian propaganda operates against the evidentiary record that the Western proxy-war architecture has produced tens of millions of deaths.

The question — operated across political-class architectures — is whether the pattern represents a structural feature of the international system that operates regardless of which great power is dominant, or whether the pattern represents a specific Western political-class apparatus that could be reorganised through political-class institutional change.

## The composite assessment

The proxy-war architecture is the system through which the great-power competition has been conducted. The combined death toll across the period is tens of millions. The combined arms-industry profit is multi-trillions. The combined displacement is hundreds of millions.

The display is the characterisation of each war as a specific local conflict — a Korean conflict, a Vietnam conflict, a Gulf War, an Iraq War, an Ukraine war, an Israel-Hamas war, a Sudan civil war. The actual architecture consists of: great-power proxy positioning for resource extraction, geographical positioning, arms-industry profit, financial-system positioning, and management of populations across the developing world.

The institutional beneficiaries are visible. The Lockheed Martin, RTX, General Dynamics, Northrop Grumman, Boeing, BAE Systems, Thales, Airbus Defence, Leonardo, Saab, Rheinmetall, Israel Aerospace Industries, Elbit Systems, Rafael, IAI, Kalashnikov Concern, Almaz-Antey, Rostec, NORINCO, AVIC, CETC, Aselsan, Bayraktar / Baykar Defence Technology, HESA, IRGC industrial complex — and the multi-million-shareholder ownership architectures of these defence contractors — have profited continuously from the proxy-war pattern. The political-class architectures — US Republican-Democratic congressional architecture, EU member-state architectures, Russian-Chinese authoritarian architectures, Iranian theocratic-IRGC architecture, Israeli political-class architecture — have operated through the arms-industry funding, the revolving-door personnel architecture, and the governing documentation of who pays whom.

The institutional victims are visible. The tens of millions of dead across the Cold War, post-Cold-War, and post-2020 wars. The hundreds of millions of displaced. The impunity for great-power patrons. The characterisation of the specific local belligerents as solely responsible for the violence.

The question — operated across political-class operators, academic operators, journalistic operators, religious operators (Pope Francis multi-year, Tedros multi-year, Quakers, Catholic Worker Movement, Mennonites, Buddhist multi-year peace operators, Islamic peace operators), civil-society operators (CAAT, World Beyond War, peace movements, anti-arms campaigners) — is whether the proxy-war architecture can be reorganised. The answer per the evidentiary record is contested. The fact: the proxy-war pattern operates continuously through the date of writing.

## Bridge to Chapter 8

The proxy-war architecture (this chapter) operates through the arms-industry profit architecture. The arms industry itself — the manufacturers, the financiers, the lobbyists, the revolving-door personnel architectures — is the signature institutional beneficiary of the proxy-war pattern. Chapter 8 documents the arms industry itself: the architecture of the Military-Industrial-Congressional-Intelligence-Media-Academia-Think-Tank Complex, the profits, the specific institutional personalities, and the politics of the arms-industry sector that the US President Dwight Eisenhower warned about in his 17 January 1961 farewell address — and that has was the structural feature of the Western political-class architecture through the date of writing.

# Part XII Chapter 8: The Weapons Industry

## Eisenhower's warning

On 17 January 1961 — three days before the inauguration of John F. Kennedy — President Dwight D. Eisenhower delivered his farewell address to the nation. The five-star general who had operated as Supreme Commander of the Allied Expeditionary Force in Europe during WWII; the 34th President of the United States; the Republican institutional operator who had presided over the Cold War apparatus from 1953 onward — used his final speech to warn the American public about the architecture that has since become the signature institutional feature of the Western political-class architecture.

The quote: "This conjunction of an immense military establishment and a large arms industry is new in the American experience. The total influence — economic, political, even spiritual — is felt in every city, every state house, every office of the federal government. We recognise the imperative need for this development. Yet we must not fail to comprehend its grave implications. In the councils of government, we must guard against the acquisition of unwarranted influence, whether sought or unsought, by the military-industrial complex. The potential for the disastrous rise of misplaced power exists and will persist. We must never let the weight of this combination endanger our liberties or democratic processes."

The record: the military-industrial complex Eisenhower warned about has operated continuously through the date of writing. It has grown more than 2x in inflation-adjusted real terms relative to the 1961 baseline. It has captured the US political-class architecture through lobbying, revolving-door personnel architecture, campaign contributions, and documentation that the arms-industry profit is the structural driver of US foreign policy.

The 2024 global military expenditure: $2,718 billion (record 10th consecutive year of rises per SIPRI). The 2025 global military expenditure: $2,887 billion (record 11th consecutive year). The 2024 SIPRI Top 100 arms producers combined revenue: $679 billion (record, +5.9% over 2023). The combined Pentagon contract awards 2020-2024: $2.4 trillion to private firms — 54% of Pentagon discretionary spending. The post-9/11 wars 2001-present: top 5 contractors received $2 trillion combined.

The record documents the weapons industry in dependency order: Eisenhower's warning and the architecture it described; the SIPRI global military expenditure record; the SIPRI Top 100 arms producers; the US "Big Five" Pentagon contractors; the European, Israeli, Turkish, Russian, Chinese, and Iranian defence-industry architectures; the US Military-Industrial-Congressional-Intelligence-Media-Academia-Think-Tank Complex (MICIMATT) political economy; the specific institutional pathways through which the arms-industry has was the structural feature of the Western political-class architecture; the verdict.

## The SIPRI record

The Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) — founded 1966 in Stockholm by Swedish parliamentary act — is the authoritative public-data architecture for global military spending and arms production. The SIPRI data documents the continuous expansion of the military-industrial complex Eisenhower warned about.

**2024 SIPRI data (released April 2025):**

- World military expenditure: $2,718 billion — record 10th consecutive year of rises.
- Real-terms increase: 9.4% over 2023 — steepest year-on-year rise since end of Cold War.
- Global military burden: 2.5% of GDP.
- US: $997 billion (3.7% of GDP) including supplemental appropriations.
- China: $314 billion estimated.
- Russia: $149 billion (+38%, 7.1% of GDP, roughly 40% of federal expenditure in 2025).
- Germany: post-2022 institutional rearmament.
- India: record spending.
- Top 5 spenders (US, China, Russia, Germany, India) = 60% of global total = $1,635 billion.
- Europe (including Russia): $693 billion (+17%) — beyond level recorded at end of Cold War.
- All European countries except Malta increased military spending 2024.
- Countries with major armed conflicts: average military burden 4.4% of GDP vs 1.9% for those without.
- Per SIPRI: "Over 100 countries around the world raised their military spending in 2024."

**2025 SIPRI data (released April 2026):**

- World military expenditure: $2,887 billion — record 11th consecutive year of rises.
- Real-terms increase: 2.9% over 2024.
- US: decline.
- Outside US: 9.2% increase.
- Europe: +14%.
- Asia/Oceania: +8.1%.
- Top 3 spenders (US, China, Russia): $1,480 billion = 51% of global total.
- Global military burden: 2.5% of GDP — highest since 2009.

The pattern: continuous increase across the Ukraine war (Chapter 4), the Gaza war (Chapter 5), the unseen wars (Chapter 6), and the proxy-war architecture (Chapter 7). Per SIPRI's Xiao Liang: "Global military spending rose again in 2025 as states responded to another year of wars, uncertainty and geopolitical upheaval with large-scale armament drives."

## The SIPRI Top 100 arms producers

The SIPRI Top 100 arms producers (SIPRI Arms Industry Database, 1989-present; including Russian companies from 2002; Chinese companies from 2015) is the authoritative ranking of the global arms industry.

**2024 SIPRI Top 100 (released December 2025):**

- Combined revenue: $679 billion — record (+5.9% over 2023).
- Multi-year growth 2015-2024: +26%.
- 30 of 39 US companies in the ranking reported revenue increases.
- 26 European companies (excluding Russia): 23 recorded increases; aggregate $151 billion (+13%).
- Czechoslovak Group (Czech Republic): +193% to $3.6 billion — sharpest percentage increase of any Top 100 company. Benefiting from the Czech Ammunition Initiative for Ukraine.
- JSC Ukrainian Defense Industry: +41%.
- 9 Middle East companies entered Top 100 for the first time:
 - 3 Israeli firms: $16.2 billion combined (+16%).
 - 5 Turkish companies: $10.1 billion combined (+11%).
 - UAE EDGE Group: $4.7 billion.
 - Combined Middle East: $31 billion (+14%).

Per SIPRI: the drivers — Ukraine war (post-February 2022); Gaza war and broader Middle East regional operations (post-October 2023).

## The US "Big Five"

The US prime defence contractor architecture has consolidated from 51 prime contractors in the 1990s to the current 5: Lockheed Martin, RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon), General Dynamics, Boeing, and Northrop Grumman.

**Quincy Institute "Profits of War" 2020-2024 figures:**

- Private firms received $2.4 trillion in Pentagon contracts — 54% of $4.4 trillion Pentagon discretionary spending.
- Top 5 combined: $771 billion.
- Lockheed Martin: $313 billion (led the field; $168 billion more than nearest competitor).
- RTX (formerly Raytheon): $145 billion.
- General Dynamics: $116 billion.
- Boeing: $115 billion.
- Northrop Grumman: $81 billion.
- US diplomacy, development, and humanitarian aid budget 2020-2024 (excluding military aid): $356 billion — roughly 15% of the contractor profit.

Post-9/11 wars (2001-present, Afghanistan, Iraq, etc.): top 5 contractors received $2 trillion combined.

**Lockheed Martin** — largest defence contractor globally. Headquartered Bethesda, Maryland. Signature programmes: F-35 Lightning II (joint strike fighter, over-budget , global proliferation); F-22 Raptor; F-16 Fighting Falcon (global exports); C-130 Hercules; Sikorsky helicopters (Black Hawk, Sea Hawk); Patriot PAC-3; THAAD; JASSM; HIMARS (deployment to Ukraine); GMLRS; ATACMS (long-range missiles transferred to Ukraine 2023-2024); PrSM; Hellfire missile (drone-strike platform); Javelin (joint with RTX); Trident SLBM; Orion crew capsule; August 2024 acquisition of Terran Orbital (satellites). 2024 defence revenue: $64.7 billion. 2023 Pentagon contracts: $61.4 billion.

**RTX Corporation** (formerly Raytheon Technologies; rebranded 2023) — missile defence and aerospace conglomerate. Headquartered Arlington, Virginia. Signature programmes: Patriot air defence (Saudi, Israeli, Ukrainian, deployments); NASAMS (Ukraine deployments); SM-3 and SM-6 (Aegis-system missiles); Tomahawk cruise missiles (Operation Desert Storm onward); AMRAAM (air-to-air missile global standard); AIM-9 Sidewinder; Stinger MANPADS (Afghanistan-mujahideen onward, Ukraine); Javelin (joint with Lockheed Martin); Coyote drones; hypersonic weapons development; Pratt & Whitney engines; Collins Aerospace; commercial aviation. 2024 defence revenue: $40.6 billion. 2023 Pentagon contracts: $24.1 billion.

**General Dynamics** — ground systems and naval shipbuilding. Headquartered Reston, Virginia. Signature programmes: M1 Abrams main battle tank (global standard, Ukraine deployment 2023-2024); Stryker armoured fighting vehicle; Bath Iron Works (DDG-51 Arleigh Burke-class destroyers); Electric Boat (Virginia-class and Columbia-class submarines, nuclear-propulsion architecture); Gulfstream business jets; IT services. 2020-2024 Pentagon contracts: $116 billion.

**Boeing** — aerospace giant (civil aviation 737 MAX issues unrelated to defence operations). Headquartered Arlington, Virginia. Signature defence programmes: F-15EX Eagle II; F/A-18 Super Hornet; AH-64 Apache attack helicopter (global standard); CH-47 Chinook; KC-46 Pegasus tanker; P-8 Poseidon maritime patrol; E-7 Wedgetail airborne early warning; Insitu drones; Phantom Works. 2020-2024 Pentagon contracts: $115 billion.

**Northrop Grumman** — strategic systems and stealth aircraft. Headquartered Falls Church, Virginia. Signature programmes: B-21 Raider strategic stealth bomber (development); B-2 Spirit (legacy); Global Hawk RQ-4 reconnaissance drone; Triton; ICBM Sentinel (replacing Minuteman III in US nuclear modernisation programme); Joint STARS; naval programmes; satellite platforms. 2020-2024 Pentagon contracts: $81 billion.

**stock performance during Ukraine and Gaza wars:**

Since 24 February 2022 (Russian invasion of Ukraine) and 7 October 2023 (Hamas attack), the major US defence contractors have operated gains. Lockheed Martin, RTX, Northrop Grumman, and General Dynamics all operated significant share-price increases. The European arms-industry signature stock performance: Rheinmetall AG (Germany) operated several-hundred-percent gains since February 2022, operating as the signature European-rearmament beneficiary.

## The European, Israeli, Turkish, Russian, Chinese, and Iranian architectures

The non-US defence-industry architectures operate continuously through specific architectures:

**European:**

- BAE Systems (UK): Eurofighter Typhoon partner; Type 45 destroyer; Astute-class nuclear submarine; CV90 IFV; AS90 howitzer. US division produces Bradley IFV.
- Airbus Defence and Space (multi-national, headquartered Leiden, Netherlands; Toulouse, France; Munich, Germany): Eurofighter Typhoon partner; A400M transport; A330 MRTT tanker; Tiger attack helicopter; NH90 helicopter.
- Thales Group (France): radar, electronic warfare, sonar, ground systems, naval architectures.
- Leonardo SpA (Italy): AW101 Merlin, AW169, AW139 helicopters; naval; electronics.
- Rheinmetall AG (Germany): Leopard 2 tank; Marder, Puma IFVs; MAN trucks; ammunition. Post-2022 Ukraine war rearmament beneficiary.
- Krauss-Maffei Wegmann (KMW; Germany): Leopard 2; PzH 2000 self-propelled howitzer (Ukraine deployment); Boxer IFV. Merger with French Nexter to form KNDS.
- Nexter (France): Leclerc tank; Caesar self-propelled howitzer (Ukraine deployment); VAB; VBCI.
- Hensoldt (Germany): radar and electronic warfare.
- Saab AB (Sweden): JAS 39 Gripen fighter; GlobalEye AEW&C; Carl Gustaf recoilless rifle; NLAW anti-tank (Ukraine deployment).
- Kongsberg (Norway): Naval Strike Missile; NASAMS partner.

**Israeli:**

- Israel Aerospace Industries (IAI): Heron, Harop loitering munition (deployment in 2020 Second Nagorno-Karabakh War and 2023 offensive), Arrow 3 anti-ballistic missile, Iron Dome partner (with Rafael), Barak missiles, naval.
- Elbit Systems: drones, electronics, artillery, electronic warfare.
- Rafael Advanced Defense Systems: Iron Dome (Gaza-rocket-defence architecture), David's Sling, SPIKE anti-tank missiles, Trophy active protection system.

**Turkish:**

- Baykar (Bayraktar TB2, TB3, Akinci, Kizilelma drones): deployment in 2020 Second Nagorno-Karabakh War, Ukraine war, Libya, Syria, Ethiopia/Tigray operations.
- Aselsan: electronics, communications, electronic warfare.
- TUSAS Turkish Aerospace Industries: Hurjet trainer; helicopters; drones.
- Roketsan: missiles.
- STM, MKE.

**Russian:**

- Almaz-Antey: S-300, S-400, S-500 air-defence systems.
- United Aircraft Corporation (UAC): Sukhoi Su-30/Su-34/Su-35/Su-57; MiG; Tupolev; Ilyushin; Yakovlev.
- Russian Helicopters: Mi-8/17, Mi-24, Mi-28, Ka-52.
- Rostec (parent conglomerate).
- Kalashnikov Concern: AK platform.
- Tactical Missiles Corporation (KTRV): Kh-101, Kh-55.
- Uralvagonzavod: T-72, T-80, T-90, T-14 Armata.
- Rosoboronexport (state arms-export agency).

**Chinese:**

- AVIC (Aviation Industry Corporation of China): J-10, J-16, J-20, J-31/J-35 fighters.
- NORINCO (China North Industries Corporation): ground systems, ammunition.
- CETC (China Electronics Technology Group Corporation): electronics, radar.
- CSSC (China State Shipbuilding Corporation): naval.
- CASC (China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation): missiles, space.
- Poly Group: arms exports.

**Iranian:**

- HESA (Iran Aircraft Manufacturing Industrial Company): Shahed-136 (deployment to Russia for Ukraine war), Shahed-129, Karrar drones.
- Aerospace Industries Organization (AIO): missiles.
- IRGC industrial complex: multi-front missile and drone architecture.

## The MICIMATT political economy

Ray McGovern — the former CIA Soviet Foreign Policy Branch analyst (1963-1990), peace-movement operator — characterised the architecture as MICIMATT: Military-Industrial-Congressional-Intelligence-Media-Academia-Think-Tank Complex. The expanded characterisation operates beyond Eisenhower's military-industrial complex to document the integration of the arms industry, the US Congressional architecture, the US intelligence community (CIA, NSA, DIA, ODNI, FBI domestic operations), the corporate media architecture (defence-contractor-funded news organisations, defence-contractor advertising), the academic architecture (DARPA grants, Pentagon-funded research, intelligence-community campus recruiting), and the think-tank architecture (CSIS, RAND, Brookings, Heritage, AEI, Hoover, Council on Foreign Relations).

**apparatus:**

- Pentagon + DOE nuclear weapons baseline 2024 budget: $886 billion (more than 2x inflation-adjusted 1961 Eisenhower-era level).
- Pentagon failed to pass a clean audit for 7 consecutive years through 2024 (the only major US federal agency unable to do so).
- Defence industry lobbying expenditure: roughly $140 million annually.
- 820 defence-industry lobbyists registered — more than 1 per member of Congress.
- Roughly 2/3 of those lobbyists came through the revolving door from Pentagon or Congress.
- Senator Elizabeth Warren's office report: roughly 700 former high-ranking US government officials, including former generals and admirals, now work for defence contractors. 91% of them became Pentagon lobbyists.
- defence-industry political action committee (PAC) contributions: to Republican and Democratic candidates across congressional districts where defence-contractor jobs operate.

**institutional pathways:**

The specific institutional pathways through which the arms industry has operated as a structural feature of US political-class architecture:

1. **Congressional district employment** — defence contractors systematically distribute subcontracting across maximum congressional districts. The F-35 Joint Strike Fighter programme operates across 45+ US states — operating each US senator and hundreds of US House members as institutional defenders of the programme regardless of performance failures and cost overruns.

2. **Revolving door** — the movement of personnel from Pentagon and Congress to contractors and back. Senator Warren's office report: roughly 700 former US officials now at defence contractors; 91% as lobbyists. The pattern: Pentagon procurement officers retire to contractor positions where they advocate for contracts they had previously overseen.

3. **Campaign contributions** — defence-industry PACs fund both political parties. Lockheed Martin, RTX, General Dynamics, Boeing, Northrop Grumman PACs fund Republican and Democratic candidates with preference for members of Senate Armed Services Committee, House Armed Services Committee, House and Senate Appropriations defence subcommittees.

4. **Lobbying** — $140 million/year defence-industry lobbying. Specific cases: September 2021 Senate effort to limit US arms sales to Saudi Arabia (using US weapons in Yemen war with UNICEF-characterised humanitarian crisis) defeated after weapons-manufacturer lobbying.

5. **Think-tank funding** — defence-contractor funding of think tanks. CSIS, RAND (originally Air Force-created), Brookings, Heritage, AEI, Hoover, Atlantic Council, Wilson Center — receive multi-million-dollar funding from defence contractors and Gulf-state and foreign-government clients. The think-tank output operates as pro-arms-spending advocacy.

6. **Media architecture** — defence-contractor advertising (60 Minutes, Sunday talk shows, news network advertising) and defence-contractor ownership architecture (e.g., NBC parent General Electric defence-industry positioning; CNN parent Time Warner-now-Warner Bros Discovery corporate apparatus; multiple defence-contractor-board members on major news organisation boards). Revolving-door of retired generals and admirals as "military analysts" on news organisations while simultaneously working for defence contractors.

7. **Academic capture** — Pentagon and intelligence-community funding of academic research. DARPA roughly $4 billion annual budget. Defence-contractor-funded chairs at universities. Intelligence-community recruiting on campuses.

8. **Intelligence-community integration** — integration of arms-industry with intelligence-community architecture. Specific examples: Booz Allen Hamilton, Leidos, SAIC, CACI as intelligence-community contractors; Edward Snowden (Booz Allen Hamilton) June 2013 NSA disclosures.

The cumulative effect: the US foreign policy operates continuously through the arms-industry apparatus. The observation by Ray McGovern, William Hartung, Andrew Cockburn, Chalmers Johnson, William Astore, Andrew Bacevich, James Risen, Seymour Melman, David Vine, and operators: the US political-class architecture cannot make foreign-policy decisions without consideration of the arms-industry institutional interest.

## The institutional pathways to war

The pathways through which the arms-industry was the structural driver of US wars:

- **The 1991 Gulf War** — operated the April Glaspie meeting with Saddam Hussein (the 25 July 1990 US institutional ambiguity about Iraqi Kuwait operations) and the Nayirah testimony (the Kuwaiti ambassador's daughter's 10 October 1990 fabricated testimony about Iraqi babies thrown from incubators, coached by Hill & Knowlton PR firm). The Operation Desert Storm produced defence-industry profits and US Persian Gulf apparatus.

- **The 2003 Iraq War** — operated through institutional disinformation about Iraqi weapons of mass destruction (Senate Select Committee on Intelligence findings 2008 and Chilcot Inquiry 2016). The invasion produced war that contributed to millions of Iraqi deaths and US defence-contractor profits including Halliburton subsidiary KBR's LOGCAP contracts (with Vice President Dick Cheney's prior Halliburton CEO position).

- **The 2011 Libya intervention** — operated Hillary Clinton institutional advocacy ("We came, we saw, he died") with characterisations by Robert Gates and others as strategic error. Libyan civil war produced defence-contractor markets across Africa.

- **The Syria intervention** — operated through CIA Operation Timber Sycamore (~2013-2017, roughly $1 billion programme arming Syrian opposition) and Pentagon programmes.

- **The Yemen war** — operated through US-UK arms transfers to Saudi-UAE coalition. The September 2021 Senate effort to limit US arms sales to Saudi Arabia defeated after weapons-manufacturer lobbying.

- **The Ukraine war** — operated through $175+ billion in US authorised supplemental appropriations 2022-2024 producing defence-contractor profits. The US weapons inventory transferred to Ukraine and the European NATO rearmament (+105% 2015-2024 per SIPRI; +143% 2016-2020 to 2021-2025) operates as rearmament-driver of the US and European defence-industry sectors.

- **The Gaza war** — operated through $21.7 billion in first 2 years US military aid to Israel (Costs of War Brown University) on top of $3.8 billion/year baseline. The US weapons used in Gaza operations including Mark 84 2,000-pound bombs, BLU-109 bunker busters, JDAM precision-guidance kits, GBU-39 small-diameter bombs, M982 Excalibur GPS-guided shells (Chapter 5) operate as defence-industry profits.

- **The May 2025 Saudi Arabia deal** — Trump-administration $142 billion arms deal characterised by Trump administration as "the largest defence sales agreement in history." The Trump-administration arms-sale architecture across Gulf monarchies (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman) operates as defence-industry record-revenue driver.

The institutional pattern: crisis architecture; emergency-response institutional rhetoric; emergency-appropriations institutional bypass of normal budgetary procedures; defence-contractor profits; crisis continuation; emergency-appropriations renewal. The structural feature: continuous war operates continuously to benefit of the defence-industry architecture.

## The structural reckoning

The weapons industry is the signature institutional beneficiary of the proxy-war architecture (Chapter 7) and the specific wars (Chapters 1-6).

The combined record:
- Global military spending 2024: $2,718 billion (record).
- Global military spending 2025: $2,887 billion (record).
- SIPRI Top 100 arms producers 2024 revenue: $679 billion (record).
- US Pentagon contracts 2020-2024 to private firms: $2.4 trillion.
- Top 5 US contractors 2020-2024: $771 billion combined.
- Post-9/11 US wars contractor revenue: $2 trillion combined.
- Multi-decade combined deaths from wars financed by the arms-industry profit architecture: tens of millions.

The arms industry is presented as a necessary national-security architecture providing military readiness against specific external threats. The underlying machinery is the structural feature: the arms-industry apparatus is the structural driver of US and Western foreign policy through specific institutional pathways — congressional district employment, revolving door, campaign contributions, lobbying, think-tank funding, media architecture, academic capture, intelligence-community integration — that have captured Western political-class architectures.

The institutional beneficiaries: Lockheed Martin, RTX, General Dynamics, Boeing, Northrop Grumman ("the Big Five"); BAE Systems, Airbus, Thales, Leonardo, Rheinmetall, KMW/KNDS, Nexter, Saab, Kongsberg (European); IAI, Elbit, Rafael (Israeli); Baykar, Aselsan, TUSAS (Turkish); Almaz-Antey, UAC, Rostec, Kalashnikov, KTRV, Uralvagonzavod (Russian); AVIC, NORINCO, CETC, CSSC, CASC (Chinese); HESA, AIO (Iranian) — and the multi-million-shareholder ownership architectures of these contractors, dominated by investors (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street ownership across US Big Five and European architectures).

The institutional victims: the tens of millions of war dead; the hundreds of millions of displaced; the opportunity cost of the $2.4-trillion-over-five-years Pentagon-contractor allocation that could otherwise have operated alternative institutional architectures of healthcare, education, infrastructure, housing, climate, and humanitarian assistance.

The Eisenhower 17 January 1961 warning has operated prophetically. The "unwarranted influence" Eisenhower warned against has become the structural feature. The "disastrous rise of misplaced power" Eisenhower warned against has materialised. The question — operated across peace-movement operators, anti-arms-trade operators (CAAT, World Beyond War, Mennonite Central Committee, American Friends Service Committee, Quaker United Nations Office, Catholic Worker Movement, Pax Christi, Veterans for Peace, Code Pink), academic operators (Hartung, Cockburn, Vine, Bacevich, Johnson, Risen, Melman, Astore, McGovern), journalistic operators, Pope Francis multi-year peace operators — is whether the arms-industry architecture can be reorganised. The institutional answer per the evidentiary record is contested. The fact: the weapons-industry profit architecture operates continuously through the date of writing.

## Bridge to Part XIII

Part XII (Chapters 1-8) has the war architecture: the Cold War proxy wars; the post-Cold-War US wars; the Iran-Iraq War; the Ukraine war; the Gaza war; the unseen wars (Sudan, Sahel, Tigray, Myanmar, DRC, Nagorno-Karabakh); the proxy-war architecture; and the weapons industry that has profited from the combined wars. The combined death toll across the wars in Part XII: tens of millions. The combined arms-industry profit: multi-trillions of dollars.

Part XIII synthesises the patterns across Volumes One and Two. The financial system (Volume One: *Behind the Mask: The Demon They Call God*) and the state apparatus (this volume) operate together as the architecture of capital — the signature institutional system that produces the war architecture, the inequality architecture, the environmental destruction architecture, and the management of populations across the global system. Chapter 1 of Part XIII documents the synthesis.

# Part XIII Chapter 1: The Devil

## The mechanism refracted

Volume One named the devil. The devil is not a person, not an institution, not a conspiracy. The devil is the four-century mechanism that began in 1694 with the Bank of England charter — William Paterson's £1.2 million loan to William III to fund the Nine Years' War — and that operates today through the central banks, the asset-management trinity (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street), the dollar-denominated petrodollar architecture, and the documented post-2008 Federal Reserve balance-sheet expansion of more than $7 trillion.

The mechanism's defining trick: money is created as debt. Sovereign states do not issue their own currency directly; they borrow it from private banking institutions at interest, then tax their populations to service the debt. The banking institutions create the principal as a book entry; they do not create the interest. The interest must therefore be extracted from elsewhere — from labour, from land, from resource extraction, from war reparations, from currency arbitrage, from speculative asset markets, from the foreclosure of the borrowers. The mechanism is mathematically guaranteed to extract because the interest cannot be repaid from the principal it was created against.

Volume One documented this mechanism's operational architecture: the 1694 founding; the 1789 First Bank of the United States under Hamilton; the Jacksonian Bank War 1832-1836; the 1913 Federal Reserve Act after the November 1910 Jekyll Island meeting (Paul Warburg of Kuhn Loeb, Frank Vanderlip of National City, Henry Davison of J.P. Morgan, Charles Norton of First National, Benjamin Strong, Senator Nelson Aldrich); the 1944 Bretton Woods system; the 15 August 1971 Nixon shock; the 1973-1974 petrodollar deal with Saudi Arabia; the 1980s deregulation under Reagan and Volcker; the 1999 Gramm-Leach-Bliley repeal of Glass-Steagall under Clinton; the 2008 crisis bailouts; the post-2008 Federal Reserve balance-sheet expansion; the 2020 COVID-era Fed-BlackRock mandate.

Volume One named the contemporary operators: Larry Fink (BlackRock); Jamie Dimon (JPMorgan); David Solomon (Goldman Sachs); the Treasury-Goldman Sachs revolving door (Robert Rubin 1995-1999, Henry Paulson 2006-2009, Steven Mnuchin 2017-2021); the Federal Reserve principals (Jerome Powell, Janet Yellen, Ben Bernanke); the City of London architecture (Andrew Bailey at the Bank of England); the ECB principals (Mario Draghi 2011-2019, Christine Lagarde 2019-); the IMF (Kristalina Georgieva); the World Bank (Ajay Banga); the BIS (Agustín Carstens).

Volume Two operates from that proven baseline. The mechanism Volume One named — the four-century debt-money architecture — does not operate in a vacuum. It operates through human beings in specific institutional positions. The state apparatus is one of those institutional positions. The state apparatus does not, in the documented record, operate as a check on the mechanism. The state apparatus, as documented across Parts III-XII of this volume, is the extension of the mechanism. The state apparatus collects the taxes that service the debt. The state apparatus enforces the property rights that secure the collateral. The state apparatus deploys the military that opens new markets, secures the petrodollar architecture, and prevents the alternatives. The state apparatus manages the populations through whom the extraction operates.

This is the through-line of the series. Capital — meaning the four-century debt-money mechanism Volume One named, the asset-management trinity Volume One named, the central-bank architecture Volume One named — is the devil. The state is one of the masks the devil wears.

## The four-century arc through the state apparatus

The mechanism Volume One named began in 1694. The state apparatus through which it operates began earlier, in some forms much earlier (per Part I of this volume), but its modern fusion with the mechanism dates to the same window.

The 1694 Bank of England charter was not, strictly, a banking transaction. It was a fiscal-military transaction. The Crown needed money for the Nine Years' War against France. Paterson and his syndicate offered to advance £1.2 million in exchange for the right to issue banknotes backed by the loan. The bank's first directors included men who simultaneously held positions in the East India Company, the Royal African Company (the slave-trading monopoly), and the Treasury. The state apparatus and the banking apparatus were not adversaries who had reached an agreement. They were the same institutional class, sitting on multiple boards, executing a coordinated capture of the sovereign's monetary function.

This is the pattern. Every subsequent expansion of the mechanism Volume One documented operated through the state apparatus. The 1789 First Bank of the United States required Hamilton at Treasury, Washington at the presidency, and the Federalist faction in Congress. The 1913 Federal Reserve Act required Wilson, Aldrich, Owen, and Glass — the 1913 Senate and House operators who carried the Jekyll Island plan into legislation. The 1944 Bretton Woods system required Roosevelt at the White House, Morgenthau at Treasury, Harry Dexter White as Treasury's lead negotiator, and the post-WWII US architecture. The 1971 Nixon shock required Nixon at the White House, John Connally at Treasury, Paul Volcker as Treasury Under-Secretary for Monetary Affairs, Arthur Burns at the Fed, and the Camp David meeting of 15 August 1971. The 1973-1974 petrodollar deal required Kissinger as Secretary of State and the Saudi-US apparatus. The 2008 bailouts required Bush at the White House, Paulson at Treasury, Bernanke at the Fed, and the bipartisan congressional architecture that passed TARP. The 2020 COVID Fed-BlackRock mandate required Powell at the Fed, Mnuchin at Treasury, and the March 2020 apparatus.

The same names recur. Volcker at Treasury (1971); Volcker at the Fed (1979-1987); Volcker on Obama's Economic Recovery Advisory Board (2009-2011). Paulson at Goldman (1999-2006); Paulson at Treasury (2006-2009). Rubin at Goldman (until 1992); Rubin at the National Economic Council (1993-1995); Rubin at Treasury (1995-1999); Rubin at Citigroup (1999-2009). Yellen at the Council of Economic Advisers (1997-1999); Yellen at the San Francisco Fed (2004-2010); Yellen as Fed Vice-Chair (2010-2014); Yellen as Fed Chair (2014-2018); Yellen at Treasury (2021-2025). Bessent at Soros (1991-2000, 2011-2015); Bessent at his own Key Square (2015-); Bessent at Treasury (January 2025-).

The revolving door is not a leak in the system. The revolving door is the system. The same individuals operate the central banks, the asset managers, and the Treasury department interchangeably, because the central banks, the asset managers, and the Treasury department are not separate institutions. They are the operational arms of the same four-century mechanism.

## The state's three functions for the mechanism

The state apparatus performs three operational functions for the mechanism Volume One named.

**First, the state collects.** The mechanism creates money as debt; the debt must be serviced; the servicing requires extraction. The state apparatus collects the extraction through taxation. The contemporary state apparatuses — the IRS in the United States, HMRC in the United Kingdom, the Bundeszentralamt für Steuern in Germany, the tax authorities across the OECD — operate as the collection arm of the mechanism. The taxes collected are not, documented, used primarily for the population's benefit. They are used to service the sovereign debt held by the banking institutions and the asset managers. The 2024 US federal debt service exceeded $880 billion — roughly equal to the documented baseline Pentagon budget Eisenhower's military-industrial complex now consumes (Chapter 8). The two largest line items in the US federal budget are debt service and military spending. Both flow to the architecture Volume One named.

**Second, the state enforces.** The mechanism extracts through the property-rights regime. The property-rights regime is enforced by the state apparatus: the courts, the police, the prisons, the bailiffs, the foreclosure architecture. When a borrower defaults, the state apparatus seizes the collateral. When a debtor cannot pay, the state apparatus garnishes wages, attaches assets, evicts from housing. The 2008 foreclosure wave in the United States produced roughly 10 million home foreclosures across 2007-2014 per RealtyTrac. The state apparatus — sheriffs serving notices, courts processing summary judgements, banks documented to have committed mass robo-signing fraud (Wells Fargo, JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, Ally Financial — the 2012 National Mortgage Settlement defendants paying $25 billion) — executed the foreclosures. The banking institutions that had created the underlying securitised mortgages, and the asset managers (including BlackRock subsidiary Acquired during the crisis) that bought them at distress prices, captured the housing stock. The state apparatus was the executor.

**Third, the state opens markets and prevents alternatives.** The mechanism requires continuous expansion. New markets must be opened; existing markets must be deepened; alternative arrangements must be prevented. The state apparatus deploys the military for this purpose. Part XII of this volume documented the war architecture: the documented Cold War proxy wars (15-25 million dead); the documented post-Cold-War US wars (2-3 million dead); the documented Iran-Iraq War (1-1.5 million dead); the documented Ukraine war (400-550K dead); the documented Gaza war (75-100K+ dead); the documented unseen wars (800K-1.1 million dead); and the documented weapons-industry profit architecture ($679 billion 2024 Top 100 revenue per SIPRI; $2.4 trillion 2020-2024 Pentagon contracts to private firms per Quincy Institute). The wars are not, as documented, acciden of mismanaged foreign policy. The wars are the third function. Every named war Volume Two documented in Part XII operated to open or defend or expand a market for the mechanism Volume One named. Petroleum: Iraq 2003, Libya 2011, Syria 2011-, Yemen 2014-, Iran. Pipeline routes: Afghanistan, Syria, Ukraine post-Nord Stream. Uranium: Niger, Mali. Lithium: Bolivia coup 2019, Afghanistan reserves. Rare-earth minerals: DRC, Myanmar, Ukraine. Agricultural land: Sudan, Ethiopia. Shipping lanes: Yemen, Red Sea. And geopolitical denial of alternative monetary arrangements: the post-2022 sanctions on Russian central-bank reserves; the multi-year sanctions on Iran, Venezuela, Cuba, Syria, North Korea; the US apparatus against the BRICS de-dollarisation efforts.

The state's three functions — collection, enforcement, market-opening — together constitute the extension of the mechanism Volume One named. The state is the devil's hands.

## The captured state

Volume One named the capture of the press, the universities, the philanthropic infrastructure, the regulatory bodies, the consensus-knowledge apparatus, and the political class. Volume Two operates from that proven baseline.

The political-class capture is the specific Volume Two condition. Across Parts III-XI this volume documented:

- The American political-class architecture: the Biden administration's financial-class integration (Larry Fink, Brian Deese ex-BlackRock to NEC Director, Wally Adeyemo ex-BlackRock to Deputy Treasury Secretary); the Trump-2 administration's financial-class integration (Scott Bessent at Treasury, Howard Lutnick at Commerce, Elon Musk's prior DOGE role); the congressional architecture across both parties; AIPAC's named lobbying disclosures and campaign contributions architecture (Dossier 17).
- The Atlantic political-class architecture: Starmer's Tony Blair Institute connections; the UK Conservative architecture under Sunak then Badenoch; the European Commission architecture under von der Leyen.
- The South Asian political-class architecture: Modi-Adani-Ambani integration (Dossier 21-22); the RSS-VHP institutional architecture (Dossier 23); the Pakistani military-civilian architecture (Dossier 24-26); the Bangladeshi architecture (Dossier 27).

The capture is not a metaphor. The capture is a documented arrangement: specific named donors, specific named lobbying disclosures, specific named foreign-agent registrations, specific named post-government commercial appointments, specific named institutional revolving-door movements.

The captured state cannot, documented, function as a check on the mechanism that captured it. The captured state functions as the mechanism's operational arm. Every named war in Part XII; every named bailout in Volume One; every named regulatory failure documented across the two volumes; every named institutional refusal to prosecute the banking principals after the 2008 crisis (no bank CEO was prosecuted for systemic fraud; the individual prosecution was Bernard Madoff, whose Ponzi scheme operated outside the systemic architecture) — all of these are not failures of an independent state apparatus. They are the documented operations of a captured state apparatus working as designed.

## The mask and the operation

The performance is the documented institutional characterisation of the state as the public's collective instrument — the democratic state, the welfare state, the security state, the rules-based international order, the liberal-democratic consensus. The reality behind the mask is the record: the Federal Reserve balance-sheet expansion of $7+ trillion since 2008 transferred to the asset managers; the post-2008 bailout architecture that protected the bank principals while foreclosing on 10 million homes; the post-2020 COVID architecture that produced roughly $5 trillion in additional Federal Reserve balance-sheet expansion while small businesses closed at record rates; the wars producing tens of millions of dead and trillions of dollars of weapons-industry profit; the tax policy that has produced documented upward redistribution culminating in the 2024 Oxfam finding that the wealthiest 1% captured roughly 63% of the post-pandemic global wealth gains.

The display is the state. The operation is the mechanism. The state is the devil's hands. The devil is the four-century mechanism Volume One named. The devil is capital.

## The contemporary operators of the integration

The contemporary individuals who operate the integration between Volume One's financial architecture and Volume Two's state apparatus are named on the documented public record.

**Larry Fink.** Founded BlackRock 1988. CEO since founding. BlackRock AUM exceeded $10 trillion in 2024 per BlackRock's own filings. The 2020 Federal Reserve mandate to BlackRock for the corporate-bond purchase programme during the COVID crisis is documented in the 23 March 2020 Federal Reserve announcement and the BlackRock Financial Markets Advisory contracts. Fink has held the documented Davos World Economic Forum board position multi-year. Fink has met with the US Treasury Secretaries (Mnuchin, Yellen, Bessent), the Federal Reserve Chairs (Bernanke, Yellen, Powell), the Presidents (Obama, Trump, Biden, Trump again) across the documented revolving-door architecture.

**Jamie Dimon.** JPMorgan CEO since 2005. The 2008 crisis beneficiary of the documented Bear Stearns and Washington Mutual acquisitions on favourable Federal Reserve terms. The 2024 JPMorgan total assets exceeded $4.2 trillion. Dimon has held the documented Business Roundtable and multi-year Treasury advisory positions across administrations.

**Jerome Powell.** Federal Reserve Chair since 2018. Re-nominated by Biden 2022. Previously: Carlyle Group partner (2010-2012); Treasury Under Secretary for Domestic Finance under Bush (1992-1993). Powell is currently navigating the documented Trump-2 administration pressure to reduce interest rates while documented 2025 inflation data has fluctuated.

**Scott Bessent.** Treasury Secretary since January 2025 under Trump 2. Previously: Soros Fund Management partner (1991-2000 and 2011-2015); founder of Key Square Group 2015. Documented 2025 architecture: documented tariff implementation against China and Mexico; documented dollar policy.

**Christine Lagarde.** ECB President since 2019. Previously: IMF Managing Director (2011-2019); French Finance Minister (2007-2011); French Trade Minister (2005-2007); Baker McKenzie chairman (1999-2005). Documented multi-year tax-fraud trial in France 2016 concerning the Bernard Tapie arbitration (acquitted but found "negligent" by the Cour de Justice de la République).

**Mario Draghi.** Italian Prime Minister 2021-2022. Previously: ECB President (2011-2019); Bank of Italy Governor (2005-2011); Goldman Sachs International vice-chairman (2002-2005); Italian Treasury Director General (1991-2001). The 2011-2012 "whatever it takes" architecture defending the euro through the sovereign-debt crisis.

**Mark Carney.** Canadian Prime Minister since March 2025. Previously: UN Special Envoy for Climate Action and Finance (2020-2025); Bank of England Governor (2013-2020); Bank of Canada Governor (2008-2013); Goldman Sachs (1988-2003). The multi-year Carney architecture across Goldman, Bank of Canada, Bank of England, UN climate-finance, and now the Canadian state.

**Tony Blair.** UK Prime Minister 1997-2007. He subsequently became a paid adviser to JP Morgan. Tony Blair Institute funded by Larry Ellison (Oracle), Bill Gates, Saudi Arabian sovereign architecture. The multi-year post-PM Blair architecture operates across multi-year Iraq War accountability (Chilcot Inquiry 2016) and multi-year private-sector advisory.

**David Cameron.** UK PM 2010-2016. Subsequent Greensill Capital scandal 2021. UK Foreign Secretary 2023-2024 under Sunak. Currently advisory architecture.

**George Osborne.** UK Chancellor 2010-2016. Subsequent BlackRock part-time appointment 2017. Subsequent Evening Standard editor 2017-2020. Subsequent advisory architecture. The multi-year Osborne architecture across multi-year UK fiscal-austerity policy and multi-year private-sector advisory captures the multi-year revolving-door pattern.

These are not isolated individuals. They are the connective tissue between the mechanism Volume One named and the state apparatus Volume Two named. They move between Goldman Sachs and the Treasury, between BlackRock and the White House, between the Bank of England and Number 10, between the ECB and the Italian Prime Ministership, between the Bank of Canada and the Canadian Prime Ministership. They are not corrupted by the system. They are the system.

## What "the Devil" means in this argument

The series uses "Devil," "Demon," and "the system" with specific operational meaning.

The Devil — capital — is the mechanism. Not a person. Not a cabal. A four-century apparatus that operates through specific named individuals in specific named positions but does not depend on any one of them. If Larry Fink retired tomorrow, the mechanism would continue. If Jamie Dimon were prosecuted tomorrow, the mechanism would continue. If the Federal Reserve were nationalised tomorrow, the mechanism would adapt and continue, because the mechanism's defining feature is not its current personnel but its mathematical structure: money is created as debt at interest; the interest cannot be repaid from the principal; extraction is therefore mathematically guaranteed; the extraction operates wherever the mechanism reaches; the mechanism's reach has been global since at least 1971.

The Demons — the masks the Devil wears — are the operational surfaces. Volume One named the financial-system mask. This volume names the state-apparatus mask. The Church and the Knowing will follow.

The system — the integrated whole — is the Devil operating through the four masks across the press, the captured universities, the captured philanthropic infrastructure, the captured regulatory bodies, the captured consensus-knowledge apparatus, and the captured political class.

This argument is not metaphorical. The argument is operational: the mechanism is real; the masks are real; the captures are real; the named operators are real; the named deaths and named extractions are real; the documentary record proves each named element. The four-volume core does not propose a theory of the Devil. The four-volume core documents the Devil's operational architecture across four institutional surfaces.

## The state demon named

Volume Two's specific contribution is the state demon — the extension of the mechanism through the state apparatus, dossiered across this volume in roughly 27+ specific named political-class operators (Dossier 1 through Dossier 27 to date), plus the war architecture (Part XII), plus the weapons-industry beneficiaries (Part XII Chapter 8), plus the pathways through which the political class is the captured class.

The state demon's masks are familiar. The legitimacy masks: democracy, the rule of law, national security, humanitarian intervention, the rules-based international order, and the multilateral apparatus. The state-form masks: the welfare state, the developmental state, the post-colonial liberation state, and the post-communist transition state. The bloc-architecture masks: the G7 architecture, the G20 architecture, and the BRICS architecture (which positions itself against the G7 architecture but operates documented multi-year through the same four-century debt-money mechanism). The institutional-architecture masks: the UN architecture, the World Bank-IMF architecture (which is Volume One financial architecture functioning as state-architecture extension), the NATO architecture, the Collective Security Treaty Organization architecture (Russian-led), the Shanghai Cooperation Organization architecture (Chinese-led), the Belt and Road architecture, and the G7-vs-BRICS narrative that frames inter-imperial competition as ideological competition.

The state demon's operations are also familiar by the end of Volume Two: tax collection servicing sovereign debt to the named asset managers; enforcement of the property-rights regime; the war architecture for market opening, alternative prevention, and weapons-industry profit; the regulatory architecture protecting the institutional positions; the revolving-door architecture moving personnel between the central banks, the asset managers, the treasuries, and the state apparatuses; the political-class capture through named donors, named lobbyists, and named foreign-agent registrations; the media architecture managing the population through the captured press and the captured platforms.

The state demon is the second mask. The Devil — capital, the mechanism Volume One named — is what wears it.

## Bridge to Chapter 2

Chapter 1 of this Part named the through-line. Capital is the Devil; the state is one of the masks. Chapter 2 names the demons — the political-class operators of this volume, characterised together as a class, with the named pattern across the multi-named individuals dossiered through Parts III-XI. The class is the layer; the class operates the mask; the mask sits on the Devil.
