# Part XIII Chapter 2: The Demons

## The class

The 27 dossiers Volume Two has produced through Parts III-XI do not document 27 individuals. They document one class — the contemporary global political class — through 27 representative members. The class shares a coherent set of features, regardless of which institutional position any individual member happens to occupy.

The features are not random. They are what the state apparatus selects for, and what the four-century mechanism Volume One named requires of its operational personnel. The class is not a conspiracy. The class is a recruitment pattern. The patterns governing entry, advancement, and retention are mechanical. They produce a class with the characteristics the mechanism needs and weed out the characteristics it does not.

The record names the class together — the demons of the state mask, characterised by their common features rather than by their individual differences.

## Feature one: wealth

The contemporary political class is rich.

US senators: 51% of US senators have net worth exceeding $1 million per OpenSecrets. The Congressional millionaire share has exceeded 50% continuously since the 2010s. The median net worth in the 117th Congress was roughly $1 million per Roll Call's analysis — twenty times the US household median.

The upper tier is staggeringly wealthy. Donald Trump: roughly $5-7 billion per Forbes 2024. Elon Musk (currently the world's richest person at roughly $400 billion per Forbes 2025; DOGE figure in early Trump 2 before public falling-out): the wealthiest individual in human history by nominal valuation. Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary in Trump 2; previously Cantor Fitzgerald CEO): roughly $2 billion. Vivek Ramaswamy (DOGE co-lead alongside Musk): roughly $1 billion. Scott Bessent (Treasury Secretary in Trump 2; Soros and Key Square hedge funds): roughly $700 million. Mark Carney (Canadian PM since March 2025; Bank of England, Bank of Canada, Goldman Sachs): roughly $10 million. Tony Blair (UK PM 1997-2007; subsequent paid advisory): roughly $80 million per *Sunday Times Rich List* multi-year estimates.

The pattern is not coincidental. The mechanism Volume One named requires that the operational personnel of the state apparatus be drawn from the class that already owns the assets the mechanism generates. The state apparatus exists to defend those assets, to enforce the property regime, and to deploy the military for the asset-owning class's geopolitical and resource-access needs (Part XII). The state apparatus would not be reliably defending the assets of a class to which its personnel did not themselves belong. It therefore recruits from inside the asset-owning class.

The handful of low-net-worth exceptions are instructive. Mike Johnson (US House Speaker since October 2023): net worth roughly $1-2 million, modest for the rank. Johnson's modesty has not produced any institutional friction with the wealth-defending function of his Speakership. Narendra Modi (Indian PM since 2014): declared assets roughly $360,000 per India Today 2024 — among the lowest of any major figure dossiered. Modi's modesty has not produced any friction with the Adani-Ambani fortune concentration (roughly $100 billion combined) that has accelerated under his government. Asim Munir (Pakistani Army Chief since November 2022): not as personally wealthy. The Pakistani military's economic empire (Fauji Foundation, Army Welfare Trust, the military-owned business apparatus) operates through Munir's position regardless of his personal asset declarations.

The pattern: personal wealth correlates with class function but does not determine it. The function is to defend the property regime. The operators are recruited from inside it, with occasional exceptions whose modesty does not produce friction with the function.

## Feature two: educational concentration

The contemporary political class is educated through a narrow set of institutions.

US Senate: roughly 75% hold graduate degrees per Pew; roughly 50% hold law degrees. The Cabinet under Trump 2 (the 2025 incoming personnel) concentrates Ivy League and elite private universities heavily. Across the post-1945 US political class, the concentration of Harvard, Yale, Princeton, Stanford, Columbia, Penn, MIT, Chicago, plus a small number of additional elite institutions (Duke, Georgetown, Northwestern, Cornell, Berkeley) approaches statistical impossibility relative to the US college-graduate population.

The UK figures concentrate Oxford and Cambridge to extreme levels. The post-2010 UK Conservative Cabinets have repeatedly run two-thirds Oxbridge. Keir Starmer (UK PM since July 2024): Oxford. David Cameron (UK PM 2010-2016): Oxford. Tony Blair (1997-2007): Oxford. Theresa May (2016-2019): Oxford. Boris Johnson (2019-2022): Oxford. Rishi Sunak (2022-2024): Oxford. The pattern operates across decades.

The French figures concentrate ENA/INSP (École nationale d'administration, replaced by Institut national du service public in 2022) and Sciences Po. Every post-1958 French president except Nicolas Sarkozy was ENA-trained or Sciences-Po-trained, with multiple presidents holding both.

The Indian figures concentrate more diversely, but the bureaucracy and elite legal cohort concentrates JNU, DU, IITs, IIMs heavily.

The Pakistani figures concentrate the Pakistan Military Academy, the Lahore University of Management Sciences (LUMS), and Oxford/Cambridge for the civilian wing.

The educational concentration is not a meritocratic phenomenon. It is a recruitment phenomenon. The elite universities operate as filtering and credentialing institutions — selecting for class-compatible behaviour, training students in class-compatible discourse, and producing graduates with the network connections the political career requires. Volume One documented the capture of the universities themselves (the Sackler buildings, the Koch buildings, the Epstein donations, the hedge-fund endowment architecture, the defence-and-intelligence money in the AI labs). Volume Two extends the documentation: the universities that captured the donors produce the personnel that captures the state.

## Feature three: religious overrepresentation

The contemporary political class is religious — disproportionately so relative to the population it claims to represent.

US Congress 119th (2025-2027): roughly 88% Christian per Pew January 2025, compared to roughly 63% of US adult population per the same source. The Christian overrepresentation is consistent since the post-1945 period.

US Senate 119th: roughly 91% Christian.

US Supreme Court: 6 of 9 justices Catholic (Roberts, Thomas, Alito, Sotomayor, Kavanaugh, Barrett); 2 Jewish (Kagan, Sotomayor — with Sotomayor identifying both as Catholic and as having Puerto Rican heritage); 1 Episcopalian (Gorsuch, raised Catholic, currently Episcopalian); 1 Jackson. The Catholic overrepresentation on SCOTUS is operating against a US Catholic population share of roughly 20%.

The Indian leadership operates through the Hindutva architecture: the BJP, the RSS, the VHP (Dossier 21-23). The Hindutva architecture characterises Indian national identity as at its root Hindu — operating against the roughly 14% Indian Muslim population, the roughly 2% Indian Christian population, the roughly 2% Indian Sikh population, and the diverse remainder.

The Pakistani leadership operates through Sunni Muslim dominance, with tensions with Shia and Ahmadi minorities. The Ahmadi community has been constitutionally classified as non-Muslim since 1974 and faces persecution.

The Israeli leadership operates through the Jewish-state architecture, with institutional differentiation between Jewish citizens, non-Jewish Israeli Arab citizens, Palestinian residents of the West Bank, Palestinian residents of Gaza, and Palestinian residents of East Jerusalem. The Smotrich-Ben-Gvir coalition under Netanyahu institutionalises the religious-Zionist framework (Part XII Ch5).

The religious overrepresentation is not a quirk. It is a class characteristic. The political class is the moral-legitimating function of the state apparatus; the religious affiliation operates as the public moral signal. The religious affiliation also operates as the class's distinguishing feature from the populations it administers, who are more diverse, more secular in many cases, more often unaffiliated.

## Feature four: age

The contemporary political class is old.

The 119th US Senate median age: roughly 64. The post-2020 US presidential elections featured oldest-in-history candidates: Joe Biden (78 on January 2021 inauguration, 81 on January 2025 exit); Donald Trump (74 on January 2021 inauguration, 78 on January 2025 inauguration of second term, will be 82 on January 2029 if completes second term); Hillary Clinton (would have been 69 on January 2017 inauguration). Multiple US senators are in their 80s: Mitch McConnell (born 20 February 1942, 84 currently); Chuck Grassley (born 17 September 1933, 92 currently — the oldest sitting US senator). Bernie Sanders (born 8 September 1941, 84 currently).

The pattern is not US-specific. Multiple global figures are above conventional retirement age: Vladimir Putin (born 7 October 1952, 73 currently); Ali Khamenei was 86 at his February 2026 death (Part XII Ch5); Mahmoud Abbas (Palestinian Authority President, born 26 March 1935, 91 currently); Xi Jinping is 72; Recep Tayyip Erdoğan is 72; Joe Biden left office at 82.

Age is a feature because longevity-in-position is what the structure rewards. The class operates through positions, relationships, and accumulation of network capital. The political career, in the record, is not a young person's career. The young person enters through a credentialing pathway (the elite university, the law firm, the consulting firm, the Wall Street firm, the congressional staff position), accumulates the network for two to three decades, and emerges into position around age 50-60.

The position itself is then retained for as long as possible. Mitch McConnell entered the US Senate in 1985 and served as Senate Republican leader from 2007-2025 — eighteen years in leadership. Nancy Pelosi entered the US House in 1987 and served as Speaker 2007-2011 and 2019-2023 plus minority leader for 12 additional years. The class does not retire. Members are removed only by death, by electoral defeat, or by intra-class realignment.

## Feature five: revolving-door integration

The contemporary political class moves between the state, the banks, the asset managers, the law firms, the defence contractors, the consulting firms, the media organisations, the universities, and the think tanks. The institutional positions are not separate careers. They are stops on a single career.

Specific examples from across the dossiered figures:

- Robert Rubin: Goldman Sachs (until 1992); National Economic Council Director (1993-1995); Treasury Secretary (1995-1999); Citigroup (1999-2009).
- Henry Paulson: Goldman Sachs CEO (1999-2006); Treasury Secretary (2006-2009); subsequent advisory positions.
- Steven Mnuchin: Goldman Sachs (1985-2002); film and finance positions; Treasury Secretary (2017-2021); Liberty Strategic Capital founder 2021.
- Janet Yellen: Council of Economic Advisers Chair (1997-1999); San Francisco Fed President (2004-2010); Fed Vice-Chair (2010-2014); Fed Chair (2014-2018); Treasury Secretary (2021-2025).
- Mark Carney: Goldman Sachs (1988-2003); Bank of Canada Governor (2008-2013); Bank of England Governor (2013-2020); UN Climate Finance Envoy (2020-2025); Canadian PM (March 2025-).
- Mario Draghi: Italian Treasury Director General (1991-2001); Goldman Sachs International Vice-Chairman (2002-2005); Bank of Italy Governor (2005-2011); ECB President (2011-2019); Italian PM (2021-2022).
- Christine Lagarde: Baker McKenzie Chairman (1999-2005); French Trade Minister (2005-2007); French Finance Minister (2007-2011); IMF Managing Director (2011-2019); ECB President (2019-).
- George Osborne: UK Chancellor (2010-2016); BlackRock part-time appointment (2017); Evening Standard editor (2017-2020); Robey Warshaw (2021-).
- Tony Blair: UK PM (1997-2007); JPMorgan paid adviser (2008); Tony Blair Institute (2016-); foundation funded by Ellison, Gates, Saudi Arabia.
- José Manuel Barroso: European Commission President (2004-2014); Goldman Sachs International Chairman (2016-).
- Neelie Kroes: European Commissioner for Competition (2004-2010); Digital Agenda (2010-2014); Uber adviser 2016; Bahamas Leaks-offshore entities.
- John Kerry: US Senator (1985-2013); Secretary of State (2013-2017); Climate Envoy (2021-2024); subsequent private-sector positions.
- Hillary Clinton: Senator (2001-2009); Secretary of State (2009-2013); paid speeches to Goldman Sachs and other financial institutions ($21.7 million in speaking fees 2013-2015 per CNN cumulative reporting).

The revolving door is as the system, not as a corruption of the system. The system is designed so that the people who staff the state apparatus are the same people who staff the banks, the asset managers, and the corporations the state apparatus regulates. The revolving door produces personnel who understand the system from inside multiple positions and who are reliably loyal to the class function regardless of which institutional position they currently occupy.

## Feature six: race, ethnicity, gender

The contemporary political class has been as racially, ethnically, and gender-monolithic relative to the populations administered, though this has shifted somewhat in recent decades.

The 119th US Congress: 25% non-white per Pew — record high but below the roughly 42% non-white share of the US population. The US Senate: roughly 12% non-white. The US House: roughly 28% non-white. The 119th Congress: roughly 28% women (~150 of 535 members) — below the roughly 51% female share of the US population.

The UK House of Commons post-2024 election: roughly 40% women — closer to population parity than the US Congress.

The Indian Lok Sabha 2024: roughly 14% women — well below the roughly 49% female share of the Indian population. The Indian Cabinet 2025: a small handful of women in major portfolios.

The Pakistani National Assembly: roughly 20% women (most through reserved seats).

The Saudi-Emirati-Qatari leaderships: 0% women in decision-making positions multi-year.

The pattern: the class has been overrepresenting men and white/dominant-ethnic citizens of the populations it administers, with gradual incremental change that has not approached representational parity. The relevant question is not whether the composition changes at the margin; the relevant question is whether changes in composition affect the function. The record across the Black, Latino, Asian-American, female, and gender-non-conforming members who have been admitted is that they have been reliably integrated into the function. The class admits members who can perform the function regardless of demographic characteristics. The class does not change in response to demographic admission.

## Feature seven: foreign-state and special-interest alignment

The contemporary political class is aligned with specific foreign states, specific industries, and specific donor networks through financial relationships.

AIPAC architecture (Dossier 17): the American Israel Public Affairs Committee plus its associated United Democracy Project (UDP) super-PAC spent over $100 million in the 2024 US election cycle per OpenSecrets — a record for AIPAC spending. The 2024 cycle saw AIPAC-backed challengers defeat Jamaal Bowman (D-NY) and Cori Bush (D-MO) in primaries — the two Democratic House members most vocal in criticism of Israeli operations in Gaza. The defeats produced an institutional signal across the US political class: criticism of Israeli operations carries campaign-financial consequences regardless of constituency preference.

The Saudi-UAE-Qatari architecture (Volume One sources cited): Gulf-state funding of US think tanks (Brookings, Atlantic Council, Center for American Progress, CSIS), think-tank-affiliated former officials, US universities, and US post-government advisory positions. Tony Blair Institute receives Saudi funding (Volume Two prior dossier sources). David Cameron post-PM advisory: Saudi connections through the Greensill Capital arrangement and other engagements.

The Indian-government-aligned architecture: the Modi-affiliated US Hindu Right and Indian-American institutional networks operate campaign-financial pathways across both US parties, with particular emphasis on the post-2014 Modi-related operations.

The defence-contractor architecture (Part XII Ch8): $140 million annual lobbying; 820 lobbyists; Pentagon revolving door producing ~700 former officials at defence contractors per Senator Warren's office report (91% as lobbyists).

The fossil-fuel-industry architecture: ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP, Shell, TotalEnergies political-action committees; Koch network operations across US politics.

The Big-Tech-industry architecture: post-2010 lobbying expansion. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon all have White House meetings, Capitol Hill lobbying budgets, Stargate Project (announced January 2025 — $500 billion AI infrastructure programme), AI policy-staffing acceleration across the 2023-2026 period.

The pharmaceutical-industry architecture (Volume One): Big Pharma PACs; FDA-PDUFA architecture; post-2008 lobbying expansion.

The financial-industry architecture (Volume One): banking-industry PACs; Treasury-Goldman pipeline; asset-manager (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street) institutional integration with US figures.

The pattern: every member of the dossiered cohort has financial relationships with at least one of these donor architectures. Their policy positions across the dossier evidentiary record correlate with the donor architectures they are aligned with. The correlation is not coincidence; it is the mechanism by which the class is funded, recruited, and retained.

## Feature eight: shared discourse

The contemporary political class shares a discourse — a vocabulary, a set of acceptable positions, a set of unacceptable positions, and a coherent style of argumentation. The discourse is reproduced through the universities (Feature 2), the think tanks (Feature 7), the media (Volume One), and the conferences (Davos, Munich Security Conference, Aspen Ideas, Sun Valley, Bilderberg, Trilateral Commission, World Economic Forum).

The discourse's contents change in detail but not in structure. The member who articulates a position outside the acceptable range — for example, that NATO expansion may have contributed to the Ukraine war (Part XII Ch4); that the Gaza war is a genocide (Part XII Ch5); that the proxy-war architecture serves arms-industry profit (Part XII Ch7-Ch8); that the central banks operate as private institutions serving private interests (Volume One) — is across the cumulative record marginalised, deplatformed, or removed from position.

The acceptable positions across the record: support for the US-led "rules-based international order"; characterisation of Russia, China, Iran as adversaries against the "free world"; characterisation of the central banks as politically independent technical institutions; characterisation of the asset managers as efficient market intermediaries; characterisation of the wars as humanitarian or counter-terrorism operations; characterisation of inequality as either a personal-merit phenomenon or a market phenomenon rather than a class-extraction phenomenon; characterisation of climate change as soluble through market-based instruments rather than through production-sector restructuring.

The unacceptable positions across the same record: characterisation of the central banks as captured private institutions (the Bank of England's 1694 charter, the Federal Reserve's 1913 founding, the multi-year asset-manager integration); characterisation of the wars as proxy operations serving arms-industry interests (Part XII Ch7-Ch8); characterisation of the philanthropic foundations as tax-arbitrage and reputation-laundering operations (Volume One); characterisation of the elections as donor-class auctions (Volume Two repeatedly); characterisation of the universities, press, regulatory bodies, and consensus-knowledge apparatus as captured (Volume One).

The member who articulates the unacceptable positions is in the cumulative record as suffering specific institutional consequences. Bernie Sanders (US Senator since 2007): multi-year ostracism from Democratic Party leadership; multi-year corporate-media characterisation as "fringe"; 2016 and 2020 Democratic Party presidential primary architecture operated against his candidacy. Jeremy Corbyn (UK Labour leader 2015-2020): 2017-2019 corporate-media characterisation as "antisemite"; post-2020 Labour leadership architecture under Starmer operated to remove him from Labour positions. Ron Paul (US Republican Congressman 1976-2013, presidential candidate): Republican Party ostracism; corporate-media characterisation. Tulsi Gabbard (US Congresswoman 2013-2021, presidential candidate 2020, Director of National Intelligence under Trump 2): 2019-2020 Democratic Party institutional sidelining; political realignment 2022.

The pattern: the class polices itself through institutional consequences for members who articulate the unacceptable positions. The discourse is the class's operational vocabulary. It is reproduced through every institutional position the class holds.

## The class function

The eight features together produce the class function: defence of the property regime; enforcement of the contracts and collateral arrangements; deployment of the military for resource access and alternative prevention; management of the populations through media architecture, education architecture, welfare architecture, surveillance architecture, criminal-justice architecture, and immigration architecture; reproduction of the class itself through recruitment, credentialing, network capital, and discourse policing.

The class is not omniscient. The class is not omnipotent. It operates through institutional positions whose occupants make mistakes, miscalculations, and policy errors. It disagrees internally about tactical questions. It accommodates dissident voices within limits. It admits new members from demographic categories it previously excluded.

What it does not do is allow the function to be disrupted. The function — defence of the property regime, enforcement of the contracts, deployment of the military, management of the populations, reproduction of the class — operates continuously across the record regardless of which individuals hold which institutional positions.

## The Devil, the Demons, and the masks

Volume One named the Devil — the four-century debt-money mechanism, the asset-management trinity, the central-bank architecture, the dollar-system architecture. Chapter 1 of this Part named the through-line — the Devil operates through the state apparatus.

The record names the Demons. The Demons are the class. The class operates the state apparatus. The state apparatus is one of the masks.

The class is not a conspiracy. The class is a recruitment pattern. The recruitment pattern produces personnel with the features the mechanism requires. The personnel staff the structural positions through which the mechanism operates. The personnel are reliably loyal because the personnel are themselves the class.

The member who is removed from position is replaced by another member. The member who falls into dissent is marginalised, defunded, or replaced. The member who dies is replaced by another member groomed for the position. The class continues regardless of any individual.

This is the demon — not the person, but the pattern. The pattern is the class. The class wears the state mask. The state mask sits on the Devil.

## Bridge to Chapter 3

Chapter 1 of this Part named the Devil. Chapter 2 named the Demons. Chapter 3 names the relationship — the puppeteer and the strings. Volume One named the puppet show; this volume names the strings. Chapter 3 documents the specific institutional pathways through which the operators dossiered in Volume One control the political-class operators dossiered in this volume — the donor architectures, the revolving-door pathways, the media architectures, the university architectures, the think-tank architectures, the philanthropic architectures, the regulatory architectures, the consensus-knowledge architectures.

# Part XIII Chapter 3: The Puppeteer and the Strings

## The puppet show and the strings

Volume One named the puppet show. The captured press; the captured universities; the captured philanthropic infrastructure; the captured regulatory bodies; the captured consensus-knowledge apparatus; and the captured political class. The 26 chapters of Volume One the captures with named donor-recipient pairings, named lobbying disclosures, named foreign-agent registrations, named post-government commercial integrations.

Volume Two has dossiered the political-class operators of the state apparatus across 27 individual dossiers and one war architecture (Part XII). The dossiers establish what each named operator does. The Part XII war architecture establishes what the captured state apparatus does collectively when the system requires open markets, alternative prevention, or weapons-industry profit.

What Volume One and Volume Two have not yet shown together, in one chapter, is the connecting tissue. The specific institutional pathways by which the financial-system operators Volume One named control — or, more accurately, integrate with — the political-class operators Volume Two named.

The record names the strings.

The strings are not metaphorical. The strings are specific, named, documented, dated, dollar-figured. The strings are the revolving-door appointments. The strings are the Davos and Bilderberg and Trilateral Commission and Council on Foreign Relations memberships. The strings are the donor architectures. The strings are the post-government commercial appointments. The strings are the foundation funding pathways. The strings are the think-tank pipelines. The strings are the media-ownership architectures. The strings are the personal relationships — the dinners, the calls, the family connections, the boards.

The puppeteer is not a single individual. The puppeteer is the four-century mechanism Volume One named, operating through the contemporary asset-management trinity (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street), the contemporary banks, the contemporary central-bank architecture, and the contemporary individual operators of those institutions. The strings are the specific institutional pathways through which that puppeteer integrates with the contemporary state apparatus.

## String one: the BlackRock-Biden pipeline

The clearest single illustration of the integration is BlackRock's 2020-2024 placement of personnel into the Biden administration's economic team.

Brian Deese: Director of the National Economic Council, January 2021 to February 2023. Before the NEC: Global Head of Sustainable Investing, BlackRock, 2017-2020. Before BlackRock: Senior Adviser to President Obama for climate and energy policy; helped negotiate the Paris Climate Agreement 2015.

Wally Adeyemo: Deputy Treasury Secretary, January 2021 to January 2025. Before Treasury: President of the Obama Foundation 2019-2020. Before the Obama Foundation: Senior Adviser at BlackRock roughly three years; briefly chief of staff to Larry Fink. Before BlackRock: Deputy Director of the National Economic Council and Deputy National Security Advisor for International Economics under Obama; first chief of staff at the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau; deputy chief of staff at Treasury under Jack Lew (Lew himself a Robert Rubin protégé who served as Citigroup COO between his Treasury stints).

Mike Pyle: Chief economic adviser to Vice President Kamala Harris, 2021-2024. Before the Vice President's office: Chief Investment Strategist at the BlackRock Investment Institute.

Tom Donilon: Senior adviser to Biden. Before the adviser role: Chairman, BlackRock Investment Institute. Before BlackRock: National Security Advisor to Obama 2010-2013. Brother of Mike Donilon, Biden's chief political strategist.

The pattern is not three or four BlackRock executives moving into the Biden administration. The pattern is BlackRock placing personnel across the Treasury, the National Economic Council, the Vice President's economic team, and the White House advisory architecture. The Wall Street Journal characterised the pattern in December 2020 as the replacement of Goldman Sachs by BlackRock as the Wall Street representative inside the White House.

The reverse flow operates in parallel. Adeyemo's career before BlackRock was government service. He moved from Treasury to BlackRock and back to Treasury — the same person, the same set of institutional relationships, the same policy positions, simply alternating between two of the arms of the same mechanism. Deese was a White House climate-policy operator who moved to BlackRock to operationalise the climate-policy positioning as a market, then moved back to the White House to direct the National Economic Council's relationship with that market.

The institutional question this raises — to what extent does the Treasury Department regulate BlackRock, and to what extent does BlackRock staff the Treasury Department? — has a clear answer. Larry Fink's stated multi-year priority, per The American Prospect cabinet-watch coverage, is to prevent BlackRock from being designated a Systemically Important Financial Institution (SIFI), which would subject BlackRock to higher Federal Reserve regulatory scrutiny. The Treasury Department is the decision-maker on SIFI designations. The Treasury Department has not designated BlackRock as a SIFI. Adeyemo, the BlackRock alumnus inside the Biden Treasury, did not push for SIFI designation during his four years as Deputy Treasury Secretary.

## String two: the Goldman Sachs-Treasury pipeline ()

The BlackRock-Biden pipeline is a recent manifestation of a pattern. For roughly three decades the pipeline ran through Goldman Sachs.

Robert Rubin: Goldman Sachs co-chairman until 1992. National Economic Council Director 1993-1995 (under Clinton). Treasury Secretary 1995-1999 (the architect of the Glass-Steagall repeal that produced the 1999 Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act). Citigroup counsellor 1999-2009 (during the period leading to Citigroup's 2008 bailout, which his Treasury successor Henry Paulson — also Goldman — engineered).

Henry Paulson: Goldman Sachs CEO 1999-2006. Treasury Secretary 2006-2009 (the architect of the 2008 bailout architecture including TARP, the Bear Stearns intervention, the AIG bailout that paid Goldman counterparties 100 cents on the dollar, and the Lehman non-rescue that benefitted Goldman's competitive position).

Steven Mnuchin: Goldman Sachs 1985-2002, rising to Chief Information Officer. Treasury Secretary 2017-2021 (under Trump 1). Liberty Strategic Capital founder 2021, raising roughly $2.5 billion including Saudi PIF investment.

Gary Cohn: Goldman Sachs President and COO. NEC Director under Trump 1, 2017-2018.

Mark Patterson: Goldman Sachs lobbyist. Treasury chief of staff under Geithner.

Dina Powell: Goldman Sachs partner. Trump 1 Deputy National Security Advisor 2017-2018; returned to Goldman after.

Steven Bannon: Goldman Sachs banker 1984-1990. Trump 1 Chief Strategist 2017.

The position of Goldman Sachs across the period: every major US economic and financial-policy initiative since 1993 has been directed by a Goldman Sachs alumnus or a Goldman client. The Glass-Steagall repeal (Rubin); the late-1990s emerging-markets architecture (Rubin and Larry Summers); the 2008 bailout (Paulson, with Geithner as Treasury principal); the Dodd-Frank implementation (Geithner, then Lew, with Gary Gensler at the CFTC); the Trump 1 tax cuts (Mnuchin, with Cohn at the NEC); the Trump 1 trade negotiations (Robert Lighthizer — not Goldman but client of Skadden Arps); the Biden BlackRock pipeline (Deese and Adeyemo — BlackRock rather than Goldman, but the same institutional category).

## String three: the international Goldman-political pipeline

The Goldman-Treasury pipeline is a US manifestation of the international pattern.

Mario Draghi: Goldman Sachs International Vice-Chairman 2002-2005. Bank of Italy Governor 2005-2011. ECB President 2011-2019 (where he committed in July 2012 to "do whatever it takes to preserve the euro" during the sovereign-debt crisis, producing the eurozone bond-purchase programme that benefitted holders of European sovereign debt including the major banks Draghi had helped advise as a Goldman International executive). Italian Prime Minister 2021-2022.

Mark Carney: Goldman Sachs 1988-2003, rising to managing director. Bank of Canada Governor 2008-2013 (managed the Canadian financial system through the 2008 crisis). Bank of England Governor 2013-2020 (the first non-British Bank of England Governor; managed the Brexit-period UK monetary policy). UN Special Envoy for Climate Action and Finance 2020-2025. Canadian Prime Minister March 2025-.

The Carney trajectory is the contemporary apotheosis of the Goldman-political pipeline. The same individual operates positions at Goldman, then at two of the G7 central banks, then at the UN architecture for climate finance, and then as the elected head of government of a G7 country. The trajectory is not coincidental. The trajectory is the system functioning as designed.

José Manuel Barroso: European Commission President 2004-2014. Goldman Sachs International Chairman 2016-. The post-Commission move to Goldman produced a EU Commission ethics complaint; the Commission ethics body ruled 2016 that Barroso had not violated the EU ethics code, despite the obvious appearance of revolving-door capture.

Petros Christodoulou: Goldman Sachs. Head of the Greek Public Debt Management Agency 2010 — during the Greek debt crisis that produced the multi-year European austerity programme. The Goldman role in helping Greece conceal its debt from Eurozone authorities in 2001 (using off-balance-sheet swaps) was the multi-year precursor to the 2010 crisis Christodoulou was then assigned to manage.

Robert Zoellick: Goldman Sachs International Advisors Chairman 2006. World Bank President 2007-2012. Trump trade adviser.

Malcolm Turnbull: Goldman Sachs Australia Chairman. Australian Prime Minister 2015-2018.

The pattern: the international Goldman pipeline operates across multiple G7 and OECD countries, placing personnel into central-bank, treasury, international-institution, and elected-government positions.

## String four: the UK financial-class-to-political-class pipeline

Tony Blair: UK Prime Minister 1997-2007. The post-PM Blair architecture has operated through: JPMorgan paid adviser roughly $5 million/year reported; Zurich Insurance adviser; Tony Blair Institute for Global Change founded 2016 (funded by Larry Ellison through the Oracle architecture, Bill Gates through the Gates Foundation architecture, and the Saudi Arabian government through multi-million-dollar contracts).

David Cameron: UK Prime Minister 2010-2016. Post-PM Cameron architecture: paid adviser to Greensill Capital, the supply-chain finance firm that collapsed in March 2021 producing the Greensill scandal in which Cameron was found to have lobbied UK Treasury officials including then-Chancellor Rishi Sunak for emergency liquidity access on behalf of Greensill. The scandal produced a UK Treasury Select Committee inquiry. Cameron returned to position as Foreign Secretary 2023-2024 under Sunak.

George Osborne: UK Chancellor 2010-2016. Post-Chancellor Osborne architecture: BlackRock part-time appointment 2017 (4 days/month, £650,000/year — roughly £162,500 per working day of advisory time). Evening Standard editor 2017-2020 (during which the Evening Standard was widely criticised for soft coverage of Osborne's commercial clients). Robey Warshaw partner 2021-.

Sajid Javid: Deutsche Bank Vice President 1991-2009; Chase Manhattan before. Conservative MP from 2010. UK Chancellor 2019-2020. Post-government: Centene Corporation board; JPMorgan Chase adviser.

Philip Hammond: investment banker pre-Parliament. UK Chancellor 2016-2019.

The UK pattern: the London financial class produces Conservative MPs and Chancellors who, after government service, return to advisory and board positions in the financial class. The same individuals operate across the financial class and the political class in either direction, often multiple times.

## String five: the Davos integration

The Davos integration is the annual architecture that integrates the financial class, the political class, the corporate class, the media class, and the academic-think-tank class.

The World Economic Forum was founded by Klaus Schwab in 1971. The annual Davos meeting in Davos-Klosters, Switzerland, hosts roughly 3,000 attendees from business, politics, media, and academia. The 2024 attendance included: Volodymyr Zelensky, Antony Blinken, Olaf Scholz, Emmanuel Macron, Ursula von der Leyen, Christine Lagarde, Larry Fink, Jamie Dimon, Sam Altman, Satya Nadella, hundreds of figures.

The WEF Board includes Larry Fink (BlackRock), Christine Lagarde (ECB), historically Mark Carney, and a rotation of banking-political-class members.

The WEF Young Global Leaders programme, founded by Schwab in 1993 (later renamed "Forum of Young Global Leaders"), has produced roughly 1,400+ alumni including: Justin Trudeau (Canadian PM 2015-2024); Emmanuel Macron (French President 2017-); Jacinda Ardern (NZ PM 2017-2023); Sebastián Piñera (Chilean President 2010-2014, 2018-2022); Sanna Marin (Finnish PM 2019-2023); Annalena Baerbock (German Foreign Minister 2021-2025); Pete Buttigieg (US Transportation Secretary 2021-2025); Tulsi Gabbard (US Director of National Intelligence 2025-); Larry Page (Google founder); Sergey Brin (Google founder); Mark Zuckerberg (Meta founder); Jeff Bezos (Amazon founder); Bill Gates (Microsoft founder); Jack Ma (Alibaba founder).

The WEF Young Global Leaders pipeline operates as the credentialing and network-building architecture for the contemporary global political class. The alumni list is not a coincidence; it is the recruitment outcome of the programme that selects candidates for global political-class positions.

The 2025 January Davos meeting saw Mark Carney attending in advisory positions before announcing his Canadian Liberal leadership run in February 2025 and winning the Liberal leadership and PM-ship March 2025. The Carney move from Davos directly to Canadian PM in roughly 60 days illustrates the speed at which the architecture operates.

## String six: the Bilderberg integration

Bilderberg is the private annual conference founded in 1954 by Joseph Retinger and Prince Bernhard of the Netherlands. Roughly 130-150 attendees per annual meeting.

The Bilderberg Steering Committee includes banking, political, media, technology figures. The attendee list includes: Henry Kissinger (attendance from 1957 until 2023, his death year); David Rockefeller (founding-era attendance); George Osborne; Tony Blair; Bill Clinton (pre-presidency 1991); Ben Bernanke; Henry Paulson; Larry Summers; Janet Yellen; Christine Lagarde; Eric Schmidt; Peter Thiel; Sam Altman.

The 2024 Bilderberg meeting was held in Madrid, Spain, May 30 - June 2. Attendees included: Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis (DeepMind / Google AI), Sundar Pichai (Google CEO), Eric Schmidt, Mark Rutte (incoming NATO Secretary General), financial and AI figures.

The 2025 Bilderberg meeting was held in Stockholm, June 2025.

The Bilderberg architecture is the private deliberative architecture in which Atlantic financial, political, media, technology, and academic figures coordinate strategic positions on multi-year basis.

## String seven: the Council on Foreign Relations and Trilateral Commission

The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR), founded 1921, is the US foreign-policy establishment with roughly 5,000 members. The membership requires nomination plus board approval. The CFR publishes Foreign Affairs, the US foreign-policy journal.

The CFR chairs across the period have included David Rockefeller, Peter G. Peterson (multi-year), Robert Rubin, Carla Hills, Richard Haass (long-serving), David Rubenstein (current since 2023; Carlyle Group co-founder; net worth roughly $3 billion). The CFR pipeline operates the US foreign-policy establishment continuously — placing personnel into State Department, Pentagon, CIA, National Security Council, media, and university positions across the period.

The Trilateral Commission was founded in 1973 by David Rockefeller and Zbigniew Brzezinski. Three regions: North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific. Members from business, political, and academic positions. The Trilateral Commission produced personnel for the Carter administration including Brzezinski as National Security Advisor, plus numerous State Department and Treasury appointments. The Trilateral Commission architecture has operated continuously.

The CFR-style architecture in other regions: Royal Institute of International Affairs (Chatham House, UK); French Institute of International Relations (IFRI); German Council on Foreign Relations (DGAP); Russian International Affairs Council (RIAC); Indian Council of World Affairs (ICWA); Brazilian Centre for International Relations (CEBRI); South African Institute of International Affairs (SAIIA). The pattern: every G20 country operates a CFR-equivalent architecture that integrates the financial, political, diplomatic, media, and academic classes.

## String eight: the foundation-funding architecture

The contemporary philanthropic foundations operate as the policy-shaping architecture that bypasses democratic accountability while operating with tax-advantaged status.

The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation: assets under management roughly $70 billion. The Gates Foundation has operated policy-shaping across global health (GAVI, COVAX, CEPI), agriculture (AGRA), and education policy. The Gates Foundation appears at every global health policy meeting, every multi-year COVID-19 policy meeting, and every global health appropriations discussion. The Gates Foundation's influence on WHO operations has approached funding parity with member-state contributions.

The Open Society Foundations (Soros): AUM roughly $25 billion post-restructuring. The Soros architecture operates funding of civil-society organisations across multi-year specific national political contexts.

The Rockefeller Foundation, Ford Foundation, Carnegie Corporation, MacArthur Foundation: established philanthropic architectures with multi-billion-dollar endowments.

Donor-Advised Funds at Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, Vanguard Charitable: combined roughly $200+ billion AUM. The DAF architecture is the fastest-growing US philanthropic vehicle, with anonymous-flow architecture that allows wealthy donors to route multi-year contributions through intermediary structures without public disclosure of ultimate beneficiaries.

The pattern: the philanthropic foundations operate the policy-shaping architecture without the democratic accountability that elected institutions face. The foundations select the research questions that get funding; the journals that get subsidies; the policy advocacy organisations that get multi-year grants; and the university faculty positions that get endowed chairs. The aggregate effect is the cumulative shaping of the policy environment in the direction the foundation principals prefer.

## String nine: the media-ownership architecture

The contemporary media architecture has been across Volume One as captured. The captures operate through direct ownership:

- Washington Post: Jeff Bezos (Amazon founder) since 2013.
- New York Times: Sulzberger family.
- Wall Street Journal: News Corp / Rupert Murdoch.
- The Atlantic: Laurene Powell Jobs majority owner since 2017.
- CNN: Warner Bros Discovery (post-AT&T spin-off).
- MSNBC, NBC News: Comcast / NBCUniversal.
- Fox News: News Corp / Murdoch.
- The Economist: Agnelli/Exor (Italian Agnelli family), Rothschild, Cadbury, plus Economist Group employees.

The pattern: the US and UK news media is owned by the billionaire class and the family-owned media conglomerates. The editorial positions of the media outlets correlate consistently with the interests of the owners. The reporters and editors are recruited from the elite-university pipeline (String 5 cross-reference); the management is recruited from the corporate world; and the board of directors of the media organisations is populated by the banking, asset-manager, corporate, and political class members across the record.

## String ten: the AI-policy capture 2023-2026

The 2023-2026 period has produced the new strand of integration: the AI-industry capture of US AI policy.

The 30 May 2023 Center for AI Safety open statement ("Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI") was signed by Sam Altman (OpenAI), Demis Hassabis (DeepMind / Google AI), Dario Amodei (Anthropic), and hundreds of AI figures. The statement positioned the AI industry as the responsible stewards of AI safety — the canonical industry-capture move of presenting the regulated entities as the expert authorities on regulation.

The 6-7 November 2023 UK AI Safety Summit at Bletchley Park (under Sunak) produced the Bletchley Declaration signed by 28 countries plus the EU including US, UK, EU, China.

The 30 October 2023 Biden Executive Order 14110 on AI was the first comprehensive US AI executive order. It was revoked 20 January 2025 by Trump.

The January 2024 onwards: Sam Altman White House meetings across Biden and Trump 2 administrations. Dario Amodei congressional testimony and White House meetings 2024-2025.

The 21 January 2025 Stargate Project announcement at the Oval Office: Trump announced a $500 billion AI infrastructure programme. The named participants: OpenAI (Sam Altman), Oracle (Larry Ellison; funder of the Tony Blair Institute), SoftBank (Masayoshi Son). The announcement included Sam Altman, Larry Ellison, and Masayoshi Son standing alongside Trump in the Oval Office.

The David Sacks named "AI and crypto czar" by Trump January 2025. Sacks: PayPal Mafia (the PayPal founders including Peter Thiel, Elon Musk, Reid Hoffman, Max Levchin, David Sacks); Craft Ventures (Sacks's venture capital firm).

The February 2025 Sriram Krishnan named AI policy adviser at the White House. Krishnan: multi-year tech investor; Andreessen Horowitz before.

The pattern: the AI industry has captured the US AI policy apparatus in roughly 24 months. The captured personnel are the AI founders themselves (Altman, Amodei) or AI investors (Thiel, Sacks, Krishnan). The captured policy questions are the questions the AI industry wants asked; the policy answers are the answers the AI industry wants produced.

## The integration synthesis

The ten strings above are not separate phenomena. The ten strings operate together as a integrated architecture.

The BlackRock-Biden pipeline (String 1) connects the Volume One asset-management trinity to the Volume Two state apparatus.

The Goldman Sachs-Treasury pipeline (String 2) is the version of the same pattern.

The international Goldman-political pipeline (String 3) extends the pattern globally — Draghi, Carney, Barroso, Zoellick, Turnbull all operating across the Goldman-central bank-political-class architecture in different G7 contexts.

The UK financial-class-to-political-class pipeline (String 4) is the London-specific version.

The Davos integration (String 5) is the annual architecture for global political-class recruitment, credentialing, and policy-coordination.

The Bilderberg integration (String 6) is the smaller private deliberative architecture.

The CFR-Trilateral Commission architecture (String 7) is the US-led foreign-policy establishment.

The foundation-funding architecture (String 8) is the policy-shaping architecture that bypasses democratic accountability.

The media-ownership architecture (String 9) is the information architecture.

The AI-policy capture (String 10) is the emerging integration of the new technology with the existing political-class architecture.

The aggregate effect: the individual operators dossiered in Volume Two are connected to the individual operators named in Volume One through multiple, overlapping, reinforcing institutional pathways. The aggregate effect is the integration of the financial system and the state apparatus into a single operational architecture, with different individuals holding different positions at different times but with the class itself operating continuously regardless of personnel.

## The puppeteer

The puppeteer is not Larry Fink. The puppeteer is not Jamie Dimon. The puppeteer is not Bill Gates. The puppeteer is not Klaus Schwab. The puppeteer is not Henry Kissinger.

These are named individuals who operate named institutional positions at named historical moments. They are not the puppeteer. They are individuals through whom the puppeteer operates.

The puppeteer is the four-century mechanism Volume One named — the debt-money architecture that began in 1694, that operates today through the asset-management trinity, the central-bank architecture, the dollar-system architecture, the petrodollar architecture, and the pathways across Volumes One and Two.

The puppeteer does not have a single decision-making body. The puppeteer does not have a chief executive officer. The puppeteer is a mechanism that operates through thousands of named individuals at any given moment, with individuals continuously replaced as individuals retire or die. The continuity is the mechanism itself, not the individuals.

The strings are the specific institutional pathways through which the puppeteer operates. The strings are visible. The strings are named. The strings are documented. The strings have dates, dollar figures, named operators, named institutional positions.

The individuals who operate the puppet show — the political-class operators dossiered in Volume Two — are not corrupted by the puppeteer. They are recruited, credentialed, funded, promoted, protected, and retained by the puppeteer through the ten strings above. They are the puppeteer's personnel. They are loyal because they are recruited from inside the class the puppeteer requires.

The question for the public is not who the puppets are. The question is whether the puppet show can continue once the strings have been named.

## Bridge to Part XIV

Part XIII has named the synthesis. Chapter 1 named the Devil — the mechanism. Chapter 2 named the Demons — the class. Chapter 3 named the strings — the pathways.

Part XIV documents the exits. The alternatives that have been historically attempted to disrupt the political-class capture: the Jacksonian populist response of the 1830s; the Greenback movement of the 1870s-1890s; the post-1929 New Deal response; the post-colonial independence movements; the twentieth-century anti-fascist responses; the twentieth-century anti-colonial movements; the contemporary non-violent civil-resistance literature (the Chenoweth-Stephan database); and the contemporary alternatives — mutual credit, cooperatives, commons, debt jubilee, parallel infrastructure, public banking, sovereign money proposals.

Chapter 1 of Part XIV documents the solutions.
