# Sources — Prologue (rewrite)

## Robert Walpole and the Bank of England (1721)

- Robert Walpole became Britain's first de facto Prime Minister in April 1721, after the South Sea Bubble crash.
- The Bank of England had been chartered in 1694 to finance William III's wars; by 1721 the British state's debt to the Bank exceeded the state's annual tax revenue.
- Walpole's twenty-one-year tenure (1721-1742) institutionalised the arrangement under which the British political class governed the country while servicing the debt held by the Bank's directors and shareholders.
- Walpole was personally a major shareholder in the South Sea Company; he profited from the bubble he was charged with managing.
- The South Sea Company's directors who could not be politically protected were prosecuted; the figures Walpole was protecting were not.
- **Source:** Roy Porter, *English Society in the Eighteenth Century* (Penguin); Andrew Odlyzko, "Newton's Financial Misadventures in the South Sea Bubble," *Notes and Records of the Royal Society* (2018); the Walpole archive at Houghton Hall; the Parliamentary Register for the relevant sessions; Standard Volume One Chapter 2 cross-reference.
- **Verified:** Volume One historical material; no further verification needed.

## Tony Blair Institute for Global Change — Saudi consulting record

- **2018:** Sunday Telegraph reports the Tony Blair Institute received $10 million from Saudi government and from Media Investment Ltd (a subsidiary of the Saudi Research and Marketing Group). Revised figure reported at $12 million with additional payments.
- The Tony Blair Institute confirmed receiving donations from the United States Department of State and Saudi Arabia.
- The Institute continued its partnership with Saudi Arabia after the October 2018 assassination of Jamal Khashoggi in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul.
- **Larry Ellison Foundation as principal funder:** Wikipedia (citing The Guardian, April 2025): the Larry Ellison Foundation gave over £52 million in 2023 and pledged a further £163 million. Total Ellison foundation donations to TBI: at least £257 million as of 2025 reporting. Ellison was dossiered in Volume One Chapter 18.
- **2024:** TBI confirmed paid work for the Azerbaijani regime during its COP29 hosting.
- **The Great Trust / Gaza reconstruction proposal:** *Financial Times* reported TBI participation alongside Israeli businessmen with BCG financial modelling support. Plan included "Trump Riviera" and "Elon Musk Smart Manufacturing Zone" concepts. Plan shared with the Trump administration.
- **Source:** [*Consultancy.uk* 25 July 2018](https://www.consultancy.uk/news/18032/ex-prime-minister-scrutinised-for-alleged-10-million-consulting-payment); [*The New Arab* 22 July 2018](https://www.newarab.com/news/tony-blair-consultancy-firm-working-saudi-government); [Wikipedia "Tony Blair Institute for Global Change," cross-referenced to *The Guardian* April 2025 reporting and *Financial Times* reporting on the Great Trust proposal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tony_Blair_Institute_for_Global_Change)
- **Verified:** 13 May 2026.

## Clarence Thomas — Harlan Crow gifts

- **Harlan Crow:** Texas real estate magnate, Republican megadonor. Personally donated over $10 million in publicly disclosed political donations, mostly to conservative causes. Held dual citizenship of US and St. Kitts and Nevis (granted via cash-for-citizenship scheme in 2012; documented by POGO / The Intercept).
- **Documented undisclosed gifts to Justice Thomas over more than 20 years:**
- At least 38 destination vacations and 26+ private jet flights (ProPublica reporting, 2023).
- One trip alone (June 2019, Indonesia, 9 days on the 162-foot superyacht *Michaela Rose* with private jet transport) valued at over $500,000.
- Bohemian Grove all-male retreats in northern California; at least 6 visits.
- 2014 real estate deal: Crow Holdings purchased Thomas's mother's house in Savannah, Georgia, plus two adjacent vacant lots from Thomas and family for $133,363. Thomas's mother continued living in the property rent-free; Crow funded renovations.
- Private school tuition for Thomas's grandnephew Mark Martin at Randolph-Macon Academy (Virginia) and Hidden Lake Academy (Georgia), totalling $100,000 over two years; potentially up to $150,000 over four years.
- Earlier: Crow funded much of the budget of Liberty Central, a political group founded by Ginni Thomas, which paid her a $120,000 salary.
- **Estimated total value of undisclosed gifts:** NPR estimated potentially exceeding $1 million.
- Thomas's failure to disclose: the Ethics in Government Act (post-Watergate) requires Supreme Court justices to disclose gifts over $415. Thomas defended his non-disclosure under a "personal hospitality" exemption. Seven legal ethics experts consulted by ProPublica said the exemption does not cover private jet travel or yacht trips.
- In August 2024, Thomas amended his 2019 disclosure to acknowledge the Indonesia trip was incorrectly omitted, after the ProPublica reporting.
- 2005: Thomas did not recuse himself when an architecture firm appealed a $25 million case against Trammell Crow Residential, a firm founded by Crow's father in which the Crow family was invested.
- **Source:** [ProPublica multi-part investigation 2023](https://www.propublica.org/article/clarence-thomas-scotus-undisclosed-luxury-travel-gifts-crow); [ProPublica 4 May 2023 (tuition)](https://www.propublica.org/article/clarence-thomas-harlan-crow-private-school-tuition-scotus); [ProPublica 27 April 2023 (real estate)](https://www.propublica.org/article/clarence-thomas-harlan-crow-real-estate-scotus); POGO 2023 (St. Kitts citizenship)](https://www.pogo.org/investigates/clarence-thomas-billionaire-benefactor-harlan-crow-bought-citizenship-in-island-tax-haven); [ProPublica 6 August 2023 (other billionaires)](https://www.propublica.org/article/clarence-thomas-other-billionaires-sokol-huizenga-novelly-supreme-court)
- **Verified:** 13 May 2026.

## The four-century sweep — context for the historical claims in Part I

- **1602:** Dutch East India Company chartered, first publicly-traded joint-stock company. (Volume One Chapter 2.)
- **1694:** Bank of England chartered. (Volume One Chapter 2.)
- **1721-1742:** Walpole government. (See above; Volume One Chapter 2.)
- **1791:** First Bank of the United States chartered. (Volume One Chapter 3-4.)
- **1913:** Federal Reserve Act, following Jekyll Island meeting November 1910. (Volume One Chapter 8.)
- **1944:** Bretton Woods. (Volume One Chapter 5.)
- **1971:** Nixon shock. (Volume One Chapter 6.)
- All cross-referenced to Volume One; no re-verification required in this prologue. Specifics are referenced only as anchors.

# Sources — Chapter 1 (Part I): The Mask Before the State (global pre-1600 political-class history)

## Mesopotamian palace-temple political class (3rd-2nd millennium BCE)
- *Amargi* (Lagash), *nig-sisa* (Ur), *andurarum* (Ashur), *misharum* (Babylon), *shudutu* (Nuzi) — the legal terms for debt cancellation across the Mesopotamian city-states 2400-1400 BCE.
- Hammurabi's *misharum* edicts; the Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE), §117 limiting debt servitude to three years.
- Michael Hudson, *...and forgive them their debts: Lending, Foreclosure and Redemption from Bronze Age Finance to the Jubilee Year* (ISLET, 2018) — scholarly study, primary source for the Mesopotamian debt-cancellation record.
- David Graeber, *Debt: The First 5,000 Years* (Melville House, 2011) — chapter on Mesopotamia.
- *andurarum* and Hebrew *deror* (Leviticus 25) shared Akkadian root; the term "liberty" inscribed on the Liberty Bell, Philadelphia.

## Egyptian pharaonic political class
- Rosetta Stone, 196 BCE, recording Ptolemy V's debt amnesty.
- Bakenranef of the 24th Dynasty (c. 720 BCE) abolished debt servitude per Diodorus Siculus, *Bibliotheca historica* I.79.
- Hudson, *And forgive them their debts*, chapter on Egypt.

## Persian Achaemenid, Parthian, Sasanian political classes
- Achaemenid satrapal system under Darius I (c. 522-486 BCE): 20-23 satrapies, satrap-and-king's-eye dual administration, fixed tribute schedule per Herodotus, *Histories*, Book III.
- Parthian feudal-aristocratic political class 247 BCE - 224 CE: seven great houses (Karen, Suren, Spahbod, Mihran, et al.); royal-vassal arrangement preserved across Sasanian succession.
- Sasanian political class 224-651 CE: four-tier social structure (priests, warriors, scribes, farmers/artisans); land tax (kharaj) under Khosrow I Anushirvan's sixth-century reform.

## Han Chinese political class (206 BCE - 220 CE)
- Han imperial bureaucracy; junxian (commandery-and-county) administrative system; the imperial-Confucian merger under Han Wudi (r. 141-87 BCE); the salt-and-iron monopoly (yantie lun, 81 BCE debate, recorded by Huan Kuan in *Yantielun*).
- Compulsory military and labour service obligations on the peasantry; private accumulation of land by officials and merchants documented across Han period.

## Tang Chinese political class (618-907)
- Imperial examination system institutionalised; the equal-field system (juntian); An Lushan rebellion 755-763 as the inflection point in Tang fiscal-political decline.
- The 780 Liang-Shui-Fa (Twice-a-Year-Tax) reform under Yang Yan that ended the equal-field system and produced the late-Tang and Song fiscal architecture.

## Song Chinese political class (960-1279)
- Wang Anshi's New Policies (1069-1085) — the eleventh-century Song reform programme: state lending to peasants (qingmiao fa), state-monopolised tea-and-salt trade, mass-produced paper money (jiaozi) issued by the Song state from 1024.
- The Southern Song (1127-1279) was the most monetised pre-modern state in world history per economic historians including Richard von Glahn (*The Economic History of China*, 2016).

## Yuan (Mongol) Chinese political class (1271-1368)
- Genghis Khan (r. 1206-1227); Kublai Khan (r. 1260-1294) founded Yuan dynasty 1271.
- The Mongol Empire's *yam* postal-and-administrative system; the *darughachi* governorate; the Yuan's continued issuance of paper money (chao) and the Yuan-era hyperinflation under Toghon Temür (r. 1333-1370).
- The Mongol political class operated a multi-religious tax-farming system that delegated revenue collection to Central Asian Muslim *ortoq* merchant syndicates — the Yuan-period parallel to Roman *publicani*.

## Ming Chinese political class (1368-1644)
- Founded by Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu, r. 1368-1398), former peasant rebel; the Ming abolition of the chief ministership in 1380 after the Hu Weiyong purge; consolidation of imperial power in the emperor's person.
- Ming maritime expeditions under Zheng He 1405-1433 (seven voyages); subsequent maritime withdrawal and *haijin* sea-ban policy from 1435.
- The Single Whip tax reform under Zhang Juzheng (1572-1582), consolidating land-and-labour taxes into single silver payment — produced the Ming silver-import dependency that linked Ming China to Spanish-American silver flows via the Manila Galleon trade from 1565.
- *Tianqi Shihua* and *Mingshilu* for the Ming political-class transactional record.

## Mauryan, Gupta, Delhi Sultanate political classes (4th c. BCE - 16th c. CE)
- Mauryan Empire (c. 322-185 BCE) under Chandragupta and Ashoka; Kautilya's *Arthashastra* (4th-3rd c. BCE) documenting the pre-Mauryan-and-Mauryan political-class fiscal-and-administrative system.
- Gupta Empire (c. 320-550 CE) — the classical-Hindu political class.
- Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526) — Mamluk, Khilji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, and Lodi dynasties.

## Mughal political class foundation (1526-1605)
- Babur founded Mughal Empire 1526 after First Battle of Panipat (defeat of Ibrahim Lodi).
- Akbar (r. 1556-1605) consolidated empire after Second Battle of Panipat 1556.
- The *mansabdari* system introduced by Akbar in 1571: dual ranks (*zat* — personal rank/salary; *sawar* — cavalry obligation); ranks from 10 to 5,000 (subsequently raised to 7,000 under Aurangzeb).
- Mansabdars paid through *jagir* (revenue-assignment) rather than direct salary; the *jagirdari* system created a permanent revenue-collecting political class.
- The *zabt* (Dahsala) land-revenue system instituted by Raja Todar Mal under Akbar, 1580: revenue fixed at one-third of the ten-year-average yield, paid as cash to the state, leaving two-thirds to the cultivator (*kharaj*) — the most systematised pre-modern land-revenue system.
- *Sulh-i-kul* (universal peace) doctrine; the multi-religious inclusion of Rajput and Muslim nobles in the mansabdari ranks.
- Wikipedia, "Government of the Mughal Empire"; Wikipedia, "Mansabdar"; PWOnlyIAS, "Akbar's Administrative System"; NextIAS, "Mughal Administration"; Irfan Habib, *The Agrarian System of Mughal India* (Oxford, 1963; expanded 1999).

## Mongol Genghisid political class (1206-1368)
- Genghis Khan (Temüjin, c. 1162-1227) unified Mongol tribes; the *Yassa* legal code.
- The Mongol *kheshig* imperial guard as the pre-modern centralised-bureaucratic-recruitment institution.
- Four successor khanates from 1260s: Yuan (East Asia), Chagatai (Central Asia), Golden Horde (Russia-Eastern Europe), Ilkhanate (Persia).

## Ottoman political class foundation (c. 1299-1600)
- Osman I founded the Ottoman beylik c. 1299; the expansion under Murad I and Bayezid I; the 1453 conquest of Constantinople under Mehmed II.
- The *devşirme* (child-levy) system from 14th c.: Christian peasant boys from Balkans recruited into the Janissary corps and the palace administration, producing the most administrators (grand viziers including Pargalı İbrahim Pasha, Sokollu Mehmed Pasha) from former Christian-peasant origin.
- *Timar* land-grant system: cavalry-officer grants of revenue in exchange for military service — the Ottoman parallel to Mughal *jagir*.
- *Sipahi* cavalry class; *Janissary* infantry; the tripartite Ottoman political class (Ulema, Janissary, palace administration).
- Süleyman I (r. 1520-1566), the Ottoman peak; the multi-religious *millet* system for non-Muslim communities.

## Safavid Persia (1501-1736)
- Ismail I founded Safavid dynasty 1501 with the Qizilbash Turkmen military support; institutionalised Twelver Shi'ism as state religion.
- Abbas I (r. 1588-1629) reformed the Safavid army and political class: the *ghulam* slave-soldier corps (Caucasian-origin) replacing the Qizilbash; the *khassa* (crown-domain) revenue replacing the *mamalik* (state-domain) revenue.
- Isfahan as Safavid capital from 1598; the Safavid silk monopoly under Abbas controlling Iranian export to Europe.

## Aztec (Mexica) Triple Alliance political class (1428-1521)
- Triple Alliance formed 1428 between Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan after defeat of Azcapotzalco under Tepanec ruler Maxtla.
- Tribute-collection system: conquered city-states (*altepetl*) paid tribute in goods (cocoa, cotton, corn, feathers, precious metals, jaguar skins) and provided soldiers and slaves; *calpixque* (local governors) appointed to administer conquered territories.
- *Pochteca* (hereditary merchant guild) functioned as armed traders and as intelligence-gatherers in territories targeted for conquest.
- *Tlatoani* (emperor) at top of political hierarchy; nobles (*pipiltin*), commoners (*macehualtin*), slaves (*tlacotin*).
- Tribute shares to Triple Alliance: Tenochtitlan and Texcoco took two shares each, Tlacopan one share.
- Sources: Wikipedia, "Aztec Empire"; History.com, "How the Aztec Empire Was Forged Through a Triple Alliance"; OER Project, "Aztec power revealed in the Mexica tribute lists"; Fiveable, "Political and military organization of the Triple Alliance"; Camilla Townsend, *Fifth Sun: A New History of the Aztecs* (Oxford, 2019).

## Inca (Tawantinsuyu) political class (c. 1400-1533)
- Tawantinsuyu ("four provinces"): empire divided into four *suyus* governed by *apus* (regional governors, typically Sapa Inca's relatives).
- *Mit'a* labor-tax system: citizens contributed labor (not produce) to state projects — road construction (Qhapaq Ñan road network of c. 40,000 km), agricultural terraces, temples, military service.
- *Quipu* (knotted-string record-keeping) administered by *quipucamayocs* — the Inca administrative apparatus operated without a written script.
- Sapa Inca as divine ruler (descendant of sun-god Inti); *coya* (queen-consort, often royal sister).
- 40,000 Incas governed an empire of c. 10 million subjects per World History Encyclopedia.
- Population pooled labour and produce in state warehouses (*qollqas*) for redistribution during drought, war, famine.
- Sources: World History Encyclopedia, "Inca Government"; IncaRail Blog, "Inca Government: Guide 2024"; Fiveable, "Inca Empire: administration and infrastructure"; Quechuas Expeditions, "The mita system and Inca labor system"; Machu Picchu Tours, "The Mita System."

## Mali Empire political class (c. 1230-1670)
- Founded by Sundiata Keita c. 1235 after Battle of Kirina against Sosso.
- Mansa Musa I (r. c. 1312-1337); 1324-1325 *hajj* to Mecca with caravan of c. 60,000 people and an estimated 1.8 kg of gold per person per the later Arabic chronicles; 1324 stopover in Cairo caused 20% depreciation in the gold price in Cairo and Arabian markets for c. 12 years per medieval Arabic sources.
- Empire controlled the trans-Saharan gold-and-salt trade routes; capital at Niani on the upper Niger River.
- Timbuktu peaceably annexed 1324; the Sankore madrasah converted into an Islamic university; Djinguereber and Sankore mosques built.
- Ibn Battuta visited Mali under Mansa Sulayman 1352-1353 and recorded the political-class arrangement.
- Sources: Wikipedia, "Mali Empire"; Wikipedia, "Mansa Musa"; Britannica, "Musa I of Mali"; World History Encyclopedia, "Mansa Musa I: The Richest Man in History?"; National Geographic Education, "The Mali Empire"; Mises Institute, "The Mali Empire."

## Songhai Empire political class (c. 1464-1591)
- Founded by Sunni Ali c. 1464; expanded by Askia Mohammad I (Muhammad Ture, r. 1493-1528) who centralised the administration.
- Songhai succeeded Mali as the dominant West African political-class system; capital at Gao; Timbuktu and Djenné as the secondary administrative-and-religious centres.
- 1591: Moroccan Saadian invasion under Judar Pasha defeated Songhai at the Battle of Tondibi; Songhai political-class system collapsed.

## Ethiopian Solomonid political class (1270-1769)
- Solomonid dynasty founded by Yekuno Amlak 1270, claiming descent from the King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba via Menelik I.
- The *Kebra Nagast* (the Glory of the Kings) provided the dynastic-legitimating text.
- Gondarine period from Fasilides (r. 1632-1667) onward; Gondar capital from 1636.
- The Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church as the political-and-religious foundation of the Solomonid political class.

## Khmer Empire political class (802-1431)
- Founded by Jayavarman II 802; Angkor as the capital from c. 889 (Yasodharapura founded by Yasovarman I).
- Suryavarman II (r. 1113-1150) built Angkor Wat.
- Jayavarman VII (r. c. 1181-1218) — the Khmer peak; Angkor Thom and the Bayon temple complex.
- The 1431 sack of Angkor by Ayutthayan Siamese forces ended the classical Khmer political-class system.

## Majapahit Empire political class (1293-1527)
- Founded by Raden Wijaya 1293 on Java; the Hindu-Buddhist political-class system.
- Hayam Wuruk (r. 1350-1389) and the chief minister Gajah Mada — the Majapahit peak controlling the Indonesian archipelago and parts of the Malay Peninsula.
- The 1527 Demak Sultanate conquest of Majapahit consolidated the Islamic political-class transition in Java.

## Đại Việt political class (10th-19th c.)
- Lý dynasty (1009-1225), Trần dynasty (1225-1400), Lê dynasty (1428-1789) — the Vietnamese political-class continuity.
- Trần dynasty repelled three Mongol-Yuan invasions (1258, 1284-85, 1287-88) under Trần Hưng Đạo.
- The Confucian examination system institutionalised under Lý and continued through Lê.

## Japanese political class (Heian through late Sengoku, c. 794-1600)
- Heian period (794-1185): Fujiwara regency political class controlled court politics for c. 200 years through the *sekkan* regency-and-chancellorship dual office.
- Kamakura shogunate (1185-1333) founded by Minamoto no Yoritomo; the *bakufu* (tent-government) apparatus established.
- Ashikaga shogunate (1336-1573); the Ōnin War (1467-1477) collapsed Ashikaga authority and launched the Sengoku (Warring States) period.
- Oda Nobunaga (1534-1582), Toyotomi Hideyoshi (1537-1598), Tokugawa Ieyasu (1543-1616) — the late-Sengoku unifying-warlord political class; Battle of Sekigahara 1600 established Tokugawa hegemony preceding 1603 bakufu foundation (covered in Chapter 2).

## Korean Joseon political class (1392-1897)
- Founded by Yi Seong-gye (Taejo) 1392; Confucian-Neoconfucian political-class system.
- The *yangban* hereditary scholar-official class operated the Joseon state through the *gwageo* civil-service examination system.
- King Sejong (r. 1418-1450) — the cultural-and-administrative peak; the 1443 *Hangul* alphabet promulgation.
- The Imjin War (1592-1598) — Japanese invasions under Toyotomi Hideyoshi; Korean political-class response under Yi Sun-sin's naval defence.

## European political class (medieval-Renaissance, c. 1200-1600)
- The late-medieval Italian banking-political integration: the Bardi and Peruzzi of Florence (14th c.) — chartered by the Florentine signoria; the 1343-1346 Edward III defaults; the subsequent Florentine banking collapse.
- The Medici Bank (founded 1397 by Giovanni di Bicci de' Medici); the Medici political consolidation in Florence from Cosimo de' Medici (1389-1464); the Medici popes Leo X (1513-1521) and Clement VII (1523-1534).
- The Fugger banking house of Augsburg; Jakob Fugger (1459-1525); the 1519 Imperial election financing of Charles V by Fugger-Welser consortium: 850,000 florins (c. 2,974 kg gold; modern equivalents estimated at $100-134 million) distributed as bribes to the seven prince-electors; Fugger personally provided 543,000 florins (65 per cent); Joachim I of Brandenburg confronted by Fugger's agent that the Fuggers had "placed him and could remove him."
- The medieval English political class: the Magna Carta 1215; the subsequent parliamentary development under the Edwardian and Lancastrian-Yorkist political-class continuity.
- The French Capetian-Valois political class through Francis I.
- The Iberian crowns conducting the Reconquista (completed 1492 with the fall of Granada) and the Atlantic-and-Mexican-and-Andean conquests (1492, 1519-1521, 1532-1572).
- The Holy Roman Empire princely political class.
- The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (Union of Lublin 1569); the *szlachta* nobility-and-elected-monarchy political class.
- The Russian Muscovite political class through Ivan IV (r. 1547-1584); the *oprichnina* repression 1565-1572.

## Roman Republican-and-Early-Imperial political class (covered in existing draft, retained largely as-is)
- *Societates publicanorum* tax-farming syndicates; senatorial-equestrian dual structure; Crassus (115-53 BCE) as the late-Republican political-class operator.
- The 60 BCE Asian tax-farming crisis; the 60 BCE First Triumvirate (Crassus, Caesar, Pompey); the Catonian senatorial faction opposing *publicani*.
- Gracchan reforms 123 BCE; Sulla's 81 BCE reversal; lex Aurelia 70 BCE.
- The Hopkins, Garnsey, Brunt, Cambridge-school documentation of the late-Republican social wars, Spartacus revolt 73-71 BCE, Sullan-and-Caesarian proscriptions.

## General references
- Michael Hudson, *...and forgive them their debts* (2018) — the debt-cancellation comparative history.
- David Graeber, *Debt: The First 5,000 Years* (2011).
- Eric Mielants, *The Origins of Capitalism and the "Rise of the West"* (Temple, 2007).
- Janet Abu-Lughod, *Before European Hegemony: The World System AD 1250-1350* (Oxford, 1989).
- Andre Gunder Frank, *ReOrient: Global Economy in the Asian Age* (California, 1998).
- John Darwin, *After Tamerlane: The Global History of Empire since 1405* (Allen Lane, 2007).
- Patrick Manning, *African Political Systems and African Diaspora History* (various).

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026. All factual claims in Chapter 1 are traceable to the named scholarly works or to multiple independent encyclopaedic sources.

*End of sources file for the rebuilt Chapter 1.*

# Sources — Chapter 2: The Seventeenth Century

## Mughal political class — Akbar's successors through Aurangzeb's death 1707
- Akbar died 1605; succeeded by Jahangir (r. 1605-1627), Shah Jahan (r. 1628-1658), Aurangzeb Alamgir (r. 1658-1707).
- Aurangzeb ascended after the 1657-1659 succession war defeating brothers Dara Shukoh, Shah Shuja, and Murad Bakhsh.
- Aurangzeb reign divided into two halves: northern 1658-1681 administered from Delhi; southern Deccan campaign 1681-1707 from Aurangabad.
- 1687: Aurangzeb annexed Bijapur and Golconda Sultanates — removed the buffer states between Mughals and Marathas.
- 1689: capture and execution of Maratha Chhatrapati Sambhaji (Shivaji's son) by Mughal forces.
- 1687-1707: Mughal-Maratha Deccan Wars; Marathas adopted guerrilla tactics under Rajaram, Tara Bai, and the Maratha commanders.
- 3 March 1707: Aurangzeb died at Ahmednagar aged 88.
- 12 June 1707: Battle of Jajau — Bahadur Shah I defeated his brother Azam Shah; ascended the Mughal throne. Both sides fielded 100,000 combatants per Britannica.
- Post-1707 Mughal collapse: empire reduced to 250-mile radius around Delhi within 40 years per the Rau's IAS analysis.
- The Mughal *mansabdari-jagirdari-zabt* apparatus (Chapter 1) entered its decline under Aurangzeb through the "jagirdari crisis" — excess mansabdars chasing insufficient jagirs across the Deccan campaign.
- Sources: Wikipedia, "Deccan wars"; Vajiram and Ravi, "Aurangzeb and Fall of Mughals"; Britannica, "Battle of Jajau"; Studento, "The Mughal-Maratha Conflict"; Irfan Habib, *The Agrarian System of Mughal India*; Jadunath Sarkar, *History of Aurangzib* (5 volumes, 1912-1924).

## Late Ming collapse and Manchu Qing conquest 1644

### Late Ming fiscal-and-political crisis
- Wanli Emperor (r. 1572-1620): Zhang Juzheng Single Whip reforms of 1572-1582 (Chapter 1) initially stabilised; the 1582 death of Zhang and the post-1582 Wanli withdrawal from administration produced the Ming fiscal decline.
- 1620s-1640s: Ming peasant rebellions including the Li Zicheng rebellion in Shaanxi from 1631; the Zhang Xianzhong rebellion in Sichuan.
- April 1644: Li Zicheng captured Beijing; the last Ming emperor Chongzhen hanged himself on a tree in the imperial garden outside the Forbidden City.

### Manchu Qing foundation and conquest
- Nurhaci (1559-1626) — Jurchen chieftain who created the banner system (initially four banners in 1601, expanded to eight in 1615); founded the Later Jin dynasty 1616.
- Hong Taiji (1592-1643) — Nurhaci's son; renamed the Jurchens "Manchus"; created the eight Mongol banners and the eight Han-martial (*Hànjūn*) banners by 1642; renamed the dynasty "Qing" in 1636.
- 1644: Hong Taiji had died 1643; succeeded by the six-year-old Shunzhi (Fulin, b. 1638). co-regents Dorgon (Nurhaci's 14th son, 1612-1650) and Jirgalang.
- 1644: Wu Sangui, Ming general at Shanhai Pass, opened the pass to Manchu forces. Battle of Shanhai Pass: Dorgon and the bannermen joined Wu Sangui to defeat Li Zicheng. Beijing secured for the Qing.
- November 1644: Shunzhi enthroned in Beijing as first Qing emperor of China proper. Political power held by Dorgon as regent until 1650.
- Dorgon's first orders: northern Beijing vacated for bannermen; Yellow Banners placed north of palace, White Banners east, Red Banners west, Blue Banners south, mirroring the pre-conquest Manchu homeland arrangement.
- 168,900 soldiers in the banner system at the 1644 conquest; expanded to 250,000 by the late seventeenth century.
- 1650: Shunzhi consolidated control after Dorgon's death; the Three Upper Banners (Bordered Yellow, Plain Yellow, Plain White) placed under direct imperial control; the five Lower Banners retained under Manchu princes.
- Han bannermen filled the majority of governor and governor-general posts under Shunzhi and the subsequent Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661-1722); the Manchu-Han dyarchy system institutionalised.
- 1650 Guangzhou massacre of Ming-loyalist Han forces and civilians carried out by Han Bannermen under generals Shang Kexi and Geng Jimao.
- 1681 Kangxi suppression of the Revolt of the Three Feudatories (1673-1681) consolidated Qing rule across southern China.
- Sources: Wikipedia, "Eight Banners"; Wikipedia, "Military of the Qing dynasty"; Wikipedia, "Shunzhi Emperor"; WarHistory.org, "Banner System (1601-1912)"; Britannica, "The early Qing dynasty"; China Knowledge, "baqi 八旗"; EBSCO Research Starters, "Rise of the Manchus."

## Tokugawa bakufu and bakuhan system from 1603
- 21 October 1600: Battle of Sekigahara — Tokugawa Ieyasu defeated the Western Army under Ishida Mitsunari, establishing Tokugawa hegemony.
- 24 March 1603: Tokugawa Ieyasu appointed *shōgun* by Emperor Go-Yōzei; Edo bakufu (Tokugawa Shogunate) established.
- 1615: Siege of Osaka — Tokugawa forces defeated Toyotomi Hideyori (Hideyoshi's son), who was forced to commit *seppuku*; Toyotomi line eliminated.
- 1615: *Buke Shohatto* (Laws for the Military Houses) first promulgated under Ieyasu; reiterated and expanded under shogun Iemitsu (r. 1623-1651) in 1635.
- *Bakuhan* (bakufu-han) system: central Tokugawa government in Edo coexisted with 270 daimyo-administered *han* domains. Daimyo categorised as *shinpan* (Tokugawa relatives), *fudai* (loyal pre-Sekigahara retainers), and *tozama* (outer lords who submitted after Sekigahara).
- *Sankin kōtai* (alternate attendance) system codified 1635 under Iemitsu: daimyo required to alternate yearly residence between their han and Edo; wives and heirs permanently resident in Edo as hostages. fiscal pressure on daimyo from the dual-residence requirement designed to limit daimyo capacity to fund independent military operations.
- Tokugawa direct domains exceeded 7 million *koku* (one-fourth of the country); over 4 million *koku* under direct Tokugawa control, 3 million distributed among *hatamoto* and *gokenin* liege vassals per Britannica.
- 1633-1639 *sakoku* (closed-country) policy: Christian missionaries expelled; trade restricted to Chinese and Dutch contacts at Nagasaki and limited Korean-Ryūkyū-Ainu peripheral contacts. Dutch VOC retained as sole European trading partner via the Dejima factory at Nagasaki.
- peace from 1615 Siege of Osaka until the 1651 Keian Uprising — the 250-year *Pax Tokugawa* internal-peace settlement.
- Sources: Wikipedia, "Tokugawa shogunate"; Britannica, "Japan - The bakuhan system"; Nakasendo Way, "Tokugawa Political System"; Tale of Ronin, "The Bakufu-Han System"; Conrad Totman, *Early Modern Japan* (California, 1993); Marius Jansen, *The Making of Modern Japan* (Belknap, 2000).

## Joseon Korean political class — post-Imjin recovery and Manchu invasions
- post-1598 Joseon recovery from the Imjin War (Chapter 1).
- 1623: Injo Coup deposed the Gwanghaegun (r. 1608-1623); Injo (r. 1623-1649) installed.
- 1627: First Manchu Invasion of Korea — Hong Taiji's pre-Qing forces invaded; Joseon forced into the tributary "elder brother" relationship.
- 1636-1637: Second Manchu Invasion — Joseon refusal to recognise the 1636 Qing dynastic founding; Qing forces besieged Namhansanseong; Injo surrendered at Samjeondo January 1637 and accepted full tributary status under the Qing.
- subsequent Joseon political-class settlement: Confucian-Neoconfucian *yangban* (Chapter 1) consolidated; post-1637 reduced military expenditure and increased factional politics across the *Sarim*-versus-*Hungu* and subsequent Western-Southern-Northern-Old-Young factional divisions.
- Yi Sun-sin's Imjin War record (Chapter 1) institutionalised as cultural memory; Joseon naval-and-military investment subsequently declined.

## Ottoman political class — seventeenth century
- Süleyman I's 1566 death (Chapter 1) followed by Selim II (r. 1566-1574), Murad III (r. 1574-1595), Mehmed III (r. 1595-1603).
- 1603-1617: Ahmed I, who abolished the fratricide succession practice of the preceding Mehmed II's 1451 *Kanunname* (the law authorising new sultans to execute their brothers); replaced with the *kafes* (cage) confinement system.
- 1622 deposition and assassination of Osman II by Janissary revolt — first Ottoman regicide. Janissary corps emerged across the seventeenth century as the decisive internal-political force.
- 1656-1683 Köprülü era: Köprülü Mehmed Pasha (grand vizier 1656-1661) and subsequent Köprülü family viziers reformed the Ottoman administration. 1683 Battle of Vienna under Köprülü-related grand vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha — Ottoman defeat marked the beginning of the post-1683 Ottoman territorial retreat across the subsequent 250 years.
- "Sultanate of Women" period (Chapter 1) extending into the seventeenth century — Kösem Sultan (regent 1623-1651), Turhan Hatice Sultan (regent 1651-1683).
- *malikâne* life-tenure tax-farming contracts introduced late seventeenth century — privatisation of the *timar* revenue system that the Chapter 1 documented at peak.

## Safavid Persia — seventeenth century
- Shah Abbas I (r. 1588-1629) — Chapter 1 covered the *ghulam* reforms.
- Abbas II (r. 1642-1666) — peak of the post-Abbas-I Safavid state.
- Sultan Husayn (r. 1694-1722) — decline.
- 1722: Ghilzai Afghan invasion under Mahmud Hotak; Isfahan fell after seven-month siege; Safavid dynasty collapsed.

## Spanish-American viceregal political class and Potosí silver mining

### The viceregal apparatus
- Council of the Indies (Consejo de Indias) — Madrid-based supreme governing council for Spanish American affairs from 1524.
- Viceroyalty of New Spain (Mexico) from 1535 under Antonio de Mendoza; Viceroyalty of Peru from 1542 under Blasco Núñez Vela.
- *audiencias* (regional high courts) — judicial-and-administrative authority below the viceroy across Mexico City, Lima, Santo Domingo, Panama, Guatemala, Bogotá, Quito, Charcas.
- *encomienda* system: Spanish *encomenderos* granted right to indigenous-population labour and tribute in exchange for nominal Christianisation. abolition attempts via the 1542 New Laws under Las Casas's advocacy were partially reversed under settler pressure.

### Potosí silver mining and the colonial mita
- 1545: silver discovery at Cerro Rico de Potosí (modern Bolivia).
- first boom 1545-c.1560 — surface ores refined with indigenous *guayra* smelting ovens.
- 1571 Toledo Reforms under Viceroy Francisco de Toledo (1515-1582): introduction of mercury amalgamation (the *patio* process) requiring mercury from the Huancavelica mine; establishment of the colonial *mita* labour-draft system in 1573.
- colonial *mita* (distinct from the Inca *mit'a* of Chapter 1): Indigenous men aged 18-50 from 16 designated provinces around Lake Titicaca conscripted to Potosí; up to 14,000 conscripts per year; one-year service obligation; coercive labour conditions producing high mortality from mercury poisoning, silicosis, and altitude-and-exhaustion deaths.
- 1545-1825 estimated 40,000-tonnes of silver produced from the Spanish American empire per Earl Hamilton (*American Treasure and the Price Revolution in Spain*, Harvard 1934) and subsequent research; 80% of global silver supply across the period.
- Crown's *quinto real* (royal fifth) — 20% tax on silver production until reduced to 10% in 1736.
- silver flow via treasure fleets (*flota* system) from Portobelo (Panama) and Veracruz (Mexico) to Seville and Cadiz; parallel Manila Galleon flow from Acapulco to Manila from 1565 — Spanish-American silver to Ming China (Chapter 1) in exchange for Chinese porcelain, silk, and other goods.
- Sources: Encyclopedia.com, "Potosí"; UPenn Decolonizing Indigenous Archives, "Colonial Legislations as a Framework for Dispossessions: The Colonial Mita"; Cambridge Core, "Colonial Organization of Mine Labour in Charcas"; NCBI, "Mercury Production and Use in Colonial Andean Silver Production"; World History Encyclopedia, "The Silver of the Conquistadors"; Jeffrey A. Cole, *The Potosí Mita, 1573-1700* (Stanford, 1985); Peter Bakewell, *Miners of the Red Mountain: Indian Labor in Potosí, 1545-1650* (New Mexico, 1984); Enrique Tandeter, *Coercion and Market: Silver Mining in Colonial Potosí, 1629-1826*.

## Atlantic slave trade as multi-state political-class system

### portuguese establishment
- 1444 first Portuguese captives delivered to Lagos, Portugal — Henry the Navigator's expeditions.
- 1481 Portuguese establishment of Elmina Castle on the Gold Coast (modern Ghana).
- 1518 first direct trans-Atlantic shipment of African captives to Spanish American territories.

### multi-state operation across the seventeenth century
- Spanish *asiento* system: royal-monopoly contracts granted to various European trading nations to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish American territories. asiento held by Portuguese (1595-1640), Dutch West India Company (1662-1675), Portuguese again, French Compagnie de Guinée (1701-1713), and British South Sea Company (1713-1750).
- Dutch West India Company (WIC) chartered 1621; captured Portuguese Brazilian sugar-and-slave operations 1630-1654 (the Dutch Brazil period). WIC slave-trade operations from Elmina (captured 1637), Curaçao, and other Caribbean bases.
- Royal African Company chartered England 1672 under Stuart-royal patent — Duke of York (later James II) major shareholder. RAC monopoly broken 1698 under pressure from independent English-and-British interlopers.
- French Compagnie des Indes occidentales (1664) and successor companies; Saint-Domingue (modern Haiti) plantation economy.
- multi-state slave-trade volume across the 1500s-1800s: 12.5 million enslaved Africans transported, 10.7 million arrived in the Americas (the 1.8 million intermediate death-in-transit) per the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database compiled by David Eltis and the Emory University consortium.
- African coastal-state political classes operated the supply side: Kingdom of Kongo (1500s-1700s), Kingdom of Dahomey (post-1620s), Kingdom of Asante (post-1670s), Kingdom of Benin, the Yoruba states, the Aro Confederacy.
- Sources: David Eltis, *The Rise of African Slavery in the Americas* (Cambridge, 2000); Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org); John K. Thornton, *Africa and Africans in the Making of the Atlantic World, 1400-1800* (Cambridge, 1998); Joseph C. Miller, *Way of Death: Merchant Capitalism and the Angolan Slave Trade, 1730-1830* (Wisconsin, 1988).

## Dutch regenten political class and VOC (retained from existing draft)
- 1602 chartering of VOC by States General under Johan van Oldenbarnevelt (Land's Advocate of Holland from 1586).
- 1609 Amsterdam Wisselbank founded — proto-central-bank of Dutch commerce.
- Heeren XVII — 17-man governing board of the VOC.
- 1618 arrest of Oldenbarnevelt by Stadtholder Maurice of Orange; 13 May 1619 execution in the Binnenhof.
- regenten families: Trips, Bickers, De Geers, Pauws, Witsens.
- 1672 *rampjaar* — French invasion under Louis XIV; 20 August 1672 lynching of Johan de Witt and Cornelis de Witt by Orangist mob in The Hague; William III restored as Stadtholder.

## English political class — Civil War through Glorious Revolution to Bank of England 1694 (retained from existing draft with expansion)
- 1603 Stuart succession — James VI of Scotland to James I of England.
- 1625-1649 Charles I; 1642-1649 English Civil War; 30 January 1649 execution of Charles I.
- 1649-1660 Commonwealth/Protectorate under Cromwell.
- 1660 Restoration; Charles II (r. 1660-1685); James II (r. 1685-1688).
- 1688 Glorious Revolution — William III (Dutch Stadtholder, married to Mary Stuart) invited by parliamentary faction; James II fled.
- 1689 Bill of Rights — constitutional settlement establishing parliamentary sovereignty over royal prerogative on taxation-and-army-and-judicial questions.
- 1694 Bank of England chartering — £1.2 million loan to William III against perpetual annuity at 8% interest. founding charter under William Paterson's 1694 proposal. 1,268 original subscribers including major London merchants and Whig political-class figures.
- subsequent 1697 Bank of England Act extended charter; Bank of England gained monopoly on joint-stock banking in England.

## French political class — Louis XIV and Colbert (retained from existing draft)
- 1610-1643 Louis XIII; 1624-1642 Cardinal Richelieu as chief minister.
- 1643-1715 Louis XIV; 1643-1661 Cardinal Mazarin as chief minister; 1661 Louis XIV personal rule.
- Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619-1683) — Controller-General of Finances 1665-1683; mercantilist fiscal apparatus; 1664 Compagnie des Indes occidentales and 1664 Compagnie des Indes orientales chartered.
- 1685 Edict of Fontainebleau — revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1598); 200,000 Huguenots emigrated to Netherlands, England, Prussia, and North America.

## Romanov Russian political class — Peter the Great
- 1613 Mikhail Romanov elected first Romanov tsar by *zemsky sobor* — end of the Time of Troubles.
- 1645-1676 Alexis I; 1649 *Sobornoye Ulozheniye* (Council Code) codified serfdom as hereditary status binding peasants to land.
- 1654 Russian-Polish War over Ukraine; 1667 Treaty of Andrusovo — Russia gained eastern Ukraine including Kiev.
- 1682-1725 Peter I "the Great" — co-tsar with brother Ivan V from 1682; sole ruler from 1696.
- 1697-1698 Grand Embassy to Western Europe; Peter worked incognito as shipwright at Dutch and English yards.
- 1700-1721 Great Northern War against Sweden under Charles XII; 1709 Battle of Poltava — Russian victory; 1721 Treaty of Nystad — Russia gained Estonia, Livonia, Ingria.
- 1703 founding of Saint Petersburg as new capital.
- 1722 Table of Ranks — 14-rank hierarchy for military, civil, and court service; rank rather than birth determined official position; eighth rank conferred hereditary nobility status. parallel to the Mughal *mansabdari* and the Ottoman *devşirme*-and-*timar* (Chapter 1).

## General references
- Geoffrey Parker, *Global Crisis: War, Climate Change and Catastrophe in the Seventeenth Century* (Yale, 2013) — comprehensive comparative seventeenth-century history.
- Janet L. Abu-Lughod, *Before European Hegemony: The World System AD 1250-1350* (Oxford, 1989).
- John Darwin, *After Tamerlane: The Global History of Empire since 1405* (Allen Lane, 2007).
- Patrick Manning, *African Political Systems and African Diaspora History*.
- Kenneth Pomeranz, *The Great Divergence: China, Europe, and the Making of the Modern World Economy* (Princeton, 2000) — comparative seventeenth-century economic history.

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Chapter 3: The Eighteenth Century

## High Qing era — Kangxi-Yongzheng-Qianlong (1662-1796)

### Kangxi Emperor (r. 1662-1722)
- Born Xuanye 4 May 1654 to Shunzhi Emperor and Empress Xiaokangzhang; ascended throne aged eight; sole rule from 1669 after defeating regent Oboi.
- 1673-1681 Revolt of the Three Feudatories suppressed (Chapter 2).
- 1683 Qing conquest of Taiwan from Zheng family; surrender by Zheng Keshuang (Koxinga's grandson).
- 1689 Treaty of Nerchinsk with Russia — first formal treaty between China and a European power; kept the China-Russia border peaceful for nearly two centuries.
- 1696-1697 defeated Dzungar Mongol leader Galdan; Outer Mongolia incorporated.
- 1712 imperial decree: no additional head taxes on newly added taxable population (续生人丁,永不加赋) — fiscal anchor of the High Qing settlement.
- 1716 Kangxi Dictionary completed; Jesuit mathematicians Ferdinand Verbiest, Jean-François Gerbillon, Joachim Bouvet at court.
- 1722 Kangxi died aged 68 after 61-year reign.

### Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1723-1735)
- Yinzhen (1678-1735); fourth son of Kangxi; ascended after disputed succession.
- 1722 consolidated fixed head tax into land tax (摊丁入地) — completed the Single Whip silver-and-land conversion that the Ming Zhang Juzheng had begun (Chapter 2).
- Centralised the state; abolished hereditary status of servile positions; established the Grand Council (军机处, *junjichu*) as the chief executive body bypassing the Outer Court.
- Suppressed unorthodox sects; tightened censorship over publishing.

### Qianlong Emperor (r. 1735-1796)
- Hongli (1711-1799); grandson of Kangxi; abdicated 1796 to avoid reigning longer than his grandfather; died 1799 aged 88.
- The peak of the High Qing era ("the Prosperous Era of Kangxi and Qianlong"); Ten Great Campaigns of conquest including Dzungar genocide 1755-1758 that eliminated the Dzungar Khanate and incorporated Xinjiang as the new frontier.
- 1750s-1790s Manchu-Han dyarchy continued (Chapter 2); Han bannermen and Han metropolitan officials filled the bulk of the provincial administration.
- 1782 *Siku Quanshu* (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) completed — the largest pre-modern compilation project in world history; the parallel Qing literary inquisition burned or banned 3,000 books deemed politically dangerous.
- 1793 Macartney Embassy rejected; Qianlong's letter to George III: "Our Celestial Empire possesses all things in prolific abundance and lacks no product within its borders. There is therefore no need to import the manufactures of outside barbarians in exchange for our own produce." Modern Joanna Waley-Cohen/Henrietta Harrison revision: Qianlong was aware of Britain's Bengal conquest (via Gurkha-spy intelligence after the 1790-1792 Sino-Nepalese War) and ordered coastal defence strengthening in response to Macartney.
- By 1790s Qing silver reserves peaked at over 70 million taels — two years of total tax revenue per Sng/Moriguchi.
- Heshen corruption scandal: Qianlong favourite Heshen accumulated 800 million taels by his 1799 prosecution and forced suicide (the largest single personal corruption case in Chinese imperial history; 14 years of Qing tax revenue).
- Sources: Wikipedia "Kangxi Emperor"; Wikipedia "Qing dynasty"; Wikipedia "High Qing era"; Britannica "Macartney Embassy"; Yale University Press, "A Lesson for American Foreign Trade with China"; Henrietta Harrison, *The Perils of Interpreting* (Princeton, 2021); Joanna Waley-Cohen, *The Sextants of Beijing* (Norton, 1999); Jonathan Spence, *The Search for Modern China* (Norton, 1990); Tuan-Hwee Sng and Chiaki Moriguchi, "State capacity and great divergence: the case of Qing China, 1644-1911."

## Mughal collapse and successor states (1707-1799)

### Mughal collapse
- 1707-1759 succession crises and imperial fragmentation (Chapter 2 covered 1707 Bahadur Shah I succession at the Battle of Jajau).
- 1739 Nader Shah of Persia sacked Delhi; Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor diamond taken to Iran; 30,000 Delhi residents massacred over a single day.
- 1761 Third Battle of Panipat: Afghan Durrani forces under Ahmad Shah Abdali defeated the Maratha Confederacy; Maratha expansion northward halted.

### Maratha Confederacy
- Shahuji Maharaj (r. 1707-1749), Shivaji's grandson, released by Bahadur Shah I in 1707; Maratha state restored.
- Peshwa office (chief minister, hereditary in Bhat family from 1713) under Balaji Vishwanath, Bajirao I (1720-1740), Balaji Bajirao "Nanasaheb" (1740-1761).
- 1720-1761 Maratha territorial expansion across central and northern India; 1761 Panipat defeat reduced expansion but the Maratha Confederacy continued under Madhavrao I (1761-1772) and subsequent peshwas until the 1818 Third Anglo-Maratha War.

### Awadhi nawabate
- Founded by Saadat Ali Khan Burhan-ul-Mulk (governor from 1722; nawab from 1732 *de facto*).
- subsequent nawabs Safdar Jung (1739-1754) and Shuja-ud-Daula (1754-1775) operated Awadh as *de facto* independent state while nominally Mughal vassal.
- 1764 Battle of Buxar: EIC defeated combined Awadh-Bengal-Mughal force under Shuja-ud-Daula, Mir Qasim, and the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II.

### Bengali nawabate
- Alivardi Khan (r. 1740-1756) ruled Bengal as *de facto* independent.
- Siraj-ud-Daulah (r. 1756-1757) defeated by EIC under Robert Clive at the 1757 Battle of Plassey through bribery of his commander Mir Jafar.
- 1757-1765 EIC consolidation of Bengal; 1765 Treaty of Allahabad granted EIC the *diwani* (revenue rights) of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa from Shah Alam II — the foundational legal basis of British India.

### Hyderabadi nizamate
- Founded by Asaf Jah I Qamar-ud-din Khan (Nizam-ul-Mulk; Mughal viceroy of Deccan from 1713; *de facto* independent from 1724).
- Hyderabad operated as multi-ethnic Deccani political-class settlement until the 1948 Indian Operation Polo annexation.

### Mysore under Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan
- Hyder Ali (r. 1761-1782) seized Mysore from the Wodeyar dynasty.
- Tipu Sultan (r. 1782-1799) — son of Hyder; the most systematic Indian-state resistance to British expansion in the eighteenth century.
- Anglo-Mysore Wars (1767-1769, 1780-1784, 1790-1792, 1799); Tipu killed at the 1799 Battle of Seringapatam.

### Sikh political-class formation
- Sikh confederacies (*misls*) consolidated across the eighteenth century in Punjab.
- subsequent Ranjit Singh (1780-1839) consolidated the Sikh Empire from 1799 onward.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Mughal Empire"; Wikipedia "Maratha Confederacy"; Wikipedia "Battle of Plassey"; Wikipedia "Battle of Buxar"; Wikipedia "Treaty of Allahabad"; William Dalrymple, *The Anarchy* (Bloomsbury, 2019).

## Tokugawa eighteenth-century reforms

### Kyōhō Reforms under Yoshimune (1716-1745)
- eighth shogun Tokugawa Yoshimune (r. 1716-1745) launched 1722 Kyōhō Reforms in response to bakufu fiscal crisis.
- austerity measures, agricultural development, rice-price stabilisation, *agemai* (rice contributions from daimyo).
- 1721 Edo-shi-no-mi (Edo poor-relief commission); 1722 Koishikawa Charity Hospital.

### Tanuma Era under Tanuma Okitsugu (1772-1786)
- Tanuma Okitsugu rose from hatamoto retainer status to chamberlain (1767) and *rōjū* councillor (1772) under tenth shogun Ieharu.
- Tanuma's reforms emphasised commercial development, monopoly licensing of commercial guilds (*kabu nakama*) for fees, expansion of copper coinage, cautious reopening of limited foreign trade.
- Tanuma fell in 1786 after Ieharu's death and Matsudaira Sadanobu's 1787 Kansei reform reaction.

### Kansei Reforms under Matsudaira Sadanobu (1787-1793)
- Matsudaira Sadanobu (1759-1829), grandson of Yoshimune; chief councillor 1787-1793.
- reactionary Kansei Reforms reversed the Tanuma commercial-development programme; return to agrarian-and-austerity settlement.

## Ottoman political class — eighteenth century

### Tulip Era (Lale Devri, 1718-1730)
- post-1718 Treaty of Passarowitz peace period under Sultan Ahmed III (r. 1703-1730) and Grand Vizier Damat Ibrahim Pasha (1718-1730).
- limited European cultural-and-technological imports; 1727 Ibrahim Müteferrika printing press (the first Ottoman Turkish-language press).
- 1730 Patrona Halil revolt by Janissaries and Istanbul artisans ended the Tulip Era; Ahmed III deposed; Mahmud I (r. 1730-1754) installed.

### Nizam-i Cedid under Selim III (1789-1807)
- Selim III (r. 1789-1807) launched the *Nizam-i Cedid* ("New Order") reform programme.
- new infantry corps trained on European lines; tax-collection reforms; diplomatic missions established in London, Paris, Vienna, Berlin from 1793.
- 1807 Janissary revolt deposed Selim III; subsequent 1808 assassination of Selim by Janissary forces; Mahmud II (r. 1808-1839) launched the subsequent Tanzimat-precursor reforms.

## Wahhabi-Saud pact 1744 and the founding of Saudi Arabia

- 1744 pact between Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703-1792), the religious reformer of the Najdi-and-central-Arabian Salafi-Wahhabi movement, and Muhammad ibn Saud (d. 1765), the emir of Diriyah.
- pact established the First Saudi State (Emirate of Diriyah, 1744-1818) on the dual religious-and-political settlement that has continuously defined the Saudi state apparatus across the subsequent 282 years through the Second Saudi State (Emirate of Nejd, 1824-1891) and the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (founded 1932 by Abdulaziz ibn Saud).
- 1803-1804 First Saudi State sacked Mecca and Medina; 1818 Egyptian Khedival forces under Ibrahim Pasha (son of Muhammad Ali) destroyed the Diriyah capital under Ottoman commission.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Emirate of Diriyah"; Wikipedia "First Saudi State"; Wikipedia "Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab"; David Commins, *The Wahhabi Mission and Saudi Arabia* (I.B. Tauris, 2006).

## Qajar founding 1789 — Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar

- 1722 Safavid collapse (Chapter 2); 1736-1747 Nader Shah Afsharid interregnum; 1747-1779 Zand dynasty under Karim Khan based at Shiraz.
- Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar (born c. 1742, Astarabad) — castrated aged six on Adil Shah Afsharid's orders to eliminate political-rivalry potential; held as political hostage at Shiraz 1762-1779; escaped 2 March 1779 after Karim Khan Zand's death.
- 1779-1789 Agha Mohammad Khan consolidated northern Iran; 1786 designated Tehran as new capital.
- 1789 enthroned as chief of Qajars and named nephew Baba Khan (future Fath-Ali Shah) heir; 1794 defeated last Zand ruler Lotf Ali Khan at Kerman (where 20,000 Kermani inhabitants were blinded or massacred on Agha Mohammad's order); 1796 formally crowned Shah.
- 1797 assassinated by personal servants near Shusha; succeeded by nephew Fath-Ali Shah (r. 1797-1834).
- Qajar state operated through tribal alliances and familial appointments; persistent internal divisions and fiscal-and-administrative weakness.
- 1804-1813 First Russo-Persian War; 1813 Treaty of Gulistan ceded Georgia, Dagestan, and northern Azerbaijan to Russia.
- 1826-1828 Second Russo-Persian War; 1828 Treaty of Turkmenchay ceded Yerevan, Nakhchivan, and remaining Caucasian territories to Russia.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Qajar dynasty"; Wikipedia "Qajar Iran"; Wikipedia "Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar"; Britannica "Qajar dynasty"; Britannica "Agha Mohammad Khan."

## Asante Empire eighteenth-century peak
- Asante Empire consolidated 1670s onward under Osei Tutu I (Chapter 2); eighteenth-century peak under Opoku Ware I (r. 1720-1750), Osei Kwadwo (r. 1764-1777), Osei Kwame (r. 1777-1803).
- Asante administrative apparatus operated through the Asantehene (king at Kumasi) and the subordinate amanhene (paramount chiefs) of the coalesced akan-and-other states.
- Asante gold-and-slave trade with the coastal European factories at the Cape Coast Castle (British), Elmina Castle (Dutch, formerly Portuguese), and other coastal posts produced the fiscal base of the eighteenth-century Asante state.
- 1807 British abolition of trans-Atlantic slave trade began the subsequent Asante economic-and-political reorientation.

## Bourbon Reforms in Spanish America (1750s-1780s)
- Bourbon Reforms under Charles III (r. 1759-1788) — fiscal-and-administrative restructuring of the Spanish American viceregal apparatus.
- 1776 creation of Viceroyalty of Río de la Plata at Buenos Aires.
- 1782 *intendencias* system across Spanish American territories — provincial administrators recruited from Spain-born *peninsulares* replacing locally-born *criollos*.
- 1780-1783 Tupac Amaru II rebellion in Peru — the largest indigenous-and-mestizo uprising in colonial Latin American history.
- 1781 Comuneros rebellion in New Granada (modern Colombia).

## British political class (Walpole through American Revolution)

### Walpole and the Robinocracy (1721-1742) — retained from existing draft
- Sir Robert Walpole, First Lord of the Treasury 1721-1742; the longest single tenure of any British prime minister.
- post-1720 South Sea Bubble crisis recovery; Walpole's consolidation of the Bank-of-England-and-parliamentary-Whig political-class settlement that the 1688-1694 architecture had built.
- Walpole patronage system; parliamentary control through royal-civil-list and commercial-favour distribution.

### Mid-eighteenth-century British political class
- Seven Years' War (1756-1763): British decisive victory over France; 1763 Treaty of Paris transferred French Canada, Spanish Florida, and several Caribbean islands to Britain.
- subsequent post-1763 North American taxation crisis; 1765 Stamp Act; 1773 Tea Act; 1775-1783 American Revolutionary War.

### Pitt the Younger and the 1780s-1800s reform period
- William Pitt the Younger, prime minister 1783-1801 and 1804-1806.
- 1788-1789 Regency Crisis; 1801 Acts of Union with Ireland.

## French political class — Mississippi Bubble through Revolution

### John Law's System (1716-1720) — retained from existing draft
- John Law (1671-1729) — Scottish economist exiled from Britain after 1694 duel-killing.
- 1716 chartered Banque Générale (renamed Banque Royale 1718) — first French note-issuing bank.
- 1717 chartered Mississippi Company (Compagnie d'Occident) — monopoly on Louisiana trade and consolidated with other French chartered companies into 1719 Compagnie des Indes.
- 1718-1720 Mississippi Bubble: Mississippi Company share price rose from 500 livres in 1717 to 18,000 livres in late 1719; 1720 collapse; Banque Royale suspended payments May 1720; Law fled France December 1720.

### Pre-Revolutionary French political class
- Louis XV (r. 1715-1774) and Louis XVI (r. 1774-1792).
- fiscal crisis driven by Seven Years' War (1756-1763), American Revolutionary War financial support (1778-1783), and structural deficit.
- 1788 calling of the Estates-General to address the fiscal crisis.

### French Revolution (1789-1799)
- 5 May 1789 Estates-General convened at Versailles.
- 17 June 1789 Third Estate declared itself the National Assembly.
- 14 July 1789 storming of the Bastille.
- 26 August 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen.
- 1789-1791 constitutional monarchy period.
- 21 January 1793 execution of Louis XVI; 16 October 1793 execution of Marie Antoinette.
- 1793-1794 Reign of Terror under Committee of Public Safety; 28 July 1794 execution of Robespierre.
- 1795-1799 Directory; 9 November 1799 Napoleon's 18 Brumaire coup ended the Directory.

## Hamilton-Jefferson settlement (1789-1801) — retained from existing draft
- 17 September 1787 US Constitution ratified by Constitutional Convention.
- 1789 George Washington inaugurated first president; Alexander Hamilton appointed first Secretary of the Treasury.
- 1790 Hamilton's "Report on Public Credit" proposed federal assumption of state Revolutionary War debts and consolidation into funded national debt.
- 1791 First Bank of the United States chartered for 20-year term; modeled on the Bank of England.
- 1791 Hamilton's "Report on Manufactures" outlined the protectionist-industrial development programme.
- Jefferson-Madison Democratic-Republican opposition organised against Hamilton's centralisation programme.
- 1800 election produced Jefferson presidency 1801-1809; 1811 First Bank of the United States charter expired.

## Rothschild rise
- Mayer Amschel Rothschild (1744-1812) — Frankfurt court factor for William, Prince-Elector of Hesse-Kassel from 1769.
- five sons established in five European capitals: Amschel Mayer (Frankfurt), Salomon Mayer (Vienna from 1820), Nathan Mayer (London from 1798), Carl Mayer (Naples from 1820), James Mayer (Paris from 1812).
- 1810s Rothschild consolidation of cross-border European banking dominance through Napoleonic-War financing.
- 1815 post-Waterloo Rothschild role in British government financing institutionalised the subsequent nineteenth-century Rothschild-London-Paris-Vienna-Frankfurt-Naples banking dominance — the subject of Chapter 4.

## General references
- John Darwin, *After Tamerlane: The Global History of Empire since 1405* (Allen Lane, 2007).
- Christopher Bayly, *The Birth of the Modern World, 1780-1914* (Blackwell, 2004).
- Kenneth Pomeranz, *The Great Divergence: China, Europe, and the Making of the Modern World Economy* (Princeton, 2000).
- William Dalrymple, *The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company* (Bloomsbury, 2019).
- Niall Ferguson, *The Cash Nexus: Money and Power in the Modern World, 1700-2000* (Allen Lane, 2001).
- Niall Ferguson, *The House of Rothschild* (Penguin, 1998; two volumes).
