# Sources — Chapter 4: The Long Nineteenth Century

## Qing decline (1796-1912)

### Post-Qianlong inflection — White Lotus Rebellion to First Opium War
- 1796-1805 White Lotus Rebellion in central China (Sichuan, Hubei, Shaanxi) — first major Qing internal-rebellion under Jiaqing (r. 1796-1820).
- 1820-1850 Daoguang reign — accelerating decline.
- 1839 Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed 20,000 chests of British opium at Canton — triggered First Opium War.

### First Opium War (1839-1842) and Treaty of Nanjing
- British Royal Navy expeditionary force dispatched 1840.
- 29 August 1842 Treaty of Nanjing — Qing ceded Hong Kong Island, opened five treaty ports (Canton, Amoy, Foochow, Ningpo, Shanghai), paid £21 million indemnity, abolished Cohong (Hong) merchant monopoly.
- 1843 Treaty of the Bogue — extraterritoriality and most-favoured-nation provisions added.

### Taiping Rebellion (1850-1864)
- Hong Xiuquan (1814-1864), Hakka from Guangdong; failed civil-service exams; founded God Worshipping Society after 1837 visionary experience.
- 11 January 1851 Hong proclaimed Heavenly Kingdom of Great Peace (Taiping Tianguo) at Jintian, Guangxi.
- 1853 Taiping captured Nanjing, renamed Tianjing ("Heavenly Capital"); subsequent 11-year Taiping rule over Yangtze valley territories.
- 1856 Tianjing Incident — internal Taiping leadership purges destabilised the movement.
- Zeng Guofan organised the Xiang Army (Hunan Army); Li Hongzhang organised the Huai Army (Anhui Army) — provincial militia armies that replaced the collapsed Banner and Green Standard armies.
- Charles "Chinese" Gordon's Ever Victorious Army (Western-officered Chinese mercenaries) reinforced Qing forces.
- 1 June 1864 Hong Xiuquan died at Tianjing (food poisoning from wild vegetables; suicide-or-illness contested).
- July 1864 Qing forces under Zeng Guofan's brother Zeng Guoquan retook Nanjing; Taiping civilian population massacred.
- Death toll: 20-30 million per multiple modern estimates including Wikipedia (5-10% of Qing population), Britannica (~20 million), History.com (~20 million), Vaia (20-30 million), History Chronicler (20-30 million).
- 17 provinces ravaged per Britannica.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Taiping Rebellion"; Britannica "Taiping Rebellion"; History.com "Taiping Rebellion: Causes, Definition & Death Toll"; History Chronicler "The Taiping Rebellion: China's Deadliest Civil War"; New World Encyclopedia "Taiping Rebellion."

### Second Opium War, Self-Strengthening, and the Cixi dowager era
- 1856-1860 Second Opium War (Arrow War); 18 October 1860 British-French sack of Yuanmingyuan (Summer Palace) under Lord Elgin and General Charles Cousin-Montauban; Treaty of Tianjin (1858) and Convention of Beijing (1860) expanded treaty-port system, legalised opium, opened interior to European merchants and missionaries.
- 1861-1895 Self-Strengthening Movement (洋务运动 *yangwu yundong*) — limited Qing institutional reform under Prince Gong, Li Hongzhang, Zeng Guofan, Zuo Zongtang focused on military-industrial modernisation. Jiangnan Arsenal (1865), Fuzhou Navy Yard (1866), China Merchants Steam Navigation Company (1872).
- Empress Dowager Cixi (1835-1908) — *de facto* ruler 1861-1908 through regencies of Tongzhi (r. 1861-1875) and Guangxu (r. 1875-1908). 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War defeat (Treaty of Shimonoseki ceded Taiwan to Japan, paid 200 million tael indemnity).
- 11 June - 21 September 1898 Hundred Days' Reform under Guangxu and reformers Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao; 21 September 1898 Cixi coup, Guangxu deposed *de facto*, six reformers executed.

### Boxer Uprising and 1911 Xinhai Revolution
- 1899-1901 Boxer Uprising (Yihequan, "Righteous and Harmonious Fists") — peasant-and-martial-arts movement against foreign-and-Christian presence.
- 21 June 1900 Cixi declared war on eight foreign powers; subsequent Eight-Nation Alliance (Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Japan, Italy, Austria-Hungary, US) invaded; 14 August 1900 Beijing relief; subsequent massive looting and atrocities.
- 7 September 1901 Boxer Protocol — 450 million tael indemnity paid over 39 years ($333 million in 1901 dollars).
- 10 October 1911 Wuchang Uprising — start of Xinhai Revolution.
- 12 February 1912 abdication of six-year-old Puyi (Xuantong Emperor); end of Qing dynasty after 268 years.
- 1 January 1912 Sun Yat-sen inaugurated provisional president of Republic of China at Nanjing.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Boxer Rebellion"; Wikipedia "Xinhai Revolution"; Wikipedia "Empress Dowager Cixi"; Jonathan Spence, *The Search for Modern China*.

## Meiji Restoration 1868 and Japanese political class

- 8 July 1853 Commodore Matthew Perry's Black Ships arrived at Edo Bay with US demand for commercial relations.
- 31 March 1854 Treaty of Kanagawa opened Shimoda and Hakodate to US trade — terminated 1633-1639 *sakoku* policy.
- 1858 Harris Treaty (Treaty of Amity and Commerce) extended commercial-and-extraterritorial provisions.
- 1860s multi-domain anti-bakufu movement under Satsuma (under Saigō Takamori, Ōkubo Toshimichi) and Chōshū (under Kido Takayoshi, Itō Hirobumi, Yamagata Aritomo).
- 3 January 1868 Meiji Restoration declared — Emperor Meiji (Mutsuhito, b. 1852, r. 1867-1912) restored to political authority; bakufu abolished; shogun Tokugawa Yoshinobu resigned.
- 1868-1869 Boshin War — pro-imperial forces defeated pro-bakufu daimyo at Toba-Fushimi, Aizu, Hakodate.
- 1871 *Haihan chiken* (abolition of han) — 270 daimyo domains converted to 47 prefectures under central-government administrators.
- 1873 conscription law replaced hereditary samurai class with universal male conscription.
- 1877 Satsuma Rebellion under Saigō Takamori suppressed; end of samurai class as military force.
- 1889 Meiji Constitution promulgated under Itō Hirobumi (modelled on 1871 Prussian Constitution); bicameral Diet with limited suffrage.
- 1894-1895 First Sino-Japanese War — Japanese victory over Qing; 17 April 1895 Treaty of Shimonoseki ceded Taiwan, Pescadores, Liaodong Peninsula (returned under Triple Intervention), 200 million tael indemnity.
- 1904-1905 Russo-Japanese War — 27-28 May 1905 Battle of Tsushima destroyed Russian Baltic Fleet; 5 September 1905 Treaty of Portsmouth recognised Japanese position in Korea and Manchuria.
- 1910 Japan-Korea Annexation Treaty incorporated Korea as Japanese colony.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Meiji Restoration"; Marius B. Jansen, *The Making of Modern Japan* (Belknap, 2000); Andrew Gordon, *A Modern History of Japan* (Oxford, 2003).

## Post-1857 British Raj

- 10 May 1857 Indian Rebellion (Sepoy Mutiny) — Bengal Army sepoys at Meerut revolted against EIC officers; subsequent multi-region uprising across northern and central India.
- 30 March 1858 British capture of Delhi; 8 July 1858 official end of rebellion.
- 1857-1858 atrocities on both sides; 800,000 Indian deaths over the subsequent multi-year aftermath including famine deaths per the demographic analysis.
- 2 August 1858 Government of India Act — EIC dissolved; direct British Crown rule established under Viceroy of India (first viceroy: Lord Canning, 1858-1862).
- 1 May 1876 Queen Victoria proclaimed Empress of India (*Kaisar-i-Hind*).
- 1885 Indian National Congress founded at Bombay by Allan Octavian Hume and subsequent moderate-and-extremist factions.
- 1905 Partition of Bengal under Lord Curzon — subsequent swadeshi (self-sufficiency) movement.
- 1906 All-India Muslim League founded at Dacca.
- 1909 Indian Councils Act (Morley-Minto Reforms) — limited Indian representation introduced.
- Famine record: 1876-1878 Great Famine of southern India (5.5-10 million deaths), 1896-1902 Late Victorian Holocausts famines (12-29 million cumulative deaths per Mike Davis's *Late Victorian Holocausts*, 2001).

## Ottoman Tanzimat through 1908 Young Turks

- 1808-1839 Mahmud II — 15 June 1826 "Auspicious Incident" suppression of Janissary corps; subsequent new modern army (Asakir-i Mansure-i Muhammediye).
- 3 November 1839 Gülhane Edict (Hatt-ı Şerif of Gülhane) launched Tanzimat ("Reorganisation") under Sultan Abdülmecid I (r. 1839-1861).
- 1856 Hatt-ı Hümayun extended Tanzimat reforms — equality of Ottoman subjects irrespective of religion.
- 1876 First Ottoman Constitution under Midhat Pasha; subsequent 1878 suspension by Abdülhamid II (r. 1876-1909).
- 1877-1878 Russo-Turkish War — Treaty of San Stefano and subsequent 13 July 1878 Treaty of Berlin partitioned Balkan territories (Romania, Serbia, Montenegro recognised independent; Bulgaria autonomous).
- 1875 Ottoman default on foreign debt; 1881 Ottoman Public Debt Administration (Düyun-u Umumiye) established under European creditor control — foreign-creditor fiscal authority over Ottoman revenues.
- 24 July 1908 Young Turk Revolution restored 1876 Constitution; subsequent 27 April 1909 Abdülhamid II deposed.
- 1908-1918 Committee of Union and Progress (CUP, İttihat ve Terakki Cemiyeti) under Enver Pasha, Talaat Pasha, Cemal Pasha — triumvirate that governed the Ottoman state through the 1914-1918 World War.
- 1915-1923 Armenian Genocide — 1.5 million Armenian deaths from 1915 CUP deportation orders and subsequent massacres.

## Qajar Iran — Constitutional Revolution 1906

- 1813 Treaty of Gulistan and 1828 Treaty of Turkmenchay (Chapter 3) ceded Caucasian territories to Russia.
- 1848-1896 Naser al-Din Shah Qajar — modernisation attempts; 1 May 1896 assassination by Mirza Reza Kermani.
- 1872 Reuter Concession — Naser al-Din Shah granted near-total monopoly over Iranian natural resources to Baron Julius de Reuter; subsequent cancellation under Russian-and-clerical pressure.
- 1890 Tobacco Protest — nationwide boycott led by Shia clergy against 1890 tobacco monopoly granted to Major Talbot of British Imperial Tobacco Corporation; Naser al-Din Shah forced to cancel concession.
- December 1905 - August 1906 Persian Constitutional Revolution — bazaar merchants, ulema, and intellectuals demanded constitutional government.
- 5 August 1906 Muzaffar al-Din Shah signed decree establishing Majlis (parliament).
- 30 December 1906 First Iranian Constitution promulgated.
- 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention divided Iran into Russian (north) and British (south) spheres of influence with central neutral zone.
- 23 June 1908 Mohammad Ali Shah's Russian-officered Persian Cossack Brigade under Colonel Vladimir Liakhov shelled Majlis building; subsequent 1908-1909 civil war.
- 16 July 1909 Mohammad Ali Shah deposed; 11-year-old Ahmad Shah installed (r. 1909-1925).
- 1911 Russian ultimatum forced dismissal of US treasury adviser Morgan Shuster — end of constitutional experiment.

## Latin American independence 1810-1825 and the subsequent republican settlements

### Independence wars
- 16 September 1810 Mexican War of Independence began with Miguel Hidalgo's "Grito de Dolores"; subsequent 11-year war ended with 27 September 1821 Plan of Iguala under Agustín de Iturbide.
- 25 May 1810 May Revolution at Buenos Aires; 9 July 1816 Argentine Declaration of Independence at Tucumán.
- 1810-1819 Bolivarian campaigns in northern South America — Simón Bolívar (1783-1830) — 7 August 1819 Battle of Boyacá liberated Colombia; 24 June 1821 Battle of Carabobo liberated Venezuela; 24 May 1822 Battle of Pichincha under Sucre liberated Ecuador.
- 1817-1818 José de San Martín's Crossing of the Andes; 12 February 1818 Chilean independence.
- 9 December 1824 Battle of Ayacucho under Sucre decisive defeat of royalist forces in Peru.
- 6 August 1825 Bolivian independence declared at Chuquisaca.
- 7 September 1822 Pedro I declared Brazilian independence from Portugal; subsequent 1822-1889 Brazilian Empire (Pedro I, Pedro II); 15 November 1889 Brazilian Republic declared.

### Republican settlements
- 1820s-1830s Bolivarian dream of Gran Colombia (1819-1831) collapsed; subsequent partition into Venezuela, New Granada (subsequently Colombia and Panama), and Ecuador.
- 1846-1848 Mexican-American War — Mexican loss of 55% of territory (Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, California, Utah, Nevada, Colorado portions) under 2 February 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.
- 1864-1867 French intervention in Mexico under Emperor Maximilian; 19 June 1867 Maximilian executed.
- 1870s-1880s Latin American caudillismo political-class settlements under multiple post-independence strongmen across multiple republics.
- 1879-1884 War of the Pacific — Chile defeated Peru and Bolivia; Bolivia lost coastal territory (Atacama).
- 1898 Spanish-American War — US acquired Cuba (protectorate), Puerto Rico, Philippines, Guam from Spain.

## Scramble for Africa and the 1884-1885 Berlin Conference

### Pre-Berlin antecedents
- 1830 French invasion of Algiers — 132-year French colonial period in Algeria.
- 1869 Suez Canal completed; 1882 British occupation of Egypt after Urabi revolt.
- 1830s-1840s Mfecane (Difaqane) — Zulu-and-related southern African demographic-and-political reorganisation.
- 1879 Anglo-Zulu War — 22 January 1879 Battle of Isandlwana (British defeat); subsequent 4 July 1879 Battle of Ulundi (British victory and Zulu Kingdom defeat).
- 1881 French protectorate over Tunisia.

### Berlin Conference November 1884 - February 1885
- 15 November 1884 - 26 February 1885 Berlin West Africa Conference convened at 77 Wilhelmstrasse Berlin under German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck at the request of King Leopold II of Belgium.
- 14 countries attended (Austria-Hungary, Belgium, Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Portugal, Russia, Spain, Sweden-Norway, Ottoman Empire, UK, US); no African nation attended or was consulted.
- General Act of Berlin (26 February 1885) established "effective occupation" principle — European claims to African territories recognised only on demonstration of actual administrative control through treaties with local leaders, administrative establishment, or military/police deployment.
- General Act regulated trade in Congo Basin, navigation of Niger and Congo rivers, forbade slave trading.
- Congo Free State (1885-1908) — personal property of King Leopold II until 1908 Belgian government takeover. Death toll: 10 million Congolese under Leopold's rubber-extraction system per Adam Hochschild's *King Leopold's Ghost* (1998).
- post-1885 European control of African territory: from 10% in 1884 to 90% by 1914 per multiple demographic-cartographic estimates.
- Sources: Wikipedia "Berlin Conference"; World History Encyclopedia "Berlin Conference 1884-5"; Britannica "Berlin Conference"; Al Jazeera "Colonising Africa: What happened at the Berlin Conference"; Adam Hochschild, *King Leopold's Ghost* (Houghton Mifflin, 1998); Thomas Pakenham, *The Scramble for Africa* (Random House, 1991).

### Post-Berlin partition (1885-1914)
- 1885 German East Africa (Tanzania), German Southwest Africa (Namibia), Kamerun, Togoland.
- 1890 Anglo-German Heligoland-Zanzibar Treaty.
- 1898 Fashoda Incident — Anglo-French confrontation in Sudan resolved by French withdrawal.
- 1899-1902 Second Boer War — British defeated Boer republics (Transvaal, Orange Free State); 31 May 1902 Treaty of Vereeniging.
- 1904-1907 Herero and Nama Genocide in German Southwest Africa — 65,000 Herero and 10,000 Nama killed under General Lothar von Trotha's 1904 Vernichtungsbefehl extermination order.
- 1908 Congo Free State transferred to Belgian government (Belgian Congo) after international outcry over Leopold's atrocities.
- 1910 Union of South Africa under British dominion status.
- 1911-1912 Italo-Turkish War — Italy seized Libya from Ottomans.
- By 1914: Liberia and Ethiopia (after 1 March 1896 Battle of Adwa defeated Italian invasion) the only independent African states.

## US political class — Civil War through Gilded Age through Jekyll Island 1910

### Antebellum and Civil War (retained foundational details)
- 1820 Missouri Compromise; 1850 Compromise; 1854 Kansas-Nebraska Act; 1857 Dred Scott decision.
- 6 November 1860 Abraham Lincoln elected president.
- 20 December 1860 South Carolina seceded; subsequent 10 other Southern states.
- 12 April 1861 - 9 April 1865 American Civil War; 14 April 1865 Lincoln assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre.
- 18 December 1865 13th Amendment abolished slavery; 9 July 1868 14th Amendment; 3 February 1870 15th Amendment.

### Reconstruction and post-Reconstruction (1865-1877)
- Andrew Johnson impeachment 1868 (acquitted); Ulysses S. Grant administration 1869-1877 — multi-scandal period (Crédit Mobilier, Whiskey Ring, Black Friday gold corner).
- Compromise of 1877 — Rutherford B. Hayes inauguration in exchange for end of Reconstruction; subsequent post-Reconstruction racial-restoration regime in the South.

### Gilded Age and the Jekyll Island plan (retained from existing draft)
- 1877-1900 Gilded Age political class — Rockefeller (Standard Oil 1870), Carnegie (US Steel 1901), J.P. Morgan, Vanderbilts, Astors.
- 1890 Sherman Antitrust Act; 1893 Panic; 1907 Panic.
- 22 November - 30 November 1910 Jekyll Island meeting — Senator Nelson Aldrich, A. Piatt Andrew (Assistant Secretary of Treasury), Henry P. Davison (J.P. Morgan), Frank Vanderlip (National City Bank, Rockefeller-aligned), Charles D. Norton (First National Bank of New York), Benjamin Strong (Bankers Trust), and Paul Warburg (Kuhn, Loeb) met at Jekyll Island Club (Georgia, owned by Morgan and Rockefeller interests) to draft what became the Federal Reserve Act.
- 23 December 1913 Federal Reserve Act signed by Woodrow Wilson — central bank with 12 regional reserve banks under Federal Reserve Board.

## Bismarck and the German political class (retained from existing draft)
- 23 September 1862 Otto von Bismarck appointed Minister-President of Prussia under Wilhelm I.
- 1864 Second Schleswig War; 1866 Austro-Prussian War; 1870-1871 Franco-Prussian War.
- 18 January 1871 German Empire proclaimed at Versailles Hall of Mirrors.
- 1872-1878 Kulturkampf against Catholic Centre Party; 1878 Anti-Socialist Laws; subsequent 1883-1889 welfare-state legislation (health insurance 1883, accident insurance 1884, old-age and disability insurance 1889) — first comprehensive modern welfare state.
- 18 March 1890 Wilhelm II dismissed Bismarck.

## French Second Empire and Third Republic (retained from existing draft)
- 2 December 1851 Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte coup; 2 December 1852 Second Empire proclaimed as Napoleon III.
- 1853-1870 Baron Haussmann's Paris reconstruction.
- 1870-1871 Franco-Prussian War; 2 September 1870 Napoleon III captured at Sedan; 4 September 1870 Third Republic proclaimed.
- 18 March - 28 May 1871 Paris Commune; subsequent bloody week and 20,000 Communard executions.
- 1894-1906 Dreyfus Affair.

## Romanov Russia — Alexander II through 1905 Revolution
- 1801-1825 Alexander I; 1825-1855 Nicholas I; 1853-1856 Crimean War defeat.
- 19 February 1861 Alexander II emancipated 23 million serfs.
- 13 March 1881 Alexander II assassinated by Narodnaya Volya bomb at St. Petersburg.
- 1881-1894 Alexander III; 1894-1917 Nicholas II.
- 22 January 1905 Bloody Sunday; 1905 Russian Revolution; 30 October 1905 October Manifesto established State Duma.

## Pre-1914 financial integration
- gold standard pre-1914 facilitated unprecedented cross-border capital movement.
- London City financial centre — Rothschilds, Barings, Schröders, Hambros — dominant pre-1914 international capital market.
- Paris-Vienna-Frankfurt-New-York-Berlin secondary centres.
- Carroll Quigley, *Tragedy and Hope* (1966), documents pre-1914 international banker network.

## General references
- Christopher Bayly, *The Birth of the Modern World, 1780-1914* (Blackwell, 2004).
- Eric Hobsbawm, *The Age of Capital, 1848-1875* (1975); *The Age of Empire, 1875-1914* (1987).
- Jürgen Osterhammel, *The Transformation of the World: A Global History of the Nineteenth Century* (Princeton, 2014).
- Niall Ferguson, *The House of Rothschild* (two volumes, Penguin, 1998-1999).
- Mike Davis, *Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World* (Verso, 2001).
- Adam Hochschild, *King Leopold's Ghost* (Houghton Mifflin, 1998).
- Thomas Pakenham, *The Scramble for Africa* (Random House, 1991).
- Carroll Quigley, *Tragedy and Hope* (Macmillan, 1966).

# Sources — Part IV South Asian Historical Chapter

## Pre-independence period (1900-1947)

### British Raj architecture
- British Raj: 1858-1947 (direct Crown rule following the Government of India Act 1858 after the 1857 Sepoy Rebellion / First War of Independence).
- East India Company rule: 1757-1858 (from the Battle of Plassey to the post-1857 transfer to Crown).
- Capital: Calcutta until 1911, then New Delhi.
- Deindustrialisation of Bengali textile industry under East India Company rule producing the transfer of textile manufacturing wealth from Bengal to industrial Britain documented in Bairoch / Maddison / Tharoor economic-history literature.
- Pre-1700: India accounted for 24% of global GDP per Maddison estimates.
- 1947: India accounted for 4% of global GDP per Maddison estimates.

### Indian National Congress (INC)
- Founded 28 December 1885 in Bombay by Allan Octavian Hume, Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee, and Dadabhai Naoroji.
- Originally a loyal-opposition institution operating within the Raj framework.
- Subsequent radicalisation under Bal Gangadhar Tilak (the "extremist" wing) from 1907 and under Mohandas K. Gandhi from 1915.

### Partition of Bengal 1905
- First Partition of Bengal: 16 October 1905 under Viceroy Lord Curzon.
- subsequent public characterisation: administrative reorganisation to reduce the governance load of a subsequent over-large province.
- subsequent actual purpose per Stephen Hay / Bipan Chandra / Sumit Sarkar / Tanika Sarkar historiography: weaken Bengali resistance to British rule by splitting Hindu-majority and Muslim-majority districts.
- partition reversed 12 December 1911 following the subsequent Swadeshi movement.

### All-India Muslim League
- Founded 30 December 1906 in Dhaka by Aga Khan III, Nawab Salimullah of Dhaka, and others.
- Originally a subsequent loyalist organisation seeking Muslim representation in colonial institutions.
- Subsequent leadership under Muhammad Ali Jinnah from 1913.
- 1916 Lucknow Pact between Congress and Muslim League.

### Bengal Renaissance
- 19th-century Bengali intellectual and cultural movement encompassing Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, Rabindranath Tagore (Nobel Prize for Literature 1913), Aurobindo Ghose, Swami Vivekananda, Jagadish Chandra Bose, Prafulla Chandra Ray.
- subsequent intellectual basis of the subsequent independence movement.

### Amritsar Massacre 1919
- 13 April 1919: Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer ordered British Indian Army troops to fire on unarmed civilians at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar.
- Official British figure: 379 dead; Indian National Congress figure: over 1,000 dead.
- subsequent 1920 Hunter Commission inquiry.
- Dyer relieved of duty in 1920; subsequent House of Lords vote of support; subsequent £26,000 fund raised by the Morning Post for Dyer in Britain.

### Gandhi non-cooperation movement
- Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (born Porbandar, Gujarat, 2 October 1869 - assassinated 30 January 1948).
- subsequent Inner Temple barrister; subsequent 1893-1914 South African legal practice.
- 1915 return to India.
- 1920-1922 Non-Cooperation Movement.
- 1930 Salt March (Dandi March): 12 March - 6 April 1930.
- 1942 Quit India Movement: launched 8 August 1942.

### Government of India Act 1935
- Single-largest statute in British parliamentary history at time of passage.
- Provided for provincial autonomy and proposed federation including princely states.
- Operational basis for the 1937 provincial elections.
- Subsequently the basis (with adaptations) for the post-1947 Indian Constitution.

### Lahore Resolution 1940
- 23 March 1940 at the Lahore session of the All-India Muslim League.
- Demanded "independent states" for Muslims in "areas in which the Muslims are numerically in a majority as in the North-Western and Eastern zones of India."
- subsequent basis for the subsequent demand for Pakistan.

### Bengal famine of 1943
- Death toll: 2.1-3 million per Wikipedia / Madhusree Mukerjee *Churchill's Secret War* (2010); 1.5 million per official British estimate; up to 4 million per the upper-range scholarly estimate.
- 22 January 2019 Geophysical Research Letters study (Mishra et al.) established 1943 as not drought-caused — the only one of six major 1873-1943 famines in India that was not associated with soil-moisture deficiency.
- Causes per the documented subsequent scholarly literature: World War II disruptions; 1942 Japanese occupation of Burma cutting rice imports; 1942 cyclone in Bengal; wartime inflation; Bengal Chamber of Commerce Foodstuffs Scheme preferential allocation to subsequent priority classes; "denial" policies (rice and boats) along the Bengal coast against potential Japanese landing; Churchill war cabinet shipping-allocation decisions; subsequent continued food exports from India during the subsequent crisis; Cherwell (Frederick Lindemann) Malthusian advisory influence on Churchill.
- Documented Churchill quotation per S.D. Choudhury *Cognizance Journal* (2021): "I hate Indians. They are beastly people with a beastly religion. The famine was their own fault for breeding..."
- 1944 Famine Inquiry Commission upheld Malthusian view blaming local population.
- Sen's 1981 "Failure of Exchange Entitlements" analysis: subsequent food availability in 1943 was significantly higher than in 1941; the subsequent cause was failure of poor Bengalis' subsequent purchasing power in the subsequent wartime-inflation period.

### Direct Action Day 1946
- 16 August 1946: All-India Muslim League called for Direct Action Day to support the subsequent demand for Pakistan.
- Calcutta riots ("Great Calcutta Killing"): 4,000 killed, 100,000 left homeless.
- subsequent cycle of communal violence across the subsequent 1946-1947 period including Noakhali riots (October-November 1946), Bihar riots (October-November 1946), and Punjab riots (March 1947 onward).

## Partition 1947

### The transfer of power
- Late 1945: British government decided to end the British Raj in India given post-war exchequer exhaustion.
- February 1947: PM Clement Attlee announced intention to transfer power by June 1948.
- March 1947: Louis Mountbatten arrived as last Viceroy.
- 3 June 1947 Mountbatten Plan: partition of British India into two dominions.
- Indian Independence Act 1947 passed by UK Parliament 18 July 1947.
- 14 August 1947: transfer of power to Dominion of Pakistan.
- 15 August 1947: transfer of power to Dominion of India.
- Radcliffe Line drawn by Cyril Radcliffe (British barrister with no prior knowledge of India); published 17 August 1947 — two days after the subsequent transfer of power.

### Death and displacement
- Death toll estimates: 1-2 million per Stanford Library guide and academic consensus; some estimates as high as 2 million.
- Refugees: 14 million displaced — the largest mass migration in modern human history.
- Punjab and Bengal were split along Radcliffe Line.
- Sexual violence: subsequent estimated 75,000-100,000 women abducted, raped, or killed per Urvashi Butalia *The Other Side of Silence* (1998) and subsequent Yasmin Khan *The Great Partition* (2007) scholarship.

### Princely states
- 565 princely states integrated into India or Pakistan via subsequent Vallabhbhai Patel-led subsequent accession negotiations.
- Hyderabad annexed by India in September 1948 (Operation Polo).
- Jammu and Kashmir acceded to India on 26 October 1947 following Pakistan-backed tribal invasion.
- Junagadh accession resolved by subsequent February 1948 plebiscite.

## Indo-Pakistani wars

### First Kashmir War 1947-1948
- Initiated by Pakistan-backed tribal Pashtun militias invading Jammu and Kashmir 22 October 1947.
- Maharaja Hari Singh signed Instrument of Accession 26 October 1947.
- Indian Army deployment 27 October 1947.
- UN ceasefire 1 January 1949 (Karachi Agreement signed 27 July 1949).
- subsequent Line of Control established.

### Second Kashmir War 1965
- August-September 1965.
- Operation Gibraltar: Pakistani infiltration into Indian-administered Kashmir.
- 23 September 1965 UN-mediated ceasefire.
- January 1966 Tashkent Declaration (USSR-brokered).

### Bangladesh Liberation War 1971
- 1970 Pakistani general election: Awami League under Sheikh Mujibur Rahman won 167 of 169 East Pakistan seats; 160 of 313 total seats — absolute majority.
- 26 March 1971: Bangladesh independence declared by Mujibur Rahman following 25 March 1971 Operation Searchlight by Pakistani military.
- Pakistani military genocide in East Pakistan: estimated 300,000 to 3 million Bengali deaths; 200,000 to 400,000 women raped (Anthony Mascarenhas / Sarmila Bose / Rounaq Jahan estimates).
- 7,235,916 Bengali refugees to India's West Bengal per official Indian government figures.
- December 1971 Indian military intervention.
- 16 December 1971: Pakistani surrender at Dhaka (Lieutenant General A.A.K. Niazi to Lieutenant General Jagjit Singh Aurora).
- 1972 Simla Agreement.

### Kargil War 1999
- May-July 1999.
- Pakistani military and Kashmiri militant infiltration into Kargil district of Indian-administered Jammu and Kashmir.
- Operation Vijay (Indian Army) and Operation Safed Sagar (Indian Air Force).
- subsequent 4 July 1999 Nawaz Sharif-Bill Clinton Washington meeting.
- 26 July 1999 Indian victory.

## Post-1971 Pakistan

### Bhutto-Zia-Bhutto cycle
- Zulfikar Ali Bhutto: PM 1973-1977; deposed by General Zia ul-Haq coup 5 July 1977; hanged 4 April 1979.
- General Zia ul-Haq: Chief Martial Law Administrator 1977-1985; President 1978-1988; killed in C-130 air crash 17 August 1988.
- 1980-1988 Pakistani support to Afghan mujahideen under US CIA-Pakistani ISI Operation Cyclone framework.
- 1980s-onward Islamization programme under Zia.
- Benazir Bhutto: PM 1988-1990 and 1993-1996 (first female PM of any Muslim-majority country); assassinated 27 December 2007.
- Nawaz Sharif: PM 1990-1993, 1997-1999, 2013-2017.

### Musharraf
- General Pervez Musharraf: Chief Executive 1999-2002 following 12 October 1999 coup against Nawaz Sharif; President 2001-2008.
- post-9/11 Pakistani alignment with US "War on Terror."

## Indira Gandhi

### Indira Gandhi biography and premierships
- Born Indira Priyadarshini Nehru 19 November 1917 in Allahabad.
- Daughter of Jawaharlal Nehru (first PM of India).
- Somerville College, Oxford and Visva-Bharati University.
- Married Feroze Gandhi 1942.
- Sons Rajiv Gandhi (born 1944) and Sanjay Gandhi (born 1946).
- Prime Minister 1966-1977 and 1980-1984.

### The Emergency 1975-1977
- 25 June 1975: President Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed declared state of emergency on Indira Gandhi's advice.
- Triggered by 12 June 1975 Allahabad High Court ruling against Indira Gandhi for corrupt electoral practices.
- 21 months: 25 June 1975 - 21 March 1977.
- Civil liberties suspended; press freedoms curtailed; opposition leaders imprisoned.
- Sanjay Gandhi-directed subsequent forced sterilisation programme: 6.2 million sterilisations in 1976 alone.
- March 1977 general election: Janata Party defeated Congress; Indira Gandhi lost her seat.
- Morarji Desai PM 1977-1979.

### Return and Operation Blue Star
- January 1980 general election: Indira Gandhi's Congress (I) won landslide; she returned as PM.
- June 1980 son Sanjay Gandhi killed in plane crash.
- 1980-1984 Punjab insurgency under Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale of Damdami Taksal.
- 1 June - 8 June 1984: Operation Blue Star.
- Casualties (Indian government White Paper 1984): 493 civilians and militants; 83 Indian Army personnel killed; 249 wounded.
- subsequent journalistic estimates: 919-930 total deaths inside Golden Temple complex; some estimates of 5,000 civilian deaths in and around the complex and Amritsar.
- subsequent damage to Akal Takht and Sikh Reference Library.
- 31 October 1984: Indira Gandhi assassinated at 9:30 AM at her Safdarjung Road residence by Sikh bodyguards Beant Singh and Satwant Singh.
- 1984 anti-Sikh riots: official nationwide death toll 3,350; independent estimates 8,000-17,000; Delhi alone 3,000 Sikh deaths.

## Bhopal disaster 1984

- 2-3 December 1984: methyl isocyanate gas leak from Union Carbide India Limited pesticide plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh.
- Immediate deaths: 3,787 per subsequent official Madhya Pradesh government figures; subsequent estimates 8,000-25,000 cumulative deaths.
- an estimated 500,000 exposed individuals.
- Union Carbide settlement: $470 million February 1989 ($750 per exposed person; characterised by the survivors-and-activists' coalition as grossly inadequate).
- Warren Anderson (Union Carbide CEO) arrested in India December 1984; released on bail; subsequent never extradited despite subsequent 1989 Indian Supreme Court order; died 29 September 2014.

## 1991 economic liberalisation

- 1991 subsequent balance-of-payments crisis: foreign-exchange reserves below three weeks of imports.
- Approached IMF and World Bank for $1.8 billion bridging loan with subsequent structural adjustment conditionalities.
- 21 June 1991: P.V. Narasimha Rao subsequent sworn in as PM following 21 May 1991 subsequent assassination of Rajiv Gandhi by LTTE suicide bomber.
- Manmohan Singh appointed Finance Minister.
- 24 July 1991: Manmohan Singh Budget speech introducing Liberalisation, Privatisation, Globalisation (LPG) reforms.
- 19% devaluation of rupee.
- Tariff cuts.
- License Raj subsequent abolished (the subsequent post-1956 subsequent industrial-licensing regime).
- 1992 SEBI empowered as statutory market regulator.
- 1992 National Stock Exchange subsequent established.
- Foreign Direct Investment subsequent automatic approval up to 51% in most sectors.
- 1999 subsequent Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) replaced subsequent Foreign Exchange Regulation Act (FERA).

## Babri Masjid demolition 1992

- 6 December 1992: 16th-century Babri Masjid in Ayodhya destroyed by 150,000-strong Hindu nationalist mob organised by Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP), Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS), and Bajrang Dal.
- L.K. Advani 1990 subsequent 10,000-kilometre rath yatra from Somnath to Ayodhya subsequent mobilised support.
- 2,000-3,000 deaths in immediate post-demolition riots.
- December 1992-January 1993 Bombay riots: 900 deaths; estimated ₹9,000 crore ($3.6 billion) property damage.
- March 1993 Bombay bombings: 257 deaths.
- 16 December 1992: Liberhan Commission established by PM P.V. Narasimha Rao.
- 30 June 2009: Liberhan Commission 1,029-page report concluded events of 6 December 1992 were "neither spontaneous nor unplanned."
- 2005 Maloy Krishna Dhar (former IB head) claim that demolition was subsequent planned 10 months in advance by RSS-BJP-VHP leadership.
- 9 November 2019: Indian Supreme Court awarded entire 2.77-acre disputed site to Hindu trust for Ram Mandir construction; 5-acre alternative site to Uttar Pradesh Sunni Central Waqf Board.

## 1998 Pokhran-II nuclear tests

- 11 May 1998 and 13 May 1998: five nuclear weapon tests at Pokhran Test Range, Rajasthan.
- PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee (BJP).
- subsequent 28 May 1998 and 30 May 1998: Pakistani Chagai-I and Chagai-II subsequent responsive tests.
- US subsequent post-tests sanctions on India and Pakistan.
- subsequent 2005 US-India civil nuclear cooperation agreement under George W. Bush; finalised 2008.

## 2002 Gujarat pogroms

- 27 February 2002: Sabarmati Express fire at Godhra station killed 59 Hindu pilgrims returning from Ayodhya.
- 28 February - early May 2002: anti-Muslim pogroms across Gujarat.
- subsequent death toll: 1,000-2,000 (Muslim-majority); subsequent estimated 230,000 displaced.
- Narendra Modi was Chief Minister of Gujarat at the time (sworn in 7 October 2001).
- subsequent 2002 US State Department report and subsequent 2005 subsequent US visa denial to Modi under International Religious Freedom Act.
- 2012 Supreme Court-appointed Special Investigation Team (SIT) gave Modi subsequent clean chit.
- subsequent 2014 subsequent post-election US visa restored.

## 2008 Mumbai attacks

- 26-29 November 2008: Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) ten-attacker subsequent assault on multiple Mumbai locations including Taj Mahal Palace Hotel, Oberoi Trident Hotel, Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus, Nariman House, and Cafe Leopold.
- 175 deaths (including 9 attackers).
- 300+ injured.
- subsequent 2012 subsequent Ajmal Kasab (sole captured attacker) execution.
- subsequent LeT Hafiz Saeed subsequent Pakistani-court conviction 2020; subsequent 33-year imprisonment.

## Post-2014 BJP-Modi government

- 16 May 2014 Indian general election: BJP won 282 seats (majority in 543-seat Lok Sabha); 31.0% of vote.
- 26 May 2014: Modi subsequent sworn in as PM.
- 23 May 2019 re-election: BJP 303 seats; 37.4% of vote.
- 4 June 2024 subsequent third-term election: BJP-led NDA 293 seats; BJP alone 240 seats — subsequent BJP lost subsequent parliamentary majority and required subsequent coalition support (Telugu Desam Party of N. Chandrababu Naidu; Janata Dal United of Nitish Kumar).

## Article 370 abrogation 2019

- 5 August 2019: Indian government revoked subsequent special-autonomy status of Jammu and Kashmir under Article 370 of the Indian Constitution; bifurcated state into two Union Territories (Jammu and Kashmir; Ladakh).
- subsequent multi-month subsequent internet shutdowns and subsequent political detentions.
- 11 December 2023: Indian Supreme Court subsequent upheld abrogation.

## 2019 Citizenship Amendment Act and National Register of Citizens

- 11 December 2019: CAA passed Lok Sabha; 12 December 2019 passed Rajya Sabha; 12 December 2019 presidential assent.
- Granted accelerated citizenship to Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, and Christian refugees from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh — explicitly excluding Muslims.
- December 2019-March 2020: anti-CAA-and-NRC protests across India.
- 23 February - 26 February 2020: Delhi riots — 53 deaths; subsequent estimated 80% Muslim victims.

## Bangladesh post-1971

### Mujib-Zia-Ershad cycle
- Sheikh Mujibur Rahman: PM 1972-1975; President 1975 (briefly); assassinated 15 August 1975 with most of his family in subsequent military coup. Daughters Sheikh Hasina and Sheikh Rehana subsequent abroad at time of subsequent coup; survived.
- Ziaur Rahman: President 1977-1981; assassinated 30 May 1981 in subsequent Chittagong.
- Hussain Muhammad Ershad: military ruler 1982-1990; subsequent eight-year subsequent martial-law period.

### Hasina-Khaleda cycle
- Khaleda Zia (widow of Ziaur Rahman; Bangladesh Nationalist Party): PM 1991-1996, 2001-2006.
- Sheikh Hasina (daughter of Mujibur Rahman; Awami League): PM 1996-2001, 2009-5 August 2024.
- subsequent 5 August 2024 Hasina ouster following student-led protests against public-sector job-quota system.
- subsequent 8 August 2024 Muhammad Yunus appointed Chief Adviser of interim government.

## Sri Lanka post-1948

### Independence and the post-1972 republican period
- 4 February 1948: Ceylon independence.
- 22 May 1972: Sri Lanka republican constitution adopted.
- 1956 Sinhala Only Act under S.W.R.D. Bandaranaike.
- subsequent 1958 subsequent anti-Tamil pogroms.
- 1972 university quota system reduced Tamil access to higher education.
- 1976 Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) founded by Velupillai Prabhakaran.
- 1983 Black July pogroms: estimated 400-3,000 Tamil deaths.
- 1983-2009 Sri Lankan Civil War.

### Mullivaikkal 2009
- May 2009: final subsequent offensive by Sri Lankan military under Mahinda Rajapaksa government against LTTE in Mullivaikkal area of northern Sri Lanka.
- estimated 40,000-70,000+ Tamil civilian deaths in final subsequent weeks (UN-OHCHR / International Crimes Investigation Group / Tamil Genocide Education Week estimates).
- subsequent 31 March 2011 UN Panel of Experts on Accountability in Sri Lanka report.
- subsequent 2014 UNHRC investigation.

### 2022 economic crisis
- April-July 2022: Sri Lankan subsequent balance-of-payments crisis.
- subsequent 9 July 2022 subsequent protesters stormed Presidential Palace in Colombo.
- 13 July 2022 subsequent Gotabaya Rajapaksa fled Sri Lanka via subsequent Maldives to Singapore.
- 21 July 2022 Ranil Wickremesinghe subsequent sworn in as President.

### 2024 NPP victory
- 21 September 2024 Sri Lankan presidential election: Anura Kumara Dissanayake of Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna (JVP) / National People's Power (NPP) coalition won 42.31%.
- 23 September 2024 subsequent Dissanayake sworn in as President.
- 14 November 2024 subsequent NPP won parliamentary election with 159 of 225 seats (two-thirds majority).

# Sources — Dossier 1: Joseph R. Biden, Jr.

## Biographical
- Born 20 November 1942, Scranton, Pennsylvania.
- Family moved to Claymont, Delaware in 1953.
- University of Delaware (1965), Syracuse Law (1968).
- Wife Neilia and infant daughter Naomi killed in car accident, 18 December 1972; sons Beau (1969-2015, died of glioblastoma) and Hunter (b. 1970) survived.
- Married Jill Tracy Jacobs 1977.
- Beau Biden's death from brain cancer at the age of 46 on 30 May 2015 became the central biographical and political reference point of Biden's later career; Joe Biden's political-class operations after 2015 are often discussed in the political-class memoir literature as taking place under the shadow of Beau's death.

## Senate career
- **Elected to the US Senate from Delaware** in November 1972 at age 29, becoming one of the youngest senators in US history.
- **Served continuously January 1973 to January 2009** — 36 years.
- ** committee positions:** Chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee (1987-1995); Chairman or Ranking Member of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee (1997-2009).
- **Bork hearings (1987):** chaired the Judiciary Committee hearings that defeated Robert Bork's nomination to the Supreme Court.
- **Anita Hill / Clarence Thomas hearings (1991):** chaired the Judiciary Committee hearings that confirmed Thomas to the Supreme Court despite Hill's sexual-harassment testimony. Biden was widely criticised by feminist legal scholars for his management of the hearing, including his decision not to call the additional women witnesses who had come forward to corroborate Hill's account. Biden later (2019) apologised to Hill for his management of the hearings.
- **Crime bill (1994):** Biden was the Senate sponsor of the Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act of 1994, which expanded federal mandatory minimum sentencing and is widely credited in the contemporary criminal-justice-reform scholarship with accelerating the late-twentieth-century US mass-incarceration boom that disproportionately incarcerated African American men. Biden defended the bill across his Senate and Vice Presidential tenure; in 2019, as a presidential candidate, he distanced himself from it.
- **Iraq War vote (2002):** Biden, as Chairman of the Foreign Relations Committee, voted for the October 2002 Authorization for Use of Military Force Against Iraq Resolution. He defended the vote across his Vice Presidential tenure; in 2019-2020 he claimed he had "opposed the war from the moment it began," a characterisation contradicted by his contemporary statements.

## MBNA, the credit-card industry, and the 2005 Bankruptcy Bill
- **MBNA Corporation** was a Delaware-headquartered credit card company; in 2006 it was acquired by Bank of America. For two decades, MBNA was the single largest contributor to Biden's Senate campaigns, with MBNA employees contributing $200,000-$241,000 to Biden's campaigns over the 1990s and 2000s.
- **Hunter Biden's MBNA employment:** Hunter Biden joined MBNA as an executive in November 1996; he reached the level of vice president before leaving in 2001 to work in the Clinton administration; from 2001 to 2005 Hunter Biden received consulting payments from MBNA reported at $100,000 per year for "online banking" work, while his father was simultaneously pushing the credit-card industry's bankruptcy bill through the Senate.
- **The 1996 house sale:** MBNA executive John Cochran bought Joe Biden's home outside Wilmington in February 1996 for the full asking price of $1.2 million, while other appraised properties at the time were selling for $100,000-$200,000 below asking price. MBNA paid Cochran $330,115 in moving expenses that year, including reimbursement of his claimed $210,000 loss on the sale of his Maryland home. Cochran subsequently donated $2,000 to Biden's 1996 Senate reelection campaign.
- **1997 paid trip:** MBNA flew Biden and his wife to Maine for a paid speech (Biden's Senate financial disclosure record).
- **Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act of 2005 (BAPCPA):** Biden voted for the bill four times across the period 1998-2005, including on final passage in March 2005 (74-25 Senate vote). The bill made it materially harder for consumers — particularly those carrying medical debt, student loan debt, and credit-card debt — to obtain Chapter 7 bankruptcy protection. The contemporaneous Consumer Federation of America legislative director Travis Plunkett described Biden as having provided "a veneer of bipartisanship" that allowed the bill to pass.
- **Biden's amendment record on the 2005 bill:** he was one of five Democrats who voted against requiring credit card companies to provide more effective warnings to consumers about paying only minimum amounts; he helped defeat amendments providing stronger protections for medical-debt bankruptcies and for military-service-member bankruptcies; he was one of four Democrats who voted with Republicans to defeat a Senator Charles Schumer amendment shifting partial liability to predatory lenders.
- **Senator Elizabeth Warren's contemporaneous testimony** before Biden's Judiciary Committee in 2005 — when Warren was a Harvard Law professor and the academic critic of the bankruptcy industry's lobbying — was the documented confrontation between Biden and Warren that Warren's later political career was, in significant part, built on. Warren and Biden later reconciled politically and Warren endorsed Biden in April 2020.

### Sources
- [ProPublica](https://www.propublica.org/article/bidens-cozy-relations-with-bank-industry-825)
- [NPR All Things Considered, "Biden's Link to Credit-Card Firm Questioned" (25 August 2008)](https://www.npr.org/transcripts/93954519)
- [CBS News, "MBNA Paid Biden's Son As Biden Backed Bill" (26 August 2008)](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/mbna-paid-bidens-son-as-biden-backed-bill/)
- [Washington Examiner](https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/news/2610152/middle-class-joe-cozied-up-to-credit-card-companies-and-made-filing-for-bankruptcy-harder/)
- [Daily News (Philadelphia Inquirer)](https://www.inquirer.com/philly/blogs/attytood/Sen_Joe_Biden_D-MBNA)
- Fox Business, Peter Navarro on Biden / MBNA, August 2020.
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

## Vice Presidency (2009-2017)
- **Sworn in 20 January 2009** as Barack Obama's Vice President.
- ** portfolios:** Ukraine policy (Obama administration "point man" on Ukraine 2014-2017); Iraq drawdown (2009-2011); Recovery Act oversight (2009-2010); China-related diplomatic missions.

### Burisma / Hunter Biden / Ukraine
- **The Maidan Revolution:** Ukrainian President Viktor Yanukovych was overthrown in February 2014 after months of Euromaidan protests. The new Ukrainian government was supported by the United States, with Vice President Biden as the Obama administration's diplomatic operator on Ukraine.
- **Hunter Biden joined Burisma Holdings board:** May 2014 (specifically appointed 12 May 2014 per US House Ways and Means Committee whistleblower-document timeline). Burisma was a Ukrainian natural gas company owned by Mykola Zlochevsky, a former Yanukovych minister who had fled into exile and was under multiple corruption investigations.
- **Hunter Biden's compensation:** up to $50,000 per month (Wikipedia, citing multiple sources); $1 million per year per the US House Oversight Committee testimony of Devon Archer, Hunter's business partner and fellow Burisma board member.
- **Devon Archer's separate role:** Archer joined the Burisma board one month before Hunter Biden, in April 2014, when the UK froze a Burisma bank account holding $23 million amid money-laundering suspicion.
- **April 2014 timeline (per US House Ways and Means Committee whistleblower disclosures):**
- 15 April 2014: Devon Archer meets with Vice President Biden at the White House.
- 16 April 2014: Hunter Biden has coffee with Greg Schultz, Adviser to Vice President Biden.
- 17 April 2014: Hunter Biden writes to his business partner that his father, then Vice President, "will be characterized as part of our advice and thinking — but what he will say and do is out of our hands."
- 21 April 2014: Joe Biden travels to Ukraine in his Vice Presidential capacity.
- 12 May 2014: Hunter Biden appointed to Burisma's board.
- 13 May 2014: At the request of the Vice President's General Counsel, a photo of Joe Biden and Devon Archer from the 15 April White House meeting is removed from Burisma's website by Eric Schwerin, Hunter Biden's business associate.
- **The Burisma email of 17 April 2015** (from Vadym Pozharskyi, an adviser to the Burisma board, to Hunter Biden): "Dear Hunter, thank you for inviting me to DC and giving an opportunity to meet your father and spent [sic] some time together. It's realty [sic] an honor and pleasure." (The email was among those recovered from Hunter Biden's laptop, the authenticity of which has been confirmed by multiple forensic examinations including those reported by the *New York Times* in 2022 and the *Washington Post* in 2022.)
- **Hunter Biden lobbied the US State Department** on behalf of Burisma in 2016 to help secure an energy project in Italy while his father was still Vice President (Wikipedia, citing the underlying *New York Post* and subsequent *New York Times* reporting).
- **Viktor Shokin firing (March 2016):** Shokin, the Ukrainian Prosecutor General, was widely criticised by Western institutions (including the IMF, the EU, and the US State Department) for blocking corruption investigations across the Ukrainian system. Joe Biden joined other Western leaders in publicly pressing the Ukrainian government to remove Shokin and threatened to withhold US loan guarantees ($1 billion). Biden recounted the threat publicly at a Council on Foreign Relations event in January 2018: "I said, 'I'm leaving in six hours. If the prosecutor is not fired, you're not getting the money.' Well, son of a bitch. He got fired."
- **The contested question of whether Shokin was investigating Burisma at the time of his firing:** the Western consensus is that Shokin was *not* actively investigating Burisma (the Burisma investigation had been shelved in 2014-2015 under Shokin's predecessor). The Republican investigative position is that Shokin's removal nonetheless terminated any prospect of the Burisma investigation being revived. Hunter Biden remained on the Burisma board until April 2019.
- **Hunter Biden's separate Chinese business activities:** Hunter Biden was a founding board member of BHR Partners, a Chinese state-linked investment company, in 2013. The BHR position was the subject of separate US House Oversight Committee investigation across 2023-2024.

- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hunter_Biden)

- [US House Oversight Committee](https://oversight.house.gov/the-bidens-influence-peddling-timeline/)

- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

## Presidency (2021-2025)
- **Inaugurated 20 January 2021** at age 78 years 2 months — the oldest person to be inaugurated US President at the time.
- **Major legislation:** American Rescue Plan Act (March 2021); Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (November 2021); CHIPS and Science Act (August 2022); Inflation Reduction Act (August 2022).
- **Foreign policy:** Afghanistan withdrawal (August 2021); Russia-Ukraine war (February 2022 - present); Israel-Gaza war (October 2023 - present, including the documented International Court of Justice provisional measures of January 2024 against Israel and the International Criminal Court arrest warrants against Netanyahu and Gallant of November 2024, in which Biden's administration provided continued US military and diplomatic support to the Netanyahu government throughout the period of the documented ICJ proceedings).
- **Classified documents investigation:** Special Counsel Robert Hur was appointed in January 2023 to investigate Biden's retention of classified documents from his time as Vice President. Hur interviewed Biden in October 2023 across two days, more than five hours total. The Hur Report was released on 8 February 2024.

### The Hur Report
- **Hur's conclusion:** "We have also considered that, at trial, Mr. Biden would likely present himself to a jury, as he did during our interview of him, as a sympathetic, well-meaning, elderly man with a poor memory." Hur concluded that criminal charges were not warranted because the evidence did not establish guilt beyond a reasonable doubt, in part because of Biden's faltering memory.
- **The audio (released May 2025):** the audio of Hur's October 2023 interview with Biden, released by the second Trump administration in May 2025, confirmed the memory lapses that the Biden White House had publicly denied at the time the Hur Report was released. The audio showed Biden struggling to remember the year his eldest son Beau Biden had died (Beau died 30 May 2015) and being prompted by his lawyers.
- **The institutional press response (February-June 2024):** the press (the major TV networks, the *New York Times*, the *Washington Post*) initially dismissed the Hur report's characterisation as "ageism" and "gratuitous" — Jeffrey Toobin of CNN called the report "an outrage" and "a disgrace"; MSNBC host Ari Melber called it "ageism snuck into a report clearing the person of any wrongdoing." This press position held until Biden's 27 June 2024 debate against Donald Trump, at which Biden's cognitive decline became publicly undeniable. The press then reversed its position. The captured-press dynamic — the captured press denying a documented fact for months until a public event forced acknowledgement — is documented of the 2024 election cycle.
- **The Tapper-Thompson book (*Original Sin*, May 2025):** Jake Tapper of CNN and Alex Thompson of Axios, both of whom had been part of the press apparatus that initially dismissed the cognitive concerns, published a book in May 2025 documenting the systematic White House concealment of Biden's cognitive decline across 2022-2024. The book is the documented post-hoc institutional admission of what the captured press had denied for two years.
- **Cognitive test refusal:** Biden declined repeatedly across 2024 to undergo a Montreal Cognitive Assessment, saying he had "a cognitive test every single day" in performing his presidential duties.

### Sources
- [NBC News, "Audio of interview confirms Biden memory lapses," 20 May 2025](https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/justice-department/audio-interview-confirms-biden-memory-lapses-rcna197548)

- [Fox News, "Credibility crisis: Press dismissed Hur report on Biden's memory issues long before concerns became undeniable," 28 May 2025](https://www.foxnews.com/media/credibility-crisis-press-dismissed-hur-report-bidens-memory-issues-long-before-concerns-became-undeniable)
- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Joe_Biden)
- Tapper and Thompson, *Original Sin: President Biden's Decline, Its Cover-Up, and His Disastrous Choice to Run Again* (Penguin, May 2025).
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

## The 27 June 2024 debate, July withdrawal, Harris endorsement
- **27 June 2024 CNN debate** against Donald Trump: Biden's performance was widely characterised by Democratic and Republican commentators as catastrophic, with extended silences, lost trains of thought, and inability to complete arguments. The debate was the public event that forced the press to reverse its prior dismissal of the cognitive concerns.
- **21 July 2024:** Biden withdrew from the 2024 presidential race and endorsed Vice President Kamala Harris.
- **The Democratic primary process:** Biden had defeated Representative Dean Phillips in the Democratic primaries earlier in 2024 with the party apparatus's full support, despite Phillips' explicit campaign-pitch argument that Biden was cognitively unfit to run again.

## Prostate cancer diagnosis
- **May 2025:** Biden was diagnosed with stage 4 metastatic prostate cancer with bone metastasis, a diagnosis that the Biden family announced in mid-May 2025.
- The diagnosis raised institutional questions about how long the cognitive decline and the underlying physical disease had been concealed from the public — particularly given that Biden's official White House physician, Kevin O'Connor, had repeatedly declared Biden fit to serve and had not ordered routine prostate-specific antigen testing.
- Barack Obama's former White House physician, Jeffrey Kuhlman, said in a June 2025 interview that Biden's doctor should have given Biden a cognitive test during his final year as president and that the results would have helped the public understand whether Biden was fit to serve.

## The Hunter Biden pardon (1 December 2024)
- **Date signed:** 1 December 2024.
- **Form:** "A Full and Unconditional Pardon For those offenses against the United States which he has committed or may have committed or taken part in during the period from January 1, 2014 through December 1, 2024."
- **Scope:** explicitly not limited to charged offences. The pardon covered any federal crime Hunter Biden "has committed or may have committed" across the 10-year-and-11-month period that included his entire Burisma board tenure (May 2014 – April 2019), his entire Chinese BHR Partners involvement, and his entire tax-evasion period.
- **The reversal:** Joe Biden had publicly stated, both before and after the November 2024 election, that he would not pardon Hunter. President Biden's spokesperson Karine Jean-Pierre had stated the same. The 1 December 2024 pardon reneged on the public commitments.
- **The Justice Department pardon document** is at [US Department of Justice](https://www.justice.gov/d9/2024-12/biden_warrant.pdf)
- **Biden's statement defending the pardon:** "From the day I took office, I said I would not interfere with the Justice Department's decision-making, and I kept my word even as I have watched my son being selectively, and unfairly, prosecuted." The statement is on the public record of the Biden White House at [Biden White House archive](https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/2024/12/01/statement-from-president-joe-biden-11/)
- **The scope question:** Peter Schweizer, the author of *Profiles in Corruption* (2020) and a documented critic of the Biden family business arrangements, noted publicly that the pardon's 10-year-and-11-month window covered the active period of investigation of potential Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA) violations by Hunter Biden — investigations that the pardon's scope terminated.

### Sources
- [White House Statement (archived)](https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/2024/12/01/statement-from-president-joe-biden-11/)
- [Justice Department pardon document](https://www.justice.gov/d9/2024-12/biden_warrant.pdf)
- [NPR, "After promising he wouldn't, President Biden pardons his son Hunter," 2 December 2024](https://www.npr.org/2024/12/02/nx-s1-5212483/after-promising-he-wouldnt-president-biden-pardons-his-son-hunter)
- [CNN, "President Biden pardons his son Hunter Biden," 1 December 2024](https://www.cnn.com/2024/12/01/politics/hunter-biden-joe-biden-pardon/index.html)
- [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/amp/news/hunter-biden-pardon-joe-biden-statement/)
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

- Volume One Chapter 6 documents the post-1971 financial-political settlement; the Biden Senate career is the political-class operator-staffing of that settlement on the credit-card-industry side.
- Volume One Chapter 8 documents the post-2008 financial-policy consolidation; the Biden presidency is the political-class operator-staffing of the post-2008 settlement.
