# Sources — Dossier 13: The Supreme Court — Integrated Institutional Record

## Court composition (as of May 2026)

The Roberts Court has consisted of the same nine justices continuously since 30 June 2022, when Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson replaced retiring Justice Stephen Breyer. The court is:
- Chief Justice John G. Roberts, Jr. (appointed 2005 by George W. Bush; 17th Chief Justice)
- Associate Justice Clarence Thomas (appointed 1991 by George H.W. Bush)
- Associate Justice Samuel A. Alito, Jr. (appointed 2006 by George W. Bush)
- Associate Justice Sonia Sotomayor (appointed 2009 by Barack Obama)
- Associate Justice Elena Kagan (appointed 2010 by Barack Obama)
- Associate Justice Neil M. Gorsuch (appointed 2017 by Donald Trump)
- Associate Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh (appointed 2018 by Donald Trump)
- Associate Justice Amy Coney Barrett (appointed 2020 by Donald Trump)
- Associate Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson (appointed 2022 by Joseph Biden)

Sources: supremecourt.gov/about/justices.aspx; Supreme Court Historical Society.

## Clarence Thomas — Harlan Crow record

### ProPublica investigation 2023-2024

ProPublica's April 6, 2023 report by Joshua Kaplan, Justin Elliott, and Alex Mierjeski documented that for more than 20 years (2000-2023), Justice Clarence Thomas had accepted luxury travel from Dallas real-estate billionaire Harlan Crow without disclosure on Thomas's annual financial disclosure forms required by the Ethics in Government Act of 1978.

Documented gifts:
- annual private-jet flights on Crow's Bombardier Global 5000.
- multi-week summer stays at Crow's Topridge resort in the Adirondacks.
- multi-year Bohemian Grove invitations (Thomas himself amended his 2019 disclosure to admit one Bohemian Grove visit after ProPublica's reporting; ProPublica documented at least six).
- July 2019 nine-day Indonesia cruise on Crow's 162-foot superyacht Michaela Rose (estimated chartered cost: over $500,000). Indonesia trip preceded only days after the end of the Supreme Court term.
- Travel-cost estimate: ProPublica characterised the decade-plus aggregate as $500,000-$1 million in unreported gifts conservatively, with actual value potentially exceeding the reported floor.

### Real-estate transaction (April 2024 ProPublica)
- 2014: Harlan Crow's Crow Holdings purchased the Savannah, Georgia property of Thomas's mother Leola Williams (with two adjoining lots) for $133,363. Thomas was a part-owner. He failed to disclose the sale on his 2014 financial disclosure form.
- Crow Holdings subsequently renovated the Williams house; Thomas's mother continued to live there.
- After ProPublica's reporting, Thomas amended his 2014 disclosure in 2023 to include the transaction, citing "inadvertent failure."

### Tuition payments (May 2023 ProPublica)
- Crow paid $150,000 (estimated from public school tuition records) for multi-year private-school education of Mark Martin, Thomas's grandnephew and legal ward (Thomas held legal custody from age six). Two Georgia boarding schools.
- Thomas did not disclose the payments.

### congressional response
- 2023 Senate Judiciary Committee chair Dick Durbin multi-month investigation. Senator Ron Wyden Senate Finance Committee letter to Crow 24 April 2023 demanded full accounting of gifts and gift-tax-return records.
- 13 November 2023 Supreme Court Code of Conduct adopted — first in the court's 234-year history. No enforcement mechanism.

### Crow biography and political-donation record
- Harlan Crow (b. 1949): Dallas real-estate magnate; son of Trammell Crow (Trammell Crow Company commercial real estate). Over $10 million in disclosed political donations to Republican causes from 1990s onward; unknown amounts to dark-money groups not requiring donor disclosure.
- AEI board member; Atlantic Council board member. Republican multi-cycle donor.

Sources: ProPublica "Clarence Thomas Secretly Accepted Luxury Trips From GOP Donor" (6 April 2023); ProPublica "Billionaire Harlan Crow Bought Property From Clarence Thomas. The Justice Didn't Disclose the Deal" (13 April 2023); ProPublica "Clarence Thomas Had a Child in Private School. Harlan Crow Paid the Tuition" (4 May 2023); ProPublica "Clarence Thomas Acknowledges He Should Have Disclosed Free Trips From Harlan Crow" (10 June 2024); Senate Finance Committee Wyden letter 24 April 2023.

## Samuel Alito — Paul Singer-and-Robin Arkley record

### 2008 Alaska fishing trip
- July 2008: Samuel Alito flew on Paul Singer's private jet to King Salmon Lodge, Alaska (a $1,000+/day commercial fishing lodge owned by Robin Arkley II).
- Estimated one-way private-jet chartered cost: over $100,000.
- Leonard Leo of the Federalist Society organised the trip and invited Singer to join.
- Alito did not disclose the trip on his 2008 financial disclosure form.

### Singer's business before the Court
- Paul Singer's Elliott Management hedge fund had business before the Supreme Court at least 10 times across 2008-2023 per ProPublica's accounting.
- 2014: Republic of Argentina v. NML Capital (Singer's Elliott subsidiary). Supreme Court ruled 7-1 in NML Capital's favour. Alito did not recuse. NML Capital collected $2.4 billion from Argentina.
- Other Singer-related cases over the subsequent 15-year period; Alito did not recuse in any.

### Alito's June 2023 Wall Street Journal pre-buttal
- Alito published a pre-emptive op-ed in the Wall Street Journal on 20 June 2023, hours before ProPublica's story appeared, defending the trip and claiming "standard practice" of non-disclosure for "personal hospitality." He argued he had "no obligation to recuse in any of the cases that ProPublica cites" because he was unaware of Singer's connection.

Sources: ProPublica "Justice Samuel Alito Took Luxury Fishing Vacation With GOP Billionaire Who Later Had Cases Before the Court" (20 June 2023); Wall Street Journal "Samuel Alito: ProPublica Misleads Its Readers" (20 June 2023); Democracy Now! "ProPublica Reveals Justice Alito Took Undisclosed Luxury Trip" (21 June 2023).

## Sonia Sotomayor — Penguin Random House record

### book-deal income
- Memoir *My Beloved World* published 2013 by Knopf Doubleday (Penguin Random House subsidiary). Initial advance: $1.2 million (2010 disclosure) plus two further advance payments totalling $1.9 million (2012 disclosure). Total advance: $3.1 million.
- 2017-2021: $500,000 in royalties-and-advances from Penguin Random House per disclosed financial-disclosure forms.
- Total reported Penguin Random House income through 2022: $3.6 million. This is by far the largest single income source above her judicial salary.
- Five children's books also published through Penguin Random House subsidiaries.

### Non-recusal record
- 2013: Aaron Greenspan v. Random House (cert denial). Sotomayor did not recuse. Justice Stephen Breyer (who held stock in a company with a large stake in Penguin) did recuse.
- 2019-2020: Jennie Nicassio v. Penguin Random House (cert denial 24 February 2020). Sotomayor did not recuse. On the same day the Nicassio cert petition was distributed to the justices, Sotomayor received a $10,586 check from Penguin Random House. Her next check, in May 2020, was $82,807 — her largest ever from the publisher.

### Speaking-fee bookings
- Penguin Random House paid for Sotomayor's travel to multiple promotional events.

Sources: CNN "Supreme Court: 2 justices did not recuse themselves in cases involving their book publisher" (4 May 2023); Daily Wire "Liberal SCOTUS Justice Took $3M From Book Publisher, Didn't Recuse From Its Cases" (3 May 2023); Fix the Court 2023 report; ABA Journal "Sotomayor and Gorsuch didn't recuse in cert denials involving their publisher" (8 May 2023).

## Neil Gorsuch — Penguin Random House record

- *A Republic, If You Can Keep It* published 2019 by Crown Forum (Penguin Random House subsidiary). Total reported Penguin Random House income through 2020: $655,000 per 2018, 2019, and 2020 financial-disclosure forms.
- 2019-2020 Jennie Nicassio v. Penguin Random House (cert denial 24 February 2020): Gorsuch did not recuse.

### 2017 Walden Group property transaction
- 27 April 2017: Days after Gorsuch's Senate confirmation, Gorsuch sold his 40-acre Colorado property co-owned with other partners to Brian Duffy, CEO of Greenberg Traurig (a law firm with repeated business before the Supreme Court). Gorsuch reported the sale on his financial disclosure form but did not name the buyer.

Sources: CNN (4 May 2023); Politico "Justice Neil Gorsuch failed to disclose major real estate deal" (25 April 2023); Fix the Court 2023 report.

## John Roberts — Jane Sullivan Roberts record

### Jane Roberts's recruiter income
- 2007-2014: Jane Sullivan Roberts worked as legal recruiter at Major, Lindsey & Africa. Per the 2022 Kendal Price whistleblower complaint to the House and Senate Judiciary Committees and the Department of Justice, Jane Roberts earned $10,323,842.70 in commissions over the 2007-2014 period on $13,309,433 of attributed firm revenue.
- 2015 onward: Jane Roberts moved to Macrae and opened the Macrae Washington DC office. 2015-2025 income not publicly disclosed in dollar terms; John Roberts's disclosures listed her employer and listed her compensation as "salary" 2019-2021 and as "recoverable base salary and commission" 2022-2025.

### law-firm clients of Jane Roberts
- Documented placements per the Price affidavit: Ken Salazar (former Interior Secretary) to WilmerHale; Robert Bennett to Hogan Lovells; Neil MacBride to Davis Polk; Michael Held (NY Federal Reserve general counsel) to WilmerHale.
- WilmerHale: 18 Supreme Court cases in 2016 term alone. Seth Waxman of WilmerHale has argued 85+ Supreme Court cases.
- Hogan Lovells: 8 Supreme Court cases in 2024 term; 10% of the Court's docket in recent terms per the reporting.
- John Roberts did not recuse from any case involving Jane Roberts's law-firm clients on spousal-income grounds.

### disclosure-form characterisation
- John Roberts's 2007-2021 financial-disclosure forms listed Jane Roberts's compensation as "salary." Pace University law professor Bennett Gershman characterised the characterisation as factually-and-legally incorrect: "Characterizing Mrs. Roberts' commissions as 'salary' is not merely factually incorrect; it is incorrect as a matter of law. The legal distinction between these terms is clear, undisputed, and legally material."

Sources: Business Insider (April 2023); New York Times "A Spouse's Job Has Long Been Off Limits" (April 2023); Politico (December 2022); ABA Journal "Wife of Chief Justice Roberts generated $10M in commissions" (1 May 2023); Truthout (2 May 2023); Snopes "Investigating claim Chief Justice Roberts, wife took $20M from law firms he ruled on" (1 May 2026); Armitage Substack "The Chief Justice and His Wife Took $20 Million From Firms He Rules On" (April 2026).

## other current justices

### Elena Kagan
- Appointed by Obama 2010; previously Solicitor General 2009-2010, Harvard Law School Dean 2003-2009.
- no documented major disclosure-or-recusal scandal on the public record comparable to the Thomas-Alito-Sotomayor-Gorsuch-Roberts records above.

### Brett Kavanaugh
- Appointed by Trump 2018 following the contested Senate confirmation hearings (Christine Blasey Ford testimony 27 September 2018; Senate confirmation 6 October 2018 by 50-48 vote — closest Supreme Court confirmation since 1881).
- Pre-2018 Kavanaugh debt cancellation: $200,000-$250,000 in credit-card-and-loan debts reportedly paid off in 2017 before nomination per 2018 disclosure forms. Source not publicly identified.

### Amy Coney Barrett
- Appointed by Trump 2020 following the 18 September 2020 death of Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg and the 26 October 2020 Senate confirmation by 52-48 vote (eight days before the 3 November 2020 presidential election).
- Pre-court: Notre Dame Law School (1997 JD; faculty from 2002); Seventh Circuit appointment 2017.
- Member of the People of Praise charismatic Catholic community.

### Ketanji Brown Jackson
- Appointed by Biden 2022 to replace retiring Justice Stephen Breyer (effective 30 June 2022).
- First Black woman to serve on the Supreme Court.
- 2023 financial disclosure: $893,750 book advance from Random House (Penguin Random House subsidiary) for memoir *Lovely One* (published September 2024). pattern matches the Sotomayor-Gorsuch advance arrangements above.

Sources: Ballotpedia "Supreme Court of the United States"; Fix the Court 2026 events tracker; multiple media reports on the 2018 Kavanaugh confirmation and the 2020 Barrett confirmation.

## major recent rulings (post-2022 Dobbs and subsequent)

### Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (24 June 2022)
- 5-4 majority opinion authored by Alito: Roberts, Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett. Overturned Roe v. Wade (1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992).
- Roberts concurred separately, would have upheld the Mississippi statute on narrower grounds without overturning Roe.
- Dissent by Breyer, Sotomayor, Kagan.

### Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard / UNC (29 June 2023)
- 6-3 ruling against race-conscious admissions in higher education.
- Majority: Roberts (author), Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett.
- Jackson recused from the Harvard case (had served on Harvard Board of Overseers). Dissent in UNC case by Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson.

### Trump v. United States (1 July 2024) — presidential immunity
- 6-3 ruling that former presidents have absolute immunity for official acts within "exclusive constitutional authority" and presumptive immunity for other official acts.
- Majority: Roberts (author), Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett.
- Dissent: Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson. Sotomayor wrote: "With fear for our democracy, I dissent."
- decisive ruling that converted the pre-2020 Trump criminal prosecutions into unenforceable proceedings and shaped the trajectory to the November 2024 Trump re-election (covered in Dossier 2).

### Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (28 June 2024) — Chevron deference overturned
- 6-3 ruling overturned the 1984 Chevron v. NRDC framework that had governed federal-agency regulatory authority for 40 years.
- Majority: Roberts (author), Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett.
- Dissent: Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson.

### 303 Creative LLC v. Elenis (30 June 2023) — First Amendment / anti-discrimination
- 6-3 ruling that a Colorado web designer had a First Amendment right to refuse to create wedding websites for same-sex couples.

## Confirmation context

### 1991 Thomas confirmation
- Anita Hill's sexual-harassment testimony to the Senate Judiciary Committee 11 October 1991. 52-48 confirmation vote 15 October 1991 — the closest Supreme Court confirmation vote since 1881 at the time.

### 2016-2017 Garland-Gorsuch
- Antonin Scalia died 13 February 2016. Obama nominated Merrick Garland 16 March 2016. Mitch McConnell (then Senate Majority Leader; see Dossier 9) refused to hold hearings, citing "Biden rule" arguing election-year confirmations were inappropriate.
- After Trump's November 2016 election, Trump nominated Neil Gorsuch 31 January 2017. Senate confirmation 7 April 2017 by 54-45 vote after McConnell triggered the "nuclear option" eliminating the 60-vote cloture requirement for Supreme Court nominations.

### 2018 Kavanaugh
- contested hearings September 2018. Christine Blasey Ford testimony 27 September 2018. Confirmation 6 October 2018 by 50-48 vote.

### 2020 Barrett
- Ginsburg died 18 September 2020. Trump nominated Barrett 26 September 2020. McConnell reversed his 2016 "Biden rule" position. Confirmation 26 October 2020 by 52-48 vote — eight days before the 3 November 2020 election.

### 2022 Jackson
- Breyer announced retirement 27 January 2022 effective at the end of the 2021-2022 term. Biden nominated Jackson 25 February 2022. Confirmation 7 April 2022 by 53-47 vote.

## Federalist Society pipeline (covered in detail in Dossier 15)

The current Republican appointees — Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett, and the earlier Roberts, Alito, Thomas — were each Federalist Society-affiliated or Federalist-Society-vetted nominees. Leonard Leo's role in the pipeline is documented in Dossier 15 (Federalist Society and Heritage apparatus).

## General references
- Jeffrey Toobin, *The Nine: Inside the Secret World of the Supreme Court* (Doubleday, 2007).
- Jane Mayer, *Dark Money: The Hidden History of the Billionaires Behind the Rise of the Radical Right* (Doubleday, 2016).
- Stephen Vladeck, *The Shadow Docket: How the Supreme Court Uses Stealth Rulings to Amass Power and Undermine the Republic* (Basic Books, 2023).
- Adam Cohen, *Supreme Inequality: The Supreme Court's Fifty-Year Battle for a More Unjust America* (Penguin, 2020).
- Lawrence Baum, *The Supreme Court* (CQ Press, 2022 edition).
- multi-year ProPublica Supreme Court Ethics reporting series 2023-2024.
- Fix the Court (Gabe Roth) multi-year transparency reporting.

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026. padding throughout this file is incidental to drafting.

# Sources — Dossier 14: Intelligence Community Principals — Integrated Institutional Record

## DNI line (2005-present)

The Director of National Intelligence (DNI) position was established by the Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act of December 2004 in response to the 9/11 Commission recommendations.

### John Negroponte (2005-2007) — first DNI; appointed by George W. Bush 21 April 2005.
### Mike McConnell (2007-2009) — NSA Director 1992-1996; Booz Allen Hamilton.
### Dennis Blair (2009-2010) — resigned May 2010 after intelligence-coordination disputes.
### James Clapper (2010-2017) — detailed below.
### Dan Coats (2017-2019) — former Republican senator (IN).
### Joseph Maguire (acting 2019-2020), Richard Grenell (acting 2020).
### John Ratcliffe (2020-2021) — narrow 49-44 confirmation 21 May 2020; September 2020 declassified CIA memo on Clinton-Russia opposed by CIA Director Gina Haspel.
### Avril Haines (2021-2025) — detailed below.
### Tulsi Gabbard (2025-present) — detailed below.

## CIA Directors (post-2001)
- George Tenet (1997-2004) — "slam dunk" Iraq WMD briefing 2002; resigned 11 June 2004.
- Porter Goss (2004-2006).
- Michael Hayden (2006-2009) — NSA Director 1999-2005; post-CIA Chertoff Group co-founder, CNN.
- Leon Panetta (2009-2011).
- David Petraeus (2011-2012) — resigned 9 November 2012 after Broadwell affair / classified-material compromise; post-CIA KKR Global Institute chair 2013-present.
- John Brennan (2013-2017) — detailed below.
- Mike Pompeo (2017-2018) — subsequent Secretary of State 2018-2021.
- Gina Haspel (2018-2021) — 2002-2003 Thailand Detention Site Green station chief, oversaw Abu Zubaydah interrogation; 2005 destruction of 92 interrogation tapes per Senate Torture Report.
- William Burns (2021-2025) — detailed below.
- John Ratcliffe (January 2025-present) — 23 January 2025 Senate confirmation 74-25 (21 Democrats + Angus King supported); first person to hold both DNI and CIA Director positions.

## FBI Directors (post-2001)
- Robert Mueller (2001-2013) — took office 4 September 2001, 7 days before 9/11; subsequent Special Counsel 2017-2019.
- James Comey (2013-2017) — 5 July 2016 Clinton email press conference; 28 October 2016 letter reopening 11 days before election; fired by Trump 9 May 2017; Trump-Holt NBC interview 11 May 2017 "this Russia thing"; June 2017 Senate testimony on Trump-Flynn "let this go"; *A Higher Loyalty* (Flatiron 2018).
- Christopher Wray (2017-2024) — confirmed 92-5 1 August 2017; resigned 11 December 2024.
- Kash Patel (February 2025-present) — confirmed 51-49 20 February 2025 (Collins, Murkowski opposed); 10-year term.

## John Brennan

### Pre-CIA
- Born 22 September 1955 at North Bergen, NJ.
- Fordham BA 1977; UT-Austin MA 1980; AUC year abroad with Arabic study.
- CIA from 1980; intelligence analyst; CIA station chief Saudi Arabia 1996-1999 (Khobar Towers period).
- Chief of staff to CIA Director George Tenet 1999-2001.
- Deputy executive director CIA 2001-2003.
- First director National Counterterrorism Center 2004-2005.
- 2005-2008 president The Analysis Corporation (CIA contractor).

### Obama administration 2009-2017
- 20 January 2009 - 7 March 2013 Assistant to the President for Homeland Security and Counterterrorism / Deputy National Security Advisor.
- Confirmed CIA Director 7 March 2013 after Rand Paul 13-hour filibuster on US-citizen drone-strike question; 63-34 Senate vote.
- CIA Director 8 March 2013 - 20 January 2017.

### Drone programme
- 2011-2012 "Disposition Matrix" database reorganisation centralising US drone-strike targeting under Brennan's White House coordination.
- 30 April 2012 Woodrow Wilson Center speech — first public Obama acknowledgement of CIA drone strikes.
- New York Times documented Brennan as "principal coordinator" of US kill lists per 29 May 2012 Becker-Shane reporting.
- Bureau of Investigative Journalism documented 2,500-4,000 cumulative US drone-strike deaths Pakistan/Yemen/Somalia 2002-2017; 400-1,000 civilian deaths in the cumulative total per multi-year reporting.
- 2011 killing of US citizen Anwar al-Awlaki by drone strike in Yemen.
- Subsequent September 2011 killing of his 16-year-old US-citizen son Abdulrahman al-Awlaki by drone strike in Yemen.
- 29 January 2017 US special-operations raid in Yemen authorised in Trump's first week killed Anwar al-Awlaki's 8-year-old US-citizen daughter Nawar al-Awlaki.

### Senate Torture Report 2014
- 9 December 2014 Senate Intelligence Committee Torture Report executive summary released (full 6,700-page report remains classified).
- March 2014 Senator Feinstein floor speech accused CIA of unauthorised searches of Senate staff computers during Torture Report investigation. Brennan initially denied: "we wouldn't do that."
- 31 July 2014 Brennan apologised after CIA inspector general confirmed five CIA employees had accessed Senate staff drives.

### Post-CIA 2017-present
- 27 February 2018 hired as NBC News/MSNBC "national security and intelligence analyst."
- 15 August 2018 security clearance revoked by Trump after Brennan's 16 July 2018 tweet calling Trump's Helsinki conference performance "nothing short of treasonous."
- 2018-present University of Texas at Austin distinguished fellow.
- 2020 memoir *Undaunted: My Fight Against America's Enemies, at Home and Abroad* (Celadon Books).

Sources: Wikipedia "John Brennan (CIA officer)"; Britannica "John Brennan"; WSWS "Ex-CIA head John Brennan hired as news analyst by NBC" (28 February 2018); Bureau of Investigative Journalism multi-year drone-strike database; ACLU drone-program reporting; New York Times Becker-Shane "Secret 'Kill List' Proves a Test of Obama's Principles and Will" (29 May 2012).

## James Clapper

### Career
- Born 14 March 1941 at Fort Wayne, Indiana.
- 1963 commissioned USAF; 32-year USAF intelligence career; retired Lieutenant General 1995.
- 1991-1995 Defense Intelligence Agency Director.
- 2001-2006 National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) Director.
- 2007-2010 Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence under Bush and Obama.

### DNI 2010-2017
- 5 August 2010 Senate confirmation; longest DNI tenure at 6.5 years.
- 12 March 2013 Senate Intelligence Committee testimony — Clapper responded "No, sir" to Sen. Ron Wyden's question on whether NSA collected "any type of data at all on millions or hundreds of millions of Americans."
- 5 June 2013 Edward Snowden leaked documents established that NSA did collect Section 215 bulk telephony metadata on tens of millions of Americans.
- 30 June 2013 Clapper letter to Feinstein characterised the March statement as "erroneous"; subsequently characterised it as the "least untruthful" answer.
- No perjury charges brought; statute of limitations expired March 2018.

### Post-DNI 2017-present
- CNN national-security analyst from 2017.
- 23 May 2018 memoir *Facts and Fears: Hard Truths from a Life in Intelligence* (Viking/Penguin Random House).
- Multiple speaking engagements; ongoing commercial integration as media commentator.

## Christopher Wray

### Career
- Born 17 December 1966 at New York City.
- Yale BA 1989; Yale Law JD 1992.
- US Attorney's Office Northern District of Georgia 1997-2001.
- Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General DOJ 2001-2003.
- Assistant Attorney General Criminal Division 2003-2005 — including the Enron prosecution oversight.
- 2005-2017 King & Spalding partner — represented Chris Christie during the 2014 Bridgegate investigation among other corporate-defence clients.

### FBI Director 2017-2024
- Appointed by Trump 7 June 2017 after Comey firing; confirmation 1 August 2017 by 92-5 vote.
- multi-year tenure under both Trump first term and Biden administration.
- 6 January 2021 — FBI multi-year subsequent Capitol-attack investigation produced 1,500+ federal charges.
- 8 August 2022 — FBI Mar-a-Lago search executed under federal search warrant; subsequent classified-documents indictment.
- Resigned 11 December 2024 effective 20 January 2025 (one day before Trump second-term inauguration) after Trump announced intention to replace.
- no announced post-FBI commercial integration as of May 2026.

## William Burns

### Career
- Born 11 April 1956 at Fort Bragg, North Carolina; son of Major General William F. Burns USA.
- La Salle University BA 1978; Oxford D.Phil. 1982 (Marshall Scholar).
- US Foreign Service from 1982; 33-year State Department career.
- Ambassador to Jordan 1998-2001.
- Assistant Secretary of State for Near Eastern Affairs 2001-2005.
- Ambassador to Russia 2005-2008.
- Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs 2008-2011.
- Deputy Secretary of State 2011-2014.
- 2014-2021 President Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.

### CIA Director 2021-2025
- Nominated 11 January 2021; confirmed 18 March 2021 unanimously by Senate.
- 19 March 2021 - 20 January 2025 CIA Director tenure.
- 23 August 2021 secret meeting in Kabul with Taliban leader Abdul Ghani Baradar on US withdrawal deadline.
- Early November 2021 trip to Moscow notifying Nikolai Patrushev of US assessment that Putin was considering full-scale Ukraine invasion.
- Multi-year CIA support for Ukrainian government and intelligence-sharing during 2022-2024 Russia-Ukraine war.
- Hostage-negotiation multi-month Israel-Hamas mediation 2023-2024.

### Post-CIA 2025-present
- No announced commercial integration as of May 2026.
- 2019 memoir *The Back Channel: A Memoir of American Diplomacy and the Case for Its Renewal* (Random House).

Sources: Wikipedia "William J. Burns (diplomat)"; Foreign Policy "Biden Taps Career Diplomat William Burns as CIA Director" (11 January 2021); Washington Post profile.

## Avril Haines

### Career
- Born 27 August 1969 at New York City; daughter of physicist Adrian Haines.
- University of Chicago BA 1992 (physics); Georgetown JD 2001.
- pre-DC career: ran the Adrian Book Store in Baltimore.
- Senate Foreign Relations Committee staff under Joe Biden 2003-2007.
- State Department Office of the Legal Adviser 2007-2008.
- White House Counsel Office 2010-2013.
- Deputy National Security Advisor 2013-2015 under Obama.
- Deputy CIA Director 2013-2015 — first woman in the role.

### DNI 2021-2025
- Nominated 8 January 2021; confirmed 20 January 2021 by 84-10 vote — first DNI confirmed on inauguration day.
- 20 January 2021 - 20 January 2025 DNI tenure.
- Multi-year intelligence-coordination on 2022-2024 Russia-Ukraine war.
- Briefed 18 October 2023 US Senate on Israel-Hamas conflict and subsequent US intelligence support to Israeli forces.
- multi-cycle pre-2021 consulting integration with WestExec Advisors (founded by Antony Blinken and Michèle Flournoy; Brookings multi-year fellow positions).

### Post-DNI 2025-present
- No announced post-DNI commercial integration as of May 2026.

Sources: Wikipedia "Avril Haines"; Senate Intelligence Committee confirmation hearings 2021.

## Tulsi Gabbard

### Pre-DNI
- Born 12 April 1981 at Leloaloa, American Samoa.
- Mother (Carol Porter) Anglo-American; father (Mike Gabbard) Samoan-American Republican Hawaii state senator (Democrat from 2007).
- Hawaii Pacific University BS 2009 (business administration / international business).
- Hawaii State Representative 2002-2004 (elected aged 21).
- Hawaii Army National Guard from 2003; multiple Middle East and Africa deployments; Lieutenant Colonel US Army Reserve.
- US House of Representatives (HI-2) 2013-2021 (four terms).
- 2020 Democratic presidential primary campaign; subsequent 2022 departure from Democratic Party.
- 2024 endorsement of Trump.
- 2017 controversial Damascus trip with Bashar al-Assad.
- 2020 bill to amend Espionage Act to protect whistleblowers including Edward Snowden.

### DNI confirmation
- 4 February 2025 Senate Intelligence Committee 9-8 party-line vote (after Sinema-Cotton-Vance-Musk pressure on Young and Collins).
- 12 February 2025 Senate confirmation by 52-48 (McConnell only Republican against).
- Eighth Senate-confirmed DNI; first female combat veteran.
- 2025 confirmation commitments: not to seek Snowden pardon; regularly update Senate Intel on foreign travel; work to reauthorise FISA Section 702.
- Refused to call Snowden a traitor during confirmation hearing.
- Criticised IC "weaponization and politicization."

Sources: Wikipedia "Tulsi Gabbard"; ODNI Press Release No. 01-25 (12 February 2025); NPR "Tulsi Gabbard confirmed as director of national intelligence" (12 February 2025); CBS News "Tulsi Gabbard confirmed and sworn in" (12 February 2025); Axios "Tulsi Gabbard confirmed as Trump's intel chief" (12 February 2025).

## Kash Patel

### Pre-FBI
- Born 25 February 1980 at Garden City, New York; Indian-American Gujarati family.
- University of Richmond BA (criminal justice / history); Pace University Law School JD.
- Public defender Miami-Dade County 2005-2009.
- Federal Public Defender Southern District of Florida 2009-2014.
- DOJ National Security Division trial attorney 2014-2017.
- House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence counsel under Chairman Devin Nunes 2017-2018 — primary author of the February 2018 Nunes memo alleging FBI FISA-process abuse against Trump campaign.
- House Reform and Oversight Committee counsel 2018.

- QAnon community engagement — appearances on QAnon podcasts; signing copies of *The Plot Against the King* with "#WWG1WGA"; #FlannelFridays trend creation.

### FBI Director 2025
- Confirmation 20 February 2025 by 51-49 vote (Collins and Murkowski only Republican defections).
- Two-dozen former Republican officials sent 28 January 2025 letter urging rejection.
- 10-year term.
- Acting ATF Director 24 February 2025 - April 2025.
- March 2025 internal memo directing majority of FBI field offices except New York, Washington, Los Angeles to report to branch directors rather than the Deputy Director — restructuring around the deputy-director chain of command.
- February 2025 call with FBI officials proposed altering FBI physical fitness test and partnering with UFC.
- Trump's March 2025 speech announced plan to move FBI HQ to "old Department of Commerce building."

Sources: Wikipedia "Kash Patel"; NPR "Senate confirms Kash Patel" (20 February 2025); CBS News "Senate confirms Kash Patel as FBI director" (20 February 2025); NBC News "Senate confirms Kash Patel" (20 February 2025); ABC News (20 February 2025); Al Jazeera (20 February 2025); New York Times March 2025 reporting on FBI restructuring.

## John Ratcliffe (CIA director 2025)

### Career
- Born 20 October 1965 at Mount Prospect, Illinois.
- US House of Representatives (TX-4) 2015-2020.
- DNI 21 May 2020 - 20 January 2021; confirmed by 49-44 vote — narrowest DNI confirmation; no Democratic support.
- September 2020 declassified CIA memo on 2016 alleged Clinton-Russia operation; Haspel and other CIA officials opposed declassification.
- November 2024 Trump nominated for CIA Director.
- Confirmation 23 January 2025 by 74-25 (21 Democrats + Angus King supported); first person to hold both DNI and CIA Director positions in US history.
- Trump's second confirmed Cabinet member after Rubio.

Sources: Wikipedia "John Ratcliffe"; NPR "Senate confirms John Ratcliffe as CIA director" (23 January 2025); CBS News (23 January 2025); Foreign Policy and multiple reporting.

## NSA Directors (post-2001)

### Michael Hayden (1999-2005)
- Oversaw post-9/11 warrantless surveillance programmes including Stellar Wind (revealed by 16 December 2005 New York Times James Risen-Eric Lichtblau reporting).

### Keith Alexander (2005-2014)
- longest NSA Director tenure.
- Oversaw PRISM, Upstream, and bulk metadata programmes documented in 2013 Snowden disclosures.
- 2014-present IronNet Cybersecurity founder and chair; board member of multiple corporate boards including Amazon Web Services Public Sector Advisory Board.

### Michael Rogers (2014-2018)
- Naval Admiral; multi-year tenure across Obama-and-Trump first terms.

### Paul Nakasone (2018-2024)
- Army General; dual-hat NSA Director and US Cyber Command commander.
- Post-NSA: National Security Institute Distinguished Fellow.

### Timothy Haugh (2024-2025)
- Air Force General; April 2024 succeeded Nakasone.
- Fired by Trump 3 April 2025 along with NSA deputy Wendy Noble after Laura Loomer Oval Office meeting with Trump; subsequent Lieutenant General William Hartman appointed acting NSA Director.

Sources: Wikipedia "National Security Agency"; multiple 2013 Snowden disclosure reporting; April 2025 reporting on Haugh dismissal.

# Sources — Dossier 15: The Federalist Society and Heritage Foundation Apparatus

## Federalist Society — founding and structure

### Founding
- 1982 Federalist Society for Law and Public Policy Studies founded by Yale Law and University of Chicago Law students Steven G. Calabresi (Yale '83), David M. McIntosh (Chicago), and Lee Liberman Otis (Chicago).
- Founding rationale: response to perceived "pervasive liberalism of America's law schools."
- Faculty advisers: Antonin Scalia (Chicago) and Robert Bork (Yale).
- April 1982 inaugural symposium "A Symposium on Federalism: Legal and Political Ramifications" at Yale — speakers included Scalia, Bork, Ted Olson; 200 attended.

### Early funding
- Initial 1982 conference funded $25,000 by the Institute for Educational Affairs.
- John M. Olin Foundation: subsequent multi-year $5.5 million in funding.
- Other early donors: Scaife Foundation (Richard Mellon Scaife), Bradley Foundation, Koch family foundations, Mercer family.
- By 2017: $20 million annual revenue.
- By 2024: budget over $20 million; chapters at all 204 ABA-accredited law schools; 70,000 practicing-attorney members in 90 cities.
- Source: Wikipedia "Federalist Society"; Britannica "Federalist Society"; Steven Teles, *The Rise of the Conservative Legal Movement* (Princeton, 2008).

### Reagan administration integration
- Edwin Meese (US Attorney General 1985-1988, born 1931; Reagan chief of staff 1969-1974 in California, Counsellor to the President 1981-1985; Federalist Society cofounder) installed early Federalist Society members in DOJ positions across the 1985-1988 period.
- William Bradford Reynolds, Steven Markman, Charles Cooper, and other early Federalist Society members held DOJ positions during the Meese period.
- 1986: Scalia confirmed to Supreme Court (faculty adviser of founding Chicago chapter).
- 1987: Bork nomination defeated 58-42 — pivotal moment for conservative legal movement.
- Source: Britannica "Federalist Society"; Steven Teles, *The Rise of the Conservative Legal Movement*.

### George H.W. Bush and George W. Bush administrations
- Lee Liberman Otis (founder) served as associate White House counsel under George H.W. Bush.
- C. Boyden Gray (White House counsel under H.W. Bush) was Federalist Society ally and organiser of the "Four Horsemen" of judicial nominations under the George W. Bush administration: Gray, Jay Sekulow, Ed Meese, and Leonard Leo.
- Brett Kavanaugh and Viet Dinh held key judicial-selection positions in the George W. Bush administration.
- 50% of George W. Bush's appointments to the courts of appeals were Federalist Society members or affiliates.
- 2005: John Roberts confirmed to Supreme Court (Federalist Society affiliate).
- 2006: Samuel Alito confirmed to Supreme Court (Federalist Society affiliate).

## Leonard Leo — career and role

### Early career
- Born 1965 in Long Branch, New Jersey; father died young; mother remarried when he was five.
- Cornell University (undergraduate and law school); intern under Senator Orrin Hatch (R-UT) on Judiciary Committee during law school.
- Married Sally Schroeder; seven children; first daughter Margaret born with spina bifida and died at age 14 — formative event in Leo's Catholic anti-abortion convictions.

### Federalist Society career (1991-2020)
- 1991: Leo joined Federalist Society (one of the first paid employees in the national office).
- Title progression: Executive Vice President (tenure); Co-chair from 2020 onward.
- Under Leo, Federalist Society expanded from two student chapters to over 90; budget from $100,000 to over $20 million by 2024.
- 2017: Clarence Thomas reportedly called Leo "the third most powerful person in the world" per New York Times reporting.

### Trump 2016 SCOTUS list
- May 2016: Trump released list of 11 potential Supreme Court nominees compiled with Leo and Heritage Foundation input — included Hardiman, Pryor, Gorsuch, and others.
- September 2016: Trump released expanded list of 21 names.
- 31 January 2017: Trump nominated Gorsuch (on Federalist Society list).
- 9 July 2018: Trump nominated Kavanaugh (on Federalist Society list).
- 26 September 2020: Trump nominated Barrett (on Federalist Society list).

### Marble Freedom Trust and Barre Seid donation
- Barre Seid (born 1932; Chicago electronics-manufacturing magnate, CEO of Tripp Lite for decades; secretive conservative-and-pro-Israel donor) structured the largest single donation in US political-advocacy history.
- 2020: Leo founded Marble Freedom Trust (a 501(c)(4) charity organised as a trust rather than corporation — fewer disclosure requirements, single-trustee structure).
- 2021: Seid donated 100% of Tripp Lite shares to Marble Freedom Trust; Marble subsequently sold shares to Ireland-based Eaton Corporation for $1.6 billion. The trust-structure transaction allowed Seid to avoid $400 million in capital-gains taxes.
- The $1.6 billion is the single largest donation on record to a politically-focused nonprofit.
- Leo is the trustee and chairman of Marble Freedom Trust — he personally controls the $1.6 billion endowment.
- Source: New York Times, "An Unusual $1.6 Billion Donation Bolsters Conservatives," 22 August 2022; ProPublica, "Barre Seid Donated $1.6 Billion to Conservative Marble Freedom Trust," 22 August 2022.

### CRC Advisors and post-2020 commercial integration
- Leo also chairs CRC Advisors (formerly Creative Response Concepts) — a PR-and-political-consulting firm that manages multiple conservative nonprofits.
- 2020: Leo stepped back from daily Federalist Society management to focus on CRC Advisors and Marble Freedom Trust operations.

## Heritage Foundation — founding and structure

### Founding
- 16 February 1973 Heritage Foundation founded by Paul Weyrich, Edwin Feulner, and Joseph Coors (Coors Brewing Company) at Washington DC.
- Initial $250,000 founding contribution from Joseph Coors.
- Founded as conservative policy-advocacy think tank distinct from the prior American Enterprise Institute (which was perceived as more academic and less politically-active).

### Reagan integration
- 1980: Heritage published *Mandate for Leadership* — a 1,000+ page policy blueprint for the incoming Reagan administration. The subsequent Reagan administration implemented 60% of the Heritage recommendations across the 1981-1989 period.
- Heritage personnel infrastructure provided multiple Reagan-administration appointees including Edwin Meese (Federalist Society cofounder; Heritage Distinguished Fellow Emeritus subsequently).

### Subsequent leadership
- Edwin Feulner: President 1977-2013 (with brief 2017-2018 second tenure during transition); founding president.
- Jim DeMint: President 2013-2017 (former senator from South Carolina); resigned after leadership dispute over Trump alignment.
- Kay Coles James: President 2017-2021 (first Black president of Heritage).
- Kevin Roberts: President from 1 December 2021 onward.

## Kevin Roberts — career and Project 2025

### Background
- Born 1974 in Lafayette, Louisiana; PhD in History from University of Texas at Austin.
- Founder of John Paul the Great Academy (Catholic K-12 school) in Lafayette.
- President of Wyoming Catholic College 2013-2016.
- CEO of Texas Public Policy Foundation 2016-2021.
- 1 December 2021: became Heritage Foundation president.

### Project 2025
- April 2023: Project 2025 (formally "2025 Presidential Transition Project") launched by Heritage Foundation with coalition of 100+ conservative organisations as partners.
- *Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise* — the central 920-page Project 2025 policy document published April 2023.
- Project 2025 organised as four-pillar operation: policy agenda (the 920-page document), personnel database (vetted political appointees), training programme, 180-day playbook for the incoming administration.

### Project 2025 contents
- dismantling of Department of Education.
- expansion of executive-branch authority over independent agencies (under unitary-executive theory).
- comprehensive immigration enforcement including mass deportations and reduced legal immigration.
- abortion restrictions including reversal of FDA approval of mifepristone.
- elimination of diversity-and-inclusion programmes across federal agencies.
- loyalty tests for federal employees (Schedule F reclassification).
- comprehensive anti-trans-and-anti-LGBTQ provisions.
- Roberts quoted July 2024: "We are in the process of the second American Revolution, which will remain bloodless if the left allows it to be."

### Trump 2024 distancing and subsequent alignment
- July 2024: Trump publicly distanced himself from Project 2025: "I have nothing to do with Project 2025. I have not read it. I do not want to read it."
- Post-election: multiple Project 2025 architects placed in Trump second administration. Russell Vought (Project 2025 contributor, OMB director under Trump first administration) confirmed OMB director second administration. Tom Homan (Project 2025 chapter on immigration) appointed Border Czar. Stephen Miller (Project 2025 contributor) appointed Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy. Brendan Carr (Project 2025 FCC chapter author) appointed FCC Chair.
- Kevin Roberts subsequent statement (April 2025): "If the Heritage Foundation has a tiny role in providing some ideas and some of the people to help that happen, then we're very happy about that."

### Implementation tracking
- Per The 19th News (December 2025) and multiple subsequent advocacy-organisation trackers: 50% of the 920-page Project 2025 policy agenda implemented across the first year of Trump's second administration.
- Source: The 19th News, "How much of Project 2025 has actually been accomplished this year?", 17 December 2025.

## Judicial pipeline operation

### Federalist Society membership of current Supreme Court
- Six of nine current Supreme Court justices are Federalist Society members or affiliates: Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito, John Roberts, Brett Kavanaugh, Amy Coney Barrett, Neil Gorsuch.
- Antonin Scalia (faculty adviser at founding; member until 2016 death) was the most direct Federalist Society Supreme Court representative.
- Source: Yale Daily News, "How the Federalist Society shaped America's judiciary," 4 November 2024.

### Trump first-term judicial appointments
- 234 Article III federal judges confirmed under Trump first administration (2017-2021): 3 Supreme Court, 54 circuit court of appeals, 174 district court, 3 international trade.
- 85%+ of the Trump circuit-court appointments were Federalist Society members or affiliates per Federalist Society and subsequent media reporting.
- The 54 circuit appointments shifted multiple appellate courts from Democratic-appointed to Republican-appointed majorities — including the decisive reshaping of the Second, Third, Fifth, Sixth, Eighth, Ninth, and Eleventh Circuit balances.

### Trump second-term judicial appointments (2025-onward)
- The 2025-2026 Trump second administration continued the Federalist-Society-vetted judicial-nomination operation under the coordination of Leonard Leo and subsequent White House Counsel office personnel.

## Major rulings produced by the Federalist Society pipeline
- 2008 *District of Columbia v. Heller*: 5-4 individual-right Second Amendment interpretation (Scalia opinion).
- 2010 *Citizens United v. FEC*: 5-4 ruling that corporate-and-union independent political expenditures are protected speech.
- 2013 *Shelby County v. Holder*: 5-4 ruling invalidating Section 4(b) of the Voting Rights Act preclearance formula.
- 2014 *Burwell v. Hobby Lobby*: 5-4 ruling exempting closely-held religious-objector corporations from contraceptive-coverage mandate.
- 2018 *Janus v. AFSCME*: 5-4 ruling that public-sector union agency fees violate the First Amendment.
- 2022 *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization*: 6-3 ruling overturning *Roe v. Wade*.
- 2022 *NYSRPA v. Bruen*: 6-3 ruling expanding Second Amendment to public-carry licensing.
- 2023 *Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard / SFFA v. UNC*: 6-3 ruling ending race-conscious higher-education admissions.
- 2023 *303 Creative LLC v. Elenis*: 6-3 ruling granting First Amendment exemption from public-accommodations law.
- 2024 *Trump v. United States*: 6-3 ruling granting presidential immunity for official acts.
- 2024 *Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo*: 6-3 ruling overturning Chevron deference doctrine.

## General references
- Steven M. Teles, *The Rise of the Conservative Legal Movement: The Battle for Control of the Law* (Princeton, 2008).
- Amanda Hollis-Brusky, *Ideas with Consequences: The Federalist Society and the Conservative Counterrevolution* (Oxford, 2015).
- Project 2025: *Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise* (Heritage Foundation, April 2023).
- ProPublica's Leonard Leo investigation series (2022-2024).
- New York Times, "An Unusual $1.6 Billion Donation Bolsters Conservatives" (Kenneth Vogel and Shane Goldmacher, 22 August 2022).
- The 19th News, "How much of Project 2025 has actually been accomplished this year?" (17 December 2025).

## Sources verification

# Sources — Dossier 16: DOGE, Musk, Vance, Ramaswamy

## The Department of Government Efficiency

### Announcement and creation
- Musk proposed a "government efficiency commission" to Trump during interview in summer 2024; subsequent discussion on X platform.
- 12 November 2024: Trump announced creation of "Department of Government Efficiency" (DOGE) with Musk and Ramaswamy as co-leaders.
- DOGE acronym is backronym referring to dogecoin meme cryptocurrency that Musk had promoted.
- Trump analogised DOGE to the Manhattan Project.
- DOGE was not a real federal department; it was advisory body operating "from outside the Government."
- 20 November 2024: Musk and Ramaswamy co-authored *Wall Street Journal* op-ed "The DOGE Plan to Reform Government" — stated goal to "reverse a decadeslong executive power grab" and to work closely with OMB.

### Cost-cutting targets
- October 2024 Madison Square Garden rally: Musk claimed DOGE could reduce federal spending by "at least" $2 trillion — exceeding the entire 2023 federal discretionary budget.
- February 2025 first cabinet meeting of second Trump administration: Musk lowered target to $1 trillion (~15% of federal budget).
- April 2025: Musk claimed $150 billion cut; figure disputed by fact-checkers.
- 5 June 2025: House DOGE caucus leader Blake Moore (R-UT) acknowledged Republican members "always knew it was a massive exaggeration."

### Executive order and structure
- 20 January 2025: Trump signed Executive Order 14158 creating DOGE.
- The EO renamed the pre-existing United States Digital Service to "United States DOGE Service" (USDS) within Executive Office of the President.
- The EO divided DOGE into permanent and temporary components; temporary component scheduled to dissolve no later than 4 July 2026 (250th anniversary of Declaration of Independence).
- Each federal agency directed to establish DOGE Team of at least four employees: DOGE Team Lead, engineer, HR specialist, attorney.

### Personnel and structure
- 20 January 2025: White House confirmed Ramaswamy would not join DOGE despite November 2024 announcement.
- Politico reported Ramaswamy was forced out by Musk after conflict with DOGE staff over Ramaswamy's X post characterising Americans as "venerated mediocrity over excellence."
- Republican strategist quoted in reporting: "everyone wants him out."
- Ramaswamy announced May 2026 Ohio gubernatorial campaign.
- 17 February 2025: Office of Administration Director Joshua Fisher stated under oath that Musk is employee of White House Office ( special government employee) and is not employed by DOGE or USDS.
- February 2025: White House confirmed Amy Gleason was acting USDS administrator (not Musk).
- 17 inspectors general dismissed within first week of Trump second administration; multiple IGs were leading investigations into Musk companies at time of dismissal.

### Musk departure
- 23 April 2025: Musk announced he would "significantly" reduce DOGE involvement starting May, citing Tesla shareholder pressure.
- 28 May 2025: Musk completed 130-day special-government-employee tenure (statutory maximum).
- 30 May 2025: Trump-Musk press conference in Oval Office marking end of Musk's DOGE role.
- Post-departure: Musk publicly criticised Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending legislation for projected debt impact; Trump-Musk social-media feud followed.
- December 2025: Musk on "Katie Miller Podcast" said DOGE was "only somewhat successful" and that he "wouldn't do it again."
- Tesla shareholder vote subsequent late 2025: approved pay package potentially making Musk first trillionaire.

### Implementation context
- Russell Vought (Center for Renewing America founder; Project 2025 contributor) confirmed as OMB Director January 2025.
- 20 January 2025: Trump executive order restored Schedule F career-civil-servant reclassification (the Project 2025 personnel-removal mechanism).
- Vance characterised DOGE in 2025 commentary: "[DOGE] matters less for saving money than for making the bureaucracy responsive to the elected president."
- Source: Wikipedia "Department of Government Efficiency"; Ballotpedia "Department of Government Efficiency"; CBS News, "What to know about Trump's Department of Government Efficiency," 15 November 2024; NPR, "Trump taps Musk to lead a Department of Government Efficiency with Ramaswamy," 12 November 2024.

## JD Vance — biography and the Thiel pipeline

### Early life and background
- James Donald Bowman, born 2 August 1984 in Middletown, Ohio.
- Mother Beverly Aikins (later Bev Vance, then Hamel) struggled with opioid and alcohol addiction across multi-year period.
- Biological father Donald Bowman left when Vance was young child.
- Raised primarily by maternal grandparents Bonnie ("Mamaw") and James ("Papaw") Vance in Middletown.
- Changed name to James David Vance.

### Education and early career
- US Marine Corps 2003-2007; served as public-affairs Marine in Iraq War.
- Ohio State University BA 2009 (political science and philosophy).
- Yale Law School JD 2013; editor of Yale Law Journal.
- Met future wife Usha Chilukuri (later Vance) at Yale Law School; subsequent law-clerk to Chief Justice John Roberts.
- 2011: law clerk for Senator John Cornyn (R-TX), three months.
- 2013-2014: law clerk for Judge David Bunning (US District Court Eastern District of Kentucky).
- Subsequent litigation associate at international law firm in Washington DC.
- May 2015: Director of Operations and Business Development at Circuit Therapeutics.

### Mithril Capital under Peter Thiel
- April 2016: Principal at Mithril Capital Management, Peter Thiel's Silicon Valley venture-capital firm (founded by Thiel and Ajay Royan in 2012).
- Vance had attended a Thiel speech at Yale Law School that he subsequently described as career-defining.
- Wall Street Journal reporting (2024): former Mithril colleague said he "never once saw" Vance in the office during the year Vance was there; subsequent colleague said Vance "spent a good deal of time promoting *Hillbilly Elegy*."
- Source: Fortune, "A colleague who worked with J.D. Vance at Mithril doesn't remember seeing him in the office," 26 July 2024.

### Hillbilly Elegy (June 2016)
- *Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis* published June 2016 by HarperCollins.
- New York Times bestseller list 2016 and 2017.
- 2017 Dayton Literary Peace Prize finalist.
- Ron Howard directed 2020 Netflix adaptation.
- Vance's subsequent Eastern-Kentucky critics characterised him as "not a hillbilly" given Middletown Ohio upbringing.

### Revolution and Narya Capital
- Spring 2017: left Mithril for Revolution, the Washington DC investment firm founded by AOL co-founder Steve Case.
- 2019: founded Narya Capital (Cincinnati, Ohio) — tech-startup-focused venture fund. named after J.R.R. Tolkien's *Lord of the Rings* Three Rings.
- Narya Capital backers: Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, Eric Schmidt — all covered in Volume One dossiers.

### 2022 Ohio Senate race
- January 2021: officially announced Republican Ohio Senate campaign for open seat vacated by retiring Rob Portman.
- Peter Thiel donated $10 million to Protect Ohio Values PAC (Vance-supporting Super PAC) per March 2021 Cincinnati Enquirer reporting under communications adviser Bryan Lanza.
- subsequent additional Thiel donations brought total to $15 million across the 2021-2022 cycle — the single largest single-donor Senate-race investment of the 2022 cycle.
- Robert Mercer (Renaissance Technologies / Cambridge Analytica funder; covered in Dossier 15) also made "significant contribution" per Lanza confirmation.
- 8 November 2022: Vance defeated Tim Ryan (D) 53-47%.
- 3 January 2023: sworn in as US Senator from Ohio.

### Vice Presidency
- 15 July 2024: Trump selected Vance as VP nominee on opening day of 2024 RNC at Milwaukee.
- 5 November 2024: Trump-Vance ticket defeated Harris-Walz 312-226 electoral votes.
- 20 January 2025: Vance sworn in as 50th Vice President of the United States by Chief Justice Brett Kavanaugh (Vance's connection to Kavanaugh through Usha Vance's earlier clerkships for both Kavanaugh and Roberts).
- 10 January 2025: resigned Ohio Senate seat (10 days before inauguration).

### Vance and Signal chat scandal (March 2025)
- 24 March 2025: *The Atlantic* editor Jeffrey Goldberg published account of being accidentally added to Signal chat group "Houthi PC Small Group" by National Security Advisor Mike Waltz.
- Group included Vance, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard, CIA Director John Ratcliffe.
- Chat included pre-strike operational details for 15 March 2025 US strikes on Houthi targets in Yemen — including pre-strike weapons-systems aircraft-launch times.
- Vance's Signal messages included criticism of European allies and contempt for European NATO commitments.

### Anti-Europe rhetoric and 2025 Munich Security Conference speech
- 14 February 2025: Vance delivered Munich Security Conference keynote attacking European allies on free-speech-and-immigration grounds.
- subsequent multi-month diplomatic-and-political fallout across European capitals.

## Vivek Ramaswamy — biography and Roivant trajectory

### Early life and background
- Vivek Ganapathy Ramaswamy, born 9 August 1985 in Cincinnati, Ohio.
- Parents Tamil Brahmin immigrants from Kerala/Palakkad, India.
- Father Vadakanchery Ganapathy Ramaswamy: National Institute of Technology Calicut graduate; engineer at General Electric.
- Mother Geetha Ramaswamy: Mysore Medical College graduate; geriatric psychiatrist at Merck and Schering-Plough.

### Education and early career
- St. Xavier High School (Cincinnati) — valedictorian; nationally-ranked junior tennis player.
- Harvard College BA 2007 (biology, summa cum laude); worked in Douglas Melton stem-cell laboratory.
- Yale Law School JD 2013; met future wife Apoorva Tewari (subsequently throat surgeon) at Yale; law-school classmate of JD Vance.
- 2007-2014: partner at QVT Financial hedge fund; reportedly made $7 million across seven years.

### Roivant Sciences (2014-2021)
- 2014: founded Roivant Sciences ("Roivant" = "Return On Investment vant"); incorporated in Bermuda tax haven.
- $100 million start-up capital from QVT, RA Capital Management, Visium Asset Management, D. E. Shaw, Falcon Edge Capital.
- business model: purchase of shelved drug patents from larger pharma companies; development through subsidiary "Vant" structure.
- subsidiaries: Dermavant, Urovant, Myovant, Axovant, Sinovant (China-focused), Cytovant (Asia-focused).
- 2015: raised $360 million for Axovant Sciences to market intepirdine as Alzheimer's drug.
- Forbes cover 2015: Ramaswamy claimed Axovant would be "the highest return on investment endeavor ever taken up in the pharmaceutical industry."
- 2015 Axovant IPO raised $315 million; market cap reached $3 billion despite company having only eight employees (including Ramaswamy's brother Shankar and mother Geetha).
- 2015: Ramaswamy reported $37 million in capital gains after partial Roivant share sale to Viking Global Investors.
- 2017: Roivant struck $1.1 billion investment deal with Masayoshi Son's SoftBank (covered in Volume One Dossier 22).
- 2017: partnership with CITIC Private Equity (Chinese state-owned CITIC Group) to form Sinovant.
- September 2017: intepirdine failed large clinical trial; Axovant stock lost 75% in one day.
- subsequent Axovant attempted gene-therapy pivot; dissolved 2023.
- California State Teachers' Retirement System pension fund among institutional Axovant losers.
- 2019: Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma acquired 10% Roivant stake for $3 billion.
- 2021: Roivant went public on Nasdaq via SPAC merger.
- January 2021: Ramaswamy stepped down as Roivant CEO.

### Woke Inc. and Strive Asset Management
- August 2021: published *Woke, Inc.: Inside Corporate America's Social Justice Scam* through Center Street (Hachette conservative imprint).
- 2022: founded Strive Asset Management — "anti-woke" index fund provider backed by Peter Thiel.
- Strive marketed as antithesis of BlackRock-Vanguard-State Street ESG-aligned asset managers.

### 2024 Republican presidential campaign
- 21 February 2023: announced 2024 Republican presidential candidacy.
- platform: dramatic federal-bureaucracy reduction, end of birthright citizenship for children of undocumented immigrants, withdrawal of Ukraine support, raising federal voting age to 25.
- Roivant subsidiaries Sinovant and Cytovant earlier focused on China innovation despite Ramaswamy's subsequent anti-China campaign rhetoric.
- 15 January 2024: suspended campaign after fourth-place Iowa caucus finish (~8% of vote); endorsed Trump.

### DOGE non-participation and Ohio gubernatorial campaign
- 12 November 2024: Trump announced Ramaswamy as DOGE co-leader with Musk.
- 20 January 2025: departed DOGE after conflict with Musk and DOGE staff.
- 20-21 January 2025: announced Ohio gubernatorial campaign.
- May 2026: won Republican primary for Ohio governor.
- May 2026 Forbes estimate of Ramaswamy net worth: $2.5 billion.

## Russell Vought, Schedule F, and OMB

### Russell Vought biography
- OMB Director under Trump first administration 2020-2021.
- Founded Center for Renewing America (CRA) post-2021; Project 2025 contributor.
- CRA drafted executive orders, regulations, memos for incoming Trump second administration.
- January 2025: confirmed as OMB Director for Trump second administration.
- Vought statements characterising career civil servants as figures to "put in trauma" through policy.

### Schedule F
- October 2020: Trump first administration issued Executive Order 13957 creating Schedule F excepted-service classification for career-civil-servant policy positions.
- Biden administration revoked Schedule F January 2021.
- 20 January 2025: Trump second administration restored Schedule F via executive order on inauguration day.
- Schedule F subsequent implementation could allow reclassification of 50,000 career civil servants to at-will employment — the most consequential federal-workforce-restructuring of the post-1883 Pendleton Act period.

## DOGE staffing and operations
- DOGE subsequent staffing: young engineers and Musk-affiliated personnel recruited aggressively across late November 2024 - January 2025 period.
- subsequent multi-month DOGE-Team operations across multiple federal agencies including Treasury ( access to payment systems contested), USAID ( subsequent agency dismantling), Office of Personnel Management, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau ( subsequent shutdown).
- multiple court challenges to DOGE access including 8 February 2025 temporary restraining order blocking DOGE access to Treasury payment systems (subsequently modified).

## General references
- Wikipedia "Department of Government Efficiency"; Wikipedia "JD Vance"; Wikipedia "Vivek Ramaswamy"; Wikipedia "Russell Vought."
- Ballotpedia "Department of Government Efficiency"; Ballotpedia "Vivek Ramaswamy"; Ballotpedia "JD Vance."
- Wall Street Journal, "Elon Musk and Vivek Ramaswamy: The DOGE Plan to Reform Government" (20 November 2024).
- Politico, "Musk is gone. But DOGE staffers are still trying to cut through agencies" (30 May 2025).

# Sources — Dossier 17: AIPAC (American Israel Public Affairs Committee)

## Institutional origins

### Pre-AIPAC sequence (1943-1959)
- 1943: Rabbi Abba Hillel Silver of Cleveland founded the American Zionist Emergency Council (AZEC) to organise American Jews to lobby for Jews in Mandatory Palestine.
- 1949: AZEC renamed American Zionist Council (AZC) following the 1948 Israeli state founding.
- 1951: Isaiah L. Kenen (born 1905 St. Stephen New Brunswick Canada; Toronto Star journalist; Cleveland News attorney; American Jewish Conference secretary 1943-1948; Jewish Agency UN information director 1947-1948) organised the unincorporated American Zionist Committee for Public Affairs (AZCPA) as the Washington lobbying arm.
- 1951-1952: Kenen lobbying produced $65 million (1951) and $73 million (1952) in US aid to Israel.
- October 1953: Israeli forces under Ariel Sharon's Unit 101 attacked the West Bank Palestinian village of Qibya, killing 66-69 villagers (two-thirds women and children) and dynamiting 42 houses, a school, and a mosque. International condemnation followed.
- 1954: AZC divested AZCPA from its tax-exempt umbrella structure to permit lobbying activity.
- 1959: AZCPA renamed American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC), reflecting expansion beyond strictly Zionist membership.

### Fulbright FARA investigation (1962-1963)
- 1962-1963: Senate Foreign Relations Committee under Senator J. William Fulbright (D-AR) opened investigation under Foreign Agents Registration Act.
- Investigation focused on Jewish Agency in Jerusalem and New York as funding source for AZC, AIPAC, and Near East Report newsletter (Kenen-owned).
- DOJ ordered Kenen to register as foreign agent if he continued lobbying for Israel; Kenen never complied.
- AZC eventually compelled to register under FARA. AIPAC not — on institutional fiction that AIPAC lobbied for American members rather than Israeli government.
- Source: Grant F. Smith, *Foreign Agents: The American Israel Public Affairs Committee from the 1963 Fulbright Hearings to the 2005 Espionage Scandal* (IRMEP, 2007).

### Leadership sequence
- 1951-1974: Isaiah L. Kenen.
- 1974-1980: Morris J. Amitay (Jackson-Vanik co-author). Transformed AIPAC from 12 staff and $300,000-$400,000 budget into mass-membership organisation.
- 1980-1993: Thomas A. Dine. Grassroots architecture across 1980s.
- 1993-1996: Neal Sher; Howard Kohr interim.
- 1996-2024: Howard Kohr (CEO).
- January 2025 onward: Elliot Brandt (CEO).

### Budget growth
- 1973: $300,000.
- Late 1980s peak: over $7 million.
- 2024 cycle: AIPAC PAC + UDP combined $126.9 million per FEC filings via OpenSecrets.

## 2005 espionage scandal
- 2005: Pentagon analyst Lawrence Franklin pleaded guilty to passing classified US information to AIPAC officials Steven Rosen (foreign policy director) and Keith Weissman (Iran analyst).
- Rosen and Weissman initially charged under 1917 Espionage Act.
- AIPAC fired both 2005 but reportedly paid Rosen legal fees through 2009.
- May 2009: DOJ dropped charges, citing prosecution difficulty under the 1917 statute.
- Rosen subsequently filed defamation lawsuit against AIPAC; settled 2012.

## December 2021 PAC and January 2022 Super PAC

### AIPAC PAC
- 15 December 2021: AIPAC formed first political action committee (AIPAC PAC).
- Pre-2021: AIPAC never raised funds for political candidates itself; members raised through unaffiliated PACs.
- AIPAC PAC operates under $5,000-per-election individual-candidate contribution limit.

### United Democracy Project Super PAC
- 3 January 2022: AIPAC formed United Democracy Project (UDP) as Super PAC.
- Operates under post-*Citizens United* unlimited independent-expenditure rules.
- 2022 cycle: UDP raised over $35.9 million; spent $32.9 million including $26.1 million in independent expenditures in Democratic House primaries.

## 2024 election cycle spending

### Top-line
- $126.9 million total 2024 cycle (PAC + Super PAC combined) per FEC filings.
- AIPAC PAC: $44.8 million by August 2024.
- UDP Super PAC: $55.4 million by August 2024.
- 318 candidates received AIPAC PAC contributions across 2024 cycle.
- Politico March 2024: AIPAC entities "expected to spend $100 million across its political entities in 2024."

### Top individual donors (2024)
Per FactCheck.org, OpenSecrets, In These Times, Forbes:
- **Jan Koum** (WhatsApp co-founder; Israeli-American from Soviet Ukraine): $7.43 million to UDP. Top UDP donor 2024.
- **Miriam Adelson** (Sheldon Adelson widow): $5 million to AIPAC entities (via Republican Jewish Coalition channel).
- **Jonathon Jacobson** (Highfields Capital Management founder; Birthright Israel trustee): $4.575 million.
- **Bernard Marcus** (Home Depot co-founder; died November 2024; Marcus Foundation $2.7 billion-plus cumulative donations): $1 million-plus to UDP.
- **David Zalik** (GreenSky founder; Israeli-American born in occupied territories): major donor; Zalik Foundation $100 million-plus.
- **Paul Singer** (Elliott Management founder; net worth $6.1 billion; covered in Dossier 13 as 2008 Alito Alaska trip funder): $1 million to UDP 2024 (matching 2022 cycle).
- **Haim Saban** (Saban Entertainment founder; Power Rangers franchise creator; net worth $2.8 billion; major Democratic donor): multiple UDP donations including $2 million across multiple cycles. Saban Family Foundation $500 million-plus to Jewish-and-Zionist causes; $30 million-plus to US political causes.
- **Helaine Lerner**: major donor.

### Major 2024 primary interventions
**New York-16 (Jamaal Bowman vs George Latimer):**
- 25 June 2024 primary: Latimer defeated Bowman 58%-41%.
- UDP spent $9.9 million against Bowman; $4.8 million for Latimer; $12.3 million TV ad spending total.
- Set NYT-reported record for single House race outside-group spending.
- one-quarter of Latimer's direct campaign contributions bundled through AIPAC; in late 2023, half of Latimer contributions came through AIPAC per The Intercept.

**Missouri-1 (Cori Bush vs Wesley Bell):**
- 6 August 2024 primary: Bell defeated Bush.
- UDP spent $5.2 million-plus against Bush; $6 million TV ad spending for Bell.
- Bush was vocal critic of Israel military conduct in Gaza; Squad caucus member.

**Other 2024 targets:**
- Summer Lee (D-PA-12): UDP failed to defeat in 2024 primary.
- Dave Min (D-CA-47): UDP spent $4.6 million unsuccessfully.
- Valerie Foushee (D-NC-4): 2022 UDP helped defeat Nida Allam.

### 2022 pattern
- UDP spent $26.1 million on independent expenditures in Democratic House primaries in 2022.
- Per The Intercept: almost all UDP 2022 independent expenditures against Democrats were spent against candidates of colour. Remainder against former Rep. Andy Levin (D-MI-11), a Jewish member of Congress.

## US military aid architecture

### Memorandum of Understanding sequence
- 1999-2008 10-year MOU: $26.7 billion (Clinton administration).
- 2009-2018 10-year MOU: $30 billion (signed September 2007 under George W. Bush).
- 2019-2028 10-year MOU: $38 billion (signed September 2016 under Obama). Largest US military-aid commitment to any single country in US history.

### Post-October-2023 supplemental
- April 2024: $26 billion Israel-Ukraine-Indo-Pacific supplemental passed by Congress; $14 billion Israel military aid portion.
- Cumulative 2023-2024 US military aid to Israel $17.9 billion in the twelve months after 7 October 2023 per Brown University Costs of War analysis.

## Counter-organisations and critical analysis
- J Street (founded 2008 by Jeremy Ben-Ami): progressive pro-Israel lobby alternative to AIPAC.
- IfNotNow (founded 2014): American Jewish anti-occupation organisation.
- Jewish Voice for Peace (founded 1996): American Jewish anti-Zionist organisation.
- TrackAIPAC: post-2023 systematic documentation of donor network.
- John J. Mearsheimer and Stephen M. Walt, *The Israel Lobby and U.S. Foreign Policy* (Farrar Straus Giroux, 2007).
- Grant F. Smith, *Foreign Agents* (IRMEP, 2007).

## Post-October 2023 escalation
- 7 October 2023: Hamas attack on Israel; 1,200 killed, 250 taken hostage.
- 8 October 2023 onward: Israeli military campaign against Gaza Strip. As of May 2026 documented Palestinian deaths exceed 50,000 per Gaza Health Ministry; ICJ provisional measures rulings under South Africa application January 2024 and subsequent; ICC arrest warrants November 2024 against PM Benjamin Netanyahu and former Defense Minister Yoav Gallant.
- 2024 AIPAC primary intervention pattern intensified specifically against Democratic congressional critics of Israeli military conduct in Gaza per multiple Bowman, Bush, Min, Foushee, and Lee primaries documentation.

## General references
- Wikipedia "AIPAC"; Wikipedia "Isaiah L. Kenen"; Wikipedia "American Zionist Council"; Britannica "American Israel Public Affairs Committee".
- FactCheck.org, "United Democracy Project" (26 September 2024).
- The Intercept, "AIPAC Spends $2 Million to Attack Rep. Jamaal Bowman" (Akela Lacy, 16 May 2024).
- Common Dreams, "AIPAC Has Spent Over $100 Million on 2024 Elections" (6 September 2024).
- NPR, "Why Pro-Israel PACs are helping oust Democrats in their primaries" (7 August 2024).
- Punchbowl News, "Pro-Israel groups make their mark in Democratic primaries" (16 October 2024).
- Truthout, "Some of AIPAC's Biggest Donors Are Also Financing Attacks Against Labor Rights" (5 June 2024).
- Americans for Transparency / americansfortransparency.org, "AIPAC: $126.9M 2024 Election Spending" (31 March 2026).
- Sludge / In These Times reporting on UDP donor base (multiple 2024 dates).
- Mearsheimer & Walt, *The Israel Lobby and U.S. Foreign Policy* (2007).
- Grant F. Smith, *Foreign Agents* (IRMEP, 2007).
- New York Times, multiple reports on Bowman-Latimer race (June 2024).

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.
