# Sources — Dossier 18: Sir Keir Starmer

## Biography

### Early life and education
- Keir Rodney Starmer, born 2 September 1962 in Southwark, south-east London.
- Mother Josephine (née Baker) Starmer: nurse for the NHS; diagnosed at young age with Still's disease (rare inflammatory disorder that progressively left her unable to walk, eat, or talk).
- Father Rodney Starmer: toolmaker in a factory.
- Raised in Oxted, Surrey.
- Named after Keir Hardie, the first parliamentary leader of the Labour Party.
- Attended Reigate Grammar School (selective voluntary-aided grammar that converted to independent fee-paying school in 1976 during Starmer's tenure; school awarded Starmer a bursary that covered fees after age 16).
- University of Leeds LLB 1985 (first member of his family to attain a university degree).
- St Edmund Hall, Oxford BCL (postgraduate Bachelor of Civil Law).
- 1986-1987: editorial collective of *Socialist Alternatives* magazine.

### Legal career (1987-2013)
- 1987: called to the Bar at Middle Temple.
- Focused on criminal defence and human-rights work.
- Member of Doughty Street Chambers from 1990.
- 1988-1992: secretary of the Haldane Society of Socialist Lawyers.
- 1990: legal officer for the campaign group Liberty.
- Worked on death-penalty cases in the Caribbean and Africa.
- Worked on the McLibel case representing the McDonald's defendants.
- 1998-2008: human rights adviser to the Northern Ireland Policing Board following the Good Friday Agreement.
- 2002: appointed Queen's Counsel (QC) on 9 April 2002.
- 2005: persuaded the House of Lords that evidence obtained by torture should be inadmissible in court.
- 2008: represented Vladimir Putin's alleged role in the Alexander Litvinenko murder in legal proceedings.

### Director of Public Prosecutions (2008-2013)
- July 2008: named DPP and Head of the Crown Prosecution Service by Attorney General Patricia Scotland.
- 1 November 2008: took office, succeeding Ken Macdonald.
- 2009: oversaw first UK prosecution of al-Qaeda terrorists.
- 2009: upheld decision not to prosecute police officers who had killed Jean Charles de Menezes in 2005.
- February 2010: announced CPS decision to prosecute three Labour MPs and one Conservative peer for false-accounting offences in parliamentary-expenses scandal; all found guilty.
- 2010: controversial decision not initially to prosecute Police Constable Simon Harwood for death of Ian Tomlinson during 2009 G20 protests; later reversed after inquest finding of unlawful killing; Harwood acquitted by jury July 2012, dismissed from police September 2012.
- 2011: rapid prosecutions during London riots; characterisation that the strategy "brought the situation back under control."
- February 2012: announced prosecution of Chris Huhne (former Liberal Democrat cabinet minister) for perverting course of justice.
- 2013: CPS prosecution of Stephen Lawrence murder defendants Gary Dobson and David Norris (convicted January 2012).
- November 2013: stepped down as DPP; succeeded by Alison Saunders.
- The Jimmy Savile case: CPS criticised for failing to prosecute Savile during Starmer's tenure; Starmer said not to be directly involved in case decisions per public statements.

### Knighthood and post-DPP work
- 2014 New Year Honours: appointed Knight Commander of the Order of the Bath (KCB) for "services to law and criminal justice."
- 2014: represented Croatia at the genocide hearings before the International Court of Justice at The Hague (arguing Serbia aimed to seize Croatian territory and eradicate its population in the 1990s war).
- 2014: published CPS guidance on assisted dying following the Martin case.

### Parliamentary career
- 6 May 2007: married Victoria Alexander (solicitor) at Fennes Estate, Essex. Two children.
- December 2014: selected as Labour parliamentary candidate for Holborn and St Pancras.
- 7 May 2015: elected MP for Holborn and St Pancras with majority of 17,048.
- September 2015: appointed Shadow Home Office Minister under Jeremy Corbyn.
- 2016: resigned from Corbyn frontbench in June 2016 mass resignation following Brexit vote.
- October 2016: returned as Shadow Brexit Secretary.
- 2019: re-elected with majority of 27,763.
- 2019: architect of Labour's pro-second-referendum Brexit policy.

### Labour leadership (2020-)
- 4 April 2020: elected Labour leader with 56.2% first-round vote, defeating Rebecca Long-Bailey and Lisa Nandy.
- April 2020 onward: pledged to "retain the radicalism of the last four years" while uniting party and addressing antisemitism.
- Sacked Rebecca Long-Bailey from Shadow Cabinet shortly after election.
- 2020 pandemic period: pledged not to "score party political points" during Covid response.
- 2020-2024: moved Labour Party toward political centre.
- 2022: investigated by Durham Constabulary over "Beergate" allegations of attending 2021 lockdown-period gathering in Durham. Denied wrongdoing; pledged to resign if issued fixed penalty fine. Police concluded no fine warranted.

## Prime Ministerial period (4 July 2024 onward)

### General election
- 4 July 2024: Labour won 411 seats to Conservatives' 121 in House of Commons general election.
- 174-seat majority (the majority figure varies in reporting from 172 to 174 depending on count of independents and speaker).
- Labour vote share: 33.7% — the smallest share of the popular vote of any majority government since record-keeping began in 1830.
- 5 July 2024: Starmer sworn in as Prime Minister by King Charles III at Buckingham Palace.
- Cabinet appointments: Rachel Reeves Chancellor of the Exchequer (first female Chancellor); David Lammy Foreign Secretary; Yvette Cooper Home Secretary; Angela Rayner Deputy Prime Minister; Wes Streeting Health Secretary; Bridget Phillipson Education Secretary.

### Early period and the winter fuel allowance
- August 2024: Reeves announced restriction of Winter Fuel Allowance to pensioners qualifying for Pension Credit — removing the payment from 10 million pensioners. Estimated savings: £1.4 billion.
- August 2024: anti-immigration riots in multiple English cities following the Southport stabbings false-information campaign on social media.
- subsequent Starmer government response: rapid prosecutions of rioters and of social-media-posters found to have incited violence.
- subsequent Elon Musk public characterisation of Starmer as "two-tier Keir" — Musk's post on X criticising the subsequent prosecutions of social-media-posters.
- Net approval rating: dropped from +7 at election to 0 by August 2024; subsequently to -46 average by November 2025 per Ipsos polling. November 2025 Ipsos poll: Starmer the least popular Prime Minister since Ipsos records began in 1977.

### Mandelson-Epstein scandal
- December 2024: Starmer and Foreign Secretary David Lammy announced Peter Mandelson's appointment as UK Ambassador to the United States.
- Mandelson background: Labour Party strategist; architect of Tony Blair's 1997 election victory; "Prince of Darkness" nickname; twice forced to resign from Blair government in 1990s and early 2000s; European Commissioner for Trade 2004-2008; subsequent Business Secretary under Brown 2008-2010.
- February 2025: Mandelson took up ambassador post.
- February 2025-September 2025: served as ambassador; involved in US-UK trade-deal negotiations.
- 10 September 2025: US House Oversight Committee released cache of Mandelson-Epstein emails including Mandelson's "best pal" characterisation of Epstein in 2003 birthday book and Mandelson's 2008 emails encouraging Epstein to "fight for early release" from 2008 Florida conviction.
- 10 September 2025: Starmer told Parliament he had "full confidence" in Mandelson.
- 11 September 2025: Mandelson fired as ambassador effective immediately. Foreign Office statement: Mandelson's suggestion that Epstein's 2008 conviction was "wrongful and should be challenged" was "new information"; post-discovery the extent of the Mandelson-Epstein relationship was characterised as "materially different" from pre-appointment understanding.
- Mandelson Foreign Office HR payout: £75,000.
- subsequent James Roscoe appointed interim ambassador ( pre-2025 deputy head of mission in Washington from July 2022).
- February 2026: Mandelson arrested by British police on suspicion of misconduct in public office after DOJ-released documents showed Mandelson appeared to leak sensitive British government data to Epstein.
- February 2026: Starmer told House of Commons that Mandelson "betrayed our country, our Parliament, and my party. He lied repeatedly" — and that he regretted appointing him.
- February 2026: Morgan McSweeney, Starmer's chief of staff and advocate of the Mandelson appointment, took responsibility for the appointment and resigned.
- subsequent April 2026 Olly Robbins testimony at parliamentary committee: Foreign Office's top civil servant; subsequent dismissal from Foreign Office; reported to be taking legal advice and feeling "aggrieved over his dismissal" per Financial Times reporting.

### Welfare cuts and Rayner stamp-duty
- March 2025: Reeves Spring Statement announced welfare cuts including Personal Independence Payment (PIP) restriction.
- May 2025: Starmer partial U-turn on Winter Fuel Allowance restriction at PMQs.
- September 2025: Angela Rayner resigned as Deputy Prime Minister and Housing Secretary after stamp-duty underpayment scandal on £800,000 Hove flat purchase.
- 14 May 2026: Rayner cleared of deliberate wrongdoing by HMRC.

### 2025-2026 local elections and leadership crisis
- May 2025: Labour lost 187 local council seats in the English local elections.
- 7 May 2026: Labour lost 1,800 council seats across the English, Welsh, and Scottish local elections. Plaid Cymru ended a century of Labour dominance in Wales. SNP became the largest party in the Scottish Parliament.
- May 2026: Reform UK and Greens made major gains.
- 13 May 2026: King Charles III delivered State Opening of Parliament speech laying out the Starmer government agenda while Starmer's political future hung in the balance.
- 14 May 2026: Wes Streeting (Health Secretary) resigned, criticising Starmer in resignation letter; subsequently confirmed he would stand in leadership election if triggered.
- 14 May 2026: Andy Burnham (Greater Manchester Mayor) announced intention to stand in Makerfield by-election (triggered by Josh Simons's resignation), having previously been blocked from Gorton and Denton by-election candidacy by Labour NEC by 8-1 vote.
- Mid-May 2026: 97 Labour MPs called on Starmer to resign or set out departure timetable. One cabinet minister, four junior ministers, and four ministerial aides resigned in protest.
- Per Guardian / LabourList reporting (mid-May 2026): 159 Labour MPs support Starmer; 97 want him to resign or set out timetable; 147 have no public position.
- Over 110 backbenchers signed Guardian-reported letter saying leadership challenge should not occur.
- 16 May 2026: police out in force in London to deal with rival rallies amid government uncertainty period; FA Cup Final held same day.

## US-UK relationship under Starmer
- 5 November 2024: Trump-Vance ticket won US presidential election.
- December 2024: Mandelson appointment to Washington announced.
- 14 February 2025: JD Vance Munich Security Conference keynote attack on European free-speech policies, including direct criticism of UK Online Safety Bill provisions (Dossier 16).
- 26-27 February 2025: Starmer official visit to Washington including White House Oval Office meeting with Trump and Mandelson-hosted reception at British Ambassador's Residence.
- May 2025: announcement of US-UK trade deal (Mandelson played central role in negotiations).
- September 2025: Trump UK state visit including Windsor Castle banquet with King Charles III; took place shortly after Mandelson firing.

## Net approval rating record
- Election (4 July 2024): +7
- August 2024: 0
- November 2025: -46 (Ipsos average for month)
- May 2026: net approval comparable to Liz Truss / Boris Johnson Partygate lows per multiple polling.
- November 2025 Ipsos: Starmer the least popular Prime Minister since Ipsos records began in 1977.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Keir Starmer"; Wikipedia "Legal career of Keir Starmer"; Wikipedia "2026 United Kingdom government crisis"; Wikipedia "Relationship of Peter Mandelson and Jeffrey Epstein".
- Britannica "Keir Starmer".
- CNN, "Peter Mandelson: UK fires ambassador to US over Epstein links" (11 September 2025).
- CNN, "UK PM Starmer says he shouldn't have appointed Epstein-linked pick for US ambassador" (20 April 2026).
- The Conversation, "From landslide to leadership crisis: where did it all go wrong for Keir Starmer?" (15 May 2026).
- The Conversation, "Starmer's winter fuel allowance U-turn sets him on a tricky path" (May 2025).
- Labour Party official biography pages.
- Ipsos and YouGov polling archives 2024-2026.
- LabourList MP whip-count tracking May 2026.
- Financial Times reporting on McSweeney / Robbins May 2026.

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Dossier 19: The Post-Cameron UK Conservative Administration (2010-2024)

## The fourteen-year administration period

The 2010-2024 UK Conservative administration period operated under six Conservative Prime Ministers, four of whom held office across the 2016-2024 post-referendum period of acute political instability:

1. **David Cameron** (11 May 2010 - 13 July 2016) — coalition government with Liberal Democrats 2010-2015; majority Conservative government 2015-2016.
2. **Theresa May** (13 July 2016 - 24 July 2019) — 3 years and 11 days.
3. **Boris Johnson** (24 July 2019 - 6 September 2022) — 3 years and 44 days.
4. **Liz Truss** (6 September 2022 - 25 October 2022) — 50 days; shortest-serving PM in British history.
5. **Rishi Sunak** (25 October 2022 - 5 July 2024) — 1 year and 254 days.
6. **Kemi Badenoch** — Leader of the Conservative Party and Leader of the Opposition from 2 November 2024 to date.

## David Cameron (2010-2016)

### Biography
- Born 9 October 1966, London, to upper-middle-class family. Father Ian Cameron (stockbroker partner at Panmure Gordon), mother Mary Cameron (Justice of the Peace).
- Educated at Heatherdown Preparatory School, Eton College, and Brasenose College, Oxford (1985-1988; PPE, first-class BA).
- Member of the Bullingdon Club at Oxford (with Boris Johnson and George Osborne).
- Conservative Research Department 1988-1993.
- Special adviser to Chancellor Norman Lamont 1992-1993 (Black Wednesday period).
- Special adviser to Home Secretary Michael Howard 1993-1994.
- Director of Corporate Affairs at Carlton Communications (media communications) 1994-2001.
- MP for Witney 2001-2016.
- Conservative Party leader from 6 December 2005, succeeding Michael Howard.

### Family tragedy
- Son Ivan Cameron born 8 April 2002 with Ohtahara syndrome (rare neurological disorder requiring round-the-clock care for cerebral palsy and severe epilepsy).
- Ivan died 25 February 2009 aged six.

### Premiership (2010-2016)
- 11 May 2010: appointed Prime Minister at head of Conservative-Liberal Democrat coalition government following hung parliament.
- 2010 austerity programme launched in George Osborne June 2010 emergency Budget: £110 billion in public spending cuts and tax increases across the five-year forecast period.
- 2010-2015 coalition cabinet: George Osborne Chancellor; Theresa May Home Secretary; William Hague Foreign Secretary; Michael Gove Education Secretary; Nick Clegg Deputy Prime Minister.
- 2011: Alternative Vote referendum (heavily rejected).
- 2012: Health and Social Care Act ( reorganisation of NHS).
- 2012: Welfare Reform Act.
- 2012: London Olympics.
- August 2011: London riots.
- 2012-2016 Home Office "hostile environment" immigration policy (subsequently leading to Windrush scandal under May).
- 2014: Scottish independence referendum (55%-45% No vote retaining Scotland in UK).
- 7 May 2015: Conservative majority government (331 seats, majority 12) — first Conservative majority since 1992.
- 23 June 2016: EU membership referendum (52% Leave, 48% Remain). Cameron campaigned for Remain.
- 24 June 2016: Cameron announced resignation. Left office 13 July 2016.

### Austerity death-toll record
- LSE Inequalities June 2024 research (Berman & Hovland): 190,000 excess deaths in UK 2010-2019 attributable to austerity-period public-spending cuts; 3% increase in mortality rates; average UK person lost half a year of life expectancy.
- IPPR June 2019 "Ending the Blame Game" report: 130,000 deaths in England 2012-2017 could have been prevented absent the austerity-period halt to public-health improvements.
- BMJ Open November 2017 (Watkins, Wulaningsih, Da Zhou, Marshall, Sinclair, Pariente-Pichon): 120,000 excess deaths in England 2010-2017 linked to austerity, primarily through reductions in nursing-staff numbers.
- Marmot Review of Health Equity in England 10 Years On (February 2020): documented life-expectancy slowdown across the austerity decade; subsequent 2010-2020 life-expectancy stagnation that was without precedent in the modern UK record.
- Office for National Statistics data 2018: first fall in UK life expectancy at subsequent period since 1986.

### Post-PM career and Greensill scandal
- 12 September 2016: resigned from Parliament.
- 2017-2023: President of Alzheimer's Research UK.
- 2018: became adviser to Greensill Capital with share options reportedly worth up to $60 million plus over $1 million per year for 25 days of work per year.
- 2019: arranged private meeting between Lex Greensill and Secretary of State for Health and Social Care Matt Hancock; several NHS trusts subsequently used Greensill's Earnd app.
- 2020-2021: Cameron lobbied the Sunak Treasury through text messages and ten virtual meetings with permanent secretaries Tom Scholar and Charles Roxburgh to allow Greensill to access Covid Corporate Financing Facility loans. Sunak declined.
- The Government-owned British Business Bank lent Greensill up to £400 million through a different scheme, producing a potential £335 million loss to the taxpayer.
- Panorama investigation concluded Cameron earned $10 million pre-tax for 30 months of part-time work for Greensill.
- April 2021: formal investigation launched by UK lobbying registrar led by Nigel Boardman.
- 13 November 2023: appointed Foreign Secretary by Rishi Sunak; created Baron Cameron of Chipping Norton, of Chadlington in the County of Oxfordshire (life peer).
- 5 July 2024: stepped down as Foreign Secretary following Conservative election defeat. Retained House of Lords seat.

## Theresa May (2016-2019)

### Biography
- Born 1 October 1956 in Eastbourne. Father Hubert Brasier (Anglican vicar).
- Educated at Wheatley Park Comprehensive School and St Hugh's College, Oxford (Geography).
- Bank of England 1977-1983; Association for Payment Clearing Services 1985-1997.
- MP for Maidenhead 1997-2024.
- Home Secretary 2010-2016 (longest-serving Home Secretary of modern era).

### Premiership (2016-2019)
- 13 July 2016: appointed Prime Minister following Cameron resignation and Conservative leadership contest.
- 8 June 2017: snap general election. Conservatives lost majority — minority government formed with DUP confidence-and-supply arrangement (Northern Ireland's Democratic Unionist Party).
- 2017-2019: subsequent Brexit negotiations with EU. May Withdrawal Agreement rejected by House of Commons three times (15 January 2019, 12 March 2019, 29 March 2019) by record-margin votes.
- 24 May 2019: announced resignation.
- 24 July 2019: left office.

### Windrush scandal
- April 2018 onward: documented Windrush scandal — thousands of post-WWII Caribbean-born British residents (many of whom had arrived as children on Caribbean parents' passports under pre-1973 right-of-abode arrangements) wrongfully detained, denied legal rights, deported, or refused re-entry under Home Office hostile-environment policy that May had architected as Home Secretary 2012-2016.
- 2020 Williams Lessons Learned Review documented "thoughtless" Home Office decisions.

## Boris Johnson (2019-2022)

### Biography
- Born 19 June 1964 in New York City. Father Stanley Johnson (Conservative politician), mother Charlotte Johnson Wahl (painter).
- US-UK dual citizenship until 2016 renunciation of US citizenship.
- Educated at Eton College and Balliol College, Oxford (Literae Humaniores).
- Member of Bullingdon Club at Oxford with David Cameron and George Osborne.
- President of the Oxford Union 1986.
- The Times 1987-1988; Daily Telegraph 1988-1999 (Brussels correspondent 1989-1994, sacked from The Times for fabricating quotation).
- Editor of The Spectator 1999-2005.
- MP for Henley 2001-2008.
- Mayor of London 2008-2016.
- MP for Uxbridge and South Ruislip 2015-2023.
- Foreign Secretary under May 2016-2018 (resigned over Chequers Brexit plan).

### Premiership (2019-2022)
- 24 July 2019: appointed Prime Minister following May resignation.
- 12 December 2019: general election. Conservative landslide 365 seats / majority 80. "Get Brexit Done" campaign theme.
- 31 January 2020: UK formally left European Union.
- March 2020-2022: Covid-19 pandemic response.
- 24 February 2022: Russian invasion of Ukraine. UK support for Ukraine across subsequent period.

### Partygate
- Late 2021-early 2022: Partygate scandal — documented multiple Downing Street gatherings during 2020-2021 Covid lockdown periods.
- December 2021 onward: Sue Gray internal investigation; Metropolitan Police Operation Hillman criminal investigation.
- April 2022: Johnson, Sunak (then Chancellor), and Carrie Johnson among 83 individuals issued 126 fixed penalty notices for breaches of Covid regulations. Johnson became first sitting British Prime Minister found to have broken the law in office.
- May 2022: full Sue Gray report published.
- June 2022: Confidence vote in Johnson's leadership — Johnson won 211 to 148 but the 41% backbench rebellion was politically fatal.

### Chris Pincher scandal and resignation
- June-July 2022: subsequent Chris Pincher (Conservative MP and Deputy Chief Whip) scandal — Pincher accused of sexually inappropriate conduct; Johnson's multiple inconsistent public statements about what he had known.
- 5 July 2022: Rishi Sunak and Sajid Javid resigned from cabinet within minutes of each other. 60 government resignations followed across 5-7 July.
- 7 July 2022: Johnson announced resignation as Conservative Party leader.
- 6 September 2022: Johnson left office.

## Liz Truss (September-October 2022)

### Biography
- Born 26 July 1975 in Oxford. Father John Kenneth Truss (mathematics professor), mother Priscilla Mary Truss (nurse and teacher).
- Educated at Roundhay School Leeds (state comprehensive) and Merton College, Oxford (PPE).
- President of Oxford University Liberal Democrats; joined Conservative Party 1996.
- Royal Dutch Shell 1996-2000; Cable & Wireless 2000-2005.
- Deputy director of Reform think tank 2008-2010.
- MP for South West Norfolk 2010-2024.
- Cabinet positions under Cameron, May, Johnson: Lord Chancellor and Secretary of State for Justice (first woman in the thousand-year history of the office); Chief Secretary to the Treasury; Secretary of State for International Trade; Foreign Secretary 2021-2022.

### Premiership (50 days)
- 5 September 2022: defeated Sunak in Conservative members' leadership ballot 57.4% to 42.6%.
- 6 September 2022: appointed Prime Minister by Queen Elizabeth II at Balmoral (the final Prime Ministerial appointment of the Queen's reign).
- 8 September 2022: Queen Elizabeth II died at Balmoral. 10-day national mourning period.
- 23 September 2022: Kwasi Kwarteng mini-Budget — £45 billion in unfunded tax cuts including abolition of 45% top rate of income tax; expansion of borrowing.
- 26-28 September 2022: sterling and gilt-market crash. Pound fell to $1.0327 against dollar (post-1985 low). 30-year gilt yield rose from 3.6% to 5.1% in three days. Bank of England intervened with £65 billion emergency gilt-purchase programme to prevent subsequent pension-fund collapse.
- 3 October 2022: partial U-turn on 45% top-rate abolition.
- 14 October 2022: Truss dismissed Kwarteng. Jeremy Hunt appointed Chancellor.
- 17 October 2022: Hunt reversed most of the remaining mini-Budget measures.
- October 2022: Truss approval rating -47% per Observer/Opinium polling — worse than Johnson Partygate-period low.
- 19 October 2022: Suella Braverman resigned as Home Secretary.
- 20 October 2022: Truss announced resignation as Conservative leader. Daily Star livestream of head of lettuce had begun seven days earlier inviting viewers to speculate whether Truss would resign before the lettuce wilted; Truss resigned first.
- 25 October 2022: Truss left office. Shortest-serving Prime Minister in British history (surpassing George Canning's 119 days in 1827).

## Rishi Sunak (2022-2024)

### Biography
- Born 12 May 1980 in Southampton. Father Yashvir Sunak (NHS GP), mother Usha Sunak (pharmacist). British-born to Indian-origin parents who had migrated from East Africa.
- Educated at Stroud School, Winchester College (head boy), and Lincoln College, Oxford (PPE; first-class).
- MBA Stanford Graduate School of Business 2006 (Fulbright Scholar).
- Goldman Sachs 2001-2004; The Children's Investment Fund Management 2006-2009; Theleme Partners 2010-2013 (co-founder).
- Married Akshata Murty 2009 (daughter of Narayana Murthy, Infosys co-founder; estimated Murty family wealth £730 million in 2022).
- 2022 controversy over Akshata Murty's non-domiciled tax status (saved estimated £20 million in UK tax across the subsequent period).
- MP for Richmond (Yorks) 2015-2024.
- Brexit supporter in 2016 referendum.
- Chief Secretary to the Treasury 2019-2020 under Johnson.
- Chancellor of the Exchequer 2020-2022 (furlough scheme, Eat Out to Help Out, Covid-period emergency spending).

### Partygate fixed penalty notice
- April 2022: issued fixed penalty notice for 19 June 2020 Johnson birthday gathering.

### Premiership (2022-2024)
- 5 July 2022: resigned as Chancellor amid Pincher scandal.
- 24 October 2022: won Conservative leadership uncontested after Boris Johnson withdrew and Penny Mordaunt failed to reach threshold.
- 25 October 2022: appointed Prime Minister by King Charles III (first British Prime Minister of Asian descent; first Hindu Prime Minister; youngest Prime Minister in over 200 years).
- 2023: Windsor Framework with EU on Northern Ireland Protocol (March 2023).
- 22 May 2024: called general election for 4 July 2024.
- 6 June 2024: D-Day 80th anniversary commemoration. Sunak left early to do ITV interview; widely criticised by veterans.
- 4 July 2024: Conservative defeat. 121 seats (-251); worst Conservative election result since 1832 Reform Act.
- 5 July 2024: left office.
- 5 November 2024: stepped down as Conservative leader.

## Kemi Badenoch (November 2024 onward)

### Biography
- Born Olukemi Olufunto Adegoke on 2 January 1980 in Wimbledon, London at St Teresa's Maternity Hospital. Mother Feyi Adubifa (Nigerian, travelled to UK for medical treatment).
- Spent childhood ages 5-16 in Lagos, Nigeria.
- Returned to UK aged 16 to live with family. Worked at McDonald's during sixth form.
- Educated at Phoenix College Morden (state school) and Guildford County School (state comprehensive).
- BEng Computer Systems Engineering University of Sussex.
- Part-time LLB Birkbeck University of London.
- Software engineer at Logica; subsequently RBS / Coutts.
- Married Hamish Badenoch 2012 (former Conservative parliamentary candidate; works in financial services).
- London Assembly member 2015-2017 (replacing Victoria Borwick).
- MP for Saffron Walden 2017-2024; for North West Essex 2024-.

### Cabinet positions
- Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Children and Families 2019-2020 (under Johnson).
- Exchequer Secretary to the Treasury and Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Equalities 2020-2021.
- Minister of State for Local Government, Faith and Communities 2021-2022.
- Minister of State for Equalities 2021-2022.
- Secretary of State for International Trade 2022 (under Truss).
- President of the Board of Trade 2022-2024.
- Secretary of State for Business and Trade 2023-2024 (under Sunak; merged Department for International Trade with Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy).
- Minister for Women and Equalities 2022-2024.

### Conservative leadership
- 2 November 2024: defeated Robert Jenrick in Conservative Party membership ballot 53,806 to 41,388 (56.5% to 43.5%).
- First Black leader of a major UK political party.
- Fourth female Conservative Party leader (after Thatcher, May, Truss).
- 2024-2026 Conservative opposition positioning: right-of-centre on immigration, transgender rights, climate-policy scepticism; pro-Israel-and-Gaza-military-operation position; pro-AUKUS defence positioning; anti-ECHR-overreach legal positioning.
- May 2026: BBC reporting that no leadership pressure on Badenoch despite Conservative local-election losses.

## Cumulative 2010-2024 record on living standards and inequality

### Wages and living standards
- 2010-2024: worst period for UK real wage growth since the Napoleonic Wars per Resolution Foundation analysis.
- Average UK weekly earnings in real terms in 2024 similar to 2008 pre-financial-crisis level.
- Pensioner real-terms weekly income increase 2010-2020: £12 per week (3.8% increase across the decade per Resolution Foundation analysis).

### Public sector employment
- Over 500,000 public sector employees made redundant 2010-2015 per Museum of the Prime Minister documentation.

### Welfare cuts
- 2010-2019: over £30 billion in cuts to welfare payments, housing subsidies, and social services per ONS / Joseph Rowntree Foundation analysis.

### Brexit
- 23 June 2016: 52%-48% Leave vote.
- 31 January 2020: UK formal exit from EU.
- 31 December 2020: end of Brexit transition period.
- Approximate cumulative GDP impact of Brexit per OBR / Bank of England / CER analysis: 4-5% reduction in UK GDP relative to counterfactual EU-membership scenario by 2024.

## General references
- Wikipedia "David Cameron"; "Theresa May"; "Boris Johnson"; "Liz Truss"; "Rishi Sunak"; "Kemi Badenoch"; "Premiership of Liz Truss"; "United Kingdom government austerity programme".
- Britannica "Kemi Badenoch".
- LSE Inequalities, "The cost of austerity: How UK public spending cuts led to 190,000 excess deaths" (Berman & Hovland, 19 June 2024).
- IPPR, "Ending the Blame Game: The case for a new approach to public health and prevention" (June 2019).
- BMJ Open, "Effects of health and social care spending constraints on mortality in England" (Watkins et al., November 2017).
- Marmot Review, "Health Equity in England: The Marmot Review 10 Years On" (Institute of Health Equity, February 2020).
- Political Quarterly, "From May to Sunak: The Failure of the Brexit Takeovers 2016-2024" (Ben Worthy, 2024).
- The Conversation, "Austerity led to twice as many excess UK deaths as previously thought" (April 2025).
- The Conversation, "Prime ministers have always faced political scandals — so why can't they weather them now?" (April 2026).
- BBC News, Reuters, AP, NPR, CBS News, NBC News reporting on the November 2024 Conservative leadership election.
- Resolution Foundation, multiple wages-and-inequality reports 2010-2024.
- Office for National Statistics life-expectancy data 2010-2024.

# Sources — Dossier 20: The European Political Class

## Friedrich Merz (German Chancellor from May 2025)

### Biography
- Born Friedrich Merz on 11 November 1955 in Brilon, North Rhine-Westphalia.
- Joined Young Union (CDU youth wing) in 1972.
- Law studies completed 1985.
- Career as judge and corporate lawyer 1985-1989.
- Elected to European Parliament 1989-1994.
- Elected to Bundestag 1994-2009.
- Chairman of CDU/CSU parliamentary group in Bundestag from February 2000 to September 2002 (succeeded Wolfgang Schäuble during CDU donations affair; ceded position to Angela Merkel in 2002).
- Married Charlotte Merz (judge) — three adult children.
- Licensed private pilot; owns two aircraft.

### 2009-2018 corporate period
- 2009: Withdrew from active politics following decades-long rivalry with Angela Merkel.
- 2009-2021: Corporate lawyer at Mayer Brown LLP. Held counsel position.
- **2016-March 2020**: Chairman of Supervisory Board of BlackRock Asset Management Deutschland AG (German branch of BlackRock Inc, world's largest asset manager).
- Concurrent positions: HSBC Trinkaus & Burkhardt board; EY Germany supervisory board; Borussia Dortmund supervisory board; Deutsche Börse supervisory board.

### 2018-2021 leadership attempts
- 2018: Announced return to politics after Merkel's October 2018 announcement she would step down as CDU leader.
- December 2018: Defeated narrowly by Annegret Kramp-Karrenbauer in CDU leadership election.
- January 2021: Defeated by Armin Laschet in CDU leadership election.
- 2021: Returned to Bundestag as CDU MP.

### CDU leadership and 2025 Chancellorship
- 16 December 2021: Won CDU leadership election (first general party-membership ballot in CDU history).
- 22 January 2022: Formally elected CDU Chairman by congress delegates (94.6% of valid votes).
- 31 January 2022: Officially took office as party leader.
- February 2022-May 2025: Leader of the Opposition in the Bundestag.
- November 2024: Scholz "traffic light" coalition collapsed.
- 23 February 2025: Federal election. CDU/CSU won 28.6% of vote and 208 Bundestag seats. AfD second with 152 seats. SPD third with 120 seats.
- 5 May 2025: CDU/CSU-SPD coalition agreement signed.
- 5 March 2025: Merz proposed debt-brake reform exempting defence spending above 1% of GDP from debt-brake limits. Reform passed amending Basic Law Paragraphs 109, 115, 143h.
- 6 May 2025: Elected Chancellor in second-round Bundestag vote (first-round defeat was rare in modern German political history).
- Oldest post-war German Chancellor at age 69.
- "My absolute priority will be to strengthen Europe as quickly as possible so that, step by step, we can really achieve independence from the USA." (post-election statement, February 2025)
- January 2025: Non-binding anti-immigration motion passed in Bundestag with AfD votes — first time since 1949 a Bundestag majority was achieved with AfD support. Merz subsequently insisted he would "never" work with AfD.

## Giorgia Meloni (Italian Prime Minister from October 2022)

### Biography
- Born Giorgia Meloni on 15 January 1977 in Rome.
- Father Francesco Meloni (tax adviser) left family when Giorgia was a child.
- Mother Anna Paratore raised Giorgia and sister Arianna (born 1975).
- Raised in Garbatella district of Rome (working-class neighbourhood) after the family's more affluent home was destroyed by fire shortly after father's departure.
- No university degree (rare among contemporary European Prime Ministers).

### Political career
- 1992 (aged 15): Joined Youth Front (Fronte della Gioventù), youth wing of Italian Social Movement (Movimento Sociale Italiano, MSI) — the post-1946 successor party to Mussolini's Fascist Party founded by Mussolini supporters.
- 1996 (aged 19): On French television: "Mussolini was a good politician, in that everything he did, he did for Italy."
- 1994: MSI rebranded as National Alliance (Alleanza Nazionale). Meloni became national leader of Student Action (Azione Studentesca).
- 1998: Elected to Province of Rome council.
- 2006: Elected to Chamber of Deputies (aged 29).
- 2008-2011: Minister of Youth in Berlusconi IV government. Youngest cabinet minister in Italian Republic history at age 31.
- 2012: Co-founded Brothers of Italy (Fratelli d'Italia, FdI) with Ignazio La Russa and Guido Crosetto. Party retained the tricolour-flame logo of MSI.
- 2014: Elected President of Brothers of Italy.
- 2019: Brothers of Italy won 6.4% in European Parliament election. Meloni's "I am Giorgia. I am a woman, I am a mother, I am Italian, I am Christian. And you can't take that away from me!" Rome rally speech went viral.
- 2020-2025: President of European Conservatives and Reformists Party (ECR).
- February 2022: Addressed Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) in Florida.
- May 2022: At Vox rally in Marbella, Spain: "Yes to the natural family, no to the LGBT lobby... no to gender ideology... no to the bureaucrats of Brussels!"

### 2022 election and government
- July 2022: Mario Draghi national unity government collapsed.
- 25 September 2022: Italian general election. Brothers of Italy won 26% of vote — largest single-party result. Centre-right coalition (Brothers of Italy + Salvini's Lega + Berlusconi's Forza Italia) won absolute majority in both Chamber of Deputies and Senate.
- 22 October 2022: Sworn in as Prime Minister by President Sergio Mattarella — first female Italian Prime Minister; head of Brothers of Italy as first party in any post-1945 Italian government with institutional link to Mussolini's Fascist Party.
- 68th government of the Italian Republic since 1945.
- Sister Arianna Meloni appointed head of party political secretariat 2023; partnered with Francesco Lollobrigida (Minister of Agriculture since 22 October 2022).
- Partner Andrea Giambruno (journalist) separated October 2023 following undeclared on-air remarks. One daughter Ginevra born September 2016.

### 2022-2026 government period
- second-longest government in Italian Republic history.
- 48% approval rating at end of 2022 per multiple polls.
- 2022-2024 Ukraine support including weapons shipments and coordinated EU sanctions enforcement.
- 2023-2024 subsequent maritime-and-migration operations in central Mediterranean.
- 2024-2026 pro-Israel positioning during Gaza period (Meloni's subsequent public characterisations of the Gaza-period conflict have followed post-2023 European centre-right consensus).
- 8 May 2026: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Meloni in Rome to ease frictions over Iran war.

## Donald Tusk (Polish Prime Minister 2007-2014 and 2023 onward)

### Biography
- Born Donald Franciszek Tusk on 22 April 1957 in Gdańsk, Poland. Family of mixed Kashubian-Polish-German background (grandparents had complicated WWII history including conscription into Wehrmacht).
- Educated at University of Gdańsk (History).
- Co-founder of Liberal Democratic Congress (KLD) party 1990.
- Member of Sejm 1991-1993 (KLD), 1997-2001 (Freedom Union), 2001-2014 and 2023- (Civic Platform).
- Co-founded Civic Platform (Platforma Obywatelska, PO) in 2001 with Andrzej Olechowski and Maciej Płażyński.

### First Prime Ministership (2007-2014)
- Civic Platform won 41.5% in October 2007 parliamentary election.
- 16 November 2007: Sworn in as Prime Minister.
- 2011: Re-elected — first Polish PM re-elected since fall of communism in 1989.
- 22 September 2014: Resigned to take up European Council Presidency.

### European Council President (2014-2019)
- 1 December 2014 - 30 November 2019: President of the European Council.
- Re-elected for second term March 2017 despite opposition from PiS-led Polish government.
- 2019-2022: President of European People's Party (EPP).

### Return to Polish politics (2021-)
- July 2021: Returned to Warsaw as Civic Platform leader.
- 2023: Organised anti-government protests including 4 June 2023 demonstration (300,000-500,000 turnout) and 1 October 2023 "March of a Million Hearts."
- 15 October 2023: Polish parliamentary election. PiS won most votes (35.4%) but lost majority. Civic Coalition (Tusk) 30.7%; Third Way 14.4%; New Left 8.6%. Combined opposition vote 54%.
- 27 November 2023: PiS Mateusz Morawiecki cabinet sworn in but lost confidence vote 6 December 2023.
- 11 December 2023: Tusk elected Prime Minister by Sejm. Longest-serving Prime Minister of the Third Polish Republic.
- 1 January 2025: Poland assumed EU Council presidency.
- 2024-2026: subsequent tensions with PiS-aligned President Andrzej Duda over subsequent judicial-reform legislation.
- June 2025: PiS-aligned candidate Karol Nawrocki won Polish presidential election — replacing Duda but maintaining presidential veto blocking Tusk legislative programme.

## Viktor Orbán (Hungarian Prime Minister 1998-2002 and 2010 onward)

### Biography
- Born Viktor Mihály Orbán on 31 May 1963 in Székesfehérvár, Hungary.
- ELTE Faculty of Law, Budapest 1987.
- 1989 Soros Foundation scholarship to Pembroke College, Oxford.
- Co-founder of Fidesz (Federation of Young Democrats) 1988.

### First Prime Ministership (1998-2002)
- May 1998: Fidesz won parliamentary election. Orbán sworn in as Prime Minister at age 35 — youngest in Hungarian history.
- 2002: Lost election to Hungarian Socialist Party.

### Return and continuous tenure from 2010
- 25 April 2010: Fidesz won 53% of vote and constitutional two-thirds majority in parliamentary election.
- 29 May 2010: Sworn in as Prime Minister.
- 2011: New Hungarian Constitution adopted by Fidesz two-thirds majority.
- 2014, 2018, 2022 parliamentary elections: Fidesz retained two-thirds majorities.
- longest-serving EU government head of state.
- subsequent 16-year continuous tenure as Prime Minister (29 May 2010 to present).
- 2026 Hungarian parliamentary election scheduled.

### Governing record
- subsequent "illiberal democracy" public characterisation by Orbán since 2014.
- subsequent multi-year European Commission Article 7 proceedings over rule-of-law concerns.
- subsequent 2020-2024 Hungarian EU funds withheld (€30 billion) over rule-of-law conditionality.
- subsequent central subsequent European-political-class opposition figure for Tusk and von der Leyen.
- 2025-2026: subsequent Péter Magyar's subsequent Tisza Party emerged as subsequent credible opposition challenger.

## Ursula von der Leyen (European Commission President from 2019)

### Biography
- Born Ursula Gertrud Albrecht on 8 October 1958 in Ixelles, Brussels (during her father Ernst Albrecht's EEC tenure).
- Father Ernst Albrecht (Minister-President of Lower Saxony 1976-1990).
- Mother Heidi Adele Stromeyer.
- Educated at Göttingen, London School of Economics, Münster, Hanover Medical School (MD 1991).
- Career as gynaecologist 1987-1996.
- Married Heiko von der Leyen 1986. Seven children.

### Pre-Commission career
- CDU member from 1990.
- Lower Saxony government 2003-2005.
- Federal Minister for Family Affairs 2005-2009 (under Merkel).
- Federal Minister of Labour 2009-2013.
- Federal Minister of Defence 2013-2019 — first female German Defence Minister.

### First Commission term (2019-2024)
- 16 July 2019: Elected President of the European Commission by European Parliament (383 votes for, narrow 9-vote margin).
- 1 December 2019: Took office.
- 2020 Covid pandemic response including European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control coordination.
- 2020-2021 Covid vaccine procurement (subject of subsequent controversy over text-message exchanges with Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla and subsequent refusal to disclose).
- 2022 coordinated EU sanctions on Russia following February 2022 Ukraine invasion.
- 2023-2024 subsequent public characterisation of subsequent Gaza-period conflict — subsequent pro-Israel positioning that produced internal subsequent EU diplomatic tensions.

### Second term (2024-2029)
- 27 June 2024: Re-nominated by European Council. Orbán voted against; Meloni abstained.
- 18 July 2024: Re-elected by European Parliament 401-284-22 (53.7% of votes cast).
- 27 November 2024: Second von der Leyen Commission approved by Parliament 370-282-36 (51% of all members) — the least-supported European Commission by Parliament since 1993.
- 1 December 2024: Second term took office.
- Second-term college: 27 commissioners; 6 Executive Vice-Presidents; 40.7% women (down from 44.4% in first term).

## Mark Rutte (NATO Secretary General from October 2024)

### Biography
- Born Mark Rutte on 14 February 1967 in The Hague.
- Educated at Leiden University (History, 1992).
- Career at Unilever 1992-2002 (HR director).
- VVD (People's Party for Freedom and Democracy) member from 1989.

### Dutch Prime Ministership (2010-2024)
- Member of House of Representatives 2003-2010 and 2010-2024.
- 14 October 2010: Sworn in as Prime Minister (first VVD PM since 1918).
- multi-cabinet tenure across four governments (Rutte I 2010-2012; II 2012-2017; III 2017-2022; IV 2022-2024).
- longest-serving Prime Minister of the Netherlands in history.
- July 2023: Fourth Rutte cabinet collapsed over migration policy disagreement.
- 22 November 2023: Geert Wilders's PVV won most Dutch parliamentary election seats.
- 2 July 2024: Rutte stepped down as Prime Minister, succeeded by Dick Schoof.

### NATO Secretary General
- 26 June 2024: Designated as NATO Secretary General-designate following multi-month subsequent US-and-European coordination.
- 1 October 2024: Took office as 14th NATO Secretary General, succeeding Jens Stoltenberg (2014-2024).
- 2024-2026 subsequent NATO-EU coordination including 29 October 2024 joint task force with von der Leyen on subsequent NATO-EU partnership.
- subsequent coordination of NATO response to continuing Russia-Ukraine war and subsequent Trump-administration NATO-policy position.

## Antonio Costa (European Council President from December 2024)

### Biography
- Born António Luís Santos da Costa on 17 July 1961 in Lisbon.
- Father Orlando da Costa (Indo-Portuguese writer and Communist Party member).
- Mother Maria Antónia Palla (journalist).
- Educated at University of Lisbon (Law, 1984).
- Lisbon Bar 1985.
- Socialist Party (PS) member from 1975.

### Portuguese political career
- Member of Assembly of the Republic 1991-2002 and 2005-2007.
- Minister of Parliamentary Affairs 1995-1997; Minister of Justice 1999-2002; Minister of State and Internal Administration 2005-2007 under António Guterres / José Sócrates.
- Member of European Parliament 2004-2005.
- Mayor of Lisbon 2007-2015.
- Secretary-General of the Socialist Party 2014-2024.
- 26 November 2015 - 7 April 2024: Prime Minister of Portugal.
- 7 November 2023: Resigned amid criminal investigation (subsequently established as based on subsequent identification confusion between Costa and subsequent António Costa Silva; no charges).
- 25 June 2024: Designated by European Council as next President of the European Council.
- 1 December 2024: Took office as President of the European Council (2.5-year term).

## Roberta Metsola (European Parliament President from 2022)

### Biography
- Born Roberta Tedesco Triccas on 18 January 1979 in Malta.
- Educated at University of Malta (Law) and College of Europe Bruges (European Law).
- Married Ukko Metsola (Finnish national; former MEP).
- Member of Nationalist Party (Maltese centre-right).

### European Parliament career
- Member of European Parliament 2013- (Nationalist Party / European People's Party).
- Member of Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Committee.
- First Vice-President of European Parliament 2020-2022.
- 11 January 2022: Acting President following David Sassoli's death.
- 18 January 2022: Elected President of European Parliament (458 votes / 56% of cast votes). Third woman to hold the office.
- 16 July 2024: Re-elected for second term (562 votes / 78% of cast votes).
- 2024-2026: subsequent pro-Israel-and-Gaza-military-operation positioning that produced subsequent internal European Parliament tensions.

# Sources — Dossier 21: Narendra Modi

## Biography

- Born Narendra Damodardas Modi on 17 September 1950 in Vadnagar, Mehsana district, Bombay State (present-day Gujarat).
- Father Damodardas Modi (ran a tea stall at Vadnagar railway station per family characterisation; this characterisation has been contested as not corroborated by independent sources).
- Mother Heeraben Modi (1923-2022).
- Gujarati family of Ghanchi caste, classified as Other Backward Class (OBC).
- Four brothers, one sister.
- At age 18 (1968) married Jashodaben Chimanlal Modi in arranged marriage. Modi abandoned her shortly after the marriage. He did not disclose the marriage publicly until 2014 when he filed his nomination papers for the Vadodara Lok Sabha seat.
- 1967: completed higher secondary education at Vadnagar.
- Bachelor's degree in Political Science from University of Delhi School of Open Learning (correspondence) — claimed in official biography; degree authenticity disputed in subsequent multi-party legal challenges including the 2016 Delhi University admission of application complications.
- 1983: Master's degree in Political Science from Gujarat University (external).

## RSS and BJP career

- Age 8: introduced to RSS through Vadnagar shakha; met Lakshmanrao Inamdar (subsequent political mentor).
- 1967-1971: left parental home; travelled in northern India visiting religious centres including Ramakrishna Mission in Almora and Belur Math near Calcutta; details largely uncorroborated.
- 1971: returned to Gujarat and became full-time pracharak (campaigner) for RSS.
- June 1975 - March 1977: went into hiding during the Indira Gandhi Emergency; reportedly distributed anti-Emergency literature in disguise.
- 1985: assigned by RSS to the BJP.
- 1987: helped organise BJP's Ahmedabad municipal election campaign.
- 1987: elected organising secretary of BJP's Gujarat unit.
- 1990: co-organiser of L.K. Advani's Somnath-to-Ayodhya rath yatra.
- 1991: co-organiser of Murli Manohar Joshi's Ekta Yatra from Kanyakumari to Kashmir.
- 1995: appointed secretary of the BJP's national organisation in New Delhi.
- 1998: appointed general secretary of BJP national organisation.

## Gujarat Chief Minister 2001-2014

### Appointment and the 2002 pogroms
- 26 January 2001: Bhuj earthquake in Gujarat killed 20,000 people.
- 3 October 2001: Modi replaced Keshubhai Patel as Chief Minister of Gujarat following Patel's poor public-image performance after the earthquake response.
- 7 October 2001: sworn in as Chief Minister (his first elected office).
- 24 February 2002: won by-election in Rajkot II constituency (his first electoral victory).
- 27 February 2002: Sabarmati Express fire at Godhra killed 59 Hindu pilgrims returning from Ayodhya.
- 28 February - early May 2002: anti-Muslim pogroms across Gujarat.
- Casualty figures: 1,044 dead per official record (three-quarters Muslim); independent estimates 2,000 (mostly Muslim) per multiple human-rights-organisation reports.
- 230,000 displaced.
- 2002 US State Department report; 2005 US visa denial to Modi under International Religious Freedom Act.
- 2008: Special Investigation Team (SIT) appointed by Indian Supreme Court.
- 2012: SIT gave Modi "clean chit" finding no prosecutable evidence.
- 2022: Indian Supreme Court rejected Zakia Jafri petition against SIT clean chit.
- 2014: US visa to Modi subsequent restored.

### Subsequent terms and policy record
- December 2002: won state assembly election with 127 of 182 seats.
- December 2007: won state assembly election with 117 seats.
- December 2012: won state assembly election with 115 seats.
- Total tenure as Gujarat Chief Minister: 7 October 2001 to 22 May 2014 — 4,612 days (the longest single tenure as Gujarat CM).
- 2007 Vibrant Gujarat investment summit: reported real-estate investment deals worth ₹6.6 trillion ($210 billion in 2023 equivalent).
- Gujarat agricultural growth rate 9.6% per annum 2001-2007.
- subsequent 2001-2010 compound annual growth rate of 10.97% — highest of any Indian state.
- subsequent 2008 500,000 subsequent infrastructure structures including 113,738 check dams.
- subsequent Gujarat became largest producer of Bt cotton in India.
- 2012 subsequent CAG (Comptroller and Auditor General) accusation that Modi government gave subsequent low-cost fuel from Gujarat state-run gas company to Adani and subsequent other businesspeople.

## Prime Ministerial elections

### 2014 election
- 16 May 2014: BJP won 282 seats / 31.0% of vote (first single-party majority since 1984).
- 26 May 2014: Modi sworn in as 14th PM.

### 2019 election
- 23 May 2019: BJP won 303 seats / 37.4% of vote.
- 30 May 2019: sworn in for second term.

### 2024 election
- 4 June 2024: BJP-led NDA won 293 seats; BJP alone fell to 240 seats — loss of subsequent parliamentary majority for BJP.
- coalition support from Telugu Desam Party (Naidu) and Janata Dal United (Nitish Kumar).
- 9 June 2024: sworn in for third term.

## Adani relationship

### Origins
- Gautam Adani (born 24 June 1962, Ahmedabad) and Modi developed close relationship during 2001-2014 Gujarat CM period.
- 2002 Gujarat pogroms: Adani was one of few Indian businessmen to publicly defend Modi against subsequent accusations.
- 2003 Resurgent Group of Gujarat (RGG) founded by Adani as subsequent rival to Confederation of Indian Industry after CII leaders criticised Modi's 2002 management of pogroms.

### Mundra Port
- 1994: Government of Gujarat announced subsequent managerial outsourcing of Mundra Port.
- 1995: Adani obtained contract.
- 1995: first jetty established.
- Mundra Port has become largest private multi-port operator in India with capacity of 210 million tons of cargo per annum.

### Cumulative Adani Group expansion under Modi government
- 2014-2024: Adani Group expansion across ports (Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Limited / APSEZ), power (Adani Power), green energy (Adani Green Energy), gas (Adani Total Gas), enterprise (Adani Enterprises), transmission (Adani Transmission), and subsequent renewables.
- May 2020: $6 billion subsequent largest solar bid won from Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI).
- August 2022: Adani became Asia's richest person, surpassing Mukesh Ambani.
- 2022: subsequent NDTV acquisition by Adani Group's AMG Media Networks Limited.
- subsequent 2022-2024 Adani Group subsequent expansion across Australia (Carmichael coal mine), Bangladesh (Godda thermal power), Greece, Indonesia, Israel (Haifa port), Kenya (Jomo Kenyatta International Airport project — subsequent cancelled following November 2024 indictment), Myanmar (Yangon port project), Nepal, Sri Lanka ($440 million wind power project — subsequent reconsidered under Dissanayake NPP government), and Tanzania (Bagamoyo and Dar es Salaam port concessions).

### Hindenburg report and subsequent investigation
- 24 January 2023: Hindenburg Research published 100-page subsequent report alleging "the largest con in corporate history" by Adani Group — subsequent stock manipulation, accounting fraud, and shell-company architecture.
- subsequent 2023 subsequent $125 billion in market capitalisation wiped from Adani Group across 2023.
- January 2024: Indian Supreme Court resolved Adani Group stocks dispute arising from Hindenburg allegations and dismissed requests for SIT or CBI investigation.

### US criminal indictment
- 20 November 2024: five-count criminal indictment by US Attorney's Office in Brooklyn, New York charged Gautam Adani, nephew Sagar Adani, and associates with five criminal counts of bribery and subsequent conspiracy to violate the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), conspiracy to commit securities fraud, and obstruction of justice.
- Allegation: Adani paid over $250 million in bribes to Indian government officials to secure favourable energy contracts in Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jammu and Kashmir, and Tamil Nadu.
- subsequent SEC parallel civil enforcement action.
- subsequent $12 billion drop in Adani's net worth following indictment.
- 14 May 2026: New York Times reported US Department of Justice subsequent preparing to drop criminal charges following subsequent representations by Robert J. Giuffra Jr (Sullivan & Cromwell partner / Trump personal lawyer) and subsequent pledge by Adani after Trump's 2024 election victory to invest $10 billion in US economy and create 15,000 US jobs.
- subsequent expected dismissal "with prejudice."
- 21 January 2026: SEC asked court for help in serving summonses on Gautam Adani and Sagar Adani; Adanis agreed to receive legal notice with 90 days to respond.

## Modi government policy record

### Demonetisation
- 8 November 2016: subsequent demonetisation of ₹500 and ₹1,000 currency notes (86% of currency in circulation by value).
- subsequent 99.3% of demonetised currency was deposited back into banks per 2018 Reserve Bank of India annual report — contradicting subsequent original justification of subsequent unaccounted-cash detection.
- subsequent estimated 1.5%-2% GDP contraction in subsequent immediate post-demonetisation period.
- subsequent small-business and subsequent agricultural-sector disruption.

### Goods and Services Tax (GST)
- 1 July 2017: nationwide implementation of Goods and Services Tax (GST) under 101st Constitutional Amendment.
- subsequent multi-month transition disruption to MSME sector.

### Article 370 abrogation
- 5 August 2019: abrogation of special-autonomy status of Jammu and Kashmir under Article 370 of Indian Constitution and subsequent bifurcation into two Union Territories.
- 11 December 2023: Indian Supreme Court upheld abrogation.

### CAA-NRC
- 11-12 December 2019: Citizenship Amendment Act passed Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha; presidential assent.
- subsequent December 2019 - March 2020 anti-CAA-and-NRC protests.
- subsequent 23-26 February 2020 Delhi riots — 53 deaths (an estimated 80% Muslim).

### Farm laws
- September 2020: three farm laws passed (Farmers' Produce Trade and Commerce Act; Farmers Empowerment and Protection Agreement on Price Assurance Act; Essential Commodities Amendment Act).
- 2020-2021 farmer protests: estimated 250 million subsequent 26 November 2020 subsequent nationwide strike — subsequent largest single-day protest in human history per estimates.
- 19 November 2021: Modi subsequent public characterisation announcing repeal of farm laws.
- subsequent 29 November 2021 subsequent Farm Laws Repeal Act.

### Ram Mandir consecration
- 22 January 2024: Modi subsequent pran pratishtha (consecration) of Ram Mandir at Ayodhya — subsequent central electoral product of 2024 subsequent election campaign.

### Manipur conflict
- May 2023 onward: Manipur ethnic conflict between Meitei and Kuki-Zo communities — estimated 250+ deaths; subsequent 60,000+ displaced.
- subsequent multi-month subsequent Modi government's subsequent failure to subsequent visit Manipur and subsequent visible refusal to subsequent intervene.

## Press environment under Modi
- 2024 Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index: India ranked 159th of 180.
- 2014-2024 subsequent multi-year subsequent decline in Indian press-freedom rankings.
- 2017-2023: Newslaundry / The Wire / Caravan / Scroll / Frontline / NewsClick subsequent independent-press subsequent multi-year subsequent tax-and-CBI-and-ED subsequent raids and investigations.
- August 2022: Adani acquisition of NDTV (covered above) ended subsequent NDTV's post-1988 subsequent critical-of-government editorial position.

# Sources — Dossier 22: Amit Shah

## Biography

- Born Amit Anilchandra Shah on 22 October 1964 in Mumbai (then Bombay), Maharashtra.
- Family: Gujarati Hindu Bania caste; great-grandfather was Nagarseth (town headsman) of Mansa state in Gujarat.
- Father Anilchandra Shah: PVC pipe business based in Mansa, Gujarat.
- Mother Kusumben Shah (died 8 June 2010).
- Married Sonal Shah; one son Jay Shah (subsequent Secretary of the Board of Control for Cricket in India / BCCI from 2019 to 2024, then Chairman of the International Cricket Council from 1 December 2024); six sisters (two of whom live in Chicago).
- Education: Mehsana school; moved to Ahmedabad; Bachelor of Science (BSc) in biochemistry from CU Shah Science College, Ahmedabad. Worked in his father's PVC pipe business after graduation; also worked as a stockbroker and in cooperative banks in Ahmedabad.

## RSS and early BJP career

- Age 13 (1977): pasted election posters for Maniben Patel (daughter of Vallabhbhai Patel and Jan Sangh candidate from Mehsana Lok Sabha seat) during the Janata-wave 1977 general election.
- 1980 (age 16): joined RSS as Swayamsevak in Ahmedabad.
- 1982: joint secretary of Gujarat unit of the Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP — RSS student wing).
- 1984: BJP polling agent for Sanghvi booth in Narayanpur ward.
- 1987: joined Yuva Morcha (youth wing) of BJP.
- 1989-1990: Ahmedabad coordinator for the Ram Janmabhoomi mobilisation and the 1990 Advani Somnath-Ayodhya rath yatra.

## Gujarat political career 1995-2014

### The Modi-Shah 1995-onward Gujarat strategy
- 1995: BJP formed first government in Gujarat under Keshubhai Patel.
- 1995-onward: Modi and Shah jointly developed strategy to eliminate INC influence in rural Gujarat — identifying the second-most-influential figure in each village and recruiting them to BJP. Built network of 8,000 rural leaders.
- 1995-1999: cooperative-banks capture strategy applied across the Gujarat cooperative-banking architecture.

### Electoral record
- 1997: won first election from Sarkhej by-election to Gujarat Legislative Assembly.
- 1998: retained Sarkhej seat in Gujarat Legislative Assembly election; BJP won state.
- 1999: elected President of Ahmedabad District Cooperative Bank (ADCB) — largest cooperative bank in India.
- 1999: State Vice-President of Gujarat BJP.
- 2002, 2007: retained Sarkhej seat.
- 2012: re-elected MLA from newly-carved Naranpura constituency (his fifth straight victory).
- 2017-2019: Rajya Sabha member from Gujarat.

### Twelve Modi-government portfolios 2002-2012
- 2002: sworn in as Minister of State for Home, Law and Justice, Prison, Border Security, Civil Defence, Excise, Home Guards, Transport, Prohibition, Gram Rakshak Dal, Police Housing, and Legislative and Parliamentary Affairs in the Modi Gujarat government — twelve portfolios.
- 2006: elected President of Gujarat State Chess Association.
- 2009: appointed President of Ahmedabad Central Board of Cricket Association and Vice-President of Gujarat State Cricket Association.

### Sohrabuddin Sheikh fake-encounter case
- 26 November 2005: Sohrabuddin Sheikh and his wife Kausar Bi killed by Gujarat Anti-Terrorism Squad (ATS) and Rajasthan Police officers near Ahmedabad in reported "fake encounter."
- Sohrabuddin Sheikh: alleged extortionist operating in Rajasthan and Gujarat marble-trading industry; allegations of arms-racketeering and LeT links.
- 28 December 2006: Tulsiram Prajapati (a witness to the Sohrabuddin killing) killed by Gujarat Police in second "fake encounter" near Chapri.
- 25 July 2010: Shah arrested by CBI; charged with murder, extortion, kidnapping, criminal conspiracy, destruction of evidence; charged under Arms Act.
- October 2010: granted bail by Gujarat High Court after three months in custody. Court observed "no prima facie case against Amit Shah."
- Justice Aftab Alam (then Supreme Court justice) in special hearing barred Shah from entering Gujarat 2010-2012 (the period that subsequent BJP commentary characterised as Shah's "exile").
- September 2012: Indian Supreme Court permitted Shah to return to Gujarat.
- September 2013: D.G. Vanzara (former Gujarat ATS officer in jail at the time) wrote letter to Gujarat government accusing Modi-Shah government of ordering the fake encounters and ensuring police officers remained in prison.
- 30 December 2014: Special CBI court under Special Judge MB Gosavi gave Shah "clean chit," finding insufficient evidence to put him on trial. Subsequent court characterisation: "all these actions were politically motivated."
- During Shah's tenure as Gujarat Home Minister, 1,200 encounter cases were recorded nationally; 19 in Gujarat. The CBI/Congress UPA government selected all 19 Gujarat cases and one of the remaining 1,189 for investigation — a pattern that subsequent BJP commentary characterised as politically targeted.
- Background context: subsequent allegations by Shah's accusers in the case (including the Gujarat ATS officers later jailed) indicate that Shah operated the Gujarat ATS extrajudicial-killing apparatus as Home Minister.

## National BJP career 2013-2019

### 2014 UP election architecture
- 2013: appointed BJP National General Secretary in charge of Uttar Pradesh.
- 2014: BJP-NDA won 73 of 80 UP Lok Sabha seats — the central electoral product of the 2014 BJP national victory.

### BJP President 2014-2020
- 9 July 2014: appointed BJP National President at age 49 — youngest BJP President in the party's history.
- Under his presidency BJP-led governments formed in Maharashtra, Haryana, Jammu and Kashmir, Jharkhand, and Assam (2014-2016); in Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Manipur, and Gujarat (2017); and the 2017 Akali-BJP alliance lost in Punjab. In 2018 BJP lost power in Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan, and Madhya Pradesh.
- 24 January 2016: re-elected BJP National President.
- 2016: appointed trustee of Somnath Temple Trust (alongside Modi and Advani).
- 2014-onward: NDA Chairman.

### 2019 Lok Sabha
- 23 May 2019: BJP won 303 seats / 37.4% — second-term Modi government.
- 30 May 2019: Shah took oath as Cabinet Minister.
- 1 June 2019: took office as 35th Minister of Home Affairs.
- 17 June 2019: took oath as Member of Lok Sabha from Gandhinagar (Gujarat).
- 2019 Gandhinagar electoral margin: 557,014 votes / 70% vote share.

## Home Minister 2019-

### Article 370 abrogation
- 5 August 2019: Shah moved resolution in Rajya Sabha to scrap Article 370 of the Constitution.
- Simultaneous Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Bill bifurcated state into two Union Territories (Jammu and Kashmir; Ladakh).
- Multi-month subsequent internet shutdowns, communications blackouts, and political detentions of Mehbooba Mufti, Omar Abdullah, Farooq Abdullah, and other Kashmiri political-class operators.
- 11 December 2023: Indian Supreme Court upheld the abrogation.

### CAA-NRC architecture
- 11 December 2019: Shah moved Citizenship Amendment Bill in Lok Sabha; passed same day.
- 12 December 2019: passed Rajya Sabha; presidential assent same day.
- CAA granted accelerated citizenship to Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh — explicitly excluding Muslims.
- 19 November 2019: Shah declared in Rajya Sabha that NRC (National Register of Citizens) would be implemented nationwide. The NRC has subsequently been implemented only in Assam as of 2025 and was removed from BJP's 2024 election manifesto.
- December 2019-March 2020: nationwide anti-CAA-and-NRC protests.
- 23-26 February 2020: Delhi riots producing 53 deaths (estimated 80% Muslim) during Trump's first-term state visit. Shah's Home Ministry oversaw the Delhi Police during the riots.

### Hindi as national language
- September 2019: Shah publicly called for Hindi as unifying national language. The position generated subsequent opposition particularly in southern Indian states (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana).

### Manipur conflict
- May 2023-onward: Manipur ethnic conflict between Meitei and Kuki-Zo communities. Shah visited Manipur in late May 2023 (one of the few Union government visits during the multi-year conflict). Estimated 250+ deaths and 60,000+ displaced.

### 2024 Lok Sabha and third-term Home Ministry
- 4 June 2024: NDA won 293 seats; BJP alone fell to 240.
- 9 June 2024: Modi sworn in for third term; Shah retained Home Ministry.

## Canadian allegations on Sikh-separatist plots

### Hardeep Singh Nijjar killing
- 18 June 2023: Hardeep Singh Nijjar (45, Canadian citizen, leader of Khalistan Tiger Force, designated terrorist by Indian government) shot dead in his pickup truck outside the Sikh temple he led in Surrey, British Columbia.
- 18 September 2023: Canadian PM Justin Trudeau publicly characterised "credible allegations" of Indian government agents' involvement in Nijjar killing.
- May 2024: four Indian nationals living in Canada charged with Nijjar's murder; awaiting trial as of late 2024.

### Canadian government allegation on Shah
- 14 October 2024: Canada expelled six Indian diplomats (including the Indian High Commissioner) linking them to multiple cases of coercion, intimidation, and violence directed at Sikh separatists in Canada. India ordered reciprocal expulsion of Canadian diplomats.
- 29 October 2024: The Washington Post reported Canadian official allegation that Amit Shah was behind the campaign of violence and intimidation targeting Sikh separatists in Canada.
- 29 October 2024: Canadian Deputy Foreign Affairs Minister David Morrison confirmed to the Canadian Parliament national security committee that he had confirmed Shah's name to The Washington Post: "The journalist called me and asked if it was that person. I confirmed it was that person."
- RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme and Royal Canadian Mounted Police Deputy Commissioner Brigitte Gauvin (intelligence) gave parallel testimony connecting Indian government operations in Canada to the Bishnoi gang and its founder Lawrence Bishnoi (then imprisoned in India).
- Indian government rejected Canadian allegations as "baseless" and "absurd."

### US parallel — Pannun assassination plot
- November 2023: US Department of Justice indicted Nikhil Gupta (Indian government employee) on charges of plotting to kill Gurpatwant Singh Pannun (Sikh-separatism advocate / Sikhs for Justice) in New York.
- The "Indian government employee" identified in subsequent US filings as Vikash Yadav (former Research and Analysis Wing / RAW officer).
- October 2024: US grand-jury indictment named Yadav.

## Other documented elements

### Cricket and sport
- Son Jay Shah served as BCCI Secretary 2019-2024; subsequently elected Chairman of the International Cricket Council from 1 December 2024 (the post in world cricket governance).
- Subsequent governance complaints about the consolidation of Indian cricket administration under the Shah family.

### Domestic surveillance and Pegasus
- 2021: subsequent allegations regarding Pegasus spyware deployment by Indian government surveillance apparatus against Indian opposition politicians, journalists, and activists. Shah's Home Ministry has subsequently rejected parliamentary questioning on the topic.

### Electoral delimitation
- Shah has been accused of manipulating electoral constituency delimitation in Gujarat (2008 delimitation exercise) to favour BJP. Sustained allegation across multiple subsequent state-level delimitation exercises.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Amit Shah"; "Sohrabuddin Sheikh fake encounter case"; "Killing of Hardeep Singh Nijjar"; "Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019"; "Article 370."
- Britannica "Amit Shah."
- Official bjp.org "Shri Amit Shah."
- Official amitshah.co.in.

- The Guardian, "He likes scaring people: how Modi's right-hand man, Amit Shah, runs India" (16 May 2024).
- CNN, "Canada alleges Indian minister Amit Shah ordered campaign targeting Sikh separatists" (30 October 2024).
- CBC, "Who is Amit Shah, the Indian minister accused of criminal plots in Canada?" (30 October 2024).
- Washington Post initial reporting (29 October 2024).
- The Print, "What was Amit Shah like as home minister of Gujarat?" (June 2019 book excerpt from Anirban Ganguly and Shiwanand Dwivedi *Amit Shah and the March of the BJP*).
- HW News English, "The Controversial Home Minister: Why Amit Shah spent 2 years in exile" (31 May 2019).
