# Sources — Dossier 23: The RSS-VHP institutional dossier (Bhagwat and the Sangh Parivar)

## The RSS

### Founding and pre-independence period
- Founded 27 September 1925 on Vijayadashami in Nagpur, Central Provinces, British India, by Keshav Baliram Hedgewar (1889-1940), a Nagpur-based physician.
- Hedgewar had been associated with the Indian National Congress in the 1920s; he had been charged with sedition in 1921 by the British administration and imprisoned for one year.
- Hedgewar was influenced by Vinayak Damodar Savarkar (1883-1966) — author of the 1923 *Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?* that produced the foundational ideological text of the Hindutva movement.
- Inspiration drawn from secret revolutionary organisations including Anushilan Samiti and Jugantar in Bengal; the Hindustan Republican Association.
- RSS broke from Congress over Congress's support for the 1919-24 Khilafat movement (a pan-Islamic campaign).

### Hedgewar period 1925-1940
- Initial objective per the founding documentation: provide character training and instil "Hindu discipline" in order to unite the Hindu community.
- The 1925-1940 RSS operated primarily through the shakha (local branch) system; daily one-hour shakha meetings combining physical drill, Hindu cultural training, and ideological instruction.
- The RSS during the colonial period: did not participate in the Indian independence movement; per Hedgewar's documented direction, scrupulously avoided any anti-British political activity.
- The "Independence Day" announced by the Indian National Congress for 26 January 1930 was celebrated by the RSS only that year and subsequently avoided.
- The Indian Tricolour was shunned in favour of the RSS bhagwa (saffron) flag.
- Hedgewar personally participated in the April 1930 Salt March but did not get the RSS involved.
- 1934: Congress passed resolution prohibiting its members from joining the RSS, the Hindu Mahasabha, or the Muslim League.
- The RSS biographer C. P. Bhishikar documented Hedgewar "talked only about Hindu organisations and avoided any direct comment on the colonial government."
- Hedgewar died 21 June 1940.

### Golwalkar period 1940-1973
- Madhav Sadashiv Golwalkar (1906-1973), "Guruji," became the second Sarsanghchalak in 1940.
- 1938 Golwalkar published *We, or Our Nationhood Defined*. Core documented passages: "The non-Hindu people in Hindustan must adopt the Hindu culture and language, must learn to respect and hold in reverence Hindu religion, must entertain no idea but those of the glorification of the Hindu race and culture..." Praise of Nazi Germany: "German race pride has become the topic of the day. To keep up the purity of the Race and its culture Germany shocked the world by purging the country of the semitic Races – the Jews. Race pride at its highest has been manifested here. Germany has shown how well nigh impossible it is for the Races and cultures having differences going to the roots, to be united into one united whole, a good lesson for us in Hindustan to learn and profit by."
- 1939 published *Bunch of Thoughts* (subsequently published in revised editions).
- Golwalkar continued the policy of RSS non-participation in the independence movement; per RSS biographer C.P. Bhishikar Golwalkar "did not want to give the British an excuse to ban the RSS."
- December 1947 Nehru letter to provincial chief ministers characterising the RSS as "an organisation which is in the nature of a private army, and which is definitely proceeding on the strictest Nazi lines, even following the techniques of the organisation."

### 1948 Gandhi assassination and first ban
- 30 January 1948: Mahatma Gandhi assassinated by Nathuram Vinayak Godse — documented former RSS member (Godse claimed at trial to have left the organisation in 1946; the broader Godse-Apte-Karkare conspiracy was documented across the trial record).
- 4 February 1948: RSS banned under the Indian Criminal Law Amendment Act.
- Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (Home Minister) wrote to Golwalkar 11 September 1948: "All their (RSS) speeches were full of communal poison."
- The 1969 Jeevan Lal Kapur Commission report on the Gandhi assassination found: "All these facts taken together were destructive of any theory other than the conspiracy to murder by Savarkar and his group."
- 12 July 1949: ban lifted after RSS agreed to adopt a constitution swearing loyalty to the Constitution of India and the national flag.

### Bharatiya Jana Sangh and the political wing
- 1951: Shyama Prasad Mukherjee founded the Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJS) as the political wing of the RSS.
- 1977: BJS merged with the Janata Party coalition that defeated Indira Gandhi.
- 1980: BJS reconstituted as the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) under L.K. Advani and Atal Bihari Vajpayee.

### Second ban 1975-1977 and third ban 1992-1993
- 4 July 1975: RSS banned during the Emergency (covered in Part IV historical chapter).
- 1977: ban lifted following Emergency end.
- 10 December 1992: RSS banned following the 6 December 1992 Babri Masjid demolition.
- 4 June 1993: ban lifted by Indian tribunal under the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act after finding no evidence of unlawful activities.

### Subsequent Sarsanghchalaks
- K.B. Hedgewar (1925-1930, 1931-1940).
- L.V. Paranjpe (acting, 1930-1931).
- M.S. Golwalkar (1940-1973).
- Madhukar Dattatraya Deoras (1973-1994).
- Rajendra Singh (1994-2000).
- K.S. Sudarshan (2000-2009).
- Mohan Bhagwat (since 21 March 2009).

## Mohan Bhagwat

### Biography
- Born Mohan Madhukar Bhagwat on 11 September 1950 in Chandrapur, then in undivided Madhya Pradesh (present-day Maharashtra).
- Marathi Karhade Brahmin family of multi-generational RSS activists.
- Father Madhukar Rao Bhagwat: Karyavah (secretary) for the Chandrapur RSS zone and subsequently RSS Karyavah for positions.
- Education: Bachelor of Veterinary Sciences and Animal Husbandry from Government Veterinary College, Nagpur; dropped out of postgraduate course in Veterinary Sciences in late 1975 to become a full-time RSS pracharak.
- Unmarried (per pracharak convention); owns no home, car, or property; lives in RSS hostels.

### RSS career
- 1975-onward: full-time pracharak.
- 1991-1999: Akhil Bharatiya Pracharak Pramukh (All-India Chief of Pracharaks).
- 1999-2000: Sah Sarkaryavah (Joint General Secretary).
- 2000-2009: Sarkaryavah (General Secretary) under K.S. Sudarshan.
- 21 March 2009: chosen as 6th Sarsanghchalak following K.S. Sudarshan; one of the youngest leaders to head the RSS after Hedgewar and Golwalkar.

### Documented positions
- 2017: first RSS chief officially invited to Rashtrapati Bhawan by then-President Pranab Mukherjee.
- September 2018: presided over three-day session at Vigyan Bhawan in Delhi; characterised the RSS as having "discarded some parts of M.S. Golwalkar's *Bunch of Thoughts* which were no longer relevant to the current circumstances."
- February 2017 Madhya Pradesh rally: "Everyone born in the country is a Hindu — of these some are idol-worshipers and some are not."
- 2019: stated the Sangh is not confined to any singular ideology or ideologue. Asserted that while the core principle of the RSS is the belief that India is a Hindu Rashtra (concept characterised as non-negotiable), it should not be categorised under any specific ideological framework, including Golwalkar's *Bunch of Thoughts*.
- November 2021: publicly opposed the Partition of India; advocated reunification: "The only solution to the pain of Partition lies in undoing it."
- January 2025: characterised India as having achieved "true independence" with the 22 January 2024 consecration of Ram Temple in Ayodhya.
- 2019 book: characterised homosexuality as a "private matter"; opposed institutional gay marriage.
- January 2023: stated LGBTQ individuals "should have their own private and social space as they are humans and have the right to live as others."
- March 2023: RSS General Secretary Dattatreya Hosabale reiterated opposition to legalisation of gay marriage; the position aligned with the BJP-ruled Government of India affidavit filed with the Indian Supreme Court against same-sex marriage.
- June 2015: Z+ VVIP security cover provided to Bhagwat by Central Industrial Security Force (CISF) following documented threats from Islamic militant organisations.
- 2 October 2025: RSS centenary on Vijayadashami (Dussehra).
- October 2025 Bhagwat speech at Mumbai centenary event: "1.25 crore Hindus in Bangladesh should unite and fight for their rights" — characterised as escalation of post-Hasina-ouster RSS rhetoric on Bangladesh.

## Dattatreya Hosabale

### Biography
- Born 1 December 1955 in Sorab, Shivamogga district, Karnataka.
- RSS pracharak since 1978.
- Sarkaryavah (General Secretary) since 2021 — operational head of the RSS.
- Re-elected as Sarkaryavah by the Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha in March 2024 for second three-year term.

### Documented positions
- Multi-year public characterisation of opposition to gay marriage, secularism, and post-1947 subsequent secular-republic framework.
- subsequent 2022-2024 subsequent public statements on subsequent caste reservation, subsequent population policy, and subsequent religious-conversion legislation.

## RSS organisational architecture

### The shakha system
- 80,000+ shakhas across India per 2025 RSS figures (the 75,000-shakha figure was the multi-source 2024-2025 estimate; the 80,000+ figure is the post-2025 RSS centenary characterisation).
- Daily one-hour pre-dawn meetings combining physical drill, Hindu cultural training, and ideological instruction.
- Pre-2014 shakha count had fallen from 51,000 (2004) to 40,000; the post-2014 BJP government tenure has produced the documented expansion to the contemporary 80,000+ figure.
- Each shakha operated by a shakha pramukh (branch chief).

### Internal governance structure
- Sarsanghchalak: chief; chosen by predecessor's nomination.
- Sarkaryavah: general secretary; elected by Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha every three years.
- Sah-Sarkaryavah: four joint general secretaries.
- Vicharak: ideologues.
- Pracharak: full-time missionaries (the cadre of the organisation).
- Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha: highest deliberative body (1,500 representatives meeting annually).

### Pracharak cadre
- Unmarried, unpaid, full-time missionaries — the institutional backbone of the RSS.
- Modi was a pracharak 1971-1985 (covered in Dossier 21); Shah was a Swayamsevak from 1980 but not a pracharak.
- The pracharak cadre operates the assignment system that places RSS-trained individuals across the Sangh Parivar 2,500-plus organisations.

## The Sangh Parivar

### Definition and scope
- "Family of the Sangh" — collection of Hindutva organisations formed by and affiliated to the RSS.
- 2,500-plus organisations across the subsequent post-1925 apparatus.

### Major Sangh Parivar organisations
- **Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)** — Indian People's Party; political wing; 202 million members as of 2024 per BJP figures; founded 1980 (successor to BJS founded 1951).
- **Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP)** — World Hindu Council; founded 29 August 1964 at Sandipani Sadhanalaya, Bombay during Krishna Janmashtami by RSS Sarsanghchalak M.S. Golwalkar and S.S. Apte with Swami Chinmayananda; founded specifically for the subsequent cultural-religious arm of the Sangh Parivar.
- **Bajrang Dal** — Army of Lord Hanuman; founded 8 October 1984 in Ayodhya as the youth wing of the VHP; major training-camp shakha system; characterised in subsequent international scholarly literature as paramilitary.
- **Durga Vahini** — women's wing of the Bajrang Dal; founded 1991 under Sadhvi Rithambara.
- **Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP)** — All India Students' Council; founded 9 July 1949; 2.8 million members as of 2011 per the organisation's figures.
- **Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS)** — Indian Workers' Union; founded 23 July 1955; 10 million members as of 2009 per the organisation's figures.
- **Bharatiya Kisan Sangh (BKS)** — Indian Farmers' Association; founded 4 March 1979; 8 million members as of 2002.
- **Rashtra Sevika Samiti** — women's wing; founded 1936.
- **Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram** — for tribal communities; founded 1952.
- **Vidya Bharati** — RSS schools network; the single largest non-government school network in India.
- **Bharatiya Janata Yuva Morcha (BJYM)** — BJP youth wing.
- **Akhil Bharatiya Adhivakta Parishad** — All India Lawyers' Council.
- **Hindu Swayamsevak Sangh (HSS)** — international wing operating in 40-plus countries including the US, UK, Kenya.
- **Deen Dayal Shodh Sansthan** — rural development; 1.7 million members.
- **Bharat Vikas Parishad** — Indian Development Council.

## Documented violence record

### Mahatma Gandhi assassination
- 30 January 1948: Nathuram Vinayak Godse (former RSS member per documented record) shot and killed Mahatma Gandhi at Birla House, New Delhi.
- Jeevan Lal Kapur Commission (1969) findings: "All these facts taken together were destructive of any theory other than the conspiracy to murder by Savarkar and his group."

### 1992 Babri Masjid demolition
- Documented RSS-BJP-VHP-Bajrang Dal organisation of the demolition (covered in Part IV historical chapter and Dossier 21).
- Liberhan Commission 2009 report concluded: events of 6 December 1992 were "neither spontaneous nor unplanned."
- 2005 Maloy Krishna Dhar (former IB Joint Director) disclosure: demolition had been planned by RSS-BJP-VHP leadership 10 months in advance and the planning had been known to PM P.V. Narasimha Rao and Home Minister Shankarrao Chavan.

### 2002 Gujarat pogroms
- Documented VHP and Bajrang Dal participation per Human Rights Watch 2002 report.
- 1,044 dead (3/4 Muslim) per official Gujarat government record; 2,000 per independent estimates (Concerned Citizens Tribunal report chaired by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer).
- Modi (then-CM) and Shah (Modi-government Home Minister) covered in Dossiers 21 and 22.

### Bombing campaigns 2003-2008
- Per documented investigation: between 2003 and 2008, nine terror attacks linked to the RSS or other Sangh Parivar organisations killed 150 people, mostly Muslims; many attacks happened near or inside Muslim places of worship or on Islamic holidays.
- Documented attacks: 2007 Samjhauta Express bombing (68 dead); 2008 Malegaon bombing (10 dead); 2008 Modasa bombing; Mecca Masjid bombing 2007 (9 dead); Ajmer Sharif Dargah bombing 2007 (3 dead).
- pattern of "Hindu terror" / "saffron terror" identification by Indian counter-terrorism investigation under 2008-2014 UPA government; which was subsequently dismantled by by National Investigation Agency under BJP government.

### Yashwant Shinde 2022 affidavit
- September 2022: former RSS pracharak Yashwant Shinde gave interview and sworn affidavit characterising training to carry out covert operations in Pakistan and false-flag terrorist attacks in India to scapegoat Muslims.
- Documented allegation: Shinde and fellow trainees bombed mosques in Jalna, Purna, and Parbhani in Maharashtra.
- Shinde implicated key members of the RSS and larger Sangh Parivar in directly planning or having knowledge of attacks, including a former RSS national executive member and a national secretary of the BJP.

### Graham Staines killing 1999
- 22-23 January 1999: Australian Christian missionary Graham Staines and his sons Philip (10) and Timothy (6) burned to death in Manoharpur, Odisha, by Bajrang Dal-affiliated Dara Singh and accomplices.
- 2003: Dara Singh sentenced to life imprisonment; 2011 Supreme Court upheld the conviction.
- 12 accomplices acquitted.
- Justice D.P. Wadhwa Commission (1999) reportedly found no evidence of orchestrated VHP / Sangh Parivar conspiracy.

### "Beti Bachao Bahu Lao" / "love jihad" campaign
- VHP-Bajrang Dal campaign tracking Hindu women in inter-faith relationships with Muslim men.
- 2016 Ramnath Goenka Award given to Scroll.in journalist Rahul Kotiyal for reporting on the campaign.
- Post-2017 multi-state subsequent "love jihad" / "anti-conversion" laws passed by BJP-ruled state governments (Uttar Pradesh 2020, Madhya Pradesh 2020, Karnataka 2021, Gujarat 2021, Uttarakhand 2018-2022, Haryana 2022, Himachal Pradesh 2022, Maharashtra 2023, Chhattisgarh 2025).

## RSS centenary 2 October 2025

- 2 October 2025: RSS centenary on Vijayadashami.
- Two-day centenary conference in New Delhi attended by diplomats, scholars, journalists, and business leaders.
- Mumbai centenary event Bhagwat speech: "1.25 crore Hindus in Bangladesh should unite and fight for their rights."
- subsequent multi-month subsequent commentary on subsequent post-Hasina-ouster Bangladesh and subsequent characterised as subsequent escalating subsequent RSS public positioning on the post-2024 subsequent Indian foreign-policy stance.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh"; "Mohan Bhagwat"; "Dattatreya Hosabale"; "Vishva Hindu Parishad"; "Bajrang Dal"; "Sangh Parivar"; "List of leaders of the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh"; "Assassination of Mahatma Gandhi"; "Demolition of the Babri Masjid"; "2007 Samjhauta Express bombings"; "2008 Malegaon bombings"; "Mecca Masjid bombing"; "Ajmer Dargah blast"; "Graham Staines."
- Britannica "Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh"; "Mohan Bhagwat."
- M.S. Golwalkar, *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* (1938).
- M.S. Golwalkar, *Bunch of Thoughts* (1939, revised editions).
- V.D. Savarkar, *Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?* (1923).
- A.G. Noorani, *The RSS and the BJP: A Division of Labour* (LeftWord, 2000); *The RSS: A Menace to India* (LeftWord, 2019).
- Christophe Jaffrelot, *The Hindu Nationalist Movement and Indian Politics 1925 to the 1990s* (Hurst, 1996); *Modi's India: Hindu Nationalism and the Rise of Ethnic Democracy* (Princeton, 2021).
- Walter K. Andersen and Shridhar D. Damle, *The Brotherhood in Saffron: The Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh and Hindu Revivalism* (Westview, 1987); *The RSS: A View to the Inside* (Penguin, 2018).

- Concerned Citizens Tribunal report on the 2002 Gujarat violence chaired by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and P.B. Sawant.

# Sources — Dossier 24: General Asim Munir and the Pakistani military political class

## The subject

- Full name: Syed Asim Munir Ahmed Shah.
- Born 1968 in Rawalpindi, Pakistan.
- Family originally from Jalandhar, Punjab; migrated to Pakistan after the 1947 Partition.
- Hafiz of the Quran (memorised the entire Quran) while serving in Saudi Arabia as military attaché.
- 11th Chief of Army Staff (COAS) of Pakistan since 29 November 2022.
- 2nd Field Marshal in Pakistan's history (after Mohammad Ayub Khan in 1959); promoted 20 May 2025.
- First Field Marshal in Pakistan's history to be appointed by a civilian government.
- First holder of the newly created post of Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) — created by the 27th Constitutional Amendment passed by the Pakistan Parliament in November 2025.
- The first Pakistani officer in history to hold both COAS and CDF simultaneously.
- Tenure as COAS extended to five years (until late November 2027).

## Education and military training
- Officers Training School (OTS) at Mangla — not the Pakistan Military Academy (PMA) at Kakul.
- The Mangla-versus-Kakul distinction was used by his opponents within the institution as a reason to challenge his promotion to COAS in 2022.
- Commissioned into the Frontier Force Regiment.
- Advanced military training in Pakistan, Japan, and Malaysia.
- MPhil in Public Policy and Strategic Security Management from the National Defence University (NDU), Islamabad.

## Career progression
- Early assignments commanding troops in the Northern Areas (Gilgit-Baltistan) including Pakistan-occupied Kashmir.
- Military attaché in Saudi Arabia — the post during which he memorised the Quran.
- Brigadier commanding an infantry brigade in the Northern Areas; caught the attention of then-X Corps Commander Qamar Javed Bajwa (who later became COAS).
- Chief of Staff of the I Strike Corps at Mangla under Bajwa.
- Director General of Military Intelligence (DG MI) from 2017.
- Promoted Lieutenant General in 2018.
- DG Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) from October 2018 to June 2019 — the shortest tenure in the agency's history.
- Replaced as DG ISI by Lt. Gen. Faiz Hameed in June 2019 following fallout with then-PM Imran Khan (reportedly over allegations Munir had raised about corruption in Bushra Bibi's family).
- Subsequent posts: corps commander of XXX Corps; Quartermaster General at GHQ.
- Was due to retire two days before Bajwa retired in November 2022. Not initially on the list of probable successors.
- 24 November 2022: promoted to General by the Shehbaz Sharif PDM (Pakistan Democratic Movement) government.
- 29 November 2022: took office as 11th COAS, succeeding Bajwa.
- 20 May 2025: promoted to Field Marshal by unanimous federal cabinet approval.
- November 2025: 27th Constitutional Amendment created CDF post; Munir became first holder.

## Operation Sindoor / Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos (May 2025)
- Early May 2025: four-day military confrontation between India and Pakistan — the "fifth war" averted between the two nuclear-armed neighbours.
- Triggered by India's Operation Sindoor (Indian strikes against alleged militant infrastructure in Pakistan).
- Pakistan's response: Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos (Arabic for "iron wall" / "leaden wall" — Quranic phrase).
- Conflict primarily air-and-missile based with drone exchanges over four days.
- Munir's promotion to Field Marshal on 20 May 2025 was officially attributed to his leadership during Bunyan-um-Marsoos.

## Pakistan military coup history

### 1958 — Ayub Khan
- 7-27 October 1958: Governor-General Iskander Mirza declared martial law and named Commander-in-Chief Gen. Mohammad Ayub Khan as Chief Martial Law Administrator.
- 27 October 1958: thirteen days after the initial declaration, Ayub Khan deposed Mirza, who was exiled to England, and assumed the presidency himself.
- Ayub ruled 1958-1969; created a centralised presidential structure.
- Ayub was promoted to Field Marshal in 1959 — Pakistan's first.

### 1969 — Yahya Khan
- 25 March 1969: General Yahya Khan declared martial law and took power from Ayub Khan.
- 1970 national elections — most open in Pakistan's history at the time. Sheikh Mujibur Rahman's Awami League won majority (167 of 169 East Pakistan seats; 160 of 313 total National Assembly seats).
- Yahya Khan and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto's Pakistan People's Party (PPP) refused to transfer power.
- 25 March 1971: Operation Searchlight; 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War; 16 December 1971 Pakistani surrender at Dhaka.
- Yahya resigned 1971; transferred power to Bhutto.

### 1977 — Zia ul-Haq (Operation Fair Play)
- 5 July 1977: Operation Fair Play — coup conducted at midnight on 4-5 July 1977 by the Pakistan Army under COAS General Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq against PM Zulfikar Ali Bhutto.
- Zia ordered the arrest of Bhutto, his ministers, and other PPP and PNA leaders.
- National Assembly and provincial assemblies dissolved; Constitution suspended.
- April 1979: Zulfikar Ali Bhutto hanged after Supreme Court upheld death sentence.
- Zia's 11-year rule (1977-1988): Hudood Ordinances (1979) Islamisation; introduced Eighth Amendment (1985) giving president unilateral authority to dismiss elected governments.
- 17 August 1988: Zia killed in plane crash near Bahawalpur along with several military officials and U.S. Ambassador Arnold Raphel.

### 1999 — Musharraf
- 12 October 1999: General Pervez Musharraf coup against PM Nawaz Sharif during the Kargil aftermath.
- Sharif had attempted to dismiss Musharraf and prevent his plane from landing on return from Sri Lanka.
- officers loyal to Musharraf arrested Sharif and his ministers.
- Musharraf's coup subsequently ratified by Supreme Court under the "doctrine of necessity."
- Musharraf assumed presidency June 2001; Legal Framework Order 2002 concentrated presidential power.
- Musharraf resigned 18 August 2008 under threat of impeachment.

### Failed coup attempts and other military actions
- 1980: Maj. Gen. Tajammul Hussain Malik plot to assassinate Zia on Pakistan Day (23 March 1980) — exposed and thwarted.
- 1984: leftist coup attempt against Zia — foiled by ISI.
- 1995: coup attempt against Benazir Bhutto led by Maj. Gen. Zaheerul Islam Abbasi — foiled.

## Summary statistics
- Direct military rule: 33 of 75 years of Pakistan's post-independence history (44%).
- Three successful coups: 1958 (Ayub), 1977 (Zia), 1999 (Musharraf).
- One additional martial law declaration: 1969 (Yahya, internal military transfer).
- The doctrine of necessity (originated by the Pakistani Supreme Court in the 1955 Federation of Pakistan v. Maulvi Tamizuddin Khan case and applied subsequently) provided judicial ratification for the 1958, 1977, and 1999 coups.

## ISI history and operations

### Founding and early history
- Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) founded 1948 by Maj. Gen. Robert Cawthome (Australian deputy chief of staff in Pakistan Army).
- Operates as the Pakistani intelligence agency reporting to the Prime Minister (formally) and the COAS (operationally).

### Operation Cyclone (1980-1988)
- 1979-1989: CIA-ISI joint operation funding and arming Afghan mujahideen against the Soviet occupation of Afghanistan.
- Code-named Operation Cyclone by the CIA.
- Estimated $3 billion in CIA funding plus equivalent Saudi matching funds.
- Channelled through the ISI to multiple mujahideen factions including Gulbuddin Hekmatyar's Hezb-e-Islami, Ahmad Shah Massoud's Jamiat-e-Islami, and Burhanuddin Rabbani's Jamiat-e-Islami.
- Operational legacy: the post-1989 Taliban (founded 1994 by Mullah Omar from Kandahar-area Pashtun madrasa students) and al-Qaeda (founded 1988 by Osama bin Laden and Ayman al-Zawahiri).

### Post-9/11 War on Terror alignment
- Post-September 2001 Musharraf alignment with US War on Terror following the US Secretary of State Colin Powell ultimatum.
- ISI was the primary Pakistani intelligence service partner of the CIA in the 2001-2014 Afghanistan war.
- Documented dual role: simultaneous ISI support for Taliban factions including the Haqqani Network even as Pakistan formally allied with the US.
- Multi-year US Congressional and intelligence community concerns about ISI support for the Taliban.
- 2 May 2011: US Navy SEAL Team 6 operation killed Osama bin Laden at his compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan — 1 km from the Pakistan Military Academy (Kakul). The location raised multi-year US Congressional and intelligence-community concerns about ISI knowledge of bin Laden's residence in Abbottabad.

### Documented human rights record
- Multi-year documented record of extrajudicial killings, enforced disappearances, and torture, particularly in Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa.
- Human Rights Commission of Pakistan, Amnesty International, and Human Rights Watch multi-year reporting on the pattern.

## Munir-Imran Khan conflict and the 9 May 2023 crisis

### Background to the conflict
- October 2018: Munir appointed DG ISI under PM Imran Khan.
- June 2019: Munir removed as DG ISI by PM Imran Khan after only 8 months — the shortest tenure in the agency's history.
- The removal followed Munir's reported corruption allegations against Khan's wife Bushra Bibi's family.
- Khan's subsequent characterisation (post-2023): "I think that maybe he has a grudge because I asked him to resign as ISI chief in 2019."

### April 2022 ouster of Imran Khan
- 10 April 2022: PM Imran Khan ousted via vote of no confidence — the first PM in Pakistan history to be removed by no-confidence vote.
- Subsequent PM Shehbaz Sharif (Pakistan Democratic Movement / PDM coalition).
- 29 November 2022: Munir succeeded Bajwa as COAS under the Shehbaz government.

### 9 May 2023 PTI attacks on military installations
- 9 May 2023: Imran Khan arrested at the Islamabad High Court in the Al-Qadir Trust Case.
- PTI (Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf) supporters launched nationwide demonstrations that descended into violent riots.
- Major incidents: attack on Lahore Corps Commander House (Jinnah House) — building torched; attack on the gates of Pakistan military headquarters (GHQ) in Rawalpindi; attacks on military installations in Karachi, Peshawar, Sargodha, Gujranwala, and other cities.
- Total damage estimated at Rs. 2.5 billion (US$8.9 million) per official figures.
- Pakistan Army termed 9 May 2023 "Pakistan's 9/11."

### Crackdown 2023-2024
- Imran Khan released two days after 9 May arrest; arrested again on 5 August 2023 in the Toshakhana corruption case.
- Multi-month subsequent prosecutions: more than 100 cases filed against Khan including the Toshakhana case (foreign-gifts sale), the Cipher case (classified diplomatic cable disclosure), and the Al-Qadir Trust Case (UK National Crime Agency £190 million / US$239 million property settlement and land donation to Al-Qadir University Trust associated with Khan and wife Bushra Bibi).
- Thousands of PTI workers and party leaders arrested.
- More than 100 PTI figures defected from the party.
- December 2023: Pakistan Election Commission removed PTI's electoral symbol (cricket bat) over "irregularities" in PTI intra-party elections.
- Multi-month subsequent media crackdown: media houses summoned to secret army meetings; Khan's name banned from print and electronic media (referred to euphemistically as "Qasim ke Abba" / Qasim's father).
- Journalists Imran Riaz Khan (kidnapped outside Sialkot Central Jail 11 May 2023; Punjab Police chief Usman Anwar blamed military agencies for the disappearance) and Sami Ibrahim (Bol News head, picked up 24 May 2023).
- Reporters Without Borders and the International Federation of Journalists raised multi-year concerns.
- Trials of civilians in military courts initiated and subsequently challenged before the Pakistan Supreme Court.
- January 2026: anti-terrorism court convicted and sentenced eight journalists and social media commentators to life imprisonment in absentia for terrorism-related offences linked to online content supporting Imran Khan.

### February 2024 elections and ongoing PTI suppression
- 8 February 2024: Pakistani general election held with PTI-backed independent candidates (PTI banned from contesting under its electoral symbol).
- PTI-backed independents won 93 of 266 directly elected National Assembly seats — largest single bloc but unable to form government.
- PML-N (Sharif) and PPP (Bhutto-Zardari) formed coalition government with Shehbaz Sharif as PM (second term from 4 March 2024).
- PTI characterised the election as fraudulent; multi-month subsequent disputes over seat allocations.
- Multi-month subsequent PTI characterisation that the 9 May 2023 attacks were a "false flag operation" engineered by the military.

## 27th Constitutional Amendment November 2025
- Pakistan Parliament passed the 27th Constitutional Amendment in November 2025.
- Created the new post of Chief of Defence Forces (CDF) with authority over all three armed services (Army, Navy, Air Force).
- Munir became the first CDF — first Pakistani officer to hold both COAS and CDF simultaneously.
- Munir's tenure as COAS extended to five years (until late November 2027).
- Air Chief Marshal Zaheer Ahmed Babar Sidhu's tenure as Chief of Air Staff extended by two years.

## Munir family
- Munir married Irum Asim Munir.
- Four daughters; third daughter Mahnoor married Captain Abdul Rehman Qasim (her first cousin, son of Munir's brother Syed Qasim Munir) on 26 December 2025 at an official residence near GHQ Rawalpindi — wedding kept discreet due to security concerns; 400 high-profile guests attended including serving and retired generals; ceremony held without public photos; coincided with UAE president's visit.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Asim Munir"; "Chief of the Army Staff (Pakistan)"; "Military coups in Pakistan"; "Military dictatorship in Pakistan"; "May 9 riots"; "Imran Khan"; "Inter-Services Intelligence."
- Britannica "Asim Munir."
- Vivekananda International Foundation, "Asim Munir: From a Struggling General to Field Marshal" (18 July 2025).
- Al Jazeera, "Pakistan promotes army chief Asim Munir to field marshal: Why it matters" (21 May 2025).
- Al Jazeera, "Can Pakistan's Imran Khan and army patch up, a year after violent clashes?" (13 May 2024).
- Arab News, "As elections near, a timeline of Pakistan's troubled history of military interventions."
- Christian Science Monitor, "May 9 crackdown: Why Pakistan army is targeting Imran Khan's PTI party" (21 June 2023).
- The Intercept reporting on May 2023 army meetings with Pakistani media houses.
- Reporters Without Borders and International Federation of Journalists statements 2023-2024.

# Sources — Dossier 25: Shehbaz Sharif and the Sharif-PML-N political-class continuity

## The subject

- Full name: Mian Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif.
- Born 23 September 1951 in Lahore, West Punjab, Pakistan.
- Family: Punjabi-speaking Kashmiri; ancestors from Anantnag (Kashmir) settled in Jati Umra, Amritsar district, Punjab at the beginning of the twentieth century; mother's family from Pulwama.
- Parents migrated from Amritsar to Lahore after the 14-15 August 1947 Partition.
- Father: Mian Muhammad Sharif (upper-middle-class businessman and industrialist; founded Ittefaq Group).
- Mother: Shamim Akhtar Sharif.
- Brothers: Nawaz Sharif (three-time PM 1990-93, 1997-99, 2013-17; longest non-consecutive PM tenure in Pakistani history) and Abbas Sharif (businessman, deceased).
- Wife: Nusrat Shehbaz Sharif (married 1973; first wife). Married Tehmina Durrani in 2003 (second wife — legally permitted under Pakistani law). Married a third time.
- Four sons by Nusrat: Hamza Shahbaz Sharif (eldest, MNA 2008-18, Chief Minister of Punjab briefly in 2022, MNA 2024-); Salman; Hassan; Sufyan. Two daughters.

## Sharif family economic and political architecture

### Ittefaq Group and Sharif Group
- Ittefaq Group: multimillion-dollar steel conglomerate; founded by Mian Muhammad Sharif.
- Sharif Group: conglomerate with holdings in agriculture, transport, and sugar mills.
- 1974: factories nationalised under Zulfikar Ali Bhutto's nationalisation policy.
- 1977: factories returned to the family following the Zia-ul-Haq coup.
- The Sharif family economic-and-political architecture: the Ittefaq-Sharif business empire (steel, sugar, agriculture, transport) is the central institutional basis of the PML-N's Punjabi industrial-class political-base.

### Family residence
- Raiwind Palace: the Sharif family compound in Raiwind, Lahore — the central physical location of the Sharif political dynasty.

### Family in politics
- Nawaz Sharif: three-time PM (1990-93, 1997-99, 2013-17). Returned from London exile 21 October 2023. PML-N supremo.
- Shehbaz Sharif: Punjab CM 1997-99, 2008-18 (three terms); PM 11 April 2022 - 14 August 2023 (first term, PDM government); PM since 4 March 2024 (second term, PML-N-PPP coalition).
- Maryam Nawaz Sharif: Nawaz's eldest daughter; Chief Minister of Punjab since February 2024 — the first woman CM of Punjab.
- Hamza Shahbaz Sharif: Shehbaz's eldest son; briefly Chief Minister of Punjab in 2022.
- Kulsoom Nawaz: Nawaz's wife (deceased 2018); First Lady three non-consecutive times.

## Education

- Saint Anthony's High School, Lahore.
- Government College University, Lahore — Bachelor of Arts.
- The Government College Lahore (also known as GCU) is the central institutional university of the Punjabi upper-class.

## Early career

- Early 1970s: joined the family Ittefaq Group business alongside Nawaz Sharif.
- The Sharif brothers' political emergence operates from the post-1977 Zia-ul-Haq regime; Nawaz was made Punjab Finance Minister under Punjab Governor Lt. Gen. Ghulam Jilani Khan (former DG ISI) in May 1980; the Sharifs are characterised by political opponents as "the product of martial law and General Jilani."

## Shehbaz political career

### 1988 Punjab Provincial Assembly
- 1988: elected to the Provincial Assembly of the Punjab in the first general election after Zia's death.

### 1990 National Assembly
- 1990: elected to the National Assembly.

### 1993 dual election
- 1993: re-elected to both the National Assembly and the Provincial Assembly of the Punjab.
- 1993-1996: served as opposition leader in the Punjab Assembly.

### First Punjab CM term 1997-1999
- 20 February 1997: elected Chief Minister of Punjab for the first time (PML-N government).
- 12 October 1999: ousted by the Musharraf coup that toppled the Nawaz Sharif federal government.
- 2000: Sharif brothers tried for hijacking a commercial airliner; Nawaz sentenced to life imprisonment April 2000 for orders telling ground control to refuse landing permission to Musharraf's Karachi-bound airliner on 12 October 1999.

### Saudi exile 2000-2007
- December 2000: Sharif family went into exile under an agreement brokered by then-Saudi Crown Prince and subsequent King Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz.
- 2000-2007: family lived in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia for seven years (originally a 10-year exile agreement).
- August 2006: Shehbaz re-elected PML-N President for second term.
- 23 August 2007: Pakistan Supreme Court ruled the Sharif brothers free to return to Pakistan.
- 10 September 2007: Nawaz attempted return from London; deported within hours to Jeddah.
- November 2007: Sharif brothers returned to Pakistan.

### Second Punjab CM term 2008-2013
- 2008: Shehbaz unable to contest the 2008 general election due to the Sabzazar case; acquitted months later.
- 2008: elected Chief Minister of Punjab for second term following PML-N victory in Punjab.
- 25 February 2009: Supreme Court of Pakistan disqualified both Nawaz and Shehbaz from holding public office (case related to Nawaz's 1993 conviction and Shehbaz's 2000 hijacking case). PPP President Asif Ali Zardari attempted to place Nawaz under house arrest.
- The 2009 Long March / Lawyers' Movement led to the 16 March 2009 reinstatement of Chief Justice Iftikhar Chaudhry and the reversal of the Sharif disqualifications.
- 2008-2013: completed five-year term as Punjab CM. Lahore Metro Bus and other infrastructure projects.

### Third Punjab CM term 2013-2018
- 2013: re-elected Chief Minister of Punjab for third term following PML-N national victory.
- 2013-2018: served third term.
- 2015: Panama Papers leak implicated Nawaz Sharif and three of his children (Maryam, Hassan, Hussain) in offshore holdings used to purchase London real estate.
- 28 July 2017: Pakistan Supreme Court disqualified Nawaz Sharif from holding public office over the Panama Papers case. Shahid Khaqan Abbasi succeeded Nawaz as PM.
- August 2017: Nawaz left for London (with wife Kulsoom seeking medical treatment); Maryam joined them in September.
- 2017: Shehbaz nominated President of the PML-N.

### 2018 election and opposition period 2018-2022
- 2018: PML-N defeated by Imran Khan's PTI in 25 July 2018 general election.
- 2018: Shehbaz elected MNA; served as Leader of the Opposition in the National Assembly.
- 4 December 2018: Shehbaz arrested by NAB on charges related to the Ashiana-i-Iqbal housing scheme case.
- 14 February 2019: released on bail.
- 28 September 2020: Shehbaz arrested in money-laundering case.
- April 2021: released on bail.
- 2019-2024: multi-year investigations and prosecutions on money-laundering charges. UK Daily Mail 14 July 2019 article (David Rose) alleging theft of UK DFID aid funds. The Sharif family rejected the allegations; the multi-year subsequent libel case operated.

### Pakistan Democratic Movement 2020-
- September 2020: PDM formed — multi-party coalition of PML-N, PPP, JUI-F, ANP, BNP-M, NP, PkMAP, Jamaat-e-Islami (briefly), and other smaller parties.
- Stated goal: reduce military establishment interference in civilian government.
- Nawaz Sharif (from London) gave video-conference speeches at PDM rallies accusing the army of orchestrating the election of Imran Khan and PTI.

### First PM term April 2022 - August 2023
- 10 April 2022: PM Imran Khan ousted via vote of no confidence — the first PM in Pakistan history removed by no-confidence vote.
- 11 April 2022: Shehbaz Sharif elected 23rd PM with 174 votes against 0 (PTI walked out).
- 11 April 2022 - 14 August 2023: served first term as PM under PDM coalition government.
- 4 March 2022: Shehbaz oversaw the federal cabinet appointment of Asim Munir to General; 29 November 2022 Munir's appointment as 11th COAS.
- 14 August 2023: Shehbaz resigned three days early to permit caretaker government and delay 2023 elections beyond the original October date.
- August 2023 - 4 March 2024: Anwaar-ul-Haq Kakar served as caretaker PM.

### 2024 election and second PM term
- 8 February 2024: general election held.
- PTI-backed independents won 93 of 266 directly elected National Assembly seats (largest single bloc but unable to form government).
- PML-N won 75 seats (second-largest); PPP won 54 seats (third-largest).
- 20 February 2024: PML-N and PPP formed minority coalition government.
- Shehbaz chosen as PM candidate over Nawaz Sharif.
- Asif Ali Zardari (PPP) chosen as coalition's presidential candidate.
- 3 March 2024: Shehbaz Sharif elected PM for second term with 201 votes (defeating PTI's Omar Ayub Khan).
- 4 March 2024: Shehbaz took oath of office for second term.
- 9 March 2024: Asif Ali Zardari sworn in as President of Pakistan (for his second term).

### Election fraud allegations
- The 8 February 2024 election was characterised by PTI and multiple international observers including The Economist, the Financial Times, and the EU Election Observation Mission as having been rigged in favour of PML-N-PPP coalition through:
- The December 2023 removal of PTI's electoral symbol (cricket bat);
- The pre-election arrest and conviction sentences against Imran Khan;
- Election-day internet shutdowns;
- Multi-day delay in result announcement;
- Late-night swings in seat counts in disputed constituencies;
- The Form-45 / Form-47 discrepancy controversy (the PTI characterisation that the polling-station Form 45 results were altered in the consolidated Form 47 results).

### Second-term governance 2024-
- May 2025 Operation Bunyan-um-Marsoos (covered in Dossier 24).
- May 2025 Field Marshal promotion of COAS Asim Munir by unanimous federal cabinet approval under Shehbaz government.
- November 2025: 27th Constitutional Amendment passed by Pakistan Parliament under Shehbaz government creating Chief of Defence Forces post for Munir.
- 2024-2026: multi-year IMF Extended Fund Facility ($7 billion in September 2024).
- Multi-year Saudi Arabia and UAE bilateral economic support.
- Multi-year continuing crackdown on PTI (Dossier 24).

## Daily Mail allegation 2019

- 14 July 2019: Daily Mail published David Rose article headlined "Did the family of Pakistani politician who has become the poster boy for British overseas aid STEAL funds meant for earthquake victims?"
- Allegation: Shehbaz Sharif stole aid funds from the UK's Department for International Development (DFID) following the 2005 Kashmir earthquake; allegedly used the aid funds to send £1 million through money laundering to frontman Aftab Mehmood.
- Context: UK had donated more than £500 million to earthquake victims in Pakistan through DFID.
- Sharif family rejected the allegations and pursued multi-year libel litigation against the Daily Mail.
- Multi-year subsequent libel litigation in UK courts.

## Sharif-Saudi relationship

- Multi-decade documented Sharif-Saudi political-and-economic relationship.
- 2000 Saudi-brokered exile under King Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz.
- Multi-year subsequent Saudi-Pakistani economic relationship (loans, deposits, oil deferred payments) operating through the Sharif PM tenures.
- November 2025 Saudi investment announcements under Shehbaz second term.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Shehbaz Sharif"; "Nawaz Sharif"; "Maryam Nawaz"; "Pakistan Democratic Movement"; "Ittefaq Group"; "Sharif family"; "Panama Papers case"; "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"; "2024 Pakistani general election"; "Pakistan Muslim League (Nawaz)"; "23rd Prime Minister of Pakistan."
- Britannica "Shehbaz Sharif"; "Nawaz Sharif."
- National Assembly of Pakistan profile of Mian Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif.
- Express Tribune, "Profile: Shehbaz Sharif, Pakistan's new prime minister" (3 March 2024).
- Anadolu Agency, "PROFILE - Shehbaz Sharif, Pakistan's new prime minister" (11 April 2022).
- Daily Mail (David Rose), "Did the family of Pakistani politician who has become the poster boy for British overseas aid STEAL funds meant for earthquake victims?" (14 July 2019).
- Panama Papers consortium 2015 leak.
- Financial Times, The Economist, Reuters multi-year reporting on the 2024 election fraud allegations.
- EU Election Observation Mission preliminary report on the 8 February 2024 Pakistani general election.

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Dossier 26: Imran Khan and the PTI record

## The subject

- Full name: Imran Ahmed Khan Niazi (Urdu: عمران احمد خان نیازی).
- Born 5 October 1952 in Lahore, Punjab.
- 22nd Prime Minister of Pakistan from 18 August 2018 until 10 April 2022 — first PM in Pakistan history removed by no-confidence vote.
- Founder of Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI / "Pakistan Movement for Justice"); founded 25 April 1996; PTI Chairman 1996-2023.
- Former international cricketer; captained Pakistan to victory in the 1992 Cricket World Cup; ICC Cricket Hall of Fame inductee.
- Imprisoned at Adiala Jail, Rawalpindi since 5 August 2023.

## Family and personal background

### Birth family
- Father: Ikramullah Khan Niazi (died March 2008, age 85) — civil engineer.
- Mother: Shaukat Khanum (died 1985 from cancer — produced the motivation for the Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital and Research Centre).
- Niazi Pashtun family; mother's side cricketers (cousins Javed Burki and Majid Khan both Pakistani cricket captains who also attended Oxford).
- Only son with four sisters: Aleema Khanum, Uzma Khanum, Rubina Khanum, Rani Khanum.

### Marriages
- 1995-2004: Jemima Goldsmith (British heiress, daughter of tycoon Sir James Goldsmith); married Paris May 1995 and London civil ceremony 21 June 1995; converted to Islam for the marriage; close friend of Princess Diana. Two sons: Sulaiman Isa (born 1996) and Kasim (born 1999). Divorced 22 June 2004 "because it was difficult for Jemima to adapt to life in Pakistan."
- 2015: Reham Khan (British-Pakistani journalist) — married 8 January 2015 (some sources note October 2014 secret nikah); announced divorce 22 October 2015 — ten-month marriage.
- 2018-: Bushra Bibi (formerly Bushra Maneka) — Khan's spiritual guide. Married 18 February 2018 (first nikah 1 January 2018 controversial because Bushra was still in iddat — the mandatory Islamic waiting period after divorce; subsequent Iddat case prosecution). Bushra had been married 28 years to Khawar Maneka with whom she had five children. The Maneka family is from Pakpattan, Punjab. The "Iddat case" March 2024 produced 7-year sentence (subsequently overturned).

### Daughter
- Tyrian Jade Khan (claimed daughter from former girlfriend Sita White; legally established Tyrian Jade as Khan's daughter in 1997 California court ruling; Khan publicly denied paternity for years).

## Education

- Cathedral School, Lahore.
- Aitchison College, Lahore — central elite Pakistani boarding school.
- Royal Grammar School Worcester, England.
- Keble College, University of Oxford — Bachelor of Arts in Philosophy, Politics and Economics (PPE); graduated 1975.
- Captain of Oxford University Blues Cricket team 1974.

## Cricket career 1971-1992

### Test debut and county cricket
- First-class debut age 16 in Lahore.
- 1971: international Test debut against England.
- 1971-1976: county cricket for Worcestershire.
- Pakistan International Airlines and Dawood Industries domestic teams.

### Captaincy
- 1982-1992: intermittent captain of Pakistan national team.
- 1987: led Pakistan to first-ever Test series wins in India and England.
- Considered one of cricket's greatest all-rounders.
- 25 March 1992: 1992 Cricket World Cup final at Melbourne Cricket Ground — Pakistan defeated England by 22 runs; Khan's man-of-the-match performance; Pakistan's only ICC World Cup victory.
- Retired from international cricket immediately after the 1992 World Cup.

## Post-cricket pre-political career 1992-1996

### Shaukat Khanum Memorial Cancer Hospital
- Founded 1994 in Lahore; opened December 1994.
- First cancer hospital in Pakistan.
- Named for Khan's mother Shaukat Khanum (died 1985 from cancer).
- Multi-million-dollar fundraising campaign across early 1990s.

### Hamid Gul / ISI / Pasban relationship 1994
- Late 1994: Khan formed alliance with former DG ISI Lt. Gen. Hamid Gul (DG ISI 1987-1989; central architect of Operation Cyclone Afghan mujahideen support) and Muhammad Ali Durrani (heading Pasban — breakaway Jamaat-e-Islami youth wing).
- Stated objective: civil society "pressure group" / watchdog.

### Sita White paternity case 1997
- 1997: California court ruling established Tyrian Jade as Khan's daughter from former girlfriend Sita White.

## PTI founding and early years 1996-2018

### April 1996 founding
- 25 April 1996: Khan founded Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI / "Pakistan Movement for Justice").
- Nawaz Sharif had previously offered Khan second-in-command position and 30 National Assembly seats; Khan declined.

### 1997 election
- PTI contested NA-53 Mianwali and NA-94 Lahore; lost both to PML-N.
- Won less than 1% of vote nationally.

### 1999 Musharraf coup support
- October 1999: Khan supported General Pervez Musharraf's coup against Nawaz Sharif, believing Musharraf would "end corruption and clear out the political mafias."

### 2002 election — first National Assembly seat
- 2002: won NA-71 Mianwali seat — only PTI seat in the 2002 elections.
- Served as opposition member from Mianwali 2002-2007.
- October 2007: resigned from National Assembly along with other politicians protesting Musharraf's presidential re-election.

### 2008 boycott and political wilderness 2008-2011
- PTI boycotted the 18 February 2008 general election (along with multiple opposition parties).
- Multi-year subsequent low electoral profile.

### 30 October 2011 Minar-e-Pakistan rally
- Mass public rally at Minar-e-Pakistan, Lahore.
- Hundreds of thousands of attendees per multiple estimates.
- Multi-year subsequent rapid PTI expansion.

### 2013 general election
- 11 May 2013 general election: PTI emerged as second-largest party by popular vote (3rd by seats).
- Formed government in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province.
- KP provincial government 2013-2018 produced multi-year police-and-judicial-reform programme.

### 2014 dharna (sit-in)
- August 2014: PTI and PAT (Pakistan Awami Tehreek under Tahir-ul-Qadri) joint sit-in in Islamabad (the "Azadi March" and "Inqilab March") protesting alleged 2013 election rigging.
- 126-day Islamabad sit-in.
- Multi-month subsequent confrontation with the Nawaz Sharif PML-N federal government.

### 2018 election victory
- 25 July 2018 general election: PTI emerged as largest party with 149 of 272 directly elected NA seats.
- Coalition formed with independents and smaller parties.
- 18 August 2018: Khan sworn in as 22nd Prime Minister.

## Prime Minister tenure 2018-2022

### Economic record
- August 2018: inherited balance-of-payments crisis from PML-N predecessor.
- Multi-year IMF bailouts including 2019 $6 billion IMF Extended Fund Facility (EFF).
- Saudi Arabia and UAE bilateral economic support.
- Shrinking current account deficit; limited defence spending; increased tax collection and investment per Khan administration figures.
- 2020-2022 COVID-19 pandemic produced economic turmoil and rising inflation.

### Ehsaas Programme and other initiatives
- Ehsaas Programme: social safety net programme launched 2019.
- Plant for Pakistan / Ten Billion Tree Tsunami initiative.
- Expanded protected areas.
- Renewable energy transition commitments.

### COVID-19 response
- 2020-2022: Khan administration COVID-19 response under National Command and Operation Centre (NCOC).
- Avoided full lockdown; selective "smart lockdowns" pattern.
- Multi-million vaccine programme.

### Foreign policy and diplomatic relations
- 2019 Khan visit to Washington and meeting with President Trump.
- Multi-year China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) Belt-and-Road framework continuation.
- 22-23 February 2022: Khan visit to Moscow on the day Russia invaded Ukraine — produced subsequent US-Pakistan diplomatic tension.
- The Khan Moscow visit became the central documented pretext of the post-March 2022 "Cipher" controversy.

## The April 2022 no-confidence ouster

### The "Cipher" controversy
- 7 March 2022: documented conversation between Pakistan's then-Ambassador to the US Asad Majeed Khan and US Assistant Secretary of State for South and Central Asian Affairs Donald Lu.
- The conversation reportedly characterised US administration concern about Khan's Moscow visit and indicated US preference for Khan's removal from power.
- The cable summarising the conversation — the "Cipher" — was reportedly received by the Pakistan Foreign Ministry.
- 27 March 2022: Khan brandished the "Cipher" paper at a public rally in Islamabad characterising it as documented US-orchestrated regime-change conspiracy against him.
- August 2023: The Intercept published purported text of the cable.
- The US State Department and the Pakistan Army subsequently rejected Khan's characterisation.

### The no-confidence vote
- 8 March 2022: opposition (PDM + dissident PTI MNAs) filed no-confidence motion against Khan in National Assembly.
- 3 April 2022: PTI Deputy Speaker Qasim Suri dismissed the motion as "unconstitutional"; Khan dissolved National Assembly via President Arif Alvi.
- 7 April 2022: Pakistan Supreme Court (5-0 ruling) restored National Assembly and ordered no-confidence vote.
- 9-10 April 2022 (overnight session): National Assembly held no-confidence vote.
- 10 April 2022 (just after midnight): 174 votes for the motion (174-0 with PTI walkout); Khan removed.
- 11 April 2022: Shehbaz Sharif elected PM (Dossier 25).

## Post-ouster political and legal architecture 2022-2025

### November 2022 Wazirabad attack
- 3 November 2022: Khan shot in the leg during his Haqeeqi Azadi Long March near Wazirabad, Punjab.
- One supporter killed; multiple wounded.
- Khan subsequently characterised the attempt as a documented assassination plot involving PM Shehbaz Sharif, then-Interior Minister Rana Sanaullah, and ISI officer Maj. Gen. Faisal Naseer.
- Khan's recovery operated from Lahore's Shaukat Khanum Hospital.

### October 2022 ECP disqualification
- 21 October 2022: Election Commission of Pakistan disqualified Khan under Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution for false declarations of assets in the Toshakhana case.

### 9 May 2023 first arrest
- 9 May 2023: Khan arrested at the Islamabad High Court in connection with the Al-Qadir Trust Case.
- The arrest came one day after the Inter-Services Public Relations (ISPR) — the Pakistan Army media wing — rejected Khan's documented allegations against ISI officer Maj. Gen. Faisal Naseer.
- Multi-day nationwide PTI demonstrations descended into violent attacks on military installations (covered in Dossier 24): Lahore Corps Commander House torched; GHQ Rawalpindi gates attacked; multi-city attacks.
- 11 May 2023: Pakistan Supreme Court deemed the arrest unlawful and ordered Khan's release.
- 12 May 2023: Khan released.

### 5 August 2023 second arrest
- 5 August 2023: Khan arrested in the Toshakhana corruption case after conviction.
- Confined at Adiala Jail, Rawalpindi continuously since 5 August 2023.
- UN experts have characterised Khan's imprisonment as "inhumane and undignified."

## The cases against Imran Khan

Pakistan courts have filed 200 cases against Khan since the April 2022 ouster. The central convictions:

### Toshakhana case (multiple)
- August 2022: first Toshakhana reference filed by PML-N MNA Mohsin Shahnawaz Ranjha alleging Khan retained and sold state gifts from foreign governments at discounted prices.
- September 2022: Khan admitted selling four gifts (Graff watch, cufflinks, ring, pen, four Rolex watches) for 58 million rupees after purchasing them from Toshakhana for 21.56 million rupees.
- 21 October 2022: ECP disqualified Khan under Article 63(1)(p).
- 5 August 2023: trial court convicted Khan with 3-year sentence (subsequently suspended by Islamabad High Court 2024).
- 31 January 2024: NAB Toshakhana case conviction with 14 years' imprisonment and ~1.5 billion rupee fine. April 2024 bail.
- 13 July 2024: re-arrested in Toshakhana 2 case.
- 17 December 2025: Toshakhana 2 case verdict — Khan and Bushra Bibi each sentenced to 17 years' imprisonment for allegedly retaining Bulgari jewellery set valued at $285,000 from Saudi crown prince (paid only $10,300); each fined 10 million rupees; 10-year disqualification from public office.

### Cipher case
- 30 January 2024: Khan and former Foreign Minister Shah Mahmood Qureshi each sentenced to 10 years' imprisonment under Official Secrets Act for "leaking state secrets" through the documented Cipher disclosure.
- 3 June 2024: Islamabad High Court (CJ Aamer Farooq and J. Miangul Hassan Aurangzeb) acquitted both Khan and Qureshi in the Cipher case.

### Iddat case
- 3 February 2024: Khan and Bushra Bibi sentenced to 7 years' imprisonment each for "contracting marriage during iddat" (the mandatory Islamic post-divorce waiting period for the woman).
- July 2024: case acquitted by district and sessions court.

### Al-Qadir Trust case
- 9 May 2023 first arrest.
- 17 January 2025: NAB accountability court sentenced Khan to 14 years' imprisonment in Al-Qadir Trust case; Bushra Bibi sentenced to 7 years. The case involved the UK National Crime Agency £190 million / US$239 million property settlement returned to Pakistan in 2019 and the alleged donation of 458 kanals of land to the Al-Qadir University Trust by property developer Malik Riaz.

### 9 May 2023 cases
- Multi-year subsequent anti-terrorism charges against Khan and PTI leadership for the 9 May 2023 attacks on military installations.
- Multi-year continuing prosecutions in military and anti-terrorism courts.

### Cumulative
- 200 total cases filed against Khan since April 2022.
- Total cumulative sentences across 6+ convictions: 65 years (subject to multiple ongoing appeals).
- Multi-month subsequent acquittals and reversals across cases.
- Imprisoned continuously since 5 August 2023.

## PTI under the 2023-2025 crackdown

### 22 December 2023 electoral symbol removal
- 22 December 2023: Election Commission of Pakistan removed PTI's electoral symbol (cricket bat) over alleged "irregularities" in PTI intra-party elections (a 13 December 2023 ECP requirement that PTI had not complied with per ECP).
- Multi-month subsequent appeal in Islamabad High Court and Pakistan Supreme Court.
- 13 January 2024: Pakistan Supreme Court upheld ECP decision.

### 8 February 2024 election
- PTI-backed independents won 93 of 266 directly elected NA seats — largest single bloc but unable to form government.
- PML-N (75) and PPP (54) formed coalition government.
- The Sunni Ittehad Council (SIC) — to which PTI-backed independents subsequently joined — was denied reserved seats by the Election Commission of Pakistan and the Pakistan Supreme Court ruling.

### November 2024 Islamabad protests
- 24 November 2024: PTI-led march on Islamabad led by Bushra Bibi from Peshawar.
- 25-26 November 2024: thousands of PTI supporters reached D-Chowk, Islamabad central area.
- 26 November 2024: post-midnight Rangers-led crackdown produced multi-PTI-worker deaths (PTI claimed 12; government denied; documented deaths contested).
- Subsequent multi-month detentions.

### July 2025 PTI protest campaign
- July 2025: PTI announced 90-day street-level protest campaign across Pakistan.
- Rallies and road blockades in multiple provinces.
- Khyber Pakhtunkhwa then-CM Ali Amin Gandapur led announcements.
- Second anniversary of Khan's jailing in August 2025: nationwide rallies; over 240 arrests.

### Documented international response
- UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention 2024 opinion: Khan's detention violates international law and lacks legal basis; Khan should be released and compensated.
- UN human rights experts condemned conditions of Khan's imprisonment.
- Reporters Without Borders multi-year reporting on Pakistani journalists' suppression.
- Amnesty International multi-year reporting.

## PTI political record summary

- 25 April 1996 founding by Imran Khan.
- 1996-2002: less than 1% national vote share; zero NA seats.
- 2002: Khan's first NA seat.
- 2008: PTI boycotted election.
- 2013: 7.6 million votes nationally (second-largest by popular vote); 35 NA seats; KP provincial government.
- 2018: 16.9 million votes (largest); 149 NA seats; coalition federal government with Khan as PM.
- 2022: April ouster of Khan by no-confidence vote.
- 2024: PTI-backed independents 93 of 266 directly elected NA seats; largest single bloc; unable to form government.

## General references
- Wikipedia "Imran Khan"; "Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf"; "Arrest of Imran Khan"; "Imprisonment of Imran Khan"; "Toshakhana reference cases against Imran Khan and Bushra Bibi"; "Cipher case"; "Bushra Bibi"; "May 9 riots."
- Britannica "Imran Khan."
- Dawn, "Imran Khan — from flamboyant cricketer to prime minister" (18 August 2018).
- Time, "Cricket Hero Imran Khan Led Pakistan's Team to Victory. As a Politician, He's Riding a Populist Wave" (28 June 2018).
- Al Jazeera, "What is the cypher case that led to jail term for Pakistan's Imran Khan?" (30 January 2024).
- Business Standard, "Toshakhana case: Pak court suspends Imran Khan, Bushra Bibi jail terms" (1 April 2024).
- The Intercept, August 2023 publication of purported Cipher text.
- The Logical Indian, "Ex-PM Imran Khan and Wife Bushra Bibi Sentenced to 17 Years Each in Toshakhana Corruption Case" (20 December 2025).
- JURIST, "Former Pakistan PM and wife sentenced to 17 years in graft case" (23 December 2025).
- UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention 2024 opinion on Imran Khan.
- Reporters Without Borders, Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch multi-year reporting.

## Sources verification
- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Dossier 27: Sheikh Hasina and the Bangladeshi political-class architecture

## The subject

- Full name: Sheikh Hasina Wazed (Bengali: শেখ হাসিনা ওয়াজেদ).
- Born 28 September 1947 in Tungipara, East Bengal (later East Pakistan, now Bangladesh's Gopalganj District).
- 10th Prime Minister of Bangladesh: first term June 1996 - July 2001; second-through-fifth consecutive terms January 2009 - 5 August 2024.
- Longest-serving Prime Minister in Bangladesh's history (20 years).
- At the time of her resignation in August 2024 she was the world's longest-serving female head of government.
- Daughter of Sheikh Mujibur Rahman — founding leader and first President of Bangladesh (1971-1975).
- Leader / President of the Bangladesh Awami League since 1981 (44 years).
- In exile in India since 5 August 2024.

## Family

- Father: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman ("Bangabandhu" / "Friend of Bengal") — Founding President of Bangladesh. Assassinated 15 August 1975 along with multiple family members.
- Mother: Sheikh Fazilatunnesa Mujib (assassinated 15 August 1975).
- Siblings: three brothers (Sheikh Kamal, Sheikh Jamal, Sheikh Russel — all assassinated 15 August 1975); one sister (Sheikh Rehana, survived because she was with Hasina in West Germany).
- Husband: Dr. M. A. Wazed Miah (1942-2009) — nuclear scientist; died 9 May 2009.
- Son: Sajeeb Ahmed Wazed (Sajeeb Wazed Joy) — ICT expert; advisor to multiple Hasina governments; US-based.
- Daughter: Saima Hossain Wazed — psychologist; subsequent Regional Director of WHO South-East Asia from 1 February 2024.
- Five grandchildren.

## Niece

- Tulip Siddiq — Sheikh Rehana's daughter; UK Labour MP for Hampstead and Highgate since 2015; UK Treasury Minister for Anti-Corruption under PM Keir Starmer from July 2024; resigned 14 January 2025 over multi-year allegations linking her to Hasina family property dealings in London (covered separately).

## 15 August 1975 family massacre

- 15 August 1975: Bangladesh Army officers assassinated Sheikh Mujibur Rahman at his Dhanmondi 32 residence in Dhaka.
- Killed: Mujibur Rahman; wife Fazilatunnesa; sons Sheikh Kamal, Sheikh Jamal, and 10-year-old Sheikh Russel; daughters-in-law Sultana Kamal and Rosy Jamal; brother Sheikh Naser; and multiple aides and family members. Total killed: 18 family members.
- Hasina and Sheikh Rehana survived because they were in West Germany at the time visiting Hasina's husband M.A. Wazed Miah.
- Subsequent exile in India (1975-1981) under Indira Gandhi government protection.
- The 1996-2001 Hasina government enacted the Bangabandhu Murder Trial proceedings; 12 convictions; multi-year executions across 2009-2020.

## Education and early life

- Tungipara primary school; Government Intermediate Girls' College, Azimpur (Dhaka).
- Eden College, Dhaka — vice-president of college Students' Union.
- University of Dhaka — Bachelor of Arts in Bengali Literature 1973.
- 1969 mass upsurge against Ayub Khan: Hasina was active participant as student leader at Eden College.
- 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War: Hasina, her mother, sister, and brother were imprisoned by the Pakistani occupation army at the Dhanmondi residence. Husband M.A. Wazed Miah was also detained.

## Political career 1981-1996

- 1981: elected President of the Bangladesh Awami League in absentia while in exile in New Delhi.
- 17 May 1981: ended six years of exile, returned to Bangladesh.
- 1981-1990: opposition leader during military-ruled period under General Ziaur Rahman (assassinated May 1981) and General Hussain Muhammad Ershad (1982-1990 dictatorship).
- 1986 Parliamentary election: won three seats; elected Leader of the Opposition in Bangladesh's Third Parliament.
- 1990 mass movement against Ershad: Hasina led the opposition mass movement that produced Ershad's December 1990 resignation; announced the constitutional formula for peaceful transfer of power through Articles 51 and 56.
- 1991 election: PM-aspirant; Awami League lost to BNP (Khaleda Zia); Hasina became Leader of the Opposition in the Fifth Parliament.

## First Premiership 1996-2001

- 23 June 1996: became 10th PM of Bangladesh after Awami League victory.
- Achievements per official record: 30-year Ganges Water Sharing Treaty with India; Chittagong Hill Tracts Peace Accord; Bangabandhu Bridge over Jamuna river; multi-year food security programmes; social safety nets including widow/disabled/freedom-fighter allowances; Shanti Nibas for elders; Ashrayan programme for the homeless; One House-One Farm scheme.
- 2001 election: Awami League lost to BNP-Jamaat coalition under Khaleda Zia.
- 15 July 2001: Hasina left office.

## Opposition period 2001-2008

- 2001-2008: Leader of the Opposition.
- 21 August 2004 grenade attack: Awami League rally in Dhaka attacked; 24 party supporters killed including Ivy Rahman (Awami League women's secretary). Hasina survived. October 2018 special-court verdict: BNP vice-chairman Tarique Rahman (in absentia) and former intelligence officials found guilty of orchestrating the attack through abuse of state power. 1 December 2024 Bangladesh High Court (post-Hasina ouster) acquitted Tarique Rahman and others.
- 2004: SAMS Kibria, Hasina's close advisor and former Finance Minister, assassinated in grenade attack in Sylhet.
- 2007-2008: military-backed caretaker government tenure under Lt. Gen. Moeen U Ahmed.
- 2007: Hasina arrested on corruption charges by caretaker government; released July 2008.

## Second-through-fifth Premiership 2009-2024

### 29 December 2008 election
- 29 December 2008 general election: Awami League landslide victory with 230 of 300 parliamentary seats.
- 6 January 2009: Hasina took oath as PM for second term.

### Economic record
- 2009-2024: multi-year GDP growth averaging 6-7% annually; multi-year ready-made garment (RMG) export expansion making Bangladesh the world's second-largest garment exporter after China; remittance-driven foreign exchange architecture; multi-year poverty reduction.
- 2022: GDP growth 7.2%; one of the fastest-growing economies in the world.
- Wealth disparities increased; the wealthiest 10% of the population controlled 41% of total income by 2022.
- Mega-projects: Padma Bridge (opened June 2022; multi-billion-dollar self-financed after World Bank cancellation amid 2012 corruption-allegation controversy); Dhaka Metro Rail (Line 6 opened December 2022); Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant (Russian-built; under construction 2017-onward); Bangabandhu Tunnel under Karnaphuli River (opened October 2023); multi-year power-generation expansion to 11,000+ MW.

### Political record
- 2009 Bangladesh Rifles (BDR) revolt: 25-26 February 2009; mutiny at BDR headquarters at Pilkhana, Dhaka; 74 dead including 57 Bangladesh Army officers killed; multi-year subsequent ICT BDR mutiny trials. The 30 November 2025 re-investigation commission (formed by post-Hasina interim government on 23 December 2024) concluded the incident was a planned operation rather than a spontaneous revolt; alleged involvement of Awami League figures including Sheikh Fazle Noor Taposh (Hasina nephew) and Hasina herself.
- 2010: revived the International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) under the International Crimes (Tribunals) Act 1973 to prosecute 1971 Liberation War crimes. Multi-year ICT trials produced multiple convictions and executions of BNP-Jamaat figures including Jamaat-e-Islami leaders Abdul Quader Mollah (executed December 2013), Mohammad Kamaruzzaman (executed April 2015), Ali Ahsan Mohammad Mujahid (executed November 2015), Salahuddin Quader Chowdhury (BNP; executed November 2015), Motiur Rahman Nizami (Jamaat-e-Islami chief; executed May 2016), and Mir Quasem Ali (Jamaat; executed September 2016).
- 2013: Jamaat-e-Islami banned from electoral participation by Bangladesh Supreme Court for inconsistency with Bangladesh's secular constitution.
- 2013-2014: widespread BNP-Jamaat-led violence opposing the ICT trials; multi-month nationwide protests; hundreds killed.

### Electoral record under fraud allegations
- 5 January 2014 general election: boycotted by BNP and other opposition parties citing absence of caretaker government provision; Awami League won 234 of 300 seats; widely characterised by international observers as not free and fair.
- 30 December 2018 general election: Awami League won 257 of 300 seats with 96% of vote share in contested seats; widely characterised by international observers as marred by vote-rigging, ballot-stuffing, and intimidation; BNP contested but won only 7 seats. Khaleda Zia had been jailed February 2018 in embezzlement and graft cases.
- 7 January 2024 general election: Awami League won 224 of 300 seats; boycotted by main opposition BNP; voter turnout 40% (official) — widely contested by Bangladeshi media and international observers.
- 11 January 2024: Hasina inaugurated for fourth consecutive term and fifth overall.

### Human rights record
- December 2021: US imposed human-rights-related sanctions on the Rapid Action Battalion (RAB — elite paramilitary force) and seven current and former officials for documented hundreds of disappearances and extrajudicial killings since 2009.
- Multi-year documented record of enforced disappearances, extrajudicial killings, and crackdown on opposition political parties, journalists, and civil society.
- Multi-year US, EU, UN, Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International reporting on the governing pattern.

### Foreign policy
- 2023 G20 New Delhi summit: bilateral meeting with PM Narendra Modi (covered in Dossier 21).
- Multi-decade close India relationship under successive Indian governments including the post-2014 Modi government.
- Multi-year Russian, Chinese, and Japanese bilateral economic relationships.
- May 2024 public characterisation by Hasina of "a white country" plotting to topple her government; alleged offer of trouble-free elections if she allowed "a white country" to set up an airbase in Bangladesh.

## The July 2024 quota reform movement and uprising

### 1 July 2024 onward — the protests
- 1 July 2024: students at Dhaka University began protests against the 5 June 2024 Bangladesh High Court reinstatement of a 30% government-jobs quota for descendants of 1971 Liberation War veterans (the so-called "freedom fighter" quota) that the 2018 quota reform movement had previously suspended.
- Protests spread to other universities across Bangladesh through early July.
- 14 July 2024: Hasina public statement characterising the protesters as "razakar" (Bengali for "1971 Pakistani collaborators") — the pejorative term that mobilised opposition.
- 15-16 July 2024: Awami League's Bangladesh Chhatra League (BCL — the AL student wing) attacked protesters on multiple campuses including Dhaka University, killing protester Abu Sayed (Begum Rokeya University, Rangpur); the documented Abu Sayed shooting on 16 July 2024 became the central iconic image of the movement.

### Death toll and crackdown
- 15 July - 5 August 2024: massive nationwide crackdown by Bangladesh Police, RAB, BGB, and the Awami League BCL.
- 19 July 2024: Rampura, Dhaka — at least 11 killed.
- 21 July 2024: Bangladesh Supreme Court reduced the quota system from 30% to 5% — but protests continued and expanded into a movement demanding Hasina's resignation.
- Nationwide internet blackouts; multi-day mobile-services shutdowns.
- 4 August 2024: nationwide internet blackout; indefinite curfew imposed at 6 PM; courts closed; three-day general holiday declared from 5 August.
- 4 August 2024 alone: nearly 100 killed.
- Total deaths estimated by UN OHCHR: up to 1,400 killed between July 15 and August 5, 2024.
- Total injured: up to 25,000 per UN figures (court testimony at ICT).
- Children killed: 66 per Prothom Alo reporting.
- Documented atrocities: Chankharpul massacre (Hasina personally implicated by ICT verdict for ordering); Ashulia immolation killings (5 August 2024 viral video of police officers stacking dead bodies on a van at Ashulia Police Station).

### 5 August 2024 resignation and flight
- 5 August 2024: Hasina resigned as PM around mid-morning.
- 5 August 2024 afternoon: Hasina and Sheikh Rehana fled Dhaka by Bangladesh Air Force helicopter and subsequently by C-130 transport aircraft to Hindon Air Force Base near Delhi, India.
- "Long March to Dhaka" by Students Against Discrimination produced protesters storming the Ganabhaban Prime Minister's official residence in Dhaka.
- 5 August 2024 termed "July 36" in Bangladeshi protest commemoration.
- The Awami League Dhanmondi 32 residence (Sheikh Mujibur Rahman memorial) was burned down by protesters.
- Multi-decade Hasina exile in Delhi commenced 5 August 2024 (continuing as of mid-2026).

## The Yunus interim government

- 5 August 2024 evening: Bangladesh Army Chief of Staff General Waker-uz-Zaman convened meeting with BNP, Jatiya Party, and Jamaat-e-Islami representatives.
- 8 August 2024: Muhammad Yunus (Nobel Peace Prize laureate, 2006) sworn in as Chief Adviser of the interim government (covered in Dossier 28).
- 23 October 2024: Bangladesh Chhatra League banned and designated as terrorist organisation under the Anti-Terrorism Act.
- 23 December 2024: interim government formed seven-member commission to re-investigate the 2009 BDR mutiny.
- May 2025: interim government banned the Awami League's political activities.
- 30 November 2025: BDR mutiny re-investigation report alleged Hasina involvement.

## The ICT proceedings and November 2025 death sentence

- 19 August 2024: ICT initiated investigations into "mass murder" cases against Hasina including charges related to the killing of 450 protesters.
- October 2024: ICT issued arrest warrant for Hasina and 45 others including former ministers.
- November 2024: Hasina failed to appear before tribunal as ordered.
- June 2025: prosecutors filed formal charges; second arrest warrant issued.
- A state lawyer was appointed to defend Hasina in absentia.
- The trial was characterised by Amnesty International, the UN OHCHR, Human Rights Watch, and Hasina's spokesperson Mohammad Ali Arafat as having multi-year fair-trial deficits — the ICT was characterised as having "lack of independence and history of unfair proceedings."
- 17 November 2025: ICT in Dhaka — three-judge panel — found Sheikh Hasina guilty of crimes against humanity on five charges: incitement of murder of protesters; ordering demonstrators be hanged; ordering use of lethal weapons, drones, and helicopters; bearing command responsibility for mass killings including Chankharpul and Ashulia immolation killings; failure to prevent or punish documented atrocities.
- 17 November 2025 sentence: death penalty in absentia.
- Former Home Minister Asaduzzaman Khan Kamal also sentenced to death in absentia.
- Former IGP Chowdhury Abdullah Al-Mamun (the only accused in custody) was pardoned from death penalty after turning state witness and confessing; sentenced to five years' imprisonment.
- Court ordered confiscation of Hasina's and Asaduzzaman's properties in favour of the state.
- 17 November 2025 Hasina statement from Delhi: rejected verdict as "biased and politically motivated"; characterised the ICT as "kangaroo court"; characterised verdicts as having been "made by a rigged tribunal established and presided over by an unelected government with no democratic mandate."
- The verdict was the first time any Bangladeshi head of government, sitting or former, had been given capital punishment through judicial proceedings.
- BNP, Jamaat-e-Islami, and the National Citizen Party (NCP) welcomed the verdict; Awami League leadership in exile rejected it.

## Extradition demands and India response

- November 2025 Bangladeshi government demands India hand over Hasina under the 2013 India-Bangladesh extradition treaty.
- The treaty Article 6 allows extradition refusal "if the offence of which it is requested is an offence of a political character."
- India has not formally responded to multiple Bangladeshi extradition demands across the 2024-2026 period.
- April 2026: subsequent India-opening of bilateral talks on extradition per South China Morning Post reporting.

- Britannica "Sheikh Hasina Wazed"; "Bangladesh."

- Al Jazeera, "Sheikh Hasina convicted of crimes against humanity – what we know" (17 November 2025).
- CNN, "Bangladesh's ousted leader Sheikh Hasina sentenced to death after crimes against humanity conviction" (17 November 2025).
- The Diplomat, "Bangladesh's Ousted Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina Sentenced to Death" (17 November 2025).
- Amnesty International, "Bangladesh: Justice for victims of 2024 massacre not served by death sentence against Sheikh Hasina" (17 November 2025).
- UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) fact-finding report on 2024 Bangladesh atrocities.

- Iowa State Archives of Women's Political Communication profile.
- BBC News, "How Bangladesh's protests ended Sheikh Hasina's 15-year reign" (5 August 2024).
