# Sources — Dossier 28: Xi Jinping

## Biographical foundation

- Born 15 June 1953 in Beijing.
- Father: Xi Zhongxun (15 October 1913 – 24 May 2002) — CCP revolutionary; member of the Eight Elders 1980s; vice-premier; vice-chair Standing Committee of the National People's Congress; one of the architects of the Special Economic Zones in the early 1980s.
- Father purged in 1962 for allegedly supporting a sympathetic novel about the party; imprisoned; further persecuted during Cultural Revolution from 1966.
- Mother: Qi Xin (second wife of Xi Zhongxun).
- Older sisters: Qi Qiaoqiao (born 1949) and Qi An'an (born 1952). Half-sister Xi Heping died during the Cultural Revolution, most likely by suicide under duress.
- Educated at the elite Beijing Bayi (August 1st) School and Beijing No. 25 School in the early 1960s.
- January 1969 (age 15): sent down to Liangjiahe village in Yan'an Prefecture, Shaanxi Province under Mao's "Up to the Mountains and Down to the Villages" movement.
- Made ten unsuccessful applications to join the CCP before being admitted in early 1974.
- Served as local Party branch chairman of Liangjiahe Brigade.
- 1975-1979: studied chemical engineering at Tsinghua University as a worker-peasant-soldier student.

## Early career 1979-2007

- 1979: secretary to General Geng Biao (then concurrently vice premier, defense minister, and secretary of military affairs commission); Geng Biao had been purged alongside Xi's father during Cultural Revolution.
- 1982: sent to Hebei province as county-level party boss (Zhengding County).
- 1985: visited US as part of corn delegation from Hebei; traveled to Iowa (stayed two nights with American family south of Des Moines), Oregon, California.
- August 1999: governor of Fujian (after string of provincial officials caught in graft dragnet).
- 1999-2002: governor of Fujian (17-year tenure in Fujian overall from earlier deputy positions).
- 2002: party secretary of Zhejiang. Province reportedly averaged 13-14% GDP growth per year during his tenure.
- March 2007: party secretary of Shanghai (replacing Chen Liangyu, who had been fired and investigated for pension fund mishandling).
- October 2007: elected to 17th Politburo Standing Committee (sixth-ranked of nine members); ahead of Li Keqiang.

## Vice presidency and ascent 2008-2012

- March 2008: elected Vice President of the People's Republic of China.
- October 2010: named Vice Chairman of the Central Military Commission (CMC).
- November 2012: 18th Party Congress — elected General Secretary of the CCP, succeeded Hu Jintao; assumed Chairman of CMC.
- 14 March 2013: named President of the PRC by 12th National People's Congress.

## Constitutional consolidation 2018

- March 2018: 13th National People's Congress passed constitutional amendments including removal of term limits for the President and Vice President. Created the National Supervisory Commission.

## Third and fourth terms 2022-

- October 2022: 20th Party Congress — Xi unanimously elected to third term as CCP General Secretary. 20th Politburo Standing Committee announced consisting of seven members all loyal to Xi: Xi Jinping, Li Qiang, Zhao Leji, Wang Huning, Cai Qi, Ding Xuexiang, Li Xi.
- 10 March 2023: secured unprecedented third five-year term as President of China; sole candidate, received votes of all 2,952 NPC delegates.
- 2027: 21st Party Congress expected to begin Xi's fourth term as General Secretary and Chairman of the CMC.
- 2032: Xi's fourth term would end if completed. Xi would be 79.
- 2035: Xi's announced midpoint year for "building China into a great modern socialist country in all respects" by 2049 ("great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation").

## Anti-corruption campaign and PLA purges

- January 2018: launched three-year Special Campaign to Crack Down on Organized Crime and Eliminate Evil (through 2020).
- July 2020: CCP launched campaign to educate and rectify political and legal teams.
- Since 2023: major military anti-corruption campaign.
- 27 June 2024: Li Shangfu (former Defense Minister) expelled from CCP.
- 28 November 2024: Miao Hua (CMC member and Director of CMC Political Work Department) suspended for investigation.
- Wei Fenghe (former Defense Minister): purged.
- He Weidong (CMC Vice Chairman): purged.
- Ma Xingrui (former Xinjiang Party Secretary and Politburo member): placed under investigation in 2026.
- Bloomberg October 2025: at least 14 generals out of 79 appointed under Xi's leadership ousted.

## PLA modernization

- 2018: People's Armed Police (PAP) placed under sole control of CMC (previously joint with Ministry of Public Security).
- 2024 PLA reforms: Strategic Support Force dissolved; Aerospace Force, Cyberspace Force, and Information Support Force established as new arms of the PLA.
- Chinese military budget more than doubled since 2012; reached record US$282 billion in 2026.
- Aircraft carriers: Shandong (2019) and Fujian (2025) entered service under Xi.

## Economic record

- Chinese GDP: ¥54.8 trillion (US$8.7 trillion) in 2012 → ¥140.2 trillion (US$20.1 trillion) in 2024.
- Chinese nominal GDP per capita: ¥40,431 (US$6,408) in 2012 → ¥99,665 in 2024.
- 2015: launched Double First-Class Construction higher-education programme (successor to Project 211 and Project 985).
- 2015: Chinese stock market bubble popped; Xi used state forces to fix it.
- Made in China 2025 plan: aimed at self-reliance in key technologies; majority of goals considered achieved by 2024.
- Since 2018 trade war: emphasis on "self-reliance" especially in technology.

## International posture

- 22 October 2015: state visit to United Kingdom (Cameron government). Drinks pint of beer with Cameron at The Plough pub, Princes Risborough.
- 8 June 2013: meeting with Obama at Rancho Mirage, California.
- 12 November 2014: announces with Obama bilateral climate agreement.
- October 2024: vows "reunification" with Taiwan, using force if necessary.
- 16 May 2024: welcomes Putin for two-day state visit to Beijing. Joint statement on energy, trade, security, geopolitics, references to Ukraine, Taiwan, Middle East.
- 11 April 2025: makes first public comments on escalating US trade war; strikes defiant note.
- 3 September 2025: Xi, Putin, Kim Jong Un stand side by side at military parade in Tiananmen Square commemorating end of WWII — unprecedented solidarity display against US and allies.
- May 2026: Xi-Trump summit; China agrees to boost US beef and poultry trade.
- 16-17 May 2026: Trump characterised Taiwan as "good negotiating chip" with China.

## South and East China Sea

- Since taking power: China rapidly building and militarising islands in South China Sea; Central Party School's *Study Times* said decision was personally taken by Xi.
- Sino-Indian relations: 2013 Depsang standoff; 2017 Doklam standoff; 2020 deadly LAC clash with soldier deaths.
- 2025: relations with Japan deteriorated after PM Sanae Takaichi's comments on potential defence of Taiwan.

## Personal

- First marriage: Ke Lingling, daughter of Ke Hua (former PRC ambassador to UK). Marriage produced no children. Divorced.
- Second marriage: Peng Liyuan (1 September 1987). Famous Chinese folk and opera singer; held rank of major general in PLA. Served as president of PLA General Political Department Song and Dance Troupe and president of PLA Art Institute.
- One daughter: Xi Mingze (born 1992). Undergraduate at Harvard University 2010-14 (psychology). Pursued graduate studies in Beijing.

## Surrounding apparatus

- Li Qiang (now Premier): served as Xi's chief of staff 2004-2007 when Li was secretary-general of Zhejiang Party Committee.
- Wang Qishan: close friend of Xi since both were "sent-down youths" in neighbouring counties in Yan'an over 40 years ago.
- Wang's father-in-law Yao Yilin was major supporter of Jiang Zemin.
- Politburo Standing Committee 20th (2022): Xi, Li Qiang, Zhao Leji, Wang Huning, Cai Qi, Ding Xuexiang, Li Xi — all Xi loyalists.

## Succession question

- Ding Xuexiang (born 1962): youngest of 7 PSC members; often mentioned as potential successor; would be 65 in 2027, 70 by 2032.
- All other PSC members born in 1950s; will be in mid-70s by Xi's fourth term ending in 2032.
- "Seven up, eight down" (qishang baxia) rule: PSC members 67 or younger may stay; 68 or older retire. Has become largely nominal in recent years.
- Politicians born in late 1960s: would have been university students during 1989 Tiananmen Square protests; viewed critically by Xi administration.
- Provincial party secretaries born in 1970s being appointed as deputy secretaries: Zhuge Yujie (Hubei), Shi Guanghui (Inner Mongolia), Zhu Zhongming (Shanghai), Yang Jinbai (Hainan), Liu Xiaotao (Jiangsu), Guo Ningning (Fujian), Lu Dongliang (Shanxi). Expected by some to be promoted to Politburo or PSC by 2027.

## Xinjiang and Uyghurs

- Multi-year mass internment programme in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region since 2017.
- US State Department January 2021 designated as "genocide and crimes against humanity"; UK Parliament April 2021 voted to characterise as genocide; multiple subsequent state designations.
- August 2022 UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights report found "serious human rights violations" possibly constituting "crimes against humanity."
- Multi-year multi-state corporate-supply-chain pressure including 2021 Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act in US.

## Hong Kong

- 1 July 2020: National Security Law for Hong Kong imposed by NPC Standing Committee, bypassing Hong Kong Legislative Council.
- Multi-year subsequent arrests of pro-democracy figures including Jimmy Lai, Apple Daily executives, Stand News.
- 2024-2025: Hong Kong Article 23 domestic security legislation passed; further protest-suppression architecture established.

- Britannica biography of Xi Jinping.
- CNN Xi Jinping Fast Facts.
- Brookings China leadership profiles.
- *China Leadership Monitor* "Plotting the Course to Xi's Fourth Term" August 2025.
- The Diplomat "Who Will Succeed Xi Jinping?" February 2026.
- The Diplomat "China's Political Calendar as a Guide to Leadership Succession" August 2025.

- Robert Suettinger, *The Conscience of the Party* (biography of Hu Yaobang).
- Ian Johnson, "Lessons of the Father," *New York Review of Books*, 24 July 2025.
- *Financial Times* PLA purges reporting.
- Bloomberg October 2025 PLA general purge data.
- Time magazine 2017 Xi succession analysis.
- *Christian Science Monitor* 2012 Xi succession profile.

# Sources — Dossier 29: The Chinese Politburo Standing Committee

## 20th PSC composition (October 2022 - present)

1. Xi Jinping (1st rank) — covered in Dossier 28
2. Li Qiang (2nd rank) — Premier of the State Council
3. Zhao Leji (3rd rank) — Chairman of the Standing Committee of the NPC
4. Wang Huning (4th rank) — Chairman of the CPPCC
5. Cai Qi (5th rank) — First Secretary of the CCP Central Secretariat
6. Ding Xuexiang (6th rank) — Executive Vice Premier of the State Council
7. Li Xi (7th rank) — Secretary of the CCDI

## Li Qiang (Premier)

- Born July 1959 in Rui'an County, Wenzhou, Zhejiang Province.
- Joined CCP in 1983.
- Undergraduate education in agricultural mechanisation at Zhejiang Institute of Agriculture's Ningbo campus (1978-82).
- First provincial government job: civil affairs department, providing welfare to rural poor.
- 2002: appointed party boss of Wenzhou at age 43 — youngest person to take that position in over three decades. Established reputation as supporter of private sector.
- 2003: joined Zhejiang provincial party committee, less than a year after Xi took control of it.
- 2004-2007: promoted to secretary general of Zhejiang Party Committee — Xi's chief of staff.
- Xi's chief of staff until Xi left for Shanghai in 2007.
- End of 2012: Zhejiang governor (after Xi took over as CCP General Secretary).
- June 2016: party secretary of Jiangsu province.
- October 2017: appointed party secretary of Shanghai (15 months after Jiangsu appointment).
- 2017-2022 Shanghai tenure:
- Opened Shanghai Stock Exchange STAR Market.
- Oversaw Tesla Gigafactory Shanghai construction (operational 2019); first wholly foreign-owned car company in China.
- Eased internal-migrant residency permit requirements.
- Created five new towns to address land supply shortage.
- 2022: two-month Shanghai COVID lockdown caused major economic damage; Li was criticised but promoted to PSC anyway.
- Per WSJ, Li was one of the few people in top leadership who wanted China to introduce Western mRNA vaccines.
- October 2022: promoted to 20th PSC at rank 2.
- March 2023: appointed 8th Premier of the State Council (succeeding Li Keqiang).
- Considered part of the "New Zhijiang Army" — Xi's Zhejiang-based faction.
- Wife worked in Zhejiang transportation bureau before retirement. One daughter who studied in Australia.
- June 2024: four-day visit to Australia, first by Chinese leader of his seniority since 2017.

## Zhao Leji (NPC Chairman)

- Born March 1957 in Xining, Qinghai Province (Shaanxi native ancestrally).
- Age 69 as of 2026.
- Long career in Qinghai province before rising to national prominence.
- Youngest ever provincial governor in China — became Party Secretary at age 42.
- Subsequently Party Secretary of Shaanxi.
- 2012-2017: head of CCP Organization Department.
- 2017-2022: Secretary of Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) — head of party's top anti-corruption watchdog. Succeeded Wang Qishan in this role.
- October 2022: retained on 20th PSC, promoted to rank 3.
- March 2023: succeeded Li Zhanshu as Chairman of Standing Committee of National People's Congress.
- Connections to Xi via Shaanxi ancestral province and Qinghai career.

## Wang Huning (CPPCC Chairman, chief ideologue)

- Born October 1955 in Shanghai (native Shanghainese).
- Age 70 as of 2026.
- Academic background — never held provincial leadership role.
- Long career as one of China's top political theorists.
- Helped craft "China Dream" framework and "Xi Jinping Thought."
- Has served as loyal advisor to three paramount leaders: Jiang Zemin, Hu Jintao, Xi Jinping. Started as Jiang acolyte.
- 2012-2017: 19th Politburo member; Director of Central Policy Research Office.
- 2017-2022: First Secretary of Central Secretariat under 19th PSC.
- October 2022: retained on 20th PSC, promoted to rank 4.
- March 2023: appointed Chairman of CPPCC (Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference) — party's advisory body.

## Cai Qi (First Secretary of Central Secretariat)

- Born December 1955 in Yongtai County, Fujian Province.
- Age 70 as of 2026.
- Spent 30+ years in Fujian and Zhejiang — overlapping with Xi's tenures in both provinces.
- 2014-2017: rose rapidly through positions in Beijing.
- 2017-2022: Party Secretary of Beijing.
- Credited with establishment of Xiong'an special economic zone.
- Responsible for hosting Beijing Winter Olympics 2022.
- October 2022: promoted to 20th PSC at rank 5; elected First Secretary of the CCP Central Secretariat — the party's organizational nerve centre.
- Characterised by analysts (Richard McGregor, Lowy Institute 2024) as the most powerful person on the PSC after Xi — "Xi's enforcer."
- Personal relationships with Xi rather than independent political achievements characterise his promotion.

## Ding Xuexiang (Executive Vice Premier)

- Born September 1962 in Nantong, Jiangsu Province.
- Age 63 as of 2026 — youngest member of 20th PSC.
- Engineer by training (materials science).
- Made his career through Shanghai municipal apparatus from the 1990s.
- Met Xi while serving in Shanghai Party Committee when Xi was Shanghai Party Secretary in 2007.
- 2013: moved to Beijing as Xi's personal secretary (Director of Office of General Secretary).
- 2017-2022: Director of CCP General Office (Xi's chief of staff) — sweeping control over information and access to officials.
- Often the few officials attending sensitive meetings alongside Xi — sobriquets "Xi's alter ego" and "Xi's chief of staff."
- Never served as a provincial-level party boss or governor — unusual for PSC promotion.
- October 2022: promoted to 20th PSC at rank 6.
- March 2023: appointed Executive Vice Premier — first-ranked vice premier; oversees development and reform, public finance, taxation, environment.
- Often mentioned as potential successor to Xi or to Li Qiang at 21st Party Congress 2027.

## Li Xi (CCDI Secretary)

- Born October 1956 in Liangdang, Gansu Province (Shaanxi connection).
- Age 69 as of 2026.
- Long career in Gansu, Shaanxi, Shanghai, Liaoning, Guangdong.
- 2017-2022: Party Secretary of Guangdong province — spearheaded development of Greater Bay Area economic region (a favourite project of Xi's).
- Family connection: longtime ally of Xi family through Shaanxi ancestral connections.
- October 2022: promoted to 20th PSC at rank 7.
- October 2022: appointed Secretary of CCDI (succeeding Zhao Leji) — head of the party's anti-corruption and supervision body.

## Politburo composition (24 members)

- Reduced from 25 to 24 members at 20th Party Congress (October 2022).
- Notable removals despite age eligibility: Li Keqiang (CYL faction; died 27 October 2023 in Shanghai), Wang Yang (CYL), Hu Chunhua (CYL), Chen Quanguo. CYL = Communist Youth League / Tuanpai faction.
- 17 non-PSC members; 13 are new.
- None close associates of Hu Jintao from CYL.
- Princelings declining: 4 of 7 PSC members were princelings in 18th PSC; only 1 (Xi) in 20th PSC.
- 10 princelings in 20th Central Committee vs 41 in 18th Central Committee.

## Other key Politburo members

- Zhang Youxia — CMC Vice Chairman; age 75 (2026); Shaanxi native princeling; widely characterised as second-most- PLA operator; reportedly under investigation in 2026.
- He Weidong — CMC Vice Chairman; age 69 (2026); purged.
- Wang Yi — CCP Central Foreign Affairs Commission Office Director; age 72 (2026); de facto top foreign-policy operator.
- He Lifeng — Vice Premier; age 71 (2026).
- Chen Wenqing — Secretary of CPLAC (Central Politics and Law Affairs Commission); age 66 (2026); law-enforcement chief.
- Li Shulei — Director of Propaganda Department; age 62 (2026).
- Shi Taifeng — Director of United Front Work Department / Director of CCP Organization Department; age 69 (2026); personnel chief.
- Ma Xingrui — former Xinjiang Party Secretary; placed under investigation 2026.

## Network analysis

- Xi has direct links to 49 of 220+ Central Committee members (Hong Kong Post 2022 analysis).
- Li Qiang: 39 connections.
- Cai Qi: 42 connections.
- Li Xi: 46 connections.
- Ding Xuexiang: 15 connections (lowest among PSC).
- Wang Huning: 15 connections.
- Zhao Leji: 21 connections.

# Sources — Dossier 30: The Hong Kong Political Class

## Constitutional foundation

- 1 July 1997: Hong Kong handover from UK to PRC. Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR) established under "one country, two systems" framework.
- 1997 Basic Law: HKSAR mini-constitution; supposed to operate until 2047.
- Article 23 Basic Law: required HKSAR to enact own national security legislation. Not implemented until 2024.

## Chief Executives 1997-2026

1. **Tung Chee-hwa** (1997-2005) — first CE; shipping magnate; forced out before completing second term; born December 1937.
2. **Donald Tsang** (2005-2012) — career civil servant; subsequently convicted of misconduct in 2017 (overturned partly 2019); born October 1944.
3. **Leung Chun-ying** (2012-2017) — surveyor; pro-Beijing; born August 1954.
4. **Carrie Lam** (2017-2022) — career civil servant; oversaw 2019 protests and 2020 National Security Law; born May 1957; sanctioned by US August 2020.
5. **John Lee Ka-chiu** (2022-present) — career police officer; sole candidate 2022 election; born December 1957; sanctioned by US August 2020.

## John Lee Ka-chiu — biography

- Born 7 December 1957 in Hong Kong; ancestral roots Panyu, Guangzhou.
- Working-class family background; grew up in So Uk Estate, Kowloon.
- Graduated Wah Yan College, Kowloon (Roman Catholic Jesuit secondary school).
- Obtained 4A grades in Hong Kong Certificate of Education Examination and 1A in HKAL Examination.
- Offered admission to undergraduate engineering programme at University of Hong Kong.
- Declined HKU offer to join the Hong Kong Police Force in 1977 (age 19) as Probationary Inspector after being robbed during a school mountain-climbing trip.
- Master's degree in Public Policy and Administration from Charles Sturt University, Australia (obtained while working in police force).
- Previously held British citizenship; relinquished August 2012 to assume Under Secretary for Security position.
- Wife and two children continue to hold British citizenship — making Lee still eligible for right of abode in the UK.
- Catholic (publicly confirmed May 2022).
- Awarded Silver Bauhinia Star by Hong Kong government in 2017.
- Nicknamed "Pikachu" by Hong Kong anti-establishment faction (resembles his Cantonese name "Lee Ka-chiu").

## John Lee — police career

- 1977: joined Hong Kong Police Force as Probationary Inspector.
- 1994-1995: cracked drinks-spiking gang case (gang injected drugs into paper-packaged drinks, committed 20+ crimes in 6 months; leader subsequently sentenced to 14 years).
- 1998: head of Organized Crime and Triad Bureau; led operation against Zhang Ziqiang (criminal with multiple looting, kidnapping, murder charges). 17 January 1998 explosives seizure (800 kg explosives, 2,000+ detonators in New Territories village). 22 July 1998 arrest of Zhang and 17 suspects; subsequent arrest of 13 more.
- 2003: Assistant Commissioner.
- 2010: Deputy Commissioner of Police.
- Career positions: Assistant Commissioner (Crime), Director of Crime and Security, Deputy Commissioner (Management).

## John Lee — government career

- 2012: appointed Under Secretary for Security under Leung Chun-ying government.
- 2017: promoted to Secretary for Security under Carrie Lam government.
- 2019: oversaw security response to anti-extradition-bill protests; permitted use of tear gas, rubber bullets, water cannons, and at times live ammunition. Was instrumental in pushing for the extradition bill that triggered the protests.
- 2020: oversaw implementation of National Security Law (imposed by NPC Standing Committee, effective 1 July 2020).
- 7 August 2020: sanctioned by US Treasury OFAC for "undermining Hong Kong's autonomy" — one of 11 individuals sanctioned (alongside Carrie Lam).
- June 2021: appointed Chief Secretary for Administration (number two position in HK government), succeeding Matthew Cheung.
- 6 April 2022: resigned as Chief Secretary to enter Chief Executive race.
- 8 May 2022: won Chief Executive Election as sole candidate; received 1,416 votes (~99%) from 1,500-member Election Committee selected by Beijing.
- 20 May 2022: appointed by Central People's Government as 6th-term Chief Executive.
- 1 July 2022: assumed office.

## Lee administration — major events

- 15 June 2022: government restructuring package passed by LegCo; new deputies for Chief Secretary, Financial Secretary, Secretary for Justice. Two new bureaux created.
- December 2022: Lee dropped pandemic "0+0" restrictions after Beijing's "green light"; did not consult Command and Coordination Group he had created.
- February 2023: trip to Middle East; encouraged Saudi Aramco to pursue secondary IPO in Hong Kong.
- July 2023: government announced bounties for 8 democrats living overseas; Lee said "we will pursue them for the rest of our lives even if they run to the ends of the earth"; called them "street rats" who should be "avoided at all costs."
- 30 January 2024: public consultation began for Safeguarding National Security Ordinance.
- 8 March 2024: Safeguarding National Security Ordinance proposed (fulfilling Article 23 obligation).
- 23 March 2024: Safeguarding National Security Ordinance signed into law — fulfilling Article 23 obligation, expanding national security enforcement powers beyond 2020 NSL.
- 5 December 2024: cabinet reshuffle began; dismissed Secretaries for Culture and Transport.

## 2020 National Security Law architecture

- 30 June 2020 evening: passed by NPC Standing Committee in Beijing, bypassing Hong Kong Legislative Council.
- Effective 1 July 2020 (23rd anniversary of handover).
- Four offences: secession, subversion, terrorism, collusion with foreign forces.
- Maximum penalty: life imprisonment.
- Extraterritorial reach.
- Office for Safeguarding National Security established in Hong Kong directly under Beijing's Central People's Government.

## Major NSL prosecutions

- Jimmy Lai (founder of Apple Daily; born December 1947): arrested August 2020; multi-year imprisonment; trial began December 2023; HK$5.8 billion in assets frozen; faces life imprisonment.
- Joshua Wong, Agnes Chow, Nathan Law (Demosistō): convictions multi-year subsequent.
- Apple Daily forced to close June 2021 after assets frozen.
- Stand News forced to close December 2021.
- Citizen News forced to close January 2022.
- 47 democrats arrested January 2021 in mass arrest (Hong Kong 47); convicted May 2024; sentencing November 2024.
- Subsequent arrests of 290+ individuals under NSL by 2024.

## 2024 Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (Article 23)

- Expanded NSL offences:
- Treason
- Insurrection
- Sedition
- Theft of state secrets and espionage
- Sabotage
- External interference
- Extended detention without charge to 16 days.
- Restricted access to lawyers during detention.
- Maximum penalty life imprisonment.
- Operates alongside 2020 NSL.

## Hong Kong economic and demographic context

- Population: 7.5 million (2024).
- GDP per capita: $52,000 (2024).
- Stock market: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing — among 5-7 largest global exchanges by market cap.
- Multi-year departure of professionals: 200,000-500,000 emigrants 2020-2024 per multiple estimates; UK BN(O) visa scheme operating since January 2021 has processed 200,000+ Hong Kong applications.
- Multi-year subsequent decline in foreign business presence (US Chamber of Commerce HK, EU Chamber of Commerce in Hong Kong, multiple multinational regional HQ relocations to Singapore, Tokyo, Dubai).

## Other key Hong Kong political-class operators

- **Carrie Lam** (born 1957): predecessor; 4th CE 2017-2022; oversaw 2019 protests and 2020 NSL imposition; sanctioned by US August 2020; subsequently appointed as a CCP Central Committee member in 2022.
- **Regina Ip** (born 1950): former Security Secretary; Convenor of Executive Council 2022-present.
- **Andrew Leung** (born 1951): President of Legislative Council 2022-present.
- **Erick Tsang** (born 1970): Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs since 2020; oversaw 2021 electoral changes.
- **Chris Tang** (born 1965): Secretary for Security since 2021; former Commissioner of Police 2019-2021; oversaw 2019 protest crackdown.
- **Paul Chan** (born 1955): Financial Secretary since 2017; PwC career background.
- **Eric Chan** (born 1961): Chief Secretary for Administration since 2022; career civil servant.
- **Joe Chow Yat-ming**: Commissioner of Police since April 2025 (replaced Raymond Siu).

## Pro-democracy political-class architecture (mostly dismantled)

- **Martin Lee** (born 1938): founder of Democratic Party; "father of democracy"; convicted April 2021 (unauthorised assembly); subsequent multi-year arrests.
- **Jimmy Lai** (born 1947): Apple Daily founder; awaiting the verdict in his NSL trial.
- **Joshua Wong** (born 1996): student activist; multiple imprisonments.
- **Agnes Chow** (born 1996): student activist; fled to Canada December 2023.
- **Nathan Law** (born 1993): fled to UK; subsequent bounty placed.
- **Benny Tai** (born 1964): legal scholar; Occupy Central co-founder; multiple imprisonments.
- **Joseph Zen** (born 1932): retired Cardinal; arrested under NSL May 2022.

## Elections architecture changes

- Pre-2021 LegCo: 70 seats including 35 directly-elected and 35 functional constituencies.
- March 2021: NPC imposed "improvement" of HK electoral system requiring "patriots" to govern.
- May 2021: LegCo restructured to 90 seats: 20 directly-elected, 30 functional constituencies, 40 selected by Election Committee.
- 2021 LegCo election: turnout 30.2% — record low; pro-democracy candidates excluded.
- 2023 District Council elections: pro-democracy candidates excluded; turnout 27.5% — historic low.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Dossier 31: The Taiwan Political Class

## Constitutional and historical foundation

- Taiwan retroceded to Republic of China sovereignty 25 October 1945 after 50 years of Japanese rule (1895-1945).
- ROC government relocated to Taiwan 1949 after losing Chinese Civil War to CCP.
- Martial law 1949-1987 under KMT one-party rule.
- 28 February 1947 incident ("228 Incident") and subsequent White Terror — estimated 18,000-28,000 killed across KMT crackdown.
- Democratic transition: martial law lifted July 1987; the first opposition party (DPP) was founded in September 1986, and the first direct presidential election took place in March 1996.
- Post-democratization presidents:
- Lee Teng-hui (KMT, but Taiwanese native): 1988-2000 (succeeded Chiang Ching-kuo).
- Chen Shui-bian (DPP): 2000-2008.
- Ma Ying-jeou (KMT): 2008-2016.
- Tsai Ing-wen (DPP): 2016-2024 (first female president).
- Lai Ching-te (DPP): 2024-present.

## Lai Ching-te (William Lai) — biography

- Born 6 October 1959 in Wanli, then Taipei County (now Wanli District, New Taipei City).
- Working-class family: father was a coal miner who died in a gas-leak accident in 1960 when Lai was an infant. His mother raised six children alone by doing odd jobs.
- First student from Wanli Junior High School to be admitted to Taipei Municipal Chien Kuo High School.
- National Taiwan University: initially studied veterinary medicine, switched to physical medicine and rehabilitation; bachelor's degree 1984.
- Worked as tutor to pay for college.
- After military service, attended National Cheng Kung University in Tainan; medical degree 1991.
- Chief resident, National Cheng Kung University Hospital, where he led a Tainan physicians' association.
- 1994: first political involvement — head of Tainan physicians' association supporting DPP gubernatorial candidate Chen Ding-nan.
- 1996: ran for Tainan City representative in the National Assembly; won.
- 1998: elected to Legislative Yuan; subsequently re-elected three times (served 1999-2010); recognised by Citizen Congress Watch as "Best Legislator" four years in a row.
- 2003: Master of Public Health, Harvard University.
- 27 November 2010: won Tainan mayoral election with 60.41% (after 2010 Tainan municipal reorganisation amalgamating Tainan City and Tainan County).
- 25 December 2010: took office as first mayor of Tainan.
- 29 November 2014: re-elected mayor with 73% — highest percentage in any county or city election since 1987 lifting of martial law.
- 2015: declared himself a "pragmatic worker for Taiwanese independence."
- 2015: localised constitutional crisis — boycotted the Tainan City Council for nearly 8 months over Speaker Lee Chuan-chiao's vote-buying allegations. 4 August 2015: Control Yuan impeached Lai for refusing to submit to interpellation.
- 8 September 2017: appointed 26th Premier of the ROC by Tsai Ing-wen.
- December 2017: Labour Standards Act amendments under Lai sparked protests — Lai's "doing good deeds" comment about longer working hours became a subject of national mockery. The reform increased allowable overtime from 46 to 138 hours over 3 months.
- 11 January 2019: resigned as Premier after DPP losses in November 2018 local elections.
- 18 November 2019: lost DPP presidential primary to Tsai Ing-wen.
- 11 January 2020: elected Vice President as Tsai's running mate.
- 12 April 2023: DPP announced Lai as 2024 presidential candidate.
- August 2023: official visit to Paraguay with US stopovers.
- 13 January 2024: won presidential election with 40.1% of vote (5.59 million votes). Hou Yu-ih (KMT): 33.49% (4.67 million). Ko Wen-je (TPP): 26.46% (3.69 million).
- 20 May 2024: inaugurated as 8th President of the ROC. Running mate: Hsiao Bi-khim (former de facto ambassador to the US).

## 2024 election context

- First time any party has won three consecutive presidential elections post-democratisation.
- DPP lost Legislative Yuan majority: 113 seats splitting between the DPP (51), KMT (52) (plus 2 Blue independents), TPP 8, others.
- Han Kuo-yu (KMT, 2020 KMT presidential candidate) elected President of Legislative Yuan with TPP support.
- China repeatedly framed election as choice between "peace and war"; characterised Lai as a "dangerous separatist" and "Taiwan independence" advocate.

## Lai presidency 2024-2026

- 20 May 2024 inauguration address: stated that the "Republic of China and the People's Republic of China are not subordinate to each other."
- 23-24 May 2024: PLA "Joint Sword-2024A" exercises around Taiwan in response to inaugural address.
- May 2024: KMT-TPP coalition passed parliamentary bills strengthening Legislative Yuan investigation/review powers; sparked DPP opposition and mass protests ("Bluebird Movement").
- June 2024: Constitutional Court upheld Lai's challenge declaring bills unconstitutional.
- June 2024: Taiwan-Thailand investment agreement signed under New Southbound Policy continuation.
- September 2024: TPP leader Ko Wen-je arrested and investigated for bribery; characterised by TPP as political persecution. Huang Kuo-chang became new TPP leader.
- December 2024: 7 Constitutional Court justices' terms expired, and the Legislative Yuan rejected Lai's new nominees; Constitutional Court entered functional paralysis.
- 2025: KMT-TPP legislative coalition passed revisions to Constitutional Interpretation Act, Election and Recall Act, Budget Act, restricting government power.
- 2025: Lai announced plans to bring back military trials for active-duty soldiers; designated China as "foreign hostile force" under the Anti-Infiltration Act.
- 23 December 2025: KMT-TPP coalition confirmed impeachment motion at plenary session.
- 26 December 2025: impeachment process initiation motion passed (all KMT and TPP in favour; all DPP against).
- 16-17 May 2026: Trump characterised Taiwan as "good negotiating chip" with China.
- 17 May 2026: Lai defended US arms purchases to Taiwan.
- 3 May 2026: Lai's trip to Eswatini (one of remaining Taiwan diplomatic allies) delayed by a lack of overflight clearance.

## DPP architecture

- Founded 28 September 1986 (during martial law).
- Founding figures: Hsu Hsin-liang, Lin Yi-hsiung, Shih Ming-teh, Yao Chia-wen, others.
- Tsai Ing-wen (born August 1956): Chair 2008-2018; President 2016-2024.
- Lai serves concurrently as DPP Chair since 2023.
- Internal factions: New Tide (Lai's faction); Tsai/Su system; others.
- The party holds 51 Legislative Yuan seats (out of 113 total).

## KMT (Kuomintang) architecture

- Founded 1894 by Sun Yat-sen.
- Ruled mainland China 1927-1949, Taiwan 1945-2000.
- Eric Chu (born June 1961): Chair since October 2021; previously presidential candidate 2016.
- Hou Yu-ih (born June 1957): Mayor of New Taipei City 2018-present; 2024 KMT presidential candidate; former Director-General of National Police Agency.
- Han Kuo-yu (born June 1957): President of Legislative Yuan since 2024; former Mayor of Kaohsiung 2018-2020 (recalled); 2020 KMT presidential candidate.
- Johnny Chiang (born February 1972): KMT former chair; Vice President of Legislative Yuan under Han.
- Ma Ying-jeou (born July 1950): President 2008-2016; visited China in 2023 and 2024 (first former ROC president to visit mainland).
- The party holds 52 Legislative Yuan seats, plus 2 Blue-leaning independents.

## TPP (Taiwan People's Party) architecture

- Founded 6 August 2019 by Ko Wen-je.
- Ko Wen-je (born August 1959): former Mayor of Taipei 2014-2022; 2024 presidential candidate (26.46%); arrested September 2024 for bribery investigation.
- Huang Kuo-chang (born March 1973): current TPP Chair (took over after Ko's arrest); former NPP convenor; legal scholar.
- Cynthia Wu: 2024 vice-presidential candidate; legislator since November 2022.
- 8 Legislative Yuan seats — kingmaker position.

## Cross-Strait posture

- Taiwan is recognised as a sovereign state by 12 states as of 2025-2026 (down from 22 in 2017; multi-month subsequent Beijing diplomatic squeeze).
- Major remaining diplomatic allies: Guatemala, Paraguay, Haiti, Belize, Saint Lucia, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Marshall Islands, Tuvalu, Palau, Eswatini, Vatican City.
- The US "one China policy," established under the 1979 Taiwan Relations Act; which commits to maintaining unofficial relations and arms sales.
- TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company): produces 90% of advanced semiconductor wafers (3nm/5nm/7nm); strategic significance for global supply chains.
- Trump administration: characterized TSMC and semiconductor production as central US strategic interest; arranging over $200 billion in TSMC US investment commitments since 2020.

## PLA pressure operations against Taiwan

- August 2022: PLA exercises following Pelosi visit to Taiwan.
- April 2023: "Joint Sword" exercises after Tsai US visit.
- May 2024: "Joint Sword-2024A" exercises after Lai inauguration.
- October 2024: "Joint Sword-2024B" exercises.
- April 2025: "Strait Thunder-2025A" exercises.
- 2024-2026: multi-month near-daily PLA aircraft and naval incursions into Taiwan's ADIZ.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

## General references

- Wikipedia entries on Lai Ching-te, 2024 Taiwanese presidential election, Hsiao Bi-khim, Hou Yu-ih, Ko Wen-je, Han Kuo-yu, Eric Chu, KMT, DPP, TPP, Taiwan Relations Act.
- Britannica Lai Ching-te biography.
- CNN Lai profile January 2024.
- Al Jazeera Lai profile January 2024.
- Ifri "Taiwan's Elections" January 2024.
- Egmont Institute "Lai Ching-te Inaugurated in Taiwan" May 2024.
- Friedrich Naumann Foundation Taiwan profile.
- Multi-year SCMP Taiwan coverage.

# Sources — Dossier 32: The Japan Political Class

## Constitutional foundation

- 1947 Constitution of Japan (the "Peace Constitution"): drafted under US occupation; renounces war (Article 9); establishes parliamentary monarchy with Emperor as symbol.
- House of Representatives (Lower House): 465 seats; primary chamber.
- House of Councillors (Upper House): 248 seats.
- Prime Minister elected by Diet (typically Lower House majority).
- Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) founded 1955; has governed Japan almost continuously since.

## Sanae Takaichi — biography

- Born 7 March 1961 in Yamatokōriyama, Nara Prefecture.
- She came from a working-class family whose parents opposed her studying away from home; she commuted long distances to Kobe University.
- She graduated from Kobe University.
- 1984: entered Matsushita Institute of Government and Management.
- She was dispatched to Washington, D.C., where she worked on the staff of a Democratic Party US Representative.
- 1989: newscaster for TV Asahi; built reputation through political analysis.
- 1992: ran unsuccessfully for House of Councillors (Upper House) seat in Nara district as independent.
- In 1993, she was elected to House of Representatives (Lower House) in Nara district as independent.
- Initially joined Shinshintō (New Frontier Party).
- In 1996, she joined the LDP after Shinshintō's collapse.
- She has been a member of the House of Representatives 1993-2003 and since 2005.
- She is married to former MP and fellow LDP member Taku Yamamoto, who legally took her surname (rare in Japan).
- No biological children; stepmother to three; grandmother to four.
- Has spoken about struggles to conceive.
- Affinities: heavy metal music; Margaret Thatcher (cites as role model, wears blue suits in tribute).

## Career under Abe

- Protégé of Shinzō Abe.
- Minister for Internal Affairs and Communications under Abe administration 2014-2017 and 2019-2020 — longest-serving MIC minister.
- She served as Minister of Economic Security under Kishida from 2022 to 2024.
- In the 2021 LDP leadership election, she ran and lost. She put forward "Plan to Strengthen the Japanese Economy" / "New Abenomics" / "Sanaenomics."
- In the 2024 LDP leadership election, she ran and lost to Shigeru Ishiba.

## Path to premiership 2025

- 7 September 2025: Ishiba announced his intention to resign.
- 4 October 2025: LDP leadership election held. Five candidates in first round; runoff between Takaichi and Shinjirō Koizumi.
- Runoff: Takaichi 185 (149 Diet members + 36 prefectural chapter votes); Koizumi 156 (145 Diet + 11 prefectural).
- 21 October 2025: National Diet elected Takaichi 104th Prime Minister.
- She was the first woman to serve as either LDP president or Prime Minister of Japan.
- Coalition challenge: Kōmeitō ended 26-year alliance with LDP just before her premiership.
- New coalition: LDP + Japan Innovation Party (Nippon Ishin no Kai, right-leaning populist).
- 24 October 2025: first policy speech at National Diet.

## Snap election February 2026

- 23 January 2026: Takaichi dissolved House of Representatives.
- 8 February 2026: snap general election held.
- Result: LDP landslide — 316+ seats in 465-seat House (outright two-thirds supermajority).
- Most seats ever won by a single party in Japan's Lower House.
- First single-party two-thirds House control since WWII.
- Voter turnout: high (specifics pending).
- Approval ratings late October-early November 2025: 65-83% — among the highest of any Japanese government in 20 years.
- Strong support among young voters: 84% in 20s; 78% in 30s; 67% overall.

## Policy posture

**Economic policy ("Sanaenomics" / continuation of Abenomics):**
- Expansionary monetary policy.
- "Flexible fiscal spending in response to crises."
- "Bold investment in crisis management and growth."
- Heavy government investment in critical strategic sectors: AI, semiconductors, nuclear fusion, biotechnology, defence.
- She considered paying for economic stimulus through bond issuance to service national debt.
- Tokyo stocks rallied to new record after Takaichi took over LDP leadership.
- She planned to shift monetary policy responsibility from Bank of Japan to government, curtailing BoJ autonomy.

**Defence policy:**
- Brought forward target of 2% GDP defence spending: from 2027 to March 2026.
- May 2025: presented spy-prevention law proposal.
- 11 May 2026 (SCMP): "Japan's new spy agency: a shield against China or 'militarist surveillance'?"

**Foreign policy:**
- US-Japan alliance: stated continuation; hosted Trump in Tokyo October 2025 (first meeting).
- APEC Summit 2025 in South Korea: met President Lee Jae-myung.
- She attended the ASEAN Summit 2025.
- She promoted trilateral cooperation among the US, South Korea, Australia, and the Philippines.
- 2025-2026: comments on potential Taiwan defence produced deterioration of China-Japan relations (per Xi dossier).
- May 2026: Korea Herald reports plans for visit to Lee Jae-myung's hometown for summit.

**Social/cultural posture:**
- Opposes same-sex marriage.
- Supports male-only succession to Japanese throne.
- Traditional view of gender roles.
- She promised "Nordic levels" of female representation in cabinet but appointed only 2 women.
- Hardline on immigration despite acknowledging need for foreign workers due to demographic decline.

## Japanese political-class architecture context

**Recent Prime Ministers (post-Abe):**
- Shinzō Abe: 2006-2007, 2012-2020. Assassinated 8 July 2022 in Nara during campaign speech (shot by Tetsuya Yamagami over Unification Church grievances).
- Yoshihide Suga: September 2020-October 2021.
- Fumio Kishida: October 2021-October 2024.
- Shigeru Ishiba: October 2024-October 2025.
- Sanae Takaichi: October 2025-present.

**Slush-fund scandal:**
- 2023-2024: LDP factional kickback scandal revealed multi-year systematic underreporting of political fundraising income across LDP factions.
- Multi-month subsequent dissolution of major LDP factions.
- This was a contributing factor to the LDP losing its absolute majority in Lower House (first since 2009).
- July 2025: LDP also lost majority in Upper House.

**LDP factional architecture (recent dissolution):**
- Seiwakai (Abe faction) — largest, dissolved January 2024.
- Heisei Kenkyūkai (Motegi faction) — dissolved January 2024.
- Shikōkai (Aso faction) — only major faction that did not dissolve.
- Kōchikai (Kishida faction) — dissolved January 2024.
- Shisuikai (Nikai faction) — dissolved.
- Most factions reformed informally as "policy groups."

**Key LDP figures around Takaichi:**
- Tarō Asō (born 1940): Vice President of LDP since 2024; PM 2008-2009; remaining faction leader; family connections through Yoshida Shigeru.
- Yoshihide Suga: former PM; kingmaker.
- Shinjirō Koizumi (born 1981): runner-up in 2025 LDP race; son of former PM Junichirō Koizumi.
- Toshimitsu Motegi: current Foreign Minister.

**Opposition parties:**
- Constitutional Democratic Party of Japan (CDP): largest opposition; centre-left; led by Yoshihiko Noda.
- Japan Innovation Party (Nippon Ishin no Kai): right-leaning populist; coalition partner.
- Kōmeitō: Buddhist Sōka Gakkai-affiliated; ended 26-year LDP coalition October 2025.
- Democratic Party for the People: centre.
- Japanese Communist Party.
- Reiwa Shinsengumi: left populist.
- Sanseitō: far-right populist; supports stronger US-Japan alliance.

**Imperial Household:**
- Naruhito: Emperor since 1 May 2019.
- Masako: Empress.
- Aiko: only daughter of Naruhito; constitutionally barred from succession (male-only succession rules).
- Crown Prince Akishino: Naruhito's brother.
- Prince Hisahito (born 2006): grandson of former Emperor Akihito; current heir presumptive.

## Japan's geopolitical position

- 4th largest economy (nominal GDP).
- GDP growth 1% (2025).
- Public debt 230% of GDP (highest in OECD).
- Demographic crisis: declining population, lowest fertility rates among major economies.
- US-Japan alliance: cornerstone of foreign policy since 1951 Treaty of San Francisco and 1960 Treaty of Mutual Cooperation and Security.
- Multi-year defence spending increases: from 1% GDP historic level to 2% target.
- 2022 National Security Strategy under Kishida: announced doubling of defence spending over 5 years.
- Quad architecture: US, Japan, India, Australia.
- Trilateral US-Japan-South Korea (Camp David Principles August 2023).

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 19 May 2026.

## General references

- Wikipedia entries on Sanae Takaichi, Liberal Democratic Party (Japan), 2025 LDP leadership election, 2026 Japanese general election, Shinzō Abe, Naruhito.
- Britannica Sanae Takaichi biography.
- Council on Foreign Relations Sheila Smith interview October 2025.
- NPR Takaichi profile 21 October 2025.
- TIME Magazine October 2025 and March 2026 coverage.
- Nippon.com Takaichi profile January 2026.
- European Parliament Research Service briefing 2025.
- Korea Herald, South China Morning Post, BBC May 2026 coverage.

# Sources — Dossier 33: The South Korean Political Class

## Constitutional foundation

- Sixth Republic of Korea established 1987 with democratic transition.
- President elected by direct popular vote since 1987; single 5-year term, no re-election.
- 1987 Constitution following the June Democratic Uprising and end of military rule.
- National Assembly: 300 seats; mixed-member proportional representation.

## Presidents 1988-2026

1. Roh Tae-woo (1988-1993) — Democratic Justice Party
2. Kim Young-sam (1993-1998) — Democratic Liberal Party
3. Kim Dae-jung (1998-2003) — National Congress for New Politics; Nobel Peace Prize 2000
4. Roh Moo-hyun (2003-2008) — Uri Party
5. Lee Myung-bak (2008-2013) — Grand National Party
6. Park Geun-hye (2013-2017) — Saenuri Party; she was impeached and removed in March 2017.
7. Moon Jae-in (2017-2022) — Democratic Party of Korea
8. Yoon Suk Yeol (2022-2025) — People Power Party; he was impeached and removed in April 2025.
9. Lee Jae-myung (2025-present) — Democratic Party

## Lee Jae-myung — biography

- Born around 8 December 1963 in Andong, South Korea (birth registered late; exact date uncertain even to him).
- She was the fifth of seven children born to a poor family.
- He worked as an underage factory labourer due to family poverty.
- He did not complete formal secondary schooling but obtained an equivalent qualification.
- 1982: admitted to Chung-Ang University in Seoul to study law.
- 1986: passed the bar exam.
- He worked as a labour lawyer; championed rights of victims of industrial accidents and residents facing eviction due to urban redevelopment.
- Married Kim Hea-kyung 1991 (first met August 1990); two children.

## Political career

- 2006: unsuccessful first bid for mayorship of Seongnam.
- 2008: unsuccessful run for parliamentary seat in Seongnam.
- 2010: won mayoralty of Seongnam on second attempt.
- 2014: re-elected mayor of Seongnam.
- 2018-2021: Governor of Gyeonggi Province (the country's most populous province, surrounding Seoul).
- 2022 presidential election: Democratic Party nominee; lost to Yoon Suk Yeol 47.8% to 48.6% — the narrowest presidential election margin in South Korean history.
- August 2022: elected leader of Democratic Party.
- January 2024: survived an assassination attempt, sustaining a stab wound to neck during visit to construction site.
- In November 2024, he was convicted under Public Official Election Act for falsely denying connection to former Seongnam Development Corporation executive Kim Moon-ki during 2022 presidential campaign.

## 2024 martial law crisis

- On the evening of 3 December 2024, President Yoon Suk Yeol abruptly declared martial law, citing "anti-state forces" and accusing opposition of paralysing the government.
- Yoon sent troops to the National Assembly building.
- Lee Jae-myung climbed over National Assembly fence and livestreamed the event.
- Lawmakers pushed past soldiers to vote unanimously against the martial law declaration within hours.
- Yoon lifted the declaration under National Assembly pressure.
- 14 December 2024: National Assembly impeached Yoon (some sources cite 28 December for completion).
- A series of acting presidents served during the impeachment process.
- 4 April 2025 (some sources 16 April): Constitutional Court unanimously upheld the impeachment, removing Yoon from office.
- Yoon subsequently stood trial on insurrection charges (punishable by death or life in prison).

## 2025 snap presidential election

- The constitutional requirement mandates an election within 60 days of presidential vacancy.
- 8 April 2025: acting president Han Duck-soo called the election for 3 June 2025.
- 9 April 2025: Lee resigned as DPK leader.
- 10 April 2025: Lee announced his third presidential candidacy.
- 27 April 2025: Lee won DPK primary with ~90% of vote.
- There was People Power Party drama: the PPP attempted to replace selected nominee Kim Moon-soo with acting PM Han Duck-soo on 10 May; PPP members rejected the resolution; Han ended his campaign 11 May and endorsed Kim.
- 17 May 2025: Yoon left the PPP and endorsed Kim.
- 27 May 2025: final televised debate.
- 3 June 2025: election held.
- **Results:**
- Lee Jae-myung (Democratic Party): 49.42%.
- Kim Moon-soo (People Power Party): 41%+ — sources vary.
- Lee Jun-seok (Reform Party): 8.3%.
- Margin: ~2.89 million votes — widest in nearly three decades.
- 4 June 2025: Lee sworn in at 11:00 KST in Rotunda Hall of National Assembly.
- His inauguration speech emphasised national unity and ending "politics of division."

## Lee presidency 2025-2026

- 4 June 2025: Lee took office.
- 10 June 2025: bilateral phone call with Xi Jinping; invited Xi to APEC summit in Gyeongju.
- 18 June 2025: his first in-person meeting with Japanese PM Ishiba at G7 summit; agreed to restart shuttle diplomacy; marked 60th anniversary of normalised Japan-Korea ties.
- 15 August 2025: "People's Mandate Ceremony" public inauguration at Gwanghwamun Square commemorating the 80th anniversary of Korean liberation from Japan.
- DPK won 168 of 300 National Assembly seats; together with smaller left-of-centre allies, the total number of leftist seats reached 185.
- December 2025: Lee commuted to Cheong Wa Dae (Blue House) for the first time — the first president to commute there since 9 May 2022 (last day of Moon Jae-in's term). Yoon had moved the presidential office to the Defence Ministry compound in Yongsan.
- April 2026: approval ratings were near 70%, among the highest of any modern South Korean president.
- 15 May 2026: Korea Herald reports Lee and Takaichi conducting hometown diplomacy in Andong.

## Legal cases against Lee

- Multiple unresolved criminal cases as of 2025-2026:
- Bribery allegations.
- Election law violations.
- Encouraging false testimony.
- March 2025: Lee cleared of previous election-law violation by Seoul High Court.
- 1 May 2025: Supreme Court overturned acquittal and returned case to Seoul High Court for retrial.
- Initial retrial date set 15 May 2025; delayed to 18 June 2025 (after the election).
- Lee has maintained charges are politically motivated.

## Yoon Suk Yeol — predecessor architecture

- Born December 1960.
- Career prosecutor.
- 2017-2021: Prosecutor General under the Moon administration (the position from which he launched his political career).
- 2021: joined People Power Party.
- 9 March 2022: won presidential election against Lee Jae-myung by 48.6% to 47.8% (0.8 percentage point margin).
- In May 2022, he was inaugurated as the 13th President.
- 2022-2024: confrontational relationship with DPK-majority National Assembly.
- Multiple scandals: First Lady Kim Keon-hee jewellery and stock-manipulation allegations.
- 3 December 2024: martial law declaration.
- April 2025: removed from office; standing trial on insurrection charges.

## DPK architecture (governing)

- Democratic Party of Korea (DPK / Minjudang).
- Centre-left/progressive.
- 168 seats in National Assembly (2024 election); 185 with allies.
- Lee Jae-myung as President.
- Other key figures include former president Moon Jae-in (2017-2022) and the DPK leadership network.
- Internal factions include the "Moon-Lee" continuity faction and other internal currents.

## PPP architecture (opposition)

- People Power Party (PPP).
- Conservative.
- Founded 2020 (successor to multiple prior conservative parties: Saenuri, Liberty Korea, etc.).
- Kim Moon-soo (born June 1951): 2025 presidential candidate; former Labour Minister under Yoon.
- Yoon Suk Yeol: left PPP May 2025.
- This led to a subsequent multi-year crisis after Yoon's impeachment.
- The party had reduced parliamentary representation after 2024 elections.

## New Reform Party

- Founded 2024 by Lee Jun-seok (born March 1985), and former PPP leader.
- Lee Jun-seok was a 2025 presidential candidate, winning 8.3% of vote.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 19 May 2026.

## General references

- Wikipedia entries on Lee Jae Myung, 2025 South Korean presidential election, Yoon Suk Yeol, Yoon's martial law declaration, Democratic Party of Korea, People Power Party.
- Britannica Lee Jae-Myung biography.
- Al Jazeera 3 June 2025 Lee profile.
- NBC News 4 June 2025 Lee victory coverage.
- Carnegie Endowment "President Lee Jae Myung and the Resetting of Korea, Inc." June 2025.
- Korea Herald May 2026 coverage.

# Sources — Dossier 34: The North Korean Political Class

## Constitutional and dynastic foundation

- Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) founded 9 September 1948 under Kim Il Sung.
- Kim Il Sung (1948-1994): founder; "Great Leader" / "Eternal President"; architect of the Juche ideology.
- Kim Jong Il (1994-2011): second-generation succession; "Dear Leader"; architect of the "Songun" (military-first) policy.
- Kim Jong Un (2011-present): third-generation succession.
- Workers' Party of Korea (WPK / KWP) is the dominant apparatus.
- Supreme People's Assembly (SPA) operates as nominal legislature.
- State Affairs Commission established 2016 (formerly National Defense Commission).
- Kim Jong Un's titles: General Secretary of the WPK; Chairman of the State Affairs Commission; Chairman of the Central Military Commission; Supreme Commander of the Korean People's Army.

## Kim Jong Un — biography

- Born 8 January 1982, 1983, or 1984 (the exact date is contested; North Korean state media has used different dates).
- Third son of Kim Jong Il and Ko Yong Hui (Kim Jong Il's consort, who died in 2004).
- Reportedly educated at the International School of Berne in Gümligen, Switzerland (under a pseudonym).
- Upon returning to North Korea, he attended Kim Il Sung National War College 2002-2007.
- January 2009: he was appointed to a leadership position in WPK and the Korean People's Army.
- September 2010: he was publicly identified as the heir apparent and appointed Vice Chairman of WPK Central Military Commission and 4-star general.
- On 17 December 2011, his father Kim Jong Il died.
- In December 2011, he was declared supreme leader.
- In April 2012, he acquired multi-title status: First Secretary of the WPK, Chairman of Central Military Commission; Chairman of National Defense Commission.
- October 2016: First Secretary title changed to Chairman of WPK; subsequently changed to General Secretary 2021.

## Wife and family

- Wife Ri Sol Ju: announced 25 July 2012; believed early 20s at time of announcement; thought to have musical background.
- Three children:
- Eldest son (unnamed publicly; reportedly born 2010; reportedly "too pale and thin" to be shown publicly per retired SK intelligence official February 2024).
- Daughter Kim Ju Ae (born 2012 or 2013); public debut November 2022.
- Third child of unknown gender (unconfirmed).

## Brutal consolidation 2011-2017

- December 2013: uncle Jang Song-thaek (longtime regent and architect of the 2011-2013 transition) executed for "treason" — the most notable purge of the period.
- February 2017: half-brother Kim Jong Nam assassinated at Malaysia's Kuala Lumpur International Airport using VX nerve agent. South Korean intelligence subsequently said Kim Jong Un ordered the assassination.
- Estimated multiple executions of military and political operators across 2011-2017 consolidation.

## Nuclear and missile programme

- 2013: first nuclear test under Kim Jong Un (third overall DPRK test, after 2006 and 2009).
- 2016 January: fourth nuclear test (claimed hydrogen bomb).
- 2016 September: fifth nuclear test.
- 2017 September: sixth nuclear test (claimed thermonuclear).
- North Korea has conducted more than 200 missile tests under Kim Jong Un — more than triple the combined number under his father and grandfather.
- The first successful ICBM launch occurred in July 2017 (Hwasong-14).
- 2017 November: claimed that the Hwasong-15 ICBM brought US mainland within range.
- 2022: ICBM and IRBM tests resumed after multi-year moratorium.
- 2023-2025: continued multi-month subsequent advanced systems testing.

## Trump-Kim diplomacy 2018-2019

- 12 June 2018: Singapore Summit with Trump — marking the first time a sitting US president met a sitting DPRK leader.
- 27-28 February 2019: Hanoi Summit (collapsed without agreement).
- 30 June 2019: DMZ meeting with Trump.
- Negotiations broke down without progress on denuclearisation.

## December 2023 unification policy reversal

- December 2023: Kim declared that DPRK formally abandoned efforts to reunify Korea.
- This was followed by the subsequent symbolic demolition of the Arch of Reunification in Pyongyang.
- DPRK constitution amended to characterise South Korea as a "hostile state."
- 2024 onwards: artillery and missile drills targeted at South Korea increased.

## DPRK-Russia alignment 2023-2026

- September 2022: Putin and Kim publicly aligned on Ukraine war.
- 2023 August onwards: DPRK exported over 1 million artillery shells to Russia.
- 12-17 September 2023: Kim visited Russian Far East; toured key sites.
- February 2024: Kim received a car gift from Putin.
- 18-19 June 2024: Putin visited Pyongyang (his first since 2000); signed "Treaty on Comprehensive Strategic Partnership" — mutual-defence commitment.
- October 2024: DPRK sent military personnel to Russia to fight against Ukraine in Russian uniforms under Russian command.
- By end of 2024: 11,000 DPRK troops deployed; 6+ million heavy artillery rounds and 100 ballistic missiles delivered to Russia per US and allied assessment.
- April 2025: Kim publicly acknowledged DPRK troops fighting in Ukraine to "annihilate and wipe out the Ukrainian neo-Nazi occupiers."
- 27 April 2026: Kim unveiled a towering bronze sculpture of DPRK and Russian soldiers in combat; praised troops who chose death over capture in Ukraine.

## DPRK-China relationship

- May 2024 and earlier: visits between Kim and Xi.
- 3 September 2025: Kim travelled to Beijing for a Chinese military parade, where he shared the parade stand with Xi and Putin (documented in Dossier 28).
- DPRK-China relationship characterised as "lips and teeth" (phrase).

## Kim Ju Ae succession architecture

- Born 2012 or 2013.
- First public appearance 18 November 2022 at ICBM launch.
- Initial state-media reference: "saranghaneun jajebun" (beloved child).
- Escalated to "jongyeonghaneun jajebun" (respected child).
- Early 2024: "hyangdo" (guidance) — a term reserved historically for leaders.
- March 2024: officially referred to as "great person of guidance" alongside her father.
- 2025: attended Choe Hyon destroyer launch 25 April; 80th Victory Day at Russian embassy 9 May; 2025 China Victory Day Parade.
- Early 2026: state media showed her firing sniper rifle with party and military officials; 19 March 2026 operating new battle tank during tactical drill.
- February 2026: South Korea NIS assessed Ju Ae as designated successor.
- 2026 NIS report to South Korean National Assembly: confirmed she had completed her training and become designated successor.
- Estimated age: 13.
- Source of name: Dennis Rodman 2013 visit account (disputed — Choe Su-yong, former NIS officer, claims actual name is Un Ju).

## Kim Yo Jong — Kim's sister

- Born 1987 or 1989 (date contested).
- Younger sister of Kim Jong Un.
- Deputy Director of the WPK Agitation and Propaganda Department.
- Vice Department Director of the Central Committee of the WPK.
- Considered the second-most-powerful operator in DPRK.
- Instrumental in creating Kim Jong Un's public image and organising state events.
- Frequently issues public statements on behalf of Kim Jong Un (often hostile toward South Korea, US, Japan).
- Has been touted as potential successor in foreign press, though Ju Ae succession architecture has now formalised.
- February 2018: travelled to Seoul for Pyeongchang Winter Olympics — she was the first member of the Kim ruling dynasty to visit since Korean War.

## Other DPRK operators

- **Choe Ryong Hae**: First Vice Chairman of State Affairs Commission; longtime political operator.
- **Pak Pong Ju**: former Premier (2013-2019, 2020-2021).
- **Kim Tok Hun**: Premier of DPRK (2020-present).
- **Ri Pyong Chol**: Vice Chairman of WPK Central Military Commission.
- **Pak Jong Chon**: former Vice Chairman of CMC.
- **Choe Son Hui**: Foreign Minister since June 2022 — first female foreign minister of DPRK.

## DPRK domestic conditions

- Population: 26 million.
- Estimated 25-30% of GDP spent on defence, including WMD development.
- Endemic food and energy shortages.
- De facto breakdown of state rationing system; rise of jangmadang (small local markets, including illicit trade).
- The multi-decade indoctrination architecture has been weakening.
- January 2024 WPK meeting: Kim publicly admitted the economic crisis was a "serious political issue" — government unable to "provide even basic necessities."
- Armed forces: 1.1 million.

## Kim Jong Un health

- April 2020: Kim absent from public for 20 days; rumours of critical illness or death.
- June 2021: returned to public after one-month absence; observers noted significant weight loss (estimated 10-20 kg / 22-44 lb).
- July 2024: South Korea spy agency reported Kim had regained weight and was suffering obesity-related health issues including high blood pressure and diabetes.
- DPRK officials reportedly sought new medications abroad for his conditions.
- Growth visible on cheek since late 2020.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 19 May 2026.

## General references

- Wikipedia entries on Kim Jong Un, Kim Ju Ae, Kim Yo Jong, Workers' Party of Korea, 2024 North Korea–Russia treaty.
- Britannica Kim Jong-Un biography.
- CNN Kim Jong Un Fast Facts.
- Carnegie Endowment "The Hollowing Out of Kim Jong Un's North Korea" April 2024 (Pak).
- 38 North "Succession at the Crossroads" December 2025.
- Foreign Policy "How North Korea Is Preparing Kim Jong Un's Daughter to Rule" May 2026.
- Anna Fifield, *The Great Successor: The Secret Rise and Rule of Kim Jong Un* (2019).
- Jung H. Pak (former CIA / Brookings) DPRK analyses.
- Multi-year South Korean National Intelligence Service annual assessments.

*End of sources file for Dossier 34.*

# Sources — Dossier 35: Vladimir Putin and the Kremlin Apex Architecture

## Putin biographical foundation

- Born 7 October 1952 in Leningrad (now St Petersburg), Soviet Union.
- Parents lost two children before his birth — one died during the prolonged Nazi siege of Leningrad (1941-1944).
- He grew up modestly in a "rat-infested communal apartment building" per his own account.
- Father Vladimir Spiridonovich Putin (1911-1999): Red Army veteran, factory worker.
- Mother Maria Ivanovna Shelomova (1911-1998): factory worker.
- He recounted the 2000 cornered-rat story from a school anecdote: "I got a quick and lasting lesson in the meaning of the word cornered."
- He studied law at Leningrad State University 1970-1975 under future patron Anatoly Sobchak.
- In 1975, he was assigned to the KGB after graduation.

## KGB career 1975-1991

- From 1975 to 1985, he worked in KGB internal operations.
- From 1985 to 1990, he was stationed in Dresden, East Germany (a relatively minor outpost; not Berlin or Moscow centre).
- In 1989, he witnessed dissolution of communism in East Germany; observed Moscow failure to respond, concluding Soviet empire was failing.
- In 1991, he retired from KGB with rank of lieutenant colonel after 16 years.

## St Petersburg / Leningrad career 1990-1996

- In 1990, he became assistant to rector of Leningrad State University responsible for international affairs.
- He subsequently became an advisor to chairman of Leningrad City Council Anatoly Sobchak.
- In June 1991, he became chairman of St Petersburg City Council's International Relations Committee.
- In 1994, he became First Deputy Chairman of St Petersburg City Government (First Deputy Mayor under Sobchak).
- In 1996, he resigned after Sobchak lost re-election.

## Moscow rise 1996-1999

- In August 1996, he became Deputy Chief of the Presidential Property Management Department under Pavel Borodin, managing the transfer of former Soviet assets to the Russian Federation.
- In March 1997, he became Deputy Chief of the Presidential Staff and head of the Main Control Directorate.
- 25 July 1998: appointed FSB Director by Yeltsin (held until August 1999).
- Reorganised and strengthened the FSB after years of post-Soviet decline.
- Assisted Yeltsin by providing a compromising videotape of Prosecutor General Yuri Skuratov with prostitutes, removing the threat of a corruption investigation of Yeltsin's circle.
- October 1998: permanent member of Russian Security Council.
- March 1999: Secretary of the Security Council.
- 9 August 1999: appointed acting Prime Minister.
- 16 August 1999: confirmed Prime Minister.
- Chechen war operations: well-organised military operation against secessionist rebels boosted his approval ratings.
- 31 December 1999: Yeltsin resigned; Putin became acting President.

## Presidential terms

- **2000-2004 (first term):** Won March 2000 election. Restored federal authority; consolidated control over oligarchs.
- **2004-2008 (second term):** Re-elected 2004. Mikhail Khodorkovsky was arrested in October 2003, and Yukos was dismantled. Increasing authoritarianism.
- **2008-2012 (Medvedev interlude):** Medvedev served as President; Putin as Prime Minister. Constitutional two-term limit observed.
- **2012-2018 (third term):** He returned to the presidency; the annexation of Crimea occurred in 2014, and the Donbas war began.
- **2018-2024 (fourth term):** the 2020 constitutional amendments removed term limits, resetting the clock to allow two more terms; the full-scale invasion of Ukraine began in February 2022.
- **2024-2030 (fifth term):** He won the March 2024 election with 88% of the vote and was inaugurated on 7 May 2024.

## 2024 election

- Held 15-17 March 2024.
- Putin: 88% of vote per official tally.
- International observers did not consider election free or fair.
- Statistical analyses suggested unprecedented levels of fraud.
- The election was held in Russian-occupied territories of Ukraine, with returns such as: Zaporizhzhia 88.12%, Kherson 92.83%, and Chechnya 98.99%.
- 22 March 2024: Crocus City Hall attack — at least 145 killed, 551 injured.
- 7 May 2024: fifth-term inauguration.

## 2020 constitutional amendments

- 16 January 2020: Putin announced a reorganisation.
- Created new office of Deputy Chairman of the Security Council; Medvedev appointed.
- 1 July 2020: nationwide vote on 206 constitutional amendments — 78% approval (officially).
- Reset Putin's term clock — eligible until 2036.
- Constitutionally recognised "traditional family values"; banned same-sex marriage; recognised faith in God.

## Family

- 28 July 1983: married Lyudmila Shkrebneva.
- Two daughters: Maria Putina (born 28 April 1985, Leningrad); Yekaterina Putina (born 31 August 1986, Dresden).
- 2013: announced divorce from Lyudmila.
- Alleged longtime partner Alina Kabaeva (former Olympic gymnast); subject of multi-year speculation.
- Reported additional children with Kabaeva (unconfirmed).

## The Yeltsin "Operation Successor" architecture

- 1998-1999: Yeltsin's circle ("The Family") sought a successor who could protect them from corruption prosecution.
- Sergei Stepashin: was a brief test candidate who failed Yeltsin's loyalty tests.
- August 1999: Putin appointed PM.
- Boris Berezovsky (oligarch in Yeltsin's circle): supported Putin's rise; later exiled to UK; died March 2013.
- Yeltsin granted Putin a presidential decree on 31 December 1999 immunising him from prosecution.
- 1999 apartment bombings (Moscow, Buynaksk, Volgodonsk): killed 293; blamed on Chechens by authorities; the discovery of FSB officers planting explosives in a Ryazan basement was explained as a "training exercise"; Putin's response justified Second Chechen War.

## Key Kremlin operators

### Nikolai Patrushev
- Born 11 July 1951.
- Longtime FSB / Security Council operator.
- 1999-2008: FSB Director (succeeded Putin).
- From 2008 to 2024, he served as Secretary of Security Council.
- Hawkish ideological hardliner.
- May 2024: dismissed as Security Council Secretary.
- Currently: Aide to the President.
- Son Dmitry Patrushev: now Deputy Prime Minister (since May 2024).

### Dmitry Medvedev
- Born 14 September 1965.
- President 2008-2012 (placeholder term).
- Prime Minister 2012-2020.
- Since 16 January 2020: Deputy Chairman of Security Council.
- Has produced multi-year subsequent hawkish public statements on Ukraine war.

### Mikhail Mishustin
- Born 3 March 1966.
- Career tax administration: Head of Federal Tax Service 2010-2020.
- Prime Minister since 16 January 2020.
- Re-appointed May 2024.
- He is a technocratic operator who manages government apparatus.

### Sergei Lavrov
- Born 21 March 1950.
- Foreign Minister since 9 March 2004 (over 20 years).
- Career Soviet/Russian diplomat.
- UN Russian Ambassador 1994-2004.
- Re-appointed May 2024.
- Age 75 in 2026.

### Andrei Belousov
- Born 17 March 1959.
- Economist; statist policy operator.
- Assistant to President 2013-2020.
- Minister of Economic Development 2012-2013.
- First Deputy Prime Minister 2020-2024.
- 12 May 2024: appointed Defense Minister, replacing Shoigu.
- Has no military background.
- Belongs to "Diveyevo Brotherhood" religious circle.
- Founded Centre for Macro-Analysis and Short-Term Forecasting.
- Russian defence spending under his tenure: approaching 6.7% of GDP (Cold War-era levels).

### Sergei Shoigu
- Born 21 May 1955.
- Defense Minister 2012-May 2024.
- 12 May 2024: transferred to Secretary of Security Council (replacing Patrushev).
- Long-serving Putin loyalist; no military training but ran Emergency Situations Ministry 1991-2012.

### Aleksandr Bortnikov
- Born 15 November 1951.
- FSB Director since May 2008.
- Career KGB/FSB operator.

### Sergei Naryshkin
- Born 27 October 1954.
- Director of Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR) since 5 October 2016.
- Former Speaker of State Duma 2011-2016.

### Vyacheslav Volodin
- Born 4 February 1964.
- Speaker of the State Duma since 5 October 2016.
- Long-time Putin loyalist.

### Valentina Matvienko
- Born 7 April 1949.
- Chairwoman of the Federation Council since 21 September 2011.
- Career Soviet/Russian operator.

### Anton Vaino
- Born 17 February 1972.
- Chief of Staff of Presidential Executive Office since August 2016.
- Estonian-born; trained in foreign service.

### Vladimir Kolokoltsev
- Born 11 May 1961.
- Interior Minister since 21 May 2012.
- Long-serving police-system operator.

### Boris Kovalchuk
- Son of Yury Kovalchuk (Putin's "personal banker" per US Treasury).
- 13 May 2024: nominated to head Audit Chamber.

### Sergei Ivanov
- Born 31 January 1953.
- Special Presidential Representative for Environmental Protection.
- Former Defence Minister 2001-2007; former Presidential Administration Chief 2011-2016.
- One of Putin's longest-serving` or `Co-longest-serving operator.

## 2023 Prigozhin mutiny

- Yevgeny Prigozhin (1961-2023): founder of Wagner Group private military company.
- 23-24 June 2023: launched short-lived "March of Justice" mutiny against Russian military leadership.
- Wagner forces took control of Rostov-on-Don, and advanced toward Moscow.
- Mutiny ended after a Lukashenko-mediated deal.
- 23 August 2023: Prigozhin was killed in plane crash in Tver Oblast (exactly two months after rebellion).
- Western intelligence: which was probably caused by explosion on board.
- Russian state involvement was widely suspected.

## Suspicious deaths under Putin

- Alexander Litvinenko: poisoned in London 2006 with polonium-210 (former FSB officer turned MI6 agent).
- Anna Politkovskaya: journalist who exposed atrocities in Chechnya and was shot 7 October 2006 (Putin's birthday).
- Boris Nemtsov: opposition politician; shot just outside Kremlin walls 27 February 2015.
- Alexei Navalny: opposition leader; poisoned with Novichok 2020; survived; imprisoned 2021; died in penal colony 16 February 2024.
- Boris Berezovsky: died in UK March 2013 in mysterious circumstances.
- Multiple journalists, activists, and former intelligence officers across the multi-year period.

## Ukraine war 2022-2026

- February 2014: the annexation of Crimea following Maidan Revolution.
- April 2014: Donbas war began.
- February 2015: Minsk II ceasefire (largely unobserved).
- On 24 February 2022, the full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
- In March 2022, the ICC issued arrest warrant for Putin for war crimes (children deportations).
- In August 2024, the Ankara prisoner exchange took place, marking the largest US-Russia prisoner swap since the Cold War, with 26 people exchanged.
- May 2024: Putin reportedly ready for negotiated ceasefire freezing front lines.
- April 2025: Trump called for peace deal via social media.
- May 2025: Russia did not agree to proposed 30-day ceasefire.
- June 2025: first peace talks since 2022 in Istanbul — Russia demanded Ukraine territorial concessions and renunciation of foreign military aid; Ukraine called these "unacceptable."
- 13-16 May 2026: Russia fired 800 drones at Ukraine despite "recent talk by Putin and Trump of possible peace"; killed at least 9 in Kyiv apartment block; death toll rose to 24 in Russian attack.
- May 2026: Putin to visit Xi Jinping days after Trump's Beijing trip.

## Approval ratings (Levada Center, with caveats about authoritarian polling)

- June 2007: 81%.
- January 2013: 62% (low during 2011-2013 protests).
- August 2014: 87% (post-Crimea).
- June 2015: 89% (all-time high).
- 2024-2026: officially 80%+, with multi-year measurement difficulties.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 19 May 2026.

## General references

- Wikipedia: Vladimir Putin, Political career of Vladimir Putin, Putin's rise to power, Security Council of Russia, Russian invasion of Ukraine.
- Britannica Vladimir Putin biography.
- History.com Vladimir Putin profile.
- Al Jazeera "Vladimir Putin: After 25 years in power" May 2025.
- Moscow Times "Reshuffle 2024" May 2024.
- Stars and Stripes "Putin turns to a technocrat" May 2024.
- NEST Centre "Transition without a successor" November 2025.
- Kremlin.ru official biographies.
- Multi-year Levada Center polling data.
