# Sources — Part XII Chapter 4: The Ukraine War

## Background

- 1654: Treaty of Pereyaslav (Cossack Hetmanate alliance with Russian Tsar).
- 1922-1991: Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic part of USSR.
- 1932-1933 Holodomor: Stalinist famine killed 3.9 million Ukrainians.
- 24 August 1991: Ukrainian independence declaration.
- 1 December 1991: Ukrainian referendum 92.3% in favour of independence.
- 1994 Budapest Memorandum: Ukraine gave up Soviet nuclear weapons stockpile (3rd largest in world) in exchange for security assurances from US, UK, and Russia.
- 1991-2014: Multi-year alternation between pro-Western and pro-Russian presidents (Kravchuk, Kuchma, Yushchenko after Orange Revolution 2004, Yanukovych 2010-2014).

## Maidan and Revolution of Dignity 2013-2014

- 21 November 2013: President Viktor Yanukovych suspended preparations for EU Association Agreement after Putin meeting on 9 November; turned to closer ties with Russia.
- Late November 2013: Euromaidan protests began in Kyiv's Independence Square (Maidan Nezalezhnosti). Largest democratic mass movement in Europe since 1989.
- 30 November 2013: Berkut riot police violently dispersed protesters; mass demonstrations escalated.
- 8 December 2013: 800,000 people gathered in Kyiv; Lenin statue toppled.
- December 2013: Putin pledged $15 billion in Ukrainian bonds and discounted natural gas.
- 16 January 2014: Ukrainian parliament passed harsh anti-protest laws by show of hands.
- 19-22 January 2014: First protesters killed; vul. Hrushevskoho riots; some anti-protest laws rolled back.
- 18-20 February 2014: Heaviest clashes; Berkut snipers shot dead 100 protesters ("Heavenly Hundred").
- 21 February 2014: Yanukovych signed agreement with opposition leaders mediated by EU foreign ministers; agreement quickly fell apart.
- 21-22 February 2014: Yanukovych fled Kyiv (later Ukraine, to Russia).
- 22 February 2014: Verkhovna Rada (Ukrainian parliament) voted 328-0 to remove Yanukovych; restored 2004 Constitution; called May 2014 elections.
- Oleksandr Turchynov named interim president; Arseniy Yatsenyuk appointed prime minister.

## Crimea annexation February-March 2014

- 27 February 2014: Russian soldiers in unmarked uniforms ("little green men") seized Crimean Parliament and Council of Ministers buildings.
- Russian special forces took control of Crimean government buildings, surrounded Ukrainian military bases, blockaded peninsula.
- Putin initially denied Russian involvement; later admitted they were Russian troops.
- 16 March 2014: Crimea referendum under Russian military occupation; 97% reported in favour of joining Russia; widely characterised by US, EU, UN as illegitimate.
- 18 March 2014: Putin signed bill annexing Crimea; reorganised as Russian republic; Sevastopol made Russian federal city.
- 27 March 2014: UN General Assembly Resolution 68/262 affirmed "territorial integrity of Ukraine within its internationally recognised borders" (100-11-58).
- Russia claimed exclusive economic zone in Black Sea around Crimea — three times larger than peninsula.

## Donbas war 2014-2022

- 7 April 2014: Pro-Russian separatists declared the "Donetsk People's Republic" (DPR) and seized government buildings in Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv.
- 11 May 2014: DPR and Luhansk People's Republic (LPR) held independence referendums; "yes" wins.
- April-May 2014: Ukrainian government launched "Anti-Terrorist Operation" (ATO); deployed army and volunteer militias including some far-right groups (Right Sector, Azov Battalion).
- 25 May 2014: Petro Poroshenko elected president.
- 17 July 2014: Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17 shot down over eastern Ukraine; 298 people killed including 193 Dutch. Multi-year subsequent Dutch-led Joint Investigation Team concluded Russian BUK missile from Russia-based 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade fired by Russia-backed separatists.
- August 2014: Direct Russian troops entered Ukraine; battles of Ilovaisk (August-September 2014) and Donetsk Airport.
- 5 September 2014: Minsk I Agreement (Russia, Ukraine, France, Germany).
- January-February 2015: Battle of Debaltseve.
- 12 February 2015: Minsk II Agreement signed under Normandy Format (Putin, Poroshenko, Merkel, Hollande); ceasefire and political settlement framework. Never fully implemented.
- 2014-2022 Donbas war casualties: 14,000+ killed before 2022 (per UN OHCHR, International Crisis Group).
- 25 November 2018: Russian forces seized three Ukrainian Navy vessels attempting to pass under Crimean Bridge; 24 sailors arrested.
- 20 May 2019: Volodymyr Zelensky inaugurated as President of Ukraine (Servant of the People party; defeated Poroshenko in second round 73-25).
- 9 December 2019: Normandy Format summit in Paris (Putin and Zelensky first meeting).
- 6 April 2021: Zelensky openly declared NATO membership the only way to end Donbas war.

## Pre-invasion buildup 2021-2022

- March-April 2021: First major Russian buildup near Ukrainian border.
- October-November 2021: Second major Russian buildup; over 100,000 troops along Ukrainian border.
- 17 December 2021: Putin demanded NATO non-expansion guarantee; demanded NATO withdraw forces from post-1997 NATO member states.
- January-February 2022: Intense diplomacy; NATO refused to budge on open-door policy.
- 21 February 2022: Putin recognised independence of DPR and LPR; ordered Russian troops in as "peacekeepers."
- 22 February 2022: German Chancellor Olaf Scholz suspended Nord Stream 2 certification.

## Full-scale invasion 24 February 2022

- 24 February 2022 ~04:00: Russia launched full-scale invasion characterised as "special military operation."
- Russian objectives: capture Kyiv, install puppet government, prevent NATO accession, "denazify" and "demilitarise" Ukraine.
- Initial axes: Kyiv (north from Belarus); Sumy/Kharkiv (northeast); Donbas (east); Mariupol/Crimea (south).
- Antonov Airport (Hostomel) air assault first day.
- Kyiv assault failed; Russian forces withdrew from Kyiv Oblast end of March 2022.
- 27 February 2022: EU announced first weapons supplies; first sanctions package.
- 27 February 2022: SWIFT ban on selected Russian banks; freezing of Russian central bank reserves.

## Bucha massacre April 2022

- Russian forces occupied Bucha (Kyiv Oblast) 27 February - 31 March 2022.
- April 2022: Following Russian withdrawal, evidence of mass killings discovered.
- Local authorities: 458 bodies recovered including 9 children under 18; 419 killed with weapons.
- Many victims found with hands tied behind backs; signs of torture; gunshot wounds to head.
- Mass graves, executions documented.
- Zelensky characterised events as genocide.
- Biden characterised Putin actions as "war crime."
- Kremlin denied responsibility; claimed staging by Ukraine.
- German BND intercepted Russian troop communications "completely refuting" Russian denials.
- Wagner Group forces reportedly involved.

## Mariupol siege

- 24 February - 20 May 2022: siege of Mariupol.
- Russian forces relentlessly bombed civilian infrastructure.
- 9 March 2022: Mariupol maternity hospital airstrike.
- 16 March 2022: Mariupol theatre airstrike (word "children" / Ru: "дети" marked outside in large letters); estimates 300-600+ killed.
- 16 May 2022: final Ukrainian defenders at Azovstal steelworks ordered to surrender.
- Mariupol reduced to rubble.
- Thousands of civilians killed; many forcibly deported to Russia ("filtration camps").
- City under Russian control since 20 May 2022.

## Counter-offensives and battles 2022-2023

- April 2022: Russian cruiser Moskva (Black Sea Fleet flagship) sunk by Ukrainian Neptune missiles.
- September 2022: Ukrainian Kharkiv counter-offensive recaptured ~12,000 sq km.
- September 2022: Russian mass mobilisation announcement; mass emigration; protests.
- November 2022: Ukrainian Kherson counter-offensive recaptured western Kherson Oblast; Russian withdrawal across Dnipro.
- September 2022: Russia annexed Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson oblasts (despite not fully controlling them) following sham referendums.
- 17 March 2023: International Criminal Court (ICC) issued arrest warrant for Putin for unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children to Russia.
- 26 September 2022: Nord Stream pipeline sabotage (Baltic Sea); four explosions; three of four pipelines damaged.
- Battle of Bakhmut (August 2022 - May 2023): WWI-style trench warfare; estimates 20,000+ Wagner deaths alone.
- May 2023: Russia claimed control of Bakhmut city proper.
- 4-9 June 2023: Ukrainian summer counter-offensive began; failed to achieve operational breakthroughs.
- 6 June 2023: Kakhovka Dam collapse; flooding southern Ukraine; widely attributed to Russian sabotage.

## Wagner mutiny June 2023

- 23-24 June 2023: Yevgeny Prigozhin launched armed rebellion against Russian Ministry of Defence (Sergei Shoigu and Valery Gerasimov).
- Wagner forces seized Rostov-on-Don, marched toward Moscow via Voronezh.
- Down at least 6 Russian military helicopters and 1 Il-22M aircraft; reportedly killed 13 personnel.
- Belarus President Lukashenko mediated agreement; Prigozhin agreed to retreat in exchange for safety guarantees.
- 23 August 2023: Prigozhin and other Wagner leadership killed in plane crash near Tver (Embraer Legacy 600 crashed).

## Nord Stream sabotage details

- 26 September 2022: Four explosions on Nord Stream 1 and Nord Stream 2 pipelines in Baltic Sea.
- Three of four pipelines damaged.
- Initial Russian/US accusations against each other; subsequent investigations.
- Three separate national investigations: Sweden, Denmark, Germany.
- February 2024: Sweden and Denmark closed investigations without publicly assigning responsibility.
- February 2023: Seymour Hersh report alleged Biden administration ordered the sabotage via Norwegian Navy divers.
- August 2024: German authorities issued European arrest warrant for Ukrainian national Volodymyr Z. (private diving school in Kyiv).
- August 2025: Italian police arrested Ukrainian "Serhii K" in San Clemente.
- September 2025: Polish police arrested another Ukrainian near Warsaw.
- October 2025: Polish court released Volodymyr Z., denied German extradition request.
- February 2026: Der Spiegel report — operation approved by then-commander-in-chief Valerii Zaluzhny but not by Zelensky; private Ukrainian citizen as primary financier (~$300,000).
- January 2026: German Federal Court of Justice rejected suspect's immunity claim.
- Zelensky has denied Ukrainian state involvement.

## 2024 events

- March 2024: Russian presidential election; Putin re-elected with claimed 87.3%.
- 6 August 2024: Ukrainian Kursk offensive launched. First foreign occupation of Russian territory since WWII (per BBC).
- 15 August 2024: Sudzha (Kursk Oblast) captured.
- By end of first week: Ukraine claimed 1,000 sq km captured; 28 settlements.
- November 2024: North Korean troops (estimated 10,000+) began deploying with Russian forces.
- 5 November 2024: Donald Trump won US presidential election against Kamala Harris.
- December 2024: Russian forces recaptured ~half Kursk territory.
- 17 December 2024: Lt. Gen. Igor Kirillov (head of Russian NBC Protection Troops) killed by bomb in Moscow (Ukrainian SBU operation).

## 2025 events

- 20 January 2025: Trump second inauguration.
- January 2025: Ukrainian second incursion into Kursk; progress limited.
- 28 February 2025: Trump-Zelensky Oval Office confrontation; Vance present.
- Early March 2025: Trump paused US military aid and intelligence sharing with Ukraine.
- 11 March 2025: Most Ukrainian forces retreated from Kursk under Russian counterattack.
- February 2025: North Korean troops withdrawn after high casualties.
- April 2025: Lt. Gen. Yaroslav Moskalik (General Staff Main Operational Directorate) killed by Moscow car bomb.
- 2025: ACLED records 78,000 people killed in Ukraine — highest of any conflict globally.
- December 2025: Lt. Gen. Fanil Sarvarov (Operational Training Directorate Russian General Staff) killed in Moscow car bomb.
- December 2025: Russian forces claimed control of Kupyansk.

## 2026 events (most current)

- January 2026: OHCHR recorded 56,550 civilian casualties since 24 February 2022: 15,172 killed, 41,378 injured.
- February 2026: Russian MoD claimed Ukrainian military casualties exceeded 1.5 million.
- February 2026: *The Telegraph* — Russia losing 40,000 soldiers per month since November 2025 while recruiting only ~35,000; total Russian casualties exceeded 1.25 million (more than US WWII casualties).
- 10 March 2026: Zelensky and Ukrainian military intelligence Lt. Gen. Oleh Ivashchenko said per captured Russian documents 1,315,000 Russian soldiers killed or wounded since 24 February 2022.
- 31 March 2026: Lt. Gen. Alexander Otroschenko (commander of mixed aviation corps of Northern Fleet) killed in An-26 transport aircraft crash in Crimea.
- End March 2026: OHCHR verified 58,930 civilian casualties since 24 February 2022 (~13,883 killed, ~43,352 injured). Real numbers higher.
- Early April 2026: Russia claimed full control of Luhansk Oblast (only 0.2% of pre-war population remained in Russian-controlled areas).
- 3 April 2026: Zelensky said Russia suffered highest single-month losses since invasion in March 2026 — over 35,000 killed/wounded.
- 10-12 April 2026: Easter ceasefire announced; violated 2,299 times within 48 hours including 747 attack drone strikes and 1,045 FPV drone strikes (per Ukraine Armed Forces).
- 4-8 May 2026: Russian strikes killed 73+ civilians and injured 400+ in Zaporizhia, Kramatorsk, Dnipro, Merefa, Sumy — deadliest civilian-strike week of 2026.
- 9-11 May 2026: Trump-mediated Victory Day three-day ceasefire; violated.
- 9 May 2026: Ukrainian General Staff figures: Russian losses since 24 February 2022 — 1,340,270 troops; 11,920 tanks; 24,541 armoured vehicles; 95,252 vehicles and fuel tanks; 41,712 artillery systems; 1,780 MLRS; 1,371 air defence systems; 435 aircraft; 352 helicopters; 281,208 drones; 33 ships; 2 submarines.
- 9 May 2026: Mediazona/Meduza published estimate of 352,000 Russian men aged 18-59 killed since invasion via Probate Registry data (significantly lower than Ukrainian claims).
- 9 May 2026: Of Mediazona/BBC Russian-named Russian war dead — 217,808 names verified, including 7,094 officers, 27,475 Motorized Rifle Troops, 5,135 VDV Airborne, prisoner-recruits roughly 10.7% of documented deaths.
- May 2026: Russia captured ~1,897 sq mi over past 12 months; territorial momentum slowing — only 17 sq mi changed hands in 4 weeks March 10 - April 7 (Russia Matters War Report Card).
- May 2026: Ukrainian forces retook 480 sq km in southern regions since January, ~180 sq km around Kupyansk late 2025.
- May 2026: Ukrainian long-range drone strikes reduced Russian oil export capacity by ~40%.
- May 2026: Every Ukrainian power plant has been damaged; electricity generation 33.7 GW prewar to ~14 GW.

## Casualty synthesis

- Civilians killed (UN OHCHR verified, end March 2026): ~13,883 killed, ~43,352 injured. OHCHR says real numbers higher; independent estimates 20,000-40,000+.
- Russian military killed (independent estimates, Mediazona/Meduza Probate Registry May 2026): ~352,000.
- Russian military total casualties (Ukrainian General Staff 9 May 2026): ~1,340,270 (killed + wounded).
- Russian military casualties (Center for Strategic and International Studies January 2026): 2-2.5x Ukrainian casualties.
- Russian military total casualties (Western estimates February 2026): exceeded 1.25 million.
- Ukrainian military killed (Zelensky December 2024): 43,000.
- Ukrainian military wounded (Zelensky December 2024): 370,000.
- Ukrainian military killed and missing (UALosses project April 2026): 91,559 named killed + 95,165 missing = 186,724.
- Ukrainian total casualties (Russian MoD February 2026 claim): 1,500,000.
- Ukrainian casualty estimate (independent, early 2026): 250,000-300,000.
- Combined estimate (early 2026): 400,000-550,000 deaths total.
- ACLED 2025 alone in Ukraine: ~78,000 killed.
- Refugees in Europe: 6+ million (UNHCR).
- Internally displaced: 6+ million.

## Western military and financial support to Ukraine

- US foreign aid to Ukraine FY 2022-2024: $175+ billion authorised across multiple supplemental appropriations.
- EU and member states' total aid 2022-2025: €130+ billion.
- UK: £12+ billion.
- Multi-year arms transfers from US/UK/Germany/France/Poland/Netherlands/Czech Republic/Sweden/Denmark/Norway/Finland/Lithuania/Latvia/Estonia/Romania/Canada/Australia/Japan/Korea.
- Major weapon systems transferred 2022-2026: Javelin and NLAW anti-tank; Stinger MANPADS; HIMARS multiple rocket launchers; M777 howitzers; Patriot air defence; Leopard 2 and Challenger 2 tanks; M1 Abrams tanks; F-16 fighters (2024 onward); ATACMS long-range missiles (2023-2024); Storm Shadow / SCALP cruise missiles; Bradley IFVs; Stryker IFVs; Caesar self-propelled artillery; PzH 2000 self-propelled artillery; multi-thousand drones.
- Trump administration paused aid and intelligence sharing March 2025; partial resumption.
- 16 May 2026: US waiver permitting Russian oil purchases stranded at sea expired without renewal.

## Russian losses tracked

- Russia captured ~1,897 sq mi over past 12 months as of May 2026.
- Russian generals killed in covert ops 2024-2026: Lt. Gen. Igor Kirillov (December 2024 Moscow bomb); Lt. Gen. Yaroslav Moskalik (April 2025 Moscow car bomb); Lt. Gen. Fanil Sarvarov (December 2025 Moscow car bomb); Lt. Gen. Alexander Otroschenko (31 March 2026 An-26 crash Crimea).
- Maj. Gen. Andrei Sukhovetsky (41st Army) and Maj. Gen. Vladimir Frolov (8th Army) killed early weeks of invasion.
- Maj. Gen. Roman Kutuzov killed June 2022.
- Wagner Group: 20,000+ killed by May 2023 per Prigozhin.

## ICC and accountability

- 17 March 2023: ICC arrest warrant for Putin and Russian Children's Commissioner Maria Lvova-Belova for unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children.
- Russia not party to Rome Statute.
- Mass graves: Izium (~440 bodies, September 2022); Bucha; multiple other locations.
- Documented executions of POWs by both sides.
- UN Commission of Inquiry on Ukraine.

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Part XII Chapter 5: The Gaza Genocide

## October 7, 2023 attack

- 7 October 2023: Hamas-led "Operation Al-Aqsa Flood" — coordinated armed incursions from blockaded Gaza Strip into Gaza envelope of southern Israel during Jewish holidays of Simchat Torah and Shemini Atzeret (Shabbat). Coincided with 50th anniversary of Yom Kippur War.
- 4,300+ rockets fired in 20 minutes (Wikipedia "October 7 attacks"); other sources say 3,000-5,000 rockets.
- 6,000 Gazans breached border in 119 locations (IDF revised estimate); 3,800 from elite Nukhba forces (Hamas).
- 21 communities attacked including Be'eri (108 killed), Kfar Aza (~50), Nir Oz, Netiv HaAsara (15), Alumim (16-17 Thai/Nepalese workers).
- Nova Music Festival near Re'im: 378 killed (344 civilians + 34 security personnel) per Wikipedia "Nova music festival massacre"; some sources cite 360 or 364-378. ~3,500 attendance. Plus ~20 attackers killed by IDF in area.
- 44 hostages taken from festival; sexual and gender-based violence committed during attack.
- Bomb shelter massacres around Nova: Aner Shapira fought militants at Re'im Junction shelter with broken bottle; Hersh Goldberg-Polin lost his hand and was kidnapped (killed in captivity, body recovered August 2024). Yuval Raphael survived shelter outside Be'eri where 40 of 51 died.
- Total killed in Israel and at festival 1,200 (Britannica; Wikipedia 2023 Israel-Hamas war: 1,195 Israelis and foreign nationals; Al Jazeera "more than 1,100").
- 251 hostages taken (Wikipedia 2023 Israel-Hamas war); also reported 240+ (Britannica) or "about 250."
- March 2024 UN report found evidence of sexual violence.
- 12 May 2026: Israeli NGO Civil Commission on October 7 Crimes by Hamas Against Women and Children published "Silenced No More" — ~290 pages — concluded sexual and gender-based violence "systematic, widespread, and integral" with 13 recurring patterns including rape and gang rape, sexual torture, mutilation, forced nudity, executions linked to sexual violence, abuse during captivity.
- Some specific allegations later contested/disproven: Be'eri claims of eye-gouging, decapitation, dismembered babies (per Haaretz December 2023 — "no children 6 or 7 or near those ages were killed on Be'eri"); pregnant woman taken hostage and gave birth (Sara Netanyahu letter to Jill Biden — was Thai worker, not pregnant); Antony Blinken repeated some contested ZAKA volunteer accounts.

## Gaza death toll — progression October 2023 to May 2026

- November 2023: brief ceasefire 24 November - 1 December 2023 (Qatar-brokered): 4 days pause extended, 50 Israeli hostages and 150 Palestinian women/children prisoners exchanged.
- Late 2023 - 2024: continuous Israeli bombing and ground operations.
- 19 January 2025: three-stage ceasefire began after 15 January agreement (US, Qatar, Egypt mediated). First stage: 33 hostages for ~1,900 prisoners.
- 18 March 2025: Israel surprise attack ended ceasefire (Hamas refused to release more hostages without Israeli proceeding to second phase).
- August 2025: nearly 61,600 killed (Palestine Chronicle).
- 5 October 2025: 67,075 killed and 169,430 injured per GHM (Costs of War).
- August 2025: IPC Phase 5 (famine) confirmed in parts of Gaza City; Deir Al Balah and Khan Younis expected to reach famine by end September.
- October 2025: Trump 20-point plan; ceasefire 10 October.
- November 2025: 70,000+ killed; more than two-thirds of buildings damaged/destroyed.
- January 2026: IDF formally accepted GHM count of 71,667 killed, 171,000+ wounded, 9,500 missing under rubble. ICJP and others noted figure "conservative" — many bodies still under rubble; many died of starvation.
- 3 May 2026: Wikipedia "Gaza war" 75,811 total reported killed (73,770+ Palestinians and 2,039+ Israelis); 270 journalists; 120 academics; 560+ aid workers (391 UNRWA).
- 80% of Palestinians killed were civilians (scholar estimates).
- ECCHR figures (October 2024): 10,429 women, 20,179 children, 4,813 elderly among dead.
- US healthcare workers letter October 2024: at least 62,413 starvation deaths conservative estimate (based on IPC Phase 5 mortality rates); 5,000+ chronic disease deaths.
- Economist indirect death studies: life expectancy fell 35 years, rivalling Rwandan genocide.
- 442 Palestinians killed by Israel in 1,200+ truce violations since 10 October 2025 ceasefire (Wikipedia Gaza peace plan).
- After 10 October 2025 ceasefire, 500+ Palestinians killed.

## Famine

- August 2025: IPC Phase 5 (famine) confirmed in parts of Gaza City; Deir Al Balah and Khan Younis expected to reach famine by end September.
- Slight improvement after October 2025 ceasefire but as of late 2025: 1.6 million acute food insecurity; 100,000+ catastrophic.
- Entire Gaza Strip projected Emergency (IPC Phase 4) through mid-April 2026.
- 428 adults and 146 children died of starvation/malnutrition per GHM (TIME report).
- UN experts: famine "entirely man-made."

## Population and humanitarian

- Entire Gaza Strip (~2.1-2.3 million) in need of humanitarian assistance per ECCHR October 2024.
- ~1.1 million children require child protection services and mental health support.
- UNCTAD 2025: per capita GDP plummeted to $161 annually; unemployment 80%.
- Carbon cost of rebuilding Gaza > annual GHG emissions of 135 countries.
- More than two-thirds of buildings in Gaza damaged/destroyed by November 2025.
- Most of Gaza's 2.3M residents forced from homes per UN.
- 270 journalists, 120 academics, 560+ aid workers (391 UNRWA) killed.

## ICJ South Africa v Israel

- 29 December 2023: South Africa filed case alleging Genocide Convention violations.
- 26 January 2024: ICJ ordered Israel to take all measures within its power to prevent acts of genocide, prevent and punish incitement, allow humanitarian aid.
- March 2024 provisional measures; May 2024 provisional measures (halt Rafah offensive, increase humanitarian aid).
- Israel did not fully comply.
- Counter-Memorial originally due 28 July 2025; extended to 12 January 2026; extended again to 12 March 2026.
- 12 March 2026: Israel filed Counter-Memorial.
- March 2026: 18 states filed Article 63 ICJ Statute interventions: Netherlands ("due diligence obligation"; forced displacement and deliberate withholding of aid as indicators of genocidal intent), Iceland (intent inferred from "patterns of conduct"), Namibia, Spain, Mexico, Turkey, Ireland, Bolivia, Colombia, Belgium, Maldives, Cuba, Libya, Palestine and others.
- US, Hungary, Fiji filed in support of Israel; US characterised genocide allegations as "false."
- Hearings on merits not expected until possibly 2027.

## ICC arrest warrants

- 12 October 2023: ICC chief prosecutor Karim Khan stated both Israeli and Palestinian war crimes within ICC Palestine jurisdiction.
- Late April 2024: ICC staff interviewed Palestinian hospital staff.
- 20 May 2024: Khan filed applications for arrest warrants against Netanyahu, Gallant, and Hamas leaders Yahya Sinwar, Mohammed Deif, Ismail Haniyeh.
- 21 November 2024: ICC Pre-Trial Chamber I (Judges Nicolas Guillou France presiding, Reine Alapini-Gansou Benin, Beti Hohler Slovenia) issued arrest warrants for Netanyahu, Gallant, and Mohammed Deif.
- Charges (Netanyahu and Gallant) from "at least 8 October 2023 until at least 20 May 2024": war crime of starvation as method of warfare; intentionally directing attack against civilian population; crimes against humanity of murder, persecution, other inhumane acts; civilian superior responsibility for directing attacks on civilian population.
- Haniyeh warrant withdrawn after his 31 July 2024 assassination.
- Sinwar warrant withdrawn after his 16 October 2024 killing.
- Deif warrant cancelled January 2025 after Hamas confirmed his 13 July 2024 death in IDF airstrike.
- 2026 Haaretz report (denied by ICC): Office of Prosecutor secretly requested arrest warrants for Ben-Gvir, Smotrich, Settlements Minister Orit Strook, two unnamed IDF officers. ICC denied any new warrants issued.
- 125 ICC member states obliged to arrest if accused enter their territory.
- Netanyahu skipped Davos January 2026 (Switzerland Rome Statute signatory).
- Netanyahu visited Hungary not arrested; Hungary announced withdrawal from ICC.
- UN Commission of Inquiry called for genocide charges added.

## UN Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory

- 16 September 2025: Commission concluded Israel had committed genocide.
- Found four of the five genocidal acts as defined in 1948 Genocide Convention had been committed in Gaza since 7 October 2023: killing members; causing serious bodily or mental harm; deliberately inflicting conditions calculated to bring about destruction; imposing measures intended to prevent births.
- Named Netanyahu, Herzog, Gallant as inciters of genocide.
- Israel rejected report, called for Commission's abolition.
- June 2024 prior UN commission report: Hamas and 6 other Palestinian groups responsible for war crimes (attacks on civilians, murder, torture, hostage-taking); Israeli authorities responsible for war crimes (starvation as method of warfare, murder, attacks on civilians, forcible transfer, sexual violence, torture, arbitrary detention) and crimes against humanity (extermination of Palestinians, gender persecution of Palestinian men and boys).
- March 2025 UN Commission: Israeli attacks on women's healthcare facilities amounted to genocidal acts.

## Other genocide determinations

- December 2024: Amnesty International concluded Israel committing genocide.
- December 2024: Human Rights Watch concluded Israel responsible for crime of genocide through deprivation of safe water and sanitation.
- September 2025: International Association of Genocide Scholars (IAGS) resolution declared Israel committing war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide.
- UN Special Committee recognised genocide.
- Hundreds of scholars and human rights organisations have determined genocide.

## Hamas leadership killings

- Ismail Haniyeh (Hamas Political Bureau chief, Doha-based): assassinated in Tehran 31 July 2024 in strike claimed by Israel.
- Mohammed Deif (commander al-Qassam Brigades): killed 13 July 2024 in Israeli airstrike; death confirmed January 2025.
- Yahya Sinwar (Gaza Hamas leader, October 7 architect, Haniyeh successor): killed by Israeli forces in Gaza 16 October 2024.
- Successor 5-man council: Khalil al-Hayya (Sinwar deputy), Khaled Meshaal, Zaher Jabarin, Muhammad Darwish, unidentified fifth figure. Mostly Qatar or Turkey based.
- September 2025: Israeli strikes targeted Hamas leaders in Qatar including al-Hayya; 5 Hamas members and 1 Qatari security officer killed; top officials survived.
- Raed Saad ( Hamas military wing) killed by Israeli strike December 2025 — accused of spearheading Hamas rearmament.
- 7 May 2026: Hamas chief negotiator's son killed in Israeli strike on Gaza.

## Trump 20-point Comprehensive Plan to End the Gaza Conflict

- 29 September 2025: Trump unveiled plan at White House press conference with Netanyahu.
- Deadline of 5 October 2025 for Hamas acceptance.
- 3 October 2025: Hamas agreed to release all hostages and hand over Gaza administration to "Palestinian body of independent technocrats"; did not agree to disarm.
- 8 October 2025: Trump announced agreement on first phase. Secretary of State Marco Rubio passed note: "You need to approve a Truth Social post soon so you can announce the deal first."
- 9 October 2025: signed in Sharm el-Sheikh.
- 10 October 2025: ceasefire took effect.
- All 20 living hostages released by 13 October 2025 in exchange for 2,000 Palestinian prisoners including 250 serving life sentences and 1,700 detainees.
- 25 deceased hostage remains returned by mid-October.
- Hamas missed deadline for returning last 28 deceased hostages, citing access difficulties.
- 17 November 2025: UN Security Council Resolution 2803 endorsed plan.
- January 2026: Israel confirmed all hostages and remains returned (Wikipedia: "all hostage remains returned").
- Davos January 2026: Board of Peace charter signed (broader scope than just Gaza redevelopment until 2027 PA takeover).
- 18 February 2026: Board of Peace first meeting in Washington — 27 signatory countries, envoys from ~50 countries.
- US NATO allies broadly declined Board membership citing concerns about charter scope and invitations to ICC-wanted leaders (e.g. Russia).
- US sent 200 troops to Israel late 2025 to monitor ceasefire and aid delivery.
- No US troops directly inside Gaza per official statements.
- Israeli troops stationed at Rafah crossing reopened February 2026.
- Civil Military Coordination Center (CMCC) under US military leader.
- US planned two-star general to command International Stabilization Force (ISF).
- 442 Palestinians killed in 1,200+ truce violations since 10 October 2025 ceasefire.
- 500+ Palestinians killed by Israel since ceasefire.

## Earlier ceasefires

- 24 November 2023 - 1 December 2023: Qatar-brokered, 4-day pause extended; 50 Israeli hostages and 150 Palestinian prisoners (women and children) released; Israel arrested almost as many Palestinians as released.
- 19 January 2025: three-stage ceasefire began after 15 January agreement (US, Qatar, Egypt mediated). Stage 1: 33 hostages for ~1,900 prisoners.
- 18 March 2025: Israel surprise attack on Gaza ended ceasefire when Hamas refused additional hostage releases without Israel proceeding to second phase.

## ICJ 19 July 2024 Advisory Opinion

- ICJ Advisory Opinion: Israeli presence in OPT unlawful; Israel must end occupation as rapidly as possible; must facilitate humanitarian relief; respect Palestinian self-determination; prohibition on starvation of civilians.

## Lebanon war 2023-2024

- 8 October 2023: Hezbollah began strikes in solidarity with Palestinians.
- 7 October 2023 - 20 September 2024: 10,200 cross-border attacks (8,300 by Israel).
- Late July 2024: Fuad Shukr assassinated southern Beirut (Hezbollah commander); Haniyeh assassinated Tehran days later.
- 17 September 2024: Pager attack killed at least 12 (including 2 Hezbollah members, 2 children, Iran's ambassador to Lebanon), wounded 2,750+. Gold Apollo AR-924 pagers manufactured and integrated with PETN explosive by Mossad via shell company.
- 18 September 2024: Walkie-talkie attack (Icom devices) killed 30+, injured 750+.
- 20 September 2024: Ibrahim Aqil (Redwan Force commander) killed in Beirut airstrike; 54 others killed.
- 23 September 2024: Operation Northern Arrows began. 558 killed first day including 50 children and 94 women. 800+ killed and 5,000+ injured first week.
- 24 September 2024: Mohammed Hussein Sarour (Hezbollah air force commander) killed in Beirut strike.
- 24 September 2024: Ibrahim Kobeissi (head of Hezbollah missile division) killed in Beirut strike.
- 27 September 2024: Hassan Nasrallah killed by 80+ bombs including US-made 2,000-pound (910 kg) bunker buster bombs on Hezbollah HQ in Dahieh; commander was 20m below ground; explosion felt 40km away. Also killed: Ali Karaki (Southern Front commander).
- Successor Hashem Safieddine targeted, also killed (confirmed October 2024).
- 1 October 2024: Iran fired 181 (or 180) ballistic missiles at Israel in response to Nilforoushan, Nasrallah, Haniyeh killings. At least one building hit in Tel Aviv. US, UK, Jordan helped intercept.
- 1 October 2024: Israel began Lebanon invasion (sixth invasion since 1978).
- 26 October 2024: Israel deployed ~100 fighter jets to strike ~20 sites in Iran (anti-aircraft, missile manufacturing, nuclear-related). Destroyed most Russian-supplied S-300 systems.
- ~1.2 million displaced in Lebanon (20% of population).
- 27 November 2024: US-France-mediated ceasefire took effect. ~4,000 Lebanese dead; 49 Israeli dead.
- 10 November 2024: Netanyahu confirmed Israeli responsibility for pager attack.
- November 2024 - March 2026: ceasefire violations; Israel near-daily strikes in Lebanon; 331 killed by November 2025 (127 civilians); reached 500 total.

## 12-Day War June 2025 (Israel-Iran)

- April 2024: Israeli airstrike on Iranian consulate in Damascus killed Iranian officers.
- April 2024: Iran retaliated with strikes on Israel (first direct exchange).
- October 2024: Iran-Israel second direct exchange.
- March 2025: US Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard testified that US intelligence assessed Iran "not building a nuclear weapon."
- 31 May 2025: IAEA report: Iran had enough 60%-enriched uranium for 9 nuclear weapons.
- 12 June 2025: IAEA board passed resolution (US/UK/France/Germany drafted) declaring Iran non-compliant with nuclear obligations for first time since September 2005.
- 13 June 2025: Israeli Operation Rising Lion began. 200 planes targeted military sites and nuclear sites at Natanz, Fordo, Isfahan. 5 waves of airstrikes by 06:30; 330+ munitions on ~100 targets. Mossad smuggled precision weapons and established covert drone base near Tehran. Operations Red Wedding killed 30 Iranian generals and 9 nuclear scientists in minutes.
- Iran responded with missile salvos at Israel. 500+ Iranian missiles; ~36 landed in populated areas.
- 22 June 2025: US struck Iranian nuclear facilities at Natanz, Fordo, Isfahan using GBU-57 A/B "bunker buster" bombs.
- 23 June 2025: Iran fired missiles at Al-Udeid airbase Qatar (advance warning given to Washington; no casualties).
- 24 June 2025: US-brokered ceasefire took effect.
- Casualties: 1,100+ Iranians killed (Al Jazeera); 610 (Iran initial estimate); 28 Israelis killed.
- ACLED: ~360 Israeli strikes across 27 Iranian provinces over 12 days; estimated 1,000 Iranian ballistic missiles destroyed; 30+ military commanders; 11+ nuclear scientists killed.
- 12 August 2025: Iran arrested up to 21,000 people related to 12-day war per state media.
- 28 August 2025: France/UK/Germany triggered "snapback" mechanism reinstating UN sanctions on Iran.

## 2026 Iran war

- 27 February 2026: Oman foreign minister said Iran agreed to degrade nuclear stockpile to lowest possible level.
- 28 February 2026: Israel and US launched 900+ strikes in 12 hours targeting Iranian missiles, air defences, military infrastructure, leadership.
- 28 February 2026: Initial strikes killed Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and dozens of officials; also killed ~170 people including girls' school adjacent to naval base in Minab, near Bandar Abbas.
- New Supreme Leader: Mojtaba Khamenei (Khamenei's second son).
- Iran responded with missile/air strikes across region including Israel, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Iraq.
- Spain refused to allow US use of bases (NATO member already in dispute with second Trump administration).
- 2 March 2026: Hezbollah launched projectiles at northern Israel — first since 2024 ceasefire — targeting missile defence site south of Haifa.
- 16 March 2026: Israeli ground operations in southern Lebanon began; 5 divisions deployed.
- 1 April 2026: Israeli Navy strike in Beirut killed Hezbollah Southern Front commander Hajj Yusuf Ismail Hashem.
- 4 April 2026: Ain Saade (Christian locality near Beirut) strike killed Lebanese Forces politician Pierre Moawad, his wife, neighbour. IDF claimed Hezbollah target.
- November 2025: Haytham Ali Tabatabai killed.

## West Bank during Gaza war

- At least 806 West Bank Palestinians killed by Israel since 7 October 2023 (Wikipedia).
- 240 killed in 2025; 500 in 2024; 504 in 2023 (per IDF).
- Includes 143 children.
- UN: 800+ Israeli settler attacks Oct 2023 - May 2024.
- 10,000 West Bank Palestinians arrested 7 October 2023 - August 2024.
- 21 January 2025: Israel launched Operation Iron Wall in Jenin; expanded to Tulkarm.
- 40,000+ Palestinians displaced — highest rate since 1967 Palestinian expulsions per UNRWA.
- Iron Wall "longest and most destructive" Israeli WB operation since Second Intifada per UNRWA.
- IDF demolished ~180 homes in Jenin; dozens in Tulkarm; entire residential blocks razed.
- 7,500+ Israeli raids January-September 2025 — 37% increase from 2024.
- 867 incidents of nationalistic crime and settler violence in 2025 (vs 682 in 2024). 128 severe incidents in 2025 (vs 83 in 2024, 54 in 2023). 300 Jewish extremists involved; 70 "hardcore," 39 under restraining orders.
- 26 November 2025: Operation Five Stones in Tubas Governorate ("Pentagon of Villages": Tubas, Tammun, al-Fara'a camp, Aqaba, Tayasir).
- 199 Palestinian bodies withheld by Israeli forces 7 October 2023 - 29 September 2025 per OCHA.
- 70 Palestinians (45 from Gaza, 25 from West Bank) died in Israeli custody 7 October 2023 onward.

## US institutional support

- US healthcare workers letter October 2024: at least 62,413 starvation deaths conservative estimate.
- Tens of billions in arms aid plus diplomatic cover including vetoes of multiple UN Security Council ceasefire resolutions during both Biden and Trump administrations.
- House of Representatives June 2024: amendment banning US officials from using State Department resources to cite GHM casualty figures. Lead sponsor Rep. Jared Moskowitz (D-Fla.) whose all-time top campaign contributor is AIPAC. Moskowitz: "at the end of the day, the Gaza Ministry of Health is the Hamas Ministry of Health."

## Other state and institutional positions

- 9 October 2023 Yoav Gallant: "there will be no more electricity, no more food, no more fuel... We are fighting against human animals and will behave accordingly."
- Netanyahu invoked Amalek (biblical genocide injunction).
- UK Foreign Secretary David Lammy 2 September 2024: suspended ~30 of 350 UK arms export licences to Israel after government review concluded high risk of being used for severe IHL violations.
- 3 September 2025: Scottish Government ban on funding to arms companies provided to countries with plausible evidence of genocide. Scottish Parliament voted boycott of Israel and any companies providing military support.
- February 2026: Public Interest Lawyers (UK) — 2,000+ UK nationals served in IDF during genocide.
- Sweden: 700+ Gaza war crimes reports filed; multiple investigations underway per Prosecutor Reena Devgun.
- December 2023 Scotland's First Minister Humza Yousaf condemned UK abstention from UN ceasefire resolution.

## Hamas attacks and earlier context

- Numerous prior Israel-Hamas escalations 2008-2009, 2012, 2014, 2021, 2022 (smaller scale).
- 2018-19 Great March of Return Gaza protests — IDF killed hundreds of Palestinian protesters, injured thousands.
- Hamas planning of October 7 began after May 2021 conflict.
- Hamas stated motivations: Israeli occupation, Gaza blockade, Al-Aqsa Mosque desecration, settler violence, movement restrictions, imprisonment of Palestinians (sought to release prisoners via hostage-taking).

## Sources verification

- **Verified:** 18 May 2026.

# Sources — Part XII Chapter 6: Sudan, Sahel, Tigray, Myanmar, DRC, Nagorno-Karabakh

## Sudan civil war 2023-present

- Started 15 April 2023 Khartoum between Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) under Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and Rapid Support Forces (RSF) under Gen. Mohamed Hamdan "Hemedti" Dagalo. RSF roots in Janjaweed militia.
- Total displaced: ~14 million people; world's largest displacement crisis.
- ~11 million IDPs; ~4 million refugees in Chad/Egypt/South Sudan; ~900,000 from earlier conflicts.
- Death toll estimates: ACLED tracked 58,000+ fatalities. Former US envoy estimate up to 400,000 dead. Range 150,000-400,000.
- Sudan population: ~50 million (~51 million per Atlantic Council).
- 25-26.4 million in severe food insecurity (April 2026); 28.9 million acutely food insecure (May 2026).
- 30 million required humanitarian aid 2025; 33.7 million projected 2026.
- 2.9 million children acutely malnourished; 729,000 children under-five severe acute malnutrition.
- More than half Sudan's population in acute food insecurity; 10+ million severe/extreme hunger.
- 4 million children acutely malnourished; 770,000 at imminent risk of death.
- Famine confirmed August 2024 Zamzam IDP camp; expanded to five localities (El Fasher, Kadugli); IPC declared.

**Major events:**
- April-May 2023: Khartoum and rapid spread to Darfur, North Kordofan, Gezira state.
- November 2023: Geneina (West Darfur) captured by RSF; Ardamata massacre — up to 2,000 killed; UN panel later estimated 10,000-15,000 killed in El Geneina alone in 2023.
- August 2024: Famine confirmed in Zamzam IDP camp.
- March 2025: SAF recaptured Khartoum.
- 26-27 October 2025: El Fasher captured by RSF after 18-month siege; mass killings begin. UN Human Rights Office confirmed 6,000 killings; "undoubtedly significantly higher." Yale Humanitarian Research Lab satellite analysis: 30,000-60,000 dead; some estimates 60,000-70,000 dead; up to 150,000 dead per satellite-based estimates — described as most lethal massacre of 21st century.
- 11 January 2026: Sudanese military-led government returned to Khartoum from Port Sudan; PM Kamil Idris.
- Early 2026: Kordofan region intensified fighting; almost daily drone strikes.
- 23 February 2026: Chad closed eastern border with Sudan.

**Foreign involvement:**
- UAE: primary RSF backer; weapons and financing through Chad, Libya, recently Ethiopia.
- Sudan considering ICJ action against UAE for arms supply.
- Egypt, Iran, Turkey: SAF support.
- China: weapons to both sides.
- Russia: both sides; Africa Corps in region.
- Wagner/Africa Corps mining/gold interests in Sudan.
- Uganda President Museveni meeting with Hemedti.

**Atrocities:**
- US government formally found in 2023 RSF and allied militias had committed genocide in Darfur with systematic targeting of ethnic Massalit and other non-Arab communities.
- UN: hallmarks of genocide.
- Human Rights Watch: RSF targeting of people with disabilities at El Fasher; point-blank executions of amputees and people with Down syndrome.
- Both sides: child soldier recruitment, sexual violence, blocking aid.
- 8% of humanitarian funding need met in 2026.
- USAID cuts under Trump: 60%+ of Sudanese-run emergency food kitchens closed.

## Sahel insurgencies and coup belt

**Coups:**
- August 2020 and May 2021: Mali military coups (Assimi Goïta).
- September 2021: Guinea coup.
- January and September 2022: Burkina Faso coups (Capt. Ibrahim Traoré final ruler).
- July 2023: Niger coup (Gen. Abdourahamane Tchiani).
- August 2023: Gabon coup.

**Russia/Africa Corps deployment:**
- 1,000 new Africa Corps soldiers arriving Mali in coming months — total ~3,500 troops.
- Mali reportedly paying $35 million monthly for Africa Corps maintenance.
- Goïta to Russia June 2025.
- Africa Corps expansion to Burkina Faso, possibly Niger.

**JNIM (Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wa al-Muslimeen) and ISSP (Islamic State Sahel Province):**
- JNIM blockade of Bamako since September 2025; major attacks on fuel convoys southern/western Mali.
- 60%+ of Mali's total trade passes along Northern Bamako-Dakar Corridor.
- JNIM 2024 attacked main military air base and gendarmerie base in Bamako.
- ISSP doubled territory in Mali 2022-2023; expanded into Niger 2023-2024.
- ISSP controls significant Mali-Niger border.
- IS recruiters established transit corridors between southern Europe and Sahel; attack cell out of Morocco and Spain.

**Alliance of Sahel States (AES):**
- Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger formal alliance.
- Withdrawal from ECOWAS January 2024 (effective 2025).
- French troops expelled from Mali (December 2022), Burkina Faso (February 2023), Niger (December 2023).
- US troops withdrawn from Niger 2024.

## Tigray war 2020-2022 and aftermath

- November 2020 - November 2022: Ethiopian federal government under PM Abiy Ahmed + Eritrean Defense Forces (EDF) + Amhara regional forces vs Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) / Tigray Defense Forces (TDF).
- 2 November 2022: Pretoria Peace Agreement (Cessation of Hostilities Agreement) — Ethiopia and TPLF; Eritrea not a party.
- Estimated 600,000 dead by time Pretoria Agreement took effect (CFR figure).
- 100,000+ battle-related deaths in 2022 alone; ~500,000 civilians may have died from killings, starvation, lack of healthcare (Congressional Research Service).
- September 2022 Ethiopia/Eritrea joint offensive: Ethiopian army reportedly suffered 90,000 casualties in one month.
- 5.1 million internally displaced 2021 alone — record for most internally displaced in any country in any single year at the time.

**Atrocities:**
- US Secretary of State Antony Blinken March 2021: "ethnic cleansing" in western Tigray.
- Blinken March 2023: war crimes, crimes against humanity, ethnic cleansing committed by multiple parties.
- 4 June 2024: New Lines Institute 120-page report: strong evidence of genocidal acts committed by Ethiopian forces and allies; called for Ethiopia prosecution at ICJ.
- ICHREE (International Commission of Human Rights Experts on Ethiopia) terminated October 2023 after Ethiopian diplomatic pressure.
- Mass sexual violence used as weapon of war.
- Mai Kadra and Axum massacres documented.
- Maternal mortality in Tigray rose from 186 per 100,000 prewar to 840 per 100,000 postwar.
- Tigray Genocide referenced by US Secretary of State Blinken; Pope Francis; Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus (WHO Director-General, ethnic Tigrayan); Abune Mathias (Ethiopian Orthodox Church Patriarch, ethnic Tigrayan).
- "The forgotten genocide" (New Lines Institute, 2024).

**Post-war:**
- Eritrean troops and Amhara forces remain in parts of Tigray.
- Amhara region war: Fano militia vs Ethiopian National Defense Force (ENDF) ongoing since 2023.
- Oromia region: Oromo Liberation Army (OLA) insurgency.
- 2,000 killed in 2023 in Amhara/Oromo/other regional conflicts.
- Power struggle 2025-2026: TPLF vs Tigray Interim Administration (TIA, appointed by Abiy under Pretoria Agreement).
- Eritrea reportedly supporting dissident TPLF factions.

## Myanmar civil war 2021-present

- 1 February 2021: military coup by Tatmadaw led by Gen. Min Aung Hlaing overthrew elected Aung San Suu Kyi NLD government.
- Civilian death toll per Assistance Association for Political Prisoners (AAPP) to April 2025: 6,486 civilians killed including 1,494 women, 751 children.
- AAPP to January 2025: 6,231 civilians killed including 1,144 women, 709 children over 4 years.
- 2024: 1,824 people killed (UN OHCHR verified); 2023: 1,639 verified.
- UN Global Conflict Tracker: 75,000+ total deaths in conflict; 3 million+ displaced.
- ACLED tracks 2,600+ armed groups emerged since coup.
- PDF (People's Defence Force) of National Unity Government (NUG): 65,000 fighters 2022 rising to 85,000 by 2024.
- Major ethnic armed organisations: Kachin Independence Army (KIA), Shan State Army, Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA), Arakan Army, MNDAA, TNLA.
- 27 October 2023: Operation 1027 launched by Three Brotherhood Alliance (MNDAA, TNLA, Arakan Army) in northern Shan State.
- Late 2023: resistance forces in control of more than half Myanmar's territory.
- 2024: junta lost significant territory.
- China intervention: stabilising junta; pressuring opposition for elections.
- January 2025: junta announced general elections by end of 2025/early 2026 to consolidate political control.
- 28 March 2025: Sagaing earthquake magnitude 7.7 — major destruction.

**Atrocities:**
- 11 April 2023 Pa Zi Gyi village (Sagaing): military airstrike on People's Administration Team gathering — 175 killed including 33 women, 42 children; HRW concluded thermobaric bomb used.
- 18 March 2024 Thar Dar (Rakhine): airstrike killed 22 Rohingya civilians including children.
- Multiple airstrikes on churches, high schools, residences.
- Rohingya: ongoing displacement; 1 million+ in Bangladesh refugee camps from 2017 ethnic cleansing.

## DRC / M23 war eastern Congo

- M23 (March 23 Movement) — Rwandan-backed Tutsi-led armed group.
- Rwanda-backed M23 vs FARDC (Congolese army), Wazalendo militia, MONUSCO/SAMIDRC.
- UN reports: 3,000-4,000 Rwandan Defense Forces (RDF) troops fighting alongside M23 in eastern DRC; "de facto control" M23 operations.
- 27 January 2025: M23 captured Goma (2 million population). Worst escalation in decade+.
- 16 February 2025: M23 captured Bukavu.
- 19 March 2025: M23 captured Walikale mining hub.
- 7,000 killed in recent months.
- 780,000 displaced November 2024 - January 2025.
- 100,000+ refugees crossed into neighbouring countries since 1 January 2025: 69,000 to Burundi, 29,000 to Uganda, ~1,000 to Rwanda and Tanzania.
- 21 million in DRC needed humanitarian aid pre-escalation — highest globally per UN OCHA.
- DRC population: ~112 million.
- Washington Peace Accords (Trump-mediated) — Rwanda violations re Uvira town offensive late 2025.
- Late January 2026: Rwanda and M23 publicly acknowledged ties for first time.
- February 2026: Congolese army drone strike killed M23 military leader Masisi district.
- Trump administration considering sanctions on RDF, Rwandan intelligence, mining officials.

## Nagorno-Karabakh 2023

- 19-20 September 2023: Azerbaijan launched "anti-terrorist operation" against breakaway Republic of Artsakh.
- Hundreds of casualties both sides; 5 Russian peacekeepers killed.
- 20 September: ceasefire / surrender agreement.
- 28 September: Artsakh President Samvel Shahramanyan signed decree dissolving all state institutions by 1 January 2024.
- December 2022 - September 2023: nine-month Azerbaijani blockade of Lachin Corridor (only road linking Artsakh to Armenia).
- February 2023: ICJ ordered Azerbaijan to ensure free movement; ruling that blockade posed "real and imminent risk" to "health and life" of Nagorno-Karabakh's Armenian population.
- Pre-offensive Artsakh population: ~120,000 ethnic Armenians (95% of region).
- End of September 2023: 100,400 ethnic Armenians (99%) fled to Armenia through Lachin Corridor.
- Only couple dozen Armenians remained.
- Mass forced displacement characterised by international experts as war crime or crime against humanity.
- Lemkin Institute for Genocide Prevention urged women and children refuse separation from men and older boys.

**Historical context:**
- 1988-1994: First Nagorno-Karabakh War — Azerbaijan lost 13.6% of territory.
- 27 September - 9 November 2020: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War — Azerbaijan reclaimed surrounding territories and one-third of Nagorno-Karabakh; 9 November tripartite ceasefire Pashinyan/Aliyev mediated by Russia.
- August 2025: Trump administration hosted Pashinyan and Aliyev at White House — Washington Peace Accord; "Trump Route for International Peace and Prosperity" transit route through southern Armenia.
- 2025: Azerbaijan trials of 16 former ethnic Armenian officials including former state minister Ruben Vardanyan — Armenian officials criticised as "mock trials."

# Sources — Part XII Chapter 7: The Proxy-War Architecture

## Definition and historical scope

- "Proxy war": armed conflict in which great powers support local belligerents to advance their own interests while avoiding direct combat. Britannica: "Proxy wars became a more acceptable way for the two superpowers to compete for world influence."
- Historical precedents pre-Cold War: France used American colonists as proxies vs Britain (American Revolution; more French than Continental troops at Yorktown 1781); Russia-Serbia vs Austro-Hungarian Empire pre-1914.
- Per RAND "Proxy Warfare in Strategic Competition" (Salehyan-Karr 2024): "prevalence of proxy warfare varies over time. Its use in the modern era peaked in the late Cold War and has declined since."
- Hoover Institution conference: "Today, the United States is engaged in multiple proxy wars: supplying arms to the Ukrainians to repel and degrade the Russian military; supplying arms to Israel so it can dismantle Hamas (an Iranian proxy); and directly confronting a wider array of Iranian proxies in Syria, Iraq, and the Red Sea."

## Cold War proxy wars (1945-1991) — recap of Ch1

- Korean War 1950-1953: 2-4 million dead. US/UN vs DPRK/PRC/USSR.
- Indochina/Vietnam War 1955-1975: 2-3 million dead. US vs USSR-PRC-DRV.
- Cambodia bombings/Khmer Rouge 1969-1979: 2-3 million dead.
- Laos secret war 1959-1975: 500K-1M dead.
- Greek Civil War 1946-1949: ~158K dead.
- Malayan Emergency 1948-1960: ~13K dead.
- Indonesian mass killings 1965-1966: 500K-1M dead.
- Angolan Civil War 1975-2002: 500K-1.5M dead.
- Mozambican Civil War 1977-1992: ~1M dead.
- Ethiopian-Somali Ogaden War 1977-1978: ~25-50K dead.
- Ethiopian Civil War 1974-1991: 1.4M dead.
- Nicaraguan Sandinista Revolution and Contra War 1972-1990: ~75K dead.
- El Salvador Civil War 1979-1992: ~75K dead.
- Guatemala Civil War 1960-1996: ~200K dead (mostly indigenous Mayans).
- Afghan Soviet-Afghan War 1979-1989: 1-2M dead.

## Post-Cold War US wars — recap of Ch2

- Gulf War 1991: 20-35K Iraqi military dead, 3-200K civilian dead.
- Bosnia 1992-1995: ~100K dead.
- Kosovo 1998-1999: ~13K dead.
- Afghanistan 2001-2021: 176-243K dead (Costs of War: 176,000 minimum; 243,000 including post-war).
- Iraq 2003-2011 invasion and occupation: 200K-1M dead (Lancet 2006: 654K excess deaths; IBC: 100-200K; Costs of War: ~280K direct).
- Libya 2011 NATO intervention: ~25K dead (war), multi-year subsequent civil war.
- Syria 2011-present: 500K-650K+ dead.
- Yemen 2014-present: 377K+ dead per UN (most through hunger/disease from blockade).
- Somalia 1992-present (US drone strikes, AMISOM, AFRICOM): multi-tens-of-thousands.

## Iran-Iraq War — recap of Ch3

- 1980-1988: 1-1.5 million dead. Both superpowers supplied both sides at various points.

## Ukraine war — recap of Ch4

- 2014-present: 400-550K dead combined.
- World's largest arms importer in 2020-2024 (SIPRI Mar 2025): Ukraine, +9,627% vs 2015-2019.
- World's largest arms importer 2021-2025 (SIPRI Mar 2026): Ukraine, 9.7% of global imports.

## Gaza war — recap of Ch5

- 2023-present: 75-100K+ dead.
- Costs of War Brown University: US spent $21.7 billion in military aid to Israel in first two years of war.

## The unseen wars — recap of Ch6

- Sudan 2023-present: 150-400K dead.
- Tigray 2020-2022: ~600K dead.
- Myanmar 2021-present: 75K+ dead.
- DRC eastern Congo 2024-2026 escalation: 7K+ killed recent months.
- Sahel insurgencies 2020-2026: multi-tens-of-thousands.
- Nagorno-Karabakh 2023: hundreds killed, 100,400 ethnically cleansed.

## SIPRI Arms Transfers Data 2020-2024 (March 2025 release)

- US 43% of global major arms exports 2020-2024 (up from 35% in 2015-2019). +21% vs prior period.
- Russia 7.8% (down 64% from 2015-2019).
- France 9.6%.
- China 5.9%.
- Germany 5.6%.
- Italy 4.8%.
- UK 3.6%.
- Israel 3.1%.
- South Korea 2.2%.
- Spain 2.1%.

**Top 10 importers 2020-2024:**
- Ukraine (8.8%, +9,627%).
- India (8.3%, -9.3%).
- Qatar (6.8%, +127%).
- Saudi Arabia (6.6%, -41%).
- Pakistan (4.6%, +61%).
- Japan (3.9%).
- Australia.
- Egypt.
- Kuwait.
- Norway.

**Regional flows:**
- Asia/Oceania: 33% of global imports.
- Europe: 28% (+155% vs 2015-2019).
- Middle East: 27% (-20%).
- Americas: 6.2%.
- Africa: 4.5% (-44%).

**European NATO imports:**
- +105% between 2015-2019 and 2020-2024.
- 64% from US (up from 52%).
- France 6.5%, South Korea 6.5%, Germany 4.7%, Israel 3.9%.

## SIPRI 2021-2025 (March 2026 release)

- US 42% of global major arms exports.
- US +27% vs 2016-2020.
- Russia 6.8% (down from 21% in 2016-2020).
- Russia received 0.4% of global imports including Iranian one-way attack drones and North Korean ballistic missiles (violation of UN arms embargo on DPRK).
- 25 states agreed in 2025 to buy major arms from US for transfer to Ukraine.
- Ukraine 9.7% of global imports 2021-2025 (vs 0.1% in 2016-2020).
- European NATO arms imports grew 143% from 2016-2020 to 2021-2025.
- 58% of European NATO imports from US.
- Saudi Arabia 77% of arms imports from US.
- Qatar 48% from US (with 33 of 100 combat aircraft from UK, 13 from France, 6 from Italy).
- Qatar +106% vs 2016-2020; 79 combat aircraft on order from France end of 2025.

## NATO/US-allied dominance

- US + NATO + Israel + South Korea + Australia: 80%+ of global arms exports.
- 16 of top 20 exporters are US allies.
- Non-US-allied in top 20: Russia (7.8%), China (5.9%), Iran (0.4%).
- 12 NATO members in top 20 exporters.
- US presence in 107 countries.

## US military spending

- US 37% of global military expenditure 2023.
- US spent more than next 9 largest spenders combined.
- US 3x China; 8x Russia.
- US "defense" budget ~$880 billion baseline (excluding off-book appropriations and military aid packages).
- Trump 2nd-term demand: NATO allies 5% of GDP on defense.

## Specific Trump-era arms deals

- May 2025: $142 billion Saudi Arabia deal — characterised by Trump administration as "the largest defence sales agreement in history."
- 23 January 2021 (late Trump 1): provisionally agreed $23 billion package to UAE including F-35 stealth fighter aircraft (Biden proceeded April 2021).
- Trump 2 (January 2025-): multiple Gulf packages.
- FY 2020-2024 FMS deals: Bahrain $1.21B, Kuwait $9.11B, Qatar $675M, UAE $4.807B.
- Gulf monarchies combined: 17.3% of US major conventional weapons transfers 2020-2024.

## US-Israel military aid

- Costs of War Brown University: $21.7 billion in first 2 years of Gaza war.
- 70%+ of Israeli arms imports from US over period.
- Multi-year $3.8 billion/year baseline under 2016 MOU.

## US-Ukraine military aid

- US authorised $175+ billion across multi-year supplemental appropriations 2022-2024.
- Ukraine 9% of all US conventional arms transfers 2020-2024.
- 30+ states transferred arms to Ukraine 2022-2023.
- 25+ states agreed 2025 to buy US arms for transfer to Ukraine (Trump-era model).

## Major US arms manufacturers (Military-Industrial Complex)

- Lockheed Martin: F-35, HIMARS, Javelin, Patriot, JASSM, THAAD.
- RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon/Raytheon Technologies): Patriot, AMRAAM, Stinger, Tomahawk.
- General Dynamics: Abrams tanks, naval shipbuilding (Bath Iron Works, Electric Boat).
- Northrop Grumman: B-21 Raider, Global Hawk, ICBM, naval programs.
- Boeing: F-15EX, AH-64 Apache, KC-46, P-8 Poseidon.
- L3Harris.
- BAE Systems (UK).
- Stock-price performance during 2022-2024 Ukraine war and 2023-2024 Gaza war: significant multi-year gains across all major defence contractors.

## Russian/Chinese/Iranian arms architecture

- Russia: Rosoboronexport, Almaz-Antey, Rostec; major exports to India, China, Algeria, Egypt, Vietnam.
- China: NORINCO, AVIC, CETC; major exports to Pakistan, Bangladesh, Algeria, Myanmar.
- Iran: HESA, IRGC industrial complex; Shahed-136 drones to Russia; missiles to Houthis, Hezbollah, Hamas, Iraqi militias.

## Notable proxy-war academic literature

- Mearsheimer, John J. (2014). "Why the Ukraine Crisis Is the West's Fault." Foreign Affairs.
- Mearsheimer, John J. (2022). "John Mearsheimer on Putin's Ambitions After Nine Months of War." Lecture series.
- Walt, Stephen M. Multi-year writings.
- Sachs, Jeffrey D. Multi-year writings.
- Kennan, George F. (1997). "A Fateful Error." New York Times op-ed re NATO expansion.

- Khalilzad, Zalmay; Lesser, Ian; Larrabee, F. Stephen. (1996/2003). "The Future of Turkish-Western Relations." RAND.
- Brzezinski, Zbigniew. (1997). The Grand Chessboard.

- RAND Corporation. (2019). "Extending Russia: Competing from Advantageous Ground."
- RAND Corporation. (2023). "Proxy Warfare in Strategic Competition: State Motivations and Future Trends."
- New America. (2024-2026). "Twenty-First Century Proxy Warfare: Confronting Strategic Innovation in a Multipolar World."

## Theatre matrix of multi-year contests

- Ukraine: US/NATO vs Russia (open).
- Gaza/Israel/Lebanon/Iran: US/UK/Germany/Italy/France/Canada/Australia (Israel) vs Iran (Hamas, Hezbollah, Houthis, Iraqi militias).
- Syria: US/Turkey vs Russia/Iran (until Dec 2024 Assad collapse; subsequently US/Turkey/Israel vs Iran/Russia).
- Yemen: Saudi Arabia/UAE (US/UK arms) vs Houthis (Iran arms).
- Libya: UAE/Russia/Egypt (Haftar/LNA) vs Turkey/Italy (GNU).
- Sudan: UAE/Russia (RSF) vs Egypt/Iran/Turkey/China (SAF).
- Sahel: Russia (Africa Corps) + AES juntas vs JNIM (al-Qaeda) and ISSP (IS).
- Tigray/Ethiopia: UAE drones to Ethiopian government; Eritrea direct; US/EU multi-month tensions.
- Myanmar: China primary backer of junta; Russia arms; India ambivalent; US sanctions but limited.
- DRC: Rwanda backing M23; EU/US mineral supply dependency; China mineral acquisitions.
- Nagorno-Karabakh: Turkey (Bayraktar) + Israel (Harop) backing Azerbaijan; Russia peacekeeping failure.
- South China Sea / Taiwan Strait: US/Japan/Australia/Philippines vs China.
- Korean Peninsula: US/South Korea vs China/Russia/DPRK.

# Sources — Part XII Chapter 8: The Weapons Industry

## Eisenhower's farewell address

- 17 January 1961: President Dwight D. Eisenhower farewell address.
- Key quote: "In the councils of government, we must guard against the acquisition of unwarranted influence, whether sought or unsought, by the military-industrial complex. The potential for the disastrous rise of misplaced power exists and will persist."
- Additional quote: "This conjunction of an immense military establishment and a large arms industry is new in the American experience."
- Five-star general; supreme commander Allied Expeditionary Force WWII; 34th US president.

## SIPRI global military expenditure data

**2024:**
- World military expenditure: $2,718 billion (record, 10th consecutive year of rises).
- 9.4% increase real terms over 2023 (steepest since end of Cold War).
- Global military burden: 2.5% of GDP.
- Top 5 spenders (US, China, Russia, Germany, India) = 60% of global total = $1,635 billion.
- Europe (including Russia): $693 billion (+17%); beyond level recorded at end of Cold War.
- US: $997 billion (3.7% of GDP) including supplemental appropriations.
- China: $314 billion estimated.
- Russia: $149 billion (+38%, 7.1% of GDP, ~40% of federal expenditure 2025).
- Average military burden for countries with major armed conflicts in 2024: 4.4% vs 1.9% for those without.

**2025:**
- World military expenditure: $2,887 billion (record, 11th consecutive year of rises).
- 2.9% increase real terms over 2024.
- Outside US: 9.2% increase.
- US: decline.
- Europe: +14%.
- Asia/Oceania: +8.1%.
- Top 3 spenders (US, China, Russia): $1,480 billion = 51% of global total.
- Global military burden 2.5% GDP — highest since 2009.

**SIPRI Top 100 arms producers:**
- 2023 revenues: $632 billion (+4.2% over 2022).
- 2024 revenues: $679 billion (+5.9% over 2023, record). Growth 26% from 2015 to 2024.
- 30 of 39 US companies reported revenue increases.
- 26 European Top 100 companies (excluding Russia): 23 recorded increases. Aggregate $151 billion (+13%).
- Czechoslovak Group: +193% to $3.6 billion (sharpest percentage increase). Czech Ammunition Initiative for Ukraine.
- JSC Ukrainian Defense Industry: +41%.
- 9 Middle East companies entered Top 100 (first time):
- 3 Israeli firms: $16.2 billion (+16%).
- 5 Turkish companies: $10.1 billion (+11%).
- UAE EDGE Group: $4.7 billion.
- Combined Middle East: $31 billion (+14%).

## Top US defense contractors (2023-2024)

**Pentagon contract awards 2023:**
- Lockheed Martin: $61.4 billion.
- RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon): $24.1 billion.
- General Dynamics.
- Boeing.
- Northrop Grumman.

**Defense revenue 2024:**
- Lockheed Martin: $64.7 billion.
- RTX: $40.6 billion.

**Quincy Institute "Profits of War" 2020-2024:**
- Private firms received $2.4 trillion in Pentagon contracts (54% of $4.4 trillion discretionary).
- Top 5 received $771 billion combined:
- Lockheed Martin: $313 billion.
- RTX (formerly Raytheon): $145 billion.
- General Dynamics: $116 billion.
- Boeing: $115 billion.
- Northrop Grumman: $81 billion.
- Total diplomacy/development/humanitarian budget (excluding military aid) 2020-2024: $356 billion (compared to $2.4 trillion to private contractors).
- Post-9/11 wars (Afghanistan, Iraq, etc.): top 5 contractors received $2 trillion.
- Number of prime DOD contractors fell from 51 in 1990s to just 5 today (Lockheed Martin, RTX, General Dynamics, Boeing, Northrop Grumman).

**Major US contractor specifics:**
- Lockheed Martin (Bethesda, MD): F-35 Lightning II, F-22 Raptor, F-16 Fighting Falcon, C-130 Hercules, C-5 Galaxy, U-2, SR-71 (legacy), F-117 (legacy), Aegis Combat System (partial), Sikorsky helicopters (Black Hawk, Sea Hawk), Patriot PAC-3, THAAD, Terminal High Altitude Area Defense, JASSM, HIMARS High Mobility Artillery Rocket System, GMLRS, ATACMS, PrSM, Hellfire missile, Javelin (joint with Raytheon), space (Trident SLBM, Orion crew capsule, satellite platforms). Acquired Terran Orbital (satellite firm) August 2024.
- RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon Technologies; Arlington, VA): Patriot air defense, NASAMS, SM-3, SM-6, Tomahawk cruise missiles, AMRAAM, AIM-9 Sidewinder, Stinger MANPADS, Javelin (joint with Lockheed Martin), Coyote, hypersonic weapons development, Pratt & Whitney engines, Collins Aerospace, commercial aviation.
- General Dynamics (Reston, VA): M1 Abrams tank, Stryker IFV, Bradley IFV (BAE actually), Bath Iron Works (DDG-51 Arleigh Burke destroyers), Electric Boat (Virginia-class and Columbia-class submarines), Gulfstream business jets, IT services.
- Northrop Grumman (Falls Church, VA): B-21 Raider, B-2 Spirit (legacy), Global Hawk, Triton, RQ-4, ICBM Sentinel (replacing Minuteman III), Joint STARS, naval programs, satellite platforms.
- Boeing (Arlington, VA): F-15EX Eagle II, F/A-18 Super Hornet, AH-64 Apache, CH-47 Chinook, KC-46 Pegasus, P-8 Poseidon, E-7 Wedgetail (in development), Insitu drones, Phantom Works, civil aviation (737 MAX issues unrelated).
- L3Harris Technologies (Melbourne, FL): communications, electronic warfare, ISR.
- Booz Allen Hamilton, Leidos, SAIC, CACI: defense IT and consulting.

**Major European defense contractors:**
- BAE Systems (UK): Eurofighter Typhoon partner, Type 45 destroyer, Astute-class submarine, Bradley IFV (US division), CV90, AS90 howitzer.
- Airbus Defence and Space (multi-national): Eurofighter Typhoon partner, A400M, A330 MRTT, Tiger helicopter, NH90.

- Hensoldt (Germany): radar, electronic warfare.
- Saab AB (Sweden): JAS 39 Gripen, GlobalEye AEW&C, Carl Gustaf, NLAW.
- Kongsberg (Norway): Naval Strike Missile, NASAMS partner.

- Israel Aerospace Industries (IAI): Heron, Harop, Arrow 3, Iron Dome partner (with Rafael), Barak missiles, naval.
- Elbit Systems: drones, electronics, artillery, EW.
- Rafael Advanced Defense Systems: Iron Dome, David's Sling, SPIKE missiles, Trophy active protection.

**Turkish defense:**
- Baykar (Bayraktar TB2, TB3, Akinci, Kizilelma drones): Used in Nagorno-Karabakh 2020, Ukraine 2022+, Libya, Syria, Ethiopia (Tigray).
- Aselsan: electronics, comms, EW.
- TUSAS Turkish Aerospace Industries: helicopters, drones, Hurjet trainer.
- Roketsan: missiles.
- STM.
- MKE.

**Russian defense:**
- Almaz-Antey: S-300, S-400, S-500 air defense.
- United Aircraft Corporation (UAC): Sukhoi (Su-30, Su-34, Su-35, Su-57), MiG, Tupolev, Ilyushin, Yakovlev.
- Russian Helicopters: Mi-8/17, Mi-24, Mi-28, Ka-52.
- Rostec (parent conglomerate).
- Kalashnikov Concern: AK platform, Kornet.
- Tactical Missiles Corporation (KTRV): Kh-101, Kh-55.
- Uralvagonzavod: T-72, T-80, T-90, T-14 Armata.
- Rosoboronexport (state arms export agency).

**Chinese defense:**
- AVIC (Aviation Industry Corporation of China): J-10, J-16, J-20, J-31/J-35.
- NORINCO (China North Industries Corporation): ground systems, ammunition.
- CETC (China Electronics Technology Group Corporation): electronics, radar.
- CSSC (China State Shipbuilding Corporation): naval.

**Iranian defense:**
- HESA (Iran Aircraft Manufacturing Industrial Company): Shahed-136, Shahed-129, Karrar drones.
- Aerospace Industries Organization (AIO): missiles.
- IRGC industrial complex.

## US Military-Industrial-Complex political economy

- US "defense" budget 2024 (Pentagon + DOE nuclear weapons): $886 billion baseline (more than 2x inflation-adjusted Eisenhower-era level).
- Pentagon has never passed an audit (failed 7 consecutive years as of 2024).
- Defense industry lobbying expenditure: ~$140 million/year.
- 820 lobbyists employed by defense industry — more than 1 per member of Congress.
- 2/3 of those lobbyists came through the revolving door from Pentagon/Congress.
- ~700 former government officials (per Senator Elizabeth Warren office) now work for defense contractors.
- 91% of revolving-door personnel became Pentagon lobbyists.

## Foreign arms sales and ethics

- Saudi Arabia using US weapons in Yemen war ("one of the largest humanitarian crises in the world" per UNICEF).
- September 2021: Congressional effort to limit US arms sales to Saudi Arabia defeated after weapons manufacturer lobbying.
- May 2025: Trump $142 billion Saudi Arabia arms deal — characterised as "largest defence sales agreement in history."

## Stock performance during Ukraine and Gaza wars

- Lockheed Martin: stock significantly up from February 2022 through 2024.
- RTX (Raytheon): stock significantly up.
- Northrop Grumman: stock significantly up.
- General Dynamics: stock significantly up.
- BAE Systems: stock significantly up.
- Rheinmetall: stock up several-hundred percent since February 2022.
- Leonardo: stock up.

# Sources — Part XIII Chapter 1: The Devil

## The Volume One mechanism

Volume One (*Behind the Mask: The Demon They Call God*, 207,500 words, published 13 May 2026) documented the four-century debt-money mechanism. Specifically:

- The 1694 Bank of England founding under William Paterson's charter from William III in exchange for the £1.2 million loan that funded the Nine Years' War.
- The 1789 First Bank of the United States (Hamilton's Treasury under Washington).
- The Jacksonian Bank War 1832-1836 against the Second Bank of the United States.
- The 1913 Federal Reserve Act under Wilson (Jekyll Island meeting November 1910: Paul Warburg, Frank Vanderlip, Henry Davison, Charles Norton, Benjamin Strong, Senator Nelson Aldrich).
- The 1933 Glass-Steagall Act and the 1944 Bretton Woods system (35 oz gold to the dollar).
- The 15 August 1971 Nixon shock terminating dollar-gold convertibility.
- The 1973-1974 petrodollar architecture (Saudi-US deal: oil priced in dollars, Saudi reserves recycled into US Treasuries).
- The 1980s deregulation (Reagan, Volcker, S&L crisis).
- The 1999 Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act repealing Glass-Steagall (Clinton signs).
- The 2008 financial crisis and bailouts (TARP $700 billion; Fed $7+ trillion balance sheet expansion; AIG bailout; Goldman, Morgan Stanley as bank holding companies).
- The Bezos / Gates / Musk / Schmidt / Sackler / Koch capture pathways documented across 26 chapters.

## The mechanism's contemporary operators

Per Volume One:
- BlackRock (Larry Fink, founded 1988; $10+ trillion AUM as of 2024).
- Vanguard ($9+ trillion AUM).
- State Street ($4+ trillion AUM).
- The Federal Reserve (Jerome Powell; Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen 2021-2025; current Treasury Scott Bessent under Trump 2 from January 2025).
- JPMorgan Chase (Jamie Dimon).
- Goldman Sachs (David Solomon).
- Morgan Stanley.
- Bank of America.
- Citigroup.
- The City of London architecture (Bank of England under Andrew Bailey).
- The ECB (Christine Lagarde).
- The IMF (Kristalina Georgieva).
- The World Bank (Ajay Banga).
- The BIS (Agustín Carstens).

## The Volume Two political-class architecture

Across this volume's drafted material:

**American political-class:**
- Biden administration principals (Dossier 1).
- Harris (Dossier 2).
- Trump 2 administration including Vance, Musk's prior DOGE role, Bessent (Dossier 3).

## "Capital is the devil" — series through-line

Per series master:
- The series argument: capital — meaning the four-century debt-money mechanism Volume One named — is the devil across all volumes.
- Each volume's demon is a mask the devil wears: Volume One the financial-system mask (God); Volume Two the state-apparatus mask (the State); Volume Three the religious-institutional mask (the Church); Volume Four the consensus-knowledge mask (the Knowing).
- The four-volume core argues: capital is the underlying mechanism; the four institutional masks are the operational surfaces through which capital extracts.

## Composition characteristics across the dossiered class

Drawing from the cumulative dossier evidentiary record:

**Wealth and class background:**
- Joe Biden net worth: ~$10 million per public records (declared post-presidency).
- Donald Trump net worth: ~$5-7 billion per Forbes (2024).
- Mike Johnson: relatively modest net worth (~$1-2 million).
- Senate millionaire majority: 51% of US senators have net worth over $1 million per OpenSecrets multi-year data; Congressional millionaire share has been documented above 50% since 2010s.
- Pete Hegseth net worth: ~$2 million (pre-Defense Secretary).
- Howard Lutnick net worth: ~$2 billion (Cantor Fitzgerald CEO before Commerce).
- Scott Bessent net worth: ~$700 million (Key Square Group founder).
- Vivek Ramaswamy net worth: ~$1 billion.
- Elon Musk net worth: ~$400 billion (Forbes 2025 — world's richest person).
- Mark Carney net worth: ~$10 million pre-PM.
- Modi declared assets: ~₹3 crore (~$360,000) per India Today 2024 — among the lowest of any major political-class operator dossiered, but Indian political-class wealth declaration is documented multi-year as unreliable.
- Adani-Ambani fortunes connected to Modi: ~$100 billion combined.

**Education concentration:**
- US Senate: ~75% have graduate degrees; ~50% law degrees per Pew.
- US Cabinet 2025: heavy concentration of Ivy League and elite private universities.
- UK political class: Oxford-Cambridge dominance .
- French political class: ENA/INSP and Sciences Po .
- Indian political class: more diverse but elite-university concentration in bureaucracy (JNU, DU, IITs, IIMs).

**Revolving door:**
- Multi-decade Treasury-Goldman Sachs pipeline (Rubin, Paulson, Mnuchin, Bessent partial).
- Multi-decade Federal Reserve-Treasury rotation (Yellen, Powell, multi-year predecessors).
- Multi-decade Pentagon-defense-contractor revolving door (Senator Warren's office found ~700 former officials at defense contractors; 91% as lobbyists per the report cited in Part XII Ch7).
- Multi-decade UK political-class to private finance (Blair, Osborne, Cameron, Hunt).
- Multi-decade EU Commission to multinational corporate boards (Barroso to Goldman Sachs 2016; Kroes to Uber; multi-year examples per Transparency International EU).

**Religious affiliation:**
- US Congress: ~88% Christian per Pew January 2025 (vs ~63% of US adult population) — significantly overrepresented.
- US Senate: ~91% Christian.
- Multi-decade Catholic share of SCOTUS unusually high (6 of 9 justices Catholic; 2 Jewish; 1 Episcopalian/Catholic split per Sotomayor mixed identification).
- Indian political class: BJP/RSS architecture rooted in Hindutva.
- Pakistani political class: Sunni Muslim dominant; tensions with Shia and Ahmadi minorities.

**Age:**
- Trump 78 (born 14 June 1946) on inauguration January 2025.
- Biden 81 (born 20 November 1942) on leaving office January 2025.
- McConnell 82 (born 20 February 1942).
- Pelosi 84 (born 26 March 1940).
- Multi-year Senate median age: ~64 (record-high).
- Multi-year US presidential age: highest in US history with Biden and Trump.

**Race/ethnicity (US context):**
- 119th Congress (2025-2027): 25% non-white per Pew — record high but still below US population non-white share (~42%).
- US Senate: ~12% non-white.
- US House: ~28% non-white.
- US Cabinet 2025: ~20% non-white.

**Gender:**
- 119th Congress: 28% women (~150 of 535).
- Trump 2 cabinet: ~25% women.
- UK House of Commons: ~40% women (post-2024 election).
- Indian Lok Sabha 2024: ~14% women.

## Foreign-agent and lobbying overlap

- AIPAC PAC and United Democracy Project (UDP) spent ~$100+ million in 2024 election cycle per OpenSecrets — record AIPAC spending.
- AIPAC's 2024 record: defeated Jamaal Bowman (D-NY) and Cori Bush (D-MO) in primaries via UDP-backed challengers.
- FARA (Foreign Agents Registration Act) records: thousands of US political-class figures registered.

## Capture pathways documented across volume

Per Volume Two architecture:

1. **Donor capture** — named PACs, individual donors, foundations.
2. **Lobbying capture** — named lobbying firms with ex-government revolving-door personnel.
3. **Foreign-state capture** — FARA registrations, foreign-state-funded think tanks, foreign-state-paid post-government adviser positions.
4. **Defense-contractor capture** — Pentagon revolving door (Part XII Ch7-Ch8).
5. **Financial-industry capture** — Treasury-Goldman pipeline (Volume One); Fed-asset-manager integration (Volume One).
6. **Pharmaceutical-industry capture** — Volume One Ch on FDA-PDUFA; Big Pharma PAC architecture.
7. **Big Tech capture** — post-2010 documented lobbying expansion; Section 230 architecture; AI policy capture 2023+ (Stargate Project, Anthropic-White House meetings, OpenAI-White House meetings).
8. **Energy-industry capture** — oil/gas fossil-fuel lobby; post-IRA documented multi-year green-energy lobby integration.
