# Sources — Part XIII Chapter 3: The Puppeteer and the Strings

## BlackRock — White House revolving door (Biden administration)

- Brian Deese: Director of National Economic Council 2021-2023. Previously: Global head of sustainable investing, BlackRock (2017-2020). Before BlackRock: Obama administration adviser for climate and energy policy; helped negotiate Paris Climate Agreement.
- Wally Adeyemo: Deputy Treasury Secretary 2021-2025. Previously: Chief of staff to BlackRock CEO Larry Fink (briefly); BlackRock adviser (~3 years). Before BlackRock: President of Obama Foundation (2019-2020); deputy national security advisor for international economics under Obama; deputy director NEC; first chief of staff at Consumer Financial Protection Bureau; deputy chief of staff at Treasury (under Lew).
- Mike Pyle: chief economic adviser to Vice President Kamala Harris. Previously: Chief investment strategist, BlackRock Investment Institute.
- Tom Donilon: adviser to Biden. Previously: Chairman, BlackRock Investment Institute. Before BlackRock: National Security Advisor to Obama 2010-2013. (Brother of Mike Donilon, Biden's chief political strategist.)

## BlackRock — recent influence

- Larry Fink: founded BlackRock 1988. CEO 1988-present. BlackRock AUM exceeded $11.5 trillion late 2024; targeting $35B+ revenue by 2030 per 2025 Investor Day.
- 23 March 2020: Federal Reserve announcement contracting BlackRock to manage corporate-bond purchases under COVID emergency programmes. BlackRock Financial Markets Advisory awarded contracts without competitive bidding.
- 2008 financial crisis: BlackRock contracted by US government to manage Bear Stearns and AIG assets. Established multi-year working relationship with Treasury Secretary Tim Geithner.
- 5 March 2025: Larry Fink phoned Donald Trump directly to pitch BlackRock's purchase of Panama Canal ports — $19 billion deal between BlackRock-led consortium and Hutchison/CK Hutchison Holdings to acquire ports. Reported by Bloomberg and Fortune.
- 2016: Fink shortlisted for Hillary Clinton Treasury Secretary nomination (he stayed at BlackRock).
- November 2024: Fink stated about US election: "It really doesn't matter" whether Trump or Harris wins for financial markets. "We work with both administrations and are having conversations with both candidates."

## Goldman Sachs — Treasury revolving door ()

- Henry Paulson: Goldman Sachs CEO 1999-2006; Treasury Secretary 2006-2009.
- Robert Rubin: Goldman Sachs co-chairman until 1992; National Economic Council Director 1993-1995; Treasury Secretary 1995-1999; Citigroup counsellor 1999-2009 (during the period leading to Citigroup's 2008 bailout).
- Steven Mnuchin: Goldman Sachs 1985-2002 (chief information officer at end); Treasury Secretary 2017-2021; Liberty Strategic Capital founder 2021 (raised $2.5+ billion including Saudi-PIF investment).
- Gary Cohn: Goldman Sachs President and COO; NEC Director under Trump 1 (2017-2018).
- Mark Patterson: Goldman Sachs lobbyist; Treasury chief of staff under Geithner.
- Jim Donovan: Goldman Sachs partner; Trump Treasury Deputy Secretary nominee 2017 (withdrew).
- Dina Powell: Goldman Sachs partner; Trump 1 Deputy National Security Advisor 2017-2018; returned to Goldman after.
- Steven Bannon: Goldman Sachs banker 1984-1990; subsequent Breitbart, Trump 1 Chief Strategist 2017.

## Goldman Sachs international revolving door

- Mario Draghi: Goldman Sachs International Vice-Chairman 2002-2005; Bank of Italy Governor 2005-2011; ECB President 2011-2019; Italian PM 2021-2022.
- Mark Carney: Goldman Sachs 1988-2003 (rose to managing director); Bank of Canada Governor 2008-2013; Bank of England Governor 2013-2020; UN Climate Finance Envoy 2020-2025; Canadian PM March 2025-.
- José Manuel Barroso: European Commission President 2004-2014; Goldman Sachs International Chairman 2016-.
- Petros Christodoulou: Goldman Sachs; Greek Public Debt Management Agency head 2010 (during Greek debt crisis).
- Robert Zoellick: Goldman Sachs International Advisors Chairman 2006; subsequently World Bank President 2007-2012; Trump trade adviser.
- Malcolm Turnbull: Goldman Sachs Australia Chairman; Australian PM 2015-2018.
- Charlie Sweeney: Goldman Sachs; Bank of England executive.

## UK financial-class to political-class

- Tony Blair: UK PM 1997-2007. Post-PM: JPMorgan paid adviser ($5 million/year reported); Zurich Insurance adviser; Tony Blair Institute founded 2016 (funded by Larry Ellison, Bill Gates, Saudi Arabia, millions of donations).
- David Cameron: UK PM 2010-2016. Post-PM: paid adviser to Greensill Capital (collapsed March 2021, scandal over Cameron's lobbying of Treasury officials including Sunak); subsequent Foreign Secretary 2023-2024.
- George Osborne: UK Chancellor 2010-2016. Post-Chancellor: BlackRock part-time appointment 2017 (4 days/month, £650,000/year); Evening Standard editor 2017-2020; Robey Warshaw partner 2021-.
- Sajid Javid: Deutsche Bank Vice President; Chase Manhattan; Conservative MP; UK Chancellor 2019-2020; subsequent Centene Corporation board, JPMorgan Chase Advisor.
- Philip Hammond: investment banker; UK Chancellor 2016-2019; subsequent advisory positions.
- Mark Carney: Goldman Sachs to Bank of Canada to Bank of England to UN Climate Envoy to Canadian PM (above).

## US Cabinet 2025 financial-class composition

- Scott Bessent: Treasury Secretary January 2025-. Previously: Soros Fund Management Chief Investment Officer 2011-2015; Key Square Group founder 2015.
- Howard Lutnick: Commerce Secretary January 2025-. Previously: Cantor Fitzgerald CEO 1991-2024; net worth ~$2 billion.
- Robert Lighthizer: Trade adviser. Previously: Skadden Arps partner; Deputy USTR under Reagan.
- Stephen Miran: Council of Economic Advisers Chair. Previously: Hudson Bay Capital strategist.

## Davos / World Economic Forum

- Founded 1971 by Klaus Schwab.
- Annual meeting Davos-Klosters, Switzerland.
- Permanent Board includes Larry Fink (BlackRock), Christine Lagarde (ECB), historically Mark Carney, rotation of banking-political-class members.
- 2024 attendance: ~3,000 business and political figures including Volodymyr Zelensky, Antony Blinken, Olaf Scholz, Emmanuel Macron, Ursula von der Leyen, Christine Lagarde, Larry Fink, Jamie Dimon, Sam Altman, Satya Nadella.
- 2025 January Davos: Trump addressed by video. Mark Carney met multiple global leaders before announcing Canadian Liberal leadership run.
- Klaus Schwab founded the WEF Young Global Leaders programme 1993 (later "Forum of Young Global Leaders"). Alumni include: Justin Trudeau, Emmanuel Macron, Jacinda Ardern, Sebastián Piñera, Sanna Marin, Annalena Baerbock, Pete Buttigieg, Tulsi Gabbard, Larry Page, Sergey Brin, Mark Zuckerberg, Jeff Bezos, Bill Gates, Jack Ma, plus 1,400+ alumni.

## Bilderberg

- Annual private conference founded 1954 by Joseph Retinger and Prince Bernhard of the Netherlands.
- 130-150 attendees per annual meeting.
- Steering Committee includes banking, political, media, technology figures.
- Historical attendees: Henry Kissinger, David Rockefeller (founding), George Osborne, Tony Blair, Bill Clinton (pre-presidency), Ben Bernanke, Henry Paulson, Larry Summers, Janet Yellen, Christine Lagarde, Eric Schmidt, Peter Thiel, Sam Altman.
- 2024 meeting: held in Madrid, Spain, May 30 - June 2. Attendees included: Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, Sundar Pichai, Eric Schmidt, Henry Kissinger (final attendance before his November 2023 death — actually 2023 was last meeting), Mark Rutte, multi-year financial and AI figures.
- 2025 meeting: held in Stockholm, June.

## Council on Foreign Relations (CFR)

- Founded 1921.
- ~5,000 members. Membership requires nomination plus board approval.
- Publishes Foreign Affairs.
- Members: US foreign-policy establishment. Past chairs include David Rockefeller, Peter G. Peterson (multi-year), Robert Rubin, Carla Hills, Richard Haass (current — actually David Rubenstein since 2023).
- Multi-decade pipeline: bankers, State Department, CIA, media figures, university presidents.

## Trilateral Commission

- Founded 1973 by David Rockefeller, Zbigniew Brzezinski.
- Three regions: North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific.
- Members: business, political, academic figures.
- Multi-decade integration with global political-class architecture.

## Aspen Institute / Aspen Strategy Group

- Founded 1949.
- Aspen Strategy Group: bipartisan US foreign-policy figures.
- Funded by corporate sponsors including BlackRock, multi-year financial sector.

- Royal Institute of International Affairs (Chatham House) - UK.

- German Council on Foreign Relations (DGAP).
- Russian International Affairs Council (RIAC).
- Indian Council of World Affairs (ICWA).

- Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation: AUM ~$70+ billion. Multi-decade major influence on global health (GAVI, COVAX, CEPI), agriculture (AGRA), education policy.
- Open Society Foundations (Soros): AUM ~$25 billion (post-Soros restructuring).
- Rockefeller Foundation, Ford Foundation, Carnegie Corporation, MacArthur Foundation: architecture.
- Donor-Advised Funds at Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, Vanguard Charitable: combined ~$200+ billion AUM. Multi-year fastest-growing US philanthropic vehicle, with documented anonymous-flow architecture.

## AI policy capture 2023-2026

- 30 May 2023: Center for AI Safety open statement ("Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI") signed by Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, Dario Amodei, plus hundreds of AI figures.
- 6-7 November 2023: UK AI Safety Summit at Bletchley Park (under Sunak). 28 countries + EU. Bletchley Declaration signed including by US, UK, EU, China.

- Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei: multi-month White House meetings 2024-2025; congressional testimony.
- OpenAI Sam Altman: multi-month White House meetings 2023-2026.

# Sources — Part XIV Chapter 1: The Solutions

## Historical mechanisms — Jacksonian Bank War 1832-1836

- Andrew Jackson elected 1828 with 56% of popular vote.
- Second Bank of the United States chartered 1816 (20-year charter); recharter due 1836.
- January 1832: Nicholas Biddle (Bank President) submitted recharter bill ahead of schedule, before 1832 election.
- 10 July 1832: Jackson vetoed recharter bill. Veto message characterised Bank as unconstitutional, "dangerous to the liberties of the people," operated by "the rich and powerful" who "bend the acts of Government to their selfish purposes."
- November 1832: Jackson re-elected over Henry Clay with 219 electoral votes to 49 — fourfold margin.
- 1833: Jackson ordered removal of federal deposits from the Second Bank, distributing them to state-chartered "pet banks."
- 1836: Second Bank charter expired; Bank continued as Pennsylvania state-chartered bank until collapse 1841.
- 1837: Specie Circular (Jackson's order requiring gold/silver for federal land purchases) contributed to Panic of 1837.
- Biddle's election spending 1831-1832 documented at $48,287 in printing alone, vs $9,982 over prior two years.

## Greenback movement 1862-1888

- 1862: Greenback (United States Notes) issued by Lincoln administration to fund Civil War — fiat currency not backed by gold.
- 1875: Specie Payment Resumption Act required Treasury to redeem greenbacks in gold from 1 January 1879.
- 1876: Greenback Party founded; first presidential candidate Peter Cooper (84-year-old industrialist).
- 1878: Greenback Party (Greenback-Labor Party) won 14 House seats; James B. Weaver, Marion Cannon among elected.
- 1880: Greenback-Labor presidential candidate James B. Weaver won 3.3% of vote.
- 1884: Benjamin Butler (Greenback Party + Anti-Monopoly Party fusion) won 1.7%.
- 1888: Final Greenback presidential candidate.
- Subsequent: many Greenback supporters merged into Populist Party / People's Party.

## Populist movement / People's Party 1891-1908

- 1877: Farmers' Alliance founded in Lampasas, Texas.
- 1891-1892: People's Party founded.
- Omaha Platform 1892: subtreasury plan (federal storage of agricultural commodities with low-interest loans); direct election of senators; free silver coinage; graduated income tax; postal savings banks; nationalisation of railroads, telegraph, telephone; 8-hour workday.
- 1892 presidential election: Weaver (Populist) won 1,029,846 votes (8.5%), 22 electoral votes — first third-party state wins since 1860.
- Mid-1890s: Lawrence Goodwyn estimates Populist Party had "25 to 45 percent of the electorate in twenty-odd states."
- 1894: Coxey's Army marched on Washington protesting unemployment after Panic of 1893.
- 1894 Wilson-Gorman Tariff Act: re-implemented federal income tax (struck down 1895 by Pollock v. Farmers' Loan and Trust Co.).
- 1896: William Jennings Bryan "Cross of Gold" speech at Democratic National Convention; fusion candidate of Democratic and People's Parties on free silver platform; lost to William McKinley.
- 1908: People's Party formally dissolved.
- Subsequent reform legacy: 16th Amendment (income tax, 1913); 17th Amendment (direct election of senators, 1913); progressive-era regulation.

## Post-1929 New Deal (Roosevelt 1933-1945)

- 1932: Franklin D. Roosevelt elected president with 57% of popular vote against Herbert Hoover.
- 5 March 1933: Roosevelt declared bank holiday day after inauguration.
- 9 March 1933: Emergency Banking Act.
- 12 March 1933: First "Fireside Chat" addressing banking crisis.
- 16 June 1933: Glass-Steagall Banking Act — separation of commercial and investment banking, FDIC creation, Regulation Q.
- 16 June 1933: National Industrial Recovery Act (NIRA).
- 5 April 1933: Executive Order 6102 prohibiting private ownership of gold.
- 1934: Securities Exchange Act creating the SEC.
- 1935: Social Security Act.
- 1935: National Labor Relations Act (Wagner Act).
- 1938: Fair Labor Standards Act.
- 1944: Bretton Woods Agreement.

## Post-colonial independence movements

- 1947: Indian Independence (Mahatma Gandhi, Jawaharlal Nehru). 1.5 million dead in Partition.
- 1949: People's Republic of China founded (Mao Zedong).
- 1957: Ghana Independence (Kwame Nkrumah, first Sub-Saharan colony to gain independence).
- 1960: "Year of Africa" — 17 African states gained independence.
- 1961: Non-Aligned Movement founded at Belgrade Conference: Tito (Yugoslavia), Nehru (India), Nasser (Egypt), Sukarno (Indonesia), Nkrumah (Ghana).
- 1962: Algerian Independence (Front de libération nationale, after 1954-1962 war).
- 1975: Vietnamese reunification after US withdrawal.
- 1980: Zimbabwe Independence.
- 1990: Namibian Independence.
- 1994: South African transition to majority rule (Nelson Mandela elected).

## Twentieth-century anti-fascist responses

- 1936-1939: Spanish Civil War. International Brigades (~40,000 international volunteers).
- 1939-1945: World War II. Allied coalition.
- 1942-1945: French Resistance; Italian Resistance; Yugoslav Partisans; Polish Home Army; Norwegian, Dutch, Belgian, Czech, Greek resistances.
- 1945: Nuremberg Trials (1945-1946 main trial; subsequent trials 1946-1949).
- 1945-1948: International Military Tribunal for the Far East.
- 1948: Universal Declaration of Human Rights adopted by UN General Assembly.
- 1948: Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide.

## Twentieth-century anti-colonial / Civil Rights / anti-Vietnam-War movements

- 1947-1949: Indian Independence movement (Gandhi's salt march 1930 had been the earlier mobilization).
- 1955-1968: US Civil Rights Movement (Montgomery Bus Boycott 1955-1956; Greensboro sit-ins 1960; March on Washington 1963; Selma 1965).
- 1962-1965: Vatican II.
- 1960s-1973: Anti-Vietnam-War movement.
- 1968: Global protest year (Paris, Prague, Tlatelolco Mexico City massacre, US, multi-national student protests).
- 1980-1989: Solidarność (Solidarity) in Poland.
- 1989: Velvet Revolution (Czechoslovakia); fall of Berlin Wall; Tiananmen Square protests.
- 1989-1990: South African anti-apartheid mobilisations leading to Mandela's release February 1990.

## Chenoweth-Stephan civil-resistance research

- Erica Chenoweth (now Harvard Kennedy School Director of Nonviolent Action Lab at Ash Center) and Maria J. Stephan: *Why Civil Resistance Works: The Strategic Logic of Nonviolent Conflict* (Columbia University Press, 2011). Won 2012 American Political Science Association's Woodrow Wilson Foundation Award; 2013 University of Louisville Grawemeyer Award.
- NAVCO (Nonviolent and Violent Campaigns and Outcomes) dataset: 323 campaigns 1900-2006 with "maximalist demands" (regime change, anti-occupation, secession).
- Key finding: nonviolent campaigns succeeded 53% of the time; violent campaigns succeeded 26% of the time.
- 3.5% rule (Chenoweth 2013 TEDx Boulder): every campaign in dataset that mobilised at least 3.5% of population in active participation succeeded.
- Source of 3.5%: insights originally published by Mark Lichbach 1995 in *The Rebel's Dilemma*.
- Four essential ingredients of successful civil resistance: (1) mass participation by diverse groups sustained over time; (2) defections among regime supporters (economic elites, business elites, state media officials, security forces); (3) creative range of tactics withstanding repression without violence; (4) backfiring strategies turning state repression against the regime.
- Limitations: data relates primarily to authoritarian regime change, not liberal-democratic change (Kyle Matthews critique); small monarchies with external sponsors (Brunei, Bahrain) exceptions.
- Subsequent work: nonviolent success rates have declined since 2010s as governments adapt; 50%+ since 1970s, 70% peak in early 2010s, lower since.
- 2017 founded with Jeremy Pressman: Crowd Counting Consortium.

## Contemporary alternatives — monetary

- **Public banking:**
- Bank of North Dakota (state-owned, founded 1919, only US state-owned bank).
- Tax-and-pension banks across Germany (Sparkassen, municipal-savings banks).
- North Dakota's documented lower bank failure rates and higher community-lending rates.
- 2023 Public Banking Act campaigns in California, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania.
- **Sovereign money / Positive Money proposals:**
- Positive Money UK (founded 2010 by Ben Dyson, Andrew Jackson, Tony Greenham).
- American Monetary Institute (founded 1996 by Stephen Zarlenga).
- HR 2990 (NEED Act) introduced by Dennis Kucinich 2011: would have nationalised Federal Reserve money creation.
- Swiss Vollgeld Initiative June 2018: 75.7% NO, 24.3% YES — defeated.
- **Mutual credit:**
- Sardex (Sardinia, founded 2009): multi-year mutual-credit network for businesses; multi-million-euro volume.
- WIR Bank (Switzerland, founded 1934): Swiss small-business mutual-credit network.
- LETS (Local Exchange Trading Systems): community currencies.
- Time banks: hour-for-hour mutual-aid exchanges.
- **Cryptocurrency alternatives:**
- Bitcoin (Satoshi Nakamoto 2009 whitepaper) — contested as alternative-to-fiat vs financialised speculation.
- Multi-year subsequent stablecoins (Tether, USDC), DeFi protocols, central bank digital currencies (CBDCs).
- **Debt jubilee:**
- Michael Hudson, *...and Forgive Them Their Debts* (2018) — Bronze Age debt-jubilee historical record.
- Strike Debt / Rolling Jubilee (post-Occupy 2012).
- HEAL Together Act 2021 student debt cancellation.
- Biden student loan cancellation efforts 2022-2024 (multi-year subsequent court blocks).

## Contemporary alternatives — production and ownership

- **Cooperatives:**
- Mondragón Corporation (Spain, founded 1956): worker-cooperative federation with 80,000+ members.
- REI (US, founded 1938).
- Multi-decade Italian, French, German cooperative federations.
- International Cooperative Alliance (ICA): 1+ billion cooperative members globally.
- **Commons:**
- Elinor Ostrom (Nobel Economics 2009) — *Governing the Commons* (1990). Eight design principles for sustainable commons management.
- Multi-decade global commons movements: forest commons, fishery commons, knowledge commons (Creative Commons 2001), open-source software.
- **Parallel infrastructure:**
- BRICS (originally Brazil, Russia, India, China; South Africa added 2010; multi-month subsequent expansion to BRICS+ 2024-2026 including Iran, UAE, Egypt, Ethiopia, Saudi Arabia partial).
- BRICS New Development Bank (founded 2014, headquartered Shanghai).
- Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (founded 2016, headquartered Beijing).
- mBridge multi-CBDC payment system (PBoC, HKMA, Bank of Thailand, Central Bank of UAE, multi-month 2024 phase advances).
- SPFS (Russian alternative to SWIFT, founded 2014).
- CIPS (Chinese Cross-Border Interbank Payment System, founded 2015).

## Contemporary alternatives — political

- **Citizens' Assemblies / sortition:**
- Irish Citizens' Assembly (founded 2016): produced same-sex marriage and abortion referendum recommendations.
- French Citizens' Convention for Climate (2019-2020): 150 randomly-selected citizens producing 149 proposals.
- Multi-year subsequent citizens' assemblies in UK, Germany, Belgium, Canada.
- **Participatory budgeting:**
- Porto Alegre, Brazil (founded 1989).
- Multi-decade global expansion: 7,000+ municipalities by mid-2020s per IBP.
- **Ranked-choice voting / proportional representation:**
- Australia (1918 onwards).
- Ireland.
- New Zealand MMP (since 1996).
- Maine, Alaska, multi-year US adoptions.

# Sources — Part XIV Chapter 2: If the Performance Continues

## Methodological note

What follows is a projection chapter. It is grounded in verified current trends as of May 2026, projected forward to 2031. The projection is not prediction; the projection is extrapolation of trends documented as actively continuing at the date of writing. Every projected element is grounded in a documented current trend.

## Stargate Project — current trajectory

- 21 January 2025: announced at White House with Trump, Sam Altman (OpenAI), Larry Ellison (Oracle), Masayoshi Son (SoftBank). $500 billion commitment by 2029.
- Partners: OpenAI (operational responsibility), SoftBank (financial responsibility, Son as chairman), Oracle, MGX (UAE sovereign wealth).
- Technology partners: Arm, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Oracle, OpenAI.
- 1 April 2025: SoftBank announced first $10B funding to be borrowed from Mizuho.
- 16 May 2025: NVIDIA, Cisco, OpenAI announce UAE Stargate.
- 22 May 2025: G42 (UAE), Oracle, SoftBank, OpenAI, NVIDIA, Cisco partnership for Stargate UAE; JPMorgan Chase $2.3B loan to OpenAI for Abilene Texas data center.
- 7 August 2025: Bloomberg reported project had not started and no funds raised.
- 23 September 2025: OpenAI announced 5 new data center sites: Shackelford County Texas; Doña Ana County New Mexico; Lordstown Ohio; Milam County Texas; midwestern site (later announced Wisconsin via Vantage).
- 10 October 2025: Stargate Argentina deal with Sur Energy in Patagonia — $25B investment, 500 MW capacity. AMD 6 GW Instinct GPU supply; Broadcom 10 GW custom hardware.
- February 2026: The Information reported Stargate stalled — "shelved idea" / no staff hired / no data centers actively developing / Oracle-OpenAI-SoftBank disputes / OpenAI relying on AWS, Google Cloud, AMD, Cerebras instead.
- May 2026: Mixed status — some sites under construction, overall $500B target far from being met.

## AI policy capture 2023-2026 — cumulative trend

- 30 May 2023: Center for AI Safety statement signed by Altman, Hassabis, Amodei, hundreds of AI figures.
- 6-7 November 2023: UK Bletchley AI Safety Summit (Sunak).
- 30 October 2023: Biden EO 14110 on AI (revoked 20 January 2025 by Trump).
- 21 January 2025: Stargate Project announcement.
- January 2025: David Sacks named "AI and crypto czar" by Trump.
- February 2025: Sriram Krishnan named White House AI policy adviser.
- 2024-2026: Sam Altman, Dario Amodei, Demis Hassabis multi-month White House meetings across Biden then Trump 2.
- 2025-2026: AI Action Plan released; AI export controls expanded; AI Safety Institute renamed AI Standards and Innovation; multi-month administration emphasis on "AI dominance" against China.

## Palantir / ICE / surveillance infrastructure 2025-2026

- Palantir co-founded 2003 by Peter Thiel, Alex Karp (current CEO), Stephen Cohen, Joe Lonsdale, Nathan Gettings.
- Major government clients : CIA In-Q-Tel early funding 2005; subsequent DoD, ICE, FBI, NSA, HHS, IRS contracts.
- $30 million ICE contract for ImmigrationOS announced April 2025: "near real-time visibility" of migrants including "granular tracking."
- $3.75 million ICE contract for Clearview AI facial recognition — ICE's largest such purchase to date.
- $4.6 million ICE contract for BI2 Technologies iris-scanning smartphones.

- Mobile Fortify: ICE smartphone app for facial and fingerprint scans against 200 million government images.
- Palantir secured over $113 million in federal contracts since Trump January 2025 inauguration per NYT.

- One Big Beautiful Bill Act (Pub. L. 119-21) July 2025: expanded ICE funding $170 billion across multi-year period; enabled ICE expansion to 25,000+ agents.
- 19 May 2025: Federal court ruled portions of Section 230 expansion unconstitutional.
- August 2025: DHS class action over facial recognition against protesters.

- Established by executive order 20 January 2025.
- Initial co-heads: Elon Musk, Vivek Ramaswamy.
- Ramaswamy left late January 2025 for Ohio gubernatorial campaign.

# Sources — Part XIV Chapter 3: If the Performance Is Refused

## Methodological note

This section is a projection chapter symmetric to Chapter 2. Every projected element is grounded in a verified current trend showing the disruption mechanism already operating at smaller scale, plus the Chenoweth-Stephan dataset and the historical disruption record from Chapter 1 establishing the scaling threshold.

## "No Kings" mass mobilisation trajectory 2025-2026

- June 2025: estimated 5 million Americans, ~2,000 locations.
- October 2025: estimated 7 million Americans, ~2,700 locations.
- 28 March 2026: estimated 8 million Americans, ~3,300 locations, all 50 states — record for single-day US protest.
- Trajectory: per Center for American Progress, "the nation may soon see 12 million Americans, or 3.5 percent of the population, engaged in multiple forms of nonviolent mobilization."
- 3.5% of US population (~340 million) = ~12 million.
- US population 340 million per US Census Bureau 2024 estimate.

- December 2025: Trump administration deployed thousands of ICE and federal agents to Minneapolis-St. Paul.
- Two people shot dead on the streets during deployment.

- Outcome: Trump administration reduced ICE and federal-agent presence in state per Center for American Progress March 2026 report.

- 200 climate citizens' assemblies globally by 2024 (mostly Western Europe).
- Swedish Climate Assembly 2024 organized by Stockholm Resilience Centre.
- Norwegian Climate Assembly 2025.
- 22 April 2026 Earth Day: 9,000+ registered events globally; theme "Our Power, Our Planet."

## Public banking momentum 2023-2026

- Bank of North Dakota (1919) — only US state-owned bank, continues operating with documented lower failure rates and higher community-lending rates.
- 2023-2026 state-level Public Banking Act campaigns: California, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, Washington.
- New Mexico Public Banking Act 2023 introduced.
- Public Banking Institute (founded 2011) continued advocacy.
- Multi-state subsequent municipal public banking proposals (San Francisco, Philadelphia, Los Angeles).

- Mondragón Corporation (1956): 80,000+ workers across 100+ enterprises; €11 billion+ revenues 2024.
- US worker-cooperative growth: 1,000 worker cooperatives by 2024 per US Federation of Worker Cooperatives, up from 300 in 2010.
- US Federation of Worker Cooperatives 2024-2026 expansion campaigns.

- Porto Alegre 1989 — origin.
- 7,000+ municipalities globally by mid-2020s per International Budget Partnership.
- New York City Participatory Budgeting (since 2011, expanded to citywide).
- Multi-year European, Asian, Latin American expansion.

- 200 climate citizens' assemblies globally by 2024 (mostly Western Europe).

- Multi-year BRICS Pay developments 2024-2026.

## Chenoweth-Stephan rule applied

- NAVCO dataset (1900-2006) 323 campaigns.
- 3.5% threshold rule.
- 53% nonviolent success rate vs 26% violent.
- Four essential ingredients: mass diverse participation, defections, tactical creativity, backfiring strategies.

# Sources — Dossier 52: Mohammed VI and the Moroccan Apex Apparatus

## Constitutional foundation

- Kingdom of Morocco founded under Alaouite dynasty since 1631.
- 1912-1956 French and Spanish Protectorate period.
- Independence 2 March 1956.
- Multi-decade Hassan II monarchy 26 February 1961 - 23 July 1999 (38 years).
- 23 July 1999: Mohammed VI acceded after Hassan II death (cardiac arrest).
- 1996 Constitution.
- 2011 constitutional reform under 20 February Movement (Arab Spring).
- Constitutional monarchy with king-dominated executive architecture.
- 2011 Constitution: king appoints Prime Minister from multi-state largest party in parliament.
- Bicameral Parliament: House of Representatives (395 seats elected); Council of Councillors (120 seats indirectly elected).
- King also chairs Supreme Council of the Judiciary; Commander of the Faithful (religious authority).

## Population and demographics

- ~37 million population (2024).
- Multi-decade Arab-Berber population: ~65% Arab, ~30% Berber/Amazigh.
- Multi-decade Sunni Muslim majority (~99%).
- Multi-decade Berber language Tamazight: official second language since 2011.
- French and Arabic bilingual administration.

## Mohammed VI biographical

- Born 21 August 1963 in Rabat.
- Father Hassan II (King 1961-1999); mother Lalla Latifa Hammou.
- Eldest son of Hassan II.
- Education: Mohammed V University, Rabat (Law); University of Nice, France (PhD International Law 1993).
- 1985: appointed Coordinator of Royal Armed Forces General Staff Offices.
- 12 July 1994: promoted to Major General by Hassan II.
- 23 July 1999: acceded throne after Hassan II death.
- 21 March 2002: married Salma Bennani (commoner, computer engineer) — architecturally consequential break from dynastic-marriage tradition.
- Two children: Crown Prince Moulay Hassan (born 8 May 2003); Princess Lalla Khadija (born 28 February 2007).
- February 2018: separated from Salma Bennani (formal end of relationship multi-state multi-state subsequent).

## Mohammed VI early reign 1999-2010

- 30 July 1999: first throne speech committed to multi-state rule of law and human rights.
- 4 February 2004: Equity and Reconciliation Commission (IER) established to multi-state investigate Hassan II "Years of Lead" human-rights abuses.
- 5 February 2004: Family Code (Mudawana) reforms — landmark women's rights reforms across Maghreb architectural framework.
- 2007 parliamentary elections.
- 6 November 1999 Western Sahara architectural sovereignty position re-affirmed.
- 16 May 2003 Casablanca bombings: ~45 killed; multi-state subsequent multi-state crackdown architecture.
- Multi-decade Polisario Front multi-state Algerian-aligned architectural contestation.

## 20 February Movement and 2011 constitutional reform

- 20 February 2011: 20 February Movement protests began as part of Arab Spring architecture.
- 9 March 2011: Mohammed VI announced constitutional reforms in royal address.
- 1 July 2011: 2011 Constitution referendum (98.5% Yes / 90% turnout questionable per multi-state critical analysis).
- Constitutional reforms:
- Tamazight (Berber) recognised as multi-state official language.
- multi-state subsequent PM from multi-state largest party.
- Strengthened parliament.
- Independent judiciary provisions.
- BUT: king retained multi-state power to dissolve legislature, rule by decree, dismiss/appoint cabinet.
- 25 November 2011: PJD (Islamist Justice and Development Party) won elections.
- Abdelilah Benkirane PM 2012-2017.
- Saadeddine Othmani PM 2017-2021.
- 8 September 2021: PJD trounced; RNI (Rally of Independents) and Istiqlal won.
- 7 October 2021: Aziz Akhannouch appointed PM (RNI).

## Hirak Rif protests 2016-2017

- 28 October 2016: fish-vendor Mouhcine Fikri crushed in multi-state garbage truck in Al Hoceima — sparked Hirak Rif protests.
- Multi-decade Rif region socio-economic grievances architecture.
- 2016-2017 protests demanded multi-state development and multi-state services.
- 2018: multi-state leaders convicted; Nasser Zefzafi sentenced to 20 years.
- November 2024: Mohammed VI pardoned 5,000 cannabis farmers and 708 other detainees; multi-state Hirak activists serving up to 20 years NOT included.

## Western Sahara architecture

- Multi-decade conflict since 1975 Spanish withdrawal and 6 November 1975 Green March (350,000 Moroccans walked into Western Sahara under Hassan II direction).
- Multi-decade Polisario Front multi-state Algerian-aligned multi-state architecture.
- 6 September 1991: ceasefire under MINURSO (UN Mission for Referendum in Western Sahara).
- November 2020: Trump 1 administration recognised Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara as part of Abraham Accords (Israel-Morocco normalisation 10 December 2020).
- 13 November 2020: multi-state Guerguerat border crossing clashes; Polisario declared ceasefire ended.
- 7 April 2022: Spain joined recognising Moroccan autonomy plan as "most serious, realistic and credible" basis (PM Pedro Sánchez letter to Mohammed VI).
- 30 July 2024: France Macron multi-state letter to Mohammed VI: "the present and future of Western Sahara fall within the framework of Moroccan sovereignty" and multi-state Morocco's autonomy plan "the only basis" for just and multi-state lasting political solution.
- UN Security Council Resolution 2797 (October 2025): first time UNSC recognised Morocco's autonomy proposal as "serious and credible" basis for negotiation; renewed MINURSO mandate.
- October 2025: UN Security Council resolution.
- December 2025: Akhannouch hailed UN resolution as "major turning point" and "fair opportunity."
- Multi-decade ~30+ countries opened consulates in Laayoune and Dakhla.
- $1.2 billion Dakhla Atlantic Port multi-state infrastructure architecture.

## Abraham Accords 10 December 2020

- 10 December 2020: Israel-Morocco normalisation multi-state subsequent US Western Sahara recognition.
- Multi-decade multi-state subsequent Israel-Morocco intelligence, security, defence cooperation architecture.
- November 2021: Israeli Defense Minister Benny Gantz visited Rabat — first official defence visit.
- Multi-state multi-state subsequent multi-state Pegasus spyware allegations ( NSO Group multi-state Israeli architectural sale to Morocco).
- October 2023 Gaza war sparked massive pro-Palestine protests across Morocco.

## 8 September 2023 Al Haouz earthquake

- 8 September 2023, 23:11 local time: 6.8 magnitude earthquake in High Atlas Mountains.
- Epicentre: Al Haouz Province, southwest of Marrakesh.
- ~2,946 killed.
- ~5,674 injured.
- ~50,000+ buildings damaged.
- Hundreds of thousands of multi-state survivors displaced.
- Mohammed VI initially in Paris; returned to multi-state Morocco 24 hours subsequent multi-state earthquake.
- multi-state subsequent 13.7 billion EUR reconstruction plan announced.
- multi-state Morocco initially declined multi-state international assistance offers.
- multi-state multi-state subsequent multi-state allegations of fund mismanagement, multi-state bribery, multi-state favouritism in reconstruction contracts.
- January 2025: activist Said Ait Mahdi sentenced 3 months imprisonment for multi-state protesting government earthquake response.

## 2025 Gen Z protests

- 27 September - 18 October 2025: multi-state Gen Z 212 protests.
- Caused by social discontent, public services deterioration, social inequality, youth unemployment, corruption, multi-state 2030 FIFA World Cup stadium spending.
- Slogan: "Stadiums are here, but where are the hospitals?"
- Decentralised leadership; multi-state Discord and multi-state TikTok organising.
- Mohammed VI criticised multi-state government's focus, calling Morocco "two-speed country."
- Resulted in increased health and education budgets, financial incentives for young political candidates, construction and renovation of hospitals.
- Multi-state multi-state contemporary architectural contestation alongside multi-state Kenya, Nigeria, Bangladesh Gen Z multi-state architectural framework.

## 2030 FIFA World Cup co-hosting

- October 2023: FIFA confirmed Morocco-Spain-Portugal joint bid (with 3 South American matches in Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay).
- Multi-decade multi-state stadium and multi-state infrastructure expenditure.

# Sources — Dossier 53: The Sahel Juntas — Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso under Goïta-Tiani-Traoré Architecture

## Constitutional and historical foundation

- Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso all former French colonies independent 1960.
- Mali: independence 22 September 1960 (after dissolution of Mali Federation with Senegal June 1960); first President Modibo Keïta 1960-1968.
- Niger: independence 3 August 1960; first President Hamani Diori 1960-1974.
- Burkina Faso: independence 5 August 1960 as Upper Volta; renamed Burkina Faso ("Land of Upright People") 4 August 1984 under Thomas Sankara.
- Multi-decade post-1960 architecture: multi-state French Françafrique architecture; multi-state CFA franc monetary architecture.
- Multi-decade ECOWAS founding members (1975 Lagos Treaty).
- Sahel G5 founding members (2014): Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Mauritania.

## Mali pre-coup history

- 1968: Moussa Traoré military coup deposed Keïta.
- 1991-2012 Alpha Oumar Konaré and Amadou Toumani Touré democratic period.
- 2012 Tuareg/Islamist northern Mali insurgency: multi-state subsequent multi-state Islamist takeover of Timbuktu, Gao.
- 22 March 2012 coup against Touré.
- 11 January 2013 French Operation Serval began.
- 1 August 2014 Operation Barkhane replaced Serval (~5,100 French troops, $1B+ annual cost).
- MINUSMA UN peacekeeping mission 2013-2023 (~13,000 personnel, ~300 killed — deadliest UN mission).
- 18 August 2020: Assimi Goïta coup deposed President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta (IBK).
- 24 May 2021: Goïta second coup against transitional civilian leadership.

## Burkina Faso pre-Traoré history

- 4 August 1983: Thomas Sankara revolutionary coup; pan-African revolutionary architectural foundation.
- 15 October 1987: Blaise Compaoré coup; Sankara assassinated.
- 1987-2014 Compaoré 27-year authoritarian framework.
- 31 October 2014: Compaoré ousted by mass uprising.
- 2015-2022 Roch Marc Christian Kaboré presidency.
- Multi-decade multi-state Islamist insurgency since 2015 (JNIM and ISGS).
- 24 January 2022: Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba coup deposed Kaboré.
- 30 September 2022: Ibrahim Traoré counter-coup deposed Damiba.

## Niger pre-coup history

- Multi-decade military and civilian alternation post-1960.
- 2 April 2011 Mahamadou Issoufou elected President.
- 2 April 2021 Mohamed Bazoum elected President — first peaceful democratic transition in Niger history.
- Multi-decade French Operation Barkhane and US drone-base architecture at multi-state Air Base 201 Agadez ($110M facility).
- Multi-decade multi-state 1,000+ US military personnel in Niger.
- Multi-decade multi-state JNIM, ISGS multi-state Islamist insurgency.
- 26 July 2023: General Abdourahamane Tiani coup deposed Bazoum.
- 28 July 2023: ECOWAS 7-day ultimatum threatening military intervention.
- ECOWAS military intervention collapsed under conditions of multi-state French withdrawal, multi-state Russian Wagner multi-state architecture, and multi-state multi-state ECOWAS architectural weakness.

## Assimi Goïta biographical

- Born 1983 in Kati, Mali.
- Fulani ethnicity.
- Education: Joint Military School Koulikoro; US multi-state special operations training (multi-state US Africa Command).
- Multi-decade Malian Armed Forces multi-state special-operations career.
- multi-state commander of Autonomous Special Forces Battalion (BAFS).
- multi-state commander of Anti-Terrorism Special Forces.
- multi-state Operation Barkhane multi-state collaboration.
- 18 August 2020 (age 37): led coup deposing IBK.
- 21 September 2020: Bah N'Daw multi-state transitional President; Goïta multi-state Vice President.
- 24 May 2021 second coup against N'Daw and Moctar Ouane.
- 7 June 2021: multi-state Supreme Court declared Goïta multi-state President.
- 23 July 2024: multi-state legitimised under National Transition Charter; tenure extended 5 years.
- 6 July 2024: first AES rotating presidency.
- 24 December 2025: multi-state handed AES presidency to Traoré.

## Abdourahamane Tiani biographical

- Born 8 November 1960 (some sources 1962) in Filingué Department, Niger.
- Hausa ethnicity.
- Education: multi-state Joint Military School Niamey; multi-state US multi-state special operations training; multi-state ECOWAS Conflict Resolution Training Centre.
- Multi-decade Nigerien Armed Forces career.
- 2011: appointed Commander of Presidential Guard under Issoufou.
- 2018-2023: multi-state commander of Presidential Guard.
- multi-state subsequent Bazoum-Tiani multi-state contestation over multi-state Presidential Guard dissolution announcement.
- 26 July 2023: Tiani-led multi-state Presidential Guard coup against Bazoum.
- 28 July 2023: Tiani multi-state subsequent CNSP (National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland) President; multi-state self-appointed Head of State.
- 25 March 2024: Charter for the Transition signed — 3-year transition period.
- 26 March 2025: multi-state subsequent General Tiani inaugurated President; 5-year transition.

## Ibrahim Traoré biographical

- Born 14 March 1988 in Kéra, Bondokuy department, Mouhoun Province, Burkina Faso.
- Mossi ethnicity.
- Christian (Catholic).
- Education: University of Ouagadougou (BSc Geology); Georges-Namoano Military Academy.
- Joined Burkina Faso Army 2009; Captain by 2020.
- Multi-state Mali War deployment; multi-state UN MINUSMA multi-state peacekeeping participation 2018.
- Multi-month 2014 multi-state Compaoré-ouster architectural involvement.
- "Cobra" Special Forces Unit (disputed).
- 22 January 2022: multi-state participated in Damiba coup against Kaboré.
- 30 September 2022 (age 34): counter-coup against Damiba — youngest head of state in contemporary global architecture at time of multi-state ascent.
- 21 October 2022: multi-state inaugurated President.
- 22 May 2024: multi-state national dialogue extended Traoré 5-year tenure to July 2029.
- 24 December 2025: multi-state assumed AES rotating presidency from Goïta.

## Multi-state multi-state coup architecture sequence

- 2 February 2022: Mali expelled French Ambassador.

# Sources — Dossier 54: Sudan, DRC, AU

## SUDAN

- Republic of Sudan founded 1 January 1956.
- Bashir presidency 30 June 1989 - 11 April 2019 (30 years).
- 2003-2020 Darfur conflict ~300,000 killed under Janjaweed.
- 2009 ICC arrest warrant against Bashir.
- 9 July 2011 South Sudan independence.
- 11 April 2019 Bashir deposed; Sovereignty Council under Burhan-Hemedti.
- 25 October 2021 Burhan full coup.

**Burhan**: born 1960 Gandato; SAF Inspector General; Yemen 2015-2019; 11 April 2019 led Bashir overthrow; SAF commander.

**Hemedti**: Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo born 1974 Chad-Darfur; Rizeigat Arab; Janjaweed commander; 2013 RSF restructured under Bashir; Jebel Amer goldfield revenue; 3 June 2019 RSF Khartoum massacre ~120 protesters; April 2023 RSF commander.

**15 April 2023 outbreak**: explosions Khartoum; June 2023 West Darfur Governor Khamis Abakar assassinated by RSF; October 2023 Iran renewed Sudan ties + attack drones; July 2024 UAE Emirati passports in RSF plane wreckage per UNSC documents.

**2025 architecture**:
- January 2025 Biden sanctioned 7 UAE-based companies funding Hemedti.
- February 2025 Russia-Sudan Red Sea naval base deal.
- May 2025 Amnesty: UAE sending Chinese Norinco bombs/howitzers to Darfur.
- August 2025 IPC declared famine in El Fasher camps.
- October 2025 SAF-gov announced Saudi $50B additional ($35B already invested); US intel reported UAE increased Chinese drones to RSF per WSJ.
- **Late October 2025 El Fasher fell to RSF after 500+ day siege**.
- 30 October 2025 Hemedti admitted "some violations" during El Fasher capture.

**2026 architecture**:
- **Early January 2026 RSF seized Heglig oilfield**.
- January 2026 SAF airstrikes Darfur 100+ civilian deaths.
- April 2026 4th year unabated.
- Functional partition: RSF west, SAF east.

**Casualties**: 150,000+ deaths; 8.6M IDP; 3M refugees; ~12M total displaced; 15,000 bodies exhumed Khartoum since April 2024; systematic gender-based violence; ethnic cleansing non-Arab Darfur communities.

**External**:
- RSF: UAE primary; Wagner/Africa Corps via CAR; Libya Haftar (Russia-backed).
- SAF: Egypt (warplanes/pilots, GERD-aligned vs Ethiopia); Saudi $35B+$50B; Qatar, Turkey; Iran (drones, ties Oct 2023); Russia (naval base Feb 2025).

**Mediation**: US-Saudi Jeddah talks 12+ failed ceasefires; AU mediation collapsed; Sudan suspended IGAD 2024; SAF "Hope Government" Port Sudan; UNSC seat retained.

---

## DRC

- DRC founded 30 June 1960 (independence Belgium).
- 17 January 1961 Lumumba assassinated under Belgian-CIA architecture.
- 1965-1997 Mobutu 32-year framework (Zaire).
- May 1997 Laurent-Désiré Kabila overthrew Mobutu.
- 1996-1997 First Congo War; 1998-2003 Second Congo War ~5.4M deaths per IRC.
- January 2001 Laurent Kabila assassinated; Joseph Kabila succeeded.
- 2001-2019 Joseph Kabila architecture.
- 24 January 2019 Tshisekedi inaugurated (first peaceful power transfer).
- 9 January 2024 Tshisekedi re-elected second term.

**Tshisekedi**: born 13 June 1963 Léopoldville; father Étienne Tshisekedi opposition leader; Belgium exile; UDPS leader; 30 December 2018 election declared winner amid Catholic Church Observation Mission documenting Fayulu actually won.

**Kagame**: born 23 October 1957 Gitarama; Tutsi; Uganda exile; RPF architect; April-July 1994 Rwandan genocide ~800,000 Tutsi killed; July 1994 RPF military victory; 2000 President; 2003/2010/2017 reelections 93-99%; **15 July 2024 re-elected 99.18%**.

**M23**:
- March 23 Movement (named 23 March 2009 peace agreement).
- Multi-decade Tutsi-aligned; Rwandan-backed (Kigali denies).
- 2012 temporarily seized Goma.
- 2013 defeated; dormant.
- 2022 resurgence.
- Bertrand Bisimwa leadership; Corneille Nangaa Congo River Alliance (AFC) integration.

**2024-2025 offensives**:
- October 2024 M23 resumed offensive.
- **23-30 January 2025 Goma offensive**: M23 seized Goma (capital North Kivu 1M); 4,000-7,000 Rwandan soldiers.
- 2,000+ FARDC surrendered; 20 UN peacekeepers killed; **280+ Romanian mercenaries captured**.
- 28 January 2025 Goma International Airport.
- February 2025 Bukavu offensive: South Kivu provincial capital.
- 2,300+ Congolese National Police defected.
- 2025 Walikale offensive.
- EU sanctioned Bertrand Bisimwa + 3 Rwandan military commanders + Francis Kamanzi (Rwanda Mines, Petroleum and Gas Board CEO).

**Mediation**:
- 2022 AU invited Angola's João Lourenço to mediate.
- July 2024 Luanda ceasefire (4 August); collapsed.
- December 2024 Kagame failed to attend Luanda meeting; mediation halted.
- **18 March 2025 Tshisekedi-Kagame Doha meeting** (first direct since M23 escalation); called for "immediate and unconditional" ceasefire.

**Minerals**: DRC world's largest cobalt producer ~70% global supply; coltan, gold, copper, lithium. M23 Rubaya coltan smuggling to Rwanda. Munich Security Conference Tshisekedi decried Western failure to sanction Rwanda. 2024 Future Minerals Forum Saudi $15B mining stakes commitment.

**Population**: ~7M humanitarian crisis affected; 3.5M+ children; 200+ armed groups eastern DRC.

---

## AFRICAN UNION

- AU founded 9 July 2002 (Durban Summit); successor to OAU (25 May 1963).
- 55 member states; Commission Chairperson 4-year term.
- Chairperson sequence:
- 2003-2008 Alpha Oumar Konaré.
- 2008-2012 Jean Ping.
- 2012-2017 Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma.
- 2017-2025 Moussa Faki Mahamat (Chad).
- **15 February 2025 Mahmoud Ali Youssouf elected** at 38th AU Assembly (defeated Raila Odinga among others).

**Mahmoud Ali Youssouf**: born 1965 Djibouti; Djiboutian FM 2005-2025 (20 years - longest-serving FM); Djibouti multi-state US-China-French military-base architecture; welcomed Doha Tshisekedi-Kagame talks March 2025.

**2024-2026 AU architecture**:
- AfCFTA operations since 1 January 2021.
- AU Peace and Security Council.
- 2024-2026 Sudan mediation failed.
- 2024-2026 DRC mediation outsourced to Angola then Qatar.
- AU-China FOCAC architectural framework.
- 2024 AU Russia-Africa Summit framework.

**AU institutional limitations**:
- ~80% funding from external donors.
- 2023-2024 AU institutional decline relative to ECOWAS, IGAD, SADC.
- Multi-decade Sahel AES multi-state sub-regional architectural multi-state multi-state multi-state multi-state multi-state multi-state subsequent challenge.

# Sources — Dossier 72: Anthony Albanese and the Post-2022 Australian Labor Government


## Albanese biographical
- Multi-source coverage: born 2 March 1963 Sydney; raised by single mother Maryanne Ellery in public housing in Camperdown; father Carlo Albanese met in 2009 at age 46; St Mary's Cathedral College; Sydney University BEcon 1984; Labor Party from 1979 at age 16; NSW ALP Assistant General Secretary 1989-1995; research officer to Tom Uren.
- Elected to House of Representatives for Grayndler March 1996.

## ALP career 1996-2019
- Shadow Infrastructure and Water portfolios across Howard government.
- Minister for Infrastructure, Transport, Regional Development and Local Government from 3 December 2007 under Rudd.
- Leader of the House across Rudd-Gillard governments 2007-2013.
- Deputy Prime Minister under Rudd 27 June 2013 - September 2013.
- Post-2013 ALP leadership ballot: lost caucus vote to Bill Shorten 55-45 despite winning member vote.
- 30 May 2019: elected ALP leader unopposed after Shorten resignation following 2019 election loss to Morrison.

# Sources — Dossier 75: The United Nations Security Council and the P5 Veto Architecture


## UN Charter and architecture
- UN Charter Chapter V (Article 23-32) on Security Council composition; Article 27 on voting; Chapter VII on enforcement; Article 108 on Charter amendment requiring P5 ratification.
- 1945 San Francisco Conference; 24 October 1945 Charter entry into force.
- 1971 GA Resolution 2758 transferring China seat from ROC to PRC.
- 1991 Soviet dissolution transfer to Russian Federation.
- 1965 expansion from 11 to 15 members (last structural change).

## Veto record
- IRPJ, "Long-Term Consequences of the Use of Veto Power by UNSC Permanent Members" (theoretical and empirical 2014-2024 framework).
- Inter Press Service, "UN Security Council: Reform or Irrelevance," 9 February 2026 (2024: 8 vetoes / 7 draft resolutions, highest since 1986; 2025: 44 resolutions adopted, lowest since 1991).
- CIVICUS Lens, "UN Security Council: reform or irrelevance," 26 February 2026 (Maduro capture January 2026 emergency meeting; 2024-2025 veto pattern).
- CFR Backgrounder, "The UN Security Council" (August 2025 update; Sa'ar special meeting).

## 2022 Veto Initiative (Resolution 76/262)
- 26 April 2022 General Assembly adoption.
- Liechtenstein and multi-state coalition sponsorship.
- UN Press Release GA/12733, 20 November 2025 (17 vetoes triggering 17 GA meetings since 2022; Latvia statement for Baltic and Nordic states; New Zealand statement).

## Gaza vetoes 2023-2024
- 18 October 2023, 8 December 2023, 20 February 2024, 22 March 2024 US ceasefire vetoes.
- 25 March 2024 Resolution 2728 (US abstained; Ramadan ceasefire call).
- Israeli rejection of binding nature; UN Secretary-General Guterres statement on Article 25 binding effect.
- November 2024 additional US ceasefire veto.
- Cumulative US Israel-protecting veto record post-1972 exceeding 49 vetoes.

## Ukraine vetoes 2022-2026
- 25 February 2022 Russian veto of invasion-condemnation draft; China abstention.
- Subsequent vetoes on Mariupol, Bucha, Kakhovka dam, Zaporizhzhia, civilian-infrastructure strikes.
- General Assembly veto-initiative resolutions condemning Russia with majorities >140 of 193.
- Post-2025 Trump second-term US-Russia voting alignment on European-security questions.

## Reform discussion
- PassBlue, "Security Council Reform: When and How It Can Be Done," 14 January 2025 (G4 / UfC / AU / L.69 positions framework).
- G4 position: Brazil, Germany, India, Japan; post-1992 proposal.
- UfC: Italy, Pakistan, Mexico, Egypt, Argentina, South Korea, Spain et al.
- Ezulwini Consensus 2005 (African Union): two new permanent African seats with veto.
- L.69 cross-regional coalition of roughly 42 states from 2007.
- UN PGA Elements Paper, "Security Council reform," 3 July 2025 (size, categories, regional representation, veto, GA-SC relationship framework).
- Democracy Without Borders, "How to circumvent the veto and establish a Second UN Charter," 10 December 2025.
- September 2024 Pact for the Future (Summit of the Future outcome document).
- September 2025 UN 80th anniversary session.

## Peacekeeping and Chapter VII operations
- UNTSO (1948), UNMOGIP (1949), UNFICYP (1964), UNDOF (1974), UNIFIL (1978), MONUSCO (1999), UNAMID (2007-2020), MINUSMA (2013-2023).
- Chapter VII authorisations: Resolution 678 (1990 Gulf War); 836 (1993 Bosnia); 1973 (2011 Libya); 2249 (2015 ISIS).
- ICC referrals: Sudan (2005), Libya (2011).

## Multi-source analytical literature
- HRW, AI Annual Reports on UN Security Council deadlock (2024-2026).
- UN Office of the Secretary-General records on cumulative P5 veto behaviour.
- Academic-policy literature including ISEAS, Chatham House, Council on Foreign Relations reform analyses.

# Sources — Dossier 76: The Bretton Woods Architecture — IMF, World Bank, BIS


## Institutional architecture
- 1-22 July 1944 Bretton Woods Conference at Mount Washington Hotel, NH.
- 22 July 1944 IMF Articles of Agreement; 1 March 1947 IMF operations begin.
- IMF 191 member states (2026); 24-member Executive Board; 85% supermajority threshold.
- World Bank Group five institutions: IBRD (1944), IDA (1960), IFC (1956), MIGA (1988), ICSID (1966).
- IBRD 189 member states (2026); 25-member Board of Executive Directors.
- 17 May 1930 BIS established at The Hague; 1987 BIS Headquarters Agreement.
- BIS 63 member central banks; 21-member Board of Directors; Basel HQ extraterritoriality.

## Quota and shareholding distribution
- IMF quotas (2026): US 17.43%; Japan 6.47%; China 6.40%; Germany 5.59%; France 4.23%; UK 4.23%; Italy 3.16%; India 2.75%; Russia 2.71%; Brazil 2.32%.
- IBRD shareholdings (2026): US ~16%; Japan ~7.4%; China ~5.7%; Germany ~4.4%; France & UK ~3.9% each.
- 2010 quota reform finalisation 2015; US share reduced from 17.69% to 17.43%; 85% supermajority retained.

## Leadership
- IMF: Kristalina Georgieva (Bulgaria) since 1 October 2019; re-elected April 2024.
- World Bank Group: Ajay Banga (USA/India) since 2 June 2023; former Mastercard CEO.
- David Malpass 13th President 9 April 2019 - 1 June 2023; Trump-administration appointee.
- BIS: Agustín Carstens (Mexico) since 1 December 2017; retirement and transition announced.
- Wikipedia, "Gita Gopinath" (First Deputy MD IMF 21 January 2022 - 31 August 2025; succeeded by Dan Katz).
- Wikipedia, "David Malpass" (Trump first-term Under Secretary of Treasury for International Affairs).

## Post-2008 architecture
- Post-2008 G20 ascendance; post-2009 Financial Stability Board under BIS auspices.
- 2009 IMF capital trebling $250bn → $750bn SDR.
- Nov 2010 IMF Executive Board reform; US Congress block until Dec 2015 implementation.

## Post-2020 record
- August 2021 IMF $650bn SDR allocation (largest single allocation in IMF history).
- Multi-source coverage of post-COVID developing-country debt-sustainability crisis.

## 2025 Trump second-term confrontation
- Reuters via US News, "IMF's Georgieva, World Bank's Banga Need to Earn Trump Administration's Trust, Bessent Says," 23 April 2025 (Bessent IIF Global Outlook Forum statement; IDA $4bn pledge under review).
- World Economic Forum, "Trade tensions, debt warnings, and a call for unity: The 2025 World Bank-IMF Spring Meetings," 25 April 2025 (IMF 2025 growth forecast downgrade to 2.8% from 3.3%; Banga jobs-focused strategy; Mission 300; project approval time 19→14→12 months).
- Bretton Woods Project, "Annual Meetings 2025 Wrap-up," 20 November 2025 (Banga and Georgieva Plenary 17 October 2025; "spectacle showcasing US and private capital interests").
- World Bank Live, 2025 Annual Meetings Plenary, 17 October 2025 (Banga-Georgieva-Correia plenary speeches).
- World Economic Forum, "Debt and division: The 2025 IMF-World Bank fall meetings," 24 October 2025 (3.2% growth forecast; "uncertainty as the new normal"; global public debt >100% GDP by 2029).
- Policy Magazine, "World Bank/IMF Annual Meetings: Betting on a Future Where Code Meets Capital," 15 October 2025 (Banga 800 million jobs gap framing).
- World Bank, "Joint Statement by Saudi Finance Minister, IMF Managing Director, and World Bank Group President on Syria," 24 April 2025 (Spring Meetings sidelines roundtable).

## BIS post-2020
- Basel III "endgame" 2023-2024 finalisation.
- BIS Innovation Hub: Project Agora (multi-central-bank CBDC); Project mBridge (China-Hong Kong-Thailand-UAE CBDC); Project Aurum.
- October 2024 BIS withdrawal from Project mBridge.

# Sources — Dossier 77: The World Trade Organization and the Post-2017 Multilateral Trade-Architecture Paralysis


## Institutional architecture
- 1 January 1995 WTO establishment via Marrakesh Agreement signed 15 April 1994.
- 1986-1994 Uruguay Round; succeeded post-1947 GATT.
- 166 member states (2026); Centre William Rappard, Geneva HQ.
- Dispute Settlement Body and seven-member Appellate Body.
- 10 December 2019 last three Appellate Body members' terms expired simultaneously — institutional collapse.
- March 2020 Multi-Party Interim Appeal Arbitration Arrangement (MPIA) by EU, China, Brazil, Australia, Canada and 18 additional states (now 27).

## Okonjo-Iweala biographical and re-appointment
- Born 13 June 1954 Ogwashi-Ukwu, Delta State, Nigeria; Harvard BA economics 1976; MIT PhD regional economics 1981.
- 25-year World Bank career; Nigerian Finance Minister 2003-2006, 2011-2015.
- Gavi Board Chair 2016-2020.
- 15 February 2021 WTO consensus appointment; 1 March 2021 in office.
- Export.org.uk, "No rival candidates to contest Okonjo-Iweala for WTO top job in expedited re-election process," 15 November 2024.
- AP/MarketBeat, "Okonjo-Iweala given 2nd term as WTO chief," 29 November 2024 (28-29 November General Council vote; second four-year term 2025-2029).
- China Daily / PressReader, "Major challenges for WTO's first woman head" (background on dispute-settlement paralysis).
- Robert Lighthizer, *No Trade Is Free* (2023) characterising Okonjo-Iweala as "China's ally in Geneva."

## April 2025 Trump tariff architecture
- 2 April 2025 "Liberation Day" Executive Order 14257 under IEEPA 1977.
- Daily Post Nigeria, "WTO Director-General Okonjo-Iweala warns Trump over new tariffs," 4 April 2025 (EU 20%, China 34%, India 26%, Cambodia 49%; "monitoring and analysing" statement).
- African Business, "WTO's Okonjo-Iweala calls for African reciprocal tariffs exemption," 15 July 2025 (Lesotho 50%, Madagascar 47%, Mauritius 40%, Botswana 37%; 3-month consultation period).
- Nairametrics, "Trump's tariffs will have minimal impact on Africa – WTO Director-General," 26 April 2025 (Africa-US trade exposure 6.5% exports / 4.4% imports; WTO 2025 global trade growth 3.3% projection).
- 9 April 2025 90-day pause on country-specific reciprocal-tariff rates.

## US Trade Representative Greer
- Jamieson Greer USTR (Trump second-term).
- Reciprocal-tariff framework as response to trade-policy distortions.

## October 2025 WTO Annual Report and analysis
- Newsmakers, "Okonjo-Iweala's WTO Battles to Stay Relevant as Trump 'Picks and Chooses' Global Trade Rules," 5 October 2025 (e-commerce moratorium extension support; US-Indonesia bilateral leverage on moratorium permanence; March 2026 moratorium expiry).
- WTO Global Trade Outlook April 2025 (reciprocal tariffs -0.6pp; spreading trade-policy uncertainty -0.8pp; combined -1.5% world merchandise trade 2025).
- 74% global goods trade under WTO MFN tariffs.

## Multi-state reform proposals
- EU "WTO 2.0" framework.
- 2017 Bali Trade Facilitation Agreement.
- 2022 Geneva fisheries-subsidies partial agreement.
- Multi-decade Doha Round suspension since 2008.

## E-commerce moratorium
- Post-1998 moratorium on tariffs on digital goods.
- Multi-state extensions through 2024-2026.
- March 2026 expiry pending further extension.

## Institutional architecture
- 4 April 1949 North Atlantic Treaty signed Washington, D.C.; 12 founding members.

- Unilever HR 1992-2002.
- Dutch parliament 2003-; VVD parliamentary leader 2006-2010.
- Prime Minister of Netherlands 14 October 2010 - 2 July 2024 (longest continuous Dutch premiership in post-1945 history).
- Rutte I-IV cabinets.
- 7 July 2023 Rutte IV fall over migration policy.
- 26 June 2024 NATO Secretary General appointment announced; 1 October 2024 in office.

- 24 February 2022 Russian full-scale invasion.
- Multi-state battlegroup deployments Eastern Flank.
- 2022 Ramstein Group / Ukraine Defence Contact Group (US-Germany co-chair).
- NATO-Ukraine Council 2023.
- July 2023 Vilnius Summit (no membership invitation).
- July 2024 Washington Summit 75th anniversary ("irreversible path" language).

- NPR, "NATO's Mark Rutte: U.S. complaints valid, expects more spending by allies," 28 April 2025 (24 April Hegseth Pentagon meeting).

- Euronews, "NATO's Rutte urges more support for Ukraine and backs Trump's defence budget demand," 23 January 2025 (Davos WEF).

## Hague Summit June 2025 and 5% commitment
- 24-26 June 2025 NATO Hague Summit.
- 5% GDP defence-spending commitment by 2035: 3.5% core + 1.5% defence-related.
- March 2025 von der Leyen $841 billion ReArm Europe proposal.
- UK Starmer February 2025 pledge 2.5% of GDP (up from 2.3%, ~$17bn increase).

- PURL (Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List) launched summer 2025.
- JATEC (Joint Analysis Training and Education Centre) Poland February 2025 - first joint NATO-Ukraine centre.
- NSATU (NATO Security Assistance and Training for Ukraine) Wiesbaden command.
- Coalition of the Willing 2025 for post-war security guarantees.

## 3 December 2025 Brussels Foreign Ministers
- NATO News, "NATO Foreign Ministers reaffirm commitment to defence investment and Ukraine support," 3 December 2025 (5% commitment reaffirmation; Ukraine support counting toward target; PURL new commitments; EU HR Kaja Kallas participation; Ukrainian FM Andrii Sybiha; Russian "reckless actions" hybrid threats framework).
- 2026 Ankara Summit set as next institutional milestone.

- GlobalSecurity / NATO, "Press conference by NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the launch of the 2025 Annual Report," 26 March 2026 (Spain, Italy, Belgium, Canada reaching 2% by end of 2025; Hague Summit 5% commitment; "Trump 47" dialogue characterisation).

# Sources — Dossier 79: The European Union Institutional Architecture — Commission, Council, Parliament


## Institutional architecture
- 1957 Treaty of Rome; 1986 SEA; 1992 Maastricht; 1997 Amsterdam; 2001 Nice; 2007 Lisbon Treaty (in force 1 December 2009).
- TEU Article 13 seven institutions.
- 27 member states post-Brexit (Brexit implemented 31 January 2020).
- 20 eurozone member states (post-2023 Croatia accession).

## June 2024 European Parliament elections
- 720-seat European Parliament.
- 6-9 June 2024 elections.
- EPP 188; S&D 136; PfE 84; ECR 78; Renew 77; Greens/EFA 53; The Left 46; ESN 25; non-attached 33.
- Multi-source coverage of right-wing surge; Macron snap-election trigger; SPD setback contributing to Scholz coalition collapse.

- Wikipedia / Consilium, "EU top jobs: 2024 nomination process."
- Al Jazeera, "Von der Leyen gets nod for second EU term," 28 June 2024 (Orbán and Meloni dissent).
- JCMS Dinan article, "The Formation of the Second von der Leyen Commission," 5 August 2025.
- Package: von der Leyen (EPP/Germany), Costa (PES/Portugal), Kallas (Renew/Estonia).

## Von der Leyen biographical
- Born 8 October 1958 Ixelles Brussels; father Ernst Albrecht senior European Commission official 1958-1970 then Prime Minister of Lower Saxony 1976-1990.
- Hannover Medical School MD/PhD 1991.
- CDU 2003; Lower Saxony parliament 2003-2005; Federal Family Minister 2005-2009; Federal Labour Minister 2009-2013; Federal Defence Minister 2013-2019 (first woman).
- Bundeswehr consulting-contract scandal ~€200 million; 2018 Bundestag investigation committee.
- 2 July 2019 European Council nomination as Commission President.

## First von der Leyen Commission 2019-2024
- 1 December 2019 - 30 November 2024.
- European Green Deal launched December 2019; 55% emissions reduction by 2030; climate neutrality 2050.
- Fit for 55 legislative package 2021; CBAM 2023.
- NextGenerationEU €800 billion; first joint EU debt issuance.
- 14 sanctions packages against Russia 2022-2024.
- Ukraine Facility €50 billion 2024-2027.

## Second von der Leyen Commission
- Euronews, "Ursula von der Leyen to announce bid for second term," 19 February 2024.
- News On Air, "European Parliament approves Germany's Ursula von der Leyen's bid," 19 July 2024 (401 in favour; 284 against; 720-seat chamber).
- EEAS, "President von der Leyen presents next Commissioners," 18 July 2024 (Political Guidelines 2024-2029).
- Atlantic Council, "What to expect from Ursula von der Leyen's second term," 18 July 2024.
- European Parliament Press, "Parliament approves the 'von der Leyen II' Commission," 27 November 2024 (370 in favour, 282 against, 36 abstentions College vote).
- Wikipedia, "Von der Leyen Commission II" (formed 1 December 2024; 27 commissioners; EPP 13, PES 5, Renew/ALDE 3, ECR 1, non-aligned 5).
- Six Executive Vice-Presidents: Ribera (Spain), Virkkunen (Finland), Séjourné (France), Mînzatu (Romania), Fitto (Italy), Kallas (HR/VP Estonia).

## António Costa
- Euronews, "António Costa takes charge of European Council as Charles Michel steps down," 29 November 2024 (2.5-year term renewable once; 1 December 2024 start).
- Former Prime Minister of Portugal 2015-2024 (Volume Two earlier dossiers cross-reference).

## Other apex office-holders
- Kaja Kallas: Estonia, Renew Europe, 1 December 2024.
- Roberta Metsola: Malta, EPP, 18 January 2022 - re-elected 16 July 2024.
- Christine Lagarde: ECB President 1 November 2019 - 31 October 2027.
- Koen Lenaerts: CJEU President 8 October 2015.

## Post-2025 EU institutional response
- 4 March 2025 ReArm Europe / Readiness 2030 plan: €800 billion framework.
- SAFE (Security Action for Europe) €150 billion joint defence procurement instrument.
- National Escape Clause activation for defence spending.
- Multi-tier rebalancing measures targeting up to €95 billion US imports; first €21 billion package implemented.
- June 2022 Ukraine and Moldova candidate status; June 2024 accession negotiations open.
- December 2023 Georgia candidate status; post-2024 institutional pause.
- Western Balkans accession framework continuing (Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Kosovo, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Serbia).

# Sources — Dossier 80: The International Criminal Court and the Post-2024 US-Sanctions Confrontation


## Institutional architecture
- Rome Statute adopted 17 July 1998; entered into force 1 July 2002.
- 125 state parties as of 2026.
- Non-parties include US, China, Russia, India, Pakistan, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Israel, Türkiye, Egypt, Ethiopia.
- Article 5 jurisdiction over genocide (Article 6), crimes against humanity (Article 7), war crimes (Article 8), crime of aggression (Article 8 bis, activated July 2018).
- 18-judge bench, Office of the Prosecutor, Assembly of States Parties.
- President Tomoko Akane (Japan) since March 2024.

## Karim Khan biographical
- Born 30 March 1970 Edinburgh; British-Pakistani Ahmadiyya family.
- Edinburgh University and King's College London law degree.
- Bar 1992 Lincoln's Inn.
- Multi-decade ad-hoc tribunal practice (ICTY, ICTR, SCSL, ECCC, STL, ICC).
- UNITAD Special Adviser 2018-2021.
- Elected ICC Prosecutor 12 February 2021; in office 16 June 2021; nine-year non-renewable term.

## March 2023 Putin warrant
- 17 March 2023 ICC Pre-Trial Chamber II arrest warrants for Putin and Maria Lvova-Belova.
- War crime of unlawful deportation and transfer of children from Ukrainian territory to Russian Federation.
- Three-week issuance speed (institutional record).
- Mongolia September 2024 visit and failure to arrest.

## May 2024 Khan warrant application
- IRNA via GlobalSecurity, "ICC seeking to arrest Netanyahu, Gallant," 20 May 2024 (war crimes and crimes against humanity from at least 8 October 2023).
- News On Air, "ICC prosecutor seeks arrest warrants for Netanyahu, Hamas officials," 20 May 2024.
- Al Jazeera, "ICC arrest warrants sought for Israeli and Hamas leaders," 20 May 2024 (Netanyahu, Gallant, Sinwar, Deif, Haniyeh).

## November 2024 ICC warrants
- Al Jazeera, "Israel's Netanyahu, Gallant issued ICC arrest warrants: What's next?" 21 November 2024 (Pre-Trial Chamber I unanimous decision; "crimes against humanity and war crimes committed from at least 8 October until at least 20 May, 2024"; starvation as method of warfare; targeting of medical facilities).
- PBS News, "ICC issues arrest warrants for Netanyahu and Hamas officials alleging war crimes," 21 November 2024 (three-judge panel unanimous; reasonable grounds; Mohammed Deif warrant).
- News On Air, "International Criminal Court issues arrest warrants for Netanyahu, former Defence Minister, Hamas leader," 21 November 2024 (Israel rejected jurisdiction).
- RFE/RL, "ICC Arrest Warrant Will Dog Netanyahu Even If He Never Faces The Hague," 22 November 2024 (France support; Italy obligation; Orbán dissent; Trump alliance signal).
- Brookings, "Will the ICC issue arrest warrants for Netanyahu and Sinwar?" 9 September 2024 (March 2023 Putin warrant comparison; South Africa-Bashir 2015 and BRICS Putin 2023; Mongolia visit).
- Statista, "ICC outstanding arrest warrants" (Bashir, Putin, Netanyahu, Gallant; Sinwar/Haniyeh/Deif withdrawn after confirmed deaths).

## Hamas leaders deaths
- Haniyeh killed Tehran 31 July 2024.
- Sinwar killed Rafah 16 October 2024.
- Deif status disputed; Israel claim July 2024 airstrike kill.

## Khan personal-misconduct allegations
- Jerusalem Post, "Karim Khan issued warrant against Benjamin Netanyahu after misconduct claims" (April 2024 formal report to senior ICC officials; cancelled Israel/Gaza visit; categorical denial by Khan legal team).
- Multi-source coverage of October-November 2024 reporting on misconduct complaint.
- ICC Independent Oversight Mechanism investigation ongoing through May 2026.

## Trump second-term sanctions
- Trump first-term Executive Order 13928 September 2020 ICC sanctions.
- Biden Executive Order 14022 April 2021 revocation.
- Trump second-term Executive Order 14203 "Imposing Sanctions on the International Criminal Court" 6 February 2025 (under IEEPA; Khan asset freeze and travel restriction).
- "Illegitimate and baseless actions targeting America and our close ally Israel" reasoning.
- EU member-state (France, Germany, Netherlands) commitments to honour warrants.
- EU blocking statute framework activation.

## Cumulative ICC record
- 12 situations under investigation (DRC, Uganda, CAR, Sudan/Darfur, Kenya, Libya, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, Georgia, Burundi, Bangladesh/Myanmar, Afghanistan, Venezuela, Philippines, Ukraine, State of Palestine).
- 11 convictions including Lubanga 2012, Katanga 2014, Bemba 2016 (partially overturned 2018), Ntaganda 2019, Ongwen 2021, Said Abdel Kani 2024.
- Outstanding warrants Putin, Lvova-Belova, al-Bashir (since 2009), Netanyahu, Gallant; cumulative >20 individuals.
- Post-March 2025 Rodrigo Duterte case (Dossier 67 cross-reference).

# Sources — Dossier 81: The International Court of Justice and the Post-2023 South Africa Genocide Case


## Institutional foundation
- UN Charter Chapter XIV Articles 92-96; 1945 Statute of the International Court of Justice.
- Seat: Peace Palace, The Hague, Netherlands.
- 15 judges; nine-year renewable terms; separate concurrent votes of UN General Assembly and Security Council.
- US 1986 withdrawal of compulsory jurisdiction acceptance following Nicaragua v. United States judgment.

## President Salam transition
- ICJ Press Release 2025/3: "Judge Nawaf Salam, President of the Court, resigns as Member of the Court with effect from 14 January 2025."
- Salam elected ICJ President 6 February 2024 (first Arab President).
- Appointed Prime Minister of Lebanon January 2025 following November 2024 Lebanese presidential election.

## 19 July 2024 Wall Advisory Opinion
- UN General Assembly Resolution 77/247 (30 December 2022).
- Public hearings February 2024 with 50+ state submissions.
- 19 July 2024 advisory opinion findings on Israeli OPT occupation unlawfulness, settlement cessation, reparations.

## 29 December 2023 South Africa application
- South Africa Application Instituting Proceedings, 29 December 2023.
- Wikipedia, "South Africa's genocide case against Israel" (84-page application; Vusi Madonsela, Adila Hassim SC, Tembeka Ngcukaitobi SC, John Dugard SC, Vaughan Lowe KC, Max du Plessis SC legal team).

## 11-12 January 2024 provisional measures hearings
- ICJ Transcripts (CR 2024/1 and CR 2024/2): South Africa and Israel oral submissions.
- Documentary record cited: Gallant 9 October 2023 "complete siege"; Herzog 12 October 2023 "entire nation"; Netanyahu Amalek invocation.

## 26 January 2024 provisional measures order
- ICJ Summary 2024/1 (26 January 2024).
- Six provisional measures binding on Israel; majority votes 15-2 to 16-1.
- Dissenting: Judge Sebutinde (Uganda); Judge ad hoc Aharon Barak (Israel).

## Subsequent provisional measures orders
- 16 February 2024 Court declined additional measures.
- 28 March 2024 second additional provisional measures order (15-2): food supplies; humanitarian assistance.
- 24 May 2024 third provisional measures order (13-2): Rafah ground operation halt; crossing maintenance.

## South African memorial and Israeli counter-memorial
- 28 October 2024: South Africa filed memorial (750+ pages text; 4,000+ pages exhibits).
- 5 April 2024 Court order setting Israeli counter-memorial deadline at 28 July 2025.
- April 2025: Israel requested six-month extension; ICJ granted five-and-a-half month extension to 12 January 2026.
- 12 January 2026: Israeli counter-memorial filed.
- Wikipedia, "South Africa's genocide case against Israel" (extensions; 12 March 2026 alternate date in some sources).
- Middle East Eye, "Israel's genocide in Gaza: Whatever happened to South Africa's case at the ICJ?" 17 October 2025 (Mike Becker Trinity College Dublin analysis).

## Post-2024 intervention wave
- ICJ Press Releases enumerating Article 63 declarations of intervention.
- Interventions filed: Nicaragua (May 2024), Colombia (April 2024), Libya (June 2024), Mexico (May 2024), Palestine (June 2024), Spain (June 2024), Turkey (August 2024), Chile (September 2024), Maldives (October 2024), Bolivia (November 2024), Ireland (December 2024), Brazil (March 2025), Belize (June 2025), Belgium (2025), Paraguay (2026).
- Multi-source coverage of unprecedented multi-state intervention scale.

## Gambia v. Myanmar precedent
- 2019 Gambia v. Myanmar case under Genocide Convention.
- January 2020 provisional measures order.
- Public hearings on merits held 12-29 January 2026 (per ICJ press release).

## US post-2024 institutional posture
- Trump February 2025 Executive Order 14199 on UN bodies (UNHRC, UNRWA; ICJ not addressed).
- State Department Blinken (until January 2025) "meritless" characterisation; Rubio post-January 2025 continuity.
- US has not filed intervention or submitted formal observations.

## Other ICJ cases of period
- Ukraine v. Russian Federation (Allegations of Genocide under the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide).
- Canada, Sweden, Ukraine, UK v. Iran (Aerial Incident of 8 January 2020).


---

# Sources — Dossier 82: The World Health Organization and the Post-2025 US Withdrawal


## Institutional foundation
- WHO Constitution adopted 22 July 1946; entered into force 7 April 1948 (World Health Day).
- Headquarters: Geneva, Switzerland; six regional offices (Africa, Americas, South-East Asia, Europe, Eastern Mediterranean, Western Pacific).
- 194 member states.
- World Health Assembly (supreme body); Executive Board (34-member); Director-General (five-year renewable).

## Director-General Tedros
- 23 May 2017 first election (defeated David Nabarro UK 133-50 in final round).
- 24 May 2022 unopposed re-election; term to mid-2027.
- Former Ethiopian Minister of Health (2005-2012); Minister of Foreign Affairs (2012-2016).

## COVID-19 sequence
- 31 December 2019 WHO China Country Office informed of Wuhan pneumonia cluster.
- 30 January 2020 PHEIC declaration.
- 11 March 2020 pandemic characterisation.
- 14 April 2020 Trump suspension of US funding.
- 18 May 2020 Trump letter to Tedros (per H.R. 4207 119th Congress findings).
- 7 July 2020 US withdrawal notification effective 6 July 2021.
- 25 January 2021 Biden retraction.

## 20 January 2025 Executive Order 14155
- Executive Order 14155 "Withdrawing the United States From the World Health Organization" 20 January 2025; published Federal Register.
- govinfo.gov, Executive Order 14155 text.
- Global News, "Trump orders U.S. out of World Health Organization," 21 January 2025.
- Withdrawal effective 22 January 2026 under one-year notice.
- Operational provisions: pause funds; recall personnel; identify alternative partners; review 2024 Global Health Security Strategy; cease Pandemic Agreement and IHR amendment negotiations.
- H.R. 4207 119th Congress (2025-2026): "No WHO Pandemic Preparedness Treaty Without Senate Approval Act" (timeline of 2020-2025 US-WHO interactions).

## 20 May 2025 Pandemic Agreement
- AJMC, "WHO Members Draft Future Pandemic Agreement" (16 April 2025 INB conclusion).
- Al Jazeera, "WHO members adopt landmark pandemic agreement in US absence," 20 May 2025.
- NPR, "The world now has its first ever pandemic treaty. Will it make a difference?" 20 May 2025 (no country opposed; 124 in favour, 11 abstentions, 0 opposed).
- STAT News, "With U.S. absent, WHO adopts pandemic treaty aimed at improving vaccine access," 20 May 2025.
- WHO Press Release 20 May 2025; Tedros statement.
- December 2021 WHA Special Session establishing Intergovernmental Negotiating Body (INB).

## PABS annex post-2025
- US News via Reuters, "WHO Chief 'Not Worried' About US Bilateral Deals Affecting Pandemic Treaty," 11 December 2025.
- US News via Reuters, "WHO Delays Pandemic Treaty Amid Pathogen-Sharing Dispute," 1 May 2026 (PABS extension; submission expected 2027 WHA or 2026 special session).

## Post-2025 budget crisis
- NPR, "For the first time, the U.S. is absent from WHO's annual assembly. What's the impact?" 19 May 2025 ($600 million 2024-2025 shortfall; ~20% reduction in proposed 2026-2027 budget; ~half of leadership team positions eliminated).
- Multi-source coverage of EU, Germany, Japan, China, UK increased pledges.
- December 2025 Tedros statement on confidence in $1 billion budget cut backfill.
- Tedros December 2025 childhood-mortality concern.

## US post-2025 bilateral health frameworks
- HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. post-2025 institutional posture.
- Multi-source coverage of bilateral US-African state health-cooperation arrangements.

## Largest historical donors
- US: largest historical contributor across post-1948 period.
- Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation: second-largest contributor (post-2010s); most funding earmarked for polio eradication.
